We also show significant changes in MCE communities subsequent to biological invasions (i.e., lionfish), based on temporal sampling. Many of our findings have been generally supported by other investigators in the Caribbean, Red Sea, and Indo-Pacific. There are also important and interesting exceptions in MCE structure and function largely driven by differences in oceanography, or geomorphology and downwelling irradiances, that help inform us on the variability observed on contemporary tropical MCEs. Finally, using the available data, and some hand waving, we will take a large scale, island-wide, perspective on the importance of sponges on the trophic ecology of MCEs from the Cayman Islands, and by extension wherever sponges are a dominant component of marine benthic communities.
4049df61:
The coral reefs of the insular shelves and surrounding slopes of the US Virgin Islands have a complex biophysical environment. The water column contains seasonally strong thermoclines and pycnoclines at mesophotic depths (30-150 meters), leading to a strong gradient of physical properties with implications of coral reef development and response to disturbance. We used benthic temperature records of 43 sites, a thermistor string deployment along the Grammanik shelf slope, and a 200 m TWR Slocum glider to investigate the variability across years, seasons, depth, and their potential oceanic drivers. Temperature records were collected by the Territorial Coral Reef Monitoring Program covering over 20 years. The glider, UVI02, was deployed by the University of the Virgin Islands Ocean Glider Lab in November 2023-24 and February 2024, collecting measurements offshore of the St. Thomas shelf near the Grammanik Bank.
Across benthic coral reef sites at a range of depths from 6m to 100m, mean temperatures were found to be increasing steadily, based on continuous measurements. Annually, temperatures generally showed warm peaks from September to November and cold peaks from January to March. Higher frequency temperature variability was strongest at depths near or below the seasonal mixed layer depth, with a strong semidiurnal frequency peak. Glider profiles just offshore of the Grammanik Bank mesophotic coral reef found strong semidiurnal internal waves causing periodic temperature variability below the mixed layer of up to ± 2 degree Celsius and ± 2 Salinity units. Glider profiles also revealed chlorophyll maximum and dissolved oxygen minimum layers at mesophotic depths around 50m. Evidence of these internal tides are seen at varying amplitudes at regional mesophotic reef sites and can provide cooling during warming periods and may help alleviate thermal stress.
8113e5e0: Mesophotic to deep-sea ecosystems off western Sumatra remain poorly understood, despite their ecological significance and potential vulnerability to anthropogenic pressures. This study investigates benthic communities across depths of 60 to 5000 meters using Underwater Visual Census (UVC) via remotely operated vehicles (ROV), during the OceanX expedition in May-July 2024. The research provides critical baseline data for informing a Large-Scale Marine Protected Area (LSMPA) design and mesophotic-deep sea conservation strategies in Indonesia. Analysis of ROV footage revealed benthic habitats dominated by flat, silty, and muddy substrates with limited structural complexity. Nevertheless, multiple faunal groups exhibited notable abundance and distribution patterns. High-density sites were dominated not only by holothurians (sea cucumbers), Malacostraca (shrimps and crabs), Asteroidea (sea stars), Echinoidea (sea urchins), but also by mesophotic anthozoans, and octocorals, which function as key habitat-forming taxa. Their presence, particularly soft corals and sea fans highlights the ecological importance of coral-associated assemblages even in deeper low-light environments, reinforcing connectivity between shallow, mesophotic, and deep-sea coral ecosystems. Preliminary findings also highlight anthropogenic threats, with plastic debris recorded at depths of 5000 meters. This study provides the first comprehensive characterization of mesophotic to deep-sea benthic communities in western Sumatra and identifies key ecological hotspots. These results offer critical scientific guidance for the design of the proposed Large-Scale Marine Protected Area, supporting the Government of Indonesia in achieving its "MPA Vision 30x45" to protect 30% of the nation’s waters by 2045.
bcbbfb27:
Mesophotic and rariphotic zones remain among the least explored marine environments due to the logistical and technological challenges of operating at depth. Advances in imaging have expanded our ability to study these ecosystems, but the resulting volume of video data has created a bottleneck for manual annotation. This study addresses that challenge by developing instance-segmentation deep learning models and applying them to high-resolution ROV video collected during the 2023 NEOM Deep Habitat Characterization Expedition in the Saudi Arabian Gulf of Aqaba. Sixteen standardized transects were analyzed across two depth zones, 70–90 m and 110–130 m, to detect and quantify coral groups along a 70-km stretch of the coast.

We trained four YOLOv11 instance-segmentation models targeting zooxanthellate scleractinians, octocorals, antipatharians, and whip corals, with precision scores of 79%, 62%, 60%, and 63%. The models were applied to all transects to extract abundance and density metrics across depth ranges and habitat types, revealing clear depth- and habitat-associated differences in community composition.

Zooxanthellate scleractinians dominated the lower mesophotic zone, reaching densities above 6 colonies m⁻², but declined by ~90% in the rariphotic zone. Antipatharians increased with depth, reaching mean densities near 1 colony m⁻², around 40% higher than in the lower mesophotic zone. Whip corals showed relatively stable densities across depths. Differences in community composition were significant (PERMANOVA, p = 0.001), and SIMPER analyses identified depth- and habitat-specific drivers of dissimilarity. Hotspot analysis revealed distinct clusters of high-density areas, while generalized linear mixed models indicated temperature and slope as key predictors of distribution.

This semi-automated workflow enabled rapid, standardized assessments of coral groups and represents the first application of instance-segmentation deep learning in the deeper reefs of the Gulf of Aqaba. The approach highlights the growing role of AI in benthic monitoring and provides a scalable tool for conservation planning.
4ff09643: Mesophotic coral reefs are renowned for their unique biodiversity and ecological significance. They have long been proposed to supplement shallow reefs and serve as refugia for shallow-water species in the face of ocean warming. Nevertheless, these deep reefs are still severely understudied compared to shallow reefs, mainly due to the technical challenges inherent to deep water environments. Here we utilized environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding to provide high-resolution spatial biodiversity profiling across the steep depth gradient in the Gulf of Aqaba (GOA) – spanning from shallow reefs to mesophotic depths and beyond. Utilizing a general metazoan COI marker, the present analysis generated over 6,782 unique amplicon sequences, accounting for over 1,500 metazoan species spanning 18 different phyla. In total, more than 130 metazoan species were validated based on available sequence references. We show that community structure differs significantly across depth, particularly between shallow reef and lower mesophotic. Depth proved to be more influential than site in shaping the respective community structures, regardless of the statutory status and protection level of the sites. Our results thus provide quantitative evidence to support current mesophotic classification but show little support to effective species overlap between deep and shallow communities. This study highlights the potential of eDNA metabarcoding as a practical tool for mesophotic reefs and deep-sea monitoring.
7fb4da4f:
Climate change is driving shifts in species distributions, which are expected to have substantial impacts on both ecological systems and human societies. Along the coat of Japan, where the warm Kuroshio Current exerts strong influence, while subtropical reefs are experiencing mass coral bleaching due to marine heatwaves, at temperate regions, seaweed beds are undergoing dramatic transitions toward tropical coral-dominated communities. These community shifts are triggering shifts in coastal marine ecosystems and are beginning to generate socio-economic consequences and conflicts among stakeholders, including fisheries and tourism sectors.
To address these emerging challenges, we are conducting an integrative study aimed at predicting and assessing the potential future ecological range shifts of marine organisms along the Japanese coastline, as well as evaluating the associated socio-ecological consequences under ongoing climate change. 
In this presentation, we will introduce several of our approaches and ongoing research, and discuss about their implementation and potential comparisons with other regions.
b468608e: Understanding the biogeography of living marine resources is essential for informed resource management, particularly in data-limited environments. Here, we demonstrate how ecological niche models, paired with open-access environmental data, can enhance the utility of existing survey programs and support monitoring of species listed under the U.S. Endangered Species Act (ESA). We developed ensemble models for two ESA-listed Indo-Pacific corals, Acropora globiceps and Isopora crateriformis using a maximum entropy (MaxEnt) framework and multi-source occurrence records collected around Tutuila, American Samoa. Environmental predictors were derived from temporally summarized, high-resolution, open-source satellite and other gridded datasets capturing heat stress, turbidity, and seafloor complexity. The models revealed distinct ecological preferences: A. globiceps was associated with intermediate depths and structural complexity, and sites with longer recovery intervals since major thermal events, while I. crateriformis showed broader environmental tolerance but greater sensitivity to recent heat stress. To evaluate real-world applicability, we designed a retrospective forecasting experiment, training models on data through 2024 and testing predictions against independent 2025 observations. Ensemble models achieved reliable discrimination (AUC = 0.71-0.74), with true skill statistics of 0.47 for A. globiceps and 0.28 for I. crateriformis, indicating useful forecast skill for both species under independent conditions. This approach provides a scalable, reproducible framework for forecasting habitat suitability, guiding targeted monitoring, and informing adaptive management of ESA-listed corals under changing reef conditions.
738c3a32:
Sydney is a biogeographic transition zone where temperate and subtropical corals overlap, providing a unique natural system for investigating their range shifts in response to environmental changes. Two coral species: Pocillopora aliciae and Plesiastrea versipora, present a range overlap but diverging responses: P. aliciae, that approaches its southern range limit, exhibits metabolic flexibility under short-term warming, while P. versipora, near its northern limit, shows limited physiological adjustment. Although P. versipora is a stronger competitor under current temperatures, this advantage disappears under ocean warming scenarios. These shifts in species-specific and community interactions are likely to affect the composition of Sydney’s transitional waters, helping forecast future community trajectories and prioritise sites for targeted monitoring. Yet, the precise locations and in situ environmental contexts of current coral communities remain unknown, limiting our capacity to predict how these species will shift under changing conditions.

Here, we combine in situ surveys, citizen-science observations, and environmental datasets to produce the first fine-scale distribution map of these species across Sydney’s Northern Beaches. At each site, coral presence, relative abundance (colonies m⁻²), and colony size were quantified and paired with a comprehensive suite of environmental data, including organic and inorganic nutrient content, turbidity, and chlorophyll a content. We assessed benthic community composition and neighbouring organism cover from photo-quadrats to evaluate potential cross-kingdom competitive influences on coral persistence. Together, these datasets informed species-specific nutrient-landscape models within a species distribution modelling framework, identifying coral “hotspots” and currently unoccupied environmentally suitable habitats.

This is the first study in Sydney’s biogeographic transition zone to explicitly link coral occurrence with ecological and nutrient measurements. Not only the resulting habitat-suitability maps provide practical guidance for evidence-based Marine Park Area planning, but improved understanding of species resilience at thermal margins, offering new insights into how nutritional ecology shapes coral range dynamics in a warming ocean.
c8810f5e: Climate change is forcing scientists and natural resource managers to contend with species range shifts and altered species interactions. These range shifts are difficult to detect and can have unexpected consequences, especially when range expanding predators feed on resident species. On tropical western Pacific coral reefs, outbreaks of the coral-eating crown-of-thorns starfish, Acanthaster cf. solaris, cause widespread coral loss. Here we document the poleward range expansion of its close relative Acanthaster brevispinus, often a non-coral-eating species, to the subtropics of eastern Australia (Solitary Islands Marine Park, 30°S). In this region, corals grow directly on rock and persist in marginal conditions at their poleward range limits. We recorded the first mixed species outbreak of A. brevispinus and A. cf. solaris (91 and 39 starfish, respectively) where both species preyed on a broad diet of primarily endemic and high-latitude corals. The unusually broad diet of the two predator species compounded the detrimental effects on vulnerable high-latitude coral assemblages, which host many long-lived and slow-growing coral species that will be slow to recover (e.g., Paragoniastrea australensis, Micromussa lordhowensis, Astrea curta). Both crown-of-thorns species also preyed on other cnidarian species including zoanthids and corallimorpharians abundant on subtropical reefs. The broad diet of the predatory starfish has potential cascading effects on benthic dynamics and associated biodiversity in this region where tropical, subtropical and temperate species overlap. We show that collaborative efforts across marine resource managers, researchers, dive operators, and citizen scientists facilitated adaptive management that led to the control of the outbreak to protect vulnerable high-latitude coral assemblages. This highlights the success of cross-jurisdictional, cooperative management in adapting to the challenge of species redistribution under climate change.
6ad52803: The tight nutritional recycling between corals and their photosynthetic intracellular algae is among the most successful and prolific associations to have evolved. Yet the warm, oligotrophic, stable, conditions that favoured this association is shifting. In the tropics, temperature anomalies that exceed a corals’ thermal maxima have become the norm, while at poleward range edges thermally suitable habitats for corals appear to be extending, with cases of coral expansions into higher latitudes. Poleward range edges present unique challenges to the coral symbiosis as low temperatures and higher turbidity can limit photosynthesis. Here we consider how the reliance of corals on their algal symbionts restricts and/or enhances their latitudinal ranges. We generated thermal performance curves of physiological data from three coral genera and their associated symbionts across four locations in the Northwest Pacific. These data indicate that acclimation/adaptation to thermal extremes experienced at high latitudes may be limited by symbiont physiology, but that symbiotic associations show flexibility across the range. Furthermore, we present stable isotope data from a subset of locations to examine the role of trophic plasticity, the ability of corals to shift away from their reliance on symbiont derived nutrients, in understanding the limits on current and ongoing coral range expansion.
5b61bd91: As oceans continue to warm, the future distribution of reef-building corals will depend on whether species can physiologically acclimate, mount sustainable stress responses, and persist at both trailing and leading edges of their ranges. Identifying such capacities could benefit from non-invasive biomarkers including those capable of distinguishing reversible thermal responses from impending collapse. Here, we combined a relative thermal-tolerance experimental design with paired tissue and environmental RNA (eRNA) transcriptomics to compare the heat-stress responses of two niche-sharing Japanese Acropora corals: A. solitaryensis (AS), a widely distributed, warm-affiliated species, and a range-restricted temperate-adapted A. pruinosa (AP), under physiologically relevant heat-stress gradient. AS adopted a high-cost fight strategy. Under moderate warming, its tissues activated a broad, energy-intensive transcriptional response, accompanied by an inverse tissue-eRNA correlation, suggesting active counter-regulatory signaling to maintain homeostasis. However, this strategy proved unsustainable at higher temperatures: under severe stress, AS exhibited a potential transcriptional collapse, characterized by a shift to strong positive tissue, eRNA correlation and a significant reduction in differentially expressed genes, consistent with passive molecular leakage of RNA and likely systemic failure. In contrast, AP employed an efficient metabolic suppression strategy. Rather than activating energy intensive regulatory programs, AP initiated a coordinated early down-regulation of metabolic pathways, evidenced by high and consistent tissue-eRNA correlation and a dominance of downregulated genes indicating a deliberate, reversible biological hold. During cooling however, AP showed synchronized gene activity between tissues and eRNA and mounted a strong recovery, confirming that this shutdown was an adaptive, not pathological, response. Although both species targeted similar functional categories, none of the specific genes overlapped, revealing distinct genetic toolkits underlying each strategy. Collectively, our results validate eRNA as a sensitive, non-invasive biomarker of species-specific acclimation modes and provide mechanistic insight into the differential range-shift potential of threatened Acropora corals.
7e5ad80a: During the 4th Global Coral Bleaching Event (GCBE4; January 2023–September 2025), an unprecedented marine heatwave occurred on the Bocas del Toro Reef Complex (BTRC) in Panama. We characterized how this heatwave impacted the health and holobiont communities of the stress-tolerant coral Siderastrea siderea at four sites across the BTRC. Tagged colonies at each site (N = 30–53 colonies per site) were visited before, during, and after the heatwave (early May 2022, mid-August 2023, and late April 2024, respectively) for the collection of images and DNA samples. In situ temperature logger data showed that sites reached maxima of 32.1–33.9°C in October 2023, resulting in the accumulation of ~12–20 maximum °C-heating weeks (DHWs). Over this period, S. siderea colonies displayed widespread bleaching (i.e., the loss of algal endosymbionts), with an increase from 8.6–33% of colonies bleached per site in May 2022 to 33–70% in August 2023, followed by a decline to 15–63% by April 2024. Colony-level partial mortality increased significantly between 2022–2024 at three of the four sites, and was observed even in colonies that were not bleached in August 2023. At three sites that experienced > 12 DHWs during the heatwave, corals hosting Cladocopium spp. algal symbionts in 2022 shifted towards less diverse communities dominated by heat-tolerant Breviolum and Durusdinium spp., and corals maintained these modified symbiont communities for months. The heatwave also reshaped corals’ bacterial microbiomes, including increases in α-diversity and abundances of pathogenic taxa (e.g., Vibrionaceae), and these shifts were similarly persistent following the heatwave. Together, these findings demonstrate that GCBE4 had lasting impacts on S. siderea holobiont health across the BTRC, underscoring that record-breaking heat can compromise even stress-tolerant coral species and induce legacy effects that will likely affect future resilience. Rapid action to minimize further ocean warming is thus necessary to safeguard reef ecosystems.
0eed358b: Coral reefs face significant threats across the globe, prompting a surge in restoration efforts aimed at mitigating their global decline. The health, resilience, and adaptability of corals are greatly influenced by their microbial communities, and while the response of coral microbiomes to many environmental stressors has been extensively studied, less is known about their natural dynamics following transplantation, which is an essential process for restoring degraded reef habitats. In this study, we integrated DNA metabarcoding (16S & ITS2) with ecological monitoring to investigate the dynamics of Symbiodiniaceae and bacterial communities in two dominant and closely related coral spp., Porites lobata and Porites cylindrica, and their different color morphs, as they underwent transplantation and an 18-week acclimatization period. We saw significant differences in microbial communities between the two Porites spp., outplanting sites, and individual coral colonies, as well as a colour morph-related difference in P. lobata bacterial communities. We saw reduced relative abundances of Endozoicomonadaceae, specifically from the genus Parendozoicomonas, following transplantation. P. lobata colonies with later Symbiodiniaceae shifts (18 weeks) had lower long-term survival. Changes in Symbiodiniaceae and bacterial communities have implications for holobiont function and colony survival, which should be considered when designing and implementing coral reef rehabilitation projects.
05a588be: Coral reefs support over a quarter of marine species and nearly a billion people worldwide but are also among the ecosystems most threatened by anthropogenic impacts. There is long-standing debate about whether coral symbioses will be disrupted or respond adaptively under future ocean conditions. Using a factorial 2.5-year future-ocean mesocosm experiment across eight coral species representing the major coral lineages, we tracked symbiont community shifts within replicate fragments from the same individual coral. Some corals exhibited stochastic divergence consistent with dysbiosis, whereas others showed deterministic, thermally adaptive shifts. Heat stress generally reduced symbiont diversity and promoted predictable restructuring, supporting deterministic processes under moderate stress but stochastic dysbiosis under extreme conditions. We propose that adaptive and stochastic responses represent endpoints along a continuum of host-orchestrated symbiont sorting. Our study bridges coral reef ecology with broader host–microbiome theory, offering an integrated perspective on how symbiotic systems may respond to environmental change.
6699f614: Scleractinian corals are highly complex organisms with a large host of microbial symbiotes both within and without their tissue. While much research has been done regarding specific key symbiotic relationships, most notably Symbiodiniaceae, other components of the holobiont, such as viruses, remain understudied. Viral control over microbial communities is well documented, identifying them as key players in holobiont interactions; however, due to the historic difficulty of studying these biological agents, much remains to be understood regarding the coral virome. This includes aspects such as fundamental insights into virome members as well as responses and alterations of virome community composition due to stress events. By taking advantage of a natural heat stress event triggered by the El Niño, we utilized metagenomics on samples of Pocillopora verrucosa and Pocillopora grandis collected from monitored reef sites in Panama to identify virome composition and abundance for these two species. Diversity analysis then identified significant alterations in the virome community due to factors such as heat stress, as well as key differences in virome composition between corals with greater and lesser degrees of heat stress tolerance. Our study provides insight into the largely uncharted coral viral communities and highlights key virome members that may potentially influence coral resilience.
81c838a0: Invasive organisms can negatively affect marine ecosystem structure and function, particularly native species. The impacts as well as the associated underlying mechanisms affecting native species often remain unclear. This study assessed the effects of the invasive red macroalga, Gracilaria salicornia, to the physiology of the scleractinian coral, Porites compressa, in Kāne‘ohe Bay, Hawai‘i. Paired studies showed that coral colonies did not die when covered by the algae for as long as seven weeks. However, corals were visually bleached within two weeks of being overgrown by algal mats. In response to shading, chlorophyll a, c2 and total carotenoids increased in remaining zooxanthellae. There was no evidence of oxidative stress or anaerobic respiration despite diurnally fluctuating hypoxic and hyperoxic conditions. When management intervention was simulated by removal of algal mats, pigment quantity recovered in coral tissues after three weeks; zooxanthellae density recovered within five weeks. Our results suggest that G. salicornia mats imposed substantial physiological stresses on the symbiosis and photosynthetic capacity of the coral Porites compressa and that recovery can be detected within three weeks if the alga is removed. Further, we demonstrated that the removal of mats is an effective management action leading to recovery from short-term stress. Thus, short-term management efforts that include removal of algal biomass are likely to enhance restoration success on reefs in Kāne‘ohe Bay and elsewhere.
7f30e734:
Agrochemical pollution is increasingly impacting coastal marine ecosystems through continuous estuarine discharge and episodic surface runoff. Among these pollutants, legacy pesticides pose a substantial threat to marine communities due to their persistence in both sediments and seawater. Atrazine, a very persistent herbicide (DT50 > 365 days) that inhibits the photosystem II in phototrophs, remains one of the most commonly detected unapproved herbicides in marine environments. As such, atrazine also compromises the health and resilience of photosymbiotic metazoans by disrupting their symbiosis with phototrophs.
Despite these risks, the effects of atrazine on host-microbe interactions, including alterations in the gene expression of both partners, host development, and photosymbiotic function, remain poorly understood. To address this knowledge gap, we utilize photosymbiotic model organisms (Exaiptasia diaphana), which live in symbiosis with photosynthetic dinoflagellates (including Symbiodiniaceae), to investigate the ecotoxicological effects of atrazine under controlled laboratory conditions. Under a range of atrazine concentrations (0.054 – 100 µg/L), which include environmentally relevant, EU Environmental Quality Standards, and experimental concentrations, we capture host and symbiont responses to short-term (7 days) and long-term (60 days) atrazine exposure. Holobiont effects of atrazine exposure are assessed by analyzing the microbial community using 16S rRNA metabarcoding, host and symbiont responses through gene expression analyses, and monitoring the photophysiology and development.
Our study provides insight into the effects of various relevant concentrations of atrazine on host-symbiont interactions under short (7 days) - and long-term (60 days) exposure, potentially informing ex-situ management strategies and microbiome-informed conservation and restoration efforts for vulnerable coastal coral reef habitats.
0b6c3e69: Some coral species have highly flexible algal symbiont communities (Family Symbiodiniaceae) that are known to vary across colonies and over time as a phenotypic response to their location, depth, colony morphology, and light exposure. These varying communities can affect the coral’s susceptibility to thermal stress and disease. This study triannually mapped intra-colony mosaics of Symbiodiniaceae taxa in the threatened Caribbean coral species Orbicella faveolata between 2022 and 2025 to investigate associations between and bleaching and disease on colonies >2 m diameter in southeast Florida. Five repeatable transects were set up on seven colonies in a radial pattern, where thirty-one biopsies were taken per colony at nine timepoints. Quantitative PCR results showed the four northernmost colonies dominated solely by Breviolum, a central colony dominated by Durusdinium, and two southernmost colonies with a mixture of Breviolum and Cladocopium. Symbiont to coral host cell ratios (s:h) temporally varied within and between colonies, with the lowest corresponding to a severe bleaching event in 2023. Five colonies exhibited active SCTLD lesions. The two colonies without recorded lesions had the lowest proportions of Breviolum. Intra-colony spatial analyses of colonies with variable symbiont communities showed significant clustering of genera that varied temporally. One colony varied seasonally and shifted s:h distributions during a disease event. Although temporally variable, the symbiont communities in the majority of colonies remained consistent except for one colony that shifted, post bleaching event, to be similar to other Breviolum-dominant colonies. This work illustrates the spatiotemporal dynamic nature of large corals with flexible algal symbiont communities. High spatiotemporal resolution symbiont community mapping provides a better understanding of the associations between algal symbiont communities and the inter and intra-colony variable responses to bleaching and disease. Further associations to shading and light exposure are currently being investigated using 3D SfM modeling.
d4ed07a0: Are the mesopredatory sharks that reside on coral reefs functionally equivalent to large teleost predators, or do they provide unique ecological roles? To answer this question, I synthesize evidence across feeding mechanics, diet and trophic ecology, demographic responses of prey, non-consumptive “fear” pathways, and cross-habitat coupling. Sharks and teleosts differ fundamentally in morphology and attack mode, yielding distinct prey-size envelopes and foraging tactics that can limit simple substitution by teleosts. Diet studies show frequent trophic overlap with predatory teleosts but also coupling between the reef and pelagic environments that is context dependent and notable individual specialization. Natural experiments and long-term contrasts reveal cases of mesopredator release and cascades mediated by behaviour following shark depletion, alongside systems where community structure appears insensitive to shark abundance with such contrasts largely explained by different spatial–temporal grains of studies, predator movement kernels, and multi-predator context. Building on recent frameworks, I identify three pathways: consumptive, risk, and coupling and specify minimal data and interpretation rules to judge substitutability versus buffering (response diversity within sharks) versus uniqueness for the role of reef sharks. I conclude that substitutability is most plausible for local consumptive effects at patch-to-reefscape grains, where prey overlap of sharks and teleosts is high; unique roles for sharks are most likely for cross-habitat coupling and broad, roving risk landscapes that teleosts seldom replicate. We highlight taxonomic and methodological gaps (especially for small resident sharks), and outline the priorities of grain-matched designs, integrated diet/movement proxies, and long-term monitoring to resolve where, when, and how sharks matter most to coral reef resilience.
ff36121e: Mesopredators play a vital role in marine food webs and are an important link between trophic levels. On coral reefs, small carnivorous fish are important mesopredators and exert strong, top down pressure regulating prey communities. However, despite this importance, few studies have directly examined the foraging ecology of small, coral reef meso-predators. To better understand this, we examined the foraging behaviour of Pseudochromis fuscus (the dusky dottyback) over three ecologically important temporal periods (the full moon, coral spawning, and the new moon) at Lizard Island on the Great Barrier Reef, Australia. We conducted a total of 56 one hour observations, each focusing on the number of strikes and successful predation attempts directed at coral reef fish recruits and invertebrates. Contrary to previous research, we did not observe a successful capture of a coral reef fish recruit, despite P. fuscus displaying a similar strike rate to previously published research. We observed that  strikes directed at invertebrates were common, and consistent across all three temporal periods, and conclude that invertebrates therefore constitute a stable food source. Interestingly, there were significantly fewer attempted strikes on coral reef fish recruits during the coral spawning period, likely due to the influx of lipid-rich food. We also observed little difference in foraging behaviour between P. fuscus colour morphs, sizes, or at different times of day. These findings reveal that the foraging behaviour of P. fuscus can be highly variable temporally and may play less of a role in the mortality rates of newly, settled coral reef fish than previously thought. Quantifying these interactions is crucial for understanding the ecological interactions of coral reef ecosystems, especially in light of significant environmental change.
03678681: Tropical coral reefs support some of the highest diversities of herbivores, and prevailing theories of competition and coexistence suggest that niche partitioning has promoted their diversification and long-term coexistence. Herbivorous fishes exert a strong top-down impact on algae, which suggests that food may be a limiting resource for these species. Moreover, herbivory provides an important ecosystem function by promoting coral reef resilience against macroalgal overgrowth. However, dietary partitioning among herbivores remains poorly defined due to the difficulty of differentiating the taxonomic identity of the food items they consume. This information is especially needed now—a time when habitat destruction is increasing and the ecological roles of herbivores remain poorly understood in the context of changing reef conditions (e.g., shifts in the abundance of corals and algae). Fortuitously, recent developments in DNA metabarcoding allow us to better discern the taxonomic identity of food items. In this study we (1) define the trophic niches of the primary herbivorous species across an algal-dominated and coral-dominated reef in Moorea, French Polynesia by using a combination of behavioral feeding observations and DNA metabarcoding, and (2) determine how the trophic niche of each species shifts with changing reef conditions. Preliminary results from behavioral data show that, in general, there is interspecific dietary portioning on coral dominated reefs, but diets shift to incorporate more turf algae and macroalgae on algal-dominated reefs, suggesting that dietary niches may converge as macroalgae increases. More specifically, diets of species that feed primarily on high-relief, rocky substrate, such as Chlorurus spilurus, shift more than those of species that feed mostly on sand and rubble, such as Scarus psittacus. We anticipate that our DNA results will reveal further taxonomic differentiation among species and individuals which will help elucidate the true degree of niche portioning versus convergence on algal-dominated reefs.
2334c0a7: The queen snapper, Etelis oculatus, is experiencing a growing fishery presence across the Caribbean, yet our understanding of its distribution, and ecology remains limited. We investigated the feeding ecology of this demersal deep-water snapper using two molecular approaches: single-species barcoding and multispecies metabarcoding. Between November 2019 and July 2020, we collected 157 queen snapper from seven locations along Puerto Rico. Our analysis identified a diverse array of prey items in the stomachs, comprising 61 species from 31 families, 18 orders, and 38 genera. Our findings suggest that E. oculatus is a large carnivore, primarily preying on squids, shrimps, and deep-water fishes. Notably, common fish prey included Diaphus brachycephalus, D. dumerilii, Dasyscopelus selenops, Coccorella atlantica, Sigmops elongatus, and Zaphotias pedaliotus. In contrast, prevalent invertebrate prey consisted of Abralia veranyi, Doryteuthis pealeii, Abralia redfieldi, Oplophorus gracilirostris, and Systellaspis debilis. Although our data suggest potential variation in diet composition across locations, definitive conclusions remain elusive. However, we observed heightened prey richness at seamount Pichincho, possibly attributable to the high structural complexity of the karst terrain on the seafloor. Additionally, we noted variation in prey species composition across size classes, with certain species more prevalent in larger or smaller queen snapper. Given the challenge of examining the prey of deep-water fish species, our study showcases the utility of both single-species barcoding and multispecies metabarcoding methodologies in characterizing the dietary range of queen snapper and similar demersal deep-water carnivorous fishes. The multispecies metabarcoding approach, in particular, offers a rapid, comprehensive, and effective means of identifying prey species. As the commercial value of the queen snapper continues to rise, our investigation into its feeding behavior provides critical baseline information for future species management efforts. By enhancing our understanding of its ecological dynamics, we aim to contribute to informed conservation and sustainable fisheries practices in the region.
c14f4c40: Coral reefs are highly diverse and productive ecosystems, primarily sustained by the efficient recycling of nutrients. Fish play a fundamental role in this process by mobilizing and transforming essential compounds such as nitrogen and phosphorus through excretion, feeding, and movement. However, reef degradation can modify these functions and alter the biogeochemical balance. This study evaluates how fish-mediated nutrient fluxes vary between healthy and degraded reefs of the Mesoamerican Reef System. We focused on four key functional groups, planktivores, herbivores, detritivores, and piscivores, considering traits such as diet, body size, and spatial behavior. Community characterization was conducted using remote video systems (stereo-video and synchronized cameras), which allowed us to estimate abundance, size, biomass, maximum speed, and movement range using the SmaxN methodological framework. In parallel, individuals were collected to measure their excretion rates through controlled incubations, analyzing dissolved compounds (ammonium, phosphate, and silicate) and particulate fecal material using colorimetric methods and elemental analyses. Preliminary results show that species such as Haemulon flavolineatum, Lutjanus apodus, and Acanthurus tractus have the highest rates of ammonium excretion, while Lutjanus apodus, Haemulon flaviolineatum, and Scarus teniopterus stand out for their high phosphate release. Further monitoring across size classes will refine per-capita excretion estimates, enabling a more accurate parametrization of nutrient-release functions at the species level. These data will support the integration of functional contributions across trophic groups under contrasting reef health scenarios. This work will help understand how shifts in fish community structure affect nutrient recycling and will offer important information for conserving the Mesoamerican Reef System amidst growing pressures such as tourism, overfishing, and coastal activities development.
8b18452b: Consumptive and non-consumptive effects of apex predators on trophic interactions and prey demography have been the focus of extensive research. However, the ability of predators to influence prey ecology by modifying nutrient pathways from the bottom up has only recently become recognised as potentially significant. Recent studies suggest that predatory fish can enhance the availability of limiting nutrients, particularly phosphorus, in nutrient-limited ecosystems such as coral reefs. Commercial fishery is on the rise globally, and it selectively harvests predatory fishes from reefs. However, there is currently a lack of knowledge about the consequences of removing this trophic group on essential ecosystem processes. Using field experiments and observational methodologies, we examined how mesopredatory fishes influence herbivory through indirect bottom-up pathways along a fishing-induced predatory fish biomass gradient in the Lakshadweep Archipelago. We show that algal growth rate and herbivore fish productivity, i.e., primary and secondary productivity, increase along the predator biomass gradient, after controlling for alternate sources of pelagic nutrient subsidies through reef aspect. Further, reef-scale and community-level herbivory increased with increasing predator biomass. Together, these results suggest that predator-induced alterations of nutrient stoichiometry can enhance herbivory in coral reefs, particularly in systems experiencing a mesopredatory release following the selective fishing of apex predators. Our study shifts the focus from the traditional top-down role of predators, highlighting an overlooked bottom-up pathway by which predators can influence the functioning of reef ecosystems. Global decline of predators could modify both top-down and bottom-up ecosystem processes on reefs, leaving them increasingly vulnerable to future disturbances.
d99e7e61: Herbivorous coral reef fishes are a diverse guild that evolved specialized traits and behaviors, creating “trophic niches” that enable coexistence in resource-limited environments. On many present‐day reefs, however, coral loss, algal overgrowth, and reductions in herbivore biomass have altered the ecological backdrop against which these species interact. As such, it remains unclear whether the feeding behaviors of reef herbivores change as coral reefs degrade. If evolved, competition‑driven behaviors remain rigid, herbivores may continue to specialize on distinct prey, thereby maintaining extensive, efficient grazing that supports reef recovery. Conversely, an abundance of basal resources could erode competition, leading to generalized feeding on a few highly preferred items and a loss of grazing efficacy. To test these scenarios, we surveyed feeding behavior and diet composition of the ten most common Caribbean herbivorous fishes, across both healthy and degraded reefs in Panama, Belize, and the Dominican Republic. Using DNA metabarcoding of three distinct gene markers (16S, 23S, and COI), we (a) defined discrete dietary signatures for each species, independent of location or reef health, (b) documented intraspecific dietary shifts across locations and gradients of reef degradation, and (c) quantified interspecific trophic niche partitioning and overlap. Finally, we integrated dietary and behavioral data to identify mechanisms underlying trophic niche partitioning. Our results reveal that evolved feeding specializations persist even in resource‑rich settings, yet new patterns of herbivore functioning emerge under altered reef states. These findings highlight both the roles of evolutionary history and ecological flexibility in shaping modern reef herbivory. Further, they offer insights into how herbivores may respond to future coral reef degradation, as well as the potential for herbivores to reverse state shifts and facilitate reef recovery.
d3a24f63: Climate change, particularly recurrent bleaching, is causing biodiversity loss on coral reefs. Obligate corallivores feeding exclusively on live coral , are among the most affected groups. While links between corallivore abundance and coral cover are established, the amount of coral tissue they consume and the drivers of this consumption remain poorly quantified. This study quantified daily coral consumption rates of corallivorous butterflyfishes at the reef edge (peripheral) and centre (interior) of the One Tree Reef lagoon, southern Great Barrier Reef, Australia, using stereo-remote underwater videos (stereo-RUVs). We evaluated drivers of consumption, including feeding behaviour (intensity and duration), assemblage structure (richness and biomass), and predation risk (piscivore biomass). At the community level, mean daily coral consumption rates were over five times higher at peripheral (mean: 17.9 g 200 m⁻² day⁻¹, 95% CI: 16.3–19.6) compared to interior sites (mean: 3.5 g 200 m⁻² day⁻¹, 95% CI: 3.2–3.7). This spatial pattern was driven by higher corallivore richness at peripheral sites. At the species level, the most dominant species, Chaetodon rainfordi, accounted for ~69% of all bites. C. rainfordi’s consumption rate was ~15 times higher at peripheral (16.5, 95% CI: 15.2–17.8 g 200 m⁻² day⁻¹) than interior sites (1.1, 95% CI: 1.0–1.2 g 200 m⁻² day⁻¹). This difference was best explained by C. rainfordi’s higher biomass and longer feeding duration at peripheral compared to interior sites, while predation risk had no observable effect. This study provides new estimates of coral consumption, which have implications for modelling trophic energy transfer, and reveals that both population dynamics (richness and biomass) and feeding behaviour (duration) are critical drivers of fish corallivory.
555766ec: Anthropogenic climate change is rapidly altering marine ecosystems. Ocean acidification (OA: low pH and altered carbonate chemistry) and ocean warming (OW: elevated temperatures) are two major factors affecting calcifying marine organisms such as coralline algae. Coralline algae are an important foundation species in coral reef ecosystems, providing essential habitat and substrate for reef organisms, yet they remain one of the most sensitive taxa to OA. However, select species of coralline algae persist at some naturally low pH/high temperature ecosystems. We wish to understand what traits these coralline algae possess that may confer resilience to future ocean conditions. We hypothesize that either species-specific physiological and ecological traits enable these coralline algae to survive at such sites, or that coralline algae at such sites persist with lower metabolic rates (e.g. calcification). We test this using a reciprocal transplant experiment where multiple coralline algal species from a natural analogue were collected and transplanted to both control and the natural analogue site. Bouraké, New Caledonia is a semi-enclosed lagoon that experiences low pH and high temperatures because of daily tidal shifts, creating conditions analogous to projected end-of-century ocean scenarios. Species diversity at each site was quantified using molecular species identification (DNA barcoding), allowing us to determine species-level composition and verify sample identity. Using this approach, we test whether coralline algae are able to acclimatize to extreme environments through phenotypic plasticity or whether survival depends on pre-existing species-specific adaptations. We assessed this by comparing short-term calcification and photosynthetic rates, and long-term calcification across transplant treatments and species. These physiological metrics allowed us to evaluate which species are capable of maintaining function under extreme conditions. By assessing which species have the potential to acclimatize to future climate conditions, this research provides critical insights into the resilience potential of foundation species in a changing ocean.
9074ad21: Rising atmospheric CO₂ concentrations are causing profound shifts in the carbonate chemistry of seawater, progressively lowering the pH of the oceans. Crustose coralline algae (CCA) play an important role in coral reef ecosystems, participating in reef accretion and acting as coral settlement cues. Ocean acidification (OA) has been suggested to alter the physiology of CCA and their associated-bacteria. However, little is known about the metabolomic responses of CCA to lower pH and how these responses relate to their ability to induce coral settlement. Here, we investigated the effects of OA on the growth, calcification and metabolomic composition of two CCA species (Dawsoniolithon spp. and Neogoniolithon cf. megalocystum) and their ability to induce coral settlement. CCA were exposed for 54 days to three pH/pCO2 scenarios, reflecting current IPCC predictions for the coming centuries (pH: 8.1, 7.8, and 7.6, pCO2: 487, 889 and 1610 µatm). While tissue mortality, growth, and calcification in Dawsoniolithon spp. were significantly affected by exposure to pH 7.6, no effects were detected in Neogoniolithon cf. megalocystum. Likewise, clear metabolomic shifts under low pH occurred in Dawsoniolithon spp., whereas the metabolome of Neogoniolithon cf. megalocystum remained stable. In Dawsoniolithon spp., reduced pH decreased fatty acyls and glycerolipids, and increased carboxylic acids, suggesting a potential metabolic reallocation from defensive lipids to compounds involved in oxidative‑stress mitigation. In contrast, both CCA species showed a reduction in their ability to induce coral settlement rates at low pH. These results highlight species-specific vulnerabilities of CCA to OA, with variable physiological and metabolomic changes, and suggest that OA may reduce the ability of CCA to induce coral settlement, even in the absence of detectable changes in their growth, calcification or metabolome. They also support that OA may negatively affect coral recruitment by reducing CCA inductive properties.
cb91a27f: Crustose coralline algae (CCA) play key roles in the development and persistence of coral reefs globally. As calcifying marine macroalgae, they stabilise benthic substrates and facilitate the settlement and metamorphosis of diverse marine invertebrates. Different genera contribute distinct ecological functions; for example, Titanoderma promotes coral larval settlement, Porolithon stabilises and cements reef frameworks, and Melyvonnea is linked to the settlement of Crown-of-Thorns starfish larvae. These roles collectively have a strong influence on coral reef ecology. Given the importance of CCA to reef ecology, it is critical to understand how CCA communities vary across gradients of water quality in the Great Barrier Reef (GBR). Current knowledge of the influence of runoff on CCA communities is based on morpho-anatomical species identifications; however, DNA sequencing can provide deeper insights into how water quality affects CCA community composition. To address this, we assessed the variability in CCA community composition across a gradient of terrestrial influence in the central GBR using DNA identification. We found that shallow-water CCA communities vary across this gradient, with inshore communities dominated by members of the Hapalidiales, and outer-shelf reefs dominated by reef-building taxa such as Porolithon and Neogoniolithon. Environmental factors such as water transparency and carbonate chemistry were strong predictors of community composition, indicating that water quality is a key determinant of CCA distribution. Most of the DNA sequences generated in our study could not be assigned to described species, emphasising the need for detailed taxonomic research in the region and underscoring the considerable diversity of this important group of reef-building organisms.
55ea25f6: This presentation synthesizes the first decade of integrative investigations of calcifying red algae on Guam’s coral reefs in the Western Pacific. Over this period, we conducted DNA barcoding blitzes, monitoring studies, in situ growth and transplant experiments, coral recruitment assays, mesophotic reef explorations, and assessments of submerged heritage sites. Collectively, this work has reshaped our prior understanding of species richness and endemism, local and regional biogeographical affinities, contributions to reef carbonate budgets, and the role of these algae in ecological community transitions. We also identified distinct distribution patterns across depth gradients and established their utility as indicator taxa on anthropogenic structures. Guam’s limited size and accessible habitats have enabled a uniquely comprehensive island-scale study. Here, we extrapolate our results from this remote island near the Coral Triangle to discuss global diversity patterns and the critical role calcifying red algae will play in the future functioning and resilience of coral reef ecosystems.
71833c94: Rising ocean temperatures and the emerging risk of deoxygenation increasingly threaten the persistence of coral reefs. Yet, the vulnerability of some key reef-building organisms to combined environmental threats remains poorly understood, particularly for non-coral taxa. Crustose coralline algae (CCA) are vital for reef stability, carbonate accretion, and coral recruitment, but there is relatively scant empirical evidence on their sensitivity to warming and virtually none on their susceptibility to deoxygenation. Here, we examined the individual and combined effects of elevated temperature and deoxygenation on two abundant CCA morphotypes from the central Red Sea: Lithophyllum sp. and an undescribed species in the order Corallinales. Fragments were exposed to control, elevated temperature, deoxygenation, and combined treatments in a 10-day laboratory experiment. Elevated temperature was the primary driver of stress in both species of CCA, significantly impairing Fv/Fm and inducing a rapid bleaching response in the undescribed Corallinales species. Lithophyllum sp. was more resilient to temperature stress than the undescribed Corallinales species and showed no visible bleaching. Both species demonstrated metabolic depression under deoxygenated conditions, providing evidence that coralline algae may be oxyregulators. These results provide the first known controlled experimental evidence of CCA responses to combined warming and deoxygenation, revealing species-specific vulnerabilities. Together, our findings suggest future ocean conditions may alter CCA community composition, with cascading consequences for carbonate budgets, reef framework stability, and coral recruitment processes.
1a5dd76f:
Calcifying algae play a critical role in coral reef flat accretion, sediment production and reef resilience, yet their vertical growth rates and contribution to reef flat accretion processes are challenging to quantify at scale in situ. Structure-from-Motion (SfM) photogrammetry offers a non-invasive, low-cost method to measure vertical and marginal growth rates of coralline algae by comparing high-resolution (sub-mm scale) digital surface models (DSMs). However, applying this technique to reliably quantify growth presents challenges associated with temporal repeatability, co-registration, and spatial scaling. 
We tested whether close-range SfM can be used to measure annual coralline algae growth rates on a fringing reef flat in Tongatapu, the Kingdom of Tonga. We also tested scaling up measurements spatially across-reef flats that are exposed at spring low tides, using drone-based SfM. Close-range DSMs were generated from handheld camera surveys of 1 m² quadrats at ~1 m elevation, repeated over three years to capture annual growth. Aerial DSMs were produced from drone imagery captured with DJI Mavic 3E at 10–15 m elevation, overlapping the quadrat locations to assess detection limits for algal accretion at broader spatial coverage. All models were processed in Agisoft, with ground-control and check-points surveyed using a Leica total station. We present vertical and marginal growth rate estimates derived from the close-range data and evaluate the sensitivity of drone-based models to detect growth through time. This presentation will discuss the successes and limitations of photogrammetric approaches for monitoring coralline algae growth on sub-aerially exposed reef flats. The importance of validating and maintaining high ground control elevational accuracy and precise co-registration is discussed.
d7ee11f1: Despite major cyclones, CoTS outbreaks, bleaching events, disease, a tsunami, and other disturbances, the living coral cover in American Samoa since 2018 has been the greatest in 40 years. We attribute this to the prevalence of the substrate-binding CCA Porolithon onkodes s.l., which is prevalent because of wave action combined with the high aragonite saturation states in American Samoa waters. Of 800 successful coral recruits observed on the forereef slope in American Samoa in 2018, 94 % were found on CCA. Recruits are prevalent in the size-classes of at least a hundred of the species of coral on the forereef slope. The reef ecosystem is resilient and stabilized by negative feedback between living-coral cover and CCA. As corals expand living cover, CCA significantly loses cover, although it commonly overgrows branching Acropora and other corals as a refuge. Conversely, when coral cover is decreased by disturbances, CCA gain cover and provide substrata favorable to coral recruitment and thereby enhance the return of coral to prevalence. However, in backreef areas with little wave action, the rubble is not bound by CCA and the coral communities are not as resilient. The relation between CCA and corals changes over a gradient in aragonite saturation state. At Rose Atoll, where wave action is chronic and the aragonite saturation state is greater than 4, the CCA generally overgrow corals, although they also enhance recruitment of corals, so the corals are prevalent and resilient, but with generally small colonies.  Abundant small colonies of corals and prevalence of CCA have been stable aspects of  the forereef of Rose Atoll since at least 2002. Negative feedback provides fundamentally stabilizing mechanisms and thereby resilience on coral reefs.
30ced59c: The cleaner wrasses of the labrid genus Labroides are among the most conspicuous fishes on coral reefs, with species in this genus exhibiting some of the best-known examples of cleaner-client mutualism in the Indo-Pacific. Because of the ecosystem services they provide, Labroides are amongst the most well-studied coral reef organisms, forming the basis for numerous studies centred on their ecology, ethology, and mimetic associations with other fishes. Yet, despite the multitude of studies directed at Labroides, the taxonomy of this emblematic group is surprisingly nebulous, though this might be explained in part by the dearth of taxonomic interest in relation to the disproportionately large number of ecological and behavioural studies. Recent molecular studies have revealed that Labroides is paraphyletic, with the monotypic, facultative cleaner Larabicus quadrilineatus nested within it. Using an integrative approach combining morphological data and genome-wide ultraconserved elements, we corroborate the relationships recovered in previous studies and resolve the taxonomy of Labroides. The genus is rediagnosed with new synapormophies and character combinations, and we provide novel insights into the trophic ecology of Labroides. Our phylogenetic reconstruction supports the hypothesis that cleaning is a derived condition, arising from the ancestral state of coral feeding. However, our results also indicate that there has been at least one instance of secondary reversion to the ancestral condition in Labroides quadrilineatus, suggesting some degree of plasticity between these trophic guilds. Finally, we describe two new species of Labroides, including a highly distinct mesophotic specialist that appears only distantly related to Labroides bicolor.
c696b244: Understanding how species arise is one of the fundamental goals of evolutionary biologists. In marine environments, physical barriers to gene flow are attenuated, yet coral reefs persist as some of the most biodiverse ecosystems on our planet. The mechanisms underlying speciation between closely related species in sympatry, for example, remain ambiguous and poorly explored. For example, in the Coral Triangle, closely related species of fairy wrasses from the Cirrhilabrus cyanopleura species complex occur in various degrees of parapatry, sympatry, and in some cases, complete syntopy. Despite the high morphological distinctiveness between species, previous studies have shown that mitochondrial DNA between members of this species complex demonstrate very low levels of phylogenetic signal. We would expect species boundaries to become eroded under these circumstances. How morphological distinctiveness between species persists in this system is yet to be explicitly tested, though sexual selection leading to assortative mating is hypothesised to prevent species erosion. In this study, we aim to characterise a) the extent of gene flow between species, and b) investigate whether sexual selection and colour perception reinforce maintenance of species integrity. We employ a reduced representation method of genome sequencing to generate a SNP dataset to explore contemporaneous gene flow between species exhibiting all modes of geographical separation. The C. cyanopleura species complex presents a case study through which we can investigate the drivers of divergent evolution when closely related species exist in a concentrated range.
9e44529c: High aspect ratio pectoral fins serve the function of enhanced swimming performance and are thought to permit successful navigation in high flow environments in some reef-associated fishes. While the relationship between habitat and swimming performance has been long established across multiple fish families, it has been minimally investigated within an evolutionary framework. Therefore, the main aim of this study was to examine the evolutionary link between different wave energy environments and pectoral fin aspect ratio in one of the most diverse and abundant families on reefs globally: the Labridae. By applying phylogenetically informed data imputation techniques, we were able to map the evolution of habitat use and fin aspect ratio across 90% of the species in the most recent Labridae phylogeny. Phylogenetic comparative analyses revealed that the earliest, most probable transition towards high wave energy habitats occurred during the early Miocene. Evolutionary shifts in fin aspect ratio primarily occurred during the late Miocene, suggesting that habitat transitions preceded morphological adaptations. Overall, wrasses inhabiting different wave energy environments appear to be exposed to different regimes of natural selection. Indeed, clustering of similar habitats corresponding to similar fin aspect ratios was observed in certain parts of the phylogeny. However, a moderate evolutionary correlation detected between wave energy and fin aspect ratio suggests that there are other influential variables. Using linear models, we further examined the effect of habitat with other ecological variables, such as water column position and trophic guilds, on fin aspect ratio. Fitting the models both with and without the phylogeny showed that most of the variation in fin aspect ratio is explained by phylogenetic relatedness. Overall, this study highlights the importance of accounting for phylogenetic relationships in ecological research, by revealing that the connection between habitat and swimming performance in wrasses is largely explained by shared ancestry.
0de32da5: Turfs and macroalgae are prevalent benthic resources on coral reefs, and central to the biology of herbivorous fishes. However, the mechanics of how algae are detached has only sparsely been studied, raising questions about the nature of diversification across herbivorous species. Surgeonfishes (Acanthuridae) represent one of the most abundant and speciose lineages of herbivorous coral reef fishes. Using high-speed filming of 15 surgeonfish species feeding on filamentous algae we uncover three main regimes based on feeding behaviour: lateral biting, ventral biting, and brushing. The prevalence of these bite types is strongly correlated with upper jaw tooth shape. Using ancestral state reconstruction, we uncover the phylogenetic history of the bite types across 38 surgeonfish species. Phylogenetic reconstructions uncover a dynamic evolutionary history between lateral and ventral biting, with six independent origins of ventral biting from a more widespread lateral biting, emphasizing a major role for transitions in feeding kinematics in the ecological diversification of this group. Furthermore, using Ornstein-Uhlenbeck evolutionary models we show the adaptive dynamics of these feeding regimes which suggest multiple adaptive optima and rates, but a constant selection strength. Overall, our results provide insights into the evolutionary adaptive dynamics of fish feeding mechanisms and how they interact with reef substrata.
e82b5051: Investigating multiple diversity facets and their spatial patterns can reveal the relative roles of evolutionary and ecological processes in community assembly. However, these processes may shift across spatial grains, particularly at global scales, and remain poorly understood for most taxonomic groups. We combined abundance, trait, and phylogenetic data for 1,855 tropical reef fish species (~30% of global richness) to assess the functional and phylogenetic structure of communities, and their drivers, across spatial scales (site, subprovince, and province). Using Functional and Phylogenetic Net Relatedness Index metrics, we quantified community structure across three marine realms, the Atlantic, the Tropical Eastern Pacific (TEP), and the Indo-Pacific, and mapped global patterns of clustering, overdispersion, and randomness. Mixed-effect models were applied to evaluate the influence of environmental and historical evolutionary covariables on community assembly across scales. Reef fish community structure differed among realms but remained consistent across spatial scales within each realm. Clustering predominated in the Indo-Pacific and Caribbean, largely influenced by past and current reef area and proximity to refugia. In contrast, communities in the TEP and the Eastern and Southwestern Atlantic tended to exhibit random structures. Province-scale clustering reflected the influence of historical–evolutionary processes, whereas local-scale clustering emphasized the role of environmental filters associated with reef area, primary productivity, and phosphate levels. Our findings demonstrate that spatial scale is crucial for detecting and disentangling the historical, biogeographic, and environmental drivers of fish community assembly. While the evolutionary history of fish lineages contributes to phylogenetic and functional clustering, contemporary environmental gradients, particularly reef area and productivity, also shape global patterns of community structure. Clustering reflects the filling of trait space by specialist species as richness increases. Notably, even assemblages characterized as functionally random retain a core set of traits essential for reef ecosystem functioning, shaped by regional species pools and constrained by dispersal limitations.
02b7bb83: Accurate species identification is essential for understanding ecological dynamics, evolutionary processes, and biodiversity patterns in coral-reef ecosystems. Yet in many scleractinian corals, species boundaries remain unclear due to morphological plasticity, cryptic speciation, hybridization, and morphological convergence, all of which hinder robust taxonomy and species delimitation. These challenges are particularly relevant in the Red Sea, a climate-change refugium and hotspot of coral endemism. Resolving species identities is crucial for assessing extinction risks, characterizing adaptive divergence, and detecting overlooked biodiversity. The genus Stylophora, particularly Stylophora pistillata, is among the most abundant and widely used model systems in coral research, underpinning studies in ecology, physiology, genomics, symbiosis, and climate-change biology. However, recent work has revealed genetic structuring within what was long considered a single widespread species, suggesting that S. pistillata may represent multiple divergent evolutionary lineages. This emerging complexity underscores the need for rigorous species-level resolution. Here, we combined multilocus genetic analyses and skeletal morphology to examine Stylophora morphospecies in the Gulf of Aqaba. We sequenced seven genetic markers (mitochondrial: COI, CR, ORF; nuclear: ITS1, ITS2, HSP70A, HSP70B) from 90 Red Sea colonies, supplemented by additional Indo-Pacific Stylophora representatives and outgroups from two other pocilloporid genera. Morphological analyses included fourteen macro- and micro-skeletal characters measured from field-collected colonies and specimens from the Steinhardt Museum of Natural History. Our integrated analyses resolved five Stylophora species, clarified species boundaries, and illuminated evolutionary relationships. While three nominal species, S. kuehlmanni, S. pistillata, and S. subseriata, clustered together in a single well-supported clade with little genetic divergence, we identified and described a new species, Stylophora sp. nov., which forms a distinct monophyletic lineage, with unique diagnostic traits. Together, our findings shed light on Stylophora diversity in the Red Sea and show that even iconic model species may harbor unrecognized evolutionary complexity essential for advancing coral-reef science.
9e43100c:
In our changing global climate, reef ecosystems also face intensifying local environmental stress. In particular, local eutrophic conditions amplify the destructive impacts of global ocean warming. Simultaneously, management and restoration are hindered by a poor foundational understanding of the degree and distribution of genetic diversity. Marginal communities at the limits of environmental tolerance, shaped by a history of human impact, may be representative of future reefs. In study sites such as Hong Kong, these corals are an opportunity to understand speciation and adaptation, which can direct sustainable conservation. 

To that end, I have undertaken the following project combining observational and experimental methods with whole-genome sequencing, the most rigorous method to identify genes associated with specific traits. I have sampled over 200 colonies of reef-building Acropora species in Hong Kong to putatively redefine species, and to contextualize the evolutionary history and diversity of China's Greater Bay Area within the greater Indo-Pacific. Demographic analysis using DILS (demographic inferences with linked selection) is used to assess past and present gene flow between populations. 

Among conspecific colonies from Hong Kong, patterns of regional genetic variation are mapped using a spatial principal component analysis (sPCA) framework.  A genotype-by-environment association (GEA) analysis – wherein genetic diversity will be characterized along the water quality gradients – then identifies genetic markers associated with eutrophication-resistance. Fragments from 123 conspecific colonies have also been deployed in a common garden experiment to evaluate physiological performance of genetically diverse individuals at varying nutrient conditions. The results will be analyzed in a genome-wide association study (GWAS). The GWAS will complement the GEA in identifying genetic markers for eutrophication-tolerance. Overall, this project will generate important genomic data for future research in coral adaptation and guide holistic, genetics-driven coral restoration.
4f0af976:
Rapid ocean warming continues to intensify mass bleaching events, yet some corals survive marine heatwaves better than others. Understanding the biological mechanisms underlying this resilience—and determining the scales at which they operate—is essential for informing conservation and restoration action. The “Global Search” project funded by the Paul G. Allen Family Foundation with support from OceanKind, NOAA, and the DFG was a coordinated, four-ocean effort spanning the Great Barrier Reef, Tropical Central Pacific, Arabian Seas, and Caribbean with the aim to apply standardized, field-deployable acute thermal stress assays (CBASS and SeaSim-in-a-Box) in conjunction with Systems Biology approaches to elucidate genetic regulators of coral resilience to thermal stress. Here we report on current and projected outcomes of this global endeavor. 

Over the course of four years, we quantified thermal tolerance thresholds for hundreds of genets across eight coral species with subsequent analyses of gene expression, algal association, microbiome assemblage, and population-genomic variation. We identified transcriptional splicing as a key mechanism reflecting the severity of thermal stress impact. Further, specific coral-algal pairings were predictive of thermal resilience, while climate bleaching metrics aligned with differences in heat stress phenotypes. We could show that both conserved and region-specific molecular signatures are associated with heat tolerance. Comparative analyses across the ocean basins will reveal the biological scales and universal applicability of genetic predictors of resilience—from individual genets to highly tolerant regional populations. 
 
Outcomes from this effort will hopefully further our understanding of thermal resilience at ecologically relevant scales to provide biomarkers active intervention. For instance, universal molecular signatures associated with resilience will provide evidence-based criteria for selecting broodstock and designing selective-breeding or assisted-migration strategies. Regional contrasts can further highlight where naturally tolerant populations occur, offering a window into what shaped past heat tolerance and how best to protect and utilize already resilient populations.
95daedd8: Organismal thermal tolerance will shape coral reef diversity and abundance of the future, as ocean warming drives extensive coral bleaching and mortality through increasingly frequent marine heatwaves. Coral thermal tolerance varies markedly among species, reflecting differences in both physiological and molecular regulatory mechanisms. Gene expression plasticity enables dynamic transcriptional adjustments to environmental stress, while alternative splicing and isoform switching further expand molecular adaptability by altering transcript diversity and abundance. Because genes function within combinatorial networks, changes in gene connectivity and in the identity of highly influential genes, or hub genes, can reveal higher-order biological reorganization under stress. Yet, the contribution of isoform switching to gene network dynamics remains unknown in corals. Here we integrate photo-physiology, gene network topology, hub genes dynamics, and isoform switching patterns across three Hawaiian corals, Porites compressa, Montipora capitata, and Pocillopora acuta to identify the molecular processes shaping thermal performance. We show that the interplay between expression plasticity, hub gene rearrangement, and isoform switching drives species-specific thermal tolerance. Network analysis uncovered extensive rewiring and temperature-responsive hub gene turnover regulated by switches in isoform usage. The most thermally tolerant species, P. compressa, shows high energy reserves, stable constitutive gene expression and continuous isoform adjustments across temperatures, while fine-tuning thermal response through dedicated hub genes. In contrast, P. acuta shows the lowest holobiont thermal tolerance, with broad transcriptomic shifts and isoform switching controlling the reorganization of network leadership at high temperatures. These findings identify isoform switching as a newly recognized regulatory mechanism in coral thermal resilience, linking isoform diversity to gene regulatory hierarchies and establishing its role in the evolutionary toolkit of reef-building corals and as a predictor of resistance and environmental resilience.
f0fb8da4: The symbiotic relationship between corals and dinoflagellate algae plays a critical role in coral biology and is thought to have evolved independently several times within Scleractinia and in other cnidarians. In this relationship, the algal symbiont is housed intracellularly in a membrane-bound organelle called the symbiosome. This membrane ultimately controls the dynamics of symbiosis by tightly controlling the molecules that are exchanged between the two partners and defines the environment for the algae. Despite playing an essential role in symbiosis, the cellular identity of the symbiosome and the molecular mechanisms enabling its repeated evolution remains unclear. Using the sea anemone Aiptasia, we generated a high-quality proteome of the symbiosome, revealing protein-trafficking mechanisms and the types of biomolecules exchanged during symbiosis. Symbiosomal enrichment of lysosomal proteins, visualization of lysosomal fusion, along with reduced symbiosis following knockdown of lysosomal genes, indicate that the symbiosome functions through extensive co-option of lysosomal proteins. We identified a symbiosomal bicarbonate/sulfate transporter, SLC26A11, and showed through CRISPR/Cas9-mutagenesis that this lysosomal transporter is required for symbiosis in both Aiptasia and the reef-building coral, Galaxea fascicularis. Together, these findings reveal that corals and anemones have repeatedly co-opted lysosomal proteins to concentrate carbon and shuttle metabolites to support photosymbiosis, providing a relatively simple path for the repeated evolution of new photosymbioses.
39a0ba0e: High-quality reference genomes are essential for the comprehensive study of organisms, providing the foundation for functional genomics, evolutionary analyses, and experimental robustness. Here, we present new telomere-to-telomere chromosome-scale genome assemblies for Exaiptasia diaphana, a widely used model for investigating cnidarian-dinoflagellate symbiosis. These assemblies, generated using long-read sequencing, Omni-C, and linkage map scaffolding, achieve full chromosomal resolution, resulting in 16 chromosomes. Using these assemblies, we conducted comparative genomics across multiple Exaiptasia strains collected from diverse geographic regions worldwide.
