This global analysis examines genome-wide patterns of sequence divergence, structural variation, and gene family evolution, including lineage-specific expansions and contractions, as well as genomic regions potentially under selection, providing insights into how local adaptation and demographic history have shaped genomic diversity. We further assessed genetic similarity between strains and tested for evidence of admixture or potential crossing events, revealing patterns of shared ancestry and introgression that inform evolutionary relationships within Exaiptasia. Together, these chromosome-scale assemblies and comparative genomic analyses provide a high-quality reference framework for Exaiptasia, enhancing its utility as a model system for studying cnidarian biology, symbiosis, and environmental resilience.
45119cfe: Extrachromosomal circular DNA (eccDNA) is found outside of chromosomes and has been documented in plants, animals, fungi and protists. eccDNA has been linked to genomic instability, structural rearrangements, gene copy number variation and heat stress responses. Despite observations of plastid minicircles in Symbiodiniaceae, it is unclear whether eccDNAs of chromosomal origin occur in Symbiodiniaceae and whether they contribute to symbiont stress tolerance. Here, we integrate multiple analytical approaches to examine the existence of eccDNA of chromosomal origin within Symbiodiniaceae for the first time. We focus on long-read sequencing technologies which are better able to span repetitive regions and resolve complete eccDNA sequences. Our first strategy used a breakpoint-linkage, graph-based approach for the reference-guided assembly of eccDNAs from publicly available Effrenium voratum, Symbiodinium tridacnidorum and Cladocopium goreaui whole genome datasets. Secondly, we carried out long-read based Circle-Seq of eccDNAs from bulk holobiont DNA extractions, followed by de novo eccDNA assembly using a tandem repeat recognition-based approach. Through this research, we reveal the presence of eccDNAs in all Symbiodiniaceae lineages examined, which are distinct from the known minicircles of the plastid genome. We characterise novel structural features and sequence motifs of eccDNAs, highlighting the extensive presence of transposable elements and repeat sequences within assembled eccDNAs. Finally, we demonstrate the presence of coding sequences within eccDNAs and discuss their potential impact on gene expression. Taken together, our integrative approach reveals that eccDNAs of chromosomal origin are an important feature in Symbiodiniaceae and may represent additional genomic substrate important for rapid responses to environmental change.
1ab2dae5: Heat shock proteins (HSPs) are molecular chaperones that safeguard proteostasis under stress. We investigated the expression dynamics of the chaperonin HSP60 across diverse cnidarians to understand its stress-responsive regulation. Using immunoblotting, we quantified HSP60 expression in Pocillopora acuta (reef-building coral), Exaiptasia diaphana (sea anemone), and Cassiopea xamachana (upside-down jellyfish). In P. acuta, HSP60 was not detected at the fragment scale under either control or heat stress conditions, whereas isolated cells exhibited transient HSP60 expression during exposure to both control and heated temperatures (+5 ºC above the optimum), indicating that HSP60 regulation in this coral is strongly context-dependent and potentially suppressed at the tissue level. In contrast, E. diaphana and C. xamachana exhibited a gradual, temperature-dependent accumulation of HSP60 over 24 h under heated conditions (+5 ºC above the optimum); however, C. xamachana also displayed constitutive basal expression under control conditions. These contrasting profiles highlight clear lineage-specific differences in HSP60 regulation among cnidarians. The consistent antibody cross-reactivity observed across all three species, along with phylogenetic analyses of HSP60 sequences, confirmed that cnidarian proteins are orthologous to the vertebrate HSP60 (human HSPD1), demonstrating deep structural and evolutionary conservation of this chaperonin across Metazoa. Collectively, these findings reveal that while HSP60 is evolutionarily ancient and conserved, its regulation under thermal stress varies across lineages and physiological contexts, reflecting complex modulation of mitochondrial proteostasis in early-diverging metazoans. This lineage- and context-dependent regulatory framework provides new insight into how chaperone plasticity contributes to cnidarian thermal tolerance and the differential susceptibility of reef taxa to bleaching under ocean warming.
2b7621b9:
Many coral populations are vulnerable to climate change because they live close to their thermal maxima, where even a few degrees of warming can trigger bleaching and death. At the same time, single coral species are often found across latitudes, where populations thrive at varying temperatures, demonstrating their ability to adapt to diverse thermal environments. A central research focus is to determine corals' genetic capacity to adapt to climate change and the molecular pathways they may use to do so. Understanding the genetic basis of this thermal tolerance variation will help predict how corals will respond to climate change.

Here, we use clonal populations of the symbiotic sea anemone, Exaiptasia diaphana (Aiptasia) from North Carolina (CC7) and Hawaii (H2), which naturally experience different temperatures, as a model to investigate thermal adaptation in corals. To identify genetic loci controlling heat-mortality variation, we performed bulk segregant analysis by heat-challenging H2xCC7 F2 larvae and sequencing the surviving individuals. This analysis revealed a CC7 haplotype that was strongly selected against during heat stress. To identify causative variants, we performed allele-specific expression analysis on H2xCC7 hybrid animals. This showed widespread cis-regulatory differences between H2 and CC7 alleles, including in multiple genes associated with heat stress. To further narrow in on causative variants, we have used CRISPR/Cas9-mediated mutagenesis to test how genes within this locus affect heat mortality in adult Aiptasia. This work will elucidate the pathways involved in heat tolerance variation in cnidarians and help us understand how corals may adapt to climate change.
10061d47: Dinoflagellate algae in the family Symbiodiniaceae, symbionts associated with many marine cnidarians, including corals, sea anemones, and jellyfish, are critical for the metabolic integrity of reef ecosystems, which are currently threatened by ocean warming and acidification. The resilience of the cnidarian-dinoflagellate symbiosis is dependent upon thermotolerance of the partner organisms; corals and sea anemone hosts that harbor heat-resistant symbionts exhibit greater resistance to bleaching. While coral responses to heat stress are well-documented, transcriptomic adaptation/acclimation of Symbiodiniaceae to elevated temperatures are limited. Here, we compare thermal responses of two genera in the Symbiodiniaceae, Symbiodinium linucheae SSA01 and Breviolum minutum SSB01. SSA01 in culture maintained photosynthetic function at elevated temperatures (34 °C) and mounted a rapid transcriptomic response characterized by early downregulation of a JMJ21-like histone demethylase coupled with prompt upregulation of transcripts associated with DNA repair and oxidative stress, which would likely contribute to enhanced resilience to heat stress. In contrast, SSB01 experienced a decline in photosynthetic efficiency and a delayed transcriptomic response (relative to SSA01) that included upregulation of transcripts encoding proteasome subunits and reduced levels of transcripts encoding proteins involved in photosynthesis and metabolite transport. These findings indicate that a rapid and moderate transcriptomic response that results in increased expression of genes related to the synthesis and repair of biomolecules might be crucial for thermal tolerance in the Symbiodiniaceae while sensitivity to elevated temperatures may be reflected by increased protein turnover and a marked decline in anabolic processes. Understanding these differences is vital for predicting coral responses to increasing seawater temperatures and for developing strategies to ameliorate the damaging effects of elevated temperature on reef ecosystems.
3181de5d:
Endosymbiosis has been a major driver of eukaryotic evolution, yet the mechanisms by which free-living microbes transition into successful intracellular symbionts remain poorly understood. Within marine systems, dinoflagellates in the family Symbiodiniaceae underpin the productivity of coral reefs, and the breakdown of these partnerships under thermal stress contributes to global coral decline. Although the basal genus Symbiodinium was taxonomically revised in 2018 and its evolutionary relationships clarified via genomics in 2022, it has not yet been broadly implemented as an experimental model clade for studying the evolution of symbiosis. 

Here, we discuss the establishment of Symbiodinium as a tractable model clade by experimentally demonstrating the symbiotic capabilities of all six species using the model anemone Aiptasia and manually morphogenized juveniles of the coral Acropora tenuis. By quantifying uptake, persistence, and proliferation, we show that only the most recently diverged lineages consistently establish and maintain endosymbiosis across hosts. We initially hypothesized that adaptation to the acidic coral symbiosome might underlie these transitions, growth assays revealed no lineage-specific differences in tolerance to acidic conditions. Instead, comparative genomics identified strong positive selection of carbohydrate-related genes, including a glycosyl hydrolase, which may be related to the remodeling of the dinoflagellate cell wall. Differential tolerances to a cellulase inhibitor further support the central role of carbohydrates in the evolution of endosymbiotic compatibility. From this, we use a combination of immunostaining and live-cell imaging to explore the fundamental, carbon-based biology of Symbiodinium within the context of cnidarian endosymbiosis.
7cd3afb3:
Effective management for reef resilience under climate change requires a clear understanding of the processes that sustain and enable adaptation in coral communities, as well as targeted actions that support those processes during periods of stress and recovery. Drawing on recent experience from the Great Barrier Reef (GBR), this presentation outlines four key aspects that underpin resilience-focused management and highlights how they interact to influence ecological outcomes in a rapidly warming climate.
First, maintaining the survival and condition of adult corals remains foundational. Adult colonies not only contribute directly to reef structure and ecosystem function, but also provide the reproductive capacity that fuels recovery following disturbances. Management actions that reduce local stressors (including high water temperatures), support water quality improvements, and mitigate cumulative impacts help preserve this critical biological capital.
Second, understanding and enhancing connectivity and larval recruitment is essential for supporting long-term reef replenishment. Recent advances in larval dispersal modelling, genetic approaches, and field-based seeding interventions are providing new insights into the sources, sinks, and pathways that shape reef recovery potential across the GBR.
Third, we need the capacity for stewardship and active intervention. This includes community-led monitoring, traditional owner leadership, targeted protection of high-value sites, as well as scalable restoration and adaptation technologies. Adaptive governance and strong partnerships are increasingly central to this capacity.
Finally, targeted research that directly informs management decisions is crucial as climate risks accelerate. Driven research–management co-design, rapid applied trials, and clear decision pathways are necessary to ensure that emerging knowledge translates quickly into on-ground action.
These are the key features of a practical, interlinked framework for supporting reef resilience. Insights from the GBR illustrate both the challenges and opportunities of managing coral ecosystems in this era of unprecedented change.
b1ce2096:
Successful scaling of reef interventions depends critically on social feasibility—whether the public supports, values, and is willing to fund different management approaches. We present research quantifying public acceptance and willingness-to-pay for reef interventions designed to achieve core ecological outcomes: increasing coral outplant survival, reducing macroalgal cover, and enhancing fish abundance. Using grazer stocking interventions (culturing and introducing native herbivores like Caribbean king crabs and long-spined sea urchins) as a focal case study, we conducted discrete choice experiments, a socioeconomic survey method, with 800 Florida residents to evaluate how intervention attributes influence public support and perceived value.
Results demonstrate strong public support for restoration action, with the vast majority of respondents preferring intervention over inaction. Willingness-to-pay was highest for coral survival improvements, followed by algal reduction, with fish abundance showing consistent but lower values. Preferences were significantly shaped by species choice (with majority support for combined species approaches over single-species strategies), sensitivity to climate risks (varying by demographics and environmental attitudes), and confidence in intervention effectiveness. While our empirical application focused on herbivore enhancement, the analytical framework is transferable to evaluating social acceptance of other interventions designed to improve coral outplant survival, increase fish abundance, or reduce algal cover across diverse reef systems.
Quantifying public support provides critical social feasibility data, but managers also need tools to optimize implementation. We complement this preference data with agent-based modeling of grazer dynamics that identifies ecological and economic optima, helping managers design stocking strategies that achieve desired algal reduction while aligning with public values and budget constraints. By demonstrating methods to quantify public support alongside ecological outcomes, our work provides decision-support tools for managers and funders seeking to prioritize interventions that are both effective and socially sustainable.
000b947b: Most coral reefs worldwide are severely affected by recurrent mass bleaching and mortality events induced by ocean warming. This underlines the urgent need for immediate interventions to prevent the widespread loss of coral reefs, while carbon neutrality is achieved. Recent findings suggest that molecular hydrogen (H₂) dissolved in seawater benefits coral photophysiology under short-term heat stress, potentially by counteracting oxidative damage. However, long-term effects of H₂ on the coral holobiont remain poorly understood. Our study therefore explored whether H₂ exposure could support coral photophysiology following a natural marine heat wave (MHW) thereby enhancing coral recovery. In 2024, the Central Red Sea experienced an extreme MHW that lasted for 202 days, with sea surface temperatures of up to 34.3 °C, triggering widespread mass coral bleaching and mortality. At the end of this event, we collected coral fragments from non-bleached colonies of the widespread hard coral Pocillopora favosa. Corals were kept in a flow-through aquarium system at ambient seawater temperatures (~ 30.2 °C) below thermal thresholds for three weeks separated into two treatments, with and without H₂, while monitoring key ecophysiological parameters. Regardless of treatment, coral fragments experienced a significant loss in coloration, Symbiodiniaceae cell density and relative maximum electron transport rate indicating a stress response even after the marine heatwave, potentially linked to the recently described “post-heat stress disorder”. By the end of the experiment, H₂-treated corals retained significantly higher photosynthetic and light-capture efficiency by 40% and 30%, respectively, compared to untreated corals. H₂-treated corals also maintained a stable chlorophyll c2:a ratio in contrast to a significant increase in untreated corals, suggesting a specific loss of chlorophyll a. In conclusion, our results suggest that H₂ can preserve photosynthetic performance of the coral holobiont highlighting the potential of H₂ as a promising tool to maintain and improve coral resilience against ocean warming.
69271a75:
The Reef Cooperative is collaboration of scientists, Traditional Owners, conservation groups and tourism operators, working together in combining academic science, First Nations ecological knowledge and public engagement to conserve reefs across the Great Barrier Reef. This functioning community model has recently surveyed over 400 reefs though the Great Reef Census. The information gathered from the Great Reef Census is analysed by the global community and scientifically validated to contribute towards management actions. From here we prioritize reefs, conduct detailed site-specific monitoring and make informed decisions on reef resilience and if site assisted recovery action is required.  

These decisions are made using our functional decision tree “Kulbul” which also considers Traditional Owner Ecological knowledge. All our projects are co-designed and co-managed with Traditional Owners, ensuring that cultural knowledge is continually recognised and safeguarded.  So far our conservation actions have included coral larval reseeding, coral predator control and stabilising coral rubble fields to increase local scale resilience. Our larval seeding has released millions of coral larvae on degraded sections of reef impacted from accumulative widespread bleaching events across two reefs. We have stabilised rubble beds from severe tropical cyclone damage with over a 1000 Reef Stars across three reefs using the Mars Assisted Reef Restoration System.  

An important development of our community model was to create a pathway for First Nations people to further develop reef health monitoring and intervention skills, that could be applied to Sea Country management.  We created a multistage reef intervention training program designed and have trained 20 First Nations youth across the Great Barrier in this world first program. This program was not only designed to build capacity but also create leaders in reef stewardship. The Reef Cooperative model based on engagement, inclusion and mutually beneficial partnerships is highly scalable to improve global coral reef resilience.
ee1056aa: At this critical juncture for the world’s coral reefs, it is essential that organizations, practitioners, and the public recognize that reversing reef decline and recovering imperiled species remain achievable goals supported by actionable pathways. The U.S. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s National Marine Fisheries Service (NOAA Fisheries) is developing a recovery plan for 15 Indo-Pacific reef-building coral species listed as threatened under the U.S. Endangered Species Act. NOAA Fisheries is working to support, coordinate, and catalyze conservation efforts across the region, and, where appropriate, implement actions directly. Effective coral recovery requires addressing both global and local threats while advancing innovative biological and environmental interventions that reduce extinction risk, enhance resilience, and buy time until global threats are mitigated. In alignment with U.S. priorities, this work integrates ecological recovery with the socio-economic well-being of coastal communities, recognizing that healthy reefs sustain livelihoods, food security, and local economies. To guide decision-making, we apply scenario planning and portfolio efficiency analysis to identify priority activities under multiple plausible futures, highlight spatial priorities across ecoregions, and elevate emerging research, management approaches, and intervention strategies. This approach aims to provide a cohesive framework that blends innovation, practical conservation expertise, and socio-economic considerations to drive systemic change to enable the recovery of threatened Indo-Pacific corals, promote ecosystem and coastal community resilience, and advance sustainable fisheries and economies.
2360cf74: The impacts of climate change on coral reef ecosystems is currently being felt globally.  Emphasis has been on quantification and management of the cascading effects of global warming press stressors such as ocean temperatures and acidity on long term coral reef health. Pulse stressors, such as climate change induced flooding, have immediate impacts on coral reef ecosystems and can result in catastrophic loss. In the region of the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park between Cairns and Townsville we have found that press stressors, annual back to back bleaching events, have had negligible impacts on near and offshore reefs.  In contrast, a pulse event, the flood associated with a prolonged tropical low for the month of February 2025, has resulted in catastrophic mortality of entire coral reef ecosystems.  Quantifying this event was possible through novel partnerships between tourism operators, traditional owners, scientists and park managers to elucidate the immediate, intermediary and future long term impacts of the event. Prior to the 2025 flood, four reefs across the region had been monitored quarterly for composition and health, and one reef weekly since 2022. Between 2022 and 2024 there were press and pulse events across the region. Back to back marine heatwaves and a flood event from Cyclone Jasper resulted in negligible bleaching and no mortality in near and off shore reefs. Before event data has been critical in informing not only the immediate impact of the event, but how intervention for long term recovery should proceed.
8a91d712: Chemical signals from crustose coralline algae (CCA) and their microbiomes regulate coral settlement, yet scalable delivery of these cues remains a critical bottleneck for reef restoration. Here, we present nanobioengineering approaches to recreate the chemical landscape of healthy reefs using biomimetic chemical microhabitats that integrate CCA-derived exometabolites, isolated settlement-inducing molecules, and living bacterial coatings. These systems mimic the signaling environment that guides larval attachment and supports early post-settlement growth. We evaluate individual and combined cue delivery, measuring settlement enhancement, coral spat health, and resistance to early competitive stress. Central to this approach is SNAP-X, a soft biomaterial incorporating silica nanoparticles, biopolymers, and CCA-derived exometabolites to create sustained chemical gradients via slow-release delivery. We further integrate lipopolysaccharides (LPS) and rationally selected microbial strains into photocurable hydrogel coatings compatible with emerging engineered microhabitats. Together, this work establishes a tunable chemical and microbial toolkit that augments coral recruitment and supports early survivorship, advancing scalable, chemically programmable substrates for reef restoration.
52fd6bf6: Rising sea surface temperatures are a major driver of coral reef degradation worldwide. One key consequence is coral bleaching, triggered by oxidative stress and excessive production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) within coral tissues, which disrupts the coral–algae symbiosis and can ultimately lead to coral death. The alarming findings of the fourth global bleaching event reported by NOAA in 2024 highlight the increasing frequency and severity of thermal stress events, underlying the urgent need for effective coral bleaching-mitigation therapies. However, delivering antioxidant treatments in the open ocean remains highly challenging, particularly in ensuring controlled drug release without contaminating the surrounding ecosystem. To overcome these limitations, a biodegradable drug delivery system represented by silk-based microparticles (MPs) was developed for the administration of curcumin - a natural antioxidant compound derived from the Curcuma longa plant – aimed to enhance coral thermal resistance. Mimicking corals’ prey in size and composition, curcumin-loaded MPs were extensively characterized in terms of morphology, physicochemical properties, release kinetics, and coral biocompatibility. Post-production thermal processing transformed the MPs from a water-soluble, α-helix silk structure into a water-resistant β-sheet configuration, enabling slow and controlled release of curcumin over time. Ingestion studies on Heliofungia fralinae corals suggested MPs' capacity to reach the gastric cavity and release curcumin locally, while a four-phase bleaching experiment conducted on Stylophora pistillata nubbins demonstrated the therapeutic efficacy of curcumin MPs. Treated corals showed significantly higher chlorophyll content and Symbiodiniaceae density compared to untreated controls, suggesting improved thermal stress resistance. These outcomes demonstrate that curcumin-loaded silk MPs represent a targeted, biocompatible, scalable delivery platform capable of improving coral resilience, offering overall, a promising therapeutic strategy for mitigating temperature-induced bleaching and supporting reef conservation efforts.
57ddf248: The use of coral probiotics, or Beneficial Microorganisms for Corals (BMCs), has emerged as a promising strategy to minimize and mitigate the negative effects of rising sea temperatures on coral reefs. Extracted from healthy corals, BMCs have been proven to facilitate higher survivorships and retention of healthy vitals in ex situ and recently in situ applications. Here, we present the use of alginate-based biotechnological tools to deliver BMCs to corals. Alginate has been well-studied in harsh conditions ranging from soil to stomach acids for its ability to preserve and protect encapsulated organisms for long-term controlled release and storage. Our experiments aimed to assess the effectiveness of encapsulation, release kinetics, retention of viable cells, and behavior underwater. This was done by testing single-strain versus consortia beads and quantifying released cells across two temperature regimes (26 and 32 °C), covering the Red Sea seasonal temperature range. We then tested the in situ application of BMCs to corals using beads as part of a restoration experiment. Alginate beads were found to retain viable BMCs and allow them to grow and be released over the maximum time tested,  three weeks, at an average rate of ~10⁶ cells hr-1 when inoculated in artificial seawater. Temperature had little to no significant effect on the release dynamics. Ultimately, Acropora spp. and Pocillopora spp. fragments inoculated with BMC beads in situ demonstrated improved health over longer-term weekly applications, indicated by Fv/Fm measurements compared to control fragments. With these results and further refinements, we aim to establish a robust, scalable, and biocompatible tool to facilitate BMC-based therapies for coral reef restoration and conservation efforts globally.
a7bb545b: The accelerating global decline of coral reefs highlights the urgent need for innovative conservation technologies that can operate at molecular and microscale levels. Nanobiotechnology and materials science offer powerful approaches for developing targeted interventions, including delivery systems for beneficial microorganisms for corals (BMCs). A major barrier to advancing microbial therapies for corals is the lack of targeted and stable delivery systems for administering probiotics to corals. In this study, we introduce natural, biodegradable alginate-based delivery systems engineered as beads, hydrogels, and macrofibers as a method for targeted probiotic release. Using Pseudoalteromonas peptidolytica as a model probiotic, we achieved encapsulation efficiencies of 7.33 × 10⁴ ± 2.5 × 10³ CFU/g of polymer, with materials exhibiting robust mechanical stability upon immersion in seawater. In vitro probiotic diffusion assays demonstrated that the encapsulated probiotic remained viable and proliferative, and migrated beyond its delivery vehicles. After an initial lag phase, the release of probiotics increased markedly, reaching a maximum at 24 hours. This enabled a targeted release during the breakdown of the alginate matrix, as confirmed by scanning electron microscopy analysis of the delivery material. During in vivo coral experiments, we compared the three alginate delivery systems and microbiome sequencing verified a partial uptake and suggested a possible temporal persistence of the introduced strain after deployment of the alginate materials, demonstrating their specificity and functional delivery capacity. Microbiome analysis and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) indicated that degradation of all three delivery systems was driven by a combination of biological and chemical mechanisms. As first underwater delivery systems engineered for coral probiotics, these alginate-based materials demonstrate how bioengineered micro- and nanoscale systems can enhance precision interventions in coral restoration. This work highlights the potential of material science to bridge the gap between laboratory development and field application, advancing therapeutic and restoration strategies for reef conservation.
38170b63: In the scope of advancing coral restoration two different functional materials are presented. First, an environmentally friendly, conductive hardening bicomponent paste was developed to transplant and anchor corals, provide them with a solid growing substrate, and enable Mineral Accretion Technology (MAT), to accelerate coral growth. The bicomponent paste consists of bio-based and biodegradable acrylate soybean oil matrix and electrically conductive carbon fillers. The paste hardens through mixing, transitioning from a Young’s modulus of ≃ 0.1 MPa to ≃ 60 MPa and reaching a strength of ≃ 5 MPa. Rheological tests demonstrate the tunability of the crosslinking dynamics of the paste. The paste exhibits a resistivity of 0.1 W∙m, with stable electrical properties for over 40 days in seawater. MAT tests show significant enhancement of coral growth rates within two weeks, doubling those of the control group. This paste offers versatility for application in aquaria and nurseries, does not require prone-to-oxidation metallic structures underwater, and can be employed on reefs. The second material is a biodegradable, non-toxic antifouling biocomposite formulated to reduce persistent plastic pollution while providing an environmentally friendly fouling-control strategy. The biocomposite pairs a beeswax matrix with Tween as natural antifoulant and bio-fillers to tune rheology and improve stability The material is compatible with conventional thermoplastic processing, including compression molding and 3D printing, and can also be applied as a conformal retrofit coating for existing structures. Antifouling action is driven by Tween-induced surface hydrophilization, which creates a hydration layer that suppresses protein adsorption and biofilm formation. The biocomposite can be integrated with existing coral restoration tools such as reef plugs, scaffolds, and 3D-printed reef structures, both reducing environmental impact and limiting adverse fouling and algal competition around coral recruits. 3D-printed antifouling coral plugs produced from this formulation supported healthy fragment attachment and early growth while limiting fouling.
c1632f9a:
Reef building result from coral calcification, a process by which corals build biomineral structures through the precipitation of calcium carbonate and the secretion of organic matrix components. Despite advances at the cellular and physiological levels, the mechanisms controlling coral calcification remain elusive. This gap is primarily due to limitations in available techniques for these organisms. To overcome these challenges, we developed an alternative methodology using in vivo injection of exogenous molecules in octocorallians (Forin et al., 2024), one subclass of Anthozoa (Cnidaria) which produces minute biominerals called sclerites.
In our search for candidate factors influencing calcification, we conducted a primary screening that identified a strong stimulator of calcification in both the soft coral Sarcophyton sp. and the precious coral Corallium rubrum.
From methodological development to hormone screening, our research ultimately pointed to thyroid hormones (TH), well known in vertebrates but scarcely studied in invertebrates, particularly anthozoans. By combining imaging analyses with ELISA assays, we showed that TH enhance sclerite calcification. We further demonstrated that one potential natural source of TH is dietary intake, thereby revealing an ecological connection between iodine originating from marine food webs and coral calcification.
The approaches developed here provide promising applications for assessing coral health and resilience, considering not only coral physiology but also the impacts of climate change and environmental stressors, particularly endocrine disruption.
1ef8c03c:
Thermal stress disrupts redox homeostasis in reef-building corals, driving the oxidative damage that underlies bleaching. While restoration efforts increasingly prioritize thermotolerance, few approaches directly address the molecular pathways that precede symbiosis collapse. Nanomedicine, long established in clinical and agricultural contexts, offers a promising avenue for targeted antioxidant delivery in complex tissues. Early applications in coral systems, including nanoceria-mediated ROS scavenging in symbiotic algae and polymer-based antioxidants in coral larvae, highlight the potential for nanoscale materials to modulate stress responses. 
Building on these foundations, this work examines how different antioxidant-active compounds and nanoparticles interact with coral tissues and influence key oxidative stress markers during thermal exposure. Symbiodiniaceae antioxidant extracts and nanoparticles engineered with catalytic redox centers or antioxidant-mimetic functional groups were introduced to isolated mixed coral cells in culture and coral fragments, enabling assessment across physiological scales. Measurements of intracellular free radical accumulation, lipid stability, mitochondrial performance, and symbiont photophysiology provide an integrated view of how nanoscale chemistry aligns with known bleaching pathways. Fluorescent particle tracking further contextualizes how material properties govern tissue distribution and access to redox-sensitive compartments.
These findings contribute to an emerging mechanistic framework for coral nanomedicine, emphasizing how nanoparticle design can shape biological interactions in stressed symbiotic tissues. Rather than offering a single solution, this work outlines a set of nanoscale strategies with the potential to complement existing restoration approaches by interfacing directly with the oxidative processes that destabilize the coral–algal partnership. By bridging advances in nanotechnology with coral cell physiology, this research defines a pathway toward therapeutic interventions that strengthen coral resilience in a warming ocean.
e123229a: Coral reef islands are environmentally and culturally important landscapes that sustain diverse ecological assemblages and are deeply significant for indigenous and local communities. Islands, as accumulations of biogenic sediment, are commonly viewed as depositional end-products of reef systems, with their futures framed primarily in terms of instability, erosion, and loss under increasing environmental, climatic, and human pressures. This plenary positions reef islands as dynamic components of reef geo-ecological systems. It synthesizes emerging understanding of the controls on island evolution, the functional links between islands and reef ecosystems, and the implications of these interactions for conservation and adaptation planning. Key geo-ecological processes governing island formation, persistence, and change across a range of spatial and temporal scales are examined. Evidence increasingly shows that many reef islands are dynamic landforms capable of adjusting their morphology in response to environmental change, revealing a wide spectrum of future trajectories. These adjustments influence both island persistence and broader reef-system resilience. The presentation highlights research priorities needed to improve predictions of future island change and support the long-term resilience of these important social-ecological systems.
33ce3a55:
Coral reefs are oligotrophic ecosystems where resources are scarce, yet they host nearly one third of marine biodiversity. This paradox is largely explained by the symbiosis between corals and Symbiodinium, which sustains high primary production and drives nutrient transfer through the food web. Some organisms, however, rely more heavily on this coral-derived production than others. Among them are symbiotic decapod crustaceans living between the branches of several Hexacorallia, particularly pocilloporids and acroporids, which are obligate associates who depend on their coral host for survival. But is this assumption accurate? Do these decapods truly rely on their host as their main food source, and if so, do they feed exclusively on coral tissue or also on other nutrient inputs?
To address these questions, we focused on the pocilloporid coral Pocillopora acuta and its two mutualistic ectosymbionts, the crab Trapezia serenei and the snapping shrimp Alpheus lottini. We first examined the morphology of their mouthparts to infer the types of particles or tissues they could ingest. We then analyzed their stomach contents through light and scanning electron microscopy. To test whether they directly consume coral tissue, corals were experimentally dyed prior to dissecting the decapods in search of dye traces within their digestive tract. Finally, stable isotope analyses were performed to integrate dietary information over longer time scales.
Our results show that these symbionts do feed on coral material but can also subsist for several days without it, as they ingest suspended particulate organic matter. However, they do not appear to forage outside their colony: neither sediment nor nearby algal material was found to contribute to their diet.
Overall, this study clarifies the long-standing assumption that coral-dwelling decapods rely almost exclusively on their host for nutrition and reveals a more nuanced trophic strategy.
1511874c: Crown-of-thorns seastars (Acanthaster spp., COTS) are best known for driving coral mortality during population outbreaks. We tracked individual feeding scars, bare coral skeletons cleared of tissue, from a low-density (non-outbreak) population of Acanthaster cf. solaris at One Tree Reef on the Great Barrier Reef. Despite variation in coral genera and colony sizes preyed on, average feeding scar size did not differ by season or COTS size. Most scars (65%) retained living coral tissue around their perimeter and were initially colonised by turf algae, with some crustose coralline algae (CCA) present by 10 weeks post-predation. By 22 weeks, 25% of scars showed signs of regenerating coral tissue. Montipora exhibited significantly greater regenerative capacity than branching genera. In contrast, Acropora scars remained turf or CCA covered despite partial predation. These results suggest that low-density A. cf. solaris exerts moderate predation pressure across a range of coral species, often leaving viable tissue remnants. This feeding pattern may help maintain coral community diversity by limiting fast-growing taxa, consistent with the intermediate disturbance hypothesis. Consistent scar size and broad coral predation may reflect a feeding strategy of low-density COTS that allows some corals to regenerate post-predation, thereby contributing to the recovery of live coral cover.
cba08eee: Sea urchins are key herbivores on coral reefs, particularly on degraded systems. However, the limited taxonomic resolution of existing urchin dietary studies hinders comprehensive assessments of reef trophic dynamics in response to their population fluctuations. Here, we present a highly resolved diet characterisation of Diadema setosum on the degraded reefs in Singapore based on COI and 18S metabarcoding, assessing among-population variation across seven reefs. We detected 31 phyla of algae, small invertebrates, and other metazoans in their diet, with an overwhelming abundance of macro- and microalgae, indicating a strong herbivorous preference in this species. Marked spatial variation in dietary profiles was observed among populations, largely reflecting the on-site algal distribution. Sargassum emerged as the dominant diet item in most urchins, challenging prior reports of aversion by D. setosum. Additionally, our results suggest preferential feeding on Lobophora by the urchins at a site with one of the highest hard coral abundances in Singapore, implying potential top-down control on this allelopathic alga and highlighting the urchin’s role in mediating coral-algal competition. The importance of Bacillariophyta (diatoms) in their diet was also evident. Given the widespread use of sea urchins as biomonitors in pollution studies, we additionally explored the relationship between diet composition and pollutant loads in urchin tissues, including seven heavy metals and microplastics previously quantified from the same individuals. Significant correlations between metal loads in urchin gonads/spines and the read abundances of four diatom species, as well as the rhodophyte Hypnea, suggest a dietary pathway for metal uptake in D. setosum. Together, these findings provide critical insights into the ecological role of D. setosum on degraded reefs and illuminate potential routes of pollutant transfer within coral reef ecosystems.
edc822e7: Coral–bivalve symbioses are widespread yet poorly understood. In the branching coral Stylophora pistillata, populations of the boring bivalve Leiosolenus lessepsianus inhabit coral skeletons, generating micro-currents through their exhalant siphons. These currents occur within the coral’s diffusive boundary layer (DBL), a narrow, laminar zone that regulates solute exchange between coral tissues and seawater, thereby acting as a physiological bottleneck for gas transfer. Extreme diel oxygen fluctuations frequently occur within this layer, characterized by daytime hyperoxia and nighttime hypoxia, which impose metabolic stress on corals. We hypothesize that the pumping activity of boring bivalves enhances ventilation of  the coral surface, attenuating these fluctuations and potentially enhancing coral resilience, particularly under low-flow conditions typical of deeper reef environments. To test this hypothesis, oxygen dynamics were quantified in colonies of S. pistillata with high (>100 individuals/colony) and low (<10 individuals/colony) bivalve densities using optical oxygen microsensors (PyroScience OXROB10-SUB). Preliminary laboratory experiments under controlled flow (3 cm s⁻¹ – 10 cm s⁻¹) revealed that corals hosting bivalves exhibited narrower diel oxygen ranges compared to uninhabited colonies, indicating DBL stabilization by siphonal jets. Building on these results, ongoing in situ measurements at 5 m and 30 m depths near the Interuniversity Institute in Eilat combine high-resolution oxygen logging with the Gypsum Dissolution Technique to characterize intra-colony flow patterns. This study provides the first direct quantification of oxygen dynamics within coral DBLs in relation to boring bivalve activity. By linking microscale hydrodynamics with coral physiology, it sheds light on context-dependent symbiotic interactions that may shift along the parasitism–mutualism continuum, thereby contributing to coral reef resilience under changing ocean conditions.
aaf44bf3: Species of the genus Peronia Fleming, 1822, are air-breathing sea slugs distributed across the tropical and subtropical Indo-West Pacific region. They primarily inhabit intertidal reef environments, especially on hard substrates such as corals, rocks, and artificial structures, like concrete breakwaters. The Ryukyu Islands in southern Japan are currently the only known region where four Peronia species cohabit, offering a unique opportunity to investigate species coexistence, adaptation, and habitat specialization. Current identification of Peronia species mainly relies on DNA sequencing or examination of anatomical characters, and field identification remains challenging due to their cryptic external morphology. This issue limits the ability to conduct comprehensive field ecological surveys, especially in areas where multiple species coexist. To address these challenges, we developed non-invasive field DNA sampling methods for sampling their feces and using a set of mini-barcode primers designed to amplify degraded fecal DNA. A  ~600 bp DNA barcode reconstruction of the COI region was obtained from three pairs of mini-barcode primer sets designed with each primer targeting ~190–250 bp fragments. We tested the primers in silico, and from tissue and fecal samples of Peronia spp. around Okinawa Island. Preliminary results show that mini-barcode primers consistently yield clean, successful amplifications from fecal samples, whereas full-length COI primers often failed to amplify. Early sequencing results suggest that the DNA barcode reconstruction is sufficient for species-level identification of the four species of Peronia in Okinawa. This DNA mini-barcoding approach could be a promising and non-invasive alternative for investigating the distribution of Peronia spp., with the potential to enable future studies on ecology and population structure.
50a70ef4: Coral reef ecosystems are rich in biodiversity, but they have been declining in recent years. One major cause is the massive proliferation of crown-of-thorns starfish (CoTS), which prey on corals. To protect corals from CoTS predation, various control measures have been implemented across multiple marine regions. While CoTS-induced coral damage is well recognized, the molecular mechanisms by which these starfish detect coral prey are largely unknown. The aim of this study was to elucidate these mechanisms to support the development of more effective control strategies, including biological approaches, for coral reef conservation. Starfish are known to detect prey odors (chemical cues) and initiate foraging behavior. Focusing on CoTS olfaction (chemoreception), this study conducted the following: (1) identifying candidate functional olfactory receptor (OR)-like genes in CoTS, (2) performing RNA-seq-based gene expression analyses, (3) using calcium imaging to test the response of the identified chemoreceptors to coral extracts, and (4) conducting behavioral assays to examine CoTS responses to coral chemical cues. CoTS from six regions possess approximately 500 functional OR-like genes (476–511), with over 70% expressed in the olfactory organs (sensory tentacles). Expression patterns appear to vary seasonally (pre-reproductive vs. reproductive periods). Two randomly selected receptors showed responses to coral extracts smaller than 3 kDa in calcium imaging assays. Behavioral experiments using live corals and coral extracts further demonstrated that olfactory cues alone could induce coral-directed behavior. The response to chemical cues from live coral varied by season. Additionally, arm raising behavior, prior to predation, was induced when exposed to coral extracts smaller than 3 kDa. These findings suggest that CoTS have olfactory receptors that can detect coral odors, which may enable them to recognize prey and perform associated behaviors. Developing substances that target these receptors and incorporating them into control programs could lead to novel, more effective biological control strategies.
9627cc2e: The acclimation abilities of reef corals living in variable environments depend not only on their own genomic response to stressful conditions but also on the reinforcing and complementary responses of their algal symbionts. Here, we analyze the gene expression profiles from populations of Porites panamensis and its endosymbiotic Cladocopium algal communities of a hydrothermal vent in Bahia Concepción, Mexico, where temperature and pH variation are the highest in the Gulf of California. Seasonal changes (winter versus summer) triggered 12-18 times more differentially expressed genes (DEGs) for the coral host and its zooxanthellae (575 and 3312, respectively) than did proximity to hydrothermal vents (47 and 180). Weighted co-expression analysis revealed host reproduction and immunity modules were sensitive to warm summer and on-vent conditions, while nutritional shifts to lipid reserves occurred in oligotrophic winter regimes. Zooxanthellae up-regulated chloroplast biogenesis and amino acid transport in summer, while metabolism and chaperone activity were up-regulated in winter. Chaperon and ribosome sensitivity were also linked to temperature and nutrient availability in the algal symbionts. Complementary transcriptomic changes between host and zooxanthellae allowed holobiont acclimatization to the marginal environmental variability of Bahia Concepción. The complementary modular responses facilitated acclimatization to the hydrothermal system with immune and metabolic trade-offs, showing that mutualistic symbiotic relations in coral P. panamensis may prevail with reduced fitness to end of century climate change projections.
84ae07c7:
Anthropogenic CO₂ emissions are intensifying ocean acidification, with major implications for coral calcification and overall reef functioning. Yet, coral populations inhabiting naturally low-pH environments provide a valuable framework for assessing the mechanisms underlying potential acclimatization or adaptive responses. In the Palau Archipelago, we conducted an integrative physiological and biochemical assessment of Porites spp. colonies from control and naturally acidified sites, quantifying parameters associated with trophic and symbiotic status, carbon acquisition pathways, energy reserves, redox homeostasis, and calcification dynamics.
Colonies from acidified reefs displayed attenuated antioxidant defenses and reduced cellular stress markers, indicative of a physiological recalibration to sustained low-pH exposure. Isotopic signatures of host tissues and symbiotic dinoflagellates, together with decreased carbonic anhydrase activity, support a shift in inorganic carbon utilization away from bicarbonate uptake toward direct CO₂ diffusion. This shift likely relaxes the energetic demands associated with active bicarbonate transport and may facilitate the maintenance of photosynthesis, respiration, and biomass despite altered carbonate chemistry. The absence of metabolic suppression, combined with increased biomass, suggests reorganization of central metabolic pathways, potentially analogous to a Warburg-type metabolic phenotype.
However, the energetic budget conferred by modified carbon acquisition strategies appears insufficient to sustain robust calcification under acidified conditions. Although linear extension rates are maintained or enhanced, skeletal density is reduced, consistent with the increased energy expenditure required to elevate pH within the extracellular calcifying fluid. This elevated energetic demand contributes to diminished lipid reserves, potentially compromising long-term stress resilience.
Collectively, our findings demonstrate that Porites spp. exhibit substantive physiological plasticity under chronic acidification, mediated by shifts in carbon metabolism and redox regulation. Nevertheless, these adjustments entail significant trade-offs that may heighten vulnerability to future multifactorial stressors. Elucidating the metabolic pathways underpinning these responses will be essential to predicting coral persistence in rapidly changing oceans.
b5412667: Mounting evidence suggests corals native to environments with strong abiotic variability possess enhanced tolerance to those stressors, which can provide insights into how corals might cope with climate change. Yet, we lack a mechanistic understanding of how such tolerance is acquired. Moreover, it remains unknown whether experimentally preconditioning corals to environmental variability can replicate stress tolerance found in corals from variable habitats, and how stressor intensity and exposure duration combine to modulate tolerance. While research has focused on the potential for thermal preconditioning to enhance heat tolerance, ocean warming and acidification act together and can reduce calcification, casting uncertainty over the future reef-building capacity of scleractinian corals. We collected Porites furcata from two sites with distinct environmental regimes in Curaçao (southern Caribbean)—a multi-stressor, variable inland bay and more stable fringing reef. We exposed corals ex situ to four variability regimes of co-varying temperature and pH by altering stressor intensity and duration in an orthogonal design. This preconditioning period lasted 12 weeks. It was followed by a two-week warming and acidification stress test simulating future climate conditions to investigate how short-term preconditioning and long-term environmental history shape stress tolerance. We quantified calcification using buoyant weight and alkalinity anomaly measurements and assessed changes in skeletal structure using 3D photogrammetry. Preconditioning produced similar effects on corals from both origins: corals exposed to long-duration variability calcified best when the intensity remained low, while corals from high-intensity treatments calcified more when variability was brief rather than prolonged. Although calcification rates were similar among bay and reef corals after preconditioning, bay corals from all treatments maintained higher calcification after exposure to severe stress, suggesting enhanced stress tolerance. Our findings demonstrate that, in this system, long-term environmental history drives tolerance to combined warming and acidification—a tolerance that may be difficult to acquire through short-term acclimation.
5a5251f2: Ocean acidification (OA) is progressing at a rapid rate due to anthropogenic stressors that are changing the chemistry of the ocean. To date, most studies have focused on assessing how OA affect groups such as scleractinian corals. However, other competitive benthic organisms including sponges, zoantharians, and soft corals remain comparatively understudied. Including these groups in surveys is essential for improving our understanding of how reef ecosystems could change under OA conditions. Recently, natural analogue sites, which have naturally low pH levels emulating or exceeding future ocean conditions, have been used to predict how reefs may respond to OA. In this study, we examined the natural analogue site of Ngerikuul (Nikko) Bay, Palau (pH =~7.8), focusing on the coverage of mainly five benthic groups: hard corals, soft corals, macroalgae, sponges, and zoantharians. Visual transects were conducted along six 10 m transects across nine sites at 4-5 m depths, and environmental DNA (eDNA) water samples were collected from each of these sites and from one additional location. Surveys were also conducted outside Ngerikuul (Nikko) Bay, which served as the reference site with ambient pH conditions (pH =~8.0). Subsequently, eDNA samples were analyzed using 12S, ITS2, and CO1 markers. Results showed that the dominant group in both low pH sites and the reference sites was hard corals. However, interesting patterns emerged in other taxa such as the inverse relationship between sponges and algae, where sponges were more abundant and algae were less prominent in low pH sites, and vice versa. Additionally, taxonomic observations using eDNA showed the absence of calcareous sponges inside the bay compared to outside. Such findings from natural analogue sites will help inform which benthic taxa may persist or become more dominant on reefs as pH in the ocean continues to decrease into the future.
4bf1ded0: The symbiotic relationship between reef-building corals and Symbiodiniaceae is threatened by environmental stressors such as ocean warming and acidification, reducing coral primary productivity. Corals from highly variable environments often show greater stress resilience. Although both stressor intensity and duration have been implicated in this increased tolerance, their relative contributions remain unknown. Furthermore, it remains poorly understood whether enhanced stress tolerance can also be achieved when two stressors co-vary, and whether preconditioning to variability can modulate stress tolerance to the same degree in coral populations from environmentally variable vs more stable habitats. In this study, we examined how co-varying temperature and pH variability (combinations of intensity and duration) affected the photophysiology of Porites furcata originating either from a variable semi-enclosed inland bay or a stable fringing reef habitat in Curaçao (southern Caribbean). After first preconditioning corals to four orthogonally designed variable temperature and pH regimes for 12 weeks, they were exposed to combined heat and acidification stress for 14 days. Responses were assessed through PAM fluorometry, spectral reflectance, respirometry, and analyses of symbiont density and chlorophyll content. We observed acclimatory responses at the end of the preconditioning phase, including changes in optical tissue properties, symbiont density, and chlorophyll content, highlighting both the flexibility and fragility of the coral symbiosis in adjusting to environmental change. After short-term heat-acidification stress, we observed bleaching in corals from both habitats, but corals from the variable bay displayed higher resistance. While preconditioning did not enhance tolerance, exposure to high-intensity temperature and pH variation during preconditioning increased stress susceptibility in reef but not bay corals. These results underscore the significance of both long- and short-term environmental histories in coral stress resilience, but highlight that short-term acclimation and adaptive strategies provide ineffective protection against rapid environmental change compared to long-term life-history acclimatization of corals in their native habitats.
241523e0:
Nearshore reefs at environmental margins, shaped by turbidity, variable water chemistry, and proximity to mangrove habitats, offer natural laboratories for understanding coral responses to future ocean conditions. Pioneer Bay, Orpheus Island, is one such multi-stressor system, where corals inhabit chronically turbid, mangrove-influenced waters and experience large diel fluctuations in temperature, pH, oxygen, and salinity. In early 2025, this already variable environment was subjected to an acute, extreme disturbance: record-breaking rainfall (3,116 mm) coinciding with a thermal anomaly.

Using three consecutive years of reef-crest surveys (2023 to 2025), we quantified how coral abundance, cover, richness, and benthic composition shifted before and after the event. Coral abundance and richness peaked in 2024 but collapsed in 2025, with richness declining by up to 82% at some sites. Bleaching and mortality affected all major taxa, resulting in the near complete loss of branching corals and significant declines in the health and abundance of massive genera. This loss of stress-sensitive corals led to a significant shift in community structure, accompanied by rapid turf-algal colonisation of newly dead skeletons, with cover increasing from 40% in 2024 to 83% in 2025. 

Flood-plume exposure produced salinity as low as 10 ppt, far below natural diel variation for this system, and likely acted synergistically with elevated temperature, turbidity, and sedimentation to precipitate widespread bleaching and mortality. The event also caused 100% mortality of a nearby giant clam population established more than 40 years ago, underscoring its severity and ecological reach.

These results demonstrate how acute, superimposed climate extremes in naturally variable, marginal-extreme habitats can drive rapid community reorganisation, offering insights into the limits of coral persistence under future multi-stressor regimes. As global change intensifies, long-term observations from such extreme systems are essential for understanding coral acclimation potential, identifying thresholds of collapse, and anticipating ecosystem trajectories in the Anthropocene.
3d3a6679: Coral skeletons form the foundational framework of coral reef ecosystems but can become increasingly porous and fragile under climate change stress, raising concerns of sublethal effects under future ocean conditions. There are however some extreme and marginal environments, such as mangrove habitats, where corals are found to maintain skeletal formation despite the presence of sub-optimal water conditions. Understanding how the structure of coral skeletons will be impacted by multiple, co-occurring climate change stressors and how different coral species will respond is of paramount importance to understand the future of coral reefs. Here, we examined the skeletal structure of corals living in a mangrove system. Mangrove environments provide a natural setting to examine the long-term effects of multiple environmental stressors on the skeletons of resident corals as well as identify mechanisms that corals may adopt to survive. Using micro-computed tomography, we investigated the effects of environmental conditions ranging from mangrove habitat to open reef on the skeletal structure of Pocillopora acuta, Acropora cf. millepora and Montipora cf. digitata, in the northern Great Barrier Reef. Corals were collected from sites with differing distance from the mangrove system at Low Isles. Species-specific structural changes were recorded in coral skeletons in response to distance from the mangroves. P. acuta had more porous and thinner skeletons closer to the mangroves, where seawater conditions were sub-optimal. Montipora cf. digitata was able to maintain a robust skeleton in the mangrove environment. This coral also possessed more dispersed but larger corallites at these sites, potentially availing of increased food availability in mangroves as a mechanism to support skeletal growth. This study suggests the integrity of coral skeletons will be compromised by climate change stress but that some coral species may be able to utilise mechanisms to maintain normal skeletal growth under sub-optimal conditions.
7bb3fddf: Intertidal coral habitats, characterized by highly variable and often extreme conditions, are understudied marginal environments. While the distribution of intertidal corals has been documented across natural reef flats, zonation patterns are typically described in relation to the horizontal arrangement of species assemblages. How these patterns are shaped by the duration and frequency of emersion stress across vertical elevations is often overlooked and remains poorly understood. Further, to our knowledge, coral vertical distribution has not yet been examined on artificial coastal defences— which represent a rapidly expanding but ecologically distinct habitat. Compared to natural habitats, these man-made structures typically exhibit steeper profiles, limiting area for colonization and intensifying competition. This makes them an interesting system for examining how coral communities are shaped across an environmental gradient in habitats characterized by high competition. In this study, we conducted 3 × 20 m transect surveys across eight seawall sites along Singapore’s mainland and southern islands. Real-time GPS measurements paired with theodolite readings enabled precise elevation data to be recorded for every coral colony. Results will allow species-specific vertical zonation patterns to be identified, offering insight into which taxa can colonise and persist in the most stressful portions of the intertidal zone. These findings will deepen our understanding on how intertidal coral communities are structured in these novel habitats characterised by both high physiological stress and strong competitive interactions.
a2a49c24: Karstic island reefscapes are common across Southeast Asia. Limestone cliffs rise hundreds of meters above fringing reefs and cast persistent shadows that alter underwater light environments. While light availability is a major driver of coral photo-physiology and community structure, the ecological implications of natural cliff-induced shading on corals remain poorly understood. Here we investigate the effects of cliff shading on light and temperature regimes, to understand how benthic communities respond to these changes across a consistent depth range. Lagen Cove located in Palawan, Philippines, is a shallow reef bordered by two karst cliffs. Three reef zones were delineated based on the duration and extent of daily shadow exposure and photosynthetic active radiation (PAR) loggers were deployed to quantify light availability within these areas. Mean daily light integrals (DLI) revealed that shaded zones received only 37–44% of the light reaching the Central Bay. Overall, shaded sites experienced ~50% shorter light duration, with light peaks restricted to midday. Temperature profiles closely mirrored light patterns, suggesting shaded zones are also cool-water microhabitats. Benthic surveys showed highest coral cover in the Central Bay, decreasing toward the shaded zones, while the opposite pattern was observed for algae and soft corals. Orthomosaics revealed clusters of bleached Fungiidae colonies within shaded areas, possibly reflecting lateral migration to escape light and heat stress during a period of thermal bleaching. Our findings demonstrate that cliff shading generates distinct horizontal zonation patterns on reefs, independent of depth. These naturally shaded reefs may also function as local refugia under thermal stress, underscoring the need to prioritise geomorphically shaded habitats in management and conservation frameworks.
c6306494: Coral populations persist through processes of survivorship, growth, and recruitment, which together describe demographic trends. Many recent studies document coral demographic patterns under extreme external forcing, such as responses to marine heat waves that result in widespread mortality. However, such reports often fail to address baseline patterns of populations under natural, stochastic change. New advances, particularly photogrammetry, enhance our capacity to investigate colony-specific change across reefs. In this study, we used large-area imagery products to track 1,000 coral colonies spanning a 10-year interval across five geographies: Curaçao, the Florida Keys, Maui, Millennium Atoll, and Palmyra Atoll. Coral colonies were digitized in Taglab, an AI-assisted software, to quantify radial change across four timepoints. These demographic trends then enabled us to contextualize overall changes in the benthic cover of major functional groups. Benthic communities remained stable across all locations; however, the autogenic pathways contributing to that stability varied between regions. Through this approach, we demonstrated distinct pathways of reef persistence. Most colonies survived and continued to grow through multiple transition periods of the study. With the exception of Millennium Atoll, corals experienced a 90% survivorship rate. Additionally, radial growth rates varied among major coral taxa and regions, yet colony-specific changes showed notable similarities in their distributions across years. This study highlights the variability of baseline coral growth and survival through a multi-region analysis of long-term monitoring initiatives. Together, these insights exemplify the natural capacity for reef persistence during a time of rapid environmental change.
bbb7ebbd:
Corals exhibit remarkable diversity in their vital rates (growth, survival, and shrinkage) dependent on taxonomic identity, colony size, and environmental conditions. Understanding how this demographic diversity changes temporally and shapes reef trajectories under escalating thermal stress remains a critical challenge.  

Here, we quantify the annual vital rates of >24,000 individually tracked coral colonies over 4 years across Australia’s Great Barrier Reef (GBR). Large-area 3D photogrammetry was used to generate spatially co-registered, scaled 2D orthomosaics for 352 permanently marked plots each year from 2021-2024. Coral colonies within orthomosaics were digitised to quantify changes in planar area and survival through time. We tested for variation in vital rates across nine coral taxa, a range of colony sizes, and across environmental gradients associated with structural complexity, depth, exposure, shelf position and latitude. Moreover, our survey period encompassed two major bleaching events: regional bleaching in the central GBR in 2022 and widespread bleaching across the full latitudinal range in 2024. 

This talk showcases the spatial, temporal and taxonomic diversity in coral vital rates at unprecedented scales, including responses of individuals to repeated heat stress. Broad analyses reveal marked variation in colony fates among taxa, colony sizes, environments and years, partly driven by bleaching events within our survey period. Focusing on colonies in the central GBR that experienced heatwaves in 2022 and 2024, we show how individual bleaching histories shape subsequent fates, including changing bleaching susceptibility, altered growth rates, and mortality likelihood. These findings reveal the demographic pathways through which repeated heat stress reshapes assemblages, identifying the environments and coral groups most likely to contribute to recovery versus those at greatest risk of decline. This integration of digital imagery with demographic modelling allows individual vital rates to be quantified across multiple disturbances, offering critical insights for predicting assemblage-level responses and future reef trajectories.
4b446a4a: Life history rates determine resistance to as well as the pace of recovery from disturbances. The main axis of variation in life histories is the fast-slow continuum: organisms are sorted according to investment in recruitment (a fast life history associated to quick recovery from disturbance events) vs. investment in survival (a slow life history whereby organisms withstand disturbances). Simultaneously quantifying life history variation among coral species through multiple disturbance events, allows mapping coral species into the fast-slow continuum and provides essential information to allow forecasting reef futures. Assisted by orthomosaics obtained through structure-from-motion photogrammetry, we have tracked the recruitment and survival of over 70,000 coral colonies, in 21 sites around Jiigurru (also known as Lizard Island) in the Northern Great Barrier Reef, Australia from 2016 to 2025. Here we partition variation in these two demographic rates, across the >300 species recorded, as well as across life stages. We examine evidence for trade-offs in the two demographic rates, and the distribution of species along the fast-slow axis of life history variation. Finally, we contextualise these demographic differences with variation of the same rates in space and time. Our quantification of demographic variation in coral assemblages can be used to scale up modelling frameworks to predict future trajectories of coral reef ecosystems.
84bf4093: Caribbean coral reefs are undergoing rapid ecological change driven by shifts in herbivore communities, habitat structure, and disturbance regimes. Understanding how these factors influence coral demographic rates is essential for predicting long-term population persistence. Here, we use Integral Projection Models (IPMs) to quantify the size-structured population dynamics of two coral species with different life-history traits, Porites astreoides and Diploria labyrinthiformis, across Culebra, Puerto Rico. Our analysis spans 2021–2023 and captures the 2022 Diadema antillarum die-off, which caused abrupt reductions in urchin density. We parameterized species-specific survival, growth, and reproduction models using colony-level measurements from orthoprojected large-area images and integrated three key covariates directly into the IPM kernels: (1) Diadema density, (2) overall herbivore abundance (including fish and invertebrate grazers), and (3) plot-level (100 m2) rugosity as a proxy for habitat structural complexity. By including these predictors in the vital rate functions, we evaluated how changes in grazing intensity and physical structure shape population growth rate (λ) and demographic resilience for each species. Our results reveal strong and species-specific demographic responses to the loss of Diadema antillarum. Declines in Diadema density following the die-off significantly reduced survival probabilities and growth rates in both coral species. Rugosity variance, representing within-plot seascape heterogeneity, exerted stronger positive effects on λ than the overall mean. Together, these findings highlight the critical role of herbivore function and habitat complexity in buffering coral populations against disturbance-driven shifts in benthic dynamics. Incorporating covariates into IPMs provides a powerful forecasting framework for evaluating how interacting biotic and abiotic drivers shape coral population trajectories in a rapidly changing Caribbean seascapes.
4b87b2f7: Range shifts and altered dominance patterns of habitat-forming species are changing the seascape structure of temperate marine ecosystems. Yet, the role of intraspecific interactions in mediating establishment and growth of newly arriving populations at range-edges remains poorly understood, limiting predictions of persistence in marginal habitats. We examined the spatial demography of the coral Pocillopora aliciae at its poleward range limit in Sydney, Australia (34°S), quantifying the spatiotemporal dynamics of adults and juveniles over five years to identify demographic mechanisms underpinning coral establishment and local expansion on temperate rocky reefs. Structure-from-motion photogrammetry was applied between 2019 and 2024 to quantify changes in density, size structure, and spatial configuration of corals. Corals were segmented from large-area images and spatial structure assessed using point pattern analyses with null model comparisons. Over five years, P. aliciae expanded its cover from 26 m2 to 73 m2 (180% increase) by forming dense clusters of large corals, with mean coral size increasing more than threefold (57 cm2 to 201 cm2). This growth led to space limitation within clusters and reduced juvenile abundance. Spatial associations between adults and juveniles reflected episodic recruitment and subsequent competitive exclusion by larger corals, with fusion likely contributing to cluster consolidation. Expansion was driven primarily by the persistence and growth of established corals rather than continuous recruitment. These findings suggest that rapid growth, brooding reproduction, and spatial clustering likely promote P. aliciae establishment on temperate reefs of eastern Australia, highlighting the importance of intraspecific interactions in shaping poleward range-edge dynamics.
699a6a58: Coral reefs are experiencing an alarming increase in the frequency and intensity of widespread disturbance events which impact both the habitat forming corals as well as associated assemblages. While our understanding of the drivers and consequences of these events continues to grow, there appears to be considerable variability associated with environmental and geographic context.  One Tree Reef, located on the southern end of the Great Barrier Reef, experienced a marine heat wave in the austral summer of 2024 which prompted wide-spread coral bleaching. This study used a combination of large-area 3D mapping of permanent sites, underwater visual census surveys and remote underwater video to track the fate of thousands of coral colonies at multiple time points through the event and evaluate the effects of this change in benthic cover on the associated abundance and behaviour (feeding rates and aggression) of corallivore assemblages (chiefly butterflyfish). Colony fate tracking indicated overall high levels of mortality across the sites but with taxa-specific state-transition dynamics. Butterflyfish assemblages were generally resilient to the effects of the change in benthic cover though changes in abundance and feeding behaviour were most pronounced for the obligate corallivores when compared to the facultative ones. Overall, this study provides an extremely robust and high-resolution assessment of the effects of a major marine heat wave on the fate of individual coral colonies and suggests a reasonably high level of resilience of butterfly fishes to these changes, at least over the course of approximately one year from the onset of the event.
d8a23ca4: Time series structure-from-motion (SFM) three-dimensional (3D) models of underwater reef images were created from repurposed historical image datasets (RHID) collected during regular monitoring in Lian, Batangas, Philippines from 2009 to 2025. The method’s novelty is its successful repurposing of data to create 3D coral models and extract surface rugosity as a 3D structure component. These datasets were primarily captured using the photo quadrat method with different cameras at variable water conditions and very limited number of images per data collection yet structure component indices were validated in situ with an accuracy of 0.97. Results showed similar trend between the rugosity and coral cover reflecting a high potential for RHID 3D structure components to proxy coral cover as a measure of reef recovery or decline over time.
2d7c0162: There are mountains of evidence that humans have caused the deaths of vast numbers of coral colonies; the evidence is undeniable.  Graphs of monitoring data for areas around the world are reviewed here.  Florida and the Caribbean have suffered huge declines of live coral cover.  Many areas in the Indo-Pacific have also suffered huge declines.  In Florida and the Caribbean, corals have not recovered.  But in the Indo-Pacific, most (but not all) have recovered.  The world’s reefs (as a whole) have not suffered huge declines in live coral cover because most reefs are in the Indo-Pacific, and most of those have recovered or not declined. There are several caveats, such as the fact that coral cover does not document coral community change, and the fact that most compilations of regional monitoring data do not extend to this year.  Past results do not predict the future, because impacts of all kinds are increasing, and especially, global warming-caused bleaching is a huge, rapidly growing threat.  But statements that imply that the world’s coral reefs are mostly dead are greatly exaggerated.
50fcdb77: Reassembly of biological communities to their pre-disturbance configuration is the epitome of resilience.
