As such, reassembly remains a common aspirational target among ecologists and managers. However, reassembly is just one of many possible community reorganisation pathways during recovery, casting doubt on the rationality of this aspiration. Here, we characterise the probability of post-disturbance reassembly to a reference community composition across 72 reefs of the Great Barrier Reef over the past 30 years. Reassembly was uncommon, occurring for 43% of recovery trajectories. A low probability of reassembly was facilitated by a wide variety of scenarios, while a high probability of reassembly was only facilitated when a narrow combination of conditions are met: 1) the first or second recovery cycle since the reference period 2) less than 21 years since the end of the reference period, 3) post-disturbance coral cover > 50% and 4) post-disturbance community at least 84% similarity in composition compared to the reference community. Notably, the probability of reassembly dramatically declines 10 years after the reference period. Community reassembly may be an unreasonable aspiration in the management of contemporary, complex ecosystems, unless there is additional evidence that the new assembly represents long lasting declines in ecosystem functioning.
a21965e4: Here we synthesize large-scale, long-term monitoring data to highlight patterns of differential resilience in the wild and to explore associations that set resilient coral reefs apart. We synthesize two specific datasets from NOAA’s National Coral Reef Monitoring Program - (1): live coral cover trends from 8,745 surveys spanning 2010-2024 from 37 central and western Pacific islands, and (2) demographic models including growth, survival and recruitment of over 10,000 coral colonies (over 15,000 unique colony-level transitions) for four dominant genera at 41 sites spanning 2013 to 2024. For the cover data (1), we first minimize the size of survey reporting zones by applying a novel hierarchical contiguous clustering technique, resulting in 181 sub-island zones. This analysis shows a mosaic of resilient and declining reefs, in which 38% of zones show Favorable coral cover trends (i.e. either increasing or maintaining high cover) and 35% show Adverse trends (declining or maintaining low cover). Given these fine-scale differential reef outcomes, we applied boosted regression tree models (BRT) to assess potential associations of zones Favorable to coral cover with 20 environmental and ecological variables. For the demographic models (2), we developed site-specific growth, mortality and recruitment models into integral projection models capable of predicting rates of coral population growth. We then repeated our BRT modeling to assess associations with 20 potential correlates, highlighting environmental and ecological patterns associated with robust population growth. In a decade and region characterized by severe marine heat wave disturbances, these models show robust associations with drivers more relevant to recovery than to those relevant to refuge from or resistance to disturbance. These associations suggest that a management focus on recovery processes may be critical to maintain these resilient remnants.
55f978db:
Resilience Dynamics and Cross-Habitat Synchrony Reveal Ecological Insurance Mechanisms in Mo’orea’s Coral Reefs

Bellini S1, Aqua A1, Boitel C1, Siu G1, Chancerelle Y1, Hédouin L1

1 UAR3278-CNRS EPHE UPVD-CRIOBE, BP1013 98729 Papetoai, Moorea, French Polynesia 


Coral reefs are collapsing, yet some systems persist. Understanding how these reefs sustain is now a central challenge for conservation. Using a unique 25-year island-scale monitoring program in Mo’orea, French Polynesia, covering three habitats (fore-reef, fringing reef and barrier reef), we examined long-term changes in coral communities, recovery trajectories and synchrony patterns among habitats and genera, to identify mechanisms that drive resilience. 
 Coral dynamics differed markedly among habitats. The fore-reef exhibited a strong resilience signature, recovering in nine years after multiple disturbances, whereas the lagoonal reefs remained stable. 
Much of this recovery was driven by Pocillopora (up to 90% of post-disturbance recovery), that acted as a pioneer facilitating later recruitment of additional taxa, and generating a delayed successional pattern similar to forest dynamics. Nonetheless, we identified a threshold: above 42% of Pocillopora cover, the establishment of other genera was inhibited. Similar Pocillopora-driven resilience patterns also emerged across other Polynesian islands, suggesting regional consistency.

Analyses revealed strong within-habitat synchrony—especially on the fore-reef—showing that sites within a given habitat tend to respond simultaneously to disturbances, increasing their vulnerability to synchronous collapses. Conversely, cross-habitat asynchrony created an ecological insurance effect: habitats responded differently through time and acted as larval sources, potentially maintaining island-scale stability through response diversity.
When focusing solely on Pocillopora, trajectories were more synchronous across habitats, suggesting that dominant genus respond coherently to regional driver, while community-level diversity maintains response heterogeneity. 
Together, these results pinpoint key habitat and genera that contribute to resilience, and reveal how habitat diversity stabilizes coral communities, offering a powerful framework to prioritize reefs that maintain resilience in a warming ocean.
6808de05: The long-projected global increases in tropical water temperatures and resulting coral thermal stress are now a reality.  In the 27 years since the first global coral bleaching event in 1997-98 bleaching has affected nearly all shallow water reefs.  In the Caribbean coral abundance, typically measured as areal coverage, has declined precipitously in this same period.  In addition to thermal stress various other stressors have been implicated in this decline, including disease epizootics, land-based sources of pollution, overfishing, and other local stressors.  We used repeated annual coral reef assessments at 20 reef sites in the US Virgin Islands across a gradient of depths (shallow to mesophotic), distance to shore, and protected status to understand the degree of change between 2005 and 2024.  We were able to show disturbances in the time series from abrupt declines in coral cover versus periods with no disturbances and gradual change, attribute the proportion of reef change caused by chronic stress and specific disturbances, and determine the stressor most responsible for driving reef decline.  Overall site-level coral cover declined from 22.8% ± 3.0 SEM to 4.8% ± 0.78 between 2005 and 2025, with the largest absolute declines occurring during the 2005 bleaching event (-9.4% ± 1.6), the epidemic phase of stony coral tissue loss disease from 2019 – 2022 (-3.9% ± 1.2), the 2023 bleaching event (-1.8% ± 0.4), and the 2024 bleaching event (-0.9% ± 0.3).  Thermal stress and bleaching caused more than three quarters of the coral cover decline in this 20 year period, an overwhelming impact relative to other modern threats to coral reefs.  Increasing ocean temperatures and persistent marine heat waves will continue to suppress meaningful recovery and are driving western Atlantic coral reefs into new configurations where living stony corals are no longer major contributors to benthic processes.
c121a9fd: Juvenile corals (<4 cm) underpin coral reef recovery, yet comparable information on juvenile dynamics across countries and taxa remains scarce across the Caribbean. Here, we draw on juvenile coral data contributed by partners across the MarineGEO monitoring network to describe broad scale patterns in juvenile densities through time. Overall, juvenile densities appear remarkably stable, with evidence for a gradual regional increase over the period 2018–2024. This suggests that, despite repeated marine heatwaves and other disturbances, the supply and survivorship of coral recruits has not collapsed at the regional scale and may even be strengthening modestly in some areas. Juveniles, however, are not evenly distributed among coral taxa. A small group of stress-tolerant or opportunistic growth forms (e.g. Siderastrea siderea, Porites astreoides) account for a large fraction of observed juveniles, while most slow-growing, framework-building, or naturally rarer taxa contribute little to the overall juvenile pool. Temporal trends also vary among locations: some sites show increasing juvenile densities through time, others show relatively stable trajectories, and a subset exhibit recent declines. Taken together, these emerging patterns suggest that regional juvenile densities have been broadly maintained or slightly increasing, but that this recovery potential is disproportionately carried by a limited suite of resilient taxa. This raises concerns about whether current juvenile supply is sufficient to sustain the full diversity and structural complexity of Caribbean reef assemblages under ongoing climate stress.
71e2a5a0:
Long-term studies are essential for guiding conservation efforts, as coral reefs are experiencing increasingly severe thermal stress and stronger typhoon impacts. This study assesses the effects of accumulated heat stress and typhoons on hard coral cover (HCC) and diversity at 14 monitoring stations in Talim Bay, located on the western coast of Luzon Island, over a 15-year period. A total of 14 degree heating weeks and over 20 typhoons have affected the reef stations since 2010.
	
The outer bay stations (n=8) recovered and surpassed the 2010 pre-disturbance HCC and TAU richness states, whereas the inner bay stations (n=6) did not recover. In both outer and inner bay stations, stable levels of cover and richness could be observed after initial shifts in coral composition. Some outer and most inner bay stations were initially dominated by corals of the Family Acroporidae. Currently, all outer bay stations were dominated by Family Poritidae.  Shifts to corals of the Family Poritidae and Family Merulinidae were observed in some inner bay stations.

Some outer and inner bay stations remained unchanged despite experiencing thermal stress and typhoons. On the other hand, some stations that showed declining HCC and TAU richness after a disturbance also showed recovery in the succeeding monitoring periods. The inner bay stations experienced more thermal stress and typhoon impacts as compared to the outer bay stations.

All reef stations sustained consecutive disturbances. The outer bay reefs, which face the southwest monsoon, showed continuous periods of recovery, while the sheltered inner reefs showed recovery but have not surpassed the 2010 pre-disturbance states. These results suggest potential priority reefs for conservation efforts.
c384f654:
Long-term time series are essential for detecting gradual or episodic ecological change and for informing evidence-based marine protected area (MPA) management. This study presents a 12-year assessment (2013–2024) of live hard coral cover (LHCC) in the Nusa Penida MPA, Bali, Indonesia, using Point Intercept Transects (PIT) at 3 m and 10 m depth across 14 permanent stations. Temporal trends were analysed using linear and polynomial regression, supported by Generalized Additive Models (GAM) to identify non-linear shifts and ecological inflection points.
Mean LHCC across the MPA was 34.09%, representing medium condition (25–50%) with strong spatial variability. Core zone reefs showed diverging trajectories: Atuh Bay (45.61%) exhibited steady increase, likely aided by natural bay protection, low tourism intensity, and early recognition as a resilient site. In contrast, Tanjung Samuh (44.27%) declined gradually, potentially linked to rising boat pressure and current exposure, indicating risk despite core-zone designation. Mangrove Point—special marine tourism zone—maintained the highest LHCC (51.15%), likely supported by ALKI-driven upwelling that enhances productivity. Ped (32.19%), a sacred site, showed shallow-water fluctuation but increasing deeper-water cover. Traditional fisheries zones—Suwehan (23.69%) and Batununggul (39.06%)—displayed high variability driven by alternating current regimes. Marine tourism sites, including Crystal Bay, Gamat Bay, Manta Bay, and Manta Point, remained highly dynamic, while upwelling-influenced Suana and Lembongan Bay sustained relatively stable LHCC.
Change-point detection revealed system-wide shifts in 2019 (post-flood disturbance) and 2022 (post-pandemic recovery). Polynomial regression and GAM indicated no monotonic trend—declines aligned with increasing tourism pressure, while pandemic-period reductions coincided with recovery.
These results demonstrate that Nusa Penida reefs remain dynamic but moderately resilient. Continued long-term monitoring is critical to refine zoning, manage visitor intensity, and strengthen climate-resilient reef management.
f6019d86:
Successful coral reproduction is critical to the persistence of coral reefs. Variation in spawning timing among species and locations is well documented but the underlying drivers across environmental gradients are still not fully understood.

Peninsular Malaysia offers a remarkable opportunity to examine how spawning seasonalities are linked to contrasting environmental conditions. Bi-annual multi-specific spawning events have been observed in March/April and September/October. The region holds an extraordinary species diversity and is exposed to two contrasting monsoonal influences on the west and east coast. The peninsula acts as a land barrier between the Andaman Sea and the South China Sea, coral populations are reproductively isolated and potentially on different evolutionary trajectories. 

Here, we investigated differences in coral spawning timing of Acropora bifurcata and Acropora intermedia on both coasts of Peninsular Malaysia. We first confirmed species identity through phylogenetic analysis, morphological metrics and reproductive traits. We then evaluated timing of spawning based on maturity assessment of 70 tagged colonies and in-situ maturity checks. Finally, we explored how variations in sea temperature, insolation, turbidity, wind, and rainfall are associated with spawning behaviour. Data were collected over two years (2024/2025) with two sampling periods per annum in the inter-monsoonal seasons. 

Our results show alternating spawning seasons between the two coasts. The main spawning time for both species is in March on the west coast and September on the east coast. There is a strong influence of environmental factors related to monsoon cycles. Phylogenetics revealed differences between taxa on both coasts but within an expected range of intraspecific variation. Morphological differences were significant for single traits suggesting environmental plasticity.

Our study is an opportunity to disentangle drivers of seasonal variations in coral reproductive phenology, particularly temperature and insolation. These insights can help us identify impacts on fertilisation success due to climate change.
cd09089e: Lunar cycles have long been linked to the timing of reproductive events in fishes, yet the strength, consistency and mechanisms of these patterns remain unclear across taxa and habitats. We conducted a systematic review of published studies examining lunar-related spawning behaviours in marine and freshwater fishes. Across 249 species from 390 studies, three broad patterns emerged. First, lunar synchrony is the most widespread rhythm among fishes. While the timing of spawning peaks is quite variable, patterns are moderately conserved within broad taxonomic groups. Second, the drivers of this timing differ among species, with evidence for higher prevalence of semi-lunar periodicity in species and locations with greater tidal amplitudes, and greater prevalence of lunar periodicity is species that aggregate to spawn. Third, the strength of lunar periodicity within species is associated with a range of other egg and larval traits, indicative of different reproductive strategies among fishes. Overall, the review highlights that lunar influences on spawning are context-dependent rather than universal, and that future work must integrate behavioural, environmental and physiological data to further resolve underlying mechanisms. These insights refine our understanding of reproductive timing in fishes and inform management strategies for species whose spawning aggregations coincide with predictable lunar windows.
f727195e:
Disruption of natural sunlight and moonlight cycles by Artificial Light at Night (ALAN) and ocean darkening threatens a core biological process on coral reefs—synchronized broadcast spawning. This synchronization depends on daily (sunlight) and monthly (moonlight) cues, yet, it remains unknown whether it is mediated by a direct response to environmental cues or by an endogenous clock. Testing this is challenging because the long reproductive cycles of reef-building corals hinder free-running experiments that reveal self-sustained rhythmicity. To address this, we exposed the sea anemone Aiptasia —a cnidarian model with a monthly reproductive cycle— to light modulation experiments to determine how daily and monthly rhythms interact to regulate spawning. We monitored
basal metabolism of three genotypes (CC7, F003, and H2) by measuring photosynthesis/irradiance curves, respiration, and daily kinetics of photosystem II photochemical efficiency. Reproductive activity was quantified through spawning frequency, gamete production, fertilization success, and asexual reproduction, revealing distinct genotype-specific differences. Furthermore, we are currently analyzing gene expression and luciferase assays to investigate putative clock genes, testing whether regulation follows conventional circadian mechanisms or distinct evolutionary pathways. Our results clarify the relative roles of endogenous and exogenous control in synchronized spawning, providing molecular insights into cnidarian reproductive timing. Understanding how cnidarians interpret solar and lunar cues is critical for predicting the impacts of ALAN and ocean darkening on reproductive success. These findings establish Aiptasia as a tractable model for mechanistic studies of light-driven reproduction and will offer a foundation for assessing the consequences of altered marine lightscapes under global change.
252530ba: Reef building corals are in steady decline in the United States Virgin Islands (USVI) and throughout the Caribbean due to anthropogenic disturbance, climate change, and most recently, stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD). Assisted sexual reproduction is a cost-effective technique for repopulation that maintains genetic diversity in corals and builds reef resilience. Programs conducting assisted sexual reproduction of corals at The Nature Conservancy, St. Croix, and at the University of the Virgin Islands, St. Thomas, have monitored spawning of the SCTLD-susceptible brain corals Diploria labyrinthiformis and Pseudodiploria strigosa since 2020 and 2023, respectively. The USVI and wider Caribbean region experienced the most intense thermal stress and mass bleaching on record in 2023 and again in 2024, the repercussions of which are still being uncovered. In 2024, near complete failure of spawning was observed for both species across the USVI. This report serves as a record of spawning observations between 2020-2025 to show that both species were observed releasing gametes in years prior, released few or no gametes in 2024, and had mixed success in 2025.  These observations suggest a potential new threat to reefs in the failure to spawn seasonally, likely due to an energy tradeoff in response to stressors including increased temperatures and bleaching. Spawning observations are combined with temperature logs, extensive bleaching surveys, and histology samples to document and better understand the extent to which extreme thermal stress can influence the long-term reproductive capacity of coral reefs. Declining coral cover and the increasing frequency and intensity of thermal stress events observed in the Caribbean indicate that natural recruitment may not be sufficient to maintain coral populations, leaving “ghost reefs” of nonreproductive corals, still standing but failing to disperse or recruit new generations of offspring.
dfe288b6: Coral reefs are facing global decline from anthropogenic-driven climate stressors; most coral species rely on large-scale, synchronous spawning events with high fertilization success to increase the recruitment of genetically diverse juveniles to reefs. The longest observations of Caribbean orbicellid coral spawning were recorded at Hospital Point near Bocas del Toro, Panama. Drs. Don Levitan and Nancy Knowlton established a transect and monitored three species of Orbicellid corals from 1998 to 2013; however, the transect remained unmonitored for the last decade. This study revisited this long-term transect in 2024 to: (1) assess coral cover, (2) determine whether the magnitude and synchrony of coral spawning at Hospital Point has changed within a species or across years, and (3) record environmental parameters to understand their potential role in long-term reproductive patterns. Using historic tags and maps approximately 50% of the corals on the transect were identified, and biomass was assessed using a combination of in situ measurements and extrapolated data from photographs using ImageJ. Orbicella annularis, O. franksi, and O. faveolata were monitored for spawning 2–8 nights after the full moon. Data collected in 2024 indicates significant partial coral mortality, a heavily reduced magnitude and quality of spawning, and a potential shift in spawning times compared to historic data. These findings could be attributed to environmental factors, such as increased ocean temperatures, changes in light intensity and spectrum from turbidity, and/or the presence of endogenous/exogenous sex steroids. Furthermore, the reduction in coral biomass may result in a strong Allee effect, in which fertilization success is reduced due to mate or gamete limitation. Determining whether changes in the timing and magnitude of annual reproduction events are occurring will help our understanding of how a changing environment and lowered population density will impact the reef ecosystem’s natural recovery.
78e122ac: Understanding the ecological and evolutionary processes that generate coral biodiversity is essential for predicting reef resilience in a rapidly changing ocean. While geographic isolation has traditionally been viewed as the primary mechanism driving allopatric speciation, emerging evidence from Western Australia highlights the important role of reproductive phenology in promoting allochronic speciation. Many Western Australian reefs display both autumn and spring spawning periods, yet the extent to which local environmental conditions shape these patterns remains underexplored. Here we show that fine-scale environmental gradients can produce markedly different reproductive outcomes at adjacent sites. Ningaloo Reef is a high-energy fringing reef exposed to Indian Ocean swell and persistent south-westerly winds, whereas nearby Bundegi Reef in Exmouth Gulf is a sheltered and turbid embayment protected by the North-West Cape. Despite their proximity, coral reproductive phenology differed sharply in spring. At Bundegi, 31% of species exhibited mature gametes, compared with only 5% at wave-exposed Ningaloo, while autumn spawning remained similarly high across both sites. These contrasting patterns reflect clear differences in hydrodynamic conditions. Low wave energy and wind-driven turbulence at Bundegi likely enhance spring fertilisation, whereas strong winds and higher wave energy at Ningaloo appear to suppress it. This case study demonstrates that local environmental conditions can generate pronounced phenological divergence over small spatial scales. Across Western Australia, differences in spawning time are increasingly linked to genetic structuring and incipient speciation. Sympatric autumn- and spring-spawning cohorts of several Acropora species show strong genomic divergence, revealing that allochronic speciation is an underappreciated contributor to cryptic and overall reef biodiversity. Recognising and characterising these phenological patterns is essential for accurate biodiversity assessments, interpreting population connectivity, and guiding restoration efforts. Integrating reproductive phenology into ecological studies and conservation planning will improve detection of incipient species, preserve adaptive potential, and ensure management strategies reflect true Indo-Pacific coral diversity.
fb6e3302: Understanding biodiversity patterns and their drivers has become a central theme in mesophotic coral ecosystem research. Studies on fishes and benthic assemblages consistently show strong depth-driven species turnover, highlighting how compositionally distinct mesophotic communities are from shallow reefs. Unfortunately, research has focused largely on conspicuous taxa, leaving the cryptic fauna that makes up most reef biodiversity largely unknown at mesophotic depths. On shallow reefs, Autonomous Reef Monitoring Structures (ARMS) are a widely used tool for sampling cryptic biodiversity, but their application in the mesophotic zone has been very limited. To help fill this gap, our team deployed ARMS at 10, 50, and 100 m across four Pacific locations. Here we report the first retrieval of 13 ARMS, conducted in October 2025 on Guam after eight years of deployment, yielding more than 2000 organisms, including several new records and new species. Sessile communities quantified at low taxonomic resolution revealed strong depth structuring. PERMANOVA showed depth as the dominant factor shaping assemblages, and phylum richness increased significantly from 10 to 100 m. The top plates displayed marked shifts with increasing depth: the shallow unit was heavily scrapped by fishes, with remaining biomass consisting of turf and macroalgae; at 50 m, crustose coralline algae (CCA) and macroalgae were most common; and at 100 m, CCA remained prevalent, but sponge cover increased. Additional depth-linked patterns included a decreasing biomass gradient, abundant mollusks and brachiopods at 50 m, and higher cnidarian diversity at 100 m, including depth-specialist scleractinians. These preliminary results show that even coarse taxonomic data capture strong depth structuring in cryptic communities, mirroring patterns known for fishes and corals. Continued taxonomic and molecular work will produce a comprehensive baseline of mesophotic biodiversity at a broad geographic scale, support evaluation of shallow–deep connectivity, and characterize the functional organization of deep communities.
ba60c5b6: Upper mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs) represent an extension of shallow coral reefs into deeper, light-limited environments, yet their fine-scale diversity patterns remain poorly understood. Most studies assess broad depth categories, potentially overlooking key ecological transitions. Here, we provide a detailed assessment of coral community structure from shallow to upper mesophotic reefs (5–40 m) in three biogeographic regions of the Philippines—West Philippine Sea, Sulu Sea, and Bohol Sea—using photo-quadrat surveys. Results show that upper mesophotic reefs (30-40 m) can maintain substantial generic richness (alpha diversity) comparable to that of shallow reefs, but community composition (beta diversity) becomes increasingly differentiated among regions with depth, in contrast to the relative similarity observed in shallow (5-10 m) reefs. These patterns suggest limited functional connectivity at the community level among deeper assemblages. While some taxa may contribute to reseeding potential, most shallow-reef genera are not well-represented in upper MCEs, indicating limited capacity for deeper reefs to serve as effective refugia for shallow coral assemblages.
e5c01017: Mesophotic coral ecosystems (30–150 m) have attracted rapidly growing research attention over the past two decades, gradually revealing how distinct these coral communities are from their shallow-water counterparts. However, substantial variation in methodological approaches to benthic community assessment, together with the concentration of research in relatively few locations, has constrained our ability to conduct robust, broad-scale comparative analyses of these ecosystems. Using a standardized photogrammetry-based approach, we quantified compositional and functional differences in benthic community structure of mesophotic coral ecosystems across two Atlantic and two Pacific regions. We developed a customized workflow and graphical user interface to characterize 3D point intercepts using a stratified grid approach within 100 m² plots at 5, 10, 20, 40 and 60 m depth at 4–5 locations each in the Kingdom of Tonga, Solomon Islands, Curaçao and Turks and Caicos. A CATAMI-based classification scheme, combined with a human-in-the-loop image classifier, was used to enable functional comparisons across depths and ocean basins. We observed consistent depth-related shifts in benthic cover and morphological diversity across all regions. Despite the strongly distinct taxonomic composition of mesophotic habitats across ocean basins, aggregating benthic taxa into functional groups revealed similar assemblages and consistent responses to depth. Compared with shallow reefs, mesophotic communities exhibited markedly lower functional redundancy, suggesting that the loss of even a few key taxa may disproportionately affect ecosystem functioning and heighten their vulnerability to disturbance. Our study highlights the value of standardized comparative analyses and hopefully provides a methodological framework to enable much broader global assessments of mesophotic coral ecosystems.
021519f1:
Mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs) are increasingly recognized as vital refuges for coral biodiversity but remain poorly explored due to depth constraints. In the South China Sea, mesophotic reefs typically occur at depths of 30 to 40 meters, forming the deeper basal reef zone. Between 7 and 11 October 2024, we conducted an ROV-based survey of hard coral communities at Pulau Tioman, Pahang, Malaysia, as part of the OceanX Expedition, using the ROV Chimaera. Seven sites were surveyed, with dives ranging from 28 to 65 meters and an average depth of 32.9 meters to target areas with highest expected coral diversity. Each dive lasted 120 to 130 minutes, allowing comprehensive video documentation and identification of coral genera.

A total of 36 hard coral genera were recorded across all sites, highlighting the richness of Pulau Tioman’s mesophotic reefs. The six most abundant genera: Leptoseris (13.24%), Goniopora (9.85%), Pavona (8.71%), Dipsastraea (8.33%), Fungia, and Plesiastrea (each 6.06%), accounted for more than 50 percent of observed diversity. Water visibility varied between sites, ranging from less than two meters to around five meters, which influenced survey clarity but not overall genus identification.

The dominance of a few genera suggests depth and site-specific structuring of mesophotic coral communities. This study provides an essential baseline for understanding coral distribution patterns in Malaysia’s deeper reefs. It also contributes to broader regional knowledge of mesophotic reef ecosystems in this region, offering insights for conservation planning and management. By demonstrating the effectiveness of ROV technology for exploring hard-to-access reefs, this research reveals hidden biodiversity and highlights the critical role of MCEs in maintaining coral diversity under increasing environmental pressures on shallow reefs.
ece293c5: The relative contribution and scales of biological and environmental filters acting on species and their traits determines local assemblage structures and connective potential between assemblages along environmental gradients. This will influence interregional migration, shape biodiversity redistribution and limit migration-related rescue effects in response to disturbances and changing environmental conditions. Mesophotic reefs have been touted and negated as potential refugia for coral reefs suffering shallow water heat stress and associated degradation. Yet, we still understand very little detail about the ecological processes driving distinct assemblages in shallow and deep reefs, and how those processes scale along other major geographic gradients. In this talk I will share my investigations of the geographic scale and depths at which random and deterministic ecosystem processes shape evolutionary and ecological distinctness of coral reef fish assemblages along all three principle spatial gradients (Latitude, Longitude, and Depth [0-130m]). Using diversity metrics informed by species’ similarities in functional traits, I will identify the spatial scales at which local reef fish assemblages are formed by similar processes and where along these gradients abrupt changes in assemblage composition are forced by deterministic processes such as environmental filtering or inter-species competition. This will reveal important controls on community structure and assembly filters that determine levels of similarity between adjacent assemblages and the future inter-compatibility of assemblage components.
bb98978c: Mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs) constitute nearly 80% of the overall reef habitat in American Samoa, but remain poorly characterized relative to their shallow counterparts. Previous studies have used visual surveys in order to characterize mesophotic reefs, often detecting conspicuous organisms. Environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding provides a much more comprehensive approach in assessing biodiversity patterns across large depth gradients. Using Riaz 12s, we examine the depth structuring, trophic composition, and community assembly of reef fishes (Actinopteri and Chondrichthyes) across a 20-100 meter gradient at three long-term monitoring sites within the island of Tutuila, American Samoa. We observed a consistent and distinct depth zonation break between 40 and 60 m separating shallow (20–40 m) and deep (60–100 m) mesophotic assemblages, suggesting that strong environmental filtering can structure fish communities even among highly mobile taxa. This community differentiation across this boundary was also primarily driven by species turnover rather than nestedness, indicating compositional replacement rather than simple richness attrition with depth. Irrespective of site, shallow mesophotic reefs supported greater taxonomic richness and internal homogeneity compared to deeper assemblages. These depth-related transitions were accompanied by strong shifts in trophic composition: herbivorous and detritivorous fish richness declined sharply with depth, lower-level carnivores decreased more gradually, while planktivores and top predators increased in deeper zones. Collectively, these results demonstrate a robust, turnover-driven depth zonation of reef-fish assemblages in American Samoa, with consistent community separation across multiple analytical frameworks. The observed ecological boundary near 40–60 m aligns with global evidence for faunal breaks within mesophotic ecosystems, underscoring the need to recognize shallow and deep mesophotic habitats as compositionally and functionally distinct ecological strata. This depth-explicit understanding of fish community structure provides a critical foundation for biodiversity monitoring, management, and the design of depth-inclusive marine protected areas within American Samoa.
cc6062b7: This study quantifies spatial and temporal variation in mesophotic reef fish assemblages at the Flower Garden Banks National Marine Sanctuary (FGBNMS) using high-resolution Mohawk ROV video transects. New surveys conducted in 2025 were compared with archived  ROV datasets from 2014-2015 at Bright, Geyer, and McGrail Banks, to evaluate decadal trends in species composition, abundance, and community structure. Analyses of the historical dataset reveal pronounced spatial and depth-related patterns: each bank hosts distinct fish communities, with Geyer Bank supporting more deep-reef specialists and Bright Bank exhibiting the highest species richness. Depth-structured assemblages were characterized by shallow mesophotic zones dominated by benthic feeders and reef-associated taxa, and deeper habitats support midwater predators and bottom-dwelling piscivores. Despite clear shifts in species, trophic structure remained consistent across banks and depths. Ongoing analyses will integrate benthic habitat annotations to assess relationships between fish assemblages, depth, and habitat complexity, providing a more holistic view of species-habitat interactions. By establishing an ecological baseline and assessing decadal shifts in fish community structure, this work will improve our understanding of mesophotic reef dynamics and inform adaptive, spatially explicit management strategies for the FGBNMS. This aim serves as the foundation of my broader dissertation, which compares shallow and mesophotic coral physiology through analyses of total lipid and fatty acid composition in two boulder coral species, investigates depth-related variation in algal symbiont community structure, algal symbiont density, and chlorophyll concentration between these species and depths, and examines stakeholder perceptions of reef conservation. Together, these data will provide a comprehensive evaluation of the ecological and socio-environmental factors shaping reefs in the FGBNMS.
cd54c105: Despite high biodiversity and the recognized importance of mesophotic habitats, most studies of coral reef community structure have focused on conspicuous taxa such as fishes and corals in shallow habitat <30 m. Here, we examined the variability of crab assemblages from Autonomous Reef Monitoring Structures (ARMS) deployed on shallow reefs across the Hawaiian Islands and along a mesophotic depth gradient on O’ahu. We tested the effects of environmental, ecological, and anthropogenic factors on shallow (8–17 m) crab assemblages. These assemblages were significantly different between the densely inhabited high islands (Main Hawaiian Islands) and the relatively pristine uninhabited atolls of the Northwestern Hawaiian Islands. Drivers of these differences include sea-surface temperature, chlorophyll-A, depth, island slope, potential larval immigration, and human impacts. We then compared shallow assemblages to those sampled along a depth gradient (12–90 m) on O’ahu. Despite considerable variability among sites distributed across nearly 10 degrees of latitude, differences along the depth gradient on O’ahu alone were greater than among the shallow assemblages across the entire archipelago. This finding suggests that 90 m of depth is a stronger driver of brachyuran assemblage structure than the latitudinal, environmental, and anthropogenic gradients across the entire ~2500 km span of the Hawaiian Islands.
35601138: As the climate crisis worsens, coral populations across tropical regions are experiencing increasing bleaching and mortality events. These populations may either adapt to elevated temperatures, perish, or expand over generations toward cooler, temperate areas. Japan hosts the northernmost coral communities in the Pacific Ocean and, due to its particular oceanographic setting, may serve as a potential warming refugia for corals evading ocean warming near the tropics. Here, we present the first regional-scale projected assessment of coral connectivity along the Japanese coast, highlighting trends and variability patterns that may influence future poleward expansion in the region. Lagrangian particle tracking simulations were conducted by coupling temperature and hydrodynamic outputs from the 2 km Future Regional Projection (FORP) dataset with generalized coral life-history traits along the Japanese coast. Simulations were run for 10 years under three scenarios: Historical (2009–2018), RCP2.6 (2091–2100), and RCP8.5 (2091–2100). Preliminary results suggest that increasing temperatures under RCP2.6 and RCP8.5 may advance spawning time by up to one to two months compared to historical conditions. The estimated coral connectivity network indicated an overall decline in connectivity strength from key southern biodiversity hotspots (e.g., Yaeyama, Miyako, Okinawa) toward northern regions, with some connections projected to be completely shut down by 2100. These changes were associated with lower flow velocities and reduced eddy kinetic energy along larval dispersal routes. While these projections carry inherent uncertainty due to future climate and hydrodynamic conditions, they provide an alarming indication that key exporter and stepping-stone reef systems in Japan may become less effective at sustaining long-distance larval exchange in the future. As such changes could have major implications for coral habitat redistribution, we hope these insights can help guide current and future conservation strategies to support coral persistence under climate change.
719beb5e: Understanding the determinants of the shape of geographic ranges is central to macroecology, yet marine systems remain understudied relative to terrestrial ones. Marine organisms, particularly coral reef fishes, inhabit highly anisotropic seascapes shaped by basin geometry, climatic gradients, and historical barriers. Here, we analyse range shapes—comparing latitudinal-longitudinal relationships to neutral isotropic expectations for over 3,100 reef fish species globally. Analyses revealed that all ocean regions deviate strongly from neutral expectations, with deviations mirroring regional geometry. In the Atlantic, a narrow basin with north–south thermal gradients, species exhibited restricted longitudinal expansion compared to latitude. The Tropical Eastern Pacific, a linear coastal corridor, displayed the steepest coupling (approaching 1:1 slopes). Conversely, the Indo-Pacific, the world’s widest tropical region, showed the shallowest slope, where species expand thousands of kilometres east–west while remaining restricted latitudinally. Consequently, oblate (east–west elongated) ranges dominate the Indo-Pacific, while the Atlantic and Eastern Pacific host more balanced or prolate shapes. We further investigated how species traits relate to range shape and contribute to regional patterns of species richness. Our findings demonstrate that ocean-basin geometry imposes fundamental limits on maximum range size, often overriding dispersal ability. Two species with identical traits may achieve vastly different realised ranges simply due to the geometry of their inhabited basin. Integrating seascape geometry into macroecological models is therefore essential for interpreting current distributions and forecasting shifts under climate change.
bdb28a7d: Recent global warming has led to the decline of coral communities in low latitudes, while simultaneously expanding coral habitats in mid-latitude regions. On the other hand, it is concerning that the negative impacts of ocean acidification, which are gradually spreading from north to south, will also adversely affect the northward-expanding coral communities. This study aims to project the coral community succession in Japan coastal areas near future by developing: (1) a detailed process-based model which responses within the coral individual to multiple environmental conditions, such as temperature, pH, based on the coral polyp model (Nakamura et al., 2013) and can simulate coral responses to environmental changes, (2) a larval dispersal model for Japan coastal areas, and (3) a coupled hydrodynamic−lower trophic level ecosystem model predicting present and future environmental parameters in coastal areas, such as flow fields, water temperature, salinity, and pH. Regarding model (1), a detailed description of reactive oxygen species (ROS) production processes associated with high- and low-temperature stresses enabled prediction of not only heat-induced but also cold-induced bleaching. This allows prediction of not only coral community decline in low latitudes but also community succession and adaptation toward mid-latitudes. Regarding (2), using present and future flow-temperature fields from the Future Ocean Regional Projection datasets in the region surrounding Japan with an approximately 2 km horizontal resolution (FORP-JPN02) version 4 (doi:10.20783/DIAS.656; Nishikawa et al., 2021), we calculated present and future spawning timing from accumulated water temperature. Additionally, using the Connectivity Modeling System (Paris et al., 2013), we calculated larval dispersal and settlement areas. Regarding (3), we predicted water quality parameters by a lower trophic level ecosystem model, Marine CoMET (Nakamura et al., in prep.), which we are developing, with FORP-JPN02 forcing. By integrating these models, we successfully reproduced/projected coral community succession following historical/future climate change.
76d5b538:
With climate warming, tropicalisation is driving changes in benthic reef communities, altering the composition and functioning of coral reef ecosystems. However, little is known about how algal communities change along environmental gradients, especially in terms of their reassembly and turnover. Here, we analyse temporal change in macroalgal communities across 32 sites in southern Japan spanning 22–35°N, using two comparable datasets collected in 2015–2016 and again in 2023–2024. We recorded species and abundance from transect photo data using CoralNet, and examined species and functional turnover along the latitudinal gradient.
We found clear evidence of tropicalisation across the region. Warm affinity macroalgal species such as Turbinaria expanded toward higher latitudes, increasing tropical species abundance and richness at cooler sites. Community changes showed a strong latitudinal pattern: turf algae increased at low-latitude reefs, while at several mid-latitude temperate sites coral cover also increased and startes to occupy space previously held by macroalgae, and at high-latitude sites the structural complexity of kelp-dominated assemblages declined. Temperature was the strongest factor influencing the diversity index, showing a clear correlation with species and functional turnover.
These findings highlight the importance of algae as a significant component of shallow reef ecosystems and the need for further research on algal dynamics in benthic communities undergoing rapid warming.
5a557878:
Marine heatwaves have become increasingly frequent worldwide, causing rapid temperature increases that threaten coral reef health. Alongside this, tropicalization is accelerating in temperate regions, often marked by macroalgal die-offs and phase shifts toward scleractinian coral dominance. Under these conditions, organisms must adapt either to higher thermal limits (temperate species facing tropicalization) or lower limits (tropical species expanding poleward). Beyond absolute extremes, seasonal temperature gaps—driven by mismatched rates of summer and winter maximum temperature increases—also impose fitness costs.
“Kyucho,” or gulf stream, is a unique oceanographic phenomenon that causes drastic water movement, sometimes resulting in damage to fishing gear such as fish cage rafts. Specifically, Kyucho events occurring in the Bungo Channel—between western Shikoku and eastern Kyushu, Japan—can raise water temperature by up to 4 °C within a few days, or in extreme cases, within just 30 minutes. This acute fluctuation, especially in summer, creates a highly dynamic thermal environment, requiring local organisms to develop adaptive strategies to survive. Furthermore, because Kyucho originates from the Kuroshio Current, it may facilitate the migration of tropical species and accelerate local tropicalization. Indeed, the west coast of Shikoku has experienced a decline in macroalgae and an increase in scleractinian coral cover over the past couple of decades.
Our project aims to investigate the genetic, transcriptomic, and symbiotic mechanisms that enable organisms to tolerate such rapid temperature changes. By identifying key traits associated with resilience, we seek to establish indicators for forecasting tropicalization risk that could be applied to other region where exposed to tropicalization threads.
842114fb: Coral reefs are facing a multitude of environmental stressors, including prolonged heatwaves, which are occurring with increasing frequency and intensity. These events can trigger mass bleaching and mortality in corals and giant clams, driven by the breakdown of their photosymbiotic relationships with Symbiodiniaceae. Early detection of potential bleaching events may help inform stakeholders about the potential risks to the ecosystems. Often, molecular changes in organisms occur before any visible physical signs of stress are apparent. Our study investigated the potential to detect stress response markers in environmental RNA (eRNA) and its potential as a molecular indicator of heat stress using the giant clam (Tridacna crocea). Tridacna crocea were housed individually in tanks with a continuous flow of seawater. They were exposed to either control conditions at 29°C or heated conditions at 32°C for two weeks. Samples were collected at 24 hours, 7 days, 14 days, during heat stress, and 4 weeks after exposure to assess recovery. All T. crocea exposed to heat stress exhibited a reduction in the maximum quantum yield of photosystem II (Fv/Fm), which recovered after four weeks. Gene expression patterns within the ctenidia were compared to those in water collected from the siphon to determine if eRNA expression patterns overlap with those within the organism. Our study aims to highlight the potential for eRNA collected from giant clams to serve as an early indicator of heat stress on coral reefs, potentially warning stakeholders of the imminent threat to reefs.
43655716: Reef-building corals are complex holobionts composed of the cnidarian host and a diverse assemblage of prokaryotic and microeukaryotic symbionts. While most studies of the coral microbiome have focused on the 1–3 mm tissue layer, the largely unexplored coral skeleton harbors a highly structured microbial community shaped by its unique physicochemical environment. Beneath the tissue resides the endolithic alga Ostreobium, which forms conspicuous green bands within massive corals and functions as a primary producer with the potential to support the coral host during stress. To investigate the composition, function, and interactions of the microbial community within the Porites lutea holobiont, we conducted a thermal bleaching–recovery experiment and performed metatranscriptomic analyses on three distinct anatomical compartments: coral tissue, the underlying Ostreobium layer, and the deeper skeleton. Ribosomal RNA analyses revealed that the microbial community of partially recovered corals closely resembled that of healthy colonies. De novo assembly of Ostreobium 16S rRNA sequences uncovered high phylogenetic diversity and dynamic compositional shifts of this endolithic alga throughout the thermal stress experiment. Preliminary RNAseq results indicate compartment-specific transcriptional activity, with coral health condition driving microbiome community diversity in both the tissue and the deeper skeleton. Ongoing mRNA analyses focus on key metabolic pathways—including carbon fixation, photosynthesis, sugar transport, and nitrogen and sulfur metabolism—to elucidate the functional roles and interactions among holobiont members during bleaching and recovery. This study represents the first metatranscriptomic comparison across coral compartments, providing unprecedented insights into the community and functional dynamics of the coral holobiont under thermal stress. Ultimately, our findings will establish a foundation for understanding the metabolic integration that supports coral resilience, with important implications for coral reef preservation in a warming ocean.
749060c4: Coastal development and poor land-use practices are a major cause of erosion and terrestrial runoff that lead to sedimentation and the decline of near-shore coral reefs on small islands such as Guam. Species of massive Porites are among the few corals able to tolerate turbid waters and sedimentation. My lab has studied the impacts of sedimentation on Guam’s near-shore reefs using a combination of in situ transcriptomics, symbiont metabarcoding, and microbiome genomics, with the aim of elucidating the mechanisms that Porites and its symbionts employ when dealing with sedimentation stress. In addition, microbiome surveys are ongoing to identify potential terrestrial sources of microbial agents and harmful genetic elements that may negatively impact near-shore reef health in Guam. Our data suggest that Porites switch between energy generation pathways to facilitate survival when faced with sedimentation. For example, reduction of aerobic respiration-associated gene expression at sites with high chronic sedimentation suggests acclimation to hypoxic conditions.  Chronic sedimentation stress was also associated with upregulation of genes with immune and cell-cell signaling functions, suggesting shifts in immune metabolism for survival. Algal (Symbiodiniacae) endosymbiont community compositions remained relatively stable across a sedimentation gradient, with Caldocopium C15 dominating across sites and seasons. Bacterial microbiome communities were dominated by coral-endosymbionts of the genus Parendozoicomonas and increasing runoff/sedimentation during the wet-season was associated with increased microbiome diversity that may have its origins in soil microbiomes. Interestingly, relative abundance of actinomycetes increased in Porites microbiomes during the wet-season. Actinomycetes are ubiquitous and a source of well-known source of antimicrobial compounds. Our working hypothesis is that some actinomycetes are transported from degraded upstream soils to Guam’s near-shore reefs as a result of erosion. Degraded soils may promote the proliferation of antimicrobial resistance genes, which could negatively impact Guam’s near-shore coral reef ecosystems exposed to such bacteria via runoff.
2f120fb2: Corals function as integrated holobiont systems, where homeostasis emerges from interactions between the host and a diverse microbial consortium. Yet, coral diseases with similar signs differ in virulence and transmissibility, hindering diagnosis. We developed a multi-omics framework integrating host and Symbiodiniaceae RNA sequencing with 16S and 18S rRNA gene metabarcoding to resolve holobiont responses to white diseases. Over 150 samples were collected from Curaçao across nine Atlantic coral species showing signs of Caribbean ciliate infection (CCI) and stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD). We sampled healthy tissue from healthy colonies, apparently healthy tissue from affected colonies ahead of the disease lesion line, transition line tissue, and necrotic skeleton. Coral diseases exhibited distinct molecular pathway signatures: In CCI, apparently healthy tissues exhibited DNA repair and G1/S control, developmental differentiation, and cilia/flagella-driven motility, while the transition line was characterized by intensified mitotic nuclear division, organelle fission, and EGFR/ERBB signaling. In SCTLD, apparently healthy tissues already showed elevated ribosome biogenesis, translation, developmental patterning, and oxidative stress responses, whereas the transition line concentrated RNA/ncRNA processing, nucleoside triphosphate biosynthesis, proton transport, and programmed cell death. Parallel microbiome profiles revealed severe dysbiosis across maladies, showing known beneficial microbial associates, such as Breviolum and Endozoicomonas abundances crashing in lesion tissues, promoting enrichment of saprophytes and putative pathogenic lineages. Differential abundance revealed specific prokaryotic and microeukaryotic taxa highlighting candidate bioindicators, such as ASVs from the order Ga0077536 potentially capable of aerobic methanotrophy in early stages of dysbiosis. Microbes core to transition line samples are revealed as primarily ciliates (Metanophrys sinensis, Ampullofolliculina lageniformis, and Philaster apodigitiformis), labyrinthulids, and several prokaryotic taxa with known pathogenic roles in other marine systems. Together, this integrative system-level approach maps a diseased holobiont landscape that identifies candidate host and microbial bioindicators and provides a foundation for developing targeted, field-deployable diagnostic tools and intervention strategies.
1bad0a78: Between 2013 and 2021, a series of thermal anomalies, disease, and extreme tide events decimated 68% of Acropora staghorn in Guam, Micronesia. In response, Guam established two ocean nurseries in 2017 as biorepositories from surviving populations. While nursery corals show high success as measured by survival, growth, and fecundity, the transplanting process remains stressful. A rapid transition to a new environment may exceed a colony’s capacity to restructure its symbiotic communities. Fragments grown on substrate must overcome different stressors than fragments suspended in nurseries and, in turn, may harbor different microbial communities. We hypothesize that transplanting corals from nurseries to substrate may trigger shifts in the microbiome that may correlate with phenotypic stress responses commonly observed post-outplaning acclimation. As a preliminary effort to document this shift, we examined coral-associated bacterial and Symbiodiniaceae diversity, as well as genetic diversity, across wild and nursery-reared colonies of Acropora virgata. We predicted colonies would share similar assemblages by site, but found the opposite. Wild colonies, regardless of reef-site conditions, showed closer similarities due to high affiliation with Endozoicomonas, a bacterium associated with healthy coral, and distinct Symbiodiniaceae assemblages dominated by either Durusdinium or Cladocopium ITS2 profiles. In contrast, nursery fragments hosted higher Symbiodiniaceae and bacterial diversity and had unique profiles specific to each nursery. Despite appearing visually healthy, nursery colonies showed lower relative abundance of Endozoicomonas across both nurseries. Colonies reared in the Piti Nursery also showed higher affiliation with Cladocopium rather than Durusdinium compared to their wild sources. We speculate that reducing outplant mortality rates may, in part, rely on minimizing microbial shifts and suggest an intermediate nursery phase to expose colonies to substrate-associated microbes to facilitate stress recovery. These preliminary results show the importance of incorporating the role of microbial assemblages in restoration success to evaluate species-specific acclimation and coral-site compatibility.
0aaac7a2: The persistence of coral populations depends on their resistance to escalating heat stress and extreme thermal events. High diel thermal variability (DTV) has been linked to reduced bleaching, with corals from more thermally variable reefs often exhibiting greater thermal tolerance and gene expression plasticity than those from more stable environments. However, DTV is also associated with shifts in other physicochemical parameters on a reef, complicating its effects and requiring further resolution to determine its specific role. In this study, we investigated how DTV and broader environmental contexts shape coral phenotypic plasticity and thermal performance. Given the potential energetic costs of these responses, we also evaluated links between environments and trade-offs among calcification, energetic reserves, and reproductive investment, key life-history traits underlying coral survival and long-term reef dynamics. We identified four sites (two low-DTV, two high-DTV) within the Bocas del Toro Reef Complex, Panamá, where populations of the reef-building coral Siderastrea siderea experience distinct DTV despite similar mean temperatures. We first characterized coral and symbiont phenomes (composite physiological metrics), trophic ecology, genome-wide gene expression, full thermal performance curves, fecundity, and historical growth records of corals from these thermally contrasting reef sites. Next, we conducted a year-long reciprocal transplant experiment between low- and high-DTV environments to examine coral phenotypic plasticity in phenomes, growth, gene expression, and thermal tolerance. We simultaneously deployed loggers at each site to capture a continuous year-long record of seawater parameters (e.g., temperature, salinity, dissolved oxygen, pH) and collected nutrient profiles at three time points to determine which variables covary with DTV or may confound its effects. Our study elucidates how environmental heterogeneity shapes the extent to which long-lived reef-building corals can employ phenotypic plasticity to persist under rapid climate change. These insights will strengthen predictions of coral resilience to future warming and directly inform effective restoration strategies.
4299d2fd: Vibrio coralliilyticus (Vcor) is a devastating pathogen of reef-building corals, threatening important reef ecosystems. One such relevant Vcor strain is OCN008 one of the agents responsible for acute Montipora white syndrome (aMSW), an acute tissue loss disease that can significantly reduce coral cover during outbreaks. However, there is a lack of information on the effect of host and environmental signals on Vcor virulence and how they trigger disease outbreaks. Past research has shown that the metabolism of different carbon sources can impact virulence factors and growth in Vcor. Further, the carbohydrates in the extracellular mucus (ECM) layer of the coral can change in composition due to host physiology or environmental perturbations. Therefore, we hypothesize that environmental and host-related factors can influence ECM carbohydrates, which in turn influences Vcor virulence. To investigate the role of metabolism in Vcor strain OCN008, this pathogen was grown with different ECM-associated carbohydrates. Depending on the main carbohydrate available, positive or negative effects on growth were observed as well as important virulence factors such as production of antibiotics, protease, and antibacterial systems, i.e., the Type VI secretion system (T6SS). To further assess the role of metabolism in important virulence factors, a deletion of the cAMP receptor protein (CRP) gene was made in OCN008. CRP is a global genetic regulator which responds to different carbohydrates. The CRP mutant had reduced protease activity, T6SS activity, and antibiotic production. Additionally, when introduced to a surrogate clam infection system, virulence of the CRP mutant was significantly reduced compared to wildtype. These results suggest that carbohydrate metabolism is critical for Vcor virulence and that carbohydrate composition in the ECM may modulate the expression of key virulence factors. Future studies will explore how specific mucus-associated carbohydrates influence infection outcomes in live coral experiments, providing insight into host-microbe interactions that determine disease susceptibility.
090cfadf: Corals can thrive in oligotrophic waters due to their relationship with endosymbiotic dinoflagellates (Symbiodiniaceae). The coral-algal partnership depends on a delicate nutrient balance between internal nitrogen (N) recycling and nitrogen availability. Previous studies suggest that the host is the main nitrogen source for the algae; keeping symbionts nitrogen limited controls symbiont density, ensuring their excess photosynthate (i.e., fixed carbon (C)) is translocated to the host. With reefs experiencing increasing nutrient pollution and thermal stress, it is essential to understand how the coral holobiont maintains a nutrient balance. Here, under in situ, nitrate enriched, thermal stress, and nitrate enriched + thermal stress reef conditions, we explored the role of nutritional mode (autotrophy vs. heterotrophy) in acquiring nitrogen and carbon and their fate in the holobiont. In outdoor incubations, Montastraea cavernosa was exposed for 24 hours to eight experimental treatments using 15N tracer to label either zooplankton or nitrate and 13C-bicarbonate. All coral were fed zooplankton. Some zooplankton-N was quickly (<1 hour) transferred to the symbionts (likely as ammonium) in both low and high nitrate treatments, showing that symbionts prefer ammonium over nitrate, which is consistent with previous studies. This transfer also occurred under both ambient and thermal stressed conditions, showing that environmental stress did not affect the symbionts’ preference for recycled nitrogen (ammonium) from the host. Both coral host and algal symbiont acquired nitrogen from nitrate quickly. The symbionts photosynthesized and passed 13C-photosynthates to the coral host, but only when light became available. Therefore, in the dark, the symbionts must have energy reserves that allow them to acquire nitrate in the absence of energy from photosynthesis. Thermal stress did not significantly alter carbon or nitrate transfer in these experiments. Compared to a previous experiment using Diploria labyrinthiformis, the role of heterotrophy is far more variable in Montastraea cavernosa.
77fc466c: Rising ocean temperatures pose the greatest threat to coral reef persistence, as thermal stress can lead to the breakdown of coral symbioses. Studies suggest that prior exposure to thermal variability can enhance coral performance under thermal stress and microbial and algal associations may further shape tolerance. However, the mechanisms shaping these networks remain largely unexplored. Here, we assess the diversity and community composition of coral-associated algal and microbial communities across four ecologically relevant scales. We collected corals from four paired inshore-offshore reefs across the Caribbean, covering a 15º latitudinal gradient that span environments that vary in their environmental conditions. We leverage algal and microbial community metabarcoding across multiple spatial scales to explore how patterns of environmental variation shape community composition and diversity of symbiotic partners. Our hypothesis is that corals from more variable environments (e.g., inshore, higher latitude) will host lower algal symbiont and higher microbial community diversities compared to less variable sites.
525323f7:
Artificial light at night (ALAN) is an emerging global change driver in coastal ecosystems, where it alters natural light cycles that regulate behavior and physiology. Here, we integrated behavioral, cellular, and transcriptomic analyses to examine how ALAN affects sleep, neural physiology, and nocturnal behavior in coral-dwelling damselfish exposed to ALAN for three to five days (acute) and five months (chronic).

Machine learning based tracking of the Blue Green Damselfish (Chromis viridis) within their coral habitats showed that ALAN increased nocturnal activity, reduced homing fidelity to coral shelter, elevated territorial aggression and feeding, and consistently decreased sleep duration. These behavioral alterations were associated with impaired nighttime neural recovery, as neurons in the dorsal pallium accumulated DNA damage under both acute and chronic ALAN exposure in laboratory tanks and during in situ experiments at the Gulf of Aqaba (Eilat).

Complementary transcriptomic analyses of the Whitetail Dascyllus (Dascyllus aruanus) brain under acute ALAN in laboratory conditions revealed that although core circadian clock genes maintained rhythmic expression, downstream pathways were widely disrupted. At night, ALAN elevated immediate early gene expression and modified immune-related transcription factors, suggesting increased neural activity and reduced restorative capacity. During the following day, ALAN-exposed brains showed broad suppression of metabolic, endocrine, and immune pathways, together with increased expression of stress-associated genes. 

Together, these findings show that ALAN disrupts the restorative function of sleep, alters diel gene regulation, and affects key ecological behaviors such as shelter use, territoriality, and foraging in coral reef fish. This identifies ALAN as a significant sensory pollutant that undermines the ecological roles and resilience of reef fish in coastal environments.
69828718:
Much of what we know about how marine organisms respond to multiple simultaneous stressors still comes from laboratory studies on single species, which inevitably misses the ecological complexity of real reefs. Nikko Bay in Palau provides a rare counterexample: a semi-enclosed system with limited connectivity to surrounding waters, where elevated temperature and pCO₂ and low oxygen have been persistent for decades. This isolation has created a natural setting to explore how reef species adjust to future-like conditions.
We examined whether environmentally driven shifts in habitat structure inside Nikko Bay influence the behaviour and brain gene expression of pajama cardinalfish (Sphaeramia nematoptera), a coral-associated specialist. We used in-situ observations to assess coral and fish patterns and a reciprocal transplant experiment to move fish individuals between control reefs and inner-bay sites and assess their short- and long-term acclimation potential.
Inside the bay, branching Porites cylindrica is largely replaced by Montipora stellata, accompanied by lower cardinalfish abundance and higher activity levels. Furthermore, S. nematoptera change their association from P. cylindrica in the control area to M. stellata in Nikko Bay. When fish from the control reefs were transplanted into Nikko Bay and exposed to M. stellata, they showed reduced activity and pronounced upregulation in brain transcriptomic profiles compared to fish kept in their original habitat (both compared to fish associated with P. cylindrica in the control area or fish associated with M. stellata originating from Nikko Bay). In contrast, transplanting fish from P. cylindrica to M. stellata within the control environment showed a less pronounced reduction in activity and a weak transcriptomic response. These consistent behavioural and molecular responses indicate that habitat restructuring and altered environmental conditions in Nikko Bay are strong drivers of both ecological and molecular change, offering insight into the limits of plasticity in coral-associated fishes under future ocean scenarios.
fa9e6f91:
Tropical reef systems are increasingly threatened by rising ocean temperatures and other anthropogenic stressors. Species with limited mobility, such as anemone- or coral-associated species, are particularly at risk because they remain exposed to the local environmental conditions around their host after settlement. In Moorea, French Polynesia, the threespot damselfish (Dascyllus trimaculatus) and Polynesian anemonefish (Amphiprion maohiensis) are both associated with anemones and occupy habitats that differ markedly in temperature, ranging from warm, shallow lagoons to cooler outer reefs from 10-20 m in depth.

Our objective was to determine whether developmental acclimation to local thermal conditions enhances physiological resilience to warming, measured through metabolic rates and circulating cortisol. Juveniles of each species from both habitat types (lagoon and outer reef) were assessed for respirometry as well as baseline and stress-induced cortisol soon after capture under ambient temperature conditions (28˚C). Individuals were then split into two groups and either held at 28˚C or acclimated to 32˚C, a predicted end of century temperature which can induce anemone bleaching and is currently considered abnormally high. Respirometry was then retested after approximately 10 days, as were cortisol levels.  

By comparing baseline and thermally-induced metabolic and endocrine responses across origin and temperature treatments, the results of this study will not only elucidate the responses of two vulnerable fish species to future warming, but also how their microhabitat-specific developmental histories influence their vulnerability to future warming. We will further improve our understanding of the physiological consequences of warming on site-attached species through comparison with known effects of anemone bleaching on these species. With limited dispersal capability past the larval phase, these findings will provide insight into the adaptive potential of site-attached reef fishes via developmental acclimation and highlight that resilience to rising temperatures may vary substantially across reef zones, even across small spatial scales.
e9983eb0: Within tropical, shallow water coral reefs, the relationship between fishes and live corals is arguably the most iconic and highly scrutinized. A strong relationship between fishes and reef-building hard corals is often assumed, a belief supported by studies that document the decline of reef fishes following coral loss. However, the extent of this relationship is often unclear, as evidenced by conflicting reports. Here, I present works from my PhD that argue for a rethink of this relationship. Beginning with a classification of extant fishes into their association with live coral, we highlight that only a small proportion of fishes are closely associated with corals, aligning with published literature. Using this classification, we examine the diversification of fishes and corals across evolutionary timescales (~millions of years). We find that fish lineages that rely on coral were decoupled from coral lineage trajectories, challenging co-evolutionary diversification theories.  Fast forward to the present, we explore potential confounders that regulate both fish and coral communities globally, highlighting how ecological and evolutionary drivers explain away much of the relationship between these two groups. We then also document changes in fish communities (abundance and species richness) across 1485 sites in 67 locations globally. Remarkably, we find decadal stability of fish populations from ecoregions up to major biogeographic realms, with population trajectories largely decoupled from mass coral bleaching events. Lastly, we investigate the magnitude of this fish-coral relationship through a meta-analysis of published literature since the 1980s. Interestingly, across trophic and taxonomic groupings, we find consistently weak associations with coral cover, even among “coral-dependent” groups. These findings challenge the longstanding narrative of a critical interdependence between fishes and corals, urging consideration of other factors influencing coral reef systems.
b6acd396: The animal kingdom showcases a variety of charismatic color patterns, yet their function remains elusive for many taxa. Often their function is unknown because of a lack of integration across several ecological and evolutionary axes to provide the proper context. Interestingly, animals within mutualisms sometimes evolve specific color patterns due to their mutualistic interactions. One such mutualism includes the clownfish-anemone symbiosis. The color patterns of clownfishes have fascinated scientists for several decades, yet only recently are we beginning to uncover their function. Clownfishes have convergently evolved three distinct color patterns based on their host anemone use. Orange fish with few stripes live within Radianthus magnifica, red fish with few stripes live within Entacmaea quadricolor, and black fish with thick white stripes are host generalists (3+ hosts). Here, we provide the ecological context under which these color patterns evolved to predict their function. We combined multiple behavioral metrics, sensory ecology, host specificity, dietary niche, and microbiome data. By studying 11 clownfish species in 4 different locations, we consistently find that the ecology and host use of clownfishes have converged with their color pattern. For two color patterns, species are host specialists, remain near their host, are hard to see in their hosts and have a narrow dietary niche and microbiome. These results suggest that the color pattern of host specialists function as camouflage within their host anemones. The third color pattern is found in species that are host generalists, venture far from their host, are easy to find in their hosts, and have a generalized dietary niche and microbiome. This suggests that the heavily striped color pattern of host generalists functions as disruptive coloration or aposematism. Our study highlights that clownfish color patterns serve as antipredator protection, but the specific form of protection varies by host specificity.
6f2d407f: Previous research on the ‘seascape of fear’ has examined how visual cover, such as that provided by macrophytes or coral reef topography, affects the grazing behaviour of prey fishes. Many studies show that herbivory decreases with increasing structural complexity because reduced sightlines limit predator detection, creating a trade-off between food consumption and vigilance. However, other work on coral reef rugosity has reported the opposite pattern, with higher habitat complexity correlating with elevated grazing rates and greater abundances of herbivorous fishes. One mechanism that may reconcile these contrasting findings is that cover, which often covaries with complexity, can function either as refuge or as background risk depending on its density. At low to moderate levels, cover can help conceal prey from predators, whereas beyond a threshold it may impair visibility and heighten perceived predation risk. In this ex-situ study, we test the possibility of a unimodal relationship between visual cover and the grazing rates of Zebrasoma scopas, a common Indo-Pacific grazing surgeonfish. Fish were introduced into an aquarium arena containing zones with differing levels of cover and exposed to a moving blacktip reef shark model to simulate acute predation risk. Conducting the study in a laboratory setting allowed for finer control of cover levels, which is often difficult in field experiments due to the influence of surrounding reef structures. This work will provide insights into the mechanisms underlying herbivore grazing patterns, predator–prey dynamics, and habitat utilization in coral reef systems. Findings may also inform conservation of architecturally complex habitats and guide ecological engineering strategies aimed at enhancing reef resilience.
6eff5b55: How reef populations respond to extreme climatic events is a fundamental question for twenty-first-century marine ecology and conservation. Documenting local collapses of iconic populations can offer insights into broader patterns of decline and help to communicate the issue to the public. Here, we monitored the anemonefish (Amphiprion bicinctus) and their host anemones (Radianthus magnifica) across three central Red Sea reefs from 2022–2024, encompassing an unprecedented marine heatwave that peaked at ~22 °C-weeks of Degree Heating Weeks. Across all reefs we observed a sequence of 100% anemone bleaching, followed by 94.3–100% anemonefish mortality, and ultimately 66.4–94.1% anemone mortality.
