To better understand why anemonefish mortality follows anemone bleaching, we conducted complementary laboratory experiments at Boston University. We monitored anemonefish (Amphiprion percula) behavior and anemone (Entacmaea quadricolor) nematocyst discharge in response to environmentally relevant heat stress. We show that fish alter their behavior and that anemone nematocyst discharge changes in ways that would likely increase fish vulnerability to predation following prolonged heat stress and bleaching. This integrative approach links catastrophic population collapses and breakdown of mutualistic interactions in the wild to their proximate mechanisms in the laboratory. Together, our studies reveal how and why marine heatwaves disrupt one of the ocean’s most iconic mutualisms. Finally, by pairing these findings with widely circulated visual documentation of bleached anemones and anemonefish (featured by National Geographic, Mongabay, and others), we also aim to leverage the familiarity of and affection for this mutualism to strengthen public engagement with climate-driven reef decline.
cd31ecf6: Marine biogenic calcifiers are under severe threat from future global change. Coralline algae are particularly susceptible to ocean acidification, affecting their physiology and mineralogy, which impacts their role as settlement substrate, reef accretors, and seed banks. Laboratory environments offer an excellent opportunity to study the impacts of key drivers under controlled conditions. However, natural laboratories, where environmental conditions are already close to predicted levels under future global change, provide a unique opportunity to study the effects of these key drivers in-situ. Bourakè, in New Caledonia is a semi-enclosed bay where tidal variation drives extreme diel changes in temperature, pH, and oxygen. Coralline algae can survive within this extreme environment, indicating that it is likely that some forms of adaptation to local environmental stressors could have occurred. Previous laboratory studies have indicated that the ability of coralline algae to shift dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC) use (from energetically expensive bicarbonate (HCO3-) to readily available carbon dioxide (CO2)), and to maintain control of their calcification physiology (i.e. high internal CaCO3 saturation state (Ω) and low % magnesium (%Mg)) under low pH, is related to tolerance. In this study, we conducted a reciprocal transplant of coralline algae living within (1) a low pH and (2) a nearby control site in Bourakè, to assess short-term acclimatisation processes and physiological responses to novel environmental conditions. Our hypotheses are: (1) coralline algae from Bourakè will show physiological differences compared to those from the control site; (2) they will display greater control of their calcification physiology (high internal CaCO3 Ω and low %Mg) and preferentially take up CO2 for photosynthesis; (3) individuals transplanted to novel environmental conditions will not be able to acclimatise over the short-term; and (4) those transplanted into the low pH (Bourakè) environment, will be most negatively affected.
5f8c70e8: Crustose coralline algae (CCA) are an important source of coral larval settlement cues; however, settlement success varies across CCA species and it remains unclear whether these cues originate from the host and/or the CCA microbiome. Furthermore, CCA microbiomes are species-specific, so potential microbial settlement cues could differ between different CCA. To investigate the presence and potential commonality of CCA-associated microbial cues, we characterized the microbiomes of 14 coralline algal species and a calcareous non-coralline alga that induced settlement for 14 Great Barrier Reef coral species with varying levels of success. We used a suite of community analyses like linear regression, differential abundance, indicator species, and random forest analyses to identify Amplicon Sequence Variants (ASVs) associated with coral larval settlement. We found ASVs that correlated with settlement for each coral species and were largely coral species-specific.  In addition to potential coral species-specific microbial inducers or inhibitors, we found ASVs associated with high or low settlement that were shared between the corals Dipsastrea favus, Echinophyllia aspera, Lobophyllia corymbosa, Mycedium elephantotus, and Platygrya sinensis, suggesting common settlement inducing or inhibiting cues.  Specifically, Candidatus Nitrosopumilus sp. and Filomicrobium sp. were found as potential shared inducers and ASVs within the Pirellulaceae and Flavobacteriacae families were identified as potential inhibitors. Shared ASVs associated with high coral settlement were typically found across multiple CCA species, while ASVs associated with low settlement were generally confined to low settlement CCA species (e.g., Lithothamnion cf. proliferum and Amphiroa cf. foliacea). These findings add insight into the identity of potential microbial taxa associated with settlement cues and what constitutes an attractive substrate to encourage coral recruitment.
830f54ea: Population outbreaks of crown-of-thorns starfish (CoTS, Acanthaster spp.) remain one of the foremost causes of coral loss on Indo-Pacific reefs. For outbreaks to initiate and spread, large numbers of planktonic larvae have to navigate settlement, grow and survive as herbivorous juveniles, and complete an ontogenetic diet shift to corallivory. Throughout this early life-history, coralline algae play essential roles as settlement cues and juvenile food and may therefore shape spatial recruitment patterns. Yet, these inherently vulnerable early-life stages and transitions remain a black box in our understanding of this notorious coral predator. In a series of experimental studies, we demonstrate that specific coralline algae, especially Melyvonnea cf. madagascariensis, disproportionally shape the success, fitness and survival of Pacific CoTS (Acanthaster cf. solaris). Larval settlement rates for 15 coralline algae from Australia’s Great Barrier Reef ranged from >90% in Melyvonnea cf. madagascariensis to less than 2% in some other species. This contrasting capacity of cues associated with different species to facilitate settlement may be linked to their surface-associated microbiomes and/or tissue-associated metabolomes. Notably, we identified strong correlations between settlement and specific metabolites, some of which were particularly enriched in M. cf. madagascariensis. Moreover, daily consumption rates of juvenile starfish were up to 11 times higher for M. cf. madagascariensis compared to other species, resulting in up to 38 times higher growth rates of individuals consuming this alga. Another experiment corroborated that starfish feeding on this alga not only grow considerably faster and larger but are also more likely to survive and transition to feeding on Acropora coral. Together, these new insights suggest that the prevalence of specific coralline algae on reefs not only enhances individual fitness and recruitment success of this starfish but may also explain some of the marked spatial heterogeneity in the incidence of their population outbreaks and corresponding reef degradation.
ee48665a: Scleractinian corals and crustose coralline algae (CCA) are the primary reef builders and their ecological interactions have generally been viewed through the lens of positive, facilitative species interactions. In addition to creating stable frameworks for corals to grow, CCA are known to induce the settlement of coral larvae. Fewer studies, however, have explored the interactions between CCA and coral larvae following settlement. To investigate this, we first assessed the ability of four common CCA species (Crustaphytum sp., Lithophyllum sp., Porolithon cf. onkodes, and Titanoderma cf. tessellatum) to induce settlement of Acropora millepora coral larvae in standard well plate assays. We then monitored the growth and survival of coral larvae that settled directly onto the surface of the four CCA species over 10 weeks. Our results showed that all four CCA species successfully induced >50% larval settlement, although significantly lower settlement was observed in Lithophyllum sp. compared to the other three CCA species. Survival of coral spat that settled on the surfaces of CCA fragment was low (<20%) by 10 weeks post-settlement. We also Different strategies were employed by CCA to kill newly settled spat: 1) T. cf. tessellatum and Crustaphytum sp. gradually overgrew coral spat, with Crustaphytum sp. exhibiting a faster overgrowth rate than T. cf. tessellatum, 2) P. cf. onkodes formed bulges that pushed spat upwards, resulting in the dislodgement of spat from CCA surface, while 3) Lithophyllum sp. adopted deep-layer sloughing process, in which thick white patches developed directly underneath settled coral spat and were subsequently sloughed off. Overall, this study provides empirical evidence that the interactions between CCA and corals are multifaceted and dynamic, from facilitative during the coral larval stage to competitive during the early post-settlement phase.
894077d2: Crustose coralline algae (CCA; order Corallinales) are coral reef foundation species that form calcified crusts cementing reef frameworks together and release chemical cues that encourage coral larval settlement. Yet despite their ecological importance, little is known about CCA diversity on tropical western Atlantic reefs. Most previous research within this region has relied on morpho-anatomical identification of species. However, DNA sequencing has revealed extensive cryptic diversity within red algae (Rhodophyta), meaning that species cannot be identified using morpho-anatomical characters. Further, in recent decades peyssonnelid algal crusts (PAC; order Peyssonneliales) have rapidly spread across shallow western tropical Atlantic coral reefs, growing to dominate the substrate in some locations. Previous research has shown that PAC microbiome communities host lower abundances of Pseudoalteromonas––an epibiotic bacterial genus known to facilitate invertebrate larval settlement and recruitment––compared to CCA. As a result, PAC may constrain coral recruitment on reefs where they dominate the benthos, potentially contributing to reduced reef resilience. By combining in situ field observations with DNA sequencing, associated microbiome community analyses, and coral larval settlement experiments, we aim to advance our understanding of the diversity and ecological importance of CCA and PAC within the Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary, USA, and the Saba National Marine Park, Dutch Caribbean. Preliminary results suggest that Neogoniolithon solubile is the most dominant CCA species at the collection sites within the Florida Keys. However, Neogoniolithon spectabile, Sporolithon dimotum, Sporolithon spp., Hydrolithon spp., Lithophyllum spp., Lithothamnion spp., Peyssonnelia spp., and Ramicrusta spp. have also been identified. Following no-choice settlement experiments with Orbicella faveolata larvae, preliminary results suggest that a Hydrolithon sp. induced the highest settlement (~40%), whereas a Peyssonnelia sp. induced the lowest (<10%). Identification to the species level and microbiome community analyses of Florida specimens are ongoing, and DNA has yet to be extracted from Saban specimens.
83ce6ed5:
Red coralline algae have been present in the geological record for over 137 million years, and over this expansive timeframe have been subjected to conditions not dissimilar to currently predicted climate change scenarios, including increased pCO2 and temperature, as well as secular changes in seawater magnesium-calcium (Mg/Ca) ratios. While these historic climatic changes have led to the rise and extinction of many coralline algae species, many have been able to withstand these climatic changes and are still thriving in our oceans today. However, despite the geological longevity of coralline algae, research over the past 25 years has demonstrated a sensitivity to ocean acidification and warming that is inconsistent with their geological record. 
To examine this contradiction, we selected three coralline algae species with different evolutionary histories and belonging to different taxonomic orders (Sporolithales: Sporolithon cf. durum, Hapalidiales: Lithothamnion cf. proliferum, Corallinales: Lithophyllum cf. insipidum) and grew them in treatments of varying seawater molar Mg/Ca ratios, pCO2 and temperatures to examine potential acclimation and adaption mechanisms in their skeletal mineralogy. The expectation is that ancient evolutionary lineages (e.g. Sporolithales) are more resistant to ocean acidification than the more recently evolved groups (e.g. Corallinales) because they were dominant during geological periods of high CO2. Preliminary results show that Sporolithon cf. durum grown under low Mg/Ca ratio treatments precipitated calcite with a lower Mg content, potentially providing an acclimatory mechanism to cope with changes in ocean chemistry.
Considering the crucial roles coralline algae play across many marine habitats, it is essential we gain a better understanding of the potential mechanisms used to acclimate to future environmental conditions.
7b3866d0: Calcifying macroalgae are vital to coral reefs, contributing to carbonate production, food webs, stabilization, and coral settlement. While calcification rates are well studied, stoichiometric datasets often overestimate organic carbon in calcifiers by reporting total carbon, conflating organic and inorganic fractions. We analyzed a reef-only global dataset of benthic seaweed stoichiometry (1975–2025), applying scaling factors derived from literature values and loss-on-ignition analyses (n = 152) to correct calcifying taxa for inorganic carbon. Our dataset included 3,104 samples (985 new from South Florida) across 55 regional reefs (263 sites) and 102 genera (18 calcifying). After correction, median molar C:N:P ratios were 1032:58:1 overall, 652:51:1 for calcifiers, and 1397:64:1 for non-calcifiers, indicating elevated carbon and nitrogen relative to phosphorus on reefs compared to global historical baselines (550:31:1) and modern observations (801:40:1) including non-reefs. Non-calcifying taxa incorporated significantly more carbon and nitrogen relative to phosphorus than calcifiers. Non-parametric tests confirmed significant stoichiometric differences between groups for C:N (p < 0.0001), C:P (p < 0.0001), and N:P (p < 0.001). Temporal trends revealed a significant decline in C:N for calcifiers (p < 0.001) but no significant changes in C:P or N:P, while non-calcifiers showed steep increases in C:P and N:P over five decades (p < 0.001), consistent with rising nitrogen relative to phosphorus on reefs. These patterns suggest fleshy macroalgae could outperform calcifiers under nitrogen enrichment, potentially accelerating reef community phase shifts and reducing calcifier abundance, with implications for reef resilience. Organic carbon ranged from 15-87.64% in calcifiers, highlighting a 22–667% overestimation of organic carbon in calcifiers in published datasets, underscoring carbonate bias in reef carbon budgets. However, given a high proportion of Florida samples in our dataset, we stress the need for expanded stoichiometric data from reefs across diverse oceanic basins to better capture global variability and improve reef nutrient and carbon models.
1ad313cc: In this talk, we will explore the practical applications of the chemistry associated with crustose coralline algae (CCA) tissues across several disciplines, including algal taxonomy, coral restoration, and reef conservation. Our focus will be on recent studies examining the metabolomes—the sets of small-molecule metabolites—of diverse and common CCA species from shallow coral reefs of the Australian Great Barrier Reef. We will present findings on the variability of metabolic profiles among species of the reef-building genus Porolithon, and discuss how these differences can aid taxonomic studies and species delineation. This approach is particularly valuable in highly diverse algal groups where species show limited morphological and anatomical variation, yet follow distinct evolutionary trajectories revealed through DNA sequencing. In the second part of the talk, we will examine the relationship between CCA metabolites and larval settlement success in a range of reef-building coral species. These insights have enabled us to propose hypotheses about potential coral settlement cues and their possible application in aquaculture to maximise coral larval settlement, with direct relevance to coral restoration practices. Finally, we will present a case study demonstrating how knowledge of CCA metabolic profiles can shed light on the drivers of larval settlement behaviour in the crown-of-thorns starfish (CoTS). Such information may also inform potential management strategies to mitigate population outbreaks of this significant coral predator. Together, these examples highlight the application of CCA chemical compound profiles in coral reef conservation, while also emphasising how little we currently know about the precise identity of these compounds.
721d5c8b: Accurate species richness measures are foundational to effective conservation, yet undescribed 'dark taxa' obscure diversity patterns. To address this, we established CoralBank, a repository integrating high-resolution imagery, skeletal vouchers, and tissue samples from over 10,000 coral colonies collected across the Indo-Pacific. Curated at the Queensland Museum Tropics, CoralBank has sequenced over 5,000 colonies using a hybrid phylogenomic strategy. We combine data from targeted capture of Ultraconserved Elements (UCEs) with low-coverage genome skimming. This approach allows us to cost-effectively harvest UCEs from skimmed samples for compatibility with capture datasets, while simultaneously generating genome-wide SNPs for fine-scale population analyses. Applying this comprehensive genomic framework to the hyper-diverse genus Acropora reveals extensive dark diversity. We identified over 200 distinct lineages, demonstrating that ~70% of species diversity is hidden by current taxonomy. Furthermore, biogeographic assessments indicate that many "widespread" species are complexes of range-restricted endemics, significantly altering our understanding of extinction risk. These findings underscore the critical role of modern natural history collections in reef science; by providing the taxonomic fidelity essential for reassessing conservation priorities. CoralBank proves we cannot effectively protect that which we have not accurately described.
5362ffa1: Understanding the evolutionary history of corals is key to interpreting how lineages diversify and persist under diverse environments. Corals of the Red Sea thrive under some of the warmest, saltiest, and most variable conditions on Earth, yet their evolutionary history at the genomic level remains largely unexplored. To investigate the evolution of Red Sea corals and the emergence and loss of symbiosis, we focused on the Pocilloporidae family. We generated high-quality genome assemblies for four species spanning contrasting ecological niches: the zooxanthellae, shallow-water reef builders Pocillopora favosa, Madracis kirbyi, and Stylophora pistillata, and the azooxanthellae deep-sea species Madracis interjecta. This contrast between shallow symbiotic corals and a deep, non-symbiotic species enabled a broad assessment of genomic features associated with environmental adaptation to mesophotic and deep-sea habitats, as well as the presence or absence of dinoflagellate symbiosis. By integrating phylogenomics, comparative genomics, and evolutionary analyses, we reconstructed the evolutionary history of the Pocilloporidae family of the Red Sea and identified gene family expansions and contractions, lineage-specific divergence, and genomic regions under heterogeneous selection linked to symbiosis state and depth-related adaptation. Together, these results illuminate the genomic mechanisms shaping diversification, symbiotic transitions, and environmental specialization within Red Sea Pocilloporidae.
c6e3e4b9:
Favites valenciennesii belongs to the family Merulinidae and is a reef-building coral widely distributed from tropical to temperate regions in the Indo-West Pacific Ocean. The colony shape is massive, and individual characteristics include polygonal corallite ranging from eight to 15 mm in diameter. A distinct feature is the “groove” that forms between individuals, which makes us easy to distinguish from others in the same genus. Fukami et al. (2009) reported two morphological types within this species: the “separation type”, which individuals are clearly separated, and the “adhesion type”, which individuals are joined together. However, no further morphological differences were found between these two types, even examination of skeletal microstructures (Huang et al. 2014).
In this study, we collected 41 samples of this species from temperate to subtropical regions in Japan. We quantified morphological data from their skeletal specimens to clarify species boundaries among these morphological types by integrating quantitative morphological and molecular phylogenetic analyses.
For morphological analysis, we measured 11 morphological features and performed variable selection using the Random Forest method prior to conducting principal multivariate analysis. In the molecular phylogenetic analysis, we used nuclear internal transcribed spacers (ITSs) of ribosomal RNA and mitochondrial intergenic region (IGR) between COI and tRNA as markers. 
The morphological analysis revealed four morphologically distinct types overall, with both separation and adhesion types divided into two more types. Moreover, the molecular phylogenetic analysis indicated that the two adhesion types formed clades within the genus Favites, while the two separation types formed clades within the genus Dipsastraea. Our results suggest that at least three distinct species are concealed within the polymorphism of F. valenciennesii.
fdc354e4: In highly connected reef systems, strong barriers are needed for lineages to diverge and persist as distinct species. Yet, it is not uncommon to find widely distributed but recently diverged lineages that are often attributed to isolation during Pleistocene low sea-level stands. The persistence of such divergence after sea levels rose implies either a post-isolation barrier to gene flow, or the formation of genomic islands, which is when divergence accumulated during periods of isolation is maintained by post-isolation selection. In the first scenario, we expect genomic divergence that is consistent with the timing of isolation, while in the second scenario, we expect genomic regions of high divergence that also exhibit signatures of selection. We tested these hypotheses by examining phylogenomic patterns in three Pachyseris speciosa lineages that have been detected from samples across Indonesia, Philippines, and Great Barrier Reef. The ‘blue’ and ‘green’ lineages are broadly distributed across the Indo–West Pacific and show more recent divergence relative to the ‘red’ lineage, which is largely restricted to the Great Barrier Reef. Divergence-time estimates and lineage distributions support the role of Pleistocene sea-level lows in isolating the ‘blue’ and ‘green’ lineages, while regional isolation in the western Pacific likely drove divergence of the ‘red’ lineage. The ‘blue’ and ‘green’ lineages exhibit heterogeneous genomic divergence, indicating that gene flow has continued following sea-level rise. Evidence of selection was detected in the most highly divergent regions, suggesting that the formation and persistence of genomic islands maintain the distinctiveness of the ‘blue’ and ‘green’ lineages despite ongoing gene flow.
1a1e17e6: Phylogenomic data are revealing that Acropora species previously considered as single morphologically variable species widely distributed across the Indo-Pacific actually comprise complexes of morphologically similar but genetically distinct lineages with much smaller geographic ranges. Somewhat paradoxically, the substantial increase in provincialism in Acropora also indicates that while the total diversity of Acropora is far higher than the 120–160 species proposed in the influential taxonomic revisions of Wallace (1999) and Veron (2000), species richness many sites is often lower than previously thought, particularly at isolated or peripheral locations. For example, phylogenomic analyses of the Acropora fauna at the remote Christmas Island in the Indian Ocean indicate that species richness is ~50% lower than previous estimates using traditional morphological taxonomy, and that the species turnover is high even between relatively proximal regions such as Ningaloo Reef. These patterns challenge the prevailing assumptions that have underpinned coral biogeography for the last half a century that local diversity can be inferred from regional species pools, and that small-ranged endemic species are rare. Instead, our results suggest local-scale factors such as isolation and habitat persistence over geological timescales exert a strong influence on species richness, taxonomic composition and endemism at any particular location. These findings highlight the need to reassess conservation priorities, as many newly recognised species have restricted ranges, increasing their vulnerability to localised disturbances and indicating that connectivity from adjacent regions will have little influence on recovery of coral populations on ecologically relevant timescales.
06355170: In combating rapid declines in coral populations, a major challenge is to link relevant information across species over spatial and temporal scales. Understanding whether patterns of genomic diversity and demographic histories inferred in studies of individual coral populations result from common evolutionary processes requires a unified framework of analysis. In this respect, comparative population genomics techniques, which study variation across related species to learn about shared evolutionary processes, have been underutilized in corals. We therefore set out to unify genomic analyses across studies and species, focusing on the reef-building species-rich Acropora genus, which have been the focus of many analyses. We collected high coverage whole genome resequencing (WGS) data from A. millepora colonies sampled from across the Great Barrier Reef and analyze these data alongside recently published WGS datasets from five additional Acropora species and high-quality reference genomes from twelve Acropora species. These species include Acropora from around the world, estimated to have diverged >50MYA, as well as closely related species with overlapping ranges. We generated a multi-species alignment and called variants using the same pipeline for all datasets and in so doing, developed a data-driven approach for quality control that we expect will be useful for other coral genomics studies. We characterized levels of genetic diversity, linkage disequilibria patterns, and population structure within each species and visualize genetic connectivity patterns across their ranges, identifying signals of asymmetric and long-range historical migration in each population and species. Additionally, we inferred a new phylogeny from whole genome data, identified genomic regions for which the genealogical history deviates from the species tree, and quantified putative introgression between species. Together, these analyses begin to paint a picture of the evolutionary history of the Acropora genus, enabling further cross-species analyses and identifying shared and unique patterns of evolution within the clade.
2cc5fbcf: Climate change is forcing many reef‑building corals at distributional margins to either adapt, shift their ranges, or go locally extinct, yet the evolutionary pathways by which tropical species give rise to temperate specialists remain poorly resolved. The temperate and highly threatened coral Acropora pruinosa, restricted to the waters of Japan and Hong Kong, is closely related to the tropical Acropora solitaryensis and thus provides a model for understanding such transitions. Here we test the hypothesis that A. pruinosa originated from an Indo‑Pacific A. solitaryensis ancestor in subtropical Hong Kong, subsequently expanded into the temperate coasts of Japan, and then diverged further into eastern and western Japanese lineages. We combined comparative skeletal morphology with genome‑wide SNP data spanning A. solitaryensis and A. pruinosa across their ranges. Colonies identified as A. pruinosa in Hong Kong display gross morphological characters intermediate between A. solitaryensis and Japanese A. pruinosa. Species‑tree inference in SNAPPER under the multispecies coalescent places Hong Kong A. pruinosa as a distinct, basal lineage that diverged after A. solitaryensis and before Japanese populations, which form two well‑supported clades corresponding to eastern and western Japan. Together with its intermediate morphology, this topology suggests that Hong Kong A. pruinosa retains more ancestral character states relative to Japanese populations and may approximate the ancestral lineage from which contemporary Japanese A. pruinosa originated. This pattern is consistent with a stepping‑stone scenario in which an A. solitaryensis‑like ancestor first colonized Hong Kong, evolved temperate‑tolerant traits characteristic of A. pruinosa, and then colonized and diversified within Japanese waters under geographic isolation. Our results reveal cryptic evolutionary diversification within A. pruinosa and illustrate how marginal environments can promote lineage formation along tropical–temperate transition zones. We further propose that Hong Kong, western Japan, and eastern Japan lineages should be treated as separate Evolutionarily Significant Units for future management.
2f440d75: The Western Indian Ocean (WIO) possesses a biogeographically significant history, shaped by the closure of the Tethys Sea and semi-permeable dispersal barriers, such as the Mozambique Channel. For the purposes of this study, we define the WIO as encompassing the coastal and island areas of Somalia, Kenya, Tanzania, Mozambique, South Africa, the Comoros, Madagascar, the Mascarene Islands, the Seychelles, and the Chagos Archipelago. The geological legacy has driven high regional endemism in many marine taxa, including fishes, echinoderms, and molluscs. In contrast, the WIO’s scleractinian coral fauna has long been considered anomalous, being dominated almost entirely by species with widespread Indo-Pacific distributions, which may imply differences in life history or ecology. However, recent phylogenomic advances suggest that morphology-based coral taxonomy has underestimated species richness and provincialism, particularly in speciose genera such as Acropora. According to current taxonomy and inferred geographic ranges, the WIO contains approximately 35 to 40 valid Acropora species, the vast majority of which are described as broadly distributed Indo-Pacific taxa, with an estimated two percent regional endemism. To reassess this framework, we used an integrated approach combining genomic-scale phylogenetics with comprehensive morphological character analysis. We reconstructed the most extensive phylogeny of WIO Acropora to date, comprising more than 300 specimens sampled across the region and complemented with material from the wider Indo-Pacific and Red Sea. Our results fundamentally revise the known biogeography of WIO Acropora and find that currently accepted taxonomy likely inflates estimates of species richness in the Indian Ocean while underestimating regional endemism, because taxa restricted to the WIO are misidentified as broadly distributed Indo-Pacific species. Phylogenomic results indicate that approximately half of the Acropora lineages in the WIO are endemic. Taxonomic recognition of this unique diversity is critical for accurate extinction-risk assessments and essential for evidence-based coral conservation, management, and restoration across the region.
164db3df: Recent advances in molecular phylogenomics have revealed an abundance of diversity across the tree of life obscured by traditional morphological taxonomy. The coral genus Acropora is no exception, with molecular data revealing that the taxonomy that has underpinned coral research for the last half century does not reflect the taxonomic diversity, biogeography or evolutionary history of the group. This issue is particularly problematic in remote and understudied locations, such as the Groote Archipelago (Gulf of Carpentaria, NT, Australia), where lack of comprehensive taxonomic assessment means the identity of species present and their biogeographic affinities remain unknown. Given that multiple mass bleaching events have been observed in recent years, establishing a comprehensive and accurate taxonomic account of coral species is paramount. Here, we present the first taxonomic assessment of the Acropora fauna from the Groote Archipelago based on an integrated approach combining molecular phylogenomics and morphological analysis and comparison to type material and original descriptions. We reconstructed a phylogeny based on 1539 UCE loci from 298 specimens collected from the Groote Archipelago and adjacent regions to identify monophyletic lineages indicative of species, which were then tested against additional lines of evidence (morphology and SNP-based species delimitation analyses) to delineate species. Our results indicate that the Groote Archipelago supports relatively few (~15-20) Acropora species; however, almost all species are either undescribed or have been synonymised with species from other regions of the Indo-Pacific. Groote’s coral fauna shows affinities with Western Australia and the Coral Triangle but not the Great Barrier Reef, suggesting the Torres Strait is a major biogeographic boundary in Acropora. This study represents the first taxonomic assessment of corals in the Gulf of Carpentaria, providing new insights into Acropora biogeography and highlighting the Groote Archipelago as a key coral-diversity junction between the GBR, Western Australia, and the Coral Triangle.
a62ec54e:
Understanding the genetic structure and evolutionary trajectories of reef-building corals is essential for predicting their responses to climate change. SNP-based approaches have proven highly effective for resolving cryptic diversity and population structure, as demonstrated in the Tara Pacific expedition. Here, we aimed to evaluate whether metatranscriptomic data could reproduce these results, offering a faster and reference-genome-independent alternative.
We first developed a set of single-copy orthologous markers by selecting conserved proteins used in BUSCO, identified across de novo metatranscriptomes generated from Pocillopora samples of the Tara Pacific expedition. Once validated, this marker set was applied to all Porites colonies from the same expedition.
Our results show that metatranscriptome-derived markers recover the same major lineages previously resolved using genome-wide SNPs. This demonstrates that transcriptome-based genotyping can achieve comparable discriminatory power while requiring significantly less time and without the need for a reference genome to pre-define and filter informative SNPs.
This proof of concept highlights the potential of standardized transcriptomic markers as a scalable, rapid, and taxonomically flexible tool for coral molecular taxonomy, particularly valuable for large-scale biodiversity assessments and conservation genomics.
c97a2301: The breakdown of the nutritional symbiosis between cnidarians and dinoflagellates underpins the global coral bleaching crisis, yet the cellular mechanisms governing the establishment and maintenance of this partnership remain poorly understood. While metabolic cooperation is well-documented, the identity, origin, and developmental plasticity of specific host cell types pre-adapted to recognize and house symbionts remain unresolved. Here, we present a high-resolution single-cell transcriptomic atlas of the model sea anemone Exaiptasia diaphana under both symbiotic and aposymbiotic conditions. Our analysis reveals substantial cellular heterogeneity and identifies a distinct symbiosis-competent cell population present in both symbiotic and aposymbiotic states, suggesting the host possesses a pre-adapted cellular framework for symbiosis regardless of algal presence. Trajectory inference from RNA velocity analysis further uncovers a condition-dependent plasticity in the differentiation of these cells. In symbiotic animals, symbiosis-competent cells predominantly differentiate from gastrodermal lineages; conversely, in the aposymbiotic state, they appear to originate from immune progenitors. This lineage switch is accompanied by divergent metabolic and immune signatures, where lysosomal activity acts as a key driver in the transition to symbiosis competence. Together, these findings suggest that cnidarian symbiosis is built on a flexible developmental program in which immune and digestive cell fates remain fluid, allowing dynamic modulation of symbiotic capacity in response to environmental and symbiotic cues.
4dfad142: Coral reef ecosystems are sustained by the metabolic partnership between cnidarian hosts and intracellular Symbiodiniaceae, which inhabit a specialized subset of host gastrodermal cells dedicated to symbiont maintenance and nutrient exchange.  Disruption of this relationship (coral bleaching), commonly through prolonged heat stress, can lead to coral mortality due to their dependence on algal symbionts for essential nutrition. As climate change drives rising seawater temperatures and increases the frequency of bleaching events, a detailed understanding of coral-algal symbiosis dynamics is urgently needed. Recent single-cell transcriptomic studies in reef-building corals have begun to illuminate host cell type diversity and symbiont-associated states; however, the cellular and molecular mechanisms that govern symbiosis establishment and maintenance remain largely unresolved. In particular, the transition from symbiont-free gastrodermal cells to symbiont-containing cells, along with the gene expression programs that define these states, are still poorly characterized. Bulk transcriptomic approaches obscure cell state distinctions by averaging gene expression across heterogeneous tissues, masking the cell-type-specific processes central to host–symbiont interactions. Here, we apply single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) to the cnidarian model sea anemone Exaiptasia diaphana (Aiptasia) to resolve host cellular diversity in both symbiotic and symbiont-free states. This approach enables us to compare transcriptional landscapes across individual host cells and characterize multiple distinct cell populations within both gastrodermis and epidermis tissue layers. We identify the cell state associated with intracellular symbiont occupancy and uncover transcriptional features that differentiate this state from symbiont-free gastrodermal cells. These findings provide a prioritized set of candidate genes and pathways for functional testing and offer new mechanistic hypotheses for how different host cell states support the establishment, stability, and breakdown of cnidarian-algal symbiosis.
60569923: Reef-building corals are complex holobionts, containing cross-kingdom symbioses, somatic genetic diversity, and internal microenvironments that differ substantially from the overlying seawater. Their two tissue layers are composed of diverse cell types with specialized roles in environmental sensing, biomineralization, and supporting photosynthetic endosymbionts. Traditional whole-organism gene expression analyses provide broad signatures of how corals respond to environmental perturbations, yet can obscure spatial context and cell-specific biology. This limits our ability to capture heterogeneity in biological baselines and also the spatial and cell-level complexity of the cascades leading from heat stress to coral bleaching. In light of these challenges, we present a series of experimental approaches to test the hypothesis that the processes that result in coral-algal dysbiosis originate in different cell types, and are therefore spatially partitioned. We demonstrated that coral tissues have spatially distinct gene expression profiles, and then applied bulk and spatially resolved approaches to study coral and symbiont gene expression under control and acute heat-stress conditions for three species representing diverse tissue morphologies. Laser capture microdissection and RNAseq revealed >1,500 differentially expressed genes involved in biomineralization, environmental sensing, and molecular transport between oral epidermis and aboral tissues in Pocillopora acuta. We generated single-nuclei expression atlases for P. acuta, Montipora capitata, and Porites compressa and identified that, in control conditions, key heat stress genes from the literature are expressed more substantially in certain cell types rather than uniformly across tissues. Lastly, our analysis of both bulk and spatially-resolved assays of an acute heat stress time series identified both shared and distinct expression patterns between these three coral species, with many heat stress genes showing spatial variation and species-specific differences in the timing of response initiation. Our high resolution approaches generate spatial and temporal information essential to disentangling the coral holobiont stress response and resilience.
d8e4339a: Coral bleaching—the stress-induced collapse of symbiosis between corals and their dinoflagellate algae—poses an escalating threat to reef ecosystems worldwide¹. Despite its ecological importance, the cellular mechanisms driving bleaching remain incompletely understood. Previously, we established Xenia sp. as a model system for studying coral endosymbiosis and developed an RNAi-based approach to investigate LePin function during the initiation of new symbiotic relationships. Here, using a menthol-induced bleaching model combined with single-cell RNA sequencing at early and late stages of bleaching, we characterize the transcriptional dynamics of coral cells under stress. We show that early in the bleaching response, coral cells downregulate adhesion-related complexes, priming themself for detachment. This precedes a coordinated upregulation of membrane-trafficking pathways and lysosomal digestion machinery at later stress stage. Together, our results provide a high-resolution landscape of cell-type-specific transcriptional changes during bleaching, establishing a foundation for deeper molecular insight into the mechanisms underlying coral bleaching.
15246cec: Coral reef ecosystems depend on the endosymbiosis between corals and photosynthetic dinoflagellates of the family Symbiodiniaceae. The relationships between coral hosts and their algal symbionts can be highly specific, but the mechanisms underlying why corals associate with certain Symbiodiniaceae species and not others are poorly understood. Establishing new coral-algal symbioses has the potential to increase coral survival in the face of climate change, but host-symbiont specificity could limit the availability of thermally tolerant species combinations. An established model to study specificity in the cnidarian-algal symbiosis is the tropical sea anemone Exaiptasia sp.. Exaiptasia sp. laboratory clone lines and populations across their geographic range are known to differentially associate with Symbiodiniaceae species, allowing us to examine the differences in gene expression that underlie distinct symbiotic pairings. Here, we develop an optimized single-cell sorting protocol to explicitly target the transcriptomic profiles of Exaiptasia cells hosting different Symbiodiniaceae strains. In combination with short-read and long-read transcriptomics, our algal-hosting cell focused approach will identify a set of candidate genes that are associated with the healthy functioning of species-specific symbioses in adult individuals. By clarifying the molecular language of specificity in cnidarian-algal symbiosis, our findings will help inform strategies to overcome host-symbiont compatibility constraints in coral reef conservation efforts into the future.
a15d4f44: The intra-cellular mutualisms between animals and dinoflagellate endosymbionts (Family: Symbiodiniaceae) are widespread and, most significantly, the foundation of Earth’s coral reef ecosystems. Symbiodiniacean dinoflagellates are diverse and associate with a broad diversity of invertebrate and single-celled hosts that require them for survival. The molecular communication between both eukaryotic partners involving signaling, recognition, and nutrient transfer is complex and poorly understood. The interface for this exchange occurs at the symbiont’s cell wall, which functions as both a barrier and filter that modulates the flux of molecules necessary to maintain this mutualism. Dinoflagellate cell walls are made primarily of cellulose, proteins and glycoproteins and bound in vesicles (called amphiesma) found beneath the plasma membrane, and are thus internal. Symbionts capable of living within and outside a host undergo a visible reduction in cell wall thickness for intracellular life, but it is unknown whether a change in cell wall molecular composition is responsible for this adaptation. Glycome profiling, an approach often used to study plant cells, was used to characterize pectin and hemicellulose related moieties in Symbiodinium microadriaticum and Breviolum minutum living in vitro and in hospite. Additionally, bioinformatic analyses of cell wall modification genes were obtained from available Symbiodiniaceae transcriptomes and compared against the CAZY (Carbohydrate-Active Enzymes) database to infer the evolution and origin of discovered carbohydrates. This data has revealed that previously uncharacterized carbohydrates in dinoflagellate cell walls such as xylans and mixed-linkage glucans make up a significant component of Symbiodiniaceae thecae and may be varying in abundance between symbiotic states.
fbd38dfa: The Great Barrier Reef faces escalating climate change impacts, necessitating a transition from proven ecosystem-based to resilience-based management strategies. The Reef Authority’s 2024 Outlook Report, Climate Change Position Statement, and Reef Blueprint 2030 collectively highlight the vulnerability of the Reef’s values to the consequences of mounting greenhouse gas emissions and the urgent need for integrated mitigation and local adaptation actions. Collaborative efforts involving scientists, Traditional Owners, policymakers, industries and the community are needed to inform and deliver adaptive management actions that protect Reef resilience. The Reef 2050 Long-Term Sustainability Plan serves as the multi-jurisdictional framework for actions aimed at protecting the biodiversity and cultural values across the Reef and its catchment. The Reef Authority’s climate resilience and adaptation strategy (Blueprint 2030) focuses on the Reef’s marine ecosystems, identifying high priority intervention options. New measures build on proven actions including Marine Park zoning and the long-running crown-of-thorns starfish control program, that have demonstrated their capacity to protect ecosystem resilience. The Reef Authority remains committed to advancing its 50-year adaptive management journey using the latest scientific guidance, climate scenarios and stakeholder engagement to safeguard the Reef’s ecosystems and values as the climate changes, consistent with the goals of the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park Act, for the benefit of all.
3703ed52:
The Great Barrier Reef (GBR) faces escalating pressures from climate change, pollution, and ecosystem degradation, necessitating advanced tools for effective management and intervention. This study introduces the development and validation of a digital twin—a coupled ocean–atmosphere numerical modelling system serving as a dynamic virtual representation of the GBR. The framework integrates a high-resolution (1 km) Regional Ocean Modeling System (ROMS) nested within the global Mercator Ocean NEMO model to provide realistic boundary conditions. Atmospheric forcing is derived from a global NCEP operational high-resolution model, supplying all variables required for heat, freshwater, and momentum exchange, while ocean tides are incorporated via modified ROMS boundary conditions. Freshwater inflows are a key driver of ocean dynamics and salinity distribution in coastal regions. In this study, we incorporated real-time hourly discharge data for major rivers within our modelling domain, obtained from the Queensland Government water monitoring network (https://water-monitoring.information.qld.gov.au/), as an additional forcing in the ROMS model. 
Since January 1, 2025, the system has operated in real-time, delivering 6-day forecasts that capture dynamically consistent reef responses under diverse scenarios. Validation was performed through comparisons with in situ observations from ocean gliders and dedicated drifters, ensuring accuracy and operational reliability. On top of the 3D ocean–atmosphere model, a Lagrangian module was implemented to simulate coral spawning enabling optimisation of restoration strategies and assessment of policy impacts prior to deployment. By providing a predictive, real-time, and interactive platform, this digital twin enhances resilience planning and supports evidence-based interventions, offering a scalable approach for large-scale marine ecosystem management.
3440bebb:
Reef interventions require strategic decisions about resource allocation, as management efforts typically reach only a small fraction of possible reefs. Determining where to intervene requires data on current reef state across vast areas, though not necessarily high-resolution taxonomic detail. Recent technological advances have transformed who can contribute such information via improved underwater photography, remote internet connectivity, and artificial intelligence.

The Great Reef Census provides the infrastructure for citizen scientists to easily collect standardised survey imagery, capitalising on public interest. Images undergo dual analysis: semantic segmentation by deep learning models and verification by online citizen scientists. This hybrid approach achieves over 95% accuracy for key coral morphologies when compared to expert assessments. The most recent annual survey programme documented approximately 380 reefs across the Great Barrier Reef within four months. The platform now operates globally, scaling up into regions including Hawaii, Red Sea, and Coral Triangle. The programme is operated through a collaborative network spanning universities, not-for-profits, Traditional Owners, government agencies, and technology companies.

To harness this infrastructure most effectively, a recent critical innovation optimises where surveys should be conducted, rather than relying on random volunteer deployment. Priority mapping algorithms identify reefs where resolving coral cover uncertainty most influences management decisions. This approach accounts for larval connectivity networks, disturbance history, and uncertainty propagation through reef networks. Several hundred strategically selected surveys can effectively inform intervention allocation across thousands of potential sites.

Applications include optimising pest control programmes that can reach less than 5% of reefs, targeting restoration efforts toward key larval source reefs, and prioritising fishing compliance enforcement. By combining accessible technology with decision-focused survey design, this approach demonstrates how broadscale citizen science can meaningfully enhance cost-effectiveness and ecological impact of targeted reef interventions.
a2fc6c6d: Coral reefs are experiencing unprecedented pressure as climate change accelerates, with coral populations worldwide approaching critical physiological limits. The world has recently experienced the Fourth Global Mass Bleaching Event (2023–present), which affected more than 80% of coral reef systems, including those in the Red Sea. Historically, the Red Sea, especially the north, has been regarded as a globally important thermal refuge, yet in 2024 the Northern Red Sea reached NOAA Coral Reef Watch’s Alert Level 5, and experienced multispecies bleaching, patchy mortality, and 28 DHW. Globally, reef management has entered an era of emergency response, requiring both proactive and reactive multi-layered approaches to mitigate potential mass bleaching episodes and anthropogenic stressors. Despite the regional expression of environmental stress in coral systems, management actions often remain localized, leading to reduced survivorship due to delayed intervention implementation. Together with regional partners, we will develop governance models for the Regional Emergency Response Plans to coordinate region-wide resilience frameworks in the face of accelerating environmental stress. Initially focusing on marine heat waves in the northern Red Sea, the framework will eventually integrate both immediate response actions with longer term proactive interventions across regional and temporal scales. The approach addresses several vectors of reef stress, including thermal anomalies, coral disease outbreaks, bleaching, loss of genetic diversity, coastal development, and ship groundings. We will present a strategy of layering interventions using key facilities and tools, including water cooling, supplemental nutrition, coral probiotic inoculation, wet-chain relocation, multi-taxa restoration, monitoring and biobanking. In conjunction with regulatory and corporate partners, mechanisms and assurances enabling targeted applied research to facilitate accelerated adaptation will be co-developed. Strengthening rapid-response capacity through the development of the Emergency Response Plans will help safeguard resilience and persistence within Red Sea coral systems and support the opportunity to replicate successful governance models elsewhere as needed.
a03a4114: The decade between 2014 and 2024 has been catastrophic for coral populations across the Caribbean, marked by the devastating spread of Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (begun in Florida in 2014 and marching across the region) as well as mass mortality from severe heatwaves both in 2014-15 and 2023-24.  Consensus is growing that this trend, along with the general failure of natural successful coral recruitment, has rendered many local coral populations in the Caribbean region as ‘small and isolated’ within a very short period of time. In small breeding populations, inbreeding depression becomes a factor that adds to local extinction risk.  In response, a range of coral restoration practitioners, previously trained in coral breeding and larval propagation by SECORE International, has come together to develop ecological best practices and work cooperatively through the formidable permitting hurdles at the local, national, and international levels to implement a plan of reciprocal coral larval exchange across eight countries. Each of these groups has identified inadequate breeding stocks of broadcast-spawning, reef-building species as a hurdle to restoration success.  The Caribbean Larval Exchange will buy time for Caribbean corals by bolstering local breeding populations and can serve as a future model for other regions of the world as well as, potentially, for more intensive interventions (e.g. selective Assisted Gene Flow or ecological replacement) as coral population decline is expected to worsen with ongoing climate change.
0517348c:
The UNDP-supported, Adaptation Fund–financed project Restoring Marine Ecosystem Services by Rehabilitating Coral Reefs to Meet a Changing Climate Future is being implemented in Seychelles and Mauritius to improve reef resilience through targeted, context-specific interventions. The project aims to reduce climate-related impacts on local communities and reef-dependent economies by restoring reef structure and function across six marine protected areas using diverse, scalable approaches.

Nearly 6 hectares of degraded reef habitat are being rehabilitated using a multi-method strategy that reflects the practical realities, constraints, and opportunities at different spatial scales. In Seychelles, partners are refining mid-water rope nurseries and trialing multiple outplanting techniques—including cement stabilisation and modular frames—to improve survival and efficiency under heat stress and high-energy conditions. In Mauritius and Rodrigues, teams are implementing seabed-mounted rope and table nurseries suited to shallow, cyclone-prone lagoons where intervention design must prioritise structural stability and maintenance feasibility. Across all sites, partners are documenting operational challenges, environmental limitations, and cost–effort considerations that influence intervention success.

To expand intervention capacity, the project is also establishing three land-based nurseries (one operational in Seychelles and two in development in Mauritius), enabling the use of both sexual and asexual propagation and ensuring a more reliable and resilient coral supply. Community participation is central to implementation: more than 100 beneficiaries from the community have been trained and employed in nursery construction, propagation, and outplanting, strengthening stewardship and long-term maintenance potential.

Running from 2020 to 2026, the project provides practical, evidence-based insights into how different restoration interventions perform in diverse ecological and disturbance regimes—and what is required to scale them effectively. Lessons learned across the Western Indian Ocean highlight opportunities to improve efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and transferability of interventions, supporting managers and practitioners seeking to strengthen reef resilience at small, medium, and large scales.
37d2c719: Coral reefs are rapidly declining due to climate stress and local pressures, making effective management essential for sustaining biodiversity and ecosystem function. Although MPAs and locally managed areas are widely promoted, there is limited empirical evidence comparing their ecological performance against unprotected reefs, especially in data-poor regions of the Coral Triangle. This study addresses this gap by assessing coral and fish community status across 60 sites in Indonesia and the Philippines with different management regimes to evaluate how management influences reef condition and resilience. We used underwater visual censuses to quantify fish biomass, abundance, and community composition, alongside assessments of macrobenthos. We measured benthic components through photo-quadrat surveys at 8–10 m depth. We calculated fish and macrobenthos diversity using the Shannon index, and quantified benthic cover using CPCe. We compared coral and fish communities across management regimes using univariate tests for biomass, diversity, and coral cover, and multivariate analyses (nMDS, PERMANOVA, SIMPER) to assess community structure and management-driven differences. Preliminary analyses of the fish data indicate that management effectiveness is beginning to show measurable ecological benefits, although these patterns are not yet consistent across all metrics. Fish biomass differed significantly among management regimes, with reefs inside Regional MPAs supporting substantially higher biomass than unmanaged sites. In contrast, locally managed areas did not show clear improvements relative to unprotected reefs. Fish diversity, however, showed no significant differences across management categories, suggesting that diversity may respond more slowly or be less sensitive to current management interventions. These findings provide early evidence that formal MPAs may deliver stronger ecological benefits than locally managed or unmanaged reefs, but further analyses integrating coral and benthic data are needed to understand the full scope of management impacts and reef resilience across the region.
2a55f46b:
Costa Rica’s South Pacific region hosts coral communities of high ecological and socio-economic importance, yet faces increasing pressures from climate change, sedimentation, and coastal development. Since 2016, Raising Coral Costa Rica has led one of the country’s most sustained and scalable coral restoration initiatives, transitioning from a proof-of-concept effort into an integrated ecological and community-centered intervention. This case study synthesizes lessons from nearly a decade of work implementing adaptive coral gardening, community-based restoration, and a pilot model of regenerative tourism within the context of a developing country with limited technological infrastructure and constrained financial resources.

Guided by the ecological restoration principles of Suding et al. (2015), the program has built resilience into restoration design by combining multiple approaches, including nursery propagation, direct substrate attachment, genotype-diverse outplanting, and multi-species cultivation, with diversified site selection across depth, exposure, and environmental regimes. These strategies have been informed by real-time ecological responses, particularly during extreme disturbances such as the 2023–2024 global coral bleaching event.

Central to the program is its commitment to strengthening local capacity and fostering long-term stewardship. Through the Jardineros de Coral, the initiative has created meaningful pathways for community members,including women and youth, to participate in restoration activities, gain marine resource management skills, and increasingly assume leadership roles. While its regenerative tourism component remains in a pilot phase, early implementation demonstrates potential for future income generation, strengthening financial sustainability, and linking visitors directly with reef restoration outcomes.

By integrating ecological restoration, community empowerment, and early-stage blue-economy innovation, the South Pacific restoration program demonstrates that locally grounded approaches can enhance reef resilience under climate stress while contributing to social and economic well-being. This model offers actionable insights for other developing nations seeking to rebuild reef function, strengthen ecosystem services, and cultivate community resilience in the face of accelerating environmental change.
b828a290: In response to declining coral reefs, restoration programs have been established to promote coral conservation and recovery by collecting, propagating, and outplanting corals to depauperate reefs. Despite extensive efforts throughout the region, prolonged evaluations of methodological factors that drive outplant success are limited. Here we synthesize the results of five long-term (3-8 years) Acropora cervicornis outplanting experiments conducted between 2008-2025 across 21 local reef sites in southeast Florida to evaluate the impact of site, outplant density, colony size, and attachment method on survival and change in live tissue area (LTA). Analysis was limited to the first three years of each experiment to facilitate comparison. From 2008-2018, single A. cervicornis fragments were outplanted using epoxy, nail and cable ties, or cement pucks and survival varied widely (0-100%). Beginning in 2019, we transitioned to outplanting multi-fragment clusters using epoxy or cement. Mean 3-year outplant survivorship of the clusters, regardless of site and attachment method, was 90% and significantly greater than single fragments (32%). Cluster survival remained consistently high for all years, while single fragment survival significantly declined over time. Additionally, for multi-fragment clusters, 3-year cement outplant survival (96%) was higher, although not significantly different, than epoxy clusters (87%) across all sites, while change in LTA was equal for both methods. Furthermore, change in cluster LTA differed significantly among sites and is likely limited by partial mortality, such that 63% of clusters did not reach their 3-year expected LTA. Our results indicate that multi-fragment A. cervicornis clusters attached with cement provide the greatest long-term outplant survivorship and LTA increase for southeast Florida. Following recent bleaching events, efforts are underway to assess nursery and outplant genotypic thermal tolerance, such that this performance metric can be incorporated into local restoration planning and ultimately improve the future viability of restored reefs in Southeast Florida.
6e02f08d: Climate change impacts on coral reef ecosystems are rapidly pushing them to tipping points that are likely to be irreversible without dramatic change in human behavior. The speed with which this is occurring varies worldwide but is accelerating everywhere, with some areas having already surpassed these thresholds. Florida is one such area where reefs are in immediate need of more radical approaches to maintain some capacity for future recovery. New approaches, such as the first-ever wild outplanting of corals bred from international sources for increased heat tolerance (“Flonduran” elkhorn corals, generated by crossing corals from Florida with corals from an exceptionally warm reef in Honduras) have already begun, under permit from authorities. Further discussions around additional, more radical approaches, such as breeding corals from different populations around the Caribbean, ecological replacement using introduced species from outside the region, and the use of genetically modified organisms, are already underway. Objections to these new approaches typically center around outcome uncertainty and a lack of readiness to give up on “natural” ecosystem recovery. Yet, having these “end-of-life” discussions and comparing them to realistic alternatives has longstanding precedent in human healthcare, changes the perspective around radical interventions, and is a responsible course of action under the circumstances. Importantly, it does not mean giving up on a future for reefs: dwindling hope for old approaches must be replaced by kindling motivation for new ones. While sites like Florida have utility as living laboratories where new scientific and regulatory innovations can be developed to help re-shape twentieth-century norms and governance frameworks, advance planning for similar approaches elsewhere must begin immediately to help frame, motivate, and permit the more radical coral reef interventions that will be needed to ensure quality of life for the increasingly modified and managed coral reef ecosystems of the future.
21ea91aa: Coral reefs will face extreme thermal stress over the coming decades, and their persistence will require evolutionary adaptive change. Various novel actions and tools aiming to promote and accelerate evolutionary adaptation are being developed and considered. Ideally, novel methods for assisted evolution in restoration are implemented in a manner that maximises ecosystem benefits and minimises risks. Without planning frameworks, however, the application of assisted evolution methods might devolve into an unstructured crisis response. To date, systematic and transparent criteria for implementing assisted evolution actions have not been developed, especially considering their demographic and evolutionary consequences across a connected reef system. Here, we present a conservation and restoration decision framework for creating system-wide benefits with assisted evolution based on changes in thermal regimes, dispersal connectivity, and the type of action. We consider three potential management objectives: 1) maintaining reefs that are pre-adapted to their local future state; 2) bolstering reefs that contribute to system-wide thermal adaptation, and 3) undertaking high-risk restoration R&D at locations that minimise system-wide risks. We consider management actions of conservation and local restoration along with restoration actions that enhance adaptive responses in the light of predicted thermal changes on a reef, and dispersal connectivity manifested as self-recruitment, inflow, and outflow of locally and system-wide adapted or maladapted larvae. We illustrate this framework with a worked example for the Great Barrier Reef reefscape to highlight reefs where different types of restoration actions will likely create beneficial not unintended (perverse) management outcomes, allowing room for both established and risky new technologies. We outline this framework of decision criteria to enable smart decision making in the era of assisted evolution actions.
f372ccd1: Marine heatwaves are triggering mass coral mortality, threatening the persistence of coral reefs under climate change. Identifying and propagating heat-tolerant corals is being considered as a strategy to enhance population resilience, yet the fitness cost of adult heat tolerance across life stages remains poorly understood. Here, we selectively bred Acropora aff. digitifera from parents with experimentally determined high or low tolerance to simulated marine heatwaves and assessed how parental phenotype influenced early stages' performance. Embryos from distinctive heat-tolerant families were exposed for 27 hours to either control conditions or acute heat stress (+3°C), after which larval survivorship, settlement success, and early recruit growth were quantified. Parental heatwave tolerance had a significant influence on offspring performance. Under ambient conditions, larvae from high-heat-tolerant parents showed reduced survivorship and settlement compared to those from low-heat-tolerant parents. Under heat stress, offspring from high-tolerant linages performed equally or better than those from low-tolerant parents. When the effects of larval survivorship and settlement were combined, overall reproductive success declined with increasing parental tolerance under ambient conditions, indicating a potential life-stage trade-off, but showed no improvement under acute stress. These results demonstrate that selection for adult heatwave tolerance can influence offspring fitness in complex and context-dependent ways, underscoring the importance of evaluating tradeoffs at different life stages and environments when designing interventions such as selective breeding for coral conservation.
5026e67c: Selective breeding of corals is a promising route towards the establishment of heat-resistant corals for reef restoration. Here, we successfully implemented the concept of post-hoc selective breeding (i.e. thermal selection at the larval stage) to improve the heat resilience of the brooding coral Favia fragum. Shortly after collection, larvae were exposed to a gradually increasing temperature until mortality exceeded 50%. Surviving larvae were allowed to settle and were raised to a recruit size of at least 3mm diameter before subjecting them to a second heatwave. At the start of this validation heatwave, only 5% of the initial batch of heat-treated larvae had survived. During the validation heatwave, the apparent photochemical yield of Photosystem II (PSII) of the symbiotic algae (an indicator for thermal bleaching) in non-selected wildtypes crashed at a temperature of 32°C, whereas PSII yield in the thermally selected recruits remained reduced yet stable at this temperature. The identity of the dominant symbiont type in the corals (Breviolum sp.) did not change after thermal selection, showing that the observed change in thermotolerance must be associated with the coral host. We conclude that post-hoc selective breeding can be a valuable addition to thermal selection of parent colonies, thus accelerating the speed of thermal improvement among cultured corals. Currently, we are further testing the concept of post-hoc selective breeding by repeating the study described above with offspring of thermally selected parent corals.
584bf5ed: As climate change continues to warm oceans, it is critical to understand the relationships between thermal tolerance and the reproductive output of corals. Selective breeding of coral in the context of restoration is ongoing in Hawaii, however this work is dominated by one coral species, Montipora capitata. Diversifying species available for breeding, especially those with different life history traits, reproductive biology and ecological function is important for effective conservation. Here, we aim to develop a new selective breeding model for P. lobata in Kaneohe Bay, Oahu, Hawaii based on direct feedback from the community expressing the ecological and cultural importance of this species. We established a model population of 100 colonies in the field and collected histology samples throughout the summer of 2025 to 1) identify the sex of the colony, 2) the timeline of gametogenesis, and 3) quantify fecundity. Fecundity was quantified by counts of all visible gametes per polyp across multiple polyps per one section per slide. Small fragments from field source colonies were collected to explore tradeoffs in this species, including the relationship between fecundity and other important traits like growth and thermal tolerance. These fragments were maintained ex situ and buoyant weighed to determine growth before being exposed to a standardized heat-stress assay that reflects natural beaching patterns and elicits. We used PAM fluorometry during this stress test to resolve continuous thermal tolerance scores, which will be compared with fecundity and growth metrics to assess tradeoffs. The development of additional selective breeding models supports selective breeding for restoration practitioners in Hawaii, creating greater ecological diversity for this conservation approach.
4a89465d: Coral reefs around the world are rapidly disappearing, primarily as a consequence of global warming. The heat tolerance of corals is largely determined by their microbial photosymbionts in the family Symbiodiniaceae. Therefore, manipulating photosymbiont communities may enhance the ability of corals to survive summer heatwaves. Heat-tolerant and -sensitive photosymbiont species occur naturally on the Great Barrier Reef, but corals that associate with the most heat-tolerant species in the genus Durusdinium are nutritionally compromised at ambient temperatures compared with those harbouring more sensitive species in the Cladocopium genus. Further, even corals that harbour Durusdinium species have been observed to bleach during summer heatwaves. We employed experimental evolution (i.e., laboratory selection) of Symbiodiniaceae cultures under elevated temperatures and demonstrate this approach can enhance their upper thermal tolerance in vitro. Some heat-evolved strains also confer improved thermal tolerance on their coral hosts; enhanced tolerance was observed across coral life stages, in taxonomically divergent corals and in the presence of native symbionts. Notably, coral growth under ambient conditions was not compromised when heat-evolved Cladocopium was present. The new symbioses with heat-evolved symbionts were shown to be stable for at least two years in aquarium experiments. Recent field trails confirmed our laboratory findings, with outplanted adult coral fragments that harboured a mix of heat-evolved and native photosymbionts exhibiting no bleaching and the highest photophysiological fitness throughout the 2024 summer heatwave, while fragments with only native photosymbionts paled and had lower fitness. Thermal enhancement of corals is a critical component of coral reef restoration, with photosymbiont manipulation having the potential to make an important contribution to this goal.
9a7388dc: To enhance coral thermal tolerance, we subjected Cladocopium goreaui to four years (~200 generations) of experimental evolution at increasing temperatures up to 31°C. The ten heat-evolved (HE) strains exhibited lower reactive oxygen species secretion, stable photosynthetic performance, and growth compared to wild-type (WT) strains (maintained at 27°C). RNA-seq analysis of in hospite HE (three) vs WT (one) strains at ambient temperature suggested constitutively elevated levels of carbon fixation and reduced photosynthesis rates contribute to thermal tolerance. In addition, we performed a genome-wide SNP analysis on the same RNA-seq data set and identified 15,640 polymorphic variants across 4,739 scaffolds (covering 36% of the expressed genome). While this approach captures only actively transcribed regions and likely underestimates genomic diversity, most SNPs were shared across strains with no strain specific private alleles. However, significantly different allele frequencies of 339 SNPs on 155 scaffolds separated WT and HE strains, with 11 additional SNPs distinguishing among HE strains. Some of the significant SNPs affected genes related to photosynthesis and thermal stress, and patterns of allele frequency variation as well as low linkage disequilibrium for a subset of the scaffolds indicated that recombination of standing genetic variation contributed to strain differentiation. These results highlight the evolutionary potential of Symbiodiniaceae through recombination of existing genetic variation and suggest that future experimental evolution studies should prioritize genetically diverse starting material to speed up adaptation.
ffaa84c7: The frequency and severity of bleaching level thermal stress events faced by coral reefs in recent years has made it clear that their natural adaptation capacity is struggling to keep pace with rapid climate warming. Interventions, such as the use of heat-evolved coral symbionts, may accelerate the rate at which corals can adapt. Recent research has confirmed that providing corals with these novel symbionts does increase their resilience to bleaching, but their potential when used in concert with other symbionts remains unexplored. Here we show that the intervention of using heat-evolved symbionts can be synergistic, with inoculum containing two heat evolved symbionts (one Cladocopium and one Durusdinium strain) increasing bleaching resilience by 6 DHWs beyond what could be achieved using individual heat evolved strains. Additionally, we show that hosting a second symbiont strain (heat evolved or wild type Cladocopium or Durusdinium), even at low abundance (< 7%), can provide a significant increase in bleaching resilience when compared to corals hosting only one strain. Our results suggest that providing corals with multiple heat-evolved symbionts could enhance resilience to climate warming further than previously expected, and that background symbionts may play a major role in dictating coral bleaching resilience.
61e7bf57: Probiotics are a powerful tool to support coral health and resilience in the face of the unprecedented coral reef decline worldwide. However, a bottleneck of probiotic-based interventions is the low culturable diversity of coral-associated bacteria. Expanding this diversity is essential to improve the scope and application of microbiome-based therapies for human-assisted evolution of corals. This study presents the development of a high-throughput culturing platform and its use for the first direct comparison of culturing diversity across marine growth media, to enhance coral probiotic development.
