We applied microfluidic single-cell sorting with systematic media comparison for high-throughput recovery of coral-associated microbes compared to traditional approaches. A total of 17,136 bacterial cells were isolated from Caribbean coral mucus samples and cultured across arrays of minimal, general, and rich media with subsequent comparisons of salt compositions and individual versus community growth. Our system allowed us to compare growth performance and the ratio of individual cells able to grow under each condition. Cultured isolates were then pooled from each condition and sequenced to assess taxonomic composition and diversity. Minimal media yielded greater isolate recovery and taxonomic richness, while Instant Ocean salts improved the recovery of unique taxa. Our platform also allowed a comparison of culturable diversity between microbes grown individually versus grown together, which followed the same general trends but highlighted strains that need a community versus those normally outcompeted and kept at low abundance. From these targeted libraries, we developed bioassays using engineered fluorescence-based assays for the rapid screening of traits like antimicrobial activity, which were combined with laboratory automation for high-throughput screening. Our results demonstrate that upgraded probiotic discovery pipelines can accelerate the identification of diverse, host-associated bacteria with therapeutic potential—paving the way for more effective, scalable solutions supporting innovative biotechnological approaches to coral reef restoration.
e23f7e35: On the Great Barrier Reef, crown-of-thorns sea stars (COTS) occur in two population states: a high-density outbreak state where aggregations overconsume coral followed by population collapse, and a long-term low-density state with stable population sizes. Despite their ecological significance, the drivers of COTS irruptions remain poorly understood because population dynamics, age structure, and the habitat conditions supporting low-density populations are largely unresolved. Transitions between low-density and outbreak phases are likely influenced by factors that include population density, the reef’s fishing history, past disturbances, and the current state of coral cover. This study investigated non-outbreak COTS at One Tree Reef (OTR) in the southern Great Barrier Reef, a reef system protected from extractive fishing and tourism for 50 years. We quantified long-term, adult abundance and distribution across three sites over 16 years as well as associated benthic community. Sampling was conducted at three lagoon sites (Shark Alley, Green Point, and the Labyrinth), recording COTS size and identity using natural markings and madreporite coding. COTS densities were highest at Shark Alley and fluctuated from 2008–2024, with peaks detected in 2019 and 2024. A quadratic model best described abundance patterns, indicating an initial decline followed by a small increase toward 2024. Size-structure analyses showed a transition from diffuse, bimodal distributions in 2008–2009 to sharper, unimodal distributions dominated by large individuals (>50 cm) from 2018 onward, with few juveniles recorded. Recaptured individuals over 15 years detected individual COTS of 10+ years of age. These findings show the important demographic insights that can be determined from low-density COTS populations where known individuals can be tracked. We show that low-density populations can persist without escalating into high-density states, potentially influenced by the long-protected status of OTR. Our results indicate that outbreaks are not an inevitable phase in the population dynamics of this coral predator.
4abb8d9d: Queen conch, Aliger gigas, were listed as threatened under the United States Endangered Species Act in February 2024 prompting special considerations before coastal construction projects. Permitters now require the relocation and monitoring of individuals of every size to avoid take from direct impacts. Relocating conch has many challenges including care for the animals during transport, survival, and integration at the receiver site. Previous relocations have reported extensive mortality from predation and migration away from the receiver site. Here we report on the largest permitted queen conch relocation effort to date in the United States. Over the course of seven days, 138 conch were collected from the 8 hectare (80,000 m²) direct impact area and the 3.8 ha (38,000 m²) 50 m buffer zone.  Each collected individual was tagged and relocated nine miles north to a 9 ha receiver site in Pompano Beach. Weekly monitoring of the 50 m buffer zone yielded an average of 10.6 conch per week migrating into the direct impact area. Initial assessment of the receiver site in September found 1,824 conch unevenly dispersed over 9 ha: 54.2% mature adults, 29.2% subadults, and 16.6% juveniles. The most concentrated hectare had 603 conch and another had 336 conch. 29 occurrences of mating, 51 conch laying eggs, and 45 unaccompanied egg masses were documented in September. Two weeks after relocation, 27 tagged conch were found in the hectare they were placed and 18 in three randomly chosen adjacent hectares. Ten occurrences of mating and six laying eggs were seen in November, well outside of their expected mating season. These results will be used to inform and adapt future conch relocation plans to reduce take during marine construction.
a30fe0c6:
Coral reef sponges remain underrepresented in restoration and management efforts despite their ecological importance and substantial species richness on Caribbean reefs. Although frequently portrayed as competitors of corals, in situ manipulations reveal that coral mortality increases by an order of magnitude when sponge neighbors are removed. Long-term census data also show that sponges are declining in both species number and volume, contradicting assertions that they will inevitably overwhelm corals under Anthropocene pressures. Much of the controversy stems from the fact that some studies emphasize the deleterious effects of excavating sponges on corals, while others highlight the beneficial roles non-excavating species provide, such as reducing bioerosion of coral skeletons and stabilizing corals with compromised bases. Interaction dynamics are further shaped by coral behaviors, including the deployment of sweeper tentacles and digestive filaments that can damage neighboring organisms and maintain space around coral tissue.
To better characterize these interactions, I conducted a series of in situ experimental manipulations that quantified how sponges interact with corals versus unoccupied reef substrate and how corals respond to direct sponge contact. Across experiments, sponges occupied vacant reef space more frequently than space occupied by coral tissue, suggesting that many interactions described as competitive overgrowth may instead reflect opportunistic colonization of available substrate. Corals exhibited species-specific relationships with different sponge neighbors, patterns consistent with behavioral differences observed in the field. I also found evidence that corals may digest neighboring sponges using extruded digestive filaments, creating localized space between living coral tissue and adjacent sponge surfaces. An analysis of a year-long observational dataset reinforced patterns seen in time-series studies: few sponges actively overgrow corals, and both taxa are experiencing rapid decline. These findings emphasize the urgency of incorporating both corals and sponges into management strategies, as they share important beneficial associations and each provides distinct ecosystem services.
f0b0a97f: Globally, coral rubble beds are becoming more prevalent and abundant due to increasing anthropogenic impacts. Despite this, our understanding of the biodiversity and community composition differences of coral rubble organisms is lacking. Here, we aim to determine the factors influencing infaunal differences in coral rubble beds in the Turks and Caicos Islands. We collected coral rubble and its associated infauna from 53 sites across the archipelago. Following identification of the specimens, we used logistical regressions models, principal component analyses, and two-way ANOVA to determine if differences in coral rubble biodiversity is influenced by abiotic factors, such as rubble patch size, volume of space within the matrix, and type of rubble present, and biotic factors, such as adjacent habitat. Analysis of the almost 2,800 specimens shows that a higher total specimen abundance is found in smaller rubble patches, and in those with live coral as the closest adjacent habitat. The likelihood of individual phyla presence was influenced by different factors, with very few driving differences in more than one phylum. These results highlight the complexity of community composition within coral rubble beds and demonstrate that additional knowledge is still needed so we can fully understand the function of these reef microhabitats.
6fd9c2de: Sea urchins in the genus Tripneustes have fast growth and boom-and-bust population dynamics. Tripneustes australiae is endemic to southeast Australia and northwest New Zealand and is currently undergoing a population increase (‘boom’ phase) in Lord Howe Island Marine Park (LHIMP), the southernmost coral reefs. Grazing pressure in population outbreaks of this species creates barrens-type habitat. We used Reef Life Survey data to document the density of T. australiae spanning 18 years (2006-2024). Two outbreaks were evident, the first started in 2006 followed by a marked decline and the second started in 2022. The Admiralty Islands appear to be a recruitment hot spot for this species. The size of T. australiae in 2024 (most < 5 cm test diameter) indicated recent recruitment. Most individuals were found at 6-12 m depth and were juveniles as confirmed by the absence of gonads. Over 18 years there was no difference in the densities of T. australiae in ‘no take’ sanctuary zones and habitat protection zones of LHIMP with the exception of the recent outbreak in 2024. The drivers of the boom-and-bust population dynamics of T. australiae are likely related to inherent demographic traits of the genus associated with fast growth to reproductive maturity and a short life span. Recruitment anomalies of T. australiae to LHIMP may be related to climate warming and altered current connectivity from the Australian continent. The population dynamics of and ecological impacts T. australiae are important to understand in the high conservation value Lord Howe Island World Heritage Area ecosystems.
35cd8bab: Coral reefs have, in many cases, experienced a phase shift away from coral dominance. Particularly in the Caribbean, degraded reefs are now often dominated by macroalgae, sediment laden turfs, octocorals, and sponges. Intense competition and strong Allee effects have resulted in a strong coral recruitment bottleneck maintaining a stable low coral cover condition and, thus, restoration efforts abound in the region. Direct enhancement of reef grazer populations offers a cost-effective, stable, and persistent strategy for mediating coral-algal interactions and increasing local coral settlement and recruitment rates. While echinoderms have long dominated the grazers enhancement conversation, the Caribbean king crab offers an opportunity to address the issues of mediating coral-algal interactions to support coral recruitment and outplant survival, and of scale throughout the Caribbean and Florida region. We found a significant (50 – 80%) decrease in algae cover on sites where crab density was enhanced, along with significant (3-5-fold) increases in coral recruitment and fish community complexity. Aquaculture of the species has proved challenging, but scalable, to support restoration efforts throughout the region. Importantly, Caribbean king crabs appear to be complementary in function with other crab and urchin species under evaluation for enhancement to support restoration. The Caribbean king crab presents a strong potential for facilitating improved restoration outcomes beyond simple increased growth and survival of outplanted corals with several key regulatory challenges to overcome, particularly in Florida (USA). Here we will relay the successes, challenges, and potential economic opportunities associated with scaled production of the species to support a holistic coral reef restoration strategy in the Caribbean and Florida.
87800425:
Small-scale fisheries (SSFs) are critical to the livelihoods and food security of millions of people globally, particularly in low-income coastal regions where alternative livelihoods are limited. In South-West Madagascar, Vezo communities rely almost entirely on marine resources, and Locally Managed Marine Areas (LMMAs) have emerged as key tools for context-appropriate, community-led fisheries management. They implement a suite of management measures, including no-take zones across coral reefs, seagrass beds and mangroves, temporary octopus closures, and bans on destructive fishing practices.
Marine macroinvertebrate gleaning, primarily undertaken by women and children at spring low tide, represents a vital but largely overlooked component of Vezo livelihoods. Despite its importance for food security, income generation, and the ecological functioning of coastal ecosystems, this fishery remains data-poor and mostly absent from monitoring and management. This study provides one of the first comprehensive characterizations of the multi-species gleaning fishery in the Velondriake LMMA. Using ecological surveys of gleaned habitats, landing surveys, and socio-economic interviews with gleaners, we assessed ecosystem condition, invertebrate populations, the structure and importance of the fishery, and its role within the broader socio-ecological system.
Results highlight the high diversity of invertebrate species harvested and the fishery’s essential contribution to household food security and, for many gleaners, their only source of income. However, sustainability concerns are evident: heavily targeted species show low abundances and small size structures, gleaned habitats exhibit degraded ecological conditions, and some species may be locally disappearing. Given the fishery’s socio-economic importance and the ecological role of macroinvertebrates in supporting higher-value fisheries, integrating gleaning into existing management frameworks is essential. These findings provide baseline data to inform adaptive, community-led decision making and to support more holistic resource management; and we discuss potential strategies tailored to an LMMA contect.
6e4b7469: Acropora palmata is a key branching coral in the Caribbean, contributing to reef complexity and providing habitat for diverse species. In recent decades, its abundance and distribution have declined due to various factors, highlighting the need to understand its ecological role, as its loss could affect a broader range of organisms than previously recognized. Visual surveys conducted along linear transects in the Limones and Picudas reefs (Quintana Roo, Mexico) identified macrofaunal species associated with A. palmata and evaluated their interactions to understand habitat use. The coverage of A. palmata colonies and the density of individuals per unit of covered area were also estimated. A total of 80 species were recorded, 38 shared between reefs. Limones exhibited more species (67) compared to Picudas (54), though Picudas had a higher density of individuals (10.68 ind/m² vs. 2.13 ind/m²). Among the groups recorded were fish, crustaceans, polychaetes, echinoderms, and mollusks. The composition of species associated with A. palmata was similar across reefs. This coral creates physical barriers that segment colony areas, generating spaces used as habitat, refuge, feeding zones, and territorial defense. Although colony areas influenced species abundance, no effect on size was detected. The disappearance of A. palmata would have a considerable impact on reef ecosystems, as no other species can replace its role as a source of structural complexity and habitat for such a wide diversity of organisms. Therefore, the absence of A. palmata would significantly reduce biodiversity and affect the functionality of reefs over the long term
45e450eb: Reef-associated macroinvertebrates play key roles in habitat structuring and benthic competition, yet many groups remain ecologically understudied. Zooxanthellate zoantharians, such as Zoanthus and Palythoa spp., despite being conspicuous and often dominant on Indo-Pacific reefs, lack quantitative assessments of the microhabitats that shape their distributions. We examined the fine-scale abiotic and biotic niches of Zoanthus sansibaricus, Palythoa tuberculosa and P. heliodiscus across three shallow reef sites in Okinawa, Japan, using colony-centred surveys conducted during SCUBA dives. Multivariate analyses revealed strong abiotic niche partitioning. The three species were separated along clear habitat gradients: Z. sansibaricus occupied shallow, low-rugosity, crevice-rich microhabitats, whereas P. tuberculosa and P. heliodiscus occurred in deeper, more rugose environments. Species also differed in niche breadth, with P. tuberculosa spanning the widest range of abiotic conditions. Microtopographic features, such as slope and crevice presence, further distinguished species, including the marked association of P. heliodiscus with vertical, high-relief walls. Benthic assemblages adjacent to colonies also varied among species. Z. sansibaricus was most frequently bordered by turf algae, P. tuberculosa by scleractinians, cyanobacterial mats and macroalgae, and P. heliodiscus by crustose coralline algae and sponges. An integrated abiotic–biotic PCoA reinforced these distinctions and confirmed significant differences in niche position and breadth. Together, these results reveal strong, fine-scale niche partitioning among common Okinawan zoantharian species living sympatrically at the same reef. By identifying the abiotic and biotic factors that filter their distributions, this study contributes to a broader understanding of how overlooked benthic invertebrates organise space on subtropical reefs. These insights provide a foundation for incorporating macroinvertebrate habitat requirements into reef monitoring, benthic community modelling and spatial management strategies.
22b61877: Small-scale fishers are among the most important yet underrepresented actors in coral-reef governance. Their livelihoods, cultures, and nutrition are directly tied to reef health, yet they are frequently excluded from decision-making in conservation and coastal development. This presentation will argue that integrating small-scale fishers into coral-reef management is not only an issue of justice, but also a key strategy for ecological and social resilience. Small-scale fisheries employ 90% of the world’s fishers, provide 40% of all aquatic foods, and support nearly half a billion people in coastal economies. Despite these contributions, dominant conservation and blue-economy approaches often marginalise their voices, overlooking their role as stewards of the ecosystems on which they depend. Drawing on global examples and emerging evidence from fisher-led networks, we will show how participatory governance and co-management arrangements can transform coral-reef systems into engines of inclusion and sustainability. When fishers are recognised as rights-holders and decision-makers, management outcomes improve across ecological and social dimensions – enhancing reef resilience, increasing food security, and strengthening local governance. These outcomes stem from local knowledge, customary practices, and social networks that underpin sustainable resource use but remain undervalued in conventional management frameworks. We propose three steps for meaningful integration: (1) reconceptualising coral reefs as socioecological systems that sustain both biodiversity and human well-being; (2) strengthening small-scale fishers’ rights and representation at all levels of governance; and (3) building partnerships among fisher organisations, governments, and researchers to institutionalise equitable co-management. Centering small-scale fishers in coral-reef governance is essential to achieving both conservation and equity goals. Their inclusion represents not only a moral imperative but a pragmatic pathway toward thriving reefs, regenerative fisheries, and resilient coastal communities.
58a86005:
Recent widespread coral mortality from bleaching and disease in Belize and across the Caribbean has intensified the urgency to identify naturally surviving, resilient coral colonies that can serve as donor stock for restoration. Belize’s 10-year Coral Reef Resilience Plan (first draft completed in September 2025) identified ecologically resilient reef areas for future replenishment, yet its initial development involved limited community participation, creating gaps in its local relevance and implementability.

This project addressed that gap by incorporating community perspectives into the selection of priority sites for coral replenishment. Workshops, focus groups, and guided reef visits were conducted across multiple cayes and coastal communities in northern and southern Belize with tour guides, fishers, community leaders, and other key community stakeholders who depend heavily on reef ecosystems. Participants identified areas of relatively good reef health, described patterns of reef use and dependence, documented observed ecological changes, and indicated where they would prioritize replenishment activities.

Community-generated insights were used to validate and refine Belize’s 10-year Coral Reef Resilience Plan’s existing site-selection criteria and to identify additional or adjusted priority sites. These community prioritized sites were then assessed for ecological suitability using established restoration guidelines and compared with national resilience priorities. The resulting set of candidate donor colonies and socially grounded replenishment sites offers a more locally supported and ecologically robust foundation for implementation of the national Plan.

This presentation will outline the participatory process, highlight key findings, and discuss how integrating community knowledge can strengthen large-scale reef restoration planning and enhance the legitimacy and long-term success of resilience initiatives.
b4407dbc: Subsistence fisheries support the food security, cultural identity, and resilience of millions of coastal households, yet they remain largely absent from fisheries governance and ecological assessments. Fisheries governance continues to prioritize commercial and industrial sectors, overlooking the multi-scalar and multi-dimensional importance of subsistence fisheries. At the same time, ecological change, shifts in species abundance, habitat degradation, and increasing competition from tourism and coastal development have begun to erode communities’ access to key finfish species. We examine these dynamics in Belize, where subsistence finfish harvests remain deeply rooted in cultural identity and well-being. Drawing on household interviews, participatory mapping, and fisheries-dependent and independent data, we explore how access, availability, and use of subsistence species are being reshaped. We found that subsistence fisheries typically target a small portfolio of species, primarily in nearshore habitats. Fishers consistently described declining abundance, smaller sizes, and reduced catch reliability, patterns that aligned with ecological monitoring data. Households also reported rising prices, reduced fishing effort, and competition from tourism operators, which collectively narrow the space for subsistence harvests. Our findings illustrate that ecological trends and social practices are tightly coupled, revealing cross-scale feedback that shapes both vulnerability and adaptive capacity. As countries expand spatial regulations and update coastal management strategies, failing to consider subsistence fisheries may undermine food security, cultural identity, and social cohesion. Integrating subsistence fisheries into policy discussions is therefore essential for designing equitable, ecologically grounded coastal governance.
9641adbd: The United States Virgin Islands (USVI) has a rich cultural history and its people have had a front-row seat for shifts in coastal ecosystems, like coral reefs and mangrove forests. Coral reefs throughout the USVI have been severely impacted by ocean warming, mass coral bleaching events, disease, coastal development, overfishing, and other sources of point and non-point source pollution. Additionally, mangrove forests are similarly under pressure from habitat loss caused by coastal development and land use changes, pollution and hydrological alterations, intense hurricanes, and slow natural regeneration. This project uses semi-structured interviews to explore how residents of the USVI perceive, remember, and respond to changes in these coastal ecosystems. Through personal accounts, we gain insights into ecosystem histories, climate change impacts, and community resilience. From these accounts, we will examine how environmental change intersects with identity, livelihood, and stewardship in island communities where the sea is both a source of sustenance and cultural meaning. Additionally, we will evaluate how to best support community participation in nature-based solutions like coral and mangrove restoration.
de1cb718:
In the Caribbean, coral restoration is increasingly explored as a Nature-based Solution (NbS) that can support biodiversity, protect shorelines, and bolster livelihoods tied to tourism and fisheries.
NbS are actions that protect, sustainably manage, and restore natural or modified ecosystems while addressing societal challenges, simultaneously providing human well-being and ecological benefits. While this definition captures the core premise of NbS, recent scholarship encourages us to move beyond a technocratic or ecological framing. Instead, NbS should be understood as transformative, collaborative, and inclusive processes that require meaningful engagement with local communities and intentional efforts to integrate local knowledge and scientific practice. When designed and implemented through this lens, NbS have the potential not only to enhance ecosystem resilience but also to redress historical exclusions and promote more fair environmental futures. In this presentation, we describe ongoing efforts with communities in the U.S. Virgin Islands to co-develop equitable and locally appropriate coral restoration plans as NbS through the Strong Coasts project. We will present results from community-based workshops, surveys, interviews, and other engagement activities, reflecting on both our success and the challenges we have faced as an interdisciplinary team working to design and facilitate inclusive community engagement and foster equitable outcomes.
8e930fb1: Social equity emphasises the importance of inclusive participation and is becoming a major aspiration in conservation and environmental management. Its importance has grown in response to global policy calling for fairness and justice in conservation practice, including in the expansion of marine protected areas (MPAs). Locally managed MPAs are a common tool in the Philippines for fisheries management and coral reef conservation - highly diverse systems that support local communities whose livelihoods depend on them. In these interconnected socio-ecological systems, social equity is not just a conservation goal but a practical necessity. Yet, despite efforts to include stakeholders in the planning and management processes of locally managed MPAs, the extent to which these MPAs deliver socially equitable outcomes remains poorly understood. To date, social equity has mostly been assessed through the distribution of costs and benefits among stakeholders; however, recent literature identifies multiple dimensions of social equity. We use the Tañon Strait, Philippines, as a case study to assess the perception of social equity among stakeholders of locally managed MPAs. Specifically, we examine three dimensions of social equity - distribution, procedure, and recognition - using a structured questionnaire administered to key local stakeholders. As data collection is ongoing, we will use the symposium to present preliminary results. Understanding social equity in conservation tools, such as locally managed MPAs, highlights the role of inclusive management in supporting both community well-being and the long-term resilience of coral reef ecosystems.
16f016e3: The coastal and marine ecosystems of Gulf of Mannar (GOM) in Tamil Nadu on the southeast coast of India have been greatly benefited by the participation of community in the management for over two decades.  The coast has coral reefs and the small-scale fishermen living on the coast depend on the reef-associated fishery resources for their livelihood.  The fast growing population and fishing pressure resulted in over exploitation, resort to destructive fishing practices and reef degradation. With the implementation of a biodiversity conservation programme funded by United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) and Global Environment Facility (GEF) in 2002, community participation in coral reef management began in GOM. Coastal womenfolk have played a lead role with the establishment of 252 Eco Development Committees (EDCs) consisting mostly of women Self Help Group members. Each EDC has 20 members. After the end of UNDP-GEF programme in 2012, Government of Tamil Nadu has been continuing the activities with funding support. Some important components are microcredit managed by EDCs as revolving fund, training-cum-loan for pursuing alternative livelihood options, and training to youth in employment-oriented programmes. Awareness creation—a key part—helped the community to understand the need for protecting and managing the coastal areas particularly coral reefs and seagrass meadows. The low-interest credit and the additional income generated through alternative livelihood options have enhanced their socio-economic status, leading to reduced fishing pressure. Based on the success, several similar follow-up community-based programmes with the participation of coastal womenfolk have been implemented in order to protect the coastal ecosystems of GOM particularly coral reefs from human and climatic impacts, improve fisher folk income, educate them of the importance of biodiversity, make them climate resilient and involve them in restoration activities, collection of discard fishnets, removal of ghost nets, ecotourism and management.
0ee4a3f2:
The Solomon Islands is located within the Coral Triangle - the epicentre of marine biodiversity – and harbours some of the most ecologically rich coral reef ecosystems. These reefs underpin local economies, food security and cultural identity, yet climate change, overfishing, and pollution now threaten these reefs. In the remote Solomon Islands province of Temotu, geographical isolation has historically limited commercial exploitation of marine resources, creating a unique opportunity to strengthen coral reef conservation. People from Temotu have strong socio-cultural connection to their reefs, relying heavily on them for food, income, and traditional practices. However, increasing population pressure and limited livelihood alternatives are accelerating marine resource dependence leading to overfishing, destructive fishing practices, and commercial fisheries trade.
To better integrate community voices and traditional knowledge into coral reef management, the Wildlife Conservation Society conducted participatory consultations with communities in Temotu. These included community visioning, SWOT analysis for coral reef conservation, and discussions to identify locally driven opportunities for sustainable management. The consultations revealed important customary practices that can strengthen contemporary management approaches, particularly Tambu (prohibited) areas, regulated by village chiefs and local food preservation practices for Nambo (bread fruit). The traditional Tambu closures, lifted only during specific times or cultural events, closely align with the objectives of government-endorsed marine protected areas. Community members noted observable ‘spillover effects’ of the Tambu areas, reinforcing their ecological value. The cultural practice of preserving Nambo, unique to Temotu, also emerged as a potential opportunity for income generation to relieve commercial pressure on marine resources. These insights highlight the critical role of customary governance and traditional knowledge systems in shaping effective, community-led coral reef conservation. Strengthening these systems alongside scientific approaches offers a pathway to more resilient reefs and empowered communities in Temotu Province.
d280075a: Coral reefs are experiencing unprecedented global declines, yet population responses can vary substantially across habitats with differing environmental conditions. Mesophotic coral ecosystems may offer refugee for some depth-generalist species, but empirical long-term demographic assessments remain scarce. Here, we evaluated the population dynamics of the depth-generalist stony coral Paramontastraea peresi across a broad depth gradient in the Gulf of Aqaba and Eilat, northern Red Sea—a region renowned for exceptional thermal tolerance. To capture depth-dependent demographic patterns, we collected demographic data across four years using long-term photographic monitoring of permanently marked reef plots and digital coral mapping. Individual colonies were tracked to quantify growth, shrinkage, mortality, and recruitment along a 5–55 m gradient. Based on these empirical rates, we built structured demographic models to examine and predict depth-specific population trajectories. Because many corals undergo morphological changes with depth, affecting coral surface area and polyp density, which are integral to demographic estimates, we quantified colony flattening with depth using high-resolution photogrammetry. Vital-rate analyses revealed clear depth-structured life-history strategies: mesophotic colonies exhibited slower growth, lower fecundity, and produced fewer recruits than shallow conspecifics. Although overall metapopulation dynamics suggested a near-stable state (λ = 1.008), depth-specific models showed contrasting outcomes, with shallow populations exhibiting positive growth (λ = 1.032) and mesophotic populations declining (λ = 0.957). Accordingly, model predictions indicate long-term increases in shallow habitats but persistent declines at depth. Together, these results challenge assumptions about the viability of mesophotic populations of depth-generalist species and their capacity to buffer shallow-reef losses. Instead, they indicate a stronger demographic dependence of mesophotic populations on shallow ones than vice versa. This study highlights the critical need for sustained, long-term, depth-explicit demographic monitoring to resolve patterns of reef resilience under ongoing environmental change.
f839c554: Marine diseases are increasing in frequency and severity throughout the Western Atlantic, threatening the resilience of coral reefs. Flower Garden Banks National Marine Sanctuary (FGBNMS) is an isolated system of reefs in the northern Gulf of Mexico (GoM) with historically low disease incidence but dense assemblages of susceptible coral hosts. However, in the summer of 2022 a disease with unknown etiology and causing multi-species acute tissue loss was observed at FGBNMS. Here we use large-area imaging (LAI) products captured before, during, and after a marine disease outbreak in a ~550 m² study site at FGBNMS to understand disease spread and mechanisms of infection. We leverage an advanced pipeline with three software’s – Agisoft Metashape, Viscore, and TagLab – to compare 2D, co-registered mosaics across three years. Through this pipeline, we track individual colonies and lesions during the outbreak to examine tissue loss, lesion spread, survivorship, and overall benthic health. We use this dataset to move beyond centroid-based spatial statistics to characterize coral disease transmission and propose novel edge-to-edge techniques using LAI data products. These methodologies elucidate high clustering of disease at the colony and lesion level across all scales of the study plot. Additionally, we utilize this pipeline to develop an agent-based model of disease transmission dynamics within the study plot. The combined photogrammetric pipeline and agent-based model facilitate our understanding of how coral diseases transmit across the reef, which highlights disease vectors and reservoirs and will aid in the design of future mitigation.
54449e7a: Low coral colony density in reef habitats can reduce population fitness, known as the Allee effect. This occurs through diminished reproductive success owing to declines in sperm concentration and thus fertilisation success with increasing distance among neighbouring colonies. For less abundant coral species, overcoming the challenge of Allee effects is critical for their persistence. Here, we create orthomosaics using large-area reef photogrammetry to examine coral density, spatial distribution, nearest intercolonial distances, and clustering patterns in a pristine reef, Uchelbeluu Reef, in Palau. We examine coral colony density patterns using six 2000 m² plots in three wave-exposed and three wave-sheltered sites. We first determine the proportion of populations that is reproductively isolated with large intercolonial distances. Our findings highlight that many species on individual reefs may occur below their critical fertilisation densities. We then estimate the total reproductive output of these reefs by modelling fertilisation success for two coral species, Acropora hyacinthus and Acropora tenuis. These results can be used to inform restoration strategies for degraded reefs, guiding the spatial configuration of outplanted corals required to establish reproductively viable coral clusters.
5906d72a: Understanding the drivers of early coral recruitment is key to predicting reef resilience. However, coral recruitment results from multiple interacting ecological processes – including larval supply and connectivity, substrate availability and suitability, and early post-settlement survival – whose combined effects remain challenging to disentangle. To investigate these mechanisms, we combined long-term surveys of larval settlement rates on tiles with photogrammetric 3D mapping of reefs across a cross-shelf gradient in New Caledonia. Settlement tiles served as proxies for larval supply, while recent advances in underwater photogrammetry enabled the conversion of permanent video transects into high-resolution maps of reef habitats. These maps captured spatial and temporal variation in coral community composition, benthic competitors (macroalgae, turf algae, sponges, and soft corals), habitat structural complexity, and the distribution of suitable substrates for coral recruitment (e.g., crustose coralline algae and unoccupied reef slabs). By comparing patterns of larval settlement – standardized to the 3D suitable surface area – with the abundance and taxonomic composition of juvenile corals (<5 cm), we inferred early post-settlement survival. Our results show that microtopography (cavities, surface rugosity) and curvature, along with the availability of favorable substrates, explain most of the variability in early survival and must be accounted for when assessing coral community recovery potential. Our photogrammetry-from-videotransect methodology enables the investigation of key ecological mechanisms driving coral recruitment. It is now being deployed across New Caledonia through local community-driven reef monitoring programs. By complementing traditional fish and invertebrate surveys, this approach delivers the first archipelago-scale characterization of coral recruitment dynamics and lays the groundwork for transitioning from classical ecosystem health assessments toward resilience-based reef monitoring.
904b848b: Ecological niche construction occurs when organisms modify environments in ways that enhance their own or other organisms’ fitness. As reef ecosystem engineers, Scleractinian corals inherently change the reef with their skeletons, modifying local environmental conditions both directly, defining habitat 3D structure, and indirectly, affecting environmental conditions such as flow and light regimes with their shapes. Here, we examine Scleractinian corals as ecological niche constructors by testing how coral presence and coral-built structures influence the coral community at early life stages. To estimate complexity in local habitats and reefs, we used structure-from-motion algorithms in both manipulative and observational studies. In a field experiment, artificial tiles placed along a gradient of local coral-built structural complexity showed a 15.7% increase in coral settler presence across the complexity range considered, indicating that greater structural complexity facilitates larval settlement. In an observational study encompassing 9 reef sites (~120 m2 each) where adult and juvenile corals were mapped in the reef through time, juvenile coral occurrence was more strongly influenced by adult coral abundance than by reef structural complexity. Together, these findings suggest that while reef structures may promote settlement, they do not necessarily ensure survival into later stages, as juvenile occurrence in the field correlates more closely with adult abundance. Nonetheless, corals meet a key criterion of ecological niche construction, as their presence creates conditions that facilitate recruitment, directly and indirectly interacting with other biotic factors that influence local coral populations persistence. These findings show how combining digital imagery with niche construction theory could improve our understanding of coral community dynamics.
46784c16: Understanding variability in the growth and survivorship of reef building corals across space, and among species, is critical to creating reliable coral reef restoration goals and establishing realistic timelines to meet those goals. Corals are known for their flexible growth patterns, including the capacity to respond to stress via partial mortality, and subsequent regrowth. However, the degree to which different coral taxa rely on these available demographic pathways is less well understood. From 2023-2025, baseline coral demographic data was collected at seven reefs spanning the Florida Keys, as part of the Mission: Iconic Reefs coral restoration project. We tracked over 10,000 individual colonies from twelve focal coral taxa, and calculated change in area to describe mortality patterns and construct population growth models for each taxon. There was support for radial growth models for all taxa, though the specific form of radial growth (e.g., constant, linear, or non-linear) varied among species. While we did not find evidence for site level differences in radial extension rates, there were differences in size specific mortality rates among species and locations. These results provide strong support that variation in coral populations, and communities as a whole, is largely driven by patterns of recruitment and mortality, rather than differences in growth rate. Additionally, results present clear evidence of predictable and geometrically consistent patterns of radial growth. The approach used in this study provides a strong theoretical and empirical framework for understanding and predicting coral growth through time that can be used as a practical tool to guide restoration planning.
1389a15c: Globally, ecosystems are being reconfigured by a range of intensifying human-induced stressors. Coral reefs lie at the forefront of this environmental transformation, bringing the need to understand trajectories of reef change and associated links to ecosystem functioning to the fore. While it is widely accepted that one of the key trajectories of reef decline is the replacement of hard corals by algae, emerging evidence has revealed that in the majority of cases this is not manifesting as coral-macroalgae transitions, but rather coral-turf transitions. Yet, turfs remain widely overlooked and poorly quantified on reefs, despite now being the single most abundant benthic cover, on average, on coral reefs globally. Here I will synthesise my recent research to explore how turfs on coral reefs are inherently linked to coral reef decline, the functioning of coral reef ecosystems, and the capacity of coral reefs to recover from disturbance. In doing so, I will reveal the marked variability in turf composition at the scale of millimeters, how this variability is linked to other reef organisms (juvenile corals and herbivorous fishes), and why this variability matters for understanding reef functioning and reef recovery potential. Moreover, I will draw on a recently developed metric, the Epilithic Algal Ratio, to explore the environmental drivers related to variation in turf composition from local to global scales. Ultimately, I will highlight the need for broader quantification of variability in turfs if we are to comprehensively understand temporal changes on coral reefs. This need arises as turfs represent a critical ecological interface through which the effects of human-induced stressors may reverberate up through coral reefs, compromising reef resilience and functioning.
b9b928f2: Many coral reefs have experienced phase shifts, changing from coral-dominated to macroalgal dominated states, driven either by top-down (e.g., declines in reef herbivores), or bottom-up (e.g., increased nutrients) forces.  These alternate states have proved to be persistent, with long-term declines in ecosystem function.  At the request of local communities, we documented a coral-to-coral phase shift, with an undescribed species of Montipora coral overtaking some reefs of Ulithi Atoll.  This shift is of concern to the indigenous communities of the atoll due to its impact upon their subsistence fisheries.  Community-led marine management has since revived traditional practices and incorporated new scientific knowledge.  In 2015, Typhoon Maysak hit Ulithi Atoll directly, causing severe damage above and below water, scouring the Montipora fields.  Three years later recruits of Acropora coral were found at two managed reefs in the northern atoll and by 2023 a full phase shift reversal with return of both coral and fish diversity was documented. The resilience of these reefs provides both hope and insight into the socio-ecological factors that can support recovery of reef ecosystems.
282b1a7e:
Zoantharian dominance, once considered rare or isolated, is increasingly recognized as a recurrent indicator of ecological imbalance in coral reefs and subtropical hard-bottom ecosystems. A new zoantharian–dominated area was detected in August 2025 on O‘ahu (Hawai'i, USA), adding to at least 19 global locations where zoantharian outbreaks have been reported. Most outbreaks are linked to human-driven stressors, as zoantharians show high tolerance to ocean warming and declining water quality conditions compared to Scleractinia corals.
Against this global background, the Canary Islands stand out as an exceptional subtropical hotspot, with 14 zoantharian-dominated areas and clear evidence of impacts on native macroalgal ecosystems. Our research shows that Palythoa caribaeorum–dominated patches exceeding 1000 m² alter mesoinvertebrate and fish assemblages, revealing broad ecological consequences. Experimental and field observations indicate that P. caribaeorum does not outcompete macroalgae in direct contact, instead, it expands when intensive grazing by the sea urchin Diadema africanum creates bare substrate. Long-term ocean warming further promotes its spread. Under laboratory conditions, P. caribaeorum performs significantly better at 28 °C, warmer than regional seawater, by adjusting the composition of its symbiotic Cladocopium proliferum community. In situ, the combination of elevated sea water temperatures and the formation of extensive barren grounds seems to have favored zoantharian colonization and benthic dominance that now show high persistence. Together, these results identify ocean warming and intense herbivorous grazing, indirectly caused by overfishing, as the primary drivers of P. caribaeorum outbreaks. 
In 2015, the first documented phase shift from coral- to zoantharian-dominated habitat was recorded. Our findings highlight that similar phase-shifts can also occur in subtropical macroalgae-dominated ecosystems. Because zoantharians are easily identifiable indicators of environmental stress, documenting new colonies can reveal early signs of ecosystem stress. Such monitoring can help guide timely management actions to prevent long-term decline of native ecosystems.
5eb2cde0: Coral reefs in the Caribbean have undergone profound ecological change over the last several decades, with Florida’s Coral Reef experiencing one of the steepest regional declines in coral cover (>75%). As structural complexity and carbonate production diminish, benthic communities have reorganized around alternative states, including long sediment-laden algal turfs (LSAT), dense, sediment-rich turf systems that may inhibit coral recovery but remain poorly quantified. Here, we integrate seasonal surveys, habitat complexity metrics, and experimental manipulations to resolve the spatiotemporal dynamics of LSAT and their ecological consequences across six sites spanning the Florida Reef Tract. LSAT occupied approximately 40% of benthic substrates and exhibited substantial seasonal variation, with both percent cover and sediment depth peaking in Summer and Winter. Habitat attributes strongly modulated LSAT development: flatter, low-rugosity surfaces accumulated deeper sediments and supported thicker LSAT assemblages, while more complex microhabitats tended to resist LSAT formation. Juvenile coral presence declined sharply with increasing LSAT sediment depth, and logistic regression models revealed the strongest negative relationship during Summer, coinciding with peak sediment loads. Sediment-removal experiments further demonstrated rapid re-establishment of LSAT within days, indicating a highly resilient and self-reinforcing benthic state. Collectively, our findings identify LSAT as a persistent, sediment-driven turf assemblage that reduces settlement-quality habitat for corals and may constrain reef-scale recovery trajectories. Quantifying LSAT’s drivers and seasonal behavior provides critical insight into how sediment dynamics, topographic complexity, and turf assemblages interact to shape future reef resilience and highlights emerging management opportunities focused on mitigating sediment accumulation and restoring functional substrate conditions for coral recruitment.
01ec08b6: Top-down (e.g. consumer pressure) and bottom-up (e.g. nutrients) pressures are strong determinants of succession after disturbances. Changes to these pressures may result in shifts in the abundance or composition of primary producers. Macroalgae and corals compete directly for space, and each can suppress the other when abundant. Given this, priority effects might have a strong influence in some environments. Understanding the biotic and abiotic conditions that facilitate the establishment and proliferation of macroalgae can help determine when priority effects play a greater role than top-down or bottom-up processes in shaping benthic community composition. Here, we used an exclosure experiment on the north shore of Moorea, French Polynesia to quantify how occupancy of macroalgae on coral reefs varies with consumer pressure and nutrient enrichment among algal taxa. We tracked benthic cover over 4 years to investigate: 1) how consumer pressure and nutrient enrichment influence the establishment, abundance, and temporal occupancy of three macroalgal taxa, and 2) how the order of arrival of species affects the temporal occupancy of macroalgae. We found that top-down and bottom-up forcing had different effects on the establishment, abundance, and temporal occupancy of three macroalgal taxa, and found some evidence indicating that priority effects impacted these associations. While Lobophora spp. generally established early regardless of consumer pressure, Halimeda spp. and Turbinaria ornata established later under higher consumer pressure. The abundance of all three taxa was lower under increased consumer pressure. In the absence of consumers, we found that the temporal occupancy of Lobophora was dependent on the order of arrival of taxa, with temporal occupancy decreasing when coral anteceded Lobophora. However, priority effects did not impact the temporal occupancy of Halimeda and Turbinaria. Our findings suggest that taxon-specific responses of macroalgae to changes in top-down and bottom-up drivers impact benthic successional dynamics following disturbance.
58a1cfa4:
The community composition of organisms on coral reefs is primarily determined by direct competition for space. Rising sea surface temperatures from global warming and Anthropogenic disturbances cause shift in dominance and give competitive advantage to organisms such as macroalgae, soft coral, ascidians, cyanobacteria, corallimorphians, and bioeroding sponges, disrupting the balance between constructive and destructive processes essential for reef maintenance. Bioeroding sponges associated with coral reefs often compete with corals by encrusting them, which can ultimately lead to coral mortality. This study aims to understand key characteristics—such as skeletal density and porosity of corals—as indicators of the impact of bioerosion on the changing dynamics of the coral reef ecosystem.
To assess the effect of bioerosion on coral skeletal integrity, Micro-computed Tomography (Micro-CT), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Light Microscopy were used to evaluate the impact of the bioeroding sponges Cliona celata and Cliona thomasii on the skeletal density and percentage of erosion in two coral genera, Turbinaria sp. and Plesiastrea sp. Species-specific differences were observed in the structural response of coral skeletons to infestation by sponges. Micro-CT analysis revealed that erosion by bioeroding sponges significantly alters the coral endoskeleton, resulting in reduced bone mineral density (BMD), increased porosity and a deformed skeletal framework. Cliona celata caused a 1.55% decrease in BMD of Plesiastrea sp. and a substantial 12.52% decrease in Turbinaria sp. The impact of Cliona thomasii was similar across species, causing a 2.67% decrease in Plesiastrea sp. and 2.89% in Turbinaria sp.
SEM and Light Microscopy images showed progressive breakdown of coral septa, leading to hollow corallites. The cavities created by infestation were filled with sponge spicules, and increased pore formation in the coenosteum made the skeleton fragile and brittle. These findings provide important insights into the weakening of coral structural integrity, increasing their vulnerability to additional environmental stressors.
35aad1cf: The balance between reproduction and mortality is a fundamental process that drives coral population dynamics. Despite being one of the most vital processes for persistence of a population, sexual reproduction is secondary to vital intercellular biological resources allocation. Thermal stress, disease, eutrophication, colony size, and age can all impact a coral’s reproductive output. Recently, increased stressors have shifted the equation to reduced fecundity and increased mortality at both the local and global scales. Investigating these dynamics on reefs that extend across latitudinal gradients of stress conditions provides an opportunity to explore the effects of stressors within a single population. Florida’s Coral Reef extends across multiple environmental and anthropogenic stress gradients from the tropics to a temperate climate with differential human-derived excess nutrient inputs. In the summer of 2021, 87 genetically similar large Orbicella faveolata colonies were sampled across Florida. A regression tree model of polyp fecundity testing against a variety of environmental (temperature, wind), disease (frequency, number of treatments), and colony specific factors (live tissue, lineage, symbionts) showed that 46.1% of the variation in fecundity was explained by region, mean temperature, and mean wind. Dry season high winds in the north (an indicator of turbidity) and high 2020 summer temperatures in the south explained the most variation. These results indicate that temperature differences across reef systems are a large driver effecting reproduction in coral populations. Additionally, increased turbidity and/or sedimentation may decrease fecundity in stressed populations near highly urbanized areas. Disease occurrence and the number of antibiotic treatments were not factors providing further evidence that disease treatments do not impede sexual reproductive success and impacts from SCTLD appear to be localized. Analyses exploring the impact of modeled nutrient loads for each colony on fecundity are underway and will be presented.
474ddb22: Rising sea temperatures have accelerated the poleward expansion of tropical species, driving the ongoing 'tropicalization' of temperate marine ecosystems. Nowhere is this process more pronounced than in the Mediterranean Sea, where Lessepsian migration (i.e., the movement of Red Sea species through the Suez Canal) has already introduced hundreds of non-native tropical species. In 2022, a new Lessepsian migrant was documented in the Mediterranean: the Indo-Pacific soft coral Dendronephthya hemprichi, first observed on gas rigs off the Israeli Mediterranean coast. Soft corals are known for their invasive capabilities and, accordingly, D. hemprichi has rapidly proliferated in the Eastern Mediterranean, forming an exceptionally dense marine animal forest within a single year. The long-term establishment of any non-native species highly depends on its capacity to survive and reproduce in the novel environment. Here, we provide the first comprehensive comparison of the reproductive biology of D. hemprichi between its native Red Sea ecosystem and its new Mediterranean range and evaluate changes relative to historical reproductive data collected from the Red Sea three decades ago. Through monthly sampling, dissections, and histological analyses, we characterized sex ratio, gametogenesis, reproductive seasonality and fecundity across regions. Histological examination confirms the presence of both male and female colonies in the Mediterranean. We found that while the Red Sea population retains its already known presence of year-round mature gametes, the Mediterranean population exhibits a markedly shorter reproductive window, with a clear seasonal peak in the summer (August). Our findings demonstrate that D. hemprichi has established a sexually reproducing population in the Mediterranean and is likely to continue expanding its range which undoubtedly indicated tropicalization of the East Mediterranean. These results will be discussed in the context of environmental differences between the Mediterranean and Red Sea that may drive shifts in coral reproductive biology.
fb3f2afa: Sexual reproduction underpins species persistence, genetic diversity, and the ability of populations to adapt to changing environments. Yet reproduction demands substantial energetic investment. Sex-allocation theory examines how organisms optimally distribute resources between reproduction and other life-functions, and between male and female gamete production. While the theory is well established for gonochoric species, sex-allocation in simultaneous hermaphrodites remains poorly understood. The limited evidence available suggests that hermaphrodites may flexibly adjust their investment in male versus female functions in response to environmental conditions, potentially optimizing reproductive success under different constraints. Most reef-building corals are simultaneous hermaphrodites that broadcast-spawn both sperm and eggs into the water column. Despite their ecological importance, the mechanisms governing sex-allocation in corals alongside the environmental drivers that shape it remain largely unexplored. Here, we quantified sex-allocation across multiple acroporid species exposed to contrasting environmental regimes. Colonies of three Acropora species and one Montipora species were sampled across depths, years, and sites with differing anthropogenic impacts. Polyps were dissected to isolate spermaries and oocytes, and dry gamete biomass was measured at microgram resolution using an ultramicrobalance. Our results demonstrate that across species, environmental context strongly influences reproductive investment. While sperm-to-oocyte mass ratios were generally conservative, total reproductive biomass declined markedly in deep reefs (40–60 m) compared to shallow reefs, with frequent occurrences of strictly male or infertile polyps at depth. Similar shifts of reduced reproductive mass and increased male-biased or infertile polyps were observed in disturbed versus protected sites and before and after heat stress. These findings reveal that coral sex-allocation is environmentally sensitive, structurally flexible, and more dynamic than usually recognized, all of which highlight reproductive allocation as a critical, overlooked axis of coral resilience in a rapidly changing ocean.
d3d95a18: Caribbean staghorn coral populations have declined over 90% since the 1970s, yet the evolutionary consequences of their dual reproductive strategy remain poorly understood. While phenotypic plasticity can enhance survival in Acropora cervicornis, its influence on the critical balance between sexual and asexual reproduction has not been examined. We tracked 263 colonies representing 10 genotypes across 9 Florida Keys reef sites over 42 months, investigating relationships between fragmentation, morphological plasticity, and capacity for sexual reproduction. Breakage patterns reflected both environmental and genetic factors, with significant genotypic variation in susceptibility demonstrating that fragmentation is a heritable trait subject to selection. Primary branch breaks created a "double negative" effect—simultaneously increasing mortality risk (HR=2.58, p=0.022) and delaying sexual maturity (HR=0.570, p=0.035). Of 177 colonies analyzed for reproductive development, 43% reached the ≥25cm diameter maturity threshold during observation. Conversely, higher morphological plasticity in surface area to volume ratio accelerated sexual maturation (HR=6.556, p=0.002), counteracting the negative effects of fragmentation. Among corals experiencing primary breaks, high-plasticity individuals (ramets) were significantly more likely to reach sexual maturity (44%) than low-plasticity individuals (27.5%, p=0.040), demonstrating that plasticity enhances post-breakage recovery rather than preventing damage. These results reveal a fundamental reproductive trade-off in A. cervicornis, where asexual reproduction through fragmentation undermines sexual reproductive potential by reducing colony size below maturation thresholds. Our study confirms that fragmentation susceptibility is heritable and identifies how plasticity contributes to fitness beyond immediate survival. These insights have direct restoration applications—selecting genotypes with lower breakage risk and higher plasticity could significantly improve population recovery in this threatened foundation species.
4b6c8f14:
Coral–bioeroder interactions have traditionally been framed as parasitic, yet growing evidence suggests more nuanced, context-dependent outcomes. We integrate two complementary datasets on the association between the coral Stylophora pistillata and its boring bivalve Leiosolenus lessepsianus in the Gulf of Eilat/Aqaba (Red Sea), combining long-term reproductive phenology with contemporary experimental measurements of coral physiology. Historical and recent reproductive data reveal pronounced shifts in the timing of gametogenesis and larval development in both partners over five decades, yet their relative reproductive synchrony has been conserved, indicating strong life-history coordination. The coral now exhibits an extended planulation period, while the bivalve’s reproductive cycle has shifted later in parallel, maintaining larval settlement during the host’s reproductive window.
To examine the physiological dimension of this coordination, we quantified seasonal coral calcification, oxygen, DIC, and total alkalinity fluxes in bored and non-bored colonies. Calcification in S. pistillata declined during winter, coinciding with peak bivalve reproductive activity. This temporal overlap may minimize skeletal overgrowth on newly settled bivalve larvae while reducing metabolic conflict between host growth and symbiont recruitment. Despite substantial internal bioerosion, bored colonies maintained net seasonal calcification rates comparable to non-bored colonies.
Together, these findings support a model of facultative mutualism mediated by seasonal trade-offs, in which reproductive synchrony and physiological compensation promote long-term stability. This work reframes coral–bioeroder dynamics along a mutualism–parasitism continuum and highlights adaptive synchrony as a potential mechanism of reef resilience under environmental change.
9cef74cc:
Coral reefs are critical biodiversity havens facing many threats such as higher temperatures, disease, overfishing, and pollution. Understanding the genomic basis of coral reproductive behavior is now to guide restoration strategies that rely on sexual reproduction. The genus Orbicella is an ideal system to study the molecular mechanisms behind sexual reproduction given decades of spawning behavioral observations in the field. The genus is comprised of three species (i.e., O. faveolata, O. franski and O. annularis) widespread throughout the Caribbean, each with unique spawning schedules, making Orbicella an unparalleled comparative system.
To investigate the genetic mechanisms that trigger synchronous, mass-broadcast spawning events in this species complex, we collected RNA-Seq data for O. faveolata during the week leading up to spawning, the night of spawning, and in the days post-spawning. We found signatures of final stage spermatogenesis occurring a week before gamete release in O. faveolata. Additionally, genes that mask circadian rhythms and blue light detection were upregulated during gamete release, indicating that spawning triggers rely on disruption of typical lunar light exposure. 
We also analyzed data from multiple timepoints during the night of gamete release for O. annularis and O. franksi to assess variation in gene expression between the other two sister species. Genes relating to muscle contraction, tissue repair, and immune response are upregulated in the hours after gamete release, however these genes were more prominently expressed in O. franksi, suggesting that reproductive events may have a more substantial impact on the health of this species. Our study indicates that reproductive timing is tied to external factors, but the molecular responses to these cues are species-specific. This highlights the need for coral restoration and conservation strategies that account for these unique, idiosyncratic responses to ensure long-term success.
30694c95: Research on Mesophotic Coral Ecosystems (MCEs; 30-150 m) has grown exponentially in recent decades, highlighting their rich diversity and broad geographic extent. Despite this, MCEs remain insufficiently protected and under-represented in marine spatial planning and conservation initiatives. A central challenge to this is the continued lack of long-term ecological data, which limits the ability to inform effective management. This study provides new insights into patterns of benthic diversity in MCE communities across the Chagos Archipelago, central Indian Ocean. Furthermore, we document the impact of climate change, specifically subsurface heatwaves, on these unique ecosystems. Using imagery from Remotely Operated Vehicle surveys collected between 2019 and 2025, we quantified invertebrate megafauna assemblages along a continuous depth gradient from 15 to 160 m, spanning the shallow-water to lower mesophotic zones. The diversity of the benthic communities decreased with increasing depth; however, even the deepest mesophotic habitats in the Archipelago supported higher species richness levels exceeding those reported from several shallow subtropical regions. Community composition also changed markedly along the depth gradient, with high dissimilarity driven primarily by species turnover, underscoring the uniqueness of mesophotic assemblages. Evidence of coral bleaching was observed at multiple sites across multiple years, highlighting the vulnerability of these deeper coral reefs to thermal stress. Overall, this study provides a comprehensive account of MCE benthic diversity in the Chagos Archipelago. Our findings demonstrate that these ecosystems harbour both high species richness and unique community composition, yet remain vulnerable to climate change, emphasising the importance of incorporating MCEs into conservation planning to safeguard the overall biodiversity of coral reef ecosystems.
da053e61: The rising threat of marine heatwaves has led to numerous predictions that shallow coral reefs will be severely degraded by the end of this century, while it remains unclear how deeper reefs will respond to such extreme conditions. Yet, more stable environmental conditions in Mesophotic Coral Ecosystems (MCEs) may regionally moderate coral bleaching by reducing thermal stress across depth during marine heatwaves. Evaluating whether MCEs can serve as full or partial refuges from thermal disturbances requires integrating thermal severity and inter-site variability responses of coral communities. We monitored the bleaching incidence and live coral cover before, during, and after a mass bleaching event (2024–25) in Reunion Island in the southwestern Indian Ocean. Surveys were conducted around the island at three sites with contrasting environmental and geomorphological conditions: across a depth gradient from 10m to 65m off a harbour, on fringing coral reefs and on volcanic lava-flows. Live coral cover and bleaching were assessed through photoquadrats and thermal stress was quantified using both satellite-derived degree heating weeks and in situ temperature data. Shallow corals suffered ~ 80% bleaching under severe thermal stress of ~ 20.0 °C-weeks. We found a marked decline in bleaching severity with depth, indicating that MCEs experienced reduced thermal stresses relative to shallow sites. However, bleaching was still persistent in MCEs, particularly where sediment-laden runoff associated with cyclone Garance reached deeper habitats. Inter-site comparisons revealed distinct disturbance signatures: port sites showed elevated sedimentation driven mortality, reefs exhibited the strongest response to thermal anomalies, while lava-flow sites displayed intermediate but spatially complex bleaching patterns. Our study shows that the impacts of marine heatwaves on corals are partially buffered across depth with high spatial heterogeneity. Identifying depth zones with higher refuge potential can help refine conservation priorities, improve resilience-based management for coral ecosystems increasingly exposed to compound climate-driven disturbances.
51b881fd: Coral framework ecosystems at high latitudes and mesophotic depths are potential climate refugia for shallow coral reefs, yet their long-term stability and resilience under intensifying global change remain unclear. Here we contrast the ecological histories of two such potential refugia: the high-latitude shallow reefs of Moreton Bay (Queensland, Australia) monitored seasonally over 3 years (2021-2024), where live coral cover declined by ~30%, and the mesophotic coral ecosystem of Eilat (Red Sea, Israel) monitored annually over decades, where upper-mesophotic coral cover declined by ~50% since 2010. Both systems exhibit ecological shifts from historically complex assemblages dominated by coral taxa to present-day states increasingly dominated by turf algae and macroalgae, while massive, stress-tolerant coral species persist in low abundance. These transitions are linked to the interaction of acute extreme events (e.g., marine heatwaves, cold spells, floods, turbidity pulses, internal wave-induced temperature anomalies) with rising background environmental variability. Although both systems are considered refugia, neither has remained static. Rather, the relative predictability of environmental fluctuations appears to underpin continued coral persistence. These findings challenge the notion that climatic refugia are defined solely by environmental stability, suggesting instead that seasonal or periodic variabilities in long-term environmental conditions can buffer coral communities against climate stress. In contrast, acute extreme events drive assemblage volatility and accelerate phase shifts from coral to algal dominance. This synthesis underscores that environmental predictability, rather than stability, better explains ecological persistence in marginal and extreme reef environments. It further implies that conservation prioritisation under climate change should focus on reef habitats characterised by predictable environmental regimes and manageable extremes, as these are better positioned than unpredictable systems to sustain coral assemblages in an era of increasing climatic uncertainty.
cfe64d40: Coinciding with the strongest El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) events, 2023 and later 2024 marked the warmest years on record at the time. The associated large-scale atmospheric and oceanic anomalies produced severe and extreme heat events across multiple regions globally. Simultaneously, sustained marine heatwaves contributed to the declaration of the fourth Global Coral Mass Bleaching Event in April 2024, with widespread shallow-water coral bleaching and subsequent mortality reported across all major ocean basins. While the impacts of heatwaves on shallow-water coral reefs are well-documented, their variation with depth and their effects on light-dependent Mesophotic Coral Ecosystems (MCEs) remain comparatively understudied. MCEs were long considered relatively protected from surface warming. Yet, emerging evidence indicates that oceanographic processes can induce severe coral bleaching at depth, underscoring the need to reassess the vulnerability and resilience of these deeper ecosystems and to test how bleaching evolves with depth. In this study, we compiled the most comprehensive dataset to date describing global patterns of coral bleaching across the depth gradient, during the 2023-2024 ENSO event. A total of 93 coral reefs in 15 territories were surveyed between 2023 and 2024, spanning depths from 5 to 160 m. Bleaching prevalence with depth varied markedly among regions, with the Caribbean and Great Barrier Reef exhibiting significantly less bleaching at mesophotic depths. Interestingly, locations with higher baseline bleaching at shallow depths exhibited a lower decrease in bleaching prevalence at mesophotic depths. Nevertheless, most of the surveyed locations displayed an uncertain depth effect on bleaching, and none showed an increasing bleaching with depth. These findings indicate that the deep-reef refug- hypothesis, at least regarding bleaching with depth, is highly context-dependent and underscore the need of assessing the vulnerability of MCEs to climate change to ensure the persistence of these ecosystems.
a483cfc5: Mesophotic coral ecosystems have garnered considerable interest following the Deep Reef Refugia Hypothesis (DRRH), which suggests that they could serve as a refuge for some shallow corals in a warming ocean. However, punctual evidence of coral bleaching at mesophotic depths has been observed worldwide, raising questions about the validity of the DRRH. Yet, no long-term information is available covering several bleaching events and revealing the long-term changes in coral communities at depth. Since 2016, three massive bleaching events have occurred in Ishigaki Island, in Okinawa. Comparing data on coral cover and community composition since 2014, we could evaluate the impact of coral bleaching across depths in Nagura Bay, Ishigaki Island. Our findings indicate that, overall, corals below 30 m were not significantly affected by the massive bleaching event, with an almost complete absence of bleached corals during the event and an increase in cover in most coral genera and total coral cover. The strongest impact on corals in Nagura Bay at shallower depth was observed after the 2016 event. However, observations in nearby areas outside Nagura Bay during the 2024 massive bleaching event illustrate the need for a better understanding of the mechanisms underlying mesophotic bleaching, as well as the protection of deep corals against bleaching and the risk of generalisations. Here, we will present these results and discuss how coral communities reacted to multiple bleaching events over a decade.
92faa1dd:
Light availability regulates coral–Symbiodiniaceae interactions, and when combined with increased temperature, intensifies the severity of coral bleaching. Mesophotic corals, living below 30m, offer unique insights into understanding how light-mediated shifts in symbiosis and energy storage translate into differences in bleaching susceptibility. 

To examine how depth-driven light environments influence photoacclimation and nutrient availability, Montipora grisea colonies were sampled from shallow (5m), mid (25m), and deep (40m) environments in The National Marine Sanctuary of American Samoa, Fagatele Bay. We combined rapid light curves, stable isotope analysis, and ITS2 sequencing to quantify depth-related changes in primary productivity, nutrient exchange, energy storage, and Symbiodiniaceae community. 

To explore how these physiological traits connect to thermal response, shallow and deep colonies were exposed to a rapid heat stress assay using the Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System (CBASS) under high-light, mesophotic-light, and no-light conditions. Fv/Fm values taken during the CBASS assay were used to assess the Effective Dose 50 (ED50, temperature where Fv/Fm decreases 50%) and to assess how light and temperature influence thermal response in corals from different light environments.  

ITS2 analysis revealed a mixed community of Durusdinium and Cladocopium in deep corals, which exhibited higher ED50 under ambient light (37.32°C) than shallow Cladocopium-dominated corals (36.33°C). Reduced light increased thermal tolerance, with significantly higher ED50s in both populations under mesophotic light and increased Fv/Fm measured under no-light conditions. Stable isotope analysis indicated lower C:N ratios in deep corals, and rapid light curves showed distinct photophysiological traits across depths. 

Together, these data give further insight into how fluctuations in the symbiotic relationship influence overall holobiont thermal tolerance.
