Understanding the biological mechanisms of thermal tolerance in corals from mutliple depths is a critical research priority and can help guide strategies for integrating mesophotic coral reefs into conservation planning.
9f5d6fbe: The 2010 Deepwater Horizon oil spill impacted marine benthic communities across a wide geography centered on the Mississippi-Alabama continental shelf and slope to a depth of 1800 m. Habitat-forming octocorals were heavily affected, prompting restoration efforts, starting with active fragmentation of corals, and passive recruitment of corals. The work used ROVs to demonstrate ex-situ fragmentation of three dominant species occurring deeper than 50 m on the Mississippi-Alabama shelf: Swiftia exserta, Thesea nivea, and Muricea pendula. Corals were collected by ROV, recovered to the surface, fragmented, and subsequently glued on to cement-based racks that were returned to sea. Of 190 fragments monitored for 500 days, survivorship reached 57%, with strong variation by species, size class, and substrate. Large fragments (≥8 cm) placed on hard substrate had 82% survival. S. exserta performed best, with 89% survival and mean growth of 2.1 cm year⁻¹ (±0.5 SEM). Mortality during the first six months was low, indicating high resilience to fragmentation. Significant losses due to burial or overgrowth emerged later, when corals were surveyed at day 355. Simultaneously, we performed recruitment studies at a 52-m mesophotic site (DSR7) and deployed 16 arrays, each containing twelve 20 cm square limestone and concrete settlement tiles for 724 days. Across 131 tiles, 100 coral recruits settled (92 scleractinians, 8 octocorals), yielding a rate of 4.6 corals m²/year. Highest recruitment (13.0 corals m²/year) occurred on upper surfaces of horizontal limestone tiles over hard substrate. Recruitment responded significantly to substrate beneath tiles, tile material, and orientation, with no significant interactions. Despite the predominance of octocorals at the site and near-absence of scleractinians, octocoral recruitment was unexpectedly lower than that of scleractinians. Our findings  suggest that restoration of nearby deeper Deepwater Horizon-impacted sites will require active propagation techniques to supplement the slow and episodic recruitment in natural and artificial substrates.
d8aa7163: Coral recruitment is an essential demographic mechanism that underpins the community structure and dynamics of coral reefs and facilitates post-disturbance coral recovery. However, information on coral recruitment patterns in mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs) is globally scarce. We present a spatially-explicit and multi-temporal study of coral recruitment at shallow (15 m), upper (45 m) and lower (75 m) mesophotic depths across three contrasted locations (nine stations) around Reunion Island in the southwestern Indian Ocean. Terracotta tiles were deployed at each station to monitor coral recruitment across one year (October 2021–October 2022) and four consecutive austral summer periods (October 2022–March 2023, October 2023–March 2024, October 2024–March 2025 and October 2025–March 2026). This study, the first to assess coral recruitment temporal patterns in MCEs, highlighted a marked decline in recruitment rate in MCEs consistently across locations and seasons. Although site-level interannual variability in coral recruitment was high, the overall geographic pattern of recruit distribution remained consistent over time. Recruit composition also varied significantly with depth, with Pocilloporidae dominating shallow habitats, while lower MCEs exclusively harbored “other” coral family recruits (i.e., other than Acroporidae, Pocilloporidae and Poritidae). Upper MCEs could constitute substantial refuges for Pocilloporidae corals, while lower MCEs are of paramount importance for “other” coral families (e.g. Agariciidae). Interannual surveys are essential for improving our understanding of the spatiotemporal variability of coral recruitment in both shallow habitats and MCEs, as well as the factors influencing early post-settlement processes. Persistent vertical patterns in coral recruitment enhance our insight into the mechanisms and conditions that underpin coral reef resilience and highlight the need to safeguard areas with high recruitment while investigating the drivers of low recruitment in contrasting habitats. This approach will strengthen the conservation of coral reefs at a time when they face climate driven changes and coastal pollution.
194c1aaf:
Global climate change is driving severe functional declines in coral reef ecosystems, threatening food security, coastal protection, and livelihoods for millions of people. Conservation efforts are predominantly reactive – often focused on restoring degraded areas after extreme events – yet such strategies are unlikely to preserve reef ecosystems at scales meaningful for ecological function and its corresponding ecosystem services. Long term preservation and proactive adaptation of local human communities instead require identifying where suitable environmental conditions are most likely to persist in the coming decades.

Here, we combine multiple complementary statistical approaches, using key reef-relevant variables outputted by multiple Earth System Models from the latest Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP6), to characterise the environmental niches that currently support shallow-water coral reefs. By calibrating statistical models within distinct biogeographic regions, we capture ecological differences shaped by regional evolutionary histories, circulation-driven connectivity, and long-term environmental exposure. Applying these models to CMIP6 projections, we produce spatially explicit forecasts of future reef suitability at scales relevant to local stakeholders. Additionally, ensemble predictions across multiple climate models allow us to quantify predictive uncertainty. Finally, we link projected shifts in suitability with national-scale dependency metrics – such as the proportion of GDP derived from reef services – to identify communities that are most susceptible to losses or gains in reef function.

This global assessment identifies where reef systems are most likely to persist, decline, or relocate under future climate conditions, and highlights the human communities most exposed to these changes. By providing a robust, uncertainty-aware foundation for anticipating ecological and socio-economic impacts, our framework offers an actionable basis for prioritising conservation investments, designing climate-responsive protected areas, and planning targeted interventions such as assisted migration and restoration. In doing so, it supports a transition from reactive management to strategic, forward-looking stewardship of the world’s coral reef ecosystems.
92f3c521:
Purpose
Climate-driven range shifts in coral reef ecosystems are reshaping local biodiversity and altering the ecological foundations that coastal social communities rely on. While the ecological mechanisms behind these shifts have become increasingly well understood, much less is known about how communities perceive and respond to such rapid socio-ecological change. This study investigates strategies that support social adaptation to range shifts associated with coral reef ecosystem changes, drawing on empirical insights from Japan.
Methods
This study combined a national coastal survey (N = ca. 2,000) with ecological indicators to examine social community responses to biodiversity change in coral reef ecosystems. Residents’ perceptions of marine environmental change and ecosystem-related resources were compared with sea surface temperature-based data on the presence of corals, seaweeds, and fish, in order to analyze gaps between perceived and actual ecological change.
Results
Cluster analysis identified three groups with distinct levels of engagement and adaptive attitudes. Recognition of coastal resources varied clearly across clusters: familiar resources such as fisheries products, ports, and tourism facilities were most widely recognized. The low-engagement group showed consistently low awareness, the conservation-oriented group the highest, and the adaptation-oriented group remained intermediate. Overall awareness of changes in coral and seaweed habitats was low, with no significant differences among clusters for coral-related recognition. Substantial municipal-level variation in subjective and objective understanding of coral reef ecosystem shifts suggested uneven access to ecological information. Differences were also observed across levels of ocean interaction, marine resource use, and demographic attributes.
Conclusion 
These findings highlight the uneven recognition of ecosystem shifts among the public and underscore the importance of strengthening locally grounded communication and community-based adaptation efforts.
a19e6eab: Three reef fish species – Taʻape (Lutjanus kasmira), Toʻau (Lutjanus fulvus), and Roi (Cephalopholis argus) – were introduced to Hawaiʻi in the 1950s for fisheries enhancement. Today, local fishers seldom target these “invasive” species, expressing concern about their potential negative impacts on native reef fish. Despite being introduced simultaneously, their distributions and densities differ markedly across the Main Hawaiian Islands (MHI) and Northwestern Hawaiian Islands (NWHI). Taʻape are observed in shallow waters across the MHI and NWHI and extend into mesophotic depths (45-100 m) as far north as French Frigate Shoals (Lalo). Toʻau and Roi are restricted to shallow waters (<45 m) up to Lalo, with no detections in the northernmost islands. We used species distribution models with >6000 sampling sites of NOAA monitoring data to examine how environmental and anthropogenic variables shape these patterns. Taʻape and Toʻau distributions are constrained by temperature extremes (<22°C and >27°C), with lower-limit annual sea surface temperatures influencing their northward range limits. However, the lower-limit temperature-distribution relationship for Taʻape and Toʻau varied by scale: positive across the full archipelago but negative within the MHI, likely reflecting greater temperature stability in the MHI, where island-scale windward-leeward temperature gradients outweigh archipelago-wide seasonal shifts. Ecologically, Taʻape occur in deeper, structurally complex habitats with lower coral cover, while Toʻau are found in higher-sediment habitats, potentially linked to freshwater outflows that are not prevalent in the low-lying NWHI. Spearfishing negatively affects detectability in all three species, highlighting the role of human pressure in limiting their presence. These results provide insight into the drivers of introduced reef fish distributions, highlighting the potential for climate-driven range expansions into the northernmost reefs of Papahānaumokuākea, which are dominated by endemic species. Our findings underscore the importance of targeted monitoring and management to mitigate the impacts of invasive fishes under ongoing oceanographic change.
1aa636c7: Worldwide anthropogenic pressures are restructuring ecosystems and driving the loss of habitat-forming organisms. In marine ecosystems, the occurrence of heat waves, an increase of nutrients and sedimentation has led to the loss of biogenic habitat-formers such as coral reefs and kelp forests, creating space for turf filamentous algae to proliferate and persist over time. Despite the growing prevalence, there is a lack of clear understanding of the processes that maintain this turf persistence and how it can vary across depths, reef conditions and realms. To examine the persistence of algal turfs across different reef states, we deployed 309 pre-conditioned tiles in reef patches dominated by live biogenic habitat-formers (corals in the tropics and kelp on temperate reefs) and on patches with low structural complexity covered in turf. Our study sites spanned four locations along Australia’s east coast, from the northern Great Barrier Reef to southeast Tasmania. Tiles were deployed to reef sites that ranged between approx. 2 and 12 metres depth to compare the variation between locations across realms, while additional turf-tiles were also deployed in each habitat type across depths (2, 5, 8, and 12 m) at a single site within each location to concurrently factor for any potential depth effects across realms and locations. In general, turf cover persisted at relatively high levels across the two reef states in the tropics, although with some reduction for shallow-water high coral cover plots. In contrast, in temperate locations, turf persistence and sediment accumulation were consistently lower in the reef state typified by high kelp cover. Our study reveals realm and depth-specific differences in turf-sediment dynamics across reef states and highlights the potential for strong positive feedback loops in intact kelp reefs to resist changes to turf dominance.
eb9b6408:
Climate change-amplified extreme weather events are reshaping ecological communities globally. On coral reefs, heatwaves typically disrupt the obligate coral–algal symbiosis, with symbiont identity a prime determinant of coral resilience to these thermal extremes. Yet, whether heatwaves have long-term effects on coral symbioses remains unclear due to a lack of longitudinal symbiont data. 
Here, we report on a decade-long symbiont survey from Kiritimati atoll (2013–2023), spanning the most prolonged tropical marine heatwave on record (2015–2016) and its aftermath. Using ITS2 DNA metabarcoding data from 856 samples belonging to 551 coral colonies and benthic surveys from 37 forereef sites along a gradient of chronic local human disturbance we document wholesale transformation of the symbiont assemblages in two coral species, Montipora aequituberculata and Pocillopora grandis, the legacy of which was persistent for seven years post heatwave. The recovery of coral cover and pre-heatwave symbioses was negligible in both species for at least three years after the heatwave (2017-2019) and by 7.5 years after the heatwave (2023) was only observed in P. grandis and not M. aequituberculata, a species driven close to local extinction by the marine heatwave. We also observe significant changes to the incidence and abundance of the heat tolerant generalist symbiont taxa after the marine heatwave, and in P. grandis, higher Durusdinium abundance associated with colonies that survived the heatwave and colonies at sites exposed to high human disturbance. 
Our study demonstrates that marine heatwaves can have long-lasting impacts on symbioses, making the recovery of symbiont communities in some corals increasingly unlikely due to the protracted duration of the recovery of pre-heatwave symbionts, the potential for local extinctions of host-adapted symbiont species, and the additional delays in recovery associated with chronic stressors, raising concern for coral resilience to future heatwaves.
b66a50f1: Scleractinian corals are well known to be intimately dependent on numerous microbial partners, with microbes contributing significantly to diverse physiological processes such as nutrient exchange and cycling. Increasing evidence suggests that these microbial partners are also essential contributors to the coral response to both stress and disease. Yet how microbes enhance or weaken coral resistance to stressors, and the degree of specificity of beneficial host-microbe associations across species, remains unknown. The recent outbreak of a devastating coral disease (SCTLD) in the Caribbean, which has affected nearly 20 species of corals, has provided unprecedented opportunities to investigate the contributions of holobiont interactions to coral disease dynamics. Here we detail a long-term sampling approach, combining ecological monitoring of individual coral colonies with repeated mulit-‘omic sampling through disease phases to identify signatures of both resistance and general host response to disease. A multi-species sampling approach allowed for the investigation of species-specific and species-independent markers of disease resistance and response. Integrating transcriptomic data from hosts and photosymbionts with microbial and viral community composition we identify key holobiont interactions contributing to disease resistance. Furthermore, we highlight species-specific and species-independent shifts in these associations across disease progression. Generally, our results highlight intimate associations between host immune gene expression and microbial community members which are likely essential to defense response. Notably, while some of these associations are species-independent, we observe a high degree of species specificity to these patterns, highlighting a need for species-specific management approaches. Combined, this powerful dataset significantly advances our understanding coral disease in a holobiont framework.
4af1bc61: Coral reefs are experiencing accelerating environmental pressures that undermine coral survival and reef functioning. In the Red Sea, recent temperature-driven coral bleaching events have drastically reduced coral cover on shallow reef flats. Yet, crustose coralline algae (CCA) remain abundant and are increasingly recognized as key ecosystem engineers that facilitate reef resilience and recovery. Because important CCA functions are microbially mediated, understanding the natural variability of CCA-associated bacteria, and their sensitivity to environmental stressors, is essential but remains poorly resolved. We investigated spatial and temporal variation in microbiomes associated with two abundant but undescribed CCA species in the central Red Sea, a region characterized by strong environmental seasonality and extreme summer temperatures. The two focal species differed in their fouling-resistance mechanisms: Species 1 regularly sloughs epithelial layers, whereas Species 2 does not. Ten individuals of each species were tagged and repeatedly sampled across four seasons at one nearshore (Abu Shusha) and one offshore (Cement Wreck) shallow reef flat. Sequencing of the 16S rRNA V3–V4 region showed that bacterial communities were strongly host-specific, clearly distinct from seawater, and significantly structured by both site and season. Season emerged as the dominant driver of microbiome composition in both hosts, with pronounced shifts during summer. Summer assemblages were enriched in opportunistic and potentially pathogenic taxa, including Vibrio spp. Recovery trajectories diverged between host species: while Species 1 microbiomes returned to pre-summer compositions after peak temperatures, Species 2 microbiomes remained dispersed, suggesting slower or incomplete recovery to summer stress. Overall, this work reveals that CCA hosts exhibit distinct microbiome responses to summer stress, emphasizing the need to further investigate how host-microbiome interactions shape CCA health and their broader ecological roles on coral reefs.
daee9934: Coral reefs are threatened by rising ocean temperatures associated with climate change, leading to a breakdown of the cnidarian-dinoflagellate symbiosis and growth of pathogenic bacteria. The host response to diverse bacteria can vary from species to species. In this study, the sea anemone Exaiptasia diaphana (Aiptasia), a prominent laboratory model for coral-symbiosis research, was used to investigate host responses to three key bacterial species associated with both Aiptasia and corals. Tritonibacter mobilis, Vibrio alginolyticus, and Alteromonas macleodii were individually inoculated into the seawater of both symbiotic and aposymbiotic hosts to determine their effects on host health. Each bacterium was hypothesized to have a different effect on host health—beneficial, pathogenic, or no effect. Various densities of each bacterium were used to assess impacts on host morphology and survival over 14 days at 27°C. Host biomass, protein content, and dinoflagellate abundance (to assess bacterial effects on host bleaching) were examined over 7 days at 27°C in response to an inoculation density of 8×10^8 cells/ml of each bacterium. When compared to un-inoculated Aiptasia, V. alginolyticus reduced survival and biomass by 27% and 54% in symbiotic and aposymbiotic Aiptasia, respectively. V. alginolyticus also diminished dinoflagellate abundance by 20% in symbiotic Aiptasia. In contrast, T. mobilis enhanced survival of symbiotic Aiptasia, biomass by 9%, protein content by 50%, and dinoflagellate abundance by 60%, but had no impacts on aposymbiotic Aiptasia. A. macleodii had no major effects on host health at all. As a result, V. alginolyticus generally has negative impacts on host health, while T. mobilis appears to be a beneficial bacterium. Moreover, host susceptibility to bacteria is dependent on whether animals have their dinoflagellates to begin with and, therefore, bleached cnidarians may experience more drastic responses to pathogens like V. alginolyticus, but no benefits from a potential probiotic like T. mobilis.
5d7e429d:
Mayotte hosts one of the largest lagoon systems in the Indian Ocean, forming a major regional biodiversity hotspot. Rapid demographic growth over recent decades has intensified land-use change,  sediment and nutrient inputs to the lagoon, underscoring the need for sustained monitoring of marine communities.
Sponges are relevant indicators of land–sea interactions due to their sensitivity to water quality and substrate conditions. Since 2020, projects have developed sponges and their microbiomes as a model system to quantify how watershed anthropisation influences (i) sponge-associated microbiomes and (ii) sponge community structure across Mayotte’s fringing reefs. To do so, microbiome analyses were conducted in 2020 across four campaigns and ecologically contrasted stations using 16S metabarcoding (two markers) on Xestospongia testudinaria (HMA) and Stylissa massa (LMA). Sponge assemblages were assessed during large-scale campaigns in 2022/2023 and 2025, surveying 30 stations around the island. Environmental covariates (anthropisation index, nutrients, substrate type, depth) were measured to identify pathways linking watershed pressures to benthic responses.
Microbiome analyses showed that watershed anthropisation influences part of the microbial community, with several bacterial lineages shifting along the disturbance gradient despite stable core microbiomes. Sponge assemblages displayed strong within-station structuring driven by depth, and anthropisation emerged as a major determinant of community composition. Stations adjacent to the most anthropised watersheds exhibited the highest sponge richness and abundance, including species favoured by enriched or degraded conditions. Causal modelling confirmed expected effects related to nutrient enrichment and substrate variables, but detailed analyses revealed that anthropisation did not act primarily through siltation; instead, it operated more subtly by altering substrate diversity. Despite major disturbances—including the 2024 mass bleaching and Cyclone Chido—sponge assemblages remained remarkably stable at the island scale.
Our results highlight sponges and their microbiomes as powerful indicators of land–sea interactions and provide a robust foundation for long-term ecological monitoring in the Mayotte lagoon.
2cdb9434: Marine heatwaves (MHWs) have increased 34% in frequency since 1925, posing significant threats to foundational reef organisms like sponges. These sessile filter feeders process substantial water volumes (up to 35 ml min⁻¹/cm³ tissue) and play crucial roles in benthic-pelagic coupling through nutrient transformation and release of dissolved organic matter that supports reef ecosystems. Thermal stress is known to alter sponge-associated microbial communities and cause host bleaching and mortality, however, the long-term potential for microbiome recovery following thermal disturbance has been largely underexplored. This research aims to explore both the acute and long-term responses of the in-situ sponge microbiome to a marine heatwave via longitudinal sampling of shallow water, emergent sponges in Florida Bay, FL. We hypothesized that sponge microbiomes will shift in composition with exposure to extreme thermal stress, and that altered microbiome recovery to a pre-disturbance state will increase with increasing temporal distance from the disturbance event. To test these hypotheses, we are analyzing sponge tissue and water column samples collected across seven time points during and after the 2023 south Florida thermal events, which included two successive heatwaves and a subsequent cold snap. Preliminary microbiome analysis indicates that microbial communities are responding to thermal stress in the short term, with detectable compositional shifts during thermal anomalies. Ongoing analysis aims to determine whether these microbiomes demonstrate resilience and recover to pre-disturbance states or undergo persistent restructuring. Given the important role of sponges in nutrient cycling on reefs, our understanding of their response to, and recovery from, marine heat waves will provide critical insights into reef ecosystem resilience.
897d0e78: This study fills a major knowledge gap in baseline data for passive acoustic monitoring (PAM), reef acoustics, and reef fish behavioural studies. To make passive acoustics a useful tool for monitoring fish and their behaviour, it is essential to associate specific sounds with species and behavioural contexts. Although recording fish sounds in captivity is possible, most species rarely vocalize in confined environments. Therefore, identifying and describing fish sounds in situ across diverse behaviours and habitats is crucial. In this study, fish sounds were recorded in the wild using a cost-effective portable audio-video platform capable of capturing species-specific acoustic signatures through a hydrophone paired with a 360° camera. This setup can be deployed across various reef habitats to collect synchronized audio-video data for defined periods. GoPro and 360° cameras were mounted on frame, and uRUDAR acoustic recorders were used. Fieldwork was conducted across multiple reef zones, including lagoons, outer reef flats, and reef slopes in the Lakshadweep Islands, India. The audio-video approach provided high accuracy in validating sounds produced by soniferous fishes and enabled documentation of mechanical feeding sounds, such as crunching noises from Scarus sp. This method proved effective across different reef environments. Using this setup, sounds from species such as Lutjanus gibbus, Stegastes nigricans, Myripristis murdjan, Acanthurus sp., and Scarus sp. were identified in association with behaviours like feeding, shoaling, aggression, territorial interactions, and mating. These sounds were classified and analysed using Audacity and RavenPro software to build the first underwater acoustic sound bank for the Indian region. This study provides essential baseline information for future PAM-based reef monitoring and offers a framework for capacity building among dive centres and other stakeholders to generate non-invasive acoustic datasets.
2a22dcfb:
On coral reefs, rabbitfish are functionally important because they forage on algae in structurally complex areas that are inaccessible to most other herbivores. They often exhibit a cooperative feeding tactic, forming symbiotic long-term pair bonds where one partner feeds and the other adopts a vigilant posture. Despite their functional importance, the environmental drivers of vigilance are unknown. This is especially relevant as coral reefs undergo rapid change, including declines in predator biomass, loss of structural complexity, and shifts toward turf-dominated benthos.

We are conducting a year-long investigation into how variation in local biotic reef conditions influences cooperative feeding behaviour in Siganus doliatus pairs. At four sites around Lizard Island (northern Great Barrier Reef, Australia), we conducted repeated behavioural observations of six pairs per site across three seasonal trips. At each site, underwater visual census transects quantified predator and competitor abundance and biomass. Within each pair’s territory, we measured structural complexity (rugosity) and epilithic algal turf cover and height. We are testing how these environmental variables predicted vigilance, foraging, and territorial defence.

We predicted more frequent vigilance with higher predator or competitor abundance and greater structural complexity. Instead, these expectations were not met in the data analysed thus far: relationships between environmental variables and cooperative behaviour were highly variable among sites and pairs. This heterogeneity suggests that rabbitfish cooperation is not uniformly driven by these environmental variables. Instead, further analysis may reveal interactions among factors, partner dynamics, or unresponsiveness to environmental variability. Unresponsiveness to environmental variability could be interesting, indicating stereotyped behaviours or overriding social factors.

Understanding when and where paired rabbitfish cooperate provides insight into the stability of their social symbiosis and its sensitivity to ongoing reef change. Such context-dependent behaviour may have implications for pair-bond fidelity, the functional roles rabbitfishes play, and their resilience within rapidly transforming reef ecosystems.
0471c6c9: Understanding how reef fish interactions respond to environmental variation is essential to predict ecological outcomes under climate change. While behavioral studies are typically restricted to local scales, applying interaction ecology to macroecological contexts is an emerging field known as macrobehavior. Here, we investigated the structure and drivers of agonistic interactions across 38 reef sites in nine localities of the Atlantic Ocean. We collected data on species richness and agonistic interactions using Remote Underwater Videos (RUVs). For each locality, 30 RUVs were sampled, totaling 270 videos (<45 hours of footage). To evaluate network structure along the latitudinal gradient, we calculated four unipartite metrics: weighted nestedness (WUNODF), modularity, centrality, and clustering. Notably, WUNODF is a new nestedness metric introduced here, designed to detect nested structure in weighted networks. Environmental drivers were tested using mixed linear models. Agonistic interactions were most frequent in tropical regions (12°N to −17°S), particularly in Curaçao Island, Rocas Atoll, and Abrolhos Archipelago. Interaction networks were predominantly modular (15 of 24 sites), and centrality structured an additional 25% of sites. Species initiating most interactions (out-degree centrality) were largely damselfishes of the Stegastes genus, reflecting territorial dominance. Among environmental predictors, only sea surface temperature (SST) significantly affected agonistic frequency, increasing ~8 interactions per 1 °C rise. Our results show that modularity strongly shapes agonistic networks across a biogeographic gradient and that SST is a major driver of interaction frequency. These findings highlight that fine-scale behavioral dynamics are modulated by large-scale climatic conditions, underscoring the need to incorporate behavior into climate-change vulnerability assessments of reef communities.
eaa89c94: The structural complexity of shallow tropical reefs comes mainly from hermatypic corals (Scleractinians). However, in certain areas of the world, ahermatypic coral species, such as Antipatharians, also known as black corals, can thrive. These black corals create complex structures that can serve as habitats for many marine species. These corals are generally found at great depths (> 50 meters), which makes it difficult to study and explains the lack of knowledge about this system. However, when certain conditions are met, Antipatharians can colonize shallow reefs (15-30 meters) offering an opportunity to study their ecological roles. In the Gulf of California (GC), a hypothesis dating back to the mid-2000s suggests that shallow black coral forests could serve as habitats or nurseries for several fish species, in particular serranids, some of which are endangered on the IUCN Red List. Studying this ecosystem is, therefore, essential to understand how Antipatharians shape reef fish diversity and will help to reveal the importance of this system for biodiversity conservation. Here, we studied the ichthyological diversity in terms of density, biomass and assemblage composition within black coral forest, black coral patches and rocky reefs in Bahia de Los Angeles in the GC using visual transects. We found higher density and richness, as well as a distinct assemblage composition in black coral forest in comparison to the other two habitats. The spotted sand bass (Paralabrax maculatofasciatus) was exclusively found in black coral forest at a higher density than that observed in other historical data on this species in the GC. Two other species (Paralabrax auroguttatus and Oxycirrhithes typus) were only found within black coral branches. Our findings reveal the importance of black coral forests for highly targeted species and call for future large-scale studies on this ecosystem in the northern part of the GC.
c7e8fbe2:
Ocean warming and acidification may profoundly affect marine organisms. To understand how coral reef fish are affected by changing conditions, we used a manipulative aquaria-based experiment to assess the response to warming and ocean acidification in the Neon Damselfish (Pomacentrus coelestis) in Shimoda, Japan. Fish were exposed to one of four treatments: elevated temperature, elevated pCO₂, combined elevated temperature and elevated pCO₂, and ambient conditions (control). Respirometry, behavioral assays, and brain transcriptomics were used to assess physiological performance, behavioral changes, and gene set enrichment, respectively.

Both warming and elevated pCO₂ decreased factorial aerobic scope, and warming alone decreased activity levels. In the combined stressor treatment, there was an increase in activity level relative to the warming treatment, leading to activity levels which were comparable to the ambient temperature treatments. Gene set enrichment analysis revealed enrichment of pathways associated with immune response, neuronal activity, metabolic activity, and vision, indicating that these fish may be equipped to acclimate to elevated pCO₂ and temperature with a robust transcriptomic response. The highest number of enriched pathways was found in the pCO₂ treatment, followed by the combined stressor treatment, with the lowest number in the warming treatment. 

Reduced activity level in the warming treatment indicates that reduced factorial aerobic scope may force these fish to downregulate their activity under warming conditions to conserve energy. However, this compensatory response is disrupted in the multiple stressor treatment, eliminating the behavioral changes seen in the warming treatment. The combined stressor treatment also reduces the number of enriched pathways relative to the pCO₂ treatment. These results imply that multiple stressors may dilute the response seen in a single stressor treatment, leading to a maladaptive synergistic effect. This study highlights the value of integrative multiple stressor experiments by demonstrating the complexity of coral reef fish response to global environmental change.
50a7b531:
Coral reef fishes are increasingly challenged by rising ocean temperatures driven by climate change. These pressures are particularly acute for highly site-attached species that live in symbiosis with sessile hosts (e.g., corals, anemones) and cannot readily relocate to avoid thermal stress. Unlike mobile species capable of escaping sub-optimal conditions, these fishes must cope and acclimate in situ. Understanding how environmental variation shapes the physiology and behaviour of these host-dependent species, and how this differs among species with varying degrees of host association (site-attachment), is essential for predicting their vulnerability under future climate scenarios.

We investigated the effect of thermal history on three coral-associated fishes with differing levels of site-attachment: the arc-eye hawkfish (Paracirrhites arcatus), yellowtail damselfish (Dascyllus flavicaudus), and spotted coral croucher (Caracanthus maculatus) on Mo'orea, French Polynesia. Individuals were collected from two different thermal environments: the warmer, more variable lagoon and the cooler, more thermally stable outer reef. Under controlled laboratory conditions, we quantified maximum and standard metabolic rates, aerobic scope, and behavioural traits (boldness, exploration, sociality) at 27 °C to assess how thermal history influences physiological and behavioural performance. We also tested whether species’ differing levels of host attachment correspond to consistent behavioural syndromes and whether these behaviours relate to metabolic performance and potential thermal acclimation.

Although analyses are still ongoing, results will be ready for presentation at ICRS 2026.
7a9711e8:
Marine photosynthesis underpins coral reef productivity and is sustained not only by corals but also by photosymbiotic invertebrates such as giant clams. Tridacna crocea is a giant clam species that inhabits very shallow reef habitats, where symbiotic dinoflagellates (Symbiodiniaceae) experience intense and spectrally variable light over small depth gradients. Efficient regulation of the internal light environment is therefore essential for maintaining photosynthetic performance while avoiding photodamage.
In giant clams, this regulation is mediated in part by mantle iridocytes—specialized reflective cells that generate structural coloration and modulate light delivery to symbionts. In T. crocea, blue mantles predominantly reflect shorter wavelengths (purple to blue), whereas brown mantles reflect longer wavelengths (yellow to red), indicating differences in optical filtering within the mantle. We hypothesized that these optical differences influence symbiont photosynthetic performance and contribute to fine-scale depth-related distribution patterns, even across meter-scale changes in shallow reefs.
To address this, we combined ultrastructural and optical analyses with field surveys across shallow depth strata (1–4 m). Transmission electron microscopy revealed multilayered guanine lamellae within iridocytes, with lamella thickness varying among mantle color morphs. Field observations showed that the relative abundance of blue- and brown-mantled individuals differed with depth, and effective quantum yield of photosystem II (Y(II)) also varied between color morphs across depths. These results provide an overview of how mantle structural coloration may shape internal light environments, photosynthetic performance, and depth distribution in T. crocea, and establish a field-based framework for future studies of marine photosynthesis in reef ecosystems.
b2e15f98: Giant clams (Family: Cardiidae) are integral to reef structures, providing food and habitat to other organisms, but are threatened by climate change and overharvesting. They rely on supplemental nutrition through photosymbiosis, and evidence shows that harbouring multiple photosymbionts may help them withstand fluctuating conditions. Globally, giant clams have been found to associate with photosymbionts of the genera Symbiodinium, Cladocopium, Durusdinium, and Gerakladium, however, this has not previously been investigated in Western Australian. Using a DNA metabarcoding approach, the nuclear ribosomal Internal Transcribed Spacer region 2 (ITS2) was targeted to amplify Symbiodiniaceae in four giant clam host species – Hippopus hippopus, Tridacna maxima, Tridacna noae and Tridacna squamosa. Across 78 samples, it was found that photosymbiont assemblages differ across spatial scales, with higher diversity observed in offshore reefs. Harbouring a combination of symbionts, including Durusdinium, known as the ‘extremophile’ genus of Symbiodiniaceae, may enhance the resilience of giant clams, improve their ability to withstand environmental change and secure their future in reef ecosystems. This study provides a foundation of genetic diversity of photosymbiont communities in giant clams of Western Australia and contributes to the building taxonomy of Symbiodiniaceae.
0036ee02:
Thermal tolerance in reef-building cnidarians is partly dictated by the stress-tolerance capabilities of their dinoflagellate endosymbionts, the Symbiodiniaceae. The presence of more thermally tolerant species such as Durusdinium, Cladocopium sp., and certain strains of Breviolum minutum are associated with a bleaching-tolerant phenotype in cnidarians. Over the years, efforts have been made through laboratory-assisted evolution, and more recently through synthetic biology approaches, to enhance thermal tolerance in free-living Symbiodiniaceae. However, the stress-coping pathways that underpin thermal tolerance in Symbiodiniaceae remain unknown, limiting our ability to identify suitable diagnostic markers for screening thermal-tolerance phenotypes.

In this study, we examined differences in thermal-tolerance phenotypes among experimentally evolved strains of Cladocopium proliferum (SS3, SS5, SS7, SS8, SS9) and compared them to their wild-type strain (WT10). We also compared thermal tolerance among three naturally occurring strains of B. minutum belonging to two different internal transcribed spacer region 2 profiles (one strain: B1–B1o–B1g–B1p; two strains: B1–B1a–B1b–B1g). A rapid short-term cumulative heat-stress assay (26–42 °C) was performed to determine the temperature at which these strains exhibited a 50% reduction in photosynthetic performance, used as a proxy for baseline thermal tolerance. We also measured several photobiological parameters related to heat dissipation through regulated and unregulated pathways.

Our results reveal a strong link between thermal tolerance and the activation of regulated heat-dissipation mechanisms, namely superquenching (re-organization of antennae complexes from photosystem II to photosystem I) and energy-dependent quenching. Observed changes in the ratio of membrane-bound photopigments (diadinoxanthin to diatoxanthin) further suggest potential chloroplast lipid-bilayer rigidification in thermally tolerant strains. Overall, this study highlights the importance of investigating regulated heat-dissipation pathways as potential diagnostic markers for screening thermal-tolerance phenotypes in Symbiodiniaceae.
b506fa38: Although coral reef framework formation is primarily attributed to scleractinian corals, a few octocoral taxa also play an important role. The genus Tubipora deposits hard calcareous skeletons organised in tubes connected by horizontal stolonic platforms constituted of fused sclerites. Although the genus is broadly distributed across the tropical Indo-Pacific, its diversity and evolutionary history remain poorly understood. Historically, ten species have been described, yet most recent treatments have recognized only the type species, Tubipora musica. Here, we used an integrative approach combining morphological and phylogenomic (based on Ultra-Conserved Element and exon loci) analyses of 136 Tubipora colonies collected across six marine provinces in the Indo-Pacific. We delimited 15 morphologically distinct and genetically strongly supported lineages. All lineages exhibited highly restricted geographic distributions, limited to single provinces, highlighting regional endemism. Endemic diversification is suggested by sister species restricted to the Red Sea, Madagascar, and Eastern Australia, as well as regional diversification around the Arabian Peninsula, in the Western Indian Ocean, and in the Western Pacific. Results show deep diversification within the Western Pacific, from which the Western Indian Ocean was colonized by a single lineage that underwent radiation there. These findings underscore the need for broader sampling across the Indo-Pacific to assess Tubipora diversity and diversification and highlight the power of genomics in clarifying species boundaries and evolutionary relationships, providing a foundation towards the taxonomic revision of Tubipora. Accurate species definition is essential for coral reef conservation, enabling precise biodiversity assessments and correctly informing management and restoration strategies, ultimately enhancing our ability to monitor and mitigate the impacts of climate change on vulnerable reef-building organisms.  Our findings challenge historical taxonomic assumptions on this group and highlight the need for a systematic revision of the genus through an integrated morphological, genomic, and biogeographic approach.
c7814af1: Members of the octocoral family Gorgoniidae form mutualisms with dinoflagellates belonging to the family Symbiodiniaceae. Many Caribbean gorgonians host symbiodiniaceans from the genus Breviolum, particularly the ITS2 lineage known as B1. However, this lineage has undergone a relatively recent adaptive radiation, and ITS2 alone does not sufficiently resolve diversity within the group. Therefore, it remains unclear to what extent Caribbean gorgonians exhibit specificity for unique symbiont species (and vice versa). In this context, using multiple high-resolution molecular markers can help clarify partner specificity and potentially provide evidence of codiversification. In this study, eight B1-hosting gorgonian species spanning six Caribbean locations were sampled to isolate host and symbiont DNA. Host phylogenies were constructed using sequences from two mitochondrial coding genes (msh1 and ND2) and two nuclear genes (SRP54 and 28S rDNA). Symbiont phylogenies were constructed using sequences from two nuclear regions (ITS1 and ITS2 rDNA), two microsatellite flanking regions (B7sym15 and CA4.86), and two plastid regions (domain V of 23S rDNA and the psbA noncoding region). The trees revealed cryptic diversity within the symbiont B1 lineage corresponding to 4-6 Breviolum species, as well as potential cryptic diversity among hosts. Species distributions were consistent with high connectivity across the Caribbean. Cophylogenetic patterns indicated varying degrees of specificity and flexibility among gorgonians. This study highlights the importance of incorporating multiple molecular markers into analyses of codiversification among coral-algal symbioses.
d46d186e: The integrated application of molecular phylogenetic and morphological analyses has revolutionized systematics and our understanding of evolutionary relationships across many marine taxa. One such group is the hexacorallian order Zoantharia, which inhabits a wide range of marine environments, from shallow coral reefs to the deep sea. Intensive research over the past two decades has led to the establishment of four macrocnemic families and 16 genera, reflecting a major revision of zoantharian taxonomy and a substantial improvement in the resolution of their phylogenetic framework. Nevertheless, these advances have also highlighted the limitations of traditional genetic markers, which frequently lack sufficient phylogenetic resolution to fully resolve evolutionary relationships within Zoantharia. As a consequence, support values for several taxonomic groups remain low, and their phylogenetic placements are often unstable. In recent years, ultra-conserved elements (UCEs) and exon-based markers have become increasingly popular, as they provide a much larger number of informative sites distributed across the genome and allow more robust resolution of both deep and shallow evolutionary relationships in Anthozoa. As such, they represent powerful tools for stabilizing zoantharian phylogeny and resolving long-standing taxonomic uncertainties. Here, we perform phylogenomic reconstruction based on UCEs and exonic loci to refine the systematics of Zoantharia. We present these results and discuss their implications for the systematics and understanding of the evolutionary history of this group.
0b5cd0de: Azooxanthellate scleractinian corals are solitary individuals or small colonies that do not contribute to reef formation. They are typically small, lack zooxanthellae, and inhabit deeper waters, which often leads to their omission in surveys. Despite their ecological importance, there is limited knowledge of their diversity and distribution in Hong Kong waters, as no comprehensive survey has been conducted to date. Previous studies on local azooxanthellate scleractinian corals have been few, with the last survey conducted over 20 years ago and involving only a limited number of sites. The recent discoveries of four new Tubastraea species in Hong Kong in 2021 and 2022 suggests that the diversity of azooxanthellate scleractinian corals has been underestimated. This has motivated a comprehensive survey of these corals in local waters. In this project, over 300 specimens were collected from 34 sites across eastern and western waters of Hong Kong, and 62 morphospecies were preliminarily identified. Delimiting species of corals is challenging due to morphological plasticity and the use of molecular markers that are either not informative or have not completely sorted. Moreover, recent molecular studies have shown that many azooxanthellate scleractinian species are polyphyletic at the genus and family levels. Therefore, an integrated approach combining morphological and molecular methods is essential for coral species delimitation. In this study, we used morphological and genomic approaches to delineate species boundaries for local specimens. Currently, approximately 31 species across families such as Caryophylliidae, Dendrophylliidae, Flabellidae, Oculinidae, and Rhizangiidae have been identified. By the end of the project, an updated inventory of local azooxanthellate scleractinian corals will be produced, and a genomic database of these corals will be established for public use.
51ba371d: Understanding geographical patterns of species distribution is pivotal for effective conservation. For marine benthic organisms such as reef-dwelling corals, species distributions have been traditionally inferred assuming long-distance larval dispersal and considering macro-morphological skeletal features. However, genomic findings are revealing that many coral species have restricted ranges, and the number of lineages considered widespread is overestimated. The genus Blastomussa is heavily targeted in the aquarium trade, therefore accurate delineation of species boundaries and biogeography is essential for conservation efforts. Here, we sampled 88 Blastomussa colonies across 27 Indo-Pacific localities, including the type localities of all five valid species, and used ezRAD data to resolve species boundaries and revise geographic ranges. Phylogenomic reconstructions revealed at least nine lineages with restricted distribution in the Pacific, Indian Ocean, or around the Arabian Peninsula. Integrating our genomic findings with morphological examination, we describe four new species, each with restricted distribution around the Arabian Peninsula or in the Indian Ocean. Two distinct and allopatric lineages are currently identified as Blastomussa merleti: the true B. merleti from the Pacific Ocean and a new lineage endemic to the seas around the Arabian Peninsula with similar gross morphology. In the Indian Ocean, we identified three monophyletic lineages, one from India and the Maldives, and two sympatric lineages from Madagascar. The Pacific lineage including Blastomussa wellsi and Blastomussa vivida remains in need of further sampling and genomic characterization. Finally, Symbiodiniaceae genotyping revealed host-driven selection and regional partitioning of symbiont association. Overall, these results corroborate recent findings regarding Scleractinia distribution patterns, with ranges smaller than previously inferred, which have important implications for species conservation.
43c0bad7:
Coral resilience depends on molecular interactions between cnidarian hosts and their Symbiodiniaceae symbionts. Host-symbiont protein-protein interactions and multi-subunit protein complexes, the molecular machines that orchestrate biochemical signaling cascades, may govern the earliest heat stress responses that lead to symbiosis breakdown. However, holobiont complexes remain poorly characterized, in part due to the lack of molecular tools to probe protein-protein interactions, such as specific antibodies and genetically-incorporated affinity tags.

Here, we performed size exclusion chromatography-mass spectrometry (SEC-MS) proteomics interactome mapping of the reef-building coral Montipora capitata. SEC-MS separates native protein complexes by size and identifies their subunits by MS without requiring antibodies or protein tagging, using correlated elution profiles as a proxy for physical interactions. We extracted protein from coral tissue, collected 48 SEC fractions, and analyzed them using DIA-PASEF mass spectrometry on a Bruker timsTOF HT. Our analysis identified 4,870 host and 1,406 Cladocopium proteins and we constructed co-elution profiles to infer protein complex membership. Mapping coral proteins to human orthologs revealed 52% of detected M. capitata proteins shared conserved orthology, enabling annotation of 253 host protein complexes with at least two detected members from the CORUM database, including the Arp2/3 cytoskeleton remodeling complex, the Retromer endosomal trafficking complex, the Vacuolar ATPase complex, and others with relevance to coral resilience.

Interestingly, we identified many host and symbiont proteins with highly correlated co-elution profiles, consistent with a physical interaction across the host-symbiont boundary. Host proteins whose elution profiles most strongly correlated with symbiont proteins were enriched in metabolic, protein transport, and cadherin binding pathways, suggesting that physical host-symbiont interactions may directly coordinate these processes. Our work is the first to globally define protein complexes at the cnidarian–Symbiodiniaceae interface, with the potential to reveal mechanisms underlying coral resilience to heat stress.
e14955ae: Many cnidarians rely on endosymbiotic algae in the family Symbiodiniaceae to provide them with photosynthate to meet their nutrient and energy needs. During symbiosis establishment in juvenile animals, symbionts must proliferate to populate the whole animal. A similar process occurs when symbionts re-populate an animal after a bleaching event. Despite the importance of symbiont proliferation in cnidarian-Symbiodiniaceae symbiosis, mechanistically this process is poorly understood. Recently we have isolated Breviolum minutum mutants that are visually distinct from wild type when imaged in hospite with bright-field microscopy. Mutant and wild type populations co-infected into the sea anemone Aiptasia can be easily tracked during proliferation. Data from these experiments was used to generate models of Symbiodiniaceae proliferation in Aiptasia that are revealing new insights into how symbionts proliferate in cnidarian hosts. These mutants can serve as new tools for microscopy-based approaches for evaluation of a wide variety of aspects of cnidarian-Symbiodiniaceae biology.
e873e334:
How do different organisms interact to unlock new possibilities for life? The photosynthesis-based symbiosis between cnidarian corals and dinoflagellate algae provides a striking example. Corals internalise algae into some of their cells, enabling nutrient transfer from algal photosynthesis and unlocking survival in nutrient-poor environments. One of the greatest ecological challenges of our time stems from the environmental sensitivity of this symbiosis, which breaks down under light and heat stress as algae are lost from corals, leading to coral death. Crucially, not all coral-algae symbioses suffer this fate; some survive whilst others breakdown under the same conditions. Understanding the mechanisms of this resilience remains a major unsolved challenge in coral symbiosis biology.

For symbiosis to remain stable, the algal photosystem must be exposed to light environments that support photosynthesis. Microsensor measurements have revealed light microenvironments within coral tissues that vary with local tissue morphology and algal clustering, indicating that the location of symbionts within a coral polyp, the individual units that make up a coral, will determine their light exposure. Despite these insights, how coral cells and algae interact to regulate the spatial organisation of symbionts within the host remains unclear. 

Using high-resolution imaging in combination with a tissue lacerate system and molecular biology approaches we have generated a cell-resolution imaging dataset capturing the spatial organisation of algae within the host as it undergoes morphogenesis into a polyp. We observe that symbionts are not passively accommodated but dynamically patterned to the budding tentacles during host morphogenesis. Using controlled light perturbations, we show that tissue-level symbiont spatial organisation is remodelled under photosynthesis-stress conditions – suggesting a dynamic adaptation to support photosynthesis. We are now taking a combined machine learning and experimental approach to uncover mechanisms through which symbiosis adapts to challenging environments, with an aim to bridge wet-lab discovery to applied conservation.
a4eb0849:
The persistence of coral reefs under climate change relies substantially on the identity and plasticity of the coral-Symbiodiniaceae mutualism. This partnership hinges on molecular-level recognition between host and symbiont, with lectin–glycan signaling emerging as a key mechanism of compatibility that may underlie partner specificity in the Cnidaria–Symbiodiniaceae symbiosis. Montipora capitata is a broadcast spawner that vertically transmits symbionts through gametes and in Kāneʻohe Bay, Oʻahu, Hawaiʻi, genotypes dominated by Cladocopium are more sensitive to warmer temperatures and bleach more readily than those hosting Durusdinium, which are more thermally tolerant and better equipped to handle future climates. To investigate the molecular and biochemical basis of these differences, we examined Cladocopium and Durusdinium across adult colonies of M. capitata genotypes, their eggs, and symbionts that were either retained or expelled during simulated bleaching events.
Symbiont cell surface properties and biochemistry differed between Cladocopium and Durusdinium. Combined host-symbiont effects strongly influenced the physiological and metabolic responses to temperature stress, highlighting the individual holobiont—not the symbiont genus alone—as the primary unit of thermal response. Elevated temperatures altered the symbiont cell-surface glycome and led to the convergence of betaine lipids biochemistry between the thermosensitive Cladocopium and the thermotolerant Durusdinium in adult corals. The changes in symbiont cell-surface glycoconjugates and membrane lipids, linked to heat-induced oxidative stress, suggest that changes in these molecular interfaces may disrupt host–symbiont specific symbioses. Deviations from stress-free symbiont glycan profiles may therefore contribute to both symbiosis breakdown and the plasticity of partner associations under stress. Understanding how Symbiodiniaceae regulate stress responses and host affinity helps identify the traits contributing to coral resilience under climate change.
b2991972: Fluorescent proteins (FPs) in reef-building corals represent one of the most sophisticated biological photonic systems known, yet their functional roles and the specific pathways through which they redistribute energy across tissues remain unresolved. While early studies identified nanometre-scale Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) among coral FPs, the extent to which both short- and longer-range mechanisms operate in vivo has remained unclear. FPs belong to green fluorescent protein (GFP-type) homologues naturally expressed by corals, spanning cyan to red emissions that are naturally produced by corals. This study used high-resolution confocal microscopy, fluorescence micro-spectral analyses and fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) to characterise the spatial distribution and energy-transfer behaviour of GFP-type Fps in corals from the Great Barrier Reef. Imaging revealed structured networks of spectrally complementary FPs across epidermal and gastrodermal layers, forming micro-domains closely associated with Symbiodiniaceae-rich tissues. FLIM measurements showed donor-lifetime shortening in the presence of intra-cellularly adjacent red-shifted acceptor pigments, demonstrating non-radiative FRET occurring at nanometre separations within intact tissues. In parallel, spectral analyses revealed radiative re-emission and re-absorption events consistent with energy transfer operating over longer distances, both within cells and extending across neighboring cells and tissues, where photons emitted by one FP population were subsequently absorbed by another. These combined findings indicate that coral FPs facilitate a dual energy-transfer system, with FRET enabling fine-scale spectral coupling and radiative pathways mediating broader redistribution of light through scattering tissues. Thus, in addition to their recognised roles in scattering and spectral modulation, FP assemblies also mediate energy transfer that further shapes the light environment experienced by symbiont chloroplasts. By providing in vivo, tissue-level evidence for both radiative and non-radiative energy-transfer pathways, this work expands current understanding of how coral hosts use FP-based photonic systems to fine-tune internal optical conditions within the coral–algal symbiosis.
aa31f13f: Corals establish symbiotic relationships only with certain genera of the family Symbiodiniaceae and can sometimes break these associations, raising the possibility that they may digest the symbionts within their tissue.  To assess digestive activity toward symbionts in corals, we used Gomori’s　staining to visualize the activity of acid phosphatase (ACPase), a lysosomal enzyme, in corals during the early stages of symbiosis and under bleaching conditions.　Firstly, Juvenile polyps of Acropora tenuis were inoculated with four culture strains of Symbiodiniaceae belonging to genus Breviolum (CCMP2459), Cladocopium (CCMP2466), Durusdinium (CCMP2556), and Fugacium (CCMP2468). After inoculation, the cell numbers of Cladocopium and Durusdinium increased in the host corals, whereas those of Breviolum and Fugacium did not. Inoculated corals subjected to Gomori’s staining occasionally showed acid phosphatase activity in symbiotic Breviolum, Cladocopium, and Durusdinium. Exposure to DCMU during early siymbiosis caused fewer symbiont cells and higher ACPase activity signals as compared to DCMU-untreated corals. Furthermore, bleached corals under light and heat stress showed strong ACPase signals around remaining colorless symbionts in the corals. These ACPase signals were also observed under electron microscopy, confirming their presence around disrupted symbionts. Although our results do not clearly demonstrate whether corals selectively digest specific Symbiodiniaceae during early symbiosis, they suggest that corals digest algae that have lost photosynthetic activity via ACPase-mediated processes.
00958a78: Given climate change and global coral bleaching events, studying resilient coral species and populations may help us to better understand the mechanisms of survivorship and coral persistence during thermal stress in the future. Many factors interact to shape resilience and it has become increasingly clear that the environment can modulate coral response to thermal stress through environmental memory, as corals from extreme habitats, such as tide pools, have been more tolerant than those from more stable environments. Despite that, cellular and molecular mechanisms behind these differences are not yet clear. In this study we aimed to elucidate some of the cellular mechanisms behind resilience in tide pool corals, by measuring phagocytosis in Siderastrea stellata from the Southwestern Atlantic. We collected corals from a tide pool (<0.5m) and shallow reef (2m) in the Northeast of Brazil during the fourth global bleaching event which resulted in heat stress up to 20°C-weeks. We collected healthy, fluorescent, and bleached corals (based on color) from the two sites and used flow cytometry to perform an immune-associate assay of phagocytosis. We hypothesized that resilient corals would present increased phagocytosis, as it can be a proxy of the coral innate immune system activity. As expected, healthy corals from both habitats presented more phagocytic cells than corals that were fluorescent and/or bleaching. Interestingly, we also found increased phagocytosis in the tide pool corals when compared to shallow reef in all three health states. Additionally, we found that while tide pool corals did bleach, they recovered faster than those from the shallow reef. These results confirm that the corals less affected by the heat stress were the ones with greater phagocytic activity and can indicate that a robust innate immune system response may increase resilience, helping corals to cope with elevated temperatures and persist under climate change.
fc6217f3:
As coral reefs decline due to intensifying marine heat waves, a new paradigm has been launched, focused on accelerating natural coral reef adaptation and recovery processes. Endorsed as the Reefs of Hope Ocean Decade action in Feb 2024, strategic battle plans have been formulated to prevent coral reef collapse and secure coral species diversity in the face of a rapidly worsening situation. https://oceandecade.org/actions/reefs-of-hope/  
The fundamental aspects of this coral-focused strategy are:
1. Prevent further loss of coral species and coral genetic diversity, with an emphasis on heat-adapted corals. This is done by collecting and translocating rare and declining coral species into gene bank nurseries located in cooler reef areas nearby, elevated and thus safe from COTS predators. Corals are sourced from unbleached survivors of mass bleaching and from pre-adapted coral populations located in the hot shallows where they are jeopardized by rising temperatures.  
2. Restore reproduction to declining and rare coral species by bringing them close together for greater fertilization and to increased larval supply.
3. Reboot natural coral reef recovery processes via densely planted “regeneration patches”, restoring strong settlement signals to enhance larval settlement to the reef, and designed to maximize fish and invertebrate habitat for improved ecological functioning.
4. Where possible, implement the work in support of existing conservation efforts; enhancing thermal resilience, ecological functionality, and fisheries habitat of MPAs. 
Corals for Conservation is implementing Reefs of Hope strategies in five Pacific Island nations under a 3-year CORDAP grant, and preliminary results are presented. 
ICRI has encouraged all those involved in saving coral reefs to consider using elements of these strategies within their projects as insurance against coral diversity loss. Reefs of Hope provides the battle strategies, and ample weapons and ammunition already exist. What is needed now are officers and troops to create and hold defensive lines.
abafa161:
As marine heatwaves intensify, coral nurseries, key tools for conservation and restoration, are increasingly vulnerable to bleaching. Ensuring their persistence now requires developing and validating practical interventions that can be deployed during heatwaves to reduce stress before damage occurs.
Here, we present one of the most comprehensive in-situ evaluations to date of two low-cost, scalable interventions that reduce light stress - shading with cloths (>90% light reduction) in shallow lagoon and depth relocation to 30m - tested across coral nurseries for 5 months during the 2024 heatwave in Mo’orea (French Polynesia). Using a broad assemblage of 14 species and 30 genotypes per species, we quantified how each tool altered thermal and light exposure, bleaching incidence and severity, mortality, growth, and photophysiological performance over 1 year, including 6 months after interventions ended.
Depth relocation provided the strongest protection by jointly mitigating irradiance and heat exposure through internal-wave cooling. At 30m, ~65% of the sensitive genotypes (bleached when untreated) avoided bleaching entirely, and bleaching-related mortality declined 6-fold. Artificial shading likewise raised thermal tolerance thresholds, preventing bleaching in ~40% of sensitive genotypes, lowering mortality 3-fold. Both interventions additionally reduced bleaching intensity when it still occurred, allowing to maintain photosynthetic efficiency.
Both approaches incurred short-term energetic costs during their deployment, mainly reduced growth under low light. Yet, several protected sensitive taxa showed improved growth rates in the end, 6 months after interventions ended, suggesting that downstream post-bleaching stress had been alleviated. Benefits were greatest when deployed early before the heatwave, underscoring timing as a critical parameter for field implementation.
Overall, shading and deep relocation emerge as practical, field-ready tools that can substantially reduce bleaching and mortality. Both can improve the resilience of nurseries, hence restoration success, or create artificial refugia that safeguard genetic and functional diversity while global efforts continue to curb global warming.
e6d477ef: Over the past two decades, techniques for producing coral recruits on artificial substrates have advanced substantially. However, scaling their deployment on natural reefs remains constrained by the labor-intensive nature of substrate attachment and the low survival of outplanted colonies. This study evaluates in two phases approaches for large-scale deployment of coral recruits using seeding units (SUs) to improve long-term restoration outcomes. Methods used ranged from active attachment (with bolts, cement, Coralclip, etc.) to individual diver placement without active attachment (by wedging SUs), to mass deployment from the surface or boat (referred to as 'mass seeding').  In the first phase, we assessed 11 combinations of seeding strategies, involving five attachment methods and four SU types, and compared them to a wedging attachment control. Based on the results, three attachment methods and two SU types were selected for further field testing with Acropora palmata and Diploria labyrinthiformis recruits, monitored six and eight months after outplanting. Results indicated that seeding costs increased with attachment materials. Cement-fixed SUs exhibited higher retention but did not improve live coral yield relative to the control. Drill-fixed SUs, although requiring approximately ten times more effort, doubled survival for D. labyrinthiformis and quadrupled it for A. palmata. In the second phase, we examined factors affecting SU retention and recruit performance (for A. palmata, D. labyrinthiformis, and Orbicella annularis) during mass seeding, including seabed roughness and SU shape. Preliminary results in this phase indicated that moderate seabed roughness was necessary to retain roughly 50% of mass-deployed SUs and more than 90% of SUs attached using active methods. Recruit performance remained comparable between mass-deployment and individual (unattached) placement by divers. These findings provide practitioners with evidence-based guidance for selecting cost-effective attachment strategies tailored to local conditions and restoration goals, facilitating the scaling of coral restoration interventions.
6a8b4ef8: Marine heatwaves, exacerbated by climate change, are causing widespread coral mortality. Novel host-associated assisted evolution interventions, like selective breeding, are now being considered to enhance coral heatwave tolerance and promote population persistence. Yet, the evolutionary constraints associated with targeting different heat tolerance traits and the risks of unintended fitness costs remain unknown. Here, we quantify key life history traits for Acropora cf. digitifera  corals in a pedigree-tracked managed population4. We find weak-to-moderate positive genetic correlations among coral’s tolerance to a one-month 32.5°C heatwave and shorter acute exposures—1-2 weeks at 34.5°C and six days of 36°C daily pulses—suggesting a partially shared genetic architecture. However, photosymbiont efficiency loss under a rapid heat shock assay reaching 38.5°C was genetically uncorrelated with bleaching and mortality-based heatwave tolerance, indicating that such widely-used assays cannot reliably identify tolerant coral genotypes for breeding and conservation. Encouragingly, we detected no genetically-based trade-offs between heat tolerance and other fitness traits—reproduction, growth, tissue biomass, and symbiont affinity—and a weak positive correlation with calcification. Evolutionary simulations show that achieving the tolerance levels required to withstand future heatwaves is theoretically possible but requires extremely strong selection that introduces additional challenges and risks. Alongside rapid emissions reductions, reef management and restoration interventions will need to carefully consider trait selection to fully harness the evolutionary potential of corals under climate change.
e9a8a828:
Coral reef managers across the Caribbean are seeking innovative strategies to build climate resilience in Acropora palmata populations following recurrent marine heatwaves, disease events, and local population declines. Assisted Gene Flow (AGF) represents a promising, evidence-based approach to restoring genetic diversity and thermal tolerance. In 2018, cryopreserved sperm from Florida and Puerto Rico was successfully crossed with fresh eggs from Curaçao, generating over 900 genetically diverse AGF juveniles now held at Mote Marine Laboratory’s International Coral Gene Bank. Recent analyses show that many AGF corals naturally acquired heat-tolerant Durusdinium trenchii during early rearing and demonstrated enhanced thermal tolerance and growth under experimental heat stress, although long-term symbiont stability varied by origin facility and through time.

This project describes the multi-year scientific and regulatory pathway underway to transfer these AGF corals from Florida to land-based nurseries in the U.S. Virgin Islands and Puerto Rico. With support from the Curaçao government, teams from Mote Marine Laboratory, the Smithsonian Institute, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, the National Marine Sanctuary Foundation, the USVI Department of Planning and Natural Resources, the Puerto Rico Department of Natural and Environmental Resources, Institute for Socio-Eclogical Research, and The Nature Conservancy have collaborated to refine transport protocols, coordinate permit requirements, develop acclimation and quarantine procedures, and assess risks and benefits of introducing AGF individuals into new jurisdictions. 

We present the results of this initial transfer as well as the decision frameworks co-developed with resource managers, including monitoring protocols for in-situ and ex-situ nurseries, redundancy planning for hurricanes, and criteria for eventual field trials. This work provides the first integrated roadmap for cross-jurisdictional AGF implementation in the U.S. Caribbean and highlights how genetic tools, coordinated permitting, and collaborative governance can accelerate climate-resilient coral restoration at ecosystem-relevant scales and serve as a management tool for Endangered Species Act listed species.
88a8c179: Climate change impacts have intensified the need to develop restoration strategies which facilitate the capacity of coral communities to withstand thermal stress. Assisted evolution has emerged as a response to this challenge; however, the application of these techniques depends on many factors. This study aims to evaluate the potential of assisted evolution to enhance thermal resistance in scleractinian corals through a literature review designed to identify the global state of the art and presents recommendations on which approaches may be effectively adapted to the social and environmental context of developing countries, where most coral reefs are located and which most urgently need active restoration efforts. Using the PRISMA methodology, a review was conducted with a cutoff date of December 31, 2024, identifying 1,451 articles. After applying the exclusion criteria (removal of duplicates, gray literature, and full-text screening), only 100 articles met the established requirements. Results show that Australia leads scientific production on assisted evolution (29%), followed by the Pacific Islands (23%) and the Caribbean (21%). No publications were identified for the Tropical Eastern Pacific or South Asia, highlighting critical gaps and vulnerable regions. The most studied coral genera include Acropora (44%), Pocillopora (17%) and Stylophora (17%). Nine assisted evolution strategy categories  were identified, with those focused on the role of symbionts and the microbiome being the most common (18%), followed by conditioning or acclimation (15%) and thermal tolerance experiments (14%). Among the five methodological  marker groups identified (physiological, genetic, mixed physiological/genetic, transcriptomics/metabolomics, enzymatic activity), physiological markers were the most frequently used. The qualitative economic and operational feasibility analysis  indicated that the most viable strategies for developing countries are those that leverage natural acclimation processes arising from prior thermal stress events, complemented by trait-based approaches that use low-cost physiological markers alongside basic genetic characterization of symbionts and the microbiome.
d11eb867: Coral reef conservation faces an estimated 700% financing shortfall, prompting urgent questions about the value of resilience-enhancing interventions, such as thermal conditioning, within large-scale restoration programs. To evaluate whether the physiological “return on investment” of conditioning is sufficient to justify its implementation, we adapted a net-present-value framework commonly used in finance to quantify the relative costs and benefits of this approach. In partnership with a major restoration organization in the Florida Keys, three genotypes of the staghorn coral Acropora cervicornis were exposed to either constant (+ 3°C) or varying (+1.5–3°C) thermal regimes for 1 week, then conditioned and naïve fragments were outplanted following standard restoration protocols. We measured more than 20 parameters related to symbiosis, metabolism, and thermal performance at two timepoints: one week post-conditioning and four months post-outplanting. Our results revealed both transient and persistent physiological costs and benefits of thermal conditioning, with outcomes varying by trait, genotype, and timescale. This analysis demonstrates the utility of applying a structured cost–benefit framework to intervention testing, supporting more informed decision-making under severe funding constraints.
