Implications for managers and practitioners—including interpretation of physiological net-present-value metrics, identification of promising response variables, and considerations for cost-effective deployment—will be discussed, along with key takeaways and recommendations for future optimization of conditioning interventions.
66f23da5: For millions of years, Acropora palmata dominated Caribbean reefs but is now critically endangered. Heat-induced mortality has exerted strong selective pressure on the species. We applied novel genomic tools to reconstruct population history, detect signatures of selection, and generate polygenic scores for heat tolerance. Samples from 100 Florida and 81 wider-Caribbean colonies were sequenced using PacBio HiFi and Illumina platforms, enabling demographic analyses that revealed an effective population size of ~10,000 in Florida and two pronounced historical bottlenecks. Using a novel haplotype reference panel, we imputed SNP and indel data for 1,285 A. palmata colonies from the STAGdb microarray database. Population structure analyses confirmed a west–east Caribbean division and indicated further substructure within each region; Florida was uniquely admixed. Effective migration surface analyses (FEEMS) identified four major barriers to gene flow: the Mona Passage, Yucatán Channel, Florida Strait, and Colombia–Panama Gyre. Short-term heat stress phenotypes were measured for 196 samples from 98 Florida genets using CBASS (Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System) assays. Genome-wide association study, combining whole-genome and imputed microarray data, revealed highly polygenic thermal tolerance. These associations informed polygenic score estimation. A Caribbean-wide comparison indicated regional variation in heat tolerance potential. Survival data from the 2023 Florida mass bleaching event showed median polygenic scores shifted toward more thermotolerant phenotypes, consistent with directional selection. Our results demonstrate that genomic tools can resolve fine-scale population structure, identify barriers to gene flow, and quantify polygenic variation in thermal tolerance. The observed post-bleaching score shift suggests that polygenic scores have practical utility in coral conservation, breeding, and recovery programs under climate change.
17ee1eed: Mass coral bleaching induced by marine heatwaves are expected to increase in frequency and intensity with global warming, threatening coral reefs worldwide. Developing active approaches such as assisted acclimation to enhance the heat tolerance of key reef-building corals are becoming increasingly necessary. This study tested the effects of stress-hardening on enhancing thermal adaptability in four common corals (Pachyseris speciosa, Porites lutea, Goniastrea aspera, Pocillopora acuta) from Singapore’s turbid reefs. Corals of known thermotolerances were exposed to two daily oscillating (12:12 hours cycle) heat “priming” treatments that reach up to +1.5 ºC and +3.0 °C above the local maximum monthly mean (MMM) of 30.5 ºC for a period of ~3–6 weeks, achieving a cumulative heat stress (eDHW) of 3 °C-weeks. Acute heat stress assays were used to re-test coral thermotolerances following a recovery period of ~2 weeks post-priming. Preliminary results found species-specific responses where Pocillopora acuta, Porites lutea, and Goniastrea pectinata primed at MMM +1.5 ºC demonstrated higher ED50 values (temperatures at which 50% photosynthetic efficiency) compared to their naïve control counterparts. At MMM +3.0 ºC priming conditions, however, all species showed lower average ED50 values compared to naïve controls. These findings suggest potential for enhancing coral thermal adaptability through sub-lethal heat stress-hardening, at least in the short-term. Nevertheless, species-specific responses and heat “training” regimes (including determining lasting effects of treatments) require further investigations to truly ascertain the potential of such an assisted acclimation strategy to be a practical tool for enhancing coral resilience.
fc3db1eb: Ocean warming threatens coral reefs as marine heat waves increase in frequency and magnitude. Assisted evolution approaches, including selective breeding, may enhance coral thermal tolerance, yet the genomic basis of heat tolerance and how it can be inherited across generations remains poorly understood. Ningaloo Reef, a World Heritage Site in Western Australia (WA), is one of the world’s longest and most diverse fringing reef systems, hosting about half the coral species in the Indian Ocean. We conducted a selective breeding experiment using colonies from Acropora tenuis and Acropora millepora from two sites ~130 km apart, Oyster Stacks and Pelican Point, the former experiencing approximately 1°C higher mean sea surface temperature. Larvae from 40 families from all possible cross combinations were exposed to control (27°C) or elevated (35.5°C) temperatures, and survival was tracked. Survival under heat stress varied significantly with parental origin. In A. tenuis, larvae from warm reef parents (O×O) showed highest survival probability (58.7%), 1.7x greater than cool reef larvae (P×P: 25.3%). Critically, interpopulation crosses demonstrated intermediate but higher survival probability compared to cool reef families: O×P crosses had 48.1% survival probability (1.5× higher than P×P), while P×O crosses reached 34.6% survival probability (1.2× higher than P×P). Similar patterns were observed in A. millepora, confirming consistency across species. Narrow-sense heritability estimates for heat tolerance were higher at 35.5 °C relative to 27 °C, generating baseline information for the area. Preliminary genomic data resulting from whole genome sequencing of 45 A. tenuis larvae revealed population-level genomic divergence and genetic variants associated with thermal tolerance. These findings combining phenotypic, pedigree, and genomic marker data, represent the first demonstration that selective breeding can enhance coral heat tolerance within a single generation in Indian Ocean corals and highlight the potential of assisted gene flow as a tool for reef restoration.
b044aece: Coral reefs have been increasingly threatened by marine heatwaves, prompting the need for proactive interventions that enhance coral resilience. Assisted evolution, which accelerates the natural rates of adaptation, has emerged as a promising approach. However, programs of assisted evolution must outpace the escalating frequency and intensity of marine heatwaves. Here, we review advances in coral biology across cellular, organismal, and ecological scales that support the feasibility of assisted evolution in coral populations. We compare experimental gains via assisted evolution in thermal tolerance with projected temperatures, finding that, current rates of adaptation are unlikely to keep pace with predicted climate change. We identify key knowledge gaps that hinder timely development of assisted evolution and propose a comprehensive research agenda to address these gaps. To accelerate progress, we recommend three overarching catalysts: (1) large-scale, multi-institutional field hubs to increase experimental scope and statistical power; (2) support long-term research at these hubs, spanning coral generations; and (3) technologies that safeguard broodstock and experimental corals from disturbances. By implementing these proposals, scientists can realize the potential of assisted evolution and help safeguard a future for coral reef ecosystems.
0434c9cb: Enhanced resilience conferred through pre-exposure to sublethal stress (i.e., priming) may be an important mechanism for reef building corals to cope with variable environments. The effect of stress priming on Acropora cervicornis thermotolerance was evaluated in the context of both elevated temperature and ammonium enrichment, 3 °C and/or 6 μM above ambient, respectively. Primed corals were pre-exposed to each stressor individually or in combination for eight days, while the non-primed corals remained at ambient conditions. After an eight-day recovery period, primed corals and a subset of non-primed corals (naive) were subjected to an acute 15-hour thermal challenge. Coral metabolism, symbiosis, and gene expression were characterized throughout the experiment. Thermal tolerance was quantified as the retention of symbionts, chlorophyll, and live tissue, along with the probability of survival through 3 days after acute heat stress. Corals in each priming treatment were more likely to retain symbionts and chlorophyll following heat stress and the rate of tissue loss was reduced in all priming treatments relative to the naive corals. Moreover, the risk of experiencing tissue loss or predicted mortality was significantly lower in corals pre-exposed to elevated temperature, with or without ammonium enrichment. Analysis of differential gene expression and functional enrichment revealed that regulation of the apoptotic process differed between primed and naive corals following the initial priming exposure and again at the onset of the secondary heat stress. While limited differential gene expression persisted between recurrent stress exposures, gene expression dynamics throughout the thermal challenge suggested that primed corals may exhibit higher transcriptional resilience which is likely linked with the lessened heat-induced physiological stress observed in this study. Altogether, these results provide support for the capacity of A. cervicornis to gain resilience through pre-exposure to ecologically relevant conditions as well as insights into the molecular mechanisms underpinning this process.
15f0a906:
For Pacific Island Nations, the ocean is home, heritage, and a lifeline. Coral reef ecosystems lie at the heart of this relationship, providing food security, livelihoods, cultural identity, and coastal protection. Yet, these ecosystems and the communities that depend on them face escalating threats from climate change, extreme weather events, and unsustainable land- and sea-based activities. Addressing these challenges requires approaches that meaningfully integrate scientific methods with Traditional Ecological Knowledge (TEK). When brought together, these knowledge systems strengthen community-based management and ensure practices are sustained across generations.
This study presents case studies from the i-Qoliqoli (customary fishing grounds) of Nakorotubu District (Ra Province) and Dama District (Bua Province), Fiji, where science and TEK have been blended to support coral reef stewardship. Historical datasets and long-standing local observations provide valuable long-term trends for understanding ecological change. Between 2024–2025, we surveyed 21 sites in Nakorotubu and 16 sites in Dama using fisheries monitoring and benthic habitat assessments. TEK identified spawning seasons and traditional closure sites, which were integrated with scientific monitoring. Preliminary results show coral cover (30–45%) and fish biomass (2000–4000 kg/ha) were significantly higher in tabu areas and reef passages compared to fished areas. Effectiveness of tabu areas was influenced by reef proximity to land, exposure to currents, and connectivity.
These findings demonstrate that co-produced approaches, supported by temporal datasets, can measurably enhance ecological outcomes, sustain cultural practices, and strengthen local capacity to adapt to climate change. By weaving science and TEK, communities in Fiji are building resilient reef management systems that safeguard both biodiversity and cultural heritage.
45131b32: Coral reefs are bio-geological structures that provide the calcium carbonate framework essential for biodiversity and shoreline protection. Globally, coral reefs are experiencing shifts in benthic community composition, including transitions from primary framework builders (scleractinian corals) to secondary calcifiers (primarily calcifying red algae). Considering this reorganization of reef-building taxa, it is important to understand how this functional shift alters the balance between reef accretion and erosion. To this end, this study investigates the relative contributions of the ecosystem's main calcifiers, scleractinian corals and calcifying red algae, to net carbonate production. Structure-from-motion photogrammetry was used to characterize reef topology and benthic community structure around Guam. Structural complexity was derived from digital elevation models, while a semi-automated segmentation and classification pipeline mapped calcifiers within high-resolution orthomosaics. To refine local carbonate-production estimates, we measured in situ growth rates and skeletal densities of common calcifying taxa in Guam’s waters. These empirical data were incorporated into the Indo-Pacific ReefBudget database and applied to the annotated orthomosaic segments to quantify carbonate production at taxon, functional-group, and community scales. This study underscores the importance of accurate, taxon-specific data on calcifying red algae, whose contributions to reef calcification are often generalized or underrepresented in existing models. As coral reef community composition continues to shift globally, resolving differences in calcification rates between these functional groups is essential for predicting the geomorphic trajectory and ecosystem services of future reefs.
b1bbfe2c: Coral reefs are among the most biodiverse and threatened ecosystems on Earth, with Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) promoted as key tools to safeguard their resilience. This research evaluates the effectiveness of Wadi El Gemal National Park (WGNP), a Category II MPA on Egypt’s southern Red Sea coast, in protecting coral reef ecosystems. A mixed-methods approach was employed, integrating in-situ reef surveys, abiotic water quality measurements, and stakeholder questionnaires. Fieldwork was conducted in July 2025 across five reef sites (three onshore, two offshore) and compared with baseline data collected in 2015/16. Using belt transects, coral demographics, benthic cover, juvenile recruitment, and associated fauna were assessed. Abiotic indicators (temperature, salinity, dissolved oxygen, and pH) were measured to evaluate local stress conditions. Results revealed significant reef degradation over the past decade. Dead coral now dominates benthic cover, while bleaching and mortality rates have increased markedly since 2015/16. Offshore reefs showed higher dissolved oxygen and slightly better resilience compared to onshore sites, though both exhibited substantial declines in live coral cover. Signs of recovery were evident in certain locations, with higher juvenile recruitment and herbivorous fish abundance suggesting potential for regeneration if stressors are effectively managed. However, local pressures—including poor water quality, inadequate sewage infrastructure, and tourism-related impacts—continue to undermine reef health. Stakeholder perspectives echoed these findings, with dive centre staff and fishermen reporting declining coral health and reduced fish diversity. The study concludes that while WGNP provides a framework for conservation, current management practices are insufficient to halt coral degradation. Strengthened enforcement of zoning regulations, improved waste management, and stricter control of tourism activities are urgently needed to support coral reef resilience in the park.
793fd5a2: Palytoxin is one of the most potent non-protein marine toxins known, posing significant risks to human health. Despite its danger, the sale of palytoxin-producing corals (zoanthids) is completely unregulated. Additionally, the biosynthetic origin of palytoxin remains unresolved—whether it is produced by the coral host, a dinoflagellate symbiont, or the holobiont. This study investigated correlations between toxin production and host-symbiont genomics from aquarium-sourced zoanthids, hypothesizing that the symbiotic dinoflagellate from the Cladocopium genus has a primary role in palytoxin synthesis. Zoanthid colonies (n35) were acquired from commercial aquarium stores and residential aquariums, and polyps were sampled and screened for palytoxin using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LCMS). DNA was extracted from remaining tissue for Sanger sequencing targeting the ITS and 16S regions to identify the symbiont and host respectively. LCMS methods detected palytoxin in 49% of all zoanthid samples, and all but one of the toxin-positive colonies fell within or above the range associated with human poisoning cases (400–7,000 μg/g tissue). Genomic analysis revealed that palytoxin is detected in multiple zoanthid clades, all harboring Cladocopium symbionts. Correlation of palytoxin concentration with Cladocopium taxonomy revealed a strain-specificity, providing further evidence for Cladocopium’s role in palytoxin production. These findings confirm that aquarium-traded zoanthids can harbor dangerously high levels of palytoxin. Understanding the symbiont’s role in palytoxin synthesis can inform the aquarium industry, which currently has no regulation in place for selling these harmful organisms to aquarium hobbyists. Gaining insight on the symbiont’s role in toxin synthesis will also further toxin-producing dinoflagellate research, which is an understudied field despite dinoflagellates’ large presence in aquatic ecosystems.
a1fbabe3: Carbonate sediment production worldwide occurs through distinct ecological and environmental systems classified as carbonate factories. Current global classifications of these factories, however, derive predominantly from clear-water environments. Despite being a global hotspot of coral reef biodiversity, quantitative assessments of modern carbonate sedimentology in tropical Asia remain underrepresented within these frameworks. This is particularly problematic given that many reefs in the region exist under turbid or marginal conditions that are distinct from the clear-water systems upon which global models are based. Here we present the physical grain properties and constituent composition of benthic coral reef sediment (n =150) collected from >20 sites across five regions in tropical Asia to evaluate whether turbid reefs represent a functionally distinct carbonate factory subtype or are degraded versions of existing tropical systems. Living benthic reef communities were assessed using ecological survey methods, and environments were determined through measured environmental parameters on reefs. Our findings show strong compositional divergence of coral reefs in tropical Asia from existing tropical factory models. Rather than displaying impaired carbonate sediment production, reefs exhibit high-diversity producer assemblages in which large benthic foraminifera (e.g., Calcarina spp.), mollusks, and calcareous red algae are important contributors to detrital sediments on reefs. On turbid reefs, where sediment production by corals and calcareous green algae is reduced, carbonate producers that typically provide a minor contribution to local sediment production, increase their relative dominance within the sediment pool. Our results demonstrate that turbid reefs warrant recognition as distinct carbonate factory subtypes, with implications for paleoenvironmental reconstructions and predictions of future sediment production trajectories under climate change.
72d9735c: Increasing anthropogenic emissions of greenhouse gases have led to ocean acidification, a major threat to marine ecosystems. Coral reefs are particularly vulnerable because reduced pH diminishes coral calcification. Ocean alkalinity enhancement (OAE) has been proposed as a mitigation strategy, and several studies have shown that elevating seawater alkalinity can improve coral calcification. However, its broader physiological and molecular consequences for the coral holobiont remain insufficiently understood. Here, we examined the physiological responses, including enzyme activities associated with energy metabolism and skeletal formation, and transcriptomic profiles of the branching coral Acropora hyacinthus and its symbiotic partners, Symbiodiniaceae and prokaryotes, under four acidity–alkalinity scenarios: acidified (CO₂ enrichment), alkaline (NaOH addition), neutralized (CO₂-enriched seawater adjusted to ambient pH using NaOH), and natural seawater. Corals exposed to manipulated acidity–alkalinity conditions showed marked physiological alterations relative to the control. Notably, enzyme activities linked to calcification-related and metabolic pathways were consistently elevated under alkaline and neutralized treatments, suggesting increased metabolic investment in skeletal formation processes. However, transcriptomic analyses revealed impaired coral developmental pathways across all manipulated treatments, indicating broader stress responses beyond enzyme-level adjustments. Compared with the acidified treatment, the neutralized condition restored certain Symbiodiniaceae functions but further hindered coral development. Spearman correlation analysis of Symbiodiniaceae transcripts associated with symbiosis indicated significantly reduced transport functions under neutralized conditions, suggesting a shift of coral-algal symbiosis from mutualism toward parasitism. GLMMLasso modeling also revealed a trade-off between coral development and calcification: enhanced calcification was supported primarily by Symbiodiniaceae photosynthesis, while prokaryotic transport functions further promoted calcification. Overall, our findings show that acidity–alkalinity manipulation induces complex physiological and molecular reorganizations within the coral holobiont. While OAE may stimulate pathways associated with skeletal formation, it simultaneously disrupts coral development and symbiosis, underscoring the need for caution when evaluating OAE as a mitigation strategy.
732ea7c5: Coral skeletal growth is one of the most commonly reported metrics to assess coral stress responses. Skeletal growth in these colonial organisms arises from the collective behavior of interconnected modules (i.e. polyps) dividing and depositing calcium carbonate. However, the mechanisms driving coordinated growth across modules remain poorly understood and has hindered our ability to predict their responses across environmental gradients. Coral colonies extend vertically when multiple modules form skeletal features called dissepiments. While dissepiment formation is regulated by a moonlight cue, it is unknown whether each module responds independently or if a stimulated module can transmit signals to neighboring, unstimulated modules. To test this, coral fragments were subjected to two different night-time conditions: a control group kept in darkness, and a treatment group where only a single polyp per fragment was exposed to a moonlight cycle, leaving adjacent polyps unexposed. Both groups had equal diurnal light regimes that assured maximum calcification rates. Skeletal growth was monitored over eight months using monthly in vivo micro-computerized tomography to track dissepiment formation and vertical extension. Skeletal analyses of the treatment group revealed well-defined monthly formed dissepiments in the illuminated polyps. This pattern was also observed in up to four adjacent, unstimulated polyps, extending up to 11 ± 6.7 mm from the illuminated polyp. In contrast, control fragments kept in darkness did not form new dissepiments and showed significantly less vertical extension. We confirmed that the mechanism by which inter-polyp communication coordinates skeletal growth is by propagating signals throughout areas of the colony. Light as a source of information is sensed and propagated to synchronize a collective behavior for colony growth that further determines colony morphology in scleractinian corals. These findings provide a mechanistic framework for the accurate (re)interpretation of coral skeletal archives.
8d38320d: Bleaching due to marine heatwaves is considered a major threat in the population of zooxanthellate reef organisms such as corals and giant clams. In corals, shifting or shuffling from less to a more heat tolerant zooxanthella types can improve their thermal resilience. However, such mechanism is not well understood in giant clams. Here we examined the zooxanthellae symbiont composition in the giant clam Tridacna crocea before, during, and after bleaching in laboratory conditions. The T. crocea was either dominated by Symbiodinium or Cladocopium regardless of sampling timepoints. Moreover, small variations in relative abundance and composition of ITS2 sequences across timepoints were observed, even in control clams. Although all sampled giant clams were completely bleached or recovered, the short duration of the experiment may not be sufficient to allow apparent shuffling or switching of zooxanthellae in giant clams. Nevertheless, the experiment was conducted in the laboratory setting and access to new zooxanthellae types maybe limited. This study advances our knowledge on how giant clams and their zooxanthellae may respond to heat stress disturbances.
1fec86b8: Reef-building corals are increasingly threatened by disease outbreaks, driven by increasing impacts of climate change combined with anthropogenic pressure, highlighting the need to better understand coral immune responses. Corals rely on innate immunity, a non-specific defense mechanism that involves mucus barriers, pathogen recognition receptors, phagocytosis, melanin production, antimicrobial peptide production, and other defense mechanisms. Recent research suggests that coral immunity is not only modulated by genetic markers but also by environmental and ecological factors, as well as the composition and density of their symbiotic algae. The goal of this study was to evaluate the natural variation in coral immune responses between two species of reef-building corals across a gradient of environmental conditions in inshore, midshore, and offshore reefs in the Middle Florida Keys, and to determine how the symbiont community composition and density influence these responses. We collected coral tissue samples from 2-5 colonies from each of two species (Orbicella faveolata and Siderastrea siderea) from eight reef sites in June 2024 and conducted transcriptome analyses (RNA-seq) and symbiont quantification. Preliminary results show clear species-specific patterns in symbiont density and community structure: Orbicella faveolata presents relatively stable symbiont densities and largely uniform symbiont community composition across reef types, while Siderastrea siderea displays more variable symbiont proportions that shift across the inshore-offshore gradient. Additionally, initial RNA-seq analyses suggest a subset of genes that may be differentially expressed among reef types, potentially indicating an environmental modulation of immune-related pathways. These findings will improve our understanding of how environmental conditions influence coral immune mechanisms and disease susceptibility across reefs, providing information that can support reef management and conservation efforts.
b0b7a14b: Corals are susceptible to sublethal predation, which results in a range of tissue and skeletal damage patterns. Although they possess extremely high regenerative abilities to overcome this damage, it is not well understood why some colonies can withstand and recover from predation, while others exhibit partial mortality. In this study, we experimentally wounded juvenile colonies from three common genera in the Indo Pacific, Acropora, Pocillopora, and Porites, using two standardized methods that mimic natural corallivory: scraping (tissue and skeletal damage) and deep tissue removal (near total tissue removal with no skeletal injury). We characterized the morphological changes between wound methods, observed native RFP fluorescence at wound margins, and used histological approaches to understand the role of algal colonization in regeneration failure. Over four weeks, all scraped wounds regenerated whereas deeper tissue removal resulted in algal colonization and regenerative failure. Healing performance also varied across genera. Pocillopora fragments regenerated more slowly and frequently retained open lesions, while Acropora and Porites often achieved full closure. To test whether skeletal damage facilitated regeneration, we crossed tissue removal with skeletal damage in the perforate Porites. Regardless of skeletal damage, deep tissue removal enabled algal colonization in the wound, which delayed or prevented complete regeneration. Although tissue regenerated at the margins in both scraped and deep wounds, algal competition significantly delayed lateral regeneration in deep tissue wounds. Additionally, RFP signaling in regenerating tissue was transient in scraped wounds (4-5 days), whereas signaling persisted along algae-colonized wound beds for over four weeks. Chronic signaling seen only in the tissue adjacent to algae suggests that algae disrupts the normal transition from inflammation to proliferation and remodeling. The results of this study provide a mechanistic understanding of wound healing under two corallivory pressures, and clarify the impact of natural predation on colony regeneration and survival.
8930af80: Coral reefs are complex ecosystems that promote biodiversity, provide shoreline protection and are a cultural foundation in Hawaiʻi, where the coral polyp is respected as an akua (deity). Recent work highlights the important role that cryptic meiofauna serve in promoting coral growth through species interactions (e.g., sediment removal by the common guard crab Trapezia intermedia). However, the full understanding of cryptic meiofauna (e.g.,  brittle stars) effects remains understudied. The objective of this project is to quantify the effects of both direct and indirect interactions between  corals and common coral-associated invertebrates to assess how known interactions modulate coral growth rates. Corals (Pocillopora meandrina and Porites lobata), and invertebrates (Trapezia intermedia and Ophicoma erianceus) were collected from Auwili, Hawaiʻi and incubated in a fully orthogonal experiment with meiofaunal species (T. intermedia and O. erinaceus), association (direct and indirect interaction) as fixed factors. After a 7 week incubation, modulation of calcification was assessed. Guard crabs are expected to have a stronger impact on branching corals and brittle stars on mounding corals, with direct interaction. Similarly, with indirect interaction, guard crabs are expected to have stronger impacts on branching corals and brittles on mounding corals, though in a smaller magnitude. By understanding the intricacies of species interactions on coral reefs, a better framework for adequate conservation efforts can be enacted to protect coral reefs.
509db5ce: The physiological response in the symbiotic dinoflagellates of the Symbiodiniaceae family is essential for the maintenance of scleractinian corals in the face of environmental variation. Therefore, the characterization of physiological indicators such as chlorophyll a concentration and symbiont density are key markers for assessing the overall coral health and resilience. In the Tropical Mexican Pacific, the genus Pocillopora are the most representative reef-building corals. In addition, the environmental heterogeneity to which they are exposed can elicit different physiological response. This study compared the chlorophyll a concentration and symbiont density of P.cf. meandrina and P.cf. capitata in two contrasting environments within Bahía de la Paz in the year 2023. Six colonies per morphospecies were sampled, and chlorophyll concentration was determined using the methods described elsewhere, while symbiont density was obtained by counting cells with a hemocytometer. Chlorophyll data were analyzed using two-way ANOVA, while symbiont density data were analyzed using a Kruskal-Wallis test. The results indicate significant differences in chlorophyll a concentration between morphospecies (F = 5.55, df =1, p = 0.03), with higher concentrations of chlorophyll in P.cf. capitata (µ =17.54  ± 12.35 µg cm-²) compared to P.cf. meandrina (µ = 9.43  ± 4.39 µg cm-2). Meanwhile, symbiont density showed significant differences between sites (X²= 6, df = 1, p = 0.01), with higher Chl a in Pocillopora corals at Punta Galeras (µ = 2.48 x 108 ± 2.35 x 108 cells·cm-²) compared to Isla Gaviota (µ = 6.68 x 107 ± 4.89 x 107 cells·cm-²) . These results indicate specific acclimatization responses in the Pocillopora genus according to the morphospecies. It is expected that the analysis of other physiological indicators, such as total protein and/or lipid concentration, will allow the identification of specific pathways of acclimatization response to different local environments and by morphospecies in Bahía de La Paz.
7576317a: The Banda Islands are located in the center of the biodiversity hotspot known as the coral triangle, designated as one of Indonesia's priority marine conservation areas. The Banda Islands Marine Protected Areas Network (BIMPAN) was initiated in 2017 to deal with threats to coral reefs, such as overfishing and destructive fishing practices that lead to a decrease in biodiversity and ecosystem resilience. Within the BIMPAN, the Banda Sea National Marine Protected Area (MPA) was designated in 2014, the Locally Marine Managed Area of Hatta Island in 2014, and Ay-Rhun Islands MPA in 2016. This study aims to investigate the efficacy of BIMPAN and the associated capacity building objectives by measuring changes in live hard coral cover (HC) over ten years. A point intercept transect (PIT) survey protocol was done every 1-3 years from 2012 to 2025 to measure HC at 21 sites within BIMPAN. The HC in 2012 ranged 5.3 - 93.0%, with an average of 54.1%, and in 2025 ranged from 28.5 - 86.7% with an average of 54.7%, and increased 0.6% but not statistically significant. Whilst there is currently a global trend of decreasing live hard coral cover, the relatively stable HC cover in the Banda Islands over a decade indicates the success of conservation efforts being carried out. However, the growing threat from global disturbances such as the increasing frequency of ocean warming events and tropical storms mean the consistent implementation of MPA regulations coupled with increased local community involvement and human resource capacity building are critical for maintaining coral reefs and the success of MPA management in Indonesia.
d2769a85: Coral reefs in the Caribbean, among the world’s most biodiverse ecosystems, are increasingly threatened by climate change, but also by coastal development, and pollution. Untargeted environmental metabolomics offers a powerful way to track anthropogenic impact, besides natural processes, by providing molecular fingerprints of dissolved organic matter (DOM). Metabolites are the resultant of reef exudates, microbial processes, and terrestrial inputs, capturing subtle chemical signals often overlooked in traditional monitoring. We applied untargeted metabolomics for comparative characterization of DOM in nearshore waters of Bonaire, Curaçao, Saba, and St. Eustatius. This high-resolution analyses revealed a pronounced regional divide in the overall DOM composition, abundances and chemical class distribution of northern–southern islands, also reflected in N:C ratios, Gibbs free energy, and DOC concentrations. Northern islands consistently exhibit higher N:C ratios and lower nominal carbon oxidation states, suggesting a biogeochemical regime enriched in nitrogenous and energy rich DOM. Chemical characterization showed that northern islands contained substantially higher proportions of organic nitrogen and organosulfur compounds, while the southern islands were enriched in lipids and lipid-like molecules. More specifically, anthropogenic pollutants (i.e., xenobiotics) accounted for 12% to 34% of the DOM fraction, with higher contributions in the northern islands (St. Eustatius 28%, Saba 25%) than in the southern islands (Curaçao 22%, Bonaire 17%). The xenobiotics categories human metabolites, food-contact chemicals, and plastic-additives were particularly elevated in the northern islands. These xenobiotics introduce anthropogenic molecular features to the DOM pool and may alter microbial and chemical processes, adding a layer of human-driven complexity with potential ecotoxicological effects on the ecosystem. We will relate our metabolomics findings with ecotoxicological data to explore this potential.
8fd2f753:
Coral tissue loss conditions and growth anomalies are increasingly reported in coral reefs worldwide, yet their underlying causes, cellular dynamics, and ecological consequences remain poorly understood. To address these knowledge gaps, we coupled gross and microscopic examinations of lesions from both tropical and temperate reef-building and anthozoans, including Indo-Pacific scleractinians and Mediterranean gorgonians. Across systems, lesions manifested as distinct expressions of growth anomalies developed in tissue or skeleton, acute tissue-loss lesion, and ciliate-associated pathologies, especially Brown band disease (Brb). Despite wide variation in their gross appearance, histopathology consistently revealed alterations of the basal body wall, depletion or redistribution of symbionts, inflammatory responses, and taxon-specific features such as mucocyte hyperplasia or epidermal thinning. In tissue-loss conditions, microscopic analyses highlighted divergent etiologies: some lesions reflected multifactorial stress responses, while others were driven by primary invasive agents, such as scuticociliates infiltrating intact tissues ahead of tissue loss margin in brown band disease. Opportunistic endolithic organisms were frequently present within lesions but showed unclear patterns of tissue invasion, underscoring their uncertain role in disease progression.
Together, these findings illustrate the value of comparative histopathology for disentangling the diverse mechanisms underlying coral tissue degradation, from chronic proliferative disorders to rapidly advancing pathogen-driven diseases. By identifying conserved and taxon-specific lesion signatures across biogeographic regions, this work highlights how microscopic diagnostics can refine coral health assessments, inform disease surveillance, and support the development of early-warning indicators within resilience-based reef management frameworks.
8cd3fd08:
Author has specifically requested this abstract be considered for a poster presentation ONLY.

Global warming is accelerating the tropicalization of high-latitude coastal ecosystems, and Jeju Island, South Korea, located in the temperate–tropical climatic transition zone, has become a hotspot of rapid coral community expansion. Despite this increase, mass coral bleaching events occurred in both 2016 and 2024, showing strong depth-dependent variation. To understand species-specific thermal responses and depth-related bleaching patterns, this study (1) experimentally evaluated the thermal tolerance of three dominant hard corals in Jeju—Alveopora japonica, Montipora millepora, and Psammocora albopicta—across 18–33 °C, and (2) examined depth-specific community structure and bleaching severity during the 2024 extreme thermal event. Thermal experiments revealed clear interspecific differences: heat stress induced bleaching in A. japonica at 28 °C and in M. millepora at 30 °C, whereas P. albopicta exhibited tissue necrosis at 31 °C without typical bleaching. Field surveys during the 2024 heatwave further demonstrated a pronounced depth gradient. Shallow habitats (5–10 m) experienced severe bleaching and high mortality under temperatures exceeding 29 °C, while deeper habitats (15–20 m) remained thermally stable (near 22 °C) and functioned as effective refugia. In shallow areas, A. japonica and M. millepora were almost completely bleached, yet both species remained largely healthy at 20 m. P. albopicta showed consistently high thermal tolerance across all depths. These results highlight strong interspecific variation in thermal tolerance and demonstrate that deeper habitats (15–20 m) can serve as critical thermal refugia during extreme heat events. As ocean warming intensifies, deeper environments are likely to become increasingly important as long-term refugia and future settlement areas for tropical coral species. Our findings suggest that coral expansion in Jeju waters is proceeding not only poleward but also downward into deeper reef zones.
a4c4dd3c:
Abstract
Pocilloporid corals host diverse animal associates, but knowledge is uneven across size classes and regions. Here we present the current status of a developing literature-based database that is being continuously expanded, with a particular focus on animals living in association with the living tissue of coral colonies. We synthesize available occurrence records to compare macrofaunal assemblages with meiofaunal symbioses (mostly copepods). Macrofauna dominates the evidence base: 2,167 occurrences from 83 sources document 946 species (291 families, 47 orders, 10 phyla) associated with 7 of 60 known Pocilloporidae species. Notably, 537 associations were reported only once. Most records come from living colonies, while 71 occurrences (67 species) were reported from dead coral substrates. Localities span 7/12 ocean ecoregions and are strongly concentrated in the Indo-Pacific, whereas the Atlantic remains poorly documented. Decapoda account for 49% of occurrences, and Pocillopora hosts the most diverse assemblage. In contrast, meiofaunal copepods are far less documented: 29 papers report 273 occurrences of copepods (Cyclopoida, Harpacticoida, Siphonostomatoida) on Pocilloporidae. Records are geographically biased, indicating substantial hidden diversity. Overall, the compiled evidence still covers only a limited fraction of potential pocilloporid host diversity, highlighting priorities for standardized reporting, broader geographic sampling, and integrative taxonomy for tissue-associated symbionts on living corals.
9826d99a:
Mass outbreaks of crown-of-thorns starfish (CoTS) are a major driver of coral reef decline. Consequently, the control efforts have been implemented across coral reef ecosystems worldwide; however, these efforts face significant challenges, including the hazards associated with handling toxic starfish and high operational costs. Understanding the annual behavioral patterns of CoTS and developing control strategies grounded in their behavioral ecology are therefore essential for effective coral reef conservation.
Conventional approaches such as diving and snorkeling are limited for long-term monitoring of the predominantly nocturnal behavior of CoTS. To complement these methods, we conducted a multi-month behavioral study using a biologging approach, in which small data loggers are attached to starfish to directly record their in situ behavior.
First, we evaluated attachment techniques capable of maintaining logger functionality for more than 24 hours under indoor aquarium conditions. The most effective method to date involved wrapping the logger in felt and sewing it directly onto an arm using 6-gauge nylon thread. Even with the logger attached, CoTS continued to move and feed, indicating that the device had minimal impact on their natural behavior.
Comparisons between three-axis accelerometer data from both indoor and outdoor deployments and synchronized behavioral observations showed that periods of large acceleration fluctuations corresponded to active movement. Conversely, intervals with low variability and linear acceleration patterns largely matched periods when the starfish were stationary. These results demonstrate that, despite challenges such as limited attachment duration and injuries following logger detachment, the biologging method can successfully capture nocturnal behaviors that are otherwise difficult to observe.
Moving forward, we plan to investigate seasonal and environmental drivers of CoTS behavior by integrating environmental data recorded by the loggers (e.g., water temperature) with visual observations of home-range patterns and predation rates.
2f62311f:
Sacoglossa sea slugs are particularly renowned for their ability to incorporate functional 
chloroplasts from their algal diet into their tissues, a process known as kleptoplasty. 
Considerable research has focused on the physiological and molecular mechanisms of the 
slugs and the alien organelles within the slugs that underpin kleptoplasty. However, other 
factors potentially relevant to establishing kleptoplasty, such as the composition of the 
microbiome and whether the microbiome is food-dependent, remain poorly understood.   
To shed light on the origin of the microbiome in Sacoglossa, we analyzed Elysia viridis – a 
polyphagous and well-established model in the research of kleptoplasty. We sampled natural 
populations and also conducted a 21-day food switch experiment to understand the influence 
of algal diet on the associated microbial community of the slug. Microbial communities were 
then analyzed via DNA metabarcoding of the bacterial 16S V1V2 region to assess taxonomic 
diversity and community structure. Simultaneously, we determined the origin and abundance 
of chloroplasts in the slugs using a novel approach based on DNA metabarcoding. 
Our results demonstrate that the microbiome of E. viridis varies depending on the collection 
site, but is not altered by our food switch experiment despite a complete exchange of 
kleptoplasts after 14 days. The latter further underlines the ability of E. viridis to adapt to 
different food sources and provides exclusive insights into the dynamics of a food switch. 
Although the results regarding the microbiome were not as explicit, Gammaproteobacteria, 
Actinobacteria, and Bacteroidia were identified as the most abundant classes in all samples. 
However, no time-spot dependent pattern could be observed.
955b25a8: Increasing anthropogenic disturbances at both local and global scales, are drivers of hard-coral decline, resulting in reduced ecosystem functions. Reproduction plays an integral part in the persistence of coral communities. The timing of gamete release influences the dispersal of coral larvae thereby affecting the local distribution and abundance of species. Thus, the timing of gamete release of coral species plays a significant role in developing management interventions to minimize population decline and enhance coral recruits’ recovery and minimize population decline. Here we present discovery of a new spawning record for Acropora cf. tenuis in southern Philippines. A total of 82 different coral colonies were tagged and sampled on 4 September 2025 at Davao Gulf Divers Marine Park, Mabini, Davao de Oro, Mindanao, southern Philippines.  Eighteen percent of the sampled colonies were Acropora cf. tenuis, and of those, 40% contained pigmented gametes. Dusk and night observations were carried out from 5 September 2025 starting from 17:00 hrs to 19:30 hrs to determine the time of spawning. Gamete release was observed on 12 September 2025 on the 4th night after the full moon, between 17:54 hr to 18:26 hr after sunset (17:39 hr), during a rising tide. No other coral species were observed to spawn. These observations provide new insights on the reproductive timing of A. cf. tenuis in the Philippines, and contrasts with previous records of spawning for this species in northern Luzon that occurred after April full moons. It also adds to the limited body of knowledge on the timing of spawning for coral species in southern Philippines and equatorial reef areas. This information can be used to collect and rear larvae to restore degraded reefs in southern Philippines.
c1ebd807:
American Samoa is a remote Pacific Island nation where substantial mitigation methods are needed to protect coral reefs from global warming, human impacts, and frequent mass bleaching events. Mass coral bleaching events have begun to become more frequent, occurring in the Samoan archipelago most recently between 2023 and 2024. These events are increasing in frequency and severity, an alarming sign of the acceleration of climate change. Scientists warn that sustained warming could result in the loss of up to 90% of corals globally. The international community, therefore, faces an urgent challenge: to take decisive measures to combat climate change and its effects, such as mass bleaching, to protect these vital ecosystems.
It is expected that mass coral bleaching events will continue to become more frequent and severe, having more regular impacts on coral cover in the coming years. To address this, the American Samoa Coral Reef Advisory Group (CRAG) has developed action plans to respond to bleaching events and mitigate their effects. These plans are modified according to the characteristics of each event and involve being attentive to bleaching alerts, recording water temperatures both regionally and locally, and regularly monitoring the territory’s coral ecosystem. This allows us to determine the progression of the event and establish measures to counteract its effects. Among these measures is the development of a coral restoration program that involves targeting specific sites and finding thermotolerant corals to address mass bleaching events. These restoration programs are in the early stages of development, so sharing information from the monitoring programs in American Samoa, particularly data on the recent bleaching event, will provide important insights and foster collaborative learning. This information exchange will help develop better strategies for coral reef conservation, benefiting American Samoa's high marine biodiversity and its communities that heavily rely on marine resources for subsistence.
41ca20c5: The consequences of coral bleaching extend beyond individual mortality, compromising reef stability. Reproductive processes can also be affected by severe stress events, altering population dynamics. In the Southwestern Atlantic, information on reproductive responses to climate stressors remains scarce, even for functionally important species such as the gonochoric hydrocoral Millepora alcicornis, which suffered nearly 50% mortality during the recent mass bleaching in the region. A widespread bleaching event associated with the 2023–24 El Niño Southern Oscillation affected the Brazilian province, placing populations of this species at risk in several coastal areas. By monitoring bleached and non-bleached colonies (N = 8 each) and collecting fragments bimonthly over one year, this study evaluated the effects of the stress event on fire coral reproduction through comparisons of medusa production and the size of each medusa stage using histological techniques. Statistical analyses showed that medusa production varied among months and was consistently higher in bleached colonies in every month in which medusae were present. For medusa size, stages I and III did not differ between health conditions over time, whereas stage II medusa from bleached colonies were larger; however, stage II medusae were recorded in only two months, with very few individuals in one of them (N = 6). Unexpectedly, all bleached colonies sampled were female, while all non-bleached colonies were male. Thus, the set of bleached female colonies produced more medusae than the non-bleached male colonies sampled. However, it remains unclear which factor—bleaching condition or sex—had the stronger influence, as baseline medusa production for each sex in this species is still unknown. Combined with the general lack of size differences across medusa stages, the 2023–24 bleaching event does not appear to have substantially altered subsequent fire coral reproduction, although the results suggest a potential sex-dependent bleaching pattern in this species.
c5d5bb75: As mixotrophic organisms, scleractinian corals rely on autotrophy through their symbiosis with dinoflagellates, but they are also able to use heterotrophy to supplement their energetic and metabolic needs. In particular, the ability to upregulate feeding has been shown to aid coral survival during instances such as bleaching, low light, or ocean acidification. Nutritional flexibility across taxa, however, is not well understood, and it is unclear how nutritional dynamics within the symbiosis change under decreased water quality, an increasingly prevalent concern with the rise of urbanization in coastal regions. Here, three genera of corals (Acropora, Porites, and Platygyra) were transplanted to sites spanning a water quality gradient to assess coral trophic plasticity and acclimatization. After six months, transplants were collected and processed for bulk tissue δ13C and δ15N stable isotope values, and Stable Isotope Bayesian Ellipses in R (SIBER) was used to determine trophic strategy via separation of paired host and symbiont ellipses. Platygyra had the largest overall host and symbiont isotopic niche separation and, along with Porites, showed greater heterotrophy correlated with decreased water quality as a likely response to higher turbidity and light attenuation. Conversely, Acropora exhibited high autotrophic reliance that did not differ consistently across sites. Compound specific stable isotope analysis of amino acids (CSIA-AA) was performed on a subset of samples and further confirmed genus-specific capacities for trophic plasticity, as well as higher coral trophic positions at lower water quality sites. The results of this study emphasize the importance of nutritional flexibility in acclimatization to environmental stressors and provide a framework for understanding coral resilience in an urbanized marine environment.
6d08c8ad: Coral bleaching can be driven by high seawater temperatures and is becoming more frequent and extensive across the globe. There was a coral bleaching event in Kane‘ohe Bay, Oahu, Hawai‘i, in 2014 and 2015. During these bleaching events, paired Montipora capitata colonies (one that bleached adjacent to one that didn’t) were tagged and monitored in the field for the extent of their bleaching and recovery. Samples were collected at multiple time points, frozen in liquid nitrogen, and then analyzed using a multi -omic approach. Results from RNAseq and gene ontology analysis showed that genes that drive multiple cellular processes (metabolic pathways, DNA/RNA processing, cell signaling, ubiquitin, Lysosome, and vitamin regulation) had differential expression. These results highlight intraspecific variation in critical aspects of physiology that influence coral resilience to thermal stress.
677bcfe2: The regrowth of experimental wounding can serve as a colony health indicator known as the coral regeneration assay. Regeneration incurs tradeoffs in allocation of metabolic resources between competing hierarchical systems, including somatic growth, immune response, and reproduction. A multitude of intrinsic (morphology, age, live tissue area, species, genotype) and extrinsic (temperature, light, eutrophication, sedimentation) factors affect growth, therefore a coral's capacity to regenerate from wounding is an indicator of a colony’s health. Stressed corals often have depleted metabolic resource pools which increase disease susceptibility and hinders growth and reproduction. Understanding the drivers of regeneration and the impact of sampling (disease and predation correlations) are needed for this to be a useful indicator. This project analyzed annual regeneration rates of core lesions on eighty-seven Orbicella faveolata colonies across a latitudinal and anthropogenic stress gradient in Florida (1,717 cores). Most colonies were in a state of repair with 25% of biopsied tissue regrown after three years. Disease outcomes from the cores were low throughout the study despite high endemic SCTLD levels (~11% prevalence). Regeneration rates differed by latitude and were positively correlated with colony fecundity and anthropogenic stress. After one year, colonies north of Government Cut Miami regenerated nearly 50% more compared to those in Key Biscayne and the lower Florida Keys. Disease history, genetic lineage, and predation were not significant in predicting regeneration rate. Despite their proximity to anthropogenic stress, higher latitude corals in Florida appear healthier than conspecifics further south. These results suggest that higher latitude reefs provide a refuge from thermal stress and highlight the value of conserving these systems for the survival of corals in a rapidly warming climate. Hydrographic modeling is underway to determine the contributions of eutrophication from terrestrial-based nutrients.
5deec8d6: Coral recruits rely on establishing symbiosis during early development, yet this process is highly variable and influences juvenile fitness. To understand whether early-life symbiont provisioning can shape symbiont community assembly and improve juvenile survival in the field, we deployed settlement devices inoculated with cultured Cladocopium C1 or Durusdinium D1, alongside non-inoculated controls (NIL). Recruits were monitored after four and nine months, with Symbiodiniaceae communities characterised using SymPortal ITS2 profiling. Symbiont communities remained strongly structured by treatment at four months. Recruits in the C1 inoculation treatment hosted Cladocopium-dominated communities (~90%) and still hosted Cladocopium (~82%) after nine months, indicating that this inoculation remained stable through time in early stages. In contrast, Durusdinium types dominated both NIL (~89%) and D1-inoculated (~98%) recruits, suggesting that inoculated D1 were maintained and that recruits naturally favoured Durusdinium supplied by the local environment. From ITS2-type profiles, C1 and D1 types were the most representative types found in recruits. Survival differed significantly among treatments after four months, ranging from 56% in NIL recruits to 82% in D1 recruits and 93% in C1 recruits. These results support the importance of inducing early symbiosis within coral restoration pipelines and justify the use of cultured symbionts to enhance juvenile survival on reefs. Future work should investigate sources of variability, including device-specific microhabitat factors that may influence symbiont stability and recruit survival.
838e215a: Coral reefs are among the planet’s most valuable ecosystems, yet are under increasing threat from anthropogenically-driven climate change. Marine heat waves, ocean acidification, and disease have all caused major coral losses, but the response of coral reefs to these stressors is not globally uniform. As such, regional studies are vital for making predictions of future coral reef survival, but the costs associated with in-situ monitoring are prohibitive for the remote regions where many coral populations have so far survived. One such region is the Eastern Tropical Pacific (ETP), which remains highly understudied despite suggestions that its coral populations may be better adapted to near-term climate change because of the thermal extremes they experience. Here I present decades-long records characterizing the historical calcification response of individual coral colonies within the ETP to natural and anthropogenically-driven climate variability. Using high-resolution micro-CT scans of Porites coral cores from two remote sites separated by thousands of kilometers - Socorro Island (Mexico) and Isla del Coco (Costa Rica) - I developed the first calcification rate records for these locations. The two records, which span 1934-2020 and 1984-2021 respectively, show no major decline in calcification, lending credence to the hypothesis that the corals in this region are resilient in the face of climate change. Just as interesting, on decadal time scales both of these records show strong spatial correlations with regional-scale sea surface temperature (SST) variability, suggesting that coral growth rates at these sites are likely modulated by processes governed by Pacific Decadal Variability. Wavelet analysis of the Socorro Island record shows a marked shift towards decadally-modulated variability in 1976-77, coincident with well-documented changes in Pacific climate variability. Taken together, our observations imply that decadal climate processes remain the principal modulators of coral calcification in the Eastern Tropical Pacific, even in the face of warming SSTs.
da39dcb1: The Great Barrier Reef (GBR) and other Indo-Pacific regions are increasingly subject to major acute disturbances, such as population irruptions of crown-of-thorns starfish (CoTS) and heat-induced coral bleaching. Given their increasing incidence, acute disturbances are likely to occur simultaneously or successively, though effects of major acute disturbance interactions remain understudied. Here, changes in the feeding behavior of CoTS during an emerging population irruption are examined at Lizard Island (northern GBR) amid the GBR-wide mass coral bleaching event in 2024. Scooter-Assisted Large Area Diver-Based surveys were conducted to investigate changes in CoTS density and feeding, and coral cover changes were examined using point-intercept transects. Between 2023-2025, coral cover declined by 49%, most of which is attributable to the mass bleaching event. CoTS feeding rates significantly declined over the course of the study, with both number of coral colonies and combined tissue surface area consumed per starfish declining by over 40%. Despite reduced feeding, CoTS density increased by 96% from 2023-2025. Relative consumption of coral genera by CoTS was consistent during the study period, with Acropora spp. making up >80% of CoTS diet throughout the study. The 2024 bleaching event may have reduced feeding rates and the ecological impact of individual CoTS; however, the longer-term effects of CoTS are likely to exacerbate the coral loss caused by mass coral bleaching, especially with continued increases in CoTS densities.
b192b624: Crustose coralline algae (CCA) are a diverse group of red algae that can calcify a calcium-carbonate skeleton, which contributes to the construction of reef frameworks. Yet CCAs are often underrepresented in benthic surveys and carbonate budget models due to their cryptic morphology and identification challenges. To gather a better understanding of the variations in CCA calcification, we calculated calcification rates for eight species of CCA.  To do so, we measured the initial length, width, and height (cm) of seven CCA chips for eight morpho-species. The chips were photographed, and the planar surface area was measured using ImageJ after five weeks to calculate calcification rates. After the initial measurements, two species exhibited a rapid decline, characterized by pigment loss. These samples were later identified through molecular analysis as belonging to the genus Neogoniolithon, which is typically found in areas of higher light intensity. To test the effects of light availability on growth and photosynthesis, half of the chips were placed in a high-light environment (141 PAR), and the other half in a low-light environment (44 PAR). Photosynthetic efficiency, quantified as the maximum quantum yield (Fv/Fm), will be measured using pulse-amplitude modulation (PAM) fluorometry. As this is ongoing research, we hypothesize that specimens 4 and 8, belonging to the genus Neogoniolithon, will exhibit greater performance under high-light conditions, while specimen 10, belonging to the genus Harveylithon, is expected to perform better under low-light conditions. Overall growth and calcification for each tile will be remeasured at the end of the light trials. This research fills a knowledge gap regarding species-specific physiological responses to light availability and can help develop more accurate reef carbonate budget models.  Further research is encouraged into how CCAs can contribute to understanding reef frameworks, resilience, and potential changes in reef growth.
de92bc19: Intensified coral bleaching events, due to a warming ocean, are driving coral reefs close and/or above their upper thermal limits. While this phenomenon affects the global coral community, variations in bleaching resilience are observed across the coral taxa. In this study, integrated field surveys during the 2025 bleaching event and controlled thermal experiments were conducted to establish appropriate indicators of coral stress behavior at multiple biological scales. Field assessments included pre-, peak-, and post-bleaching benthic surveys, long term in situ temperature records and nutrient records in Belle Mare, east of Mauritius Island. Photosynthetic performance alongside chlorophyll content and zooxanthellae density of coral samples were measured. Field surveys revealed high genus-level bleaching percentages with the genera Acropora (80.0%) and Montipora (76.6%) most affected and suffering from mass mortality whilst other genera such as Pavona (36.7%), Porites (33.3%) and Fungia (26.7%) relatively less affected. Following thermal stress trials with Pavona, P. decussata demonstrated almost no bleaching (<3.0%) whilst P. cactus (73.0%), P. frondifera (50.0%), P. danai (55.0%) were significantly affected. P. decussata consistently exhibited high photosynthetic efficiency, Y(II) = > 0.5, under peak thermal stress unlike its congeners. During post bleaching field observation, Pavona species demonstrated full recovery and 100% survival. The Pavona genus was hence subjected to a controlled short term thermal exposure at 26oC, 30ºC and 34oC. After 3 hours of exposure to heat stress (34◦C), P. decussata, P. frondifera and P. danai showed no signs of bleaching, whilst P. cactus showed early pigment loss along with a decrease in photosynthetic efficiency, Y(II) = 0.431±0.02. P. cactus showed signs of recovery after 12 hours at 26oC. These findings on bleaching responses and resilience reveal the potential of Pavona becoming a dominant coral on future reefs and its use in reef rehabilitation efforts in the wake of a globally warming ocean.
c513b011: The depth refugia hypothesis proposes that corals find refuge from heat-stress induced coral bleaching at depth. In addition, geomorphologically complex reefs may provide additional micro-refugia. However, evidence for pervasive community-level depth and micro refugia has been equivocal, necessitating a nuanced exploration of local coral bleaching responses. We assess the effects of depth and morphological habitat heterogeneity on coral bleaching with Bayesian generalised mixed models. We include over 6000 coral colonies between two and eighteen meters of depth over a heat stress gradient from 1 to 6 degree heating weeks, and with varying site-level habitat heterogeneity. Our results demonstrate strong interactions in bleaching susceptibility along gradients of depth, heat stress, and habitat heterogeneity that were taxa-specific and varied with colony level bleaching severity. Community-level bleaching susceptibility decreased with depth, but severe bleaching of colonies (> 50 % of coral surface) increased with depth. The coral community as a whole was more susceptible to bleaching as cumulative heat stress exposure increased, but did not vary uniformly with large-scale habitat heterogeneity. Genus-level bleaching susceptibility mostly followed expectations. However, Acropora corals bleached less than expected overall, and in unexpected patterns along interacting depth and heat stress gradients. For example, severe bleaching of Acropora colonies increased with depth where cumulative heat stress was low, but was equal across depths where heat stress was highest. Overall, our results demonstrate that bleaching susceptibility varies substantially with multiple interacting biological and environmental factors, and that the influence of these can change with the level of bleaching assessed. Consequentially, depth effects on coral bleaching can be highly nuanced and care should be taken when investigating and reporting on the ‘depth refugia’ potential for coral bleaching and when interpreting community-level bleaching outcomes that are naïve to taxa and water depth.
70f8aeb6: Indonesian reefs are situated in the heart of the Coral Triangle, comprising approximately 16% of the world's total coral cover, or around 40,000 km² of reefs housing 76% of the world's coral species. With more than 17,000 islands, coral larval dispersal among Indonesia’s reefs could significantly enhance the resilience potential of these reefs through sink-and-source mechanisms. However, little is known about the genetic connectivity of coral in this region.  To fill this gap, we sampled table coral, Acropora hyacinthus, from seven provinces spanning the Indonesian archipelago (approximately 4,000 km; n = 360). We employed 2bRAD, a reduced-representation genotyping approach, to assess population structure and genetic diversity among sites using dimension reduction and model-based clustering methods. Symbiont types, composition, and diversity will also be identified using amplicon sequencing of the ITS2 region. Preliminary results from South Sulawesi samples yielded 1967 SNPs and revealed three clusters of A. hyacinthus with a few admixed individuals, potentially indicating the presence of cryptic lineages. However, preliminary genus-level characterization of their dinoflagellate endosymbiont communities indicated all are dominated by symbionts in the Cladocopium genus. A. hyacinthus is known for its cryptic lineages: A recent taxonomic study of A. hyacinthus from this region characterized A. nyinggulu sp. nov. as a novel species of table coral in South Sulawesi. However, this taxonomic study only covers one locality of South Sulawesi. Thus, our study aims to help disentangle subspecies from population structure within and across the archipelago. An understanding that would facilitate more informed decisions about managing and preserving coral genetic diversity in this megabiodiverse region.
b60fa9c7:
Abstract 
Climate change and ocean acidification are major environmental stressors affecting the physiological stability of marine invertebrates. The azooxanthellate coral Tubastraea aurea inhabits shallow sea water environments, where temperature and pH fluctuations are increasingly common, yet its biochemical and ultrastructural responses under combined stressors remain poorly understood. This study investigates the influence of controlled temperature regimes 21 C, 25 C, and 29 C and pH levels 8.1, 7.1, and 6.1 on metabolic, antioxidant, and cellular characteristics of T aurea. Coral fragments were maintained under experimental conditions representing present and projected future ocean scenarios. A suite of biochemical parameters including total protein, glucose, and antioxidant biomarkers such as superoxide dismutase SOD, catalase CAT, glutathione S transferase GST, glutathione peroxidase GPx, glutathione reductase GR, reduced glutathione GSH, and lipid peroxidation LPO were assessed to characterize physiological stress.
Ultrastructural observations were performed using scanning electron microscopy SEM and transmission electron microscopy TEM. SEM analyses provided information on skeletal surface features, tissue interfaces, and external morphology, while TEM enabled detailed visualization of intracellular structures including epithelial layers, mitochondrial organization, and cytoplasmic architecture.
Integrating biochemical and ultra structural analyses offers a comprehensive understanding of how T aurea responds to temperature and pH variations, highlighting the species physiological sensitivity to climate linked stressors. This study provides essential baseline data for predicting coral resilience, stress thresholds, and potential ecological outcomes under future ocean conditions.

Keywords:
Tubastraea aurea, climate change, ocean acidification, antioxidant biomarkers, biochemical stress, SEM, TEM, azooxanthellate
1560b88e: Warming sea surface temperature is one of the major factors driving coral mortality through the disruption of coral-algae symbiosis, ultimately leading to reef degradation. However, the reef-building octocoral, Heliopora coerulea, a brooding species commonly known as the blue coral, has been reported to grow optimally in warmer waters, dominating heavily perturbed reefs. Although the transcriptome of adult blue coral colonies is well-documented, gene expression profiles of early developmental stages are unknown. Here, we compared gene expression patterns of 0-day-old planulae and 3-week-old post-settled juveniles to the adult coral to further identify traits that might contribute to resilience. Initial analysis revealed that genes involved in environmental sensing and response were enriched in the planulae, immune response pathways that may putatively help establish symbiosis with algal endosymbionts were enriched in the juvenile stage, and biomineralization-related genes and pathways were highly expressed in adult colonies. Our findings provide insights into how the genes expressed at early developmental stages may play a crucial role in the survival of the blue coral in Anthropocene conditions.
88b5e1ba: Adaptation of reef-building corals to climate change is critical for the persistence of coral reef ecosystems. However, genetic adaptation operates intergenerationally, leaving corals unable to cope with sudden and dramatic environmental changes typical of the Anthropocene. Under these conditions, the coral microbiome (e.g., Symbiodiniaceae and bacteria) plays a critical role in helping the cnidarian host to physiologically adapt to local environmental conditions. We predict that reefs with higher habitat heterogeneity contain more ecological niches and thus exhibit higher species diversity. Here, we examined whether the bacterial community composition of Pachyseris is correlated with site-wide taxonomic diversity, levels of habitat heterogeneity, and other local environmental factors such as sea surface temperature, which could affect the potential for coral adaptation. We profiled bacterial communities via 16S rRNA gene sequencing to assess differences in community structure and functional potential (e.g., for metabolic processes). Microbiome composition varied with habitat heterogeneity, with beneficial taxa (e.g., Endozoicomonas) enriched in structurally complex reefs, while opportunistic and detrimental taxa (e.g., Vibrio) dominated in more degraded environments. These shifts reflect microbiome-mediated adaptation to changing thermal and environmental regimes. Our work highlights the role of bacterial symbionts in coral adaptation, complementing other work on adaptation in coral hosts.
c47f3a72: Ocean warming induces range extension of coral reef-associated organisms toward higher latitudes. Southwestern Japan is one of the best places to study the range extension. Biogeographic distributions of large-sized benthic foraminifera (LsBF) along the Ryukyu Archipelago (the Ryukyus) indicate a change in assemblage composition between the central and northern regions. Characterizing benthic foraminiferal fauna in higher latitude zones such as Kochi Prefecture in Shikoku Island and comparing it with the Ryukyus gives a clearer picture of the progress of their habitat extensions. We examined LsBF assemblages in 36 surface sediments collected from a beach, shallow lagoon, and reef crest from the central and northern regions of the Ryukyus and compared with published datasets from the area. We also examined LsBF assemblages in 9 surface sediments collected from shallow-water patch reefs in Kochi to compare with our Ryukyus data set and older datasets from the area. The biogeography of LsBF species, particularly Calcarina and Amphistegina species, in southwestern Japan broadly agrees with global trends. Calcarina gaudichaudii and Calcarina quoyi have not yet been found north of the central Ryukyus. Baculogypsinoides spinosus, however, was found in the northern Ryukyus, further north than in prior literature. There were also traces of Calcarinids in deeper waters in Kochi Prefecture. There was a predominance of hyaline symbiont-bearing taxa in the Ryukyus, which lessened in Kochi. Assemblage compositions were similar between southern and central Ryukyus, but were variable in the northern Ryukyus, and entirely different in Kochi: Calcarinids were dominant in the Ryukyus, while Amphistegina lobifera was the dominant species in Kochi, making up almost 50% of all specimens identified. These findings show that there is possible poleward range extension in LsBF species and that the northern region of the Ryukyus is the front line of biogeographic extension of shallow-water calcarinids, which needs to be monitored.
0857e4a6: Coral reef ecosystems face increasing pressure from phase shifts dominated by fleshy macroalgae. These shifts alter the habitat structure for reef-associated species, yet the baseline composition of the communities thriving within these algal areas remains poorly characterized. This study addresses the need to understand the overlooked inhabitants of Sargassum, a common fleshy brown macroalgae group. Sargassum species often demonstrate seasonality, can form harmful algal blooms, and are key players in coral reef phase shifts. We employ a multiplexed DNA metabarcoding approach using nanopore sequencing to investigate epiphytic communities associated with specific organs (holdfast and fronds) and seasons (December high abundance and June low abundance) of different Sargassum species (e.g., S. ilicifolium). By amplifying COI, 28S, and 18S molecular markers from samples collected across Singapore’s tropical reefs, we aim to establish spatio-temporal patterns of epibiotic diversity hosted by Sargassum macroalgae. This genomic surveillance allows us to better assess how community structures shift as Sargassum populations bloom or shift on coral reefs. This information is needed to better understand the general diversity across reef-associated organisms and to interpret how these communities may be affected by disturbances such as storms and reef restoration actions such as sea-weeding.
2d2b9b01:
In 2022, the Independent State of Samoa partnered with the Waitt Institute through the Blue Prosperity Coalition, Conservation International, and Scripps Institution of Oceanography, through the Samoa Ocean Strategy to conduct a standardised assessment of coral reef condition along the northwestern and southwestern forereefs of Upolu and Savai‘i. Across 36 sites, the expedition collected data on benthic community composition, reef fish and macroinvertebrate assemblages, coral recruitment, reef rugosity, and water quality, including resurveys of 13 sites first assessed in 2017 and 2019. These data provide a robust ecological baseline supporting the implementation of the Samoa Ocean Strategy and provide information on trends over a five year period.

Results show mean coral cover of 20.9%, with higher cover and reef structural complexity on Upolu than Savai‘i. Time-series comparisons indicate coral cover on Upolu increased steadily since 2017, suggesting a recovery trajectory following the 2016 bleaching event, while Savai‘i reefs had lower overall cover. Juvenile coral densities were similar on both islands (mean 5.7 individuals m⁻²). Reef fish densities were comparable across islands, but biomass was higher on Upolu, suggesting larger fish in general near this island. Commercially important macroinvertebrates were recorded at very low densities, highlighting potential overharvesting pressures.
