Taxonomic composition, and more recently, functional gene profiles of reef-associated seawater microbial communities on the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) have been shown to correlate with environmental and reef health metrics. As such, we hypothesise that gene expression patterns of seawater microbial communities are also influenced by the reef environment and potentially associated with ecosystem health. Here, we collected seawater in triplicate from two inshore and two mid-shelf reefs on the GBR every three months for one year, for metagenomic (total DNA; community composition) and metatranscriptomic (total RNA; gene expression) sequencing. Inshore reefs generally showed higher concentrations of nutrients such as particulate phosphorus and silicate, while mid-shelf reefs had higher concentrations of dissolved organic carbon. Seawater microbial community composition significantly differed across reef (R² = 0.26, P<0.001, PERMANOVA), season (R² = 0.22, P<0.001, PERMANOVA), and their interaction (R²=0.73, P<0.001, PERMANOVA). Species richness was consistently highest in the November samples at all reefs; and relative abundance of the most abundant genus Parasynechococcus was decreased. For RNA mapping, 2775 metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) passing quality filter (completeness – 3x contamination ≥50 in CheckM1 or CheckM2) were generated through metagenome assembly and genome clustering. These MAGs represent 607 unique species at 95% average nucleotide identity threshold. RNA mapping to the MAGs revealed distinct gene expression activity in inshore vs. mid-shelf reefs, with 217 MAGs significantly more transcriptionally active in inshore reefs and 249 MAGs in mid-shelf reefs (adjusted p <0.05, DESeq2). Members of the family Puniceispirillaceae were transcriptionally active on inshore reefs while members of the family Poseidoniaceae and genus Pelagibacter were more active in mid-shelf reefs. These results indicate microbial community composition and gene expression are closely associated with spatial and temporal variables.
59c7338d:
The Caribbean Sea hosts approximately 8% of the world’s coral reefs, supporting vital ecological services and a major portion of the regional ecology. However, these reefs are undergoing dramatic shifts; while scleractinian corals decline, octocorals are showing remarkable resilience and increasing abundance across the region. Emerging research on multiple Caribbean gorgonians confirms a diverse and species-specific trophic strategies. This highlights a broader knowledge gap: the trophic continuum across the gorgonian community remains unquantified. Therefore, in this study we aim to develop a systematic comparison of nutritional strategies (from autotrophic to heterotrophic dominance) to resolve the physiological mechanisms behind octocoral dominance and resilience and its consequences for Caribbean coral reefs.
We applied Stable Isotope Analysis (SIA) of δ¹³C and δ¹⁵N to investigate the trophic dynamics of multiple gorgonian species. Using Stable Isotope Bayesian Ellipses in R (SIBER), we quantified and compared the isotopic niche space of hosts and their symbionts independently. We further applied the novel Host Evaluation: Reliance on Symbionts (HERS) score, to evaluate species along a continuum from highly heterotrophic to highly autotrophic.
Our SIBER analysis revealed a spectrum of host-symbiont nutritional coupling. The sea fan Gorgonia ventalina showed a moderate niche overlap (43.7% of host area, 83.5% of symbiont area) with a HERS score of 0.40, indicating a mixotrophic strategy. Interestingly, Eunicea flexuosa displayed extensive ellipse overlap and the highest HERS score (0.83), suggesting a predominantly autotrophic nutrition strategy. In contrast, Plexaurella sp. and the sea fan Gorgonia flabellum showed near-complete niche separation, revealing strong heterotrophic nutrition.
This approach reveals that Caribbean gorgonian communities utilize diverse trophic strategies, from tightly integrated autotrophs to nutritionally independent heterotrophs. Future work will expand this framework to additional Caribbean gorgonian species to build a more comprehensive understanding of nutritional diversity within this ecologically important group.
b6ba3821: Stress resistance and resilience of coral holobionts depends not only on the coral host’s ability to mitigate and respond to stressors,      but also on the interaction with their endosymbiotic zooxanthellae.  While most coral species associate with specific algal species (Symbiodiniaceae), these associations can vary and influence the thermal stress performance of the holobiont. As part of a global project investigating the short-term heat stress response of corals using standardized assays, we sought to characterize symbiont taxonomic profiles for four coral species across three locations in the Caribbean. We utilized complementary methods to construct these profiles. First, we PCR-amplified and sequenced the ITS-2 marker gene to generate profiles with the SymPortal analytical framework. Second, we generated transcriptomes using RNA-seq and retrieved ITS-2 sequences from the cDNA libraries. By comparing these concurrent symbiont profiling strategies, we assess the validity of transcriptome-generated profiles and the potential underestimation of symbiont diversity masked by amplification biases in the PCR-based approach. While the generation of such profiles allowed for the characterization of inter- and intra-specific genotypic variation in the symbiont assemblages, the high dimensionality of the resulting data poses difficulties for quantifying compositional change. We therefore applied diffusion maps, a manifold learning method, to reduce the dimensionality of the data and identified the main dimensions of the compositional variation. By exploring the application of complementary methods to characterize symbiont assemblages and novel methods to quantify compositional shifts, we hope to improve the characterization of coral-symbiont associations from existing data to inform further stress-tolerance experiments, field surveys, and reef restoration practitioners.
64bbc404:
The Royal Charlotte Bank (RCB) is the second largest biogenic bank in the tropical Southwestern Atlantic, yet it has remained one of the most poorly studied marine regions of the entire basin. Located approximately 100 km north of the Abrolhos Reef Complex and spanning nearly 7,000 km², the RCB persisted as a major scientific gap until the first exploratory expedition in 2020. Even after this initial effort, fundamental information on its biodiversity, habitat structure, and ecological functioning remained largely unknown. To address this critical gap, a second multidisciplinary expedition was conducted in 2022 to investigate the RCB using complementary geophysical and biological approaches. High-resolution seafloor mapping was combined with extensive biological surveys to characterize geodiversity, identify carbonate constructions, and detect potential biogenic reefs capable of supporting diverse communities. Ecosystem functioning was evaluated through fish functional diversity to explore aspects of resilience and community stability.
Our results reveal a previously undocumented geomorphological complexity, including at least 17 carbonate constructions and paleochannels that enhance habitat heterogeneity and promote diverse biological assemblages. Biological assessments increased the known biodiversity of the RCB to 140 species, including 66 new regional occurrences and records of threatened and endemic taxa. Preliminary functional diversity analyses indicate relatively low functional diversity but high redundancy, although additional sampling is required for a more comprehensive assessment.
This study provides the first integrated ecological and geological baseline for the RCB and underscores its ecological significance within the Southwestern Atlantic. By revealing the richness and complexity of this once-overlooked region, our findings highlight the urgent need for conservation planning and long-term monitoring to protect this key component of the South Atlantic reef system.
f5a55d20: Coral reefs are declining globally at an alarming rate due to climate and anthropogenic stressors. Poleward shifts in tropical communities have been hypothesized as a potential refuge from temperature stress; however, this may coincide with increased anthropogenic stressors. Corals that persist in stressed environments offer critical insights into resilience capabilities under shifting conditions. This study focuses on a set of five unique continuous, large, and persistent patches of Madracis auretenra located off the Southeast Florida coast. These patches reside within a subtropical transition zone subject to strong influence from rising water temperatures, anthropogenically enriched nutrients, upwelling, and exposure to endemic coral diseases like Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease. Their persistence amidst these challenges suggests these patches are either resilient to stress or they are in an environmental refuge. Next Generation Sequencing (NGS) will be used to examine genetic connectivity, patterns in population structure, and geneflow across the Florida Reef Tract. Histological analysis of monthly samples will determine their fecundity and identify the timing of spawning. Hydrographic modeling of exposure to coastal inlet water, eutrophication, and historical in situ water temperature will be used in a statistical model to determine correlations between distinct genotypes or heightened fecundity with resilience and their environment. The findings from this study aim to gain a greater understanding of the environmental and biological characteristics of habitat refugia and identify potential poleward coral refuges amidst increasing anthropogenic stressors and changing climate. By integrating genetic and histological techniques, this research aligns with ongoing efforts to bridge science and management for sustainable marine ecosystems in a changing world.
709b7ffb: Temperature and ocean warming are one of the key impacts on coral reefs globally with recurring mass bleaching events in the last decade. With such sparse in situ records, natural archives of temperature provide high-resolution data. We assess Porites sp. coral cores collected at two sites between shallow depths of 2-5 m along the north coast of Timor-Leste, Beloi and Com, by the USAID and National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration mission in 2012. The cores roughly 30 cm in length, 3 cm diameter were cleaned, slabbed, x-rayed, and UV photographed at the Australian Institute of Marine Science. Prior to slabbing, the cores were CT scanned. Major and trace element analysis was performed on one core per site by an Agilent 8900 Laser Ablation Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometer split stream system at the Central Analytical Research Facility at the Queensland University of Technology. The timeframe spans roughly 1995-2012 with an annual growth rate of 1.5-2.0 cm. Thermometry will be determined through assessing Sr/Ca ratio and correlations with El Nino Southern Oscillation. Ga and Mn concentrations will give indications of inputs from land such as sediment. Timor-Leste lies within the Indonesian Throughflow which has complex oceanography including intrusive upwelling which decouples in situ temperatures from satellite temperature readings during the wet season. The x-rays show sub-annual high-density banding in one core which could be indicative of very localized upwelling or other oceanographic processes. We aim to develop a method to use thermometry and in situ temperature records to correct sea surface temperature from satellites, providing more accurate and robust picture of reef temperature climatologies in Timor-Leste.
0113c83d: Coral reefs globally are under direct threat from a number of factors that all play a role in shifting reefs away from a coral-dominated system, creating synergistic and nonlinear responses. In the Caribbean in particular, the spread of Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease and bleaching-related mortality from an unprecedented 2023 marine heatwave have most recently caused large declines in coral populations, reshaping the benthic community. Across The Bahamas, these two threats affected reefs to varying degrees: SCTLD has been present in some areas since 2019 while the disease has yet to arrive in others, and some areas experienced much higher heat stress in 2023 than others. The coactive and independent effects of these two factors on corals at the community scale are not yet well understood. We established permanent photogrammetry monitoring plots at dozens of reef sites throughout the Bahamas between 2013 and 2020 using the ReefShape protocol, predating the onset of SCTLD and the 2023 mass bleaching event, and have resurveyed the sites between 2 and 4 times since, culminating with post-bleaching surveys in late 2023 through 2025. These co-registered time-series datasets facilitate the fate-tracking of thousands of individual colonies at each site, giving a detailed look into the shifting nature of the coral communities as they weathered these dual threats. The datasets range from areas of early onset SCTLD and severe bleaching to no SCTLD presence, mild bleaching, and various other combinations of the factors, helping to understand how they are shaping the benthos and what reefs might look like going into the future. We present the results of our analysis as well as engaging reef visualizations, providing a case-study for the utility of long-term photogrammetry-based monitoring and documenting changes on reefs in response to the two most severe threats to date to Caribbean corals.
70e13028:
Underwater photogrammetry is a popular tool for coral community surveys, however, comparing 3D maps over time requires a complicated set of operations. 
Primarily, it is difficult to align dynamic scenes in order to visualize and measure changes because different components in the map move in different directions and rates. 
Moreover, analyzing 3D maps for coral community structure involves image annotation which is laborious and requires expert knowledge. 
Here, we use differential alignment and Model Assisted Labeling to solve the aforementioned problems. 
Instead of aligning our full maps, we crop them into sub-parts and align them separately enabling us to copy the location of 3D survey points between years and conduct a direct pointwise  comparison. 
To annotate the points, we used classifications from a deep neural net trained by us and reviewed by a human annotator, resulting in substantial time savings. 
Our results show that differential alignment is effective for 3D model registration over time, and that although our classifier reached precision values typically around 60 percent for key classes, this was efficient in expediting the annotation process. 
We perform a pointwise temporal comparison of benthic composition and examine key metrics including percent coral cover, coral diversity, and structural complexity.  
The results show that while structural complexity decreased over time, coral cover showed site specific trends warranting further research in the region to determine how these changes may affect reef resilience and recovery.
ce4b8c68: As part of our coral selective breeding project, two sub-populations of Acropora digitifera, one bred from high heat tolerance parents and the other from low tolerance parents, were exposed to a natural mild heat stress event. Despite visual bleaching being similar, the proteomes are significantly different, revealing a tiered heat stress response.
70aec075: Corals play a crucial role for sustainable marine ecosystems and global carbon cycle. Thus coral restoration is important in ocean engineering. The function of coral microbiome is more critical in non-symbiotic, azooxanthellate corals and the complex of community leads to healthy coral life and also the pattern of abundance and diversity shapes coral resilience and adaptation to environmental change. The high latitude corals are facing a decline or a distribution change by increased seawater temperature like as coral reefs around tropical area encountering a tremendous decline and bleaching event. In this study, we investigated the microbiome composition in azooxanthellate coral, Dendronephthya gigantea and its larvae and compared the microbiome composition change after heat stress. The majority of OTUs significantly shifted in corals under reproduction period and in coral habitat seawater indicated distinction in the relative abundance of bacteria compartment/site‐wise. Richness and diversity were higher, and more taxa were enriched in the corals under reproduction period and coral habitat seawater in summer. Flavobacteria and alphaproteobacteria dominated corals under reproduction period and coral habitat seawater in summer. Flavobacteriaceae and Oceanospirillaceae showed the most dramatic difference between corals in reproduction period and non-reproduction period. Flavobacteriaceae and rhodobacteriaceae showed the biggest composition difference between coral habitat seawater and non-habitat seawater. In the larvae settlement experiment, coral larvae settled on the microbiome coated surface 70% higher than non-coated surface and the normal polyp development were enhanced in group on the microbiome coated surface. We suggest that coral restoration through their microbiome could be a self-sustaining tool in worldwide coral decline. This work was supported by Marine Biotics project (20210469) funded by Ministry of Ocean and Fisheries, Korea
f37b9fb2: One of the fundamental challenges in understanding the population dynamics of marine species is identifying the larval dispersal pathways that facilitate connectivity among populations. While oceanographic dispersal models and genomic sequencing approaches provide proxies of population connectivity on different timescales, rare are examples of direct, contemporary tracking of larvae moving between sites, especially for marine invertebrates. Spiny and slipper lobsters constitute important coral reef fisheries globally, and have a high dispersal capacity, with larval durations approaching two years in some species. Notably, these phyllosoma larvae transition through multiple stages while in the pelagos, providing a location-specific age for each phyllosoma. Here, we leverage an archipelago-wide plankton dataset in Hawaiʻi with the goal of mapping the density and distribution of lobster phyllosoma through time. Zooplankton were collected from 88 sampling sites across the Hawaiian Archipelago using an Isaac-Kidd midwater trawl at night during the NOAA Hawaiian Islands Cetacean and Ecosystem Assessment Survey (HICEAS) cruises from July to November of 2023. Phyllosoma were separated from bulk zooplankton, and 61 of the 88 locations had at least one phyllosoma, with a total of 616 phyllosoma collected across all sites. We have  identified 244 of the phyllosoma to date using the cytochrome c oxidase subunit I (COI) barcoding gene, and expect the fully-sequenced dataset to be completed in February 2026. Most specimens match the endemic Panulirus marginatus or the Indo-Pacific, P. penicillatus, spiny lobster species, but there are also phyllosoma of three native slipper lobsters: Scyllarides haanii, Parribacus antarcticus, and Arctides regalis. Putative larval aging of Panulirus penicillatus using has identified distinct clusters of early-stage larvae at specific sites and times. The combination of species- and age-specific densities across the archipelago improves our understanding of the spatio-temporal distribution of this crucial, but mysterious larval life phase connecting lobster populations across the Hawaiian Archipelago.
7e2d9296: Throughout Hawaiʻi, many communities traditionally maintained loko iʻa (fishponds) to store abundant fish resources to feed important visitors or their communities in times of need. Fishponds rely on larval recruits from wild fish to enter the loko iʻa and grow until they are too large to escape. This study assessed the distribution and abundances of fish larvae across Kāneʻohe Bay, Oʻahu to better understand the potential of local iʻa fish production, and to search for some of the elusive larvae of prized loko iʻa fishes. Plankton tows were conducted across four regions of Kāneʻohe Bay (North, South, and two Central) at paired distances from stream outflows (250–500 m and 2500–3000 m) using a 0.5 m diameter, 335 uM mesh plankton net (N=108 tows) towed one meter below the surface. Collections occurred from January to March in 2021 and 2022 as part of an undergraduate class laboratory. Larval fish were sorted, identified via DNA-barcoding at the MiFish and/or FishCOI gene regions, and species-specific densities were calculated from tow volumes. We identified 956 total fish larvae from 21 different families. Larval fish assemblages in 2021 were dominated by Engraulidae (24%) and Gobiidae (38%); in 2022 Callionymidae (29%) and Gobiidae (25%).  The Engraulidae and Blennidae families consistently drove regional dissimilarities across years, indicating stable temporal-community structuring (SIMPER: percent contribution=11%- 20%; overall consistency=0.79-0.83). Fish larval assemblages were significantly different across the four regions of Kāneʻohe Bay within each year (PERMANOVA, 2021: F=1.6, R2=0.09, p=0.04;2022: F=4.2, R2=0.31, p=0.0001), but consistent within regions across years (p=0.11).Unfortunately, few larval individuals from important loko iʻa food fishes were identified; however, the high prevalence of cryptobenthic fish families in the nearshore ichthyoplankton community aligns with other recent work documenting the importance of cryptobenthic fish larvae in carbon cycling on reefs.
1d54eb24: As research in coral biology progresses, <i>Endozoicomonas<i> bacteria are emerging as coral symbionts, comprising up to 99% of the microbiome in some hard coral species. The ecology and physiology of <i>Endozoicomonas<i> and its effect on host health remain uncertain due to the challenges of 1) collecting diverse or endangered corals across environmental gradients, 2) recovering sufficient <i>Endozoicomonas<i> signal in omic datasets, and 3) using microscopy to visualize <i>Endozoicomonas<i> within the calcareous skeleton of hard corals. These challenges might be mitigated if an appropriate model system could be identified. In a recent study, we evaluated the distribution of <i>Endozoicomonas<i> in corals at Little Cayman Island. Using 16S rRNA gene profiling, we quantified the diversity and relative abundance of <i>Endozoicomonas<i> in diverse soft and hard corals across depths ranging from shallow (4.5 m) to upper mesophotic zones (36.6 m) at four sites around Little Cayman Island. <i>Endozoicomonas<i> was significantly and consistently more abundant in soft corals (53.8%) compared to hard corals (16.8%), representing near complete dominance (>90% of microbiome DNA sequences) in some individuals. While <i>Endozoicomonas<i> was abundant in certain hard coral taxa (e.g., 54% mean abundance in <i>Porites astreoides<i>), it was a minor fraction of the microbiome (<10%) in other hard corals. Soft coral-associated <i>Endozoicomonas<i> communities were also genetically distinct from those of hard corals. The prevalence, abundance, and host specificity of <i>Endozoicomonas<i> in soft corals, coupled to the ease of sampling and performing microscopy on corals lacking a calcareous skeleton, identify soft corals as a tractable and ecologically relevant in vivo model system for studying the <i>Endozoicomonas<i>-coral relationship.
79ef1ccf: Marine heatwaves (MHWs) are increasingly recognised as a more immediate threat to the persistence of coral reefs than the longer-term impacts of climate change. Sponges are functionally important to coral reefs globally and house diverse microbial communities that contribute to the health of the sponge host. We conducted a simulated MHW experiment on four ecologically important New Caledonian sponges from two abiotically contrasting sites, the highly fluctuating and thermally extreme mangrove lagoon at Bouraké, with daily variation of 6.5°C and a summer maximum of 33.8°C, and the relatively stable Nouméa lagoon, measuring both physiological (respiration and photosynthesis) and microbial community (16S rRNA gene sequencing and ITS2 genotyping) responses to gain a whole-holobiont perspective. We hypothesised that sponges from Bouraké would show thermal tolerance, in stable physiology and microbial communities, revealing mechanisms for adaptations to extreme MHWs, and that those from NL would be sensitive to short-term temperature increase, showing physiological stress and microbial shifts. Under thermal profiles based on the site-specific monthly mean temperatures (26°C at NL; 28°C at Bouraké), significant sublethal effects on respiration and photosynthetic efficiency were found in Cliona orientalis (both sites) and Spheciospongia vagabunda (NL), up to +4°C and +6°C for Bouraké and NL species, respectively. In contrast, Rhabdastrella globostellata (Bouraké) and Cliona jullieni (NL) exhibited only minor signs of stress at these temperatures. Above ~33.5°C, both species from Bouraké presented sudden and acute stress (bleaching, necrosis and mortality), mirrored by significant destabilising microbiome shifts, while NL species, exposed to just 32°C, persisted. All four species, and independent of the collection site, showed holobiont tolerance up to at least 32°C. These findings indicate that tropical sponges from New Caledonia have an inherent ability to tolerate future warming and MHW scenarios up to a strict thermal threshold, and that current environmental variability may not increase adaptation potential.
4fe07585: Our understanding of reef communities, among the most diverse ecosystems on Earth, is mostly based on shallow-water studies. In contrast, mesophotic and deep coral ecosystems remain little explored, despite hosting taxonomically and functionally distinct assemblages shaped by low to no light and cooler temperatures. To address this shortfall, we evaluated the taxonomic, phylogenetic, and functional structures of azooxanthellate corals from New Caledonia deep-water reefs, a region with one of the world’s most complex underwater geomorphologies. Using azooxanthellate species data from 12 to 3,740 m across 1,493 stations collected between 1980 and 2016, we built species checklists with seven functional traits (colony type and form, pseudocolonial form, polyp integration if colonial, polyp size, and solitary or fixed form) and analyzed it on the light of a phylogeny. Among the 262 species, the highest taxonomic richness was found between 200 and 600 m. Functional richness remained intermediate down to 1,000 m, but decreased sharply at greater depths, with particularly low values observed below 2,000 m. Most azooxanthellate species lack colony (or pseudocolonial) forms and, therefore, do not have polyps integration, being mostly solitary, conical (trochoid), and fixed to the substrate. Phylogenetic richness followed a similar pattern, declining with depth from 0 to 1,000 m. Our results highlight the importance of assessing multiple facets of deep coral community structure, as these species are essential for maintaining the 3D complexity and functioning of reefs that remain largely overlooked, providing critical information for conservation strategies in deep environments.
3b547213: Increasing environmental pressures on corals and their large-scale consequences have sparked research interest in the observed differences of their resilience and acclimation capacity, from interspecific to intracolonial levels. This variation in physiological responses may be traced back to differences in transcriptional regulation and consequently gene expression, which may be an acquired trait based on environmental history of an individual. Here, we investigated differences in transcriptomic responses to acute heat stress in Exaiptasia diaphana that were pre-exposed to different temperature regimes. We chose the clonal line CC7 to exclude responses caused by genotypic variation. Anemones were pre-exposed to a short- (1 week) or long-term (3 years) sub-lethal heat treatment at 32C, followed by a week of recovery at 25C. Then, anemones from both above treatments, as well as from the 32C (acclimated treatment) and 25C (naïve treatment) groups were exposed to an acute heat stress at 34C. Both symbiont density and Fv/Fm measurements showed a greater resilience of pre-exposed treatments, significantly so for the long-term primed and acclimated treatment. We performed a weighted gene co-expression network analysis on RNA-Seq data at 3 hours past onset of acute heat stress, yielding 17 modules, and categorized the fold change of genes in each module relative to the naïve control into pre-defined reaction norms (e.g. frontloading, priming, constitutively higher/lower expression, among others). The short-term primed treatment showed the highest occurrence of priming, the long-term primed treatment showed a dominance of silencing responses, while the acclimated treatment tended towards constitutively lower or higher expression compared to the naïve treatment. Most prominently, genes involved in translation processes showed a significantly higher induction in pre-exposed treatments.  Overall, our results show the beneficial effects of thermal pre-exposure, while demonstrating that different thermal histories can induce a variety of transcriptional response strategies to increase organismal resilience to heat stress.
7c1ec9d4: Mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs) have received attention for their potential as refuges for organisms, such as fishes, that face threats in shallow reefs. However, studies have shown that MCEs can harbor compositionally distinct communities from their shallow counterparts and are similarly affected by disturbances. Whether these findings are consistent across varying reef profiles, ranging from near-vertical walls to gentle slopes and discontinuous patches/plateaus, remains to be elucidated. In particular, limited studies have examined how fish community composition varies among different reef profiles along the shallow-to-mesophotic depth gradient. Here, we investigated differences in fish community composition from 5-40 meters depth among reefs with contrasting profiles at different sites in the Philippines. Overall fish abundance and species diversity along the depth gradient were more similar at wall sites than at gentle slope and discontinuous sites. Reef profile type also influenced which dominant species occurred at specific depths, with greater overlap observed at wall sites, likely because depth-generalist fishes can more readily move across depths on these horizontally less-extensive profiles. These results highlight reef profile as an important factor in structuring fish spatial distribution across depths. Targeted protection of shallow to upper mesophotic reefs with different profile types may therefore contribute to conservation of distinct habitats and their corresponding fish communities.
55077d39: Turbid reefs have been shown to exhibit high coral cover, diversity, and resilience to disturbances, which may suggest hidden, robust functionality. In contrast to less dynamic clear-water reefs, turbid reefs span a wider range of environments and disturbance regimes. These conditions both likely shape distinct coral communities that employ diverse strategies to cope with local pressures, and have also impeded ecological investigations of these systems at meaningful spatial scales. Consequently, we know little about the ecology of these potentially valuable ecosystems.  Here, we address the lack of baseline ecological data for turbid reefs by applying photogrammetric techniques optimized for turbid conditions across 10 sites in Asia (Singapore, Philippines, Thailand, and Sri Lanka). We quantify community composition, coral demography, and gather morphological traits (colony-level rugosity and height) to assess how coral assemblages contribute to local structural complexity and ecosystem functions. Assemblages showed marked variability in both morphotaxa diversity and demographic structure between sites, with some reefs exhibiting high diversity and coral cover (8-69%). Several sites were comprised of monospecific communities but the dominant genera varied between reefs. Our results reveal substantial ecological and spatial heterogeneity within turbid settings, as reef assemblages comprised multiple community types rather than a single degraded state. Consequently, the assumption that turbid reefs lack ecological function should be abandoned in favour of targeted management that explicitly accounts for local community composition and function.
e1268d7e: With an area covering just under 1 million km², Australia’s Coral Sea Marine Park (CSMP) is among the largest marine protected areas globally. Yet, the demersal deep-reef fish fauna of the CSMP remains poorly described, due in part to its remote location, as well as its sheer size, much of which is inaccessible below conventional SCUBA diving depths. Recent remotely operated vehicle (ROV) explorations conducted by the Schmidt Ocean Institute provided an opportunity to explore the benthic ichthyofauna of deep mesophotic and rariphotic coral ecosystems. Contributions to the deep, demersal ichthyofauna of Australia’s CSMP are herein supplemented with an integrative dataset consisting of ROV imagery and specimen vouchers. Sixty-two new records of fishes from 26 families are reported from the CSMP. Of these, 45 are new Australian records, one of which is also a new family record for Australia. Four species are new records for the Southern Hemisphere, and 21 species are potentially new to science. We report new voucher material for 29 species, including several species previously identified as new for the CSMP with no voucher specimens.
d62a1c7b: Coral reefs support the livelihood of many coastal indigenous communities globally but there is a lack of data to evaluate reef health and to make informed decisions for marine resource management. Many benthic monitoring programs have transitioned from in situ measurements of benthic cover, e.g. the Point-Intercept Transect method, to photographic surveys.  Photographic surveys not only create a permanent archive suitable for subsequent analyses but also tend to greatly reduce in-water survey times.   However, analyzing the large number of images produced is a labor-intensive procedure requiring coral taxonomy expertise. CoralNet (https://coralnet.ucsd.edu/) has been developed to greatly accelerate the annotation process using an Artificial Intelligence Convolutional Neural Network.  Users have uploaded more than 6,530 data sets consisting of over 5 million images and 276 million point annotations. We evaluated changes in hard coral cover between photo quadrat surveys in the Beqa Lagoon, Fiji, in 2017 and 2023.  We photographed 16 (1m2) quadrats along eighteen permanent 30m transects at 1-2m depth, for a total of 288 photo quadrats each year. For CoralNet training, a total of 388 independent images were collected and annotated via CPCE with 75 points per images giving a total of 29,100 points. These training annotations were then imported into CoralNet to train the classifier, which was then used to evaluate the images from the fixed transects.  Hard coral cover declined significantly from a mean of approximately 40% in 2017 to a mean of approximately 20% in 2023.  During the period between surveys, there were 2 major ocean warming events, a category 5 cyclone (Harold) that passed only 50 km south of the study site and an infestation of Crown-of-Thorns Starfish, that combined are likely the cause of the observed decline. This study clearly demonstrates that CoralNet can be used to document changes on coral reefs over time.
b93da449: A decade after the third global mass-bleaching event, coral-reef recovery trajectories remain heterogeneous: while some reefs remain degraded, others have regained coral cover but often exhibit taxonomically simplified host and symbiont assemblages. Environmental gradients can further modulate these recovery trajectories by promoting unique coral assemblages and symbiont associations, even among closely related corals. On Kiritimati (Republic of Kiribati), coral cover declined by 89% on the atoll’s shallow reefs during the event, driving a major ecological shift in coral and symbiont assemblages across a strong gradient of human disturbance. Identifying how certain abiotic factors (wave exposure, human disturbance) and biotic variation in Symbiodiniaceae community composition collectively modify post-disturbance recovery trajectories is key to understanding why certain coral species dominate recovering ecosystems. We investigated the spatial distributions of two Acropora species with differing growth forms (tabulate and corymbose) across 37 sites in 2023 spanning a human-impact gradient and using ITS2 DNA metabarcoding on 109 co-collected tissue samples from 13 sites, to characterize host–symbiont relationships between Symbiodiniaceae communities and their hosts. Acropora dominance shifted predictably with disturbance: tabulate corals dominated sites exposed to low levels of pollution, coastal development, and fishing pressure, whereas corymbose corals dominated sites where these pressures were high. Although symbiont communities partially overlapped, each species hosted distinct ITS2 lineages identified by indicator-species analyses. Unifrac dissimilarity confirmed significant host-specific structuring of symbionts and revealed differences across the human-impact gradient. These results suggest that the dominance of tabulate Acropora on previously pristine reefs one decade after mass coral mortality is shaped by interactions between species-specific traits and local disturbance regimes. The ecologically restricted recovery of both species, and their growth-form-specific symbionts, may reduce resilience to future stressors. These findings show that human impact and symbiont-community structure jointly predict which Acropora species and holobiont structures are likely to dominate future reefs.
56287e1b: Coral bleaching is known to be caused by a complex combination of cellular pathways including apoptosis, exocytosis, and phagocytosis. Additionally, the bleaching response has also been proposed to be an immune response. One specific area of immunity not well explored in bleaching is the process of inflammation. We hypothesize that an important component of coral bleaching is the inflammatory response whereby a systemic response caused by inflammatory factors leads to bleaching. To test this idea, we exposed heat-stressed corals to a set of immune-modulating treatments to determine whether altering inflammation influences bleaching outcomes. Corals were subjected to controlled thermal stress and multiple treatment conditions. We then quantified changes in symbiont populations, including those retained within host tissues and those released during bleaching. These measurements allowed us to evaluate cellular responses related to stress and symbiont health. Understanding these interactions is an important step toward identifying the functional mechanisms driving coral bleaching.
7182d93d:
Submitter has requested this abstract be considered as a poster ONLY.

Ocean warming is accelerating the poleward redistribution of marine species, reshaping ecological connectivity and driving ecosystem transitions across temperate and subtropical regions. To evaluate how early-life stages of small pelagic fishes respond to these changes, we developed a high-resolution coupled bio-physical individual-based model (IBM) for chub mackerel (Scomber japonicus) larvae in the western North Pacific, a region undergoing rapid marine tropicalization. The model accurately reproduced the present-day transport pathways structured by the Tsushima Warm Current, linking spawning grounds in the southern East China Sea with nursery habitats in the Korea Strait and the Japan/East Sea. Simulations for the 2010s revealed a pronounced peak in larval biomass in May. Under projected mid-century warming (2050s), larval biomass increased substantially and the distribution expanded northward toward the Yellow Sea and the subarctic domain of the Japan/East Sea, indicating a poleward shift of spawning and recruitment habitats.
These projected range shifts align with broader warming-driven ecological changes observed across reef and non-reef systems, where tropical and warm-temperate taxa increasingly extend into higher latitudes. Such redistribution may modify trophic interactions, larval connectivity, and regional fisheries productivity, with potential socio-ecological implications similar to those documented in coral reef ecosystems experiencing tropicalization. Our findings provide mechanistic evidence of how climate change may restructure the early-life habitats of small pelagic fishes and highlight the importance of integrated monitoring, international data sharing, and adaptive management strategies in a rapidly changing ocean.
Acknowledgment: This research was supported by the Korea Institute of Marine Science and Technology (KIMST) funded by the Ministry of Oceans and Fisheries (RS-2024-00406249 and RS-2025-02304432).
e9fde770: Traditional transect-based biodiversity surveys often miss fine-scale ecological patterns and are difficult to scale across heterogeneous reef habitats. We present a low-cost, integrated approach that combines GPS-equipped diver-tethered buoys, compact CTD sensors (temperature, depth, conductivity, light), and diver-operated stereo video systems for synchronized environmental and visual data collection. All instruments are equipped with synchronized real-time clocks, for automated post-processing of stereo video and physical data, allowing 3D seafloor reconstruction with precise georeferencing and subsequent extraction of quantitative metrics including coral colony size, volume, rugosity, and substrate composition. Pilot deployments at long-term monitoring sites reveal emerging microhabitat biodiversity patterns as a function of substrate type, geomorphology, depth zonation, and small-scale physical gradients. By linking these spatially explicit habitat metrics with environmental conditions – including stratification, local currents, and site-specific oceanographic features – we explore how pelagic forcing shapes sessile community assemblages and habitat transitions. Initial results indicate that variation in local physical conditions and reef–ocean connectivity may influence the distribution of corals, sponges, oysters, and associated benthos across gradients extending from coral communities into adjacent habitats. This combined toolset provides a scalable and repeatable method for generating high-resolution biodiversity heatmaps and detecting environmentally driven spatial change. By coupling low-cost tools with quantitative spatial analysis, the approach supports improved understanding of how oceanic processes structure coral ecosystems across scales.
9f736a38: Pocillopora corals provide habitat for fishes and invertebrates, with skeletal architecture critical for reef accretion and for maintaining reef-associated abundance and diversity. In light of this, we examined the relative abundance of Pocillopora spp. across 12 sites around the island of Mo’orea French Polynesia to address two questions: (1) How are Pocillopora spp. of various size classes distributed across lagoonal reefs?, and (2) Which abiotic and biotic factors contribute to shaping these distributions? Our genetic dataset (n > 3,400), confirmed the presence of six species in the lagoon, dominated by P. meandrina. Significant differences in Pocillopora species and size classes were found across space, with a higher abundance of P. effusa on the North compared to East or West shores, and a lower abundance of P. acuta on the East compared to North or West shores. The oceanographic gradients around Mo’orea provide a natural setting to test environmental plasticity in both colony density on the benthos and skeletal density. Our analyses show predictive capacity for species and size class distributions, with low latent-variable effects with the exception of P. acuta, which exhibits an unexplained variation potentially linked to microscale circulation or brooding reproductive mode. We identified wave energy, and nutrients as major predictors of community-level patterns in Pocillopora species of all sizes, as well as of sizes <5cm, which are of the age most temporally linked with the environmental data. Bulk density (n > 1,500) across species correlates with dominant environmental features of each shore, with higher skeletal densities on the high wave energy West and East shores compared to the North, consistent with our previous findings. Our investigation also characterizes how wave energy influences the detailed responses of skeletal traits using SEM and microCT analyses under different biotic and abiotic environmental conditions and across size classes.
c35c06c7: Geochemical variability in the calcium carbonate skeletons of stony corals serves as a powerful archive of seasonal-to-interannual tropical paleoenvironmental variability. However, comprehensive decoupling of natural and anthropogenic forcing in these environments necessitates higher resolution records on scales of weeks to months. Laser ablation inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (LA-ICP-MS) is a versatile microanalytical technique that can provide the high analytical resolution needed to increase the resolution of coral geochemical records. Unfortunately, LA-ICP-MS is known to have a lower signal-to-noise ratio than traditional geochemical analyses of coral skeletons leading to a decrease in analytical precision. This limits the establishment of accurate and precise paleoenvironmental reconstructions from coral geochemical variability, even when records are highly resolved. This study explores the combined usage of LA-ICP-MS with solution phase inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) to generate accurate and precise records of weekly-to-monthly geochemical variability from Caribbean surface corals. We report Sr/Ca ratios measured both via ICP-OES (1 mm sampling resolution) and LA-ICP-MS (85 μm resolution) along thecal walls of modern specimens of Pseudodiploria strigosa from St. Croix, USVI. Using the coarser (sub-seasonal) yet more precise (± 0.1 % RSD; 0.2 °C) OES data as accuracy tiepoints, we synthesize these datasets to create a comprehensive temperature-based chronology for Crucian corals at sub-monthly resolution. Such a robust and highly resolved chronology will prove essential in future applications of LA-ICP-MS that explore geochemical variability in corals related to non-seasonal and/or baseline changes in the ocean/land conditions. Future work will use this chronology to explore proxies of land use change (ex. Pb/Ca, Ba/Ca, Y/Ca) preserved within modern and fossilized Common Era (last 2000 years) Crucian corals to showcase the utility of these archives in obtaining objective records of environmental change before, during, and after the Transatlantic Slave Trade’s impacts on the US Virgin Islands.
0d77712d: Symbiosis with microorganisms is vital for reef-building corals. In addition to Symbiodiniaceae, bacteria are thought to provide many essential nutrients to their hosts. Genomic and transcriptomic data have been used to propose multiple functional roles of the bacterial genus Endozoicomonas, one of the most widespread coral associates. Some of these functions include the synthesis and provision of metabolites such as B-vitamins, hormones, and antimicrobial substances. Here, we characterised the metabolites exuded by a coral-associated Endozoicomonas in pure culture under different conditions (temperature, salinity, pH) using liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry. These data will provide a baseline of its biosynthetic potential and identify target metabolites that will be knocked out in future experiments in hospite to assess their function in the holobiont.
f498e09b:
The Red Sea is a peripheral sea with exceptionally high levels of endemism. Of the more than 1,100 fish species recorded from the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, over 19% are endemic. This proportion is exceeded only by Hawaii and Rapa Nui yet represents a larger absolute number of endemic species than both combined. The most species-rich fish family in the region is Gobiidae, with over 140 recognized species. Although new species continue to be described, and over a quarter are already considered endemic, most Red Sea gobies remain poorly studied. Many nominally widespread species likely represent undescribed, cryptic Red Sea endemics. Because gobies are small, highly resident microhabitat specialists with short generation times, they likely reflect patterns of Red Sea diversification at an accelerated scale, providing a model for processes occurring across other reef fish families.
As part of an extensive multi-year survey, we have collected, photographed, and sequenced more than 100 Red Sea gobiid species from across the Red Sea and other locations in the Indo-Pacific. Our findings reveal numerous entirely new species and a high prevalence of cryptic endemicity within taxa previously thought to be broadly distributed. Our results indicate that the true proportion of endemism in Red Sea gobies is likely far higher than currently recognized.
In this presentation, we highlight several newly discovered species, emerging patterns of cryptic speciation, and the broader biogeographic context of Red Sea diversification across the Indo-Pacific, including notable exceptions. As the most species-rich fish family in the region, gobies substantially elevate the already high endemism of this global marine biodiversity hotspot.
e15da6b1: Crown-of-thorns starfish, (Acanthaster cf. solaris, CoTS) are among the largest and most efficient corallivores on the Great Barrier Reef (GBR). Although CoTS are native on the GBR, these organisms become detrimental to reefs during population outbreaks which can hinder ecosystem resilience and short-term recovery, with cascading effects lasting from years to decades. Active culling operations have been implemented as the most direct method to mitigate CoTS outbreaks and preserve remaining coral assemblages throughout the GBR. While extensive research has been conducted on CoTS—particularly regarding their responses to direct culling; there is limited understanding of how CoTS behavior and outbreaks may be influenced by coral mortality and rising sea surface temperatures. Our research aims to improve the management of CoTS populations through enhanced detection, in-situ surveillance procedures, and understanding ecological responses of CoTS under changing environmental conditions. To address these knowledge gaps, this project will integrate experimental and field-based approaches to examine how coral composition, water temperature, and reef degradation affect CoTS feeding behavior and detectability during in-situ surveys. This research will have practical applications for industry and managing CoTS on the GBR, strengthen collaborations between research institutions and culling operators, and support the long-term resilience of the GBR.
fbf0e2b1: Studying systems where hybridization occurs between recently-diverged species offers a unique glimpse into the process of speciation and provides a deeper understanding of how species barriers are formed and maintained in nature. In marine systems, hybridization events are common due to a lack of physical barriers to gene flow, especially in coral reef fishes. Holacanthus angelfishes in the Tropical Western Atlantic (TWA) provide an ideal system to explore the impacts of hybridization and assess the strength of species barriers with continued gene flow. The two sister species in the TWA–the queen angelfish, H. ciliaris, and the blue angelfish, H. bermudensis–diverged ~1.5 Mya and share a large sympatric range throughout the Gulf of Mexico. H. ciliaris are found beyond this shared distribution throughout the Greater Caribbean, whereas the southern extent of H. bermudensis’ range ceases at the onset of the Yucatán Channel. Hybrids of the queen and blue angelfishes, previously described as a distinct species (“H. townsendi”), are frequently observed and display intermediate phenotypes relating to body color pattern. The role hybridization plays in introducing genetic variation or, conversely, blurring the lines between parent species through homogenization of genetic material, remains undetermined. Similarly, the initial driver of divergence and forces maintaining species boundaries between H. ciliaris and H. bermudensis despite continued gene flow are unknown. For this study, we conducted whole-genome sequencing on sampled populations of both parent species and hybrids throughout their shared and unique ranges to investigate 1) the extent and consequences of hybridization for Holacanthus in the TWA and 2) the ecological and/or genomic mechanisms driving the origin and maintenance of species boundaries. The findings from this work illuminate the impact of hybridization on the persistence of species barriers separating queen and blue angelfishes and the genetic architecture of speciation.
c3b214b4: Despite its wide distribution throughout the Indo-Pacific and the Red Sea, the reef building coral Porites rus remains largely understudied. Porites rus is resistant to environmental stressors, making it a good candidate for coral reef restoration. The Banda Islands are an oceanic archipelago of 11 small islands in the Moluccas, Indonesia. Here, a ‘forest’ of P. rus colonies located on a lava slope of the islands’ volcano has received attention since 2024. A transoceanic study showed that P. rus spawns synchronously ~1.5 hours after sunrise 5 days after full moon from October to April in the Southern hemisphere. This finding, together with participation in a global citizen-science spawning monitoring event initiated by French Polynesia, inspired for an Indonesian monitoring network under the CORDAP COSMARINDO project. Observations showed that spawning synchrony reported in other Indo-Pacific regions was also true at the Banda Islands, with slight variations in season. To disentangle the environmental factors responsible for these variations, a strong capacity in monitoring P. rus spawning was developed, and gamete capture and larval propagation methods are currently being explored. Advances on these methods allow collection of physiological and behavior parameters to implement a small-scale connectivity model to better understand source-sink dynamics of P. rus around the Banda Islands. This international collaborative research contributes to unravelling the uniqueness of the species’ reproductive biology to assist in protecting P. rus forests, and restoring damaged coral reef areas across its distribution range.
f3eefe9a:
Tracing the flow of energy through marine food webs is fundamental to understanding how ecosystems function and how they respond to environmental change. However, directly quantifying trophic interactions in fishes through methods such as stomach content analysis and feeding observations can be logistically difficult, expensive, and limited to short-term inferences. Stable isotope analysis (SIA) provides an alternative, offering time-integrated insights into resource use and trophic position with comparatively small sample sizes.
Norfolk Island, located in the subtropical South Pacific, supports coral and rocky reef habitats that vary in productivity, wave exposure, and resource availability. Parma polylepis, a large-bodied pomacentrid damselfish that is abundant across subtropical reefs, exhibits strong site fidelity, slow growth, and a generalist algal/invertebrate diet—characteristics that make it an ideal indicator species for detecting spatial variation in trophic pathways.
To evaluate the suitability of fin tissue as a sublethal proxy for muscle tissue in isotope studies, we analysed paired dorsal muscle and fin samples from 73 lethally sampled P. polylepis. We quantified the relationship between tissues for both δ¹³C and δ¹⁵N, comparing mean-offset and linear regression approaches. Regression models outperformed offset methods, allowing us to derive correction factors that were applied to an additional 20 fin-only samples collected through non-lethal sampling in a no-take zone. Corrected values were then incorporated into Bayesian isotopic niche models to assess trophic structure across sites and habitat types. Isotopic niche analyses revealed clear spatial structuring, with P. polylepis occupying distinct niche spaces across rocky reefs, lagoonal, and ocean-exposed coral reefs. These differences suggest that local habitat strongly influences carbon sources, nitrogen baselines, and trophic position. These results validate fin clips as a reliable, non-lethal substitute for muscle tissue in subtropical reef fishes and demonstrate that P. polylepis serves as an effective species for identifying spatial trophic variation across heterogeneous reef systems.
65710977: The coral holobiont is vital for coral health and resilience to bleaching events. In subtropical Hong Kong, considered a safe haven for corals with 84 reef-building species, five significant bleaching events have occurred in the two decades, affecting over 90% of corals at some sites. This situation underscores the urgent need to understand how local corals respond to stress and recover. To address these questions, we conducted a multi-omics study using three main approaches. First, from 2023 to 2024, we performed a quarterly baseline survey focusing on two bleaching-susceptible species, Acropora solitaryensis and Montipora peltiformis, and two resilient species, Pavona decussata and Platygyra carnosa, across three contrasting environmental conditions. We measured zooxanthellae density, chlorophyll-a content, and microbial communities using next-generation sequencing (NGS) techniques. Second, we compared these corals during the 2022 bleaching event to identify microbial contributors to resilience. Third, we monitored bleached Acropora corals in an 11-week recovery experiment, integrating physiological metrics, NGS sequencing, and gene expression studies. Preliminary findings reveal that Acropora, Pavona, and Platygyra adjust their zooxanthellae types, mainly Cladocopium C1 subclades, while Montipora associates with C21. Notably, Durusdinium was found in bleached corals, indicating its temporary presence during stress. Non-bleached corals exhibited beneficial microbial enrichment, whereas bleaching disrupted key metabolic processes. During recovery, significant increases in host genes related to stress response were observed. This research provides essential insights into the factors influencing coral resilience in a changing climate.
1e1daba9: Atoll islands are dynamic, low-lying (<3 m) sandy islets composed primarily of reef-derived material that accumulate on fossil reef platforms. As human-induced climate change accelerates globally, atoll island environments are among the most sensitive coastal systems to disturbance. Lalo, an open atoll within the Papahānaumokuākea Marine National Monument (PMNM), was severely impacted by Hurricane Walaka (Category 3) on October 4, 2018, which devastated terrestrial and marine habitats and nearly erased several sandy islets. To assess the stability and resilience of sandy islets within Lalo, this study applies a new geospatial framework, the Reef Island Geomorphic Activity Assessment (RIGAA), to quantify changes in reef-island area, shape, orientation, and position using complete island footprints. High-resolution satellite and aerial imagery (1981-2024) were analyzed in ArcGIS to evaluate morphodynamic change across four periods: (1) pre-disturbance, (2) post-disturbance, (3) 12-month recovery, and (4) total historical change. Wind and wave data will be incorporated in the future to identify external forces driving island reconfiguration. RIGAA offers a more holistic approach to analyzing reef island change by accounting for entire island footprints and delivering geospatial data products that support resource management and conservation priorities. Grounded in a kuanaʻike Hawaiʻi (Hawaiian worldview), this study recognizes the natural cycles of reef-islands and their ability to disappear into pō (darkness) and re-emerge into ao (lightness) as intrinsic expressions of their ancestral identity.
37e76868:
Uncovering the mechanisms involved in naturally occurring coral reef recovery is fundamental to progress within restoration and conservation applications. Calcifying organisms and their microbial partners provide a foundation for coral reef ecosystems by creating structural habitat and recycling essential nutrients, respectively. During coral decline, microbial functions are lost, impacting the highly coupled exchange of organic carbon and nutrient transformation. The loss of reef-building corals in Moorea was caused by a severe marine heatwave in 2019, followed by an outbreak of the crown-of-thorns starfish. This study aims to determine the roles that surviving calcifying organisms have on a reef’s benthic microbial and metabolomic communities, two factors important to coupled nutrient cycling.

Influences on microbial and metabolite community composition were assessed for three calcifying organisms observed to be prevalent on the recovering forereefs of Moorea, French Polynesia: Millepora platyphylla, Porites lobata, and crustose coralline algae. To characterize the resource contributions of surviving reef calcifiers, we measured contributions through three mechanisms: 1) microbial density regulation via organismal grazing pressure, 2) enrichment of unique microbial communities, and 3) exudation of unique exometabolite pools released by each organism. 

Replicated aquaria for all three reef-building organisms were sampled repeatedly for three days. Samples were collected for bacterial and viral abundances, microbial community composition, and for characterization of dissolved exometabolites via tandem mass spectrometry. Results are beginning to reveal contributions of reef calcifiers through the enrichment of unique microbial seawater communities, with varied microbial density regulation over diurnal cycles. These molecular techniques reveal a better understanding of the highly coupled processes between the benthic microbial community and stabilizing biochemical feedbacks fundamental to reef survival and recovery.
5737657f: Coral reefs face increasing threats from anthropogenic impacts, including bleaching, disease, overfishing, and declining water quality. A critical component of water quality degradation is increased turbidity from coastal development, dredging, boating, and intensifying storms. While sediment effects on corals are well-studied, impacts on reef fish remain poorly understood. We examined turbidity effects on behavior and epigenetics of bicolor damselfish (Stegastes partitus) through aquarium experiments. Epigenetics refers to the study of heritable changes in gene expression that do not alter DNA sequence, and these can enable rapid environmental adaptation. For social, mobile species, epigenetics illuminates the molecular mechanisms underlying behavioral changes, though reef fish epigenetics remains severely understudied. In this experiment bicolor damselfish were exposed to control (0-1 NTU), medium (5-15 NTU), or high (20-30 NTU) turbidity for pulse durations of one, six, or twelve hours. Results indicated that turbidity impacted food reaction time, with high and medium turbidity groups consuming significantly less food than controls. In turbid conditions, damselfish also reduced swimming distance from shelter, consistent with wild observations. Following trials, damselfish were dissected and tissues preserved for epigenetic analysis using Methylation-Sensitive Amplification Polymorphism (MSAP) to investigate modifications in three genes associated with stress response, neuroplasticity, and oxygen transport. These results are among the first applications of combined behavioral and epigenetic approaches to reef fish stress responses in the Western Atlantic. Epigenetics provides a novel framework for understanding rapid environmental adaptation in marine ecosystems. This work emphasizes the critical importance of turbidity and water quality changes in the marine system, as behavioral and epigenetic responses may significantly affect reef fish fitness.
e6fae24e: Achieving accurate underwater fish recognition requires large amounts of precisely annotated imagery, yet manual annotation is slow, labor-intensive, and highly challenging in complex coral reef environments. Factors such as irregular reef structures, variable water turbidity, and shifting illumination further complicate annotation. To overcome this bottleneck, this study presents an automated annotation framework that integrates a lightweight YOLOv12 fish detection model with ByteTrack multi-object tracking. Underwater video was captured at 3-meter depth in the nearshore waters of southern Taiwan at 30 fps. Using a small set of manually annotated images for ten common local fish species, a preliminary YOLOv12 model was trained. The automated system then selects the three most suitable target fish every ten minutes—those with larger bounding boxes and simpler backgrounds—from the first frame of each interval. ByteTrack subsequently locks onto and continuously tracks these targets. During the next three minutes, the system automatically extracts and annotates five images at 30-second intervals. This mechanism efficiently produced thousands of annotated images featuring diverse fish poses and coral reef conditions, substantially supporting iterative model refinement. Experimental results show that the combined YOLOv12–ByteTrack workflow rapidly generates high-quality annotated datasets and significantly improves detection performance. After incorporating the automatically generated imagery into training, YOLOv12 achieved over 90% recognition accuracy for the ten target species under complex coral reef environments. The proposed framework greatly reduces dependence on manual annotation while preserving high precision and consistency in the generated labels. It accelerates dataset expansion and offers a scalable solution for automated ecological monitoring, contributing to advanced marine biodiversity assessment.
947a4f6d: Marine heatwaves and high irradiance drive coral stress, highlighting the importance of climate refugia for species persistence. We investigated the physiological variability of the reef corals Montastraea cavernosa and Mussismilia hispida in a cross-shelf gradient and distinct micro-habitats (reef tops vs. walls) in the Abrolhos Bank during the 2025 Marine Heatwave. We hypothesized that light attenuation in vertical walls and turbid coastal sites would function as climate refugia, sustaining higher symbiont densities and pigments compared to high-irradiance tops. Specifically, we predicted this effect would be maximized in coastal walls and for the low-light specialist M. cavernosa. Data from 104 colonies were analyzed using Generalized Linear Models (Gamma distribution). Results revealed context-dependent responses rather than a universal refuge pattern. For Symbiodiniaceae density, we observed a significant Species × Site × Habitat interaction. Starkly contrasting our hypothesis, M. hispida in clear midshelf waters exhibited ~6 times higher symbiont density on reef tops compared to walls. This suggests that the large depth disparity at this site (top ~7.7m vs. wall ~18.9m) likely imposes light limitation on the deep walls rather than protection, with M. hispida preferring moderate irradiance levels found on tops. Conversely, coastal sites showed negligible differences between habitats, likely due to limited depth variation homogenizing the light environment. Unexpectedly, M. cavernosa defied the refuge hypothesis, displaying physiological stability across all habitats. Chlorophyll-a concentration was influenced by a Species × Habitat interaction and positively driven by depth; the latter explained ~11% of the pigment variability, suggesting active photoacclimatization where deeper colonies accumulated more chlorophyll. We conclude that reef walls do not serve as universal refugia, as their protective capacity is strictly modulated by local bathymetry, turbidity, and species-specific strategies. To fully unravel the drivers of physiological resilience, future analyses will incorporate remote sensing data (e.g., SST, DLI) alongside additional ecological metrics.
3d266c0f: Global biodiversity is declining at an unprecedented and accelerating rate, largely driven by environmental change that is reshaping ecosystems faster than species can adapt. Despite its potential to revolutionize biodiversity assessment of vulnerable species or fragile ecosystems, species-specific non-invasive underwater DNA sampling remains largely understudied for marine invertebrates. Comprehensive species-specific DNA barcode databases are essential for accurate species identification and taxonomic assignment, particularly at a time of increasingly employed metabarcoding monitoring of marine biodiversity. We present a novel in situ swab-based protocol adapted for underwater collection of genetic material, using Red Sea echinoderms as a case study. We sampled over 300 individuals from over 50 species of echinoderms using a newly designed underwater sampling kit applying sterile buccal swabs and an underwater sampling container. The novel sampling protocol was compared with traditional tissue-based DNA extractions and tested for preservation conditions (fixatives, temperatures and durations). While DNA yield from swabs was naturally lower than from traditional tissue biopsies, it proved sufficient and of high quality for all downstream applications. Overall PCR amplification success was 88% (240/274 echinoderm swabs), with a subsequent 94% sequencing success rate (202/214), and no significant difference in DNA integrity between swab and tissue-based methods. We further demonstrate the broad applicability of this method by compiling dozens of new barcodes for Red Sea invertebrates, including cryptic, rare, and undefined species. Our results demonstrate that underwater swabbing is a rapid (< 1 min per sample), cost-effective, and non-destructive, suitable for generating high-quality genetic data under challenging field conditions. We propose this protocol as an alternative to traditional DNA sampling, providing an efficient approach for studying at-risk ecosystems and species while prioritizing conservation and sustainability and facilitating large-scale genetic screening of wild populations.
7086496e: Artificial reefs are increasingly implemented to enhance marine biodiversity and ecosystem services, though their long-term ecological performance remains context-dependent. This study presents a baseline ecological assessment of coral communities and habitat condition along the inner wall of the artificial breakwater at Hambantota International Port , southern Sri Lanka. Using underwater belt transects, photo quadrats, visual fish and invertebrate surveys, and microbial water-quality analyses, we evaluated community structure, trophic composition, and indicators of ecosystem health. Substrate assessments revealed that approximately 65% of the surveyed area (Zone 1) supported healthy benthic communities, characterized by an average of 35% live scleractinian coral cover under favourable water-quality conditions. Coral assemblages displayed high structural diversity (Shannon index H′ = 2.5), comprising a range of growth forms including branching, encrusting, massive, sub-massive, digitate, tabular, and foliose across genera such as Acropora, Montipora, Porites, Pocillopora, Goniastrea, and Platygyra. Soft corals, sponges, ascidians, and gorgonians contributed additional three-dimensional complexity. Reef fish diversity was similarly high (H′ = 2.3), with representatives from 15 families dominated by herbivorous and carnivorous guilds, reflecting a well-defined functional trophic structure. Invertebrate richness included long and double spined sea urchins (Diadema setosum, Echinothrix calamaris), feather stars (Comaster schlegelii), giant clams (Tridacna spp.), oysters (Saccostrea spp.), and spiny lobsters (Panulirus versicolor). Coral predation was minimal, with only one Acanthaster planci observed. In contrast, Zone 2 demonstrated early signs of degradation, including coral rubble, crustose coralline algal overgrowth, and localized bleaching, suggestive of mild thermal or nutrient stress.  Recorded bacterial abundance at 2.3 ± 0.1 × 10⁵ cells ml⁻¹, with Vibrio cholerae and Shigella spp. prevalent in degraded areas, consistent with early stages of microbialization. Sediments contained 2% organic matter trends of active bioturbation. These findings highlight ecological value of the Hambantota artificial reef while emphasizing the need for continued monitoring to ensure long-term ecosystem sustainability.
39780f6f: Coral reefs are complex, dynamic systems on the frontline of climate change. As combined global and local stressors accelerate ecosystem shifts, we must quickly advance understanding of the ecological processes—and their adaptability—that drive recovery. New ecological approaches, modeling frameworks, and monitoring technologies are transforming reef science, providing pathways to identify, prioritize, and protect reefs. We present how the KAUST Coral Restoration Initiative – one of the world’s largest and most innovative restoration programs is driving these transformations to achieve monitoring and decision-making at unprecedented resolution and scale. Coupling image-based methods with integrated population models enables tracking of thousands of colonies to reveal how demography varies across species, environments, and scales. Large area image (LAI) mosaics and 3D models of entire reef patches establish demographic baselines—species composition, size–frequency distributions, spatial clustering, and condition—at ecologically meaningful extents. These products directly inform outplanting design and success by guiding site selection, microhabitat targeting, spacing and density rules, genotype placement, and performance auditing. When integrated with population and trait-based models, LAI supports forecasts of recovery trajectories and stress test interventions under future scenarios. At the same time, nursery monitoring is a major capacity bottleneck. Both in situ and ex situ operations require frequent, consistent imaging and annotation to quantify growth, health, and readiness for outplanting. We are evaluating innovative techniques—capture protocols, color/size calibration, AI-assisted detection and segmentation, automated quality control and uncertainty reporting—to reduce effort while increasing outputs and maintaining accuracy, and to connect nursery performance with downstream reef outcomes. Delivering restoration at landscape scale demands a step change in monitoring: standardized digital assets, interoperable data pipelines, and AI-enabled analytics that upscale from colony to reefscape while preserving ecological fidelity.
3c92245b: The necessity for large-scale monitoring in coral reef restoration demands new technological integration and robust analytical frameworks. The KAUST Coral Restoration Initiative (KCRI), a groundbreaking Red Sea program utilizing cutting-edge technology and research to restore coral ecosystems, addresses this need by developing technologically advanced data collection platforms. Utilizing Large Area Imagery (LAI) mosaics, which capture vast reef sections using high-resolution digital imagery, we facilitate the detailed annotation of individual coral colonies to the species level, enabling identification and tracking over space and time. This data is used directly for restoration management, including the long-term tracking of outplanted corals for survivorship analysis and optimization of restoration protocols. Beyond restoration, this comprehensive digital dataset, coupled with dynamic ecological data, serves as a powerful integrative tool for research. The dataset supports a broad range of detailed studies, including but not limited to, species distribution analysis, depth modelling, size-frequency distributions, and the long-term tracking of individual colonies. This unique combination of high-resolution imagery and ecological data opens the door for querying a myriad of complex ecological questions and refining reef trajectory models. Finally, the structured nature of the annotated dataset unlocks the potential of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AI/ML) protocols. We are actively working towards automated species identification, paving the way for the scalable, autonomous reef monitoring necessary for future conservation success.
c22b314b: Most shallow-water octocorals engage in a symbiotic relationship with photosynthetic dinoflagellates of the family Symbiodiniaceae. Their inherently mixotrophic nature, combining autotrophic and heterotrophic feeding, provides a flexible energy acquisition that supports their metabolic demands. This nutritional plasticity depends on the functional integrity of symbiosis, which can be disrupted by thermal stress, potentially disturbing oxidative balance and causing symbiosis breakdown (i.e., bleaching). Although temperature effects are well known in scleractinian corals, octocoral ecophysiological responses remain poorly understood, especially in South Atlantic reefs. In this study, we used two octocoral species, Neospongodes atlantica and Plexaurella grandiflora, to test the hypotheses that elevated temperature (+3.5 °C, 14 days) induces bleaching by triggering oxidative stress, and that enhanced heterotrophic feeding occurs after heat stress. Symbiosis was evaluated through symbiont density and chlorophyll-a content; trophic ecology was assessed using fatty acid markers of autotrophy and heterotrophy; and oxidative status was analyzed through total antioxidant capacity and lipid peroxidation. Both species bleached under thermal stress, but via distinct mechanisms. Plexaurella grandiflora showed a reduction in symbiont density, which led to a decline in autotrophic input that was compensated by an increase in heterotrophic feeding. In contrast, N. atlantica decreased chlorophyll-a content, but its trophic balance remained stable. Oxidative status was unaffected in both species, suggesting that bleaching may have mitigated potential oxidative imbalances, or that bleaching occurred independently of oxidative stress - possibly linked to impaired photosynthetic carbon translocation. These findings underscore octocoral susceptibility to warming oceans and reveal species-specific physiological strategies.
1caafdb4: Southwestern Atlantic reefs are largely confined to Brazilian coastal waters and are strongly shaped by continental runoff. Octocorals are key benthic habitat-formers in these systems, and many shallow-water species depend on symbiosis with Symbiodiniaceae to sustain their energetic demands.
