Overcoming these barriers will enable the integration of Symbiodiniaceae in global functional ecology and strengthen predictions of coral reef trajectories under climate change. We outline pathways forward, including refined taxonomy, systematic trait measurements in hospite and ex hospite, and the creation of a centralized Symbiodiniaceae trait database. By establishing the conceptual and methodological basis for trait-based symbiont ecology, this work provides a necessary foundation for incorporating Symbiodiniaceae functional diversity into models of reef resilience.
d1ecb3e7: Ocean acidification threatens the stability of reef ecosystems, but whether host–microbe associations reorganize in predictable ways under chronic low pH remains unresolved. CO₂ seeps provide valuable natural laboratories to better understand future conditions, and among these sites, Ambitle Island in Papua New Guinea represents a comparatively “pure” CO₂ seep environment and thus provides a critical opportunity to generate new comparative data. This study characterized bacterial and Symbiodiniaceae communities associated with the zooxanthellate zoantharian Palythoa tuberculosa at Ambitle under high, medium, and control pH levels. Preliminary analyses revealed that bacterial richness showed a tendency to be highest at the mid-CO₂ site, while bacterial community composition differed significantly among sites with similar multivariate dispersion. Notably, Symbiodiniaceae at the high-CO₂ site were dominated by Durusdinium or variable Cladocopium types, while at all other sites they were dominated by Cladocopium C1, which has been seen in other sites across the Indo-Pacific.  These results, when compared with recent results from the same host species in Japan and across the Pacific, indicate that perhaps holobiont responses to acidification follow consistent patterns across regions. Thus, P. tuberculosa could serve as a useful model for predicting holobiont change under future ocean acidification. The study improves our ability to assess how reliably natural CO₂ seeps can be used to understand holobiont resilience in high-CO₂ oceans.
29ae65a1: Acoustic Enrichment (AE) is a restoration approach that uses underwater speakers to broadcast healthy reef sounds to make degraded reefs acoustically resemble natural reefs, attracting settling larval fish and coral larvae. Here we demonstrate how pairing AE with low-cost cameras enables non-invasive, scalable monitoring of larval fish responses towards reef restoration interventions. We deployed a fully autonomous AE unit (3.8 kW-hr capacity) in Kāne'ohe Bay, O'ahu, across four spawning seasons (August 2023, June-July 2024, June 2025) at artificial reef sites at 5m depth, separated by 42 m and alternated treatment and control sites. Each site was equipped with autonomous cameras monitoring artificial reef structures, hydrophones, and underwater speakers that broadcast reef soundscapes nightly for three weeks. The cameras captured 3-5 burst images every five minutes from dawn to dusk. The resulting burst-images enabled manual and semi-automated larval counts and revealed that although mature fish abundance was not impacted between sites, larval densities at acoustically enriched reefs were 4 to 10 times higher than at the silent control site, with recruitment peaks coinciding with new moon periods. The ability to measure motion across burst images proved critical in distinguishing larvae from passively drifting flotsam. Image analysis exposed practical challenges: maintaining consistent exposure under variable lighting, identifying small, transparent larvae in complex images, and engineering compact long-term power systems for multi-week autonomous operation. To enable automated monitoring at scale, stereovision integration is a crucial next step. Stereocameras would provide real-time 3D depth perception and distance measurements, enabling discrimination of larvae from flotsam, size estimation of recruiting fish, and robust tracking of larval settlement patterns across complex reef structures. This work advances non-invasive monitoring, highlighting both AE's promise to enhance larval recruitment and the critical need for stereovision-enabled algorithmic automation to make passive visual monitoring viable for ecosystem assessment in reef restoration initiatives.
9550f006: Tropical coral reefs are facing unprecedented threats from anthropogenic warming, ocean acidification, and infectious diseases, all of which have driven rapid global declines in healthy coral coverage in recent decades. The foundational species that support these vital ecosystems, Scleractinian corals, rely heavily on their diverse community of bacteria, archaea, viruses, and eukaryotic microorganisms to defend against disease and provide nutrients. The composition of these microbial communities, particularly the dominance of specific bacterial taxa, have been linked with coral disease susceptibility. Caribbean reefs have suffered disproportionate losses from disease outbreaks, most recently stony coral tissue loss, making them crucial study sites to link microbial communities with disease susceptibility. Alongside corals, crustose coralline algae (CCA) and associated microbes play a critical structural role in reef ecosystems by producing chemical cues that either induce or inhibit coral larval settlement, making them essential for reef stability and regeneration. Evaluating the microbial communities associated with these reef calcifiers can therefore provide insights into potential reef disease resistance and resilience. In collaboration with Mission Iconic Reefs, we assessed 3 compositionally and structurally distinct Caribbean reefs along the Florida Reef Tract: Eastern Dry Rocks (CCA dominated site), Looe Key (coral dominated site), and Newfound Harbor (high CCA and coral species evenness). We assessed macrofauna community composition using video transect and photoquadrat data. We then profiled microbial communities from corals and CCA along each transect using 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing to score sites for current health status and potential for restoration. Our findings will be validated against past ecosystem health status and complemented by ongoing geochemical work to assess reef site chemical stability and calcification. The results of our study contribute to generating a spatial, epidemiological assessment of reef resilience that can inform restoration prioritization and outplanting efforts for optimal regeneration.
a93af3a6: The coral holobiont is implicated in the health and resilience of corals to a variety of climate and anthropogenic stressors. Evidence suggests that elucidating key microbes in the coral holobiont could be leveraged to improve restoration outcomes through microbiome engineering and beneficial microorganisms for corals (BMCs). Coral-associated microbial aggregates (CAMAs) are distinct clusters of bacteria within the tissue layers of corals typically dominated by Endozoicomonas. Significant knowledge gaps remain regarding the functional and ecological roles of CAMAs, especially outside of the Indo-Pacific. Current postulations about CAMAs consider them key secondary symbionts between the coral and Symbiodiniaceae as well as important providers of conditional benefits to the coral host during stress events. To expand our knowledge on Endozoicomonas-CAMAs and their relationships to global Endozoicomonas populations, we are studying U.S. coral species from Hawaii to Florida. This includes North Carolina Astrangia poculata and Oculina arbuscula that can survive with or without Symbiodiniaceae, providing an ideal system for exploring CAMA dynamics with the coral and other microbes in vivo during simulated stress events. We are also characterizing CAMAs in important species from Florida (Acropora cervicornis, Siderastrea siderea, Orbicella spp., etc.) and Hawaii (Montipora capitata, Porites compressa) through histology and sequencing (metagenomic and 16S) to further understand the dynamics between Endozoicomonas aggregation, environmental factors, and coral health. Together, these studies will improve our understanding of Endozoicomonas-CAMAs and can inform restoration practitioners and researchers about the potential to use microbial engineering techniques to target Endozoicomonas-CAMAs to improve restoration and conservation efforts.
39171e3e: The endosymbiosis between tropical reef-building corals and dinoflagellate algae of the family Symbiodiniaceae underpins the healthy functioning of coral reef ecosystems. Although dominant host-Symbiodiniaceae pairings differ across spatial scales (e.g., bioregion, reef zone, and depth), corals often associate with multiple Symbiodiniaceae. Where diverse Symbiodiniaceae occur together, the local-scale ecology of symbionts remains poorly understood. Here, we integrated high-throughput sequencing of the ITS2 gene region with digital 3D photogrammetry to evaluate host structural complexity and contextualise patterns of symbiont distribution. Symbiont assemblages were characterised along a vertical gradient (coral apex to base) across 50 Hydnophora microconos colonies from Rarotonga, Cook Islands. We identified dominance by the genera Durusdinium and Cladocopium, with inter-genus co-occurrence present in 60% of coral colonies. Geometric quantification from digital 3D reconstructions of coral colonies indicated that single-symbiont colonies exhibited reduced morphological range compared to those associating with mixed symbiont communities. Where Cladocopium-Durusdinium communities were present, Durusdinium prevalence increased toward the colony base, corresponding to regions with the greatest occlusion of modelled downwelling light. Disturbance imposed via in situ manual bleaching across a subset of host colonies resulted in a marked shift toward Durusdinium dominance, whereas community structure persisted in the absence of disturbance pressure. Collectively, these findings suggest that coral structural heterogeneity supports Symbiodiniaceae diversity and highlight the value of resolving microhabitat-level variation to understand the community dynamics of co-occurring symbionts.
f22d56c7: Reef-building corals, known for their heightened susceptibility to environmental shifts, are expected to face deleterious consequences as climate change intensifies. Despite the looming threats, the existing body of knowledge focuses mainly on specific locations and does not fully explore intraspecific and interspecific coral sensitivity across reefs, leaving a substantial gap in our understanding of the broader ecological landscape. During the Tara Pacific expedition, 900 samples from Pocillopora spp. and Porites spp. were collected on 31 islands across the Pacific from 2016 to 2018 to assess phenotypic signatures using a multi-biomarker approach. The biomarkers analyzed were related to the trophic and symbiotic status of the colonies, metabolism, and oxidative stress. By an integrative analysis of these biomarkers, we uncovered distinct physiological profiles that clearly reveal the condition of corals across the Pacific. In particular, our results show similarities in phenotypic profiles across the Pacific Ocean at an island scale. A complementary multivariate analysis identified the main environmental and genetic factors that shape the environmental signatures and phenotype of coral colonies. Sparse Partial Least Squares analysis identified the main environmental factors shaping the different coral phenotypes and revealed species-specific responses despite the distribution of the coral species in the same environment. Furthermore, Genome-Wide Association analyses uncovered a set of genes involved in local adaptation, laying the foundation for understanding the resilience capacity of coral reefs. Overall, this study, conducted at an unprecedented scale for coral reef ecosystems, demonstrates the acclimation and adaptation potential of species within the genera Pocillopora and Porites. Ultimately, this research seeks to provide conservationists with essential tools and insights to improve coral resilience and restoration efforts, thereby supporting sustainable strategies to safeguard these vital ecosystems against ongoing environmental challenges.
5cab1965: RNA editing is an epigenetic regulatory mechanism that modifies RNA molecules after transcription without changing genomic DNA, enabling rapid and reversible phenotypic plasticity that may be particularly important for reef-building corals under acute thermal stress or during reproduction. Here we present two independent datasets that map and quantify RNA editing in corals under (i) experimental heat stress in Galaxea fascicularis and (ii) spawning-associated biological contexts in Orbicella faveolata. In the heat-stress experiment, we analyzed a controlled thermal time series across multiple G. fascicularis colonies, aligning RNA-seq reads to a curated reference assembly with gene models, marking duplicates, and using pileup-based variant calling to identify candidate editing sites. To minimize false positives, we applied stringent quality filters, restricted analyses to canonical deamination signatures (A>G and T>C), and, where matched DNA was available, implemented a DNA “veto” strategy based on depth and allele-fraction thresholds to exclude genomic polymorphisms, yielding a high-confidence catalogue of heat-associated editing sites and enabling quantification of editing fractions across exposure points. In the O. faveolata spawning dataset, we centered analyses on a focal colony with matched genomic DNA, using the same reference-consistent pipeline to rigorously distinguish true RNA-editing events from SNPs, quantify editing fractions across reproductive contexts, and classify edited sites into coding versus non-coding regions, prioritizing those with potential functional relevance. Across both datasets, we identified coherent, high-confidence RNA-editing signatures and clear context-specific variation in editing levels, and downstream functional enrichment analyses link edited targets to cellular stress responses and reproductive physiology. Together, these results establish RNA editing as a measurable epigenetic layer of regulation in corals and provide scalable, genome-aware resources for connecting molecular regulation to coral performance under environmental stress and during reproduction.
37404791: Reef fish abundance and distribution are strongly influenced by the availability of key resources such as food and shelter, both of which can vary with environmental and habitat conditions. However, the extent to which these resources influence territorial damselfishes remains poorly understood. Territorial damselfishes are a group of herbivores that defend reef territories where they cultivate algal “farms,” remove unwanted benthic components, and ward off intruders. Due to their close association with the benthic substratum and their limited home ranges, these fishes may be particularly sensitive to variation in habitat structure and resource variability. This study examined potential differences in the abundance of the territorial damselfish Stegastes lacrymatus across three reef sites - Cangaluyan, Cabungan, and Magsaysay - in northwestern Philippines that vary in benthic composition. Fish abundance was quantified at each site using underwater visual surveys. Algal cover and composition, quantified at both the site level and within S. lacrymatus territories, served as indicators of food availability. Fish specimens were also collected for gut content analysis to identify ingested prey items (a proxy for diet). Preliminary results showed that Cabungan and Magsaysay reefs had higher turf algal cover compared to Cangaluyan. In contrast, Cangaluyan had the highest overall algal cover and supported a wider range of algal types (i.e., calcareous algae, macroalgae, and turf algae) at both site and territory scales. Correspondingly, S. lacrymatus abundance was highest in Cangaluyan, suggesting that the species may be more closely associated with higher total algal cover and algal diversity rather than with turf algal cover alone. Further analyses of gut contents are underway to provide additional insights into dietary patterns and resource use.
d31f4392:
Epilithic algal matrices (EAM), comprise of short algal turfs (< 2 cm) multispecies assemblages and other non-algal components including detritus, inorganic sediment, and microbes.  EAM are proliferating on degrading coral reefs, and may outcompete other benthic organisms, including crustose coralline algae (CCA) under specific environmental conditions such as sediment accumulation.  Epilithic algae-CCA interactions remain poorly understood, but increasing epilithic algae cover and sediment accumulation may negatively impact CCA growth and calcification by reducing available light and altering localized conditions.
Importantly, CCA play a key role in driving carbonate production on most contemporary coral reefs.  However, it remains unclear how changing environmental conditions and increasing epilithic algal cover may impact CCA’s ecological function.  Recent evidence suggests that CCA can match or even exceed the contribution of hard corals (Scleractinia) to the carbonate production.  This may become more relevant as coral cover declines and benthic communities become increasingly dominated by epilithic algae, particularly on marginal, high-latitude reefs where CCA may be critical to maintaining reef framework.
This study will examine epilithic algae-CCA interactions, and quantify how epilithic overlay, spatial and seasonal variability, and accumulated sediment influence CCA calcification on the Florida Reef Tract (FRT) and in the Persian Gulf.  Results will provide the first comparative analysis of epilithic algae-CCA interactions across extreme, high latitude reef systems and will advance understanding of carbonate dynamics on marginal reefs.
201bef1d: We catalogued  the abundance of polyketide synthase domains in dinoflagellate transcriptomes (Williams, Bachvaroff and Place, Evolutionary Bioinformatics 17: 1-24, 2021) and found that all members of the Symbiodiniaceae are capable of making polyketide toxins.  This was substantiated by the polyketides zooxanthellamides and zooxanthellatoxins being isolated from two free-living Symbiodiniaceae— Symbiodinium pilosum (strain no. Y-6) and S. natans (strain HA3-5)— but whether symbiotic species also produce these bioactive molecules has remained largely unexplored. To address this research gap, we isolated material from free-living S. natans CCMP 2548 as well as symbiotic species S. linucheae (SSA01) and Breviolum minutum (SSB01) via a standard methanol extraction and a methanol LC gradient elution used for sterolysin purification. HPLC fractionation revealed a high abundance of material absorbing at UV 225 nm in each of the 60% MeOH fractions. Time-of-flight mass spectrometry analyses further revealed the most abundant neutral monoisotopic masses produced by S. natans were Peak 1 2781.37 amu (C127H220N2O59S2) and Peak 2 2935.52 amu (C141H238N2O57S2). SSA01 was found to produce two smaller polyketides with parent masses Peak 1 920.53 amu (C49H80N2O10S2) and Peak 2 964.55 amu (C51H84N2O11S2). SSB01, however, does not appear to produce any major polyketides.  We are extending this analysis to the  sea anemone Exaiptasia diaphana (“Aiptasia”) infected with SSA01 or SSB01 to see if the same findings exist in hospite.
28d8c928: There is much is left to be learned about the underlying genes involved in coral symbiosis and their evolution, despite their importance in understanding coral sensitivity and resilience to climate change. Endosymbiosis with Symbiodiniaceae has arisen multiple times in Scleractinia but few studies have specifically addressed similarities and/or differences in the underlying mechanisms among these distinct clades. To help address this, we have taken a combined experimental and comparative genomic approach to better understand the underlying molecular mechanisms of coral symbiosis. Using comparative genomic and phylogenomic approaches, we have examined gene family evolution among clades of endosymbiotic and nonsymbiotic corals using coral genomes and transcriptomes to assess genomic evolution related to endosymbiosis and its maintenance. Then, we performed a one month RNAseq experiment using Orbicella faveolata in four treatments of heat and nutrient stress (i.e. control, heat stress, nitrate stress, heat + nitrate stress). We included nitrate treatments due its implicated effects as a local stressor on coral health and bleaching susceptibility, so that we could assess at the molecular level, how external nitrogen availability effects the relationship between the coral and its Symbiodiniaceae, given the role of nitrogen in symbiosis maintenance. We found that heat stress had a greater effect on the gene expression of Durusdinium, whereas the combined heat and nitrate stress had the greatest impact on O. faveolata gene expression. Interestingly, we found several genes related to neurotransmitters (e.g., GABA receptors) to be differentially expressed in our experiment. The results of these analyses will provide functional evidence that genes identified by our comparative genomic analyses are involved in symbiosis and/or its breakdown. Key groups will be tested for rates of evolution and gene family expansion to inform predictions to test in future experiments and assess during bleaching.
39d00a99: Severe bleaching events leading to mass coral mortality are increasing in intensity and frequency worldwide. Consequently, live coral cover decreases and it is unclear whether this is directly related to an induced imbalance in the population dynamics of reef-building corals. This study investigated live coral cover using LIT surveys (n=3) and recruitment patterns, determined by 1mx1m quadrats placed on alternate sides along a 30m transect (n=3), at the Belle Mare reef of Mauritius Island in October/November months in years 2008, 2012, 2020 and 2024. The live coral cover percentage was 85.67±8.80, 55.07±5.50, 57.22±13.98, and 83.00±11.00 at Belle Mare reef in 2008, 2012, 2020 and 2024, respectively. Total coral recruit density (individual m-2) was higher in 2008 (6.37±0.93) and 2012 (7.42±0.90) compared to 2020 (2.53±0.25) and 2024 (1.60±0.36). Recruitment was mostly dominated by Acroporidae followed by Pocilloporidae family and then variably by Poritidae and other coral families. Belle Mare reefs have known to be severely bleached and suffered mass coral mortality due to thermal anomalies in years 2009, 2016 and 2019. These findings imply that following mass bleaching events in 2009, 2016 and 2019, the coral cover has recovered in 2024. In 2012 recruitment remained high following the 2009 bleaching event and though it was less than half its density in 2020 and 2024, following the bleaching events in 2016 and 2019, the 2024 coral cover regained its percentage as it was in 2008. These results indicate that Belle Mare reefs tended to be resilient to bleaching-related disturbances at least partly due to its coral recruitment capacity. Further investigations on a yearly basis on natural and potential assisted coral recruitment for a long-term period is warranted to thoroughly understand the role of coral recruitment and subsequently assist in boosting recovery and resilience to climate change-induced mass coral bleaching impacts.
f114e613: The Symbiodiniaceae family contains unicellular dinoflagellates which commonly form symbioses with reef-building corals, sharing photosynthetic products to their coral host, and receiving inorganic nutrients (e.g., nitrogen and phosphorus) in return. Nutrient cycling within the symbiosis allows corals to thrive in oligotrophic tropical oceans, and breakdown of this nutrient exchange can lead to coral bleaching and death. There is evidence that different Symbiodiniaceae species have unique nutritional requirements and therefore unique biogeochemical niches, which could have significant impacts on coral symbioses. However, we don’t yet have a comprehensive understanding of the full range of variation in nutritional requirements across Symbiodiniaceae species. The current lack of information in this area is partly because Symbiodiniaceae laboratory experiments generally use flask-based culturing methods, which are relatively time consuming and therefore limit the number of nutrient environments to explore. When considering the impact of different nutrient environments, both the absolute amount of each element (e.g., nitrogen and phosphorus) and the stoichiometry of the elements (e.g., N:P) have significant impacts on microalgae functioning. Here we present data on a developed high-throughput phenotyping platform for Symbiodiniaceae using 96-well plates. Our approach allows different concentrations and stoichiometries of nutrients to be created, and use of spectrophotometry and fluorometry to assess cell physiology in a high-throughput fashion, over a two-week growth period. We apply this high-throughput phenotyping approach to assess the effects of different nitrogen (nitrate and ammonium) and phosphorus (phosphate) concentrations on the performance of two common Symbiodiniaceae species (Cladocopium proliferum and Durusdinium trenchii), under both ambient and heat stress conditions. Our results provide new insights into the fundamental nitrogen and phosphorus requirements of these two competing Symbiodiniaceae species and furthers our understanding of their optimal biogeochemical niche.
31c566b4: Corals possess complex structures that provide ecosystem services such as biodiversity support and coastal protection. However, these exact structures are delicate and prone to wounding due to both biotic and abiotic factors. As holobionts, corals house diverse microorganisms, including Symbiodiniaceae and bacteria, that confer benefits to the host’s immunity and recovery.  Despite having high regenerative ability, seasonal temperature variations have been found to influence the coral wound healing, which hotter temperature during summer found to degenerate coral wounds. Additionally, climate change-related ocean warming  may make wounded corals more susceptible to infections and mortality. To determine the repercussion of  heat stress on injured coral recovery,  integrative physiological, microbiome and transcriptomic analyses were done on  artificially wounded Acropora digitifera from Sesoko, Okinawa, Japan. The corals were subjected to 29°C (control; average summer temperature) and 32°C (heat stress; +3°C from control) for 14 days. Wounded individuals at control showed apparent tissue recovery progression over time, but mortality was also observed in a small portion. Meanwhile, heat stress treatment elicited 100% mortality, corresponding to the decreased Fv/Fm, net photosynthesis, algal density, pigment concentration and  light calcification in both wounded and unwounded individuals, clearly impeding wound healing. Some microbial taxa, including potentially pathogenic opportunists (e.g. Paracoccaceae), were present at early timepoints, while potential wound healing-supporting groups (e.g. Endozoicomonadaceae) were present at the later timepoint in the majority of wounded corals under control temperature condition. Heat stress caused a shift in the abundance of certain microbial taxa (e.g.  Saprospiraceae and Limnothrix), accompanied by mortality. Transcriptome analysis also revealed differentially expressed genes driven by both wounding and heat stress. Overall, these findings provide evidence that injury itself can already act as a stressor, and elevated temperature can exacerbate this stress in corals that may result in the disruption of reef function.
8fa19df3: Mesophotic coral reef ecosystems (MCEs) have been hypothesized to serve as refugia for species that face threats in shallow reefs. However, our understanding of these ecosystems is based largely on one-off biodiversity assessments, with contributions to ecological processes across time and depth being relatively overlooked. Understanding how species use these deeper reef habitats over longer temporal scales will provide greater understanding of their dynamics. This study investigates differences in feeding patterns (number of bites, bites/minute)  of the reef fish Ctenochaetus binotatus with time of day (morning at 7:00 a.m.; midday at 11:00 a.m.; afternoon at 4:00 p.m.) and among depth zones (at 10, 20, and 30 meters) in Bolinao, northwestern Philippines using unbaited remote underwater video systems (RUVs). We recorded the highest number of bites at 20 meters during midday (1578 bites   from 706 minutes of video), while the highest bite rate occurred at 30 meters during midday (22.4 bites/minute). There was a greater availability of feeding substrata at depth (e.g., sediment accumulation) and a greater abundance of other grazing surgeonfishes (e.g., Acanthurus nigrofuscus and Ctenochaetus striatus) in shallower areas and this may have shaped the general pattern of increasing feeding with depth. The higher feeding at midday compared to morning and afternoon is consistent with the diurnal feeding hypothesis. These findings highlight the role of MCEs in shaping overall ecological dynamics in reef systems and underscore the importance of multidimensional approaches when examining relatively understudied deeper reef ecosystems.
5e8f3ece: Corals are treatened by global warming. Bleaching is one immediate effect of global warming, resulting from the loss of photosynthetic endosymbiont dinoflagellates. Understanding host-symbiont associations are critical for assessing coral’s habitat requirements and its response to environmental changes. Cladocopium (formerly family Symbiodiniaceae clade C) are dominant endosymbionts in the reef-building coral, Mussismilia braziliensis. This study aimed to investigate the effect of temperature on the biochemical and cellular features of Cladocopium. Heat stress increased oxygen (O2) and decreased proteins, pigments (Chla + Chlc2), hexadecanoic acid- methyl ester, methyl stearate, and octadecenoic acid (Z)- methyl ester molecules. In addition, there was an increase in neutral lipids such as esterified cholesterol and a decrease in free fatty acids that may have been incorporated for the production of lipid droplets. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) demonstrated that Cladocopium cells subjected to heat stress had thinner cell walls, deformation of chloroplasts, and increased lipid droplets after 3 days at 28°C. These findings indicate that thermal stress negatively affects isolated Cladocopium spp. from Mussismilia host coral.
eef0a0e9:
Long-term monitoring of coral reef ecosystems provides critical insights into temporal dynamics, reveals shifts in community structure, identifies patterns of change, and informs evidence-based conservation strategies. This study assessed spatio-temporal variation in coral abundance, distribution, and recruitment across protected and non-protected sites around Mauritius and Rodrigues Islands, Western Indian Ocean, between 2020 and 2024. Live coral cover (LCC) was measured using Line Intercept Transects along three 50 m transects per site, while juvenile coral density was quantified with triplicate 1 m² quadrats and identified to genus level. Shannon-Wiener diversity (H’) was calculated for each site. Coral reef condition was assessed using four ecological indicators: hard coral cover, juvenile density, macroalgal cover, and diversity, and sites were classified into Protect, Recover, or Transform categories based on total scores and temporal trends.
Results revealed marked spatio-temporal variation in coral cover and recruitment (p < 0.05). In Mauritius, protected Flat Island increased +41.5% in live coral cover, while Ile aux Aigrettes declined −32.7%. Non-protected sites showed mixed trends: Belle Mare Reef +25.8%, Castle +4.2%, and Pineapple Rock −18.2%. Recruitment declined at non-protected FEF Reef and Le Morne Aquarium, while Belle Mare Reef maintained high Acroporidae representation. In Rodrigues, protected Pate Reynieux increased +7.7% in live coral, whereas Acroporidae declined −24.7% and Poritidae increased +40.3%. Recruitment at non-protected sites shifted toward non-Acroporidae families, increasing by 69.0% at Aquarium and 68.4% at Ti Trou.
Threshold analysis classified high-cover, high-diversity sites, including protected Silvana, Japanese Garden, and Dolphin’s Point as Protect, whereas low-cover or low-recruitment sites such as Boulder Reef, Flamant, Belle Mare Aquarium, Hermitage Island, and Var Brulee were assigned Recover. No sites required Transform interventions. These results indicate that global warming reduces sensitive coral taxa and alters recruitment dynamics, emphasizing the importance of protection in sustaining reef resilience and informing adaptive management strategies.
a3f837e6: Coral reefs are declining worldwide, leading to rapid loss of resilience due to a variety of concurrent global and local pressures. Dendrogyra cylindrus, an ecologically unique reef-building columnar coral, has become from rare to virtually extinct across the Caribbean following epizootic events of Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD). Conservation efforts, including selection of refugia for area-based protection and recruit production via assisted fertilization, are complicated by the paucity of population studies on this species. In early 2022, we conducted the first thorough population study of D. cylindrus in southeastern Dominican Republic (DR), mapping its distribution and abundance right before the arrival of SCTLD. In 59 acres, we found a healthy population, recording 1,314 colonies with most colonies being 50-100 cm and <50 cm in diameter. This first population and distribution study showed that in the Dominican Republic, prior to SCTLD arrival in late 2022, D. cylindrus was not rare as in other Caribbean regions (e.g., Florida reef tract, Mesoamerican reef). In 2025, after SCTLD became endemic, sites with the highest recorded density (up to 156 colonies/ha) were re-evaluated. After 3 years of SCTLD arrival, 96.7% (ranging from 93-100%) of colonies were lost on average, with half showing active SCTLD lesions, most colonies still being 50-100 cm and <50 cm in diameter but with >60% partial mortalities. To respond to this loss, we have been leading efforts to repopulate this species through assisted sexual reproduction. Over the past 6 years, we have produced and grew-out in an in-situ nursery setting over 35 one-year-old recruits, with average growth depending on the cohort, 0.4-0.5 mm after 1 or 2 years, losing cohorts born before 2022 due to SCTLD infection. Our results highlight the importance of conducting population studies to inform strategies to repopulate key reef builders and promote their resilience.
b153d59a: Endemism is a fundamental pattern in biogeography, with important implications for understanding evolutionary processes and conservation prioritisation. Endemic species are typically characterised by restricted ranges shaped by isolation, evolutionary history, and environmental conditions. However, quantifying endemism poses significant challenges, particularly in marine systems where the lack of clear biogeographic bounds complicate range definitions. For coral reef fishes, the definition of endemism depends on the spatial metric used to quantify geographic range size and the extent over which evolutionary history is considered, leading to variability in estimates across regions. Despite this variability, oceanic island locations have been consistently recognised as hotspots of reef fish endemism, harbouring unique assemblages. These isolated locations often support disproportionately high levels of endemism relative to their species diversity, yet the factors driving these patterns remain poorly understood. While island biogeography theory suggests that isolation, area, and age shape species distributions, the relative importance and impact of these historical and geographic factors versus contemporary environmental conditions in driving endemism have not been systematically tested using phylogenetically informed approaches. This study examines large-scale drivers of reef fish endemism on oceanic islands across multiple ocean basins. Using refined geographic distribution data and comprehensive phylogenetic trees, we compile historical and geographical attributes of oceanic islands (e.g. age, size, reef area, isolation) alongside environmental variables (e.g. sea surface temperature, current velocity, primary productivity). We calculate weighted endemism (species richness inversely weighted by range sizes) and phylogenetic endemism (branch length weighted inversely by descendant clade ranges), as these metrics capture different facets of endemism. Using phylogenetically informed Bayesian regression analyses, we then test which factors better predict endemism patterns, providing a quantitative framework for understanding elevated endemism on oceanic islands. Altogether, our results add fundamental knowledge on the processes generating unique biodiversity in isolated marine systems.
9d25e773:
The cryptic genetic diversity in the reef‑building coral Montastraea cavernosa suggests that some lineages may be specialized for distinct environmental niches.  In Honduras, M. cavernosa colonies on the turbid, nutrient enriched reefs of Tela Bay frequently show daytime polyp extension and active heterotrophic feeding, in contrast to colonies on the clear, oligotrophic reefs of nearby Roatan where daytime feeding is rare. This study tests whether Tela Bay M. cavernosa represent a distinct adaptive lineage within this cryptic diversity complex and whether their elevated daytime feeding is primarily genetically determined or environmentally induced.

Population genetic structure will be assessed using 2b‑RAD sequencing of 175 M. cavernosa colonies collected across eight sites in Tela Bay and four sites in Roatan. These data will be integrated with existing Caribbean‑wide 2b‑RAD datasets to determine the genetic relationship of Tela and Roatan populations to previously defined cryptic lineages associated with different environmental regimes. Differentiation of Tela Bay corals from known lineages will support a genetic adaptation hypothesis, whereas genetic similarity combined with site‑dependent feeding behavior will indicate strong behavioral plasticity.

Results from these analyses will guide a subsequent reciprocal transplant experiment designed to test survival, condition, and feeding behavior of lineages across turbid and clear‑water habitats, representing degraded versus “typical” reef conditions.By explicitly linking cryptic genetic structure to habitat type and feeding behavior, this work will clarify how individual M. cavernosa lineages contribute to reef persistence in marginal environments and provide a framework for incorporating cryptic diversity and behavior into restoration planning.
94b69401: Coral reefs experienced the fourth global bleaching event in 2024, with prolonged thermal stress severely impacting coral reef ecosystems across the Maldives. Declines in net carbonate production associated with coral bleaching are implicated in reef structural collapse and pose cascading risks to critical wave-breaking zones. Here, we present a carbonate budget assessment of an ocean-facing rim (reef crest and reef flat) in a high wave energy environment in the southern Maldives. This study also provide the first post-2024 bleaching reef flat data for the central Indian Ocean. Our results reveal an overall positive carbonate budget (1.3 ± 2.7 G), with the highest rate of carbonate production occurring on the outer reef flat (4.3 ± 3.2 G). Vertical accretion across the reef flat is estimated at 3.4 mm yr-1. Scleractinian corals were the primary carbonate producers, while crustose coralline algae (CCA) served as important secondary contributors to reef accretion. These findings underscore the significant role of reef flats in sustaining reef growth and contribute to the limited number of studies quantifying contemporary accretion rates in critical wave dissipation zone. Thereby, improving projections of future reef development under climate stress.
1b8e72bb: Giant clams (Tridacninae) are prevalent on eastern Indian Ocean reefs off the coast of Western Australia where they are commercially harvested for the aquarium trade and under threat due to ongoing heatwaves. Like corals, giant clams host Symbiodiniaceae that supplement filter-feeding and facilitate rapid calcification. Recently, symbiont profiles were developed for giant clam samples across the Western Australian coast, revealing substantial differences in photosymbiont communities when comparing giant clams to other coral reef hosts, and when comparing offshore reef and nearshore reef habitats. Simultaneously, genetic research revealed differences in the distribution of giant clams, specifically Tridacna, across the Western Australian coast. Tridacna noae has been found to be the dominant species in inshore areas from Ningaloo up to the Kimberley, while T. maxima has only been genotyped from offshore reefs, including Rowley Shoals and Cocos-Keeling Islands in the Indian Ocean Territories. Across this work, three key coral reefs were not sampled for giant clams and their photosymbionts. These include the high latitude Houtman Abrolhos Islands located in a subtropical marine transition zone, the tropical oceanic offshore Scott Reef system, and Christmas Island, which is an isolated tropical oceanic reef in the Indian Ocean Territories. Trends in Symbiodiniaceae communities from these previously unsampled sites are discussed in the context of both giant clams and other Symbiodiniaceae hosts of the region. Together this work begins to form a synthetic baseline of host and symbiont biodiversity and biogeography from these iconic coral reef molluscs in the Indian Ocean.
185324f2: The extreme and fluctuating environmental conditions of the intertidal zone shape distinctive stress tolerance traits in resident organisms. Intertidal corals, being persistently exposed to thermal stress, serve as valuable natural models for elucidating their heat resistance mechanisms. Studying the thermal adaptation strategies of these corals helps uncover unique evolutionary adaptations that have developed over time, offering scientific insights critical for coral reef conservation and management amid global climate change. This study focused on two reef-building coral species from the intertidal area of Sanya, China—Acropora digitifera and Porites lutea—to systematically investigate their thermal resistance mechanisms. We conducted a controlled warming experiment (29°C control vs. 33°C treatment, lasting 7 days) combined with metabolomic profiling. Results demonstrated that intertidal populations of both species possess superior thermal tolerance compared to subtidal counterparts. Metabolomic analysis further indicated that heat stress induced fewer alterations in the metabolite profiles of intertidal corals, though notable interspecific differences in acclimation strategies were observed. In contrast to A. digitifera, intertidal P. lutea exhibited widespread downregulation of metabolites following warming—a pattern mirroring subtidal conspecifics—implying that its thermal adaptability may be more constrained. This divergent metabolic response may also underlie the observed size reduction in intertidal P. lutea compared to subtidal populations. Notably, intertidal corals of both species showed consistently higher baseline levels of various vitamins, alkaloids, and functional metabolic intermediates, which remained elevated under thermal stress. This suggests a key adaptive mechanism contributing to their heat resilience. In particular, antioxidants such as betaine and stachydrine were persistently highly expressed in intertidal specimens, highlighting their potential as biomarkers for identifying and cultivating thermally resilient corals in future reef conservation efforts.
68877774: The world’s ocean has recently experienced the 4th global bleaching, another episode of extreme temperatures that trigger coral decline. Coral bleaching causes corals to be prone to coral diseases and death contributing to coral reef decline. The Bolinao-Anda Reef Complex (BARC), northwestern Philippines experienced a mass bleaching event  between May and August 2024. As a response, this study was conducted to evaluate the impact and recovery potential of the study site. In particular, this study aimed  to characterize the population structure of BARC reefs one year after the 2024 coral bleaching event specifically to determine the 1) adult (>5cm) and juvenile (<5cm) populations, 2) size frequency distribution of colonies, and 3) recruitment status. In this study, adult and juvenile populations were quantified across two reef areas within BARC, each with four stations, using photoquadrat method. Size measurements were done using SizeExtractR, and recruitment was quantified by deploying coral settlement tiles. Results show relatively higher adult coral density in Anda (13.88 ± 6.27 m-2) than in Bolinao (9.52 ± 2.29 m-2). Meanwhile, juvenile densities were higher in Bolinao (40.67 ± 15.43.70 m-2), compared to Anda (32.00 ± 10.11 juveniles m-2). Size frequency analysis showed predominance of smaller colonies, likely resulting from mortality and fragmentation caused by the bleaching event. Recruitment rates in BARC showed to be low compared to previous years (6.65 ± 0.55 recruits m-2), with relatively higher recruitment in Bolinao (8.78 ± 0.89 recruits m-2) than Anda (4.16 ± 0.65 recruits m-2). The majority of remaining adult and juvenile corals were massive and submassive Merulinidae and Poritidae corals, while recruits were primarily dominated by corals from the Poritidae family. Overall, the results indicate low recovery potential and are likely to decline with projected increase in sea surface temperature in the coming years.
c37ed528: In nearshore coral reefs, submarine groundwater discharge (SGD) delivers freshwater with distinct physical and chemical properties that can alter the physiology of reef calcifiers and influence ecosystem function. Modification of coastal watersheds through human land use can result in the transport of elevated concentrations of inorganic nutrients and anthropogenic contaminants into reef habitats. This study employs untargeted metabolomics to characterize the composition of groundwater-derived organic matter and track its transformation along a spatial gradient across a fringing reef in Moorea, French Polynesia. Groundwater entering the fringing reef is cooler, fresher, lower in pH, and enriched in humic substances, creating distinct chemical conditions that elicit strong physiological responses in calcifying reef producers. These primary producers release a substantial fraction of their photosynthetically fixed carbon as dissolved exometabolites, stimulating microbial activity in the water column. Thus, physiological responses to groundwater can reshape biogeochemical gradients by shifting the types and quantities of metabolites released into the environment, reflecting stress or response to excess nutrient exposure. To test whether groundwater exposure drives differential metabolite release, a mesocosm experiment was conducted to capture and isolate exometabolites exuded into the seawater by foundational reef calcifiers under both high and low groundwater exposure. The content of organically bound nitrogen and the release of proteinaceous compounds were altered under groundwater exposure in both coral and crustose coralline algae treatments. Together, these results illustrate how metabolomic fingerprints of submarine groundwater discharge, combined with organism-level responses to groundwater exposure, can strengthen environmental monitoring efforts and support more informed management of nearshore coral reefs.
b9f38852:
The deep sea is Earth’s largest ecosystem, yet it remains among the least explored. Strong depth-related gradients in light, temperature, oxygen, and salinity structure distinct ecological zones, each supporting specialized biological communities. The rariphotic zone (130–300 m) represents a critical transition between mesophotic coral ecosystems and the deep sea, hosting unique assemblages of corals, sponges, and fishes. Although the Gulf of Eilat is one of the most extensively studied regions of the Red Sea, research has focused primarily on shallow and mesophotic habitats, leaving the rariphotic zone—and particularly submarine canyons—largely uncharacterized.
Early submersible surveys documented deep coral frameworks and suggested high levels of endemism at these depths, while more recent studies have revealed pronounced changes in community composition with depth. However, the combined effects of canyon morphology, depth gradients, and environmental conditions on rariphotic communities remain poorly understood.
Here, I present an ongoing study addressing these gaps through a structured survey of the rariphotic zone in submarine canyons of the Gulf of Eilat. Using an integrated approach that includes ROV-based visual surveys, three-dimensional photogrammetry, baited remote underwater video systems (BRUVS), and sediment environmental DNA (eDNA) analyses, I characterize benthic and fish communities across multiple depths (100, 200, and 300 m) inside and outside submarine canyons. Long-term mooring stations deployed at corresponding depths provide continuous physical data to support biological observations.
By integrating visual, molecular, and environmental datasets, this study establishes the first comprehensive ecological baseline for rariphotic canyon habitats in the Gulf of Eilat and provides new insights into the structure and biodiversity of deep coral-associated ecosystems.
e6b61a1c:
Submarine groundwater discharge (SGD) exerts a major influence on nutrient supply, carbonate chemistry, and water quality in coral-reef environments, yet it remains difficult to quantify and even harder to reconstruct over time. Terrestrial waters typically exhibit δ234U signatures markedly different from seawater, thus making SGD the dominant driver of δ234U variability in reef settings where groundwater fluxes are significant. Because sea-level changes modulate SGD flux, we show that δ234U measured in corals also provides a powerful means of reconstructing past sea-level variability. 
We present the first spatially resolved assessment of uranium-isotope variability in SGD within the volcanic island and reef system of Tahiti, French Polynesia. Coastal seawater maintains δ234U values near the open-ocean baseline, indicating conservative behaviour and rapid uranium mixing, whereas SGD samples display pronounced spatial heterogeneity in both δ234U and uranium concentrations. These variations reflect differences in aquifer flow paths, residence times, and redox conditions, and are modulated by tidal forcing, with elevated δ234U at low tide signalling enhanced discharge.
A decade-long δ234U record from a coral at Maraa, Tahiti captures interannual SGD variability that closely tracks ENSO-driven sea-level fluctuations, demonstrating that sea-level changes are directly influencing SGD flux and recorded in coral skeletons. To test the broader applicability of this proxy, we examine a comparable coral record from the Yucatán Peninsula, a contrasting hydrogeologic system where SGD dominates coastal hydrology. The Yucatán record, spanning the past century, reveals an even stronger correspondence between annual variations in δ234U and centimetre-scale sea-level variations. 
Taken together, these results demonstrate that δ234U in corals robustly encodes groundwater–sea-level interactions, enabling quantitative reconstruction of high-resolution sea-level variability in reef environments. This approach provides a new, proxy-based pathway for deciphering past sea-level change in regions where traditional indicators are limited or absent.
facca918: Marine heatwaves are causing increasing mass coral bleaching events, threatening coral reefs worldwide. Under these circumstances, the recovery potential of corals after bleaching is a key determinant of reef resilience. However, Ocean acidification (OA) may influence the recovery process, and its effects may be exacerbated as climate change progresses. While numerous studies have assessed the effects of OA on coral physiology, how OA affects the recovery of corals following heat stress remains unknown. In this study, we focused on the fast-growing coral Acropora aff. tenuis, to evaluate the effects of OA on both their bleaching and subsequent recovery. We conducted a three-month tank experiment in which coral fragments were kept under four treatments: Control (25.5°C; pH8.1), Heat (31°C; pH8.1), OA (25.5°C; pH7.8) and OA+Heat (31°C; pH7.8). Nine colonies of A. aff. tenuis were sampled from the reef at Sesoko island, Okinawa, Japan and coral fragments were distributed into 12 indoor tanks. The temperature in the heated treatments was ramped up to 31°C and maintained until at least 50% of fragments showed signs of bleaching.  Temperature was then lowered to 25.5°C for 2 months during the recovery period. Throughout the experiment, coral health was monitored by PAM fluorometry, and routine mortality checks were conducted using optical microscopy paired with UV light. Additionally, photosynthesis, respiration and calcification rates, symbiont density, chlorophyll a+c2 concentrations, SOD and CAT activities were measured at three timepoints (T0: after bleaching; T1: 2 weeks of recovery; T2: 2 months of recovery). Fv/Fm in both the Heat and Heat+OA treatments showed similar recovery trajectories during the recovery period. However, significantly higher mortality occurred in the OA+Heat treatment, suggesting synergetic effects of OA on heat-stress recovery. We will further discuss potential mechanisms underlying these OA-mediated impacts on coral bleaching and recovery.
f3d70856: Autonomous Reef Monitoring Structures (ARMS) provide a globally standardised framework for characterising the cryptobiome of coral reefs. ARMS studies focusing on the metazoan community rely on metabarcoding based on the cytochrome c oxidase subunit I (COI) gene, yet biodiversity estimates are expected to differ substantially due to variations in amplification conditions. Because the mitochondrial COI region is highly variable across marine metazoans, primer–template mismatches may lead to differential amplification, introducing biases in richness estimates, taxonomic representation, and perceived community structure. Despite this known issue, few studies have directly compared primer sets on identical ARMS-derived assemblages, limiting our ability to evaluate the magnitude and implications of primer-driven variation. This study examines how two widely used reverse primers, the inosine-containing jgHCO2198 and the degenerate LoboR1, shape metabarcoding outcomes from ARMS deployed in Singapore’s highly urbanised reef ecosystems. Twenty-one ARMS were installed across seven sites for one year. Extracted ARMS samples are analysed through COI metabarcoding targeting the 313 bp Leray fragment (mlCOIintF paired with either jgHCO2198 or LoboR1). We compare primer sets in terms of recovered MOTU richness, taxonomic breadth, and primer-specific influences on community composition. By characterising these differences on identical ARMS samples, this study aims to clarify the extent to which primer choice affects biodiversity estimation accuracy in cryptobiome research. Our findings will inform methodological refinement for global ARMS metabarcoding protocols and support more robust, comparable assessments of reef cryptofauna.
6747404b: Coral reef ecosystems in the Seychelles Islands are experiencing accelerated degradation due to recurrent climate-induced bleaching events and mounting anthropogenic pressures. These stressors have led to significant declines in coral cover, structural complexity, and ecosystem functionality. In response, the Marine Conservation Society Seychelles (MCSS), with support from the Adaptation Fund, is implementing assisted reef recovery strategies that combine ecological innovation with advanced monitoring. The project centers on the strategic deployment of nursery-reared coral colonies, selected for stress tolerance and adaptive potential under shifting environmental conditions. These genotypes are propagated and outplanted across degraded reef sites to promote structural regeneration and ecological stability. To evaluate restoration effectiveness, MCSS employs underwater photogrammetry, a non-invasive, high-resolution imaging technique that enables precise quantification of coral cover, growth dynamics, and habitat complexity. Photogrammetric surveys generate large-scale 2D orthomosaics that enable spatial analysis and temporal monitoring to assess the success of restored sites. This data-driven approach supports adaptive management by informing site selection, optimizing restoration protocols, and guiding future interventions. By integrating ecological innovation with scalable monitoring tools, this project offers a replicable framework for climate-adaptive reef restoration. The combination of stress-tolerant coral genotypes and photogrammetric assessment represents a promising pathway for restoring degraded reef systems and safeguarding biodiversity in the Western Indian Ocean.
6ad828ac: Mass coral bleaching has become increasingly frequent, intense, and long-lasting, critically impacting shallow coral reefs worldwide. Although South Atlantic reefs historically experienced lower bleaching-related mortality than Indo-Pacific and Caribbean reefs, due to high turbidity, nutrification, and massive species dominance, no organism-level comparison of thermal tolerance across these regions has ever been conducted; this study is the first to do so. Here, we assessed whether corals from South Atlantic, Indo-Pacific, and Caribbean differ in thermal stress responses using photosynthetic parameters and changes in Symbiodiniaceae assemblage. Nine coral species, three representative species per region, including massive and branching morphologies (Millepora alcicornis, Mussismilia hispida, Siderastrea sp., Acropora tenuis, Favites pentagona, Seriatopora hystrix, Diploria labyrinthiformis, Orbicella franksi, and Porites divaricata) were maintained 21 days at 27°C (control) or 30°C (stress). Photosynthetic efficiency, symbiont density, chlorophyll-a and mortality were assessed and Symbiodiniaceae assemblages characterized via ITS2 sequencing. Results indicate differences in coral responses to increased temperature among provinces. All South Atlantic species showed reduction in photosynthetic efficiency, despite unchanged Symbiodiniaceae density and chlorophyll-a. In the Indo-Pacific, Se. hystrix increased symbiont density and chlorophyll-a, but exhibited no change in photosynthetic efficiency, whereas other species showed no alterations for any parameter. Caribbean responses were variable: D. labyrinthiformis and P. divaricata decreased Symbiodiniaceae density, O. franksi increased it, and in D. labyrinthiformis chlorophyll-a declined. Thermal-stress-related fragment mortality only occurred in Mi. alcicornis (80%) and O. franksi (20%). Notably, Symbiodiniaceae assemblage composition did not alter significantly with thermal stress in any species, thus no symbiont shuffle was observed. Some relationships between assemblage composition and photosynthetic responses were found: Mi. alcicornis, with the highest thermal-associated mortality, had Breviolum, a potentially temperature-sensitive genus, dominating its Symbiodiniaceae. These findings suggest South Atlantic thermal tolerance is shaped more by regional oceanographic conditions than by intrinsic species resilience or, in some cases, Symbiodiniaceae assemblage.
faa74ec1: Symbiotic relationships between dinoflagellates of the family Symbiodiniaceae are commonly observed in various organisms living in coral reef ecosystems. Large benthic foraminifers, unicellular protist secreting calcite tests, contribute up to 5% of the total reef carbonate production. Although foraminifera in the subfamily Soritinae host Symbiodiniaceae, the genetic structures of holobionts and symbiotic diversity at population-level are not yet understood. Here, we investigated the holobiont population genetic structures and diversity of the Symbiodiniaceae community in the soritid foraminifers Amphisorus kudakajimensis, Amphisorus hemprichii, and Sorites spp. Foraminifers were collected from different depths (<32 m) at four reef slopes, and from a single depth (<2 m) at fifteen reef flats on Ryukyu Islands, Japan. Next-generation sequencing technology and the SymPortal pipeline specific to analyses of the internal transcribed spacer 2 region of the ribosomal RNA gene of Symbiodiniaceae yielded 97 lineages in more than 600 specimens. Multiple lineages in single foraminiferal specimen were composed of dominant genera of Cladocopium, Freudenthalidium, and Halluxium. Halluxium was dominant in populations living in reef slopes, whereas it was scarcely detected in populations living in reef flats. Greater genus richness of Symbiodiniaceae communities were observed in the populations in reef slopes than in the populations in reef flats, where soritids host specific combinations of Cladocopium and Freudenthalidium. Low genetic diversity in holobiont populations based on genome-wide SNP genotyping is consistent with convergence in low-diversity Symbiodiniaceae communities in reef flats. Symbiodiniaceae communities and genetic populations structures imply that uniformity of holobiont communities in the shallow reef flats is attributed to geographical isolation and large environmental fluctuation.
d80beea3:
Through mass bleaching events and constant stresses, coral reef ecosystems are losing foundational structure. As we lose more coral, we are not only losing unique geometry on a species-per-species level, but also at an intraspecific level. This makes it essential to learn all we can about how corals grow to create diverse and complex forms so important to the rest of the life on the reef. Traditional metrics such as linear extension and chain rugosity have helped form our current understanding of coral growth and form. Now, novel advances in imaging technology, machine learning, and photogrammetry open the door to new ways of characterizing coral structure. Here, we focus on photomosaic images of coral restoration sites collected as annual time series by Fragments of Hope near Placencia, Belize. This monitoring has been crucial to tracking how coral cover has changed species-by-species over the years, but also contains a yet-to-be tapped wealth of information on geometric growth and intraspecific variation in outplanted acroporid corals.

Much work to date has focused on the 2D orthoprojected products from the photomosaics or the combination of orthoprojections and digital elevation models. Here, we show how machine learning focused only on the point-cloud data underlying 3D photomosaics provides a novel way to uncover features of coral structure that may have been previously hidden. We have created a modified version of the PointNet deep-learning model to classify corals by species, which has shown to be a promising starting point. The features uncovered by our analysis show overlap with conventional metrics, but also captures considerable variation in colony morphology not explained otherwise. We then take a preliminary look at how these features might also discern intraspecific phenotypes in Acropora palmata and discuss how this data mining can streamline our learning of restoration from both past and present.
c2784574: Phytoplankton form the base of photosynthesis in coral reef ecosystems and respond to seasonal environmental fluctuations. This study examined monthly phytoplankton samples from three stations at Narara Reef, Gulf of Kachchh, over two consecutive years (2024–2025), alongside water-quality measurements including temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, and salinity. A total of 29 phytoplankton species were identified, with pronounced seasonal variations in species composition and functional groups. Centric diatoms (66.9–77.2%) were consistently dominant, while dinoflagellates (4.5–8.7%) remained non-dominant. Minor groups included pennate diatoms (8.7–14.5%), colonial diatoms (4.8–5.9%), and other algae (4.8–8.5%). The diatom:dinoflagellate (D:D) ratio peaked during winter and early pre-summer (20:1) and declined during the monsoon (9:1), reflecting increased dinoflagellate contribution with rising temperatures. Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) and Canonical Correspondence Analysis (CCA) were performed to assess seasonal trends and environmental drivers of the D:D ratio. While the correlation with temperature was moderately significant, the seasonal trend indicates that the D:D ratio may serve as a preliminary bioindicator for the onset of warm-season conditions. These results demonstrate the value of integrating phytoplankton functional ratios with environmental parameters, with a focus on the D:D ratio providing a quantitative framework for monitoring phytoplankton dynamics and potential indicator metrics for reef ecosystem changes in the Gulf of Kachchh.
28e158dd: Thermal preconditioning has been demonstrated to enhance bleaching tolerance in tropical reef-building corals, offering potential applications for coral restoration and assisted evolution strategies. Although experimental evidence supports the efficacy of thermal preconditioning in increasing tolerance in corals, the molecular mechanisms underpinning this process remains poorly understood. Limited investigations have been conducted on the molecular basis, with only few coral species studied. Therefore, in this study, colonies of Pocillopora damicornis were subjected to thermal preconditioning through controlled exposure to sub-lethal temperature (28 °C, for 2 weeks), simulating conditions that induce increase tolerance in previous studies. Non-preconditioned colonies were maintained under lower stable conditions (25 °C). All experimental groups were then subjected to a heat stress of 33 °C. RNA was extracted from coral samples and RNA sequencing was employed to determine the transcriptome-wide changes in gene expression. Bioinformatics analyses were conducted to identify differentially expressed genes, pathways, and functional categories. The transcriptomic analysis revealed significant differences in gene expression profiles between preconditioned and non-preconditioned colonies. Following preconditioning, preconditioned corals exhibited higher expression of genes related to antioxidant response and DNA repair. During heat stress, preconditioned colonies showed delayed bleaching compared to non-preconditioned colonies. Preconditioned colonies showed increased expression of mitochondrial genes associated with oxidative phosphorylation, energy production, antioxidant defense, homeostasis, and efferocytosis. Overall, these responses suggest that preconditioned corals activate pathways that sustain energy balance and limit oxidative damage during thermal stress. The non-preconditioned colonies displayed a less pronounced stress responsive transcriptomic signature, suggesting reduced preparedness for thermal stress. These findings provide a comprehensive understanding of the molecular dynamics governing thermal preconditioning in coral. Overall, this investigation will help bridge the knowledge gap surrounding the molecular mechanism of thermal preconditioning and provide further insights for the practical implementation of this technique in restoration strategies.
46fc9cd4: Submarine groundwater discharge (SGD) areas, particularly in volcanic regions, release warm, acidic, nutrient- and CO₂-rich waters, creating distinct biogeochemical conditions that mimic future climate change scenarios for coral reefs. SGD areas offer an opportunity to explore the effects of rising seawater temperature and acidification on corals and their symbionts in the natural setting. Coral survival under these stresses is partly attributed to its photosynthetic symbionts, dinoflagellates belonging to the family Symbiodiniaceae. To examine the impact of SGD conditions on these symbionts, a coral translocation experiment was performed in Tingloy, Batangas, a known volcanic region influenced by SGD. Pocillopora verrucosa corals were translocated into established sites in Tingloy: (1) “Vent” site, an SGD-visible coral reef with active shallow hydrothermal vents, and (2) “Non-vent” site, a coral reef without any visible SGD emanations. Translocated fragments were sampled after 1- and 7- months for analysis. Results show that fragments translocated in the “Vent” site significantly differed in terms of symbiont density from those translocated into the “Non-vent” site. This is especially prominent during the short-term deployment. Differences are also evident in symbiont community composition in both short- and long-term deployments. Identification of these short- and long-term influences of SGD conditions on coral symbionts provide insights into potential adaptation mechanisms of coral hosts in response to the unique environments of SGD areas, advancing our understanding of coral community resilience to climate change and its impacts on Philippine coral reef biodiversity.
39609f7f: Understanding how oceanographic variability regulates coral physiology is essential for predicting reef responses to climate‐driven heatwaves. Upwelling is widespread on coral reefs, but the physiological interactions between localized cooling and nutrient delivery on coral thermal tolerance are poorly understood. On an offshore reef in the central Red Sea, we paired 14 months of continuous environmental monitoring with monthly sampling of tagged Pocillopora favosa coral colonies across a 5-25 m depth gradient to evaluate how resource supply and thermal stress shape coral physiology. During spring and summer in the Red Sea, regular upwelling transported cool, nitrate- and plankton-rich waters onto the reef slope, creating strong vertical gradients in temperature, nutrient supply, and food availability. However, a heatwave during July-September 2024 increased stratification of the water column and shut down upwelling, exposing all depths to anomalously warm surface waters. This disruption resulted in depth‐specific outcomes, with complete bleaching and mortality at 5-15 m and minimal impact at deeper depths. To investigate the mechanisms underpinning these outcomes, we quantified how upwelling-driven shifts in nitrate availability and food supply influence coral nitrogen metabolism, redox balance, and symbiosis stability using biomarkers of biomass, chlorophyll a, symbiont density, C:N ratios, and protein nitration (PN) - the latter indicating peroxynitrite formation - a highly reactive nitrogen species that can impair cellular function severely under combined heat stress and altered nitrogen metabolism. Coral and symbiont C:N ratios reveal depth-stratified nutrient regimes, while PN levels captured how oxidative–nitrosative stress varied across depths and time by integrating thermal driven reactive oxygen species production with nitric oxide derived from nitrogen metabolism. By quantifying natural fluctuations in nutrient delivery and temperature before and during a severe bleaching event, our dataset offers a unique framework for understanding how nutrient dynamics modulate oxidative–nitrosative stress pathways that compromise symbiosis and, ultimately, bleaching patterns across depth.
54a6f274:
The Class Octocorallia (subphylum Anthozoa, phylum Cnidaria) are colonial organisms inhabiting seafloors worldwide including on numerous coral reef areas. Octocorals provide shelter and food for small animals such as crustaceans, molluscs, polychaetes and others. Furthermore, within coral reef ecosystems, some octocorals are thought to be comparably more resistant to bleaching than many hard scleractinian corals. After scleractinian corals die off, the ecosystem may shift to other organisms, including soft corals.
Although octocorals are an important part of coral reef ecosystems, because of difficulty in identification and a comparatively small number of octocoral experts, ecological aspects of octocorals are often understudied. As well, there are only a few morphological traits useful for classification that accurately reflect molecular phylogeny, leading to long-standing taxonomic confusion. The small crystal CaCO3 skeletal elementals that occur in the tissues of colonies are called sclerites, and have served as key taxonomic characters for over a century. Yet, subjective descriptions of sclerites based on expert experience are common, posing challenges in ensuring objectivity. While some papers have performed quantitative analyses using certain gorgonian genera exist, no such attempts exist for soft corals that lack an axis. Therefore, we aimed to construct an auto-classification model for sclerite images using deep learning, focusing on efficient extraction of quantitative data such as length, width, and circularity of the objects. By using this approach to collect quantitative data on sclerites and visualize the regions used by AI as judgment criteria during automatic classification, it was possible to explore new classification traits that ensure objectivity. Here, as a first step, we report on the results of investigating whether AI can extract quantitative trait states of sclerites, enabling automatic image classification.
25e98a3c:
Crustose coralline algae (CCA) are key reef builders that promote calcification, coral 
settlement, and serve as food for herbivores. Their morphology is plastic, shifting between 
crustose and foliose forms in response to grazing pressure. This study aimed to test 
whether herbivory directly influences the morphology of CCA. Specifically, we examined 
whether grazing by reef herbivores, particularly parrotfishes, drives the shift between 
crustose (flat) and foliose (rose-like) forms. The experiment was conducted within the 
Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary (Molasses Reef). Sixteen limestone tiles (10 × 10 
cm) were deployed on the reef for nine months to allow natural algal colonization. After this 
period, the tiles were collected and maintained in the laboratory for nine months under 
controlled conditions with low light and no herbivore grazing pressure. During this time, 
several individuals of CCA developed foliose morphology. The 16 tiles were then returned 
to the reef to conduct feeding assays. Photographs were taken before exposure to 
herbivores. The tiles remained in the reef for 3 hours, after which each tile was 
photographed to quantify percent cover and height and estimate biomass. Herbivory 
occurred rapidly in the field. Within the assay period, all rose-like CCA individuals (100% of 
tiles with this morphology) were consumed by herbivores, while crustose individuals were 
barely grazed (<5 bites). The main grazer responsible for removing the rose-like morphology 
was the browsing parrotfish. Our findings demonstrate that CCA morphology is strongly 
influenced by herbivory pressure. The foliose form was highly susceptible to herbivory, 
while the crustose form remained resistant. These results support the hypothesis that 
herbivory is a key ecological force shaping the morphology of coralline algae. The 
morphology of CCA is at least partially controlled by herbivory pressure.
ad933203:
Indonesia, with over 17,000 islands, is home to 18% of the world’s coral reefs with the 60% of the world's coral species. Despite this status, coral reef management in Indonesia faces challenges due to outdated national coral reef data. The most recent national coral reefs dataset was produced in 2013. This data gap leads to reliance on the Coral reefs data by Allen Coral Atlas produces in 2023 which have limitations in accurately capturing the complex and diverse coral reefs environment across region in Indonesia.
To address this, the Ministry of Marine Affairs and Fisheries, in collaboration with the Indonesian Seagrass Mapping Partnership and Yayasan Konservasi Alam Nusantara, conducted a shallow-water benthic habitat analysis in 2025. This initiative aimed to map the distribution of coral reefs and seagrass across Indonesia using Sentinel-2 satellite imagery providing detailed and comprehensive update to the country's coral reef data. The resulting maps must be rigorously validated to ensure that the data are accurate and representative of all regions of Indonesia.
To validate this map, a validation and data collection process was conducted using a crowdsourcing method. This participatory process gathered input from 45 data contributors representing central and local government institutions, universities, practitioners, and NGOs that are involved in coral reef management, yielding 10,158 data points. The dataset comprises diverse data types, including coral and seagrass monitoring and ground truthing data, spanning from 2014 to 2025.
The crowdsource process ensures the map validation involving public participation from stakeholders nationwide.  The contingency table for the validation dataset shows random pattern, indicating no systematic misclassification relationship between the map model and the field validation points. Instead, the pattern highlights areas of reef extent that were not detected by the model, suggesting the need to refine reef boundaries and improve the coral and seagrass mapping analyses.
e2a5545e: Structural complexity underpins critical ecological functions on coral reefs and is central to multiple quantitative metrics used in reef assessment and monitoring. It is associated with high biodiversity, enhanced wave attenuation, and increased ecosystem resilience. Concerningly, coral reefs worldwide are experiencing an unprecedented decline in structural complexity and are shifting from live coral to rubble-dominated states due to the increasing frequency of harmful disturbance events. The geo-ecological health of a coral reef, defined as its capacity to maintain structural integrity, is closely linked to benthic structural complexity and represents an important metric in reef monitoring. However, complexity is often measured within a narrow range of spatial scales and methodological approaches, despite its strong scale dependence, and frequently overlooks the contribution of dead and mobile components to seabed rugosity. In this study, we investigated how structural complexity varies across spatial scales and assessed the contribution of key benthic classes to overall seabed rugosity. Structure-from-Motion photogrammetry was used to generate sub-centimetre-resolution orthomosaics and digital elevation models for two contrasting reef environments at Heron Reef: one dominated by rubble and one dominated by complex branching coral. Seabed structural complexity was quantified using the Vector Ruggedness Measure across nine spatial scales, and benthic classes (including coral rubble as a distinct class) were classified using Object-based Image Analysis. Results revealed that roughness increased logarithmically with spatial scale. Higher-magnitude roughness at coral-dominated locations occurred at finer scales, whereas the highest roughness values at rubble-dominated locations occurred at broader scales.
