Although the greatest structural complexity was associated with coral-dominated areas, coral rubble contributed between 18% and 48% of total benthic roughness across all scales. These findings demonstrate that rubble can contribute substantially to reef structural complexity and highlight the importance of including non-living and mobile components in future assessments of reef structural change.
e1b90da5:
Planktivorous fishes are an important component of coral reef ecosystems, transferring energy from plankton to higher trophic levels. Yet, despite their importance, the spatial patterns of planktivorous fish biomass and the factors influencing them in Indonesia remain relatively poorly understood, limiting our ability to predict how climate, anthropogenic pressures, and changing reef conditions may affect coral reef trophic dynamics. This study aims to identify the key environmental and habitat drivers shaping variation in planktivorous fish biomass across coral reefs in Eastern Indonesia, distinguishing between small reef-associated and large shoaling planktivores.

We collected fish species biomass estimates through Underwater Visual Census (UVC) surveys at 84 sites and quantified hard coral cover from CPCe-processed benthic photographs at the same sites. We analyzed the relationships between biomass of small and large planktivorous fishes and biological, environmental (sea surface temperature, chlorophyll-a, photosynthetically active radiation - light, habitat heterogeneity, and Kd490), and human influence predictors using generalized linear mixed models (GLMMs). 

Our preliminary results show that small planktivores exhibit significant responses to environmental gradients. Turbidity (Kd490) has a significant negative effect on their biomass, while productivity indicators, including light availability, have significant positive effects. Coral cover is positively related to biomass across small, large, and total planktivores, and habitat heterogeneity shows negative relationships for small, large, and total planktivores. Fishing pressure appears weak and non-significant.

This study provides an understanding of how habitat conditions and environmental pressures shape the biomass of planktivorous fish in regions with high biodiversity and serves as a foundation for further analyses of ecosystem responses to climate change in a data-poor region.
8c9ab5ca: Tunda Island is one of small islands in the north-western of Java Island, Indonesia, with diverse corals, that is a potential source of coral larvae for coral recruitments for self-reef passive restorations. This study aims to collect coral spawning data in Tunda Island and to model their larval dispersal to estimate its contribution to surrounding recruitments. The spawning observations were done visually during night dive around full moon from January to November 2025 and the model were developed using OpenDrift. The results show that there were two coral spawning seasons, which were February to April and September to October.  November should still be a part of the spawning season, but bad weather made the spawning might be postponed or cancelled out. The most common coral to spawn were Porites lobata. The other corals that were observed spawning were Favia, Favites, Goniastrea, Galaxea, and Porites cylindrica. They were mostly observed to spawn between one day before full moon and five days after full moon. The larval dispersal model suggested the potential contribution of Tunda Island corals as the source of coral recruitments in surrounding areas up to 150 km south and 700 km east. Further study to prove this model includes the genetic analysis to estimate the gene flow along the current direction of larval dispersal.
5fc46dcf: Coral reefs are under increasing stress from rising seawater temperatures combined with nutrient enrichment from land-based sources (runoff, coastal development, sewage). Benthic Cyanobacterial Mats (BCMs) have become more prevalent on Caribbean reefs over the past two decades, and recent reports indicate rising abundance in the Indo-Pacific. BCMs host diverse microbial communities, yet how nutrients and temperature shape their composition in natural systems remains poorly understood. Our natural sampling settings were reefs and marine lakes of West Papua, Indonesia. We sampled reefs and marine lakes in West Papua, Indonesia. Eight reefs were surveyed across a gradient of water quality and human impact, and eight marine lakes - landlocked seawater bodies -which spanned a range of temperatures. Our study aimed to (1) characterize BCM microbial communities in Raja Ampat for the first time using visual surveys and 16S rRNA metabarcoding, and (2) test whether community composition shifts with nutrient levels and temperature. The BCMs microbial consortium across sites was not dominated by Cyanobacteriota, but by members of the Phylum Protobacteriota. A shared core microbiome was present across habitats (90% sample prevalence), with only 5% of unique ASVs present in each habitat. On reefs, mats near human activity (resorts, villages) showed elevated phosphate and dissolved inorganic nitrogen (DIN), with several Proteobacteria and Cyanobacteriota genera decreasing as phosphate and temperature increased. In marine lakes, all measured environmental variables influenced the community dispersion, with DIN, phosphate and salinity influencing the abundance of most Phyla. Our main findings indicate that BCMs in Raja Ampat are influenced by both nutrient enrichment and increased temperature, underscoring their potential as indicators of reef health under changing conditions. Monitoring their occurrence and community dynamics provides critical insight into ecosystem responses and can inform management strategies in this globally significant yet increasingly impacted region.
fee002c9: Following the discovery that personal care products may harm corals, many cosmetics companies, particularly within the sunscreen sector, have marketed and labelled products as “reef safe” without adequate validation, as there are no standardised verification methods or regulatory oversight that currently exists. This has resulted in potentially misleading or unsubstantiated claims that may increase or obscure risks to the environment. Notably, 48% of sunscreens labelled as “reef safe” still contain ingredients potentially toxic to corals. We thus introduce the Reef Protection Factor (RPF), a standardised, three-tier certification framework comprising of bronze, silver, and gold ratings. The RPF certification framework allows for both the scientific assessment and an easily understood assignment of a safety level for any cosmetic product, indicating its likely impact on corals and other reef-associated invertebrates. The RPF evaluation protocol and ranking criteria are both transparent and rigorous. The method is also practical for widespread adoption, as testing costs are low. We anticipate that this will support broad implementation of the RPF system and ensure that the framework is accessible to companies of all sizes.
0c09c6d6: Coral reefs are among the most diverse marine ecosystems, and their physical structure is recognized as a key element for maintaining fish assemblages at a global scale. While studies conducted in the Caribbean and Indo-Pacific have demonstrated clear relationships between the structural complexity provided by branching corals and high levels of fish diversity, little is known about how these complexity-diversity relationships manifest in the Southwestern Atlantic. Reefs in this region are considered structurally simpler, as they exhibit overall low coral cover and are dominated by massive coral growth forms. Our study aimed to evaluate how structural complexity mediated by coral cover influences fish richness, abundance, and biomass across 20 degrees of latitude along the southwestern Atlantic coast of Brazil, encompassing biogenic, sedimentary and metamorphic reef habitats. We integrated metrics of rugosity, refuge availability, and topographic height variation to understand how gradients of structural complexity shape the attributes of the associated fish assemblages. Our results indicate that complexity play a role for fish assemblage attributes but only in areas with higher coral cover, particularly in regions where massive corals form more consolidated three-dimensional structures. These patterns suggest that, even in a reef system characterized by relatively low coral abundance compared to other tropical regions, corals continue to play a central role in sustaining reef fish assemblages. Given the increasing thermal disturbances that have been reducing coral cover in the Southwestern Atlantic, understanding how structural integrity influences reef fishes along the Brazilian coast is fundamental to ground conservation and management strategies in one of the most unique reef regions in the world.
458bea92:
Tubastraea aurea is an azooxanthellate, non-reef-building scleractinian coral widely distributed in the Indo-Pacific. Although this species reproduces sexually through brooding and asexually via extra-tentacular budding, the environmental drivers governing its clonal propagation remain insufficiently characterized. Given that climate change is increasing the frequency and intensity of marine heatwaves, clarifying how temperature influences asexual reproduction is essential for understanding the persistence of T. aurea populations and the dynamics of coral communities in warming oceans.
This study provides the first systematic evaluation of temperature-dependent budding performance in T. aurea. After polyp separation, individual polyps were maintained under five thermal treatments (21 °C, 23 °C, 25 °C, 27 °C, and 29 °C). Budding rates exhibited clear thermal sensitivity. Polyps at 25 °C showed the highest budding output, with one-way ANOVA indicating significantly greater bud production than all other treatments (p < 0.05). The 27 °C treatment also yielded significantly more buds than the 21 °C and 23 °C groups. In contrast, more than half of the polyps at ≤ 23 °C produced no buds, whereas the 25 °C group had the greatest proportion of individuals generating three or more buds.
Temporal analyses revealed that budding persisted throughout the experimental period at ≤ 25 °C, while performance declined sharply at 27 °C and 29 °C after 16 and 20 weeks, respectively. These patterns indicate a clear temperature-dependent trade-off, in which prolonged exposure to elevated temperatures suppresses sustained asexual reproduction.
Overall, this study advances understanding of the thermal limits of T. aurea’s budding capacity and provides important implications for predicting the species’ reproductive resilience under future ocean warming scenarios.

Keywords: Budding behavior; Climate change; Tubastraea aurea
5353189c: Historical underwater photographs provide rare windows into the past condition of coral reefs. These archives offer empirical site-specific data for reconstructing ecosystem baselines and improving the evidence base available to managers and policy makers for adopting conservation strategies. Despite their inherent value, these ‘dark data’ are being lost to the ages due to a lack of suitable infrastructure and community effort to organise, curate, and preserve these archives. As part of a new initiative to build a sustainable, open-access repository for legacy reef imagery and metadata, we showcase a digitised collection of 1,480 photographs taken by Eileen Graham in the mid-1960s near Discovery Bay, Jamaica. These images—now freely accessible through the UK Natural History Museum’s Data Portal—include precise spatial and temporal information and document reef communities before their widespread decline in the 1980s and prior to the establishment of long-term Caribbean monitoring programmes.  These archives range from fine-scale close-ups of individual corals to broad reefscape views. The collection captures ecologically meaningful features such as benthic assemblage composition, coral cover, and structural complexity. We propose developing a global network of historical reef-image repositories, capitalising on the unique opportunity presented by the first generation of SCUBA-enabled scientists who are now retiring and seeking long-term stewardship for their photographic records. Our experience highlights the importance of working directly with photographers to document site locations, dive histories, and contextual details while this knowledge is still accessible. Finally, we emphasise the essential role of Museums and Archives as enduring custodians of these materials—unlike short-term research projects, these institutions can provide long-term stability, dedicated curation expertise, and open-data infrastructures to ensure that historical reef imagery remains accessible for future scientific, conservation, and educational use.
d339d965: Recent advances in geochemistry have enabled the detection of photosymbiosis in fossil corals using geochemical proxies (e.g. 15N/14N). While promising, its application requires rarely available exceptionally-preserved specimens, particularly for aragonitic scleractinian corals. Consequently, upscaling its application to all extinct coral taxa is unfeasible. In contrast, morphological characters are documented for nearly all described taxa. Here, using the full diversity of extant corals (>1700 species) for which photosymbiotic state is known, we test whether objectively measured morphological traits (e.g. corallite width and integration, coloniality, growth form) can reliably predict photosymbiotic state across taxonomic levels. Our analyses extend previous work with far greater taxonomic, spatial, and environmental coverage. We present a preliminary classification framework for inferring the photosymbiotic state of extinct corals and evaluate its validity using independent evidence from the published literature, including available geochemical proxy data. Together, our results offer insight into the capacity of morphological traits to serve as a robust and scalable approach for reconstructing photosymbiosis in fossil scleractinian corals across evolutionary timescales.
c88c876d:
An innovative marine stewardship model is emerging in Zenadth Kes (Torres Strait Islands), co-developed by the Envirotech–Coralz Group and centred on the Indigenous-led PuipiKak Malu Indigenous Corporation. PuipiKak Malu serves as the organisational hub for a growing ecosystem of island-based groups dedicated to cultural and language revitalisation, marine stewardship, community enterprise, and the transmission of traditional ecological knowledge. This model positions Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities as leaders in inter- and transdisciplinary conservation and development programs across communities, islands, and the wider region.
The model directly responds to a persistent barrier to effective marine conservation and restoration: the shortage of accredited, skilled professionals able to integrate scientific and Indigenous knowledge in practice. Overcoming this barrier requires training systems that empower communities and authorities with recognised skills, cultural legitimacy, and practical socio-economic pathways to lead stewardship of their marine and coastal environments.
A defining pillar is the integration of accredited vocational training, particularly the Marine Habitat Conservation and Restoration qualifications, a unique, nationally accredited and globally recognised vocational and technical training program developed by Coralz and delivered by Envirotech Education with PuipiKak Malu as a community delivery partner. Co-designed and co-delivered with Elders, rangers, youth, and local leaders, these pathways cultivate Indigenous trainers, create employment in conservation and in training delivery, and enable Indigenous organisations to ultimately hold the intellectual property of co-designed training programs embedding traditional knowledge, safeguarding cultural and Indigenous intellectual property while strengthening governance autonomy.
Training is grounded in applied, participatory research that documents Indigenous practices, languages, values, and relationships with species and habitats. Each cohort becomes a platform for research, cultural practice, community planning, and new island-led initiatives.
Anchored in Indigenous leadership, the model is scalable and transferable, offering a culturally grounded and technical pathway for transformative social-ecological systems of Indigenous seascapes.
dec38f47: Over the last decade, Guam’s reefs have been impacted by successive coral bleaching events, causing a significant decline in live coral cover. Following the decline of Acropora pulchra, Pavona decussata appears to have increased in relative abundance, often overgrowing A. pulchra skeletons. The physiological mechanisms and symbiont dynamics that drive this ecological shift remain unclear. In this study, I conducted an ex situ heat stress experiment to evaluate the metabolic responses of A. pulchra and P. decussata dominated by either Cladocopium or Durusdinium. Coral fragments were exposed to control or heat treatment conditions, and monitored over four timepoints for changes in net photosynthesis, respiration, and Symbiodiniaceae community compositions using closed-chamber respirometry and qPCR. Results showed that A. pulchra dominated by Cladocopium experienced significant declines in photosynthesis and relative symbiont abundance under heat stress. In contrast, A. pulchra fragments dominated by Durusdinium maintained stable metabolic function and symbiont composition across treatments. Pavona decussata dominated by Cladocopium also maintained relatively stable function and symbiont abundance across treatments. Multivariate analyses confirmed that physiological variation was more strongly associated with coral species-symbiont pairings than with treatment alone. These findings offer a physiological basis for observed shifts in coral community composition on Guam’s reefs. This study contributes to management and restoration efforts by providing crucial information on the role of Symbiodiniaceae in thermal stress on Guam’s reefs.
62e3dd8b: Ancient coral reefs are rich archives of historical process and vital baselines for future development. Coring gives a window into the growth of ancient coral reefs and the fossil coral community’s response to paleoenvironment. Well NK-1 drilled from Meiji Reef is the longest scientific core with the highest recovery in the South China Sea. This study focused on the upper section of the Well NK-1 accumulated during the Holocene. From 8200 yr BP, Holocene reef initiated on the substrates of Pleistocene limestone and stopped accreting vertically at 4800 yr BP. A total of 11 coral genera and 16 Acropora species were identified from the Holocene segment of the core. The diversity of coral communities had an impact on reef growth. Coral communities with less genera and absolute predominance of branching Acropora were corresponded with a rapid vertical accumulation of reef framework with higher accretion rate. Acropora corals with rapid growth and dispersion by fragmentation were principal reef-builders during the Holocene, with A.valida being the most prevalent coral species. The highest Acropora species richness occurred at the geological era of 5400-5300 yr BP when reef accumulated with the fastest upward accretion rate of 23.1mm yr-1. This intricate and detailed pattern presented a new and novel model for the development of Holocene reef, indicating that the coral community and key reef builders were crucial to reef growth. These findings are extremely helpful for understanding the current ecological state of modern coral reefs as well as their potential response to global climate change.
d3e3b578: Quaternary fossil reefs provide critical insights into climate and sea-level fluctuations and their effects on reef ecosystem, but data from glacial lowstands and transitional periods remain scarce. Submerged fossil reefs offer a unique record of these events, particularly along rapidly subsiding margins like Hawai’i, where expanded reef sequences have the potential to provide higher resolution records of sea-level and reef growth histories. In 2023, IODP Expedition 389 (Hawaiian Drowned Reefs) drilled 35 holes into fossil reef terraces around Hawai’i at depths of 132–1242 m, penetrating up to 75 m and recovering 425 m of core with 66% average recovery. These cores span the last 500 kyr and primarily consist of reef frameworks composed of corals, coralline algal crusts, and microbialite. We documented the taxonomy, growth form, and context (in situ vs. non in situ) of fossil corals, coralline crust thickness, bioerosion intensity, and vermetid and Homotrema abundances. We show Porites has been the dominant reef builder for at least 500 kyr with varied growth forms: branching, columnar, (sub)massive, and laminar. The main associated coral taxa include Cyphastrea, Pocillopora, Montipora, and Agariciidae. Eleven distinct coral assemblages indicate environments ranging from shallow (<20 m) to lower mesophotic (>60 m) depths. Thick successions of branching and columnar Porites on two distinct leeward terraces suggest similar responses to sea-level rise during climate cycles 200 kyr apart. Laminar corals are more common on windward sites, possibly reflecting higher energy or deeper paleowater conditions. Small encrusting Cyphastrea colonies consistently occur within thick algal crusts, while Acropora appears only at one windward site, implying long-term rarity in Hawai’i. Lithologic and fossil trends at reef tops are complex, lacking a single drowning signature, although several holes on the windward margin and South Point are capped by rhodoliths.
f0d09e43:
It is common for fishes to aggregate on coral reefs for the purpose of spawning. The objective of this study was to address the physical and biological drivers influencing the formation of a multispecies fish aggregation and evaluate existing paradigms regarding the causal factors influencing aggregation. A persistent fish aggregation site was identified on the seaward side adjacent a narrow reef pass in the Great Barrier Reef. During flood tides when oceanic water travels through the reef pass into the coral reef lagoon, hundreds of large fishes from many species aggregated. 

This study selected three conspicuous species and showed each species displayed a clear and consistent pattern of annual aggregation that persisted over the 13-year investigation. The within year periodicity of aggregation was maintained despite interannual fluctuations in sea temperature and major perturbations including cyclones and coral bleaching. Seawater temperature and daylength were the proximate cues for a benthic invertebrate feeding haemulid and an omnivorous acanthurid forming seasonal aggregations. In contrast flooding tidal phase was the dominant cue towards the piscivorous lutjanid forming daily aggregations all year. The behavioural study concluded, that the haemulid aggregated to reproduce while the lutjanid aggregated to forage, although seasonal reproduction appears likely. Further, the omnivorous acanthurid aggregated seasonally to forage and reproduce. 

The daily and seasonal hydrodynamic cycles produced clear patterns of aggregation for fishes to perform vital tasks such as feeding and reproduction. We found spawning fishes coincided aggregation with flood tides to release gametes with slack water and maximise chances of newly formed larvae being advected rapidly from reef planktivores. Aggregation sites created by geomorphology and predictable
hydrodynamics produced predictable fish behaviour, critical to coral reef ecosystems by concentrating and connecting fishes. We conclude that the biological importance of small-scale reef passes in the Great Barrier Reef has been underestimated.
26c3f43c: The current oxidative model of coral bleaching holds that elevated temperatures trigger the production of reactive oxygen species by algal symbionts, ultimately leading to nonspecific oxidative stress and toxicity in the host coral. Another possibility is that the regulatory pathways involved in bleaching may be modulated through transient protein oxidation. To test this theory, we used an oxidative proteomics approach to track changes in the oxidative state of the cysteine proteome during heat stress in the model organism Exaiptasia diaphana. Both symbiotic and aposymbiotic Exaiptasia were exposed to a heat ramp from 26 to 34 °C over 10 days, and the oxidative state of cysteines was assessed at the beginning, during, and at the end of the heat ramp. A large diversity of cysteine-containing proteins was identified, and their involvement in regulatory pathways was inferred through homology mapping to the human interactome. As expected, the preliminary results show that several proteins associated with the host mitochondria and the endoplasmic reticulum underwent significant changes in their oxidative state between control and heat-stressed individuals. By integrating oxidative protein modifications with the regulatory pathways in which these proteins operate, we aim to determine whether a molecular regulatory cascade could be initiated during bleaching, and to better understand the molecular mechanisms underlying bleaching.
52837a41: Mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs) have gained interest because of both their refuge potential and their ability to host unique and distinct biological communities. In terms of reef-building corals (order Scleractinia), Leptoseris is among the most dominant genera at lower mesophotic depths throughout the Indo-Pacific. Interestingly, based on conventional taxonomy, many of the reported species at mesophotic depths appear to be depth-generalists; however, detailed molecular studies targeting mesophotic members of these genera remain limited. While traditional molecular markers have helped delineate species in both genera, assessments have been hindered by their limited resolution; these can now be conducted in greater detail using reduced-representation genome sequencing approaches. Here, we provide a phylogenomic assessment of the dominant mesophotic species in the genus Leptoseris (based on nextRAD sequencing) and their associated endosymbionts (through ITS2 amplicon sequencing). All nominal species were successfully resolved; however, substantial undescribed diversity was uncovered across three of the four focal species, concerning sympatrically occurring and mesophotic-associated lineages. Species delimitation analyses confirmed the presence of several undescribed species, which are now awaiting further taxonomic assessment. Regarding the endosymbiont community, we observed almost exclusive association with the genus Cladocopium, comprising 17 ITS2 profiles grouped into four clusters. Although symbiont types were shared across depths, statistical analyses indicated a certain degree of host-symbiont specificity and significant variations influenced by geographical and ecological factors. Overall, this study sheds light on the hidden diversity of this ecologically important genus.
8f577044: As the ocean temperatures become more frequent and extreme, coral reef ecosystems face increasing threats from climate-change-driven bleaching events. In the 2024 global bleaching event, several Philippine reefs suffered moderate to severe bleaching. As climate stressors persist, disturbances now exceed the natural ability of corals to adapt, leaving reefs highly vulnerable. This study examined coral assemblage before (2023), during (2024), and after bleaching (2025) in five reef sites in Iba, Zambales, Western Philippines. Data processing focused on colony density, coral composition, colony sizes of key taxa, and the size classes most affected by bleaching. In situ temperature data, satellite-derived sea surface temperature (SST) and other physical drivers were also analyzed. In 2023, the coral assemblage exhibited high colony density, dominated by acroporiids and massive Porites. By 2024, 66-80% of coral colonies had bleached, with acroporiids, Favites and Dipsastraea experiencing severe bleaching (40-100%), while massive Porites and Galaxea showed lower bleaching (14-40%). In 2025, Iba reef sites lost half of their coral density (except at BAN02), accompanied by shifts in coral taxa dominance favoring stress-tolerant corals (e.g., massive Porites and Favites) compared to 2023. The size frequency distribution also shifted toward smaller sizes, and partial mortality was observed for most corals. Temperature loggers recorded 1–2°C higher maxdailySST from March-July 2024 than in 2023, although data gaps occurred from lost/damaged loggers. Additionally, satellite-derived average dailySST in 2024 was significantly higher than in 2023 (p=0.002) and 2025 (p<0.001). Coral assemblages in Iba reef experienced pronounced shifts following the 2024 bleaching event, with select sites showing resilience. In the absence of further perturbations, surviving coral colonies exhibiting thermal tolerance may have the potential to replace lost colonies. Nevertheless, the recovery rates remain uncertain; thus, continued monitoring and adaptive management are essential to assess long-term recovery and guide reef conservation and restoration.
bb1dbb33:
Coral reef ecosystems are increasingly threatened by escalating thermal stress causing recurrent bleaching events and local stressors that compromise coral survival. Therefore, coral restoration initiatives have been increasingly implemented worldwide. In the Maldives, a country that relies on coral reefs and with 50+ coral restoration projects, 𝘈𝘤𝘳𝘰𝘱𝘰𝘳𝘢 𝘮𝘶𝘳𝘪𝘤𝘢𝘵𝘢 has been used as a target species for restoration due to its elevated survivorship and fast growth rate in nurseries. However, long-term molecular monitoring studies following the fate of outplanted colonies to natural reefs remain scarce. This study investigates the two-year transcriptomic response of the common Indo-Pacific coral 𝘈. 𝘮𝘶𝘳𝘪𝘤𝘢𝘵𝘢 following its successful outplantation from a restoration nursery site to natural coral reef area in Magoodhoo island, Faafu Atoll, Maldives. 
Seven outplanted colonies were monitored for two years, and samples collected 3 times a year for a total of 6 sampling times. Illumina RNA-Seq data were subsequently analysed with bioinformatics pipelines in Linux and R. eggNOG was used to obtain GO, COG and KEGG annotation. Analysis of differential gene expression and enrichment revealed that 𝘈. 𝘮𝘶𝘳𝘪𝘤𝘢𝘵𝘢 colonies sampled few months following their transplantation, activated cellular pathways related to stress response and cellular regulation. This appeared to be consistent with the outplantation in a different environment compared to the nursery resulting in an adaptive molecular response. Over subsequent months, colonies showed a progressive physiological stabilization, with enhancement of genes involved in growth and metabolism. Colonies were also followed across the 2024 mass bleaching event which also severely affected the Maldives. Surprisingly, most of the colonies survived showing an interesting plasticity and transcriptomic response. 
Overall, our findings elucidated the transcriptomic responses of the corals after their transplantation to natural reefs, and support the selection of 𝘈. 𝘮𝘶𝘳𝘪𝘤𝘢𝘵𝘢 as a valid candidate for restoration projects, possessing a plastic transcriptomic response, enabling a successful long-term survivorship.
b6fefe5e:
Coral reefs worldwide confront escalating threats, characterized by increased sea water temperatures due to climate change, resulting in mass coral bleaching events. Endosymbiosis with Symbiodiniaceae algae and bacteria is fundamental for coral survivorship and adaptation to environmental stressors. However, how the spatial-temporal dynamics, inter- and intra-specific difference influence these communities is still not clear.
In this study, we present a multi-site, temporally resolved research focused on algal symbiont and microbiome characterisation of two common reef building coral species, Porites lutea and Acropora muricata, in three different Maldivian islands (Magoodhoo in Faafu Atoll and Thudufushi, and Athuruga in Ari Atoll), across the 2024 mass-bleaching event. Specifically, more than 80 colonies were tagged and sampled before, during and after the bleaching event, and the different fates of bleaching, recovery, or mortality were identified.  In addition, using ITS2 and 16S metabarcoding, we characterized the diversity and composition of both symbiotic microalgae and bacterial communities, aiming to identify community patterns associated with coral survivorship and post-bleaching recovery. We analysed data using established bioinformatic pipelines, specifically using SymPortal for the taxonomic assignment of Symbiodiniaceae. We evaluated temporal and special patterns in microbial and algal symbiont density, focusing also on functional profiles. 
Our results showed as the two different species displayed a different bleaching susceptibility and even within the same species, corals from different locations responded differently to the temperature increase. Moreover, community-level analyses further suggest shifts in both bacterial and Symbiodiniaceae assemblages across bleaching stages, with emerging patterns that may be linked to differential resilience and recovery trajectories.
Overall, our findings highlighted as both local condition and coral’s endosymbiosis may play a role in coral holobiont tolerance and resilience. This study sheds light on the role of these symbioses in tropical corals and how their reorganisation after extreme thermal stress may impact their health.
fa2639e3:
Submitter has requested this only be considered as a POSTER.

The coral communities (i.e., soft and hard corals) in Korean waters at the northern limit of their high-latitude Asia-Pacific marginal distribution zone are thriving, exhibiting high resistance and successful adaptation to their comparably stressful environment, specifically the unfavorable winter water temperature. Here, by photographic documentation, we report the first demise of soft corals by a severe slumping event. Slumping in organisms without exo- or endoskeleton is herein coined as the loss of structural integrity by collapse of their hydrostatic skeleton, leading from drooping to deflation, death, and disintegration. We propose the term “slumping event” to describe the observed phenomenon, which affected at least several species of non-symbiotic octocorallian soft corals of the family Nephtheidae. We define four major physiologically abnormal morphological stages involved in the soft coral slumping event, namely the slumping stage, the flaccid stage, the dangle stage, and the disintegration stage. General causes of hydroskeletal deflation are briefly discussed and compared to in-situ, multiple-year-comparison of summer sea surface temperature and salinity data. For future quantifications of salinity conditions during observed soft coral slumping events, we propose the introduction of the metric Degree Freshening Week (DFW) as a measure of hypo-saline stress accumulated over time, similar to the already well-established Degree Heating Week (DHW) measuring heat stress on hard coral reefs.
3ce81393:
Coral reef ecosystems, such as those surrounding Okinawa Island in southern Japan, have high levels of biodiversity, especially among benthic communities. Over the last century, major coastal development and reclamation works have been carried out along the coast of Okinawa Island, and there are concerns about their adverse effects on the communities of organisms living in the coral reef areas. Examples of methods for investigating the
distribution and ecology of organisms that live on the seabed, as well as environmental impacts, include several transect and photo quadrat techniques. However, such survey methods aim to roughly grasp the benthic biota at a spatial scale that does not target relatively small (< 5 cm) benthic organisms. Therefore, in this study, we focused on the microenvironment and conducted surveys of benthic organisms using quadrats of 0.0025 m ² (5 cm × 5 cm) (hereinafter referred to as micro-quadrats). The results from our micro-quadrats survey revealed significant differences in benthic communities between reclaimed and non-reclaimed seafloor areas. Sites categorized as non-reclaimed were more likely to have hard coral, ascidians, and CCA (crustose coralline algae) present, and that sites categorized as land reclamation were more likely to have sand present, suggesting a potential increase in sedimentation rates. By focusing on more minute scales than conventional methods, this study demonstrates the importance of understanding the biota in microhabitats at different spatial scales.
1c8c5f3f: Previous studies have attempted to characterize metabolic and physiological diversity within Symbiodiniaceae to better establish what factors influence host specificity, maintenance, and resilience in the cnidarian-algal partnership. These studies are not easily comparable across the literature due to regular changes in taxonomic definitions, inconsistent data collection parameters, and restricted examination of only a few Symbiodiniaceae representatives. A novel cell culture device, Resipher™, measures oxygen consumption rate (OCR) in a continuous and non-invasive manner of cells in a 96-well plate format using a patented fluorescent technology. Because of this device’s capacity for high-sample throughput, we extended Resipher’s application by illuminating the plate from below with LEDs to measure photosynthesis and dark respiration during defined photoperiods. Our modified Resipher protocol was then used to compare physiology across S. natans (CCMP 2548), S. linucheae (SSA01), B. minutum (SSB01), C. goreaui (C1), D. trenchii (Mf2.2b), and E. voratum (SSE01). By treating each sample with metabolic inhibitors DCMU, rotenone, propyl gallate, and SHAM, we were able to identify differences in how each species and strain exploits various metabolic pathways in the chloroplasts and mitochondria. Additionally, we identified differences in Symbiodiniaceae physiology when simulating in situ symbiosome conditions like increased acidity and nutrient enrichment. Finally, we found variations in photosynthesis and respiration rates obtained under environmental stressors such as increased temperature. While research has shown that the cnidarian-algal symbiosis is dependent on several factors including the flexibility in host specificity, the stability of environmental conditions, and the availability of Symbiodiniaceae populations in a given geographic region, the cellular and molecular pathways involved in this interaction remains elusive. Our results highlight the extensive diversity in symbiont metabolism and physiology that impact the abilities of each species to infect its host and/or resist stress.
e96a9526:
Effective coral reef restoration and research require monitoring tools that capture biological and ecological change across multiple scales such as from individual coral colonies to entire reefs. Photogrammetry enables this multi-scale approach, but its strongest value emerges when the technology is intentionally applied at the scales most relevant to the underlying questions or focus of the project. Sharing examples from the Restore with Resilience initiative in Hawai‘i and the CORDAP-supported Reefs of Hope project in Fiji, we highlight how combining individual coral to the site-scale photogrammetry provides a more complete understanding of reef dynamics than any single scale alone.

For example, individual coral 3D models have been used to quantify fine-scale growth, morphology, bleaching severity, and geometric changes during restoration and thermal tolerance studies. These models reveal biological changes that cannot be measured as accurately in 2D images or using other survey methods. At the reef site scale, 3D models and imagery provide essential context for understanding spatial patterns, outplant placement and survivorship, micro-habitat formation, and broader ecological responses.

Findings show that multi-scale workflows uncover variability in growth rates, survivorship, species performance, and reef-level outcomes that would be missed by single-scale monitoring. They also demonstrate that relevant metrics derived from 3D models and imagery serve as more sensitive indicators of early restoration success or experimental responses than traditional methods. Additionally, photogrammetry has streamlined logistical efficiency while improving the accuracy and precision of information collected. The power of photogrammetry for coral reefs lies not simply in producing 3D models, but in applying the right tool at the most relevant scale for the question of interest.
501f9920: Coral reefs worldwide are increasingly impacted by climate-driven disturbances that are rising in both frequency and severity, reshaping community composition and reducing ecological function. Understanding the environmental and ecological drivers in shaping population size structure is key to identifying demographic processes that underlie these shifts. In our study, we examine the size structure and spatial distribution of functionally important branching coral taxa across the lagoon in Moorea, French Polynesia. We analyze population distributions in conjunction with a large environmental dataset to test correlations between coral size structure and abiotic and biotic variables with linear models. Our variables included fish functional groups, nutrient concentrations, temperature, and spatial position around the island. We find that median colony size is significantly predicted by benthic macroalgae cover and island spatial positioning.  Coral colony density was also heavily influenced by macroalgal cover, and additionally, the average daily temperature range and density of excavating fish species. Our results support previous work underlining the importance of macroalgae presence in inhibiting coral settlement, health, and survivorship. We take this a step further to highlight specific environmental variables that strongly influence specific coral size classes, such as the density of excavating herbivorous fishes, which was found to be a significant predictor for juvenile coral colony density. Additionally, we examine these coral distributions across coarse size bins and use a K-means cluster analysis to determine whether these populations fall into predictable groups. We report the mean differences between distributions across cluster pairs using a Kolmogorov–Smirnov (KS) test. This study provides a current look into branching coral populations around the island of Moorea, following several large-scale disturbances in the last decade. This work underscores the importance of linking coral size classes to environmental conditions to further understand long-term changes in coral populations under ongoing climate stress.
284c747b: Coral skeleton cores from the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) provide an untapped molecular record of reef microbiomes spanning major disturbance and recovery events. While geochemical proxies in massive Porites spp. colonies have long revealed environmental change, the persistence of ancient DNA (aDNA) within coral skeletons opens new opportunities to directly examine historical coral–microbe associations through time. Here, we apply aDNA extraction and metagenomic sequencing to dated Porites cores from inshore and offshore reefs of the central GBR. These archives capture bleaching and flood events extending back more than 250 years. In parallel, we will capture high-resolution geochemical profiles from micro-milled transects of the same Porites cores to reconstruct past temperature, freshwater flood plume events, and nutrient dynamics, providing the environmental context to target distinct disturbance events for the aDNA-based holobiont reconstructions. By recovering metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs), we reconstruct the taxonomic and functional composition of coral-associated microbiomes and symbiont associations before, during, and after major stress events such as the 1998 and 2002 mass bleaching events. Comparisons with extant Porites microbiomes from the same localities will allow us to test whether microbial community structure and functional potential have shifted through time and whether certain taxa persist as long-term symbionts or stress-responsive opportunists. Our work will contribute to emerging frameworks that integrate ancient DNA, geochemistry, and modern ecology to reconstruct coral holobiont evolution and resilience.
18c915d4: The scientific understanding of coral physiology has expanded to encompass the coral holobiont, including the coral-algal symbiosis. This encompasses the coral host and its diverse microbial associates, including the algal symbionts (Family Symbiodiniaceae), bacteria, archaea, fungi, viruses, and microeukaryotes. Within this framework, the role of bacteria in supporting algal growth, survival, and function remains largely speculative. Although some studies suggest that these symbionts rely on bacterial partners, experimental evidence confirming whether such relationships are essential or facultative remains limited. Microbial partners may influence nutrient cycling, thermal tolerance, or can be leveraged in microbiome engineering, such as probiotic development to enhance coral resilience under disease stress. Axenic cultures of coral-associated Symbiodiniaceae are rarely available due to the difficulty of removing bacteria without compromising algal viability. Cultures of Symbiodinium, Breviolum, and Durusdinium are being developed using multistep antibiotic treatments. Pilot trials indicate substantial reductions in bacterial load following a single exposure, with algal cells remaining viable and photosynthetically active. Refinements included sequential treatments in liquid and solid media, followed by recovery in antibiotic-free conditions. Bacterial presence will be screened for via plating, PCR, and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Using axenic cultures, bacterial isolates derived from parent cultures and corals will be introduced individually and in groups to evaluate their impact on algal growth and physiology. Functional assays will screen isolates for traits relevant to symbiosis, including nitrogen fixation, phosphate solubilization, phytohormone production, siderophore activity, and vitamin biosynthesis. Confocal, SEM, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging will visualize bacterial-algal interactions, distinguishing surface associations from potential internal colonization. It is hypothesized that isolates expressing traits that improve nutrient availability or promote algal cell growth will enhance algal performance and form closer associations with host cells. Understanding these microbial contributions to Symbiodiniaceae function could reshape approaches to coral restoration, holobiont manipulation, and microbial intervention strategies.
c70d9747: Outbreaks of Crown-of-Thorns Seastars are a major contributor to coral reef degradation in the Indo-Pacific, but their localised impacts on fish assemblages remain poorly understood. The current study examined the relationship between Crown of Thorns Seastar densities and reef fish assemblages in Koh Tao, situated in The Gulf of Thailand. Data was analysed using Bayesian generalised additive models. Seastar population densities exceeded regional outbreak thresholds across three sites over a five-year period, indicating sustained outbreaks, but effects on fish communities were generally subtle and context dependent. Total fish abundance showed a weak positive association with seastar density, though with high uncertainty. At the functional group level, herbivores, particularly Scarus spp. showed a moderate positive response, potentially indicating changes to substrate composition associated with Acanthaster sp. predation and subsequent increased food availability such as with macroalgal abundance following coral mortality. Contradictory to previous research, coral-associated Chaetodon spp. showed a weak positive trend with increasing seastar density, providing insights into the relative impact of Acanthaster sp. on coral reef ecosystems in the region. Overall, outbreaks of Crown of Thorns Seastar may influence specific fish taxa but are unlikely to cause wholescale restructuring of reef fish assemblages at the observed outbreak densities. These findings enhance the understanding of ecological dynamics driven by Acanthaster sp. outbreaks in the Gulf of Thailand.
431b294a:
Global warming and ocean acidification are intensifying chemical stressors in coral reef ecosystems, where shifts in carbonate chemistry can alter coral calcification, physiological performance, and sensitivity to other environmental pressures. To characterize such chemical exposures, reliable quantification of carbonate parameters in small-volume samples is essential. Total alkalinity (TA), in particular, provides a well-constrained indicator of seawater buffering capacity and acidification-related changes. This study evaluates the applicability of a compact titration-based detection system for characterizing carbonate chemistry in environmentally sensitive reef environments. 
To support microscale and field-based studies, we developed a compact centrifugal CD-type microfluidic device integrated with an ion-sensitive field-effect transistor (ISFET) pH sensor. The prototype performs automated stepwise titration by sequentially releasing titrant aliquots from micro reservoirs through rotation-controlled actuation, enabling pump-free reagent delivery at microliter resolution. This configuration allows small-volume titration measurements while maintaining a simple and portable analytical platform.
As a proof-of-concept, 300 µL of 0.1 mol/L Na2CO3 solution was titrated with sequential 4 µL aliquots of 0.1 mol/L HCl. The system produced clear and reproducible equivalence points consistent with theoretical expectations, confirming its suitability for controlled carbonate chemistry analysis. Building on this foundation, we aim to extend the system toward TA determination in natural seawater by optimizing reagent delivery using a 3D microchannel structure and adapting the workflow for the ionic composition of seawater. These developments will provide a robust, low-volume, pump-free titration platform for future applications in coral reef chemical monitoring.
91bbdd85: Predators, such as pufferfish, are key for maintaining trophic balance, controlling the population of primary consumers (e.g., sea urchins), but the ecological role of tetraodontiforms and their impact on the trophic chain of reef ecosystems remain understudied. This work analyzed the distribution pattern and habitat use of Diodontidae and Tetraodontidae individuals, focusing on the description of the behavior of the common spiny pufferfish (Chilomycterus spinosus) in rocky shores of southeastern Brazil. Data was obtained at four sampling sites (Prainha, Praia do Forno, Pedra Vermelha and Fortaleza) using free diving. To characterize tetraodontiforms abundance, 20 timed censuses (10 min) were performed, counting all pufferfish individuals within a 2m wide strip. Habitat was characterized on 30x30 cm images extracted from six videotransects of each site. For the behavioral patterns analysis, individuals of C. spinosus were observed for 10 minutes, with all actions recorded. Complementary, we analyze the pufferfish's prey detection method using a prey detection assay with five treatments: for visual detection, two transparent containers containing a sea urchin were placed, one open and one closed, along with a fake sea urchin. For olfactory detection, two opaque containers with holes, one containing a sea urchin and the other containing algae, were attached. Finally, an opaque, empty container was used as a neutral control. In total, benthic cover was dominated by macroalgae and turf, besides sand at the reef interface, and pufferfish showed higher association with non-consolidate substrate. We observed nine different behaviors: “swimming”, “non-agonistic interaction”, “investigation”, “chewing”, “static”, “inflation”, “regurgitation”, “biting”, and “open mouth”. The most observed behavior was “swimming”, with approximately 58.9 ± 84.1s. The pufferfish proved to be non-aggressive animals, interacting with nine other fish species. Prey detection assays indicated that C. spinosus is a visually oriented predator which applied energy conservative strategies, possibly targeting high energy content prey.
12c2a8f6:
Coral reefs provide critical ecosystem services but are increasingly threatened by climate-induced bleaching. Reefs around the world, including Mauritius Island, Western Indian Ocean suffered from a severe bleaching event in 2024-2025. The aim of this study was to assess species responses in nursery-grown corals through evaluation of bleaching severity, mortality, and early recovery of corals to inform restoration.  

Three nursery species, namely, Acropora muricata, A. selago, and Pocillopora damicornis, were surveyed. Surveys were conducted during the bleaching event and ten weeks post bleaching. Bleaching severity was scored using a standardized scale (Obura 2014), where corals were classified as: Healthy = 1, Pale = 2, 0–20% bleached = 3, 20–50% bleached = 4, 50–80% bleached = 5, 80–100% bleached = 6, and Dead = 7. Fragment size was categorized as small (<5 cm), medium (5-10 cm), or large (>10 cm). Mortality and recovery were recorded post bleaching. The non-parametric tests Kruskal–Wallis was used for species and size comparisons.

Bleaching severity differed significantly among species (p < 0.001), with A. muricata showing the highest scores, A. selago intermediate, and P. damicornis the lowest. Recovery patterns were species-specific: P. damicornis showed a significant decrease in bleaching severity (p < 0.001), whereas Acropora species worsened (A. muricata, p < 0.001; A. selago, p = 0.028). Smaller fragments (<5 cm) experienced higher bleaching and mortality than medium-sized fragments (p = 0.044). 

These findings indicate that bleaching susceptibility and early recovery are species and size-dependent and modulated by local conditions. Restoration should prioritize thermally tolerant species, medium to large-sized fragments, and environmental matching to enhance coral survival and reef resilience in Mauritius and similar shallow lagoonal reef systems.
dcd45830: Fishing communities are increasingly affected by climate-related pressures, yet their vulnerability and adaptive responses are shaped by complex sociocultural dynamics. We previously found that intersectional dimensions are rarely addressed in gender mainstreaming policy, creating critical gaps in understanding community-level impacts and adaptive capacities. Here, we further deepen our understanding, employing a mixed-methods design to analyse how gender, religion, and marital status intersect with (i) domestic roles and responsibilities, (ii) resource dependency, (iii) access to and control over resources, and (iv) adaptive capacity in the context of small-scale fisheries. We integrate household surveys and focus group discussions to examine how multilayered social identities shape gendered roles in fishing activities and responses to climate stressors. The findings demonstrate that intersecting forms of social inequality produce differentiated vulnerability profiles and distinct adaptive pathways within a coastal community. By combining quantitative and qualitative approaches, our study offers a comprehensive account of how structural and identity-based factors condition climate adaptation in coastal fisheries. These insights aim to inform the development of equitable, gender-responsive strategies to strengthen resilience in local fishing communities.
02983ccf: The intensification of thermal anomalies, combined with local pressures, has reshaped our understanding of coral species’ thermal tolerance potential on a global scale. Here, through detailed monitoring, we individually tracked the health trajectories of eight reef-building species along Brazil’s northeastern coast before, during, and after the fourth global mass-bleaching event. We found striking differences in species vulnerability, largely driven by intrinsic life-history traits and minimal fine-scale spatial variability. Generalist species such as Montastraea cavernosa and Siderastrea spp. showed high thermotolerance, with mortality of only 10% and 14%, respectively. In contrast, Millepora alcicornis, Mussismilia harttii, and Agaricia humilis approached their survival limits, each exceeding 90% mortality. These species, however, exhibited distinct bleaching and mortality trajectories over time. No variation in the response of mature colonies of different sizes was detected for M. alcicornis and M. cavernosa, consistent with previous events and suggesting potential barriers to population recovery. Mortality rates for Mussismilia hispida (78%), Solymia wellsi (70%), and Porites astreoides (48%) were intermediate to high, raising concerns about their future under increasingly frequent thermal extremes, even for species previously considered thermotolerant. Using historical monitoring and exposure to varying Degree Heating Week levels, we evaluated the responses of five species and found that escalating thermal stress has pushed several close to their survival thresholds. This pattern reinforces ongoing biodiversity loss and the likelihood that reefs will become increasingly dominated by thermotolerant taxa. We also documented a predominance of filamentous and turf algae on dead skeletons, highlighting potential challenges for the recovery of severely affected populations. Overall, our findings underscore the vulnerability of South Atlantic coastal reefs to climate change, reveal species-specific differences in resilience, and provide critical insights for management actions and the reassessment of conservation priorities.
2e3c5214: Corals engage in a mutualistic symbiosis with intracellular photosynthetic dinoflagellates. This intimate partnership forms the trophic and structural foundation of coral reef ecosystems. This presentation will examine the cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying the establishment, maintenance and breakdown of the symbiosis in coral- and sea anemone-dinoflagellate partnerships. The talk will over two principal areas of inter-partner regulation. First, host innate immunity and symbiont strategies for modulating this immune response are central to the stability of the symbiosis. These innate immune mechanisms have been a key focus of the Weis group and will be summarized during the presentation. Second will be inter-partner regulation of biomass: how the partners keep pace with one another as the host grows and the algal population increases, will be presented as another focus of the Weis Lab. Finally the presentation will cover the development of the model organism Aiptasia as key tool for discovery in coral biology, aimed at accelerating solutions for assisting in the coral reef crisis.
cf4d7f71:
Coral populations are being progressively thinned by climate change, which elevates the 
risk of reproductive failure from Allee effects during fertilization. Studies have shown that fertilization success improves during more intense and synchronized spawning, but the local dependence of fertilization on coral density remains unknown in wild populations. In a series of experimental and observational studies in Australia and Palau, incorporating three species of Acropora, we find strong evidence of spatial Allee effects. In A. hyacinthus, fertilization averaged 30% when colonies were in close proximity (<0.5 m), but this declined rapidly to less than 10% at a separation of 10 m and virtually zero by 15 to 20 m. While colony spacing is consistently important in driving fertilization outcomes, other factors, including depth and current velocity, exert important impacts on fertilization success. Paternity assignments verify that most coral fertilization events occur between colonies found within 3 m of one another. To explore the long-term consequences of Allee effects we incorporated our results into a complex and field-validated simulation model (ReefMod) and projected coral futures under the current emissions trajectory. We find that Allee effects hasten the onset of local (within-reef) population extinction by a decade. Early extinction is less likely in reefs with greater larval connectivity. In principle, reactive restoration can mitigate Allee effects and we discuss the scope of such actions to help maintain coral population viability.
ac08983c:
Scalable microbiome interventions for coral reefs are hindered by the uncertain long-term residency of administered probiotics, limiting their durable benefits. To address this bottleneck, we introduce a novel selection framework for next-generation probiotics that prioritizes evolutionary genomic signatures predictive of persistent host association. We first identify candidates based on hallmarks of a trajectory toward host dependency, such as insertion sequence expansion and metabolic gene pseudogenization, and then empirically test for a functional benefit.

Applied to the cosmopolitan genus Ruegeria, this framework identified population MC10. A controlled laboratory assay in the model cnidarian Exaiptasia diaphana showed that MC10 inoculation was associated with a significant increase in host thermal tolerance. Crucially, in an 8-month field trial with Acropora pruinosa, a one-month, twice-weekly nursery inoculation enabled MC10 to persist in coral tissues throughout the monitoring period without reapplication. During a natural bleaching event, MC10-inoculated corals exhibited reduced visual bleaching and higher retention of photosynthetic efficiency than placebo controls. Key characteristics of MC10, including its unanticipated H2O2 sensitivity in a laboratory setting, would have disqualified it from conventional probiotic screens that prioritize robust in vitro performance. Supporting multi-omics data reveal that MC10 possesses a unique functional profile, including enhanced biofilm formation and a host-induced “motility-to-sessility” proteomic shift, consistent with stable colonization. 

This work provides a proof-of-concept for an evolution-guided pipeline that selects probiotics for innate colonization potential. By aligning microbial intervention with host-microbe coevolution, we address the key scalability bottleneck of persistence, offering a generalizable approach to developing effective probiotics for science-informed reef restoration.
81299635: Host-microbe symbioses form a central part of coral biology since corals rely on their associated symbionts for organismal functioning and survival. In the face of escalating environmental stressors, the targeted manipulation of microbial communities emerges as a potential intervention to enhance coral resilience. While probiotic inoculation has demonstrated potential to improve recovery and survival following coral bleaching, the mechanisms driving coral-bacteria interactions remain poorly understood. This is partly due to the complexity of coral microbiomes and a lack of standardized diagnostics to assess the impact of microbial modifications. Here, we present a standardized screening framework using the cnidarian model Aiptasia, integrating bacterial isolate libraries, phenotype pre-screening, and CBASS acute thermal assays to identify beneficial bacterial strains. Through this effort, we could identify bacterial isolates that increased Aiptasia heat tolerance by up to 1.4 °C. One candidate, Microbacterium ginsengisoli, is a robust probiotic candidate that consistently increased thermal tolerance across Aiptasia strains, suggesting that some bacteria may exert beneficial effects across host boundaries. These findings establish a platform to dissect the molecular mechanisms underlying microbiome-mediated thermal tolerance. Ultimately, this approach may contribute to developing targeted probiotic therapies aimed at enhancing coral resilience, thereby supporting ongoing efforts in coral reef conservation.
14dde036: Coral reefs stand out as one of the most ecologically, environmentally, and socioeconomically important ecosystems in the marine realm, offering a myriad of direct ecosystem services and provisions to millions of people worldwide. However, rapid environmental change and escalating human pressures on coastal zones are threatening the enduring survival of coral reefs. It has become evident that relying solely on climate mitigation may not be sufficient to safeguard the future viability of corals and coral reefs. Simultaneously, targeted active restoration actions are deemed necessary, but the long-term sustainability of current efforts raises uncertainties. Environmental factors consistently hold a pivotal role in determining restoration success, yet they are underreported in the existing reef restoration literature; indeed, we find that 55% of restoration initiatives appear to report no consideration of environmental parameters in their approaches. Using a global meta-analysis, we show that integrating environmental considerations into restoration strategies will reduce the risk of restoration failure, and accelerate innovation towards the development of reefs-of-tomorrow – which work with environmental change rather than working against it. Although not without challenges, we demonstrate that embracing an environmentally-aware perspective when using the full continuum of restoration intervention approaches, from passive protection to active restoration with novel intervention, has the potential to enhance the long-term sustainability of corals and coral reef ecosystems.
68c86d3f: Coastal systems are in decline, prompting repeated calls for their restoration to rebuild biodiversity and to ensure the continued provision of valuable services. Many restoration projects initially require a source of individuals or propagules, generally from an intact donor system. However, very few efforts consider the impact that extraction has on the donor system in assessing overall restoration success. If the structure and function returned by the restoration does not replace or exceed that which was lost from the donor system, the act of restoration may lead to a net negative outcome—a phenomenon we refer to as the “donor paradox” of restorarion. Here, we define the restoration donor paradox and consider the ecological and ecosystem consequences of extractive restoration. We then empirically evaluate the donor paradox using IKI’s Coral Carib case study in the Dominican Republic, Central Caribbean. Finally, we provide recommendations for practitioners to mitigate or reduce the impacts of the donor paradox when planning future restorations efforts.
bf9295c8:
Coral bleaching is devastating coral cover globally with projections of losing >90% by 2050 if global warming exceeds 2°C above pre-industrial levels. The Global Tipping Points Report 2025 has highlighted that coral reefs passed their tipping point, emphasizing the urgent need for action to protect these vital ecosystems. Current coral reef restoration is criticized due to the limited evidence supporting their long-term success and a lack of long-term monitoring. A rational design of approaches to assess restoration success based on research-informed criteria is a critical gap in supporting biodiversity conservation and restoration. As such, their efficacy could be significantly enhanced through evidence-based, science-informed strategies, especially in regions with limited access to advanced technologies.

In collaboration with SeaChange Indonesia, we have established a model restoration site in Gili Asahan, where we assess the impact of thermal pre-screening and resilient donor-based microbiome transplantation on long-term survival and restoration success. We are further developing a standardized monitoring framework. This framework incorporates growth/survival tracking, automated underwater image analysis, and eDNA-based approaches to measure long-term increase in reef biomass, micro- and macro-biodiversity, and ecosystem services. 

Our goal is to develop an adaptable and scalable framework for coral reef restoration grounded in science. By prioritizing practical, real-world transformative, and evidence-based interventions aiming at a high return of investment, we hope to build a replicable model that can contribute effectively to supporting reef preservation at scale through adapting research-based coral conservation and restoration to local community stake holding.
47379cf4:
South Florida  is fortunate to have notable populations of parrotfishes along its reef tract, a boon that simultaneously represents both an ecological asset and a significant obstacle for coral reef restoration in Palm Beach, Broward, and Miami-Dade counties. Although parrotfishes play an important role in maintaining reef health through herbivory, they also exert intense predation pressure on newly outplanted corals. During the first six months post-transplantation, parrotfish grazing can severely damage or remove juvenile corals, leading to markedly reduced survivorship of  40%  in some instances. This loss threatens restoration success and dramatically increases the labor, time, and financial investment required to meet long-term restoration goals. The challenge is expected to grow as restoration programs increasingly rely on ex-situ–reared corals.

Current deterrent methods, such as mesh barriers or temporary exclusion cages, have shown limited effectiveness. They often delay predation rather than prevent it and become too costly, cumbersome, or inefficient for utilization at scales required to meet restoration targets. To address this need, this project evaluates a marine-biodegradable predator exclusion device constructed from polyhydroxyalkanoate (PHA) and engineered specifically to prevent parrotfish predation while minimizing ecological footprint and logistical burden.

A preliminary trial conducted during 2022–2023 yielded promising results for two massive coral species, Orbicella faveolata and Porites astreoides. Building on those findings, this study will assess a mass-produced version of the device—the Coral Fort™—using a more comprehensive experimental design. Metrics will include , frequency and severity of predation events, growth rates based on linear extension, and overall survivorship. A parallel cost-benefit analysis will compare labor hours, material costs, cost per surviving transplant, and cost per cm^2 of living tissue for corals transplanted with and without the device. The results will inform restoration practitioners and stakeholders about the practicality, scalability, and value of incorporating the Coral Fort™ into coral outplanting strategies.
2ebf1722: Back-to-back marine heatwaves in 2023–2024 caused significant bleaching and localized mortality in shallow reefs of the Central Red Sea. This impact reinforced an urgent need for climate-smart, mechanistically grounded restoration approaches capable of re-establishing structure, function and reef resilience. These reefs often include vertical walls, habitats for which purpose-built restoration methods remain limited. We present the first implementation of an integrated, multi-taxa microbiome-informed restoration program tailored to post-bleaching Red Sea conditions. Our approach combines: (i) outplanting using standardized Mars Assisted Reef Restoration System (MARRS) over dead reef substrates, paired with microbial therapies to enhance fragment survivorship; (ii) testing MARRS prototype modules adapted for coral outplanting on vertical faces; and (iii) a multi-taxa assemblage restoring successional processes and functional redundancy, including Pocillopora favosa, Acropora hyacinthus, and Acropora cf. plantaginea as a first-line assemblage, with an expansion into a broader diversity-line, plus soft corals, holothurians, tridacnids, and sponges. Slow-release probiotics will support outplant survival and long-term resilience while restoring key microbial functions. Collection and husbandry of donor fragments began in November 2024 and continued throughout 2025 in coral nurseries (trees and mid-water tables). Coral performance under elevated summer temperatures in 2025 helped guide the selection of first-line and diversity-line species. Outplanting efforts commenced in November 2025, with around 800 fragments deployed and ~6,000 planned for early 2026. Monitoring integrates paired non-restored controls, Structure-from-Motion photogrammetry, pulse-amplitude-modulated fluorometry, high-frequency physicochemical logging, organic matter flux,  and Autonomous Reef Monitoring Structures (ARMS) to assess early community reassembly. This study establishes a field platform to test multi-taxa, holobiont-level interventions while evaluating a scalable approach for restoring vertical reef walls in the Red Sea. By integrating engineering innovation, microbiome modulation, and ecological succession, we aim to accelerate reef recovery, develop transferable approaches for climate-resilient restoration,  and strengthen multi-institutional collaborations for broader implementation.
6dde73cd: Filipino small scale commercial fishers (SSCFs) are losing access to key fishing grounds in the Western Philippine Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) due to encroachment and harassment by foreign naval, militia, and coastguard vessels, putting their safety and livelihoods at risk. This issue has recently intensified, as conflicts between China and the Philippines over areas of the South China Sea within the Philippines’ EEZ have increased in frequency and severity. Like other artisanal fishers in the Southern Hemisphere, the livelihoods of Filipino SSCFs are closely intertwined with the health and geography of coral reefs.  In August 2025, we conducted semi-structured interviews and focus group discussions with 92 SSCFs across seven fishing communities in the provinces of Palawan and Zambales to systematically document the socioeconomic impacts of foreign vessel encroachment. Our results highlight the ways that intersectional engagement with coral reef fishing communities can inform more comprehensive approaches to the mitigation of hazards for fishers and contribute to effective fisheries management. For instance, by stratifying our interviews by preferred gear type and target species, we identified important differences in exposure to and impacts from Chinese harassment, with implications for context-specific mitigation measures. We also stratified by seniority to capture the voices of crewmembers alongside captains and boat owners (cultural norms predispose figures of authority to be most vocal in community dialogues) and discovered income-based discrepancies in vulnerability and adaptive capacity to harassment impacts. Our findings reveal that SSCFs are non-monolithic. Socio-cultural, political, and economic dynamics within these diverse fishing communities play important roles in structuring vulnerability. It is critical to understand these discrepancies to implement inclusive management and mitigation approaches, particularly surrounding foreign fleet incursion. This effort was spearheaded by the Fish Right Program, a collaboration between the United States and Philippine governments geared towards improving marine biodiversity and the fisheries sector.
56baa3d7:
Climate change is affecting the socioeconomic conditions of coastal communities and has affected catch yields as well as exacerbating gender inequalities in fishing activities and livelihood shifts. Women have limited access to fisheries resources and a low level of climate knowledge. This study analyzes the relationship between gender roles, vulnerability of fishing households, traditional knowledge, and adaptive management strategies in facing climate change in 386 communities in the Maluku Province of Indonesia. We used  193 household surveys to measure vulnerability indices, 386 in-depth interviews to understand gender dynamics and traditional practices, coupled with field observation to identify adaptation patterns and 18 focus group discussions to explore gendered experiences in coping with climate change in fisheries livelihoods. We analyzed vulnerability components using Climate Change Vulnerability Assessments and qualitative analysis. Results indicate that the vulnerability of fishing households is strongly influenced by the division of gender roles,  resource access, and the level of integration of traditional knowledge in fisheries management practices. Women have less resource access but play an important role in strengthening adaptive capacity through household economic management and livelihood diversification. These findings highlight the need for more gender-explicit and locally informed fisheries policies to enhance the resilience of coastal communities to climate change.

Key words : climate change vulnerability,  fisheries household, gender dynamic, livelihood, 
traditional knowledge
47a1a22f:
The Republic of the Maldives comprises around 1,200 islands originating from corals, highly threatened marine organisms. Reefs sustain the livelihoods of the Maldivian population through ecosystem services, thus degradation translates directly into socio-economic impacts, including coastal erosion, sea-level rise, and the risk of population displacement. Yet, ocean literacy and environmental awareness on local islands are often limited, hampering the creation of a network of ocean guardians for a more sustainable ocean governance.
To address this gap, the community-based project "Playing with Corals” was launched in May 2024 in Faafu Atoll. It combines education, social engagement, and reef restoration using the Maldives' most popular sport, football, to raise awareness about marine conservation. Football serves as a tool to engage young people, build local capacity (SDG 4), improve physical health and enhance children's resilience (SDG 3), increase climate change awareness (SDG 13) and promote marine conservation (SDG 14).
The aim is to nurture a new generation of ocean stewards and to build local capacity through theoretical and practical workshops for local trainers, conducted twice a year by a team of researchers from the University of Milano-Bicocca and trainers from Inter Campus, the CSR department of FC Internazionale Milano. Thereafter, trainers carry out activities independently in their home islands. Marine-focused environmental games are integrated into football training sessions, and Inter Campus's educational principles help develop children’s personalities, promoting marine awareness and more positive attitudes towards the marine ecosystem. In reef restoration, reef stars are used as educational tools to foster a sense of ownership and collective responsibility. The project has propagated around 500 corals, conducted over 150 training sessions, reached 100 children aged 10 to 14, and 20 Maldivian trainers. Before-and-after questionnaires document changes in children’s environmental knowledge and closeness to the ocean, providing the basis for creating an exportable and replicable model.
