Recent transcriptomic analyses revealed that Vp infection triggers robust activation of cnidarian pattern recognition receptors belonging to the cGAS like receptor (cGLR) family, homologous to the mammalian cGAS STING axis. We have engineered stable human heterologous cell lines expressing individual cGLRs and employed reporter systems (IRF 3, NF κB) to quantify downstream signaling, second messenger production (2′3′ cGAMP, 3′3′ cGAMP), and STING phosphorylation. Inhibitor guided studies using a small molecule cGAS blocker (G140) allow us to assess the functional contribution of cGLRs in vivo during Vp challenge, integrating whole organism transcriptomics to map gene expression changes.  Parallel phylogenetic and site directed mutagenesis work targets evolutionarily conserved residues in cGLRs that appear essential in human cGAS, uncovering mechanistic convergence and potential regulatory elements of cnidarian immunity. Our findings will illuminate how ancient immune systems sense and defend against bacterial pathogens, providing insights into conserved residues that shape pathogen–host interactions across 600 M years of evolution.
9ddc6d50: The loss of corals in the wild and within coral nurseries in Florida during the summer of 2023 highlighted the importance of identifying resilient coral species and genotypes. New biomass is traditionally added to coral nurseries through acquiring either sexual recruits or wild collected corals of opportunity. However, keeping these corals in nurseries is labor intensive, and significant time and effort in coral care is often expended before practitioners select fit genotypes for incorporation into the restoration pipeline. This study aimed to identify coral traits indicative of resistance and/or resilience to environmental stressors through the evaluation of microbiomes and metabolomics during the collection, propagation, and grow-out of corals within an in-water nursery. Colpophyllia natans and Diploria labyrinthiformis corals of opportunity were collected from nearshore reefs in three regions of the Florida Keys. Survival analysis identified differences according to genotype, collection location, and initial size. Metabolomics showed significant differences initially by collection location with the upper keys site separating from the others. After 2 months in the coral nursery, metabolomes became more diverse as compared to initial collections, and the middle keys site now separated out. Because of the location specific differences in survival and metabolomics, we are identifying specific metabolites that may be associated with survival. Microbiome analysis revealed that communities were significantly influenced by genotypic differences and temporal variation. In addition, specific bacterial families were associated with survival in both C. natans and D. labyrinthiformis in the months after transport to the coral nursery. By identifying specific microbes and metabolites positively or negatively associated with coral survival, we can incorporate a new tool to select for potentially resilient corals for restoration.
0014299b:
Coral disease outbreaks have increased in occurrence and severity in the Caribbean. Stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD) has caused unprecedented coral mortality, and its etiology remains unresolved. SCTLD affects >30 coral species that differ in their associations with dinoflagellate endosymbionts (Family Symbiodiniaceae). Thus, symbiont lineage-level dynamics may underlie variation in holobiont disease phenotype. We analyzed 491 apparently healthy and SCTLD-affected corals in Florida (USA) and the United States Virgin Islands, representing nine coral species sampled in situ or during a SCTLD transmission experiment. Based on the internal transcribed spacer-2 (ITS-2) region of rDNA, Symbiodiniaceae composition varied significantly by coral species, disease susceptibility group, tissue health state, and region. In the corals Orbicella annularis and Colpophyllia natans, lesion-adjacent tissues had reduced abundances of a Symbiodinium and a Cladocopium lineage, whereas a Durusdinium lineage was enriched. Accounting for biogeography and host identity, our results suggest that in corals harboring multiple Symbiodiniaceae lineages, more susceptible lineages are lost first, leaving relatively resistant lineages in lesion-adjacent tissue until sloughing occurs. These findings indicate SCTLD acts as a selective filter on coral symbiont assemblages. Our results provide the first multi-species, multi-region evidence of fine-scale associations between Symbiodiniaceae lineages and SCTLD, establishing a framework for testing lineage-specific disease susceptibility in future experiments. 
We further examined Symbiodiniaceae and associated virus-like sequences and particles from an unknown coral tissue loss disease in the Flower Garden Banks (northwest Gulf of Mexico). We analyzed 30 apparently healthy and diseased Pseudodiploria strigosa corals sampled 1-7 months after the disease outbreak. Symbiodiniaceae composition was consistent; however, putative virus-like particles, replication structures, and sequences were associated with diseased tissue and/or histopathological indicators of the Flower Garden Banks disease based on electron micrographs and metatranscriptomes of diseased and healthy corals. Our findings highlight the diverse influences of microbes on coral disease phenotypes.
cea17fda: Octocorals are increasingly threatened by climate stress, yet the interplay between ocean warming and fungal infection remains experimentally underexplored. Here, we demonstrate that thermal stress exacerbates the pathogenicity of the fungus Aspergillus sydowii, driving physiological collapse and functional reorganization in the Red Sea octocoral Sclerophytum sp.. We deployed a 34-day mesocosm heat-stress experiment ramping 1°C per day from 28 to 33°C, holding at peak temperature for 10 days, then cooling at the same rate followed by a 7-day recovery phase. We quantified photosynthetic performance, oxygen fluxes, mortality, and taxonomic and functional microbiome shifts. Combined heat and fungal exposure proved highly lethal, causing 45% mortality compared to 7% under heat exposure alone. Re-isolation from diseased and dead nubbins implicates A. sydowii as the causal agent. Metagenomic profiling indicated that physiological collapse was underpinned by a functional transition from mutualism to antagonism in the microbiome. Specifically, combined heat and fungal stress triggered a depletion of ankyrin- and WD40-repeat proteins - hallmarks of symbiotic stability - concurrent with a surge in chitin degradation (e.g., enrichment in GH19 endochitinases and chitin deacetylation genes) and secondary metabolite biosynthesis potential. This functional shift was corroborated by the specific enrichment of chitinolytic taxa, including Chitinophagales and Rhodothermales after combined stress, suggesting a targeted microbiome response to the chitinous fungal invader. While surviving holobionts recovered full photosynthetic efficiency after combined stress, their microbiomes did not revert to baseline. Instead, they assembled into a taxonomically distinct configuration characterized by enrichments of sulfate-reducers (Thermodesulfobacteriota) and thermotolerant phototrophs (Thermosynechococcales), suggesting that while host physiology recovered, the internal chemical microenvironment and microbial community retained a legacy of the thermal and biotic stress. These findings highlight divergent responses of host physiology and microbiome composition, demonstrating that while some corals may survive severe climate-driven disease, they emerge as ecologically reorganized entities.
fcdd1b35: Coral reefs face mounting pressures from local and global stressors, yet the role of the complex coral microbiome network in mediating holobiont resilience remains underexplored. This study investigates how eutrophication (E), a chronic local stressor in Guam, influences coral holobiont resilience by examining the microbiome community assemblages of different functional groups in two reef-building scleractinian species – Porites cylindrica and Pavona decussata – which differ in life history traits and environmental sensitivity. This research utilized a one-year in situ reciprocal transplant experiment to assess how nutrient exposure (E) influences coral-associated microbial communities. Coral fragments were collected from two reef sites with contrasting nutrient levels: Tumon Bay (↑E) vs. Luminao (↓E) in western Guam, characterized using isotopic analyses of δ¹⁵N levels. Microbiome structure was assessed using molecular techniques, including rbcL gene barcoding for Ostreobium algae, ITS2 metabarcoding for Symbiodiniaceae algae, and 16S rRNA metabarcoding for bacterial communities. Coral health and fitness were assessed using ecological metrics, such as partial mortality. While Symbiodiniaceae associations remained highly specific and inflexible in both coral species, in situ monitoring indicates that P. decussata exhibits greater resilience with lower mortality and faster bacterial microbiome stabilization (dominance of Endozoicomonadaceae), whereas P. cylindrica experienced prolonged bacterial dysbiosis under similar environmental conditions. In particular, P. cylindrica corals transplanted from Luminao (↓E) to Tumon (↑E) showed lower resilience, suggesting sensitivity to nitrogen enrichment. In contrast, skeletal-associated microbiome communities (Ostreobium) remained compositionally and functionally stable under nutrient stress. Together, these findings highlight that coral holobionts respond to environmental stress in distinct yet interconnected ways. Tissue-associated bacteria are sensitive bioindicators of stress, while Symbiodiniaceae and Ostreobium display compositional stability but differ in functional responsiveness. This underscores the need to view the holobiont as a dynamic system to better predict coral resilience.
c31dcacb: Bacteria are a vital member of the coral holobiont, contributing to nutrient cycling, metabolic productivity, and defense. However, stressors can cause a shift in the microbiome from commensal to harmful, altering disease dynamics and host susceptibility. Vibrio coralliilyticus is a widely distributed marine bacteria that can cause acute coral tissue loss. Notably, strain OCN008 has been shown to cause acute Montipora white syndrome, a rapid tissue loss disease in the Pacific coral, Montipora capitata. Despite being an etiological agent that infects various corals, shellfish, and urchins, molecular mechanisms involved in the pathogenesis are not fully understood. However, the protein ToxR was found to be essential for V. coralliilyticus infection of corals and shellfish larvae. In other vibrios, ToxR is a regulatory DNA binding protein, which can alter transcription of a network of different genes in response to environmental conditions. In V. coralliilyticus, deletion of the toxR gene (ΔtoxR) attenuated virulence and increased the pathogen’s susceptibility to antimicrobials produced by probiotic bacteria. These results suggest that genes in the ToxR regulatory network are responsible for antimicrobial resistance and survival in interbacterial competition. Transcriptomic data of wild type V. coralliilyticus strain OCN008 identified differentially expressed genes compared to a ΔtoxR strain. Several outer membrane porins and the transcriptional regulator, LeuO, were found, suggesting that antibiotic resistance may be modulated via ToxR through membrane permeability, environmental sensing, and activation of downstream regulatory networks. To investigate these genes, in-frame deletions will be created and assessed for altered resistance to probiotics and antibiotics. These results will not only identify which genes drive V. coralliilyticus survivorship and reveal the underlying, environmentally regulated molecular mechanisms involved, it will  be the first genes  identified in  a coral pathogen to confer resistance to probiotics. Ultimately, this research aims to support targeted disease intervention strategies and promote coral holobiont health.
dae7e301: Ocean acidification (OA), a phenomenon resulting from diffusion of excess anthropogenic CO₂ into seawater, can negatively impact corals by increasing energetic costs of calcification. Oculina arbuscula is a facultatively symbiotic coral common on subtropical reefs of the South Atlantic Bight, USA, which exhibits resilience to OA. Both symbiotic and aposymbiotic O. arbuscula fragments can maintain calcification rates under OA despite having fundamentally different energetic budgets. However, the mechanisms allowing corals with contrasting nutritional modes to sustain calcification under acidified conditions remain unclear. This study investigated the impact of heterotrophy on calcification in aposymbiotic O. arbuscula under OA conditions in a 90-day laboratory experiment. Fragments were exposed to one of three feeding levels and a pH of either 7.8 (OA) or 8.1 (control). Metabolic chamber incubations, using the alkalinity anomaly technique combined with changes in dissolved oxygen, were performed at four different time points over the experiment to estimate instantaneous calcification and respiration rates. Net calcification rates were also measured via the buoyant weight technique. Results demonstrated that greater food consumption was associated with higher instantaneous and net calcification rates at the end of the experiment, independent of pH. Although both methods produced similar calcification estimates by the end of the experiment, it took time for the chamber-based calcification rates to diverge between the feeding treatments, indicating a delayed response to the different energetic regimes. Conversely, the effect of food supply was immediate on respiration rates, with higher respiration rates in the higher food treatments throughout the experiment. Respiration also showed a significant response to OA, with fragments exposed to low pH having lower respiration rates. The complex relationships between feeding, respiration, and calcification demonstrate how energetic balance is important in driving cellular calcification in O. arbuscula and how responses change through time.
6959713d: Coral nurseries are central to conservation and restoration efforts, providing platforms to accelerate reef recovery. However, they remain exposed to heatwaves, which compromise coral survival and fitness. Maintaining broad genetic and phenotypical diversity is essential for the long-term management of nursery-grown corals. Despite extensive research, mechanisms underlying differential thermotolerance among coral populations remain unresolved, in part due to the complexity of holobiont responses, which result from the integrated contributions of host, symbionts, and microbial communities shaped by environmental history. To investigate these dynamics, we used a standardized heat-stress assay (CBASS) to quantify thermal tolerance and holobiont responses in Acropora spp. from two Fijian reef sites with contrasting bleaching histories, Serua and Beqa. Fifty colonies per site were transplanted for six months to a common-garden nursery, after which 6–8 from each source underwent an 18-hour CBASS assay (ambient, +3°C, +6°C, +9°C). Photochemical efficiency (Fv/Fm) and reflectance (R) were measured with a Diving-PAM-II+mini-spectrometer. Fv/Fm was used to fit dose-response curves (DRC), while reflectance was used to estimate pigment profiles and tissue optical properties. Thermotolerance, expressed as ED50 from DRC, was similar between populations, yet several phenotypic traits showed significant Source×Temperature interactions. Beqa corals had higher initial pigment content and a linear decline in pigment and absorbance with heat-stress, suggesting strong baseline investment but limited flexibility. Serua corals began with lower pigment levels but displayed a hormetic response, with a modest increase at moderate temperatures before inhibition, consistent with inducible pigment-based protection and greater short-term acclimatory capacity. These contrasting strategies indicate that both populations achieve comparable thermal tolerance through different trait combinations, shaped by environmental history. Microbial community and host taxonomy analyses are underway to further contextualize these responses. Incorporating corals with contrasting trait signatures in restoration nurseries could enhance overall fitness and phenotypic diversity, supporting long-term reef resilience.
093e286b: Farmer damselfishes are ubiquitous coral reef fishes that maintain territories of palatable algae and have been found to have both positive and negative impacts on reef-building corals. However, the mechanisms of this variation in coral impact are not fully understood. Damselfish farming behavior can be classified as intensive (aggressive defense, small territory, long algal turf) or extensive (weaker defense, large territory, short algal turf). We hypothesized that farming intensity plays an important role in coral condition, as algal productivity could harm corals via competition and overgrowth, while territorial behavior could benefit corals via indirect protection from corallivores. We also aimed to assess how damselfish simultaneously influenced multiple aspects of coral condition: disease, predator damage, and live tissue cover. In Bonaire, Caribbean Netherlands, we assessed patches of coral for damselfish density, farming intensity (i.e., territorial behavior and algal turf length), and coral condition on two ecologically important corals: Acropora cervicornis and Orbicella spp. We found that damselfish density positively correlated with farming intensity in A. cervicornis, such that patches with greater damselfish density exhibited stronger territorial behavior per damselfish and had longer algal turf. Farming intensity was a better predictor of coral condition than damselfish density in A. cervicornis, but not Orbicella spp. We did not observe simultaneous positive and negative associations between damselfish and coral condition. Disease prevalence in both coral taxa and dead coral cover in A. cervicornis increased with damselfish density, but there was no evidence of increased predator protection. In fact, fireworm predation damage on A. cervicornis increased with damselfish density. Farmer damselfish have increased on coral reefs in recent decades and have been suggested to both help and harm coral restoration outcomes. This study improves our understanding of the relationship between farmer damselfish and corals and can help inform coral restoration planning in the Caribbean.
01827a5e: Effective conservation of reef manta rays (Modula alfredi) depends on understanding how habitat health influences population stability, particularly on isolated islands where local declines can't be offset through immigration. POPAN mark-recapture models were created using long-term photo-ID databases with 3,093 sightings from Guam (U.S. territory [2008–2024]) and the Federated States of Micronesia (Yap [2008–2025] and Pohnpei [2010–2025]). We investigated how coral reef habitats impact abundance, recruitment, and demographic structure. POPAN models consistently indicated high survival rates (φ ≈ 0.92–0.95), low entry rates (pent ≈ 0.05–0.06), and small abundance estimates (95% CI) of 68 for Guam (64–71), 79 for Yap (73–85), and 139 for Pohnpei (122–156). Populations seem stable, with detection probabilities varying by site (Guam = 0.68; Yap = 0.50; Pohnpei = 0.24), reflecting differences in habitat accessibility and behavioural predictability. Sex and maturity ratios were significantly skewed across all locations (Guam p = 0.0124; Yap p = 3.80e-09; Pohnpei p = 7.97e-18), indicating limited juvenile recruitment typical of a K-selected species. Model fit patterns matched habitat structure: Yap and Pohnpei showed strong model fit, where manta rays utilise lagoonal and channel cleaning stations within well-developed barrier and fringing reef systems. Guam demonstrated variability in detection, reflecting its more limited habitat, with overdispersion likely arising from manta use of fish-spawning aggregation feeding sites that fluctuate with lunar-driven shifts, though this mechanism remains to be tested. These differences show that manta population stability, detectability, and recruitment are fundamentally linked to coral reef complexity, cleaning station persistence, and prey availability. Micronesian manta rays show high site fidelity; the loss of even a single reef feature can cause population declines with no new individuals moving in to offset these losses. Therefore, habitat integrity is a key factor limiting the reef manta ray population across Micronesia.
469875b8: Macroalgal beds are increasingly recognised as important nursery habitats for coral reef fishes. Yet, the degree to which they function primarily as feeding grounds or as refuges from predation remains poorly understood. In this study, we examined the distribution, feeding behaviour, diet, and predation risk of juvenile rabbitfishes within macroalgal habitats at Orpheus Island, Great Barrier Reef. Juvenile Siganus doliatus were largely restricted to the macroalgal-rich reef flat, while adults were abundant on the reef crest. Juvenile S. doliatus primarily fed on epiphytes on Sargassum (80% of bites), with relatively few bites taken on turfs or Sargassum itself. Stomach content analyses showed juvenile S. doliatus primarily fed on filamentous algae. Similarly, the stomach contents of juvenile S. doliatus, S. canaliculatus, and S. spinus collected from macroalgal habitats were broadly comparable and dominated by filamentous algae and detritus. Interestingly, we detected no relationship between predator abundance and juvenile rabbitfish abundance across sites. Our results indicate that juvenile rabbitfishes use macroalgal habitats on this and potentially other inshore reefs as a food source, underscoring the importance of this habitat for juvenile reef fishes.
400137fe: A functional entity is defined as a unique combination of ecological or functional traits. Functional redundancy is a situation where many species perform similar functions. When a functional entity is represented by only one species, it is called functional vulnerability. In the present study, the artificial reef formed from a swimming pool submerged during the 26th December 2004 Tsunami was studied. Surveys of ichthyofaunal assemblages were conducted using fish belt transect and underwater visual census. Functional Diversity was assessed based on various ecological characteristics: Diet (11 categories), Activity Period (3 categories), Mobility (3 categories), Size (6 categories), Schooling pattern or gregariousness (5 categories) and Position in the water column (3 categories). A total of 151 species of fishes belonging to 88 genera and 47 families were recorded during the study. 106 functional entities were identified, with a mean of 1.42 species per entity. 75% of these entities had a single species representing them, indicating high functional vulnerability.  26 functional entities were redundant, expressed by a maximum of 5 species. The family Pomacentridae had the highest number of functional entities. Although functional richness increased strongly with species richness, several families deviated from this expectation. Some families exhibited higher functional richness than predicted, indicating functional diversification, while others showed lower-than-expected functional richness. A high functional vulnerability is prevalent in this region. It could be predicted that if there is species loss among these fishes, their functional role will also be lost, having a high impact on coral reef resilience. The results from this study can be applicable in similar cases around the world. This study is the first of its kind from this region.
2219171d:
Natural processes that support coral growth and resilience are becoming increasingly critical as reefs face accelerating decline and warming oceans. Planktivorous damselfishes (Pomacentridae) closely associated with branching corals excrete nitrogen (ammonium) and phosphorus wastes. These nutrients are essential for metabolic processes within the coral holobiont and for sustaining photosynthesis, yet their scarcity in reef systems limits coral growth. Corals hosting resident damselfish exhibit faster growth rates and support denser endosymbiont populations, indicating active utilisation of fish-derived nutrients. Because ammonium and phosphorus supplementation enhances bleaching tolerance and recovery, and given the ubiquity of damselfish–coral associations, this nutrient transfer from fish may strengthen coral resilience to heat stress disturbances.
I will first showcase results of current research which, for the first time, proves and quantifies the transfer of fish-derived ammonium to corals using stable isotope techniques. I will then share results from recent experiments that integrated field data with controlled laboratory work to test how fish-derived nutrients influence coral physiology under both normal and elevated temperatures. These findings demonstrate the probable role of resident damselfish as sustained nutrient sources for branching corals, with measurable physiological benefits evident even at low nutrient concentrations. This work highlights the importance of holistic reef management and reveals the potential for this research to drive innovative strategies in coral reef restoration and conservation that harness natural ecological interactions.
f3c9ffba: Progressive marine heatwaves are forcing consumer species to adapt their foraging strategies to meet metabolic demands across restructured resource landscapes. Coral bleaching events are impacting reef-associated species in profound ways, with corallivorous fishes—which rely on live coral for all or part of their diet—facing negative selective pressures as reefs become increasingly disturbed. The loss of live coral cover and declining nutritional quality of bleached corals amplifies the need for efficient foraging whilst simultaneously intensifying competition among foragers. Previous studies have shown that corallivorous species can adapt their foraging behaviour and investment in territoriality and sociality in response to changing resource availability, offering some flexibility to changing systems. Following a widespread coral bleaching event on the southern Great Barrier Reef in 2024, we conducted a manipulative field experiment at One Tree Island to examine 1) the foraging response of dominant corallivorous groups to varied prey condition, and 2) their investment in territoriality and tolerance to sociality. We offered bleached and healthy coral colonies to corallivores in situ and assessed their foraging patterns, rates of feeding, and interactive behaviour during the bleaching event. Our results reveal differential foraging responses among corallivorous groups to prey condition, with some species showing increased tolerance to conspecifics (sociality) whilst others expressed high rates of aggression (territoriality). These findings underscore the capacity of corallivores to behaviourally adapt to changing prey availability but contribute to mounting concern about the persistence of specialists in progressively disturbed reef systems.
b81227cf:
Coral reefs are experiencing increasing anthropogenic pressure worldwide, driving more frequent and severe mass bleaching events. As many reef fishes rely closely on coral structure and condition, understanding faunal responses to bleaching provides critical insight into how disturbances shape reef ecosystems. Corallivorous butterflyfishes (Chaetodon spp.), which depend directly on live Scleractinian corals, are among the taxa most likely to respond sensitively to coral health declines. Although tropical reefs often show strong, linear reductions in obligate corallivore abundance following bleaching, responses within subtropical reefs remains poorly documented, despite their rising ecological importance.
During the 2023/24 Austral summer, Norfolk Island in the subtropical South Pacific experienced its second recorded coral bleaching event within its lagoonal reef. Notably, local coral responses diverged from patterns commonly observed on tropical reefs, indicating possibility of similarly atypical responses among coral-dependent fishes. As bleaching in these marginal reef environments is expected to increase, understanding how associated taxa respond bears importance.
To investigate this, we conducted 15-min roving snorkeller surveys across six sampling periods between December 2023 and June 2025 (n = 10 surveys  per period) within Kingston Lagoon to quantify Chaetodon diversity, abundance, and assemblage structure before, during, and after bleaching. Multivariate analyses revealed shifts in species composition across sampling periods. Contrary to expectations from tropical reef studies, obligate corallivores remained stable following bleaching. In contrast, facultative species exhibited divergent, species-specific trajectories: Chaetodon auriga declined in abundance, whereas C. flavirostris and C. tricinctus increased during and after bleaching. These findings provide the first detailed assessment of corallivore responses to bleaching for this high-latitude ecosystem and suggest an unexpected degree of resilience among specialized coral-feeding fishes. The results also highlight that subtropical reefs may exhibit distinct ecological dynamics relative to tropical systems and underscore the importance of incorporating marginal reef environments into broader assessments of climate-driven disturbance impacts.
72878750: The Philippines, located at the apex of the Coral Triangle, harbors exceptionally high marine biodiversity, particularly within the Visayas Region, which has long been considered a hotspot for shorefish diversity. Despite this ecological prominence, the region continues to face substantial natural and anthropogenic pressures that threaten coral reef conditions. Butterflyfishes (Family Chaetodontidae), widely used as reef health indicators, do not always exhibit straightforward relationships with high coral cover, highlighting the need to examine broader ecological drivers. This study investigates how environmental factors shape spatial patterns in chaetodontid diversity and assemblage structure across the Central Philippines. Data were collected over a ten-year period (2010–2019) using standard non-destructive fish visual census techniques across 60 sites with triplicate surveys. A total of 36 chaetodontid species were recorded, with the lowest diversity observed in the Visayas Region and the highest along the eastern Philippine coast, consistent with the group’s hypothesized evolutionary origins in the Pacific. Chaetodon octofasciatus, Chelmon rostratus, and Chaetodon lunulatus were the most frequently encountered species, occurring at 87%, 67%, and 58% of surveyed sites, respectively. Cluster analysis of species composition and abundance revealed three major site groupings, which aligned with discriminant analysis results where 90.6% of predicted sites were correctly classified based on environmental variables. Macroalgal cover, fisher density and slope degree emerged as the primary environmental predictors differentiating assemblages. These findings reveal complex spatial patterns in chaetodontid communities and emphasize the influence of both ecological conditions and human pressures on reef-associated butterflyfish populations.
7c741f4b: Associations between herbivorous reef fishes, such as group formation, are well documented. However, the drivers of variation in these associations, and the functional consequences of variation in grouping behaviour are not well resolved. Social context can modify foraging behaviour, potentially cascading to effects on ecosystem functions including herbivory – which is critical for the resilience of rapidly changing reefs. Typical proxies for functions, such as biomass or fish densities, may not capture this behavioural variation. We examined how grouping behaviour of an abundant parrotfish species, Chlorurus sordidus, influences the critical function of herbivory on coral reefs in the Lakshadweep Archipelago and evaluated the role of density dependence and territorial aggression from competitors in driving group formation in the entire herbivorous fish assemblage. Feeding rates increased with group size and decreased with aggressive encounters, with parrotfish in larger groups consuming about 110% more algae per capita than solitary individuals.  Group foragers, which formed both single-species and multi-species groups, also benefitted marginally from reduced territorial aggression from competing herbivores. The propensities of herbivores to form groups, and their group sizes were positively density dependent, and, to a lesser extent, were driven by access to resources defended by territorial competitors. Our study highlights the importance of incorporating behavioural variation and interspecific associations in assessments and predictive frameworks of ecosystem functioning. As our results indicate, for social animals, key ecosystem functions are particularly sensitive to animal densities and interspecific interactions, especially when individual function varies with social context. Rapidly changing environments, with altered resource distributions, depleted fish densities, and altered intra- and inter-species associations could modify ecosystem functioning.
35c21235:
Large-area imagery, also known as photogrammetry, is increasingly used to reconstruct three-dimensional “digital twins” for studying and visualizing reef change over time. Once used solely by scientists at well-resourced universities, large-area imagery is on the verge of becoming mainstream in coral reef science, though uptake in the restoration community remains limited. More accessible technology, as well as streamlined and intuitive workflows, would expand practitioner access.

In 2024, Seatrees, the Scripps Institution of Oceanography, and the University of California, San Diego began collaborating with Samsung Electronics to develop a large-area imaging workflow grounded in accessible mobile technology for coral reef restoration. This includes developing a custom “Ocean Mode” camera setting on Samsung Galaxy devices to improve underwater image capture in accordance with existing large-area imaging needs, as well as an Android app (“Oasis”) to streamline workflows for data collection, organization, 3D model reconstruction, visualization, and analysis.

Restoration practitioners in Bali, Fiji, Florida, Costa Rica, and the Galapagos are currently using Galaxy devices to monitor change over time in land-based nurseries and outplanting sites. Scripps scientists and their partners have also tested device functionality across multiple research expeditions. By evaluating performance across multiple use-cases and environmental conditions, this collaboration has leveraged field tests to further refine Ocean Mode and Oasis. Current priorities include automating time-intensive analysis workflows and streamlining the user experience based on restoration practitioner feedback. This will enable restoration practitioners to use large-area imagery for quantifying reef change in response to restoration efforts, producing actionable insights for restoration and management.

Co-designing technology with a community of users, developers and scientists has been the key driver of success to date. This collaboration is an example of how industry can authentically support restoration and conservation efforts by providing expertise, hardware, engineering resources, and sustained support to make technology more accessible to practitioners.
d3c0e594:
Large-area imaging (LAI) of coral reef ecosystems has become a widespread methodology used by scientists, resource managers, and restoration practitioners to document the benthos of coral reefs. International objectives in monitoring biodiversity and restoration outcomes can be met in part through image-based benthic data, which is notable for its accessibility to broad audiences, transparency in ecosystem monitoring, and comparability between data sources. Despite continual advancements in this methodology and growing international engagement in the field, fragmented innovation and isolated access to certain datasets or workflows remain as challenges. Given the significant quantity of existing LAI data and its inherent strengths when applied to monitoring and stewardship, an opportunity exists to rapidly advance the scale, access, and interoperability of image-based benthic data through targeted investment from the private sector. 

We seek to address these accessibility challenges and advance the collaborative strengths of LAI through a regional case study approach supported by the Delta Electronics Foundation. The foundation has invested in collaborative infrastructure for coral reef science and monitoring, enabling stakeholders to build a technology-enabled community of practice for LAI in Taiwan. To this end, we have produced training materials, hosted proactive workshops, integrated multi-source datasets, and developed interactive geospatial visualizations to facilitate the wider use of LAI data. This collaboration between academia, non-profits, and the private sector highlights how cross-sectoral partnerships can improve the accessibility, transparency, and standardization of data through the scaling of both computational infrastructure and community engagement. With strategic support, the private sector can create the enabling conditions for shared capacity and innovation. This talk will discuss the benefits and challenges associated with building this technology-enabled community of practice and regional hub of expertise in Taiwan, and share key takeaways regarding how cross-sectoral partnerships are positioned to advance coral reef science and management.
a60a890b: Coral reef ecosystems are complex acoustic environments, dominated by fish and cryptic invertebrates. Hardware innovations over the past two decades have enabled passive acoustic monitoring (PAM) to become a promising cost-effective and scalable remote sensing method for studying ecosystem function and health. However, most coral reef acoustic studies have used non-directional hydrophones, which makes it difficult to associate particular sounds with particular coral structures. Acoustic vector sensors are compact sensor packages that measure both acoustic pressure and particle velocity, which allows them to measure the arrival azimuths and elevations of sounds over a broad range of frequencies. Here we present observations using acoustic vector sensors to characterize the spatial and temporal organization of sounds from several coral reefs off the Hawai'ian Islands. Initial pilot studies in 2019 established that a small network of these sensors could map 2-D positions of sound sources over 100 m by 100 m regions of reef.  However, at the time the sensors could only process frequencies up to 500 Hz, which limited the analysis to a subset of fish sounds. In 2024 and 2025 we applied new-generation sensors to study coral reef directional characteristics at frequencies up to 7 kHz, a bandwidth that includes invertebrates as well as fish. In partnership with NOAA and the Hawai'ian Institute of Marine Biology (HIMB), we have collected a few weeks of directional acoustic datasets at multiple reefs off O'ahu and Maui. The vector sensors mapped directionality of snapping shrimp activity between 1-7 kHz and fish activity between 1-4 kHz. The temporal scale of our deployments allowed us to note the directionality of diel acoustic patterns in the reefs. Our results suggest that the judicious placement of relatively few acoustic sensors can monitor biological reef activity over hundreds of square meters.
0506f928: Microfluidic/Mesofluidic systems commenced a new era of coral research, offering precise experimental condition control while reducing material consumption, and minimizing negative impacts on natural environments. Comparing to tank-operated experiments, the reduced size also allows continuous microscopy during experiments at unprecedented spatial-temporal resolutions and ensures greater homogeneity of environmental factors among samples. Here we present a new coral chips that enables long-term (over a month), stable coral cultivation in the laboratory without laborious maintenance. Important culturing factors including temperature, water flow, salinity, nutrition, and pathogen inoculation were precisely controlled in the coral chip, enabling efficient investigation on the growth and health of the cultured corals. We demonstrated the usefulness of the mesofluidic system by examining the resilience of the cultured coral under stress of heat, pathogen, and salinity.  By employing coral microcolonies, i.e., small coral fragments, our multiple-well system is suitable for a wide spectrum of corals and is compatible with various analyses, compared to polyp-based microfluidic systems in previous studies. The miniaturized system presented here considerably extends the applicability of existing coral-cultivation chips and provides a convenient, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly platform for future coral studies and restorations.
986fbefa:
Conventional coral restoration heavily depends on fragmentation, which inherently injures donor colonies and limits scalability. To address this critical bottleneck, we present a regenerative medicine-inspired, minimally invasive propagation strategy that enables polyp harvesting without breaking coral branches. Building on mechanistic parallels between vertebrate osteogenesis and coral calcification, we leveraged titanium (Ti) - a material known for its osteoconductive properties - as a functional substrate to induce spontaneous polyp migration.
In our approach, small Ti rods (“clips”) were placed in direct contact with the surface tissue of Acropora branches. Polyps consistently extended onto the Ti within several days, and by approximately four weeks, new skeletal deposition at the coral-titanium interface was confirmed. Detaching the clips allowed the selective harvesting of viable polyps anchored to a thin layer of newly secreted skeleton. Importantly, this procedure was minimally invasive: although a small mark remained on the donor colony, complete tissue regeneration occurred within two weeks.
Harvested polyps on the Ti clips survived and expanded in ex situ culture, forming enlarged tissue patches. To demonstrate applicability to reef restoration, the polyp-bearing clips were mounted onto calcium-carbonate 3D-printed scaffolds. Polyps successfully migrated from the clips to the scaffolds and initiated de novo colony formation, confirming that the harvested units function as effective propagules.
This “Clip-and-Grow” platform represents a significant advance towards sustainable, non-destructive coral propagation. It enables the generation of propagation units without physical fragmentation and provides a modular interface for integrating living polyps with artificial reef substrates, offering a scalable pathway for next-generation restoration strategies.
0262b610: Corals reefs in the Florida Keys have been on a trajectory of degradation for more than a half century. With myriad stresses and insults resulting in a near complete extirpation of key reef builders, strong Allee effects, and a strong recruitment bottleneck, active restoration is critical to managing the existential threats facing Florida’s Coral Reef. Mote Marine Laboratory focuses on science-based, resilience-focused, and holistic restoration interventions in the pursuit of restoring form and function to this iconic reef ecosystem. While the scalability of interventions is paramount, so too is the application of innovative technology to the planning, execution, and monitoring of restoration efforts. Mote Marine Laboratory has recently established the Artificial Intelligence in Marine Science Center to develop and implement machine learning, artificial intelligence, and automation tools to support the organization’s restoration efforts. Integrating AI and machine learning into the labor and computationally intensive monitoring pipelines, particularly the processing and annotation of large area imagery, offers a strong potential for increasing workforce efficiency and productivity. Similarly, algorithmic tools whose purpose are a priori and post hoc decision support based on adaptive management principles offer opportunities to increase the efficacy and efficiency of interventions and the expenditure of limited human and financial resources. We will discuss the targeted and collaborative development of new AI technology designed to increase the productivity, efficacy, and efficiency of large-scale coral reef restoration efforts in the Florida Keys.
c0363e8a:
Reconstructing past reef ecosystems requires reliable geochemical and mineralogical interpretations of fossil corals. For decades, coral evolution has been framed by the assumption that major hexacorallian groups differed mineralogically through time: Paleozoic Rugosa formed calcitic skeletons, whereas Mesozoic and modern Scleractinia produced exclusively aragonitic skeletons. This paradigm has influenced phylogenetic models, interpretations of diagenetic alteration, and the widespread use of corals as paleoenvironmental archives. However, growing evidence indicates that this temporal and mineralogical dichotomy may be overly simplistic.
	Ordovician “scleractiniamorphs” from Scotland, now regarded as early scleractinians, show mouldic preservation compatible with aragonite, yet they co-occur with rugose corals whose calcitic skeletons are equally dissolved, obscuring mineralogical inference. In Cretaceous deposits, heavily altered (presumably aragonitic) corals occur alongside exceptionally preserved Coelosmilia, interpreted as having an originally calcitic skeleton. The modern coral Paraconotrochus appears to form a bimineralic skeleton containing both aragonite and calcite.
	These evidences suggest that calcite in post-Paleozoic corals, traditionally considered diagnostic of diagenesis, may in some cases be primary, while mineralogy alone cannot explain contrasting preservation within the same sediments. Scleractinian corals exhibit diverse calcium carbonate fiber arrangements, organic-matrix distribution patterns, and trace-element distributions that may confer different diagenetic pathways and preservation potentials.
	To evaluate these controls directly, we conducted controlled experimental diagenesis on five modern coral taxa representing distinct microstructural types. The results highlight how microstructural diversity can bias the fossil record. Recognizing and understanding these biases is essential for refining paleoenvironmental reconstructions.

This project was supported by the National Science Centre (Poland) research grant 2019/35/D/ST10/04378 to KJ.
0b1968ca:
Coral reefs generally thrive under high light conditions. As light decreases with depth, corals may adapt their morphology to optimise light capture. However, these same changes in morphology (e.g., platy forms) may also occur in response to light attenuation caused by turbidity within shallow waters. In the fossil record the occurrence of turbid shallow-water ecosystems has been largely restricted to the Meso- and Cenozoic. Only a single example of an ecosystem functionally analogous to modern turbid reefs has been identified from the Palaeozoic (Devonian of Queensland). 

Here we report a Silurian (~ 425 Ma) reef ecosystem from Gotland, Sweden, composed predominantly of platy tabulate corals (Anthozoa: Tabulata). Sedimentological data suggest shallow depths, high sedimentation rates and an unconsolidated substrate. We interpret the Gotland reef to be functionally similar to modern turbid reefs, making it the oldest known turbid
reef system, extending records by nearly 40 million years. This suggests that platy growth forms and colonies similar to modern funnel-shaped forms first emerged during the Silurian as a novel strategy that facilitated coral expansion in turbid, low-light environments. 

The recurrence of these forms across geological time suggests strong functional convergence driven by similar environmental pressures, evolving at least twice in both tabulate and scleractinian corals.

This work is a contribution to the research grant 2022/45/B/ST10/00345 of the National Science Centre of Poland.
9fa43747:
Reefs are key marine ecosystems but are currently threatened by anthropogenic and natural threats. Ancient reef crises and subsequent recoveries are important analogues for modern conservation, and one of the most severe reef collapses of the Phanerozoic occurred during the Pliensbachian–Toarcian transition (Early Jurassic). During the Pliensbachian, lithiotid bivalves, corals, sponges, and microbialites built bioherms and biostromes; however, these reefs were decimated by environmental changes associated with the Pliensbachian/Toarcian boundary event and Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (T-OAE) ~183 million years ago.

Interdisciplinary research in the Central High Atlas Mountains of Morocco has identified a dynamic record of reef proliferation and collapse. During the late Pliensbachian, coral-sponge-microbial bioherms and lithiotid-coral biostromes thrived in shallow-water depositional environments. These reefs were home to diverse communities with abundant macro and microfauna. Just after the Pliensbachian/Toarcian boundary, the climate regime shifted, resulting in a pulse of terrigenous clastic material and nutrients that poisoned carbonate ecosystems, leading to a first reef collapse. Lithiotid bioherms and biostromes returned to shallow-water settings quite rapidly and were often the first to colonize these settings. Conversely, corals, sponges, and foraminifera did not recover quickly, with large benthic foraminifera being absent from early Toarcian reefs and corals only occupying minor roles in lithiotid communities. It was not until just before the onset of the T-OAE that corals and sponges reappeared as reef builders, producing small patch reefs in several locations just before the onset of the event. Reef recovery was short-lived as the T-OAE caused the extinction of the lithiotid bivalves and a second reef collapse. Despite the severity of the T-OAE, coral and microbial reefs were quick to recover, albeit with an extremely depauperate foraminifera and sponge communities. This high-resolution record of multi-phased reef crisis and recovery is a rare and important analogue for modern reef ecosystems.
38035594:
Corals and sponges are major reef-builders of the Phanerozoic. Three key groups are: corals (rugosans, tabulates, scleractinians), hypercalcified sponges (archaeocyaths, stromatoporoids, sphinctozoans, inozoans, chaetetids), and synsedimentarily lithified siliceous sponges (lithistids and some hexactinellids). They differ in temperature and oxygen requirements, as well as in their abilities to calcify. These influences can be discerned in the Phanerozoic abundance and environmental distribution of reefs over 25-50 Ma timescales. 

Reefs formed by lithified siliceous sponges are common in the Cambrian-Ordovician and Triassic- Cretaceous, often with microbial carbonates. This time-distribution appears to be positively correlated with widespread environmental conditions, such as anoxia and elevated temperature, that favored their synsedimentary calcification. In contrast, they declined during intervals with cooler temperatures and increased oxygenation such as the Cenozoic. 

Hypercalcified sponge and coral reefs were abundant, and commonly co-occurred, during intervals of moderate temperature and oxygenation. Both show marked episodes of decline at mass extinction events linked to increased temperature and anoxia. Cenozoic hypercalcified sponges are only conspicuous in cryptic or relatively deep forereef habitats. They appear to be azooxanthellate and lack the size, and probably growth rate, that made stromatoporoids important reef-builders in the mid-Paleozoic. With a few exceptions, siliceous sponges appear to have lacked the synsedimentary calcification necessary to form lithified reefs during the Cenozoic. In contrast, increase in scleractinian corals may be related to cooling and oxygenation favoring the temperature-sensitive algal symbionts (e.g., zooxanthellae) that assist their shallow-water growth and calcification, and to the evolution of branched morphotypes that aid reproduction and recovery from disturbance. 

Thus, global fluctuations in oxygenation and temperature have influenced sponge and coral reef formation throughout the Phanerozoic, as well as during mass extinction events. Cenozoic oxygen increase and global cooling likely favored scleractinians over hypercalcified sponges in shallow tropical reefs. With Holocene global warming, these conditions may be changing.
ba1c63cd:
Molecular signaling dynamics that underpin the coral–dinoflagellate symbiosis remain poorly understood, particularly those mediated by phosphorylation. To investigate how phosphorylation influences symbiosis and thermal stress responses, we performed global mass spectrometry–based phosphoproteomics and abundance proteomics on three major reef-building corals from Hawai‘i (Pocillopora acuta, Porites compressa, and Montipora capitata) across an acute heat-stress time course. We identified 11,292 unique proteins in P. acuta, 9,706 in P. compressa, and 9,206 in M. capitata, along with 38,125, 23,846, and 21,607 unique phosphosites, respectively.

Heat exposure induced widespread phosphorylation changes independent of abundance shifts, including 4,600 significantly regulated sites in P. acuta, 3,090 in M. capitata, and 2,512 in P. compressa. Mapping coral proteins to human orthologs revealed that acute heat stress modulated phosphorylation across pathways involved in pattern-recognition receptor signaling and regulation of neurotransmitter secretion, with notable species specific differences. We also detected regulation of innate immune pathways, including cascades that in humans control T-cell activation; suggesting that corals may repurpose antigen-recognition-like circuitry within their cells.

To identify upstream regulators of these phosphorylation shifts, we applied The Kinase Library, a motif-scoring algorithm that infers kinase activity from local sequence context and phosphosite regulation. This analysis highlighted strong activation of MAP kinase pathways, including p38, ERK, and JNK cascades. To further probe kinase function, we developed an experimental platform enabling pharmacological perturbation of signaling in live corals, integrating measurements of pathway activity (e.g., phosphoproteomics) with bleaching phenotypes.

These findings establish a framework for understanding phosphorylation-driven signaling during the earliest stages of coral heat stress, offering new insight into mechanisms of thermal sensitivity and resilience. To our knowledge, this represents the first phosphoproteomic analysis of reef-building corals and substantially advances proteomic and bioinformatic capabilities within the coral research community.
fec472de:
Rapid changes in the marine environment, including rising sea surface temperatures, are reshaping ecosystems worldwide. Coral reefs, which rely on a delicate symbiosis between corals and their algal partners (Symbiodiniaceae), are particularly vulnerable. Elevated temperatures disrupt this relationship, triggering mass bleaching events that threaten one of the ocean’s most critical biodiversity hotspots. While the impacts of coral bleaching have received considerable attention in recent years, the fundamental neurobiological pathways by which corals sense environmental stressors, regulate homeostasis during stress, and manage their symbiotic relationships remain largely understudied. Understanding these processes is crucial for addressing the underlying mechanisms driving coral resilience to climate change. 
In this study, single-cell and bulk RNA-seq datasets were integrated to investigate neurogenic responses to heat stress using Stylophora pistillata as a model system. Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) associated with thermal stress were first identified from bulk RNA-seq data. To pinpoint genes specifically related to neuronal processes, these DEGs were cross-referenced with single-cell transcriptomes, focusing on genes highly or uniquely expressed in neuronal cell clusters. This integrative approach revealed several strong candidates, including APLP2, CHRNA7, GluCl, and HVCN, which are implicated in synaptic signaling, neurotransmitter reception, ion transport, and pH regulation. These processes are likely essential for coral stress perception and cellular communication. The expression of these neuro-related genes was further validated using independent RNA-seq datasets from Acropora cervicornis subjected to comparable heat-stress conditions. Orthologs showed consistent expression patterns associated with thermal stress, suggesting conserved neurobiological pathways mediating coral stress responses across species. Together, these findings demonstrate that integrating single-cell and bulk transcriptomic data enables fine-scale resolution of stress-associated molecular mechanisms and provides new insights into the cellular basis of coral resilience in a warming ocean. This open-access computational pipeline will also allow researchers to explore their own questions at fine cellular resolution beyond neuronal processes.
dfe88d2b: Reef-building corals and other cnidarians rely on a crucial symbiosis with dinoflagellates (Symbiodiniaceae), which can supply the host with up to 90% of its energy. With climate change driving more frequent and severe bleaching events, host stress and starvation result under elevated temperatures due to the expulsion or degradation of its symbionts. Immune modulation is increasingly recognized as a key regulator of symbiosis establishment, maintenance, and breakdown. Transforming growth factor beta (TGF-β) is a conserved cytokine involved in innate immune modulation, and prior work shows that TGF-β signaling promotes symbiont tolerance, whereas its inhibition can exacerbate bleaching. However, how TGF-β signaling integrates the diverse cellular and immune processes involved in symbiosis remains unclear. To address this, I identified binding sites of Smad3, the canonical transcription factor downstream of the TGF-β receptor, during early heat stress in symbiotic and aposymbiotic Exaiptasia diaphana. I paired chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-seq) for Smad3 with RNA-seq to provide evidence of direct regulation of transcription by Smad3. Smad3 showed greater differential binding between symbiotic and aposymbiotic anemones than between temperature treatments, suggesting TGF-β/Smad3 signaling is central to host–symbiont regulation under both ambient and heat-stress conditions. Additionally, all differentially bound Smad3 peaks were more enriched in symbiotic versus aposymbiotic anemones. GO enrichment analysis was performed for genes with enriched Smad3 binding in their promoter-proximal regions. Between symbiotic states, Smad3-associated genes showed enrichment for metabolic, signaling, and stress response pathways consistent with known mechanisms of cnidarian–algal symbiosis. This represents the first application of transcription factor ChIP-seq in a symbiotic cnidarian, providing a novel framework for directly investigating host transcriptional regulation.
272fa323:
Climate change has accelerated coral bleaching and the destruction of coral reefs, threatening the survival of these crucial hotspots of ecological, cultural, and economic services for millions worldwide. Despite the threat of increased bleaching events, many aspects of the cellular mechanisms of coral-algal symbiosis remain under-studied. A greater understanding of these fundamental aspects of coral biology is essential for predicting how corals respond to ongoing environmental change and developing successful conservation strategies. 
Sphingolipids are important lipid signalers in many cellular processes related to host-microbe interactions. We investigated the functional role of the sphingolipid pathway during symbiosis establishment in Exaiptasia diaphana (commonly known as Aiptasia), a model system for coral-algal symbiosis studies. We combined RNAi-mediated gene knockdown and chemical inhibition of sphingosine kinase (SPHK) to study sphingolipid regulation during symbiont uptake. We tested effects in symbiotic animals and those aposymbiotic animals inoculated with their endogenous symbiont Breviolum minutum. Symbiont density was quantified in living host tissues with epifluorescent microscopy and tissue homogenate with an automatic cell counter. Our results showed that RNAi successfully knocked down gene expression in symbiotic animals and SPHK inhibition significantly reduced algal colonization in re-colonizing animals. These results indicate that SPHK activity and sphingolipid signaling are essential for successful symbiont establishment, highlighting their important role in regulating coral-algal symbiosis.
005fb143: Coral bleaching is driven in part by oxidative stress, however the mechanisms that regulate free radical movement across cell membranes and that initiate bleaching-associated signaling cascades remain poorly understood. Aquaporins (AQP) are a family of membrane-bound proteins that enable passive, gradient-driven transport of water and other small molecules such as hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) in several animal models. Elucidating the transport mechanism of H₂O₂, a key reactive oxygen species produced in aerobic metabolism and known to increase in association with coral bleaching responses, would reveal a previously uncharacterized mechanism linking intracellular redox balance to bleaching susceptibility. Preliminary proteomic analyses of Pocillopora acuta identified four aquaporin-like proteins (PaAQP3, 4, 9, and 12) sharing high sequence identity with human and Pocillopora damicornis homologs. Among these, PaAQP3 was selected for further investigation due to its known localization in animal cell and phagosomal membranes, suggesting a potential role in both the coral host cell membrane and the phagosome-like symbiosome membrane. Importantly, mammalian AQP3 is a transporter of H₂O₂ due to its electrochemical similarities to water. Immunoblotting combined with immunofluorescence helped probe live P. acuta cells for the expression and localization of AQP3-like proteins (PaAQP3, 20-25 kDa). In parallel, molecular dynamic simulations of aquaporins indicate that the presence of these channels lowers the energetic barrier for H₂O₂ diffusion across lipid bilayers, supporting its role as an H₂O₂ transporter. Together, these preliminary findings support the involvement of AQP3-like proteins in mediating intracellular H₂O₂ signaling in coral cells during stress. Understanding how coral aquaporins contribute to free radical transport provides new molecular insight into coral bleaching mechanisms and may inform strategies for identifying or engineering more resilience in corals.
3c9783ec:
Coral reefs are experiencing rapid decline, highlighting critical gaps in our understanding of coral immunity and survival mechanisms. Corals are continuously exposed to potential pathogenic bacteria, making innate immune defences essential for their protection. Among these defences, antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) are near-universal effectors across eukaryotes, yet their roles in coral immunity remain poorly investigated. While AMPs have been extensively studied in the model cnidarian Hydra, limited attention has been given to coral-derived AMPs.
Recently, we identified a novel hydramacin-like AMP, previously characterized in Hydra, in the coral Heliofungia actiniformis. This study investigates its antimicrobial activity and its influence on coral-associated microbial aggregates (CAMAs), providing insights into host–microbiome interactions and immune regulation. Furthermore, our findings explore the relationship between Heliofungia and its bacterial symbiont Endozoicomonas, elucidating its potential role in coral immunity. Collectively, this research advances understanding of coral immune strategies while highlighting the dual ecological and biomedical relevance of this newly identified AMP, which exhibits potential activity against both coral and human pathogens.
56d8fab1: Increasing temperatures due to climate change have led to heat waves becoming more frequent and severe over the last decade, triggering mass coral bleaching events. Novel approaches such as genetic engineering may be used to increase adaptive capacities. Thermophilic organisms have naturally evolved heat-shock proteins (HSP) that remain functional under extreme temperatures, making them promising candidates for enhancing stress tolerance in other organisms via genetic engineering, such as corals and their symbiotic microalgae (Symbiodiniaceae). Candidate HSP genes from thermophilic bacteria,  archaea and stress-resistant plants (n = 20), as well as coral and Symbiodiniaceae (n = 8) were heterologously expressed under different promoter strengths (strong: Gpd15, medium: Tef2 and weak UASF/Tef2) in yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae), as a standardized and tractable eukaryotic screening tool. To test the genes’ performance and ability to confer thermatolerance, 24-hour heat stress assays were performed with temperatures of up to  40°C. Analysis of final optical density (OD600nm), growth rate, doubling time, and lag phase showed that 7 potential candidate genes from thermophile microorganisms and stress-resistant plants could significantly enhance yeast growth by around 50-100% at 37°C-40°C, confirming that a gene’s thermal origin influences its ability to confer thermal tolerance to yeast. Functional performance of coral and microalgal HSPs showed varying performance, with the staghorn coral’s (Acropora cervicornis) HSP16 enhancing growth the most. These findings show promising candidates for synthetic biology approaches and provide first insights into the functional performance of coral and algal HSPs, laying a foundation for future applications in conservation techniques using biotechnology tools.
877de5e6:
Environmental stress disrupts key physiological processes in corals, yet the molecular pathways mediating these responses remain incompletely understood. Identifying these mechanisms is essential for clarifying how corals regulate energy balance during bleaching. Previous transcriptomic analyses revealed glucose-regulation components in corals that resemble human energy-metabolism pathways. Building on this, our work in Pocillopora damicornis identified insulin, the insulin receptor (INSR), and 67 of 75 human insulin-signaling proteins as likely conserved through remote homology detection. These findings led us to hypothesize that insulin signaling operates in corals and that stress-induced dysregulation of this pathway may contribute to bleaching.
To test this hypothesis, we developed an experimental platform to validate predictions from remote-homology modeling. Using Pocillopora acuta nubbins, we performed tissue dissociation, total protein extraction, SDS-PAGE, Western blotting, and mass spectrometry analyses. Pocillopora acuta exhibited positive detection of INSR as well as phosphorylated and non-phosphorylated ERK1/2 using human antibodies, with mammalian cell assays confirming band identity and detection specificity.
These results provide the first direct biochemical evidence for an insulin-signaling cascade in Pocillopora. Given insulin signaling’s conserved role in regulating metabolism, growth, and stress responses across metazoans, its presence in corals suggests a potential mechanism underlying shifts in energy balance during environmental stress. Dysregulation of this pathway may contribute to the onset or progression of bleaching. This work offers new insight into coral physiology and identifies a promising molecular target for future studies aimed at improving coral health and resilience.
07f4770a:
Coral reef restoration is emerging as a key intervention strategy  in the face of global declines in reef health. However, achieving restoration at ecologically meaningful scales requires significant improvements in coral propagation methods and technologies. Established ex situ propagation workflows managing a million or more gametes are labour intensive and remain primarily manual, particularly during coral spawning events, when timely gamete capture and processing demand large operational teams. Here we present the AutoSpawner – a modular coral  aquaculture system designed to overcome spawning related bottlenecks, improve workflow efficiency and minimise manual handling of broodstock, while maintaining offspring performance.
The core AutoSpawner configuration includes a broodstock holding tank with an overflow skimmer for gamete bundle collection, linked to an AutoFertilisier tank for automated gamete separation, sperm dilution, fertilisation, and wash-down of fertilised eggs. The system was formally tested against traditional manual methods using four coral species from the central Great Barrier Reef (GBR), Australia. The AutoSpawner reduced the labour required by up to 113-fold relative to conventional workflows, while producing larvae of equal quality. Subsequently, the system has been successfully used for spawning 11 simultaneously spawning hermaphroditic species (Acroporidae, Merulinidae, Faviidae) and one asynchronous hermaphroditic species (Lobophyllidae), with resulting larvae used for large-scale coral seeding trials on the GBR.
The modular design is adaptable to fit the operational requirements and available infrastructure on a case-by-case basis. This has also facilitated integration into containerised aquaculture platforms (e.g. ReefSeed), which have been deployed internationally. Additionally, capacity building and industry adoption are underway as part of large-scale coral seeding programs on the GBR (e.g. the Pilot Deployments Program). By substantially improving temporal and spatial production efficiency, and reducing labour demands across the spawning workflow, the AutoSpawner demonstrates that upscaled ex situ larval production is both feasible and practical for accelerating reef restoration efforts.
9702605a:
In 2012, the Horniman Museum and Gardens began developing techniques to induce predictable ex situ broadcast coral spawning events through a programme known as Project Coral. A novel mesocosm system was designed to facilitate induced spawning and is now widely used around the world to support coral research and reef restoration initiatives. This mesocosm design presents unique opportunities to investigate the environmental conditions responsible for triggering coral reproductive events.

Broadcast coral spawning correlates strongly with several environmental signals, including seasonal temperature, solar irradiance, and lunar and diel cycles. However, few robust experimental studies have examined the individual roles of these putative cues in initiating spawning. In situ, many of these parameters exhibit strong collinearity, making it difficult to determine which factor exerts the greatest influence on seasonal spawning. As a result, significant debate remains in the scientific literature. A reliable and highly controllable ex situ mesocosm system therefore provides a unique opportunity to investigate phase-shifted spawning at various temporal scales, enabling multiple reproductive events within a single year and increasing access to gamete material for experimentation.

Here, we present a three-year phase-shifting broadcast coral spawning study (2019–2021) using the reef-building corals Acropora millepora and Galaxea fascicularis. This study explores the role of sunlight dynamics and temperature regimes in controlling out-of-season spawning events.

In addition, we discuss examples of manipulating the reproductive seasons of corals from different geographical regions to explore the potential for inter-geographical hybridisation, and how this approach may help us better understand the boundaries of reproductive isolation between species.
aa9aa718: Restoring coral populations requires increasing genotypic diversity to enhance resistance to disease and improve thermal resilience. However, knowledge of coral reproductive timing in the Seychelles remains limited, impeding practitioners’ ability to utilise sexual reproduction as a restoration tool. To address this, a collaboration between Nature Seychelles, Coral Spawning International and Canon EMEA established the first Western Indian Ocean land-based coral spawning facility. Here, we report the first ex-situ spawning event from this facility in the corymbose reef-building coral Acropora tenuis cf. macrostoma, providing foundational insight for assisted breeding in the region. Fifteen genotypes spawned across three consecutive days following the full moon on 5 November 2025, within one hour after sunset. High fertilisation rates (96.13 ± 0.96%) resulted in just under 800,000 embryos, which were reared in shallow embryo tanks. Due to the number of larvae produced, several settlement approaches were implemented. A total of 7,774 spat were produced from larvae settling on 10,000 specially designed settlement plugs and were grown for a period of six months prior to outplanting. A proportion of larvae (33,000) were used in a direct larval seeding (DLS) experiment, where larvae were added to three 2 × 3 m tarpaulin plots on the reef within the Cousin Island Special Reserve. Orthomosaics were produced for the three DLS plots, along with three control plots of the same size, to assess temporal change over time. In addition, excess larvae were settled onto approximately 500 pieces of reef rubble, which were outplanted following a three-month land-based grow-out period. This talk will cover the establishment of this land-based coral spawning facility, the results of the first ex-situ coral spawning events in the Seychelles, and how corporate funding, novel ex-situ system design and in-country NGO expertise can work synergistically to produce genetically diverse corals for restoration.
ff55eef6: In response to the decline of coral populations throughout Florida’s Coral Reef and the subsequent recruitment bottleneck within most species, scientists are using ex situ assisted sexual reproduction (ASR), a form of managed breeding, in tandem with novel post-settlement interventions to increase the genetic diversity and adaptive potential of coral populations. Mote Marine Laboratory houses a diverse inventory of sexually mature Caribbean corals, across multiple in situ and ex situ nurseries, as a result of decades of wild coral collections and ex situ ASR efforts. Mote’s science-based coral reef restoration strategy and managed breeding program are informed by more than eight years of resilience experiments testing responses to environmental stressors and disease, as well as microbial community analyses, and detailed outplant survivorship monitoring data from large-scale disturbance events, like the 2023 marine heatwave. We have identified specific families, and individuals, that are more likely to host thermally tolerant algal symbionts and are more tolerant to stressors, such as disease, high temperatures, and ocean acidification conditions. Our managed breeding program has targeted these known resilient parents for performing two-parent crosses to produce sexual recruits with potentially inherited resilient traits. We then paired this managed breeding plan with novel interventions in our ex situ nursery, such as the use of cryopreserved sperm, settlement enhancement methods, and early life stage algal symbiont seeding, to determine intervention successes in producing sexual recruits with higher post-outplanting survivorship than naturally occurring genotypes. Indeed, sexual recruits created from known thermally tolerant Acropora cervicornis parents, and produced using ASR, had the highest outplant survival after the 2023 marine heatwave. This approach highlights the unique importance of ex situ coral sexual propagation and the role these interventions provide in both answering foundational research questions and enhancing practical coral restoration efforts by increasing coral resilience.
410b27ff: The past decade has seen the rapid development of land-based coral reproduction as a complementary tool to produce coral biomass and increase genetic diversity in reef restoration.   As Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease and repeated marine heatwaves have devastated Florida’s Coral Reef, two species have recently been declared functionally extinct by field researchers. The Florida Aquarium’s Coral Conservation and Research Center holds founder broodstock of both Dendrogyra cylindrus and Acropora palmata providing not only a living biobank but also a resource center for larvae and juveniles. This presentation will discuss recent efforts such as production of hybrid Acropora prolifera, international breeding efforts between Honduran and Floridian elkhorn corals for the purpose of genetic rescue, and also how genetic management considerations have impacted our procedures in ex situ coral reproduction.  We will also discuss advancements in cryopreservation and the use of cryopreserved material for both gene banking as well as workflows during spawning events to increase reproductive success in highly unsynchronized species. Maximizing post-settlement survival is critical when dealing with low numbers of larvae from targeted crosses, and we will discuss improvements such as the use of chemical settlement cues, co-culture of herbivorous grazers, pest limitation, and medical treatments on juvenile corals.
e564d010: Coral larval settlement and post-settlement survival are the main constraints to the success of ex situ sexual propagation of corals for reef restoration. To optimize the production of Caribbean corals, we assessed the effect of larval stocking density on percent settlement and the effect of initial settler density on post-settlement survival up to eight weeks— the time-point at which the corals are typically large enough to be isolated and grown on individual tiles. The percent settlement of Colpophyllia natans was 46% on average (range 18.7-77.4%) and it increased as the larval stocking density increased, from approximately 3 to 8 larvae/cm^2 of settlement substrate. For Orbicella faveolata, percent settlement was 51% on average (range 16.9-80.3%) and was only significantly higher when the larval stocking density was approximately 4 larvae/cm^2 of settlement substrate compared to lower densities (down to 2 larvae/cm^2). Post-settlement survival over time was not significantly affected by initial coral settler density. Survival was 50% or higher at a density of ≤17 settlers/cm^2 for C. natans and ≤4 settlers/cm^2 for O. faveolata.
