From a production perspective, the aim is to obtain about 1.5 coral spats/cm^2 at 8 weeks post-settlement, as a density higher than that would prevent different corals from being able to be isolated (cutting of original tile with a band saw or tile nipper would kill part of the corals when attempting to separate two corals in close proximity). To achieve this density for C. natans and O. faveolata, we recommend providing 6 larvae for each cm^2 of settlement substrate since larval settlement was approximately 50% and post-settlement survival at 8-weeks was at least 50%. From a labor efficiency perspective, this larval stocking density would lead to an initial settler density of 3 settlers/cm^2 which is also manageable to clean manually.
558e01f9:
Effective, scalable restoration requires sustained local capacity and community stewardship. The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Restoration Center (RC), a US federal resource agency, plays a critical, behind-the-scenes role in empowering local partners and community groups to actively plan and implement coral reef restoration efforts across the US Pacific Islands (American Samoa, CNMI, Guam, and Hawaii).

This presentation outlines NOAA RC’s strategy for building sustainable community-based restoration capacity in this vast and culturally diverse region. Our approach centers on providing essential resources and technical expertise while prioritizing local autonomy and indigenous knowledge. Key mechanisms include: (1) Funding Support: Implementing grant competitions designed to prioritize projects with strong community involvement and locally-driven objectives, thereby providing the necessary financial infrastructure. (2) Training and Workshops: Developing and implementing specialized regional workshops focused on critical restoration skills, such as restoration planning, rubble stabilization, Structure-from-Motion (SfM), and sexual propagation. These training sessions ensure partners have access to cutting-edge science and best practices. (3) Technical Expertise and Assistance: Providing direct technical support and co-developing restoration plans, ensuring projects are scientifically robust, scalable, and tailored to local conditions.

By focusing on facilitating local capacity and supporting partners in implementing restoration, the NOAA Restoration Center demonstrates how a federal agency can serve as a vital catalyst, empowering Pacific Island communities to become the primary stewards and implementers of successful and enduring coral reef restoration. This model is crucial for accelerating recovery and fostering long-term resilience in the face of a changing climate.
bf6f0c76:
The West Hawai'i Division of Aquatic Resources (DAR) has launched a community-based coral restoration initiative in Kahalu'u Bay in partnership with Reef Teach staff and volunteers. This project aims to conserve and restore the bay’s natural ecosystem through collaborative science, ultimately identifying best practices for community-led restoration. Success of reattachment methods applied in the Bay will be evaluated by monitoring the health and distribution of corals through monthly surveys that track the number, size, species, and health conditions of both loose and attached corals. These surveys are conducted within two distinct zones: a restoration zone where active reattachment occurs, and a control zone for comparison.

The reattachment process, which occurs, on average, over 3 days every three months, employs cement and two-part epoxy adhesives to reattach detached corals greater than 5 cm in size. Snorkelers collect detached corals, cache them at a protected central location, and then replant them onto suitable substrates, ensuring that each coral is securely attached and photographed for documentation. To track progress, 20-30 corals are tagged each quarter for long-term monitoring. The ongoing surveys (20-40 per quarter) assess the effectiveness of reattachment methods and determine if these interventions are outpacing natural detachment rates.

After the first year, survey data will be analyzed to identify the optimal timing and conditions for reattachment and the most suitable sub-habitats for restoration based on environmental factors like wave action, sedimentation, and substrate type. The second year will focus on empowering Reef Teach to lead the project independently, including assisting with their permitting process, training staff and volunteers, and facilitating their transition into full project ownership. This initiative will set a precedent for community-led restoration projects for the conservation of West Hawai'i’s coral reefs.
16e0838e:
Rapidly intensifying impacts from climate change and local stressors are beginning to outpace the capacity of traditional passive management practices alone to sustain coral communities on some reef areas creating a need to integrate climate-smart, ecologically relevant active interventions to help bolster coral community resilience. Tailoring active coral restoration to local contexts and management practices can be challenging in Fiji and other developing nations, particularly with limited access to finance and other resources; however, when implemented in conjunction with other marine management tools and using locally available materials and cost-effective methods, such approaches can provide scalable opportunities for ecological gains while strengthening local stewardship.

Here we share lessons learned and strategies utilized from a 19-year collaboration with the Vanua Davutukia in Fiji’s Korolevu-i-wai district where active coral restoration has been integrated into ongoing community-based marine management efforts to achieve a suite of ecological and socio-economic objectives. Scaled up in 2015, this effort strategically propagates and transplants 9000-10,000+ corals a year to complement the outcome from other ongoing marine management efforts.  Key approaches include using low-cost, locally sourced materials to implement locally led coral-gardening programs, the integration of traditional knowledge with applied science into restoration planning, using resilience-based methods that prioritize genotypes likely to withstand future climate impacts, and incorporating coral predator control efforts. By presenting both successes and challenges from nearly two decades of restoration efforts, we provide practical insights for other Pacific Island communities seeking to bolster coral community health and resilience, climate adaptation, and local stewardship under resource-limited conditions. The lessons shared underscore the value of context-specific, culturally informed yet scientifically designed, scalable approaches for supporting coral reef resilience in a rapidly changing climate.
a8d16001: Island communities often rely heavily on coral reefs for coastal protection, food, income from fisheries and tourism, and cultural and recreational activities. Unfortunately, the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands (CNMI) was among many regions globally that experienced catastrophic coral die-offs due to unprecedented marine heatwaves from 2013-2017. Additionally, the CNMI was hit by two super typhoons in 2015 and 2018 that caused severe destruction to island ecosystems and infrastructure, negatively impacting social systems and community wellbeing. In response to these events and dire future climate projections, local and federal resource managers began to prioritize reef restoration efforts to improve ecosystem and community resilience to ongoing climate change. Johnston Applied Marine Sciences (JAMS) is a CNMI-based independent marine research and conservation organization that has been working in collaboration with local and federal agencies to develop a climate-informed coral reef restoration program in the CNMI. We are using proven and innovative approaches to improve the scale and resilience of restored coral communities, while navigating the unique challenges associated with operating in a small, remote island community with limited resources. This presentation will provide an overview of our progress to date and next steps.
5a0a4ef1: As marine heatwaves continue to intensify, coral restoration programs must incorporate multiple species of genetically diverse, heat-resistant corals. However, the current costs and technical capacity required to identify heat-resistant colonies are not scalable, and research-driven restoration projects often struggle to engage local community members and agencies in project design and implementation. Community participation is crucial for building public buy-in and long-term support, especially in remote or developing nations. To address these challenges, the Coral Futures Academy has adopted a set of training programs that streamline the identification of bleaching-resistant corals, provide low-cost testing equipment and simple experimental designs, and offer local capacity-building opportunities for establishing heat-resistant coral nurseries. By ensuring that every component is affordable, robust, and feasible in remote settings, where most coral reefs are found, the Coral Futures Academy aims to deliver significant global impact. Our approach has been successfully piloted in Palau in the western Pacific. Using these methods, more than 900 heat-tolerant corals from 24 species have been identified and maintained in local nurseries by local researchers, supplying colonies for restoration efforts tailored to local needs. We have also empowered local leaders at our project sites to adopt additional restoration strategies appropriate to their contexts through knowledge exchange within our network. This scalable framework, first implemented in Palau, is now being expanded to new sites in Majuro (Marshall Islands) and Pohnpei (Federated States of Micronesia), with plans to reach other regions. Through these efforts, we aim to support communities in proactively sustaining their reefs and the ecosystem goods and services upon which they depend. This network also serves as a foundation for expanding collaborative restoration and other scientific efforts across the region. Furthermore, the lessons we have gained from applying this model offer important information on how it performs within this regional context.
7e321d31: Reef-building corals face unprecedented decline, necessitating novel, scalable restoration approaches that enhance genetic diversity. While sexually propagated corals offer these advantages, logistical challenges, including clumped larval settlement, early mortality, and labor-intensive recruit remounting have limited reef-scale implementation. To help resolve these challenges, we tested biopolymers (sodium alginates, chitosan, iota-carrageenan, kappa-carrageenan) as engineered substrates to facilitate spatial control of settlement and manipulate the microbial environment during settlement (e.g., algal symbiont and bacterial inoculation). Using larvae from four Caribbean coral species (Colpophyllia natans, Pseudodiploria strigosa, Diploria labyrinthiformis, Orbicella faveolata) across four spawning years (2022-2025), we evaluated survival, settlement, and symbiont establishment in biopolymers using generalized linear mixed models. All polymers supported survival, with lower concentrations (0.5%-1.5%) yielding better outcomes. Iota-carrageenan was unique in maintaining survival equivalent to filtered sea water controls (89.7% vs. 89.8% at Day 21, p = 0.98) while promoting 8-fold higher settlement (15.7%, p < 0.001). Chitosan supported survival but inhibited metamorphosis and settlement, while sodium alginates showed reduced survival (72-80%). Pre-treatment of larvae with tetrabromopyrrole (TBP) significantly enhanced survival (+19%) and settlement (+19-36%) in iota-carrageenan only, demonstrating polymer-specific chemical cue facilitation. Symbiont biocompatibility assays (Symbiodinium microadriaticum, Breviolum minutum, Durusdinium trenchii) revealed iota-carrageenan maintained or enhanced photosynthetic efficiency (B. minutum: +50%), while chitosan caused severe impairment (~82% reduction in S. microadriaticum). Coral recruits (P. strigosa, O. faveolata) settled within iota-carrageenan and sodium alginate also successfully acquired symbionts (Durusdinium trenchii). Iota-carrageenan uniquely supported complete coral ontogeny from larva to symbiotic recruit, establishing the biocompatibility required for hydrogel-based encapsulation platforms that could enable scalable, genetically diverse, intervention-enabled restoration at reef scales.
8d2b53f0: Scalable, science-informed restoration increasingly relies on assisting coral evolution through holobiont manipulation. However, the translation of laboratory-reared, heat-resilient genotypes with manipulated holobiont to the reef can be compromised by microbial risks in the receiving environment. To address this, we integrated long-read nanopore sequencing into a Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) framework to profile transplantation sites in Singapore’s urbanized reefs. We characterized the baseline microbiomes (with full-length 16S metabarcoding sequencing) of four target coral species, Pocillopora acuta, Goniastrea pectinata, Pachyseris speciosa and Porites lutea, across candidate transplant sites to identify biological risks prior to the introduction of the corals. Moving beyond traditional ecological monitoring, this high-resolution genomic approach allowed us to detect specific pathogen reservoirs. Analyses revealed elevated latent disease risk at specific sites that may compromise transplant survival. These microbial risk profiles were weighted alongside ecological viability (e.g., coral cover change over time, genetic connectivity, etc), environmental suitability (e.g. temperature, turbidity, etc.) and socio-economic factors, to generate a site suitability heatmap. We demonstrate that pre-transplantation risk profiling is a critical step in future-proofing restoration, ensuring that climate-adapted corals are excluded from zones where microbial dysbiosis threatens their resilience.
614ecfb9: We are advancing a suite of complementary approaches that apply ecological precision medicine and AI-enabled assisted evolution coupled with scalable deployment platforms to restore entire reef ecosystems in a climate-wise manner. Specifically, we have developed single-polyp multi-omics methods that allow for holobiont-informed interventions, including phage therapies that harness historically overlooked viral regulation of coral resilience, as well as precision probiotics, and mucus/microbiome transplants to enhance coral stress tolerance and disease resistance. In parallel, we integrate resilience-linked biomarkers with AI-enabled assisted evolution to forecast and amplify thermal tolerance. Using novel AI architectures, we have identified metabolomic biomarkers that predict coral bleaching susceptibility with up to 100% accuracy, enabling high-confidence selection of individuals for selective breeding. Corals produced through this pipeline exhibit >20% increases in heat tolerance in a single generation, representing an unprecedented leap for how adaptive capacity can be built into restoration. Because coral-only restoration efforts often fail, we have embedded these techniques and others within a whole-ecosystem restoration approach using Coral Reef Arks, modular midwater reef structures designed to rebuild entire coral reefs along with their associated biodiversity, trophic interactions, and ecosystem services. In early deployments, Arks have proven more than twice as effective as conventional restoration measures. This whole-ecosystem approach provides a pathway from laboratory innovation to field-scale, climate-ready reef restoration. Together, this integrated framework links holobiont-centered precision medicine techniques, AI-guided selective breeding, and whole-ecosystem restoration into a scalable roadmap for future-proofing reefs under accelerating environmental change.
056614f1:
Coral Reef Arks are a new type of artificial reef that aim to provide ideal environmental conditions for corals reef communities by elevating them off degraded benthos. Arks are geodesic spheres that can be seeded with both translocated corals, and key cryptic reef biodiversity passively accumulated on Autonomous Reef Monitoring Structures (ARMS) units. Two 8-foot geodesic coral Arks were deployed in Vieques, Puerto Rico in the midwater several miles from shore. As hypothesized, this location indeed provided improved water quality conditions that led to significant improvements in reef health metrics compared to benthic control sites: higher coral survival, growth, and recruitment, less fleshy macroalgae and turf algae, more biodiversity, and more piscivorous fish. ARMS units were added to the Arks partway through the deployment, allowing separate testing of how that intervention affected various aspects of reef resilience. Most notably, ARMS seeding led to rapid increases in coral growth and fish biomass. After 2.5 years in the midwater, the Arks were then relocated, intact, to benthic sites closer to shore, keeping the Arks at the same depths but securing them directly to the seafloor, to test whether the improvements achieved in the midwater could be retained.   
Efforts to reverse the decline of coral reefs around the world are hampered by degraded benthic conditions that caused much of the decline in the first place. Coral Reef Arks provide a useful approach to provide healthy conditions to regenerate small reef units that are more ecologically functional than degraded natural reefs. Once established communities develop on the Arks, they can re-seed adjacent natural reefs with larvae or the Arks can be translocated back to adjacent degraded locations to support re-establishment of reefs. Results from the Vieques Coral Arks demonstration project will be presented, identifying how this intervention influences various aspects of reef resilience.
9d313cec: Coral reefs are rapidly degrading due to climate change and local anthropogenic pressures, creating an urgent need for innovative restoration strategies. Traditional approaches primarily prioritize coral propagation over the broader ecological interactions that underpin reef resilience. Emerging research indicates that incorporating positive interactions between corals and other reef taxa can enhance restoration outcomes in real-world applications. As a proof of concept, we are initiating a multi-taxa restoration experiment at Al Fahal Reef, Saudi Arabia—an area severely impacted by the 2023–2024 bleaching events—by co-cultivating sponges, giant clams, soft corals, and sea cucumbers with reef-forming corals. The aim of this experiment is to determine whether corals restored within a multi-taxa assemblage can (1) achieve higher initial survivorship after outplanting, (2) exhibit faster growth, and (3) maintain increased survivorship over the summer. Two assemblages of the Mars Assisted Reef Restoration System (MARRS), each containing 30 Reef Stars and 450 coral fragments from Pocillopora favosa and Acropora spp., were constructed: one coral-only and one multi-taxa. While the inclusion of giant clams (Tridacna maxima), soft corals (Xenia sp.), and sea cucumbers (Holothuria scabra) will be completed in the next month, preliminary results show that the sponge Hyrtios sp. remains visually healthy and is already exhibiting growth over the Reef Stars after 12 days. Pilot nursery trials further demonstrate that donors and explants of the sponge Callyspongia crassa survive, heal, and maintain health post-fragmentation—key traits for species used in restoration. Integrating functional species throughout the restoration pipeline, from nursery co-cultivation to ecological outplanting, has the potential to transform coral reef restoration into a more resilient and effective approach for safeguarding reef ecosystems and the communities that depend on them.
6dc2207e: Caribbean coral reefs are undergoing accelerated decline due to climate-driven thermal stress, altered hydrological regimes, and cumulative local pressures, highlighting the need for restoration approaches that are both science-informed and scalable. The Zapata Swamp National Park in southwest Cuba, a comparatively well-preserved and hydrologically distinctive wetland system, offers a valuable reference site for developing future-proof coral reef restoration strategies. This initiative integrates cutting-edge ecological research with industrial innovation to establish a comprehensive framework for resilience-based reef restoration. A zero-emission research platform operating entirely on renewable energy provides the infrastructure for high-resolution environmental monitoring, physiological assessments of coral stress tolerance, and in-situ experiments aimed at identifying adaptive mechanisms that can inform restoration at larger scales. Industrial partners contribute through the deployment of modular, renewable-energy-powered water treatment systems that address freshwater scarcity in nearby communities while reducing land-based pollution and other watershed-derived stressors that indirectly affect coastal reef health. In parallel, the co-development of sustainable ecotourism models generates stable income streams that are reinvested into scientific monitoring, restoration operations, and community-led conservation programs, establishing an economic–ecological feedback loop that enhances long-term project viability. Capacity-building efforts, including training, knowledge exchange, and participatory environmental governance, ensure that restoration practices remain locally embedded and adaptable to shifting environmental baselines. Collectively, this integrative science–industry approach aligns with UN Sustainable Development Goals 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation), 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities), 13 (Climate Action), 14 (Life Below Water), and 15 (Life on Land), while contributing to SDG 4 (Quality Education) and SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth). By coupling emerging scientific insights on coral adaptation with scalable industrial technologies and socio-economic mechanisms, this model provides a robust pathway for future-proofing coral reefs in the face of accelerating environmental change.
f6c98562: International conservation organisations are well positioned to advance effective and equitable coral reef management by empowering coastal communities – many of whom rely on small-scale fisheries - to govern resources through secure rights and meaningful access to decision-making processes. A human rights-based approach to conservation, which focuses on protecting and promoting the basic human rights of small-scale fishers, can support just and effective stewardship of coral reefs. This research seeks to understand how international conservation organisations operating in coastal areas globally, support human rights principles through their policies and practices. While progress has been made, critical gaps remain in policy commitments by conservation organisations to certain rights, such as social development and decent work and recognizing local groups' rights and knowledge. Case studies of conservation programs working in small-scale fisheries in Colombia, Indonesia, and Kenya, also reveal that program effort needs to focus on improving the socioeconomic conditions of coastal communities, their rights to access reef resources, and equitable value chains of coral reef fisheries. Given the multitude of challenge faced by small-scale fishing communities, it is imperative that conservation organisations utilise their position of influence to create a supportive environment for them, fostering sustainable development, economic viability, and improved social wellbeing.
ef79bd25: Long-term and sustainable coral reef management requires governance models that center the knowledge, priorities, and lived experiences of the communities most closely tied to nearshore ecosystems. The Kōkua Community-Based Monitoring (KCBM) Program, housed within the Division of Aquatic Resources (DAR) under the State of Hawaiʻi Department of Land and Natural Resources (DLNR), was created as part of the Holomua Marine Initiative to support the Initiative’s goal of more effectively engaging communities and ensuring that local knowledge guides marine management planning. The KCBM Program supports communities across Hawaiʻi in defining, designing, and implementing place-based monitoring rooted in kilo (holistic observation methodology) and local stewardship priorities and practices. The program was named Kōkua, meaning “to help, aid, or assist,” reflecting its purpose to partner with communities to develop robust, culturally grounded monitoring programs. The case study presented focuses on limu (seaweed), an important subsistence resource not currently monitored by DAR, and algal-bench surveys at Ko Olina, Oʻahu. These efforts take place within Hawaiʻi’s coral reef social ecological system, where nearshore algal dynamics, water quality, and community resource use directly influence reef health, partnership development, and management decisions. This presentation examines how the KCBM framework facilitates meaningful and inclusive participation in monitoring, leading to informed management. Through co-developed monitoring questions, flexible partnership pathways, shared agreements, and culturally grounded training, the KCBM Program supports communities in shaping the knowledge that informs reef-related decision-making. Insights from the Ko Olina case study highlight successes and trade-offs, including balancing scientific rigor with community feasibility, navigating capacity limitations for agencies and community partners, and addressing structural inequities experienced by rural and Native Hawaiian communities in state-managed resource systems. Lessons learned from the KCBM Program offer a replicable model for embedding community voices and kilo in nearshore monitoring and planning to inform fisheries, restoration, and ecosystem management decisions.
c306ccaa: Coral conservation is an emerging field rooted in preserving and restoring tropical coastal ecosystems. While advances in Western science have allowed for improving the efficiency and impact of restoration, incorporating traditional knowledge systems passed down from kūpuna (ancestors) is needed. Our coral restoration framework is built with the kaiāulu (community) o Keaukaha as its backbone, and educating haumāna (students) at the Ka ʻUmeke Kāʻeo Hawaiian Immersion School to preserve the knowledge held about coral reefs. We developed bilingual (English and ʻŌlelo Hawaiʻi) lessons in topics such as coral biology, ecology, benefits to humans, and also highlighted emerging human stressors on coral reefs, such as marine heat waves, ocean acidification, marine debris pollution, etc. We teach through interactive games and experiential snorkeling activities to reinforce the concepts we cover and get them engaged. These lessons expose haumāna to koʻa (coral) at a young age and spread awareness of the type of work that can be done in their kaiāulu. We culminate each academic year with an open house, held for the community to communicate our progress, listen to ongoing concerns, and build pilina (bonds and connections) with researchers at our facility. We will continue our work, expanding to other communities, so we can pass down our ʻike (knowledge) to future generations. We hope our coral conservation program can be a useful model to other researchers and communities hoping to weave ʻike kupuna (knowledge from ancestors) with Western techniques.
e0af80bc:
Across Papua New Guinea (PNG) and the broader Pacific, indigenous women continue to play a leading role in community stewardship of marine resources. This knowledge, leadership, and daily engagement with reefs and near-shore fisheries form a vital and longstanding foundation for many locally-managed marine areas (LMMAs). These LMMAs consist of governance rooted in cultural practice, customary tenure, and intergenerational knowledge. The Sea Women of Melanesia (SWoM), an indigenous, female-led organisation in PNG exemplifies this leadership through its role in driving community-based reef management across PNG.
This presentation highlights how SWoM is advancing coral reef management by blending indigenous knowledge, practices, community priorities, and innovative digital tools, particularly the use of ReefCloud, a cloud-based image analysis and data-sharing platform co-developed in partnership with the Australian Institute of Marine Science (AIMS). ReefCloud is locally adaptable because it is being shaped through an ongoing, collaboration between SWoM and the Australian Institute of Marine Science (AIMS). Through this partnership, AIMS also learns from SWoM’s field experience, cultural knowledge, and monitoring needs to improve ReefCloud’s design and usability. SWoM-led monitoring programs demonstrate an approach that supports local communities to design and propose new marine reserve areas and management plans, blending scientific reef assessments with community knowledge about fishing practices, fish species, and customary use zones.
We share four key lessons emerging from this partnership: (1) the importance of co-design; (2) the application of digital tools to meet community-grounded monitoring needs; (3) strengthening data autonomy; and, (4) ensuring that technology supports and does not replace local expertise. We offer recommendations for expanding this model: (1) scaling up women-led monitoring across further LMMAs; (2) strengthening training and capacity; (3) improving coordination between communities, NGOs, and regional agencies; and (4) continuing to co-develop tools like ReefCloud so they remain culturally appropriate, locally usable, and scientifically robust.
77e36491: Black corals (Antipatharia), locally known as "Akar Bahar", hold deep cultural and economic value in the Karimunjawa Archipelago, where they are commonly used as talismans and souvenirs. Despite their significance, these corals face increasing threats from overharvesting, species misidentification, and limited public awareness of their ecological roles. This study investigates the cultural narratives, usage patterns, and ecological status of "Akar Bahar", while also identifying knowledge gaps and conservation opportunities. Preliminary research activities—such as focus group discussions, interviews with diverse community members, and reef surveys across five conservation zones—have been conducted, with analysis underway. The participatory approach ensures the inclusion of local perspectives across age and gender, recognizing the varied roles community members play in coral use and conservation. Expected results include baseline data on species distribution, cultural practices, and trade patterns, as well as insights into potential misidentification between black corals and gorgonians. This study aims to inform conservation planning that is both culturally respectful and ecologically sound. Anticipated outputs include localized education materials, community awareness programs, and recommendations for sustainable management. The research contributes to inclusive, community-based strategies for safeguarding marine biodiversity in Indonesia’s small island regions.
a0ecc01b:
Funafuti’s reefs have been heavily damaged by repeated bleaching events and long-standing local pressures, leading to widespread coral mortality and the collapse of earlier donor colonies and nurseries. In response, the UNDP Tuvalu Coastal Adaptation Project (TCAP) has launched a renewed coral rehabilitation initiative centred on local knowledge and community leadership. Key partners include the youth-led NGO Fuligafou, the Funafuti Kaupule, island youth groups, and the Tuvalu Fisheries Association—reflecting the Pacific’s long tradition of community-based marine stewardship and customary management.
Recent lagoon and ocean-side scoping missions, supported by Corals for Conservation, identified small but resilient colonies of Acropora, Pocillopora, and Porites. These remnant populations—documented through snorkel transects, reef-flat walkovers, drone mapping, and ROV-assisted surveys—form the basis for selecting new donor sites and establishing nursery locations. Site selection blends scientific and cultural criteria: coral health, water flow, sediment load, predator pressure, accessibility for local teams, traditional use areas, and weather exposure. Guidance from youth fishers and elders, particularly on historical spawning grounds and current patterns, has strengthened the reliability of site assessments.
However, the programme faces significant constraints. Limited boats, fuel, and equipment reduce monitoring frequency; community volunteers balance restoration with livelihood demands; and ecological challenges—CoTS and Drupella predation, algal growth, and uncertain spawning—affect recovery. Sedimentation from nearby reclamation works adds further stress. Ensuring sustainability requires targeted capacity-building in coral husbandry, taxonomy, and nursery maintenance, supported by predictable funding and stronger coordination between Fisheries, the Kaupule, and Fuligafou.
Lessons from similar initiatives in Fiji, Samoa, and Kiribati underscore that long-term success relies on empowering local institutions and embedding restoration within cultural practice. This community-driven model provides a practical pathway for rebuilding coral resilience in atoll environments under climate pressure.
7ef51bee: The Philippines is a global biodiversity hotspot within the Coral Triangle and continues to face rapid coral reef degradation driven by climate change, unsustainable coastal development, overfishing, and pollution. Despite numerous conservation and restoration initiatives, long-term effectiveness remains limited by insufficient community integration, inconsistent local participation, and misalignment with sociocultural contexts, reducing ecological impact and sustainability. This study introduces a comprehensive framework for coral reef conservation and restoration - Preventive Actions, Passive Restoration, and Active Restoration (PPAR) - with community engagement as a central pillar. Drawing on marine ecology, coral reef restoration, marine protected area (MPA) management, participatory governance, and social science, the framework emphasizes structured community consultation, informed engagement,  multi-sectoral collaboration, support from local governance, partnerships with national agencies, effective MPA management, civil society involvement, co-production of knowledge, inclusive decision-making, and shared stewardship among scientists, local authorities, and coastal communities. Active restoration is implemented through science-based, community-friendly design. Case studies from Bohol, Siquijor, and Negros Oriental (2013–2025) demonstrate that community-centered approaches enhance feasibility, social acceptance, and ecological outcomes. Under the PPAR Framework, approximately, over 600 hectares of coral reefs in the central Philippines have been conserved or restored. These results indicate that integrating socioecological and governance dimensions with evidence-based interventions strengthens both ecological effectiveness and long-term sustainability. The PPAR framework offers a scalable, community-responsive model that reinforces coral reef resilience and local stewardship. By prioritizing socioecological integration alongside scientific restoration, it provides a viable pathway for coral reef recovery in the Philippines and other climate-vulnerable regions.
f4a4af94:
Raja Ampat, located at the heart of the Coral Triangle, is globally recognized as one of the most important centers of marine biodiversity. The region harbors approximately 75% of the world’s coral reef species, including 553 coral species and 1,437 reef fish species, and provides essential ecosystem services and livelihoods for more than 40,000 people. Coastal and marine resource management in Raja Ampat is deeply intertwined with local culture, particularly through customary governance systems such as sasi, a traditional knowledge and practice that regulates the temporal and spatial use of marine and coastal resources to ensure sustainability.
Within this socio-ecological context, environmental education plays a strategic role in strengthening awareness and stewardship from an early age. Yayasan Konservasi Alam Nusantara, in collaboration with local partners, developed an environmental education module for elementary school students in Raja Ampat Regency. Launched in 2016 and formally institutionalized through a Regent’s Decree, the module has been integrated into the elementary school curriculum. The learning approach combines classroom-based instruction with outdoor and experiential activities, including direct environmental observation, while incorporating local wisdom such as sasi as a foundational element of resource management.
The module content emphasizes sustainable environmental management, with a focus on three critical coastal ecosystems: coral reefs, seagrass meadows, and mangrove forests, while contextualizing scientific concepts within traditional practices and community values. By positioning the surrounding environment and local knowledge as key learning resources, the program aims to enhance students’ analytical skills, environmental literacy, and appreciation of customary conservation systems. Ultimately, this approach supports the development of future generations who are informed, culturally grounded, and committed to sustainable coastal management in Raja Ampat.

Keywords: Raja Ampat, environmental education, coral reefs, students, mangroves, sasi
73f2e8d1: Indonesia's archipelago has 41% of worldwide climate-resilient reefs, making it strategically necessary to strengthen active involvement with local communities to ensure long-term conservation success. Reef Check Indonesia provides lessons learned on developing national and sub-national HUBs within bio-climatic units to support locally driven and inclusive conservation of climate-resilient reefs. In the archipelago, several unique and successful approaches were found from local and indigenous communities through their traditional knowledge and vision for climate-resilient reef conservation. Community in Bali and East Nusa Tenggara with Awig-awig and a temporary "open and close" principle like Sasi (eastern Indonesia) and Hadi Mulung (in Alor Islands) shows that socio-cultural connection to their reefs, relying heavily on food, income, and traditional practices. However, increasing local stresses on coral reefs stem primarily from direct human activities in coastal areas, and global threats to ecosystems are also leading to declining of “our hope” climate-resilient reefs as well as social and economic decline. To better integrate local knowledge and vision into national coral reef management, supported by the GEF7 CRR Project, Reef Check Indonesia initiated an inclusive National Coral Reef HUB, a platform laid in national government structure where a range of reef stakeholders may collaborate and coordinate, as well as get support from the technical working group in HUB, for conserving climate-resilient reefs. An innovative “buddy” system was approached in HUB. Partnering HUB’s community members with local NGOs or entities to enable and encourage inclusive representation in conservation decisions. Local remote fisher group in Anambas, with Anambas Foundation improves knowledge on conserving their climate refugee reef. Also community in Alor with local university and WWF, community group in Baranglompo Makasar, and Negeri Ameth Ambon. Strengthening these systems alongside scientific support and ownership ensures a pathway to more resilient reefs and empowered communities in an archipelagic country like Indonesia.
fa4da201:
Coral bleaching has emerged as a major environmental issue, primarily driven by rising sea surface temperatures, extreme weather events, and pollution that impede coral recovery. This global study analyzes the spatial and temporal dynamics of coral bleaching events from 1982 to 2023, focusing on long-term patterns and the impact of El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) phases on bleaching prevalence. Utilizing the Global Coral Bleaching Database (GCBD), the research examines maximum and minimum percent bleaching across 32,554 records collected over four decades.
Advanced geostatistical techniques were applied, including annual central feature analysis to evaluate spatial dispersion, Emerging Hot Spots Analysis (EHSA), and optimized outlier analysis to assess space-time clustering and spatial autocorrelation. Analyses were weighted by bleaching severity and geospatial attributes, such as density and distance. Results highlighted three main types of spatial clusters for percent maximum bleaching: sporadic hot spots (22.78%), oscillating cold spots (13.51%), and oscillating hot spots (3.09%). For percent minimum bleaching, only oscillating cold spots (13.51%) and sporadic hot spots (7.34%) were observed.
A key finding was the reversal in significant spatial clustering (p-value ≥ 0.05) between El Niño and La Niña phases. High-high clusters were common during El Niño years, especially in the Indian Ocean, Southeast Asia, and Northeast Australia, while La Niña years saw low-low clusters in those same regions. This indicates a pronounced spatial and temporal response of coral reefs to shifting oceanographic and climatic conditions.
Overall, the study finds that the frequency and severity of coral bleaching are likely to increase due to ongoing climate change and human activities. Analyzing spatial patterns provides critical insight into the distribution of bleaching events and ENSO’s role, emphasizing the urgent need for conservation strategies to bolster coral resilience against environmental stressors.
f74196d2: Extreme waves generated by tropical cyclones intermittently cause catastrophic damage to coral communities on reefs, diminishing their ability to perform key ecosystem services like shoreline protection and habitat provision for fish.  People often assume that such storms will become more damaging to reefs as the climate continues to warm. However, for the Great Barrier Reef, numerical wave models run for thousands of future cyclone tracks generated by a global climate model for both the current climate and a near future (2050) time frame at a worst case 8.5 degree scenario show that this is not the case.  For both relatively common (return time 10 years) and rare (return time 100 years) cyclone events, the modelled exposure of the vast majority of reef communities to cyclone generated waves capable of damaging most vulnerable corals either does not change or declines when compared to the current climate.  We tested this for a range of modelled wave parameters including significant wave height and bottom stress, and separately for each of 8 incoming wave directions, and for the magnitude of wave energy as well as its duration over a presumed damage threshold. While exposure to long lasting damaging waves and extreme wave heights was highly spatially variable within and between reefs, the lack of an increase in exposure was consistent across the vast majority of the Great Barrier Reef. These results illustrate the importance of spatially explicit modelling of reef exposure to cyclones instead of applying ‘common sense’ approximations. Importantly, however, this modelling informs the risk of exposure to but not actual damage from cyclone waves.  Actual damage depends on local scale characteristics of corals that affect their structural vulnerability (size, growth form, history of other disturbance, strength of attachment to the substratum) which are poorly known for most reefs.
c617c007: The primary consequence of global warming for reefs is coral bleaching, often leading to extensive coral mortality. Although bleaching is well-documented globally, the thermal stress and bleaching experienced by the unique South Atlantic reefs remain  largely unknown due to insufficient monitoring on both spatial and temporal scales. Therefore, this work aimed to reconstruct past thermal stress episodes across South Atlantic reefs, and assessed whether episodes are becoming more intense, longer-lasting, and more frequent. We retrieved daily 5 km-resolution Degree Heating Week (DHW) data from the U.S. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Coral Reef Watch server for 33 reef sites spanning the last 40 years. For each thermal stress episode, we assessed the intensity (maximum DHW value), duration (number of continuous days under stress), and frequency (number of days between episodes). Generalized linear models were fitted to intensity, duration, and frequency data to evaluate the influence of latitude and the time x region interaction as predictors. We recorded multiple thermal stress episodes, increasing from 2010 onwards, ranging from 10 episodes between 1985–89 and 75 between 2020–24. Intensity and duration increased over time across the entire South Atlantic. Frequency also increased across the Southwestern Atlantic coast and oceanic islands, but not for Africa. Episodes at higher latitudes were more intense, prolonged, and frequent. The validity of the thermal stress history reconstruction was groundtruthed using information from the Abrolhos Bank, the only consistently monitored reef site in the South Atlantic—DHW data accurately matched the observed bleaching episodes at this site. With this, our dataset shows that multiple bleaching episodes likely occurred in the South Atlantic, but went undocumented in the field. Therefore, the information currently available for the South Atlantic likely underestimates the extent of bleaching occurring in the area, which is experiencing increases in intensity, duration, and frequency of thermal stress.
d6517e2e: Reef islands are small, low-lying landforms composed of loose reef-derived sediments, making them highly dynamic and susceptible to climate-driven coastal hazards. Previous research has largely concentrated on planimetric (2D) shoreline changes to assess island persistence, leaving an important gap in understanding how these changes translate into three dimensions (3D). This study addresses that gap by mapping and monitoring reef island shorelines in both horizontal and vertical dimensions and examining whether 3D changes correspond with 2D trends. Field surveys were conducted on Ulithi Atoll (Micronesia) using Structure-from-Motion (SfM) photogrammetry and mobile laser scanning. Shoreline indicators such as vegetation line, beach berm, and beach volume were analyzed to compare 2D and 3D changes. Findings reveal that 3D changes do not consistently mirror 2D patterns and vary across islands and indicators, indicating that long-term planform studies reporting stability or accretion may need to be interpreted with caution. This research demonstrates that 3D shoreline dynamics can be effectively quantified using drone-based SfM and laser scanning, though each technique involves trade-offs in acquisition time, cost, resolution, and coverage. The results refine conceptual and methodological approaches for monitoring reef island evolution and underscore the importance of selecting appropriate shoreline proxies. A better understanding of 3D changes improves predictive models of island futures and informs climate adaptation strategies, critical for atoll nations facing severe climate uncertainty and limited relocation options.
9ad97324:
Bleaching-driven shifts in coral-Symbiodiniaceae  symbiosis are well documented, but the relative roles of host-internal processes versus environmental symbiont reservoirs to community restructuring remain poorly resolved. To better understanding coral bleaching, and to improve future bleaching predictions, we must improve our understanding of the mechanisms of bleaching occurrence and recovery within this  tripartite system. Here, we addressed this by investigating the Symbiodiniaceae community dynamics between corals , the surrounding water column and the interstitial sediment water  before and during bleaching. Tracking 75 coral colonies (Porites lutea) during 2022 (non-bleaching year) and 2024 (severe bleaching year ) around Mayotte, Western Indian Ocean, we investigated associated Symbiodiniaceae communities over space and time, using ITS2 & psbA metabarcoding. Our results show that, during periods of low bleaching stress (2022), symbiont communities exhibited predictable spatial and seasonal structures that were closely aligned to the environmentally based symbiont communities in the water column and sediment. 
However, during periods of high thermal stress (the 2024 bleaching event), coordinated shifts within and between Symbiodiniaceae communities associated with the coral host, water column and interstitial sediment water, far beyond the variability displayed during non-bleaching time. This suggests that bleaching restructures the tripartite host-water column-sediment dynamics, through acclimatory or selective filtering process  es within and between compartments. 
Although we do not yet know if these shifts will persist, the alignment of changes across compartments points to a critical role for environmental reservoirs in regulating post-bleaching host-symbiont dynamics. The additional impact of tropical storms (e.g. Chido in December 2024, Dikeledi in January 2025 in this region) may further complicate recovery trajectories, highlighting the need to consider multiple interacting stressors in future assessments.
2e6acc54:
Bleaching and coral disease are escalating globally, yet confirmed pathogen–disease relationships remain exceptionally rare. The complexity of coral microbiomes makes establishing causality difficult, and worldwide, only six studies have experimentally fulfilled Koch’s postulates for any coral disease. Here, we report the first confirmed identification of Vibrio owensii as the cause of rapid tissue loss in Acropora cf. hemprichii, making it the 7th verified coral pathogen globally. Two symptomatic A. hemprichii colonies exhibiting rapid tissue loss were sampled for bacterial isolation. Full-length 16S rRNA gene sequencing revealed a single Vibrio isolate (VBM40) uniquely associated with diseased tissue. Phylogenetic analysis placed this isolate within the Vibrio harveyi clade - a group containing several known marine pathogens.
To test pathogenicity, healthy A. hemprichii fragments were exposed to placebo (3.5% NaCl solution) or VBM40 (106 cells/ mL) at 25 °C and 33 °C. Corals exposed to VBM40 developed multifocal lesions characteristic of white syndromes, with disease onset accelerated under heat stress  (21 days at 33 °C and 30 days at 25 °C). Mortality was significantly higher in VBM40 treatments at both temperatures. Whole genome sequencing of the bacteria re-isolated from diseased fragments confirmed>99.97% ANI with the original inoculum, fully satisfying Koch’s postulates. Comparative genomics identified VBM40 as Vibrio owensii, sharing >96% ANI with the type strain DY05.
This study provides the first validated report of Vibrio owensii VBM40 as an etiological agent of rapid tissue loss in a coral genus other than Montipora, more specifically in A. hemprichii, and represents one of only seven confirmed coral disease causations worldwide. Our findings underscore the need for targeted pathogen surveillance and proactive reef health management as environmental pressures increase.
5ae55183: The combination of coral bleaching and disease outbreaks have caused substantial declines in staghorn corals (Acropora spp.) in Guam, Micronesia, over the past decade. While the effects and mechanisms of coral bleaching are well-studied, the process driving coral disease dynamics remain poorly understood. Acropora White Syndrome (AWS), characterized by rapid tissue loss progressing from an active disease margin, is a major source of mortality in rehabilitation efforts, yet its transmissibility and population-level susceptibility remain gaps in our knowledge. A direct transmission experiment was conducted in closed mesocosms at the University of Guam Marine Laboratory. Healthy fragments of Acropora cf. pulchra from three reef flat source populations (southern Cocos Lagoon, west-central Agat, and northern Urunao) were paired in direct contact with either visually healthy fragments or fragments bearing active AWS lesions collected from Tumon Bay during an outbreak. Disease transmission and progression rates were monitored over two weeks using photography and ImageJ analyses, and recovery was assessed for an additional two-weeks post-exposure period. Tissue samples collected prior to exposure, 24 hr post-transmission, and at the end of the experiment are being analyzed using 16S rRNA metabarcoding to characterize host-associated bacterial communities and ITS2 barcoding to profile Symbiodiniaceae genera. Preliminary observations suggest evidence for direct transmission and population-level differences in susceptibility to AWS. Agat-sourced fragments exhibited the highest susceptibility with 90% of disease-paired fragments showing active lesions, compared to 30% for Cocos Lagoon-sourced fragments and 40% for Urunao-sourced fragments. Additionally, observations suggest a lower mortality rate and slower progression rate in Cocos Lagoon-sourced fragments. Ongoing microbiome analyses aim to identify dysbiosis patterns underlying intra-specific differences in AWS susceptibility across Guam’s Acropora populations. Together, these results will help identify vulnerable and resilient source populations to AWS, providing critical guidance for targeted rehabilitation and conservation strategies aimed at mitigating future disease-driven declines.
f7025eaa: Managers in American Samoa have prioritized coral bleaching and disease for research and management but effective strategies requires understanding disease processes.  In American Samoa, acroporid corals are especially vulnerable to diseases including Acropora growth anomalies (AGAs).  Although the cause of AGAs are unknown, they are associated with reduced colony growth and reproduction and can lower population fitness.  We surveyed Fagatele Bay National Marine Sanctuary in 2011 and 2012 to: (1) quantify AGA prevalence and incidence, (2) assess impacts on colony growth, mortality, and reproduction, (3) measure disease progression, and (4) test whether lesion removal is a viable management tool.  AGA prevalence was 0.55% (SE+0.21) in 2011 and 0.79% (SE+0.17) in 2012.  Between 2011 and 2012, AGA-affected A. hyacinthus colonies decreased in size and most loss appeared due to breakage rather than tissue death. GA colonies developed partial mortality on prior healthy sections as well as within growth anomalies, and the AGA case fatality rate was 8.3%.  Reproductive output (eggs per polyp) did not differ between healthy control colonies and healthy tissue on affected colonies but egg numbers were sharply reduced within growth anomalies. AGA is a progressive disease; 10 of 12 tagged colonies (83%) developed between 1 to 8 new growth anomalies between 2011 and 2012. In 2011, we removed GAs from five A. hyacinthus colonies to test removal as a treatment.  All removal lesions healed well; however, AGAs regrew in 4 of 5 colonies, both at the removal margins and at new sites elsewhere. Treated colonies appeared more resilient, showing less breakage and partial mortality than untreated colonies.  The small sample size warrants further study.
2543a3a9:
More than 65% of coral disease studies still depend on underwater identification guides, despite evidence of inconsistency and frequent misclassification. Although existing morphologic lesion terminology provides an essential foundation, the field still lacks a comprehensive diagnostic framework that integrates macroscopic morphology, skeletal pathology, physiological impairment, microbial etiology, and disease progression. This gap constrains accurate diagnosis, early detection, and effective disease response.
We applied a holobiont-integrated diagnostic framework to seven infectious coral diseases across the Republic of Mauritius: White Band, White Plague, White Syndrome, Brown Band, Black Band, Skeletal Eroding Band (SEB), and Yellow Blotch. Lesion characterisation coupled with scanning electron microscopy revealed significant alterations in corallite diameter, septal width, and micro-architecture in White Band, SEB, and Yellow Blotch (p < 0.05) - signatures not discernible via field-based methods.
Photo-physiological assessments using Diving- and Imaging-PAM fluorometry showed consistent reductions in Fv/Fm, rETRmax, and NPQmax across all diseases, demonstrating substantial impairment of symbiont photochemistry in infected tissues. Molecular pathogen screening confirmed hallmark etiological agents: Scuticociliatia ciliates in Brown Band and SEB, and Vibrio coralliilyticus, Vibrio shilonii, and Aurantimonas corallicida in White Plague lesions. Disease progression measurements revealed rapid advancement, with White Band (0.64 ± 0.25 cm/day) and White Plague (0.54 ± 0.02 cm/day) spreading fastest, followed by Brown Band, Black Band, and SEB.
Accurate identification of each infectious syndrome and assessment of prevalence levels during surveys was only possible after adopting the diagnostic framework, particularly morphological characterisation, which enabled consistent lesion staging, differentiation among visually similar conditions, and detection of significant spatio-temporal differences in prevalence around Mauritius Island. By integrating morphology, microscopy, physiology, molecular diagnostics, and progression rates, this study provides one of the first holistic infectious coral disease diagnostic frameworks for the Western Indian Ocean, substantially improving diagnostic accuracy and supporting earlier, more effective management strategies.
82f4850a: Pink-pigmented inflammations are frequently observed in corals in response to biotic or abiotic stress. However, the influence of pink-pigmentation on coral defense and subsequent stress recovery is largely uncharacterised. Hence, this study aims to investigate the genomic characterisation of healthy and pigmented colonies of Porites lutea. The pigmented response showed differential expression of coral defense responsive genes such as 2-alkenal reductase, which is involved in the degrading reactive carbonyl species, upregulation of trypsin which acts against pathogens and predators, ROS scavengers like peroxidase, pattern recognition receptors such as ficolins, apoptosis regulators like Ras-related proteins and xenobiotic transporters such as xenobiotic transporting ATPase, etc. This suggests the pink inflammatory responses may play a role in enhancing the immune system towards coral resilience from the ongoing stress. Additionally, this was further supported by transcriptome analysis exhibiting the expression of the FoxO signaling pathway involved in defense mechanisms, the coral growth-promoting Wnt signaling pathway, the Fanconi anemia pathway involved in DNA repair, activation of the Notch signaling pathway responsible for recognising native coral symbionts and expression of apoptosis-related gene essential for coral development, morphogenesis and immunity. Furthermore, the proteomic study identified the expression of proteins such as red fluorescent protein, green fluorescent protein, serine protease and metalloprotease, etc., which are involved in innate immune responses. Thus, the pink inflammatory responses characterised in the present study may help to elucidate mechanisms underlying coral survival and stress tolerance.
5146feba:
Coral reefs are a critical example of diverse and highly productive marine ecosystems, yet they are increasingly affected by diseases that contribute to declines in coral populations. Human impacts such as overfishing and pollution can accelerate disease prevalence, making it essential to improve our understanding of their distribution and causative agents. Examining the diversity of organisms associated with coral diseases can provide insight into the interactions that drive disease dynamics. Our objective was to identify agents causing multifocal pink spots (MPS) on Porites, reported in 2021 and 2022 in Mo’orea, French Polynesia. MPS are a distinctive immune response of Porites, characterized by single swollen pink polyps. Causative agents such as digenetic trematodes and endosymbiotic barnacles have been documented to trigger this response, which can reduce colony growth by up to 50% due to predation. 
Surveys along the north shore were conducted in June–July 2021, April–May 2022, and August–September 2022. To determine causative agents, we employed photography, histology, and dissections. Photography targeted visible external agents, histology enabled cellular-level identification, and dissections examined internal agents. Across these methods, endosymbiotic barnacles were consistently detected, while nematodes and potential digenetic trematode cysts were primarily identified through dissections. Notably, barnacles and various helminths frequently co-occurred within individual polyps, revealing complex multi-organism associations. DNA analyses are needed to further confirm trematode cysts, highlighting the importance of multiple approaches for assessing causative agent diversity in coral diseases such as MPS. Overall, this study documents, for the first time in this region, the occurrence of multiple agents associated with MPS on Porites, advancing our understanding of coral disease distribution and multi-organism interactions across the Indo-Pacific.
143d35fe: Human pathogenic agents capable of surviving in marine environments represent a growing threat to public health and ocean biodiversity. Hospital acquired, antibiotic resistant bacteria (E. coli, Enterococcus, Salmonella) as well as intrinsically virulent marine species (Vibrio spp.) are now being detected in coastal waters, where they can infect humans through consumption of seafood and could cause disease in marine organisms. Virulence factors and antibiotic resistance, often plasmid borne, can spread by horizontal transfer to non pathogenic bacteria in the marine community, thereby amplifying emerging risks.  Our project aims to unravel the molecular and ecological mechanisms that allow these pathogens to survive, persist, and infect marine hosts. Adopting a One Health perspective with reciprocal concerns for human and environmental wellness, we will investigate four main components of marine zooanthroponosis : 1 – Environmental parameters – we examine the impact of salinity (0–40 g L⁻¹) and temperature (20–37 °C) on the survival and virulence of human pathogens. 2 – Chemosensory signaling – we assess the detection of host signals and the expression of virulence factors through digital PCR. 3 – Persistence dynamics – we measure the functional half life of strains in saltwater, with or without a marine host (anemone, oyster), combining viable counts, flow cytometry, and Weibull survival models. And, 4 – Horizontal transfer – we assess the potential for transfer of virulence and resistance genes between pathogens and marine microbiomes using fluorescent markers (GFP) and phenotypic assays.  Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for predicting the ecological and health consequences of contaminating marine waters with human pathogens, and for evaluating emerging risks associated with the evolution of these infectious agents in a climate change context.
01c51a65:
The coral reef ecosystem in Karimunjawa National Park (KNP) is experiencing increasing pressure from climate change and land-based human activities, including shrimp pond expansion, rapid tourism growth, and inadequate waste management. These pressures have degraded water quality and coral health, evidenced by bleaching, coral diseases, structural damage, and algal blooms across several sites. This study aimed to assess spatial variation in water quality across KNP to understand how human activity intensity influences coral reef condition. 
Water quality monitoring was conducted at 21 sites over 18 months (2024 to 2025), representing gradients of anthropogenic impact. Key parameters, including nitrate, phosphate, E. coli, dissolved oxygen, pH, salinity, water temperature, and total dissolved solids, were measured and compared with Indonesia’s national marine water quality standards (IS). 
The results indicate that at the sites where exceedances occurred, several parameters surpassed IS values, including phosphate (IS = 0.15 mg/L; KNP = 0.16–0.048 mg/L; n = 13 sites), E. coli (IS = 200 units/mL; KNP = 240–557.5 units/mL; n = 6), salinity (IS = 33–34 ppt; KNP = 19.3 ppt; n = 1), and dissolved oxygen (IS > 5 mg/L; KNP = 3.1–3.3 mg/L; n = 2). Water quality declined progressively from nearshore to offshore, indicating a strong land-based influence. Correspondingly, 10.5% of outlet sites, 5.1% of shallow sites, and 1.3% of reef sites showed at least one exceedance. By dominant activity, exceedances were highest at shrimp ponds (9%), followed by seaports (6.4%) and settlements (5.1%).
These findings highlight how terrestrial and coastal development degrade water quality and jeopardize coral reef health in KNP. The evidence informed the closure of four shrimp farms and is now driving WCS and KNP’s co-development of an integrated sanitation and wastewater management approach to enhance island-wide water quality and secure long-term ecosystem and livelihood sustainability.
14d1aea6:
The Great Barrier Reef (GBR) in Queensland Australia is a large and iconic marine ecosystem under sustained pressure from anthropogenic activities. It extends approximately 2,600 kilometres along the Queensland coast and covers a total area of 345,000 km2. The adjacent catchment area, which is intimately connected to the marine ecosystem, also covers an area of 423,000 km2 and contains 35 major basins. A spatial management prioritisation method was developed for the to provide robust, scientifically defensible guidance for managing land-based runoff and reducing marine pollution. The project integrated catchment characteristics, pollutant sources and loads, pollutant exposure, and ecosystem condition into a decision support framework to guide priorities for water quality management at a drainage basin scale. This framework combines stressor based goals, which aim to reduce pollutant pressures, with outcome based goals, which focus on maintaining or improving ecosystem condition. Four levels of management response were identified: Foundational (maintaining ecosystems and baseline practices and compliance), Proactive (localised interventions for smaller scale and/or emerging issues), Accelerated (targeted, multi land use strategies for moderate pollutant exposure), and Intensive (urgent, large scale and integrated actions for high pollutant exposure). Ecosystem condition further refines responses, with tailored actions for good, moderate, and poor condition systems.
The assessment highlighted priority areas by basin and pollutant type. The framework provides decision-makers with evidence-based spatial priorities, balancing immediate pollutant reduction with long-term resilience. Regular updates are recommended to ensure relevance, with strategies tailored to local contexts while maintaining a whole-of-system perspective.
2bb582ec:
Coral reef ecosystems are experiencing unprecedented decline due to the combined pressures of climate change and persistent anthropogenic pollution. While global assessments project widespread loss of positive net carbonate production, limited field data linking in situ metabolic rates with carbonate chemistry constrain the development of site-specific management strategies. To address this gap, we quantified the environmental controls of Net Community Production (NCP) and Net Community Calcification (NCC) in the shallow lagoon of Kavaratti Atoll, Lakshadweep, one of India’s most population-intense coral reef systems.
Using the alkalinity anomaly method integrated with a Random-Forest-RFE–GAM modelling framework, we achieved high predictive accuracy (Adjusted R² = 0.96 for NCP). Results revealed strong non-linear regulation of NCP by pCO₂, temperature, and live coral cover (LCC), with pCO₂ showing the highest non-linear effect (EDF = 3.27), indicating susceptibility to metabolic tipping points under rising CO₂. NCC was mainly influenced by aragonite saturation state (Ωarag) and Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR), indicating partial metabolic resilience but with significant energetic constraints.
The lagoon showed persistent signs of pollution, marked by chronic nitrogen limitation (N/P < 16), elevated ammonium, and intermittent spikes in dissolved inorganic phosphorus (DIP), likely influenced by land-derived organic inputs. These inputs enhance community respiration and elevate lagoon pCO₂, creating a compounded stress pathway where local pollution interacts collectively with ocean acidification to suppress both NCP and NCC.
This study establishes the first predictive metabolic threshold framework for Lakshadweep reefs, offering managers actionable tools to anticipate shifts from autotrophy to heterotrophy and from net accretion to erosion. The findings highlight the urgency of wastewater reform and nutrient-input controls. Reducing land-based pollution can directly buffer carbonate chemistry, strengthen metabolic resilience, and provide a scalable science–policy model for pollution management in coral reef lagoons globally.
67e37b0d: Siquijor’s growing tourism economy has heightened local concern about the condition of freshwater sources and emerging coastal water-quality issues. To provide a low-cost, actionable baseline for decision-making, Wildlife Conservation Society implemented the Coastal Pollution Toolkit alongside a Sphere Handbook–aligned WASH Technical Assessment across all six municipalities (2024–2025). The situation assessment combined policy review, source-to-sea mapping of 20 freshwater bodies, site inspections, and key informant interviews with households, tourism workers, and barangay officials. Policy documents—including CLUPs, LCCAPs, and municipal sanitation plans—show limited guidance on managing nutrient and sediment inputs. Field mapping documented unprotected springs, communal washing points, and river crossings with frequent bathing and livestock use. Residents consistently reported that coastal algae had become more visible over the past five to seven years and that some water sources turn turbid after rainfall. Sanitation gaps were widespread: the province has no septage treatment facility, and in Larena 98% of households (3,304) rely on single-chamber or bottomless septic systems situated in highly permeable karst terrain. Several rivers in Lazi and Larena have been converted into straightened flood-control channels that transport runoff efficiently to the coast. This integrated toolkit assessment allowed LGUs to identify priority areas for intervention without the need for laboratory testing or high-cost equipment. The findings point to specific upstream drivers, including informal sanitation, washing practices in freshwater bodies, and altered drainage corridors, that inform where septic upgrades, alternative washing sites, watershed rehabilitation, or community IEC efforts can most effectively reduce land-based pollution. By demonstrating how simple diagnostic tools can translate local observations into management priorities, it gives emphasis on moving from knowledge to action in small-island contexts.
a1f49006: Despite the rapid expansion of coral reef restoration projects globally, few explicitly incorporate the principles of resilience-based management (RBM) to effectively adapt to and mitigate the impacts of changing ocean conditions. The newly released US National Coral Reef Resilience Strategy establishes a multidisciplinary framework where restoration is positioned not as an end in itself, but as a key strategic tool within a broader RBM approach. This presentation will offer a detailed overview of the Strategy’s restoration focus area, outlining its role in catalyzing collaborative action across federal agencies, state governments, academic institutions, and non-governmental organizations to meet the evolving needs of resource managers. We will specifically examine the objectives to "enhance population resilience," which places high priority on targeted research into the mechanisms of thermal tolerance and the systematic application of genetic management in restoration planning and implementation. The talk will also demonstrate how the Strategy effectively links high-level conservation goals—such as restoring diverse, reproductively viable coral populations —with concrete, practical objectives. These objectives include scaling up national capacity for sexual propagation techniques and accelerating the deployment of "stress-hardening" interventions. By aligning local action plans with this robust national strategy, practitioners can significantly improve their ability to secure sustained funding and vital support necessary for executing RBM restoration efforts.
2f4fc119: Effective collaboration between countries is essential for the conservation, protection and restoration of coral reefs. Coral researchers and restoration practitioners often collaborate internationally to exchange genetic material or live corals for research and conservation purposes. However, because stony corals are protected under national and international law, stakeholders must obtain various permits to transfer these types of samples between countries. Specific regulatory frameworks governing international exchange of coral include the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES), ratified in 1975, and the Nagoya Protocol on Access and Benefit Sharing which was initiated in 2010. Coral researchers and restoration practitioners are increasingly concerned that these regulatory processes will delay or complicate urgently needed interventions such as assisted gene flow and live biobanking. We are conducting surveys and interviews of coral researchers, restoration practitioners, and governmental stakeholders across the Caribbean to understand experiences with the process of obtaining/approving CITES and Nagoya permits for exchanges. In parallel, we undertook a literature review to elucidate where exchanges are occurring and to recruit participants. Results to date from interviews with 12 researchers representing 12 countries of export indicate that all respondents were required to obtain a CITES permit; however, only two acquired Nagoya permits despite most of the countries of sample origin having ratified the Nagoya Protocol. Further, experiences with obtaining CITES permits were highly variable – some respondents reported ease in acquiring permits, while others reported significant barriers including specific administrative processes, time delays, and financial challenges. Preliminary findings indicate substantial variation in experiences with permitting processes, and through continued analyses we aim to elucidate how these processes may impact the implementation of novel restoration interventions in the Caribbean. Finally, we aim to synthesize recommendations for stakeholders to facilitate a regulatory environment that facilitates use of novel interventions.
983ae10b:
The coral biota of Aotearoa New Zealand is remarkably rich and diverse, encompassing more than 300 legally protected species. Many corals thrive offshore in deeper waters and numerous research surveys and biodiversity inventories have greatly expanded our knowledge of their distribution and diversity. These voyages have also built a national collection of catalogued specimens, which provides an invaluable resource for ongoing research. Our understanding of deep-sea corals has also advanced through the work of government fisheries observers, who collect data and specimens from commercial vessels. As corals frequently occur as bycatch in New Zealand’s deep-sea trawl fisheries, their observations continually underpin critical insights into coral ecology and anthropogenic impacts. 

The Department of Conservation, the government agency responsible for wildlife management, administers the Conservation Services Programme (CSP). CSP supports research into bycatch and the broader environmental impacts of fishing through the administration of cost-recovered levies from quota owners of commercial fish stocks. Since these impacts include direct effects on protected species, CSP runs a dedicated scientific programme to better understand and mitigate fishing pressures on marine fauna. This includes corals in four taxonomic groups as described in legislation: all species in the Octocorallia, Scleractinia, Antipatharia, and the Stylasteridae.

Funding through conservation service levies is legally entrenched, providing ongoing CSP support for research relevant to managing the impacts of commercial fishing. Research has spanned diverse themes—connectivity, species distribution modelling, reproduction, genetics, and biodiversity. This talk highlights the breadth of coral research undertaken through CSP, now in its 30th year, and explores the programme’s distinctive model, its scientific contributions, ongoing challenges, and future directions for both research and coral conservation in New Zealand.
4ec67cb9: Ocean- and land-based coral restoration nurseries have been established in many locations throughout the greater Caribbean and are run by a mix of for-profit, governmental, academic, and non-governmental organizations, each with their own goals, timelines, and restoration plans. Despite widespread propagation efforts, the once dominant and currently threatened Caribbean elkhorn coral (Acropora palmata) has continued to undergo precipitous declines. An effort began in 2020 in Florida to coordinate in situ and ex situ conservation efforts for A. palmata among researchers, managers, and restoration practitioners to better manage captive and outplanted population segments in a more collaborative and strategic way. We convened institutions and practitioners maintaining A. palmata and gathered genetic and demographic data on collections, propagation, and outplanting. The goal is to use this information to make recommendations and informed decisions designed to maintain demographic stability and protect existing gene diversity, ultimately safeguarding the species from extinction. Through a comprehensive status assessment, we found that there are only about 150 unrelated genets (founders) remaining in Florida, the vast majority of which have been extirpated in the wild and are only found in human care. Many of these genets were held in a single location in a single institution, but through coordinated efforts, all founder genets are now represented in two land-based gene banks as recommended in annual management guidelines developed as part of the coordination. Future short-term recommendations and goals of coordination include distribution of all founder genets among all institutions, equalizing representation of founder genets in human care so that none are over or under-represented, and minimizing mean population kinship through breeding recommendations to minimize risks from inbreeding and loss of diversity important for adaptability.
