This process for developing scientific population management for A. palmata in Florida can be used as a model for other threatened coral species worldwide.
b40aea0b: Coral reef ecosystems are under threat globally from anthropogenic activities, prompting efforts to manage and restore these vital habitats. Evaluating the success of such efforts requires rigorous, scalable, and repeatable monitoring methods. Advances in acoustic recorders and AI now make passive acoustic surveys a promising tool for large-scale reef monitoring. Here, we present results of passive acoustic surveys we conducted to expand monitoring duration at coral research sites and measure effectiveness of reef restoration efforts.  We deployed marine automated recording (ARU) units from August 2023 to November 2024 at six sites around Mo’orea, with one ARU in each site across reef habitat: fore reef, fringing reef, and back reef.  Additional ARUs were placed at a coral outplanting restoration site and a nearby unmanipulated control site.  We developed a convolutional neural network model to detect and classify reef-related sounds.  We present data from four ecologically important signals: ‘parrotfish grazing’ and ‘damselfish calls’,  a ‘knock’ signal produced by unknown fish species, and a metric of phonic richness for signals generated by fish.  We present differences in acoustic detection rates between three coral and three algal dominated sites. Preliminary results between coral and algal sites were not significant for any metric.   We also compared the detection rates at the experimental coral restoration and control sites for the four metrics, but with sample size n=1, these results are anecdotal.  We found all four metrics to be different between reef types. This study shows the potential for passive acoustic monitoring of coral reef communities, highlights some of the challenges that still need to be addressed to improve on species detection and classification, and provides practical examples of ways to efficiently measure outcomes of coral reef restoration efforts.
6a0e57b4:
Global coral reefs face catastrophic decline due to climate change and anthropogenic stressors. While restoration efforts are expanding, conventional fragmentation techniques remain labour-intensive and limited in scalability. This study introduces a novel, scalable approach: regenerating coral colonies from isolated individual polyps anchored on upcycled industrial waste. Drawing on the remarkable similarities between coral calcification and vertebrate bone formation, we hypothesised that titanium - widely recognised in biomedical implants for its osteoconductivity and biocompatibility - could serve as an ideal substrate for coral growth. To explore this potential while promoting circular resource use, we investigated the utility of titanium swarf (machining waste) as a scaffold.
Titanium swarf, characterised by its ribbon-like geometry, high surface roughness, and corrosion resistance, was compacted into disks. This process created a complex, porous structure mimicking natural reef crevices, ideal for initial polyp settlement. Coral polyps were isolated using a salinity-adjustment method to induce polyp bailout without chemical agents and subsequently seeded onto these scaffolds.
Observations revealed that polyps adhered firmly to the titanium surface and remained attached for over 30 days, demonstrating strong substrate compatibility. Tissue expansion across the irregular metallic surface was evident, followed by the rapid deposition of a calcium carbonate skeleton. Microscopic analysis confirmed the formation of skeletal ridges and growth lines physically integrated with the titanium micro-texture, comparable to osteointegration. Furthermore, isolated polyps exhibited clear signs of asexual reproduction, including budding and tissue spreading. These results indicate that even minimal units can initiate viable colony formation when provided with a structurally and chemically favourable substrate. Consequently, upcycled titanium swarf scaffolds offer a dual solution: a robust platform for high-efficiency coral regeneration and a sustainable strategy for industrial waste management.
c0575696:
Fish-i, a patented semi-automated reef fish visual census system, is designed to democratize marine biodiversity assessments by enabling consistent, expert-level analysis through AI-assisted video processing. Initially tested across the Philippines, Fish-i has now expanded globally, demonstrating versatility across diverse ecological and management contexts. This presentation explores three major deployments—in Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, and the Philippines—highlighting how Fish-i adapts to MPA assessment, environmental impact analysis, and site-specific monitoring.
In Banggai, Indonesia, Fish-i supported MPA performance evaluations by enabling local partners to independently conduct surveys using standard Fish-i rigs. The system captured high-resolution species assemblage profiles, helping validate protection effectiveness and guide future zoning strategies.
In the Red Sea, data gathered using Fish-i demonstrated the system’s adaptability beyond the Coral Triangle and its potential use in varied environmental contexts. These deployments show how future initiatives in the region—and similar settings—can integrate Fish-i to support standardized monitoring and assessment workflows.
In the Philippines, Fish-i continues to evolve through DeepFish 360 integrations for mesophotic exploration and the initial field validation of the Fish-i Box in Tandag, Surigao del Sur—a portable, site-specific black box enabling communities to process data on-site without high-computing infrastructure.
These applications showcase Fish-i as a flexible, scalable tool bridging science, technology, and local capacity. By making reef assessment faster, more accessible, and more standardized, Fish-i empowers governments, communities, and researchers to respond more effectively to accelerating reef degradation and management challenges.
a798ae77:
Coral reef ecosystems are increasingly threatened by global warming, which is driving more frequent and intense bleaching events. However, coral restoration efforts aim to preserve reef surface and biodiversity. Shading corals to reduce bleaching has been proposed, but like many interventions, its implementation remains limited in scale due to high costs. 

In French Polynesia, limited land availability but extensive lagoon areas create an opportunity to test marine renewable energy technologies. This context led to a unique collaboration between Total Energies and CRIOBE to develop a floating photovoltaic (FPV) project in the lagoon of Raʻiātea. The project aims to evaluate whether FPV could contribute both to low-carbon electricity production and support coral reef ecosystem resilience. 

The project is deliberately multidisciplinary. Ecological monitoring assesses how prolonged shading affects coral energy budgets in nurseries and the long-term growth of coral pinnacles under different light regimes. In addition, a life-cycle assessment evaluates the impacts of FPV in a lagoon on resource use and pollutant emissions, while a remote-sensing-based assessment examines whether FPV is the most suitable technology across the different Polynesian islands and evaluates its socio-technical potential.

By combining ecological monitoring, engineering development, and spatial feasibility studies within a research-industry collaboration, this project aims to generate evidence on whether FPV can simultaneously support coral reef resilience and accelerate the transition toward low-carbon, locally produced energy. Ultimately, this project aims to inform French Polynesian and South Pacific decision-makers and energy companies about the conditions under which FPV could become a viable contribution to regional energy independence while potentially supporting coral reef ecosystem resilience.
c35769af: Climate change driven sea-level rise and increase in frequency and severity of storms is exacerbating coastal erosion and flooding risk. Existing coastal defence solutions prioritise civil engineering requirements while the ecological costs of these artificial structures are often overlooked. By applying ecological engineering principles, it is possible to integrate natural components into coastal defences to create ‘hybrid’ shorelines. Coral reefs are valued not only for their high biodiversity, but also for their role ability to dissipate wave energy. By designing breakwaters that encourage coral growth, there is an opportunity to increase both coastal protection and ecological function. Here we examine the wave-attenuating properties of breakwaters seeded with corals. Through 72 wave flume experiments, we explored the effects of two coral densities on the wave dissipation provided by a submerged breakwater model (1:5 scale) at three water depths and 16 wave conditions. 3D-printed coral colony models (1:5 scale) accurately replicated four common coral colony morphologies. We found that the breakwater model populated with 3D-printed corals dissipated more wave energy. Energy dissipation was highest at lower water depths, higher wave heights, higher wave periods, and with more coral models attached. These results demonstrate that integrating corals into breakwater design can simultaneously enhance coastal protection, support marine biodiversity, and provide a model for climate-resilient, ecologically-informed coastal infrastructure.
4d802376:
Coral reef conservation continues to be constrained by limited funding, high operational costs, and unequal access to emerging technologies. These barriers often prevent coral-dependent communities from contributing fully to monitoring, mapping, and managing their own reef systems. In Kenya, where coastal livelihoods rely heavily on healthy reefs, bridging this technical divide is critical. Our multi-institutional partnership uniting Kenyan research institutions (KMFRI, ERACOMA, CES, CORDIO, REEFolution), community organizations, international partners (IUCN, University of Technology Sydney), and the Allen Coral Atlas at Arizona State University demonstrates how cross-sector collaboration and youth-centered capacity building can accelerate reef-monitoring innovation and community stewardship.
Across a year-long, two-phase program, we assessed local knowledge, identified technological gaps, and co-designed community-led training on geospatial mapping, coral identification, participatory monitoring, and use of freely available satellite-derived reef data. A major innovation was the localized adaptation of global tools, including translating the online course Remote Sensing and Mapping for Coral Reef Conservation into Swahili and enabling community members to teach eight new marine management technical tools. This two-way exchange illustrates how global technologies become more accurate, accessible, and equitable when local users shape their evolution.
Our successes include establishing three youth-led technical training hubs along the Kenyan coast, enabling community members to analyze reef maps, validate satellite observations, and integrate monitoring outputs into marine protected area planning. Challenges included limited digital access in remote regions, youth career advancement, and the need for sustained funding to maintain monitoring momentum after the project period.
This work highlights the potential of cross-sector partnerships to democratize reef-monitoring technologies, strengthen local leadership, and enhance collaborative innovation for coral reef resilience. Our lessons learned provide a scalable model for integrating community expertise, technological tools, and global scientific networks to meet the growing demand for coral conservation solutions.
8f9873c6: Global marine biodiversity is concentrated in the Indo-Australian Archipelago today, where corals and other marine organisms achieve their greatest species richness. However, the fossil record suggests at least four marine biodiversity hotspots existed throughout the Cenozoic, waxing and waning in response to global environmental changes: (1) Western Tethys (Eocene–Oligocene); (2) Arabian (Eocene–Miocene); (3) Caribbean (Oligocene–Pliocene); and (4) Indo-Australian Archipelago (Miocene–Recent). While broadly recognised, fundamental gaps in our understanding of these biodiversity hotspots exist including the timing and drivers of their origin, persistence, and ultimate decline. Understanding these long-term dynamics is crucial for anticipating how modern biodiversity hotspots may respond to accelerating environmental change over the next century. Through the EDiTH project, we are investigating the diversity dynamics within Cenozoic marine tropical hotspots to reconstruct their macroevolutionary history and identify their long-term drivers. Through comparison across multiple marine biodiversity hotspots, we will identify—if any—general drivers of such concentrations of biodiversity. Here, we present initial work focused on reconstructing the evolutionary history of the Caribbean marine biodiversity hotspot. Using a comprehensive dataset of fossil scleractinian coral occurrences and sampling-standardisation approaches, we first estimate alpha, beta, and gamma diversity for the region. Extending upon these analyses, we apply state-of-the-art Bayesian modelling to estimate diversification rates throughout the Cenozoic. Our results provide an evolutionary baseline for the Caribbean marine biodiversity hotspot, enabling comparison with other Cenozoic marine biodiversity hotspots.
8880b0ba: Marine heatwaves are increasing in frequency and severity over the past decades causing extensive coral mortality mainly across the euphotic zone. In contrast, mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs), including both turbid, low-light shallow settings (“brown” MCEs) and the clear, low-light reefs of deeper waters (“blue” MCEs), often show greater resilience to thermal stress. This has led to the suggestion that MCEs may function as climate refugia as sea surface temperatures continue to increase and global warming intensifies. However, to which extent MCEs can play a refugia role in the long-term remains uncertain, because the temporal scale in which these processes operate far exceed our observational records. In this talk, we discuss the critical value of integrating palaeontological evidence to address this knowledge gap. The fossil record is unique in offering the necessary long-term perspective by integrating sedimentological, ecological, geochemical, and palaeogeographic proxies. Our preliminary analyses of reef systems –with corals as primary or secondary builders– across 485 million years (Ordovician to Pleistocene) show that during major rapid warming events, reef distributions consistently shifted toward higher latitudes and deeper (blue mesophotic) settings, demonstrating their potential as refugia. However, the recovery period often required millions of years. Further work will focus on reassessing the current data for more granularity within the classification framework, new depth and light proxies, and standardized databases to enable meaningful comparisons between fossil and modern reefs. By bridging neontological and palaeontological perspectives, we aim to improve understanding of MCEs’ capacity to buffer coral biodiversity and inform conservation strategies in a warming ocean.
46ac1847:
Rising ocean temperatures drive the poleward expansion of marine species, leading to the tropicalisation of subtropical and temperate marine ecosystems. In the past, historical glacial cycles have caused coral reef expansion and contraction, but potential similarities in community transitions during these periods remain unclear. By examining past coral distributions and life history traits, we gain insights into potential future tropicalisation and the functioning of higher-latitude coral communities in a warming world.

We predict coral genus distribution and trait compositions during the last glacial maximum (LGM) and mid-Holocene using Hierarchical Modelling of Species Communities (HMSC). We first model modern coral distribution along the Pacific coast of Japan with coral genus occurrence (1929-2019, 643 sites), environmental, and life history trait data. We then explore coral distribution and functioning in the mid-Holocene and last glacial maximum (LGM) by hindcasting our model using HadCM3 climate data.

Our findings reveal shifts in community and trait composition along Japan's latitudinal gradient in both modern and fossil coral assemblages. Genus prevalence and richness were higher in the modern period (1929-2019), closely linked to higher maximum temperatures that exceeded those of the mid-Holocene and Last Glacial Maximum. We found a shift in trait complexity through time from the stress-tolerant, simple and homogenised communities of the LGM to the more complex and diverse communities of today.

Our results suggest that within the Ryukyu Islands, the lower latitude islands retained high species richness throughout the LGM and mid-Holocene, allowing recolonisation at higher latitudes when conditions were more favourable. Future high-latitude coral communities may act as refuges for subtropical and temperate corals adapted to marginal conditions. However, they are unlikely to facilitate recolonisation of lower latitude reefs, even if sea surface temperatures cool, due to ocean currents and other biogeographical constraints.
52d2f93e: Contemporary carbonate budget models are widely used to assess reef growth potential, yet their performance across geological timescales remains largely untested. This study compares census-based net carbonate production estimates with Holocene accretion data from two reef cores in the Maldives. Radiometrically constrained accretion rates were converted into net carbonate production using a mass-balance framework incorporating aragonite density and framework porosity. These core-derived estimates are evaluated against hindcasted production values generated by applying modified ReefBudget equations to inferred coral cover. The results reveal a persistent mismatch: the ReefBudget approach yields near-zero or negative net production during periods of documented vertical reef accretion, whereas core-derived estimates indicate sustained positive accumulation of 1.8–8.1 kg CaCO₃ m⁻² yr⁻¹. This “carbonate production gap” reflects the cumulative effects of sediment retention, framework infill, and early lithification — processes recorded in reef stratigraphy but absent from census-based models. Stratigraphic archives, therefore, provide essential constraints for recalibrating carbonate budget approaches, ensuring long-term production estimates remain consistent with observed accretion histories across millennial timescales.
f1b40ceb: The ongoing threat to thriving coral reefs is currently attributed to several factors, including ocean warming, acidification, pollution, and disease outbreaks. Consequently, conventional reef development models may not accurately predict how reefs will respond in the future. In the Atlantic, non-traditional reef settings have been largely overlooked as potential analogues for past and future reef development. This is the case for Bermuda, an isolated atoll-like platform, which is located at 32°N. Rotary drill cores and vibracores have been extracted and analysed in detail. Key drivers of Holocene reef development indicate a “suppressed accretion” mode. The reefs of Bermuda exhibit a steady vertical accretion in the Holocene with an average rate of 2.18 ± 0.66 m/kyr, significantly lower than the reported average rates of 3–4 m/kyr for Western Atlantic reefs. The “suppressed accretion” mode is a complex process that reflects the combined influence of multiple factors, including the composition of the coral fauna (dominated by massive, stress-tolerant taxa and lacking competitive acroporids), environmental energy, sediment flux, bioerosion, taphonomic framework loss, and sea-level rise. Together, these circumstances result in the formation of reefs that have remained permanently submerged for several metres, establishing a submerged keep-up relationship between the living reefs and sea level throughout their entire development. In contrast to Western Atlantic reefs, which commonly show a declining trend in accretion rates throughout the mid to late Holocene, the continuously submerged nature of Bermuda reefs has prevented them from undergoing the same decline, enabling virtually uninterrupted accretion. The development model emerging from these mid to late Holocene reefs provides a valuable analogue for understanding reef responses under comparable environmental conditions and circumstances, whether in the geological past or in an increasingly uncertain future.
5d9eb6f2: One of the most promising techniques for reef rehabilitation is sexual coral propagation, which enables the production of genotypically diverse offspring and supports assisted evolution. However, this approach is limited by high costs, labour intensity, and low survivorship, restricting the current scale of application. There are no commercially available “off-the-shelf” devices specifically for settling, rearing, and outplanting sexually propagated corals that can be applied at scale across multiple species and environmental conditions. The lack of effective, scalable tools for coral sexual propagation is a major barrier to advancing reef restoration and assisted evolution. To address this, we have developed the Coralassist Plug (CAPs), a directly attached device designed to enhance the efficiency and scalability of coral sexual propagation. CAPs incorporate key innovations: efficient handling for settlement and rearing, built-in microrefugia to reduce early mortality from grazing, and a design that allows rapid, adhesive-free attachment to reefs. This allows a three-person dive team to outplant ~120 CAPs in one 90-minute shallow dive (just over two minutes per CAP per person). Long-term field trials in Palau, Micronesia using the corymbose coral Acropora aff. digitifera have demonstrated high rates of survivorship. With long ex-situ nursery durations (6-months) it was possible to achieve 36% yield (i.e., proportion of devices with a surviving coral) 4-years post-outplant. With ex-situ nursery durations shortened to 1-month, we were able to attain 16% yield four-years post-outplant. Microrefugia significantly enhanced post-outplant survivorship leading to an 11% increase in yield 4-years post outplant compared to devices without microrefugia. Outplanted corals had reached adult size, were self-attached and were reproductively mature after 4 years. Here we will discuss our vision to make CAPs available to a wide range of end users as a practical tool for applying coral sexual propagation at scale as part of reef restoration and assisted evolution efforts.
fb0e2044: A cornerstone of successful reef restoration is the recruitment and survival of coral larvae, which underpin the replenishment of coral populations. However, these early life stages experience high mortality rates, creating a significant bottleneck in population recovery. Optimizing settlement substrates is one proposed mechanism for improving restoration outcomes. Here, we investigate how ion supplementation, substrate porosity, and surface topography influence the settlement success of coral larvae from two Red Sea coral species currently used in restoration efforts. Coral larvae from Acropora spp. were exposed to various substrate types in controlled settlement assays: commercially available restoration substrates (Coralassist plugs, SECORE stars) and custom-engineered ClimateCrete tiles that incorporated variations in inorganic enrichment (CaCO₃ content, Mg²+, Sr²+, Fe³+), porosity, and surface topography (flat vs. grooved). Multiple trials were conducted during the 2024 and 2025 spawning seasons. During settlement assays, we quantified larval behavior, settlement rate after 48h, and early post-settlement survival after 7 weeks. In the first set of assays, Acropora spp. settlement rates were highest on ClimateCrete tiles with low carbonaceous sand content (22% settled) and high carbonaceous sand content tiles (18% settled), relative to Coralassist plugs and SECORE stars. In the second set of assays, the engineered ClimateCrete substrates incorporating grooves, Fe³+ ion additives, or increased porosity outperformed all other materials, achieving the highest settlement rates (38-41% settled) and the highest 7-week survival rate (33% survival). These findings demonstrate that CaCO₃-rich substrates, specific inorganic ion enrichments, and increased structural complexity can significantly enhance both larval settlement and early survival, with promising implications for substrate design in large-scale reef restoration applications.
f6232191: Coral restoration efforts have expanded globally in response to the declining natural recovery capacity of most degraded reefs. However, many emerging approaches remain experimental and under development, including coral larval enhancement through sexual propagation. Collecting gravid colonies for ex situ spawning and larval culture may result in handling stress on donor colonies, and in situ sampling of corals can damage polyp tissues. Here, we evaluated the effects of handling-induced stress on donor colonies of Favites abdita used for ex situ sexual propagation. A total of 110 healthy, reproductively mature colonies (>8cm diameter) were tagged within and around a 1000m2 monitoring site at Caniogan Reef, Anda, northwestern Philippines. During the May 2025 spawning period, colonies were randomly assigned to four treatments: in situ injury from fragment sampling (IS), ex situ spawning of uninjured colonies (TS), ex situ spawning of injured colonies (ITS), and non-handled controls (NS). Colonies collected for ex situ spawning were returned to their original sites after spawning, and all colonies were monitored monthly in situ. Four months after spawning, the highest colony mortality occurred in the ITS colonies (31.5%), followed by TS (18.2%) and IS (14.3%), with no mortality in NS. The mean live tissue cover (±SE ) was 100%±0 for NS, 79%±39.2 for IS, 72%±42.1 for TS, and 56%±44.8 for ITS. In addition, moderate bleaching was observed in some colonies of the ITS and TS groups, while Drupella corallivory was present in a few colonies from the ITS and IS groups. Overall, these results demonstrate variable gravid breeding-colony vulnerability to handling, with the greatest impacts on colonies experiencing both injury and ex situ propagation handling. This study highlights the importance of monitoring donor colonies and improving restoration practices to ensure that viable reproductive colonies surviving on reefs are not compromised by restoration activities.
38312ab7: Information on the reproductive behaviour of Arabian Gulf corals is relatively sparse, especially on the stress-tolerant species, such as Coscinaraea monile. Although C. monile is among the most thermally resilient reef builders, able to withstand extreme temperatures and high salinities of the Arabian Gulf, its reproductive biology remains undocumented with no published data on reproductive cycles and spawning timings. Here, we present the first dataset on the spawning periodicity of C. monile based on ex-situ spawning observations collected in the first coral hatchery in Qatar. We confirmed that the species is a gonochoric broadcast spawner and recorded a total of 53 spawning observations, spread over 25 days, between 10 April 2021 and 6 July 2024, under environmental conditions (temperature, photoperiod and lunar cycle) synchronous with natural seasonal cycles: 6 in April 2021(13-20 nights after full moon); 13 in May 2022 (6-13 nights after full moon); 3 in May 2024 (5 nights after full moon); 20 in June 2024 (5-16 nights after full moon) and; 11 in July 2024 (11-16 nights after full moon). This dataset reveals that, in the Arabian Gulf, C. monile has a relatively long spawning period, during spring and early summer. Because colonies are gonochoric, variation in spawning timings was also observed between male and female colonies. The male colonies usually released sperm (median: 20 minutes after sunset, range: 2-49 minutes) earlier than female oocyte release (median: 36 minutes after sunset, range: 12-88 minutes), a pattern commonly considered optimal for fertilization success in broadcast spawners. These initial records provide a baseline dataset that is of critical importance for future research, conservation and restoration efforts on this species.
d05d67a7: Cryopreservation is emerging as a critical tool for safeguarding coral genetic diversity and supporting assisted reproduction as reefs continue to decline globally. Although the Red Sea hosts some of the world’s most thermally tolerant coral lineages, the region has lacked a coordinated approach to long-term germplasm banking. Here, we report the establishment and initial content of the first frozen coral biorepository for the Red Sea, created to preserve priority genetic resources and support future breeding and restoration efforts. In April 2022 in the central Red Sea (Thuwal, Saudi Arabia), sperm was collected from Acropora eurystoma (n = 7), A. hemprichii (n = 13), and Platygyra daedalea (n = 3) and cryopreserved using controlled-rate freezing (20 °C/min), yielding 53 archived vials. Fresh sperm collected at a target concentration of 10^7 cells/mL exhibited species-specific differences in motile concentration, with A. eurystoma showing the highest motile concentration (1.8 x 10^7 cells/mL), followed by P. daedalea (1.4 x 10^7 cells/mL) and A. hemprichii (0.7 x 10^7 cells/mL). Post-thaw assessments of sperm cryopreserved at a target concentration of 10^9 cells/mL revealed high retention of motile sperm in both Acropora species across multiple cryopreservation runs (1.5-5.0 x 10^8 cells/mL). P. daedalea displayed markedly reduced post-thaw motility (1.5 x 10^7 cells/mL), a pattern consistent with cryopreservation challenges reported for P. daedalea globally. These results demonstrate that sperm from key Red Sea coral species can be successfully cryopreserved with substantial recovery of motile cells, establishing a foundational resource for assisted fertilization, selective breeding, and genetic management. As part of the region’s diversifying coral management strategy, the biorepository is integrated within NEOM’s KAUST Coral Restoration Initiative, the world’s largest coral restoration program. Embedding cryobanking within this large-scale framework strengthens the Red Sea’s capacity to secure thermally resilient coral lineages and support interventions that enhance reef resilience under climate change.
13bf2983: Coral populations on Florida’s Coral Reef have experienced dramatic declines in the past few decades, and natural rates of sexual reproduction have been insufficient to replace lost biomass and maintain genetic diversity in the region. To address these issues and improve recruitment outcomes, managed breeding approaches (ex-situ spawning and settlement, recruit rearing, and outplanting) have been applied to boost the production of native Florida coral species, but this approach, although effective, is both costly and labor-intensive. To increase the efficiency of rearing early-stage recruits in land-based facilities while also maximizing outplant survival of key reef-building species, we outplanted lab-reared recruits of three Caribbean brain coral species (Diploria labyrinthiformis, Colpophyllia natans and Pseudodiploria strigosa) to reef sites off Miami, Florida, USA, at 5 and 8 months of age. Each cohort was further subdivided to include acclimation periods in an offshore nursery of 0, 1, and 3 months. This design allowed us to test how outplant age and offshore nursery acclimation interact to affect restoration outcomes. Juvenile survival, health (i.e., bleaching, disease, predation), and growth were monitored for one year following their release from the land-based facility. This project helps identify minimum viable outplant ages and assess the potential incremental benefit of adding an offshore nursery acclimation stage prior to outplanting, ultimately guiding future strategies for scalable sexual recruit restoration. These results will help guide restoration practitioners and managers in Florida and elsewhere on how best to integrate recruit growout pipelines  into ongoing outplanting and restoration efforts.
d20d0bec: Generating sexually produced juvenile coral has become an invaluable tool to support reef restoration. The Florida Aquarium (TFA) has worked with various partners and practitioners to increase the genetic diversity of in situ nurseries and outplanted corals in the Florida Keys, USA. Having developed effective techniques for spawning and rearing Florida corals, TFA has closely collaborated with University of Florida to improve coral rearing and outplanting success.  One such strategy is to increase post settlement survival, growth, and coral health using artificial LED lighting as compared to natural, greenhouse lighting prior to outplanting. In April of 2022, a total of 600 genetically unique 8 month old corals of three species (Pseudodiploria strigosa, Pseudodiploria clivosa, and Colpophyllia natans) were held in closed aquarium systems with two different lighting sources (Greenhouse vs. LED) for a total of 11 months prior to outplanting. Buoyant weight and surface area were measured and compared between all groups.  Ultimately, Pseudodiploria strigosa, Pseudodiploria clivosa, with the addition of Diploria labyrinthiformis corals where outplanted in June 2023 at two depth profiles (14ft and 25ft) each at three inshore patch reefs off of Grassy Key in the Florida Keys. Initially, corals were going to be monitored for early light related stress across all groups. However, these juvenile corals were outplanted prior to the record-breaking marine heatwave that resulted in a mass coral bleaching event for much of the Florida Reef Tract which changed all expectations. Post outplant, the juvenile corals were monitored at 1 month, 5 month, and 1 year for survivorship, bleaching, and growth.
e6b71231: In June 2025, scientists observed rapid tissue loss on multiple coral species concurrent with an algal bloom within the Piti Marine Preserve, Guam. Within 1 week, 350 outplanted staghorn Acropora colonies representing 6 species showed 85% mortality. Here, we report our investigation into this event, its cause, and implications for restoration. Mortality was limited to a 2.5-ha area around a cluster of 6m-deep karst sinkholes, the site of coral nurseries and outplanting activities. We observed 23 species with partial to full mortality during survey swims. Antecedent environmental triggers included heavy rainfall following prolonged drought, water temperatures exceeding 33°C, and doldrum-like conditions. A point source of nitrogen, debris, and anoxic water was identified as a large stream draining into the Preserve. Further investigation noted greatly reduced flow due to a blocked culvert from debris accumulated during a 2023 typhoon. We surmise that these concurrent conditions, coupled with sinkhole bathymetry, triggered a cascade of environmental impacts, initiated by the algal bloom which elevated biological oxygen demand and caused a drop in dissolved oxygen at the bottom of the sinkholes. These combined effects resulted in rapid, acute tissue loss. Ongoing histopathology and eDNA analyses will elucidate any pathogen involvement. This confluence of events has not been previously observed in 15 years of monitoring of this Marine Preserve. Pro-active responses are in development by multiple agencies to safeguard restoration efforts, and include installing DO meters for additional vigilance through seasonal rainfall transitions; involving local stakeholders in a river clean-up; reconfiguring outplanting structures; and limiting coral restocking to two resilient species that showed higher survival and rapid recovery. Learning from, and responding to, unpredictable events is of urgent importance to restoration efforts. Our highly collaborative effort, involving cooperation among five agencies and sharing resources and data, was critical to our success.
5c10c08c: Staghorn Acropora are dominant reef-builders that form essential fish habitats on shallow reef flats in Guam, Micronesia. Successive mass bleaching events from 2013 to 2017 caused a 36% loss of live staghorn coral cover, reducing four out of eight species to single surviving populations. These events highlighted an urgent need for targeted restoration. In response, two in situ coral nurseries were established to culture, prune, and outplant all eight staghorn species. Since 2021, more than 5,000 fragments have been outplanted using three attachment methods suited for hard or sand substrate. Outplants of the seven most at-risk species were established at two environmentally distinct sites, Piti Bomb Holes and Cocos Lagoon, both of which historically supported extensive staghorn populations. Piti is a marine preserve with high herbivore biomass, high flow, and relatively high water quality, whereas Cocos Lagoon experiences upland watershed runoff, increased turbidity and nutrients, higher fishing pressure, and lower herbivore biomass. To assess outplant performance, colonies were monitored monthly for the first six months post-outplant and annually thereafter. Monitoring documented disease, predation, CoralWatch color scores, and survival. Our initial assessments show that survival varied among species, sites, and outplanting methods. Three-year survival was higher and less variable at Piti (70.5% ± 33.5%; mean ± SD)  than at Cocos (53.2% ± 42.9%; mean ± SD), likely reflecting more favorable environmental conditions. Species-specific patterns were also evident; Acropora acuminata showed the highest three-year survival at 90% ± 22.1% (mean ± SD) and A. virgata showed the lowest survival at 35.9% ± 44.2% (mean ± SD). This three-year dataset provides the first assessment of longer-term staghorn outplant success on Guam, spanning multiple bleaching seasons and a Category 4 typhoon. Further analysis will inform adaptive restoration strategies to reduce extirpation risk of these highly vulnerable species.
3ead4dd4:
As the most geographically isolated island chain in the world, with urbanized coastlines and nearshore fringing reefs, Hawaiian coral reefs are extremely unique and face distinct challenges for coral restoration. Unlike most other reef ecosystems in the Pacific and around the globe, Hawaiian reefs exhibit low species diversity, high endemism, and lack dominance by fast-growing branching corals. These characteristics have required Hawai‘i-based restoration practitioners to create novel techniques for working with slow-growing massive corals, rare species, and restoration in environments with heavy tourism and human use. To address these hurdles, the State of Hawai‘i Division of Aquatic Resources (DAR) established the Hawai‘i Coral Restoration Nursery (HCRN) in 2015 to pioneer multiple innovative approaches to expedite the growth of Hawaiian coral species to large sizes in a significantly shorter duration compared to natural growth rates. 

As the first land-based coral restoration nursery in Hawai'i, the HCRN initiated a combination of microfragmentation and advanced aquarium techniques to produce large corals for restoring damaged and degraded reef sites and enhancing natural ecosystem services and functions. The 1-2 year process produces 40-cm and 1-m coral colonies which represent 20-100+ years of natural growth. With over a decade of developments, DAR will share lessons learned, experimental results, and novel techniques from the Nursery’s process and history. From microfragment sizing and species-specific light regimes to site-specific acclimation techniques, the HCRN has tested and proven a suite of methods to improve coral growth and outplant success. These protocols were developed through small scale experiments, trial and error, and collaborations with researchers and practitioners. DAR aims to share lessons learned from the evolution of the HCRN to support information sharing, best practices, and collaboration between coral restoration practitioners across our oceans.
da24cb29: In Hawai‘i, like many Pacific islands, geographic isolation has resulted in many rare and endemic coral populations that play a crucial part in maintaining reef biodiversity. As anthropogenic stressors continue to increase, these populations are at risk of extirpation and extinction. The unique, locally adapted, slow growing reefs of Hawaiʻi make restoration challenging, especially for rare corals that are extremely limited in range and vulnerable to land-based impacts on Hawaʻi’s nearshore reefs. To preserve and restore these populations, the State’s Division of Aquatic Resources (DAR) created a land-based Rare Hawaiian Coral Ark at its Hawaiʻi Coral Restoration Nursery (HCRN) for holding and restoring rare, uncommon, and endemic coral species. One of the success stories of the Coral Ark has been the reintroduction of the possibly extirpated Knobby Finger Coral (Porites duerdeni) in Kāneʻohe Bay on the island of Oʻahu. P. duerdeni is a critically endangered reef-building coral that is endemic to Hawai‘i. In July 2015, in anticipation of a warm water bleaching event in the Hawaiian islands, the HCRN collected fragments of P. duerdeni from Kāneʻohe Bay to add to the Rare Coral Ark. After the bleaching event, no living colonies of P. duerdeni could be found in the Bay. The collected pieces of P. duerdeni were later microfragmented and grown onto two concrete pyramid modules at the HCRN, and in 2019 they were outplanted back in Kāneʻohe Bay. In 2024, after five years of growth, HCRN staff collected fragments from the original modules to continue growing and outplanting additional coral modules. This success story shows how early preparation can lead to successful preservation and restoration of rare corals and can be a model for restoration in other areas with high endemism.
f6251ce7: Guam hosts a remarkably diverse coral community, with approximately 400 species of stony corals forming reefs that provide coastal protection and essential ecosystem and economic services. Restoration has become a crucial component of local coral reef management as increasingly frequent bleaching events, extreme weather conditions, and other disturbances have pushed Guam’s reefs past the point of natural recovery. In June 2024, Guam Coral Reef Initiative’s (GCRI) pilot in situ coral nursery was installed in the Piti Bomb Holes Marine Preserve to support ongoing restoration efforts by incorporating a wide variety of coral morphologies and species, with the goal of enhancing reef resilience by targeting functional and genetic diversity. The first year of this project focused on establishing best practices for nursery husbandry and propagation of six understudied coral species and evaluating species-specific preferences and nursery success by monitoring survivorship, health, and growth metrics. As a small program in a remote location, GCRI faced many challenges due to limited capacity, complex reef ecology, lack of resources, limited data availability regarding various coral species, and several unexpected impacts. Here, we highlight several of these challenges and lessons learned, supported by relevant data products and analyses, and GCRI's resulting adaptations and management outcomes. Topics will include creative low-cost solutions, variable successes of experimental propagation methods, nursery structure modifications, and addressing health impacts such as bleaching, sedimentation, and predation. During the planning process, GCRI encountered challenges finding detailed information on project methods, logistics, species selection, and successes or failures of other Indo-Pacific projects, indicating a need for more regional knowledge exchange and collaboration. GCRI aims to promote the open discussion of challenges unique to the Pacific Islands and the sharing of failures, lessons learned, and unique methodologies between practitioners, especially as restoration efforts continue scaling up amid increasing local stressors and climate impacts.
ad6a794a: Mass bleaching events from 2013 to 2017 resulted in a drastic decline in Guam’s live coral cover. In response, coral restoration was initiated, focusing on particularly vulnerable staghorn Acropora species. Guam’s eight staghorn species are currently grown in two ocean nurseries established in contrasting protection, turbidity, depth, and water-flow environments. In this study, finger-sized fragments (avg 7.6 cm) of eight species, including a few from different source populations, were collected from surviving wild populations and cultured on coral trees for later pruning and outplanting. To determine growth rate trends between these species and between the two nursery environments, growth was tracked monthly for one year, using both physical measurements and image analysis. Total linear extension (TLE) was calculated by summing all visible branch lengths on each fragment, capturing new branch growth every month, providing comprehensive changes to colonies overtime. All species in both nurseries experienced slower growth for the first 6 months, after which growth accelerated, demonstrating the value of a nursery acclimation period to facilitate recovery after fragmentation. Overall, the contrasting nurseries revealed clear, environment-dependent growth trajectories showing different species-specific responses indicating that the lower-light, and low-flow environments may enhance growth in Acropora coral. For nearly every species, A.acuminata, A.muricata, A.virgata, A. pulchra, and A.vaughani reared in the deeper nursery had a significantly higher growth increase compared to the corals in the shallow nursery. Stable temperatures, lower light, and reduced water flow may decrease heat stress or energetic demand, allocating more energy to skeletal extension in the deeper nursery. In contrast, the well-illuminated and shallower nursery, Piti, may push corals to their thresholds, slowing growth, despite higher light availability. These results underscore the need to account for both environmental and physiological factors in coral restoration, showing that corals achieve optimal growth when conditions minimize stress and maximize energy efficiency.
0e50ad2d: Seascape genomics uncovers how connectivity and adaptation are shaped by reef landscapes and environments. Robust evidence-based knowledge of these eco-evolutionary dynamics are essential to model coral reef future, evaluate the efficacy of proposed actions, and guide conservation efforts by managers and practitioners. But the high diversity of coral species and their geographic spread hinder generalising results from single studies to other species and locations. Drawing on empirical studies of corals from the Great Barrier Reef, I will discuss patterns uncovered by comparative seascape genomics. We find that: dispersal distances are well predicted by coral reproductive mode (brooding vs broadcast spawning); spatial and environmental structuring of symbionts often exceeds that of their coral hosts; many genes can contribute to coral thermal adaptation; common coral species often comprise multiple cryptic taxa that range from completely to partially reproductively isolated, and these purported species can differ in their responses to heat stress and occupy different microenvironments; and, finally, gene exchange (introgression) between taxa can be an important contributor to rapid adaptation. Such emerging generalities may help to guide the difficult decisions that lie ahead.
39b04311: Coral reef restoration has accelerated as a key approach in the race to enhance reef resilience under intensifying global change and local stressors. As the field continues to expand, effective restoration requires context-specific guidance to align policy goals with on-the-ground practice. Here, we present the largest global synthesis of coral restoration efforts to-date, pairing restoration data with environmental information to spatially resolve coral recovery outcomes as a function of restoration methods, socio-ecological conditions, and compounding stressors. Specifically, we combined data on more than 1300 coral restoration projects occurring over the last five decades to (1) examine how the coral restoration field has evolved, (2) evaluate the field’s trajectory, (3) understand how methodological choices, ecological contexts, and environmental conditions drive restored coral survival, and (4) suggest how methods can be leveraged to enhance restoration potential in the future. Through this global synthesis, we found that the number of restoration projects has increased >14-fold since 2020, expanding to diverse reefs characterized by differences in coral cover, tourism, bleaching (degree heating weeks) and larval connectivity. Despite mounting climate extremes, restoration efficacy has incrementally improved over time with 15% increased survival since 2020, driven largely by methodological improvements. When evaluating predictors of restored coral survival and relative restoration performance, our results highlight high and low resilience pathways associated with historical climate that can inform restoration outcomes under harsher current conditions. Applying these insights to predict coral survival at un-restored reefs >= 10 km from restoration sites further revealed that methods can play an important role in shifting (by up to >50%) recovery trajectories under variable conditions. Integrating lessons learned through this global synthesis with expert and local knowledge provides a timely framework for informed restoration planning and implementation that compliments climate action and local conservation to ensure effective outcomes as efforts continue to scale.
9a8b65d2: Coral populations are rapidly declining due to global warming and local anthropogenic stressors, with nearly all living corals at risk if temperatures rise beyond 1.5°C. As reversing climate change becomes increasingly unrealistic, effective local actions are essential to mitigate its impacts and support coral recovery through targeted restoration and protection efforts. Biophysical models that simulate coral larval dispersal at reef-scale resolution are crucial for guiding these actions. However, the high computational cost of such models has limited most studies to a few species and spawning events, limiting insights into interannual and interspecific variability. Here, we used the multi-scale ocean model SLIM to simulate larval dispersal for six key reef-building coral species (Diploria labyrinthiformis, Acropora cervicornis, Pseudodiploria strigosa, Colpophyllia natans, Montastraea cavernosa, and Orbicella faveolata) across Florida's Coral Reef over a 10-year period (2012–2021), incorporating experimentally calibrated larval dynamics. Our results show that connectivity indicators are most strongly correlated among species with similar spawning windows. Notably, incoming and outgoing connections (i.e. in/out-degrees) exhibited the highest interspecific and interannual correlations. By integrating these metrics into a restoration indicator, we identified large clusters of reefs in the Dry Tortugas and northern Broward-Miami regions with significant restoration potential. The in- and out-degrees displayed limited interannual variability, with most fluctuations observed at outer shelf reefs in the Lower Keys and Dry Tortugas, where the ocean circulation is more variable. By providing long-term connectivity estimates for multiple reef-building species, this study offers valuable insights to inform marine conservation strategies.
8dd597de: Marine ecosystem restoration has entered an era of unprecedented ambition, with billions invested globally to recover coral reefs, kelp forests, and other foundation habitats. Yet restoration outcomes remain highly variable, and planning often relies on precedent or expert judgment rather than structured evaluation. We propose Restoration Strategy Evaluation (RSE), a simulation-based framework adapted from Management Strategy Evaluation in fisheries science, to guide more effective and adaptive marine restoration. RSE integrates operating models that simulate ecological dynamics with iterative feedback from field monitoring, enabling practitioners to compare alternative strategies, quantify uncertainty, and refine approaches as new data emerge. The framework comprises seven components: setting restoration targets, building operating models that capture demographic processes and environmental variability, simulating candidate restoration strategies, defining performance metrics, implementing preferred strategies, monitoring outcomes, and updating models through feedback loops. Unlike traditional fisheries applications, RSE emphasizes place-based socioecological outcomes—from coral cover and ecosystem function to local employment and community engagement—aligned with practitioner and community objectives. We illustrate RSE through a coral reef restoration case study where size-structured population models simulate colony growth and survival under climate stress, comparing strategies including thermally tolerant genotype deployment, variable outplanting densities, and herbivore protection zones. Performance metrics encompass both ecological indicators and socioeconomic benefits such as restoration-related jobs and tourism revenue. By embedding current scientific knowledge into dynamic decision-support tools, RSE creates a structured platform for collaboration among analysts, practitioners, and stakeholders. This approach offers a practical pathway for moving coral reef restoration from experience-based planning toward predictive, evidence-based decision-making capable of addressing the urgent challenges of climate change and biodiversity loss.
1437fc58: Ecosystems are complex assemblages of micro- and macroscopic organisms interacting with non-living components. The interaction of this milieu generates biological and ecological functions commonly called ecosystem services, such as water purification, atmospheric gas exchange, and biomass generation for human consumption. Ecosystem services are being lost due to the ongoing decline of Earth’s ecosystems at the hands of humanity, giving rise to ecosystem restoration–an active and theoretically holistic process so essential that the UN declared the 2020s the Decade on Ecosystem Restoration.  But most ecosystem restoration efforts focus on one or a handful of foundation-forming species, not the greater community. Implicit in this approach is the assumption that restocking organisms like corals and trees will produce reefs and forests because everything else will follow. Existing data do not support this assumption on coral reefs. Therefore, there remains a need for reef restoration approaches that reconstruct the biological and non-biological complexity of these ecosystems. Here, I describe aspects of ecosystem restoration that are currently underutilized within the area of “community seeding” on coral reef systems and highlight case studies for: (1) restocking communities/assemblages rather than species, (2) using passive organism collection approaches rather than intensive nursery rearing, (3) conferring beneficial viral and microbial communities as reservoirs of raw material for rapid adaptation and acclimatization, and (4) building food webs in addition to rebuilding habitat structure. These approaches and strategies demonstrate a path toward next-generation reef restoration by emulating natural ecosystem complexity and are thus more likely to produce resilient, self-sustaining reefs in the face of ongoing environmental change.
e9987b4b: The accelerating decline of coral reefs worldwide has created an urgent need for restoration approaches that can operate effectively at large scales. Meeting this challenge demands tight alignment between scientific evidence and operational decision-making. In this context, KCRI (KAUST Coral Restoration Initiative) was launched in 2021 in NEOM (Northern Red Sea) as one of the world’s largest and most innovative coral restoration programs, supporting Saudi Vision 2030. KCRI’s science program was designed to address implementation gaps and inefficiencies by generating targeted knowledge, refining on-the-ground practices, and accelerating innovation in support of a resilient reefscape. We present how >50 focused trials and pilot studies to date directly inform restoration and operational activity, with emphasis on coral health, stress response, sexual reproduction, and genetic management. Specifically, our studies provide the empirical foundation needed to optimise species selection, outplanting approaches, and nursery methodologies. In doing so, the rapid development and testing of hypotheses provide a mission-critical, agile feedback loop that improves restoration outcomes whilst strengthening the translation of scientific insight into practical guidance. We also discuss how these solutions are cultivated through >45 diverse academic partnership projects to advance technologies in ecological restoration, assisted reproduction, innovative monitoring tools and frameworks, coral stress detection, and reefscape flow-dynamics modelling. By embedding research, field practice, and technological development into a single program, we enable promising concepts to be evaluated, adapted, and deployed within a unified strategic framework. Through integration of field-led questions, targeted experimentation, and strategic innovation, we are building a more resilient, knowledge-driven foundation for coral reef restoration in a rapidly changing environment.
bc3f11d8: The KAUST Coral Restoration Initiative (KCRI) at Shushah Island, one of the largest and most innovative reef restoration projects, is transitioning to its scale-up phase, where high-capacity coral propagation systems offer a unique platform for testing science-based interventions to enhance reef resilience at scale. To support this effort, we developed a microbiome stewardship strategy to guide the responsible use of microbial interventions. Our strategy builds on the inherent flexibility of coral microbiomes, which enables targeted manipulation, or bioaugmentation, of microbial partners to rehabilitate disrupted coral health. Current strategies at different stages of implementation, through in-situ and/or land-based trials, include: (1) identifying ecological or physiological bottlenecks where microbial therapies can meaningfully improve coral performance; (2) isolating and characterizing native, stress-resistant microbiomes to serve as stocks for tailored-designed probiotics; (3) administrating validated probiotics to enhance the fitness and survivorship of outplanted corals, which we also (4) monitor off-target organisms to support risk assessments; (5)  application of postbiotics (heat-killed cells), which demonstrate thermal-stress protection comparable to their probiotic counterparts while offering extended shelf life and potentially safer application; (6) applying probiotics to coral parents prior to the reproductive season, resulting in enhanced intergenerational benefits; and (7) extensive coral disease monitoring to (8) identify potential pathogens and (9) develop targeted probiotic solutions. Our microbiome stewardship framework also incorporates the assessment of genetic and metabolic interactions between introduced microbes and their coral hosts, supported by previous in-situ and land-based trials showing that probiotics can induce restructuring of the coral microbiome and metabolome. While microbial interventions have mostly focused on adult corals, this strategy extends to additional life stages, including larval settlement and recruitment . Together, these efforts underpin an integrated, evidence-based approach for incorporating microbial tools into large-scale restoration, illustrating KCRI’s role as a model for advancing safe and effective microbiome-based solutions for reef recovery.
ba4c816d:
Photogrammetry has emerged as an important tool for underwater reef monitoring and addressing a wide range of research questions. However, these methodologies are typically labor-intensive and demand specialized expertise in survey techniques, image processing workflows, and three-dimensional modeling pipelines, frequently limiting the development of accurately scaled models.

We introduce RSP (Reef Stereo Photogrammetry), an open-source toolkit designed to facilitate the creation of scaled 3D models for large underwater reef areas without scalebars. RSP features a user-friendly interface comprising four primary components: 1) GoPro synchronization scripts; 2) an image processing application for batch organization and picture enhancement; 3) Agisoft Metashape Pro integration scripts for automatic scaling; and 4) calibration scripts to establish camera-to-camera distance. Our tool supports multi-threaded processing and employs Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization to enhance underwater image colors and details.

RSP reduces survey and image processing time compared to manual workflows while improving image quality for 3D reconstruction. The stereo calibration workflow allows precise distance measurements, ensuring accurate models with scaling errors of < 0.002 m over areas exceeding 400 m². The toolkit efficiently processes images from consumer-grade cameras, including GoPro systems commonly used for marine research.
RSP facilitates broader application of underwater photogrammetry by providing fast, cost-effective, and user-friendly tools requiring minimal training, suitable where small scaling errors do not compromise survey objectives. The open-source nature and graphical user interface make photogrammetry accessible to citizen scientists, students, and researchers in developing regions where expensive tools may be prohibitive for including photogrammetry in benthic surveys.
ccea368e:
Accurate underwater object detection is essential for marine ecosystem monitoring, fisheries management, and environmental decision-making. However, underwater imagery presents persistent and unique challenges for object detection: light attenuation, turbidity, color distortion, and highly imbalanced class distributions. Not every species is detected equally well, yet underlying causes remain unclear.  This study investigates two central research questions: 1) What factors beyond data quantity drive class-specific performance disparities? 2) How can we systematically improve detection of under-performing marine species? 

Using the DUO and RUOD benchmark datasets, we isolate and analyze the two core components of object detection: localization and classification. We perform controlled dataset manipulations and conduct experiments focused on a known under-performing class, scallops, to disentangle the effects of data imbalance, inter-class feature dependencies, and inherent visual properties. Localization experiments, conducted using YOLO11 and evaluated through the TIDE error analysis framework, demonstrate that foreground–background discrimination is the dominant failure mode across all data distributions, a critical finding for detecting camouflaging species. Increasing data quantity alone does not resolve this, indicating that the challenge arises from fundamental visual ambiguity rather than dataset size.

Classification experiments further show that significant precision gaps persist even when classes are perfectly balanced, suggesting that intrinsic feature-level limitations drive persistent under-performance. Our findings underscore that improving under-performing species in underwater detection systems requires algorithmic advances—particularly within localization modules—rather than solely collecting more data.  These insights lead to practical recommendations in dataset design: applications prioritizing recall, such as conservation monitoring, should use balanced training, while commercial operations needing high precision should prefer imbalanced training. All code and modified datasets are publicly released to support further research into reliable detection of marine species.
ec66bf74: Large area imagery (LAI) in coral reefs, obtained through underwater digital photogrammetry techniques has become a powerful tool for characterizing and assessing coral reef benthic assemblages. The use of 61 MP multispectral sensors (10-bands) for surveying reef biogeomorphic units (spurs), and processing those images with SfM techniques results in orthomosaics of super-high spatial resolution (0.2-0.4 mm) with at least 7 useable bands: 405, 430, 450 nm in the blue range, 550, 560, 570 nm in the green range, and 710 nm in the red-edge. This spectral resolution allows for a robust discrimination (≥ 1.9 Jeffries-Matusita) of benthic components including coral at species levels, different types of macroalgae, sponges, etc. For this level of discrimination between benthic components, the red-edge band plays a substantial role, red-edge is known to capture much better the concentration of pigments than the near-infrared spectra which -additionally- cannot be recorded properly underwater. These multispectral products can be analyzed using supervised classification and segmentation to produce highly accurate (>80% overall accuracy) benthic cover rasters for the whole area of interest, and the data can be used to characterize sites without the previously identified bias from transects, lines, and quadrats-based methods to estimate benthic cover and diversity. An important advantage of this approach is a faster processing for benthic categories than hand-digitizing approaches, as well as absolute measurement of covers, rather than sampled cover estimates in large areas. Disadvantages shared with other LAI digital photogrammetry approaches are the need for high-end computing hardware, long computing times for orthomosaics generation, and particularly for our multispectral approach are the massive file sizes and the steep cost of the sensors. Also, additional products, as Digital Elevation Models (shared with other SfM approaches) can be used to extract a proxy of the function of refuge and to estimate bottom rugosity changes.
597540f0: Accurate observations of coral reefs fishes are important for research and monitoring studies. Traditional visual census methods can be time-intensive, dependent on specialized observer expertise, and limited by depth and bottom time. Computer vision offers a scalable alternative for automated analysis of underwater imagery but the lack of extensive, annotated datasets specific to the Hawaiian Archipelago’s coral reef fishes was a significant barrier to deployment. This study presents the development of a large-scale, image dataset designed to train object detection and classification models for Hawaiian reef fish. We aggregated thousands of images from video surveys across the main Hawaiian Islands. Using Roboflow as the primary data management and annotation platform, we manually annotated over 20,000 instances across 70+ classes of common families and species ensuring robustness against variable lighting, turbidity, and complex benthic backgrounds. To validate the dataset's utility, we benchmarked performance using two state-of-the-art object detection architectures: YOLO (You Only Look Once), utilized for its real-time inference speeds suitable for edge computing, and RF-DETR (Receptive Field/Detection Transformer), employed to test transformer-based accuracy in complex occlusion scenarios. Furthermore, to move beyond static frame detection towards robust population counting in video, we integrated ByteTrack, a high-performance multi-object tracking algorithm, to associate detected fish across consecutive frames. Preliminary results indicate high mean Average Precision (mAP) scores for detection and promising tracking accuracy, demonstrating that region-specific training data significantly outperforms generic pre-trained weights. This workflow establishes a reproducible pipeline for rapid ecological assessment of coral reef fishes in Hawaii. By automating the identification, tracking, and counting process, we aim to accelerate data availability for managers and researchers, allowing for more responsive management strategies in the face of changing ocean conditions.
8826e452:
Accurate, standardized color measurements from underwater imagery remain a longstanding challenge because optical conditions in the ocean vary with depth, location, and time of day. While there are color standardization pipelines for images taken under natural light, images taken using artificial illumination introduce additional complexity for color standardization due to the variable intensity and spectral composition of a given light source, as well as the spatial non-uniformity of its illumination pattern. Therefore, in the case of imaging with artificial lights, we lack an image acquisition and calibration pipeline to reliably standardize colors.

To bridge this gap, we present a framework for calibrating artificial light in underwater imaging, enabling consistent, quantitative color assessment. During image acquisition, the method requires a gray calibration target that spans the field-of-view for capturing the spatial illumination pattern, a color calibration target that remains in the frame, and a RAW-capable camera. There are no limitations on the type and number of artificial lights. For post-processing, we provide instructions and codes to process the data to achieve standardized colors.

To demonstrate the utility of our method, we focus on the ecological problem of “black” coloration in marine invertebrates. Organisms described as black can differ in how their darkness is produced and perceived — through variation in reflectance, ambient light, angle-dependent effects like iridescence, and limitations in the viewer's visual system — yet are often described as simply "black". These distinctions may have ecological implications for ontogeny, diet, signaling, and UV protection. Using invertebrates in the Gulf of Aqaba as a case study, we show that our calibration method for artificial light works consistently and reveals biologically meaningful color variation obscured in uncorrected images. Our results establish a methodological advance with broad applications for biodiversity surveys, ecological monitoring, and comparative studies of color in the marine environment.
bbe2e6f6: Video belt transects are faster and easier to take than photo transects and could potentially yield data on coral size structure and the spatial relations of reef biota, and not just benthic cover. To have a better visualization of the transect videos, we developed CorStitch, an open-source software that creates mosaics from video data acquired by divers or snorkelers along a transect. CorStitch produces two primary outputs: video mosaics and marked mosaics. Video mosaics are constructed using a Fourier-based image correlation algorithm in which the central horizontal region of each successive frame is compared with the preceding frame, and their alignment is determined by the location of the point of highest correlation. Marked mosaics are generated by segmenting the video mosaics into 1-meter and 5-meter intervals and randomly placing twenty points per square meter, allowing spatially distributed sampling that effectively simulates a Coral Point Count sampling. Then, we evaluated CorStitch on video data collected under varying conditions, including non-uniform illumination, lack of white balance correction, and substantial optical distortion. Across all test cases, the algorithm successfully produced continuous mosaics, which is evident in the continuous benthic features such as corals and rocky substrates. However, stitching inaccuracies were observed. These errors may result from camera rotation during collection, limited image features in certain frames, and inherent constraints of the correlation-based stitching method. Collectively, these factors introduced inconsistencies in the apparent size of benthic organisms within the mosaics. Despite these limitations, the reef biota in the mosaics remain identifiable, which establishes CorStitch as a robust open-source platform with substantial potential for broad application in coral reef monitoring and spatial ecology studies.
d7f89391:
Effective management of Polynesian coastal ecosystems requires reliable, repeatable, and large-scale monitoring of key benthic communities. Among these, sea cucumbers (Holothuroidea) are of particular interest due to their commercial value and ecological role. However, current survey methods, including conventional diver-based surveys, are labor-intensive, risky, and restricted in spatial and temporal coverage. To address these limitations, we developed an integrated imaging-and-AI pipeline for  automated, rapid, and scalable evaluations of holothurian population abundance and density.

To this purpose, we designed a modular stereoscopic system with paired GoPro cameras and adjustable baselines, enabling depth estimation through triangulation similar  to human vision. This compact platform records continuous underwater video suitable for object detection, segmentation, and size reconstruction. Using an annotated dataset of several thousand images representing five Polynesian holothurian species, we fine-tuned recent deep-learning-based detection models and obtained high performance in identifying individuals across variable reef environments. Furthermore, to capture fine-scale morphology and derive biomass-relevant measurements, detections were passed to a segmentation module that extracts masks from bounding-box prompts. By coupling stereo geometry with keypoint correspondence between left and right frames, we obtained accurate estimates of holothurian body length and elongation even under variable poses. Further refinements involve combining visual outputs with morphometric and species-level trait data to minimize prediction errors and improve species identification.

By integrating underwater stereo-vision, deep-learning-based detection and segmentation, and geometric size reconstruction, we established a foundation for scalable, automated holothurian surveys across Polynesian lagoons, supporting ecological assessments and sustainable harvesting., The system is versatile, operable by divers, and can potentially be adapted to other platforms (e.g., ROVs, AUVs) enabling participatory monitoring and providing data to support adaptive co-management of benthic resources.
076529b2:
Local and Indigenous knowledge plays a critical role in understanding coral reef ecosystems and designing management strategies that are ecologically effective and culturally grounded. In the Western Province of Solomon Islands, a partnership between community fishers and conservation practitioners conducted surverys of local fisher knowledge survey to document traditional observations of fish behaviour, seasonal patterns, and spawning sites. The survey revealed multiple fish spawning aggregation (FSA) sites within Marovo Lagoon, including the presence of Symphorus nematophorus, a species rarely encountered in scientific monitoring and for which limited ecological data exists.

These findings became a central component of a collaborative local marine management area (LMMA) planning process with a Marovo Lagoon community. Through discussions that integrated ecological science with customary knowledge particularly around lunar and seasonal cues that shape fishing practices community leaders identified FSAs as both highly valued and highly vulnerable. This facilitated an informed and proactive decision to establish a year-round lunar-based catch ban targeting key aggregation periods. The management measure, rooted in customary stewardship principles and reinforced by scientific understanding of FSA sensitivity, represents a tangible example of how local knowledge can guide adaptive, community-driven fisheries governance.

This presentation highlights the value of co-produced knowledge in identifying cryptic or previously undocumented FSAs, improving reef fisheries management, and strengthening community leadership in conservation. It demonstrates how bridging traditional ecological knowledge with scientific tools can generate scalable lessons for Pacific LMMA systems and broader regional efforts to safeguard reef fish populations under increasing environmental and socioeconomic pressures.
7bf20869: Periodic closures, whereby areas that are temporarily closed to fisheries, are a common customary management strategy in indigenous communities throughout the Pacific. Numerous studies have shown that these systems can allow biomass to rebound after harvesting periods. However, evaluations of this management strategy have focused on a limited suite of indicators (standing biomass, abundance, or fish behavior), and very few incorporate time-series data. This study provides the first evaluation of how periodic closures can affect standing biomass-productivity relationships. Specifically, we ask: How do different life-history mechanisms contribute to biomass production under periodic closures? Using a 9-year dataset from a customary rotational closure system in Papua New Guinea, we found that somatic-growth productivity was lower during closure periods, while reproductive productivity was higher. Particularly, somatic-growth productivity was 19% lower during closure periods than in open-access periods for the same value of standardized biomass. Fish community composition showed little variation between management regimes, and the differences observed were driven by higher abundances and body sizes in periodic closures. In contrast, reproduction potential (measured as the total number of eggs produced by mature females per hectare) increased during closure periods. Our findings highlight the capacity of different productivity mechanisms -somatic growth versus reproduction- to dominate biomass production under customary periodic closures, revealing distinct trade-offs depending on how productivity is defined. Somatic growth-based productivity is higher during harvesting periods, when smaller and fast-growing fishes dominate as larger competitors are removed. Conversely, reproductive productivity is higher during closure periods, when larger and more fecund individuals prevail. These results underscore the importance of incorporating multiple productivity pathways when informing closure duration and evaluating the sustainability of customary periodic closures in coral reef fisheries.
1c33ce6c: Moi (Polydactylus sexfilis) is a highly valued resource species in Hawaiʻi, traditionally monitored by lawaiʻa (fishers) and konohiki (managers of ahupuaʻa land divisions ). Moi experienced significant declines in the mid 20th century, prompting a seasonal fishing ban aligned with then-recorded spawning periods. Master lawaiʻa (fisherman) Uncle Mac Poepoe, recently observed spawning season changes, likely due to habitat and environmental stressors, indicating that the ban is not effective for protecting moi during extended spawning periods. In response, Uncle Mac formed Nā Kai ʻEwalu, a collective of lawaiʻa, to study these changes across islands.
