This dance of science, policy, and intervention could be made more meaningful if we (1) are more specific about conservation and recovery goals, (2) operate under a rational policy focused on assisted enabling of natural regenerative processes, and (3) we develop accountability and efficiencies in the experimental process of inventing coral reef stewardship strategies that are adaptive in today’s strange world.
d8d78f31:
In the USA and across the border, the consensus is that we need to move quickly if we want to not only restore our coral reefs but, more importantly, if we want to avoid a mass extinction of coral species. When that imminent need is transferred into practice, the urgency is not necessarily experienced when sorting out all the necessary processes and permits needed to start in-situ and ex-situ nurseries and the actual restoration of the coral reefs. 

Based on Puerto Rico and US based experiences in starting and carrying out restoration efforts, we will present a review of local and national requirements compared to international scenarios and requirements (or lack there of). The purpose of this review is to identify limitations and realities to consider or take into account when basing policy development on science needs and on the ground reality, that might challenge the Precautionary Principle.
08930349:
A critical channel through which climate change affects human welfare and economic development is the degradation of ecosystems that underpin livelihoods and nutrition. Coral reefs are a prime example: they are highly vulnerable to climate-induced coral bleaching while simultaneously supporting fisheries that are central to food security in many coastal regions, in particular in developing countries. Using a novel large-scale coral bleaching dataset created based on satellite imagery and exploiting long-run sea surface temperature variation as an instrument, this paper provides causal evidence on how coral bleaching translates into socio-economic damages relating to fisheries, incomes, consumption, and health in East Africa.

The paper documents a clear chain of impacts from ecosystem degradation to human outcomes. Coral bleaching significantly reduces fish catch, leading to declines in household protein consumption and increases the prevalence of child stunting, an indicator of early childhood malnutrition. At the same time, it forces households to adapt by selling agricultural assets, while other theorized adaptation channels like migration or increased work effort do not appear to play a role. These findings highlight that coral bleaching is not only an environmental concern but also presents a considerable threat to the economic well-being, health, and long-term human capital formation in coastal communities.

From a policy perspective, the results highlight the importance of considering coral bleaching when developing climate adaptation strategies, social policies, and aid interventions. They suggest that coral reef conservation and restoration can generate substantial co-benefits beyond biodiversity protection, and that communities and entire countries can benefit in terms of improved fishery yields, better health outcomes that matter for long-term wellbeing, incomes - and consequently economic development. More broadly, the findings support the case for policy frameworks that explicitly account for the role of coral reefs in sustaining livelihoods and human well-being.
9d6f0bd4:
Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) has reshaped coral reef management across the Caribbean, catalyzing rapid emergency responses, novel scientific collaborations, and unprecedented management interventions. Yet as SCTLD transitions from an acute ecological shock to a chronic, persistent stressor, many reef systems now face a familiar governance challenge: how to sustain effective management once the initial crisis attention, funding, and political urgency have waned. We examine how coral reef policy and management frameworks can move beyond short-term disaster response toward durable, incentive-aligned governance capable of addressing ongoing ecological decline.

Building on empirical and policy insights from SCTLD response efforts, this study analyzes the institutional, financial, and behavioral incentives that shape reef management decisions in the post-crisis phase. It asks three central questions: Which best practices introduced during SCTLD response, such as coordinated monitoring, rapid decision-making, and cross-sector collaboration, are most vulnerable to erosion over time?  What misaligned incentives undermine long-term coral stewardship once emergency funding and media attention recede? And thirdly, how can incentive structures be redesigned to embed adaptive management, compliance, and local stewardship into routine reef governance?

Using a comparative policy analysis informed by conservation finance, marine spatial planning, and disease ecology, we propose an incentive-based framework for sustaining coral reef management under chronic stress. We highlight the need to shift from crisis-driven interventions toward governance systems that reward prevention, learning, and long-term ecological outcomes. By reframing SCTLD not only as a disease outbreak but as a stress test for coral reef governance, this research offers actionable insights for policymakers, managers, and funders seeking to build resilient reef management institutions in an era of compounding threats.
4b01e7d6: Coral reefs in the tropical shallow waters of southern Taiwan have undergone severe degradation due to anthropogenic pollution and climate-driven disturbances, particularly marine heatwaves and typhoon impacts. Average coral cover has declined from approximately 60% in the 1970s to around 25% in recent years, with many sites exhibiting signs of ecological decline or collapse. Interestingly, photo-transect monitoring combined with AI-assisted image analysis (CoralNet) revealed that coral cover on certain seawall wavebreakers designated as in-situ coral nurseries was higher than on adjacent natural reefs. 3D photogrammetric monitoring further indicated that several coral taxa – including Acropora, Galaxea, Heliopora, Isopora, Montipora, Pocillopora, Porites, and Turbinaria – maintained positive growth even after the unprecedented marine heatwaves and typhoons of 2024. Parallel efforts in ex-situ coral nursery using artificial seawater recirculating aquaculture system (RAS) have successfully completed the life cycle of the brooding Pocillopora acuta, achieving stable larval release and second generation propagation. The RAS also supported recovery of bleached Seriatopora caliendrum and Euphyllia glabrescens collected from the wild, where controlled feeding and husbandry enabled full recovery and resumption of larval release. Ongoing research aims to improve culture efficiency and thermal conditioning through two complementary systems: a vertical, multi-layer RAS equipped with independently adjustable light spectra and intensities to optimize performance across life stages and maximize spatial use, and a robotic arm system that automates coral transfer among temperature regimes to enhance thermal resilience and contribute to the development of heat-resilient strains. These advances are enabled by cross-sector collaboration among scientists, aquarists, citizen scientists, corporate volunteers, managers, and policymakers. This integrated framework supports scientific, scalable, and site-adapted strategies to accelerate coral aquaculture and reef restoration outcomes in southern Taiwan.
35b811a5:
Florida’s coral reefs are in a state of near-constant degradation concomitant with ever-increasing coastal development and associated anthropogenic impacts. Government agencies, non-profits, and concerned citizens have spent significant time and resources combating these negative impacts. One primary method of mitigating damage to coral reefs is to transplant corals onto degraded reefs using nursery-grown corals. While many challenges of reef restoration have been overcome, parrotfish predation on freshly transplanted corals remains a significant issue in South Florida and in the overall greater Caribbean. Parrotfish are recognized as an essential species on healthy reefs, responsible for the removal of macroalgae, but can also hinder reef restoration efforts by biting young and newly transplanted corals. This project endeavored to create the world’s first 3rd party certified marine biodegradable predator exclusion device (PED), the Coral Fort™.
This maintenance-free, innovative device increases the success of transplant activities and promotes efficiencies in the restoration workflow to reduce costs and labor associated with coral transplants. Initial Coral Fort™ prototype testing was conducted in Broward County, Florida, using an experiment that evaluated the prototypes’ ability to reduce instances of predation and mortality, as well as the PED’s influence on the coral’s growth. Further experimentation in the last 3 years has improved the initial design, analyzed the biodegradation rates via mass-loss and SEM, and used 16S rRNA to characterize the microbiome of the degrading polyhydroxyalkanoate (PHA) from which the Coral Fort™ is constructed from. Additionally, an official partnership with WinCup, Inc., through their Phade® brand, which is a major industry innovator in the biodegradable food service products sector, has helped to make the Coral Fort™ an affordable, scalable solution by offering assistance with materials engineering, manufacturing processes, and public awareness.
f1b783b2:
Coral reefs are among the most biodiverse ecosystems on Earth, yet are experiencing rapid declines driven by anthropogenic climate change. Conventional restoration methods, such as propagation in nurseries and outplanting, lack resilience against climate-driven impacts. Some coral genotypes, particularly those inhabiting shallow “hot pockets”, display naturally high thermal tolerance, representing a critical genetic resource. However, these environments are becoming lethal, and there are currently no systematic or scalable methods readily available to identify thermally tolerant corals or the habitats that foster their adaptation.

Coral phenotyping, the identification and measurement of desirable traits across corals and populations to understand their responses to environmental conditions, is an emerging field. Forest phenotyping, by comparison, has developed robust in-situ and multi-scale workflows using advanced sensors to quantify how tree genotypes express traits in different environments and silvicultural regimes. Parallels between forestry management and coral restoration suggest strong potential for cross-pollination of methods.

This study reviews current coral phenotyping approaches, with particular emphasis on remote and proximal sensing, and compares them with state-of-the-art forest phenotyping practices. A substantial gap persists in coral reef management, where remote sensing tools, such as UAVs, remain underutilised, and efforts largely focus on satellite products and in-water photogrammetry. This work aims to accelerate the adoption of scalable tools that can improve decision-making and prioritisation in coral restoration under accelerating climate pressures. 

The review reveals a rich suite of forestry methodologies, from UAV-based structural metrics to hyperspectral trait mapping, that could be adapted to coral reefs with minimal modification. These approaches offer pathways toward more efficient, larger-scale reef management by enabling the identification of thermally resilient coral populations. The emerging Reefs of Hope Ocean Decade Action framework aligns strongly with this potential, integrating advanced sensing, stress-testing and genetic tools to identify resilient coral populations and optimise restoration investments.
58f5d2be: Coral reef degradation in the semi-enclosed Bay-of-Ranobe, Southwest Madagascar threatens both ecological functioning and the well-being of coastal communities. To accelerate recovery and strengthen local resilience, we developed an integrated restoration approach that combines limestone-aggregation artificial reefs with biodiversity seeding using Autonomous Reef Monitoring Structures (ARMS). In this system, ARMS serve a dual function: documenting cryptic biodiversity and early colonisation processes across habitats, and transferring these assemblages onto newly built artificial reef modules to stimulate ecological succession. ARMS units rapidly accumulated diverse cryptic communities, and when translocated to artificial reefs, they enhanced fine-scale structural complexity and facilitated the early establishment of key benthic functional groups. Ecological analyses from 33 sites revealed faster development of microhabitats in seeded modules, while in situ observations indicated favorable shifts toward crustose coralline algae and increased herbivore visitation—conditions that support coral settlement and long-term ecosystem regeneration. A strong participatory component underpinned this work. Through a series of over 15 community workshops and 5 governance agreements, fishers co-designed restoration sites, contributed to reef construction, and directly participated in ecological, fishery and human health monitoring, along 14 villages. This co-design structure strengthened stewardship, reinforced ecological literacy, and linked ecological recovery with local aspirations for food security, livelihoods, and well-being. Building on these results, the restoration program is now expanding into a multi-trophic, multi-habitat framework. Ongoing efforts integrate coral larval enhancement, herbivore restocking, and the introduction of ecosystem engineers such as sea urchins and sea cucumbers to accelerate functional recovery. Additional innovations include community-built coral nurseries and “oyster arks” as new microhabitat-forming reef units. Parallel governance strengthening aims to translate these ecological gains into long-term, community-led management. By merging biodiversity seeding, ecological engineering, and participatory governance, this model offers a scalable pathway for climate-resilient coral reef restoration and improved socio-ecological well-being across Madagascar and the Western Indian Ocean.
d7328d7c: Coral reef restoration urgently requires innovative yet accessible technologies that can be applied across diverse socio-economic contexts. In Thailand, we demonstrate how cross-sectoral partnerships can drive practical and scalable restoration solutions by integrating scientific innovation with low-cost technologies suitable for developing countries. This study presents a restoration framework developed through collaboration among universities, the Plant Genetic Conservation Project under the Royal Initiative of Her Royal Highness Princess Maha Chakri Sirindhorn, the Royal Thai Navy, government agencies, private sector partners, and coastal communities. Our team has advanced sexual coral propagation, microbial enhancement, and the early development of cryopreserved gamete banks to increase genetic diversity and climate resilience. Importantly, we also developed a low-technology hatchery system, using simple materials, gravity-fed seawater flow, low-energy filtration, and locally sourced components, that significantly reduces operational costs while maintaining high survival of coral larvae and recruits. This system has enabled reliable annual production of thousands of coral juveniles at Samaesan Island in the Upper Gulf of Thailand, making sexual propagation more accessible to regions with limited resources. These innovations, co-designed with Navy divers and coastal communities, have resulted in improved settlement success, enhanced early growth, and the integration of thermally resilient broodstock into restoration plots. The low-tech hatchery model has further empowered community partners to participate in coral culture, strengthening local capacity, stewardship, and long-term monitoring.
23394c96:
Mass coral bleaching on the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) in Australia between 2016 and 2025 was driven by high sea surface  temperatures. The likelihood of temperature-induced bleaching is a key determinant for the future threat status of the GBR, but the long-term context of recent temperatures in the region is unclear.
In this study, we show that the January–March Coral Sea heat extremes in 2024, 2025, 2017 and 2020 (in order of descending mean SST anomalies) were the warmest in 400 years, exceeding the 95th-percentile uncertainty limit of our reconstructed pre-1900
maximum. The 2016, 2004 and 2022 events were the next warmest, exceeding the 90th-percentile limit. Climate model analysis confirms that human influence on the climate system is responsible for the rapid warming in recent decades. This attribution, together with the recent ocean temperature extremes, post-1900 warming trend and observed mass coral bleaching, shows that the existential threat to the GBR ecosystem from anthropogenic climate change is now realised. Without urgent
intervention, the iconic GBR is at risk of experiencing temperatures conducive to near-annual coral bleaching, with negative consequences for biodiversity and ecosystem services. A continuation on the current trajectory would further threaten the
ecological function and outstanding universal value of one of Earth’s greatest natural wonders.
1c94851f: The Last Interglacial (Marine Isotope Stage 5e, ~129-116 ka) was characterized by global mean sea surface temperatures 1-2 °C warmer than pre-industrial, making it a key reference period for assessing modern and future warm-world climate sensitivity. However, the structure of variability and extremes in this period remains poorly constrained, particularly in marginal seas such as the Red Sea, which limits our ability to place ongoing marine heatwaves and bleaching events into a longer-term context. Here, we present the first 80-year-long, sub-seasonally resolved multiproxy coral record from MIS 5e in the central Red Sea, derived from Porites colonies collected from raised reef terraces near Rabigh (Saudi Arabia). The corals are exceptionally well preserved, as confirmed by CT imaging, XRD, and microscopy, which document pristine aragonite and clear annual density banding. We combined δ¹⁸O, δ¹³C, Sr/Ca, Li/Mg, Mg/Ca, and U/Ca to characterize seasonal to interdecadal variability in sea surface temperature, hydrology (δ¹⁸Osw), and carbonate chemistry at a time when background climate was warmer but atmospheric CO₂ remained near pre-industrial levels. Preliminary analyses reveal clear annual cycles and coherent relationships among temperature- and chemistry-sensitive tracers, indicating the high fidelity of the archive in recording MIS 5e climate dynamics in the central basin. We use these records as a benchmark for modern warming by comparing the amplitude and frequency of reconstructed warm anomalies and hydrological extremes with satellite-era SST and heat-stress observations from the same region, where corals today are close to their thermal limits and bleaching thresholds. These first results provide a baseline to assess whether 21st-century Red Sea heatwaves fall within, or exceed, the range of natural variability in a past warm world.
685ade09:
The Great Atlantic Sargassum Belt first appeared in 2011 and quickly became the largest interconnected floating biome on Earth. In recent years, Sargassum stranding events have caused substantial ecological and socio-economic impacts in coastal communities. Sargassum requires both phosphorus (P) and nitrogen (N) for growth, yet the primary sources of these nutrients fuelling the extensive Sargassum blooms remain unclear. Here we use coral-bound N isotopes to reconstruct N2 fixation, the ultimate source of the ocean’s bioavailable N, across the Caribbean over the past 120 years. Our data indicate that changes in N2 fixation were primarily controlled by multidecadal and interannual changes in equatorial Atlantic upwelling of ‘excess P’, that is, P in stoichiometric excess relative to fixed N. We show that the supply of excess P from equatorial upwelling and N from the N2 fixation response can account for the majority of Sargassum variability since 2011. Sargassum dynamics are best explained by their symbiosis with N2-fixing epiphytes, which render the macroalgae highly competitive during strong equatorial upwelling of excess P. 

Complementary satellite and in situ observations reveal that, in contrast to explosive growth in the tropical Atlantic, Sargassum biomass in the north Sargasso Sea has declined sharply since 2015, alongside a collapse in its historic seasonal growth cycle and a switch to advection-dominated supply from the GASB. When considered alongside extended coral-based reconstructions, these contrasting trajectories raise the possibility that recent tropical proliferation may be altering basin-scale pathways of limiting nutrients in ways that affect the Sargasso Sea.
be480375: Reefs that lie adjacent to major urban centers experience a high degree of localized anthropogenic stress on top of that imposed by a warming ocean. Long-lived corals that persist in these environments preserve a history of these compounding stressors and provide examples of resilience from which we may be able to take useful lessons. On Varadero Reef (Cartagena Bay, Colombia), a century-long history of dredging in the Dique Canal to expand and maintain shipping access has resulted in high levels of sedimentation and turbidity. Local industry and wastewater further reduce water quality. Despite this environmental degradation, corals persist at this site. Here we present a 60-year monthly reconstruction of hydrological variability from the geochemistry of an Orbicella faveolata colony from Varadero Reef that reflects the discharge of the Magdalena River, which feeds the Dique Canal. Skeletal oxygen isotope and Ba/Ca ratios both track historical discharge, which is linked to the position of the Intertropical Convergence Zone and the state of the El Niño/Southern Oscillation system. A decreasing trend in skeletal carbon isotope ratios is consistent with reduced water clarity and increasing heterotrophy since the mid-20th century. The skeletal geochemistry provides a continuous environmental context for paired reconstructions of coral holobiont diversity through ancient DNA analysis of the same skeletal materials. Together, these methods offer new ways of exploring why some corals are resilient to significant environmental degradation.
21061cc0:
The Great Barrier Reef (GBR) is increasingly threatened by rising sea surface temperatures (SST) and the growing frequency and severity of mass coral bleaching events. Accurately reconstructing past SST from coral geochemical proxy records is vital to understanding climate trends prior to the instrumental period, contextualising these changes. However, proxy behaviour can vary with local environmental and ecological conditions, complicating their interpretation. To strengthen the usage of coral-based SST proxies, we compare multiple SST proxies across environmental gradients along the GBR to evaluate their relative robustness. Additionally, we experimentally test whether short-term thermal stress produces a consistent and detectable geochemical signature of coral bleaching.

The evaluation of proxy-temperature relationships uses records in the Great Barrier Reef Coral Skeletal Records Database. Records were compared to satellite and logger SST data and showed substantial variance in proxy fit with latitude, distance across the continental shelf, and analytical technique. Specifically, linear regression correlations were consistently and significantly lower at near-shore (median of the medians for each proxy r2 = 0.29) and southern sites (0.19), in contrast to at offshore (0.71), central and northern reef sites (0.65) across all proxies examined. Methodological differences were also significant, with solution chemistry showing higher proxy-temperature correlations (0.68) than laser ablation (0.22). Cross-variances between this set of comparisons are being investigated. Across the dataset, combining multiple proxies better replicates the instrumental SST signal than reliance on any single proxy, consistent with previous studies on multi-proxy SST reconstructions.

In identifying how various SST proxies perform more reliably and in demonstrating the importance of combining multiple proxies, more robust reconstructions of past reef conditions can be developed. Analysis of tank experiment samples will investigate possible bleaching geochemical signals in coral skeletons, improving the capacity to interpret long-term records in the context of increasing thermal stress across the GBR.
088aec82: Sea surface temperature (SST) is a key parameter for understanding marine ecosystem dynamics, including the health of coral reefs. This study investigates SST trends in Banten Bay and their relationship with annual linear growth rates of Porites corals, as well as links to ENSO and IOD phenomena. Six Porites cores were collected from three locations—Panjang Island (PPSBP1), Kalih Island (PKS1P1, PKS1P2, PKS2P1), and Tunda Island (PTS1P1, PTS2P2) using grab sampling to preserve growth structures. Coral growth was analyzed with CT-Scan imaging (processed in ImageJ) and X-ray imaging (analyzed in CoralXDS). SST data combined in situ measurements (HOBO U24 and Tidbit V2 loggers near Panjang Island) and daily OISST satellite data (2009–2024), from which annual means were derived. Results show a warming trend of 0.0017 °C per year over 2009–2024. Annual Porites growth ranged from 0.826–1.236 cm/yr with colony ages between 3–16 years. Statistical tests revealed no significant differences between transects or analysis methods; thus, transects with lowest deviation were selected as representative. The highest average annual growth rate occurred in PPSBP1 (1.120 cm/yr), followed by PKS1P2 (0.865 cm/yr) and PTS2P2 (0.771 cm/yr). All three colonies showed declining growth, with the steepest decline in PKS1P2 (0.0194 cm/yr). Correlations between SST and coral growth varied by site: Panjang Island showed a weak but significant positive relationship (r = 0.179, p = 0.031), Kalih Island a weak nonsignificant negative relationship (r = −0.178, p = 0.561), and Tunda Island a moderate, near-significant negative correlation (r = −0.598, p = 0.056). Correlations between SST and climate indices (ONI, DMI) were weak and nonsignificant, suggesting local SST variability is not strongly driven by ENSO or IOD. Overall, Porites growth responses in Banten Bay appear location-dependent and exhibit non-uniform responses to climate variability.
3b20bc54: Ocean acidification is expected to be detrimental to coral reef calcification, yet in some locations, recent evidence suggests that coral reefs can be exposed to restricted periods (diel) of high pCO2 / low pH while continuing to calcify and survive. However, beyond a few years, we know little regarding longer term in situ changes in coral reef carbonate chemistry trends, as the diel variability in the physicochemical environment of coral reefs often masks such trends. Here, we reconstruct a seasonally resolved coral reef history of in situ water pCO2 using Raman spectroscopy using replicated annually-banded Rhodoliths from One Tree Island on the Great Barrier Reef (GBR). We show relatively stable pCO2 between the 1960s-2000s followed by a defined upturn in pCO2 after the mid-2000 occurring during both summer and winter. Indeed, the consistent presence of changing pCO2 during both winter and summer suggests this observation is not driven primarily by coral reef metabolism (as occurs at diel time scales; evidenced in diel variability of DIC-AT patterns), but rather, evidences a longer-term change of background carbonate chemistry in the waters bathing One Tree Island.
d84b01cb:
Corals thrive in tropical and subtropical regions. They harbour Symbiodiniaceae in order to obtain nutrients, but the mechanisms involved are not fully understood. When juvenile polyps acquire Symbiodiniaceae, the number of symbiosis-related proteins should increase in proportion to the number of Symbiodiniaceae cells. In this study, we explored the proteins that increased in line with the number of Symbiodiniaceae cells and elucidated their function.
The juvenile polyps of Acropora digitifera and A. tenuis were acquired with Cladocopium sp. or Durusdinium sp. and reared for several weeks. As the number of Symbiodiniaceae cells increased, the polyps were collected and proteomic analysis was carried out. Zoxanthellal proteins, Peridinin-chlorophyll a-binding proteins (PCP), proportionally increased according to the number of Symbiodiniaceae. Thus, the proteins correlated with PCP were explored. Saposin, ApoD and collagen α5(IV) were found in A. digitifera with Durusdinium sp. CCMP2556. Saposin, long-chain-fatty-acid-CoA ligase, and collagen α2 (IV) were found in A. tenuis with Cladocopium and Durusdinium. Notably, A. tenuis with Cladocopium contained proteins related to the energy production pathway. As Saposin and ApoD are related to lipid metabolism and transport, the lipid content of juvenile polyps was examined. Juvenile polyps of A. tenuis with Cladocopium contained more triglycerides than those with Durusdinium, and larger lipid droplets accumulated in the epidermal cells of the former. These results suggest that photosynthates from Symbiodiniaceae are transported and stored as lipids in the epidermal cells. Collagen (IV) was reported to form holes in the mesoglea to transport substances between gastrodermal and epidermal cells in Hydra spp. This suggests that Acropora harbouring certain Symbiodiniaceae species promote lipid accumulation and transport to epidermal cells.
2646b87c:
The photosymbiosis between corals and dinoflagellates is physiologically coupled to coral calcification, which underpins reef structure. While hundreds of studies report how light enhances coral calcification, the mechanistic basis underlying the interaction of symbiont photosynthesis with coral calcification remains unclear.
We investigated metabolic coupling between symbiont photosynthesis and coral calcification by focusing on a key energy-dependent step in the calcification mechanism: pH regulation of the calicoblastic epithelium and calcifying medium. Using live tissue imaging of the growing edge of coral microcolonies (Stylophora pistillata) we characterized energy dependant pH recovery in the calcifying ‘calicoblastic’ cells and explored how it responded to light and to prolonged periods of darkness. In parallel we also established the effects of light and darkness on new skeletal crystal formation at the growing front of the skeleton. Our findings reveal that despite that fact that symbiont photosynthesis and coral calcification are spatially separated in different epithelia, light exposure exerts a strong influence on the capacity of the calcifying cells to regulate their pH. Only prolonged darkness, exceeding 12 hours, impaired pH recovery, indicating that energy reserves in the calcifying cells sustain normal pH regulation over natural night periods but not during extended absence of photosynthesis. Ongoing work is disentangling the respective roles of oxygen supply and fixed-carbon availability in modulating this response.
These findings reveal metabolic links between symbiont photosynthesis and calcification, highlighting the energetic dependence of calcifying cell pH regulation, an essential factor in shaping the capacity of corals to maintain calcification under ocean warming and acidification.
4d8dbff7: Climate change destabilizes coral reef ecosystems by disrupting the cnidarian-algal symbiosis and impairing coral recruitment. Yet, the mechanisms underlying the thermotolerance of corals throughout their life cycle remain poorly understood. Combing physiological, sing-cell RNA-seq and NanoSIMS imaging, we show that thermotolerance of corals varies markedly with life stage: larvae are the most resistant, whereas young recruits and adult corals are more susceptible to heat stress. These differences correspond to shifts in host energy reserves and carbon translocation rates in the symbiosis. Ongoing analysis of NanoSIMS and scRNA-seq data will permit linking metabolic and molecular interactions in the symbiosis at the single-cell level. Together, our findings suggest that large energy stores and the absence of calcification underpin the high thermotolerance of larvae, and reveal that symbiotic nutrient cycling and host nutritional status are key drivers of thermal tolerance in the cnidarian-algal partnership.
e7b127ee: Selenium (Se) is an essential trace element with well-established antioxidant functions, including modulation of redox signalling pathways and enhancement of cellular defence systems. It may therefore play a critical role in the resilience of the cnidarian holobiont under thermal stress; however, how corals acquire Se, how it is partitioned within the holobiont, and how it is used at the cellular level remain entirely unknown. Here, we developed a novel, non-destructive radiolabelled ⁷⁵Se tracing technique to quantitatively track Se uptake and distribution in the cnidarian holobiont under both ambient and elevated temperature conditions. This approach provided real-time insight into Se assimilation dynamics and revealed that uptake depends on the presence and metabolic activity of the endosymbiotic algae, although animal tissues accumulated on average 57% (± 6%) more Se than algal cells. Under thermal stress and subsequent bleaching, total Se accumulation by the holobiont was reduced by approximately half relative to control conditions, accompanied by shifts in the relative proportions of organic versus inorganic Se compounds. These changes in Se dynamics have important implications for the stability of the symbiosis during stress and for potential recovery pathways post-bleaching. Our innovative methodological framework offers a powerful new tool for monitoring trace element fluxes in situ and may inform strategies to enhance coral resilience.
bf5c28ae: Many cnidarians, including reef-building corals, establish symbiotic relationships with photosynthetic algae in the family Symbiodiniaceae. These partnerships are sensitive to temperature anomalies, which cause symbiont loss and increased mortality. Several studies have implicated host innate immunity as playing a central role in the maintenance of cnidarian-algal symbiosis and it has been shown that establishing symbiosis with different genera of algal symbionts modulates thermal tolerance. Over the last several years our group has investigated interactions between symbiotic state, algal symbiont type, innate immunity, and thermal tolerance across different life history stages in a model anemone Exaiptasia pallida (Aiptasia), two facultatively symbiotic corals (Oculina arbuscula, Astrangia poculata), and a tropical reef-building coral (Pocillopora damicornis). Using experimental and genomic approaches, our results demonstrate that host innate immunity is modulated by symbiotic state and that establishing symbiosis with specific algal strains modulates host innate immunity in distinct ways, which lead to differences in thermal tolerance and responses to microbial challenge in the lab. Overall, this presentation will integrate results from several recent studies integrating imaging, molecular, genomic, and single-cell RNAseq technologies that further support the role of host immunity in the maintenance of cnidarian-Symbiodiniaceae partnerships and shed light on how different symbiont genera can modulate this immunity and ultimately influence host resistance to pathogens and bleaching.
bb307cbf: Coral reefs face escalating threats from rising sea temperatures, triggering widespread mass bleaching and mortality events. Thermal preconditioning, a process in which corals exposed to sublethal thermal stress become more tolerant to subsequent thermal stress through the development of environmental stress memory, is increasingly recognized as a promising possible strategy to aid restoration efficiency. However, its underlying molecular mechanisms remain unclear. Furthermore, species-specific responses to thermal preconditioning must be understood to ensure its broad applicability. In this study, we conducted a comprehensive multi-molecular biomarker analysis to investigate the physiological and molecular effects of thermal preconditioning on two coral species, Pocillopora damicornis and Stylophora pistillata, by exposing them to a sublethal thermal stress, followed by an acute stress. In both species, preconditioned corals showed delayed bleaching, maintaining chlorophyll a and c2 concentrations, along with Symbiodiniaceae density, for longer periods compared to non-preconditioned corals. At the molecular level, this increased thermal tolerance could be linked to the higher activity of antioxidant enzymes measured, suggesting enhanced protection against oxidative stress and consequently lower levels of lipid peroxidation damage. Preconditioned corals also exhibited a slower downregulation of heat shock proteins (Hsps) or sustained Hsp expression above basal levels during acute heat stress, possibly reflecting prolonged cellular homeostasis. Additionally, they demonstrated delayed or reduced Hsp gene expression, indicating a less immediate response to thermal stress. Furthermore, the acquired thermal tolerance lasted longer in P. damicornis, highlighting a species-specific response to thermal preconditioning. Our study provides crucial molecular insights into the complex mechanisms of thermal preconditioning, which will be essential for developing more effective and species-targeted preconditioning protocols for practical application in coral reef restoration efforts.
612a09c6:
Marine heatwaves are intensifying worldwide, driving bleaching events that threaten the persistence of coral reef ecosystems. Understanding the genotype-level resilience and mechanisms behind coral resistance and recovery is crucial. The Gulf of Aqaba, northern Red Sea, presents a potential refuge, providing unique insights into the adaptability of corals, yet, recent observations indicate that resilience to thermal stress varies among coral taxa and genotypes. 
To investigate mechanisms of thermal resistance and recovery, we exposed Stylophora pistillata, Pocillopora verrucosa, and Porites spp. to diurnally fluctuating heatwaves (+3.5°C and +7°C) for ~3 weeks, followed by an equal recovery period, tracking photophysiology, pigments, symbiont density, and metabolic rates, supported by transcriptomic analyses. The species exhibited distinct thermal sensitivity and recovery, with P. verrucosa showing the highest overall thermal resistance. It maintained comparatively stable photochemical performance, even during peak stress periods of above 33°C. S. pistillata bleached quickly, revealing strong genotypic variation, but also showed rapid rebound capacity. In contrast, Porites experienced slower but significant photophysiological impairment and showed limited recovery, suggesting acute sensitivity under the applied heatwave regimes. Colony-level variation was evident among all species, with some genotypes maintaining photochemical efficiency despite severe stress equating to more than eleven degree heating weeks, potentially reflecting differences in prior environmental histories. 
These findings align with gene expression responses of both branching pocilloporids in this experiment, which revealed species-specific cellular stress programs and strong genet signatures. Together, these physiological and molecular insights highlight that species identity, intra-specific variation, and potentially habitat-derived preconditioning critically shape bleaching resistance and recovery. Our results highlight the importance of incorporating genotype-level performance and biodiversity into climate-resilient reef management, suggesting that targeted selection of resilient genotypes may substantially strengthen adaptation strategies in a warming ocean.
28a03533: The success of restoration using sexually propagated corals is dependent on the settlement (attachment and metamorphosis) of swimming planula larvae onto appropriate substrates. Current practices involve the use of conditioned/biofilmed substrates or the application of crustose coralline algae (CCA) to induce settlement. Neither approach is scalable (i.e., labor costs are high and CCA supply is limiting) and both can have inconsistent outcomes (i.e., settlement is not even across substrates). Therefore, the development of a consistent, low-cost settlement cue that could be applied to substrates or to the water column to enhance settlement would greatly increase the effectiveness of restoration via sexual propagation. Tetrabromopyrrole (TBP) is a marine bacterial compound that induces coral settlement (attachment and metamorphosis). However, TBP is unstable at room temperature and has the potential for off-target activity if added directly to the environment. To address these issues, we have developed a short-term, pretreatment approach to induce coral settlement using TBP. Briefly, larvae are exposed to 250nM TBP for 1-3 hours before being rinsed and added to the appropriate settlement conditions. This approach has proven to be effective in small bench top assays and larger mesocosm scale assays with multiple Caribbean corals species. It has also been successfully applied to both in-situ and ex-situ restoration pipelines. Furthermore, treatment with TBP did not negatively impact recruit survival or growth and in some cases improved it. Treating larvae with TBP allows for increased settlement while eliminating the need for substrate conditioning. The ability to pretreat larvae with TBP and subsequently remove the majority of the TBP before adding larvae to either ex-situ or in-situ settlement conditions greatly reduces concerns for off-target effects and requires larvae to be held in a closed system for as little as an hour, making TBP a promising addition to the restoration toolkit.
5b90f315: Global coral populations are declining at alarming rates, underscoring the urgent need for effective coral restoration strategies. Ex situ coral sexual reproduction offers a scalable pathway to produce coral settlers for reef restoration, yet the optimal light spectrum to maximize survival and growth remains unclear. This study investigated how spectral shifts influence coral post-settlement survival, growth, and algal endosymbiont acquisition. Over a 28-week experiment, sets of 36 Pseudodiploria clivosa coral settlers were transitioned from a reef mimic spectrum to a blue light spectrum at ten different time points. Coral survival and symbiont acquisition were monitored every 2 weeks, while growth was assessed every 4–6 weeks. Results revealed that shifting from reef mimic to blue light at 4 weeks post-fertilization yielded the highest survival and growth rates. More broadly, spectral shifts within the first three months, particularly by 10–12 weeks, significantly enhanced survival. Faster acquisition of algal endosymbionts was strongly correlated with improved survival and growth outcomes. These findings provide critical insights into coral photophysiology and highlight the importance of early spectral manipulation in ex situ coral aquaculture. By identifying optimal light regimes, this work supports the upscaling of coral restoration efforts and contributes to addressing the global coral reef crisis.
c17da1de: Reef restoration intends to minimize the ongoing decline of coral populations and accelerate recovery. The sexual propagation of corals adds diversity to the populations. While the techniques to induce corals to spawn ex situ and rear their larvae are relatively well-established, the grow-out, i.e. rearing from newly settled to a size suitable for outplanting, remains yet to be fully optimized. Specifically, light spectrum, food quantity and quality, and algal control require optimization. This study investigated the post-settlement survival and growth of the Caribbean reef building corals Colpophyllia natans and Pseudodiploria strigosa under three light spectra: (1) near surface (natural sunlight), i.e. outdoors grow-out at land-based nurseries; (2) 10m reef depth (using radion lights); (3) blue-shifted (using radion lights) peaking between 400-450nm. The experiment was conducted at both Nova Southeastern University and The Florida Aquarium using the same larval cohort. All treatments underwent the same irradiance biweekly increase from 25 µmol photons m²s¹ at week 0 to 200 µmol photons m²s¹ at week 19. After seven months, the blue-shifted light spectrum presented a slight increase in survival rates for both species. Pseudodiploria strigosa corals grew similarly under the reef-mimic and blue shifted light spectra, but the reef-mimic light spectrum C. natans reached almost twice the size of the blue-shifted light spectrum. The near-surface spectrum produced smaller corals. This study highlights that light spectra impacts the grow-out of corals, and artificial light spectra has the potential to increase the production efficiency of ex situ coral sexual propagation facilities.
02e2309e: The sexual propagation of corals ex situ is a successful practice to produce corals for reef restoration, but further optimization is needed to increase production and scale up restoration. A major bottleneck often observed ex situ is low larval settlement and high mortality rates in the early weeks following settlement. Using two Caribbean bouldering coral species, Colpophyllia natans and Pseudodiploria strigosa, this study assesses whether colored substrates resembling those of crustose coralline algae (CCA), a known settlement-inducing cue, can improve settlement success and whether coral growth orientation contributes to survival and growth. Coral larvae were settled and reared in three indoor recirculating systems over two months to (1) compare the proportion of larvae settled on pink, white, vs. mixed-color settlement tiles, and  (2) determine how orientation (diagonal 60° and horizontal 180°) affects post-settlement survival and tissue area growth. C. natans did not show a preference for color scheme, whereas P. strigosa had significantly higher settlement in the pink or white only color schemes (28% and 33% respectively)  compared to the mixed color scheme (22.5%). At two months, corals grown on diagonal pink tiles were significantly larger than those grown on different orientation and tile color combinations, specifically, 50% larger than horizontal pink tiles, 34% larger than diagonal white tiles, and 38% larger than horizontal white tiles. Grow-out orientation significantly affected survival regardless of the tile color. Corals grown diagonally had a four times greater chance of survival compared to those grown horizontally. These findings suggest that while pink tiles may not increase settlement, they can help improve growth post-settlement, and the combination of pink tiles and a diagonal grow-out orientation can further optimize the ex situ sexual propagation of corals.
3a9b73f8: The mass production of new, genetically unique corals via ex situ sexual propagation remains laborious and expensive, hampering the ability to upscale reef restoration. Recent innovations to the three phases of ex situ sexual propagation have led to increased production and better use of equipment and labor. Spawning of the corals has been staggered to increase year round space and labor efficiency. This was achieved by shifting light and temperature cycles in the different induction systems by 4 and 8 months so spawning occurs every 4 months. Creating out of season spawning schedules to have three distinct spawning windows allowed for a year-round usage of the lab space dedicated to the larval rearing, settlement and early grow-out (i.e. the first 3-4 months of culture) and improve labor allocation. An integrated larval rearing, settlement and early grow-out recirculating system allowed hands-free culture from fertilization to one month post-settlement. Larvae are reared in recirculating conical tanks equipped with large filters at the bottom to increase water exchange rates, and Hartford loops and water spray bars to prevent clumping of larva. When larvae become motile, they are drained by gravity and water flow to a large settlement basket on an adjacent, within-system raceway. The larval settlement baskets were made using a PVC pipe frame with an acrylic bottom tray, wrapped in a 105 µm mesh bag removable once settlement was complete. Once the mesh bag is removed, the settlement tiles remain in the bottom tray of the now dismantled basked, allowing the newly settled corals to remain in the same tank for early grow-out and thus reducing early mortality. Late grow-out (3-4 months to a year) is conducted in outdoor tanks in 60° sloped racks which increases coral survival and growth (through reduction of algal overgrowth) and doubles tank space available for grow-out.
608c6fa1: Palau’s resilient coral reefs are vital to biodiversity, food security, and cultural identity. Although these reefs have remained relatively unaffected by recent global bleaching events, climate change and marine heatwaves are projected to drive local degradation in the near future. To address this challenge, the Palau International Coral Reef Center, in collaboration with international institutions, conservation organizations, and local communities, is developing and testing a diverse restoration strategy to strengthen reef resilience of Palau’s reefs. In partnership with The Nature Conservancy -Micronesia and Polynesia, Newcastle University, and Stanford University, this includes the experimental use of heat stress assays to explore species-specific tolerance, spawning and rearing of selectively bred corals, and a range of coral outplanting approaches that utilize coral fragmentation and sexual propagation. These approaches not only expand the scientific toolkit for reef restoration but also foster active community participation in rehabilitation efforts. All these methods are currently being trialed and monitored in different sites in Palau, with thousands of corals having been heat stress tested and thousands outplanted. How many of these corals reach sexual maturity and provide local ecological benefits, and the extent to which assayed heat tolerances are retained during natural marine heatwaves and help to reduce heatwave impacts will be critical research avenues in the future. By integrating scientific research, technical training, and community engagement, the Center contributes to both local and global conservation goals. The adoption of diverse restoration methods underscores the importance of knowledge exchange and collective action in mitigating climate impacts on coral reef ecosystems. Ultimately, this work lays the foundation for safeguarding the survival of Palau’s reefs, ensuring they continue to sustain ecological, cultural, and economic well-being for generations to come.
822e3eca:
Coral restoration is increasingly recognized as a vital strategy for rehabilitating reef ecosystems impacted by climate change and local disturbances. In Palau—a Pacific archipelago known for its relatively resilient reefs amid rising ocean temperatures, but some areas have been severely damaged by typhoons in the 1990s and haven’t fully recovered. In this study, we assessed the heat tolerance of multiple scleractinian species with different morphologies and explored the use of identified heat-tolerant corals for coral restoration. Replicate coral fragments from source colonies in source reefs in northwestern Palau were collected and exposed to varying temperatures using a 4-day long heat stress assay. Corals were then categorized into different heat tolerance levels (i.e., low, medium, high). Larger fragments from the source colonies were also collected, attached, and are being monitored in field nurseries. 
To date, we have tested a total of 23 species with over 900 colonies attached to nurseries, and further monitoring will be done and exploration of heat tolerance of other species will continue. In our analysis using 11 species, corals classified to have high heat tolerance exhibited a survival rate of 80%, compared to 60% survival of medium and low tolerance corals.  These initial findings demonstrate that these corals can persist and grow under natural reef conditions, offering a promising foundation for climate-smart restoration. This approach enhances coral reef resilience while also supporting fisheries and community livelihoods, contributing to the long-term sustainability of Palau’s marine ecosystems.
4942fc36: Restore with Resilience is a multi-disciplinary initiative proactively restoring coral reefs in Hawai'i via selective propagation of thermally tolerant corals of opportunity. Combining hands-on restoration with community partnerships, the project has shown a scalable model for locally led restoration. Today, six restoration sites across the state are managed by different partner organizations, each tailoring efforts to the needs of their community. Following intensive training with coral researchers, nonprofits and community volunteers are now able to carry out coral restoration independently via their own permit approvals and funding, cultivating meaningful local ownership, capacity, and long-term stewardship that can persist beyond the lifespan of a single project. At ICRS 2022, we shared our intention to co-develop a set of best practices for coral restoration with the community. Four years later, we are proud to present the product: “Restore with Resilience: A Guide to Community-Driven Coral Restoration.” This resource reflects the collective knowledge, challenges, and successes of communities restoring the reefs that sustain them.
3673f25d: Coral reefs are facing unprecedented decline due to myriad stressors, both global and local, and The Hawaiian Archipelago is no exception. In 2015 Hawaiʻi experienced its strongest bleaching event on record resulting in nearly 70% mortality in some areas. Alongside thermal stress, many of the reefs throughout Hawaiʻi are impacted by high levels of turbidity, untreated wastewater infiltration, and high wave energy, making active coral restoration challenging at best. Maui was once revered for its coral diversity, rare and endemic coral species, and coral coverage; today many of its reefs are declining, some with less than 5% coral coverage remaining. In response to this stark decline, The Marine Institute established the only land-based nursery on Maui. At this nursery, we collect corals of opportunity, test them for thermal tolerance, selectively propagate, and differentially outplant the corals back on their home reefs. This work is done in partnership with the community of Maui and research institutions throughout the US. Through partnership with the Restore with Resilience network, climate wise coral restoration efforts were established at Olowalu, West Maui. Olowalu was selected by the community for its cultural significance, recreational usage, and its status as “The Mother Reef of Maui Nui” for its potential larval distribution reach. Over the last two years more than 1,500 coral have been planted on this reef. This presentation will cover current project status, results of the thermal tolerance assays, lessons learned, and potential pathways forward for restoration on Maui’s leeward shores.
1793e91b: The Kanu Ko‘a Initiative, a community-based coral restoration program, evaluated four coral restoration techniques at two environmentally distinct sites along the west coast of Hawai‘i Island: Kahuwai Bay and Kealakekua Bay. This systematic comparison assessed direct reattachment of whole Porites lobata colonies, direct outplanting of P. lobata fragment arrays, nursery-grown P. lobata fragments, and direct outplanting of Pocillopora meandrina. Structure-from-Motion photogrammetry and in-water surveys over approximately one year revealed significant site-specific differences in restoration outcomes. At Kahuwai Bay, survival rates reached 100% for fragmented arrays and 94% for directly reattached P. lobata colonies, while Kealakekua Bay showed lower rates (60-70%). Statistical analyses revealed that time on the reef negatively affected survival at Kealakekua Bay but positively influenced growth at both sites. Pocillopora meandrina exhibited the highest growth rates and lowest survival at both sites. A cost-benefit analysis identified direct reattachment of whole P. lobata colonies as the most cost-effective technique at both sites, costing only 6% as much per colony as the most expensive technique, nursery-grown arrays. This research provides practical guidance for optimizing reef restoration success in Hawai‘i and beyond and underscores the importance of tailoring restoration strategies to the local context. These findings contribute to a growing scientific foundation for coral reef restoration in Hawai'i and offer valuable insights for practitioners seeking to enhance reef resilience while maximizing the benefit of their restoration investment.
109f24bb: Between 2013 and 2018 the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands was faced with three mass bleaching and mortality events and two super typhoons, resulting in over 60% decline in overall shallow water coral cover. Eighty seven percent of staghorn Acropora spp. in Saipan Lagoon were lost, leaving behind hectares of dead staghorn thickets.  These structurally complex thickets are extremely important as habitat for other organisms and coastal protection. Without intervention, however the dead thickets will lose complexity, slowly collapsing on themselves and forming rubble fields. In 2021, to see if we could mitigate, or even reverse, this process, we conducted a pilot project to recolonize a dead staghorn thicket. Eighty seven 10 cm long Acropora pulchra fragments were outplanted to a 10m x 10m section of dead framework using the skeletons to support the outplants, mirroring natural staghorn colony extension. Coral cover, structural complexity, and reef volume were monitored yearly using structure from motion photogrammetry at the restoration plot and a nearby control plot where no corals were outplanted. From 2021 to 2024, coral cover at the restoration plot increased by over 3,000% and structural complexity and reef volume also increased. The control plot, however, showed no change in coral cover and declines in structural complexity and volume. Using the existing natural framework, we successfully restored a staghorn thicket, decreasing the need for man made structures to keep the coral in place and off the sand, saving resources and manpower, both of which are limited in remote Pacific islands. While restoration and monitoring is ongoing at this site, the initial assessment suggests that outplanting directly on dead structures helps mitigate the loss of natural reef structure, increases its rugosity, and is a low resource, nature first approach to staghorn restoration.
a1480fff: As anthropogenic disturbances continue to increase in frequency and magnitude, the weathering of deceased coral skeletons into rubble has accelerated. Persistent rubble beds, or accumulations of coral skeletal fragments, have created a unique physical challenge for reef function as their inherent instability renders them unsuitable substrate for coral growth and recruitment. Consolidation of rubble by calcareous encrusting organisms naturally occurs over time, but overwhelming coral mortality and weathering has resulted in large swaths of pulverized or temporarily intact skeleton lacking live coral and the sustainable structural complexity it provides. Research and mitigation thus far has focused on stabilization of small rubble pieces, as opposed to highly rugose branching coral skeletons, which provide appealing habitat for organisms critical to reef recovery via recruitment. This study will examine the effectiveness of outplanting Acropora pulchra, Pavona decussata, and Porites rus fragments onto 1m2 plots of complex, interlocking staghorn Acropora skeletons on sandy substrate. Our aim is to compare the ability of these three species to cement and stabilize coral skeletons to create structurally complex reef habitat. We will utilize staghorn Acropora skeletons generated from a longstanding restoration site recently exposed to an unprecedented water quality event that resulted in extensive mortality. Coral growth, mortality, and the rate of resheeting of live coral over dead skeleton will be measured via ImageJ software over the course of six months. We will also monitor sediment stabilization under plots, and recruitment of coral-associated sessile invertebrates, algae, and fish. Results of this study will elucidate a role that high-complexity coral rubble can potentially play in restoration. Utilizing complex dead coral skeleton as substrate for outplanted corals creates habitat immediately attractive to motile reef organisms and provides a novel use for intact and complex coral skeleton in future restoration efforts.
d921efc6: The escalating frequency and intensity of acute disturbance events, including mass coral bleaching, storms, and continued ship groundings, are causing increasing concern over the loss of reef complexity across the Pacific. Substrate destabilization and the associated increase in unconsolidated substrate inhibit the survival of young corals and coral outplants. Hawaiian reefs are particularly susceptible to declining complexity due to slow coral growth rates, low crustose coralline algae abundance, and high wave energy. This limited natural capacity for rubble field consolidation suggests that active substrate stabilization may be warranted, but has yet to be tested in Hawaii. Using a BACI design, this study tests two manual rubble stabilization methods—live rock piles cemented into 1 x 1 x 0.5m pyramids and 2 x 2m PVC-coated mesh. We are conducting this study on a rubble field on West Oahu caused by a large ship grounding. Specifically, we are investigating which method is most effective at facilitating reef growth, increasing coral populations, enhancing habitat provisioning, promoting outplant survival, and stabilizing rubble. Structures were installed in Fall 2024, followed by the addition of coral outplants to enhance stabilization and coral recovery. Preliminary results in the first year after installation are promising. There was a significant 2-fold increase in juvenile coral abundance on the boulder piles, as well as a significant 3-fold increase in fish diversity at both active treatments. Despite initial successes, challenges such as heavy biofouling by macroalgae, corallivory, bioerosion from urchins, and rubble inundation are being observed. This project highlights the critical role of rigorous science to guide effective restoration and why we should be prioritizing restoration strategies that address bottlenecks to coral survival. Through close collaboration among managers and practitioners, we’re building local capacity and advancing scalable coral restoration across Hawaiʻi.
9323566d: In-situ coral nurseries are designed to provide a safe, ideal environment for corals to grow, increasing survival and growth rates compared to direct transplantation and providing a renewable stock of live coral material for restoration projects. Understanding the growth rates of corals in nurseries can aid in establishing outplanting schedules, and determining the factors affecting growth rates can improve the efficacy of coral restoration projects overall. Growth rates are calculated by taking repeated measurements of coral sizes over time, but measuring corals in-situ can be a tricky and time-consuming task. The Guam Coral Reef Initiative (GCRI), a program of the Guam Department of Agriculture, launched a new coral restoration program in 2024 with a goal of developing effective, easy, and low-cost methods that can be reproduced on any budget and with limited resources. GCRI has developed a semi-automated, high-precision method of measuring corals in-situ using a camera in a simple lightbox made with upcycled materials, the free software R, and CoralNet Toolbox, a free and open-source tool for AI-powered image segmentation and annotation. With this method, GCRI produces ellipsoid volumetric measurements of corals comparable to those obtained by hydrostatic displacement.  This method provides accurate and precise measurements for a variety of morphologies with a fraction of the effort, time, and cost required by other popular methods of measurement, such as outline tracing, total linear extension, 3D scanning/photogrammetry, and water displacement.  In this presentation, GCRI will demonstrate the workflow, tools, requirements, and results of this method, showing that this is a practical method of measuring corals for any size restoration program on any budget.
b326d9a9: Waikīkī is the most iconic beach in Hawai’i, yet is one of the most degraded reefs in the islands, with low coral cover, structural complexity, and fish abundance. REEFrame is a collaborative coral restoration project to foster coral growth and improve reef habitat by using innovative concrete technology to create naturally-shaped structures providing ideal habitat for corals, fishes, and other reef life. The permanent structures will be placed about 0.75 mi off Waikīkī Beach on sand flats ranging from 40 to 60 feet deep. Corals of opportunity (colonies dislodged by disturbance) will be attached to the structures as nurseries for outplanting, and eventually natural succession will result in REEFrame structures becoming de facto coral reefs.  A preliminary feasibility study, initiated in 2016, has already demonstrated the effectiveness of concrete structures in this location. The test structures have successfully attracted naturally settling coral larvae, fish, and invertebrates. Furthermore, both naturally settled colonies and corals of opportunity transplanted onto the test structures have shown high survival and growth. This success provides confidence that a scaled-up nursery will be effective for restoring Waikīkī reefs. The restoration of this environmentally and culturally significant ‘āina is central to REEFrame’s mission, guided by the Hawaiian proverb, e hāpai wa‘a kākou – if we all help to lift and carry the canoe, we can move it forward. REEFrame is a three-year project led by Conservation International, the University of Hawai‘i at Mānoa, the Hawai‘i Division of Aquatic Resources, Natrx, Oceanit, ClimbHI, and Pacific Consulting Services Inc., and NOAA’s Office of Habitat Conservation.
3f5e4119: Resilience-based management is essential to protect coral reef ecosystems facing the increasing and cumulative impacts of Climate Change and human use. Crown-of-thorns Starfish (CoTS, Acanthaster cf. solaris) are coral predators that periodically reach outbreak densities and cause extensive coral loss and degradation of reef habitats. Here we overview the operations and outcomes of the CoTS Control Program in the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park, Australia. Drawing upon long-term reef monitoring data, we assessed the degree to which targeted surveillance and manual culling suppressed starfish densities and protected coral across entire reefs and regions (sectors). CoTS outbreaks were effectively suppressed, and live hard coral cover was maintained across sectors that received timely and sufficient CoTS control effort, while sectors that received limited to no control effort experienced sustained outbreaks and significant coral losses. Outbreaking reefs that received higher levels of culling effort had net increases in coral cover, while the rate of coral loss was more than halved on reefs with lower levels of cull effort. Our findings demonstrate that proactive, targeted, and sustained CoTS control is an effective and scalable action that augments the benefits of Marine Park Zoning management to protect coral and support the Reef’s resilience.
d9a3b943:
Science-based interventions have emerged as a necessary complement to global emissions reduction to secure a future for coral reefs. Outbreaks of coral-eating crown-of-thorns starfish (COTS) pose a threat to coral reefs and their associated industries across the Pacific, Indian Ocean and Red Sea, compounding the impacts of climate-related stressors. On Australia’s Great Barrier Reef (GBR), the COTS Control Program has become a core management intervention to protect coral reefs and support socio-ecological health and resilience. To our knowledge, it is the largest-scale active reef intervention program globally, with a fleet of vessels and trained crew controlling starfish numbers to ecologically sustainable levels across approximately 200 reefs each year along the 2,300km length of the GBR. The Control Program’s activities and impact are underpinned by research partnerships that support improvements in efficacy and efficiency at scale.

Over the past four years, the COTS Control Innovation Program (CCIP) has delivered an AUD $10 million portfolio of research that is mission-focused on improving the prediction, detection and control of COTS outbreaks. This talk will outline the collaborative partnerships that enabled 11 institutions and over 90 people across research, management and industry to effectively work together. We will provide a synthesis of key discoveries and capabilities developed through CCIP, including COTS monitoring and control technologies and decision-support tools and models. These innovations are enabling increased effectiveness of the COTS Control Program’s large-scale intervention effort. We will also share our active approach to engagement and integration of research into operations, recognizing that outputs of R&D effort need to be accessible and fit-for-purpose for reef managers and industry practitioners. Finally, we will reflect on key lessons learned in delivering our innovation program and will use this session to explore opportunities for coordination and knowledge sharing across other research and intervention efforts aimed at enhancing reef resilience.
38f5f6f4: Resilience-based management (RBM) has been adopted to address climate-driven change on coral reefs, yet few examples exist of effective RBM at scale. The Crown-of-Thorns Starfish (COTS) Control Program on the Great Barrier Reef provides a practical example of a meta-adaptive approach to RBM: a deliberate strategy of starting simple, then incrementally adding scale and complexity while maintaining on-ground resilience outcomes. Here we outline the core principles of the COTS control program's meta-adaptive approach and its evolution.  Using two case studies we illustrate how modelling and decision support has enabled the scaling of site-based COTS control intervention  to deliver regional-scale resilience benefits. First, we outline the Program’s decision-support architecture using established Business Intelligence software repurposed into in-house Conservation Intelligence dashboards that integrate monitoring, control and opertional data. These tools provide near real-time analysis on outbreak severity, coral condition and program performance, and are explicitly structured around key decision points (e.g. site status, annual reef prioritisation), targeting and supporting each element of the adaptive management cycle. Second, we present emerging fine-scale species distribution models as a complementary decision layer to guide within-reef targeting by identifying high-risk habitats and to maximise coral protection per unit effort. Together, these case studies show advanced and mature decision support platforms enable effective RBM and provide clear pathways for emerging science to be incorporated into COTS Control Program decision making across spatial scales. These examples underscore how meta-adaptive principles of learning by doing alongside continuous modelling, assessment, evaluation and adjustment around clear decision points can turn a nominally local intervention into a scalable and self-reinforcing RBM program. The COTS Control Program offers a transferable template for other reef systems seeking to link decision-support, emerging science and on-ground action to support reef resilience across scales.
a3e0b061: Crown-of-Thorns Starfish (CoTS; Acanthaster spp.), a voracious corallivore, are a major driver of coral loss on the Great Barrier Reef. Despite being naturally present at low densities, CoTS undergo episodic irruptions (outbreaks) that cause widespread coral mortality. These impacts are compounded by disturbances driven by climate change, such as marine heatwaves and cyclones, undermining reef resilience. The Reef Authority’s CoTS Control Program (CCP) uses direct culling with vinegar or bile-salt injections to maintain CoTS densities below ecological thresholds that allow reef recovery. Given the GBR’s vast spatial extent, CCP applies ecological modelling to prioritise reefs annually by outbreak risk, as well as ecological and economic value, ensuring control efforts are directed where they will be most effective. This targeted approach has enabled the program to successfully suppress CoTS outbreaks and prevent their spread to other vulnerable reefs. However, climate change threatens CCP’s long-term effectiveness as culling efficiency declines drastically during bleaching events. CoTS feeding scars become less distinguishable against bleached corals, and heat stress may alter starfish behaviour, making them harder to locate and cull. Adaptive strategies during bleaching, such as redirecting culling efforts to reefs experiencing lower thermal stress or temporarily switching to monitoring, could improve culling efficiency and optimise resource allocation. We use ReefMod-GBR, a spatially explicit ecological model that integrates coral and CoTS demography and disturbance regimes (bleaching, cyclones), to forecast outcomes across 3,806 reefs in the GBR under different management strategies. ReefMod-GBR simulations quantify the impacts of adaptive management strategies on coral cover, species diversity, CoTS density, and outbreak frequency, guiding decisions that maximise CoTS control benefits. These results directly inform CCP management to enhance reef resilience and offer a transferable framework for CoTS control across the Indo-Pacific, which will become increasingly important as climate change drives more frequent and severe bleaching events.
60b396b6: Increasingly, mass coral bleaching events driven by climate change are devastating the world’s coral reefs. Shading coral during high-stress periods can ameliorate a portion of the environmental strain on corals that comes from sun exposure and reduce bleaching. However, shading at reef-level scales is not feasible using shade cloths and other rigid structures. A proposed solution to shade corals at larger scales involves aerosolising seawater to create a fog layer that reduces irradiance. However, seawater fogging is limited to lower and more variable levels of shade (15–30%) when compared to rigid structures and the effect of seawater fogging on coral has yet to be empirically tested. Here, we evaluated the irradiance reduction capabilities of a miniaturised saltwater fogging machine in two aquaria-based coral bleaching experiments. We found that lower levels of fog (21.5%) improved coral colouration and symbiont retention in Pocillopora meandrina, but resulted in other mixed responses, including reduced photosynthetic efficiency. A higher fog density (33.3%) was tested in a second experiment on Acropora hyacinthus and Pocillopora damicornis. The denser fog resulted in a 55% reduction in mortality risk in A. hyacinthus and improved Fv/Fm and reduced whiteness in both species. Fog at 33.3% shade also continued to provide benefits to both species when applied after the heat-stress ended during the recovery period. Based on these findings, we hypothesise that higher fog densities/shade levels may provide a more consistent benefit over a wider range of species and that fogging/shading technologies may be useful for supporting coral recovery alongside bleaching mitigation during the heat-stress period. However, further experimental work, particularly in-situ is needed to understand the mixed effects of lower shade levels from fogging and the differing responses between coral species.
ae18a6d0: Climate change is driving ocean warming beyond the thermal limits of many coral–algal symbioses. Experimentally evolved, heat-tolerant algal symbionts (heat-evolved; HE) offer a promising solution by enhancing coral holobiont thermotolerance in a relatively short timeframe. To effectively translate the benefits of heat-evolved symbionts into restoration practices, scalable delivery methods must be developed. Coral tissue fusion—a natural process involving gastrovascular integration between colonies— may provide a novel delivery pathway for transferring HE symbionts into wild corals. To test this, adult fragments of Galaxea fascicularis and Psammocora columna hosting heat-evolved Cladocopium proliferum (strain SS8), along with chemically bleached, SS8-naive recipients, were cut and isografts were paired to promote tissue fusion. To account for potential acquisition of expelled SS8 cells from the surrounding water, a subset of unfused recipients was also housed within the same recirculating system. Corals were maintained for ~50 days, during which time fusion progression, tissue repigmentation, and algal photochemical efficiency were regularly assessed. Successful tissue fusion occurred after four days in G. fascicularis and eight days in P. columna. In both species, fusion was rapidly followed by elevated symbiont densities and enhanced photochemical efficiency near the recipient’s fusion interface, relative to unfused controls. SS8 was detected at low levels (<10%) in over 94% of fused G. fascicularis recipients and 77% of unfused recipients. While the latter provides strong evidence that expelled SS8 cells are being acquired horizontally from the water column, the higher detection rate in fused recipients—together with spatially localised pigmentation and photophysiological recovery—indicates that fusion has likely contributed to SS8 acquisition. These findings provide the first empirical evidence that coral tissue fusion can facilitate HE symbiont acquisition and accelerate post-bleaching recovery, potentially offering a complementary pathway for deploying beneficial symbionts on coral reefs and improving the scale of the intervention.
3470ae2d: The current loses of reef systems has been exacerbated by local human-induced agents (e.g., pollution, increased nutrients, and overexploitation) and by the increased frequency and intensity of El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) events. In response to this rapid degradation of coral reefs, human-assisted interventions have grown exponentially in all coral regions. However, most of these short- to long-term programs were significantly affected by the severe 2023-2024 El Niño event, both in Mexico's reefs and around the world. In this context, the present study evaluated the efficiency of using deep refuges (18-19 meters) although mobile-nurseries as a conservation tool for fragments reservoirs of the main reef-building coral, Pocillopora, in the Mexican Pacific during the 2023-2024 ENSO event.
