We then conducted regression analyses to estimate how microplastic concentration influences population-level effects (growth, feeding, and mortality rates) across trophic groups. Finally, we used an ecosystem model, Ecopath with Ecosim, to simulate ecosystem-level outcomes under present and projected microplastic concentrations, based on the reefs in Nanwan Bay, Taiwan, and using environmental microplastic data from Liuqiu Island, Taiwan, and Singapore’s Southern Islands.

Regression results showed mostly negative microplastic effects across trophic groups, with corals among the most sensitive. Ecosystem simulations predicted cascading disruptions throughout the food web as pollution intensifies. At higher concentrations, biomass of corals, sea anemones, soft corals, and polyp-feeding fishes declined markedly. Zooplankton biomass increased despite expected adverse effects. Overall, total reef biomass declined. The model suggests that reefs at Liuqiu Island are likely already experiencing microplastic impacts, whereas the impact on Singapore’s reefs is not yet noticeable. These findings highlight microplastics as a significant threat to coral reef ecosystems and emphasize the need for effective mitigation strategies.
20473c5d:
As of 2022, nearly one billion people live within 100 km of a coral reef, driving rapid land-use change, deforestation, agricultural expansion, and coastal urbanization that have sharply increased sediment, nutrient, and contaminant pollution in reef ecosystems. These pollutants reduce coral cover, structural complexity, biodiversity, and fisheries productivity, and they exacerbate bleaching mortality and disease outbreaks, making land-based pollution one of the most widespread and solvable threats to coral reefs globally. Yet despite its importance, most managers still lack the actionable, spatially explicit information needed to identify pollution sources, assess ecological risk, and prioritize interventions. Existing tools are often technically demanding, too coarse for local decision-making, or disconnected from on-the-ground management needs.
To address this gap, we are developing Global Pollution Watch (GPW): the first fully integrated, spatially explicit global system that maps where land-based pollution threatens coral reefs. GPW combines global land-use, hydrology, and watershed models with high-resolution hydrodynamic simulations of tides, currents, and wave-driven transport to produce dynamic estimates of pollution exposure for every coral reef worldwide. These outputs feed into an open-access Web App, enabling users to look at pollution exposure trends through time, rapidly identify vulnerable reefs, and trace pollution back to its source.
By delivering decision-ready information through an accessible user interface, Global Pollution Watch removes a major barrier to effective pollution management and restoration. This system allows practitioners, governments, and communities to prioritize action where it matters most, which in turn supports more strategic, collaborative, and climate-resilient conservation outcomes for reefs and the communities that depend on them.
1d6f746e: Accelerating loss of foundation reef-coral assemblages caused by climate change and direct human impacts is degrading most reef systems globally, requiring new approaches and interventions to effectively support recovery. Loss of breeding corals limits natural larval supply for settlement, recruitment and reef regeneration, creating negative feedback loops that further erode resilience and recovery. Moreover, degraded reef communities may not produce the sensory cues that attract or induce planktonic larvae to settle, further limiting recruitment. Here, we demonstrate for the first time using a large-scale reef experiment that playback of healthy reef soundscapes by underwater speakers can significantly enhance settlement rates of wild larvae on degraded reef sites. Using 24 plots at six reef sites at Lizard Island, Great Barrier Reef, we found that wild larval settlement was four times greater on acoustically enriched plots compared with ambient sound control plots. We also tested the effects of enhancing larval supply using millions of reef-cultured larvae (~1 million per larval plot), reared from wild coral spawn slick samples in a floating larval pool, that were slowly released onto larval enhancement plots using innovative larvalbox chambers. Enhanced larval supply increased settlement by 70x compared with natural larval supply. When acoustic and larval enhancement interventions were combined, a 1.5x superadditive effect on coral larval settlement rates was recorded on acoustically enriched plots supplied with reef-cultured larvae, representing a 107x increased compared with background settlement on control plots. These outcomes clearly demonstrate that wild coral larvae respond positively to sounds of healthy reef, and that settlement rates can be greatly amplified through acoustic enrichment to attract and anchor larvae. Combining acoustic enrichment with mass larval culture and enhanced supply creates new opportunities for catalysing much larger-scale larval settlement on degraded reefs and supporting reef recovery in the future.
dc6eb288: Tamil Nadu’s 1,069-km coastline supports one of India’s most diverse tropical seascapes, comprising coral reefs, seagrass meadows, mangroves, seaweed beds, and island ecosystems that collectively sustain high biodiversity and reef-dependent fisheries. The Gulf of Mannar (GoM), with 21 coral-bearing islands extending across ~160 km, represents India’s most biologically productive reef–seagrass continuum, supporting >4,000 marine species and underpinning livelihoods for thousands of small-scale fishers. However, climate-driven ocean warming, bleaching episodes, coastal pollution, over extraction, and plastic-derived stressors, especially abandoned, lost, and discarded fishing gears (ALDFG) pose escalating threats to ecosystem integrity and socio-ecological resilience. In response, the Government of Tamil Nadu has launched the Tamil Nadu Coastal Restoration Mission (2023–24) and the World Bank-supported Tamil Nadu: Sustainably Harnessing Ocean Resources and Blue Economy (TN-SHORE) programme - India’s first integrated state-wide coastal restoration framework linking science, policy, and community action. TN-SHORE adopts a multi-sectoral strategy encompassing coral and seagrass restoration, island ecosystem rehabilitation, reduction of land- and sea-based pollutants, and livelihood diversification through a circular and climate-resilient blue-economy approach. Core reef-focused interventions include: (i) expansion of long-term ecological monitoring of coral health, bleaching susceptibility, and reef–seagrass connectivity; (ii) restoration of priority reef patches and degraded seagrass beds; (iii) island vegetation restoration to improve sediment stability and reef water quality; and (iv) community-implemented plastic and ALDFG retrieval programmes coupled with incentive-based recycling. Capacity-building initiatives target fisher communities, youth, and local governance institutions to strengthen stewardship and co-management. TN-SHORE represents a scalable model for sub-national reef conservation integrating ecosystem restoration with social protections, climate adaptation, and nature-positive development. This presentation highlights the programme’s science-policy design, monitoring architecture, and early outcomes, demonstrating how state-level frameworks can operationalise national and global coral-reef resilience priorities in rapidly developing tropical coastlines.
7118096d: Coral reef restoration has been practiced for decades in response to local pressures but accelerating climate threats have rendered traditional approaches limited in scale and easily overwhelmed by single large disturbances. New approaches that simultaneous upscale the technology for production and delivery while accelerating the pace at which scientific knowledge is generated are urgently needed. To this end, global restoration R&D has been reshaped through major programs such as RRAP, CORDAP, DARPA, and KAUST. RRAP exemplifies a mission-driven, cross-disciplinary approach integrating science, engineering, and social engagement. Over five years, 300+ contributors across 20+ organizations achieved significant advances in upscaling both knowledge generation through critical R&D and the scope of intervention intensity through engineering solution. This led to pilot deployments, with a third phase involving full-scale implementation within reach in the near future. But despite the amazing advances to date we are not yet on a trajectory for long-term success. We propose to build on current progress to understand heat tolerance and survival to further focus on rate changing R&D to deliver corals that can better survive current and future conditions. We will present a concept based on local resilience hubs that operate as locations to progressively improve coral resilience to future conditions, provide a local restoration benefit and the larvae for broader restoration using the advance conservation aquaculture methods currently being rolled out. Linked to biobanking, training and capacity building efforts, these networks of hubs will protect biodiversity and engage local partners and industry to build supply chains and finance for scalable restoration. The coral reef restoration challenge remains immense and will require a moonshot of innovation and collaboration. But the potential benefits delivered via hubs to sustain reef functions and the billions who depend on the are equally profound. If not now, when. If not us, who.
2a2cdffb: Since the 1980s, coral reef interventions have expanded from local restoration activities to more complex approaches, including assisted evolution and environmental modification. However, ecological risk management frameworks have not kept pace. As interventions increase in scale and ambition, structured approaches are needed to assess potential ecological risks alongside anticipated benefits and societal risk tolerance. We conducted a systematic review to identify, categorise and define the ecological risks associated with active coral reef interventions, compiling the results into a structured, searchable database. Five primary ecological risk categories were identified: genetic, physiological, invasive, physical and ecosystem risks. Most literature focuses on ecosystem- and physiology-related concerns, particularly in regions such as Australia and the Caribbean. While many risks are perceived to be theoretical, they warrant explicit consideration as interventions move toward larger-scale application. Our analysis shows that how risks are framed depends on intervention type, spatial and temporal scale, and regional context. This underscores the importance of transparent, context-specific risk assessment processes rather than generic assumptions of safety or harm. The ecological risk database will be publicly available to support researchers, managers and practitioners. It enables users to examine how risks have been characterised across interventions and regions, identify knowledge gaps, and inform decision-making within specific environmental and regulatory settings. The database has also supported a CORDAP global landscape study to identify research and development priorities in a Roadmap. Collectively, these efforts strengthen applied ecological risk assessment capability through the Australian Institute of Marine Science and its partners, contributing to the global evidence base for responsible evaluation and governance of emerging reef interventions.
4f05233e: The impacts of Ocean Alkalinity Enhancement (OAE) were tested on sediments of the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) in the context of Carbon Dioxide Removal (CDR) and remediation of ocean acidification. OAE is a proposed method of CDR that enhances the ocean’s natural process of absorbing carbon dioxide. This is done by increasing Total Alkalinity (TA) in ocean water, which increases pH, allowing the ocean to both absorb more carbon dioxide and alleviate pH stress on calcifying organisms such as corals. Localized treatments of OAE may be necessary to target coral reefs to reduce stress from ocean acidification, and OAE operations are likely to take place over sediments rather than the reef framework itself. To simulate methods of OAE, benthic advective chambers were used to isolate calcium carbonate sediment patches for incubation. Sodium hydroxide treatments were used to elevate total alkalinity within those chambers. Three time points were taken over 24 hours to collect data on daytime and nighttime biogeochemical processes under the manipulated water chemistry. From these samples, total alkalinity, pH, dissolved inorganic carbon, dissolved oxygen and calcium fluxes were calculated to determine how these sediments may respond under OAE. Sediment cores were taken to determine composition of the tested sediments, and continuous light and temperature data was taken from within the chambers. These treatments were repeated across seven reefs in the GBR to determine spatially how OAE will affect different reefs depending on distance from land, composition of sediment and change in latitude.
c8a0d9c3: Coral reefs occur in over 100 countries and territories, support global biodiversity and underpin the livelihoods of hundreds of millions of people, generating over USD $9 trillion in ecosystem services annually. As one of the most vulnerable ecosystems on the planet to anthropogenic pressures, evaluating and reporting on the global status and trends of coral reefs is critical to guide conservation actions and raise awareness of global and local drivers of coral loss. Here, we present results from the latest and most comprehensive global compilation and analysis of monitoring data through the Global Coral Reef Monitoring Network (GCRMN). The GCRMN is an operational network of the International Coral Reef Initiative (ICRI) that aims to provide the best available scientific information on the status and trends of coral reef ecosystems for their conservation and management. The network comprises scientists, managers, and organisations across 10 regional nodes that monitor coral reefs at their local scales. The “Status of Coral Reefs of the World: 2025” report describes decadal trends in key coral reef indicators at global, regional, and sub-regional scales, based on a quantitative analysis of a global dataset comprising 87,701 surveys conducted across 124 countries and territories, at 37,799 sites between 1973 and 2025. The scale of this analysis provides an in-depth global picture of the condition and common challenges facing the world's coral reefs. The results will be presented both as a global synthesis and as regional analyses to examine region-specific and common patterns of change over the last four decades. The data collation and report production also provided valuable lessons and key insights into pathways to address known challenges in collecting, analysing, integrating, and applying monitoring data to inform conservation actions in the 21st century.
40154d50:
Caribbean reefs cover 24,230 km², representing 9.7% of global reef area, yet they have undergone substantial ecological decline. The Status and Trends of Caribbean Coral Reefs: 1970–2024, led by the International Coral Reef Initiative (ICRI) and the Global Coral Reef Monitoring Network (GCRMN)-Caribbean node, provides the most up-to-date regional assessment of Caribbean coral reef condition. Synthesizing nearly two years of collaboration among ~300 contributors, the report integrates 72 datasets encompassing 13,864 monitoring sites and 23,742 surveys conducted between 1973 and 2024. Machine learning models were applied to correct for spatial and temporal sampling biases and estimate long-term trends across five major benthic categories: hard coral, macroalgae, turf algae, coralline algae, and other fauna, as well as three key coral genera (Acropora, Orbicella, Porites).
Hard coral cover decreased by 48% from 1980 to 2024, with major declines in 1998, 2005, and 2023 linked to mass bleaching events and coral disease. Acropora corals, once dominant reef builders, remain critically depleted, averaging just 1.8% cover, far lower than their early 1970s coverage near 16%. Meanwhile, massive corals such as Orbicella and Porites have remained stable or increased slightly, driving a shift in species dominance and likely reducing overall reef architectural complexity.
Macroalgae increased by 84.8% over the same period, reflecting declines in herbivorous species, such as parrotfish and sea urchins, and elevated nutrient inputs from land-based sources. Climate driven stress continues to intensify: mean sea surface temperatures across Caribbean reef areas rose by 1.07°C between 1985 and 2024, a warming rate of 0.27°C per decade. Human pressures are also expanding, with populations living within 20 km of reefs increasing by 27.6%: 13 million additional people between 2000 and 2020. The report concludes with a set of recommendations for urgent, coordinated regional action and strengthened science-based management to ensure the future of Caribbean reefs.
d2b2136b: Palau is a Pacific Island nation characterised by extensive coral reefs that provide coastal protection, support dive tourism, and sustain community livelihoods. Over recent decades, several major disturbance events, including mass coral bleaching events and two major cyclones, have reduced coral cover and altered community composition. Understanding the cumulative effects of these disturbances and the capacity of reefs to recover is essential for effective management in a rapidly changing climate. Here, we present 22 years (2002-2024) of coral reef monitoring data collected across 23 sites through a national long-term monitoring program led by the Palau International Coral Reef Center (PICRC). Benthic cover was quantified using ReefCloud, an AI-assisted image analysis platform that accelerates data processing by having machine learning trained on human annotations. Across the full monitoring record, we show how coral cover and community composition have fluctuated in response to different disturbances, with recovery to pre-disturbance levels taking approximately 10 years. Such long-term, continuous monitoring datasets with taxonomic detail and spanning more than 20 years are rare in the Pacific outside of Hawaii and Australia, making this time series scientifically invaluable. Dedicated monitoring programs from PICRC are critical for understanding the long-term capacity of reefs to absorb and recover from disturbance. With the increasing severity and frequency of these disturbances, it is essential to understand how management interventions, such as marine protected areas, can mitigate impacts, alongside the aforementioned long-term monitoring to enable rapid responses to these disturbance events. Insights from this comprehensive dataset will be discussed in the context of Palau’s climatological impacts and the National Environmental Management Strategy. Lessons learned and recommendations from this assessment will be discussed to guide reef monitoring and management efforts across the wider Pacific.
f45dffd9: The Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary (FKNMS) encompasses the largest coral reef system in the continental United States along with extensive seagrass and mangrove habitats, forming a complex ecosystem of immense ecological and socioeconomic value. Since its designation in 1990, the Sanctuary has aimed to protect biodiversity while supporting sustainable uses such as commercial fishing, recreation and tourism. Early management plans introduced regulations and zoning to safeguard critical habitats and reduce user conflicts. However, the first Sanctuary “report card” released in 2010 documented accelerating declines in coral cover, reef fish populations and habitat quality due to local, regional and global stressors. These findings made clear that the existing management framework was insufficient to reverse ecosystem degradation. In response, FKNMS and its partners spent over a decade identifying lessons learned and using them to develop more targeted conservation measures. The resulting Restoration Blueprint, released in 2025, establishes updated boundaries, new and expanded managed zones, revised regulations and a coordinated restoration and management strategy designed to rebuild degraded habitats, enhance resilience and mitigate ongoing stressors. Evaluating the effectiveness of this new management plan is essential for guiding adaptive management and ensuring long-term ecosystem recovery. This comprehensive monitoring and assessment framework will integrate 1) ecological fish and benthic surveys; 2) passive acoustic monitoring to characterize human use and biological activity; 3) in-situ experiments on primary productivity and community composition; 4) restoration-based monitoring, including Mission: Iconic Reefs; 5) assessment of habitat-support structures; and 6) social and socioeconomic indicators. This integrated approach will assess compliance with spatial protections, the efficacy of new zone configurations, changes in biodiversity and habitat condition, restoration outcomes and community impacts. Together, these multidisciplinary tools provide a scientifically rigorous basis for evaluating the Restoration Blueprint, informing future management decisions, and offering a transferable model for assessing marine protected area effectiveness globally.
84949190:
Coral reefs in the ROPME Sea Area (RSA), including the Arabian/Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, and western Arabian Sea, persist under extreme conditions, with summer temperatures above 35 °C, salinities exceeding 42 PSU, and intense coastal development. Despite their resilience, assessments indicate a 40 percent decline in coral cover since the mid 1990s, with losses above 80 percent in some areas. Although countries have monitored reefs for decades, the preparation of the 2020 Global Coral Reef Monitoring Network (GCRMN) report showed that inconsistent methods, sparse regional coverage, fragmented datasets, and limited access restricted the ability to interpret long-term trends.

To address these challenges, experts from seven RSA nations launched a data diplomacy initiative to harmonize monitoring, improve data integration, and enable collaborative interpretation of regional data. A key step was the establishment of a regional data sharing agreement, which created a protected framework for open access while guaranteeing reciprocal credit to contributors. Three principles guided the collaboration: standardized monitoring methodology and metadata protocols, open but protected data access with contributor credit, and neutral custodianship by a regional institution.

The initiative adopted ReefCloud and MERMAID as core platforms, establishing a workflow from image processing to centralized archiving and integration into global databases. By March 2025, more than 2,000 georeferenced transects from 380 sites collected between 1985 and 2024 had been compiled, representing a fivefold increase since 2020 and expanding both temporal and spatial coverage.

Through this process, the RSA shifted from data scarcity to active participation in global assessments, with standardized datasets now supporting the first regional and global IUCN Red List of Ecosystems assessment for coral reefs. This regional experience shows how methodological alignment, neutral stewardship, and open access principles can transform fragmented monitoring into a scalable and policy relevant observatory capable of informing management and coordinated conservation planning.
e5a79890:
Long-term, large-scale coral reef monitoring is essential for detecting ecosystem change, evaluating management actions, and supporting ecological assessments. Since 2000, NOAA Coral Reef Conservation Program’s (CRCP) National Coral Reef Monitoring Program (NCRMP) has surveyed more than 25,000 individual sites, recording over 20 different benthic, fish, and climate indicators, including coral cover and demography, fish biomass and abundance, temperature and carbonate chemistry. In recent years, NCRMP has worked on streamlining data collection, improving data pipelines, standardizing QA/QC, adopting advanced technologies, and integrating multi-disciplinary datasets. These advances have increased both the accessibility of data to managers and communities, and the suitability of these data to support local and regional management actions, fisheries management plans, coral restoration siting, and ecosystem condition reports. NCRMP surveys also allow for comprehensive ecological assessments of uninhabited, remote islands under different management regimes, offering a unique opportunity to evaluate the impacts of human activities and management effectiveness.

NCRMP's "wide but thin" approach of surveying each U.S. Pacific archipelago every three years with island-scale data delivery, still has limitations for local management needs. To address this, NOAA’s CRCP provides project-based funding to support direct collaboration with local managers and communities to co-develop monitoring efforts that leverage the wider NCRMP datasets, while providing training and support for data collection, management, and analyses, and the development of local and regional technical products, including a restoration guidance framework, a U.S. Pacific-wide resilience assessment, and an Environmental Data Summary tool that links survey data with environmental information to better understand drivers of ecological observations. Future plans to increase the impact of NCRMP survey data include improved analytical workflows, creation of interoperable data products, increased data visualization tools, and a focus on developing ecological analyses and indicators tailored to meet priority coral reef management needs.
36298c97:
This presentation will illustrate how long term, citizen science monitoring can provide early warning insights, inform management responses to both global and local stressors, and highlight the importance of open-access, standardised datasets in meeting future monitoring and management needs.

Since 2002, CoralWatch has contributed a globally consistent, long-term coral health dataset generated through community based monitoring using the scientifically validated Coral Health Chart. This simple colour based tool enables volunteers, students, tourism operators, and scientists to quantify coral bleaching with high repeatability, resulting in more than 530,000 observations from 2,400 reefs across 81 countries, all publicly available through the Atlas of Living Australia. 

This long term dataset demonstrates the value of citizen science based monitoring in multiple ways. First, CoralWatch participants can mobilise quickly during bleaching events, detecting onset within days and providing early warning information. This rapid response also allows the detection of local stressors, such as land reclamation and dredging, complementing traditional monitoring approaches. Second, comparative studies, including research from the Heron Island on the Great Barrier Reef, show that CoralWatch data can capture spatial variation in bleaching severity, supporting decision-making at local and regional scales. Third, community contributions often include reef sites known locally but overlooked by formal scientific programs, expanding the spatial coverage of monitoring.

CoralWatch’s open-access platform makes data immediately available, and includes interactive data visualisation tools that support communication with managers, educators, policymakers, and the public. These tools help translate complex bleaching data into intuitive formats, strengthening cross-sector dialogue and improving science communication. However, challenges remain, particularly in quantifying coral mortality and recovery, highlighting the importance of collaborative monitoring efforts and the need to advance data standardisation and interoperability across regions and sectors.
29660419:
Adaptive conservation planning to manage local impacts on coral reefs while accounting for climate change pressures is often constrained by insufficient information on coral reef status and trends, due to limited resources, insufficient monitoring capacity, and difficulties accessing existing data.
Technological solutions offer significant potential to overcome these challenges. However, they can introduce risks, such as reliance on external experts or specific tools, limited adaptability across diverse contexts, and uncertain long-term support. Considering this, scientists and monitoring practitioners from Australia, the Pacific, and Southeast Asia collaborated to design ReefCloud, a digital platform hosted by the Australian Institute of Marine Science (AIMS). ReefCloud.ai uses artificial intelligence to analyse coral reef images, store and analyse monitoring data, and produce standardised reef condition reports.
Partner feedback, feature testing and iterative improvements of ReefCloud’s functionality enabled the establishment of a standard framework for reef monitoring. Capacity-building activities focused on building local expertise and fostering an international community of practice, supporting the protection and management of coral reef ecosystems in Southeast Asia and the Pacific.  
The deliberate co-design approach accelerated coral reef monitoring capabilities in partner countries within three years, illustrated by four case studies showing how technology enables agile decision-making when local needs and priorities are considered: In Brunei Darussalam, the project catalysed the formulation of a draft National Coral Reef Monitoring and Management Plan. In the Philippines, ReefCloud was formally adopted as the standard monitoring method, directly supporting coastal Zoning in the province of Palawan. In Samoa, the application of ReefCloud was central for providing training, monitoring resources and guidance for the design of a National Coral Reef Monitoring Plan supporting the Samoa Ocean Strategy. In Vietnam, nine MPAs are now conducting phototransect monitoring supported by ReefCloud, and data have been applied to inform adaptive management of coral reefs impacted by thermal bleaching.
43542564:
Coral reefs are dynamic environments where temporal changes occur within and across reefs as a result of natural drivers and impacts such as bleaching, cyclones and diseases. Due to mounting climatic pressures, there is an urgent need to better understand reef dynamics at extensive spatial and temporal scales to aid reef management. Recent developments in low cost cameras and GPS, machine learning and earth observation have rapidly increased our ability to monitor reefs at various spatial and temporal scales. 

Here we present some of the innovations that let to the analyses of over 20 years of annual coral reef benthic monitoring combining, citizen science, photo quadrates, satellite imagery and machine learning applied at Heron Reef (28 km2), Southern Great Barrier Reef, Australia. 

Annual surveys included: CoralWatch Colour Health Chart surveys, +4000 geolocated photo quadrats covering +30 km of transects, and coincident high resolution multi spectral satellite imagery. The photos were analysed through machine learning using ReefCloud/CoralNet, and were integrated with high spatial resolution multi spectral satellite imagery, water depth, and waves using object based analysis and random forest classifier to produce high resolution habitat maps. These data sets provided benthic composition at individual photo quadrat, site, geomorphic zone and reef scales over time. Analysis gave us further insights into: benthic cover change, carbonate budgets, bleaching-cyclone-disease impacts, and reef resilience. 

Our findings highlight recent innovations that increase our ability to monitor reefs at various spatial scales, while also making research methods more accessible, repeatable, and robust. The developed approaches formed the basis for various mapping initiatives locally to globally scales. We also show that coral benthic communities vary within and across reefs over time and space, and site selection is crucial for understanding reef dynamics. Future research directions will focus on real time analysis, autonomous monitoring and integration with hyperspectral imagery.
7b120fa1:
Abstract
Coral reefs are among the most biodiverse and ecologically significant ecosystems, supporting the livelihoods of millions globally. However, they face mounting threats from both local and global stressors. Effective conservation strategies require robust baseline data to assess long-term ecological changes and inform adaptive management. This study presents the most recent and thorough large-scale assessment of coral reefs in the Saudi Red Sea, providing critical baseline information to support national conservation efforts. Between February 2023 and April 2024, surveys were conducted at 589 sites spanning from Haql to the Farasan Islands using standardized underwater transect methods. A custom Reef Health Index (RHI), integrating Coral Health Index (CHI) and Fish Health Index (FHI) metrics on a scale from 0 to 1, was developed to assess reef condition. The RHI indicated “Healthy” reefs in the Central region (Yanbu, 0.66) and predominantly “Fair” reefs in the South (Al Lith, 0.41). Hard coral cover was highest in the North (48%) and Center (45%), while macroalgae cover peaked in the South (11%). Coral communities were dominated by branching, massive, and encrusting forms (76.9–95.9%), with Acropora spp. prevalent in the North and Center, and Porites spp. dominant in the South. Key stressors included anchor damage (Center, South), discarded fishing gear (North), and widespread coral bleaching (>40% of sites), especially near the Farasan Islands and Banks. These findings highlight the need for region-specific conservation actions to address ongoing pressures and support Saudi Arabia’s Vision 2030 goals through science-based reef management and restoration.
9412dea0: The widespread decline of biologically and economically vital coral reefs, often resulting in phase shifts to macroalgal dominance, necessitates the study of resilient outliers. The reefs of Flower Garden Banks National Marine Sanctuary (FGBNMS), located 190 km offshore from the Texas and Louisiana coasts in the United States, have demonstrated exceptional stability over decades. A long-term photographic monitoring program, initiated in 1989, has produced a continuous dataset critical for assessing community-level trends and fine-scale benthic changes. We analyzed a 32-year time series (1992–2024) of benthic composition (coral, macroalgae, CCA, sponge) using point-count analysis from both random and permanent monitoring stations at East and West Flower Garden Banks (EFGB and WFGB). Our findings reveal sustained high coral cover and growth. Key results show EFGB maintained stable mean coral cover (46% in 1989 to 50% in 2024), while WFGB exhibited a significant increase from 47% to 62%. Species composition remained consistent, indicating increasing cover was due to sustained growth of the existing community. Macroalgae showed interannual variability and a slight increase (EFGB: 18% to 24%; WFGB: 13% to 17%), but not degradation-level phase shifts. These results contrast sharply with widespread Western Atlantic/Caribbean reef degradation, establishing the reefs of FGBNMS as a critical example of sustained coral dominance and reinforcing the value of consistent, photo-based, long-term monitoring. Crucially, FGBNMS represents a globally significant reference site to actively study mechanisms of resilience against thermal and anthropogenic stress. In turn, the data directly inform decision-making and policy, producing status and trend condition reports, vulnerability assessments, and setting the foundation for the sanctuary's next management plan, directly addressing 21st-century management needs.
363e24ba:
Coral reef ecosystems are facing a critical situation due to climate change, particularly from elevated sea temperatures. Therefore, conservation and management approaches based on scientific evidence, such as the identification of Ecologically or Biologically Significant Marine Areas (EBSAs), are becoming increasingly important. In coral reef ecosystems, connectivity among populations and regions promotes population maintenance, recovery, and genetic diversity. It provides essential ecological information for assessing the EBSA criterion of “vulnerability, fragility, sensitivity, or slow recovery.” However, in mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs) below 30 m depth, especially in the western Pacific, information on vertical and horizontal connectivity remains limited. In this study, we conducted genome-wide SNP-based population genetic analyses of Seriatopora hystrix and Leptoseris papyracea inhabiting MCEs from the Ryukyu Islands, Japan. 
A total of 79 colonies of Leptoseris papyracea were collected from three sites deeper than 30 m, and 368 colonies of Seriatopora hystrix were collected from 12 sites ranging from shallow to mesophotic depths. Phylogenetic analyses of S. hystrix (9,671 SNPs) identified three cryptic lineages unrelated to depth or geographic distance. After splitting the three lineages of S. hystrix, STRUCTURE, AMOVA, and migration estimates revealed a stronger genetic connectivity between shallow and mesophotic populations within or between nearby sites than between geographically distant populations at the same depth. Nevertheless, the extent of genetic connectivity across depths varied depending on lineage and the continuity of habitats spanning different depth ranges. These findings suggest that vertical and horizontal connectivity in coral reef ecosystems across the Japanese Archipelago depend on the dispersal ability and adaptive capacity of species or lineages, as well as on their habitat characteristics. Analysis of L. papyracea is still ongoing, and the presentation will include results for both S. hystrix and L. papyracea.
dc1e24b1:
As coral reefs face accelerating impacts from global warming and human pressures, mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs) have emerged as a key frontier for understanding reef resilience. Situated between 30- and 150-meters depth, these deep reef communities remain poorly characterized, particularly regarding their biodiversity and their potential demographic links with shallow reefs. Central to the Deep Reef Refugia Hypothesis (DRRH) is the assumption that MCEs can replenish degraded shallow reefs, yet this relies on the existence of effective vertical connectivity.
Here, we present one of the most extensive assessments of vertical and horizontal genetic connectivity in two islands of the southwestern Indian Ocean, using 9 hydroid target species as a model system. Across 57 mesophotic dives together with 50 shallow water dives around Réunion and Mayotte islands, we collected and genotyped over 2,200 specimens using microsatellite libraries, seven of which were newly developed for this study.
Using a comparative population genetics framework combining differentiation indices, STRUCTURE, AMOVA, Mantel tests, and Bayesian estimates of contemporary gene flow (BayesAss 3), we identified a greater genetic diversity in Mayotte than in Réunion, a lack of connectivity between the islands and different patterns of vertical and horizontal connectivity within each island. 
While only two species in Mayotte exhibited strong vertical differentiation between euphotic and mesophotic depths, three markedly contrasting connectivity patterns emerged in Réunion: (1) horizontal stepping-stone dispersal, (2) vertical stepping-stone connectivity across adjacent depth zones, and (3) near-panmictic population structure.
Despite these overarching patterns, each species displayed distinct connectivity signatures that differed between islands, highlighting the complexity of mesophotic biodiversity and the need for further investigation. Critically, at Réunion Island, four species showed bottom-up gene flow consistent with the DRRH, providing strong evidence that MCEs can contribute to the replenishment and long-term resilience of shallow reefs.
0df79bc0:
Mesophotic coral reefs remain vastly understudied and yet highly biodiverse ecosystems. In the face of global climate change, it has been suggested that mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCE’s) may provide a thermal refugia against coral bleaching.  However little is known about the thermal limits or bleaching thresholds of mesophotic corals, and those that are known are generally restricted to the upper mesophotic zone (> 60m). Here we assessed the thermal threshold of Leptoseris cf. striatus, a depth-specialist scleractinian living in symbioses with both endosymbiotic (Symbiodiniaceae) and endolithic algae (Ostreobium spp.) We collected L. cf. striatus specimens from the Saudi Arabian Red Sea, across a south to north gradient at varying depths from 68-126m.
To quantify the thermal threshold of these corals, we conducted a thermal ramping experiment, increasing the temperature by 1.0°C per day from the ambient collection temperature of 23.0 °C. No corals showed any signs of mortality until the temperature reached 34.0 °C, +6.0°C above the highest temperature we recorded in-situ at the time of collection and +11.0 °C above the ambient temperature. Our results challenge the notion that most tropical marine species are generally living close to their thermal limits and uncovering the mechanisms behind this observed thermal resistance may help us to understand how mesophotic reefs will respond to future warming events.
d59b4cfc:
Black corals (antipatharians) are sessile colonial animals ubiquitous across oceans, thriving until great depths (> 8,000 m). Particularly abundant in mesophotic zones, they form dense assemblages often referred to as “Marine Animal Forests”, which create biodiversity oases supporting a wide range of marine life. Due to the challenges associated with sampling and exploration at depth, our knowledge on this taxa remains very limited, leaving critical gaps in understanding their ecological success in such environments. 
This study explores how morphology and ciliary flow shape mass transport in black corals that were collected down to 80 m depth through tech-diving operations. We compared two co-occurring species with contrasting morphologies: the whip-like Stichopathes sp. and the branched Antipathella wollastoni. Using multi-scale flow visualization (particle image/tracking velocimetry), and metabolic assays (oxygen-sensitive sensPIV and respirometry), we explored how morphology influences flow dynamics and ecological performance. 
Our findings revealed that ciliary flow at the microscale plays a central role in mass transport, with species-specific roles. Stichopathes sp. relies strongly on ciliary flow to enhance ventilation and feeding, compensating for its limited exchange surface, whereas A. wollastoni benefits from greater exchange surface, but uses ciliary flow for local redistribution of small food particles. Additionally, we observed bidirectional particle movement in gastrovascular systems and tentacles, with flow characteristics shaped by skeletal spine morphology. 
Our results highlight the critical influence of morphology and the diverse functions of cilia in shaping the ecological success of black corals in mesophotic and deeper habitats.
618d0744: Photosymbiotic corals create the structural foundation of coral reef ecosystems, which support high biodiversity and productivity in oligotrophic waters throughout tropical and subtropical oceans. These light-dependent corals can exhibit high abundance at depths >100 m and their distribution extends to extreme depths (>190 m). However, knowledge about coral reef ecosystems is primarily gleaned from shallow water studies using coral taxa restricted to the upper half of the photic zone (<80 m). In oligotrophic waters, the lower half of the vertical habitat for photosymbiotic corals receives <3.2% of surface PAR, and the photosymbiotic coral community is dominated by depth-specialist species typically within the family Agariicidae. For example, species of the genus Leptoseris are consistently the deepest taxa found throughout Indo-Pacific locations where the full depth range has been explored. With few exceptions, very little reliable information exists about the physiological function and ecology of photosymbiotic corals in the lower half of their depth range. Despite ample contradictory evidence, photosymbiotic corals have long been assumed to increase their reliance on heterotrophy with depths as light exponentially attenuates and the potential for photosynthesis declines. To definitively address the knowledge gap on the trophic ecology of photosymbiotic corals in the lower photic zone, deep-water Leptoseris spp. (≥100 m) were sampled from three disparate locations (Red Sea, East China Sea, and South Pacific) and analyzed via δ15N CSIA-AA to determine their trophic position. Results of this unprecedented analysis will be presented at the conference and placed within the context of the known physiology and ecology of Leptoseris spp. Important implications of these results will be discussed, including a long-overdue paradigm shift in the role of heterotrophy in photosymbiotic corals.
15f51a83: While carbon and nitrogen bulk tissue stable isotope analysis (SIA) has revolutionized food web studies, their specific application to photosymbiotic organisms in coral reef ecosystems involves significant complexities which are not present in nonsymbiotic organisms or most terrestrial ecosystems. The use of bulk tissue carbon and nitrogen SIA metrics to infer relative contribution of autotrophy versus heterotrophy in photosymbiotic organisms (i.e., δ13C(host), Δ13C(host-symbiont), δ15N(host), Δ15N(host-symbiont), SIBER, MixSIAR) has proliferated in recent years due in part to their convenience and low cost. However, a comprehensive review of the literature reveals that the theoretical foundations and assumptions underlying their validity can be quite tenuous, especially for natural studies spanning significant spatial scales. To date, the available empirical evidence does not support the efficacy of these bulk tissue SIA trophic metrics as several factors can exert a dominant influence on them independent of an organism’s trophic status. A review of previously published datasets reveals a lack of correlation between these metrics and with higher resolution trophic analyses (i.e., compound specific stable isotope analysis and lipid analysis) confirming that they are primarily influenced by distinct factors. These findings have important implications for a large body of literature on photosymbiotic organisms, their previous conclusions, and the prevailing generalizations based on these studies. Specifically, historical assumptions about the energetics of photosymbiotic corals have long assumed that they must increase their reliance on heterotrophic feeding as light attenuates due to increasing depth or turbidity. In the absence of a commensurate reduction in metabolic demand, this assumption of increased reliance on heterotrophy appears reasonable. Based on context-limited interpretations of select bulk tissue SIA data, this generalization has become widely accepted as dogma and is routinely repeated in contemporary primary literature. However, a holistic reassessment of available data, including data from higher-resolution tools, now unambiguously refutes this generalization.
73300a87: Leptoseris corals span a broad depth range and are among the most common photosymbiotic taxa in mesophotic ecosystems. Yet, the molecular processes that enable these corals to tolerate depth-associated environmental variability remain poorly understood. Here, we conducted a reciprocal transplantation of Leptoseris colonies between shallow (10–15 m) and upper mesophotic (30–40 m) depths in Bolinao, Pangasinan, Philippines, and quantified transcriptome-wide gene expression responses over time. Initial findings revealed that transplanted colonies rapidly shifted their transcriptional profiles toward those of depth-matched controls. Shallow-origin corals transplanted to the mesophotic site upregulated pathways involved in cell signaling, mitochondrial function, and metabolic regulation. In contrast, mesophotic-origin corals moved to the shallow site upregulated pathways linked to UV stress responses, protein folding and turnover, metabolism, and calcification. These results highlight depth-specific molecular programs in Leptoseris and identify pathway shifts that underpin their capacity to acclimatize across contrasting reef environments.
1a2022a0: Increasing ocean temperatures and the intensification of marine heatwaves continue to drive widespread bleaching and mortality across Caribbean coral reefs. Mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs), found at depths of 30-150m, have been proposed as potential thermal refugia due to their cooler, more stable conditions, which has led to the deep reef refugia hypothesis (DRRH). However, support for the DRRH remains inconsistent across regions and species. In some cases, MCEs appear buffered from thermal stress, while others, including research on Orbicella spp., suggest that corals may be acclimated to local depth-specific conditions, making them just as susceptible to temperature anomalies. This study investigated resilience and recovery in Orbicella franksi from a shallow fringing reef (Flat Cay; 15m) and a mesophotic reef (Hind Bank; 40m) in St. Thomas, USVI, under experimentally simulated thermal stress. We hypothesized that mesophotic O. franksi would exhibit greater bleaching susceptibility than their shallow reef counterparts due to their acclimatization to cooler, lower light environments. Ramets from five genets per depth were exposed to three temperature treatments representing ambient conditions, moderate warming, and severe heat stress. Throughout the experiment, corals were assessed via daily visual health checks, quantitative image-based bleaching analysis, and photosynthetic efficiency. Symbiont density and chlorophyll content were measured across bleaching stages, while symbiont community composition was analyzed only pre-bleaching and post-recovery to detect shifts from Cladocopium to heat-tolerant Durusdinium. Physiological samples are being processed, but preliminary results show that mesophotic colonies bleached faster than Flat Cay colonies, supporting our hypothesis that deeper corals are adapted to their local thermal environment and are therefore more sensitive to elevated temperatures. Findings from this study will improve our understanding of depth-specific responses to thermal stress in O. franksi, helping assess the capacity of MCEs to function as climate refugia in the U.S. Virgin Islands.
afeec941: Low-lying atolls of the Republic of the Marshall Islands (RMI) are projected to become uninhabitable by 2040 due to inundation of their shallow freshwater resources. Thus, it is imperative to understand the impact of climate change on the location and intensity of rainfall associated with the Intertropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ). Located in the core of the ITCZ’s northmost extent, small changes in the position or extent of the ITCZ, such as during El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) events, leave a strong imprint on regional rainfall and salinity. Stable oxygen isotopes measured in corals (δ18Ocoral) from RMI therefore provide a critical archive of ITCZ-related salinity variability associated with changes in broader Pacific climate state (with over 60% of the δ18Ocoral variability driven by precipitation-driven changes in δ18Osw).  Here, we leverage a network of modern and fossil coral cores spanning the 13th to 21st centuries to reconstruct salinity variability over the past millennium and assess the sensitivity of ITCZ-related rainfall to natural forcings and internal modes of climate variability.  Our reconstruction is characterized by a preindustrial period with strong decadal to multidecadal variability and prolonged wet and dry intervals, whereas in the last few decades, ENSO-related interannual variability has increased. Reconstructed salinity was strongly inversely related to both the N-S interhemispheric temperature and precipitation gradients, and positively related to temperature over the Niño 3.4 region, supporting the strong link between ITCZ position and the cross-equatorial temperature gradient across interannual to decadal timescales. Finally, the continuous reconstruction from ~1750 – 2024 depicts a southward shift in the ITCZ and salinification until ~1870, and a broad freshening trend with increased interannual variability thereafter. This long-term climate reconstruction highlights that future freshwater security in the RMI will depend on how warming changes the ITCZ, with likely increased interannual rainfall extremes.
281af0e1: COR-Resilience project aims at reconstructing, quantifying and evaluating past, present and future impacts of anthropogenically-driven climate change, and local stressors on the calcification and growth mechanisms of two of the main reef-building coral genera (Porites and Diploastrea) found in the Indo-Pacific region. To achieve this objective, the project will apply a combined “biogeochemical data – modelling” approach and reconstruct long-term key environmental parameters over the 2-5 last centuries based on coral geochemistry. Decadal to centennial scale records of past seawater temperature, carbonate chemistry and heavy metal concentration will be reconstructed and compared with the skeletal growth parameters to improve predictions of coral calcification and growth responses to natural and human-induced environmental changes. Such comparisons are rare given that they require a large effort in collecting multiple information on coral growth parameters, dynamics of skeletal colonization by microborers and geochemical proxies (d18O, d13C, d11B, B/Ca, Sr/Ca, Li/Mg, etc.). The project working hypothesis is that coral growth and skeletal macro- and micro-structures are not directly affected by the ambient seawater parameters, but by the direct influence of abiotic/biotic factors on the chemistry of the calcifying fluid from which aragonite precipitates. The main purpose of COR-Resilience project is to provide: i- a full characterization of the present-day and past variability of the carbonate chemistry within the calcifying fluid of several colonies of Porites and Diploastrea from the Indo-Pacific region. Coral geochemistry will be also used to reconstruct the long-term SST variability and heavy metal contents of the ambient seawater; ii- a “data – model” comparison of the seawater carbonate chemistry and SST changes over the last centuries; iii- a comprehensive evaluation of the effects of multiple stressors on the growth mechanisms of massive tropical corals. Halfway through the project, we propose to present the project's progress while illustrating some initial results.
5a119289:
Subtropical gyres are wind-driven, strongly stratified ocean regions that cover 40% of Earth’s surface. These so-called ‘ocean deserts’ are characterized by nutrient impoverishment and low biological productivity, and their perennial warmth has profound impacts on atmospheric circulation and climate. Satellite observations suggest that subtropical gyres have been expanding and migrating polewards since the late 1990s in response to anthropogenic global warming. However, significant uncertainty remains as to the sign and magnitude of such changes because they are easily obscured by interannual and decadal climate variability and because of the relatively short length of the satellite record. 

Here we present records of the 15N-to-14N isotopic ratio of organic matter preserved in the skeleton of shallow-water scleractinian corals from the Mozambique Channel, extending back 145 years. Toward the present, these corals increasingly record the nitrogen isotopic signature of the Indian Ocean subtropical gyre (IOSG), suggesting that the IOSG has been expanding since early in the 20th century. While the observed global expansion of the atmospheric Hadley cells may have contributed, we find evidence that trade wind enhancement of the South Equatorial Current acted as a significant control on IOSG expansion over the 20th century. Such a driver would likely have enhanced the leakage of water from the Agulhas Current into the Atlantic basin over 20th century, which may have worked to stabilize the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation in the face of global warming.
abf13b38: Caribbean coral reefs are facing escalating pressures at unprecedented scales. In the Mesoamerican Reef System, these include intensifying local anthropogenic impacts driven by rapid population growth and inadequate wastewater treatment, recurrent and increasingly severe thermal stress event, most notably the extreme bleaching event of 2023, and the growing influence of sargassum influxes, whose impacts on reef health and water quality remain insufficiently quantified. Here, we assess multi-decadal variability in coral skeletal growth and apply a suite of geochemical proxies to reconstruct environmental change, with particular focus on the recent 2023 thermal stress event. By combining high-resolution growth measurements with trace-element ratios and nitrogen stable isotopes, we aim to link variations in calcification to interacting drivers including temperature, nutrient enrichment, and possible sargassum-related inputs. Our results aim to provide new insight into how local and global stressors are shaping reef resilience in one of the Caribbean’s most threatened reef systems.
38ad81e0: Coral skeletons are a source of high-resolution proxy data for reconstructing past temperature, salinity, and ocean conditions. However, the aragonite skeleton can undergo post-depositional alteration (diagenesis) that disrupts the original geochemical proxy climate signal. Secondary aragonite, a precipitate common in early marine diagenesis of coral skeletons, can introduce significant errors in ocean temperature reconstructions even in small amounts. In addition, secondary aragonite is difficult to detect by the most common screening methods such as x-ray diffraction, as it has the same crystal structure as the primary coral skeleton. Here we present a screening framework for low levels of secondary aragonite, and we apply the framework to ancient coral samples that date to up to 500,000 years old. These coral samples were retrieved from a globally unique sequence of drowned fossil reefs offshore Hawai’i, collected as part of the International Ocean Discovery Program Expedition 389. The framework is based on continuous Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) imaging of the corals along their major growth axis, the same location where geochemical samples will be milled. The results show that the SEM framework can identify sub-millimetre-scale areas of secondary aragonite, which can be avoided with careful sample collection, and provides a threshold below which coral geochemistry should be reliable for climate reconstructions. The results will be compared to other secondary aragonite screening methods. This new framework for screening secondary aragonite will complement published screening guides that focus on calcite and dissolution and will lead to more reliable paleoclimate and paleoenvironment reconstructions from corals.
4e3e9411: The impact of global warming on the ocean’s oxygen-deficient zones (ODZs) is uncertain, partly because of a lack of data on past changes. We report monthly resolved records of coral skeleton–bound nitrogen isotopes (CS-d15N) to reconstruct denitrification in the Eastern Tropical North Pacific (ETNP) ODZ over the last 80 years. The data indicate strong decadal variation in ETNP denitrification, with maxima during the cool North Pacific phase of Pacific Decadal Variability. The maxima in denitrification (and thus oxygen deficiency) were likely due to stronger upwelling that enhanced productivity leading to greater oxygen demand in the thermocline. Prior findings of multidecadal-to-centennial ODZ trends were likely biased by this variability. ODZ evolution over the next century will depend on how global warming interacts with the decadal oscillations.
259057c9:
Oxidative stress within the coral symbiocyte in response to thermal stress is considered a leading cause of coral bleaching. Thermal stress disrupts redox homeostasis, inducing a state of oxidative dysregulation whereby the production of reactive nitrogen species (RNS) and reactive oxygen species (ROS) surpasses the antioxidant capacity of the coral symbiocyte. Study of the prooxidant-antioxidant balance is therefore key to understanding the mechanism of coral bleaching. 
In this study, we measured intracellular RNS and ROS and the corresponding antioxidant response in live Pocillopora acuta cell cultures under controlled thermal stress. The activities of three primary enzymatic antioxidants were measured: catalase (CAT), superoxide dismutase (SOD) and glutathione peroxidase (GPX). Pairing analysis of antioxidant activity with RNS/ROS production provided a robust method to identify the state of oxidative stress in the coral symbiont and the physiological impact of this stress. With the use of applied thermal stress and the addition of exogenous prooxidants, it was possible to identify a critical inflection point in the antioxidant-prooxidant balance in live cell culture where enzymatic defenses become saturated resulting in runaway stress. This approach provides insight into redox equilibria in the coral symbiocyte, its disruption in response to thermal stress, and highlights potential opportunities for targeted intervention with exogenous antioxidants to mitigate bleaching.
71d29242: The collapse of the cnidarian–algal symbiosis during bleaching is driven by a breakdown of mutualistic nutrient cycling that ultimately results in host starvation. Yet the cellular processes underlying both the destabilization and the potential recovery remain poorly resolved. Here, we combine physiological measurements, proteomics, Raman microscopy, and a newly developed CryoNanoSIMS workflow to characterize the heat-stress response in the Aiptasia model system at subcellular resolution. We show that heat stress induces a gradual loss of host control over nitrogen availability, leading to excess nitrogen supply to algal symbionts. CryoNanoSIMS imaging reveals that symbionts rapidly assimilate host-derived catabolic nutrients to support their own growth, coinciding with a reduction in chloroplast volume and a collapse of carbon translocation to the host. Furthermore, we find that symbionts develop large nutrient-storage vacuoles—occupying up to 50% of the cell volume—that are enriched in essential elements and contain immense nitrogen reserves in the form of crystalline guanine. These guanine stores facilitate rapid symbiont proliferation following bleaching, thereby promoting recovery of the symbiosis. Taken together, our results identify algal nutrient storage as a critical mechanism of post-bleaching resilience in cnidarian–algal partnerships.
fac5be1e:
The oxidative stress hypothesis is currently considered the leading explanation of coral bleaching, the most significant ecological threat to coral reefs today. Reactive nitrogen species (RNS), particularly nitric oxide (NO), have been implicated in this stress response cascade. Notably, NO can react with superoxide to form peroxynitrite (ONOO-), a highly reactive oxidant that bridges nitrosative and oxidative stress pathways. Unlike many ROS that are neutralized by antioxidant defenses, ONOO- inflicts direct oxidative and nitrative damage to proteins, lipids and organelles, linking environmental stress to irreversible cellular injury.
In this study, we measured intracellular ONOO- in live Pocillopora acuta cell cultures, providing real-time insight into the dynamics of the coral-symbiont relationship under thermal stress. Peroxynitrite production was measured relative to total reactive oxygen species (ROS) within individual coral cells through a dual-labeling fluorescence approach. Probes specific to intracellular ROS and RNS were optimized for use in the live cell culture, and spectral unmixing via confocal microscopy was employed to disentangle dye-specific fluorescence from the endogenous fluorescence This allowed us to test the hypothesis that overall ROS accumulation is not the primary driver of bleaching or symbiont loss, but that specific, highly cytotoxic RNS-derived oxidants such as ONOO- play a more direct mechanistic role in initiating cellular damage and disrupting symbiosis.
d7da2981:
Autophagy is a primary pathway contributing to coral bleaching and can be induced by various stressors, notably heat stress. This highly conserved process of recycling cellular components occurs across eukaryotes and is regulated by the mechanistic target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway. In cnidarians, mTOR functions as the central nutrient-sensing pathway, detecting amino acid and glucose levels in host cells to regulate autophagy and cell growth. Myo-inositol is a precursor to the signaling lipid PI(3,5)P₂, which in model eukaryotes recruits mTORC1 and its downstream substrates to lysosomes - an essential step for mTOR activation. Recent evidence shows marked shifts in myo-inositol abundance in coral host cells during heat stress, alongside declines in other nutrient cycling processes implicated in bleaching. These patterns suggest symbiont-derived myo-inositol could be essential for host PI(3,5)P₂ synthesis, mTOR activation at the symbiosome, and overall holobiont stability. 

Here, we test whether PI(3,5)P₂ mediates recruitment and activation of mTOR at the symbiosome, and whether symbiont-supplied myo-inositol is used to synthesize host PI(3,5)P₂. Custom-made antibodies are used to measure the shift in localization of mTOR from the symbiosome in the symbiotic Exaiptasia diaphana following pharmacological inhibition of PI(3,5)P₂ synthesis. The results reveal the importance of PI(3,5)P₂ in the recruitment and activation of mTOR. Moreover, blocking myo-inositol translocation from symbiont to host with lithium chloride and measuring host PI(3,5)P₂ abundance explores the host dependence on symbiont-supplied myo-inositol for PI(3,5)P₂-mediated mTOR activation. From this approach, our results provide a model in which heat-induced reductions in symbiont myo-inositol transfer may limit host PI(3,5)P₂ production, prevent mTOR recruitment to the symbiosome, and thereby trigger autophagy. This work highlights a nutrient-signaling mechanism underpinning symbiosis stability and helps explain how its disruption contributes to bleaching under thermal stress.
eed32e79: To help understand how the two main drivers of coral decline, bleaching and disease, affect coral and their algal endosymbionts, we collected 15 colonies of Orbicella franksi of various health states following a White Plague outbreak in St. John’s, USVI. Health states included: apparently healthy with no signs of active lesions (HH), diseased colonies with active White Plague lesions (DD), the apparently healthy tissue from a diseased colony (HD), and slightly bleached but otherwise healthy colonies (H-BL). In order to investigate the underling mechanisms of these phenotypes, we looked at host and symbiont gene expression and histopathology including symbiont size, vacuole size, and tissue degradation. Host gene expression shows DD corals are mounting the strongest response and have the most differentially expressed genes . Furthermore, many genes associated with immune and inflammatory responses, apoptosis, and extracellular matrix were identified in DD corals. H-BL corals upregulated known calcification-related mechanisms such as calcium-ion binding receptors and carbonic anhydrases as well as immune mechanisms related to apoptosis and inflammatory response like those seen in the DD corals. Overlapping immune related genes and processes between these two stressors will be identified and further explored to identify the most important responses to both bleaching and disease in the host. On the symbiont level, HH colonies were exclusively dominated by Durusdinium whereas H-BL and diseased (HD and DD) colonies were largely Symbiodinium dominated. Symbiont gene expression will determine if dominant symbiont is driving a unique response in the host or within itself. Histopathology measurements will be correlated to host and symbiont gene expression to contextualize the genotypic response to each stressor. By integrating cellular and molecular metrics we hope to find health state specific responses to aid in our understanding of how the coral immune system responds to these environmental stressors.
0a0cd65d: The ecological success of coral reefs is underpinned by their association with symbiotic dinoflagellates, which provide their hosts with photosynthate in return for a supply of inorganic nutrients. The breakdown of this relationship in response to environmental changes has led to widespread bleaching events. These events may be further exacerbated by the increase in stratification predicted to accompany sea surface warming, leading to nutrient limitation for future reefs. While early evidence has suggested that corals can digest their symbionts to obtain nutrients, the process has not been well-characterized. In this study, we endeavored to observe and quantify markers of symbiont digestion during starvation in the model anemone Aiptasia. Following eight weeks of feeding or starvation, anemones and their symbionts were sampled for physiological measurements and proteomics. Starvation decreased algal populations and host protein content, indicating a state of nutrient deprivation. The proteome, which is pending but will be presented, is predicted to reveal proteins related to symbiont digestion, including those involved in immunity, phagosomal maturation, and autophagy. In addition, we are currently using confocal microscopy to immunolocalize markers for the lysosome and late endosome. This will allow us to confirm whether symbiont digestion is occurring and, along with the results of the proteome, distinguish between potential digestive mechanisms. This research will provide insight into a strategy that corals may use to survive periods of nutrient limitation during future warming events and shed light on an understudied, potentially additional mechanism of bleaching.
e0eb5999: Coral sexual propagation carries great potential for large-scale coral reef rehabilitation, but feasibility rests on overcoming key bottlenecks in efficiency, infrastructure, and deployment. We developed an integrated framework to streamline production and outplanting using Coralassist plugs (CAPs); these are coral settlement devices with microrefugia that have demonstrated enhanced juvenile coral survivorship on the reef. A modular framework of PVC trays (50 x 30 x 2 cm) with threaded rods was designed for use in all stages of the production workflow. Each tray holds 500 CAPs and can be used sequentially for conditioning, settlement, grow-out in rearing tanks (10,000 CAPs per tank), transportation in coolers, and in trays for reef deployment, whilst minimizing handling and improving workflow continuity. The modular rods enable rapid microscope assessment for coral recruits and can be mounted on nursery trees for in-situ rearing. To increase outplant efficiency, we engineered two modified Coralclip® designs for attaching CAPs to the reef: a single-bend design and a looped design to prevent CAPs from spinning. The performance of both Coralclip® devices was compared to commonly used attachment methods: cement, epoxy, adhesive, nails, nylon push mounts, and self-drilling screws. The looped Coralclip® was preferred by divers for ease of use, with the fastest attachment method (35  2 seconds per CAP), and achieved the highest overall yield (77%) with 92% retention and 80% survivorship after four months on the reef. These results demonstrate that rapid attachment methods can also deliver high yields. As outplanting juvenile corals remains the most resource-intensive step in coral sexual propagation, improvements in logistical and deployment efficiency directly translate to reduced costs and increased rehabilitation output. Our modular framework and optimized attachment method demonstrate a scalable, field-tested solution for applying coral sexual propagation to reef rehabilitation at meaningful scales.
7a9f1cee: KCRI (KAUST Coral Restoration Initiative) was launched in 2021 in NEOM (Northern Red Sea) as one of the world’s largest and most innovative restoration program in support of Saudi Vision 2030, providing global demonstration in delivering industrial-scale restoration. Central to production of millions of corals over the next 5 years has been construction of the world’s largest land-based coral nursery, comprising 400 x 3000L tanks over a 28,000m2 footprint, completed in 2026. Preparing to operate this facility and propagate coral at unprecedented scale was the critical activation of a neighbouring pilot facility ( “Development Nursery”) since March 2024 – a ‘sister facility’ one tenth in scale designed to provide the fast fail learnings in local coral propagation, life support and building operability at scale; including, tackling unknowns in propagating and moving large coral volumes in a remote location. Solutions have included major technological developments in (a) hardware, software and digital infrastructure to monitor, maintain and manage propagation at scale, (b) end-to-end engineering (from production to planting) for both asexual and sexually derived material, (c) design and operation considerations that optimize tank maintenance and operation and role of the “human in the loop”. We will showcase the capacity and operation of the first few months of the new facility, the technological steps – both in place and planned, as well as operationalization of the lessons learned through fast fail. We will demonstrate how successful establishment of this unique platform for global restoration efforts has rested on critical multi-sector partnerships, including construction and engineering, coral propagation, aquaculture life support, and marine operations. We discuss the logistical, governance and technological challenges in building and commissioning the facility, and the strategy to ensure relevant approaches and technologies are widely accessible and transferrable to coral propagations efforts – of all scales – globally.
18cca5f5: Scaling reef restoration to achieve ecosystem-level impacts requires transformative innovation in coral production workflows. This study synthesizes five years of research from the Coral Aquaculture and Deployment (CAD) subprogram of the Reef Restoration and Adaptation Program (RRAP), which focused on optimizing ex situ sexual coral production methodologies for large-scale deployment on the Great Barrier Reef. Here, we evaluated technological and process innovations across the coral production pipeline—from gamete capture and larval rearing to symbiont inoculation, settlement, and device assembly. Key advancements include the automation of fertilisation using an AutoSpawner system, high-density larval culture with computer vision monitoring, optimized conditioning and preservation of settlement substrata, novel bioreactor systems for symbiont culturing, and development of biofouling- and predation-resistant seeding devices suitable for diver-free deployment. Comparative analyses revealed reductions in labour of between 1.4 and 28.6-fold across key processes, resulting in up to as much as a 20.4-fold increase in production capacity per unit area in the facility, and a 4.3-fold reduction in seawater use, while preserving biological performance and genetic diversity. These outcomes demonstrate the feasibility of producing millions of coral spat annually within a single facility. Together they establish a practical and scalable pathway toward ecosystem-scale coral reef restoration under accelerating climate change.
bf705221:
Effective large-scale coral restoration will require a clear understanding of the true cost of producing healthy fragments, yet major data gaps remain regarding how diet influences both coral performance and production expenses. This study will evaluate the total cost of ex situ propagation for three ecologically important stony coral species in the Florida Reef Tract; Montastraea cavernosa, Orbicella faveolata, and Porites astreoides. A cost–benefit analysis will be conducted across multiple dietary regimes, quantifying labor, feed and supplement costs, any necessary medical interventions, and artificial seawater inputs. These factors will be related to monthly fragment growth, survivorship, and production efficiency (expressed as $/cm² tissue area and $/fragment). To assess physiological responses to diet, photosynthetic efficiency will be measured monthly, and stable isotope signatures (δ¹³C, δ¹⁵N) will be collected from source colonies at the start of the study as well as from a subset of fragments after one year in a recirculating aquaculture system. These comparisons will provide insight into in situ versus ex situ nutritional sources for each species.
The resulting dataset will inform optimized diet recommendations for cost-effective coral aquaculture and will provide foundational isotope data for a companion field study that will examine how coral nutrition influences susceptibility to parrotfish predation following outplanting. Together, this work will advance both the economic and ecological understanding needed to improve coral restoration outcomes and aquaculture cost projections.
ef54d2c6: Accelerating coral reef restoration at ecologically meaningful scales remains a global challenge. Propagule-based approaches scale well in plant systems, but larval-based coral restoration has been difficult, particularly for delivering competent larvae to reefs. Across a series of trials, we develop and test techniques to collect, culture, and deploy coral larvae at ecologically relevant scales. Demographic modelling indicates that harvesting wild coral spawn slicks, culturing them to release competent larvae offers a low-impact pathway to produce and translocate genetically diverse larvae over large areas. Collection scales to >30 million embryos per pass using active pumping onto a small barge, and hundreds of millions via repeated passes with hand-held skimmers. Culturing in pools yields hundreds of millions of competent larvae. To deploy larvae across large spatial footprints, we developed the larval seedbox: a modular, passive delivery system that enables delayed release of competent larvae near the benthos, increasing substrate encounter rates without net enclosures that previously limited seeding to small areas. An initial trial at a clear-water mid-shelf reef delivered ~14 million larvae using five seedboxes across ~2 ha. Settlement occurred on 85% of tiles with densities 9–56x above background, with spatial modelling estimating enhancement over ~3,000 m² via local retention and tidally driven dispersal. A second trial at a turbid inshore reef delivered ~6 million larvae using ten seedboxes across ~1.6 ha of reef.
