Preliminary analysis shows settlement was low throughout the bay (26% of tiles with 1 or more spat), hotspots of settlement remained high (>100x above background) but were largely restricted to tiles adjacent to seedboxes. Knowledge gaps remain on optimal seedboxe placement depending on reef topography, fine-scale currents, and benthic predators or competitors. Seedboxes are simple, modular, and readily transferable to operators, offering a practical tool for scaling coral larval restoration over large, degraded areas.
5c5f0e5a: Larval-based coral seeding techniques have huge potential for upscaling the restoration of genotypically diverse coral populations using industrial approaches. Such industrial approaches include coral aquaculture systems that create controlled conditions for maximising fertilisation success and preventing gamete wastage, while improving early survival of coral larvae and newly settled corals for use in seeding. Yet, industrial technologies often involve complex infrastructure. ReefSeed is a stand-alone, transportable aquaculture system that can be packed into two shipping containers to support the rapid production and deployment of newly settled corals onto seeding devices. ReefSeed makes technologically advanced coral seeding practices accessible to small island developing states and other remote reefs with limited infrastructure, enabling locally led reef recovery where it is needed most. Here we present results from the first system trials in Australia in late 2024 and the Maldives in early 2025 and 2026, reporting on system capabilities for spawning, larval rearing and settlement. For example, in the Maldives in April 2025, a total of 24 broodstock colonies from 3 hard coral species produced ~2.9 million gametes over an 11-day period, which led to an eventual deployment of 720 devices in North and South Male Atoll. However, previous trials in March 2025 yielded no viable gametes as coral fecundity assessments revealed that most corals were reproductively immature. These trials have highlighted the challenges of applying large-scale coral seeding projects in regions with limited knowledge of coral reproductive timing. ReefSeed has been operated independently by trained but new users of the system, despite these challenges. Overcoming these constraints through shared expertise, science-based decision-making, and strong partnerships will be essential to realising the pathway toward the industrial production of corals for >ha-scale restoration.
582ef1bb:
For effective climate-resilient reef restoration at industrial scales demands technological innovation and a trained, well-supported workforce capable of deploying these tools in diverse and remote environments. Yet across the Pacific, community-led restoration initiatives face persistent constraints that limit their ability to adopt and implement emerging techniques for mass propagation, broodstock management, and standardized monitoring. Through a rapid assessment in 2023 of more than 75 organizations, including NGOs, dive centers, hotels, and emerging grassroots groups, this study documents four gaps hindering restoration: (1) a shortage of trained personnel, with most projects lacking dedicated coral restoration technicians, field managers, or marine scientists; (2) unstable and insufficient funding, often limited to short-term grants or tourism-dependent support; (3) limited diagnostic capacity to identify drivers of local reef decline, constraining appropriate intervention design; and (4) inconsistent monitoring systems, with few programs applying long-term, third-party scientific evaluation.
These gaps collectively reveal that the Pacific restoration sector is not yet equipped to engage fully with industrialized propagation technologies or large-scale outplanting methods. A few emerging models aim to fill this capacity bottleneck by providing targeted technical training, diagnostic reef assessments, and standardized monitoring frameworks tailored for community practitioners. By partnering with regional scientific networks (IUCN, SPREP, SPC, AIMS, CRC, TNC, WCS, WWF, RARE) and drawing insights from global hubs identified through ICRI and international restoration literature, we identify strategies for strengthening the human resource base necessary for large-scale coral production systems.
Our findings emphasize that workforce development paired with diagnostic accuracy and consistent data collection is an essential enabling condition for the adoption of high-throughput coral propagation technologies, containerized mobile systems, and genetic management approaches. Addressing these foundational gaps will accelerate technology transfer, reduce cost-per-unit effort, and better position Pacific communities to participate in and benefit from the next generation of mass restoration technologies.
e752995b: Benthic marine cyanobacteria are becoming increasingly abundant in tropical and subtropical habitats worldwide, and many species produce diverse, sometimes toxic, natural products. Blooms of cyanobacteria occur regularly throughout Florida and on coral reefs worldwide. Over the past 20 years, we have made progress in characterizing the species composition and natural products chemistry of benthic cyanobacteria on coral reefs. Crude extracts and pure compounds from some cyanobacteria deter feeding by generalist herbivores such as fishes and sea urchins. Cyanobacterial metabolites may serve multiple ecological functions, including feeding deterrence, inhibition of microorganisms and competition for space. The role of allelopathy (chemical inhibition) in mediating interactions between cyanobacteria and different life history stages of corals has been tested. Extracts and isolated compounds from multiple cyanobacteria negatively influence the settlement of coral larvae. On reefs experiencing increased abundance of chemically rich benthic cyanobacteria, the restocking of adult coral populations may be slowed due to recruitment inhibition caused by cyanobacterial metabolites. One species of interest in coral disease ecology is Roseofilum reptotaenium, a filamentous marine cyanobacterium that is a major component of Black Band Disease (BBD), a lethal disease that occurs on many species of corals worldwide. BBD is abundant on Florida’s Coral Reef and throughout the Caribbean, especially in summer months. To decipher chemical signaling among microorganisms in BBD, we investigated the natural products chemistry of BBD and nonaxenic cultures of R. reptotaenium and identified lyngbic acid, which inhibits quorum sensing (QS) in Vibrio harveyi QS reporters, and macrocyclic depsipeptides, which we named looekeyolides. Lookeyolides are not toxic, but may protect the cyanobacteria from reactive oxygen species, which are coral’s frontline defense against pathogens.
aa91a062:
Benthic cyanobacterial mats (BCMs) are expanding across Caribbean reefs as coral mortality from bleaching and SCTLD creates extensive bare substrate rapidly colonised by these groups. These mats are highly active microbial assemblages that can strongly influence reef biogeochemistry through nitrogen fixation, organic matter production, and the release of bioactive secondary metabolites. Yet, their ecological roles and potential risks remain poorly quantified.
We surveyed 28 sites along the leeward coast of Curaçao (10 m depth) to assess BCM distribution, biomass, nitrogen fixation, and toxicity. Average BCM cover was 10%, dominated by red mats. Biomass analyses of the most abundant mat types enabled calculation of ash-free dry weight per m² of reef. Nitrogen fixation rates were quantified using the acetylene-reduction assay (per unit dry weight) and extrapolated to reef-scale estimates. Culturing of dominant cyanobacterial strains revealed substantial variability in N-fixation potential, identifying species most relevant to the reef nitrogen budget.
Most BCM morphotypes exhibited toxic potential in Thamnocephalus platyurus bioassays, with LC₅₀ values indicating the presence of bioactive or toxic secondary metabolites. Untargeted metabolomic analyses (LC-MS/MS) confirmed diverse chemical profiles and candidate compounds likely contributing to toxicity.
Together, these results show that BCMs on Curaçao’s reefs are abundant, metabolically active, and often toxic. Their capacity for substantial nitrogen input, combined with secondary metabolite production, suggests that BCMs already play a significant role in shaping reef biogeochemistry and may influence benthic community dynamics. Understanding the ecological functions and impacts of BCMs is essential for predicting future reef trajectories in an era of coral decline.
748a1ca1: The world’s oceans are being fundamentally changed by multiple human impacts, and tropical coral reefs are among the most impacted ecosystems. Widespread reports have documented coral reef shifts characterised by losses of scleractinian corals and emerging dominance of alternate groups such as macroalgae and sponges. Benthic cyanobacterial mats (BCMs) have until now received very little research attention, but are becoming increasingly common around tropical coastlines. In Fiji, blooms can occur annually on nearshore coral reefs during warm months, but in recent years some BCMs have begun to prevail through the cold season as well. Little is known about how these BCMs influence underlying reef processes and functions, and many fundamental knowledge gaps remain. Our research aimed to better understand the drivers and dynamics of BCMs, and their impact on coral reef communities in Fiji, a Pacific Island country that has a high dependence on reef-associated ecosystem services. Research objectives included (i) to compile data from reefs across Fiji to generate baseline data on BCM spatial distribution and to determine how they relate to environmental features, (ii) to conduct fine-scale surveys into BCMs and environmental conditions at a set of inshore reefs to generate data on temporal dynamics of BCMs, and (iii) understand the impact of BCMs on reef-associated organisms. Our findings reveal that BCMs are ubiquitous across many of Fiji’s reefs, even at surprisingly remote reefs distant from land. Their abundance seems to be connected to increased temperatures and rainfall, with highest levels found in surveys between January and March. Additionally, our findings have highlighted the negative impact BCMs have on fish herbivory and their ability to rapidly cause bleaching and mortality as they overgrow hard coral tissue. A key challenge in reef monitoring is our ability to identify BCMs, often leading to significant underestimations of their abundance.
96f126b1:
Cyanobacteria are being increasingly recognized as an emerging threat to coral reef functioning, yet their ecological impacts remain understudied compared to more widely discussed stressors such as overfishing, nutrient pollution, and macroalgal dominance. While benthic cyanobacterial mats (BCMs) contribute to nitrogen fixation and primary production, their growing abundance—driven in part by ocean warming and local disturbances—has been implicated in the disruption of key ecological processes that underpin coral reef resilience. Of particular concern is their influence on herbivorous reef fishes, whose grazing and scraping activities maintain reef surfaces in a favorable condition for coral recruitment.

This study quantified interactions between BCMs and two important herbivorous functional groups: browsing fishes and territorial Stegastes damselfishes. Feeding assays were conducted to compare bite rates of browsing herbivores on brown macroalgae with and without overgrowing BCMs across three different Fijian reef systems (Namada Village, Leleuvia Island, and Barefoot Manta Resort) selected to represent a gradient of environmental conditions and human pressures. Video assays were conducted at Vatuolalai Village to examine whether Stegastes spp. actively remove cyanobacteria placed on corals within their territories. Browsers exhibited an 84% reduction in bite rates when macroalgal substrates were overgrown by BCMs, demonstrating a strong deterrent effect with potential implications for the efficiency of herbivory on BCM-dominated reefs. In contrast, Stegastes damselfish removed BCMs almost immediately, suggesting a possible localized mechanism for mat suppression on corals within defended territories.

These results underscore the growing need to consider BCMs in the management of coral reefs given these contrasting top-down controls on algal growth. As BCMs continue to expand across tropical reefs, understanding their deterrent properties, ecological toxicity,

and interactions with key herbivores will be essential for developing climate-smart, adaptive reef management approaches. Future research should prioritize the chemical ecology of BCMs and their influence on multiple herbivore guilds.
f1ad708c:
Ciguatera poisoning (CP) is a serious seafood-borne illness caused by ciguatoxins (CTXs) produced by benthic dinoflagellates of the genera Gambierdiscus and Fukuyoa. The South Pacific accounts for more than 80% of global CP cases, and climate-driven range expansion is expected to further increase risk. However, only a small number of Gambierdiscus species have been directly linked to CTX production. Here, we investigated the toxic potential, phylogenetic position, and environmental occurrence of Gambierdiscus holmesii from the Great Barrier Reef (Queensland, Australia).
Using LC–MS/MS toxin profiling, G. holmesii was confirmed to produce M-seco-CTX4A/B, making it only the second Gambierdiscus species with chemically confirmed CTX production in the Pacific after G. polynesiensis. In addition, G. holmesii produced an uncharacterised compound previously detected only in G. polynesiensis, although its bioactivity remains unknown. Phylogenomic analyses placed G. holmesii within Clade III, closely related to G. polynesiensis and the newly described G. bagnisii sp. nov.
To enable targeted environmental surveillance of this CTX-producing species, we developed a species-specific quantitative PCR (qPCR) assay targeting the ITS region of G. holmesii. The assay was calibrated using synthetic gBlock standards and cell-based standard curves, and demonstrated high specificity, efficiency, and sensitivity, with no cross-reactivity against closely related taxa. Application of the assay to environmental samples from Heron Island detected G. holmesii at 5 out of 12 reef sites.
Together, these results firmly establish G. holmesii as a CTX-producing species in Australian waters and provide a robust molecular tool to support early detection, improved monitoring, and management of CP risk to public health and fisheries.
0ee7658f: Ciguatera Poisoning (CP) caused by ciguatoxins (CTXs) from marine microbial eukaryotes (Gambierdiscus spp., Dinophyta, Alveolata) has significant worldwide economic and social impacts and is directly impacted by climate change. However, significant knowledge gaps remain in understanding the link between Gambierdiscus community ecology and CTX concentrations in fishes within a local area, inhibiting prevention strategies.  To examine site-specific CTX risk factors, we coupled a tailored high-throughput sequencing metabarcoding approach and quantitative PCR, with CTX determination in novel Gambierdiscus strains, in an omnivorous fish (Maito, Ctenochaetus striatus) with a limited home range as well as fishes with wider distributions, in Rarotonga (Cook Islands), which has amongst the world’s highest incidence rate of CP. Of the novel Gambierdiscus strains, only G. polynesiensis isolates produced described Pacific CTXs (CTX3B, -3C, -4A, -4B, iso-CTX3B/C, -4A/B). While dinoflagellate assemblages were heavily dominated by Gambierdiscus spp., only sites with higher abundances of G. polynesiensis closely reflected the CTX3B levels in C. striatus, unrelated to fish age or size. Artificial and macroalgal substrate sampling gave similar assemblage alpha diversity of dinoflagellates, which was significantly lower at a site near a major tourist hub, with relatively lower water quality. Using neuroblastoma cell-based assays and liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry, CTXs were detected in samples from nine carnivorous and herbivorous fish taxa, with levels varying among species. Based on these results, site-specific information on sentinel fish species and regular quantification of targeted Gambierdiscus taxa using molecular approaches could play an important role in CP prevention. We are ultimately working towards fit-for-purpose monitoring techniques that are rapid, cheap, and easy to use for the communities that need them.
28536ec3:
Located in the heart of the South West Pacific, New Caledonia hosts the world’s largest lagoon, a coral reef ecosystem increasingly exposed to anthropogenic pressures and climate change. The rise in sea surface temperature, the increasing frequency of marine heatwaves, widespread coral bleaching, and the proliferation of dead substrates may promote the colonization of macroalgae, which in turn provide a favorable habitat for benthic and potentially toxic microalgae. Among these, the genus Gambierdiscus is known to produce ciguatoxins responsible for ciguatera. This seafood poisoning is the most widespread non-infectious disease in the tropics. 
In the context of climate change, Gambierdiscus may alter their dynamics, distribution, and toxicity, representing a growing threat to human health, marine ecosystems, and the local community. This study aims to better understand how thermal variations, whether acute or gradual, influence the genetic diversity, chemodiversity, and toxin production of Gambierdiscus in New Caledonia.
To this end, monthly in situ sampling across several sites in the lagoon were carried out using artificial substrates (window screen (WS)) and passive samplers (solid phase adsorption toxin tracking (SPATT)) to collect benthic dinoflagellates and their dissolved toxins, respectively. Artificial substrates coupled with molecular analyses (qPCR, eDNA) allow us to assess genetic diversity and dynamics, and high-performance chemical analyses (LC-HRMS/MS) enable us to identify and quantify toxins, including potential biomarkers. 
Expected outcomes include a better understanding of ciguatera dynamics in the lagoon across seasons and during marine heat wawes, the establishment of a reference culture collection of benthic microalgae from New Caledonia, and explore the ability to predict risk areas for coral reefs in New Caledonia. This study aligns with the theme “Harmful cyanobacteria and microalgae on coral reefs: Drivers, dynamics, and ecological impacts” theme of the Symposium, by proposing a multidisciplinary approach combining ecology, molecular biology, and analytical chemistry.
3c330485: Modern Scleractinian corals have engineered complex reef frameworks for 240 million years, at times sheltering a quarter of all marine species.  Anthropogenic climate change, however, has breached critical thermal and chemical thresholds, destabilising the fragile symbioses between coral hosts and endosymbiotic dinoflagellates. As global sea surface temperatures have reached 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels, marine heatwaves, ocean acidification, intensifying storms, and declining calcification rates have increasingly jeopardised these vital ecosystems.  Current interventions, including assisted evolution, large-scale restoration, and dynamic management, falter when scaled beyond localised trials. Furthermore, Earth system modelling warns that passing critical emission boundaries risks triggering large-scale tipping points, such as the collapse of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC). Such a collapse would radically alter poleward heat transport and exacerbate destructive thermal extremes across coasts and oceans.  Forty years of limited conservation success demand an urgent, two-pronged strategy: (1) Accelerate decarbonization and rapidly transition to a 100 % global carbon-free renewable infrastructure by 2030, and (2) Protect areas that have the best chances of success in climate change, while preserving and protecting the genetic reservoirs necessary to reseed ecosystems once the climate has stabilised. Failure to mitigate risks of ecological collapse will expose at least 500 million people to acute food insecurity, severe coastal hazards, and lost livelihoods. While dismantling reliance on fossil fuels poses formidable socioeconomic hurdles, international climate governance must accelerate efforts to rebuild the atmosphere by rapidly transitioning to renewables and transforming land-use. Global frameworks must build and enforce science-based policies to address the imminent threat to the biosphere.
7919cdeb: Despite sustained advances in science and the growing global investment in coral restoration, future reefs are expected to look markedly different. Many projections anticipate major declines in zooxanthellate hard corals, with some predicting functional extinction under worst-case scenarios, resulting in reefs progressively degrading, eroding, and losing ecological function. This session at ICRS focuses on the emerging field of reef regeneration - a complementary suite of approaches aimed at sustaining biodiversity, ecological processes, and the delivery of critical ecosystem services such as coastal protection, fisheries productivity, and tourism. As the session description notes, regenerative approaches draw on a broader range of climate-resilient taxa, ecological functions, and intervention strategies expected to persist under future conditions. This includes work on assisted evolution, assisted reproduction, selective propagation, and managed migration of corals and other key reef organisms. The regenerative approach also focuses on improving enabling conditions for reef health, and on applying multiple regenerative tools in combination, including targeted removal of coral predators or seaweed competitors. Regenerative approaches can further complement the hard engineering required to safeguard low-lying islands, adding ecological function and biological complexity to these structures. Here we present the results of a meta-analysis of the global state of research and development in reef regenerative approaches, and introduce the Coral Reef Regeneration Co-Lab project, supported by Earthkeeper. The purpose of the Co-Lab is to build a global community of practice to accelerate, coordinate, and mainstream regenerative methodologies alongside existing coral restoration efforts. Achieving meaningful scale will require not only scientific and technical innovation but also the development of new financial mechanisms capable of mobilising resources at levels commensurate with the challenge. If widely adopted, reef regeneration has the potential to support, extend, and enhance the success of coral restoration, producing better outcomes for future reefs and the people who depend on them.
2b5c4cea: Coral probiotics can enhance coral thermal tolerance, improve coral health, and restructure holobiont microbiomes in laboratory and field trials. However, their broader ecological implications - particularly whether benefits extend beyond the coral host and help mitigating community-level impacts during extreme warming - remain unexplored. Here, we provide the first long-term in situ evaluation of how the application of probiotics on corals influences adjacent reef biodiversity, microbial networks, and functioning before and after a record marine heatwave. We applied two native probiotic consortia to Pocillopora favosa and Acropora sp. colonies three times per week for 18 months across 12 Red Sea reef patches (n=4 per treatment,  control). To assess off-target effects, we used replicated, customized, biomimetic Autonomous Reef Monitoring Structures to quantify changes in cryptobenthic and microbial communities through visual surveys of mobile and sessile invertebrates, molecular profiling (16S/18S rRNA gene, COI), as well as community metabolism and calcification rates. Prior to the 2023 marine heatwave, probiotic-treated reef patches showed similar or enhanced cryptobenthic community structure, invertebrate abundance, and functional performance relative to controls, with no harmful off-target effects. After the heatwave and associated bleaching, probiotic-treated patches retained markedly higher biodiversity and microbial stability. Mobile invertebrate abundance was approximately three times higher than in controls, sessile invertebrate losses were less severe, and microbial co-occurrence networks stayed cohesive rather than fragmenting as in controls. Metabolic assays indicated that, while control communities shifted to net dissolution, probiotic-treated patches maintained positive or rising calcification rates, likely due to retained calcifying algae. These findings show that coral probiotics benefit both treated colonies and nearby microhabitats, helping to stabilize communities and reduce biodiversity loss during extreme thermal stress. They position microbiome-based interventions as promising ecosystem-level tools for reef resilience and underscore the need to identify host–probiotic pairings that can deliver community-wide gains under accelerating climate change.
5f760eaa:
Healthy Reefs for Healthy People leads a collaboration of >70 partners focused on improving reef ecosystem health. Since inception in 2004, we have recognized the fundamental linkages between ecosystem function, structural and social components, and primary drivers of change. Strategies to enhance reef health and regeneration initially focused on increasing MPAs, then the fully protected zones, which reached 9% of coral reef area by 2024. However, this relatively small area, combined with the escalating impacts of climate change, requires additional broader interventions. Our twenty years of reef monitoring have shown that the over-abundance of macroalgae is the most persistent threat to reef health. The fastest route to increasing herbivory was to fully protect parrotfish that had recently become part of the main fishery. The entire Mesoamerican Reef has now fully protected parrotfish, with increasing herbivore biomass and decreasing fleshy macroalgae becoming evident. In 2019 we also initiated a project focused on the largest herbivorous crab, the Caribbean King Crab (Maguimithrax spinosissimus), to develop restorative mariculture and reseeding of their natural populations. The project has developed successful protocols for capturing, breeding, hatching, and cultivating juvenile crabs, along with the Mexican Fisheries Institute aquaculture center.  Approximately 200 crabs have been released onto local reefs, some in tandem with coral outplanting and assessing their ecological impacts.  
Financing these solutions is crucial and requires new models, such as MAR+Invest: a GFCR funded blended finance mechanism that integrates a business acceleration pipeline and capacity-building programs, with a strong M&E component. By leveraging public and private investments, MAR+Invest acts as a catalyst for impactful solutions, including the King Crab research program and business model development. 
Integrating these sustainable provides a holistic and restorative approach to reef restoration, based on interdisciplinary collaboration and innovative thinking to restore the conditions that support biodiversity, natural regeneration and local livelihoods.
3e94deef: Coral reefs host nearly one third of global marine biodiversity, protect coastlines, and support close to half a billion people through fisheries and tourism. They nevertheless illustrate the global decline of biodiversity. In this context, growing efforts in coral restoration aim to help reefs recover from disturbances. However, restoration initiatives remain largely focused on branching coral genera, which are among the most thermosensitive, raising concerns about their long-term effectiveness. In terrestrial ecosystems such as forests, restoring with higher species diversity has been shown to enhance both productivity and resistance to environmental stress, a pattern still poorly understood in coral reefs. This talk will investigate how algal removal and the diversity of transplanted coral assemblages influence the productivity and resistance of restored reefs. Fifteen 6 × 6 m quadrats were established on the outer reef slope of Mo’orea: three untreated controls, three cleared of macroalgae but not transplanted, and nine transplanted quadrats representing three distinct assemblages: (1) a monoculture of Acropora hyacinthus, documented as thermosensitive; (2) a monoculture of Pocillopora spp., the pioneering genus on Moorea; and (3) a polyculture of ten species including Pocillopora spp. and Acropora hyacinthus. Restoration was conducted on a site that had dropped to ~0% coral cover following a severe Acanthaster spp. outbreak in 2023, providing a genuine real-world restoration context. Over six months, polyculture assemblages exhibited markedly higher growth rates than monocultures, supported greater fish diversity, and showed enhanced coral larval recruitment. Moreover, A. hyacinthus displayed fewer signs of predation in multispecific assemblages, highlighting the key role of diversity in supporting the survival of the most sensitive coral genera. Together, these findings demonstrate that coral diversity enhances both ecological functioning and recovery capacity, establishing it as a critical lever for strengthening the resilience of restored reef habitats in an era of intensifying environmental disturbances.
50439114: Southwestern Atlantic reefs are experiencing increased bleaching-associated mortality, prompting the proposal of active coral restoration as a solution. However, this approach faces challenges such as genetic bottlenecks, cost, scale, survival amidst local and global impacts, and long-term efficacy. Thus, we reviewed the major attributes of Southwestern Atlantic reefs and their coral species to assess the feasibility of active coral restoration in this unique biological and oceanographic setting. We also examined existing restoration techniques and their applicability to Southwestern Atlantic coral species and reef sites. Few species meet both suitability and priority criteria for restoration, with only Millepora alcicornis, Mi. braziliensis, and Mi. nitida, and, to a lesser extent, Mussismilia braziliensis emerging as relevant candidates. In this low-diversity region, suitable species are scarce, and identifying suitable restoration sites is challenging due to widespread local impacts and ineffective policy enforcement. We propose that practitioners (i) master species-specific fragmentation, handling, care, and monitoring techniques; (ii) align restoration practices with scientific knowledge; (iii) avoid combining multiple interventionist techniques; (iv) acknowledge potential conflicts of interest in restoration practices; and that (v) government agencies oversee restoration activities, and that transparency reports be generated. Southwestern Atlantic reefs need custom-designed conservation measures, and baseline data is essential for identifying suitable restoration sites. Although few species are viable candidates for restoration, and its effectiveness in restoring degraded reefs in the Southwestern Atlantic is unproven under current climate conditions, following the proposed ethical and ecological guidelines may help sustain threatened species and ecosystem services in degraded areas until climate change policies take effect.
dd97ad89: Corallivory poses a major challenge to coral reef restoration, particularly during the early stages of transplantation when juvenile corals are most vulnerable to predation. Territorial damselfish Stegastes nigricans exhibit aggressive behaviour that can influence surrounding reef communities and potentially deter roaming corallivores. This study compared the influence of both live S. nigricans and ‘scarecrow’ S. nigricans models on coral predation at Port Ghalib in the Egyptian Red Sea. Bite densities on established Pocillopora verrucosa colonies were quantified across three treatments: within live S. nigricans territories, in non-territory areas, and alongside model S. nigricans. Additional experiments measured the survival of transplanted Acropora humilis fragments placed alongside each treatment. We found that live S. nigricans did not provide consistent protection to corals, and often acted as antagonists, increasing coral tissue damage through farm-maintenance behaviours that promoted algal overgrowth. A. humilis fragments were actively removed (55% success rate over 4 weeks) or rapidly overgrown by filamentous algae characteristic of damselfish farms. In contrast, scarecrow models of S. nigricans significantly reduced corallivory on P. verrucosa without the associated damage of territory maintenance, supporting a new strategy of Model-Based Deterrence. Although the deterrent effect plateaued over time, likely due to corallivore habituation and biofouling of the models, these findings represent the first empirical evidence that artificial fish models can reduce coral predation in a natural reef setting. The use of S. nigricans scarecrows offers a novel, low-cost approach to protecting vulnerable corals during the initial stages of restoration, avoiding the damaging effects associated with live territorial damselfish. While their effect was transient, refinements to model design, such as enhanced realism, movement, or multisensory cues, could improve efficacy and longevity. This study demonstrates the potential of behavioural deterrents as practical tools for coral restoration and highlights the value of integrating ecological behaviour into coral reef restoration.
5977a5ae: Despite being among the most critical ecosystems in the world, coral reefs have experienced significant declines due to the increased frequency and intensity of global, regional, and local stressors. Coral reef degradation often drives algal-dominated phase shifts, leading to reduced biodiversity, limited structural complexity, and increased bioerosion compared to coral-dominated systems. Reversing a phase-shift to a stable coral-dominated state is particularly challenging, especially in systems such as Florida’s Coral Reef, where Allee effects are strong and coral recruitment is minimal. However, one approach to mitigating or reversing such a phase shift is to bolster the critical function of reef grazers. Maguimithrax spinosissimus, commonly known as the Caribbean king crab (CKC), is a large invertebrate grazer native to the Western Atlantic, including reefs in the Florida Keys. The species has demonstrated potential to reverse the phase shift on algal-dominated reefs via substantially increasing grazing intensity. Efforts are currently underway to increase the population of M. spinosissimus along Florida’s barrier reef tract by releasing crabs cultured in land-based nurseries; however, little is known about the current genetic connectivity of CKC across the 350km of Florida’s barrier reef. Understanding the population structure of M. spinosissimus can guide broodstock management, including determining whether broodstock from different biogeographic regions can be managed as a single genetic population. Using 2b-RAD sequencing, we aimed to quantify the genetic diversity of M. spinosissimus of wild crabs between four biogeographic regions of Florida’s barrier reef. Preliminary results indicate that within Florida’s barrier reef, M. spinosissimus comprises one putative population with high genetic connectivity among the lower, middle, and upper Keys and Southeast Florida. These results suggest that broodstock from different biogeographic regions can be interbred and their progeny released across a broad geographic range to increase the genetic diversity of the species on Florida’s Coral Reefs.
7188e33d:
Coral reef interventions are evolving rapidly in response to accelerating ecological decline, with approaches such as assisted gene flow, thermal-tolerant symbiont inoculation, shading, hybridization, and microbiome manipulation moving toward field deployment. Yet intervention science is advancing faster than the governance systems needed to evaluate, authorize, and steward their responsible use. Drawing on a synthesis of the 2024 Reef Futures interdisciplinary workshop (80+ participants), the Coral Restoration Consortium (CRC) policy brief, and a cross-case review of emerging interventions in the Caribbean and beyond, this study examines how accelerating stress conditions reshape assessments of readiness, risk, and appropriateness.
We present an updated intervention readiness–risk framework that integrates three dimensions: (1) expected ecological effect under worsening thermal regimes, (2) context-dependent risk profiles, including ecological, genetic, and socio-political risks, and (3) governance requirements for responsible deployment. Using expert-derived ratings and qualitative uncertainty assessments, we identify mismatch patterns in which interventions with high potential utility under climate stress face severe governance barriers, while lower-risk interventions often lack the enabling conditions for scaling.
Our findings show that:
• ecological urgency compresses timelines for evaluation, heightening the significance of institutional readiness;
• perceived risks vary substantially across regions due to differences in regulatory capacity, community trust, and histories of intervention;
• community-led stewardship and transparent decision-making processes increase intervention acceptability across diverse stakeholder groups.
We conclude by identifying critical governance gaps—including permitting complexity, coordination challenges across agencies, and limited policy frameworks for interventions beyond traditional restoration. Addressing these gaps is essential for ensuring that emerging interventions can be scaled responsibly and equitably as reefs enter a period of heightened instability.
dbca165b:
Human rights matter for marine conservation because people and nature are inextricably linked. A thriving planet cannot be one that contains widespread human suffering or stifles human potential; and a thriving humanity cannot exist on a dying planet. While coral reef conservation increasingly considers human well-being, it retains a legacy in some places of protectionism, colonialism, and fortress conservation. In this presentation, we examine how a human rights-based approach (HRBA) can transform coral reef governance to achieve social justice and ecological resilience.

We focus on four dimensions: (i) how human rights principles—including participation, non-discrimination, accountability, and access to justice—apply to coral reef management; (ii) examples where conservation goals have conflicted with the rights of small-scale fishers, Indigenous peoples, and coastal communities; (iii) legal and ethical obligations of states and conservation actors to respect, protect, and fulfil these rights; and (iv) practical strategies for integrating HRBA into reef policy and practice. Evidence from reef regions across the Pacific, Caribbean, and Indian Ocean shows that when communities are excluded, conservation measures can erode livelihoods, food security, and cultural identity. Conversely, when rights and participation are embedded, reefs and communities both thrive.

However, the global context for applying HRBA is shifting. As rules-based international order faces erosion and key states question human rights and environmental conventions, the challenge becomes how to advance equitable and sustainable reef governance amid this “polycrisis” as global consensus fragments. 

Integrating human rights into coral reef conservation is not optional—it is essential. Equity must be treated as a rights-based condition, not a charitable gesture. Embedding HRBA principles strengthens trust and enhances ecological outcomes. Recognizing coral reefs as socioecological systems—where biodiversity, culture, and livelihoods are interlinked—creates the foundation for conservation that is inclusive, legitimate, and enduring. Protecting reefs therefore requires protecting the rights of the people who depend on them.
65ede94d:
In French Polynesia, rāhui is an ancestral practice that involves temporarily restricting access to a space or resource. Since the 2000s, this practice has been reappropriated in a hybrid arrangements that combine Indigenous knowledge and principles with public marine management and conservation policies. This institutionalized rāhui model has expanded rapidly across multiples islands, supported by governmental institutions and increasingly highlighted in global conservation arenas post-UN Ocean Conference as a community-led pathway for coral-reef protection.
However, the scaling up of rāhui systems, while allowing for adaptive co-management, reveals tensions that complicate their portrayal as uniformly community-driven. Based on applied anthropological research conducted across several islands, this study examines representativeness - political, symbolic, procedural - as a critical factor shaping the effectiveness and legitimacy of scaled rāhui initiatives. Findings show that social cohesion, local leadership structures, and the mechanisms used to integrate community voices strongly influence whether rāhui governance genuinely reflects plural local interests or, conversely, enables subtle forms of elite capture. In several sites, management committees reaffirm participation in principle yet reproduce unequal power dynamics in practice.
Scaling also reshapes lagoon appropriation. While municipalities claim renewed authority over lagoons through rāhui frameworks, their autonomy is constrained by national regulations defining the sea as public domain. This juxtaposition creates tensions between traditional feelings of ownership of lagoons and reefs within district boundaries, community expectations, and regulatory constraints. Additionally, a paradox arises :  the widespread promotion of “rāhui” enhances its visibility locally and internationally, yet may simultaneously dilute cultural knowledge and weaken traditional foundations.
These dynamics highlight that scaling-up community-led reef conservation is not only an ecological or institutional challenge, but also a social and political one. Enhancing the legitimacy of rāhui-based pathways requires participatory processes that engage with local social structures, strengthen shared leadership, and maintain cultural integrity while navigating multi-level governance.
22927a42:
“They came, took the fish, said nothing to anybody - There were no limits back then.” This “sensing of the system” by a fisher captures the paradox at the heart of Hawaiʻi’s commercial aquarium trade: coral reefs that appear ecologically vibrant while communities experience them as spiritually, culturally, and materially depleted. This study examines the aquarium trade as a multi-scalar extractive system. One that links localized reef impacts with state governance contradictions and global ornamental markets, also highlighting how indigenous stewardship offers a cross-scale pathway for transformation rooted in kuleana, or relational responsibility.  

Drawing on ethnography conducted across community and governance settings, this research integrates participant observation at Board of Land and Natural Resources hearings, Division of Aquatic Resource and community network meetings, analysis of Chapter 91 rulemaking, environmental review documents, legal decisions, public testimony; and interviews with kanaka ʻōiwi fishers and community leaders across Hawaiʻi. These data reveal how regulatory authority is repeatedly fragmented or deferred, enabling extraction while obscuring accountability. Sustainability narratives deployed at state and global scales further mask structural inequities and ecological decline.

At the local scale, lawaiʻa describe declines in culturally significant species, shifting recruitment patterns, and the erosion of intergenerational knowledge and practice. These grounded insights “sense the system” from the peripheries, revealing how extraction reverberates across social, cultural and ecological dimensions. Yet these same local perspectives also illuminate entry points for cross-scale transformation: strengthening Indigenous political authority, revitalizing community stewardship, and reorienting management toward place-based responsibilities.

By analyzing the aquarium trade across local, state and global scales, this paper demonstrates how community testimonies serve as diagnostic tools for understanding broader governance failures. The analysis argues that transformative coral reef conservation requires strengthening Indigenous political authority and restoring kuleana-based stewardship to shift management away from ornamental extraction and toward just and enduring reef futures.
1c52f0d0:
Global agreements under the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) and the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) increasingly emphasize integrated, multi-scalar action that links community-led stewardship with national and global policy commitments. Yet how countries translate these frameworks into national strategies remains poorly understood, even for ecosystems—and communities—highly vulnerable to warming, such as tropical coral reefs. Bleaching-level heat stress has affected over 85% of global reefs and likely impacted at least 97 of 101 reef-holding countries, threatening the social, cultural, and livelihood benefits that reef-dependent communities rely upon.

We assessed how the 25 countries with the largest reef areas (“high-coral countries”) are operationalizing global commitments by analyzing National Biodiversity Strategies and Action Plans (NBSAPs) and Kunming–Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KM-GBF) targets, as well as Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) and National Adaptation Plans (NAPs) under the Paris Agreement. As of August 2025, 71% of high-coral countries had submitted KM-GBF–aligned targets, yet none included measurable commitments for coral reefs. Only one country linked climate threats to quantitative Target 3 commitments, and eight referenced marine ecosystem action under Target 8. Under the Paris Agreement, fewer than half (48%) mentioned coral reefs in NDCs, and only three included measurable reef-related commitments; seven countries referenced reefs in NAPs, none with quantitative targets.

These findings reveal a significant disconnect between global ambition and national implementation, leaving reef managers and local communities without the enabling policy environment needed to scale effective, bottom-up conservation. We propose three priorities to close this gap: (i) co-develop national coral reef strategies that integrate local knowledge and stewardship; (ii) elevate coral reefs within NBSAPs as biodiversity and climate-resilience priorities; and (iii) include measurable reef commitments within future NDCs and NAPs. Strengthening coherence across biodiversity and climate frameworks—and building multi-scalar pathways that amplify community-led stewardship—is essential to securing climate-resilient coral reefs.
b15a38fc:
Eight years after implementing the Indigenous Partnerships Policy at the Australian Institute of Marine Science (AIMS), we reflect on progress made and lessons learned in our mission to strengthen relationships with Traditional Owners of northern Australia’s sea Country, and amplify their knowledge in sea Country science and stewardship. Across our expansive research portfolio, AIMS has implemented an institutional framework to seek Free, Prior and Informed Consent (FPIC) for projects. The AIMS Indigenous Partnerships Policy is underpinned by the United Nations Declaration of Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP) and domestic guidance including the Australian Institute of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Studies (AIATSIS) Code of Ethics for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander research. 

The AIMS approach is unique, and existing frameworks for executing FPIC in marine research are limited and rarely applied elsewhere. Bespoke FPIC procedures are required to account for the diversity of Traditional Owner groups around Australia and differing engagement protocol preferences. We share learnings from navigating FPIC, proposing strategies to overcome common pitfalls. For example, we identified points of tension between scientific and FPIC processes, particularly around timelines, communication styles, and partnership expectations. We also combat logistical challenges associated with managing a large FPIC database while ensuring the privacy and confidentiality of Indigenous partners.

Looking beyond FPIC, we discuss practical tools required to facilitate meaningful co-designed projects and support Indigenous leadership and equity in marine science, including procedures to safeguard Indigenous Cultural and Intellectual Property (ICIP) in ways Intellectual Property law cannot. While we have made significant progress in building meaningful relationships with Traditional Owner groups, we remain committed to continuous learning and improvement. Ultimately, we aim to create spaces that elevate Indigenous Peoples as the first marine scientists, ensuring the best possible outcomes for tropical marine environments including coral reefs and the communities who care for them.
e94b847d:
RRAP Indigenous Futures was established to prepare Great Barrier Reef Traditional Owner groups for the emerging reef restoration and adaptation industry through genuine capacity building grounded in Indigenous pedagogies and repeated hands-on learning in coral larval restoration. The program aimed to demonstrate that Traditional Owner Rangers can deliver deployment-ready larval restoration interventions on their sea Country and establish their place in future RRAP deployments.
Across three years, approximately 30 Indigenous Rangers from eight Indigenous Ranger groups completed practical training in larval pool mobilisation, coral spawn collection, larval culturing, device-based settlement, larval cloud releases, device deployment, site selection for larval restoration, marine monitoring and cultural mapping. The program was co-designed with Indigenous governance groups and the Indigenous project lead to ensure it was built from Indigenous foundations, and was strengthened by additional Indigenous participation including support staff, interns, trainees, researchers, media specialists and respected Elders. All engagement followed cultural protocols and the principles of Free, Prior and Informed Consent, guided by the Australian Institute of Marine Science’s Indigenous Partnerships Policy and Plan.
This presentation reports final-year outcomes, where three-years of capability development culminated in Ranger teams delivering coral slick collection, larval rearing, settlement and deployment with targeted support. Their performance was assessed against published larval restoration benchmarks to evaluate operational readiness. Marine monitoring training focused on foundational methods relevant to coral larval restoration, combining diver-less approaches such as RUVS, ReefScan and ReefCloud annotation workflows with diver-operated benthic surveys to support consistent assessment of early-life coral survival and site condition.
The program also progressed the development of structured training materials that can support broader delivery across First-Nations sea Country. RRAP Indigenous Futures demonstrates a scalable model for preparing First Nations ranger groups to lead and deliver reef restoration and adaptation activities on the Great Barrier Reef and in other Indigenous-managed marine environments.
fddbf910:
The Holomua Marine Initiative, led by the Hawaiʻi Department of Land and Natural Resources Division of Aquatic Resources (DAR), represents a transformational, place-based approach to nearshore marine resource management centered around community leadership, Indigenous knowledge systems, and collaborative governance. Grounded in the recognition that Hawaiʻi’s coral reefs and nearshore ecosystems are inseparable from the cultural identity, livelihoods, and well-being of Native Hawaiian communities, DAR seeks to institutionalize traditional practices and values as foundational components of marine management.
Through a community-led, island-scale planning process, DAR convenes local community members including fishers, cultural practitioners, researchers and practitioners to co-design nearshore management strategies that reflect the unique ecological, cultural, and social dimensions of place. This effort emphasizes equitable participation, blending diverse perspectives and worldviews to inform decision-making. Locally derived science,  research, and Native Hawaiian knowledge of ocean conditions, species behavior, and stewardship practices are intertwined in this process to shape adaptive management.
The Holomua Marine Initiative focuses on addressing community-identified priorities and leverages its position as a state agency to facilitate partnerships among community organizations, funders, and government agencies with local communities to implement and support key management actions. Some of these include prioritized support for community-driven monitoring and data collection, expanded training and capacity-building for local resource stewards, and the co-development of management frameworks that identify culturally grounded indicators of reef condition. These approaches are supported by outreach, compliance, and inter-agency collaboration to ensure that management actions reflect local priorities and maintain long-term accountability.
The Holomua Marine Initiative offers a case study in placing Indigenous authority, community governance, and cultural continuity at the center of nearshore resource management. This transformative approach to weave together local experiences, ecological research, and Native Hawaiian knowledge and values is an effort to develop effective and culturally aligned solutions to sustain the ocean for generations to come.
a41c5dce: Coral reefs support a quarter of known marine biodiversity and provide food security to a majority of Island Nations, while occupying less than 1% of the ocean floor. In the anthropocene we face major environmental challenges, requiring rapid and realistic action to salvage our biome. Human prosperity is often considered when prioritising reef conservation, yet when the people who rely on and live amongst these ecosystems are insufficiently included in the reefs’ future plans, then all foresight is restricted, and thus ineffective. My PhD will aim to visualise the benefits of weaving scientific practice and Melanesian culture to evaluate the temporal fluctuation of a customary fringing reef. To complement geospatial surveys, I will co-develop a pictograph with Vavanga Village to visualise their Traditional Ecological Knowledge (TEK), Local Ecological Knowledge (LEK), and culture. I will use low-cost drone photogrammetry to geospatially map the marine substrata and biota, creating an orthomosaic of the customary marine area. Aerial transects and underwater visual census will be conducted to embed species distribution and abundance into the geospatial map for multivariate analysis. To record the community’s TEK and LEK, semi-structured interviews will be held with key stakeholders. Once expert knowledge is collected, community meetings will be held for all stakeholders to co-develop a story map. The result will depict knowledge of population dynamics through Solomon Island Western Province iconography. Interactive physical and digital models of the reef will share specialist ecological knowledge with the wider community. Geographical Information Systems (GIS) will enable changes in the condition of the reef between surveys to be mapped to help avoid, remedy, or mitigate adverse changes. An interactive website relating the qualitative story map to the quantitative geospatial map will provide complimentary context to both data visualisations and allow Vavanga to continue using the digital story map into the future.
a16d9b9f: Occurring adjacent to the Indo-Malay centre of marine biodiversity, Western Australia’s Kimberley region hosts some of the world’s most diverse and least impacted tropical reefs. These ecosystems have been protected and managed by Aboriginal people since time immemorial as part of their rights and cultural responsibilities for Sea Country. Today, First Nations Australians work with managers and scientists through co-designed, Indigenous-led frameworks, including Indigenous Protected Areas. The Kimberley Reef Connect project, initiated in 2022 and funded by Parks Australia, exemplifies ethical collaboration. It brought together Western Australian Museum and Curtin University scientists with Wunambal Gaambera Uunguu Rangers and Traditional Owners to document the biodiversity, ecological condition, and cultural values of reefs in the Kimberley Marine Park, and to generate new knowledge to support the protection of the ecological and cultural heritage of the Uunguu Wundaagu Indigenous Protected Area and the North Kimberley Marine Parks. A 10-day biodiversity survey of previously unsurveyed reefs collected over 2,500 specimens representing more than 1,000 species. New species, range extensions, critical habitats, refuges for threatened species, unique ecosystems, and invasive species were identified, and new genetic data were generated to support taxonomic research, eDNA monitoring and management. Traditional voices were elevated during a saltwater knowledge-sharing camp that engaged elders, youth, and stakeholders in cultural site mapping and intergenerational knowledge transfer. This was complemented by an Emerging Curator workshop at the Western Australian Museum, where Rangers gained taxonomic and curatorial skills. Project outcomes were showcased in a public exhibit, highlighting biodiversity and cultural values while identifying priorities for future research and management. This “right-way science” approach emphasises the importance of building relationships, practising respectful listening and patience, recognising history and intellectual property rights, and fostering an inclusive, solutions-focused mindset to integrate Indigenous knowledge with Western science to safeguard Australia’s tropical marine heritage at a critical time.
7886fb29: Understanding how biotic interactions vary across space and time is central to predicting how reef ecosystems respond to environmental change. However, standardized and globally comparable datasets remain scarce. The ReefRUV Global Network provides a unified framework to quantify fish behavior and fish–benthos interactions across habitats and biodiversity gradients, enabling broad-scale assessments of ecological processes and anthropogenic change. Remote underwater videos (RUVs) were deployed using a consistent design (2 m² area, 10-min replicates) across coral reefs, subtropical rocky reefs, and tide pools worldwide. From each video, we extracted species richness, abundance, biomass, feeding rates, consumption events, and additional behaviors (e.g., cleaning, agonistic interactions), along with observations of other benthic and mobile organisms. Analyses included latitudinal and longitudinal comparisons, network characterization of agonistic interactions, and functional and phylogenetic evaluations of species co-occurrence. Latitudinal analyses showed that sea surface temperature (SST) strongly predicts feeding-pressure rates, providing a mechanistic basis for forecasting ecological responses to ocean warming. Longitudinal assessments revealed that agonistic networks scale with species richness but maintain a consistent nested structure dominated by highly interactive, site-attached herbivores. Ongoing work on co-occurring species per unit area enables tests of the limiting similarity hypothesis across functional and phylogenetic axes, from species-poor systems to hyperdiverse reefs in the Coral Triangle. This standardized global dataset enables unprecedented comparisons of reef-fish feeding ecology across spatial and temporal scales. Upcoming research will quantify functional and phylogenetic dimensions of feeding-pressure interactions across global richness gradients and revisit sites first surveyed 15 years ago to detect climate-driven ecological change. These efforts contribute to emerging predictive frameworks essential for managing reef ecosystems under rapid environmental shifts.
bdead16a: Biophysical drivers play a central role in structuring coral reef communities. The diversity of environmental gradients and reef assemblages across the insular Pacific provides an ideal natural context to explore links between biophysical conditions and reef change. Through our team’s primary research initiative–the 100 Island Challenge–we used a combination of in situ fish belt transects, large-area imagery, and satellite-derived oceanographic data, we investigated how central Pacific coral reef communities respond to episodic climatic and biophysical forcing events. We examined resistance mechanisms in key coral and fish groups to assess their capacity to buffer anomalous conditions (e.g., El Niño–related reductions in nearshore upwelling and elevated sea-surface temperatures). Species survival was strongly dependent on life-history traits: fast-growing, short-lived species were less able to tolerate disturbances and often experienced dramatic population declines, whereas larger-bodied fishes and massive corals demonstrated greater resistance. Biophysical disturbances caused species-specific reductions in coral cover and shifts in fish assemblage structure; however, many reduced species retained the capacity for rapid population recovery. Following a major disturbance, we observed a complete reorganization of the reef community, with gradual rebuilding toward pre-disturbance structure. Our results highlight the variability of coral reef assemblages over time and emphasize the role of biophysical forcing in shaping reef resilience–a dynamic likely to become increasingly important as anomalous events become more frequent and severe.
7c6da3e2: Coral reefs are crucial habitats for many fish species due to their function as feeding and shelter sites. However, due to their sensitive to environmental changes, reef degradation has led to a drastic decrease in fish and reef assemblages, which has also been reflected in related economic activities. The loss of branching corals Acropora palmata and Acropora cervicornis reaches up to 90% in some areas, which means a reduction in the abundance and diversity of associated fish populations. In two time periods, between 2011-2012 and 2023-2024 throughout the Colombian Caribbean, the fish associated with the Acropora formations were evaluated in relation to the size and health status of both species. The most important patches of A. palmata were found in the Tayrona National Natural Park and A. cervicornis in the reefs of the Gulf of Urabá. 113 species from 34 families were associated with A. palmata, with the best-represented families being Labridae, Haemulidae, Pomacentridae, and Serranidae. Thalassoma bifasciatum, Microspathodon chrysurus, and Stegastes adustus were the species common to all reef locations, with the highest number of species found in the Tayrona National Natural Park (55). In A. cervicornis, 78 species belonging to 25 families were recorded, with Labridae, Pomacentridae, and Haemulidae being the most abundant. Stegastes planifrons was the only species common to all locations, and the highest number of species was found in San Bernardo Islands (39). Although the state is critical throughout the Caribbean, and with the bleaching of 2023-2024, the populations of A. palmata practically disappeared from two localities, the loss of structure is not yet reflected in the associated ichthyofauna.
9fa00b47:
Long-spined sea urchins are key herbivores that strongly impact benthic reef communities through their interactions with macroalgae and grazing activities. Yet, their long-term dynamics in the Pacific region remain poorly understood, even though mass mortality events in the Caribbean since the 1980s have revealed their critical ecological roles. Here, we describe the decline of long-spined urchin populations on barrier reefs surrounding the island of Mo’orea (French Polynesia) using three time-series datasets (1990–2025), comprising 20 sites across three coastlines, and assess concomitant changes in reef assemblages.

Due to differences in hydrodynamic regimes and local pollution, each coastline was analyzed separately. This spatial approach revealed strong geographic heterogeneity in the timing and magnitude of decline. On the north coast, combined densities of Diadema spp. and Echinothrix spp. dropped by more than 75% in the late 1990s, while the east coast experienced a pronounced mass mortality event between 2013 and 2015, during which densities fell from more than 20 individuals/10 m² to nearly zero. In contrast, the west coast never exhibited notable populations of long-spined urchins. The mortality events were followed by increased macroalgal cover and rises in herbivorous fish biomass, suggesting a compensatory response by fishes after the loss of echinoid grazing pressure.

The contrasting trajectories among coastlines highlight how varying local conditions may have shaped both the timing of collapse and the ecological responses that followed. They indicate that multiple environmental stressors may have jointly weakened urchin populations, facilitating widespread mortality around the island. Overall, this study underscores the value of long-term, spatially resolved monitoring for detecting gradual declines in key functional groups. It also emphasizes the vulnerability of herbivores to climate- and human-driven stressors, and the need to restore urchin populations as a nature-based solution to rising macroalgal cover.
e5347643: Defense mechanisms of macroalgae include the production of secondary metabolites in some species (e.g., Dictyota spp.), generation of a calcium carbonate skeleton (e.g., Amphiroa spp), or rapid regeneration rates (turf algae). Each strategy entails a response to herbivores and distinct resource allocations that can determine the outcome of species-species interactions. We investigated the influence of herbivory pressure on the distribution and abundance of palatable algae (Jania spp. and Amphiroa spp.) as single species or in mixtures with unpalatable species (Dictyota spp.). During the summer of 2015, we quantified the frequency of algal assemblages and the biomass of herbivorous fish of six reefs in the Florida Keys. Additionally, we conducted a field experiment in which we tracked the succession of palatable and unpalatable algal species-clusters and mixes, caging them to exclude herbivory. Across the six reefs, Scarid biomass was higher than Acanthurid biomass. Reefs with high herbivore biomass had a high frequency of Dictyota-palatable spp. mixes, and there were no single species clusters of palatable species present. After excluding herbivory for 24 days, the absolute percent cover of Dictyota spp. in Dictyota-Amphiroa and Dictyota-Jania mixes decreased from >25% to <2% and the absolute percent cover of palatable species increased from <10% to >28%. When herbivores were excluded, the unpalatable species decreased, and palatable species increased over time. Thus, in coral reefs with high herbivory, unpalatable species (Dictyota spp.) can serve as refuges for palatable species. These results show the contribution of herbivory pressure in determining algal abundance, species interactions, and ultimately, community composition of reef benthic communities.
e27e024e:
The compounding pressures of the 2023 worldwide coral bleaching event and the regional spread of Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) have accelerated ecological degradation across the Caribbean reefs. To evaluate the population effect of these disturbances on San Andrés Island (Colombia), we integrated high-resolution photomosaic surveys with in situ coral spawning monitoring from 2023 to 2025. This combined approach enables assessment of both structural and functional components of reef resilience and is aligned with long-term monitoring priorities for effective reef management.
Over three years of continuous monitoring, live coral cover declined sharply from an average of ~10% in 2023 to ~3% in 2025. Photomosaics revealed substantial mortality of both SCTLD-susceptible species and bleaching affected species. Also, it indicates a shift toward algal dominance and localized losses of structural complexity. Concurrently, reproductive assessments documented a near-total collapse in coral spawning: almost no spawning activity was detected in 2023 and 2024, and only limited gamete release by a small number of colonies of four of the most important coral species was observed in 2025. These results suggest severe energetic depletion and fecundity decrease following consecutive thermal and disease stress, with implications for the long-term regenerative capacity of populations.
Encouragingly, the partial return of reproductive activity in 2025 coincided with declining SCTLD prevalence, indicating the first signs of functional recovery. The integration of photomosaics with reproductive monitoring proved essential for detecting subtle early recovery signals not captured by cover metrics alone.
Our findings underscore the value of sustained, standardized monitoring frameworks for identifying thresholds of decline, documenting disease and bleaching impacts, and informing science-based management actions. These results contribute to regional understanding of temporal ecosystem change and highlight the need for continued, high-resolution tracking of both benthic state and reproductive output to support reef conservation across the Caribbean.
8122ce4a: Ecosystems worldwide are facing a suite of anthropogenic stressors driving rapid biodiversity change, with significant consequences for ecosystem function and resilience. Both alpha and beta diversity can help disentangle how anthropogenic stressors are altering communities over time. Coral reefs are an iconic ecosystem threatened by local anthropogenic stressors (e.g., fishing pressure and nutrient pollution), in addition to escalating physical disturbances such as tropical cyclones. However, it remains unknown if and how physical disturbances alter the effects of local anthropogenic stressors on biodiversity and community composition. We conducted a four-year field experiment that emulated the impacts of overfishing and nutrient enrichment with an acute physical disturbance, mimicking a tropical cyclone. We evaluated how this altered coral reef benthic community composition and diversity over time, with an emphasis on two main benthic groups: stony coral and macroalgae. In intact areas (not physically disturbed), nutrients and consumer pressure were both important in structuring the benthic community. Nutrients were the primary driver of coral community composition and nutrient enrichment reduced coral beta diversity through time by preventing decreases of coral taxa abundances at high consumer pressure. Consumer pressure and nutrients interacted to influence macroalgal communities in undisturbed plots, with temporal alpha diversity being highest under a combination of elevated nutrient levels and low consumer pressure. However, in physically disturbed areas, consumer pressure was the primary driver of diversity and community composition. Under low consumer pressure, increases in macroalgal alpha diversity were highest and increases in coral alpha diversity were lowest. Our results highlight that physical disturbances can alter the impacts of local anthropogenic stressors on diversity and increase the importance of top-down control by consumers, emphasizing the need to conserve consumer populations in this era of escalating physical disturbances.
457cd1dd: The critical ecosystem services that coral reefs provide rely on the production and persistence of complex, three-dimensional habitat. Reef framework accumulates when calcification by marine organisms exceeds erosion, and thresholds of habitat persistence–termed Darwin Points—mark the geographical limits beyond which net carbonate production can no longer keep pace with sea level rise. As coral reef growth rates decline globally, reefs will increasingly transition toward net erosional states and the location of Darwin Points may consequently shift. In the 1980s, strong latitudinal gradients in coral cover, calcification, and carbonate production rates were documented across the Hawaiian Archipelago, with the northernmost atoll, Kure, identified as the Darwin Point. Forty years later, we reassessed spatial patterns and drivers of Hawaiian coral reef carbonate production and investigated the present day location of the Darwin Point threshold. Using in-water and image-based ReefBudget assessment techniques refined for the U.S. Pacific Islands, we evaluated benthic cover and estimated carbonate production and biological erosion rates across twelve islands and atolls in the main and Northwestern Hawaiian Islands. We observed significant declines in coral cover and carbonate production at most islands over the past four decades, likely driven by repeated bleaching events, ocean acidification, invasive species outbreaks, and other localized environmental stressors. While most reef sites and islands were net producing, several locations were already at or approaching net erosional states. In addition, although methods differ between studies, our findings suggest a more southerly Darwin Point for the Hawaiian Archipelago than previous estimates. Our study demonstrates the importance of these functional metrics in resilience-based management and highlights the increasing spatial constraints on reef growth in the face of ongoing climate change.
225ce7ab: Conventional statistical methods provide powerful tools for understanding patterns in nature, but they often rely on assumptions that ecological systems routinely violate. Coral reefs are shaped by nonlinear interactions, interdependent processes, and complex spatial heterogeneity. When such data are analyzed with conventional models, subtle yet important patterns in ecosystem function can remain hidden. This study applied topological data analysis, a mathematical framework that examines the geometric structure of complex datasets. This approach uses principles of topology and homology to detect multidimensional features that persist across scales, allowing meaningful ecological signals to be distinguished from background noise. When applied to coral reef datasets, these tools reveal how ecological functions emerge from the interplay of many interconnected species and habitat features. To test the applicability of this approach, benthic composition, three dimensional habitat metrics, and fish community composition and functional trait data were integrated across a broad spatial gradient in West Hawaii. Topological methods were then used to characterize the shape and structure of their multivariate relationships in high dimensional space. By mapping sites according to intrinsic similarity, the analysis uncovered natural ecological clusters and transitions that remain undetected through traditional approaches. To identify early indicators of stress and resilience, nonlinear smoothing was applied to the derived coordinates, and the first and second derivatives of the resulting response curves were evaluated. Threshold points were identified where small changes in habitat complexity or functional biomass correspond to rapid shifts in ecological state, occurring at locations of maximal curvature that mark an acceleration in ecological change. These threshold locations provide quantitatively defined reference points that can serve as indicators of ecosystem function, emerging stress, and potential recovery trajectories. By integrating geometric data structure with nonlinear threshold detection, this approach identifies actionable ecological indicators that support ecosystem assessment, management, and monitoring of coral reef resilience.
bb5e2afc: Coral reefs have been the archetype of highly productive oceanic ecosystems. This high productivity is driven in part by the efficient cycling of organic and inorganic carbon, which are the foundation for coral reef metabolic function. Coral reef metabolic rates, such as net ecosystem production, ecosystem respiration, and net ecosystem calcification, scale up to affect all living organisms on coral reefs by filtering carbon through the food web and building the calcium carbonate structures that support the high biodiversity of reefs. These metabolic processes are primarily driven by light, temperature, and water flow and are modulated by the composition and biomass of organisms (e.g., relative amount of producers or calcifiers). Each of these environmental conditions are shifting as a result of anthropogenic climate change which may threaten the critical metabolic rates that maintain coral reef ecosystems. Based on an 18-year time-series from Mo’orea, French Polynesia, we showed significant declines in gross ecosystem production, ecosystem respiration, and net ecosystem calcification. Using a Bayesian structural equation model, we demonstrate that coral loss was the primary driver of these dramatic declines in reef metabolism, which was driven primarily by a depression in ecosystem respiration. There is an urgent need to integrate time-series measurements of both organic and inorganic carbon cycles to truly understand how global climate change and other local stressors are interacting to influence critical coral ecosystem functioning.
1c0a3504: Coral reefs are highly vulnerable to thermal stress, yet the physiological mechanisms driving their recovery remain incompletely understood. Recovery is not merely the visible reversal of bleaching; it is a complex, time-intensive process involving multiple interdependent physiological pathways. This study examines the multifaceted nature of coral recovery, with a focus on symbiosis with dinoflagellate partners, heterotrophic feeding, energy acquisition, and biomass accumulation. The interactions among these components are assessed to determine their influence on coral resilience, resistance, and long-term persistence under thermal stress. Results indicate that biomass serves as a critical integrative measure, reflecting the energy reserves and overall physiological state of corals. Recovery trajectories are closely linked to the capacity of corals to rebuild biomass through coordinated symbiont repopulation and energy acquisition via feeding and photosynthesis. Corals with higher initial biomass and effective energy accumulation pathways demonstrate enhanced resistance and faster recovery, whereas those with depleted reserves exhibit prolonged stress and reduced persistence. These findings underscore the importance of monitoring biomass alongside traditional metrics such as symbiont density or chlorophyll content to better predict recovery potential. The results highlight the inherent complexity of coral physiology, demonstrating that successful recovery requires the integration of multiple physiological processes over extended timescales.
