By elucidating the relationships between biomass, symbiosis, and energy dynamics, this study provides a framework for understanding coral resilience under climate-driven thermal stress and informs management strategies aimed at sustaining coral populations and reef ecosystems.
67a533fe:
Ecological networks map the interactions that link species and functional groups, providing a systems level view of how ecological processes are organised and how they respond to disturbance. Although they are powerful indicators of functional change, ecological networks remain uncommon in marine research and are entirely absent for Caribbean coral reefs. The pronounced community shift detected on the turbid reefs of Tela Bay following the 2023 bleaching event provides the empirical basis for establishing these networks for the first time.
The primary aim is to construct an interaction matrix that synthesises established ecological relationships, including functional roles, trophic pathways, and spatial competition patterns, among the coral, algal, benthic, and targeted reef fish taxa monitored across Caribbean reef systems. Once completed, this matrix will serve as a regional framework for developing site specific co-occurrence, functional, and disturbance focused ecological network models.
Co-occurrence networks will provide a baseline view of community organisation by highlighting patterns of species association. Functional networks will illustrate how species interact through grazing, predation, competition, and habitat provision, helping to identify which ecological functions are maintained by which taxa and which roles may weaken as communities reorganise after bleaching. Disturbance simulations will assess the vulnerability of these pathways to bleaching-related disruption and will identify where key ecological roles become concentrated in very few species.
With climate driven disturbances reshaping marine ecosystems rapidly, ecological network approaches offer a valuable advance beyond traditional monitoring by detecting early shifts in functional structure. The Caribbean provides an ideal model region, where well documented disturbance histories and contrasting reef conditions allow network reorganisation under climate stress to be examined. This context also enables key functional processes such as grazing, habitat provision, or coral settlement support to be identified as drivers of recovery, which can help inform management strategies.
3dfc5c76: Coral reefs in Okinawa, Japan are highly diverse and exhibit substantial variation in their structural complexity and benthic community composition, which can be attributed partly to the biophysical conditions. Essential ecosystem services—such as habitat for fisheries species and shoreline protection—depend heavily on reef complexity, therefore it is crucial to understand both the drivers of changing reef structure and the impacts of those changes. To better characterize the relationship between hydrodynamics and reef structure on Okinawa’s reefs, we deployed instruments less than 50 cm off the bottom that recorded flow velocity across eight sites along the island’s west coast for two weeks in August 2025. We paired these measurements with Structure-from-Motion photogrammetry to quantify coral community composition and structural complexity. Photogrammetry surveys covered 100 m2 at each site and were used to generate digital elevation models for rugosity calculations and orthomosaic images. Orthomosaics were analyzed in CoralNet to assess community composition at the genus and morphological functional levels. Flow regimes differed among sites, with average speeds ranging from 0.80 to 6.28 cm s-1 and max speeds from 6.13 to 25.01 cm s-1. Reefs with higher current magnitudes were observed to support a greater abundance of tabular colonies, whereas reefs with lower current magnitudes were dominated by more massive coral morphologies. This work demonstrates the importance of taking an integrative approach to coral reef monitoring, providing insights into the interconnectedness between hydrodynamics and  community composition on one of the most biodiverse reef ecosystems in the world.
8f4fb7af:
Coral reefs cover less than 0.2% of the ocean’s surface yet sustain nearly one-quarter of all marine species, making them among the most biodiverse ecosystems on Earth. However, reefs are increasingly threatened by multiple anthropogenic stressors, including ocean warming, acidification, and pollution. Among these, plastic pollution has emerged as a pervasive but underexplored driver of coral reef degradation. An estimated 0.8–2.7 million metric tons of plastic debris become entangled in Asia–Pacific coral reefs annually, and this amount is projected to increase by up to 40% by 2025. Despite growing awareness, field-based studies investigating chronic plastic exposure under natural conditions remain scarce.
This study presents an integrated field investigation of macroplastic embedment and associated stress pathways in coral reefs around Nanao Island, Guangdong Province, China. Combining field surveys, physiological assays, and electron microscopy, we examined coral colonies with and without macroplastic exposure. Corals entangled with plastic exhibited significantly reduced symbiont density and chlorophyll a content compared to unaffected colonies, indicating impaired photosynthetic capacity. Some colonies were found dead, frequently covered with black algal  that may facilitate microbial infection and disease progression, while others survived with plastic fragments embedded in their skeletons. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) revealed clear physical patterns of plastic penetration and skeletal deformation, highlighting long-term interaction between coral tissue and plastic debris.
Ongoing microbiome and RNA-seq analyses will further elucidate how macroplastic embedment disrupts coral-associated microbial communities and activates stress-related molecular pathways. This study provides the first mechanistic field evidence of how macroplastic debris induces chronic physiological stress and structural damage in reef-building corals, contributing essential insights for managing coral health and mitigating plastic impacts in the South China Sea.
95cbdac6: This study provides the first documented report of microplastic ingestion by corals in historical collections. We examined the presence of microplastics in 19 Lobophytum and 20 Sarcophyton soft coral museum-archived specimens collected in 2011–2012 and compared them with fresh 15 Lobophytum and 19 Sarcophyton samples collected in 2023 to assess temporal trends in microplastic pollution. Preliminary results of microplastics recovered and isolated from coral tissues after peroxide digestion indicated an increase in abundance between 2011 and 2023. FTIR analysis revealed that microplastic particles were dominated by fragments, followed by fibers. The major polymers identified were polystyrene (PS), polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polyethylene-polypropylene (PE-PP) co-polymer and polyethylene (PE). Our results, which also documented the first ingestion of microplastics by corals in Okinawa Island, indicate an increase of microplastic abundance in Okinawa marine environment over the past decade. Given the scarcity of long-term data on microplastic occurrence in marine ecosystems, this study highlights the importance of natural history collections for reconstructing historical patterns of microplastic pollution.
3d1bc341:
Hermatypic corals form the structural foundation of tropical reefs, which are among the planet’s most biodiverse ecosystems. Although the impacts of climate change on coral reefs are well documented, coral reefs also increasingly suffer from emerging threats such as plastic pollution. Although microplastics’ effects on coral physiology have been studied, microplastics smaller than 60 microns and nanoplastics remain largely unexamined. In this study, we investigated the impacts of microplastics (MPs) and nanoplastics (NPs), (1 × 10⁵ and 2.5 × 10¹² particles L⁻¹, respectively) on two tropical coral species, Stylophora pistillata and Turbinaria reniformis. We assessed the integrity of the coral–algal symbiosis, the photosynthetic performance of the algal symbionts, and the energetic budget of the coral holobiont.

Our results indicate that both coral species and particle size significantly influenced physiological responses. In S. pistillata, MPs induced symbiont loss, or “bleaching,” following a significant decrease in photosystem II efficiency and chlorophyll content. Both coral species presented a significant decrease in energy reserves such as proteins and carbohydrates, and prolonged exposure to MPs further amplified these effects. Nanoplastics induced milder effects, but the concentrations used in this study were below environmentally relevant levels resulting from microplastic fragmentation. Future studies using higher nanoplastic concentrations are necessary to fully assess their potential impact on coral health.
1a27b7b0: Microplastics are ubiquitous pollutants on coral reefs, where they can adhere to coral surfaces or be ingested by coral polyps. Corals also face increasing thermal stress from marine heat waves, which can cause bleaching and alter behaviors including mucus production and feeding. The combined effects dictating how bleaching influences adhesion and ingestion of different common microplastic types remain relatively unknown. We conducted a multi-factor experiment to investigate the interactive effects of thermal stress, bio-fouled microplastic type (polyethylene; PE, polypropylene; PP, and tire particles; TP), and feeding choice (microplastic vs. Artemia nauplii) on two dominant coral species from Hawaiʻi, Montipora capitata and Pocillopora acuta. Coral fragments were exposed to plastic particles and/or Artemia in controlled, 1-hour feeding trials. For M. capitata, plastic adhesion rates shifted under bleaching conditions, with relatively more PP and PE adhesion to fragments that had bleached. In P. acuta, both bleached and nonbleached fragments adhered PP more than other plastics, regardless of the presence of Artemia prey. While M. capitata fragments adhered more microplastics than Artemia, the opposite was observed in P. acuta. The P. acuta fed on microplastics only when Artemia prey were present, with ingestion rates of PP ≈ PE > TP. Ingestion in M. capitata was too low to analyze across treatments. Bleached fragments of both species showed extremely low feeding, suggesting that severe thermal stress suppressed feeding activity and/or disrupted particle selection mechanisms. While particle surface charge and density could stimulate adhesion of certain plastics (e.g., polypropylene), plastic ingestion seems to be stimulated by nutritional prey cues and inhibited by thermal stress. Regardless, adhered particles could transport pathogens and/or exacerbate reactive oxygen species production in bleached corals. Understanding how coral mucus mediates adhesion and ingestion, and whether its function changes during bleaching, will be critical to project the impacts of these stressors.
57b76562:
The Derawan Conservation Area in Berau Regency, East Kalimantan Province, covers 285,548.95 hectares and is divided into three management zones: the Core Zone, the Limited Utilization Zone, and Other Zones. Although the region holds substantial potential for marine tourism and fisheries development, it faces increasing environmental pressures, particularly the accumulation of coastal and marine debris originating from both domestic and tourism-related activities. Data from 2023 show that 83 non-household establishments, including accommodations and restaurants, generated up to 630.8 kg of waste per day during the holiday Idul Fitri peak season. Waste found in surrounding waters does not solely originate from local sources, raising concerns about the long-term sustainability of ecosystems within the Derawan Marine Protected Areas (MPA).

Research demonstrates that plastic debris has severe impacts on coral reefs. Lamb et.al. (2018), indicate that plastic can facilitate microbial colonization by pathogens associated with marine disease outbreaks, increasing the probability of coral disease from 4% to 89% upon contact. Structurally complex coral species are disproportionately affected, posing risks to reef-associated habitats and fisheries. WWF Indonesia (2024) recorded daily waste generation of approximately 572.95 kg on Derawan Island, dominated by organic waste and multilayer plastics. Monitoring by the Plastic Free Ocean Network (PFON) from June 2024 to July 2025 reported an average of 5.88 kg of marine debris, primarily soft plastics, indicating ongoing irresponsible disposal practices.

Mitigation measures include establishing a Material Recovery Facility (MRF), conducting awareness campaigns, strengthening policy advocacy, and organizing regular coastal clean-ups. Between February and September 2025, around 410 residents participated in environmental activities, supported by 12 public infographics and 77 media publications reaching nearly 99,703 accounts. The MRF facility can process approximately 3,159.2 kg of inorganic waste per month. Continued assessments and enhanced multi-stakeholder collaboration are essential to sustain plastic waste management and protect coastal and marine ecosystems.
255a89fc: Coral reef ecosystems are increasingly exposed to pollutants derived from plastic products, including phthalates, phenols and bisphenols. These additives can leach into surrounding waters, accumulate in benthic organisms, and disrupt key physiological processes. Despite growing concern, monitoring plastic-derived contaminants in reef invertebrates typically relies on destructive sampling, limiting our ability to assess contamination without harming the organisms used for detection. This study aims to establish a non-lethal in-situ method for detecting plastic additives in sessile filter-feeding reef invertebrates. We are developing an analytical protocol using Solid-Phase Microextraction (SPME) needles to detect phthalates and related additives, including DMP, DBP, DEHP, BPA and 4-nonylphenol, directly from living marine organisms. We evaluate this approach in ascidians (Microcosmus exasperatus and Polycarpa mytiligera) and in the giant clam Tridacna maxima, selected for their ecological importance as filter feeders and their role as bioindicators of reef health. Sampling is conducted in-situ across seven locations in the eastern Mediterranean Sea and the Red Sea, providing an opportunity to compare contamination patterns across sites while avoiding destructive harm to the organisms. Our findings will provide new insight into the presence and distribution of plastic additives in coral reef invertebrates. More broadly, this work offers a validated non-lethal method for contaminant monitoring, enabling long-term ecological assessments without compromising sensitive reef communities.
9773327e: Healthy coral reef ecosystems are a product of abundant and diverse species assemblages. Coral reefs’ most abundant and diverse residents are small invertebrates (<5 centimeters) such as worms, clams, snails, shrimp, and crabs, collectively known as cryptofauna. Cryptofauna are critically important because they constitute the base of coral reef food webs and nutrient cycles. Understanding cryptofauna community dynamics, including the composition of species from different trophic levels and functional roles, is critical to understanding the fundamental health of coral reefs. Little is currently known about cryptofauna communities in the Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary. We are investigating cryptofauna settlement, succession, and biodiversity patterns in the middle Florida Keys at two reefs situated along an environmental gradient: Coral Gardens (a nearshore reef with high rugosity and high coral cover) and Stag Party (an offshore reef with low rugosity and low coral cover). We deployed a total of 10 Autonomous Reef Monitoring Structures (ARMS) with attached Onset HOBO temperature and light data loggers across these reefs in July 2024. One ARMS was retrieved from each reef in August 2024, January 2025, June 2025, and June 2026 after 1-month, 6-month, 1-year, and 2-year immersions, respectively. The remaining ARMS will be retrieved in June 2027 after a 3-year immersion. In accordance with Smithsonian Institute protocols, ARMS processing involves (1) identifying motile organisms to the lowest possible taxonomic level, and (2) photographing ARMS plates to annotate in CoralNet and determine percent cover of sessile organisms. Results from this project will improve our understanding of how cryptofauna communities are influenced by environmental conditions (water temperature, light availability, reef location, coral cover, and rugosity), and how sampling results are influenced by ARMS immersion duration. We aim to contribute valuable information to help inform ARMS survey design and better enable replication and standardization of future cryptofauna studies.
60c77fd7: The richness and diversity of life remain one of the most fundamental questions in science, yet a precise estimate of it continues to elude us, particularly in lesser-studied animal groups such as marine invertebrates. Marine benthic communities, which support a rich biodiversity of such invertebrates, are ubiquitous and major sources of socio-economic importance, but are often understudied in biodiversity research. In particular, Southeast Asia, where the Coral Triangle is situated, is the richest hotspot in global marine biodiversity, but is threatened by habitat loss and degradation. Against a backdrop of accelerated anthropogenic-induced change, we are potentially losing species faster than we can document and describe them. There is thus a need to extensively document the region’s rich marine biodiversity to better track changes in spatial and temporal patterns in diversity. This presentation will introduce the Marine Biodiversity of Southeast Asia (MBSSEA), an ongoing five-year initiative (2024–2029) aimed at characterizing the marine hard-bottom biodiversity of key sites in Southeast Asia with Autonomous Reef Monitoring Structures (ARMS). ARMS are artificial units that provide a standardised way of sampling hard-bottom benthic communities. When combined with DNA sequencing technology, ARMS allows for high-throughput assessment of community richness and diversity across local and regional scales. It also presents a yet untapped opportunity to characterise new species and local records. This presentation will cover the objectives and goals of MBSSEA, provide an overview of the ongoing ARMS work, and serve as a launchpad to gather interest and collaboration with researchers or stakeholders working in the region and field.
f74037e9: “Cryptobenthic” is the term used to define a group of fishes associated with the substrate in which at least 10% of the species within a family reach a maximum body size of only 50 mm. Their population dynamics—characterized by rapid growth, continuous reproduction, and high mortality rates—were recently described as the “fuel” of coral reef functioning, due to the transfer of energy from microscopic prey to higher trophic levels. However, the scarcity of information on cryptobenthic fishes, particularly in the Southwestern Atlantic, prevents an accurate understanding of the factors that determine their distribution across rocky reefs. The aim of this project is to compare different methodologies for obtaining community parameters (such as species richness and abundance) and to understand the structure of cryptobenthic fish communities and the factors driving their distribution on subtropical rocky reefs. To achieve this, four different methodologies will be applied synchronously at three sites in Arraial do Cabo, Rio de Janeiro, and at two depth ranges (3–5 m and 7–9 m): underwater visual censuses, interference visual censuses, quinaldine stations, and FARMS. Community structure will be evaluated using standard univariate tests and multivariate analyses, including Bray–Curtis dissimilarity, PERMANOVA, and nMDS. Using all four methodologies in combination will allow for appropriate and synchronous comparison, as well as provide a significant amount of baseline data on the community, enabling investigation of the drivers of species distribution. Research efforts focused on cryptobenthic fishes are of utmost importance given the global knowledge gap for this group, particularly in the South Atlantic, where few studies have been published. By integrating multiple survey techniques, this research will help fill a critical knowledge gap and establish a foundation for advancing studies on cryptobenthic fishes in the South Atlantic.
da116041: Understanding how energetic constraints shape the assembly and turnover of cryptobenthic reef fish communities is central to predicting reef resilience under accelerating environmental change. Across tropical reefs, small-bodied cryptobenthic taxa dominate fish diversity and contribute disproportionately to biomass production through rapid growth, short generation times, and high turnover rates. Yet despite their ecological importance, the functional mechanisms structuring their distribution remain poorly resolved. Here we integrate high-resolution morphological measurements, trait-based community analyses, and emerging otolith geochemical approaches to examine how energy acquisition, expenditure, and growth strategies influence community structure across reef habitats. Using more than 400 standardized photographs of cryptobenthic fishes collected around Lizard Island (Great Barrier Reef) and a complementary dataset from the Belizean Barrier Reef, I quantified fine-scale functional morphology in traits linked to feeding mechanics, locomotor performance, and energy allocation. Community-weighted trait analyses and ordinations reveal consistent patterns of functional differentiation among habitats, suggesting that environmental gradients play an important role in organizing energetic trait space and shaping local cryptobenthic assemblages. To extend these functional insights toward direct physiological inference, I am developing a novel dataset coupling otolith-derived age and growth trajectories with otolith δ¹³C-based proxies of field metabolic rate. Preliminary analyses suggest broad correspondence between habitat-specific energetic regimes inferred from morphology and patterns of growth and metabolism recorded in otoliths. Together, this framework offers a pathway for linking individual-level physiology to emergent community patterns. By integrating morphological, energetic, and life-history perspectives, this work establishes a mechanistic foundation for understanding how environmental variation shapes cryptobenthic fish communities. More broadly, it offers a scalable approach for predicting functional turnover and biomass production potential in these critical but understudied reef assemblages.
9330b151: Cryptobenthic fishes are among the most abundant and diverse group of fishes found on coral reefs and are increasingly recognized for their importance in maintaining ecological processes and trophodynamics on reefs. Yet the characteristics that define their ecological value also suggest they are likely to be highly vulnerable to anthropogenic impacts such as climate change and habitat loss. Coral reefs in the southern Arabian Gulf exist along an environmental gradient the extends from the relatively benign Musandam Peninsular, Oman (mean summer temperatures 31°C) to the hottest reefs in the world in Abu Dhabi, UAE (mean summer temperatures 34°C but can exceed 35°C for weeks), providing a natural laboratory for studying the effects of climate change on cryptobenthic fish communities. To assess the effects of changes in habitat and temperature, cryptobenthic fish were collected from 32 anesthetic clove oil stations across eight reefs along this gradient. Analyses revealed that cryptobenthic fish communities fell into two distinct groups associated with a switch from complex, diverse reefs fringing the headlands in the Musandam to the flat, shallow, low complexity reefs of the UAE. These changes in community were characterized by a ~65% reduction in the number of species per reef and a switch in the dominant species, with Asterropteryx semipuctata dominating Musandam reefs and Coryogalops anomalus dominating UAE reefs. Together these two species represented ~60% of all individuals caught. The density of cryptobenthic fishes on reefs, however, was consistent across the cooler Musandam reefs but gradually declined along the increasingly extreme reefs of the UAE. This study suggests that habitat loss is the primary threat cryptobenthic fishes, impacting communities before the physiological effects from rising temperatures. It also shows cryptobenthic fishes can still persist under end of the century climate conditions and some species even thrive in these extreme environments.
8bc6edf9:
Marine reef caves are microhabitats that provide refuge and nursery habitats for many fishes, yet the communities they support and their contribution to reef function across environmental gradients remain poorly understood. These cavities are dominated by cryptobenthic fishes, which contribute disproportionately to cave-level abundance, diversity, and turnover. Because shelf position shapes habitat structure and predator pressure, cave assemblages are expected to shift from inshore to offshore. Identifying these patterns is essential for understanding how cave microhabitats fit within broader reef dynamics.
Eighteen reef caves were surveyed in the central Red Sea (6 inshore, 6 mid-shelf, 6 offshore), with paired sampling on sheltered and exposed reef faces. Conspicuous fishes were visually surveyed, and cryptobenthic and cave-associated fishes were collected using rotenone. Collected individuals were identified, photographed, measured, and preserved in ethanol. Community composition, species richness, biomass, size structure, and functional traits were quantified across the shelf gradient and between reef exposures.
Strong community differentiation was observed across the shelf. Inshore caves showed the highest abundances and were dominated by small gobiids (Trimma spp.), whereas offshore caves exhibited greater evenness, higher richness, and larger proportions of predatory taxa such as scorpaenids. Biomass and average body size increased from inshore to offshore reefs, indicating a shift toward higher trophic levels across the shelf. This pattern resembles fishing-driven trophic gradients, where nearshore predator depletion allows small cryptobenthic fishes to dominate, suggesting that cave assemblages may reflect broader ecosystem pressures.
Overall, this study highlights the overlooked role of reef caves as microhabitats that contribute to reef productivity and diversity. It provides one of the first shelf-wide assessments of cave-associated fish communities in the Red Sea and demonstrates how biomass and trait data can vary across environmental gradients, contributing to ecological variation.
d1e3b68d:
This presentation synthesizes current knowledge of coral-associated copepod diversity based on 160 published taxonomic studies covering all copepods associated with scleractinian corals, together with molecular datasets from samples collected across multiple regions of the Indo-Pacific and the Caribbean. Special emphasis is placed on the coral families Pocilloporidae and Euphylliidae (including Galaxea) and on the highly diverse copepod family Anchimolgidae.  
The integrated taxonomic and molecular evidence reveals hidden diversity, previously unrecognized host associations, and a remarkable zoogeographic fragmentation among copepod assemblages distributed across distinct coral reef ecoregions, a unique pattern not previously documented for other coral-associated invertebrate symbionts or for coral algal symbionts. These findings indicate strong regional structuring and a wide spectrum of host specificity across taxa.
The presentation identifies major knowledge gaps and emerging biogeographic and ecological patterns essential for understanding coral–copepod symbioses across tropical reef systems.
5670a3c6: In this study we report the presence of three albino basket star individuals belonging to the species Astrocaneum spinosum from Bahía de Los Ángeles (BLA), México, a Biosphere Reserve. These specimens were collected in three different sites and depths within the area, close to Angel de la Guardia Island, which is separated from the continental area by a deep channel, Canal de Ballenas. The first ophiuroid was found in La Ramita, the northernmost site at 18 m depth with a disk diameter dd=2.13 cm; the second in La Ventana at 10 m depth with a dd=1.47 cm, located in the middle part of the Reserve, and the third in Cabeza de Caballo, the southernmost site at 18 m depth with a dd=2.42 cm. They were found coiled on different species of soft corals, mostly of the genus Leptogorgia. All three individuals showed a clearly white disk, yellow-whitish arms and were considered albinos. Other specimens of the species found in the study site showed the typical brown color. The albino individuals were separated approximately 14 km apart from each other, and each site showed different substrate types. We suggest that the prevalence of albinism is high for a relatively small area within the Reserve. Since albinism is genetically determined, this might point out to considerable connectivity within the area. The oceanographic conditions in the area show the presence of a cyclonic gyro in the northernmost part of the Canal de Ballenas and an anticyclone in the southernmost part of the channel that cause a circular current pattern, which might help in dispersing the larvae in the area, but at the same time restricting their dispersal within the channel.
e873050b: Puerto Rico’s Long-Term Coral Reef Monitoring Program (PRCRMP), established in 1999, is the longest-running (1999–2025) coral reef monitoring initiative in the archipelago. It is under the Department of Natural and Environmental Resources’ Coral Reef Conservation and Management Program. Currently based on a biannual cycle at 42 sites across the archipelago, the program provides an essential record for understanding long-term ecological trends of coral reefs under increasingly frequent disturbances. Across 26 years of benthic and fish community data (1999–2025), the program documents substantial ecological change, particularly in very shallow and mid-depth reefs. Major declines—often exceeding 60%—were observed in live cover of key Caribbean reef-building stony corals such as Acropora and Orbicella species, mostly due to thermal anomalies and subsequent epizootic events, coral diseases (namely coral bleaching and Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease) and strong hurricanes. In contrast, more stress-tolerant, opportunistic coral species have increased in abundance. Macroalgal cover showed pronounced increases following disturbance events and generally remained elevated, while sponge cover increased before stabilizing. Fish communities displayed similarly distinct temporal patterns. Sustained declines in mid-sized carnivores, including groupers, snappers, nurse sharks, and other demersal predators, occurred while herbivorous fish—particularly recruits, juveniles, and small-bodied adults—became increasingly dominant. These fish community trends paralleled benthic changes, suggesting that shifts in habitat structure and benthic composition have contributed to long-term alterations in fish assemblages. Through multivariate time-series analyses and standardized monitoring approaches, the PRCRMP demonstrates the critical value of supporting adaptive management. The coral monitoring program is expanding its capacity through the integration of diver-based and autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) photogrammetry to enhance documentation of benthic change. Collectively, these findings inform understanding of future reef trajectories and highlight key knowledge gaps in the drivers and consequences of ecosystem change.
497278fe: The U.S. National Coral Reef Monitoring Program (NCRMP) Atlantic Benthic Team conducts biennial biological monitoring across Florida, Puerto Rico, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and Flower Garden Banks National Marine Sanctuary. In addition to scleractinian coral cover and demographic metrics, NCRMP collects crucial, often overlooked, datastreams that provide a holistic understanding of coral reef ecosystem function. NCRMP collects cover data of other benthic sessile invertebrates, such as sponges and gorgonians. Once thought to be “ecological winners,” these taxa were significantly impacted by the 2023–2024 record heat stress in the Atlantic, similar to corals. Data is also collected across various algal functional groups (e.g., sediment-bound turf algae, cyanobacteria, invasive encrusting red algae: Ramicrusta), which act as competitors and inhibitors of coral settlement and growth. Furthermore, density data is collected for critical mobile invertebrates. These include the ecologically important herbivore, the long-spined sea urchin (Diadema antillarum); Caribbean spiny lobster (Panulirus argus), a valuable fishery species; and Queen Conch (Aliger gigas), an important fishery species recently listed as “Threatened” under the U.S. Endangered Species Act, making monitoring critical. NCRMP employs a stratified random sampling approach that generates greater spatial variation, serving an important "Eyes of the Reef" function that provides valuable context to local monitoring programs. Crucially, NCRMP continually interfaces with reef managers to ensure we collect data on what is most critical, including novel threats. In response to manager requests, we have recently integrated monitoring of Diadema mortality events, Xestospongia disease outbreaks, and the presence of invasive octocorals, and developed GIS platforms to rapidly report this data. With over a decade of continuous data, NCRMP uniquely provides reef managers with a comprehensive suite of indicators, capturing temporal trends of diverse benthic taxonomies beyond scleractinian corals, and enabling data-informed decision-making for management and policy in a rapidly changing environment.
69677ef7: In the past decade, coral reefs in American Samoa (AS) have faced significant disturbances, including two major marine heat waves, a tropical cyclone, and ongoing local stressors (e.g., pollution, coral disease). These disturbances can affect the resilience of coral reef ecosystems – their ability to resist disturbances and/or recover to a pre-disturbance state. While resilience-based assessments are useful management tools, such assessments are generally conducted at a single time point, lacking the temporal resolution required to understand how resilience changes over time. Here, we addressed this gap of knowledge by gauging the resilience of coral reefs in Tutuila (AS) across three time points (2015, 2018, and 2023). To align with local managers' spatial scale of interest, we subdivided the island into seven sectors using a downscaling method that group areas of similar benthic composition using spatially contiguous clustering. To conduct our reef resilience assessment, we integrated long-term monitoring datasets from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s National Coral Reef Monitoring Program, the National Park of AS, and several AS Department of Marine and Wildlife Resources programs. From these datasets, we calculated a composite resilience index derived from eight indicators: coral diversity, herbivorous fish biomass, macroalgae cover, coral recruitment, bleaching, coral disease, fishing pressure, and temperature variation. We found that the resilience of all Tutuila sectors has increased between 2015 to 2023, with the exception of the North sector that displayed a net decrease. Coral recruitment and temperature variation were most strongly associated with higher sector resilience scores. Conversely, decreases in bleaching resistant taxa and coral recruitment were the primary indicators driving the observed decline in the North sector resilience scores. This temporal resilience assessment provides information that can be used to assist in management strategies for Tutuila’s coral reefs.
8adb3f7c: Coral reefs at the islands along the east coast of Peninsular Malaysia are affected by the annual monsoon season, possibly shaping the leeward (westward facing mainland) and windward (eastward facing South China Sea) reef compositions. Furthermore, a devastating Pabuk Typhoon in January 2019 hit the northern part of the region and significantly damaged the reefs. This study aimed to examine effects of the acute and chronic weather phenomena on the reef composition. Coral reef compositions were recorded, based on Reef Check Malaysia standards, across nine islands (70 sites) along East Coast Peninsular Malaysia from 2017 to 2023. The composition parameters analyzed were sessile benthic cover, and populations of fish and invertebrates.  PERMANOVA statistical methods, with data stratified by site were used to reduce variation. The analysis revealed distinct patterns between northern and southern island groups, as well as between leeward and windward sites. Northern leeward sites showed significant compositional changes across years, particularly a notable hard coral decline after Pabuk Typhoon (2019-2020), replaced primarily by nutrient indicator algae. Northern islands also exhibited increased populations of parrotfish, sweetlips, and snappers. Southern islands displayed different patterns, with 2023 showing the most degraded benthic conditions rather than 2019-2020. Fish composition varied more by island than by year in the south, while invertebrates and disease patterns remained inconsistent. The findings suggest geographically distinct reef dynamics, with northern reefs experiencing earlier degradation compared to southern reefs. The global and local increase in human activity following the COVID-19 pandemic may have been the primary cause of the degradation in 2023. This study highlighted the importance of region-specific coral reef management strategies, and different incidence affecting the coral reefs.
1fd14174:
The U.S. National Coral Reef Monitoring Program (NCRMP) conducts standardized surveys of fish and coral populations across the U.S. Atlantic and Caribbean, providing a foundation for evaluating coral population dynamics and, in turn, assessing conservation priorities. Between 2013 and 2025, NOAA’s Atlantic benthic NCRMP team conducted over 12,500 stratified, randomized benthic surveys across Florida, the U.S. Virgin Islands, Puerto Rico, and the Flower Garden Banks. An explicit component of this survey protocol is to record the presence or absence of the Caribbean scleractinian species listed under the Endangered Species Act (ESA; Acropora cervicornis, A. palmata, Dendrogyra cylindrus (Endangered), Orbicella annularis, O. faveolata, O. franksi, and Mycetophyllia ferox). These species are mostly critical reef builders that contribute disproportionately to coral reef structure and function. Despite the protections and resources afforded to them by the ESA, they have continued to decline since their listing, primarily driven by disease outbreaks and recurring thermal stress events.
Analyses revealed sharp declines in the proportion of surveys recording Acropora (i.e., occurrence), with the rate of occurrence dropping by more than 90% in Florida, and Caribbean populations experiencing substantial losses following bleaching events. Dendrogyra cylindrus populations remain functionally extirpated in Florida, and declined from an occurrence of 10-13% in the Caribbean to <1%. Orbicellids remained relatively stable in the Flower Garden Banks, though slight increases in old mortality suggest potential long-term threats. While occurrence rates are still relatively high, Caribbean Orbicellids experienced 60-90% density losses following stony coral tissue loss disease. Long-term monitoring programs like NCRMP generate datasets crucial for ESA status reviews and corroborates that these species are declining in the face of disease and thermal stress events. Furthermore, this monitoring can help reef managers identify remaining populations for gene banking and prioritize species or location-specific measures by identifying areas suitable for intervention or restoration.
4a94ec11: Indonesia is recognized as a biodiversity hotspot within the Coral Triangle and is rapidly expanding its network of Marine Protected Areas (MPAs). MPAs are critical tools for conserving coral reefs, and MPA management plans serve as the foundational guidelines for conservation. Their effectiveness depends partly on how adequately the stressors of coral reefs are addressed and integrated into actionable mitigation strategies. This study assessed the inclusion of stressors in current government-issued Indonesian MPA management plans. We analyzed the inclusion of stressor words within the comprehensive management plans and reviewed the action plan. By 2022, only 20% of Indonesian MPAs had comprehensive management plans, which typically included an introduction, zoning plan, and action plan. We found that most plans address stressors related to fishing activities. In contrast, less than one-third of the plans address land-based stressors, with nutrient pollution and plastic waste largely overlooked. While climate change was identified in about half of the plans, specific climate change impacts, such as rising sea surface temperature, were identified in only very few plans. Most management plans were broad, non-specific, and highly similar across locations, with stressors identified in the introduction rarely integrated into zoning and action plan sections, which may limit site-specific conservation efforts. Nevertheless, some plans showed a more targeted approach by addressing local stressors and proposing actionable responses. This study highlights the need for more site-specific and adaptive plans for managing coral reefs within MPAs. It offers a checklist to assess stressors in future Indonesian MPA management plan development, guiding increased responsiveness to evolving environmental stressors on corals.
24744c4d: East Caicos is one of the largest uninhabited islands in the Caribbean. Its isolation positions it as an ideal natural laboratory and marine protected area candidate. However, establishing evidence-based management requires long-term monitoring data, which is challenging to secure in resource-limited small island developing states. In 2018 The Turks and Caicos Reef Fund (TCRF) led the first research trip to East Caicos with funding from a BEST 2.0 grant with an aim to identify Key Biodiversity Areas. During this expedition the first full reef assessments were done using GCRMN Caribbean and AGRRA protocols. Collaborative workshops with the Department of Environment and Coastal Resources and stakeholders utilized these data for management recommendations, with standardized monitoring intervals planned. However, due to COVID-19, resampling was not possible in 2020 and with the arrival of Stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD) in the Turks & Caicos in 2019, the need to return to East Caicos was more important than ever. In 2021, TCRF launched RumPowered Research®, securing funding through partnerships with rum producers to establish annual monitoring expeditions, earlier that year SCTLD was confirmed in East Caicos. Since 2021, annual expeditions have collected baseline data across East Caicos, West Caicos, and French Cay. The 2024 expedition documented six sites using standardized methodologies: 60 fish transects, 30 benthic transects, 900 benthic quadrat photographs, and large-scale 3D photomosaic surveys (three 10×10m, one 25×25m). Over the years RumPowered Research® has evolved to integrate citizen science, training participants in coral & fish identification, disease assessment, and nursery maintenance. This model addresses critical gaps in small island states: sustainable funding and local capacity for long-term ecological monitoring. Using a combination of innovative funding, standardized protocols, stakeholder engagement, and publicly accessible data, a new framework for evidence-based  and long term monitoring in the Turks & Caicos has been established.
2ee1b497:
Indonesia’s coral reefs are globally significant, hosting more than fifty percent of the world's known coral genera. This extraordinary biodiversity has motivated decades of monitoring, scientific research, and collaborative initiatives. Long-term efforts have produced extensive datasets to maintain healthy, resilient coral reefs. 

Building on this foundation, Indonesia has developed the first Ocean Accounting (OA) Dashboard, an integrated platform that brings multidimensional data on coastal ecosystems, including coral reefs. The OA framework is internationally recognized and, in Indonesia, is mandated by law and regulation to support marine spatial planning and the blue economy.

The OA dashboard integrates spatial and temporal information on coral reef extent, condition, ecosystem services, and economic value, and is supported by a dynamic model that simulates the management impacts. Spatial data were derived from national-scale mapping using satellite imagery (Sentinel and SPOT) for 2018 and 2021, validated through ground-truthing and secondary data. The results were organized into 10 x 10 km marine-based spatial units, covering 11 fisheries management areas, 34 provinces, and 11 OA pilots across Indonesia. Ecosystem services and economic values were generated through the Andalusia (Making Ocean Capital Visible) initiative, which synthesized 173 economic valuation studies nationwide. Coral reef condition data from four OA pilots were also used to validate the dynamic model.

The OA dashboard has been applied to monitor the progress of 10 National Marine Protected Areas (MPAs), supporting management effectiveness and reporting toward Indonesia's MPA Vision 2045. Moreover, this platform also guides permit issuance by assessing investment risks and rehabilitation needs to safeguard coral reef ecosystems, enabling the country to move Beyond Gross Domestic Product and sustain Life Below Water.

As pressures from climate change and human activities intensify, the Indonesian OA dashboard responds to 21st-century management challenges and offers a scalable, transferable approach for other coastal and reef-dependent nations.
91178617:
Mass coral bleaching events are intensifying worldwide, underscoring the urgent need for innovative, scalable interventions capable of mitigating thermal stress directly in the field. Here, we present the first successful in situ proof-of-concept of an artificial cooling technology designed to protect reef-based coral nurseries during a marine heatwave. At Tetiaroa (French Polynesia), we deployed a deep-sea water-derived cooling system adapted from a SWAC (Sea Water Air Conditioning) pipeline, continuously injecting 1,000m cold water (11°C) at 13m depth over nurseries. Operating for 5 months during the 2024 heatwave, the sprinkler system reduced ambient temperatures by 0.7°C on average (up to 2°C), providing the first demonstration of sustained sub-surface cooling applied directly to potential coral conservatories.
We tested 15 genotypes of 3 coral species (Acropora globiceps, Pocillopora tuahiniensis, Pavona duerdeni) across cooled and ambient nurseries, with repeated in-situ monitoring before, during, and after the heat wave. Coral health was assessed through photobiology (symbiont and photosynthetic pigment densities, antioxydant activity (catalase), oxidative damage (lipid peroxidation)) and holobiont physiology (growth, survival, symbiont composition dynamics).
Artificial cooling prevented bleaching across all species, from the highly sensitive A. globiceps (all uncooled genotypes bleached) to the more tolerant P. duerdeni (27% bleached). By suppressing thermal stress, cooling prevented oxidative damage, maintained symbiont densities, and avoided the shift toward thermotolerant but low-productivity symbionts. Consequently, energy reserves were preserved and corals grew faster during and after the heatwave, showing no ecological or physiological debt once cooling ceased.
This talk will demonstrate that in-situ artificial cooling is a viable, field-ready technology capable of preventing bleaching and preserving coral function during extreme warming events. By coupling engineering innovation with high-resolution ecological monitoring, our work establishes a scalable pathway for climate-resilient reef conservation and restoration and opens a new frontier for protecting vulnerable coral reefs in a rapidly warming ocean.
be909cc1:
As the frequency and severity of marine heatwaves continue to exceed the natural coping capacity of many reef-building species, there is a growing need for innovative and scalable mitigation strategies. Artificial upwelling (AU), geo-engineered transport of cooler, deeper water into shallow reef environments, has emerged as a promising technique to locally reduce temperature and alleviate thermal stress. In this study, we experimentally evaluated the potential of AU as a mitigation tool by simulating a six-week marine heatwave across two consecutive summers using a controlled mesocosm system. Each day during the heatwave, a pulse of cool water was delivered to all tanks, with treatments differing in both the duration of the daily pulse (1-12 h) and the depth (temperature) of the source water. These scenarios allowed us to assess how varying cooling regimes influence coral physiological responses.

Fragments of three dominant Bermudian coral species with contrasting bleaching susceptibilities were exposed to all AU treatments. A comprehensive suite of physiological metrics was measured to identify the most responsive indicators of heat stress and AU effectiveness, including respiration, photosynthesis, calcification rates, bleaching scores, symbiont density, pigment concentrations, total proteins, total lipids, and maximum photochemical efficiency (Fv/Fm). Several AU scenarios, particularly those involving longer daily pulses (>5 h) and water sourced from greater depths (>50 m), produced notable improvements in key symbiont-related parameters. Enhancements in symbiont density, photosynthetic capacity, and Fv/Fm indicate that AU can partially counteract heat-induced declines in coral holobiont function.

This study provides the first multi-year experimental evaluation of AU under heatwaves and identifies the most effective operational parameters for mitigating coral thermal stress. Beyond informing future AU deployment strategies, these results establish a foundation for investigating broader ecological questions, including whether AU-driven temperature variability can enhance coral heat tolerance over time and how AU may shape coral reef community dynamics.
f1f07b49:
Novel reef interventions require thorough testing before implementation. We evaluated the efficacy of pulsed artificial upwelling (AU)—the uplift of cooler, deeper water from within the euphotic zone—as a tool to reduce coral thermal stress. Pulsed rather than continuous upwelling was applied to moderate, but not eliminate, thermal stress and promote gradual acclimatization to warming oceans. In summer 2025, we conducted a simulated AU experiment in Bermuda using an outdoor, community-scale mesocosm system. The 7-week experiment used twelve 500-L flow-through seawater basins: four ambient (28°C), four heated (30.5°C), and four heated+AU (30.5°C interrupted by 28°C for 8 h per day). Each basin contained common reef taxa, including adult and juvenile hard corals, soft corals, gorgonians, and juvenile fishes. We measured coral and fish metabolic rates and coral photosynthetic integrity (Fv/Fm) as indicators of physiological plasticity and stress.
The reef-building corals Diploria labyrinthiformis and Montastraea cavernosa showed high photosynthesis, respiration, and Fv/Fm in ambient and heated+AU, but reduced performance under heat alone. Porites astreoides exhibited minimal responses, and spat maintained consistently high Fv/Fm. The hydrozoan Millepora alcicornis showed strong declines in metabolic rates and Fv/Fm in both heated treatments. Calcification generally declined with temperature (ambient → heated+AU → heated), except in D. labyrinthiformis, which calcified least under heated+AU. The gorgonian Gorgonia ventalina and soft coral Plexaura flexuosa showed low susceptibility to heat. Among fishes, only the princess parrotfish increased respiration with temperature. The four-eye butterflyfish, however, experienced high mortality (50%), with more deaths under heated+AU.
Overall, AU reduced coral bleaching, though benefits varied among taxa and some trade-offs occurred (e.g., D. labyrinthiformis calcification). Most other sessile cnidarians and reef fishes showed limited temperature responses, except butterflyfish. AU may serve as a localized, mid-term intervention to mitigate coral thermal stress during marine heatwaves.
a86c9a1e: A major challenge in reef restoration and nature-positive infrastructure is designing structures that reliably support coral recruitment and early survival. Over the past three years, the Geometric Ecology Lab has iteratively developed and tested five generations of settlement modules, each informed by previous field results. A central finding is the strong influence of microhabitat geometry: spiral-shaped (helix) recesses attracted up to 80-fold more larvae than linear ones, with optimal dimensions supporting settlement that persists for years after deployment. Hydrodynamics also played a key role—higher flow increased larval entrainment into helix recesses and boosted settlement. These design rules were robust across module shapes and construction materials, with cylindrical units in both concrete and clay performing comparably. Small shifts in recess size altered post-settlement survival, emphasizing the need to balance initial recruitment and long-term persistence. Although helix designs maximized total recruits, more complex fractal structures supported greater species diversity, revealing trade-offs between abundance and richness. Together, these results provide a mechanistic basis for scalable coral-supporting structures and offer new insight into the ecological drivers of larval settlement and survival.
77739eb7: Coral reef degradation has intensified over recent decades, calling for restoration strategies that are not only ecologically responsible but also scalable and resilient to support long-term ecosystem recovery. To address this challenge, we rely on a multidisciplinary team including marine biologists, materials scientists and designers to cover issues from materials formulation to field deployment. Our work introduces an innovative coral-restoration solution based on the design of artificial reefs produced through various shaping and consolidation strategies. The engineered structures have geometries, mechanical behavior, and functional properties that can be tailored to specific environmental objectives. More specifically, additive manufacturing offers freedom in structural complexity combining micro- and macro-scale features known to influence larval settlement and long-term habitat formation. A key contribution of this study lies in the material system developed for the reef units. Following a biomimic approach, we chose oyster-shell powders as the primary source of calcium carbonate powders. Coupled with low-carbon cements in low quantities and biosourced binders derived from local resources of French Polynesia (such as alginates), the as-obtained composite materials minimize environmental impact, valorize marine waste from invasive species, and anchor the project within circular-economy principles. From a materials-science perspective, the formulations offer tunable porosity, surface roughness, and dissolution behavior, which are critical for promoting biocompatibility and resilience of early coral recruits. The structures are manufactured both using a standard casting method used as a reference and advanced 3D-printing by Binder Jetting. This way, such robust and cost-efficient manufacturing techniques support fine architectural control and large-scale production. Coral species to be integrated in the next phase of the project are still being defined in collaboration with local biologists. Field deployment is planned in the Moorea Lagoon. The next steps will consist in functionnalizing our substrates with particular chemical species, known to further enhance coral larvea settlement and survival
f77ee7b2: Coral biodiversity and reef health are declining worldwide under immense anthropogenic pressures, driving many reef stakeholders to implement local-scale restoration interventions alongside conventional management practices. Multiple approaches centred on coral restoration are now in various stages of deployment, spanning  micro-fragmentation, coral propagation via nurseries, and “hybrid” reefs where artificial structures are used to augment recovery of reef geomorphologies. Across these various  approaches, substrate building blocks (ranging from coral plugs to artificial structures) are required to kick start coral growth and framework development. From an engineering standpoint, we examine a unique 3D printed material made of Calcium Carbonate Photoinitiated (CCP) ink to create biologically inert, yet customized substrates. We fabricated and examined coral replicas of four different surface designs with various textures and porosities to optimise coral tissue growth based on “re-skinning” over the substrate surface. Increase in tissue surface area observed over 374 days varied from ~12-22% across different designs, with the smoothest design showing complete coral tissue coverage of the entire structure by day 374. Specifically this design comprised a smoother surface promoting encrusting growth (and whilst presumably limiting fouling via microhabitat availability), resulting in faster and more consistent coral coverage. Additionally, the observed increase in volume over time illustrates the relationship between encrusting and vertical growth patterns, depending on the structure’s surface design. The Pulse-Amplitude-Modulated Fluorometer - Imaging (PAM) was used to study  photosynthetic activity and its fluctuations, enabling the evaluation of stress responses and growth patterns. Furthermore, weight assessment and incubation respirometry techniques were employed to understand and study metabolic processes and gain deeper understanding of tissue growth. All three techniques revealed a positive coral survival rate, suggesting that 3D printed materials based on CCP are effective in supporting coral fragment growth.
33af32c0: Restoring coral reefs at meaningful scales requires technologies that enable rapid, cost-effective, and locally deployable interventions. In this talk, I will highlight nature-inspired innovations that act as catalysts for reef repair by leveraging insights from coral ecophysiology and stress physiology. We develop nature-inspired materials and substrates that enhance larval recruitment, improve outplanting performance, and accelerate early reef assembly. Drawing on coral light management, skeletal growth, microbial symbiosis, and benthic competition ecology, we create functional settlement gels, antifouling coatings, and bio-engineered microhabitats that enable scalable, process-driven reef restoration. These technologies are designed for integration into existing, community-deployable platforms, such as MARS Reef Stars and modular coastal defense reefs, supporting local manufacturing, low-cost deployment, and adaptation to site-specific ecological stressors. We outline a pathway for translating ecophysiology into practical restoration tools, demonstrating how nature-inspired materials can shift efforts from coral replacement to engineered ecosystem assembly. This approach expands local capacity and supports reef recovery in a changing ocean.
dbf38b6c:
Coral reef restoration is crucial for mitigating the decline of marine ecosystems due to climate change and other anthropogenic stressors. Tetrabromopyrrole (TBP), a bacterial-derived compound, has been recently identified as a potent inducer of coral larval settlement. However, natural sources of TBP are limited, necessitating the development of synthetic alternatives. 
Here, we present the synthesis of TBP and its incorporation into engineered surface coatings that can be applied to a wide range of substrates used in restoration. These coatings are designed to stabilize TBP, control its release, and extend its functional lifespan, ultimately providing a practical method for delivering settlement cues in the field.
In this project, we evaluate TBP’s efficacy in inducing larval settlement under controlled laboratory conditions. Additionally, we assess the influence of TBP on survival rates across different coral species. Our findings provide insights into the potential of synthetic TBP coatings as a scalable and reliable tool for coral restoration initiatives.
7086fe19: Landscape scale coral reef restoration is constrained by limited understanding of how corals respond to context-dependent environmental and ecological conditions across a region. Orbicella faveolata (OFAV) is a major reef-building coral whose mass reproduction has been designated as critical to Caribbean restoration efforts. Identifying factors affecting its growth and survival and any context-dependent effects therein is imperative. There is strong evidence that the parent colony source (genotype), culture conditions (nursery location and system type), and the interaction of these factors affect the success of OFAV cultured for restoration. This research evaluated the effect of source coral colony location (KJCAP and the Florida Keys), ex-situ nursery system characteristics (five systems across Nova Southeastern University, University of Miami, and Mote Marine Laboratory), and in-situ nursery placement (Florida Keys, Key Biscayne, and Broward County) on tissue regeneration and post-fragmentation survival. Preliminary results indicate that several Florida Keys locations consistently outperformed others in both in- and ex-situ settings, and that in-situ fragments generally fared better than ex-situ fragments regardless of facility. Early findings also suggest no apparent effect of source colony on in-situ and ex-situ corals. Enhanced growth in the Upper Keys may reflect favorable environmental conditions, such as seawater quality and temperature stability. These trends align with previous studies indicating that lower light and variable nutrient conditions in land-based nurseries can contribute to tissue recession and overall ex-situ performance. High-flow systems can restrict nutrient availability, while excessive feeding may lead to eutrophication, tissue recession, and mortality. Upcoming analyses of PAR / water quality data will help clarify how light and nutrient regimes contributed to these outcomes. This research identified optimal existing nursery systems for OFAV propagation and support the standardization of methods needed to scale restoration capacity.
7d94d7bf: Over the past two decades, through systematic, integrated exploration employing multibeam bathymetric mapping, remotely operated vehicles (ROVs), and advanced technical SCUBA diving, we have documented widespread mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs) at depths of 30–150 m throughout coastal Florida, the Gulf of Mexico, Cuba, Mesoamerica, and the broader Caribbean region. Stratified-random and targeted survey approaches consistently reveal high biodiversity and significant fisheries resources associated with these MCE habitats. Across more than 40 expeditions, we have identified and characterized previously unknown MCEs, evaluated genetic evidence of coral connectivity and algal symbiont variation across depth gradients and geographic distances, developed larval dispersal models linking shallow and mesophotic reef populations, and identified priority areas for conservation and restoration. MCEs in the Gulf of Mexico, Florida, Mesoamerica, and Cuba are extensive, ecologically significant, and exhibit substantial variability in both horizontal and vertical genetic connectivity. These results underscore the role of MCEs as potential refuges for certain coral species and have informed marine protected area expansion, adaptive management frameworks, and novel restoration strategies. While shallow coral reefs have experienced severe declines, MCEs in many regions exhibit low prevalence of bleaching and disease and support disproportionately high biodiversity and fisheries resources. Continued efforts to discover and characterize additional MCEs, determine their capacity to function as refuges, assess the efficacy of assisted coral (or gamete) migration, and evaluate emerging threats are essential to ensure MCE persistence under accelerating environmental change and increasing anthropogenic pressures.
90fcbc6b: The Mexican Pacific spans from 15–32° N and serves as a natural laboratory for studying climate-change effects on ecosystem structure and function. The region already exhibits projected future conditions—including lower alkalinity, rising sea-surface temperatures, increasing frequency and intensity of extreme meteorological events, and coastal upwelling that enhances nutrient supply and primary production. These conditions generate turbid waters with low light penetration, producing a shallow mesophotic zone (16–63 m), depending on site and season. Between 2021 and 2023, we conducted 384 visual transects using remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) across shallow (>10% incident light) and mesophotic (<10% incident light) strata to examine fish assemblages and assess potential refuge capacity in three mesophotic locations. We also collected water samples at multiple depths for eDNA and carbonate-system analyses. We identified 141 fish species across all sites and depths: 85 were shared between strata, 37 were exclusive to shallow waters, and 19 to mesophotic depths. We calculated taxonomic (α-diversity, Shannon, Simpson, β-diversity) and functional (richness, divergence, evenness, mean pairwise distance) diversity indices and found no significant differences (P > 0.05) between depth strata. The eDNA analysis of 81 samples produced 16,441,008 reads and identified 66,246 OTUs, 74.8% of which corresponded to cellular organisms. Eukaryotic diversity included protists, metazoans, and fungi. The results supported the deep-refuge hypothesis, with 4,262 OTUs shared between shallow and mid-water layers, 1,747 between shallow and deep layers, and 914 between mid-water and deep layers. Deep samples showed lower species richness, suggesting more homogeneous communities. Overall, the observed similarity across depths likely reflects the characteristically shallow mesophotic zones of the eastern tropical Pacific and does not necessarily indicate that the mesophotic zone functions as an ecological refuge.
43ae0a58:
Mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs) are biodiversity hotspots and potential climate refugia, yet they are increasingly threatened as human and climate pressures extend into deeper waters. We present DeepCore, a deep-coral restoration case study at Punta de la Mona (southern Alboran Sea, Granada MPA, Spain). Since 2021, the project has addressed the collapse of temperate mesophotic habitats formed by Dendrophyllia ramea at 30–60 m depth. Lost fishing gear and debris have broken colonies and triggered severe pathologies, affecting ~75% of the local population and reducing reef structural and functional complexity. DeepCore combines multidisciplinary research, active restoration, and social co-management with public authorities, fishing guilds, and scientific institutions to tackle both impacts and their root causes.

Research comprises high-resolution mapping of D. ramea distribution, oceanographic characterization, population assessments (disturbance, connectivity, genetics, epibionts/parasites), and physiological studies (biometry/photogrammetry, reproduction/histology, pathology). This evidence base guides restoration and supported a 2025 management plan regulating fishing and anchoring.

DeepCore is among the first efforts to restore MCEs in situ using technical divers and closed-circuit rebreathers. Work occurs under demanding conditions (≈50 m depth, currents to 7 m/s, ~12°C, low visibility), yielding new knowledge on a poorly known deep species and the logistics of deep restoration.

Restoration follows a depth-adapted pipeline: (1) removal of ghost nets and garbage for recycling; (2) rescue of fragmented colonies and transport to deep nurseries via scooter-assisted dives; (3) recovery nurseries at 40 m with repeated treatment of pathologies, sediment and parasite removal, and monitoring; and (4) transplantation with a precise, low-impact resin-anchoring technique to rebuild reef patches and three-dimensional complexity.

To date, 4.5 t of debris have been removed, >5,700 m² of habitat restored, and ~1,000 colonies rehabilitated or outplanted. Survival exceeds 80% after five years, alongside increases in associated fauna (+45%) and fish abundance (+75%).
684b6492:
Distribution models are widely used in marine habitat mapping and to predict and map ecological patterns. Knowledge generated from these approaches can inform marine spatial management measures. Species occurrence data compiled in open-access databases often provide model training data; however, sampling bias can strongly influence predictions. In Atlantic Canada, for example, species occurrence data relies heavily on bottom trawl surveys conducted across the continental shelf, yet trawl nets cannot be deployed across steep or complex seabed terrains. Therefore, taxa associated with complex, high-relief habitats that support Temperate Mesophotic Ecosystems (TMEs) are systematically under-sampled, and their contribution to regional seabed biodiversity estimates remains unknown.
Here, we present a Hierarchical Habitat Suitability Model (H-HSM) framework designed to predict the distribution of ideal habitat ideal for TMEs. TMEs characterized by rugged seabed geomorphology and dense aggregations of soft-coral taxa were discovered for the first time in 2024 in the Funk Island Deep (FID) marine refuge off the coast of Newfoundland, Canada. This discovery provided an opportunity to assess whether broad-scale model projections align with true seabed conditions inside a complex, data-poor environment.
The H-HSM comprises soft coral occurrence data obtained from bottom trawl surveys and nested Random Forest classifiers integrating broad-scale ocean-climate (11 km) and fine-scale seabed-terrain predictors (0.2 km). We ground-truthed H-HSM model predictions using drop-camera imagery collected inside FID, and used a binomial GLM to define an appropriate suitability threshold. Results show that most areas containing low-density soft coral assemblages were projected as highly suitable, while the spatial extent of high-density coral gardens was underestimated. This likely reflects sampling biases inherent in trawl-derived occurrence data. We demonstrate how incorporating imagery from complex seabed habitats can account for bias in regional model predictions, enhance interpretation of suitability projections, and provide a scalable approach for targeting and exploring unmapped potential marine biodiversity hotspots.
14ea3fc6: Countries of the South-West Indian Ocean (SWIO) have substantially expanded their networks of Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) in response to global biodiversity commitments, notably Aichi Target 11 and the Global Biodiversity Framework Target 3 (30×30). This expansion has been particularly important for vulnerable ecosystems such as coral reefs and seagrass beds, which play essential roles in biodiversity conservation, fisheries productivity, and climate resilience. However, despite their recognized importance, the effectiveness of MPA management remains a major regional concern, and significant knowledge gaps persist regarding the most relevant indicators of management success.This study assessed the management effectiveness of 19 MPAs across six SWIO countries (Madagascar, Kenya, Tanzania, La Réunion, Seychelles, and Comoros). Using the Integrated Management Effectiveness Tool (IMET), data were collected through structured interviews with MPA managers and key stakeholders. A total of 42 effectiveness indicators were analyzed and classified according to the six elements of the management cycle: context, planning, inputs, processes, outputs, and outcomes. Average scores (± SD) highlighted relatively strong performance during the design and planning stages (66.83 ± 11.91 for context and 62.43 ± 15.61 for planning), reflecting managers’ capacity to define clear visions, objectives, and threat-reduction strategies. In contrast, weaker performance was observed for inputs (47.99 ± 15.22) and management processes (49.05 ± 14.34), revealing limitations in staff availability, financial resources, and access to adequate equipment and infrastructure. These findings emphasize the urgent need to strengthen resource adequacy and to identify regionally relevant effectiveness indicators to better inform future MPA management plans in the SWIO. They also support further adaptation of the IMET tool to enhance learning-based and adaptive MPA management in support of biodiversity conservation and evidence-based decision-making at both regional and global policy levels.
3738d0a9: Mesophotic reefs in the Coral Triangle are ecologically significant yet understudied ecosystems. Whilst often subjected to anthropogenic effects such as artisanal fishing, climate change, and pollution, they are assumed to function as biodiversity reservoirs, including for higher trophic levels. Revealing the structure of fish assemblages, including their trophic dimensions, is essential to understand reef ecosystem health and manage key fish taxa. Baseline data on how fish assemblages vary across depths, including in the poorly studied mesophotic zone, is essential to monitor how these assemblages may change in response to anthropogenic pressures and thereby design effective, depth-inclusive conservation strategies. Our research provides insights into depth-related shifts in reef fish diversity, abundance, behaviour and functional (trophic) composition across a mesophotic gradient at three sites in Tulamben on the north coast of Bali, Indonesia. Using closed-circuit rebreathers, dive teams installed reef-mounted, mono-baited remote underwater video (BRUV) systems concurrently across reef depths (10 – 60 m). Replicate BRUV deployments recorded a 60-minute standardised soak, capturing species identities, MaxN, and behavioural interactions (including bait-engagement) utilising human and AI-assisted video processing. Fish abundance and diversity generally decreased with depth, whilst higher-trophic-level, larger, predatory fish were only observed at depth, specifically 30 m or deeper. Fish behaviour displayed a general trend from bold (increased individual interactions with the bait) at shallower depths to more timid (lower or no interaction) behaviour at depth, potentially suggesting larger predatory fish have learnt not to engage with bait. We provide baseline data on fish assemblage structure for understudied mesophotic reefs in Bali, demonstrating the importance of mesophotic depths as potential refuges for larger marine predators threatened by fishing activity.
b67397c8: The Saya de Malha, a shallow submerged bank of the Mascarene Plateau, harbours coralline red algae (CRA), octocorals and hard corals in its mesophotic coral ecosystems (MCEs) in the middle of the Indian Ocean. Though thermal responses of many shallow-water corals have been extensively studied, investigations on thermal vulnerabilities of mesophotic photosynthetic organisms are yet to be conducted. This study compared the thermal photo-physiological vulnerabilities of a CRA (Hapalidiales), gorgonian (Plexauridae, Plexaura), and hard coral (Acroporidae, Acropora) collected from 38, 47 and 30 m depth from the poorly studied MCE of Saya de Malha. Experimental exposures at 26, 29 and 32oC for a duration of 22hr were conducted to measure the effective quantum yield at the photosystem II (ΦPSII) initially, and after 6, 10 and 22 hrs of exposure using an Imaging-Pulse-Amplitude-Modulated (I-PAM) fluorometer on board the S.A. Agulhas II during the Monaco Explorations: Indian Ocean Expedition in November 2022. At 26oC, the relative to initial ΦPSII value (rΦPSII) remained unchanged for all treatments during the experiment, except for a slight decline at 22hrs for Acropora. At 29oC, rΦPSII in Plexaura remained unchanged while it decreased significantly to 80% and 60% in CRA and Acropora, respectively. At 32oC, the CRA rΦPSII behaved similarly to those at 29oC, while the Plexaura and Acropora showed a downward shift in the rΦPSII responses over time, declining to about 60 and 20%, respectively, after 22hr exposures. The Acropora bleached and the gorgonian had darkened coloration, while the CRA looked unchanged after 22hr exposure at 32oC. These findings indicate that the hard coral tended to be the most thermally vulnerable, followed by the gorgonian and the CRA and thus could provide appropriate information for protection and conservation measures.
0c3a4b35: Caribbean reefs have experienced major human-driven changes to their coral and fish communities, yet how these changes have affected trophic dynamics remains poorly understood due to challenges in reconstructing the trophic structure of pre-human-impact reefs. Advances in fossil-bound protein organic nitrogen isotope (15N/14N) analysis now enable the reconstruction of ancient trophic dynamics, as the 15N/14N ratio reflects an animal’s trophic position. Here, we apply this method to modern and prehistoric (7,000-years-old) fish otoliths (ear stones) and corals from Caribbean Panama and the Dominican Republic (DR), focusing on fishes occupying low to middle trophic levels. We find that while trophic level typically declined in high-trophic-level fishes over time, it increased or remained unchanged in low-trophic-level fishes, indicating that modern food chains are 60-70% shorter than on the prehistoric reefs in both Panama and the DR. In addition, across all trophic groups, we observed a marked reduction in dietary variation, with a 20-70% lower trophic range on the modern compared to prehistoric reefs. This pattern is best explained by less dietary specialization in modern reefs, consistent with less ecological complexity than in prehistoric reefs. These differences document and quantify the trophic simplification that has occurred on modern Caribbean reefs, a change that may increase their vulnerability to ecosystem collapse.
9d649d16: Reef-building corals that host Symbiodiniaceae derive energy from both symbiont photosynthate and heterotrophic feeding, and this nutritional flexibility strongly influences their response to environmental stress. Experimental studies show that enhanced heterotrophy can mitigate the impacts of bleaching on corals.
