f42a1117: The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s National Coral Reef Monitoring Program conducts long-term, large-scale observations of benthic, fish, environmental, and socioeconomic indicators in coral reef ecosystems in U.S. states and territories. The linked datasets span more than a decade across the Atlantic and Pacific ocean basins. Because of the extensive spatial and temporal coverage, the resulting raw data are valuable and support science-based management decisions. Managers across jurisdictions have requested a user-friendly tool to quickly visualize trends, aggregate data of interest, and download it quickly for subsequent analysis and utilization. In order to accommodate these needs, the program developed a Geographic Information System-based tool for data querying and visualization. The tool offers customizable, place-based indicator and species selection, instantaneous generation of simple figures, and the ability to download data in an analysis-ready format. The tool allows a user to visualize interdisciplinary data streams in one place, including benthic cover, bleaching and disease prevalence, coral density, key reef fish species abundance and biomass, and seawater carbonate chemistry. Additionally, human connections have become increasingly important for decision-making, and the tool offers access to human usage and social science data which is unique among global coral reef monitoring programs. The tool has seen high engagement rates since its release, especially with managers in capacity and resource-limited areas, highlighting the utility of optimizing accessibility for management-relevant datasets. While primarily designed for managers, the tool is beneficial for a diverse user base like the general public and decision makers that may be interested to see results that are not overly scientific or difficult to interpret. The tool is one of many web-based products like web stories, social media, and highly synthesized glossy trifold reports used to engage the public in today’s increasingly digital world where scientific publications and technical reports alone are not sufficient.
07afae78: Analysis of time series data is fundamental in ecology for understanding the drivers of community dynamics. However, ecological time series commonly contain missing values, which arise from methodological changes in monitoring programs, poor weather conditions, logistical constraints, or human error. For example, long-term monitoring of reef fish populations on the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) changed from annual to biennial sampling after 2005. The impact of missing data on the reliability of community dynamics models fitted to such time series remains largely unexplored, particularly in species-rich systems. We used state-space community dynamics models to analyze ecological time series with missing data by estimating unobserved true abundances as latent variables, even for census occasions that lack observations. We evaluated model performance across time series of varying lengths and sampling frequencies by using simulations informed by empirical reef fish counts from the GBR. Despite missing observations, models fitted to longer time series produced more accurate and precise estimates than shorter, complete-case time series. Additionally, higher spatial replication with uneven census intervals improved parameter estimates more than maintaining census frequency at the expense of reducing the number of sites. Empirical reef fish data showed that modeling longer time series with changing census intervals yielded lower estimated population growth and weaker intraspecific density dependence, while estimates of interannual fluctuations in species’ population growth remained consistent across sampling designs. Our findings have important implications for long-term community monitoring. First, monitoring programs should prioritize extending time series duration over strict census frequency. Second, although longer time series yield more accurate and precise estimates, analyses of shorter time series still provide reliable estimates, indicating that our approach remains useful even when only decadal time series are available. Lastly, broad spatial coverage enhances inference, highlighting the value of sampling more sites even at the expense of temporal gaps.
49bd24a9:
Australia is one of the world’s leading exporters of live corals for the aquarium trade, most of which are sourced in the wild from coral reefs. Despite the steady growth of the industry, substantial knowledge gaps remain regarding consumer preferences within both Australian and global retail aquarium markets. Our project seeks to address these gaps by developing key metrics from online retail data for the marine invertebrate trade to support evidence-based conservation planning. 
To evaluate online trade, we deployed manual and automated webscrapers to collect retail data from 73 websites across 16 countries over a 12-month period. Here, we present the first set of findings from this work. In the first four weeks of data collection, we documented 642 unique taxa (corals = 335, other marine invertebrates = 307) across thousands of lines of data, with a further 1,857 trade varieties of corals and other marine invertebrates counted. These numbers continue to rise as data collection progresses.
Additional components of our aquarium trade research will be presented to complement these findings, including a global literature review on the coral trade, and a colour-analysis of traded corals from retail images. Overall, our research aims to enhance international understanding of coral trade practices, ultimately contributing to improved conservation outcomes for coral reef ecosystems.
eb82a7c3: Anthropogenic stressors, including climate change, excessive nutrient runoff and ocean acidification, have contributed to reef degradation in the Florida Keys. Therefore, Florida’s Coral Reef is a target for coral restoration efforts to maintain the ecological and economic benefits that are associated with coral reefs. Coral restoration practices currently focus on development of land-based and in situ nurseries for initial growth of multiple genotypes of coral species prior to outplanting. Land-based nurseries that provide ideal conditions for coral growth likely require substantially different water quality from land-based nurseries to be sustainable. To evaluate these differences and possible effects on restoration corals, discrete water samples were collected from land-based nurseries (water source and a representative raceway where corals are held) over a 2-3 year period. Samples were analyzed for ammonium, nitrate/nitrite, phosphate, total seawater alkalinity, and dissolved inorganic carbon. Salinity, temperature, dissolved oxygen, and pHNBS were also monitored throughout the study. All parameters were compared between land and available field-based nurseries. Our goal is to assist in development of best practices for increasing coral reef restoration success along Florida’s Coral Reef.
bceb59cd:
Mapping, monitoring, and modelling ecosystems across relevant spatio-temporal scales is essential for effective reef management and conservation. A significant challenge is the lack of baseline knowledge regarding the spatial distribution, size distribution, and vital rates of dominant coral taxa, and their relationships to the environment.

The Ecological Intelligence for Reef Restoration and Adaptation (EcoRRAP) program addresses this knowledge gap by creating empirical data from Reference Sites that span from the southern end of the Great Barrier Reef up to the Torres Strait, encompassing cross-shelf , depth, and wave exposure gradients. This program established the largest single dataset of benthic community composition, structure, habitat complexity and coral demography.

Over five years, we collected 3D longitudinal data across more than 17 hectares of reef, digitised ~25,000 coral colonies, and developed robust standard operating procedures for data acquisition, processing, extraction, and integration. We applied novel benthic monitoring technologies to maximise efficiency and effectiveness at scales spanning millimetres to hectares, generating crucial population and landscape ecological insights. This talk will present a selection of studies utilising EcoRRAP's data, showcasing its breadth and potential for monitoring coral reef dynamics and informing management. 

Case studies will include:
(1) Method development for efficient data collection, high-performance data throughput, AI-assisted extraction and analysis.
(2) Ecological research examining the impact of multiple massive bleaching events, coral demography, and population models to inform coral reef restoration planning. 

The program generated critical information for evidence-based decision-making, which has the potential to significantly enhance the success of coral reef restoration efforts, ensuring the continued ecological and societal contributions of these vital ecosystems.
df8ec0cf: Sexual reproduction in coral propagation offers significant advantages in terms of genetic diversity and the mass juvenile coral production. We have focused on the advantages and have been continuously working on coral reef restoration using sexual reproduction since 2006. The series of initiatives is divided into three phases, and we will introduce the results achieved thus far. During the first phase from 2006 to 2008, we focused on developing mass production techniques using sexual reproduction methods. We have developed a technique to induce spawning of adult corals collected from sea in land-based tanks, enabling the mass production at once. We continue to utilize this technology as a highly important one even beyond the second phase. During the second phase from 2009 to 2017, we worked on developing Propagation Technology using Sexual Reproduction, which consists of three stages: mass production, farming in nurseries, and outplanting. In mass production, we have developed a method that can be completed entirely in the sea by using a larval cradle. In the second Phase, the issue was that it was difficult to expand the scale of coral reef restoration because people transplanted juvenile corals onto natural substrates. Therefore, in third Phase from 2018 to 2025, the scheme aimed to restore coral reefs by creating large adult coral colonies and continuously supplying large quantities of coral larvae to surrounding waters from them. In this scheme, the creation of large-scale adult coral colonies, termed coral spawning farms, is of utmost importance. To efficiently create coral spawning farms, it is crucial to develop artificial base suitable for coral larvae settlement and growth, along with techniques for efficiently releasing coral larvae. We developed these techniques through multiple tests in the sea. We will continue to advance coral reef restoration utilizing coral spawning farms.
d602c3c5: With climate change and its impact on the worlds’ coral reefs expected to worsen, enormous effort is going towards producing the next generation of resilient corals to sustain coral populations until global warming is arrested. However, on reefs off of Southeast Florida, U.S.A., predation by corallivorous finfish has emerged as a major restoration bottleneck. Newly-outplanted corals face intense predation pressure in the critical early weeks, limiting their survival and undermining investments in the production of climate-resilient corals. Effective, scalable solutions are desperately needed to address this problem. Over the last five years, we tested a suite of solutions to mitigate the impact of finfish corallivory on nursery-grown corals, including physical deterrents, chemical treatments, and novel outplanting designs. In this talk, we look across these experiments to compare their effectiveness and scalability, discuss common patterns, and chart a path forward. Addressing the sources of early post-outplant mortality is imperative as coral reef restoration programs around the world continue to scale up.
51d67788: Active restoration interventions in coral reefs have increased exponentially over the past decade in response to recurrent global mass-mortality events. In the Central Mexican Pacific (CMP), a science-based restoration program has been implemented and adapted in response to the region's natural and anthropogenic stressors. This program primarily focuses on Pocillopora, the dominant reef-building genus in the Eastern Tropical Pacific (ETP). Direct transplantation of fragments has proven to be the most effective approach, with efficient growth of 5.29 ± 1.74 cm yr⁻¹ and survival rates of≥75%, resulting in a more than 3-fold increase in live coral cover over five years. The results also showed a measurable shift in the biomass of key associated taxa, including fishes and echinoderms. Although asexual propagation may be considered potentially detrimental to reef resilience by increasing clonal structure, haplotypic diversity in the region is inherently low (Hd ≤ 0.4), and it has been proven that the genus harbors Durisdinium symbionts regardless of depth or location along the CMP. However, in response to the 2023 massive bleaching event, the asexual propagation integrating nursery-based approaches was implemented to provide thermal refugia and enhance survival. Growth rates were 15% lower, likely reflecting the energetic costs associated with coping with extreme thermal stress. Additionally, non-invasive tools such as bioacoustics have been incorporated into the monitoring framework. Acoustic signatures revealed that, despite coral loss, degraded sites exhibited soundscape patterns similar to those of non-degraded sites, suggesting that acoustic metrics may be included as markers of restoration potential. Given that the short-term projections show increasingly intense thermal and anthropogenic stressors, future efforts not only in the CMP but in the ETP must focus not only on scaling coral cover but also on evaluating and refining effective, feasible, and scalable restoration tools for maintaining the resilience of coral communities.
0b6058a0: The 10 communities of the Beqa-Yanuca Seascape are dependent on healthy coral reefs for their livelihoods. The Pacific Blue Foundation has been working with these communities for over 20 years, and we are working to bring new innovative methods in coral restoration to improve the sustainability and resilience of these communities. For this work we have partnered with the Beqa Lagoon Resort, the largest commercial stakeholder in the region, to develop a sustainable, community-led restoration program that will be funded by tourist involvement. This program includes adapting modular, 3D printed ceramic artificial reef structures developed at the Technion to be made by hand by local communities. In February 2025 we led a ceramics workshop including youth from all 10 communities who were able to deploy over 120 hand-made modular structures using local Fijian clay and hand extrusion, with traditional firing methods (using a lovo or pit oven). From this workshop the community members were able to co-create three different structures and build an artificial reef that is over 1m in diameter and 1.5 m tall, next to a 3D printed modular artificial reef that was installed at the same time. We have been monitoring the structures since installation to evaluate which design and method is most effective. We have seen coral recruitment on all structures and rapid coral growth of coral fragments planted on the structures. We are now developing a sustainable economic model with the Beqa Lagoon Resort where guests can pay for the community-made modular structures and become involved with the restoration efforts.
e40f596e: Community-led restoration programs commonly employ coral propagation and outplanting to improve local reef conditions. Species-selection decisions are typically based on operational considerations such as ease of propagation or outplanting, fast growth, and historically high survivorship. However, whether considering functional traits into this process – by expanding the growth forms used to include massive and sub-massive corals which require relatively greater investments to propagate and plant - can meaningfully enhance ecological outcomes remains largely untested. Restoration efforts therefore continue to favor branching corals, despite massive species contributing distinct ecological functions that may enhance long-term site resilience. To address this gap, a factorial outplanting experiment was established at a Coral Nurture Program (CNP) site in the Whitsundays, central Great Barrier Reef. Coral species present at the site were selected to represent distinct functional groups, enabling evaluation of the ecological value of expanding trait space, incorporating functional redundancy, and combining growth forms. Five outplanting approaches were tested; (1) single-species branching, (2) two-species branching, (3) mixed assemblage (two branching and two massive), (4) two-species massive, and (5) single-species massive. High resolution photogrammetry was used to track survival, growth, structural development, and functional trait contributions over a 16-month period. This study provides a targeted assessment of how growth-form diversity and trait representation influence ecological performance within a community-based restoration workflow. We further evaluate how the additional investments required for incorporating non-traditional target species for restoration (e.g. resource and time requirements) relate to their ecological contributions during early-stage outplanting (<2 years post-deployment). Consequently, we provide unique insight into evidence-based decision-making on when, and to what extent, massive corals should be prioritized in coral restoration efforts on the Great Barrier Reef.
ab295958: Coral reefs are declining worldwide due to anthropogenic-driven environmental change. The foundation and health of these ecosystems rely on the harmonious functioning of all members of coral holobionts, i.e., cnidarian host, symbiotic microalgae, and associated microbiome. Coral stress responses often involve shifts in the taxonomic makeup of their symbionts and microbiomes. Tracing back changes in coral holobiont composition over prolonged time periods can help us reconstruct health history of reefs and gain a better understanding of coral response to current stressors. Here, we focused on a major Caribbean reef-builder coral, Orbicella faveolata, from Varadero Reef, Colombia. This reef has undergone extensive freshwater sediment discharge for decades as result of urbanization and the centuries-long Magdalena River watershed man-made modifications. We show, for the first time, that paleogenomic and paleoclimatic approaches can be combined to reconstruct historical coral holobiont dynamics–including associated algal symbionts, bacteria and viruses–potentially associated with anthropogenic disturbances.
17eed79a:
The critically endangered Caribbean coral, Acropora palmata, has experienced severe declines due to global change and anthropogenic stressors, yet its long-term evolutionary responses to environmental change remain poorly understood. While population genomics of present-day samples has revealed contemporary population structure, ancient population level patterns and host–microbiome dynamics are largely unexplored because of challenges in recovering endogenous ancient DNA (aDNA) from reef cores. These challenges include poor preservation of dead coral tissues in dynamic marine settings and overwhelming co-extracted microbial DNA that often obscure low abundance host sequences. 
To overcome these barriers, we utilized hybridization capture sequencing targeting approximately 5,000 loci previously shown to resolve population structure in modern A. palmata - thus enabling enrichment of host nuclear aDNA while retaining important genomic information. We studied ancient coral fragments from Florida and US Virgin Islands reef cores, spanning two-time intervals bracketing a “reef gap” dated to ~3300–2900 years before present. By comparing the ancient and extant coral population structure, we showed the shifts in A. palmata population structure through time (before and after the time gap) happened differently in these two different regions. Additionally, we leveraged the abundant non host reads to study the ancient coral associated microbiomes and showed that geographic location, rather than core age, is the primary driver of microbiome diversity across samples, although specific reef sites exhibited notable temporal changes in microbial composition. Together, this study demonstrates a scalable capture-based approach for retrieving coral aDNA from reef cores, establishes a foundation for future palaeogenomic work in corals, and illuminates how A. palmata and its microbiomes had responded to past environmental variability, a knowledge that may inform conservation effort under ongoing climate change.
a52aba9e:
Understanding how corals respond to rapid environmental changes is crucial for predicting their future under global warming. Thermal tolerance in scleractinian corals arises from complex interactions between the cnidarian host and its symbiotic Symbiodiniaceae communities. While shifts in symbiont associations are a known short-term plastic response to heat stress, the long-term evolutionary dynamics of coral holobionts at the colony scale remain poorly documented.
Here, we explored symbiosis stability across the lifespan of a massive Porites lobata living colony from New Caledonia by combining ancient DNA (aDNA) recovery from a coral core (named coraDNA) with a high-resolution metabarcoding approach. Symbiodiniaceae diversity was characterized by ITS2 metabarcoding and using the SymPortal analytical framework, revealing 53 defining intragenomic variants (DIVs) spanning the last ~100 years of colony growth. The number of DIVs per sample (5–22) showed no relationship with sample age or sequencing depth, indicating robust detection of symbiotic diversity through time. Overall, the Symbiodiniaceae community was strikingly stable and dominated by clade C15. However, a pronounced shift in community structure was detected during an exceptionally warm winter event, suggesting episodic disruption of a persistent symbiosis. Additionally, we detected DNA from Azadinium spinosum (Dinophyceae) DNA in two adjacent core. 
Our findings provide the first centennial-scale insight into the evolutionary ecology of coral holobionts at the colony level, opening new perspectives on coral adaptation pathways under climate change.
83750a80:
Coral skeleton fossils represent a valuable archive of past reef ecosystem change yet the retrieval of ancient DNA (aDNA) from such samples remains challenging, and successful retrieval from millennial-scale fossils is still limited. Improving extraction methodologies is therefore essential for expanding the temporal scope of coral holobiont reconstructions using aDNA.

International Ocean Discovery Program (IODP) Expedition 389 recovered coral reef cores from drowned terraces around Hawai‘i, providing a unique opportunity to investigate patterns of DNA preservation from the coral skeletal matrix. This project aims to optimize aDNA extraction from fossil Porites by systematically assessing how components of the extraction workflow influence DNA yield and quality. This study uses Porites subsamples from the youngest and most shallow Hawaiian drowned reef terrace (H1) which drowned approximately 14.7ka years ago. Building on established bone aDNA methods, we are testing how aDNA yield varies with sample input mass, predigestion duration, and DNA binding system. 

By identifying the extraction workflow that best retrieve aDNA from coral skeletal cores, this study will provide practical guidelines for future aDNA work on this underutilised archive type. Such methodological advances are important for determining the feasibility of reconstructing reef-building coral communities and coral-Symbiodiniaceae associations from ancient reefs. Ultimately, our project aims to contribute to broader multi-proxy approaches for understanding long-term reef ecosystem responses to environmental change.
8001502c: Rising ocean temperatures and associated heat stress have emerged as the primary threats to coral reefs, which already exist close to their physiological limits. Marginal, high-latitude reefs, such as Florida’s Coral Reef (FCR), have been proposed as potential refugia from thermal stress or areas of tropicalization, allowing reefs to persist in subtropical regions as the effects of climate change intensify. Understanding the thermal history and temporal trends of these locations is critical to evaluate this potential. However, the paucity of long-term in situ temperature data collected over a wide latitudinal gradient has hindered our ability to explore thermal histories over ecologically relevant timescales. Here we leveraged almost 20 years of in situ benthic water temperature data collected as part of two long-term monitoring programs, the Southeast Florida Coral Reef Evaluation and Monitoring Project and the Coral Reef Evaluation and Monitoring Project, to evaluate spatiotemporal changes in temperature regimes and thermal stress across the entirety of FCR. From 2007-2024, water temperature was recorded bihourly at 78 permanently established sites located throughout southeast Florida, the Florida Keys, and the Dry Tortugas. We calculated key metrics to evaluate spatiotemporal variations in temperature regime and trend for each site, including accumulated thermal stress, annual variability, and maximum, minimum, and mean trends. Cluster analyses were performed to characterize temperature regimes at the region and habitat levels. Regression analyses were used to evaluate spatiotemporal trends. Our findings revealed that mean water temperature increased approximately 0.06 °C/year in each FCR region and heat stress events were more frequent, although the rate, magnitude, and variability were spatially heterogeneous. This study highlights the importance and utility of long-term monitoring of environmental variables and provides historical context for future ocean warming, which may assist in the identification of reefs resilient to thermal stress.
a7ef0630: The Republic of the Marshall Islands (RMI) is an atoll nation which spans some of the most remote, pristine coral reefs to some of the most densely populated and heavily used reef systems. This study utilizes data collected sporadically over the past 10 years across 12 atolls in RMI. We evaluated how environmental and geomorphologic gradients interact with emerging anthropogenic stressors, including increasing human population density and climate‐driven thermal indicators, to assess trajectories of coral communities, benthic condition and net carbonate accretion. Environmental gradients remained important with low wave exposure reefs exhibiting higher proportions of slow growing heterotrophic corals, with faster growing autotrophic corals representing a consistently higher proportion of communities on high exposure coasts. Atolls with minimal direct local human pressure typically retained higher coral cover, greater representation of fast-growing taxa, and more favorable overall calcified benthic composition. Atolls that have experience more frequent and severe thermal stress had consistently lower coral cover, selective loss of fast-growing sensitive taxa, and a higher proportion of small weedy corals. In the most extreme cases, when thermal stress was paired with high human pressure, a noticeable a shift in benthic substrates from coral and crustose coralline algae dominated communities toward fleshy macroalgae and other non-calcified substrates became evident. Our results show that (1) low human impact can buffer, but not fully prevent, climate-driven degradation, (2) high thermal stress alone can drive reef transitions to slightly diminished calcification states, (3) the combination of intense local use and repeated thermal events produces phase shifts to degraded, net erosional reefs. At the global level this study highlights the urgency of reducing local stressors through effective management while simultaneously advocating strong global action on climate change to sustain coral reef atolls into the future.
ff5b70e0: As thermal anomalies decimate scleractinian corals, excavating sponges are also expected to succumb to bleaching or macroalgae dominance. In this study, we followed the population demographics of the azooxanthellate excavating sponge Cliothosa delitrix over 20 years and the ecological succession patterns on the leftover substratum following its death on San Andrés reefs in the Colombian Caribbean. Coral substratum (live and dead coral species), sponge size, and sponge abundance were measured on haphazardly placed belt transects (40 m²) in 2005 and 2024 at six reef sites (6-10 m depth) along the west shallow terrace. The frequency of all benthic organisms found on the bioeroded skeleton left by dead sponges was recorded in 2024. To complement, 44 sponge-colonized coral colonies were marked in 2004 and monitored in 2005, 2011, and 2024. Contrary to expectations, the C. delitrix population in San Andrés has remained stable from 2005 to 2024, with high recruitment, while senescence claims mature sponges. In fact, the C. delitrix population has not succumbed to macroalgae dominance or to the thermal anomalies of 2005 and 2014-2017, and sponge recruitment has not been inhibited by macroalgae competition for available substratum. Succession on the bioeroded skeletons left by dead sponges occurs in a stepwise manner, with crustose coralline algae remaining the primary successors associated with both recently and older bioeroded colonies; macroalgae dominate in the long term, but they account for less than 50 % of space, while sponges (11.83 %) and other organisms also occupy the substratum; new excavating sponges and other sponge species recruit simultaneously with macroalgae. Our findings indicate that neither C. delitrix has overgrown the reef nor have macroalgae completely overtaken coral skeletons after the sponge’s death, suggesting that sponges and macroalgae are key organisms in future reefs, maintained through the "vicious circle” transfer of organic matter.
3b2fde55: Widespread ecological degradation across tropical western Atlantic reefs has substantially reduced live coral cover, leaving extensive areas of substrate occupied by diverse non-coral benthic assemblages. This shift raises important questions regarding (1) the capacity of these reefs to support vertical accretion and (2) how effectively census-based carbonate budget approaches capture accretion potential under contemporary, increasingly coral-poor configurations. To evaluate temporal changes in elevation under present-day conditions, we implemented a novel in situ approach to measure fine-scale surface-height changes on Mexican Caribbean reef crests, the zone most important for coastal wave energy dissipation. Annual fixed-benchmark measurements (~600 points per site) were collected using a bespoke submersible adaptation (ReSET) of the wetland Surface Elevation Table over two years at two ecologically contrasting reef configurations: one heavily degraded and one retaining relatively high coral cover. ReSET deployments quantified an overall net reef-surface denudation of –0.2 mm yr⁻¹ [95% CL: –1.1 to +0.67]. The degraded site was net eroding (–0.6 mm yr⁻¹ [–1.0 to –0.2]), while the less-degraded site showed negligible elevation change (~0.1 mm yr⁻¹ [–0.9 to +0.5]). In contrast, larger-scale census assessments (10-m transects) suggested an essentially static surface at the degraded site (–0.1 mm yr⁻¹ [–0.3 to +0.3]) and low-end but positive accretion at the less-impacted site (~1.1 mm yr⁻¹ [–0.4 to +4.7]). Estimates from both approaches indicated limited accretion potential and were broadly comparable, particularly given that ReSET frames were placed on specific contemporary substrates and therefore did not capture broader-scale contributions from remaining large coral colonies. These outcomes reflect the distinct processes and spatial scales each method addresses: census-based approaches integrate community-scale production and erosion and are suited to larger-scale assessments, whereas ReSET provides site-specific ground-truthing and yields nuanced insight into the accretionary or erosional status of increasingly prevalent substrate microhabitats (e.g. turf-dominated, CCA-dominated).
cd3aa58b: The severity and frequency of acute rubble-generating disturbances is increasing on reefs globally. Through a body of work spanning the past nine years across the Maldives, Indonesia and Australia, our understanding of rubble bed dynamics and recovery processes has substantially advanced. We now have a clearer understanding of the flow threshold velocities that generate rubble, move rubble, and break apart bound rubble. We also better grasp the broad variance in binding strength and binding rates among the organisms that naturally stabilise loose rubble. Most recently, we determined that the threshold velocity required to break apart bound rubble at most experimental sites increased from ~1 m/s after 4 months of stability and binding succession, to ~3 m/s after 18 months. We then scaled up these findings across the Great Barrier Reef, combining them with time series hydrodynamic data to estimate rubble mobilisation risks and rubble bed recovery potential across the region. This modelling provides a practical tool for triage, helping to identify coral reefs that have a low likelihood of natural stabilisation and recovery. For these ‘at-risk’ reefs, rubble stabilisation methods can be very effective. However, our investigations revealed that many methods are still considered small-scale, labour-intensive and expensive, although innovative techniques are emerging. Rubble remains a pervasive and key management challenge worldwide and will continue to shape reef recovery outcomes into the future. This talk will consolidate what we have learned to date and outline current research directions related to: improving modelled recovery potential predictions; formalising damage assessment protocols; advancing approaches for monitoring temporal trends in rubble generation and persistence; improving rubble-detection techniques; and enhancing the effectiveness of stabilisation methods.
3c3c22c6:
ResilienSEA Immersive's mission is to connect communities with ocean conservation and climate action through immersive experiences and theatrical storytelling. A few years ago, we created a show where we told the story of how climate change impacts coral reefs. See the recap video here: www.youtube.com/watch?v=K6v6efBqBEc. The show featured roaming, interactive parrotfish characters, Polynesian-dancing coral reef dancers, a fossil-fuel corporate character, and Sea Nymph ocean warrior dancers. Through a combination of projection mapping, dance, and interactive theatre, we connected the audience to the threats that corals face (especially with temperature increases and acidification) as well as to specific community-driven solutions that reduce carbon emissions. During the performance, the fossil fuel character, Noxxe, rudely tore the brightly colored fabrics off the coral dancers to reveal white bodysuits underneath, freezing them. That bleaching prompted the ocean warrior dancers to appear and task the audience to identify solutions. Those who volunteered their actions then rebuilt the reef by restoring the torn fabrics, demonstrating the power of collective action. (See more about this show here: www.youtube.com/watch?v=XgVZkfjo22o or here: escholarship.org/content/qt1c21z41p/qt1c21z41p.pdf )

We would love to bring this show to ICRS 2026. Our method of creating science-based immersive experiences offers a unique format for learning about coral reefs in highly experiential and compelling ways. We integrate scientific storytelling and conservation messages into our interactions, our costumes and props reflect scientific accuracy, and we gift messages-in-a-bottle that provide tangible calls to action. We can do a variety of different kinds of storytelling, including a stage show, complete with projections and full theatrical lighting and sound, and/or we can create simpler roaming acts, engaging attendees throughout the crowds. We can also modify as needed and create new characters to exemplify any theme or story. We are looking forward to connecting more!
596efd44:
Little-known viruses, bacteriophages or phages, kill bacteria, which is good for coral reefs. This simple biological truth, known as viralization, is the conceptual anchor of my artistic practice. Viralization describes the dynamics when viruses restrain runaway bacterial growth, allowing reef ecosystems to recover from microbial stress. 

For over a decade, my work has unfolded through ongoing collaboration with the Rohwer Lab, where I explore how artistic methods can illuminate and generate new scientific research lines. I first created dozens of bacteriophage illustrations for Life in Our Phage World. Those images emerged through long, recursive conversation; looking closely so art and science became inseparable. What began as representation became speculation and a shared attempt to understand how viruses shape the future of coral reefs.

This inquiry expanded into the development of Coral Reef Arks, midwater reef-building structures designed to study and restore coral ecosystems. Sculpture became a research instrument. I helped design, engineer, and fabricate structures, including the DNARK, a limestone and travertine underwater sculpture modeled on the icosahedral geometry of a bacteriophage capsid entwined with a DNA helix. Its form served as a test of how internal geometries influence water flow, organism settlement, and cryptic biodiversity.

Through these works, I approach art not as an illustrative tool but as a mode of ecological thinking. Drawing and sculpting rehearse scientific hypotheses by creating physical analogs for conceptual frameworks like viralization. The process generates forms that scientists can adapt and test: art catalyzing new questions in coral reef biogeochemistry. By embedding scientific ideas into structures that live underwater and accumulate life, I aim to expand the vocabulary we use to talk about coral reef conservation and restoration. My works invite people to recognize ecological change hinges on microscopic actors and like a phage, art can enter a system quietly and transform it.
10a6b4a2: For more than two decades, Dr Anya Salih has collaborated with leading artists, filmmakers and musicians to bring the unseen cellular life and fluorescence of corals into public consciousness. Her fluorescence imaging, confocal 3D animations and scientific advising have been central to internationally exhibited projects, including Lynette Wallworth’s Hidden/Depths, HOLD, Coral: Rekindling Venus, Awavena, and large-scale immersive installations such as at the World Economic Forum. These works have consistently elicited strong emotional and intellectual responses; at WEF their impact was such that political leaders called spontaneous round-table discussions to address reef decline and the urgency of climate action. As Scientific Producer for Mourning the Great Barrier Reef with Oscar-nominated musician Anohni, Salih contributed scientific content, underwater fieldwork and interviews with coral experts to the 2025 Sydney Opera House performances, later extended through Anohni and the Johnsons’ European tour. Pre- and post-performance surveys (n = 264 pre; n = 143 post) demonstrated significant increases in ecological concern, emotional connection to the reef and belief in the value of art–science collaboration. During the performances, her message “Find the place in nature that you love. Go to it and think of it as your kin”, became a defining articulation of the work’s ethical resonance. Building on this trajectory, Salih has begun a new collaboration with University of Sydney’s Sydney Environment Institute to develop Coral as Kin: a project connecting coral biology with multispecies justice, ethics of attunement and public engagement. This initiative incorporates augmented-reality visualisations of coral fluorescence and stress signatures as future tools for policy communication and environmental decision-making. This presentation examines how immersive art–science collaborations grounded in rigorous scientific expertise can transform public understanding of coral reefs, cultivate kinship-based ethical relationships with marine life and provide powerful pathways for strengthening conservation values and action.
1d0b2eb0: Art provides a joyful, low-stress way to invite people (kids and associated adults) to experience first-hand what positive action towards a better future taken in community and collaboration with other people feels like. For example, in 2021, I asked kids to draw what they love most about trees as a way to help cool the earth – to help penguins, coral reefs and people (https://kidscareaboutclimate.org).  2,629 kids from 213 schools and 33 nations drew pictures of trees, and in 2022 the tree planting company ‘15 Trees’ (https://15trees.com.au) planted a native Australian tree for each drawing.  A global judging panel selected winners, who either won zoom calls with artists, authors and scientists or had their drawings featured in Australian Geographic Magazine. The grand prize-winning drawing circumnavigated Antarctica with sailor Lisa Blair as a small flag on her yacht Climate Action Now as she became the fastest person to ever do so unassisted and non-stop. I mosaic’d all the drawings to form a bigger picture of a penguin planting a tree on Earth and printed it in sections for form a GIANT 7 m high by 4.25 m wide banner. Nearly 9000 kids in 15 nations have since seen and touched the giant banner, and we’ve filmed many of them via drone – spelling messages for world leaders like HELP, ACT NOW, SAVE EARTH, and TOO HOT. Videos of this have been shown at four United Nations climate meetings, and one was part of the Global Stocktake of climate action around the world. In 2025, the giant banner was part of a marathon week-long tree hug in Perth, Australia that coincided with the first week of #COP30. A further 930 people from 46 nations submitted tree hug photos in solidarity with the nearly 13,600 people involved in the initiative (#kidscareaboutclimatechange) since 2018.
9dbb5e6c: Coral reef restoration efforts are expanding rapidly in response to accelerating ecosystem degradation, yet the recovery of ecosystem functions often lags behind the return of coral cover. One contributing factor is the widespread use of small, nursery-grown outplants that fail to replicate the ecological roles of large, mature colonies lost during disturbance events. Mature corals provide essential functions including habitat creation, nutrient cycling, larval retention, and modulation of fine-scale hydrodynamics and light. To address this gap, we developed Large Engineered Colonies (LEC), engineered biological entities composed of 5 or 10 isogenic Stylophora pistillata colonies (each at transplantable size) affixed to a 3D-printed base and designed to approximate or exceed the functional size of natural adult colonies. Over one year of post-transplant monitoring, we compared the performance of the two LEC sizes to conventional single-colony outplants. LEC entities rapidly supported diverse associated communities: resident invertebrates and fishes established within the first month and stabilized by month four, with size 10 LEC hosting richer community than size 5 (median Shannon diversity index 1.5 ± 0.43 and 1.2 ± 0.4, respectively). All colonies within each LEC fused within three months, further increasing structural complexity. In contrast, single-colonies failed to sustain a stable community of coral inhabitants Trapezia cymodocee and Neopomacentrus miryae (median Shannon diversity index 0 ± 0.4) over the same period, indicating markedly slower community development. Additionally, initial biophysical measurements show that LEC modify near-colony flow, enhance larval retention potential, and influence local nutrient concentrations more effectively than single-colony outplants. Our results demonstrate that LEC restore key ecological functions at substantially more rapidly than standard restoration practices. As such, they represent a promising, scalable tool for enhancing the functional recovery of degraded reefs. Future work will optimize materials, evaluate long-term structural performance, and test scalability across diverse reef environments.
e96f31cb: With the expansion of ocean sprawl, many natural coastlines have been replaced by man-made structures, resulting in biodiversity loss and reduced ecosystem services. In response, researchers and managers are increasingly exploring hybrid shoreline solutions that merge the protective functions of traditional coastal armouring with the ecological benefits of natural habitats for future coastline development. However, the ecosystem functioning of hybrid shores remains poorly understood, particularly in comparison to natural coastal systems. Sloping seawalls share similar geomorphic profiles with natural reef slopes, such as slope angle, depth, and hard substrate, and therefore have the potential to support comparable fauna and exhibit similar ecosystem functions. Additionally, natural colonisation of corals on artificial structures, including granite revetments, breakwaters, and vertical seawalls, has been documented, suggesting that engineered coastlines can, under suitable conditions, act as reef surrogates. In this study, we will compare the functional performance of coral-colonised subtidal seawall slopes and adjacent natural coral reef slopes among the Southern Islands of Singapore. We will assess key ecosystem processes including calcium carbonate production and bioerosion, as well as herbivory and primary production. Together, these metrics offer a better understanding of the ecosystem functions supported by coral-colonised seawalls relative to natural reefs and highlight opportunities to enhance the ecological performance of hybrid shorelines. These insights will help guide the design and development of future coastal protection strategies that maintain the protective benefits of grey infrastructure while more closely emulating natural coral reef ecosystems to achieve multifunctional coastal resilience.
fceca17f: Corallivory has long been a natural stressor for reef-building corals, but its severity in Florida has intensified in recent years due to declining coral cover and increased outplanting of smaller and more vulnerable coral fragments during restoration efforts. Small-scale research studies and restoration outplant monitoring have revealed species- and reef-specific patterns of predation by corallivorous finfish. However, substantial gaps remain in understanding both predator preference and predation intensity of coral outplants. Additionally, restoration practitioners would greatly benefit from a predictive predation index to support site selection and improve the efficacy of labor-intensive restoration projects. To address these needs, we synthesized research and long-term monitoring datasets that tracked the influence of corallivorous fish predation on coral growth and survivorship across reefs. Because these datasets relied upon visual assessments of newly formed predation scars, the responsible predator could not be confirmed. To overcome this limitation, we developed a coral-baited remote underwater video system (CBRUVS) to directly observe fish predation incidence, identify the predator to species and size class, and quantify bite rates. We used CBRUVS deployments across multiple reef sites to evaluate and refine a predictive predation index. Patterns suggest that predator communities and predation risk vary among reefs and regions of Florida’s Coral Reef, with implications for long-term survivorship of corals at the species level. Improving our ability to anticipate fish predation risk, particularly for slow-growing coral species, should be prioritized to support more effective and resilient coral restoration in a rapidly changing ocean.
03f02dab:
Coral reefs are increasingly under threat from coastal development as nations respond to rising sea levels, land scarcity, and expanding infrastructural needs. Such development often leads to habitat degradation or loss, prompting the need for science-based mitigation measures. One strategy to mitigate the biodiversity loss at the impacted coral reefs is to outplant the impacted corals to suitable alternative habitats.

As part of such impact mitigation strategies, we conducted large-scale coral transplantation and monitoring efforts. In this presentation, we describe multiple case studies from Singapore, involving the relocation of more than 6500 coral colonies from at least 49 genera to various recipient sites, most of which ranged from 10cm to 49cm in size. A subset of these transplants was monitored alongside naturally occurring colonies for periods ranging from 9 months to 5 years to track survivability. Overall, transplant survival across recipient sites ranged from 65.0% to 100.0%, with notable differences in survivability across taxa. 

Overall, the findings suggest that large-scale coral relocation can yield promising outcomes when supported by careful site selection, robust installation methods, and sustained post-transplant monitoring. Such approaches provide a valuable tool for retaining local genetic diversity and mitigating resilience loss in development-constrained coastal environments.
66e0d8ce:
Coral transplantation remains a central intervention in reef rehabilitation, yet comparative evaluations across distinct biophysical settings and time frames remain limited in the Philippines. This study presents a spatio-temporal assessment of coral transplant performance using a uniform methodology applied across three ecologically contrasting sites in Mindanao Island: Kiwalan, Iligan City (L1, 2020); Calangahan, Lugait (L2, 2021); and Lawigan, Bislig (L3, 2023). Coral fragments were extracted from donor colonies using controlled manual detachment and transported underwater to minimize handling stress. Each fragment was mounted in plastic-cup bases with concrete adhesive and anchored to the substratum using concrete nails, ensuring standardized stability and comparability across sites. Coral taxonomy and survival metrics were assessed using the Taxonomic Amalgamation Units (TAUs) system following Licuanan (2020), enabling consistent, resolution-appropriate comparisons.

One-year post-transplant survival remained consistently high despite environmental variability: 88.72% (38 TAUs; n=564) at L1, 88.06% (31 TAUs; n=579) at L2, and 95.64% (36 TAUs; n=459) at L3. The significant survival at L3 suggests site-specific conditions conducive to enhanced early transplant establishment. This multi-site, multi-year analysis demonstrates the robustness of a standardized transplant protocol across diverse reef environments and provides one of the first TAU-resolved comparative datasets for Mindanao. The findings emphasize the value of harmonized restoration techniques for scaling regional reef rehabilitation strategies amid accelerating climate and anthropogenic pressures.
c1bfd980: Coral reefs are increasingly compromised by local- and global-scale environmental stressors, but their persistence relies on successful reproduction, larval settlement and early post-settlement growth of reef-building corals. However, corals are highly sensitive to sedimentation, pollution and the growing frequency and severity of marine heatwaves, resulting in reduced growth, failed spawning cycles and mass mortality. In response, research teams globally are developing in situ and ex situ approaches to assist reproduction, enhance settlement, and improve early survivorship. A major bottleneck to upscaling interventions is settling free-swimming coral larvae onto transportable substrates for later out-planting. Coral larvae utilise a range of environmental cues, including chemical signals from crustose coralline algae and biofilms, and acoustic cues produced by fishes and invertebrates in healthy reef communities. Here, we present a study from Baa Atoll, Maldives, testing whether local healthy soundscape recordings can enhance coral larval settlement in in situ and ex situ contexts. Our ex situ experiment quantified settlement rates of nursery-spawned Leptoria phrygia larvae exposed to standard aquaculture noise, degraded-reef soundscapes or healthy-reef soundscapes. We observed a highly significant 112% increase in settlement on plugs under healthy-reef sound relative to controls. Our complementary in situ experiment compared settlement of naturally occurring larvae on pre-conditioned ceramic plugs deployed at three reef sites under paired acoustic-playback versus ambient-sound controls. Preliminary results indicate a 45% increase in total settlement on sound-enriched tables (248 recruits) compared with control tables (171 recruits). Outer-table zones exhibited notably greater settlement under sound treatments (162 recruits) relative to controls (55 recruits), suggesting a potential enhancement effect that has inspired further replication. Our results indicate that acoustic enrichment can support coral restoration initiatives, providing an active, economically viable and scalable technique to enhance settlement and contribute to local-scale biodiversity and habitat recovery.
d6863c0d: Active coral restoration is being increasingly used across the tropics to combat damage to reef ecosystems. However, most restoration projects to date have primarily used live coral cover to monitor their success, with few projects or studies investigating how reef fish communities respond to coral restoration. Reef fish, especially those closely associated with live coral, are vulnerable to disturbance and coral loss, making them essential indicators of restoration success. Recently, functional trait-based approaches have offered a framework for assessing ecological communities' responses to habitat change. Here, we evaluate reef fish community composition using a functional traits approach to determine the community-level impacts of coral restoration. Working on one of the world's largest coral restoration projects, located on Bontosua Island, South Sulawesi, Indonesia, we compared the composition of reef fish communities across healthy, degraded, and restored habitats. Our findings demonstrate that coral restoration has led to increases in coral cover, fish abundance, and the number of functional entities (FEs) of fish. Individual FEs exhibited varying abundance patterns across habitat types and restoration stages: some FEs showed consistent increases over time post-restoration, while others peaked at intermediate stages of restoration or declined over time. These varied responses highlight that restoration does not lead to positive responses from all fish functional groups, but creates ‘winners’ and ‘losers’, depending on species’ traits. Understanding these dynamics is essential for holistic evaluation of restoration success, and for designing strategies that help to restore diverse and functionally rich fish communities.
1dfb0089:
Long-term ecological monitoring is essential for evaluating the effectiveness of coral reef restoration, yet few projects in Indonesia have published outcomes beyond the first years of intervention. We present a seven-year assessment (2018–2025) of two restoration sites in the Nusa Penida Marine Protected Area, Bali, established on degraded reefs dominated by rubble. Restoration interventions included deployment of modular artificial reef structures, rubble stabilization, and transplantation of over 35 coral species. Project monitoring focused on both colony-level and landscape-level metrics, to monitor recovery trajectory of the restoration sites.

These projects provide rare long-term evidence that community-driven restoration, supported by citizen science monitoring, can achieve ecological recovery. Our findings contribute to restoration ecology by illustrating predictable recovery trajectories, highlighting the value of citizen science for sustained monitoring, and demonstrating how community-based approaches can simultaneously restore reef ecosystems and engage local stakeholders in conservation.
06b5f3c4: Widespread coral decline in the Maldives has reduced natural larval supply and compromised the recovery potential of degraded reefs across the country. Larval-based restoration offers a scalable and cost-effective alternative to conventional fragmentation-based methods. Here, we present the results of the first coordinated, low-cost, community-implemented larval-restoration effort in Huvadhu Atoll, supported by a CORDAP-funded project. In November 2025, eight consecutive nights of spawning surveys were conducted across southern Huvadhu reefs, following ongoing monitoring of the reproductive condition of tagged coral colonies during previous months. Three nights of multispecies Acropora spawning were recorded, including extensive releases from Acropora hyacinthus table colonies. Field assessments showed heterogeneous reproductive maturity among tagged colonies of Acropora c.f. gemmifera, with some colonies spawning over two nights, and other colonies retaining pigmented eggs after the spawning period. Longer-term monitoring of gravid A. tenuis colonies showed maturing eggs prior to the November spawning period recorded for other corals, and absence of eggs and sperm after this period, indicating that these colonies also spawned during this period. Spawned gametes were collected and transferred into an inflatable larval culture pool moored on sand near the reef, and larvae were reared until competent to settle. Dead coral pieces were collected from nearby reef areas and deployed into the larval pool as settlement substrates. After larvae had settled they were transferred to reef locations for ongoing monitoring of survival and growth of recruits. The successful trial of reef-based larval restoration in Huvadhu forms the foundation for expanded larval culture and settlement trials during upcoming extended coral spawning periods that characterize reef-coral reproductive patterns in the Maldives. This work also demonstrates the feasibility of integrating local divers, community partners, and council teams into an effective larval-restoration team on Huvadhu Atoll, which will support future larval restoration expansion in the southern Maldives.
8af2a717:
Coral nurseries have become one of the most widely used strategies for propagating asexually produced corals and supporting reef rehabilitation. Over the last two decades, these approaches have expanded beyond academic institutions to include a diverse set of restoration practitioners, particularly Indigenous and Afro-descendant community groups. For these communities, long-term success depends not only on traditional ecological knowledge but on sustained access to scientific training.
Between 2021 and 2023, Colombia’s national program Un Millón de Corales trained multiple coastal communities in microfragmentation and nursery management, who gained significant technical capacity but had limited success in advancing restoration to the outplanting and monitoring stages. In several cases, outplanted corals suffered high mortality due to gaps in species-specific site selection, ecological understanding, and adaptive management.
During a Caribbean coral identification workshop with community leaders, these capacity gaps were identified, leading to the co-design of an extended training program focused on the full coral restoration cycle. From July to December 2025, the program has been implemented with two local groups: Jardineros de Coral de Islas del Rosario and Un Coral Más Vivo, integrating hands-on instruction in fragmentation, coral growth in nurseries, outplanting protocols, ecological monitoring, and data recording. Workshops also addressed proposal development, governance competencies, and strategies for long-term financial sustainability.
Following implementation, both groups demonstrated improved nursery management, enhanced fragmentation success, better monitoring practices, and a clearer understanding of species-specific ecological requirements for outplanting. Community members began preparing independent project proposals and establishing partnerships to ensure continuity.
This case illustrates a scalable model for community empowerment that links technical restoration training with governance and institutional capacity-building. The outcomes contribute to broader conversations on how community-led initiatives can serve as leverage points for multi-scalar transformation, supporting more just, resilient, and culturally grounded approaches to coral reef conservation.
e46e24c0:
Tun Mustapha Park (TMP), Malaysia’s largest multiple-use marine protected area (898,762 ha), contains the country’s second-largest coral reef system but faces severe degradation from destructive fishing, overexploitation, and recurring bleaching events. These pressures have altered reef structure and function, threatening ecological integrity and the livelihoods of dependent coastal communities. In response, a large-scale ecological rehabilitation programme was initiated using the Mars Assisted Reef Restoration System (MARSS) to accelerate recovery at heavily damaged sites.

Across five restoration locations, 1,600 Reef Star frames were installed through a collaborative effort involving government agencies, NGOs, universities, tourism operators, and community-based organizations (CBOs). Ecological performance was assessed comparatively at restoration sites, degraded reference sites, and natural reef controls, focusing on live coral cover, substrate stabilization, reef fish abundance and biomass, and invertebrate density. Local community members actively participated in every stage of the monitoring process, including field data collection, preliminary analysis, post-survey discussions, and sharing of results during stakeholder workshops. This participatory approach ensured both scientific rigor and local ownership of restoration outcomes.

After two years, restoration sites demonstrated clear ecological improvements relative to degraded sites. Live coral cover increased by 11.5%–29.1%, supported by enhanced substrate stabilization as coral fragments fused across the structures. Fish assemblages showed marked recovery, with abundance and biomass increasing from 37.5 kg/ha to 146.4 kg/ha, approaching values observed at natural reef sites. Invertebrate densities particularly key benthic functional groups also showed upward trends, indicating strengthening ecosystem processes.

These results highlight that MARSS, combined with active community engagement, can restore structural complexity, accelerate biological recovery, and bridge the ecological gap between degraded and natural reef habitats. Through capacity building of over 250 stakeholders, TMP is emerging as a national model for integrated, science-driven, and community-supported coral reef restoration.
c8582a49:
Community-led conservation can offer vital leverage points for transforming coral reef governance across scales. In Mauritius’ Blue Bay Marine Park, Eco-Sud’s UNDP-funded coral restoration programme demonstrates how local knowledge, inclusive participation, and lived social realities can shape broader pathways for just and effective stewardship. The initiative engages vulnerable coastal households particularly women, who represent 67% of direct beneficiaries in all aspects of reef rehabilitation, from coral husbandry and nursery maintenance to the outplanting of approximately 20,000 coral fragments. Most participants are from low-income households reliant on informal or seasonal work, reflecting livelihood vulnerabilities common across reef-dependent regions in the Western Indian Ocean. Traditional ecological knowledge (TEK) played a defining role. Beneficiaries’ insights on historical species-specific coral presence, lagoon flushing, turbidity patterns, and areas with minimal boat traffic directly informed nursery siting and outplanting strategies. This integration of TEK and scientific practice exemplifies how place-based knowledge can inform adaptive, multi-scalar restoration planning.

Training and involvement fostered strong social outcomes: 87% of beneficiaries reported increased conservation awareness and confidence, even though material gains were limited, with only one household reporting a small income increase. Community perceptions reveal systemic stressors 28 of 30 beneficiaries observed long-term declines in both fish availability and coral cover, while major livelihood constraints included decreasing fish stocks (29 responses), lack of alternative employment (29), inadequate infrastructure (22), coral degradation (21), and high input prices (15). By linking community experience to wider patterns of vulnerability, agency, and ecosystem change, the Blue Bay initiative illustrates how locally grounded restoration can “sense the system,” generate insights relevant to regional policy, and contribute to emerging global narratives on justice-centered, community-driven reef governance.

Keywords: coral restoration, inclusive community-led coral restoration, livelihood survey, traditional ecological knowledge
71101932: Coral reefs in the Anthropocene have experienced rapid and unprecedented changes on a global scale. In the Caribbean and the Dominican Republic, Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) outbreaks and the 2023-2024 bleaching events produced profound impacts in coral communities. Specifically, in Punta Cana (FPC), live coral cover decreased by 90-95%. This situation has prompted Fundación Puntacana to strengthen its conservation efforts through a restoration program. We have developed and implemented technologies (e.g. QR code for monitoring ex situ nurseries, the use of biodegradable nets for outplanting, and Structure from Motion)  with multiple partners, and monitoring protocols have been implemented both ex situ and in situ to maintain scleractinian corals. Since 2022, our land-based nursery has safeguarded 233 genotypes of corals and produced 17,900 fragments, which are monitored daily to ensure their health and growth.  FPC also incorporated a harvesting program for Diadema antillarum and Mithraculus sculptus to enhance herbivory in our restoration sites. More than 70 D. antillarum recruits and approximately 10 pairs of M. sculptus have been collected, with gravid females and larvae observed. These organisms are used in the ex situ nursery and later introduced into coral outplant sites to help control algae overgrowth. Between 2023 and 2024, 4,811 coral fragments were outplanted into degraded reefs. A 52% survival rate has been recorded in our experimental plots, with Orbicella faveolata standing out at 85%. Assisted sexual propagation has also been successfully incorporated into our program with 278,844 Acropora palmata coral spats produced in the lab and outplanted to the in situ nursery.  Our coral restoration program illustrates the importance of combining different approaches to harvest and outplant corals and other reef organisms and the relevance of multidisciplinary partnerships in adapting to uncontrolled stressors that coral reefs are facing .
db42af14:
Building capacity within local communities is increasingly recognized as essential for effective coral reef conservation, particularly in regions where institutional support is limited or inconsistent. In 2025, Corales de Paz, in collaboration with local and international partners, strengthened this approach with the community of Bocachica, an island in Cartagena Bay (Colombian Caribbean). The initiative developed diving, monitoring, and restoration skills among five community members, beginning with SCUBA diving and buoyancy control training, followed by Reef Check EcoDiver certification and instruction in the MARRS restoration method. Participants subsequently joined the active restoration program at Varadero Reef, near Tierra Bomba Island.

Varadero Reef, discovered in 2014 and designated as a Mission Blue Hope Spot in 2018, persists despite intense sedimentation, pollution, and its location within a major navigation channel. Its resilience is challenged by fishing pressures and historical destructive practices, highlighting the need for active intervention. Using the MARRS method, Corales de Paz and the newly trained community members deployed 400 Reef Stars, restoring 400 m² of degraded reef. Six-month monitoring shows approximately 90% coral survival, demonstrating the ecological resilience of Varadero and the effectiveness of locally grounded restoration stewardship. 

Beyond technical capacity building, the program also fostered broader community engagement. An educational workshop reached 200 children in Bocachica, promoting early awareness of coral reef ecology and cultivating future local custodians. Recognizing the importance of diversifying sustainable livelihoods, the project is also piloting a guided diving experience led by the trained community members, creating opportunities in ecotourism and positioning the restoration site as an attractive location for aquatic activities. 

Together, these efforts demonstrate how community-led capacities, ecological restoration, and livelihood diversification can intersect to support long-term, just, and locally grounded coral reef stewardship. This model highlights the potential for place-based initiatives to strengthen socio-ecological resilience and transformative reef conservation at multiple scales.
d62d1901: Tun Mustapha Park (TMP) is Malaysia’s largest multi-purpose marine park, covering more than 898,000ha at the northern tip of Borneo. Despite the early wins for TMP, the threats from illegal fishing, particularly fish bombing, remain an ongoing challenge with 1,495 blasts per annum on average recorded endangering coral reefs and coastal livelihoods. In this regard, the Banggi Coral Conservation Society (BCCS), a community-based not-for-profit organization, emerged as a local partner in TMP protection. BCCS are beneficiaries of specialized capacity building in terms of being appointed as Honorary Wildlife Warden with the Sabah Wildlife Department, honorary Park Rangers with Sabah Parks and training as Mars Assisted Reef Restoration System (MARRS) Practitioners. These roles give the village some official power, as well as technical know-how, in working to enforce regulations and restore their reef. Building on this capacity, BCCS leads and supports coral restoration using reef stars, coral reef surveys, anti-marine crime patrolling focused on fish bombing and fish poisoning, and fish stock assessments to monitor ecological trends. These activities are part of the co-management process in BMMA and TMP, led by the District Office and Sabah Parks (BMMA) as well as Sabah Parks respectively, that create a space for community representation in planning together with government authorities. Outcome results prove the success of this community-based approach. 2023 blast fishing incidents are 54% lower than the 2019 baseline, demonstrating a heightened de- Terrence and compliance. Concurrently, 710 reef stars have been planted out to restoration sites with coral survivorship of 85.5–92.9% and a projected ~25% return in coral coverage. This case highlights how empowering a community-led organization within formal management systems can reduce marine crime and accelerate coral reef recovery in a large, multi-use marine protected area.
ee62b70b: The conservation of sharks, rays, and skates (elasmobranchs) has often been framed through a dichotomy opposing marine ecosystem integrity and the subsistence of coastal communities. Here, we propose a conceptual framework ultimately articulating the global significance of elasmobranchs across three interrelated domains: (1) as sources of heritage, (2) exploited species, and (3) in ecological functioning. The heritage domain, encompassing values and relationships that are meaningful to Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities (IPLCs), remains largely overlooked in conservation research and management. Our framework highlights the porous boundaries between these domains, positioning elasmobranchs as boundary objects. We argue that, in many coral reef social-ecological systems, elasmobranchs operate as biocultural keystone species, central to both ecological processes and cultural continuity. We illustrate this argument through a two-year study conducted in the southwestern Indian Ocean, which examines the Local Ecological Knowledge of artisanal fishers on the islands of Mayotte, Comoros, and Nosy Be (Madagascar) regarding elasmobranchs. Such holistic approaches, which integrate diverse knowledge systems and reciprocities, are essential to anchoring transformative and socially just coral reef conservation.
6025fb20:
In French Polynesia, the rāhui is an ancestral practice involving the temporary prohibition of access to a space or resource. Since the 2000s, this practice has been reappropriated in a hybrid arrangements that combine Indigenous knowledge and principles with public marine management and conservation policies. In this context, the rāhui appears to partially embody features associated with Social-Ecological Keystone Places (SEKP), positioned at the critical intersection of ecological function, cultural significance, and the empowerment of Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities (IPLC). This study examines whether the rāhui truly operates as a SEKP, and under which social, cultural, and ecological conditions.
The analysis draws on comparative surveys conducted in multiple islands of French Polynesia integrating ethnographic and ecological datasets. In several cases, the rāhui exhibits characteristics associated with SEKP status: empirical evidence of ecological recovery, socially recognized spillover effects, and the central role of culturally and economically important species such as giant clams, crabs, or octopuses, which can act as both ecological and cultural keystones.
However, a generalized classification of rāhui as SEKP is limited. The degree of cultural anchoring varies widely across islands, ranging from strong customary legitimacy to more instrumental uses of the term within public management frameworks. This creates a paradox: the widespread promotion of “rāhui” enhances its visibility locally and internationally, yet may simultaneously dilute cultural knowledge and weaken traditional foundations. Moreover, heterogeneity in governance structures, objectives, and levels of participation reveals issues of social equity, particularly related to representation and leadership within communities that influence the inclusiveness of rāhui governance. Overall, rāhui can only function as SEKP under conditions where cultural legitimacy, ecological objectives, and governance inclusivity are aligned. The findings highlight the need for a context-specific approach that recognizes the diversity of rāhui practices and their varying capacity to function as key socio-ecological sites.
64605163: Native forage fish species in Hawai‘i estuaries historically provided a valuable fishing bait and food resource that supported subsistence and commercial based fisheries. Following nearly a century of commercial exploitation, the conservation status of these species remains uncertain due to a range of concurrent threats including habitat loss and introduced species competition. Declines in native forage fish populations observed by local fishermen have generated concerns in communities where these fish once provided an important fishing resource spanning generations. This presentation will highlight ongoing efforts to preserve traditional fishing knowledge and assess the dietary composition of the Hawaiian endemic species known as nehu (Encrasicholina purpurea), and ‘iao (Atherinomorus insularum) in addition to an introduced species known as goldspot herring (Herklotsichthys quadrimaculatus). Forage fish diets were characterized using DNA metabarcoding techniques and compared to identify potential competitive interactions among the three species which were collected from historically important baiting areas on the islands of O‘ahu, Maui and Hawai‘i. Gathering the perspectives of fishers, scientists and Hawaiian cultural practitioners provided additional insights related to the timing, scale and severity of threats believed to negatively impact native forage fish survival. Outcomes from this research aim to inform the co-development of management recommendations to support the replenishment of native forage fish populations and improve our current understanding of food web dynamics in Hawai'i estuaries.
c5e19c7f:
Increasingly frequent marine heatwaves have escalated the prevalence and extent of mass coral bleaching events. Global-scale coral bleaching events (GCBE) can unfold when marine heatwaves impact reefs across all tropical ocean basins within a common period. Here, we define an objective, quantifiable index to compare periods of satellite-derived coral heat stress accumulation globally from 1985–2025 and examine their relationship to confirmed GCBEs. We uncover an uninterrupted and ongoing period of global coral heat stress from 2018 to 2025, affecting an unprecedented 87% of reef areas globally. The average intensity has also been record-setting, with the median global heat stress accumulation on reef areas nearly 50% greater than the previous GCBE record. This is largely driven by general ocean warming, as recent baseline global surface ocean temperatures (2019-2023) are comparable to extreme anomalous conditions observed ~25 years ago. The development of a strong El Niño event in 2023, superimposed upon the already anomalously warm ocean, culminated in the 4th GCBE, the most extensive and intensive on record. Qualitative observations of coral bleaching have been reported from 84 countries spanning all coral-containing ocean basins. Further, an analysis of the extent and magnitude of heat stress suggests that bleaching may have impacted 98 of the 102 countries with coral reefs. GCBE4 (2023–present) marks a period of heat stress that is unprecedented by every investigated metric. However, global-scale coral bleaching-level heat stress has persisted for almost the entirety of the last decade, bringing many reef areas into an era of near-annual bleaching. 
Disclaimer: The scientific results and conclusions, as well as any views or opinions expressed herein, are those of the author(s) and do not necessarily reflect the views of NOAA or the Department of Commerce.
ad958734: Ocean warming has accelerated the disturbance regime across the Great Barrier Reef (GBR), producing an unprecedented sequence of six widespread mass coral bleaching events since 2016. The 2024 marine heatwave was the most intense in the observational record, exposing reefs across all three regions of the GBR to prolonged temperature stress causing bleaching and mortality.
