Yet, the extent to which thermal exposure, depth, and community composition influence bleaching severity, and subsequent impacts to the reef community through change in coral cover and composition, remains poorly quantified across large reef ecosystems. Here, we integrate more than 260 in-water surveys spanning all three GBR regions with Bayesian hierarchical modelling to quantify taxon-specific thermal susceptibility across depths and regions. We also used this approach to build site-level bleaching susceptibility and coral mortality indices that capture reef community sensitivity to bleaching related mortality as function of local taxa thermal thresholds and abundance patterns across the ecosystem. Together, these analyses allow us to identify how exposure to variation in thermal heat stress (eg. NOAA Degree Heating Week stress accumulation) interacts with taxonomic composition across a range of habitats and depths during the 2024 global bleaching event, and how these interactions translate into patterns of recovery or impacts to the ecosystem. Using these relationships, we develop a predictive framework capable of identifying risk of bleaching impacts across the GBR based on community composition from long-term monitoring programs prior to the next heatwave. Finally, we compare the susceptibility patterns observed in 2024 with those from the 2016–2017 bleaching events to assess if repeated heatwaves have reshaped the thermal tolerance of coral assemblages through time. Our results provide a new, quantitative approach for identifying GBR locations at highest risk during future marine heatwaves and reveal how community composition mediates ecosystem-scale bleaching impacts under accelerating ocean warming.
8ac04781: Above average ocean temperatures leading to marine heatwaves have implications for many marine ecosystems. The impacts felt include mass coral bleaching and mortality, altered aquaculture yields, changes in wild fish migration patterns, and marine ecosystem deterioration. Impacts are primarily related to marine heatwave severity and duration. Seasonal forecasts of marine heatwave risk covering timescales from weeks to seasons from dynamical ocean-atmosphere models can be very useful tools for marine managers, businesses and researchers. The Australian Bureau of Meteorology’s seasonal forecast model ACCESS-S2 currently produces operational real-time forecasts of sea surface and subsurface temperatures. In partnership with CSIRO, marine heatwave metrics such as and probabilities of exceeding the 90th percentile derived from subseasonal to seasonal forecasts are now operational. These new products will complement existing operational accumulated thermal stress forecast products developed for coral bleaching to improve both the understanding and the management of MHW events.
3d69b019:
In 2023 and 2024, coral reefs in the Caribbean experienced consecutive, record-setting marine heatwaves. The spatial extent and severity of marine heatwaves on coral reefs are commonly assessed using satellite sea surface temperatures (SST) and heat stress accumulation metrics provided by NOAA’s Coral Reef Watch (CRW) program. While critical for understanding the nature and evolution of marine heatwaves at synoptic scales, SST-only algorithms consist of smoothed and gap-filled satellite data over relatively large areas (usually much larger than the data grid size of 5km x 5km) and provide one temperature estimate per day at the surface sub-skin layer of the ocean (~20μm). Therefore, SST-based algorithms do not capture small-scale temperature dynamics over complex reef environments, especially at coral-relevant depths, where wind stress, surface stratification, internal waves, tides, upwelling, bathymetry, and reef composition and structure can all influence the severity and extent of coral’s response to heat stress (i.e., bleaching and mortality). Here, we compile in situ temperature data at the surface (~1m) and at depth (1–15m) from several monitoring programs across the Caribbean to compare with CRW’s SST and Degree Heating Week (DHW) metric during the 2023 and 2024 marine heatwaves. Further, we explore patterns in coral bleaching and mortality to identify areas where DHW algorithms over or under predict heat stress, and compare these with in situ temperatures to better understand regions of high diurnal temperature variability. Incorporating in situ temperatures at depth to offset heat stress accumulation derived from satellites at the ocean’s surface will ultimately improve CRW algorithm interpretation and performance.

Disclaimer: The scientific results and conclusions, as well as any views or opinions expressed herein, are those of the author(s) and do not necessarily reflect the views of NOAA or the Department of Commerce.
6dbcf92e:
Satellite sea surface temperature (SST) observations are essential for understanding ocean conditions, weather patterns and marine ecosystems. The NOAA Geo-Polar Blended SST (GPB) is a daily global dataset at 0.05º resolution, widely used in ocean forecasting, storm tracking, fisheries management and coral reef monitoring. This study focuses on the nighttime-only near real-time subset of GPB, which underpins NOAA Coral Reef Watch’s bleaching monitoring tools. 

The GPB integrates observations from multiple polar-orbiting and geostationary satellites. Uncertainties arise from differences in measurement capabilities and processing algorithms across satellite data sources, as well as from the gap-filling technique used to produce a gap-free global field. These uncertainties can affect the reliability of SST values and, in turn, influence the reliability of coral bleaching alerts.

Here, I present ongoing efforts to evaluate the reliability of the nighttime near real-time GPB dataset. Specifically, I analyse key diagnostic variables available within the processing system, including but not limited to the quantity of measured temperature values from geostationary and polar-orbiting satellites (i.e., local data density); the spatial scale over which the gap-filling algorithm operates (which vary in space and time); and the recent temporal variability in SST. These variables form the foundation of a new reliability metric designed to highlight areas and periods of higher and lower confidence in the SST values. This work will support improved bleaching predictions to provide more informed decisions for coral reef conservation.
76060d70: Intensifying thermal extremes are pushing reef-building corals beyond their physiological limits, leading to frequent mass bleaching events. Most established coral bleaching prediction tools rely on empirical thermal thresholds and have been effective in monitoring bleaching risk regionally and globally. However, coral bleaching is a result of multiple interacting physiological mechanisms influenced by additional environmental factors beyond temperature such as light irradiance, water quality, and prey availability. As a result, approaches considering only temperature extremes could raise false alarms or miss mass events. To that end, we propose and evaluate the applicability of a mechanistic bioenergetics model for predicting coral bleaching. Specifically, we implemented a coral–symbiont Dynamic Energy Budget (DEB) model in the Red Sea, forcing it with regional environmental data from satellite observations and high-resolution hydrodynamic–biogeochemical models to simulate coral bleaching in relation to temperature, light, nutrient supply, and prey availability. The model performance was assessed against in situ bleaching observations from multiple years in the Red Sea, including the major 2015 event and the unprecedented 2024 bleaching event that affected historically resistant northern reefs. The DEB model showed strong agreement with observed bleaching patterns and successfully distinguished bleaching from non-bleaching conditions. Basin-scale simulations reproduced the spatial distribution of bleaching in 2015, which, although severe, remained largely restricted to the central and southern Red Sea, as well as the 2024 event which extended to the northern Red Sea. Model diagnostics reveal region-specific mechanisms: in the less turbid, oligotrophic northern waters, elevated light intensity lowers the thermal threshold for bleaching, whereas southern corals tolerate longer heat exposure due to reduced light levels and higher nutrient availability in the water column. The results demonstrate the capacity of physiology-based multi-stressor models to capture real-world bleaching dynamics and identify the mechanisms shaping regional vulnerability, highlighting their added value alongside established prediction tools.
dfabb718: Genotypic diversity leads to increased resistance to disease in coral populations. However, it is unknown how increased genotypic diversity influences coral responses to other important global stressors, such as high temperatures. One underlying mechanism underlying increased resilience under stress is contributions from the coral microbiome. We hypothesize that when corals are in diverse mixtures, their microbiomes shift towards increased bacteria associated with thermal resistance. We tested how genetic diversity influences coral growth, thermal stress, and microbial responses in a coral nursery in Rarotonga, Cook Islands.  We arranged genets of Acropora hyacinthus corals from five sites around the island of Rarotonga in monocultures (single genotypes) and polycultures (mixtures of genotypes). Corals grew in these arrangements for one year, and following one year, they were exposed to an acute heat stress experiment. Our results suggest that the net biodiversity effects of polycultures depend on coral sensitivity to thermal stress: sensitive corals grow more in polyculture. For most coral responses (e.g., physiological characteristics, such as lipid content, carbohydrate content), the effects of polyculture depended on genotype. Continuing work will assess if mixtures of coral genotypes lead to a) an increase in thermal stress resistance and b) convergent microbial communities associated with lower thermal stress. Our results provide insight into whether population diversity can buffer thermally sensitive corals to stress, and if a mechanism underlying this response is due to adaptive shifts in the coral microbiome. We expect this research to inform coral restoration practices by quantifying the individual, population, and ecosystem benefits associated with increasing coral genotypic diversity.
f3a367a1: Coral reefs are threatened by rising ocean temperatures which continue to degrade reef ecosystem structure and function. Reefs in Okinawa, Japan maintain high coral biodiversity and cover, providing an ideal place to assess interspecific trait variation. Trait based analyses to predict heat stress-resistance are key to projecting coral survival, and thus future reef community structure and dynamics. In this study, we examined interspecific variation in thermal performance and physiology of ten coral species from Okinawa, Japan. We utilized thermal performance curves (TPCs) of coral metabolism (respiration and photosynthesis) to directly measure functional coral stress tolerance. Furthermore, we quantified differences in holobiont physiology including chlorophyll a concentration and host protein content for all species, and tissue biomass and symbiont density for seven species. We then correlated these metrics with coral thermal performance to assess potential physiological drivers of thermal performance. We showed variation across species for both bulk physiological metrics and thermal performance. Dominant reef builders such as Porites rus consistently had the highest bulk physiology values and metabolic rates while thinner, plating corals such as Montipora aequituberculata displayed lower physiological and thermal performance metrics. Notably, corals Favites complanata and Montipora vietnamensis maintained the highest thermal optima across our dataset. By linking physiological traits to thermal performance, we identified tissue biomass as a key physiological predictor of coral thermal optima. These findings highlight interspecific variability in coral responses to thermal stress. Furthermore, these results allow us to understand which coral species may survive under rapidly increasing temperatures, and are thus targets for restoration as they are likely to persist under future climate change. We advocate for incorporating interspecific differences in stress tolerance and physiology into targeted management and restoration strategies for coral reefs.
ea808f4e: Community diversity within natural ecosystems can enhance ecosystem stability and resilience in the face of disturbances and environmental variability. On coral reefs, biodiversity may be impacted by thermal anomalies, resulting in shifts in community composition and ecosystem functioning. Seasonal storm activity, while potentially damaging to coral reef structure, can help reduce sea surface temperatures (SST) within coral thermal tolerance ranges in summer months. Okinawa, Japan, a region that typically benefits from cooling by seasonal typhoons, experienced unusually high SST on coastal coral reefs in 2024 due to storm trajectories shifting northward, leading to a widespread coral bleaching event. In this study, we tested the physiological response (gross photosynthesis [GP] and respiration [R]) of coral reef communities to these elevated SST to understand the impact of thermal anomalies on common coral species as well as the potential buffering effect of community diversity on physiological performance. In August 2025, we collected duplicate fragments from 10 common coral species near the Okinawa Institute of Science and Technology Marine Science Station to measure the paired individual rates of GP and R. We then placed those 10 individuals in community assemblages to measure paired community physiological rates across the two temperature treatments. GP was greater in elevated temperatures for Acropora sp., however fully assembled communities showed no temperature effect on the GP rates, indicating that community diversity may buffer the effects of elevated SST on coral reefs. Meanwhile, R was augmented by elevated temperatures for Porites rus and Favites complanata. Despite only two species displaying this trend, assembled communities also showed increased R under elevated temperatures, suggesting uneven species contributions to ecosystem metabolism when scaling from individual fragments to community level.
83e3cdaa: Two habitat-forming groups, scleractinian corals and crustose coralline algae (CCA), underpin the structure and function of coral reefs, yet both are increasingly threatened by climate-driven stressors. Marine heatwaves are well-established triggers of bleaching, but ocean acidification (OA) has historically been reported to induce bleaching under certain conditions. However, the physiological pathways through which decreased seawater pH influence bleaching are complex and may differ from initial expectations. This study investigates whether OA directly induces coral and coralline algal bleaching, and whether any observed bleaching is instead driven by exposure to elevated temperature, high light, and reduced pH. An eight-week multistressor experiment was conducted at the Australian Institute of Marine Science (AIMS) National Sea Simulator (SeaSim) facility, in which four pH levels spanning near-future projections to extreme low-pH conditions were crossed with high- and low-light regimes and maintained at either ambient or elevated temperatures. This design enabled us to evaluate whether OA can induce bleaching independently of thermal stress or in combination with high light across multiple coral and CCA species. We assessed bleaching and stress physiology using high-resolution photophysiological measurements, calcification-related analyses (including Raman spectroscopy), and a novel application of NDVI to quantify bleaching in both corals and CCA. Together, these complementary approaches provide a mechanistic perspective on how these taxa respond across gradients of temperature, light, and pH, with a focus on distinguishing stressor-specific versus shared bleaching responses. These findings refine our understanding of the environmental conditions that initiate bleaching and alter key physiological functions in corals and CCA, providing context for interpreting early shifts in reef condition and offering actionable insight for reef management.
b0ca9ac0: Global climate change has led to the ever-present and increasing threat of thermal stress on coral reefs. Thermal stress can induce numerous physiological changes in the coral holobiont, but how these changes might manifest in the surrounding water column remains an open question. Recent mesocosm experiments suggest that thermally stressed corals alter the adjacent water column by releasing compositionally distinct, labile dissolved organic matter (DOM), which then enriches copiotrophic and putatively pathogenic bacterioplankton. This mechanism could further harm stressed corals and act as an early indicator of thermal stress in the water column. However, the relevance of this mechanism in situ has yet to be determined. To address this knowledge gap, we sampled the water column of a patch reef in Kāne‘ohe Bay, O‘ahu, Hawaii, throughout a thermal anomaly, collecting data on microbial biomass, community structure (16S rDNA amplicons), dissolved organic carbon (DOC), and DOM composition (untargeted metabolomics via LC-MS/MS). We collected temporally resolved depth profiles across the reef and explicitly sampled the boundary layer of corals throughout the thermal anomaly. Depth profiles indicated that thermal stress induced a pulse of compositionally distinct, labile DOC from the benthos that increased DOC concentrations, altered water column DOM composition, increased microbial loads, and restructured bacterioplankton communities. Boundary-layer sampling further supported this, showing that thermal stress yielded elevated Gibbs energies of oxidation and nitrogen content of metabolites in the coral boundary layer. Interestingly, longitudinal samples of the coral boundary layer across an entire year revealed a time lag between DOM and bacterioplankton shifts. Changes in DOM occurred immediately at the start of bleaching, while microbial community shifts manifested weeks after peak reef-wide bleaching, suggesting that DOM changes, rather than bacterioplankton community shifts, are more useful as early-warning indicators of reefs undergoing thermal stress.
b43ee455:
In coral colonies, the combination of highly variable reef conditions and the intense metabolic activity of the host and its symbionts creates steep microscale gradients within a single colony. Exposed branch tips experience rapid fluctuations, whereas the sheltered interior remains more stable but weakly ventilated. These intracolonial contrasts likely structure spatial heterogeneity in coral physiology, yet the mechanistic links remain poorly understood.
In this study, we investigate how corals exchange compounds with the surrounding seawater across different microenvironments and flow conditions. These exchanges, collectively referred to as mass transfer, are bidirectional, from the release of oxidative stress molecules to the uptake of oxygen and nutrients. These are shaped by both ambient flow and ciliary activity, which drive active transport. We quantify mass transfer using complementary approaches: Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) is used to resolve flow fields and characterize cilia-driven transport, which, when combined with chemical sensing through sensPIV (Ahmerkamp et al., 2022), reveals oxygen distributions and quantifies oxygen-related fluxes across microenvironments. We then integrate these experimental data into a 3D numerical model to test how flow conditions and ciliary activity influence mass-transfer efficiency across the colony. We hypothesize that ciliary activity compensates for reduced flow in sheltered intracolonial regions, thereby mitigating limitations in mass transfer relative to more exposed areas.
By resolving these microscale transport processes, our study advances our understanding of how coral colonies function as spatially heterogeneous systems and highlights the importance of considering ciliary flow to understand their performance. 


Ahmerkamp, S. et al. (2022). Simultaneous visualization of flow fields and oxygen concentrations to unravel transport and metabolic processes in biological systems. Cell Reports Methods, 2(5).
69f6828a: Past and ongoing nutrient enrichment is a major stressor of coral reefs throughout the Caribbean. Microbial community shifts have been proposed as early warning signals of reef eutrophication and subsequent reef degradation. We evaluated this potential by assessing relationships between inorganic nutrient concentrations in the water column and microbial communities in sediment and seawater at multiple sites along the coasts of Bonaire, Saba, and St. Eustatius, Caribbean Netherlands. At each site we measured dissolved nitrogen and phosphorus on a bi-weekly basis over two years. Sediment and seawater samples were collected at the beginning of the nutrient monitoring scheme for characterization of the microbial communities through metabarcoding. We assessed the correlation of microbial community structure to both long-term average concentrations and those measured at the time of sampling (concurrent). Sediment microbial communities showed significant correlations with average PO4, NH4, and NO3 concentrations on all islands, accounting for greater variation in community structure than concurrent nutrient measurements. Seawater microbial communities correlated only to average and concurrent NH4 concentrations. At the island level, indicator species analysis revealed that microbial communities in both sediment and water from Bonaire were characterized by taxa previously associated with coral-dominated reefs (Alphaproteobacteria and Cyanobacteria), reflecting the island’s dominant benthic communities. In contrast, the microbial communities of Saba and St. Eustatius, which have low or no coral cover, were characterized by taxa associated with algae-dominated reefs (Gammaproteobacteria, Bacteroidota, and Firmicutes). Our results, which integrate concomitant sampling of microbial communities and dissolved inorganic nutrient concentrations, support sediment microbial communities as a time-integrated indicator of nutrient concentrations on reefs.
b6062eee: Marine pollution is understudied throughout the Pacific Islands but is of critical importance to island nations that traditionally rely on fish as their primary source of protein. To better understand the distribution of contaminants in fishes throughout the Pacific, we conducted a systematic review of studies reporting pollutant concentrations. Of the 5405 studies examined, 44 met criteria for inclusion in the systematic review, which spanned 226 species from 115 locations representing 66 taxonomic families and six trophic levels. Results of the systematic review are contextualized with targeted studies measuring contaminant concentrations in fish from 20 sites in Hawaiʻi and six atolls in the Republic of the Marshall Islands. These locations have been impacted by marine pollution from historic and ongoing military activities, urbanization, and agriculture. Sites were chosen that vary in land use and human population. Metals, pesticides, polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) were examined in reef fishes from multiple trophic levels. The taxa included are commonly consumed throughout the Pacific Islands and have important cultural, economic, and nutritional value. Of the pollutants examined, metals and PCBs were most frequently found at elevated concentrations. Though there was evidence of biomagnification to higher trophic levels for certain contaminants, other pollutants (e.g., arsenic and PCBs) were found in both lower and higher trophic level taxa. These case studies and the systematic review provide baseline information that will aid in the development of fisheries management guidelines and recommendations, and they contribute to a data gap that persists for marine pollution data throughout the Pacific Islands.
6add3e47:
Land-based sediment pollution is one of the primary local stressors contributing to coral reef decline worldwide. Sediment pollution alters chemical, physical, biological, and ecological processes in reef systems, and interacts synergistically with other local and global disturbances. Managing sediment pollution is therefore essential for enhancing reef health and supporting their recovery ability after disturbance events. Recent international initiatives have increasingly highlighted the urgency for managing and reducing local pollution to ensure the long-term persistence and resilience of coral reefs in the changing climate. However, there are major gaps in knowledge and information that hinder the ability to meet these targets, particularly in data-poor regions that host most of the world’s coral reefs. These gaps include limited understanding of how specific land-use land-cover (LULC) changes drive sediment pollution, their trajectories over time, and their spatial relationship with and subsequent implications for coral reefs. These gaps result in managers not having the appropriate spatially and temporally-explicit information, and tools to design and implement the integrated land-sea management required to safeguard coral reefs from land-based sediment pollution.

This study presents a global, watershed-level assessment of LULC changes and associated sediment export from over 21,000 watersheds draining into coral reef habitats from 2000 to 2020. Using harmonized global datasets and spatially explicit sediment load modelling, we quantify spatial and temporal trends in watershed sediment export and identify regions where landscape transitions (i.e., deforestation, agricultural expansion, and urbanization) pose the greatest threats to these ecosystems. The products of this study provide information on sediment export dynamics and highlight priority areas where targeted land-based management can substantially reduce sediment pollution exposure to coral reefs. Additionally, by integrating LULC trajectories with sediment export modelling at global, yet locally applicable scales, this research provides critical spatial and temporal information to guide integrated land-sea management.
4582953f:
Sediment runoff is a globally increasing threat to coral reefs. However, vulnerable nearshore reefs often function as critical nursery habitats. We investigated the impact of runoff on recruitment patterns, body condition, and growth of two common herbivores: the parrotfishes Chlorurus spilurus and the surgeonfish Acanthurus triostegus. Abundance was quantified along a gradient from sediment-impacted fringing reefs to clear-water offshore reefs. Additionally, Length-Weight Relationships (LWR) were modeled for juvenile C. spilurus and A. triostegus, and otolith-based age determination was conducted for C. spilurus.
Parrotfish recruits (<5cm) occurred in significantly higher densities on disturbed reefs, whereas larger size classes dominated offshore sites. For C. spilurus, while body condition (LWR) remained constant across sites, otolith analysis revealed significantly suppressed growth rates in high-runoff areas (p < 0.05). Conversely, A. triostegus juveniles were abundant at disturbed sites but displayed a distinct ontogenetic shift: small recruits were heavier than those at control sites, but exhibited significantly lower weight gain with growth (negative allometry).
These results suggest that nutrients from runoff create a high-density but low-quality nursery ("junk food" hypothesis). Species adopt contrasting trade-offs to cope with nutritional stress: C. spilurus sacrifices growth speed to maintain condition, whereas A. triostegus sacrifices condition to prioritize size. Preserving connectivity between these productive but stressful nurseries and offshore adult habitats is essential in sediment-impacted seascapes.
0dd64012: Sedimentation from land-based runoff, erosion, and resuspension affects multiple aspects of coral reef health and ecosystem function. With global declines in coral cover, coral restoration has become a key strategy to restore reef resilience, yet how sedimentation influences restoration success and early recruitment remains unclear. We conducted a year-long, multi-metric study at Olowalu Reef, a 939-acre site along West Maui that seeds local and neighboring reefs, to evaluate how spatial variability in sediment exposure influences benthic communities, coral outplant performance, and natural recruitment. We outplanted Porites rus and Porites compressa fragments and placed ceramic recruitment domes at twenty stations spanning two depth zones and a sedimentation gradient. Continuous water quality (light, temperature, salinity, turbidity) and quarterly sediment deposition captured chronic background conditions and episodic sedimentation events. Photogrammetry and benthic surveys quantified partial mortality, growth, survivorship, and coral cover. This integrated design allowed us to assess how chronic and event-driven sediment dynamics shape reef communities. Water quality monitoring showed that sedimentation was primarily driven by resuspension during the summer high-wave season rather than episodic runoff during the winter rainy season. High attrition of coral outplants occurred throughout the study, likely due to the site’s energetic environment and limited post-fragmentation recovery periods. Coral recruitment exceeded expectations, with abundance and early growth more strongly influenced by microhabitat on recruitment domes (protected vs. exposed surfaces) than station-level sediment exposure. These results highlight the importance of fine-scale flow and deposition processes when identifying ecologically meaningful sediment limits. Together, these datasets provide a foundation for developing water-quality thresholds that account for spatial variability and episodic sediment events, key considerations for aligning ridge-to-reef management strategies. Our approach offers a replicable framework for co-developing sediment targets with managers and community partners and can guide where coral restoration and erosion-reduction efforts can enhance local reef resilience and habitat quality.
9e475f54:
Upland restoration, particularly through the removal of excess ungulates, has been identified as a crucial strategy for improving upland vegetation and reducing sedimentation in coastal areas. However, quantitative data on how upland restoration affects vegetation cycles and drought resilience have been limited by the computational power needed to investigate these factors seasonally over years and decades, and in relation to key restoration actions on land.
Using new remote sensing tools combined with available rain data, stream discharge, and downstream sediment observations, we analyzed changes in vegetation cover over time, drought indices, and their relationship to downstream coral reef systems. We calculated the Normalized Vegetation Difference Index (NVDI) for three central Hawaiian islands that are subject to drought and ungulate pressure, rainfall rates and totals, and drought indicators in three-month intervals between 2000 and 2024, considering both annual and decadal weather patterns. We analyzed sediment load in streams where possible, and incorporated coastal sedimentation data from south shore Moloka’i, Lana’i and Olowalu Maui in relation to upland fire and ungulates. Lastly, we used coastal water quality data to look at long term (decadal) trends in salinity, temperature, turbidity and sedimentation rates.
Our findings indicate that cycles of drought and flood are significantly impacting the management of the Maui Nui watersheds for coastal health. Coastal waters are increasing in salinity (average of 34.3 ppt to 35.6 ppt), and turbidity (2.9 FNU to 3.3 FNU) in conjunction with less rainfall and vegetation and increased deer pressure.Measures to manage ungulates, especially during drought years, might be necessary to reduce the risk of sediment loading to nearshore areas. These findings are considered in relation to other ongoing efforts to reduce erosion and maintain vegetation in the new climate eras in dryland Moloka’i, Lana’i, and Maui.
e9fc5f2a: Coral bleaching is widely recognized as a heat-induced stress response under elevated temperature and light. However, bleaching can also occur under severe light limitation, leading to the loss of coral symbionts and pigmentation. Despite strong mechanistic understanding from experimental studies, mass bleaching under low-light conditions has rarely been documented in the field. Here, we report community-scale dark bleaching on an equatorial reef during a turbidity event that occurred under normal thermal conditions. Measurements of seawater optical constituents revealed high chromophoric dissolved organic matter (CDOM) and fine suspended sediments prior to bleaching. These conditions compressed the euphotic zone to ~5 m depth and reduced underwater irradiance to near-dark levels across the reef slope. Depth-stratified ecological surveys of 836 coral colonies showed clear vertical zonation in bleaching severity that closely matched the light profile. At 3 m depth, most colonies remained healthy (82%), whereas at 5 m approximately 75% of colonies exhibited intense orange–red fluorescence, and at 7 m more than 90% of colonies were fully bleached. Repeat surveys after 30 days showed improved underwater light conditions as CDOM concentrations declined with the transition between monsoons. Corals remained bleached or fluorescent, and only minor mortality was observed. These findings demonstrate that extreme light deprivation can induce mass bleaching comparable in severity to heat-driven events, disproportionately affecting deeper coral assemblages as coastal water quality declines.
298855ff:
Black-band disease (BBD) is a globally significant coral disease associated with rapid tissue loss and high mortality in reef-building corals. Within Hawaiʻi, the disease has only been recorded on the north shore of Kauaʻi. At ʻAnini Reef, a long-standing outbreak affecting Montipora species has persisted since its initial confirmation in 2004. This study quantified BBD prevalence and evaluated colony-level traits, spatial patterns, and environmental drivers, including indicators of land-based pollution, to better understand factors shaping disease dynamics at this culturally and ecologically important reef system.
In summer 2024, surveys of more than 4,600 Montipora colonies across 171 inner-reef-flat plots documented a BBD prevalence of 17.8%, considerably higher than previously reported for ʻAnini. Colony size and species were significant predictors of susceptibility, with larger colonies and Montipora patula disproportionately affected. Zero-inflated beta regression models supported a frequency-dependent transmission pattern, where BBD prevalence declined with increasing colony density, indicating waterborne or environmentally mediated spread rather than direct density-dependent transmission.
A random forest model explained 57% of the variance in BBD prevalence and identified freshwater influence and water depth as the strongest predictors of disease distribution. Spatial analyses revealed localized hotspots of high BBD prevalence that did not necessarily correspond with areas of high colony density. This pattern suggests that environmental stressors, likely influenced by watershed inputs and variable hydrodynamics, are driving outbreak persistence
These findings highlight the importance of environmental conditions in shaping BBD dynamics in Hawaiʻi and illustrate how local stressors can interact with global change to undermine reef resilience across Oceania. By identifying environmental predictors and spatial patterns of disease, this work supports targeted monitoring, watershed management, and coral restoration strategies. Results can inform efforts aimed at reducing disease impacts on vulnerable reef habitats, contributing to regional efforts to “work together to ensure a future for coral reefs.”
af4a829f: Urbanization-driven water quality changes, including nutrient enrichment, turbidity, and sedimentation, directly influence coral reef communities and their resilience. We investigated benthic composition and coral community assemblages across reef zones in Suva, Fiji, one of the Pacific’s most urbanized reef systems, to assess how land-based stressors influence reef ecosystems. Our benthic surveys revealed that Suva’s reefs persist in a degraded-but-stable state, with mean coral cover around 30%, lower than Fiji’s typical for undisturbed reef range but consistent with previous surveys 20 years ago on this system. Reef zone was the dominant driver of community structure, exceeding proximity of the reef zone to the urban area in shaping benthic assemblages. Predictive modeling identified temperature as the primary driver of benthic composition, while turbidity and nutrient enrichment contributed to shifts toward alternative substrate types, highlighting interactive effects of local and global stressors. Coral community analyses recorded 27 genera, with Acropora and Porites dominating cover. Juvenile coral distributions showed genus-specific responses to local environmental conditions: Acropora thrived in stable, high water quality sites, Porites exhibited clustered patterns tolerating moderate stress, and Goniopora and Pocillopora showed restricted or sporadic recruitment. Our findings emphasize the capacity for urban reefs to persist under chronic anthropogenic pressures while remaining vulnerable to further degradation. Integrating water quality management, and habitat-specific protection can enhance reef resilience. Collectively, these results underscore both the resilience and vulnerabilities of urban reef systems, highlight them as priorities for future research, and demonstrate the need for adaptive, habitat-based management strategies in Fiji and comparable Pacific settings.
2a90728c: Coral reefs in high island ecosystems are heavily influenced by freshwater inputs including submarine groundwater discharge (SGD), which can alter carbonate chemistry, temperature, nutrients, and pollution exposure on reefs. SGD may also create microclimates where the effects of heatwaves and ocean acidification vary. For example, SGD in tropical islands is typically colder than ambient seawater, potentially providing localized cooling during heatwaves, while, conversely, SGD often contains low-pH waters that may exacerbate ocean acidification. However, these important microclimates are difficult to quantify because the magnitude and quality of SGD vary widely over space and time. Prior research on SGD has primarily focused on single seeps or case studies that do not capture coast-wide spatial variability in SGD influence. To address this gap, we are developing low-cost, island-scale remote sensing methods using Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) to map SGD plumes across the coastline of Moʻorea, French Polynesia. We demonstrate the feasibility of island-wide SGD mapping using thermal UAV imagery within monitoring schedules and cost limitations appropriate to public agencies or local managers. Results reveal: 1) a high risk of undersampling groundwater discharge when relying solely on in situ measurements, 2) key physical parameters (e.g., altitude, wind speed, tidal height, and solar conditions) required for groundwater mapping at island scales, 3) new insights into the spatial distribution of SGD seeps, and 4) future directions and limitations of aerial measurements of SGD in reef systems. Overall, we aim to provide a scalable framework for monitoring groundwater influences on coral reef ecosystems, improving predictions of reef responses to climate stressors, and informing integrated land–sea conservation and restoration.
1b200a5e:
Submitter has requested a 15min oral presentation.

The microbiome plays a critical role in the resilience of corals to global change stressors including warming temperatures, and we are just beginning to understand the coral microbiome response to ocean deoxygenation. Even less is known about the potential response of the microbiome to the combined stressors of warming and deoxygenation, despite these stressors commonly co-occurring. Here, we employed an experimental approach that compared the response of two Caribbean reef-building corals, Acropora cervicornis and Orbicella annularis, to elevated temperatures and lowered oxygen. We exposed both species to high temperature (31.5 degC) and low oxygen (0.5 mg/l) conditions simultaneously in a fully factorial lab experiment that include control conditions (29.0 degC and 6.0 mg/l, respectively). We found that A. cervicornis, the species recognized as more vulnerable to deoxygenation, had its greatest shift in microbiome community composition when exposed to a combination of both stressors – a response that is less apparent in O. annularis. Moreover, A. cervicornis had more differentially abundant microbial taxa than O. annularis in the combined stressor treatment, including several potential coral pathogens. We also tested whether sequential exposure to stressors would affect the microbiome response by having conducted the above lab experiment with corals that had been previously outplanted over the summer at either of two reefs in the Florida Keys that differed in thermal regime. We found that prior exposure to more stressful reef temperatures muted the subsequent response of several microbial taxa to warming and deoxygenation, suggesting a “stress hardening” response that carried over from thermal to deoxygenation stress. Our work reveals the importance of testing deoxygenation in a multiple-stressor context, and that differences between coral species can be altered by the history of stress exposure.
adb30660: Continued loss of dissolved oxygen (O₂) in our oceans under the combined effects of climate change i.e., ocean warming driven deoxygenation and local pollution, are heavily impacting coral metabolic function and fitness, with associated changes to reef assemblages. With models predicting lower O₂ environments for reefs under further anthropogenic stress, ability to maintain O₂ consumption (i.e., respiration) despite environmental change will be crucial for predicting performance under future deoxygenated reefs.  Yet little is known in how O₂ consumption can be maintained relative to declining ambient concentrations (i.e., “oxyregulation”) in reef-building corals, or how environmental history regulates capacity. Using hypoxia response curves, we examined several tropical coral taxa from contrasting reef ecosystems across the Great Barrier Reef, evaluating hypoxia thresholds and oxyregulation capacity under declining O₂. Our findings indicate long-term local adaptation to “extreme” and persistently variable seawater conditions (e.g., fluctuating temperature, O₂, pH) in a shallow mangrove lagoon enhanced the hypoxic thresholds (i.e., Pcmax) of Pocillopora acuta relative to populations on the neighbouring reef. Retention of this aerobic performance in mangrove corals moved out of their native ecosystem for a one-year period suggests fixed phenotypic differences specific to their extreme environmental history. In contrast, variation in oxyregulation capacity for Acropora aspera, Isopora palifera and Pocillopora damicornis measured at different depths on Heron Island was predominately attributed to short-term changes in seawater conditions (resulting in increased average regulation capacity; Tpos) rather than reef-of-origin. These physiologically plastic responses to immediate environmental stimuli reveal the short-term ecological flexibility of coral aerobic physiology, with clear taxonomic separation in capacity and performance. These – and previous HRC-based observations – suggest that scale of environmental history regulates aerobic phenotypes for corals, with ecological drivers strongly influencing short-term variability in taxa that could be better equipped for lower and/or more variable O₂ regimes in a future ocean.
b309d77a: Oxygen plays a critical role in coral physiology and fitness. Accelerating deoxygenation is emerging as a major global threat to marine ecosystems, with hypoxic events already occurring on many coral reefs. Warming oceans are projected to amplify and prolong hypoxic events, yet the functional response of corals to these compounded stressors remains poorly resolved. We quantified the functional responses of two key Caribbean reef-building coral species, the branching coral Acropora cervicornis and the massive coral Orbicella faveolata, to deoxygenation and heat stress combined. In controlled aquaria, we measured physiological impacts in corals exposed to four treatment combinations: two dissolved oxygen treatments (ambient: 6 mg L-1 and low: 1 mg L-1) crossed with two temperature treatments (ambient: 29C and high: 31.5C). Both A. cervicornis and O. faveolata were most strongly affected by deoxygenation and heat stress combined, exhibiting the biggest decline in symbiont density and photosynthetic efficiency relative to the other treatments within days of exposure. The two species showed distinct physiological responses to the combined deoxygenation and warming treatment. Orbicella faveolata had lower respiration rates and showed visible swelling of its tissue, while A. cervicornis experienced tissue loss and mortality after less than a day of treatment exposure. Together, these results reveal that elevated temperatures increase the sensitivity of both branching and massive reef-building corals to deoxygenation, underscoring the heightened vulnerability of Caribbean reefs to increasing co-occurrence of low-oxygen, high-temperature conditions.
f1347f97: While mass coral bleaching is a known consequence of marine heatwaves (MHWs), recent severe MHWs have increasingly triggered rapid coral mortality—a phenomenon distinct from bleaching that results in mortality within hours to days, precluding any chance of recovery. We hypothesise that acute thermal stress—which simultaneously inhibits the photosynthetic oxygen supply and elevates holobiont oxygen demand—leads to an intrinsic oxygen deficit within the coral tissue, driving rapid mortality via coral tissue disintegration. We experimentally induced acute thermal stress in six Red Sea and Great Barrier Reef coral species. Using oxygen microsensors, we consistently observed that oxygen concentration at the tissue surface underwent rapid deoxygenation to levels consistent with severe hypoxia or anoxia, immediately preceding the first signs of tissue disintegration. This rapid decline was driven by the collapse of photosynthetic oxygen production, and compounded by mass transfer-limited oxygen supply and elevated respiratory demand. Furthermore, we demonstrate that oxygen deprivation alone is sufficient to induce tissue disintegration across all tested species, and that high flow rates (enhancing oxygen supply) delayas the onset of coral tissue disintegration and mortality. Our findings reveal intrinsic oxygen deprivation as a route to rapid coral mortality under acute heat stress. We propose that physical constraints on oxygen supply, dictated by holobiont physiology and boundary layer dynamics, define a new, more immediate thermal tipping point for corals. This process is often visually indistinguishable from the aftermath of bleaching, suggesting potential misdiagnosis in current reef health datasets, and a potential shift from mass bleaching to direct mortality in response to intensifying MHWs. This work underscores the critical need for refined stress metrics that capture heating rates, incorporating high-resolution local weather monitoring at micro- and macro-scales, and developing tools to distinguish between bleaching and tissue disintegration to accurately predict and manage coral reef collapse in the Anthropocene.
0028372a: Managing coral reefs to maintain ecosystem function and maximize resilience requires identification of resilience indicators and clear ecological reference thresholds. In the absence of local resilience thresholds, targets or long-term data, managers can assess reef condition by comparing locally collected indicator data to established and published broadscale thresholds that incorporate large spatial and temporal variability. In this study, we assessed the reef condition prior to the 2016 coral bleaching event at Aldabra Atoll, a remote Marine Protected Area and UNESCO World Heritage Site in Seychelles relative to broadscale thresholds for structural complexity, total fish biomass, herbivore fish biomass, juvenile coral density, and trophic level fish biomass. The results of this assessment were synthesized into a resilience index and sites were classified into ‘management strategies’ using a published reef management framework. We then compared the resilience index scores to observed coral cover changes following the 2016 bleaching event, tracking recovery through to 2022. The findings showed that seven of the eight assessed sites displayed the resilience expected of a remote, well protected MPA, with five sites meeting or exceeding all assessed broadscale thresholds. The research station and associated human activity appeared to have minimal negative impacts on reef resilience at Aldabra. This study demonstrates how broadscale thresholds can provide meaningful ecological baselines even in data-limited contexts and, when paired with long-term monitoring data, can validate and refine resilience-based management frameworks. By linking resilience indicators with empirical recovery trajectories, the approach supports adaptive, evidence-based management and offers a scalable model for other reef systems facing increasing climate stress. To improve future assessments and aid management decisions, we recommend integrating site-specific factors by assessing site-level oceanographic, biological, and chemical conditions.
44530b2b: Accurate long-term monitoring of coral bleaching is essential for understanding climate-driven changes in reef ecosystems and informing management strategies. Yet global bleaching databases rely on in situ surveys that are rarely conducted at the peak of thermal stress. Therefore, bleaching severities recorded too early—before peak heat stress—or too late—after partial recovery—may underestimate the true severity experienced by corals. Despite the extensive use of these datasets in global assessments and climate-risk analyses, the magnitude of this possible timing bias remains unquantified. Here, we assess the potential underestimation of bleaching severity in a global database. We matched 21,008 bleaching observations (1963–2017) to NOAA CRW 5-km daily DHW values at the survey date, and extracted all daily DHW fields within ±6 months to reconstruct each site’s thermal stress trajectory. From this window, we obtained the maximum heat stress reached during the event (MAX_DHW), and the time offset between the survey and MAX_DHW. Using these metrics, we developed two Bayesian cumulative ordinal regression models: a pre-peak model relating severity to DHW at the survey and a post-peak model relating severity to MAX_DHW and time since the thermal peak. These models quantify how the probability of each severity category shifts with these metrics. Using four rule-based criteria for identifying reports that underestimate bleaching severity, preliminary results show that 20–27% of reports were consistently flagged. Underestimation increases when surveys occur ≥42 days before or ≥80–90 days after the heat-stress peak. Globally, mild and moderate bleaching appear systematically under-reported. By identifying when and where underestimation is most likely – and by establishing severity-DHW probability relationships – this work provides actionable guidance for field monitoring programs, strengthens interpretation of long-term databases, and enhances the reliability of large-scale assessments supporting 21st-century reef management.
1e6757fe:
The world’s coral reefs are heterogeneous, spanning wide swaths of the ocean and myriad habitats across coastlines. The variability across reefs is created by the biodiversity of species as well as the diversity of physicochemical conditions across geographies. Further, evidence indicates that coral reef community structure can vary widely through time, driven by external disturbances and ecologically mediated transitions. A challenge thus emerges to develop generalizable ecological insights given the extreme contextual heterogeneity.

Working with a global community of colleagues and collaborators, we have collected a snapshot of the spatial and temporal variability of coral reefs. Our core sampling effort, the 100 Island Challenge, used a natural experimental approach to consider the independent and interactive effects of oceanographic context, island geomorphology, and anthropogenic forcing on the structure and functioning of reef communities. 

The data reveal some general patterns that relate well to existing understandings of reef ecology. Increased levels of nearshore productivity are linked with increases in biomass growth and standing stock potential. High levels of anthropogenic impact largely reduced biomass of fishes and coverage of reef-building corals. More surprising were the results revealed from time-series analyses. Across reef geographies, the modal demographic fate of coral colonies is growth. Certainly, impacts such as marine heat waves or predator irruptions can have striking impacts on coral fate. However, under many circumstances the corals respond to impacts by following pathways toward colony recovery or assemblages can demonstrate coral-driven reassembly. 

Describing geographic patterns of variation in reef structure and dynamics provides a robust platform for next analytical steps. A goal for reef ecology should be to develop predictive models that incorporate geographic and contextual variability, offering insights into how the tremendous biodiversity of these ecosystems may contain mechanisms to meet mounting management challenges.
c5f14994: Resilience assessments, which rely on ecological indicators to predict future ecosystem resilience across a management jurisdiction, are used to prioritize limited conservation resources toward “resilient” locations with the best chance of surviving climate change. While resilience assessments have been widely applied to inform management, particularly for coral reefs, their precision and accuracy have rarely been validated. We used a timeseries of coral reef 3D models to 1) conduct multiple resilience assessments of fixed sites over time, and 2) track the resistance and recovery of those same sites over a decade. This allowed us to compare resilience predictions across assessments (precision) and test whether resilience predictions aligned with observed patterns of resistance and recovery (accuracy). We found that resilience assessments are capable of generating consistent resilience predictions over time, but in our case those predictions were not correlated with observed resistance or recovery dynamics. We recommend modelling resistance and recovery as distinct processes, and emphasize the importance of validating predictions with long-term monitoring. We caution against using resilience assessments in isolation to drive conservation decision making. Instead, spatial prioritization should be driven by local community input, with management of priority sites informed by functional indicators of resistance and recovery.
0b855e2a: Pahang’s coastal waters have a remarkable coral diversity, particularly in the marine protected area, Tioman Marine Park, which has become a conservation and study hotspot for researchers. However, unprotected areas like Pulau Ular remain neglected despite their ecological importance and significant value to nearby coastal communities. In addition, more anthropogenic sources that potentially threaten the coral reef have been observed in the areas and recognized as the catalyst for coral diseases that induce significant mortality on reefs. Therefore, assessing the current status of coral health and diversity at Pulau Ular is crucial for establishing baseline data to support sustainable reef ecosystem management. Coral survey was conducted using the Coral Video Transect (CVT) method at two selected reef sites in Pulau Ular and the ReefCloud platform was used to determine the coral diversity and disease prevalence. The present result indicated that Pulau Ular reefs fall in a ‘Good’ (66% ± 2.03) coral conditions category. For coral diversity, a total of 17 coral genera were identified with Porites (32.40% ± 9.17) being the most abundant, followed by Galaxea (18.50% ± 7.61), Platygyra (13.70% ± 6.74) and Acropora (11.80% ± 6.32). Meanwhile disease assessment revealed five types of coral diseases with White Syndrome/ WS (0.60% ± 1.0) has the highest prevalence, followed by Unexplained Growth Anomalies/ UGA (0.50% ± 1.0), Atramentous Necrosis/ AtN (0.40% ± 0.08), Skeletal Eroding Band/ SEB (0.30% ± 0.07) and Ulcerative White Spots/ UWS (0.02% ± 0.02). As a conclusion, the finding of this research provides essential baseline information on coral health, diversity and disease prevalence in Pulau Ular, supporting a proactive approach of coral disease detection and guiding a long-term future coral health monitoring efforts particularly in Pahang’s reef ecosystems.
93728c42: Following the 3rd global coral bleaching event, the world has experienced a multi-year period of regional mass coral bleaching events, culminating with the declared multi-year 4th global coral bleaching event. This global event led to record-setting coral bleaching in Florida and the Caribbean across 2023 and 2024 due to extreme temperatures, with greatly expanded temporal and spatial coverages, rapid intensification, and increased accumulation of bleaching heat stress. This new paradigm of active and rapidly developing marine heatwaves has prompted the need for a new, near real-time monitoring and assessment tool for coral reef management. This tool must quantitatively monitor the daily change in reef exposure to heat stress and assess spatial and temporal evolutions of heat stress at all relevant scales (daily to annual and local to global) as well as provide concise and easy-to-read summary information for any given day in a historical context. Here, we examine temporal and spatial expansions of reef areas exposed to severe bleaching-level heat stress on a daily basis using NOAA Coral Reef Watch’s (CRW) satellite data record of 1985–2025. A daily climatology documenting which coral reef grids have been exposed to select levels of bleaching level heat stress since 1985 (at Degree Heating Week thresholds of 4, 8, 12, 16, and 20 °C -weeks, a key index of the CRW satellite product suite) was developed as the baseline for near real-time tracking of the daily exposure of reef grids. The numbers of reef grids exposed to high stress levels on many individual days of year have dramatically increased over the years, indicating these reef grids now start to experience high stress much earlier and/or stress dissipates later than in the past. This tool will more effectively and efficiently provide coral reef managers and scientists with near real-time bleaching heat stress information.
8ced5bed: Maintaining the structural framework of coral reef habitats is crucial to preserve their ecological function and the services they provide, yet reefs are in a critical state of decline. As part of NOAA’s National Coral Reef Monitoring Program, census-based carbonate budget surveys are performed every three years at long-term monitoring sites to assess the state of reef habitat and inform federal management. The carbonate budget of a coral reef represents the balance between calcification (by scleractinian coral, crustose coralline algae) and bioerosion (by parrotfish, urchins, micro- and macroborers), ultimately yielding net carbonate production (NCP), a metric that encapsulates reef habitat persistence. We analyzed carbonate budgets from six permanent sites over 12 years, spanning a depth gradient of 4m - 20m: Flower Garden Banks National Marine Sanctuary, Dry Tortugas National Park, Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary, Puerto Rico, as well as two sites in the U.S. Virgin Islands. Carbonate budgets were paired with high-precision temperature recorders to provide in situ temperature data and assess multiple disturbance events. We found that three of the six study sites have had a significant decline in carbonate production and one is already in a net erosional state. Comparatively, sites with more stable thermal histories and sustained coral cover maintained net positive NCP, highlighting the role of local conditions and community composition in temperature resilience. Bleaching events associated with thermal stress, as well as disease and hurricanes, have played a role in the decline of NCP. As coral reefs continue to face thermal stress events and other environmental threats, maintaining robust data time series will be essential for detecting shifts in reef-building capacity to combat sea level rise, informing predictive models and guiding management strategies to safeguard remaining reef structure.
fed7f4c7: Megabenthos, such as mussels, sea cucumbers, and large benthic invertebrates, play a crucial role in maintaining the ecological function of coral reef ecosystems. They contribute to environmental balance through nutrient cycling and the maintenance of habitat structure. In recent years, megabenthos have become recognised as sensitive indicators of reef health, particularly in tropical MPAs in the Indo-Pacific region, which are vulnerable to climate change and human pressure. Based on this understanding, this study examines the diversity and structure of megabenthos communities as a practical approach to assessing the ecological condition of tropical coral reef MPAs. Field surveys were conducted on 12 transects around Mare Island, North Maluku (Indonesia), using a standardised belt transect method (70 m × 2 m). The analysis revealed 32 species from 20 families, dominated by molluscs and echinoderms. Diversity values were low to moderate (H' = 0.5-2.4), evenness was relatively high (E = 0.65-0.95), and dominance varied among stations (C = 0.1-0.65), indicating a simple community structure and vulnerability to anthropogenic pressure. Economically valuable species such as mussels, sea cucumbers, trochus snails, and lobsters dominated the target population, indicating their crucial ecological role and potential for overexploitation. A higher abundance of megabenthos was found in areas with healthier coral conditions, confirming the role of habitat complexity in maintaining the resilience of benthic communities. These results confirm that megabenthos are effective ecological indicators for monitoring the health of coral reefs and supporting adaptive management of marine protected areas in tropical regions.
0bf7ba91: Wetar Island, located in the southern Coral Triangle, hosts high potential marine biodiversity, yet scientific knowledge of reef ecosystem conditions in this region remains limited. To support Indonesia’s Marine Protected Area (MPA) Vision 30×45, the Maluku Provincial Government proposed West Wetar Island as a marine protected area in 2022. This study evaluates the ecological condition of coral reef ecosystems in West Wetar Island to provide scientific evidence for MPA spatial design and to identify areas most suitable for designation as no-take zones. We conducted ecological surveys across 14 reef sites in West Wetar Island. We used underwater visual census to quantify reef fish metrics. We assessed benthic components using photo-quadrat surveys and quadrat surveys at 8–10 m depth, and documented coral species composition through random sampling at depths of 3–20 m at each site. We analyzed fish diversity using the Shannon diversity index, extracted benthic cover data using CPCe software, and explored community patterns using non-metric multidimensional scaling (nMDS) in R. West Wetar supports exceptionally rich and abundant reef fish communities and records the highest values. Species richness ranged from 53–65 species per site, with consistently high abundances and moderate to high diversity (H′ = 2.4–3.9). We recorded 204 fish species and 10,629 individuals. Target fish biomass reached approximately one metric ton and showed dominance by Pterocaesio tile. Coral reefs generally occurred in moderate to good condition, with high live coral cover, strong habitat complexity, and benthic community structure dominated by hard corals. nMDS results revealed strong spatial structuring of reef communities associated with coral dominance and habitat complexity. These findings provide robust ecological evidence to guide science-based MPA zoning, particularly for the placement of no-take zones, buffer areas, and ecological corridors, while supporting long-term fisheries productivity, reef resilience, and effective conservation planning in the southern Coral Triangle.
57126ef7: Coral feeding on zooplankton increases as zooplankton concentrations increase. In addition, resilience is higher in corals that eat more zooplankton. To leverage the advantage that zooplankton feeding has on coral resilience, we developed the Underwater Zooplankton Light Enhancement Array (UZELA) to locally concentrate natural zooplankton and give corals more to eat. UZELA is programmable, autonomous, waterproof, and deployable for six months on a single battery. In the field, UZELA increases local zooplankton concentrations by 7-fold and increases coral feeding rates by 10 to 50-fold. Field tests on Hawaiian and Floridian corals shows that UZELA can enhance coral growth, survivorship, and health metrics (see also abstracts presented by Shannon Dixon, Nick Wilson, and Ann Marie Hulver). New advances in UZELA include a self-cleaning wiper, rechargeable batteries, and an easy-to-use software interface that gives end-users options in the frequency and timing of the illumination and wiper functions. Technological interventions like UZELA can be part of the solution for enhancing restoration success.
e81ff0a6: The global decline of coral reefs has accelerated the need for technological interventions that enhance coral resilience and reef recovery. Coral recovery from bleaching can be aided by increased rates of feeding on zooplankton. However, this strategy is limited in nature by natural zooplankton concentrations on the reef. The Underwater Zooplankton Enhancement Light Array (UZELA) is a device that capitalizes on the phototactic behavior of zooplankton and locally concentrates zooplankton. We hypothesized that heat-stressed corals exposed to UZELA would have increased feeding and accelerated recovery following heat stress. To test this hypothesis, bleached and non-bleached Montipora capitata and Porites compressa ramets were deployed on the reef with UZELA for six months. Feeding rates, survivorship, and physiological parameters including calcification, branch extension, biomass, lipids, algal endosymbiont density, and pigmentation were measured throughout the experiment. Twenty-eight ramets were sampled after 10 days, six weeks, and six months for M. capitata. P. compressa was only collected after 10 days as the remaining ramets died shortly thereafter of a parasitic infection. At all timepoints, bleached and non-bleached M. capitata and P. compressa corals demonstrated higher feeding rates with UZELA than controls. Bleached M. capitata and P. compressa  corals with UZELA did not differ physiologically from bleached corals without UZELA. Non-bleached M. capitata corals with UZELA had higher calcification and branch extension at the expense of their lipid concentrations than non-bleached corals without UZELA.  These findings demonstrate that UZELA can be used to increase growth of healthy M. capitata corals in nurseries or on reefs post-disturbance. With the continuing decline of coral reefs, technologies that can enhance coral feeding could play a critical role in coral resilience for coral restoration and conservation efforts.
659a2aa3:
Coral Restoration Foundation (CRF) initiated a collaborative in-situ spawning and larval propagation trial to address a persistent barrier to the recovery of Florida’s Coral Reef Tract: the extremely low natural recruitment of Caribbean Acropora spp. Although asexual propagation and outplanting are widely implemented across the region, long-term restoration success depends on self-sustaining restored populations through successful sexual reproduction, larval settlement, and recruitment in the wild. 

In August 2025, CRF documented spawning in 27 genotypes of Acropora cervicornis at its in-situ nursery in Tavernier, Florida—the highest number recorded to date. This observation provides strong evidence that 2023 post-bleaching survivors retained reproductive capacity and suggests resilience traits are worth further investigation. 

In collaboration with Shedd Aquarium, we investigated bottlenecks in ex-situ sexual propagation techniques and in-situ recruitment processes aboard the Shedd’s research vessel, the R/V Coral Reef II (CRII). Moored directly above the nursery, the vessel functioned as a floating laboratory with a larval rearing system integrated into its live-well tanks. This setup supported controlled fertilization and rearing while enabling rapid deployment of competent larvae into settlement structures in direct proximity to parent colonies. 

Gametes from 11 A. cervicornis genotypes, sourced across the Florida Keys, were collected at the nursery on August 12–13 and fertilized aboard the vessel. Approximately 25,000 larvae were deployed into two settlement crates placed on the sand within the nursery, each covered with a mesh net of varying size. Crates contained settlement-inducing substrates, including CCA-treated wax tiles, rugose silica tiles, reef arch inserts, and live rock, enabling evaluation of larval settlement preference and post-settlement survival. 

Ongoing monthly monitoring will inform refinements to substrate design, deployment strategies, and larval delivery systems for a second-generation spawning hub in 2026. Advancing in-situ sexual propagation is essential for identifying ontogenetic bottlenecks and increasing the long-term resilience of restored Acropora populations.
c0f74860: Accelerating degradation of coral reefs globally due to ocean environmental changes makes the development of large-scale coral restoration technologies an urgent priority. We have focused on developing large-scale restoration techniques utilizing sexual reproduction, targeting Acropora corals, the most dominant genus in the Indo-Pacific reefs. We consider establishing larval supply sources, termed coral spawning farms, to be particularly crucial. This necessitates the efficient mass production of juvenile corals. We developed a coral sand-based square tube settlement device (cube tube) as an artificial substrate and established a “larval cradle” system capable of producing seedlings consistently in the sea, from egg collection to larval settlement. However, the yield rate of seedlings (the percentage of cube tubes with at least one surviving juvenile coral) is unstable, varying significantly depending on the nursery location and year. Therefore, to establish a stable mass production method for coral seedlings, we compared the results of various juvenile coral rearing results conducted to date and made improvements. The results showed that: ① During the first six months after settlement (summer), utilizing algae-grazing gastropods reduces competition with small algae (cage mesh size 5–25 mm), ② From six months to one year, enlarge the cage mesh size to 90 mm to allow herbivorous fish to remove algae, thereby stably increasing yield. The future goal is to establish methods for spreading mass-produced coral seedlings over wide areas to realize large-scale coral restoration.
ebcf36b8:
Scaling coral aquaculture is essential for delivering the numbers of climate-resilient corals needed to restore reefs such as the Great Barrier Reef (GBR). A critical early-stage requirement in this pipeline is continuous monitoring of coral spawn and larvae in aquaculture facilities. Spawn counts inform key decisions around resource allocation, larval density, fertilisation assessment, and overall system health. However, current monitoring methods rely heavily on manual sampling, which is labour-intensive, intermittent, and increasingly infeasible as production increases in scale, which limits the data and scientific insights available during mass spawning events.

To address this challenge, we present the Coral Spawn and Larvae Imaging Camera System (CSLICS), a low-cost, modular imaging platform that automates density monitoring in larval rearing tanks. CSLICS integrates compact waterproof cameras with a machine-learning pipeline that leverages human-in-the-loop annotation strategies to build efficient, high-quality training datasets. The system detects, classifies, and counts coral spawn during embryonic and larval stages, offering continuous, non-invasive monitoring throughout the rearing period.

Here, we describe the system design, dataset development, and computer vision methods underpinning CSLICS, along with an evaluation using imagery collected during mass coral spawning events on the GBR. Our detection models achieve an 82.4% F1 score for detecting surface spawn during embryonic stages, and a 65.3% F1 score for detecting sub-surface larvae—an especially challenging imaging condition. Compared to manual sampling at equivalent frequency, CSLICS provides an estimated labour saving of 5,720 hours per spawning event. Validation against expert manual counts demonstrates CSLICS’ ability to accurately quantify fertilisation success and track sub-surface culture dynamics.

By enabling continuous, scalable, and accurate monitoring, CSLICS enhances the efficiency and scientific resolution of coral aquaculture operations. This technology provides a step change for upscaling restoration efforts, improving the feasibility of delivering the millions of coral recruits required to bolster reef resilience under accelerating climate change.
6d90ad91: Human-induced climate change is intensifying marine heatwave events, triggering the breakdown of symbiosis that leads to coral bleaching. Bleaching might be lethal for corals, as photosynthate transfer represents the main energy source for the majority of scleractinian corals. However, several laboratory studies have shown that corals benefiting from an external food supply (heterotrophy) are more resistant to bleaching or recover better. Still, the influence of heterotrophy on coral thermotolerance remains poorly studied in the field settings. In New Caledonia, areas rich in plankton and organic matter (mesotrophic reef) provide natural laboratories for studying the role of nutrition in coral thermotolerance. To test the hypothesis that food availability in the water column improve physiological performances of corals thriving in mesotrophic reefs, we transplanted coral colonies of Acropora tenuis from oligo- to mesotrophic reefs. After five months of transplantation, skeletal growth was measured and was generally similar among transplantation treatments. Acute heat stress assay (CBASS) was also conducted on A. tenuis, which consists of four temperature regimes (28, 30, 33, and 36℃), with the maximum temperature maintained for three hours. One hour post-stress at 36°C, mesotrophic coral colonies exhibited the highest maximum photosynthetic efficiencies (Fv/Fm ≒ 0.55). This trend persisted 12 hours after the stress, with oligotrophic corals exhibiting severe damage to the photosystem (Fv/Fm < 0.2). Our results suggest that higher exogenous food availability enable corals to mitigate photophysiological damage during thermal stress. Ongoing physiological and isotopic analyses will enable us to further elucidate acclimatization mechanisms to mesotrophic reefs and their role in coral fitness.
f21753a1: Acoustic enrichment (AE), broadcasting healthy reef ambient noise towards degraded reefs, is an emerging tool to accelerate recruitment of fish and coral larvae. Previous research has shown that AE can elevate coral and fish larval settlement in controlled settings, but reef-scale applications during natural spawning events remain understudied. Between 2023 and 2025 four deployments were conducted 5m deep on a sandy bottom flat off Kāneʻohe Bay, Oʻahu, Hawaiʻi, testing whether AE enhances fish larval recruitment on artificial reef structures.  One of the deployments (July 2024) also tested whether AE increases coral larvae response.  Autonomous cameras collected burst photos of fish and fish larvae at both a treatment site and control site separated by 42m range, with both sites containing custom-printed fish habitat modules (FHM) surrounding an active (treatment) and dummy (control) speaker.  A fully autonomous playback system broadcast local healthy reef sounds from the treatment site nightly over three weeks around new moon spawning, with source levels calibrated so that playback levels merged with ambient at 21m range.  For the coral larvae study, 19 individual 3D printed Coral Settlement Modules (CSMs) mimicking mesoscale reef roughness were deployed along two transects 1–42 m from the active speaker.  Also deployed were 12 Microhabitat Cinder Block (MCB) units engineered to attenuate flow.  Six MCB units were also coated with a nano-engineered "Brink" biofilm to chemically induce settlement.  AE treatment sites were found to have 4 to 10 times greater fish larvae presence relative to control sites, but had no statistically significant effect on mature reef fish presence.  In addition, coral settlement densities on both the CSM and MCB structures were significantly correlated with the presence of Brink coating and treatment speaker distance.  Combining AE, custom artificial structures, and bioengineered materials show promise in providing scalable, cost-effective pathways to enhancing recruitment from multiple taxa.
4ac37302:
In the past 20 years, there has been a sharp rise in the establishment of coral reef restoration programs, which generally aim to restore key services provided by healthy, natural reefs. However, the effect of restoration on arguably more important metrics related to ecosystem functioning is rarely considered, with most programs focusing their monitoring on static variables (e.g. coral cover) only. With crucial metrics related to ecosystem functioning left largely unmonitored, it is difficult to assess the outcomes of reef restoration through a truly ecological lens.
