We therefore propose that ecosystem functioning should be placed at the forefront of reef restoration, and suggest the following focus points for improvements in the field: (1) Implement a set of standardized methods to monitor key functional processes on restored reefs (and neighboring natural reefs), (2) better understand how reef restoration will support ecosystem functioning in a changing ocean, and the identity of organisms that carry most of the “functionality load” on reefs, and (3) adopt a function-centric approach that implements techniques specifically targeted at protecting the respective species and processes of interest. 

Too often, reef restoration is seen as a sprint to cover the seabed with coral as fast as possible. In reality, it’s a marathon of rebuilding the complex web of relationships that sustain the entire reef. Restoration efforts must take this long-term functional perspective into account in future planning and management, and ensure that programme design and implementation truly maximise ecological value.
4d4e9ad9: For the last 20 years a group of island leaders across the island regions of the world have both catalysed and been inspired by a set of initiatives which have had a significant impact on island and ocean biodiversity. Starting with the announcement of the Micronesia Challenge in 2006 (at the same time parties to the Convention on Biodiversity adopted the Programme of Work on Island Biodiversity),  the Coral Triangle Initiative, Caribbean Challenge Initiative, the BEST Challenge - in Europe overseas, the Seychelles Debt Swap, Niue's large scale MPA and the Aloha+Challenge this set of leaders has faced the implementation challenge - how you move from science and policy to on ground implementation. These large political commitments are really a story as well about how we move to scale implementation and a great willingness between these islands to share lessons learned and create a global platform for island advocacy. They have also led the development or strengthening of national and regional trust funds (Caribbean Biodiversity Fund and national trust funds in the Caribbean region, Niue Oceanwide Trust, CTI CTF, Micronesia Conservation Trust, Seychelles Conservation and Climate Adaptation Trust Fund) which have been a game changer for these islands in terms of building stronger capacity for financing conservation through locally led mechanisms that last beyond project cycles. This presentation will speak to what has been learnt throughout 20 years of significant island leadership and how islands can continue to play this leadership role in catalysing action for what comes next.
f3fb2a53: Micronesia harbors extraordinary biodiversity and abundant resources, including over 480 species of hard corals, 1,300 reef fish species, and nearly 30% of the world’s tuna. Yet these ecosystems face mounting threats from overharvest, unsustainable development, and climate change. To safeguard nature and livelihoods, five nations launched the Micronesia Challenge (MC), a bold commitment to effectively manage at least 50% of marine areas and 30% of terrestrial areas, strengthen climate resilience, and improve community well-being by 2030. Turning this vision into reality requires lasting financial solutions. Building on nearly two decades of progress, the MC governments, The Nature Conservancy (TNC), Micronesia Conservation Trust (MCT), and other key stakeholders are advancing a groundbreaking Project Finance for Permanence (PFP) through the Enduring Earth collaboration. Formalized by a Memorandum of Understanding at the 27th Micronesian Islands Forum in 2025, this approach secures full funding and enabling conditions upfront, unlike traditional short-term donor models. The PFP will unlock multi-million-dollar investments to expand new protections of over 150 million hectares, restore ecosystems, strengthen ocean governance and management, and enhance community resilience. By leveraging regional leadership, traditional knowledge, and innovative financing, the MC PFP aims to protect biodiversity, cultural heritage, and food security across 678 million hectares, ensuring a thriving Micronesia for generations to come.
06f7c7f2: Data informed decision making is often the aspiration of many natural resource management processes, but the reality of how information is used and by whom is often more complex than we might hope. Likewise, human and natural systems are complex and uncertain, and those uncertainties are more evident than ever in a changing climate. Within this context, I will share how collaborative science to support effective nearshore management in Hawai‘i is allowing for greater uptake of data in decision making, with examples of challenges and opportunities for resilience-based management, community-based management, and for managing tradeoffs among fisheries, pollution, and ecosystems. The ability to bridge data and decisions stem from the efforts of the Hawaiʻi Monitoring and Reporting Collaborative (HIMARC) to bring together diverse datasets inclusive of both reef status and trends and associated drivers at spatial and temporal scales commensurate with management needs. These datasets are combined from multiple sources and involve collaborations among scientists, managers, and community leaders resulting in consensus building that increases the potential for uptake in management. The talk will focus on how innovative scientific products can be most useful for achieving successful conservation outcomes when working collaboratively to define and answer questions at the right scale, build trust, and deliver the information at the right place and time.
12889f59: Coral reefs provide essential ecological, economic, and climate-resilience benefits to coastal communities in the Philippines. Effective coral reef management requires integration into long-term spatial and development planning, yet the degree to which this is realized at the local government level in the Philippines remains unclear. This study assesses the extent to which coral reef management is integrated into Comprehensive Land and Water Use Plans (CLWUPs) across selected coastal municipalities in the Philippines and in the suite of national-level planning guidelines issued by sectoral and interagency institutions. Using a structured content analysis framework, key informant interviews, and alignment scoring against established coastal management, biodiversity conservation, and climate-resilience criteria, the research evaluates how coral reef management provisions are reflected in land and water use zoning, environmental management strategies, and development priorities. Results highlight varying levels of mainstreaming influenced by institutional capacity, data availability, interagency coordination, and local prioritization of coastal issues. Findings identify gaps, good practices, institutional challenges, and enabling conditions that influence the quality of integration. The study provides evidence-based recommendations to strengthen policy coherence, improve local planning capacities, and enhance the long-term sustainability of coral reef ecosystems within local governance processes.
58c7e4f9:
Efforts to advance evidence-based marine management often rely on collaborations between governments, coastal communities, non-governmental organizations, and scientific institutions. These partnerships offer powerful opportunities for interdisciplinary and cross-cultural approaches to managing coral reefs. Yet the practicalities of such collaborations are inherently complex. 

Planning and executing surveys across large, diverse seascapes introduces logistical and strategic challenges, from aligning scientific aims, cultural priorities and government mandates to coordinating between ministries, NGOs, community leaders, and technical teams. As partnerships scale up, so do questions about who drives the agenda, who interprets the data, and how decisions can be made to reflect local values rather than external expectations. Similarly, these interdisciplinary partnerships also raise the issue of balancing data sovereignty with increasing pushes for open-access data, ensuring that national and community partners retain meaningful control over their own information while still utilizing data resources to their potential.

Navigating these complexities often requires us to think past how scientists and managers work in isolation and identify new ways of working across disciplines. Partner organizations often share ultimate goals, but approach them in ways which are not always immediately compatible. Through experience, we have learned that collaborations function best when leadership, benefits, and authority are shared, but we also recognize that achieving this balance is an ongoing process rather than a fixed model.

We draw on case studies from diverse international partnerships, highlighting both successful outcomes and valuable learning opportunities. These examples demonstrate the potential of cross-sector collaboration and the importance of thoughtfully integrating scientific tools with local sovereignty, cultural context, and long-term stewardship. By sharing these evolving insights and lessons, we aim to catalyze meaningful cross-disciplinary dialogue.
7ff57e98:
The Micronesia Challenge (MC) began as a political pledge to conserve 30% of marine resources and has recently expanded to include 50% effective management of target populations by 2030. To reach these ambitious targets, regional working groups have integrated MC goals into governmental programs, scientific research, community engagement, and funding strategies under a coordinated conservation framework. Twenty years later, the MC has led to the growth of protected area networks, several forms of species-based management, watershed planning, and is now poised to integrate climate-change mitigation to the extent possible.

Lessons learned over two decades show that successful coral-reef and fisheries management was often grounded in simplified science paired with real-time outreach. For example, approximately 60% of Micronesia no-take MPA have documented significant improvements to their fish stocks and reef health relative to reference locations, with effect sizes that exceed much of the global literature. While regional outcomes are encouraging, continuous data streams frequently revealed intermittent poaching, which typically prompted corrective actions within approximately two years. Similar patterns emerged for other policies, such as seasonal protection of grouper spawning aggregations, which enhanced abundances and fisher landings despite occasional lapses in enforcement.

We believe that online access to clear, science-based messaging improved accountability across communities and governments. Moving forward, these data streams recently provided a basis for species-based management by integrating fisheries-dependent and independent streams, and they pave the way for identifying reefs with unusually high resistance and/or recovery to help tailor potential mitigation. We close by reviewing the details of standardized, yet imperfect, protocols that have produced comparable ecological data across a large portion of the tropical Pacific to support the MC and related initiatives.
327def02:
In the face of challenges to coral reefs it is important to understand responses to climatic and environmental changes at multiple spatial and temporal scales. Yet, there is currently a disconnect between understanding past reef growth, modern disturbance regimes, and the potential of future reef growth. To bridge the gap between biological and geological timescales we here present the results of ~500 U-Th dated corals obtained from reef matrix cores, death assemblages and fossil microatolls from eleven continental island fringing reefs in the Keppel Islands, southern Great Barrier Reef (GBR). Data from reef flat cores show that reefs initiated prior to 7500 years before present (yr.BP-1950), although not contemporaneously among sites. Reef accretion rates following initiation were comparable to reefs elsewhere on the GBR, but rapidly outpaced average rates reported for central and northern inshore GBR sites in the subsequent 1500 years when sea levels were higher, and sea surface temperatures (SST’s) were ~1°C warmer. Reef slope cores show limited net accretion from ~500 yrBP to present at two reef sites suggesting these reefs have reached their geomorphological limits. U-Th dates from coral death assemblages were highly variable until a multi-site peak centered around the 2006 bleaching event, suggesting unprecedented modern regional scale mortality. Death assemblage data was also used to spatially interpolate past coral mortality and compare this data to in situ observations of mortality following known bleaching events. 
Data obtained from the Keppel Islands provides evidence for optimal growth phases, site specific geomorphological limitations and underestimation of acute mortality events when compared to observational reports. This information can be used to prioritise active reef restoration efforts and guide regional management strategies and expectations.
cbaeabbe: Fringing reefs and their associated reef flats provide critical ecosystem services by attenuating waves and reducing shoreline erosion. Despite their importance, the long-term accretionary processes and carbonate-producing community dynamics of fringing reef flats remain poorly understood compared to other reef zones. This is particularly true for mainland-attached fringing reefs in Southeast Asia, where significant terrestrial sediment input creates mixed carbonate-siliciclastic environments. This knowledge gap limits our ability to predict how reef flats will respond to future environmental and ecological change. Here we investigate Holocene reef flat accretion histories using coral cores collected from an exposed intertidal fossil fringing reef in Inopacan, Leyte, Philippines. We applied radiometric dating (U/Th and ¹⁴C), luminescence band analysis, and micro-CT scanning to reconstruct reef growth chronology, past coral communities, and sediment composition. Our results show that the fringing reef was dominated by massive coral colonies, primarily Porites and Favites. Siliciclastic sediment was present on the initiating surface of corals, and terrestrial particles were incorporated within coral skeletons throughout their growth history, indicating persistent turbid conditions. The cores also revealed varying degrees of bioerosion. These findings demonstrate how fringing reef flat formation and accretion dynamics respond to sustained terrestrial sediment input, with implications for predicting reef flat development under future environmental change.
548a705f: Predicting future coral reef responses and climate-related risk to sea-level rise requires robust historical records of reef vertical accretion rates (VARs) and their ecological drivers. However, comparable empirical data on past reef growth and framework bioconstruction are often spatially and temporally limited, precluding detection of regional patterns and long-term trends. Here, we compiled, evaluated and standardized Holocene VAR and coral community structure data from 288 Indo-Pacific paleo-reef records across 92 reef sites to examine both intrinsic and extrinsic drivers of reef accretion. Our findings reveal that reef formation and long-term accretion are shaped by a complex interplay between sea-level change and eco-geomorphological factors. Historical VAR records indicate limited capacity of many Indo-Pacific reefs (98% of reef sites) to keep pace with projected rates of sea-level rise, particularly under high-emissions scenario. Critical thresholds suggest that reef accretion is very unlikely (>90% probability) to be maintained when relative sea-level rise rate exceeds 5.3 mm yr-1, a scenario likely to be surpassed within 35 years. Without substantial reduction in global emissions, many coral reefs are at increasing risk of submergence, structural collapse and loss of critical ecosystem services, especially where communities are dominated by generalist and weedy taxa.
b1896724: Quaternary coral reefs provide key insights into past environmental changes and reef development. On Okinawa Jima, the Minatogawa Formation (MF) records relative sea-level fluctuations and reef growth during the late Middle to Late Pleistocene. This formation, up to ~20 m thick, consists of coral and foraminiferal limestones divided into four units separated by unconformities. We examined core sections from 12 boreholes totaling 188 m, including two shore-perpendicular transects (T1: Holes 1–6; T2: Holes 1B–5B) and one intermediate hole (C). Our objectives are to reconstruct the 3D facies architecture and clarify the depositional, diagenetic, and sea-level history of the MF. We used core logging, X-ray CT imaging, and microfacies analysis to characterize the core material, and bulk-carbonate δ¹⁸O and δ¹³C measurements to identify subaerial exposure surfaces. Six facies were identified: five coral-rich types (framestone, rudstone, floatstone, grainstone, carbonate mud-rich packstone) and one well-sorted foraminiferal grainstone. Coral assemblages differ between transects. T1 is dominated by Porites with morphologies ranging from massive near the coast (Holes 1 and 2) to laminar and branching in generally finer sediments inland (Holes 3 and 4), suggesting a less exposed setting in inland holes. T2 features in situ corals only in Holes 1B and 2B near the coast, dominated by robust branching Acropora and encrusting Merulinidae, suggesting a shallow high-energy environment. Bioclastic grainstones contain mainly large benthic foraminifers, and fewer planktic foraminifers likely sourced from older Shimajiri Group siltstones. Stable isotope data, erosional surfaces, and paleosols indicate five subaerial exposure events. Coral framestones in Holes 1B, 2B, and C preserve three cavity infill stages—carbonate mud, LBF grainstone, and soil-derived clay—reflecting regression, flooding, and modern exposure. Our study suggests that sea level change and wave energy control the spatiotemporal distribution of coral assemblages and lithofacies in the MF.
ce550af3:
Coral reefs provide a critical archive of past sea-level variability because framework-building corals grow within narrow depth ranges and can be precisely dated across glacial–interglacial cycles. The drowned reefs surrounding the island of Hawai‘i offer an exceptional opportunity to reconstruct coral growth history in relation to rapid sea-level change, as the island simultaneously experienced steady volcanic subsidence and repeated reef drowning events. International Ocean Discovery Program Expedition 389 recovered an unprecedented suite of reef cores spanning the last ~500 kyr. This study focuses on corals collected from the two youngest reef terraces, H2 and H1 which drowned during the penultimate (133-134 ka) and last deglaciation (12-14 ka).

We quantify annual skeletal extension using high-resolution X-ray computed tomography (CT) and the CoralCT analysis framework to measure density banding in Porites corals. These measurements are compared to independently derived sea-level reconstructions and atmospheric pCO2 concentrations to assess extension rate variation across sea-level conditions. We test the hypothesis that corals exhibit faster extension during intervals of accelerated sea-level rise (“catch-up”), slower growth during highstands (“keep-up”), and growth failure during extreme rises (“give-up”).

Seventeen minimally altered coral cores from H1 and H2, collected from leeward and windward island settings, allow evaluation of spatial and temporal variability in coral growth response. Resulting extension histories will refine estimates of the rates of sea-level rise these reefs could tolerate before drowning, and illuminate the environmental thresholds governing reef survival. Preliminary results suggest that corals from both H1 and H2 have an average extension rate (0.95 cm/yr) lower than modern-day Hawaii Porites spp. (1.09 cm/yr), regardless of sea level. However, extension rates are lowest during periods of rapid change relative to periods of stable sea level. This work ultimately advances understanding of how coral reef systems respond to rapid, climate-driven sea-level change past and future.
34081418:
Coral reefs have a long evolutionary history of succession and disturbance. Their persistence through repeated environmental stress provides critical insight into Earth’s climatic history and associated reef response. International Ocean Discovery Program (IODP) Expedition 389 (Hawaiian Drowned Reefs, 2023) recovered fossil reef cores around Hawai’i Island's rapidly subsiding margins (~2.5 m/ka), offering a unique, high-resolution record of climate variability, sea-level change, and reef development over the last 500 kyr. Paleo-reef frameworks are dominated by a few genera that exhibit strong morphological variation. This low diversity poses a challenge for paleoenvironmental reconstructions, as fewer taxonomic indicators are available to distinguish among assemblages. Modern analogues of coral community structure are widely applied to improve our interpretations of Quaternary reef succession. The new drowned reef record around Hawai’i Island highlights the need for an updated framework of modern coral zonation, with particular interest in morphology as a valuable proxy for environmental setting and water depth. 

We test how windward-leeward environmental gradients shape modern Hawaiian coral morpho-taxa assemblages, and whether these patterns provide functional analogues for various depositional and ecological settings in IODP 389 cores. All data were compiled from modern benthic surveys (NOAA National Coral Reef Monitoring Program), geospatial rasters, satellite-derived environmental products, and hindcast datasets. We employed non-parametric, similarity-based multivariate analyses to identify distinct morpho-taxa communities across depth zones. When benthic data were limited, we included a semi-quantitative review of associated taxa (mesophotic corals, coralline algae, and vermetid gastropods) to build a conceptual depth-zonation profile for Hawai’i Island. Preliminary results reveal an island-wide environmental gradient transitioning from high-energy windward regimes to nutrient-enriched, thermally elevated, leeward regimes, with distinct morpho-taxa communities. These modern patterns align with several recurring coral assemblages in the 389 cores, while notable deviations in paleo-community structure highlight intervals of ecological reorganization under past climate stress.
276d5e48: Microbial life found in and on coral hosts plays a critical role in their physiology, ecology and evolution. Although both niche and neutral forces structure the bacterial assemblages found across coral holobionts, the neutral forces that play a role are often overlooked. We applied a neutral ecological model that originated from macro-ecology theory to analyze coral microbial assemblages across taxonomy and anatomy. We first characterize the role ecological stochasticity (i.e., random community assembly) has in shaping coral holobionts and then analyzed the taxa and predicted functional traits that deviate from neutral model predictions. Our results showed that a majority of coral microbiomes seem to be assembled by ecological stochasticity rather than by niche-based factors, and that mucus associated microbial communities across coral species and regions had a stronger model fit to neutrality compared to tissue and skeleton. Taxa that significantly deviated from the neutral model represent microbes predicted to be influenced by host  and taxon-specific differences. Subsequent comparative analysis between non-neutral (i.e., over- and under-represented taxa) and stochasticity distributed taxa show distinct taxonomic and functional characteristics. We also modeled microbial dispersal patterns within coral anatomy and detected a bottom-up flow of bacterial taxa found in the skeleton into tissue and tissue into mucus but not the other way around. Finally, we identified strong dispersal from sediment microbiomes into coral skeleton, but not tissue or mucus. Overall, this study approaches microbial ecology by characterizing both niche and neutral forces that influence coral microbial community assembly; reveals which microbial taxa and associated functions capabilities are most visible to selection; and identifies sources and sinks in the transmission of microbes within corals, across species, and between corals and their environments.
3fe51ef8: Large genome databases have markedly improved our understanding of marine microorganisms. Although these resources have focused on prokaryotes, genomes from many dominant marine lineages, such as Pelagibacter and Prochlorococcus, are conspicuously underrepresented. Here, we present the Great Barrier Reef Microbial Genomes Database (GBR MGD) comprising 5283 prokaryotic metagenome assembled genomes (MAGs) obtained from GBR seawater samples collected across 48 reefs using Nanopore and Illumina sequencing. We show that standard Illumina short read assemblies miss underrepresented populations due to a combination of strain heterogeneity and low GC% sequencing bias, wherein few MAGs were recovered above 0.01 nucleotide diversity (a measure of strain heterogeneity) and <40% GC. Species-level dereplication of the 5283 MAGs resulted in 876 unique species at 95% average nucleotide identity, of which 584 (66.7%) have not been previously represented in the Ocean Microbiomics Database, a curated collection of MAGs derived from global marine metagenomes. These MAGs may therefore represent either newly recognised species potentially specific to the GBR, or taxa present elsewhere but are not represented in public databases due to difficulties with assembly. The GBR MGD also comprises 20 chromosome level picoeukaryote and 808,585 viral genomes, including a newly described clade of marine Crassvirales. We demonstrate the utility of the GBR MGD to identify indicator taxa that can reliably predict the effects of reef management practices, such as the establishment of marine protected zones on the GBR with around 71% average accuracy (>85% in certain GBR sectors).
a6f19320: Coral reefs are among the most biodiverse marine ecosystems on Earth, providing a habitat for one third of all described marine species. Yet, we are uncertain of the prokaryotic diversity underlying this macrofauna diversity and what factors contribute to differences in the coral microbiome composition.  Prokaryotic diversity in coral reefs has previously been estimated to approximate the total estimated global microbial diversity. This most recent estimate of about 2.8 million distinct prokaryotic taxa (ASVs)  was based on plankton, fish, and coral samples from the Tara Pacific expedition across the Pacific ocean. However, coral-associated microbial diversity was represented by only three species, leaving a knowledge gap to what extent comprehensive coral species sampling may alter such an estimate. Here, we report on an extensive sampling effort using a novel Tara Pacific dataset comprising 2450 samples across 47 coral genera. Integrating data from these samples into prokaryotic diversity estimates, we show that the contribution of corals to reef microbiome diversity is up to three times higher than previously estimated, prompting an upwards correction of the reef microbiome diversity alone to over 8 million ASVs. This number supersedes the current total estimated bacterial diversity on Earth. Our data further show  that coral species harbor highly uneven microbiomes with some super-diverse coral hosts. Furthermore, we analyze the core microbiomes of the most common coral genera and whether there are functional differences between genera that have been observed to be resilient and those that might be more sensitive to heat stress. In addition, based on a comprehensive environmental data collection, we explore the abiotic and biotic drivers that shape coral microbiome composition. Our work highlights the large currently undescribed microbial ‘dark matter’ of coral reefs, at risk of being lost before we can fully appreciate and understand its importance to reef function, resilience, and survival.
0c5c9f57: Coral reefs support diverse microbial communities that underpin key biogeochemical processes. Yet, the turnover of seawater microbiomes across archipelagic reef systems in the Coral Triangle and the ecological drivers of these patterns remain poorly understood. Here, we characterized seawater microbial communities from 60 reef sites in the Philippines and Indonesia using 16S rRNA gene metabarcoding to test whether biogeography and habitat complexity shape microbial assemblages. We examined variation in community composition using alpha and beta diversity metrics derived from Bray–Curtis dissimilarities and ordination, and quantified patterns associated with differing levels of habitat heterogeneity using benthic Shannon diversity as a metric of reef structural complexity. Community composition differed significantly between countries, likely reflecting broad biogeographic separation and contrasting oceanographic settings. Dominant prokaryotic phyla included Pseudomonadota, Cyanobacteria, Bacteroidota, Actinomycetota, Verrucomicrobiota, Thermoplasmatota, and SAR324, with their relative abundances varying between Philippine and Indonesian sites. Alpha diversity was higher in more structurally complex reefs, and beta diversity increased with differences in habitat heterogeneity, indicating stronger taxonomic turnover among sites with contrasting reef complexity. These findings provide a comparative baseline for seawater microbiomes across two major reef regions in the Coral Triangle, and demonstrate that both regional biogeography and fine-scale habitat variation shape prokaryotic assemblages in reef-associated waters.
3258f3e9: Host-microbe symbioses are crucial to host and ecosystem health. Herbivorous coral reef fishes are widely recognized for controlling algal overgrowth and preserving resilient reefs, but the role of microorganisms in facilitating this has only recently gained attention. Herbivorous reef fishes rely on their gut microbiomes to digest complex algal polysaccharides and release metabolites. We investigate how seasonal upwelling alters herbivore microbiomes, microbial function, and host diet. We take a multi-omics approach, integrating DNA metabarcoding, metabolomics, and stable isotope analyses (SIA), to study host-microbe symbioses in species from two families: damselfishes (Pomacentridae) and surgeonfishes (Acanthuridae). We sampled fish gut microbiomes across two gulfs in Panama’s Tropical Eastern Pacific before, during, and after upwelling. Additionally, we conducted fish surveys during the upwelling and non-upwelling seasons to assess whether fish communities changed seasonally. We find that while the composition of fish communities does not change significantly between seasons, our four herbivores’ diets do shift. This includes an enrichment in cyanobacteria in most fishes’ diets during upwelling (23S rRNA data), as well as a slight decrease in 𝛅15N values (SIA), suggesting fishes may be eating at lower trophic levels during this period. Contrary to our expectations, the seasonal effect of upwelling on fish microbiome communities is relatively minor compared to the strong effect of host species, with gut microbiomes clustering tightly by species rather than season or region. Overall, these findings suggest that herbivorous fishes and their microbiomes are resilient to upwelling-driven environmental shifts: they appear to slightly modify their diets seasonally yet maintain similar feeding ranges and gut microbial communities. Seasonal environmental variability may confer greater resilience to environmental disturbances, allowing these fishes and their microbiomes to maintain their control of algal overgrowth. However, as climate change intensifies, it may outpace their adaptive capacity and drive vulnerable reef ecosystems past a critical threshold.
e4d8eb0a:
Octocorals are vital components of tropical, temperate, and cold-water benthic marine ecosystems. Their associated microbiomes, comprising microeukaryotes, prokaryotes, and viruses, are increasingly recognised as central to host health, nutrient cycling, and chemical defence. Metabarcoding and metagenomic approaches have uncovered taxonomic and functional complexity within these microbial communities, revealing patterns of host specificity and health status, along with seasonality and geographic structuring. However, anthropogenic stressors, particularly those associated with global climate change, exert intense pressure on coral-dominated ecosystems, leading to complex and poorly understood local and regional patterns of octocoral expansion and mortality. Microbial interactions may be a main driver of these contrasting outcomes by mediating the ecological resilience of octocorals to environmental stress. 

In this presentation, we synthesise the current state of research on the diversity, organisation, and function of the octocoral microbiome, and identify critical knowledge gaps on octocoral holobionts relative to scleractinian corals. We showcase a meta-analysis of 79 publicly available bacterial genomes from octocorals and reveal group-specific specialisation in denitrification and nitrate assimilation, along with widespread capacities for essential amino acid, cofactor, and vitamin production, suggesting important contributions to nutrient cycling in the holobiont. While sampling efforts between cultured and uncultured lineages are even, our genomic survey reveals strong sampling bias toward the Atlantic Ocean, temperate gorgonians, and healthy host states, whereas bacterial genomes representing the pathobiome, tropical and/or deep-sea regions, and other octocoral taxa remain underrepresented. Accordingly, we propose future research directions to advance understanding of octocoral microbiome ecology and its role in the resilience of tropical, temperate and cold-water coral reefs.
621b1585: Octocorals are key ecosystem engineers that enhance marine biodiversity across a wide range of habitats, from shallow to deep waters. However, knowledge of their microbiomes, particularly the prokaryotic components that are crucial for understanding and evaluating coral health and adaptive capacity, remains limited. In particular, the twilight zone represents an environment where steep depth-related gradients and unique regional conditions may give rise to distinct microbial communities compared with those in shallow or deep waters, yet studies focusing on this region are still very limited. In this study, we analyzed the prokaryotic community structure of 100 individuals of the precious coral Corallium japonicum collected from the Ryukyu Archipelago, Kochi, and the Ogasawara Islands, Japan using 16S rRNA metabarcoding. A total of 539 taxa at the family level were identified, with the microbiome being predominantly composed of Endozoicomonadaceae and Spongiibacteraceae. The overall community structure was significantly explained by latitude and depth, both of which showed low multicollinearity. Moreover, the relative abundances of Endozoicomonadaceae and Spongiibacteraceae exhibited complementary trends along latitudinal and depth gradients, Endozoicomonadaceae increasing in shallower and higher-latitude sites, while Spongiibacteraceae increased in deeper and lower-latitude sites. These patterns suggest that C. japonicum may adapt and acclimatize to environmental variation through shifts in its associated microbiome. In addition, network analysis further revealed taxa with strong potential ecological importance, including Bacillaceae, which showed numerous positive correlations with other taxa, and Staphylococcaceae and Saprospiraceae, which displayed negative correlations. Although these families were relatively low in abundance, they exhibited high centrality within the microbial network. Collectively, these findings provide essential baseline information for understanding the mechanisms of coral holobionts in the twilight zone, a region where sampling remains technically challenging.
5d2a8e62: Coral holobionts emerge from complex associations between the Cnidarian host and diverse symbiotic partners, yet the relative influence of host identity and environmental conditions in shaping holobiont structure remains difficult to disentangle. Strong host fidelity is often observed, but resolving host identity at the species level may overlook fine-scale structuring driven by intraspecific genetic variation. In this context, hydrozoan reef-builders (Milleporidae) remain particularly understudied despite their key ecological roles in tropical reefs. Here, we investigated host–symbiont associations in three co-occurring Millepora species (Millepora cf. exaesa, M. tenera, and M. cf. platyphylla) across three environmentally distinct habitats on Réunion Island (inner reef flat, outer reef flat, mid-reef slope), combining microsatellite genotyping of coral hosts with ITS2 and 16S rRNA metabarcoding of Symbiodiniaceae and prokaryotic assemblages. Symbiodiniaceae communities were largely dominated by Symbiodinium (99.2%), primarily from A3 and A16 lineages. Cladocopium occurred at very low abundance (0.8%) and predominantly associated with M. cf. platyphylla and M. tenera. Most Symbiodinaceae profiles were restricted to particular species and genotypes within habitats. Similarly, prokaryotic assemblages were primarily structured by host genotypes, though to a lesser extent than Symbiodiniaceae. Millepora hosts consistently associated with the Spirochaetaceae, Kiloniellaceae and Desulfobacteraceae bacterial families across all habitats, suggesting key evolutionary-maintained associations. Within habitats, each Millepora species harbored a distinct microbiome, yet both Symbiodiniaceae and prokaryotic assemblages also shifted along the habitat gradient, reflecting flexibility of host-symbiont associations and potential acclimatization or adaptation to local environmental conditions. Our results indicate that fire coral holobionts are primarily structured by host-driven processes, with the environment acting as a secondary selective layer for symbiont acquisition. This study provides the first microbiome description of M. tenera and M. cf. exaesa, and sheds light on how host identity and environment jointly shape unique host-symbiont associations contributing to the ecological success of these overlooked coral holobionts.
46f3baec:
Climate change research on corals has largely focused on the direct physiological impacts of warming and acidification. Yet corals exist within complex ecological networks where direct effects can influence other species through altered ecological interactions. These indirect pathways have the potential to modify community structure and ecosystem functioning at scales equal to or greater than direct effects. However, they remain poorly quantified and rarely incorporated into models of future reef trajectories. 
Synthesising multiple empirical studies from the Great Barrier Reef and a naturally acidified reef in Papua New Guinea, we evaluate how climate change alters two fundamental ecological interactions, competition and predation. We then assess how these altered interactions affect coral performance and community dynamics. Across both warming and acidification contexts, we show consistent declines in interaction strength: competitive encounters become less frequent and less intense following mass bleaching and under acidified conditions, while predation pressure from corallivorous butterflyfishes is reduced due to combined changes in fish community composition, feeding behaviour and coral availability. These shifts indicate that climate-driven bleaching and acidification have the potential to weaken interaction networks that have historically shaped coral assemblages. 
We provide examples of competitive release and altered predator-prey dynamics, including for individuals that appear physiologically tolerant of climate stress. The extent of these changes varied with site, genus, size structure, coral cover, and colony abundance and distribution. By considering the multiple stressors of broad-scale climate change and local ecological interactions, we show that mortality responses were largely predictable from additive or multiplicative models, suggesting that incorporating indirect effects can improve the accuracy of future projections. Together these results highlight that indirect, interaction-mediated pathways are critical determinants of coral resilience. To forecast the structure and function of future reef ecosystems, indirect effects must be integrated alongside direct physiological responses, in predictive models and management frameworks.
da8352ab: Coral reefs are among the most diverse ecosystems where macroalgae are commonly abundant (up to 100% benthic cover) and diverse (over 40 orders with Rhodophyta presenting the highest richness). While larger and upright seaweed taxa (e.g., Sargassum, Dictyota) are relativity easy to identify/quantify, others such as crustose coralline algae (CCA) and turf-forming species (e.g., Digenea, Ceramium) and regularly clumped together as a single group. The oversimplification of these cryptic species groups limits our ability to understand fully their ecological functions. We conducted high-taxonomic resolution field surveys paired with morphological and molecular identifications to assess the functional diversity of crustose and turf-forming algae of Florida’s coral reefs. Benthic surveys also included sessile animals (e.g., corals) and multiple abiotic factors (e.g., sediment accumulation, slope, rugosity) to evaluate Florida's coral reefs' overall composition and functioning. We found low CCA taxonomic diversity (less than five species dominated most corals), high turf-forming species, and evidence of reef habitat-specific distribution. Light intensity, substrate slope, and herbivory shape macroalgal distribution at a small scale (within centimeters). Additionally, we reported low coral cover (~1%) and low density (~ 1 coral m2) of small adult corals, which likely reflects the cumulative effects of years of disturbances. The presence and density of juvenile corals were negatively correlated with the depth of the sediment layer trapped within long, sediment-laden algal turfs, which is particularly abundant (>50% cover) in low complexity reef habitats (low-relief groove, low-relief spur top, and high-relief groove). Our results indicate that current unsuitable habitat conditions (high sediment load) for early-life-stage corals may be an essential factor preventing coral recovery.
7fa19a00: In an era of increasing disturbances, understanding the capacity for coral reefs to recover and the drivers that underpin resilience is critical for predicting the health of future coral reefs. Traditional resilience paradigms for Western Atlantic reefs suggest that high herbivore biomass and low macroalgal cover are tenets of resilient systems. Yet, it is clear that some locations, such as Florida’s reefs, and potentially other locations, are not well explained by this paradigm. Here, we suggest that the proliferation and increased dominance of long sediment-laden algal turfs (LSATs) creates an additional negative feedback process that helps explain the compromised resilience of Florida’s reefs and further extends the existing Western Atlantic model. Collectively, coral mortality, reef flattening, and intense grazing appear to have created a series of negative feedback processes that reinforce a flat, sediment-laden benthos that impedes the recovery of already diminished coral populations. Importantly, feedbacks associated with LSATs could be strengthened in the presence of high herbivore biomass, thereby undermining foundational expectations of resilience based on grazing pressure alone. Here, we outline this destructive cycle and provide support for the mechanisms that drive these feedbacks. Although we focus on the ecological context of Florida’s reefs, our expansion of the conceptual framework will likely apply to other reef systems in the Caribbean with similar ecological attributes. Mounting evidence suggests that LSAT dominance represents a novel ecosystem state for Western Atlantic reefs.
4c9d67bc:
Since time immemorial, Pacific Island nations have relied on coastal resources and marine ecosystem services for shoreline protection, development, nutrition, and cultural practices. However, with the unprecedented climate-change-induced coral decline worldwide, the resilience and economic development of these insular territories are being compromised. In French Polynesia, decadal data from the Mo’orea Coral Reef Long Term Ecological Research program show that reefs have suffered significant structural damages from past cyclonic events, ongoing anthropogenic pressures and predator outbreaks (Taramea or Acanthaster planci), resulting in once coral-dominated benthic assemblages shifting to macroalgal dominance. 
In this project, we seek to test the microbialization hypothesis, wherein phase shifts disrupt bacterioplankton recycling, leading to inefficient organic matter metabolism and furthering coral decline. We hypothesized that an increased macroalgae abundance in benthic communities will alter seawater chemistry and organic matter flux with indirect effects on the composition and abundance of microbial communities consistent with microbialization. Mesocosm experiments simulated coral-dominated, algae-dominated, and post-disturbance reefs (Taramea and cyclone disturbances) to assess the effects of benthic cover on compositional dynamics of microbial communities and dissolved organic matter. Organic matter (fDOM and DOC) was quantified via fluorescence spectroscopy and catalytic oxidation, while microbial communities were analyzed using flow cytometry with amplicon next generation DNA sequencing. Algae-dominated mesocosms displayed higher DOC production, compositionally distinct organic matter exudates, and lower inorganic nutrient concentrations. Altered chemical outputs in algae-dominated aquaria were associated with significantly different bacterial abundances and community composition. Results of this project provide evidence supporting microbialization of Mo’orea reefs undergoing phase shifts to algal dominance and enhance our understanding of the interactions between bacterioplankton communities and benthic species, with implications for biogeochemical processes in the oligotrophic ecosystems of the South Pacific.
4c97bd02: Coral reefs are in dramatic global decline, with outbreaks of coral diseases playing a significant and increasingly frequent role in recent decades. We recently demonstrated that loss of sea cucumbers, which have been overharvested for decades, made corals in both French Polynesia and Palmyra Atoll dramatically more susceptible to disease. Here we show that deposition of drifting seaweeds greatly exacerbates this problem and that sea cucumbers act as marine janitors that clean reef sediments and suppress coral disease. Surveys conducted in Moorea, French Polynesia, revealed that sea cucumbers were naturally more abundant in reef areas receiving greater seaweed deposition. A complementary feeding-choice experiment found that individuals preferentially fed on sediments where we experimentally deposited seaweed on the benthos. In exclusion experiments spanning a gradient of natural seaweed deposition, sea cucumber removal exacerbated disease-associated coral tissue loss at sites experiencing the highest seaweed inputs. Finally, when seaweeds or white rice (as a proxy for organic enrichment without seaweed-derived compounds) were experimentally deposited on otherwise “clean” sediments, corals in contact with those sediments suffered elevated tissue death relative to controls, demonstrating that organic loading can trigger disease. Together, these findings highlight the cascading impacts of overfishing on reef ecosystem function. Removal of herbivores promotes blooms of seaweeds that break off and accumulate elsewhere as mobile organics, while overharvest of sea cucumbers eliminates the detritivores needed to repackage this material into invertebrate biomass. In their absence, seaweed deposition may exacerbate coral losses to disease on reefs in the Anthropocene.
a790250b: Macroalgal communities exhibit vertical stratification, yet canopy–understorey interactions in tropical systems remain poorly understood, with previous research largely focused on herbivory or competition with corals. We investigated the spatial patterns and temporal dynamics of macroalgal assemblages across nine inshore reef sites in the central Great Barrier Reef every 7 weeks for 14 months. Specifically, we surveyed three turbid island systems (Orpheus, Havannah, Magnetic Island) characterized by seasonal sediment inputs and high resuspension. Community structure varied markedly across space, with Orpheus Island showing greater heterogeneity than Havannah and Magnetic Island. The primary driver of temporal change was the seasonal succession of the canopy. Canopy structure (i.e., height and density) peaked between November and March (austral summer), reaching up to 1.5 m in height and 40 thalli m^-2 in density. The canopy’s structure drove the overall community composition through a seasonal trade-off between competition and facilitation. During the summer peak, high canopy height was strongly associated with lower community richness and a lower abundance of understorey macroalgae (competitive exclusion). In contrast, canopy density was positively associated with higher understorey abundance in winter, indicating a temporal shift toward facilitation, where the residual canopy provides structural refuge. Winter increases in understorey abundance and blooms of ephemeral macroalgae resulted in increased community richness. At the taxon-specific level, multivariate latent variable models suggested distinct co-occurrence patterns, with strong negative associations within canopy-forming macroalgal genera (e.g., Sargassum and Sirophysalis), and more diffuse, sometimes positive, interactions among understorey macroalgal taxa. Taxon-specific trajectories were highly spatially variable, indicating that local conditions mediate community turnover. Together, these findings highlight the dynamic nature of tropical macroalgal assemblages and underscore the importance of interactions between taxa and spatial context in shaping community structure and composition on inshore reefs.
68bf14eb: Coral reef decline requires responses that integrate ecological science with social strategies. In Venezuela, migration has become a collective condition: some have left the country or their cities, while others have migrated into new professions or trades as strategies for survival. This fractured social fabric reflects a “broken pact” that parallels the ecological collapse of coral reefs, where symbiotic relationships dissolve and habitats no longer provide refuge. Such ruptures generate overlapping grief—personal, social, institutional, and ecological— that call for new resilience. Vivimos el Mar (“We live the Sea”) is an initiative that explores how artistic and psychological practices can complement marine science to foster awareness and resilience. Two pilot interventions—the Marine Oracle and the Refuge of Emotions—use literary and psychodramatic methods to connect participants with reefs. The Marine Oracle employs illustrations and narrative to evoke identification with reef species, highlighting ecological ruptures such as the dissolution of coral–zooxanthellae symbiosis. The Refuge of Emotions applies psychodrama as a bridge, creating collective scenarios where unconscious dimensions of grief and hope emerge, transforming despair into rituals of resilience. In community workshops, reef species are introduced through familiar metaphors, linking them to everyday experiences. These references are complemented with information on their population status and practical ways to support their conservation, ensuring that symbolic connections are grounded in ecological realities. Participants are then invited to “write a letter to your reef,” an exercise that transforms coral ecosystems into interlocutors of care and empathy. These letters receive pedagogical responses with scientific explanations that translate ecological processes into accessible narratives, while psychologists use them to bridge individual and collective emotions, fostering resilience and shared strategies of care. This work shows how interdisciplinary practices function as epistemic models for reef conservation, addressing ecological grief collectively and providing replicable tools for community resilience.
6281686b: Performing arts span music, drama, dance, music, magic, poetry, puppetry, circus arts, comedy and more.  Alongside visual arts activities, this presentation seeks to encourage coral reef scientists, managers and other stakeholders in the area of performance – through individual expression and via collaboration with established artists.  Recent recognition for culture and heritage has included the proposed inclusion of indicators within the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) adaptive strategies.  Performing arts has the capacity to support needed change through engaging a wide variety of audiences to promote: acceptance of climate science; understanding of likely natural and community impacts; and cooperation on societal changes to minimise negative outcomes.  In the words of Afghan artist and academic Shamsia Hassani,  “Art changes people’s minds and people change the world.”
d4807c44: This practice-led research investigates contemporary eco-installation art and art-science practices that respond to the urgent ecological issues of the Anthropocene and that challenge the anthropocentric worldview underpinning this epoch. It critiques anthropomorphic strategies in the field of contemporary visual art and proposes ecocentric ways to engage with the more-than-human world by exemplifying interspecies entanglement through resulting artworks. The research proposes a new art-science model of creative production grounded in an intensive study of coral symbionts and asks how contemporary installation art is capable of fostering new kinds of ecocentric multi-species relationships. The study is grounded in a new collaboration with a marine ecological research team who have monitored the decline in health of Queensland’s Moreton Bay coral communities, and is informed by qualitative methods of field observation, semi-structured interviews, co-creation, and studio experimentation with both marine ecologists and contemporary eco-artists. The project’s primary outcomes include a series of immersive installations which incorporated scientific visual data amongst coral-inspired sculptures, and the development of a novel framework for art-science collaboration. Ultimately, the project seeks to trial anti-anthropomorphic, ecocentric approaches to eco-installation art that enhance the public accessibility and impact of ecological research, whilst also presenting the ecosophical perspectives urgently needed in this anthropocentric era.
4735f27c:
Think back to your youth, to what inspired your curiosity, passions, and career path. Put yourself in the shoes of a child seeing something spectacular for the first time, a community striving to save their way of life and meet critical needs, or the individual on a spiritual path balancing nature and beliefs. To achieve impactful conservation, we must first look to the core of coral reef-dependent communities: their traditions, belief systems, indigenous knowledge, community dynamics, and individual drive. By integrating these foundational elements with marine ecosystem sciences, hands-on, inspirational experiences, artistic practices, and individual expression, we can better inform communities to advocate for the roles and benefits of coral reefs, bridging the ever-changing past, present, and future possibilities. 

By empowering a selection of underserved, highly talented artists in reef-dependent communities across continents, providing them with inspirational coral reef experiences and knowledge, and pairing them with climate scientists and cultural advisors to develop, publish, and socialize science and culturally informed climate change art, we can create a community-wide to global cascade of awareness. This joint compilation of fine art pieces will visually describe existing climate-induced changes, as well as best- and worst-case scenarios projected for the future. Not only will this build skills, knowledge, and networks for underserved community members, but this effort, grounded in cultural and scientific knowledge has the potential to share a greater, more wide-spread understanding of how climate change impacts and mitigations affect communities across the world. 

Having worked closely with organizations such as the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration and U.S. Agency for International Development, as well as diverse stakeholders in marine ecosystem-based management, and the National Geographic Society Pristine Seas on scientific illustration, I have the skill/experience to achieve this; I need the partners (which I can build upon), funding, and support. Interested?
0ab40ad2:
The Atlantic Literature Program, developed by the Coral Vivo Institute, integrates artistic creation, literary narratives, and ocean literacy to strengthen emotional and educational engagement with marine conservation in Brazilian public schools. The program’s main publications combine poetry and storytelling to address climate change, marine biodiversity, and human–ocean relationships. This approach aligns with studies that recognize poetry and artistic expression as powerful tools for shaping environmental awareness and fostering affective connections that motivate ecological action. Through these creative pieces of work, the program seeks not only to inform but to cultivate new ways of sensing, imagining, and caring for the sea.
The program operates through partnerships with county offices of education along the Brazilian coast, donating thousands of printed books to public schools for use in classrooms and interdisciplinary projects. These materials support the inclusion of ocean themes in curricula and provide teachers with accessible resources to explore marine conservation through art, literature, and community engagement. Complementarily, the program promotes educator training through Coral Vivo Education Network Meetings, a national platform for exchanging successful practices that combine creativity, science, and environmental responsibility.
Between 2024 and 2025, qualitative evidence revealed strong connections between students, teachers, and the UNESCO–IOC Ocean Literacy framework (Brazil has stood out at the national level). Schools formally incorporated ocean content and organized artistic exhibitions, field activities, and community events inspired by the program’s materials. During this period, dialogues were held with 27 county offices of education, and 35.712 books were distributed, potentially reaching 230.000 students.
By positioning art and literature as epistemologies for environmental learning, the Atlantic Literature Program demonstrates how creative practice can catalyze educational engagement in marine conservation. Its experience reinforces the potential of artistic approaches to inspire care for coral ecosystems and contribute to the movement of reimagining reefs through interdisciplinary collaboration.
736a2335:
Accelerating coral reef decline calls for conservation approaches that reach beyond academic communities and cultivate partnerships among scientists, educators, and young learners. This talk highlights creative and collaborative approaches to marine science education that strengthen community partnerships, foster scientific literacy and stewardship in early learners, and promote communication and teaching skills in graduate student trainees.

We developed a ‘Coral Reefs & Our Changing Oceans’ 4th–5th grade lesson series integrating ecological concepts with creative storytelling and art. Students learn about trophic interactions, co-construct a food web, explore how human impacts alter reef ecosystems and consider management interventions. Finally, they design a ‘Reef Superheroes’ comic linked to careers in science communication. Initially focused on Texas’ Flower Garden Banks, the lessons were shared in Texas schools and with the Texas State Aquarium. Through a collaboration with Jamaican educators, we adapted the curriculum to Jamaican ecosystems, conservation initiatives, and teaching standards for a scientist-educator STEM camp in Negril. We published lessons in English and Spanish as Open Educational Resources on OER Commons for free use and regional adaptation.

This educational outreach model helped inspire the implementation of a University of Texas Marine Science Institute graduate training program in evidence-based teaching practices. Graduate students learn about student-centered pedagogy, develop K–12 lessons, and gain teaching experience in local schools (Batterton et al. 2024, JMBE). K–12 students build ecological literacy and a commitment to marine stewardship, while early-career scientists strengthen teaching and community-engagement skills. Standardized test trends suggest improved student performance in partner schools and graduate trainees report increased confidence in evidence-based teaching and communication. Conserving coral reefs for future generations begins with cultivating genuine partnerships among scientists, educators, and the communities they serve. This collaborative approach–rooted in creative science education and graduate pedagogical training–offers a scalable model to build trust in science and foster marine stewardship.
43e3f751: Inclusion in education is a legally guaranteed right and a pathway toward a more equitable society. Within the context of marine conservation—particularly the protection of reef environments—strengthening dialogue between universities and coastal communities is essential. The project “Para Que Serve? O que faz um cientista que trabalha com o mar?” (What’s it for? What does a marine scientist do?) promotes inclusive and participatory knowledge exchange, introducing students to themes associated with marine ecology, reef conservation, and the protection of marine species. To support accessible learning, we developed playful activities and multisensory educational materials designed to be inclusive for students with different visual abilities. We evaluated the effectiveness of these science activities in enhancing the understanding of basic ecological concepts, including those related to reef environments and ecosystem conservation. Six sessions were conducted in two schools located in a coastal city in Rio de Janeiro. All activities incorporated multisensory materials handcrafted with common, low-cost materials. Twenty-nine students between 5-17 took part; 36% were blind or low-vision, and 64% were sighted. Across all sessions, multisensory materials elicited higher engagement and facilitated comprehension for both groups, demonstrating their value in supporting learning about marine and reef ecosystems. Nonetheless, educator mediation played a critical role, highlighting the need for trained facilitators for inclusive conservation education. By integrating accessibility, hands-on learning, and marine conservation themes, this project strengthened the relationship between academic and coastal communities and fostered early awareness of reef ecosystem importance. We hope these results stimulate the development of more inclusive outreach initiatives, broaden public understanding of marine and reef environments, and inspire actions that promote scientific knowledge beyond traditional academic boundaries.
4a98691a: This is a story of a little girl who had no idea coral reefs existed. This little girl lived inside of a polluted metropolitan city. Politics were strife with corruption, society focused on economic growth, the concept of nature was foreign. How does this little girl who can barely swim, who gets severely seasick, grow up to fall in love with the ocean? The answer - art, science and community.
0b03d2f5:
The diverse coral reefs of Guam are central to the CHamoru people’s identity, subsistence, and spirituality, yet they face accelerating degradation from climate change, land-based sources of pollution, and overuse. In an effort to strengthen coral reef stewardship across generations, the Guam Department of Agriculture created a museum exhibit titled “Gineftao: Understanding the Ocean, Honoring our Home” to integrate interactive art, Indigenous knowledge, and experiential learning with community engagement. The heart of this project is the concept of Gineftao, meaning selfless generosity, which is used as a creative practice to teach and generate knowledge related to being a steward of the ocean and its coral reefs.  

Surrounding the central framework of Gineftao are culturally grounded components developed with local practitioners, including talåya (cast-net) experts, fisherfolk, youth artists, and educators. Visitors explore topics that range from traditional fishing narratives and local Guam legends to coral reef ecology and the broader ridge to reef concept while participating in hands-on activities.  

Rather than simply translating science into aesthetic forms, Gineftao positions art as a mode of inquiry that reflects CHamoru relationships to the reef rooted in respect, reciprocity, and collective responsibility. Early participant feedback indicates increased emotional connection to reef ecosystems, greater awareness of local threats to reefs, and strengthened recognition of Indigenous perspectives in conservation dialogue. 

This presentation will explore the development and collaboration process of the co-created art, culturally grounded design, and community participation of the exhibit. A virtual tour of the exhibit installations will display resources for fellow practitioners and educators to optimize visitor engagement to catalyze ocean literacy and influence conservation behavior. As Guam faces rapid environmental change, Gineftao offers a model for how creative practice can build a culture of coral reef stewardship that is scientifically informed, culturally resonant, and deeply place-based.
aba92593: Culture is often understood through tradition, ancestry, and place. Yet culture evolves continuously, shaped by science, technology, and the lived experiences of people responding to environmental change. However, modern conservation knowledge is often excluded from this cultural narrative. In the wake of the 2024 global mass coral bleaching event, we created Is This Love, Mama?, an illustrated storybook that translates this ecological crisis into an emotionally resonant narrative for children, families, and non-scientists. Grounded in real field data from Malaysian coral reefs, the story integrates accurate ecological information, local biodiversity, and conservation practices, while weaving in cultural and emotional themes relatable to local audiences. The development process involved iterative feedback from Malaysian NGOs, coral reef scientists, conservationists, illustrators, and storytellers to ensure biological accuracy while maintaining emotional resonance. Through themes of curiosity, loss, and resilience, the story also depicts simple reef-supporting behaviours, such as debris removal and coral monitoring, modelling conservation as something relatable rather than remote. By portraying conservationists as community members rather than distant heroes, the story reframes their work as part of a living cultural identity. The storybook will be made freely available for digital download to extend its reach while minimising material footprint. We propose that integrating scientific practice into nature storytelling allows it to enter cultural memory, shaping how modern communities understand and value their relationship with the natural world. In a time when reef decline is accelerating faster than public awareness, storytelling offers a pathway to reach diverse audiences whose identities extend beyond traditional cultural boundaries. Positioning conservation as a shared cultural practice through storytelling may be essential for empowering the next generation of reef guardians in Malaysia and beyond, helping them recognise their responsibility at a moment when the future of coral reefs is being shaped in real time.
84771392: Growing coastal cities urgently require protection strategies that sustain the ecosystems essential for their long-term resilience. In land-scarce Singapore, where sea-level rise is projected to exceed 0.5m in this century, almost 70% of the shoreline has been hardened. This has contributed to the loss of 60% of coral reefs, 90% of mangroves, and 45% of seagrass cover. Yet, substantial portions of this tropical nation’s artificial shorelines are becoming ‘hybrid shores’ through natural colonisation by corals and other habitat forming taxa, adding both biodiversity and coastal protection. Such incorporation of nature alongside grey structures, intentionally or serendipitously, reintroduces lost and additional ecological ecosystem services (EES). However, the EES potential of Singapore’s heterogeneous hybrid shorelines remains poorly quantified. A better understanding will improve nature-inclusive coastal protection, restoration planning, and the non-monetary valuation of coastal protection designs. This study presents a systems-level framework to identify, map, and compare the EES potential of grey and hybrid systems along Singapore’s mainland and offshore shorelines. Using high-resolution (1×1m) transects, habitat composition is characterised perpendicular to the shore and linked with relevant EES. Using habitat distribution as a proxy visualises the fine-scale distribution of EES potential across coastal systems. The application of the InVEST Coastal Vulnerability Model will help visualise the coastal protection attributed to these natural features, including corals, seagrasses, and mangroves. By making the ecological contributions of hybrid systems comparable, this modelling informs nature-inclusive coastal protection strategies. Field sites span and combine natural intertidal reef and mudflats, seagrass and mangrove areas, steep seawalls, revetments, breakwaters, and boardwalks. They include planted, naturally recruited, and preserved habitats. Hybrid shorelines support greater intertidal area and habitat diversity than hardened structures, enhancing and diversifying service provision potential. These insights from Singapore’s highly modified shorelines inform system-based, restorative designs while strengthening the long-term resilience of future nature-based solutions.
b8476956: Innovative finance mechanisms such as conservation bonds are emerging as promising tools to mobilize capital for coral reef protection, restoration, and regeneration at scale. However, the success of these instruments depends on establishing scientifically robust key performance indicators (KPIs) that can credibly measure ecological outcomes over multi-year to decadal timeframes while remaining interpretable to investors. The challenge lies in bridging ecological complexity and the financial sector's need for transparent, reliable metrics that support sustained funding. We outline a framework for translating best available science into actionable KPIs for reef conservation finance. Using predictive modelling and data from the MERMAID platform (datamermaid.org), we evaluate how management interventions—such as strengthening marine protected areas or implementing restoration—affect measurable outcomes like fish biomass and coral cover over time. We demonstrate translating such ecological recovery curves into KPIs with a real-world case study of the World Bank's Indonesia Coral Reef Bond. Our approach explicitly incorporates uncertainty, recognizing that ecological predictions inherently contain variability that must be communicated effectively to risk-averse investors without undermining confidence in long-term conservation outcomes. We examine critical considerations in indicator selection: (1) identifying fish families that serve as reliable proxies for reef health while minimizing susceptibility to stochastic events and sampling artifacts; (2) establishing thresholds that reflect meaningful ecological change, particularly as environmental baselines shift; and (3) developing uncertainty communication strategies for financial audiences unfamiliar with ecological science. This work contributes to the growing interface between conservation science and finance by providing guidance on designing evidence-based performance metrics that serve both ecological integrity and financial sustainability. As conservation bonds scale globally, establishing standardized yet adaptable approaches for science-based indicator development will be essential for channeling sustained capital toward initiatives that achieve long-term reef health while maintaining scientific accountability.
e1585176: The Gulf of Mannar (GOM) located in the southeast of India has coral reefs, seagrass beds and mangroves. Major reefs are distributed around twenty-one islands that lie in a coastal stretch of 160 km, the area covering which was declared as GOM Marine National Park in 1986. The reef-associated fishery is the main source of livelihood for the dependent fisher folk. Till 2000, this ecosystem was facing severe degradation due to coral mining, destructive fishing practices and pollution. Government’s action and awareness creation helped to stop coral mining in 2005 and reduced other human impacts. But, climatic factors like elevated temperature pose a serious threat and GOM has lost vast areas of pristine reefs.  The loss of reefs caused serve erosion on island shores threatening their existence. Measures like coral restoration initiated in 2002 and seagrass restoration started in 2008 in GOM have helped restore over 26 acres of degraded reefs (with 43,000 sq.m transplantation area in clusters) and over 20 acres of degraded seagrasses (with 38,000 sq.m transplantation area in clusters). Thirteen islands have mangrove patches and small-scale restoration was done in some islands.  The highly eroded Vaan Island was restored through deployment of multipurpose artificial reef modules, which also serve as substrates for natural coral recruitment and other epifauna. The restoration of Kariyachalli Island is in progress. Involvement of the local community in the restoration activities enabled them to understand the importance of these resources for healthy environment and their wellbeing. Though the climatic and non-climatic factors continue to threaten these ecosystems, the integrated restoration approach in GOM in the last two decades, supported by long-term monitoring, helps not only to protect and conserve the coral reefs, seagrass meadows and submerging islands but also to enhance biodiversity richness, fishery wealth and dependent livelihood.
bf9d9752: Mangroves and coral reefs provide many benefits to coastal communities, including those in the U.S. Virgin Islands (USVI). In 2017, two Category 5 hurricanes hit the territory, devastating USVI mangrove forests. Data from the Territorial Mangrove Monitoring Program of 21 sites, reveal delayed recovery and limited natural regeneration, indicating restoration is warranted. Meanwhile, USVI coral reefs have declined sharply due to overfishing, coastal development, ocean warming, and disease. In response, the University of the Virgin Islands (UVI) developed the GRROE USVI Mangroves and Reef Response programs, to restore these critical habitats through community partnerships, including education. One such effort, Mangroves in the Classroom (MIC), began in 2021, as a collaboration between UVI, the Virgin Islands Department of Education and local businesses. MIC has engaged >1,500 middle and high school students in the science and conservation of local mangroves. The program consists of 8-10 lessons co-designed with paid, local educators, a semester-long experiment where students grow mangroves in their classrooms, plus two field trips where students kayak and snorkel through a local mangrove lagoon, visit UVI’s land-based mangrove and coral nurseries, and participate in mangrove outplanting at local restoration sites. UVI undergraduate and graduate students act as paid classroom facilitators to enhance science learning outcomes and relatability, promoting a near-peer mentoring model while also providing training in education and outreach. Program evaluation using pre/post surveys and interviews of students, revealed significant gains in understanding mangrove ecosystem benefits, self-efficacy for learning and doing science, environmental awareness, and stewardship values. Program strengths included hands-on learning, inspirational facilitators, and real-world relevance. Building on MIC’s success, Corals in the Classroom is being piloted in Fall 2025. Together, these programs and partnerships develop innovative, community-based solutions that promote environmental literacy and stewardship of coastal ecosystems and strengthen these islands’ ecological and cultural resilience.
b27f2667: Much of the lowland forest of West New Britain, Papua New Guinea, has been replaced by oil palm plantations and many mangrove forests along the shore have disappeared. This habitat loss, further exacerbated by climate change impacts such as more extreme weather events and rising sea levels causing higher tides, results in increased erosion, run-off during flooding, eutrophication of the nearby coral reefs, while also resulting in adverse social impacts on coastal communities.  In partnership with palm oil companies such as New Britain Palm Oil and Hargy Oil Palms LTD, Mahonia Na Dari Research and Conservation Centre is working to restore mangrove forests along the shores of Kimbe Bay, thereby creating and extending the vital ecological buffer zones that benefit both the terrestrial and marine environments. Since 2017, more than 16000 mangroves have been planted. We integrate these efforts with our long-running marine education and community engagement programmes. Now for the first time we are looking at implementing systematic monitoring of our mangrove restoration to understand how the new forests are used by the communities and what the impacts are on the wider Kimbe Bay ecosystem. Using this data, we are hoping to attract more buy-in from palm oil companies, as well as diversified funding and research to expand mangrove restoration in Kimbe Bay.
00ee963b: Coral reefs, seagrass meadows and mangroves are critical biodiversity hotspots that support fisheries, coastal protection and tourism, valued at more than US$2.7 trillion annually. Despite this importance, these ecosystems are rapidly declining due to human pressures, with around 50% of global coral cover lost since the 1970s. Restoration is therefore urgent.
