In 2023, gametes were collected from bleaching tolerant and wild-type parent colonies and settled to produce selectively bred and wild-type juveniles. These selectively bred corals represent phenotypes that may dominate future reefs, allowing us to explore how future populations respond to different environmental conditions across the bay. Three-year-old juveniles were outplanted to reefs with varied flow, light, temperature, sediment, and salinity. We monitored coral growth, structural complexity, survivorship, symbiont community composition (ITS2 profiling), and biochemical responses (metabolomic profiling) through time to assess performance. Results from this longitudinal study demonstrate how selectively bred M. capitata juveniles perform across different reef environments, providing insight into the persistence of thermal tolerant traits under varied environmental parameters and offering context for restoration and management decisions.
ca43059f: As the demand for coastal protection intensifies in the face of climate change, alternatives to traditional hard engineering such as ‘hybrid’ shorelines are gaining traction. Natural shorelines tend to be structurally complex, yet efforts to actively manipulate and enhance complexity of hard-engineered infrastructure has generally been restricted to small-scale interventions. To maximise the beneficial ecological effects, the design and planning of future coastal defences should consider incorporating complexity across scales ranging from millimeters to 100s of meters, thereby widening the variety of ecological niches available. In this project, we propose a ‘meandering shoreline’ concept that integrates artificial bays, headlands, coral reefs, and estuaries. Our aim is to investigate the potential benefits of scaling up complexity in the context of hybrid shorelines, spanning intertidal zones to subtidal coral reefs. Here we present results of surveys for biodiversity present in artificial headlands and bays versus linear seawalls and take a landscape design approach to enhancing coastal complexity through artificial estuaries. We also explore options for combining 3D construction printing with traditional casting techniques for fabricating topographically complex artificial reef units. Finally, to evaluate the ecological resilience of tropical hybrid shoreline system, we assess ecosystem functioning, including productivity, carbon and nutrient cycling. Overall, findings from this project will provide strategies for increasing the ecological benefits of human-constructed coastal protection systems.
f9d67253: In the wake of the 2023-2024 mass bleaching event, which devastated Caribbean coral reefs, it is clear that demographic rescue alone is no longer a sufficient restoration strategy in this region. There is a critical need for regional coordination to research, develop, and implement advanced intervention strategies that incorporate climate resilience into reef restoration at scale. We conducted a three-day workshop at the Reef Futures Symposium in 2024 that brought together researchers, restoration practitioners, and managers from throughout the Caribbean to identify promising restoration interventions and prioritize research and development needs to advance their implementation. Interventions were grouped into six categories based on the 2019 U.S. National Academies of Science Report : 1) abiotic interventions, 2) algal symbiont manipulations, 3) biotic interventions, 4) host genetic & reproductive interventions, 5) microbiome manipulations, and 6) physiological interventions. Participants were asked to consider importance, efficacy, and risk in addition to context-dependence, accessibility, scalability, and permitting to identify hurdles to implementation. On the final day of the workshop, participants assigned intervention strategies to one of four quadrants based on axes of risk and efficacy using an anonymized survey. A consent-based approach was then used to develop final ranks to prioritize these strategies. Nuances on rationales for the finalized ranking were gleaned from pre-workshop survey data, interviews, and transcripts of workshop discussions. Specifically, we examined differences in the assignment of risk/efficacy by participants' professional and geographical affiliation to distinguish potential regional and sector-level disagreement over intervention readiness. On average, participants placed the largest number of intervention strategies in the low risk, high efficacy quadrant, indicating broad agreement that many of the strategies discussed are ready to be deployed now. Here, we will outline main findings and consensus on recommendations for incorporating these interventions into a climate-resilient strategy for reef restoration in the Caribbean.
032b0ff7:
Understanding how reef-building corals respond to environmental stress is critical for predicting the long-term resilience of populations under climate change. Combining physiological assays with population genetic analyses allows us to identify reefs with enhanced thermal tolerance, as well as highly connected populations that may serve as conduits for thermally tolerant recruits.

Across a strong thermal gradient in Majuro Atoll, we sampled colonies of Porites rus from both warm and cool lagoonal sites. Using high-coverage genome-wide SNP data, we investigated fine-scale population structure and the genetic basis of thermal resilience in these populations. Early results reveal clear spatial genetic differentiation across short distances (<40 km), suggesting restricted gene flow and potential local adaptation. A genome-wide association study (GWAS) between thermally contrasting sites also identified candidate genes associated with increased thermal tolerance.

In Maui Nui, traditional ecological knowledge identifies Olowalu in West Maui as a Mother Reef that seeds reefs on nearby reefs and islands such as South Molokai. We assessed the thermal performance of P. compressa using photochemical efficiency (Fv/Fm) and Visual Bleaching Scores (VBS), allowing us to identify thermally tolerant populations across both Maui and Moloka‘i. Physiological results suggest striking differences in thermal sensitivity between sites, despite minimal differences in thermal environments. Genetic data from these populations are still forthcoming and will help identify reefs with high connectivity and pinpoint genes associated with increased thermal tolerance.

Together, these studies emphasize the importance of understanding coral connections, adaptations and resilience across a set of linked, local reefs. By integrating genomic and physiological data, and incorporating place-based knowledge, this work provides insights that can inform conservation and restoration strategies under climate change.
df302f54: Hinatuan Island, Talavera, Taganaan, Surigao del Norte, an area bordered by ecologically important coastal habitats, including mangroves, seagrass beds, and coral reefs, is operated by a mining company. To evaluate the potential impacts of mining activities on these ecosystems, a comprehensive coastal resource assessment was conducted across four stations: Panubigon, Samputan, New Camp, and the newly surveyed Parcel 2. The study focused on benthic lifeform composition, coral cover status, reef health, and temporal trends from 2018 to 2025. Using a digital fixed photo-transect method, the percent cover of hard corals, soft corals, macroalgae, dead corals, and abiotic substrate was quantified, and coral health was assessed using established national reef condition indices. Results showed that live hard coral cover remained low across all stations (7.19–13.26%), indicating poor to fair reef health and suggesting widespread degradation. New Camp exhibited the highest hard (13.26%) and soft coral cover (11.60%), while Samputan recorded the lowest hard coral cover and the highest abiotic substrate (46.21%). High dead coral cover (26.81–56.75%) across stations points to chronic stress likely linked to sedimentation, past bleaching events, storm impacts, or anthropogenic disturbances. Macroalgae was prominent, particularly in Parcel 2 (19.11%), indicating potential phase shifts toward algal dominance. Temporal analysis revealed a substantial decline in coral cover since 2018, with formerly healthier sites such as New Camp shifting from good–excellent condition (33.20–47.68%) to poor–fair status. Samputan and Panubigon likewise showed persistent degradation, limited coral recruitment, and increasing abiotic cover, signaling weakened resilience. Overall, findings demonstrate declining coral reef health across Hinatuan Island`s coastal area, emphasizing the need for immediate management interventions. Recommendations include mitigating sediment and nutrient inputs, regulating fishing activities, protecting herbivorous fish populations, and implementing reef restoration to support recovery and prevent further ecological decline.
0bf899cc:
The accelerating loss of Caribbean coral reefs necessitates approaches that integrate ecological restoration with cultural and community engagement. In the U.S. Virgin Islands, we constructed the territory’s first artificial reef, intentionally designed using motifs from Taino petroglyphs found on St. John and outlying cays of St. Thomas. These symbols are widely recognized locally and appear in numerous artisan projects. The design provided a culturally resonant framework to foster community awareness, stewardship, and connection to Indigenous heritage.

Beyond its aesthetic and recreational value, the project incorporates scientific monitoring and experimentation. A coral outplanting comparison study is being conducted on the artificial reef and the adjacent natural reef to evaluate survival and growth using 3D modeling techniques. In conjunction, fish surveys, employing a stationary point count method, measure abundance and size distribution, are being conducted quarterly to evaluate how the artificial reef is affecting the local fish community. Preliminary results indicate comparable coral survival between reef types, with early observations of fish aggregation on the artificial structure primarily comprising of juvenile recruits rather than adults migrating from the natural reef. Despite its small footprint, data indicate the structure is reducing wave energy and modifying flow dynamics, aiding coastal protection.

This project demonstrates how culturally informed design can bridge art and science in practice. By embedding Taino symbolism into reef construction, restoration is elevated from a purely scientific intervention to a vehicle for cultural continuity and community engagement. This initiative illustrates the potential of interdisciplinary approaches that integrate science, art, and Indigenous knowledge into reef restoration.
2eaec458:
Aquarium hobbyists and aquarists maintain thousands of coral colonies in home and public aquaria, yet these systems are rarely used as structured observatories for coral reef science. At the same time, effective reef management increasingly calls for approaches that meaningfully integrate community members into monitoring and decision-making. We developed Project ReefLink, a citizen science initiative that links aquarists, public aquaria, and researchers to co-generate microbiome data and contextual metadata from aquarium corals.
We designed an accessible sampling kit and protocol that enabled participants to collect small fragments from stony corals and paired water samples, record detailed metadata on husbandry practices and tank conditions, and return preserved samples by mail for downstream culturing and DNA sequencing. In 2025, we used this workflow to collect 100 coral fragments spanning multiple scleractinian species and a range of aquarium environments.
Here we present the programme design, early participation patterns, and pilot microbiome results from these diverse systems. We show that community-generated samples can yield high-quality metagenomic datasets that capture both known coral-associated taxa and putative functional groups relevant to nutrient cycling, disease resistance, and stress tolerance. Standardized metadata from each participant allows us to link microbial variation to environmental parameters such as temperature stability, nutrient inputs, and coral health status.
By positioning aquarists as collaborators rather than sample providers, Project ReefLink demonstrates how community-led monitoring of coral-associated microbiomes can expand geographic coverage, build microbial baselines, and strengthen community voices in shaping questions and applications in coral reef management. We aim to continue this study, expand this community-driven coral dataset, and contribute open-source workflows and openly accessible microbiome and metadata resources to support broader use by the research community.
dc5f9c6d: The coral reefs in the Philippines are among the most diverse reef systems globally and provide essential ecosystem services to local communities. In these reef-dependent regions, the establishment of locally managed marine protected areas (MPAs) is a common approach for conserving and managing these coral reefs. MPAs can generate considerable ecological and socioeconomic benefits when well-designed, but they can also negatively impact vulnerable coastal communities when design and implementation are inadequate. Given the Philippines' commitment to global efforts such as the KMGBF to expand the conservation and protection of its marine resources, greater effort is needed to optimise the design and planning processes for successful locally managed MPAs. Here, we consider the design of a network of equitable, locally managed MPAs in Tañon Strait (Philippines)  that generate ecological and economic benefits to local communities. We (i) synthesised design guidelines for a variety of MPA objectives, (ii) estimated the costs of MPAs for different social contexts, and (iii) assessed the perception of social equity distribution among the stakeholders of Tañon Strait. Together, these components and lessons learned will be integrated into a spatial prioritisation model to design a network of MPAs within Tañon Strait. The symposium will be a great opportunity to share preliminary results and receive feedback from researchers, practitioners, and managers. By focusing on coral reef systems in the Philippines, this project contributes critical knowledge to support the development of successful, socially just, and ecologically resilient MPAs in the Philippines and beyond.
5e204314:
This submitter has specifically asked this abstract to be considered as a poster ONLY.

Corals are facing environmental stress and recovery, leading to serious coral disease problems. Coral ciliate disease is a serious coral disease that is highly contagious and has a high mortality rate. Infected corals may bleach, rot, or die. This experiment infected two different corals to assess the presence of specific parasitism. For the food, 5 and 10 polyps were used; and three microalgae: Tetraselmis chui, Isochrysis galbana tml and Nannochloropsis oculata, at concentrations of 1.5×106 cell mL-1 and 3.0×106 cell mL-1, respectively. A 5-day infection test was conducted to assess the impact on ciliate growth and development, as well as on survival. The results showed that the ciliates identified in this experiment were Scuticociliatia sp. The group fed with B. violacea (10 polyps) had the best proliferation rate, which was significantly different from the proliferation rates of other control groups (p < 0.05). All ciliates died within 48 h of being fed microalgae, indicating that microalgae cannot serve as food for ciliates, and their diet or infection may be specific.
db3c924e: The coral communities on underwater pinnacle play an ecological role similar to coral reefs, operating as offshore habitat hubs that sustain high biodiversity. These structurally complex ecosystems provide critical habitat, food resources, and nursery functions for corals, fishes, and benthic invertebrates, supporting recruitment and trophic stability. They also offer potential for SCUBA tourism, boosting local economies and promoting sustainable tourism practices. This study focuses on Hin Khao Dang, a newly discovered dive site in Chumphon province, the Gulf of Thailand, to characterize its coral community structure and evaluate its potential for low impact ecotourism development. The results showed that the dominant coral species found on the underwater pinnacle were Porites lutea, Galaxea fascicularis, Turbinaria mesenterina, and Goniopora columna. The most dominant macrobenthic invertebrates were Heteractis magnifica, Diadema setosum, and Bequina semiorbiculata. The most abundant reef fish were Chromis cinerascens, Pterocaesio pisang, and Thalassoma lunare. The present study underscores the ecological importance of underwater pinnacle, which are under increasing pressure from climate change and chronic environmental stressors. The findings further reinforce that this newly identified offshore underwater pinnacle functions as a climate resilient biodiversity hotspot, sustaining structured coral assemblages and diverse benthic and reef associated faunal communities. Effective, low impact spatial governance and adaptive zoning are therefore essential for maintaining long-term ecosystem integrity and securing marine-dependent livelihoods. These emerging underwater systems provide strategic foundations for expanding sustainable ecotourism and strengthening local stewardship, directly supporting Thailand’s nature positive and biodiversity aligned tourism development pathways.
146382aa: Corals are foundational ecosystem engineers that sustain the biodiversity and functioning of tropical coral reefs worldwide. Yet, with nearly half of reef-building species currently threatened with extinction, concerns are growing over the potential consequences of species loss for the maintenance of coral functional strategies. Here, we combined occurrence data for ~800 reef-forming coral species with trait information and the most recent IUCN extinction risk assessments to: (i) map extinction risk within the global coral functional space, (ii) test whether extinction risk is randomly distributed across trait axes, and (iii) simulate extinction scenarios to predict changes in trait diversity at global and regional scales. Preliminary results indicate that extinction risk is not evenly distributed across trait space. Instead, specific functional strategies, particularly those associated with large, branching species characterized by low skeletal density, high growth rates, large surface-area-to-volume ratios, large interstitial space, and narrow corallites, are globally more threatened. In addition, extinction simulations suggest that the high functional redundancy (species with overlapping trait combinations) within coral fauna may buffer against widespread loss of functional strategies globally. However, this buffering effect is weaker at regional scales, where functional erosion is projected to occur, varying markedly among regions. Overall, our findings suggest that key coral functional strategies underpinning critical ecological processes are already at risk, underscoring the need for conservation efforts explicitly considering trait diversity.
36757c3f: Increasing ocean temperatures are the greatest threat to coral reef persistence worldwide. Thermal anomalies of 1–2 °C above the typical summer maximum can induce severe physiological stress in corals, often leading to mass bleaching and mortality. While satellite-derived sea-surface temperature products are invaluable for monitoring broad-scale thermal conditions, high-accuracy subsurface measurements more precisely capture the temperatures experienced by reefs at depth. Here, we quantify seasonal dynamics, multi-year trends, and extreme thermal anomalies from in situ observations at 110 sites spanning 1–35 m across the western Atlantic. High-accuracy, low-drift subsurface temperature recorders were initially deployed between 2013 and 2015 across mesophotic reefs in the Flower Garden Banks, subtropical reefs in South Florida and the Florida Keys, and Caribbean reefs in Puerto Rico and the U.S. Virgin Islands. Instruments were replaced every 2–3 years, with the most recent retrievals completed in fall 2025. Seasonal temperature variability was greatest in South Florida and the Flower Garden Banks, with differences between maximum and minimum monthly mean temperatures of 5–9 °C, compared with 2–3 °C at the Caribbean sites. Approximately half of all monitored locations exhibit positive, statistically significant warming trends, with annual rates of 0.01–0.20 °C per year, observed at both shallow and deep sites. Across most sites, the strongest cold anomalies occurred in winter 2018 (January–March), and the most pronounced warm anomalies occurred in summer 2023 (July–October), regardless of depth. This long-term, regionally integrated dataset highlights the distinct seasonal regimes in western Atlantic reefs and underscores the pervasive influence of ocean warming and heatwaves across spatial and depth gradients in the region.
9c3e87b9: Corals in the eastern tropical Pacific (ETP) inhabit harsh conditions outside the typical range of most tropical reefs, experiencing high temperature variability, high nutrient levels, and relatively acidified waters. In the Galápagos, upwelling and recurrent ENSO events further expose corals to extreme cold and warm anomalies that can lead to bleaching. To examine how corals persist under such extremes, we conducted an archipelago-wide survey of Galápagos coral tolerance to heat and cold stress using a portable bleaching stress system. Thermal tolerance assays on Pocillopora and Pavona across seven islands revealed clear spatial patterns: corals from the coolest region (Isabela) were the most heat-sensitive, while those from the warmer northern region (Darwin and Wolf) exhibited the broadest thermal tolerances. We then tracked the fate of the relatively vulnerable Isabela corals at a new restoration site over a two-year period. We found higher coral genetic diversity, variation in growth rates, and more diverse coral-symbiont associations in pocilloporids than previously reported. When combined with the archipelago-wide results, these findings suggest that Isabela pocilloporids have higher adaptive potential and suitability for restoration than expected. Our findings highlight the Galápagos as a natural laboratory for understanding how corals persist in extreme and fluctuating environments and reveal physiological traits that may shape coral resilience under future ocean conditions.
1b5ca7d1: Large-scale coral restoration projects in Florida have been active since the early 2000’s but the recovery of coral populations has been slow due in part to high predation and mortality of juvenile coral outplants. Therefore, the development of outplanting strategies to increase initial survival of newly outplanted coral species is critical to improving outplant survival and increasing outplanting success. Land-reared sexual recruits created from broodstock rescued from stony coral tissue loss disease were acclimated within an offshore coral nursery prior to outplanting and biodegradable predator exclusion devices (PEDs) were deployed around outplants to evaluate potential reductions in predation and increases outplant survival. Corals acclimated prior to outplanting suffered less tissue loss and resulting mortality from fish predation while the addition of PEDs further increased survival. However, findings from this study indicate that outplant size plays the largest role in survival during the first year on the reef with larger outplants successfully recovering from predation impacts better than smaller outplants. Based on a cost-benefit analysis, additional time and resources spent growing corals to a larger size prior to outplanting provides corals that are better adapted for outplanting while including additional efforts to acclimate land-reared corals to in-situ conditions further increase outplant survival and restoration success.
6e9fa701: Sea surface temperatures (SSTs) are increasing globally, with thermal anomalies occurring more frequently and persisting for longer periods of time. Coral reefs, including those at higher latitudes previously considered climate refuges, which are now experiencing record high temperatures. Lord Howe Island, the world’s southernmost coral reef, has experienced four bleaching events 1998, 2010, 2019, 2024, with the 2024 event being one of the most intense and prolonged marine heatwaves recorded for this reef system. Temperatures reached 27°C, 3°C above normal summertime peaks and resulted in a second bleaching event within five years. At the same time, the reef experienced a negative 20cm low tide anomaly that persisted for one week. This study examined the effects of these two stress events over three timepoints in the summer-autumn of 2024 (January; pre-bleaching. March; peak bleaching. May; post bleaching). Coral cover was monitored throughout the study and bleaching was recorded across all sites, with bleaching prevalence varying through time and location. The highest bleaching cover of 53% was recorded at Sylphs Hole during peak bleaching in March. Neds Beach recorded the greatest proportion of coral mortality from bleaching at 23%, while Comets Hole and North Bay exhibited the highest mortality associated with the low tide anomaly (34% and 28%). Pocillopora spp. and Porites spp. were the most susceptible taxa to bleaching.  In contrast, Isopora cuneata and Acropora spp., which are typically susceptible to bleaching in tropical regions, only exhibited paling. Understanding how the Lord Howe Island reef responds to repeated bleaching events within short time frames is important, as continued increases in SSTs are likely to result in more frequent and prolonged bleaching events. This study thus provides insight into the spatial and temporal variability of bleaching and mortality impacts within this isolated high-latitude reef system.
b3d6f8dd:
PLEASE REVIEW FOR A POSTER ONLY. PLEASE CONSULT SIMON DAVY ON THIS PROJECT.
The International Junior SeaKeepers Program (IJSK) is an educational program designed to increase marine science literacy in high-school aged children. Based on the strong foundations laid by the Junior SeaKeepers Program (JSK) based out of SeaKeepers Headquarters, this novel international approach utilizes online tools, a shared curriculum, and hyper-localized instruction to run simultaneous, linked programming with individualized impact. In 2025, the first IJSK program was initiated in New Zealand, United Kingdom, and Singapore. In Aotearoa New Zealand, the first cohort of 21 students embarked on an in-field hands-on learning opportunity, visited Goat Island Marine Reserve Te Hawerea-a-Maki’s Discovery Center to conduct water quality analysis, and analyzed images from an international Citizen Science program, Citizens of the Reef. The second IJSK cohort in Aotearoa New Zealand consists of 25 students and will build on the previous cohort’s experiences by leading their own independent research project on a marine ecosystem of their choosing and creating a scientific poster to share in an annual SeaKeepers research summit. Across all Junior SeaKeepers programs (JSK + IJSK), more than 101 students were impacted in 2025, and an anticipated 99 students in 2026. The Aotearoa New Zealand cohorts made up 21% and 25% of these students respectively. Thanks to the international nature of these combined programs, students in 2025 had access to expert presentations from 3 countries, with 9 presentations from SeaKeepers staff, and 15 presentations from external experts in marine science (6 of which were specifically offered to students from Aotearoa New Zealand). As a preparatory tool for students interested in marine science, this program equips the next generation of academics, reef managers, restoration practitioners, research scientists, and ICRS presenters.
867c314f:
Kapoposang island is one the islands in the Spermonde archipelago. Its beauty truly attracts the attention of both locals and international visitors, especially its coral reefs and surrounding marine life. There are approximately 120 islands decorating Spemonde.

It is located on the western coast of South Sulawesi Province in Indonesia.  This island with over 400 inhabitants, almost all of its inhabitants are fisherman. This island is relatively not too hot and has lush vegetation. Its coastal area is special because it has stretches of exotic coral reefs. 

The silhouette of the sea is very captivating, all that beauty becomes an opportunity for research and coral reef conservation efforts. 

On this island, there is a community movement concerned with coral reef conservation, known as the web spider conservation activist community. It was established in 2015 and has carried out numerous conservation efforts using coral reef duplication methods. By taking a sample from the core, the duplication process then begins with a web-like spider network. 

Through this effort, we hope to contribute to the preservation of the marine environment in the Spermonde archipelago area, and more, specifically on Kapoposang island.
ab734538:
A multi-site carbonate-budget assessment has been conducted around the reef platform of a small Southeast Asian island, which has suffered severely from historical bomb fishing. A combination of high-resolution benthic surveys, in situ calcification and bioerosion measurements was used to calculate a standard reefbudget model. A heterogeneous benthic ecosystem with a wide range of carbonate production levels was revealed, including large areas of unproductive rubble, mixed assemblages of macroalgae, sponges and octocorals, as well as a few recovering scleractinian-dominated sites. 

The results show that benthic reassembly following destructive fishing can result in pronounced spatial divergence with disparate carbonate budgets. The reef-crest zone was predominantly net erosional due to large expanses without any framework-building coral cover, very low levels of crustose coralline algae, and elevated bioerosion from endolithic borers and Bumphead parrotfish. The consequences of the platform scale reduction of carbonate production are substantial. The loss of an accreting reef-crest increases the exposure to wave energy, reduces the natural coastal protection and alters the provision of ecosystem services. This study provides an empirical foundation to identify priority zones for restoration in order to maximise the recovery of ecosystem functions and long-term platform stability.
c44a6b0b: Coral propagation ex situ is emerging as a viable intervention to restore coral cover. Outplanting sexually propagated corals on the reef is particularly important as it contributes to greater genetic diversity and potentially overall reef resilience. However, these corals typically experience high levels of predation following outplanting, significantly reducing survival, and thus constitute a major bottleneck to the success of restoration efforts. This study assessed if simulated predation events on sexually propagated coral juveniles prior to deployment offshore can prime them to generate a physical and/or chemical anti-predatory response mechanism. Sixteen-eighteen month old (4.8-47.6mm diameter) corals from six species grown ex situ were divided into two groups: a group where individuals were poked with a needle 2 and 4 days prior to outplanting, and a control group where individuals were not exposed to simulated predation. Following that, both groups were outplanted to an in situ nursery, with half of each group being either cemented to modules(Concrete blocks 1m off seafloor) or fastened to a coral nursery tree. Outplants were monitored after 7, 18, 34, and 81 days. The effectiveness of simulated predation and in situ grow-out structure on the post-outplant survival and growth of the coral juveniles was assessed. Corals cemented on modules had significantly higher survival rates than those attached to trees, with modules showing 59% survival compared to 53% for trees after 81 days. Coral growth varied significantly over time, but no differences in growth were found between poked vs. unpoked corals or between different nursery structures. Initial coral size influenced predation outcomes, with smaller corals being less likely to be predated but having lower survival if predated.
88c74a00: The Central Mexican Pacific harbours coral communities that have so far been resistant to the effects of thermal anomalies associated with ENSO events, but the region has also become a tourist hotspot over the last 40 years, increasing the anthropogenic pressure and potential vulnerability on the coral communities. Marine sediments can accumulate plastic particles over several years, serving as a marker of the extent of contamination in coral areas and elucidating its potential ecological consequences. The presence of potential microplastic particles (pMPs) in the marine sediment from three sites: Playa del Amor and Zona de Restauracion, located in an NPA with high and low touristic pressure, respectively, but with a coral restoration program implemented, and Las Virgencitas with intermediate touristic activities.  The results show evidence of pMPs at all sites, with the highest abundance at Playa del Amor (0.54 pMPs/g) and the lowest at Las Virgencitas (0.06 pMPs/g). A total of five types of pMPs were identified (fibers, fragments, films, foams, and fiber assemblages), with fibers being the most predominant form at all three sites (>90%). The presence of pMPs at both highly visited sites and those categorized as having low human impact suggests that tourism is one of the most important vectors for the introduction of pMPs; however, indirect carriers, such as rainwater runoff, river flow, and wind transport, should be considered. The constant input of pMPs may hinder coral restoration efforts by affecting the survival and the long-term maintenance of the restoration site, extending its negative effects beyond the local scale to the regional level. Therefore, these approaches play a critical role in safeguarding marine biodiversity and maintaining the resilience and sustainability of the ecosystem services provided by coral reefs, which are vital to the well-being of coastal communities and the regional economy.
e6b532d5: Decentralized science (DeSci) integrates Web3 technologies, biotechnology, and community governance to transform coral reef conservation. This study presents a framework with three components: a decentralized AI agent managing research collaboration, data analytics, and governance; a standardized biotechnology toolkit for in situ coral restoration; and modular, portable wet labs at reef sites for propagation and genomic studies. Additionally, a monitoring component is integrated to assess ecosystem-level effects beyond restoration success metrics. This enables transparent, community-driven innovation and adaptive funding, powered by AI and blockchain, to accelerate reef regeneration. The AI agent optimizes resource use and inclusive decisions, while the biotech tools empower practitioners to enhance coral resilience. Portable labs facilitate real-time experiments and adaptive management. Together, these form a scalable, transparent model promoting ecosystem recovery amid climate and human pressures. The framework democratizes conservation through decentralized governance and technological innovation, supporting rapid improvements in coral health and ecosystem resilience. Its modular design fits diverse reef contexts globally, tackling marine biodiversity threats with collaborative, tech-enabled strategies. This approach redefines reef restoration by merging Web3, AI, and biotech into a community-powered platform. Aligned with decentralized defensive acceleration (d/acc), a philosophy prioritizing secure, inclusive, responsible innovation to boost ecosystem resilience and community governance, this framework exemplifies sustainable solutions for urgent environmental challenges. d/acc promotes accelerating innovation with defense, decentralization, and democratic governance. It empowers diverse stakeholders in transparent, adaptive collaboration for resilient environmental restoration. This study illustrates how d/acc principles enable secure, community-led coral reef regeneration driven by decentralized science and advanced technologies.
156b0ead:
Abstract
Herbivorous sea urchins provide essential ecosystem services on coral reefs by grazing macroalgae, maintaining open substrate for coral recruitment, and reinforcing benthic biodiversity. However, population declines driven by habitat degradation, disease, and harvesting have weakened this natural control mechanism, increasing the need for labor-intensive manual algal removal in reef management. To address this growing challenge, we developed a cross-sector innovation that integrates circular bio-resource utilization with urchin aquaculture to support scalable, biodiversity-based reef interventions.
This study repurposes high-salinity brine waste from pickled-vegetable processing as a culture medium for Dunaliella salina, producing a nutrient-rich algal protein that functions as a sustainable fishmeal alternative for rearing the herbivorous urchin Tripneustes gratilla. The resulting feed significantly improved juvenile growth, survivorship, and physiological condition—traits that are critical for restoration stocking and post-release performance.
By linking food-industry waste valorization with coral reef restoration needs, this work demonstrates a replicable pathway for producing large numbers of herbivorous urchins in a low-carbon, cost-efficient manner. Our findings highlight how cross-sectoral partnerships can expand technological access, reduce dependence on manual algal clearing, and strengthen coral–urchin facilitation processes that underpin reef resilience under climate change.
Keywords
Herbivorous sea urchin; coral–urchin facilitation; circular bio-resources; macroalgal control; Tripneustes gratilla; reef r resilience
920de076: Coral reefs have undergone dramatic shifts in community composition in recent decades due to increasing pressure from local and global stressors. In the face of global declines in coral cover, weedy coral species have seen an increase in relative abundance on certain reefs, likely facilitated by their rapid growth rates and predominantly asexual reproduction. Yet, despite the growing importance of these weedy species, molecular studies investigating their genetic diversity remain scarce, leaving questions about the resilience of these increasingly relevant coral species at a reefscape scale. One of the main challenges in studying these species can be their occurrence as dense aggregations or “monospecific stands”, where indistinct colony boundaries complicate systematic assessments. Here, we present a scalable, a priori sampling workflow that combines structure-from-motion photogrammetry with a top-down grid projection to enable unbiased and systematic sampling of monospecific stand-forming corals. Using this approach, we generated a reefscape-genomic dataset for the Caribbean coral Madracis auretenra, which has increased in relative abundance on reefs in Curaçao and Bonaire. Across 678 samples, collected at one-meter intervals, we identified two genetically distinct clades with different depth ranges and frequent co-occurrence in the same monospecific stand. Despite the presence of numerous clonal groups across all sites, monospecific stands showed notable genotypic diversity, with clonal dispersal predominantly following a downslope pattern. This spatially-explicit approach not only enables inference about the reproductive processes underlying the observed genetic patterns, but can also provide guidance to maximise genotypic diversity when selecting ramets for restoration interventions. Overall, this approach offers an efficient and reproducible method for classifying spatial genetic diversity in systems where traditional colony-based sampling is unreliable, and provides valuable insights for conservation and restoration projects that focus on these resilient species.
e2e8be41:
Coral reef restoration requires actionable forecasting of heat, light, oxygen, and flow conditions at the scale of individual coral colonies beyond qualitative best practice learned through experience. To address this gap, we deployed 35 autonomous instruments across a reef patch on Shusha Island, located in the northern Red Sea off the coast of Saudi Arabia. These sensors log light, dissolved oxygen, temperature, pressure, and fluid velocity/angle, enabling coupled inference of local tidal regimes. Field measurements are merged with a modeling pipeline built around three pillars: temporal regime prediction, angle reconstruction, and computational fluid dynamics (CFD).
First, a Hidden Semi-Markov model (HSMM) infers the discrete tidal phase. Second, we model each instrument unsteady flow angle as a stochastic process conditioned on the tidal phase prediction. These angle dynamics are assimilated with in-situ measurements via a Kalman smoothing algorithm to fill gaps in the acquired data. The final piece of our pipeline uses a database of CFD solutions, each representing a different inflow angle in 1° increments. At each observation hour, the angle measurements are used to select the best matching solution from the CFD, producing a temporal evolution of the inflow angle, from which the evolution of the entire velocity field follows. 
Our approach is practical and efficient. Once the CFD simulations are completed, the data-driven parts of the model can be updated quickly continuously, allowing us to track changes in reef flow conditions at very low ongoing cost. Future work will add boundary models for temperature and oxygen, helping us predict heat and oxygen exchange at the coral surface. Even in its current form, the developed combined modeling and sensing approach offers reef practitioners a new, economical tool for observing and forecasting environmental conditions at the coral colony scale, supporting smarter decisions for coral survival risk and restoration planning.
e80e7034:
Typically on the GBR lagoon in summer, upper ocean turbulence distributes incoming solar energy over a buoyant surface mixed layer 30 or more metres deep. However, as reported by Long et al. (2025), recent mass coral bleaching events on the GBR have been associated with acute periods of intense stratification, brought about by extended periods of low winds reducing upper ocean turbulence. This manifests as reduction in vertical mixing, lower benthic water movement, less sediment re-suspension (higher surface temps, lower flows and more light reaching the corals). Using data from a range of instruments & models (IMOS sea gliders & GBR ROMS model) we closely followed the hydrodynamics of stratification and destratification of the water column in the GBR lagoon associated with “bleaching weather” during summer 2025-26 (bleaching weather being characterised by a shoaling of the surface mixed layer driven by low winds). Using the model in forecast mode we were able to successfully predict the formation of shallow (10 – 12 m depth), hot surface layers, and relatively cooler deeper water below, approximately one week prior to their occurrence. Periodic natural destratification of the water column due to the return of winds was also predicted by the model and observed by the gliders.

We designed and built a mixer involving a bespoke pump and deployed it at Moore Reef in cooperation with our reef tourism partners in preparation for a forecast stratification event. The mixer’s effectiveness in localised destratification and cooling was evaluated across test and control sites using a range of in-water sensors including temperature, flow, CTD casts, and underwater PAM fluorometry of sentinel coral colonies.  

This bespoke pump is lightweight , modular and customisable for a range of supplementary mixing applications on reefs where failure of normal vertical mixing is an acute contributor to coral bleaching stress.
c55099be: The world’s coral reefs are increasingly transforming to become algal turf dominated ecosystems. Given the prevalence of turfs in these transformed ecosystems, and the fact they interact closely with a range of key ecosystem processes such as herbivory and coral recruitment, understanding the structure and composition of turfs on transformed reefs is likely to be critical in shaping how these reefs function. These issues are brought to the fore across Florida’s coral reef tract. Given the high latitude nature of this reef system, existing at the edge of environmental limits, and the fact it is dominated by high algal turf cover, it is likely to offer valuable insights into how future reefs may operate under continuing global change. Yet, our understanding of turf structure and subsequent interactions with key ecosystem processes remains in its infancy for this reef system. Here I will provide an overview of our recent research based on in-situ quantification and experimental manipulation of turf communities on Florida’s coral reef tract. Specifically, we assessed (1) the relationship between algal turf length and sediment mass, (2) spatial differences in algal turf bound sediments (3) how sediment loads affect grazing intensity, (4) spatial variability in herbivory across reef habitats, and (5) the identity and functional roles of the dominant turf grazing fishes. Together, this research is suggesting close associations between turf length and sediment mass, as well as marked cross-shelf gradients in turf structure and composition for Florida’s reef tract. Patterns of turf structure also appear to be closely associated with herbivory patterns, with initial evidence of increased sediment loads suppressing grazing intensity. The emerging results underscore the importance of considering the structure and composition of turfs on transformed coral reefs, and the potential for increased sediment loads to constrain key ecosystem processes such as herbivory.
49796819: Herbivory is a primary function on coral reefs as it controls macroalgae cover and facilitates coral recruitment. The Diadema urchin die-off in the 1980’s has left parrotfish (family Scaridae) as the primary herbivores on many Caribbean reefs, including those in Florida, USA. While larger parrotfish remove exponentially higher amounts of macroalgae, some species contribute to higher rates of corallivory and reduce coral restoration success through direct consumption and extirpation of outplants. Previous studies have revealed that parrotfish densities differ in three main ecoregions in the Florida Reef Tract, with the Dry Tortugas and Florida Keys having substantially higher abundances than Southeast Florida. Despite their protected status, these populations have undergone severe declines due to hurricanes and habitat degradation, leaving the Florida Reef Tract dominated by small individuals. As different parrotfish species and size classes contribute uniquely to overall herbivory and corallivory, understanding spatial dynamics of their populations is essential for reef restoration. We identify functional size classes by analyzing species-specific habitat shifts through ontogeny in a ten-year dataset (2014-24). Biennial emerging hotspot analyses by region revealed spatiotemporal patterns in parrotfish populations. Preliminary results indicate widespread shifts and local consistency of parrotfish hotspots in all three ecoregions. Populations in all ecoregions were dominated by small individuals with persistent hot spots of large individuals in protected areas. Juveniles in all bioregions were often associated with mangroves, which was increasingly apparent in the highly developed Southeast Florida ecoregion. Multiple species showed shifts in hot spot location over our study period. These spatiotemporal patterns were then compared against known outplant predation data to determine if biennial patterns are associated with higher-frequency practitioner data. These analyses advance the understanding of parrotfish corallivory to inform restoration strategies by identifying sites where precautions like predator-exclusion devices may be necessary to ensure outplanting success.
ae556148:
In Maunalua Bay, O’ahu, Hawai’i, a unique initiative led by Malama Maunalua (MM) is demonstrating how a small non-profit can leverage and integrate government, tourists, community, corporations, NGOs, and researchers into protecting and restoring the region’s coral reefs. The Maunalua region is heavily urbanized and houses over 50,000 residents. Over 90% of the coastal strip has been paved with impervious surfaces, causing significant stormwater runoff and pollution to enter the Bay. This means that to adequately restore these reefs, MM must address a wide range of threats throughout the watersheds that feed into Maunalua Bay. With its partners, MM is undertaking a holistic ridge-to-reef approach that draws inspiration from the Native Hawaiian ahupua’a management concept. This talk will address the various components of the effort, the lessons learned, and its replicability.
MM is working with community partners to fragment and outplant thermally resilient corals. Thus far, over 4,000 fragments have been planted with monitoring being undertaken by expert staff and regenerative tourism. Complementing these efforts, MM has established a Fisheries Management Area in the region to protect herbivores and incorporate fishers in restoration. Further, MM’s Huki project has removed over 4 million pounds of invasive alien algae and welcomed over 46,000 volunteers since 2006. Terrestrially, MM is leading efforts to improve coastal water quality by managing and reducing stormwater runoff and pollution. MM is working with a range of partners to capture runoff by installing green infrastructure and to restore the ecological functions of the forest and stream ecosystems that feed into Maunalua Bay. The success of this program can largely be attributed to the collaborative efforts of MM’s partners, mentored volunteers, and a robust internship program that helps undertake critical science. Through this talk, MM offers its insights with communities interested in replicating and scaling similar approaches.
1ad3cd5a:
Mesophotic reef ecosystems (MCEs), extending from 30 to 150 meters, remain among the most challenging marine habitats to study due to depth-related risks and logistical limitations. This study demonstrates an integrated methodology combining the DeepFish ROV platform with the Fish-i semi-automated reef fish assessment system to characterize mesophotic fish communities across three Philippine regions: Pangasinan, Batangas, and Apo Reef, Mindoro.
A stereo-camera system mounted on a compact Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) allowed safe and repeatable surveys at depths inaccessible to traditional SCUBA-based methods. This presentation will highlight the integrated approach used to deploy the ROV and collect video data for Fish-i analysis. It will outline how ROV-based imaging and Fish-i’s semi-automated pipeline work together to enable consistent data capture, processing, and interpretation across mesophotic reef environments.
Across the three survey sites, the combined ROV–Fish-i system documented diverse and representative mesophotic fish communities. The results highlight unique assemblage patterns and depth-related variations observed across the regions, demonstrating the capability of the integrated approach to capture broad ecological signatures and community structures at multiple locations.
The combined ROV–Fish-i approach substantially reduces depth-related risk, increases survey efficiency, and standardizes community assessments across multiple sites. This work highlights the potential for scalable, technology-enabled mesophotic monitoring that bridges observational gaps between shallow and deep reef systems. As climate change increasingly threatens deep-reef refugia, such innovations are essential for informing conservation strategies, supporting marine spatial planning, and strengthening the evidence base for long-term reef resilience.
64836b2f:
Across Micronesia, coastal communities are responding to accelerating climate and human pressures with approaches that draw equally from local leadership, traditional knowledge, and applied science. The Kiwa Initiative’s MiCOAST Project—implemented across Palau, the Federated States of Micronesia, the Republic of the Marshall Islands, and Nauru—aims to strengthen Community-Based Fisheries Management (CBFM) as a scalable Nature-based Solution for food security, biodiversity protection, and climate resilience. Working through locally grounded partners, MiCOAST supports community planning, fishery monitoring, compliance and enforcement, GEDSI integration, and livelihood diversification, while elevating Indigenous knowledge and local governance systems.

This work directly contributes to the evolving vision of the Micronesia Challenge (MC), particularly its 2030 goals, which now extend beyond protected areas to encompass sustainable use of nearshore fisheries. MiCOAST partners contribute to this movement by supporting locally meaningful monitoring, elevating Indigenous governance systems, strengthening compliance and enforcement, and ensuring women, youth, and marginalized groups have a voice in decision-making. The stories emerging from this work highlight a shared regional narrative: that resilience grows when communities themselves define the rules, track what matters to them, and learn across island boundaries. The project also helps strengthen regional coherence by linking community actions to national policies and to the Pacific Framework for Action for Scaling Up CBFM.

Early observations suggest that MiCOAST provides an enabling model for catalyzing cross-jurisdictional learning, supporting adaptive decision-making, and reinforcing the MC’s long-standing “ridge-to-reef” ethos with community-led fisheries solutions. This presentation will weave together examples of community-led actions, early lessons from cross-jurisdictional collaboration, and reflections on how MiCOAST is helping reaffirm Micronesian stewardship within the broader MC vision; demonstrating that community-rooted solutions remain one of the region’s most powerful pathways for sustaining reefs, fisheries, and island futures.
5694d18d:
Environmental stressors and/or climate change strongly influence the physiological performance of marine organisms, including scleractinian corals. The azooxanthellate coral Tubastraea aurea (Dendrophylliidae) that dominantes in the natural high acidic hydrothermal vent (HV) ecosystem off Kueishan Island, northeast Taiwan providing an excellent study model for coral stress tolerance assessment. In this study, we examined oxidative stress and antioxidant responses in T. aurea exposed to multiple environmental stressors at HV site. We quantidied the activities of key antioxidant enzymes, including superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT) and glutathione peroxidase (GSH-Px), and characterized the tissue-specific expression and regulation of heat shock proteins (HSPs: taHSP25, taHSP60, taHSP70, and taHSP90). The results revealed that HV corals exhibited significantly (p < 0.05) elevated SOD and CAT activities compared with both non-vent (NHV, control) and HV corals maintained under control aquarium conditions, whereas GSH-Px activity did not differ among groups. All four taHSPs were expressed across multiple tissues, including whole tissue, tentacles, oral and aboral regions, theca, mesentery filaments, and gonads. Notably, taHSP60 and taHSP70 were upregulated markedly in HV corals at 4.53- and 10.69-folds higher than the NHV population, while taHSP25 and taHSP90 showed no significant difference. Under the natural acidic hydrothermal vent extreme environment, the T. aurea remains abundance off the vent site highlighting its exceptional physiological plasticity adapt to adverse environment. These findings provide valuable molecular insights of the coordinated roles of antioxidant defenses and HSP mediated stress responses in coral tolerance, enhance our understanding of coral resilience and adaptation under future climate change scenarios.

Key words: scleractinian coral, hydrothermal vent, acidified environment, oxidative stress, hypoxia, climate change
20dbb9cc:
The Red Sea coast of Saudi Arabia extends over 1,800 km, along most of which are fringing coral reefs. Offshore are further well-developed reef complexes, notably around the Strait of Tiran, through the Al Wajh Lagoon, and between Al Lith and Al Shuqaiq, an area including the Farasan Bank. These reefs were in pristine condition until the 1980s, when over 100 sites were proposed as Marine Protected Areas (MPAs), including National Parks on the Al Wajh and Farasan Banks. Although none of these proposals were implemented at the time, in the early 2000s the Saudi Wildlife Commission produced detailed proposals for MPAs in the southern Gulf of Aqaba, on the Tiran Islands and coast, and covering the Al Wajh Bank.
Since then there have along the Saudi coast been numerous coastal and island-based developments, including ports, marinas, global trade and energy hubs, and tourist resorts. These projects have incorporated efforts to protect marine habitats and ecology, but their promoters need to further consider how easily cumulative and synergistic processes can impact coral reefs.
For example, the Red Sea has since 1998 become increasingly subject to coral bleaching.  On central and southern Saudi reefs this is now a near-annual occurrence, while in the northern Red Sea mass mortality has only more recently been reported. Warming seas will make the region more sensitive to other anthropogenic impacts.
Here we report on the work of a new Saudi Arabian Foundation, CURA, launched with the intention of promoting positive, innovative approaches to the conservation of the Red Sea’s unique marine ecosystem. On the one hand it is promoting means by which the direct and indirect impacts of coastal development can be minimised. On the other it has initiated research to identify genes underlying the greater tolerance of some coral genotypes to elevated sea-surface temperatures.
d3b0624b: ABSTRACT: Mare Island’s coral reefs in Tidore Kepulauan City, Northern Maluku, support diverse reef fish communities that contribute to local food security and safeguard coastal ecosystem functions. To better understand their current condition, this study examined how reef fish trophic groups vary across depth zones within the island’s marine protected area. Surveys were carried out between 23 and 29 August 2025 using Underwater Visual Census (UVC) along belt transects. The community was primarily composed of planktivorous reef fishes, especially members of Pomacentridae and Caesionidae, suggesting that the plankton-based food web remains intact. Commercially valuable predators (Serranidae, Lutjanidae, Scaridae) occurred at only a few stations, suggesting habitat constraints or fishing pressure. Coral-dependent indicators (Chaetodontidae, Zanclidae) were consistently observed, reflecting live coral support for key trophic processes. Diversity peaked at mid-depths (9-10 m), while deeper areas showed reduced habitat complexity. These patterns reinforce the need for adaptive conservation through regulated fishing, protection of critical habitats, and long-term ecological monitoring. Continued research linking fish community dynamics with benthic conditions will help ensure the effectiveness of this small-island marine protected area.
067f4737: The concentration of Manganese (Mn), Chromium (Cr), Zinc (Zn) and total nitrogen contents (TN) in Porites corals from Dar es Salaam and on the Zanzibar coastal areas, located in the western and eastern parts of Zanzibar Channel respectively, within Tanzania continental shelf were determined to assess the spatial and temporal contamination.  After coring, the corals cores were thoroughly cleaned with water and then distilled water. The growth rate or growth bands of the sliced coral slabs, were visually determined with the use of UV-light, and were found to be 7 to 12 mm/year. Every fifth growth band was sub-sampled for analysis. The mean maximum concentration (n=3) were found to be 11.4±1.5 μg/g Mn, 43.0±3.0 μg/g Cr, 8.0±1.1 μg/g Zn and 0.29 % TN in the skeleton coral growth bands. The Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometer (AAS) graphite furnace and CHN Elemental analyzer were used for metal and nitrogen analysis respectively. The results showed the level of Mn, Cr, Zn and TN in western part of Zanzibar Channel were higher than those of eastern part of the channel (p<0.01). This suggest that the western Zanzibar Channel is highly affected by anthropogenic activities and favorable to cyanobacteria. Notably the sewer pipe from Dar es Salaam city Centre, terrestrial runoff from highly polluted Kizinga and Msimbazi Rivers, and anthropogenic activities at Dar es Salaam port and its vicinity are presumed to be the cause of contamination in these coral reef areas It is therefore, important for the conservation and monitoring processes to start to be in place to avoid pollution in the channel and its ecosystem in general.
04fcfe18: Coral reefs worldwide are increasingly experiencing thermal stress events resulting in widespread mortality that highlight the need for restoration approaches that improve long-term thermal resilience. Thermal preconditioning has emerged as a promising tool to boost coral physiological tolerance, but is understudied in slow-growing, massive species. This study aims to determine whether brief, repeated thermal exposures ex situ can enhance the post-outplant performance of juvenile Pseudodiploria clivosa, a massive, Western Atlantic reef-building species. Two year old sexual recruits (n=288) were reared ex situ, exposed to either ambient-control conditions (27 °C) or heat-treated preconditioning durations (4-, 12-, 24-hour exposures) at 32 °C, each followed by a 7-day recovery period. Corals were outplanted across two U.S. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Mission: Iconic Reef sites (Newfound Harbor and Looe Key, Florida) using a balanced, spatially randomized experimental design. Survivorship, bleaching severity, and growth were monitored quarterly over one year using standardized visual assessments, CoralWatch color scores, and planar area measurements. Metabolic performance (photosynthesis and respiration) was measured using a Community In Situ MEtabolism instrument on a subset of recruits, to evaluate physiological function under field conditions. Preliminary monitoring shows that corals at Looe Key had lower pigmentation and higher algal overgrowth and tissue recession than those at Newfound Harbor, regardless of treatment. Net photosynthesis did not differ across sites or treatments, suggesting strong site effects (i.e. temperature, light levels, and water quality) and limited early influence of preconditioning. This study is the first to pair laboratory preconditioning, outplanting, and cutting-edge noninvasive metabolic monitoring in situ. By directly testing a scalable preconditioning approach, this research contributes to the development of evidence-based strategies for enhancing coral survivorship in the face of climate change. If effective, this relatively low-cost method could be integrated into restoration techniques to increase outplant success across regions.
656db7d5:
Understanding patterns of coral disease is essential for predicting coral reef resilience and guiding management actions. This study presents the first assessment of coral disease patterns across four survey periods in the Al Wajh Lagoon, northern Red Sea. Disease signs were recorded in multiple taxa, with Acropora, Cyphastrea, Echinopora, Goniastrea, Platygyra, Pocillopora and Porites exhibiting lesions during at least three of the four monitoring periods. Several diseases were documented at outbreak levels during the September 2024 global bleaching event, including Acropora White Syndrome (WS), Black Band disease (BBD), and Porites WS with cyanobacterial bands.
The data reveal clear patterns of differential disease susceptibility among coral taxa, providing managers and practitioners with valuable information for targeted monitoring during routine conditions and thermal stress events. These insights also support species selection for coral reef restoration and offer guidance for in-situ and ex-situ nursery management by highlighting which endemic lesions are most likely to occur on cultured corals.
Disease prevalence was further compared between natural and artificial habitats and between developed and undeveloped coastal regions. No significant differences were detected across any survey period, suggesting that current levels of coastal development do not yet have a detectable influence on coral disease in the lagoon. In parallel, four invertebrate coral predators were recorded: Drupella spp., Coralliophila spp., Culcita spp., and Acanthaster spp.. Drupella was consistently the most common predator, underscoring the importance of continued monitoring of this species.
Overall, this study establishes a baseline understanding of coral disease prevalence, coral predator presence, and coral reef resilience and recovery patters.  These findings represent a critical first step toward predicting coral reef health trajectories and supporting adaptive management and marine regenerative planning in a rapidly changing Red Sea environment.
08709c62: Coral reef ecosystems harbor rich biodiversity, with community composition exhibiting dynamic temporal patterns between day and night. Many reef species display distinct diurnal and nocturnal behaviors, likely biasing the observable biodiversity. Traditional visual surveys often miss these temporal variations, potentially underestimating species’ richness and ecological complexity. Environmental DNA (eDNA) provides a non-invasive tool for monitoring biodiversity by capturing biomaterial left by organisms in their environment. While previous studies have documented diel patterns in marine eDNA, comprehensive multi-marker assessment of temporal turnover remains limited. This study investigates diurnal and nocturnal biodiversity on coral reefs in the Florida Keys using a multi-marker eDNA approach. Three 5-liter water samples were collected per site and time period from five reefs using a Smith-Root eDNA backpack sampler during both daylight and nighttime slack tides. Community composition was characterized by three markers (uniCO1, 12S MiFish, and 16S). We detected nearly twice as many species at night compared to day, with notable community turnover between time periods. Day samples captured primarily benthic sessile taxa (e.g., cnidarians, gastropods) and photosynthetic cyanobacteria such as Synechococcus. Night samples detected mobile nocturnal predators, including eunicid polychaetes, and heterotrophic bacterial groups. Fish assemblages also showed diel shifts, further demonstrating that temporal sampling is critical for accurately capturing coral reef biodiversity. eDNA can be a useful tool for supplementing biodiversity inventories, particularly at night. By capturing both day and night community compositions, our approach reveals hidden temporal patterns essential for comprehensive biodiversity assessments and ecosystem understanding. This multi-marker approach reveals cycle-driven ecological shifts across taxonomic levels, from bacteria to vertebrates. Single-timepoint sampling (typically conducted during the day) may overlook entire groups, particularly cryptic or nocturnal taxa difficult to detect through traditional survey means. These findings underscore the need to integrate diel sampling into future reef monitoring and conservation strategies.
4b25346f:
The Holomua Marine Initiative, led by the Hawaiʻi Department of Land and Natural Resources, Division of Aquatic Resources (DAR) represents a transformative, community-driven approach to nearshore resource management, offering valuable insights for scaling coral reef conservation across regions and governance contexts. Holomua seeks to convene local communities to develop comprehensive, island-wide strategies through place-based processes based in cultural relevance, community decision-making, and adaptive management. In the recognition that Hawaiʻi’s coral reefs and nearshore ecosystems are inseparable from the cultural identity, livelihoods, and well-being of its people, this process emphasizes equitable participation grounded in local community knowledge and sustainable solutions.
 The Holomua Marine Initiative leverages its position as a state agency to facilitate inclusive partnerships among community organizations, funders, and other government agencies with local communities to implement and support key management actions based on community-identified priorities. This multi-faceted approach aims to not only to address near-term pressures on coral reefs such as unsustainable use, climate impacts, and declining ecosystem resilience, but also to shift the underlying systems that shape stewardship by strengthening inter-jurisdictional collaboration, resourcing community leadership, and institutionalizing participatory governance.
This presentation shares examples from Holomua highlighting how community-led planning processes and alignment with regional policy can generate leverage points for systemic transformation. In particular, we highlight how the initiative’s emphasis on co-management, equity, and cultural grounding challenges traditional agency-centric models and opens pathways for more just and effective stewardship, honoring the strong connections the people of Hawai‘i and other island communities have with their coral reef ecosystems.
By “sensing the system” through Holomua’s early experiences, this process offers insight into how diverse place-based strategies can inform larger-scale narratives, decision structures, and inclusive, cross-sector partnerships. As global reef crises intensify, the Holomua Marine Initiative illustrates how locally rooted approaches can catalyze broader shifts in governance, resilience, and collective responsibility.
c2b10586: Coral reef fisheries play a crucial role in supporting food security, livelihoods, and ways of life for communities worldwide, yet many face growing pressures from climate change, habitat degradation, and overexploitation. Understanding how different management strategies influence ecological outcomes is therefore critical for sustaining coral reefs and the communities that depend on them. In Hawaiʻi, historical reconstructions have revealed that Indigenous fisheries management practices supported high fisheries harvest from reef resources for centuries, without evidence of overharvesting. Here, we investigate ecological mechanisms that may have contributed to this historical abundance and assess their relevance to contemporary regulations, with a focus on population size–structure, seasonality, and predator-prey relationships . We developed a stage-structured, predator-prey model that incorporates harvest across trophic levels and life-history stages. Using this framework, we evaluated historical strategies that may have protected large, fecund adults, imposed harvest closures during spawning events, and accounted for trophic interactions, and compared them with modern approaches that use similar approaches, but differ in implementation. Our analyses demonstrate that maximum size limits yielded the greatest harvest, while slot limits supported the greatest range of harvest intensities, suggesting that protection of large adults is a key component of maintaining high biomass and harvest. Simulations of temporal closures highlight the benefit of aligning closures with spawning events, but also indicate that ideal closure conditions depend on harvest intensity and species biology. We further show that harvest of the predator population can enhance prey yield under certain ecological conditions. These findings indicate that the ecological mechanisms embedded in Indigenous stewardship practices, such as protection of large adults, attention to spawning cycles, and consideration of trophic interactions, can enhance sustainability and may help guide current nearshore regulations in Hawaiʻi.
9c6042b7:
Coral reefs worldwide are under increasing pressure from climate change, overfishing, and habitat degradation, and the Solomon Islands is no exception. Central Province, comprising the Russell Islands, Ngella (Florida) Islands, and Savo Island hosts globally significant marine ecosystems that sustain biodiversity and local livelihoods. These habitats include deep-water zones vital for whales and coastal areas that provide nesting sites for turtles and habitats for sharks, crocodiles, and diverse reef fish species. Over 3,000 people depend directly on these ecosystems for food security and well-being. Yet decades of unsustainable fishing practices, such as widespread use of spider nets, have degraded reef health, reduced herbivore populations, and increased reliance on imported canned food. These trends highlight the urgent need for nature-based solutions to safeguard ecosystems and strengthen resilience for over 30,000 residents.
Since 2023, the Wildlife Conservation Society (WCS), in partnership with national ministries, regional organizations, and the Central Provincial Government, has supported three communities in developing Ecosystem-based Adaptation (EbA) Plans. These plans integrate science and traditional knowledge such as tabu areas and zoning to prioritize reef recovery zones, protect herbivores, and reduce land-based runoff, enhancing coral resilience under climate stress. Two plans are in final draft and one under review, with legal gazettement anticipated early next year. Complementary initiatives, including baseline surveys, training, and governance strengthening, aim to empower Resource Management Committees for effective implementation.
At the symposium, we will share lessons learned from this community-driven approach, highlight strategies for integrating cultural practices with modern conservation, and discuss how EbA planning in the Solomon Islands offers a scalable model for safeguarding biodiversity and improving climate resilience in vulnerable coastal regions worldwide.
99f72f9f: For many decades, white band disease (WB) and white pox disease (WPX) have been severely impacting populations of the reef building corals Acropora palmata and A. cervicornis throughout the Caribbean region. Even after the major outbreaks in the 1990s and early 2000s, these diseases still occur annually in certain regions of the Caribbean, including in the United States Virgin Islands (USVI). Given the high virulence and infection rates of WB and WPX, mitigation treatments should be explored. It is highly suspected both diseases have bacterial pathogens, even though Henle-Koch’s postulates have not been fulfilled to determine the causative agent for WB nor WPX observed in the USVI. A specialized antibiotic paste utilizing amoxicillin trihydrate has successfully been used in situ to mitigate another disease impacting stony corals, stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD). Considering the past success of this topical paste, this study aimed to determine if this treatment could be used on A. palmata and A. cervicornis to treat WPX and WB, respectively. The topical antibiotic paste did not significantly halt disease lesion expansion, nor stop new lesions from forming. Therefore, other mitigation treatments for WB and WPX should be considered.
0da3a72d: The leading treatment for stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD) is a paste composed of amoxicillin trihydrate and silicon Base2B, combined at a standard ratio of 1:8, respectively. This treatment is designed to release antibiotics into the coral tissue over the course of three days. However, in the field, wave action and predation can interfere. Errors such as undermixing the antibiotic in the base can cause variances in dosages throughout the paste. Water temperature and light intensity may also affect the susceptibility of the host, virulence of the pathogen, absorption of the antibiotic or attachment of the base to the coral, resulting in variable efficacy between seasons. This study compared the efficacy of different ratios (1:8, 1:16, & 1:32) in a dosage experiment and then compared the efficacy of applying the standard ratio treatment for different durations (three-hours, one-day, and two-days) on SCTLD-affected coral colonies of the species Orbicella annularis. To test whether seasonality impacts the effectiveness of the treatment, both the dosage and duration experiments were conducted twice: once during the cooler dry season and once during the warmer wet season. Adjusted pairwise comparisons on Fisher’s Exact tests at most timepoints showed no significance between treatments whether duration or dosage, only slightly when compared to controls. This suggests that the lower dosages halted the lesion expansion as often as the standard treatment and that even the three-hour exposure duration time was effective at halting lesion expansion. Dosage lesion progression indicated faster expansion during the cool season. All analyses showed untreated controls to have the highest mortality, active lesions, and faster lesion expansion. Therefore, it is better to apply the antibiotic to the SCTLD affected lesion than to leave it untreated.
e41240a2:
Understanding how environmental stressors suppress key ecological functions is essential for developing sensitive indicators of reef condition. Herbivorous fishes are widely recognised as critical contributors to coral reef resilience, yet their functional roles may be substantially constrained by sedimentation—a pervasive stressor on inshore reefs. We integrate trait-based functional metrics with behavioural observations to assess how sedimentation filters both the composition and functional performance of herbivore assemblages.

We surveyed herbivore assemblages across six fringing reef sites in Fiji that varied in sedimentation rate and composition. For each species present, we defined a potential functional role using key morphological and ecological traits linked to food acquisition. We then used video-based foraging observations to evaluate how much of the potential function was actively realised through foraging. These were compared using multivariate and fourth-corner analyses to link trait expression with sedimentation variables.

Realised functional space (species foraging) occupied ≤1–45% of the potential functional space (species present) across sites, suggesting strong environmental filtering of functional performance. Nearly half of the herbivore species present were never observed feeding, particularly those most sensitive to sediment ingestion. This presented a disconnect between potential and realised functional roles. Foraging was disproportionately concentrated on vertical substrates, which accumulate significantly less sediment than horizontal surfaces, further suggesting that sedimentation restricts where functions are realised.
