These findings suggest using PBC lighting for coral nurseries and restoration because PBC lighting is beneficial for decreasing bleaching.
41362b99: With the aid of memorable statistics such as: “coral reefs take up less than one percent of the sea floor yet make up an estimated 25% of global marine life”, these systems are in an ideal position to encourage public engagement. Yet given their small footprint, we become challenged to reach a broad audience. We explored how creative practice grounded in scientific research, community passion and inexpensive distribution might expand the reach and conversation around coral reef community ecology and conservation. The project was piloted using our local ecosystem: the rocky intertidal. Through community conversation and local outreach, we developed and illustrated a comprehensive list of species. These illustrations were combined into an “infinity sticker” which was designed to wrap around a water bottle continuously, taking the viewer through the zonation of this iconic ecosystem, where benthic ecology was born and fostered. The sticker became a mobile gallery that allowed community members to see their coastal identities reflected in the ecosystem’s complexity, and learn more deeply about how we explore the complex dynamics present. Building on this success, we are piloting a parallel project for coral reefs, co-designed with community partners and informed by ecological research from the Moorea Coral Reef Long Term Ecological Research project. This coral-focused design highlights culturally significant species and key interactions currently under investigation, translating complex dynamics into shareable artwork. In doing so, it aims to connect people who may never visit a coral reef with the ecological stories unfolding beneath the surface, expanding the research into geographically distant communities. We aim to sell the sticker at various conferences and meetings, with proceeds supporting work of local educational nonprofit, Reva Atea. Through widely distributable, community-rooted artworks with low ecological footprint, we explore how impact can scale socially and geographically, activating a global, living gallery.
b010e437: Mass coral bleaching events are increasing in frequency and severity, and natural rates of thermal adaptation for many coral species may no longer match the pace of warming. Since corals inhabiting thermally variable environments often exhibit thermal resilience, ex situ exposure to temperature fluctuations has been hypothesized as a potential assisted-evolution strategy to enhance thermal tolerance before restoration. Thermal conditioning experiments have yielded mixed outcomes, particularly in massive species, and trade-offs in restoration contexts remain unclear. Here, we evaluate whether short-term thermal conditioning can enhance heat tolerance and restoration outcomes of the massive Caribbean coral Montastraea cavernosa. In an initial assessment, fragments from 12 coral genotypes were exposed to either constant elevated temperatures (~30.1 °C) or daily fluctuating temperatures (~28.2–30.2 °C) for ten days, reaching an equivalent eDHW corresponding to previously established ED5 thresholds. Following a recovery period, thermal tolerance will be quantified using a heat-stress assay in which fragments are ramped to 34 °C and held until bleaching. Throughout conditioning and the assay, photosynthetic efficiency (PAM fluorometry) and standardized coral color (color-referenced images) were monitored. Guided by these results, we will scale up the experiment by exposing a larger set of coral fragments to ambient, constant elevated, or daily fluctuating temperatures. A subset will undergo post-conditioning heat-stress assays, while the remainder will be outplanted to St. Lucie Reef, Florida, to evaluate in situ responses. Fragment growth, survival, color, and photosynthetic efficiency will be monitored quarterly to determine whether conditioning affects outplant performance or reveals growth trade-offs. After one year, a subset of outplants will undergo heat-stress assays to test the persistence of conditioning effects. Collectively, this work will clarify the potential and limitations of short-term thermal conditioning as an assisted-evolution tool for a priority restoration species and provide insight into trade-offs and field performance associated with fluctuating thermal conditioning.
b10690ad:
Coral reefs worldwide are declining rapidly, with projections estimating losses of up to 90% of reefs by 2050 under current warming trajectories. In response, methods and technologies for reef restoration interventions have undergone rapid development. However, to achieve ecologically meaningful impact for large reef systems like the Great Barrier Reef (GBR), these workflows must be scalable. Mass coral aquaculture, where sexually produced coral larvae are settled and grown under controlled conditions before deployment using e.g. coral seeding devices, offers a promising pathway: recruits are more resilient at later developmental stages, and genetic diversity is preserved. However, monitoring of recruits during the rearing stage remains profoundly labour-intensive. Manual counting and health assessments for recruits represent major operational bottlenecks, and quickly become infeasible as operations increase in scale.

We present the Coral Growout Robotic Assessment System (CGRAS), a novel robotic imaging and AI-driven analysis platform designed to automate recruit monitoring during ex situ rearing. Developed through a collaboration between roboticists and coral scientists as part of the Reef Restoration and Adaptation Program, CGRAS integrates a high-resolution submersible camera with an ingress-protected robotic arm for precise, repeatable and semi-automated imaging. The system repeatedly captures precise, high-magnification images of coral recruits across large batches of growout tiles, producing consistent, high-quality data while reducing manual labour.

State-of-the-art computer vision and machine learning (YOLOv8) algorithms automatically detect, count, and track the growth of individual coral recruits down to 0.25mm in diameter over time, enabling scientists to quantify treatment effects and optimize conditions. CGRAS was deployed at the National Sea Simulator (Townsville, Australia) and demonstrated to reduce counting time from two hours to eight minutes per tile, a 15-fold reduction compared to manual assessments.  This represents a major step toward scalable, data-driven conservation aquaculture, supporting Australia’s goal of delivering millions of climate-resilient corals to the GBR annually.
21bee929:
Growth measurements in scleractinian corals are important to monitor and evaluate various physiological processes. However, this has been somewhat challenging, especially for small colonies, fragments and over short time periods. This is often the case in laboratory ecotoxicological studies where precise measurements are essential to reliably detect detrimental effects at high sensitivity.
Common methods for measuring coral size include (1) buoyant weight determination and (2) the evaluation of their footprint from a top-down view. Both methods have limitations: (1) buoyant weight measurements can be affected by setup variability, such as minor changes in water temperature or salinity, and the growth of non-target organisms (e.g., algae). (2) Footprint measurements avoid these issues but may underestimate complex three-dimensional (3D) growth. However, recent advances in reliable 3D-scanning technologies offer consistent size measurements with increased accuracy.
In this study, we tracked growth of three coral species (Montipora digitata, Acropora austera and Porites cylindrica) in an aquarium facility over three months, using buoyant weight, footprint area, and 3D-scanning. Small fragments had different growth forms: M. digitata spread encrustingly across hard substrates, A. austera grew in complex shapes along a primary axis, and P. cylindrica grew as relatively massive digitate branches. Growth rates were calculated, and accuracy and correlations among the methods were analyzed. Compared to 3D-scans, buoyant weight showed higher variability, while footprint area was less accurate for more complex growth forms. For encrusting fragments (e.g., M. digitata), footprint area was highly correlated to 3D surface area.
Our findings demonstrate that 3D-scanning offers the highest accuracy of the tested measurement methods for coral growth . Footprint area evaluation can serve as a precise proxy for less complex growth forms (i.e., M. digitata). Using these improved methods enhances reliability and sensitivity, paving the way for coral growth as a sub-lethal endpoint in standardized chronic ecotoxicological testing.
d3175ddb:
The recovery of reef building corals requires the development of effective transplantation techniques. This study examined the growth enhancement effect of exposing corals to an electric field. 
A table-shaped iron frame with a 10 cm grid was constructed using 10 mm steel bars. In the experimental group, aluminum alloy anode plates were attached to generate a weak current, exposing corals to an electric field (10–50 mA/m²). In June 2025, fragments of Acropora muricata approximately 8 cm in length were collected at Nishidomari, Otsuki Town, Kochi Prefecture, Japan, and fixed to the intersections of the frame. Lateral photographs were taken at 0, 3, 12, and 24 weeks. Branches were categorized as primary, secondary, and tertiary, and their lengths and numbers were measured.
Both groups showed marked growth between Weeks 3 and 12, and growth enhancement in the experimental group was observed at Week 12. While the number of secondary branches did not differ between groups, the experimental group exhibited a clearly higher number of tertiary branches. “Secondary branches bearing multiple tertiary branches” which were rarely observed in the control group, were frequently recorded in the experimental group. No differences were detected in linear extension per branch. Comparison of fragments with higher branching showed that the experimental group retained a greater number of branches at Week 24. These results suggest that exposure to an electric field promotes branching patterns not previously reported in A. muricata, which may contribute to overall growth enhancement.
65fa605e: Coral reefs across the Caribbean have suffered dramatic declines due to recurrent mass bleaching events and stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD). In contrast, the reefs of Tela Bay, Honduras, remain remarkably intact, exhibiting low incidence of SCTLD and exceptionally high coral cover (>60%). This despite chronic exposure to thermal stress, high turbidity, and elevated nutrients from estuarine and riverine inputs. This study examines the algal symbiont communities of eight massive coral species across two geographically distinct regions in Honduras: Tela Bay and Roatán. We analyzed a total of 254 biopsy samples using ITS2 sequencing across environmental gradients at both locations and found that in Tela, for five of the eight species surveyed, corals harbor significantly higher proportions of Durusdinium trenchii compared to their conspecifics in Roatán. Within Tela, symbiont assemblages varied predictably across a nearshore-to-offshore gradient, with nearshore, more anthropogenically influenced sites exhibiting the highest abundances of Durusdinium. Meanwhile, across Roatán’s sites, Durusdinium was uncommon or rare across all species, with symbionts in the genus Breviolum most prominent. These patterns suggest that long-term associations with thermally tolerant Durusdinium play an integral role in driving the resilience of Tela’s massive corals. However, the substantial presence of other, non-Durusdinium dominant partnerships in Tela indicates that multiple mechanisms - potentially including host genetic adaptation, local acclimatization, or unique Symbiodiniaceae subspecies, may be at play. The relative absence of Durusdinium in Roatán suggests these coral communities may lack an important buffer against thermal stress and SCTLD. Together, these findings establish Tela Bay as a valuable natural laboratory for understanding coral resilience and highlight its potential as a source of climate-resilient corals. Integrating Tela’s massive corals into local and regional restoration, translocation, and assisted gene flow pipelines could meaningfully enhance Caribbean reef resilience under future ocean conditions.
2a290386: Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) is an invaluable tool for the prediction of future coral reef ranges in times of global change. ENM algorithms learn niche dimensions automatically from labeled training data and output continuous variables that express habitat suitability of unsurveyed areas, thus performing a regression task known as supervised machine learning (ML). Predictions outside of the training area, or into the future, are called extrapolations or generalizations. Extrapolations are especially prone to errors because predicted instances were not encountered during training. One way to improve generalization accuracy is to use an ensemble of models, drawing on the complementarity of their predictions to reduce the likelihood of model under- or overfitting to the training data. We introduce a novel approach to ensemble learning that combines model outcomes into a probabilistic framework and applied it to the Siderastrea Atlantic Complex as a working example. We employed 9 ENM algorithms and used information theory to build uncertainty surfaces. Our method uncovered surprising amounts of spatially-explicit variation in model performances, both within and among species, that cannot be captured by statistics traditionally employed in ML evaluation (Receiver Operating Characteristic, True Skill Statistic, etc.). We also introduced an entropy weighting strategy into ENM that integrates uncertainty into probabilistic ensemble predictions and, at the same time, prevents ensemble contamination by the chaotic variance of overfitted models. Our approach uncovered cross-area species transferability between the Caribbean and Brazil and a highly suitable stretch for two species of the genus Siderastrea along the Western African coast, a result that was recently corroborated by citizen-science reports. Although employed in the context of spatial extrapolation, the method is readily extensible to temporal projections, whose reliability must be carefully assessed to ensure sensible management decisions. The approach was fully implemented in a free R package.
a7d92bf2: White plague (WP) and stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD) are two detrimental diseases in the Caribbean that affect many of the main reef-building corals. WP has produced sporadic outbreaks for about 40 years, while SCTLD emerged in Florida in 2014 and rapidly spread through the Caribbean. Because the diseases can co-occur and share similar phenotypes, understanding their molecular similarities and differences may improve early diagnosis and differentiation. Since 2017, the Mydlarz lab has generated numerous datasets from corals with WP and SCTLD from the USVI and the Flower Garden Banks, including two parallel transmission experiments across multiple coral species and field-collected disease samples. Synthesis of these datasets using comparative transcriptomics and machine-learning approaches reveals important similarities and differences in host and symbiont responses. WP and SCTLD share core molecular responses, including induction of immune, signaling, and extracellular matrix–related (ECM) genes, and reliance on intercellular protein trafficking that underpins species-level resistance. In both diseases, the autophagy–apoptosis axis is a key determinant of susceptibility, with exposed but uninfected corals upregulating autophagy and susceptible species shifting toward apoptosis. The major differences reflect disease severity. WP triggers hypoxic stress, inflammation, and ECM-related changes, but with comparatively moderate immune and metabolic disruption, consistent with a more generalized immune response. In contrast, SCTLD shows dysbiosis between host and algal symbiont, metabolic collapse, and intense immune activation, along with pronounced symbiophagy driven by photosystem disruption. These qualitative and quantitative comparisons will be presented in this poster in the context of identifying targets for field assay development to support disease identification and treatment.
dc9b788a: Coral reefs are facing unprecedented degradation due to climate change, driving the need for innovative restoration strategies. Selective breeding harnessing within-population genetic and phenotypic variations offers a promising pathway to enhance climate resilience in corals. In this study, we utilized naturally heat-tolerant coral colonies as robust parents for cross-fertilization, aiming to produce thermally resilient offspring. We further unraveled the genetic and physiological mechanisms underlying heat tolerance, thereby bridging selective breeding with mechanistic discovery. We focused on Sanya's Luhuitou fringing reef—a highly fluctuating environment characterized by extreme summer temperatures (up to 36°C), turbidity, and wave action. Despite severe disturbance, residual coral colonies persist, suggesting evolutionary adaptations. We monitored and cross-fertilized five tagged A. muricata colonies, creating 18 larval lineages. Adults and larvae were exposed to acute thermal stress (33°C for 5 days and 35°C for 36 hours, respectively). Survival was recorded, and samples were collected for transcriptomic and SNP analysis. Our integrated analysis revealed conserved heat stress responses across life stages, including activation of DNA repair pathways, protein homeostasis mechanisms, and apoptosis regulation. Life-stage-specific responses were identified: larvae prioritized genes supporting rapid growth and development (cell adhesion, nucleotide biosynthesis, neuronal development), while adults upregulated processes for long-term homeostasis (precise protein folding, ion transport, and complex signaling networks). SNP analysis pinpointed alleles under thermal selection, including genes involved in ion transport, catabolic processes, providing genetic markers for thermal tolerance. This study identifies key molecular mechanisms and genetic markers of thermal resilience. Our findings provide a scientific foundation for selecting robust genotypes for breeding. We are advancing a bench-to-reef approach, expanding offspring production and initiating outplanting trials to validate restoration success in natural reef environments.
f3b1211d: Coral reef and seagrass ecosystem outside formal marine protected areas (MPA) remain ecologically significant yet often lack effective governance. As Other Effective Conservation Measures (OECM) gain global attention, empirical examples of shared governance arrangements in tropical marine environment are still limited. This study examines the implementation of a shared governance OECM model at Cempedak Island, Indonesia, a site outside existing MPA designation. The approach integrates community and private sector actors in co-management arrangements aimed at enhancing coral reef and seagrass ecosystem resilience while supporting local livelihoods. Key elements include the establishment of co-management committees with delegated authority, adaptive management protocols, and transparent benefit-sharing mechanisms. Economic incentives are supported through diversified livelihood activities such as enhanced sustainable fisheries and value-added processing, as well as ecotourism. Ecological monitoring integrates in situ surveys to assess habitat extent, species composition, and overall ecosystem condition, while socioeconomic outcomes are evaluated through baseline and follow-up assessments of household income, perceptions, and communitty engagement. Participatory monitoring is emphasized, engaging community members directly in ecological data collection and interpretation to strengthen local stewardship and build capacity for adaptive decision-making. Preliminary findings suggest that the shared-governance OECM model at Cempedak Island can potentially increase community participation, improve compliance with conservation measures, support early signs of ecosystem health improvements, and generate additional livelihood opportunities. This pilot provides insights into how OECM-based shared governance can serve as replicable, scalable conservation strategy aligning with national and global biodiversity targets while advancing community welfare.
94308c3f: Gulf of Mannar (GoM), one of the major reef ecosystems in India, is the main source of livelihood for thousands of coastal fisherfolk. The major reef formations occur around the twenty-one uninhabited shallow islands. The islands and their coral reefs give protection to the 160 km long mainland coast from erosion, cyclone and tsunami. The protection status enjoyed by GoM under different declarations/recognitions like Marine National Park (1986), Biosphere Reserve (1989) and Ramsar Site (2022) and the dedicated funding for protection, research, capacity building and awareness creation have substantially helped to protect the GoM reefs. However, the conservation and management of GoM corals face challenges due to the increasing coastal population and access to fishing infrastructure and facilities. For example, from 2005 to 2016, the number of fishermen families increased 12% and the number of motorized fishing crafts increased from 50.3% to 94.8%. With a majority of the families living below the poverty line, they greatly depend on reef fishery for their livelihood, and the consequent high fishing pressure has led to overexploitation and the use of destructive fishing methods (bottom trawling, bottom-set gill nets, purse seine, trap fishing, spear fishing, surface-supplied diving and anchoring), which have significantly damaged the reefs. Besides, the climatic factors remain a constant threat to the corals. There has been a relative decline of -47% in live coral cover during the past two decades affecting all the associated benefits. Initiatives like formation of village level eco-development committees, eco-tourism, awareness campaigns, alternate livelihood options and capacity building trainings, and involving community members in restoration projects have helped greatly in reducing the livelihood-linked threats. However, the cumulative effects of human pressure and climate change impacts pose a threat not only to GoM coral reefs but also to the livelihood of the dependent coastal communities.
a1f1556c: Turbid coral reefs are increasingly recognized as potential refugia for coral ecosystems under thermal stress and excessive solar irradiance. However, the environmental drivers that create light-limited habitats remain poorly quantified, leading to difficulties in defining turbid reef settings. Most existing studies have regarded elevated suspended sediments as the primary cause of light attenuation on turbid reefs. Yet, multiple optically active constituents, such as phytoplankton, coloured dissolved organic matter (CDOM), non-algal particulates, and even the inherent absorption of seawater, also play significant roles in determining both the intensity and spectral quality of light available for photosynthesis. A lack of quantitative datasets of optical constituents has largely oversimplified light-limiting processes on reefs. Here we conduct comprehensive optical measurements of 15 reef sites in Southeast Asia, from naturally turbid to extreme anthropogenically-influenced reefs, to quantify the relative contributions of the four major optical constituents to the diffuse attenuation of photosynthetically active light (kdPAR) and evaluate how shifts in optical composition alter underwater light fields. Our results show variability in optical regimes (kdPAR: 0.19 - 0.68) as water properties change between reefs. This variability demonstrates that turbid environments are not a single habitat type but instead exhibit distinct optical “signatures” that may influence coral photophysiology, depth distribution, and community structure. Our findings highlight the need to refine how these ecosystems are conceptualized in environmental assessments. By establishing a standardized approach for measuring underwater optical properties, we provide a baseline for future global comparisons of the relevant optical parameters to characterize coral reef environments.
d68c2f5f: Mass coral bleaching events and disease outbreaks from 2013 to 2017 resulted in a severe decline of live coral cover on Guam, Micronesia, especially targeting critical staghorn Acropora species. Rehabilitation of Guam’s eight Acropora species began in 2015 with fragments from surviving populations grown out in two ocean nurseries, then pruned and outplanted. Staghorn corals on Guam form extensive clonal thickets, increasing their susceptibility to disease and bleaching in a changing climate. Here, we explore polyculture as an alternative outplanting protocol. Polyculture has established benefits for cultured organisms, but its facilitative role has not been quantified in the context of coral restoration. We compare outplanting responses in monoculture vs. polyculture plots in two restored species, Acropora pulchra and Acropora vaughani, and one common reef species, Porites rus. We investigate how these contrasting approaches influence survival, susceptibility to disease and bleaching, and growth of our target species. Further, we test for differences in the coral’s microbiome and coral ecosphere between these environments, and their influence on our performance metrics.  Growth is assessed monthly, via photo analysis using ImageJ© Image Analysis Software, to calculate Total Linear Extension. Monthly health assessments record tissue color, partial and full mortality, disease, and predation.  Associated bacterial and endosymbiotic algal communities are being characterized at set time points, to determine whether polyculture influences shifts in the coral microbiome. Microbial exchange between these species may change their holobiont composition, which could increase diversity and improve resilience, survival, and growth. This study aims to inform future restoration methods designed for thicket-forming Acropora, and address knowledge gaps on the role of microbiome interactions between cohabiting species.
d0841fde:
Coral reefs worldwide face critical challenges from acute stressors (mass bleaching, COTS outbreaks, cyclones). The frequency and intensity of these acute disturbances have led to substantial coral loss, demanding prompt assessment of ecosystem viability. The National Science Foundation (NSF) International Research Experiences for Students (IRES) Advanced Studies Institute in Australia provides graduate students with crucial, immersive, and transdisciplinary field experience, focusing on solutions to environmental challenges. This project stems directly from the course's mandate to apply an integrated social-ecological understanding to assess the current status and resilience of the reefs surrounding Lizard Island following significant decline.

This research conducted a comprehensive transdisciplinary assessment of water quality parameters and coral cover. Underwater autosamplers were strategically deployed across diverse reef-associated habitats (coral reef, mangrove, rubble shore) to analyze the critical diurnal and weekly shifts in key water chemistry parameters, including alkalinity, dissolved oxygen, and salinity. This continuous temporal sampling strategy aimed to precisely capture natural baseline variability and potential metabolic signals, providing a fine-scale understanding of the environment. These chemical assessments were rigorously coupled with in situ ecological surveys, utilizing the transect line method to assess current coral cover against historical data.

Analysis of water samples determined that all measured parameters were within optimal ranges conducive to the growth and health of reef-building corals. Furthermore, ecological assessments demonstrated a measurable and encouraging increase in coral cover compared to previous post-disturbance assessments.

The convergence of chemical evidence (optimal water quality) and visual evidence (increasing coral cover) strongly indicates that the Lizard Island coral reefs are actively rebounding from previous declines. This finding highlights the potential for high natural resilience within these northern Great Barrier Reef ecosystems, suggesting effective local management and successful ecological processes, and exemplifies the value of IRES transdisciplinary training in developing holistic, effective strategies for long-term ecosystem recovery.
b6c0d775:
A conceptual model known as the Biological Condition Gradient (BCG) was adopted for use on coral reefs as the frequency and scale of various anthropogenic stressors has been gradually increasing over time making it difficult to track and effectively manage. The BCG relates biological condition to increasing levels of anthropogenic stress on a 1-6 scale and can be used to identify biological attributes and measurable increments of change from biological condition assessments. Level 1 on the scale is representative of a pristine, undisturbed environment while level 6 reflects sites with extreme environmental stress from major changes in biotic structure and ecosystem functions.
The BCG model was run in two phases: phase 1 consisted of historical data between 2005-2021 while phase 2 encompassed current data from 2022-2024. Hot spot analyses run on both the BCG historical and current datasets identify statistically significant spatial clusters of high BCG values (hot spots) and low BCG values (cold spots) revealing some areas of interest surrounding the Kristin Jacobs Coral Aquatic Preserve (CAP) and the Dry Tortugas region. The CAP transitions from a region of extreme hot spot clustering to sporadic hot spot clustering possibly due to the natural reef structure of the CAP region being narrower than traditional reefs possibly impacting coral communities more than anthropogenic pressures. The Dry Tortugas initially exhibits a cold spot clustering that transitions to an area of hot spot concentrations which may likely be due to the onset of stony coral tissue loss disease as it was officially reported in the Dry Tortugas on June 2nd, 2021. To help justify some of the differences exhibited in scores, other long term environmental data such as temperature and water quality, will be tested against the BCG to help explain some of the differences seen in the scores through time.
576d28d2:
Climate change has accelerated the intensity of storms and increased the need for emergency repairs to decades-old U.S. owned critical coastal infrastructure in the Pacific. Underwater portions of marine infrastructure often contain opportunistic coral aggregations; and in areas of high productivity, coral cover can be significant and/or contain U.S. Endangered Species Act (ESA)-listed corals. Infrastructure may also occur in an area federally deemed as Essential Fish Habitat (EFH). If EFH or ESA-listed species are likely to be affected, federal consultation with pertinent regulatory bodies is required to determine if avoidance, minimization and/or mitigation measures are necessary. Coral translocation is increasingly being utilized as one such measure.
 
This session discusses the operational and scientific techniques utilized during a large-scale coral translocation and monitoring project as part of critical repairs to a U.S. Navy-owned breakwater (Glass Breakwater) in Apra Harbor, Guam. Initial calculations estimated that approximately 1,200 coral colonies would need to be relocated from the project footprint, however, these estimates were based on data collected during the preliminary design phase. Refined design documents revealed that the construction area would be smaller than anticipated, and so additional surveys were conducted to produce a more precise assessment of coral presence in the area. Results from these surveys revealed that the actual number of coral colonies requiring translocation was substantially lower— reduced by approximately half— which decreased the number of corals subject to the stress of translocation. A monitoring program has been initiated, and orthomosaic survey techniques will be used to track changes in coral health and growth over time. 

This session examines the lessons learned and the challenges associated with fast-paced emergency repairs timelines and execution of work in dynamic environments all while maintaining the rigor and pace of the scientific and regulatory process.
3f1db695: The ongoing loss of coral reefs has led to growing interest in using artificial reefs to aid in the restoration and rehabilitation of these critical habitats. Habitat complexity is considered an important driver of biodiversity, but its application in artificial reef design has not been fully realized due to the limited techniques available to fabricate highly complex structures. In this study, we used 3D construction printing (3DCP) combined with low-carbon ‘green’ concretes, to construct highly complex artificial reefs. For the green concretes, we partially replaced ordinary Portland cement (OPC) with ground granulated blast furnace slag (GGBS) or calcined marine clay. Three artificial reefs were designed, printed, and deployed on the reefs of Singapore’s Southern Islands. To capitalize on the flexibility of additive manufacturing, each design was modular and tailored specifically for each reef zone: reef flat, reef slope, reef base. Findings from this study will provide insights into the ecological benefits of using additive manufacturing and green concrete for constructing artificial reefs. This in turn will help to inform future artificial reef designs and large-scale deployments to bolster ongoing reef restoration efforts.
abda933d: Coral reefs provide critical ecological, economic, and climate-resilience benefits to coastal communities in the Philippines.  Integrating coral reef management into long-term spatial and development planning is essential, yet its implementation at the local government level remains poorly understood. This study evaluates the extent to which coral reef management is integrated into Comprehensive Land and Water Use Plans (CLWUPs) across selected coastal municipalities in the Philippines and within national planning guidelines. Using a structured content analysis framework, key informant interviews, and alignment scoring against established coastal management, biodiversity conservation, and climate-resilience criteria, this research evaluates how coral reef-related management provisions are reflected in land and water use zoning, environmental management strategies, and development priorities. Results reveal different levels of mainstreaming for the local governments assessed, which are influenced by institutional capacity, data availability, interagency coordination, and local prioritization of coastal issues. Analysis of national guidelines shows that while coastal management principles are present, reef-specific provisions were often fragmented or lack operational detail, limiting effective mainstreaming at the local level. We recommend revising these guidelines to include clearer management standards, stronger linkages to climate adaptation and practical tools and trainings for local implementation. Findings from this study inform recommendations to strengthen policy coherence and improve local planning capacities to support long-term sustainability of coral reef ecosystems and scaling it up from local practices to national governance frameworks and processes.
dba4d03e: Coral reefs are immensely valuable ecosystems, providing key ecosystem services like access to seafood, recreational activities, coastal protection, and cultural value to millions of people worldwide. However, these systems are quickly degrading under stress from anthropogenic impacts. Climate change, overfishing, and pollution have caused massive global declines in coral cover, pushing reef ecosystems past stability thresholds and often triggering shifts away from coral-dominated states towards macroalgal-dominated states. While global climate change is a primary driver, its impacts are often exacerbated by local, non-climate stressors. Disentangling the relative influence of these co-occurring drivers is a critical management challenge, particularly in the Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary (FKNMS), where the region has lost nearly 90% of its coral cover since the 1970s despite decades of local management.  The objective of this project is to assess the relative influence of climate (e.g., temperature variability, heat stress) and non-climate stressors (e.g., fishing pressure, diving pressure, water quality) on long-term benthic community changes in the FKNMS. We quantify the drivers of both the decline in percent live coral cover and spatial and temporal shifts in coral community composition. To approach this, we have synthesized over 25 years of ecological data from the Coral Reef Evaluation and Monitoring Program (CREMP) and integrated it with spatial data on human pressures and remotely-sensed temperature data. Utilizing this longitudinal ecological data within a highly managed, exploited system, we identify the specific drivers most strongly associated with long-term coral decline and those influencing community changes. Findings highlight the spatial heterogeneity in these relationships, parsing out the effects of chronic spatial pressures and acute temporal disturbances. Understanding these complex interactions is critical to predicting high-risk areas, identifying potential climate refugia, and prioritizing local management actions within the Sanctuary.
9a42f839: Coral reef ecosystems are embedded within a mosaic of interconnected habitats, spanning shallow reefs to offshore/deeper waters. Many reef-associated organisms move among these habitats over daily, lunar, or ontogenetic cycles.  Additionally, diel vertical migrations link mesopelagic predators and deep-water food webs to surface communities. Despite the ecological importance of these cross-habitat exchanges, their expression in environmental DNA (eDNA) signatures remains poorly resolved. We addressed this gap with high-resolution spatiotemporal sampling of a shallow lagoon, an adjacent epipelagic site, and a deeper (~960 m) mesopelagic/mesophotic site on Tetiaroa Atoll.  We sampled these locations both day and night, across four lunar phases, and during a lunar eclipse. We asked: (1) Do fish communities exhibit consistent diel differences across a lunar cycle? (2) Are such diel patterns stable or moon-phase dependent? (3) Do pelagic and demersal organisms show similar or distinct lunar dynamics? and (4) Can eDNA detections provide an index of species’ relative abundance?  Community ordinations showed strong spatial structuring among habitats and clear diel shifts in assemblage composition. Species detections and relative eDNA concentrations varied with the interaction of lunar phase, diel activity guild, and habitat. Epipelagic daytime communities were comparatively stable across the lunar month, whereas nighttime communities exhibited pronounced lunar-phase-specific peaks, especially for diurnally active species at the new moon and nocturnal species at both the new and first-quarter moons. Lagoon communities were more variable overall and showed distinct responses to waxing phases and a coincident lunar eclipse. Pelagic and demersal taxa displayed divergent lunar signatures across habitats.  Across all habitats and lunar conditions, species richness was strongly and linearly related to relative eDNA concentration, with diel activity guilds showing distinct relationships. These relationships indicate that eDNA detections can serve as a consistent index of relative abundance while resolving fine-scale diel and lunar dynamics across connected reef–ocean ecosystems.
a87a9b4f: Coral reefs in Hawai‘i are ecologically, culturally, and economically essential, yet they are increasingly threatened by ocean warming and local stressors. As coral restoration becomes a key tool for supporting reef resilience, identifying the biological and environmental factors that drive outplant success is critical. This pilot study investigates the drivers of coral outplant survival and performance at Olowalu Reef, a priority restoration site on Maui, Hawai‘i. Through the Super Reefs Alliance—a collaboration among The Nature Conservancy, Stanford University, and the Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution—we experimentally evaluated thermal tolerance in coral colonies from eight reef habitats across West Maui characterized by different historical temperature regimes. We outplanted 259 unheated control fragments representing a range of heat-tolerance scores into paired warm and cool plots at Olowalu. Fragments are monitored biannually for survival, growth, and bleaching, with analyses examining how biological traits and environmental conditions influence performance. Preliminary observations indicate variation among species and across site conditions, with full-year results expected to clarify these patterns. To complement outplant monitoring, parent colonies are being tracked at three sites to determine whether experimentally measured thermal tolerance predicts bleaching responses in situ. By integrating genet identity, species, measured heat tolerance, origin site, and environmental context, this study aims to identify patterns that can guide adaptive coral restoration under climate change. Ultimately, linking laboratory-based thermal tolerance with field-based outplant outcomes can support more effective, climate-informed restoration strategies in Hawai‘i and beyond.
fcb29c60:
The submitter has requested this abstract is considered for a poster ONLY.

Octocorals exhibit resilience to a range of local and global stressors in the Caribbean Sea, a trait that their rapid vertical growth may partly explain. This study aimed to quantify the growth rate, survival, and mortality of two octocoral species across a eutrophication gradient in Cuban reefs. The gradient was characterized using microbiological variables, nutrient concentrations, and stable nitrogen isotopes. At each site, a permanent belt transect (250 × 2 m) was established to monitor colony height, width, and area over one year. The mean vertical growth rate of Eunicea flexuosa was 2.4 cm yr⁻¹ (range: 0.1–7.7 cm yr⁻¹), while Plexaura kükenthali exhibited 1.9 cm yr⁻¹ (range: 0.1–9.4 cm yr⁻¹). The growth rates of both species were significantly higher in impacted reefs by polluted river basins, and the eutrophication gradient, water visibility, and sediment accumulation on the bottom explained 36–78% of the observed variability. E. flexuosa had 40.1% mortality and 59.9% survival, whereas P. kükenthali showed 35.2% survival. Spatial differences in mortality and survival were not explained by eutrophication gradient, water visibility, or sediment accumulation, but hydrodynamic stress accounted for 59% of mortality variability across species. These results highlight the resilience of both species, which grow faster in eutrophic reefs with reduced water visibility and higher sedimentation, contributing to the ecological success of octocorals in the Caribbean Sea.
09685f4c: The long-term success and continued feasibility of coral restoration efforts necessitate that they be both scalable and cost-effective. This means finding ways to produce corals faster, cheaper, and more efficiently. One simple and inexpensive, yet understudied technique that could accelerate the production of coral outplants in land-based nurseries is the manipulation of seawater alkalinity. Preliminary evidence from aquarists suggests that alkalinity enrichment (AE) stimulates coral growth, and preliminary research has shown increased calcification.  Many questions remain, however, concerning the effectiveness of AE on species targeted for restoration, the manner in which growth is affected (i.e., skeletal density versus linear extension), as well as potential ontogenetic differences in response, particularly at early life stages. Using a novel robotic dosing system, we show that a 1.8-times increase in seawater alkalinity produces a 125% and 98% increase in total calcification and linear extension in sexually mature Acropora cervicornis, a commonly gardened Caribbean coral. Additionally, we present preliminary results from a second experiment assessing impacts on the density and microskeletal structure of juvenile coral recruits reared in AE using microcomputed tomography. Together, these results explore whether AE can scale both sexual and asexual coral propagation by accelerating growth while maintaining or improving skeletal integrity, ultimately producing more resilient outplants.
530f0a41: Coral restoration success has traditionally been measured by coral survivorship and growth, often overlooking the goal of reestablishing biodiverse benthic communities. Our study moved beyond coral-centric metrics to assess whether different coral restoration strategies—monoculture, mixculture, and polyculture—enhance the recruitment of benthic biodiversity compared to unseeded controls. We deployed Autonomous Reef Monitoring Structures (ARMS) for a three-year coral restoration experiment in a marginal reef to standardize the sampling of associated fauna. Combining morphological counts with COI metabarcoding of motile and sessile fractions, we found that proximity to an existing reef was a dominant driver of community composition, exhibiting a strong pattern of distance-decay. Our data also showed that the restoration strategy significantly influenced total biodiversity: active coral seeding yielded higher overall Operational Taxonomic Unit (OTU) richness than passive control substrates, which seemingly became dominated by oysters. Yet, the distinct culture types (mono-, mix-, poly-) did not yield starkly different communities. Our results demonstrate that active coral restoration is superior to passive substrate deployment for recruiting a diverse cryptobenthic community. Additionally, we advocate for long-term monitoring to allow the development of coral biomass and structural complexity, which may unlock the full biodiversity benefits of diverse planting strategies.
00a2e13c: Over the last several years, Sea Ventures Marine Response Unit (SVMRU), in collaboration with the Coral Restoration Foundation (CRF) and the National Oceanographic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Restoration Center, implemented large-scale Acropora palmata restoration at multiple sites in Puerto Rico. During the summers of 2023 and 2024, the coral reefs in the area were exposed to the worst thermal anomalies on record for Puerto Rico, with NOAA’s Coral Reef Watch virtual buoys indicating up to 16 and 19 degree heating weeks, respectively. Bleaching and subsequent mortality significantly impacted the restoration efforts.  Monitoring conducted using high-resolution photomosaics, provided detailed metrics on coral survival, growth, and percent cover. Coral mortality varied significantly across sites and regions. Thanks to this monitoring, SVMRU implemented a variety of techniques to respond to the thermal anomalies including genetic transfer and genotype-preserving relocation efforts guided by monitoring results. Relocating A. palmata fragments in Puerto Rico aims to promote sexual reproduction and support the recovery of the species in areas where it is at the brink of local extinction sites that, prior to the 2023 bleaching event, sustained healthy and robust populations. Collectively, these efforts expand the spatial distribution, genetic diversity, and monitoring resolution of A. palmata restoration across Puerto Rico, strengthening resilience in the face of recurrent thermal stress.
adc0774e: Understanding variation in coral thermal tolerance is critical for predicting reef persistence under future ocean warming. To quantify this variation across environments, we conducted a reciprocal transplant experiment and paired thermal‐performance assays in two dominant Hawaiian reef builders, Montipora capitata and Porites compressa. Corals were collected from three sites across Kāneʻohe Bay and one in nearby Kailua Bay (Ulupaʻu). Fragments from 40 genotypes were exposed to heat-stress assays, and photosynthetic efficiency was monitored using PAM fluorometry. Thermal tolerance was quantified as Effective Dose 30 (ED₃₀). All genotypes exceeded 4 eDHW, the commonly referenced bleaching threshod. P. compressa exhibited higher tolerance overall (6.84 eDHW) than M. capitata (6.33 eDHW). Within Kāneʻohe Bay, thermal performance was remarkably consistent across sites for both species, suggesting either shared bleaching histories or strong gene flow shaping a common tolerance baseline. By contrast, bay-level differences were more pronounced. P. compressa from Ulupaʻu had significantly higher thermal tolerance than Kāneʻohe conspecifics and all M. capitata populations, potentially linked to its compact growth forms in Ulupaʻu’s higher-energy environment. To assess stability of thermal tolerance in the field, all genotypes were reciprocally transplanted between bays and reassessed nine months later. Survivorship at Ulupaʻu was low, yet fragments that survived underwent a second heat-stress test. Across both species and transplant sites, surviving genotypes maintained high overall thermal tolerance, indicating resilience of these traits following transplantation. ED₃₀ values exhibited only a slight decline of ~1eDHW relative to initial measurements and likely reflect seasonal physiological shifts rather than true loss of tolerance. Ongoing analyses including 3D-growth, ITS2-symbiont profiling, and 16S-microbiome sequencing will further evaluate how holobiont composition, and growth dynamics contribute to thermal tolerance variation and can inform climate-ready coral selection for restoration in Hawai‘i.
de8f63eb: Low dissolved oxygen (DO) and pH compound events are projected to increase, becoming a threat to marine ecosystems, though they remain severely understudied on coral reefs. Almost no studies have assessed whether corals can cope with concurrent hypoxia and acidification, and it remains unknown (1) if routine exposure to sub-lethal nighttime low DO and low pH can induce conditioning that enhances future tolerance, and (2) whether this is frequency-dependent. Here, we pre-conditioned hypoxia-sensitive Agaricia tenuifolia and hypoxia-tolerant Siderastrea siderea to one month of high-frequency (HF, six pulses), low-frequency (LF, three pulses), or no pre-conditioning, with each pulse consisting of two consecutive nights of low DO (2.6 mg L⁻¹) and low total pH (7.75). The pre-conditioning phase was followed by one week of acute stress (DO 2.2 mg L⁻¹, pHT 7.69) or ambient conditions (DO 6.6 mg L⁻¹, pHT 7.98). Species-specific and frequency-dependent responses emerged during both pre-conditioning and the acute stress test. In A. tenuifolia, HF corals already exhibited 33% mortality during preconditioning, and survivors showed a 32% reduction in photosynthetic efficiency and 42% lower symbiont densities relative to unprimed corals, while biomass and calcification remained stable. In contrast, S. siderea maintained broad physiological stability across pre-conditioning treatments, but LF corals showed enhanced biomass maintenance at the end of acute stress compared to HF and unprimed corals. These results demonstrate that the frequency of low-DO, low-pH exposure modulates baseline physiology and short-term conditioning capacity. Environmental oxygen history has been shown to enhance coral hypoxia tolerance, yet frequency-based priming in this study did not yield comparable outcomes. Investigating key features of pre-conditioning duration, frequency, and intensity, and the interaction with co-varying stressors such as temperature, will be essential for resolving the mechanisms that support coral conditioning to future multi-stress scenarios.
f506602a: Coral reefs are among the most diverse and vibrant ecosystems on the planet. From fish to invertebrates, from corals to algae, color is woven into every level of life. Whether for camouflage, conveying a message, reproduction, or adapting to changing conditions, each organism carries its own distinct palette. For this project we are looking into the commercially important, yet little-studied corallimorph Ricordea florida (mushroom coral) from a perspective of color, looking at how it could have an influence on their ecology, where first, we surveyed reefs across Almirante Bay, Panama, to document the distribution and abundance of color morphs across sites and depths; its physiology, in which we evaluated whether color corresponds to physiological differences by comparing thermal thresholds and stress responses among morphs; and lastly genetics, where we examined potential genetic and symbiont variation associated with coloration, all this under the premise that there might be undescribed cryptic species of R. florida in the Caribbean and the fact that we could potentially have them here in Panama. With our ecological surveys we have found clear distinct ecological patterns between color morphs that are being backed up by the results of our physiological analysis and the corals’ response to thermal stress, and we are currently utilizing molecular approaches to further prove our hypothesis. This project not only would expand the limited knowledge we have about the biology of this fascinating group of animals, but will also provide us with valuable insights necessary for the adequate management, protection, and sustainable use of this species, ensuring their persistence in the ecosystems they inhabit and their genetic diversity.
b537ca3d: Tropical coral reefs in Australia and worldwide are increasingly facing threats from many different climate stressors, including ocean warming, acidification, and deoxygenation. While there has been extensive research on the effects of high temperatures and low pH on corals, less is known about how corals tolerate low oxygen stress. In this study, we show that naturally variable ecosystems such as mangrove lagoons adjacent to traditional coral reefs can serve as natural laboratories for investigating coral tolerance to climate stressors in situ. Using data from custom-built coral incubation chambers, we compare seasonal differences in coral dissolved oxygen thresholds for Porites sp. corals from both reef and mangrove sites over a single year. To further explore the connectivity between the reef and mangrove systems at Low Isles, we also report dynamic seawater carbonate chemistry, dissolved oxygen concentrations, water flow patterns, and nutrient content along a gradient between the reef and mangroves from discrete seawater samples and in situ autonomous sensors. These results highlight the highly dynamic environments that mangrove corals are able to withstand, suggesting potential refugia for reef corals under climate change.
13a989f0:
Turbid reef systems remain comparatively understudied across the Caribbean. Tela Bay, Honduras, is an inshore turbid reef system that historically supported unusually high coral cover for the Caribbean, leading it to be regarded as a regional bright spot. However, in 2023, a heatwave caused a mass bleaching event leading to a stark decline in coral cover. The aim of this study was to document how a marginal, turbid reef that resisted past regional heat stress responded to the 2023 heatwave, and to characterise the resulting collapse in benthic community structure. 
Benthic surveys at 15 m depth in Tela Bay and Utila showed that Tela maintained consistently high coral cover (≃35-60%) and low macroalgal cover (≃7-30%) until 2023, when thermal stress caused a sharp decline in coral cover to ≃7% and an increase in macroalgae to ≃35-45%. Prior to 2023, z-scores placed Tela’s coral cover above the regional mean and showed little year-to-year variation, indicating a stable state. Benthic assemblages in Tela were consistently dominated by hard corals, particularly Agaricia spp, with little change across years. Post bleaching, coral cover dropped to below the regional mean and showed greater variability.  Benthic community collapsed and gave way to a community composition more typical of contemporary Caribbean reefs where soft coral and millepora dominated and coral community become governed by Stephanocoenia spp, Dichocoenia spp and Madracis spp.
These findings illustrate how a historically stable, coral-dominated turbid reef that had previously demonstrated resistance to regional heat stress collapsed rapidly following the 2023 bleaching event, showing that earlier stability does not ensure continued protection under escalating thermal stress.
f6a1e0b4:
Coral recruits experience high mortality during their earliest life stages, especially on inshore reefs where macroalgae are abundant. Seeding devices with laboratory-reared and settled coral spat offer a promising method to accelerate reef recovery; however, their success is strongly dependent on local benthic conditions. Identifying which macroalgal traits most strongly influence coral spat performance is critical for deployment decisions and optimizing restoration outcomes.
This study investigates how macroalgal traits (e.g., canopy height, cover, biomass, and taxonomic richness), turf traits, and fine-scale benthic processes (e.g., device fouling and species-specific interactions) affect the survival and growth of coral spat on seeding devices deployed across inshore reefs in the central Great Barrier Reef. Here, we present results from devices deployed following the November 2025 spawning, identifying which macroalgal metrics most strongly influence coral recruit survival and growth, and distinguishing the relative importance of canopy-level versus micro-scale benthic interactions. We expect certain canopy traits to emerge as key predictors of spat performance, while localized interactions will reveal conditions under which seeding devices are most or least effective.
By linking functional ecology with applied restoration, this work will define the ecological limits of coral–macroalgae coexistence during early life stages, provide actionable guidance for site selection, and directly inform strategies to scale coral-seeding technologies to improve reef recovery in increasingly macroalgae-dominated ecosystems.
da294386: Understanding reef resilience across managed and unmanaged areas is essential for developing adaptive conservation strategies in Indonesia. Despite the country’s expansive network of Marine Protected Areas (MPAs), direct comparisons of ecological resilience indicators between managed and unmanaged reefs using a unified framework remain limited. This study assessed the resilience of 30 coral reef sites inside and outside MPAs in Eastern Indonesia by applying a set of widely used resilience indicators and established methodological guidelines. Resistance metrics, such as temperature variability, coral diversity, and herbivorous fish biomass and richness, along with recovery metrics including macroalgae cover and substrate suitability for coral settlement, were combined into composite resilience scores through a structured normalisation and anchoring process. Of the 30 sites surveyed, ten  showed high resilience and nine moderate resilience. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) biplots were employed to examine indicator patterns and identify the main drivers of variation across high, medium, and low resilience clusters. Herbivorous fish biomass emerged as the strongest contributor to differences in resilience, particularly in analyses that excluded temperature variability. High-resilience sites, including Ameth, Aboru, Rutong, and Porto, were characterised by elevated coral cover and diversity, greater herbivore biomass, low macroalgae cover, and reduced temperature variability. Conversely, medium-resilience sites were more strongly associated with higher temperature variability and increased macroalgae cover, reflecting heightened ecological stress. Coral cover, coral diversity, fish biomass, and coralline algae contributed positively to resilience scores, while algal abundance explained minimal variance due to its narrow range across sites. Overall, combining resilience indicators with multivariate analyses provided a clear differentiation of resilience patterns inside and outside MPAs, offering a valuable framework for prioritising management interventions in Eastern Indonesia’s coral reefs.
d06b4a7a: Coral reefs in Nosy-Be play a vital role in local livelihoods and tourism but face increasing degradation. This study, conducted from April to July 2024, assesses the ecological health of reefs and their integration into the tourism economy as a conservation tool. Surveys were conducted at seven sites (Nosy Tanikely, Nosy Komba, Nosy Sakatia, Nosy Antsôha, Ambario, Dzamandzar, and Nosy Tanga) using the Random Snorkeling method. Based on initial assessments, Nosy Tanikely, Nosy Komba, and Nosy Tanga were selected on which 30 transects with 750 quadrats were carried out to determine in detail their benthic cover and evaluate the abundance of fish and macroinvertebrates by the photo quadrat and transect methods. Results indicate Nosy Tanikely has the highest coral cover (20.49%), with 11.5% healthy coral and 9.08% bleached, while Nosy Tanga is the most degraded, with only 7.5% live coral cover. Fish diversity is highest in Nosy Tanikely (12 families), whereas macroinvertebrate diversity remains low across all sites (four families on average). A socio-economic study involving conservation organizations, local authorities, fishers, and tourism stakeholders highlights destructive fishing as a major threat to reef health. Findings underscore ecotourism as a viable strategy for conservation, community engagement, and sustainable economic development. Integrating marine protected area management with community-led ecotourism initiatives could enhance reef resilience while fostering inclusive economic opportunities. Strengthening governance, promoting reef-friendly tourism, and reinforcing local stewardship are key recommendations for ensuring long-term sustainability.KEYWORDS: Coral reefs, random snorkeling, photo quadrat et transect, SocMon, conservation, degradation, ecotourism.
4ef3b52d: Climate change, coastal development, and emerging diseases are causing global declines in reef corals, disrupting reef ecological functions and reducing ecosystem services. Many generalist reef corals, however, are thriving in non-reef habitats associated with reefs, such as mangroves, and seagrass lagoons. Though these habitats often present harsher environmental conditions compared to reefs (e.g., high and variable temperatures, low light levels). One theory posits that these habitats may serve as resilience reservoirs that, by exposing corals to more hostile conditions, strengthen their tolerance to environmental stressors. If these non-reef habitats harbor locally adapted corals, they could serve as critical sources of genetic diversity, potentially rescuing reef populations as environmental conditions continue to change. The Mustard Hill coral, Porites astreoides, is a generalist reef coral that plays a crucial role in filling structural gaps and maintaining benthic coral cover throughout the Caribbean. As a member of the ‘weedy’ life history guild, P. astreoides is resilient to environmental fluctuations, making it a key player in reef ecosystems. On Turneffe Atoll, Belize, Porites astreoides is found widely across coral reefs, seagrass meadows, and the mangrove epibiont community.  To elucidate whether these populations are locally adapted or acclimating to these incredibly variable environments, we conducted a reciprocal transplant experiment across Turneffe Atoll’s mangrove-lagoon-reef mosaic. We’ve seen these corals perform differently in each environment, exhibiting variation in survival, growth, and morphology. This implies different acclimatory capacities of populations across these systems, with the habitat from which the corals were sourced playing a consequential role in their acclimatory responses across the seascape.
c941dc98: Coral disease outbreaks continue to reshape reef ecosystems worldwide, yet their detection and monitoring remain constrained by expenses and logistical challenges, especially in remote regions. Norfolk Island, situated in the South Pacific, hosts a unique sub-tropical coral reef ecosystem affected by agricultural runoff which catalyses disease outbreaks after heavy rainfall. Here, we present a novel application of autonomous surface-vehicle technology to quantify disease prevalence and severity. Using a Blue Robotics BlueBoat uncrewed surface vessel (USV), we conducted high-resolution georeferenced surveys that integrated underwater imagery, environmental data, and spatially explicit transects to rapidly assess coral health at a scale and speed previously not possible through diver-based efforts. Using images from a GoPro mounted face down on the BlueBoat we were able to successfully and accurately identify prevalence and severity of disease across Montipora sp and Acropora sp colonies and track the onset of disease through time after it's initial outbreak following heavy rainfall. This research demonstrates the feasibility of integrating robotics and quantitative ecological approaches to strengthen coral disease surveys. By reducing the time, cost, and risk associated with traditional field methods, USV-based monitoring offers a scalable platform for rapid outbreak assessment, especially in regions with limited diving capacity or challenging sea conditions which represent hazards to in water surveyors. Our findings contribute to broader efforts aimed at developing innovative tools and analytical techniques to understand the drivers of coral disease dynamics, identify early indicators of stress, and support proactive management responses. This work underscores the value of autonomous technologies as part of an emerging toolkit for global coral disease monitoring and intervention planning.
e55d3166: Coral reefs in the Philippines are renowned for their rich fish diversity but face growing threats by coastal development, sedimentation, and overfishing. The fringing reefs of Jabonga, Agusan del Norte, lie adjacent to a proposed windmill and port projects, yet their ecological status remains poorly known. This study provides the baseline assessment of reef fish temporal dynamics in the area. Underwater visual census was conducted at three sites during dry season (March 2025) and two sites during wet season (July 2025) using standard protocols and evaluated using Hilomen et al. (2000) and Nanola et al. (2004) categories for Philippine reefs. Species richness, density, biomass, and biodiversity indices were quantified. Results showed that dry season assemblages were richer (48-60 species), more densely populated (1,460-3,327 ind/1000m2), and had higher biomass (29.8-72.1 MT/km2) than wet season assemblages (37-49 species; 1,429-1675 ind/1000m2; 13.1-23.9 MT/km2). Noticeable lower biomass values were recorded for larger, target food fishes during the wet season. Diversity remained moderate to high (Shannon H’ = 2.80-3.21; Simpson 1-D =0.88-0.94), while low dominance (D = 0.06-0.12) and moderate to low evenness (0.27-0.47) indicates an uneven community distribution that is driven by few abundant families (Pomacentridae, Labridae and Serranidae) shaping the community structure. Indicator families such as Chaetodontidae were present but few. Planktivores, omnivores and herbivores were the richest functional groups, with coralivores and piscivores present in smaller number. Despite seasonal declines in richness and density, overall community composition remained relatively stable. These findings emphasize the importance of temporal monitoring to understand reef ecosystem dynamics and support sustainable management and coastal biodiversity conservation. By establishing baseline data in a site facing imminent development, this study contributed to the broader effort to identify patterns and drivers of change in Philippine coral reef ecosystem.
68e8fc7e: Coral reefs are becoming increasingly threatened by rising sea surface temperatures, yet some populations remain resistant. In Ngermid Bay, a semi enclosed bay in Palau, corals thrive despite higher sea surface temperatures and pH levels similar to projections for 2100. To better understand what the future of coral reefs will look like, more research is needed to determine the physiological mechanisms behind coral responses to stressors, particularly in resilient populations. This project investigates physiological responses to heat stress and recovery in Porites cf. lobata populations from three sites within Ngermid Bay in comparison to a reference site with more oceanic influences. In collaboration with the Palau International Coral Reef Center (PICRC), a 7-day heat stress and 15-day recovery experiment was conducted, and coral fragments were assessed for buoyant weight to evaluate calcification rates and color intensity via the health chart to evaluate bleaching and recovery status at Days 0, 7, and 15. Changes in calcification and pigmentation across the stress and recovery periods will be used to identify physiological indicators of thermal tolerance in P. cf. lobata, along with recovery potential from stress events. Anticipated results from this project will advance understanding of the physiological mechanisms that contribute to coral resilience and adaptation. Linking these findings to reef management will support strategies that target conservation and protection of corals in a changing ocean.
6168d86f:
Gene expression modulation is a key acclimation mechanism in reef-building corals, as it allows rapid responses to environmental stressors. Consequently, molecular biomarkers provide powerful tools for the early detection of physiological stress and for linking cellular responses to local environmental conditions. During 2023-2024, identified as one of the most extreme and spatially widespread thermal stress events ever recorded on coral reefs. We analyzed the expression of heat shock protein (Hsp70) in Orbicella faveolata and Montastraea cavernosa from the remote Cayo Arenas reef system on the Campeche Bank in the southern Gulf of Mexico. Thermal conditions prior to sampling differed markedly between years: in 2023, the historical annual maximum temperature for the site (29.56 °C) was exceeded for 28 consecutive days, while in 2024 only 12 days of thermal stress were recorded, likely mitigated by Hurricane Beryl.
In July 2023 and 2024, 1 cm² tissue fragments were collected from ten colonies per species on six reefs at depths between 7 and 9 m. Hsp70 expression was quantified using qPCR. High-resolution orthomosaics were generated using underwater photogrammetry to relate gene expression to local habitat-scale variables, enabling the analysis of seascape metrics such as colony connectivity, colony area, patch complexity, coral cover at the species level, and the depth of individual colonies.
In 2023, O. faveolata showed positive expression of Hsp70 in 30 colonies and M. cavernosa in 22, with significant differences between reefs but no apparent relation to the variables analyzed. Samples from 2024 are still being analyzed. The spatial variability observed in 2023 may reflect localized acclimation processes and the thermal resilience of corals in this remote reef system, underscoring the importance of integrating molecular, ecological, and seascape data to better understand coral responses to increased thermal stress.
0cf5e53e: Indonesia plays a leading role in the global ornamental coral trade, exporting over two million coral fragments annually from 71 species. Sixteen species are particularly favoured in the industry and are classified into three growth-rate categories: fast (harvestable in 3–6 months), medium (8–12 months), and slow-growing (over 24 months). However, these classifications are based solely on time and lack biological validation. This study evaluated the growth and survival performance of 16 species across two depths (10 m and 20 m) in an in-situ husbandry facility. Coral growth was assessed using linear and vertical extension metrics over a 13-month period. Depth had no significant effect on overall survival or growth across most species, though Cynarina lacrymalis showed notable sensitivity. Growth performance varied significantly among categories, with fast-growing species achieving ≥ 7.5 cm/year in linear and ≥ 6 cm/year in vertical extension. Medium and slow-growing species exhibited consistently lower rates. These biologically derived growth thresholds can be used to refine Indonesia’s national coral mariculture guidelines, ensuring that harvest decisions are based not only on time but also on species-specific growth performance, supporting more sustainable and standardised coral farming practices.
cabdef95: Southeast Sulawesi lies in the heart of the Coral Triangle and serves as Indonesia’s primary source region for the coral harvesting trade. Yet despite its global ecological importance, the population-level consequences of intensive coral extraction remain poorly quantified. This study provides the first empirical assessment of how harvesting pressure influences the population structure of ten commercially targeted coral species across reefs under contrasting management regimes. Understanding these impacts is essential for evaluating the sustainability of Indonesia’s coral trade and for safeguarding the ecological integrity of coral reef ecosystems in their global epicentre. Underwater photo surveys were conducted at six harvested and six non-harvested reef sites using 150 m² belt transects. High-resolution images were processed in ImageJ using the SizeExtractR software to extract standardised metrics of colony presence, frequency, and size distribution. These indicators of population structure are being compared across management zones to detect potential signatures of extraction, including reduced colony abundance, truncated size classes, or shifts in species representation on harvested reefs. Preliminary image assessments reveal spatial variation in colony occurrence, and ongoing analyses aim to quantify the magnitude and patterns of population differences attributable to harvesting pressure. By integrating image-based monitoring with a comparative design across Indonesia’s most heavily exploited coral source region, this study provides novel, urgently needed evidence on the ecological costs of coral extraction. The findings will provide a scientific basis for improving harvest regulations, optimising MPA design, and informing sustainable management strategies within the Coral Triangle, where maintaining coral population resilience is vital for global biodiversity, fisheries, and reef conservation.
44470e40: Acropora palmata—a keystone reef-builder across the Caribbean—has experienced widespread decline under rapid ocean warming. In San Andres Island (Colombia), within the 180,000-km² Seaflower Biosphere Reserve, one of the world’s largest MPAs, long-term ecological data (1992–2024) reveal a decades-long deterioration of A. palmata, culminating in a severe collapse following the 2023 mass-bleaching event. Few locations globally possess such an extensive species-specific time series within a high-protection context, making this case a uniquely powerful lens for evaluating the limitations of passive marine management under climate extremes. To characterize the status of the surviving population, we conducted standardized field surveys along a 0–280 m reef axis (30 m cross-shore width), recording colony presence, size structure, and post-bleaching condition. In parallel, genetic analyses using STAGdb SNP arrays are underway to establish the first genomic baseline for A. palmata in the Seaflower MPAs. These analyses will clarify clonality, genetic structure, and the extent to which the remnant colonies retain variation relevant to future resilience. The integration of a three-decade ecological trajectory with emerging genomic information provides an unprecedented opportunity to inform A. palmata recovery strategies at a regional scale. This work advances a genetic-resilience approach to restoration—one that prioritizes the identification of resilient donor colonies, incorporates genetic diversity into nursery designs, and strengthens adaptive management frameworks under recurrent bleaching. The San Andres case underscores both the vulnerability of Caribbean foundation corals and the urgent need to embed genetics and long-term monitoring into restoration planning across climate-impacted MPAs.
3600a4d9: Coral bleaching events are globally monitored by satellite-derived sea surface temperature (SST), which is used to track heat exposure on coral reefs and predict the degree of bleaching without direct in situ observations. However, satellites provide temperatures at resolution coarser than most coral reefs, causing them to miss small-scale temperature variability, leading to spatial and temporal differences in predicted versus observed bleaching severity.  In situ temperature data, collected directly on the reef rather than remotely sensed, are essential for capturing these small-scale dynamics and improving our understanding of reef-water temperature variance. Therefore, we developed the Coral Reef In Situ Product (CRISP), the first global-scale repository of in situ coral reef temperature data. The repository currently contains over 2,500 consistently formatted raw temperature-logger files gathered from peer-reviewed studies, gray literature, and community submissions. CRISP is publicly accessible online, where users can access the database, view metadata for each logger, and download the raw temperature data immediately. Using CRISP, we are developing statistical models to predict fine-scale temperature variability such as daily temperature range calculations on reefs globally. We are also leveraging a variety of reef characteristics and environmental drivers including reef geomorphology, depth, distance to shore, and distance to open water. Our aims are to (1) evaluate the accuracy of satellite-derived SST through systematic comparisons between satellite product and in situ data, and (2) develop predictions of reef-water temperatures.
2d8bef87: Characterizing when fish produce sounds is essential for interpreting ecological rhythms. Although diel, lunar, and seasonal patterns are widely documented among soniferous fishes, they are rarely examined together within a single study. To investigate temporal variation in calling activity, we examined a year of recordings focusing on two dominant sonotypes attributed to Sciaenidae and Ophidiidae, families known for their prominent use of acoustic communication. Our goal was to characterize fish acoustic activity at three complementary scales: diel, lunar, and seasonal, in a subtropical rocky-reef system (Brazil). We analysed twelve 24-h recordings collected monthly between February 2018 and February 2019 during the new-moon. Each day was subsampled using one-minute files recorded every ten minutes.
