Fv/Fm values were also surprisingly consistent between all treatments. Recruit survival was strongly reduced on uncaged substrate due to predation by butterflyfish, independent of treatment. So far, we conclude that heat stress with or without AU has no negative effect on the very early life stages of P. astreoides, while the monitoring of longer-term legacy effects is currently still ongoing.
3bc985f8:
Corals are an important source of structurally diverse bioactive compounds with increasing value in biomedical research. This study examines the biological activities of organic extracts and green synthesized zinc oxide nanoparticles derived from the coral Tubastraea aurea collected from Kueishan Island, Taiwan. The coral extract and nanoparticles were characterized using UV Vis spectroscopy, FTIR, XRD, dynamic light scattering, zeta potential analysis, FE SEM EDX, and HR TEM. Chitosan based ZnO nanocomposites were also prepared to enhance stability and biological performance.

The nanoparticles and nanocomposites demonstrated strong antioxidant activity and broad spectrum antibacterial effects against Proteus vulgaris, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Escherichia coli, and Salmonella typhi. In mosquito vector assays, the nanoparticles showed potent larvicidal and pupicidal activity against Aedes aegypti under laboratory and field conditions, reduced adult fecundity and longevity, and lowered egg viability. They also improved copepod predatory efficiency in treated aquatic habitats, suggesting potential for integrated vector management.

Beyond vector control, the nanoparticles exhibited significant antiplasmodial activity against chloroquine resistant and sensitive Plasmodium falciparum strains and inhibited dengue virus serotype two. Wound healing studies showed that chitosan ZnO nanocomposites promoted HMEC 1 cell migration and accelerated wound closure without cytotoxicity.

Cytotoxicity studies in human lung cancer cells revealed dose dependent antiproliferative effects with low IC50 values and clear apoptotic morphological changes. Overall, Tubastraea aurea is a valuable natural resource for eco friendly synthesis of zinc based nanomaterials. The resulting nanoparticles and nanocomposites display multifunctional activities with promising biomedical and public health applications.
5e8d0a24:
Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) is the deadliest coral disease recorded, afflicting more than 22 coral species throughout the Caribbean for over a decade and continuing to spread into previously unaffected territories. Current treatments using CoralCure Tissue Loss Disease (Base2B):Amoxicillin achieve roughly 80% success in halting lesion progression with no known negative environmental or coral impacts. However, broadscale interventions face regulatory hesitation due to concerns over antibiotic use. To provide non-antibiotic alternatives, Ocean Alchemists has developed two new products, CoralCure Black Band Disease Formulation K (CCBBD-K) and CoralCure Universal Putty combined with chlorine (CCUP-C). 
CCBBD-K is a treatment paste containing a proprietary blend of natural antibacterials originally for Black Band Disease (BBD). These compounds may suppress bacterial communities associated with SCTLD, and preliminary trials indicate up to 80% efficacy in halting SCTLD lesions. CCUP-C is being formulated to diffuse chlorine as the active ingredient over an extended period of time, providing a continuous multi-day dosage regime to the SCTLD lesion margin. This new treatment methods improves on earlier chlorinated epoxy treatments, which were impeded by the rapid cure rates of epoxy and limited sustained release. 
This study evaluates the efficacy of these non-antibiotic disease interventions through field trials in the British Virgin Islands (February 2026) and SE Florida (May 2026). Diseased colonies from multiple species will be located and randomly assigned to treatment groups: CCBBD-K, CCUP-C, or untreated controls. Lesion progression will be measured at treatment, one week, one month, and three months using the linear progression single-nail method. Overall treatment efficacy will be analyzed with One-way ANOVA and Fisher’s Exact Test. Two-way ANOVA will assess species-specific responses. Results from the BVI and preliminary findings from Florida will be presented. Overcoming the risk of broadscale antibiotics will remove the current hurdles for the commencement of large-scale disease intervention campaigns.
c1c4a6dc: Assisted sexual reproduction is an effective strategy for enhancing fertilization rates in coral populations with reduced density and for increasing genetic diversity, improving adaptability to environmental stressors such as bleaching. Since 2020, The Nature Conservancy’s Coral Innovation Hub in the U.S. Virgin Islands has implemented this approach, primarily rearing recruits in field-based nurseries until 2024. Following the 2023 mass bleaching event and in anticipation of further thermal stress in 2024, a subset of Diploria labyrinthiformis recruits settled in May 2023 at our Hub was transferred to a land-based nursery for protection. After nearly one year, these lab-reared recruits exhibited substantially greater growth compared to counterparts maintained in field nurseries. To assess whether this growth advantage translates to improved survival and resilience post-outplanting, both treatment groups (field-reared and lab-reared) were transplanted to the reef and monitored for six months. This presentation will review comparative results on growth, retention, and health metrics, informing optimal rearing strategies for post-outplant success.
77e5c382:
Coral reefs in East Portland, Jamaica, are in a degraded state, reflecting a persistent coral-macroalgae phase shift. This shift is primarily driven by anthropogenic stressors including high nutrient loads from in-land agricultural run-off and the loss of key herbivores due to overfishing and disease. The condition is quantified by a poor Coral Reef Health Index value of 2.4 (2023), as coral cover, commercial and herbivorous fish abundance were critically low, while macroalgal benthic coverage exceeded 50%. This dominance has resulted in a significant loss of biodiversity and structural complexity within the area which has impacted the reef’s capacity to provide ecosystem services and its ability to recover from disturbances.   
To test a scalable restoration intervention, manual macroalgal removal was combined with coral outplanting over a two-year period (2024-2026). On three fringing reefs: Salt Creek, Turtle Crawle, and Winnifred, fleshy macroalgae were periodically removed by scraping and pulling from 25 m2 experimental plots. 162 'heat-tolerant' Orbicella faveolata macrofragments were outplanted within selected plots and benthic cover changes were monitored using 3D Photogrammetry. 
An average of 51.18kg of wet macroalgae was removed per cleaning event and the mean initial area of fragments outplanted in March 2025 was 13.43cm2. From March to September 2025, fragments had an average daily growth of 1.02mm2 and 1.06mm2 in algal removal plots and macroalgae plots respectively.  Survivorship of Orbicella faveolata fragments was 99.2% over the first 7 months, with one fragment mortality from an algal removal plot.  
Potentially, manual macroalgal removal paired with coral outplanting could be a cost-effective, low-tech intervention which reduces algal competition and facilitates coral recovery. This strategy is a viable, replicable and sustainable (less extractive) method for reef restoration in Jamaica’s Marine Protected Areas and can be implemented by engaging local communities.  

Keywords: Coral restoration; Macroalgal removal; Orbicella faveolata, Community-based management; Jamaica
d15c1dd9:
Assisted sexual coral propagation is crucial in rebuilding Jamaica’s coral resilience as it results in greater genetic diversity, increasing the chances of coral survivorship as they face unprecedented challenges. Restoration in Jamaica has been heavily biased towards asexual coral propagation, which can result in a loss of biodiversity and decline in species resilience in response to increasing and frequent stressors, such as disease, storms and bleaching events. The Alligator Head Foundation in partnership with The Nature Conservancy under the IKI CoralCarib project, became the first institution to successfully execute a coral assisted fertilization programme in Jamaica. Here, we provide a detailed description of the processes and results of the programme, including the lessons learned during its implementation.  
In September 2025, Orbicella faveolata and Orbicella annularis gamete bundles were successfully collected and fertilised from 28 colonies across 3 different sites in the East Portland Fish Sanctuary. Approximately 1500 sexual coral recruits were developed in AHF’s ex-situ laboratory and were seeded on 3 different shaped substrates. Variability was observed in spat settlement numbers as they ranged from 0-103 spats per substrate and in spatial preference on substrates. Substrates were monitored and allowed to develop both ex-situ and in-situ in 25m2 experiment plots.  
The program has shown that it is possible for Jamaica to successfully engage in assisted sexual propagation techniques increasing the genetic diversity within our restoration programs and local reefs. We hope through the establishment of AHF’s programme that other local entities may learn and be able to replicate components and contribute to the expansion of sexual coral restoration efforts in Jamaica. 

Key words: Assisted Coral Sexual Propagation; Restoration; Orbicella faveolata; Orbicella annularis; Jamaica
ee37b51f:
Over the past four decades, coral reef health in East Portland, Jamaica have been negatively affected by compounding stressors. Historically, local restoration efforts focused on asexual reproduction of Acroporids with varied success, and little attention to slow growing reef builders. After the massive 2023 bleaching event affected reefs, including our IKI CoralCarib project site in East Portland, there was a need to adapt and change previous restoration plans 
Three different morphologies of Orbicella faveolata were observed in East Portland, all displaying resilience towards Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease and high sea temperatures. Morph 1, a rugose colony with a surface forming multiple bumps, was selected for macrofragmentation as each bump potentially could be a colony outplant. In 2025, 3-6 OFAV bumps approximately 5-10cm in length were ‘macrofragmented’ per colony in-situ using a mallet and chisel. Wounds were sealed with 2-part epoxy sculpt putty and OFAV fragments were secured to the reef within 5km of the donor colonies. Sixty-five colonies across two sites were macrofragmented. At Site 1, an average 63.8% of the wound area was healed over 9 months, while at site 2, 58.2% of the wound area was healed over 4 months. 
A sample of 6 donor colonies from each site, alongside non-donor colonies were monitored for spawning in September 2025. 75% of the colonies spawned, with at least two donor colonies spawning on both September 13 and 14.
This methodology is a low-tech sustainable technique which can be used to restore slow growing species on reefs without the need for a nursery. Results have so far shown that colonies can heal, remain healthy and maintain reproductive potential. 

Keywords: Macrofragmentation; Wound Healing; Restoration; Orbicella faveolata, Jamaica
055e214f:
Engagement of fisheries communities in designing a Marine Protected Area (MPA) in Indonesia was introduced in the 2000s. However, their primary role was mainly restricted to key informants for fishing ground distribution as part of the ‘cost’ in designing an MPA. The Teon, Nila, and Serua (TNS) Islands, located in the remote Inner Banda Arc, are one of the priority areas for a Marine Protected Area (MPA) development. This study presents a community-driven spatial fisheries assessment that places fishers at the center of both fishery-dependent data collection and zoning decision-making to support MPA design.
A series of data collection methods were employed, combining a biophysics survey to map out key ecosystems, a participatory frame survey to document fleet structure, fishing gear types, threat location, and locally recognized fishing grounds, a mounted tracking devices on vessels to generate real-time spatial fishing activity, as well as the Crew Operated Data Recording System (CODRS) to document catch composition, length data, and operational trip characteristics. Processing spatial data produced layers of benthic habitat distribution, juvenile fish hotspots, vulnerable coral habitats, and fishing ground as key features for Spatial analysis to develop the MPA zoning. 
Of 517,5 hectares of critical areas for reef-associated fisheries, approximately 184,9 hectares are secured for a high level of protection during the consultation phase. A prominent challenge, as found in many MPA development processes, is the initial mistrust due to the fear that the MPA would restrict their access to the sea. By addressing a transparent, inclusive, tailored approach to community needs, while aiming for coral reef protection, an intensive dialogue transformed community skepticism into a sense of ownership, which ultimately strengthened the legitimacy of the management.
6647f79e: Marine plastics represent a major pollutant along Thailand’s coasts, accounting for over 45% of total marine debris. This study examined microplastic (MP) contamination in ten coral species; Acropora millepora, Astreopora myriophthalma, Diploastrea heliopora, Echinopora lamellosa, Favia pallida, Favites halicora, Galaxea fascicularis, Pocillopora damicornis, Porites lutea, and Turbinaria decurrens, which collected from Samae Sarn Island, Chonburi Province. The results showed that all coral species contained MPs, with Favia pallida showing the highest contamination (0.78 MPs /polyp) and Echinopora lamellosa the lowest (0.015 MPs /polyp). Fibers dominated (89.3%), followed by films, granules, and styrofoam. Most MPs were <1 mm (65%), and black particles were most common (41.7%). Six polymer types were identified, mainly nylon (43.2%), followed by polypropylene, polyester, PET, polystyrene, and PVDC. Larger polyps exhibited higher MP abundance, suggesting ingestion is influenced by mouth size. The dominance of fibrous black nylon reflects local anthropogenic sources such as tourism and fishing. These findings, the first for Samae Sarn, highlight microplastics as an emerging threat to coral health and underscore the urgency of targeted management and awareness initiatives in Thailand’s coastal regions.
03759fea:
Photogrammetry provides the ability to reconstruct 3D models and imagery from photos of corals and coral reefs. Even with its growing implementation globally, many often rely on approaches that produce “good enough” results with lower-accuracy data and inconsistent model and imagery quality. This gap between widespread adoption and technical rigor continues to grow, leading to ecological interpretations that are difficult to compare, reproduce, or defend scientifically.

Drawing from applied work across global coral programs and projects, it is evident that recurrent issues undermine the quality of coral reef photogrammetry. These often include insufficient photo quality, lack of project planning, improper use of ground control references, and suboptimal software workflows. Such limitations not only influence the accuracy of 3D models and imagery produced, but also risk misleading management decisions based on incomplete or inaccurate data. Among the many reasons why this occurs, there is one commonality that shapes how photogrammetry is applied to coral reefs: the educational and training pathway used to learn the technology. Without a high-quality, unified, and consistent pathway for learning how to apply photogrammetry to coral reefs, the very reasons we claim to use this technology to replace older methods (i.e., better data, scalability) are being undermined once again.

To improve the overall quality, scalability, and standard of applying photogrammetry across diverse projects, a more robust educational framework is needed. Currently, two primary pathways exist for learning coral reef photogrammetry: the self-learning path and the mentor-mentoree path. Although they both offer essential lessons that can be built upon, both are incomplete and fragmented. To address this, a new pathway is proposed: a global coral reef photogrammetry certification program. This tiered program is built upon photogrammetry fundamentals and coral reef–specific lessons while being designed to equip anyone with the technical skills needed.
93e8b70a: Global coral reef decline has accelerated under climate change, with Florida’s Coral Reef (Florida Keys, USA) experiencing substantial losses in coral cover and ecosystem function. Ensuring the future of coral reefs will require technological interventions that bolster coral resilience amid rapidly intensifying environmental stress. UZELA (Underwater Zooplankton Enhancement Light Array) is a technology that could help address this need. UZELA is an underwater light device that locally concentrates zooplankton, increasing coral feeding rates by 10- to 50-fold. Here, we tested the effect of UZELA on the mounding reef building coral Orbicella faveolata. Eighty ramets were placed in the Coral Restoration Foundation’s nursery in Tavernier, Florida, with and without a UZELA from January to October 2025. Coral planar surface area was quantified from photographs taken at six and ten months. At ten months, all ramets were collected for physiological analyses of chlorophyll concentration, symbiont density, stable isotopes, and lipid content. Preliminary results from the first six months show that the planar area of O. faveolata ramets exposed to UZELA increased by 11%, while control colonies decreased by 4.3%. We anticipate that this pattern will continue throughout the ten-month timepoint. Additionally, ongoing laboratory analyses will allow us to evaluate other health metrics in response to UZELA. We hypothesize that mounding corals exposed to UZELA will have higher biomass and symbiont density, and isotopic values corresponding to increased feeding, which are traits that correlate with coral resilience. Technological interventions like UZELA have the potential to aid in reef restoration efforts in the Florida Keys and beyond, by giving corals the boost they need to grow faster and be more resilient in the face of climate change stressors.
31e968d9: Historical destructive fishing has long been a major driver of rubble-bed formation across Indonesia’s coral reefs. Although such practices are now prohibited through area-based protection, climate-driven increases in tropical cyclones continue to destabilise rubble, hindering coral recruitment and slowing reef recovery. We surveyed three rubble-bed locations, each containing unrestored and restored sites, that were all fished destructively in the early 2000s, with one location subsequently exposed to significant storm damage. At each site, we quantified rubble-bed and fragment properties (slope, bed thickness, fragment morphology, hard coral recruits, stability, and binding) and documented the associated binding community. We then modelled the biophysical drivers of rubble stability, binding likelihood, and coral recruit abundance using Generalised Linear Mixed Models (GLMMs). Despite clear differences in rubble composition among locations, stability remained uniformly low (<10%) across both destructive-fished only and destructive-fished+storm rubble beds, and did not differ even at sites where restoration had been attempted. Binding likelihood ranged from 3–18%, with striking contrasts in binder composition, where mixed-disturbance rubble beds was dominated by early-stage binders like macro- and turf-algae, whereas rubble formed solely by historical destructive fishing supported a higher prevalence of calcifying binders such as crustose coralline algae (CCA) and hard-coral recruits. Longer rubble fragments and thicker rubble beds increased stability, binding, and hard-coral recruits, while steeper slopes suppressed all three metrics. Rubble originating solely from historical blast fishing supported over two orders of magnitude more coral recruits than rubble additionally impacted by storm disturbance. Our findings show that both destructive-fished and storm-reworked rubble remain locked in states of chronic instability, offering little chance of natural recovery. Immediate stabilisation is therefore essential across all rubble types, not only those recently disturbed. Crucially, long-term maintenance is indispensable; without continued care, restored rubble quickly loses structural integrity and fails to support sustained coral recruitment.
7fefd973: Coral reef protection through Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) is now widespread, but the ecological pathways through which they enhance reef fish production remain poorly  understood. Ongoing losses of coral cover and shifts in life-history composition degrade habitat and reshape ecosystem function. To understand how protection influences these dynamics, we examined how coral life-history traits, benthic composition, and management interventions shape associated fish assemblages across MPAs in Indonesia, the Philippines, and Tanzania (Zanzibar). We used Generalized Linear Mixed-Effect Models (GLMMs) to quantify the effects of coral life-history strategies (competitive, stress-tolerant, generalist, and weedy), benthic attributes, management status on fish communities at whole assemblage, trophic- and family-level. Protected areas showed mixed effects on overall fish biomass and abundance, yet significantly increased several highly targeted families biomass and length (including Epinephelidae, Lutjanidae, Acanthuridae). Coral traits also played a key role, where stress-tolerant corals increased both biomass and abundance, while competitive corals mainly increased abundance. Trophic groups and family-level indicators responded strongly to coral trait composition as well as benthic attributes. Protection status by itself is not a reliable indicator of fish community responses, and adding compliance and fishing pressure will likely improves our understanding of management outcomes. Coral life-history traits and benthic attributes offer valuable insight into habitat quality and significantly influence trophic structure, and family-level indicators reveal patterns that whole-assemblage metrics may obscure. The link between habitat condition and the biomass and abundance of highly targeted fishes is non-linear, and recovery under MPA management is shaped not only by protection status but also by coral functional composition, benthic structure, and management effectiveness. This highlights the need to incorporate coral functional traits and family-level metrics of targeted fishes into monitoring frameworks to improve evaluations of ecological recovery and pinpoint the coral assemblages most critical for sustaining ecosystem function within MPAs.
84dd03aa: The ultraviolet (UV) filter octocrylene has been detected in waters surrounding coral reefs and in coral tissues, however, limited studies and effects on corals have been reported. To address this we conducted definitive acute (96 hour) and chronic (21 day) toxicity tests, based on standard US EPA and OECD guidelines, with the Atlantic staghorn coral Acropora cervicornis. Multiple biological endpoints were employed to assess response including those pertinent to risk assessments (i.e. mortality and growth) along with other coral specific assessments, such as visual bleaching, coral condition, polyp retraction and photosynthetic efficiency. Acute toxicity was not observed with octocrylene at concentrations in the mg/L range and far exceeding solubility estimates (i.e., 40 µg/L). Two chronic toxicity tests were conducted using concentrations below estimated solubility and at levels measured in seawater (i.e. 0.05 – 36 µg/L nominal; 0.01 – 15.51 µg/L measured concentrations). In the first test no treatment effects were observed for photosynthetic efficiency, but statistically-significant treatment effects were observed at 17 and 21 days  for the coral condition index and growth rate. In the second chronic test, no statistically-significant effects were observed for visual bleaching, photosynthetic efficiency or coral condition index at any concentration or time point during the 21-day exposure. However, at 21days there was a significant reduction in growth rate at the highest dose of 1.47 µg/L (measured; 4.05 µg/L nominal), with a IC10 of 1.01 µg/L (measured; 1.95µg/L nominal) and NOEC of 0.427 µg/L (measured; 1.35 µg/L nominal). These values are similar to the 21-day reproduction NOEC (2.66 µg/L measured) in Daphnia magna. Further studies are required to investigate the impact of octocrylene on a range of morphologically diverse and geographically distributed coral species using both adult and early-life stages in addition to the extent of bioaccumulation and mechanism of action of octocrylene in coral species.
13f42a5d:
Ultraviolet (UV) filters are used in a wide range of products, including sunscreens, and have been detected in various environmental matrices, including, water, sediment and biota. Orders of magnitude different concentrations have been observed in environmental matrices and in toxicity thresholds reported for the same UV filters. Differences may in part be due to the varying sampling and analytical methods used. Adequate and accurate assessments of the concentration of the UV filter under study in toxicity testing is critical and underpins the reliability and defensibility of the toxicity thresholds derived from the study. We summarize our near decade of lessons learnt and recommend appropriate extraction procedures and analytical assessments for working with UV filters from field-collected environmental matrices and toxicity test samples with the goal to develop standard analytical methods for UV filters. 

An LC-MS/MS-based analytical method was developed to detect and quantify twelve organic UV-filter compounds, with limits of detection ranging from 0.02 to 9 ng/L. In order to test the effectiveness of the extraction method and the utility of the recovery standards, spiked solutions of all analytes at two different concentrations were prepared in deionized water and artificial seawater (n=6). These samples were prepared, extracted, and analyzed using the same method as environmental samples. In addition, standard solutions in artificial seawater were prepared and kept in the dark at room temperature, 4°C and -20°C before being analyzed in order to test the analytes’ stability. The use of isotopically-labeled recovery standards added before solid-phase extraction increased both recovery and reproducability. All compounds tested had a recovery of 80% or greater after recovery standard correction. This indicates that the extraction and analytical methods are well-suited for the analysis of UV filters in seawater samples. Additionally, all UV filters were shown to be stable over short periods at all tested temperatures.
10a51eab: Larvae-propagated coral restoration is emerging as a promising strategy to accelerate reef recovery, yet optimizing settlement substrates remains a key challenge, especially since there is an urgent need to move away from artificial substrates. The 1988 lava flow at Gunung Api, Banda Islands, is widely known for its remarkable natural coral regeneration, offering an opportunity to better understand how substrate characteristics can support larvae-based restoration. Within a capacity building project, we evaluated how lava rock influences the settlement success and early development of coral larvae. Gametes collected during a mass-spawning event at the lava flow reef in November 2023 were cultured to planulae and deployed in a damaged reef area ~2 km from the donor site, where natural recruitment is typically low. Lava rocks and smoother non-lava rocks were arranged in paired settlement plots, each covered with fine nets to retain larvae during the first seven days (n = 17 plots). In parallel, additional lava and non-lava rock plots (n = 15) were deployed to quantify natural recruitment, allowing direct comparison between larvae-propagated and non-propagated restoration treatments. Recruit abundance, survival, growth, and genus-level composition were monitored over a two-year period. Our results demonstrate clear differences in early coral performance linked to substrate type and highlight the strong potential of volcanic lava rock microstructures to enhance larvae-propagated restoration outcomes. These findings provide practical guidance for substrate selection in larval-based reef rehabilitation and underscore the broader value of integrating locally available natural materials into restoration strategies across Indonesia.
18482072: Tropical Cyclone Seroja, which occurred in April 2021, generated large waves and caused severe destruction of coral reefs in Rote and Sabu within the Savu Sea Marine National Park. Understanding of the ecological consequences of cyclone impact on coral reef is essential for informing Marine Protected Area (MPA) management unit to improve protection of coral reef ecosystems and support the livelihood security of small-scale fishing communities. This study assesses the impact of Cyclone Seroja on coral reef conditions in Savu Sea Marine National Park, with a particular focus on Rote Island. We investigated the coral cover and genus composition across eight monitoring sites, four years after the natural disturbance. Average coral cover reached 44.80 ± 14.33%, with the highest value recorded at four locations along the eastern to northwestern coasts of Rote Island. These four sites were heavily impacted by the cyclone, exhibiting extensive structural damage, as indicated by coral rubble accumulation along shoreline that have formed barrier reef like structures. Despite this disturbance, coral cover in these areas remains relatively high due to the dominance of Porites sp. Evidence of natural recovery was also detected, indicated by the presence of new coral recruits, particularly from Pocillopora sp. This genus known for its rapid colonization and ability to quickly respond to environmental change.  Together, Porites sp. and Pocillopora sp. constitute the most dominant coral genera across all surveyed sites. We recommend conducting annual survey of the study sites, including assessment of coral recruitment, to better understand the recovery rate of coral cover post-disturbance. This approach will provide MPA managers with more robust information for evidence-based policy formulation and timely management intervention to support coral reef restoration in areas impacted by Cyclone Seroja.
ce99282d: Coral reefs are structurally diverse, house significant biodiversity, and provide essential ecosystem services to coastal communities, with each function tightly linked to the spatial distribution of reef benthic communities. Understanding the spatial variability of reef habitats and the co-occurrence of key, broad scale benthic features (e.g., hard corals, macroalgae, sand) is essential for the accurate delineation of benthic features across broad spatial scales derived from Earth Observation image pixels. Here, we used >100,000 benthic photoquadrats collected over 20+ years, which provide high spatial resolution (<1 m²) in situ benthic cover data from multiple reef environments (e.g., oceanic atolls, fringing reefs). Benthic cover data was utilised to identify distinct assemblages of co-occurring benthic features at varying spatial scales based on existing and planned satellite-based sensors (pixel sizes of 1-300 m). We derived probabilities of co-occurrence for groups of benthic features at different sites, to identify variations in benthic configuration between locations for benthic cover types relevant to monitoring with spaceborne sensors over regional scales and larger (10’s 100’s of km²). Co-occurrence relationships were defined using simple presence/absence metrics as well as proportional cover, derived from multiple decades of benthic photoquadrats classified using an expertly trained machine learning model (ReefCloud). We identified key spatial thresholds (e.g., pixel sizes) at which co-occurrence relationships vary significantly, for the context of satellite-based coral reef monitoring. These probabilities of co-occurrence help quantify the spatial heterogeneity across a reef system while providing guidelines, rules, and thresholds for habitat mapping classification tools. We highlight which ecologically relevant features should be considered for understanding what an Earth Observation based pixel consists of, supporting not only our understanding of reef composition but also what Earth Observation imagery can identify.
672abcc6:
Corals of dendritic morphologies often regenerate from small fragments of branch to form dense clonal populations. Utilizing this high regeneration ability, asexual reproduction has been applied to artificial coral propagation for reef restoration. But this method is applicable for fast-growing coral species such as Acropora. For massive coral species, this method is not practical due to slow growth. As an alternative way of asexual propagation of massive corals, “reskinning” was proposed as a method to cover various shape substrates including artificial structures by thin coral tissues grown rapidly from small fragments arranged in certain distances to each other on a whole substrate. However, this reskinning method requires many clonal fragments excised from a single colony to allow fusion of grown fragments, resulting a heavy damage on the donor colony. In order to improve this aspect, we developed a method to multiplicate donor fragments repeatedly.
A massive coral Porites lobate was used as a donor, and thin fragments of approximately 3cm diameter were excised. Twenty five pieces of 3cm-square tile were gathered, and four donor fragments obtained from a single colony were fixed on four-corner tiles surrounding the center tile. Donor fragments spread over neighboring tiles after 11 months of culture. Then tiles were separated 12 months post fixation, and each covered tile as a second-round donor fragment and each original donor tile was surrounded by new tiles. Another set using another colony showed similar results, and it was operated as above 20 months post fixation. Not only the recycled donor fragments but also the second-round donor fragments spread over surrounding tiles one year after the second setting. These results reveal that repeated propagation of donor fragments for reskinning is available and suggest that reskinning could be achieved by reducing damages on donor massive corals.
a89d2f21: The Caribbean elkhorn coral Acropora palmata has suffered severe declines across the tropical western Atlantic, with Florida populations considered locally extirpated following the 2023 marine heat wave. Despite this, A. palmata remains the focus of active restoration efforts due to its iconic nature and ecological role as a wave-breaking reef crest species. However, restoration efforts draw from dwindling genetic pools, potentially constraining adaptive capacity under rapid environmental change. Leveraging the broader reservoir of genetic variation across the species’ range may therefore support recovery and resilience, with variation in thermal tolerance among regional populations suggesting that combining genetic lineages through assisted gene flow (AGF) could enhance climate resilience. In June 2024, branches from 13 A. palmata colonies were collected under permit from Tela, Honduras and imported as broodstock to the USA under CITES permits. Using ex situ spawning systems and controlled 1:1 fertilization crosses, three types of F1 cross were generated: Florida × Florida (Fl♀ × Fl♂), Florida × Honduras (Fl♀ × Hnd♂), and Honduras × Florida (Hnd♀ × Fl♂). These offspring were outplanted at one year, under license, to a wild reef off Miami, FL, in anticipation of a bleaching event in summer 2025 to investigate whether combining genetic lineages can enhance performance in a changing climate. Tissue samples were collected before outplanting (June 2025), after corals experienced eight degree heating weeks (September 2025), and during recovery (October 2025), with additional samples taken at three-month intervals to assess changes in symbiont community and symbiont-to-host cell ratio during heat stress, recovery, and subsequent seasonal environmental changes. An ex situ heat-stress experiment will also be undertaken in Spring 2026 to compare host:symbiont cell dynamics and characterize host physiological responses to elevated temperatures across lineage types.
6d536f75:
Coral reefs provide economic goods and services worth about $375 billion each year, yet their protection often relies on public support during crisis events. While agencies like NOAA have strong systems for monitoring the biological health of reefs, there are few tools available to track social sentiment and how the public feels and reacts during these disturbances. This project fills that gap by using Python-based web scraping to track public attention on social media.

Using a dataset of Reddit post titles from 2010 to 2025, we tracked how often people posted about coral reefs during major storm seasons. To ensure we captured actual public conversation rather than academic discussions, we tracked the everyday words people use to talk about storms, like "hurricane damage" and "storm surge", rather than complex scientific terms. Results show that public attention spikes immediately after events like Hurricane Maria, but these spikes are often very short.

Building on these findings, this project is evolving into an interactive dashboard for resource managers. This tool will allow users to filter data by specific hurricanes or regions to see real-time shifts in public concern. We are also expanding the work to include professional networks like LinkedIn to compare how the general public reacts versus how professionals respond. This social monitoring tool will help managers time their outreach and funding requests to match the moments when the public is paying the most attention.
2bdb27af: Fish biomass, a key driver of coral reef ecosystem functioning and associated ecosystem services, is strongly affected by human disturbances. Estimating fish biomass, understanding its spatial distribution, and assessing recovery potential are essential for evaluating reef condition and informing effective management. Coral reef ecosystems and their ecological benefits are particularly important around the reefs of Mauritius, yet data on fish biomass under varying management regimes remain limited. This study examined spatial and temporal changes in total fish biomass (TFB) and target biomass (TB), the biomass of fish having a high market value, around Mauritius in 2020 and 2024, across 20 reef sites categorized as protected or non-protected based on formal management status. Fish ≥ 3 cm total length were surveyed using triplicate 50 × 2 m belt transects, and species-specific length–weight relationships were applied to estimate biomass (kg/ha). At non-protected sites, the highest TFB recorded was 2317.45 ± 1299.22 kg/ha in 2020 and 5818.19 ± 851.73 kg/ha in 2024, with corresponding TB of 1883.84 ± 1123.80 kg/ha and 4365.52 ± 409.93 kg/ha. At protected sites, the highest TFB was 2779.57 ± 1342.69 kg/ha in 2020 and 2725.09 ± 280.41 kg/ha in 2024, while TB reached 2553.25 ± 1244.87 kg/ha and 2402.16 ± 280.41 kg/ha. There was a significant increase in TFB (p = 0.004) and TB (p = 0.001) at non-protected sites between 2020 and 2024, but no significant change at protected sites. No significant differences were detected between protected and non-protected sites in either year. These results indicate that the reefs of Mauritius maintain relatively high fish biomass despite limited MPA coverage and enforcement. Temporal increases at non-protected sites highlight resilience where fishing pressure is moderate. Strengthened enforcement, adaptive site-specific management, and continued monitoring are essential to sustain reef fish populations and associated ecosystem services.
953876f8:
The coral reef of Mauritius faces accelerating pressures from climate change, overfishing, and habitat degradation. The UNDP/Adaptation Fund project Restoring Marine Ecosystem Services by Rehabilitating Coral Reefs to Meet a Changing Climate Future responds by restoring degraded reefs with thermally tolerant coral species while strengthening long-term community stewardship and livelihood resilience. Implemented by Reef Conservation with UNDP and the Ministry of Blue Economy, the project places coastal communities, particularly women and youth, at the center of reef recovery.

A gender-responsive selection process identified 45 vulnerable participants, later stabilizing at 24 as some transitioned to permanent employment. Prioritizing women-headed households, providing stipends, and offering practical restoration training catalyzed a major shift in participation: by mid-2025, 75% of interviewed direct beneficiaries were women. Many reported increased confidence, financial independence, and leadership, indicating meaningful transformation in gender roles within marine conservation.

Livelihood surveys (2022–2025) confirmed that more than 90% of households remain highly dependent on fisheries, yet participants increasingly recognize coral restoration as vital for safeguarding fish stocks and diversifying income opportunities. Despite considerable operational challenges, including cyclonic damage, prolonged high sea temperatures, bleaching at donor sites, and restricted sea access, the project generated important technical lessons. Deeper nursery placement, larger fragment sizes, and the superior resilience of rope nurseries emerged as key methods for enhancing coral survival under climate stress.

By 2025, beneficiaries viewed coral restoration not only as an environmental intervention but as a pathway to strengthened identity, economic security, and community leadership. The project demonstrates that coupling restoration science with inclusive, gender-responsive capacity building can accelerate both ecosystem recovery and socio-economic resilience in a changing climate.
ad7a815a: Gender shapes people's climate change vulnerability and resilience across a multitude of social-ecological systems, including small-scale tropical coral reef fisheries. However, studies that address gender-specific climate vulnerabilities in this context are limited. Through a comprehensive meta-analysis of peer reviewed and local language publications, we identified factors that contribute to these vulnerabilities in two countries with high reliance on reef fisheries and vulnerability to climate impacts: Indonesia and the Philippines. We found these factors to include 1) the specific climate change hazards and risks that threaten small-scale fisheries, 2) gendered fishing roles, which influence exposure to these hazards and corresponding vulnerabilities, and 3) adaptive capacity and adaptation approaches, which also vary by gender. For instance, cultural beliefs and stereotypes about women’s suitability to fish offshore as well as gendered expectations surrounding childcare and home responsibilities mean that women across both countries more typically participate in nearshore fisheries, whereas men tend to fish offshore. This role division exposes men and women to different climate risks: nearshore areas are vulnerable to sea level rise, e.g., while offshore vessels are more likely to encounter threats via the intensification of storms. Adaptation approaches also varied by gender, with women more likely to have diversified livelihood strategies that may serve as an important bulwark against climate impacts. However, women also face unique vulnerabilities, including power imbalances, limited representation in fisheries governance contexts, and relative lack of resources. Collectively, our findings suggest that gender plays an important role in shaping fishers’ experiences of climate change, their resilience to impacts, and fishery resilience writ large. Gender is therefore a critical dimension in supporting effective adaptation strategies and inclusive fisheries governance approaches that ensure fishery resilience and sustainability for all. Our work contributes to Climate REEFS, a transdisciplinary, multi-national project advancing climate resilient reef fisheries.
0381812c: Adaptive management proposed for the Pūpūkea Marine Life Conservation District (MLCD) on O‘ahu, Hawaiʻi exemplifies the integration of community voices in coral reef management. The locally-based community stewardship non-profit organization Mālama Pūpūkea-Waimea (MPW) was contracted by the local government to execute a three-year Biological Carrying Capacity (BCC) study to provide empirical data and recommendations for adaptive management related to the potential overuse of the marine reserve by humans.The BCC study focused on the heavily impacted Kapoʻo (Sharks Cove) region to collect rigorous scientific data to create management recommendations. Results demonstrate clear ecological degradation linked to persistent, elevated visitor density. Since 2022, in-water human presence in the Kapoʻo Tidepools has increased by 214%, remaining persistent year-round. BCC surveys confirmed that physical damage to coral increased significantly with human density, with an estimated 40% probability of observed damage on coral colonies in hotspots of visitor use. Concurrently, non-schooling fish abundance and species richness declined year-over-year across zones. Using non-linear analysis of the relationship of non-schooling fish abundance and surrounding human density, we quantified a conservative threshold of 1 human per 10m radius in the Kapoʻo Tidepools after which precipitous declines in fish abundance are likely, and extrapolates to a maximum acceptable visitation of 27–31 individuals at any given time for the entire area.These findings culminated in recommendations to the State and City governments for adaptive management. This project provides a useful case study in academic partnership, local stewardship, and natural resource management.
b9b52aae: The collection of organisms for the aquarium trade typically occurs on coral reefs, adding pressure to an already threatened ecosystem. Large-scale production through marine ornamental fish aquaculture offers a potential solution. One major bottleneck to scaling up production is the prevalence of pelagic-spawning species in the trade, while marine ornamental fish aquaculture seems to rely more on demersal spawners. This study investigated whether marine ornamental fish aquaculture is biased toward species with simpler husbandry characteristics associated with demersal spawning. Six reproductive, embryonic, and larval development traits were analyzed: sexuality, spawning type, egg size, duration of embryonic development, larval size at hatching, and duration of larval development. Data were compiled for 527 globally traded ornamental species, comprising 80 mass-cultured and 447 non–mass-cultured species. Sexuality showed no trend among mass-cultured species, but all other traits showed that mass-cultured species tend to have demersal spawning, produce larger eggs with longer embryonic development, and hatch larger larvae when compared with the overall species pool. Duration of larval development was the only exception, likely due to limited sample size. Therefore, demersal spawning facilitates ornamental aquaculture: larger eggs are easier to handle, longer embryonic periods yield more developed larvae capable of feeding on larger prey, and larger larvae at hatching improve survival and reduce production costs. However, while some pelagic spawners have established protocols, most are rarely mass-cultured, indicating that larviculture is the main bottleneck. To reduce fisheries pressure over already degraded coral reefs, technological advances are urgently needed to develop protocols for all ornamental species and enable large-scale production. The aquarium industry should target on diminishing this existing bias, focusing on the preservation of wild populations.
2c66793a: Guam hosts over 5000 marine species, including over 400 species of corals. Culturally and economically important, Guam’s coral reefs support traditional CHamoru fishing practices, attract hundreds of thousands of tourists annually, and provide coastline protection. Despite their high value, these reefs have experienced extensive declines from local and global stressors to the point where active restoration is needed. To address this decline, in June of 2024, the Guam Department of Agriculture’s Coral Reef Initiative (GCRI) established a new coral nursery in Piti Bomb Holes Marine Preserve (PBHMP) focusing on six understudied species. Early assessments revealed notable differences in survivorship and growth across species and genotypes. Acropora latistella emerged as one of the top performers, exhibiting rapid growth rates and nearly 100% survivorship. However, in July 2025, the PBHMP suffered an unprecedented poor water quality event, triggering a coral disease outbreak that killed hundreds of soft and stony corals within the preserve, including nursery corals. Species and genotypic differences in susceptibility to disease were observed. Notably, A. latistella was particularly susceptible with 38.6% of fragments succumbing to disease, significantly more than the other 5 restoration species. Within A. latistella, genotype mortality ranged widely: 0 to 88.6%. Given the considerable variability both within and between species, the importance of species and genotype diversity in reef restoration is highlighted. To explore the long-term effects of disease, survivorship and growth rates will be measured and compared with pre-disease values as well as with unaffected genotypes. Understanding these differences is critical for improving nursery design, prioritizing resilient corals, and demonstrates that diverse assemblages are more likely to withstand disturbance events, thus maintaining long-term survival and growth of restored corals.
3335ee5c: Small-scale fisheries (SSF) comprise 90% of the world’s fishers, and although the concept only emerged in the early 2000’s, it has since grown rapidly. The concept has been widely successful in distinguishing small-scale fisheries from their industrial counterparts, and in mobilising directed research, governance, and funding. However, a focus on the politics of representation has separated their study into predominantly social or natural science foci and led to a homogenization of SSF, undermining understanding and sustainability of some of the most socially important, ecologically biodiverse, and vulnerable socioecological systems globally. Here I re-embed an interdisciplinary understanding and appreciation of the diversity of coral reef SSF, encompassing biodiversity, catch, livelihoods, nutrition, and governance. Although SSF differ considerably, by geography, habitat, and scale, they can all be characterised by diversity. Across multiple domains, diversity is a critical, interdependent, and reinforcing component of coral reef fisheries and inherent in building and maintaining resilience. Governance approaches to support and maintain diversity will therefore be key to the future sustainability of coral reef SSF and the ecosystems that support them.
1ebbba74: Tropical coastal seascapes of coral reefs, seagrass beds, and mangroves form interconnected mosaics that collectively support biodiversity and provide essential ecosystem services. Despite the growing use of the seascape concept in management, ecological assessments are often conducted at the habitat patch level, overlooking the functional linkages between habitats. This gap is particularly significant in tropical coastal systems, where many fish species depend on multiple habitats throughout their life cycles. Moreover, ecological integrity, which underpins ecosystem services, is poorly defined and quantified. To address this gap, We define seascape integrity through three measurable dimensions—habitat condition, extent, and connectivity—each linked to structure, composition, and function. Structure captures the spatial arrangement and linkages among habitat patches; composition reflects the variety and distribution of habitats and associated fauna; and function represents ecological roles and cross-habitat processes, particularly fish movements across life stages. By encoding differential habitat use across taxa and life stages, we integrate these dimensions into a unified framework. We combine habitat condition maps, biophysical modelling, and fish functional traits to map integrity for Kenyan reef seascapes. This integrative approach provides a comprehensive understanding of ecosystem health and resilience, offering insights for ecosystem-based management and policy development in a resource dependent tropical region.
033c85ae: Tropical marine ecosystems are defined by mosaics of interconnected habitats, where cross-habitat foraging links energy pathways across seascapes. In the Florida Keys, Sanctuary Preservation Areas (SPAs) are designed to protect reef fish assemblages but contain disproportionately little seagrass relative to reef and hardbottom habitat. Because many reef mesoconsumers frequently feed in surrounding seagrass beds, understanding their reliance on seagrass-derived energy sources is essential for evaluating how effectively SPAs protect the resources that support reef fish populations. This need is especially relevant across the gradient of the Upper, Middle, and Lower Florida Keys, where the spatial arrangement of seagrass meadows and patch reef mosaics differ significantly. Variability in habitat distribution, reef–seagrass proximity, and local oceanographic conditions create distinct resource landscapes that shape how mesoconsumers access and integrate production across these regions. This study investigates four reef mesoconsumers (Haemulon plumierii, Lutjanus analis, Ocyurus chrysurus, and Sphyraena barracuda) trophic dynamics and examines their reliance on reef-seagrass derived energy sources. Fin clips of the four target species and basal energy sources (i.e., seagrass, macroalgae) were collected and sampled for stable isotope analysis (δ13C, δ15N, and δ34S). Bayesian mixing models estimated proportional contributions of basal sources, and posterior distributions informed hypervolume analyses to evaluate trophic niche size, overlap, and regional variation. Across species and regions, mesoconsumers derived a greater share of their energy from shallower seagrass meadows rather than from seagrass patches adjacent to reefs. These results highlight the critical role of cross-habitat subsidies and show that reef fish depend heavily on production generated outside the reef-focused SPAs. Determining energy contribution of reef-seagrass resources to reef mesoconsumers helps to understand the functional connectivity between benthic habitats, which should be incorporated into SPA designs. Further, identifying mesoconsumers niche space allows a better understanding of their resource utilization, identifying niche overlap, and inferring trophic relationships.
96b984da: Mangrove restoration can enhance coastal biodiversity by creating valuable nursery habitats for reef-associated fishes, yet the ecological outcomes of planting mangroves over existing habitats such as seagrass beds remain debated. This study investigated how 10- and 30-year-old planted mangroves established on former seagrass beds support juvenile reef fish communities and how reef proximity (<100 m, <300 m, <600 m) shapes assemblage structure. Fish sampling across tidal stages used a beach seine net (7.5 m × 1 m, 5 mm mesh) with 15 standardized tows per site. A total of 1,035 individuals representing 36 species from 16 families were recorded, the majority of which were juveniles. Reef-associated fishes, particularly from Lutjanidae and Siganidae, were consistently present, demonstrating that planted mangroves provide shelter and feeding opportunities for juvenile reef fishes. Species richness and abundance generally increased with distance from coral reefs, indicating that planted mangroves can expand available nursery habitat within the coastal mosaic. However, shifts in habitat use were evident, such as the presence of Halichoeres spp. in younger plantations but their absence in older stands, reflecting ecological changes following the conversion of seagrass beds into mangrove monocultures. These findings underscore that while planted mangroves can function as important nursery areas for reef fish, restoration must be implemented responsibly, avoiding seagrass conversion and ensuring that site selection aligns with ecological suitability. Such considerations are essential for maximizing restoration benefits for fisheries, reef-mangrove connectivity, and coastal biodiversity conservation.
42f50cdf: Larval dispersal is a fundamental ecological process that links coral reefs with the surrounding coastal mosaic, including mangroves, seagrass meadows, and other marine and socio-ecological systems. These cross-habitat connections are essential for maintaining ecological integrity, supporting population persistence, facilitating recovery from disturbances, and shaping long-term evolutionary dynamics. Yet despite its central importance, larval dispersal remains one of the most difficult processes to quantify across species and seascapes. Limited empirical data and the complexity of hydrodynamic environments hinder our ability to generalize dispersal outcomes or predict how connectivity will respond to local and global stressors. The DisKer (Dispersal Kernel) project aims to close this gap by combining genetic, ecological, and oceanographic approaches to develop the first data-assimilated biophysical models of larval dispersal across multiple reef seascapes. We apply isolation-by-distance (IbD) theory to estimate mean parent–offspring dispersal distances (σ_IbD) for six reef fish species representing diverse life-history traits. These species were co-sampled and RAD-seq genotyped across Fiji, Vanuatu, and New Caledonia, allowing us to compare dispersal patterns across environmentally and culturally distinct archipelagos. The resulting empirical connectivity estimates are used to constrain biophysical models run over twenty years of high-resolution hydrodynamic data, enabling robust tests of how species traits, habitat configuration, and seascape features interact to shape dispersal kernels. By embedding larval movement in a multi-habitat context, our work highlights how connectivity among reefs, mangroves, and seagrasses underpins ecosystem functioning and resilience across tropical seascapes. These data-informed models will support a new conservation portfolio framework for designing managed-area networks that explicitly incorporate temporal variability in dispersal and cross-habitat linkages. Through engagement with local communities and stakeholders in each archipelago, the project aims to translate cutting-edge connectivity research into practical, culturally grounded management strategies that strengthen seascape integrity and enhance reef resilience in a rapidly changing world.
0e5be9b9: Ocean warming disrupts coral larval connectivity through two major mechanisms, including altered larval development in response to warming and reduced reproductive output from a shrinking coral population. The extent to which these combined impacts can subsequently alter dispersal patterns across the vast coral reef ecosystems has not been extensively studied. Using a 1 km coral larval dispersal model, climate model projected sea temperatures during spawning months, and a spatially-explicit model of coral population dynamics, we examine how climate change disrupts the connectivity of the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) from 2025 to 2100 under four climate scenarios. Under the highest emission scenarios of SSP5-8.5, the combined impacts reduce the number and strength of source-sink connections by 49.7% and 99.6%, respectively, by the end of the century. The mean number of reef clusters increases by 6%, while the mean size of clusters decreases by 46%, suggesting potential habitat fragmentation. Conversely, under the lower emission scenario of SSP2-4.5, the number and strength of connections are predicted to be reduced by 12.4% and 74.4%, respectively, and the clustering of reefs remains consistent by 2100. The weakening of larval connectivity and the increasingly isolated reef patches will potentially reduce the exchange of genetic materials between coral populations and the potential for rescue effects of larvae after local extirpation of corals from disturbances. We have also identified key source reefs, mainly distributed in the northern and southern regions, that have a high potential to replenish many other reefs consistently in over half of the hydrodynamic scenarios, given that larval production remains stable. Conservation actions should be prioritised in these reefs that maintain connectivity to increase the post-disturbance recovery potential of the GBR.
915b7f98: Financing the establishment and long-term management of marine reserves remains a significant challenge for many coastal communities. Resource users, such as scuba divers, are known to place a premium on pristine ecological conditions typically found inside marine reserves, and several countries have introduced a user fee for diving in marine reserves to serve as a revenue stream for their conservation efforts. We conducted a willingness-to-pay (WTP) study with 170 scuba divers in the Great Barrier Reef region to quantify their WTP for various changes in reef attributes and to assess whether the divers would pay a premium to access highly protected dive sites. We found that divers assigned the highest WTP to experience high coral cover, fish diversity, and abundance. Divers were willing to pay 14.50 AUD to dive in a marine reserve even if the biological conditions inside the reserve were identical to those in less protected areas. We also found that most divers were willing to pay a user fee when the purpose of the fee (i.e., to support conservation) was explicitly stated. Our findings can assist the dive tourism industry and Reef managers in their planning for future coral reef tourism opportunities that will help contribute towards the costs of Reef protection initiatives against the backdrop of environmental change.
af4687f2: Coral conservation in small island developing states faces persistent challenges. The Turks and Caicos Reef Fund (TCRF) works with the tourism sector, positioning conservation as an economic asset, using community participation to create self-sustaining mechanisms. One model is Citizen Science Tourism, which helps fund research while engaging visitors directly. RumPowered Research®, launched in 2021 through partnerships with rum producers, has generated more than $24,000 per annual research expedition. It has since evolved to integrate paying citizen scientists aboard the Turks & Caicos Explorer II. More than 40 volunteers have been trained in coral identification and disease recognition, and eight monitoring sites off East Caicos have been revisited three times in five years. The 2025 expedition trained six visitors with no prior experience in basic coral and fish identification, photomosaic surveys, disease treatment, and coral nursery maintenance. Sustainable Tourism Operator Engagement is equally important. In 2025, TCRF secured £49,434 through Darwin Plus Local Round 5 for Conservation Connections, installing six large educational signs at strategic marinas, producing videos on mooring practices and wildlife interactions, and embedding conservation messaging into required tourism certifications (TIDES, STCW). The Reef Keeper program in participating hotels further empowers visitors to donate and get involved. Partnerships with dive operators, government agencies, and the water sports and hotel association ensure messaging reaches 100,000–300,000 annual visitors. Biodiversity Enhancement is central to current conservation needs. TCRF’s Living Coral Biobank is the first kind in the UKOTs and open to the public allowing for visitors and volunteers. TCRF also partners with The Reef Institute and AGRRA and through the CORDAP Coral Rescue & Restoration Roadmaps grant headed by AGRRA, serves as a mentor to others in the region establishing biobanks. Having a public-facing facility where visitors can see, learn, and interact with corals has proven highly effective and rewarding.
eeacbed7: Anthropogenic noise pollution has become one of the most pervasive stressors affecting the quality of marine habitats in recent decades. In coral reefs, noise pollution affects the foraging, reproduction, and recruitment of reef-associated organisms. The pervasive nature of noise makes physical no-entry boundaries ineffective against this stressor, and new strategies are necessary for impact mitigation. We aimed to identify and measure the challenges and opportunities in reefs under varying protection statuses by contrasting habitat quality and levels of anthropogenic noise. We investigated noise levels, juvenile fish abundance, adult herbivore abundance, and substrate cover in two no-entry zones, three no-take zones, and three general-use zones in Kenting National Park, Taiwan's oldest Marine Protected Area (MPA). Noise indicators were divided into two biologically relevant frequency bands: 0.2–1.5 kHz, which corresponds to fish sounds, and 2–20 kHz, corresponding to shrimp snaps. We found that anthropogenic noise from motorized vessels generates louder and longer-lasting sounds compared to fish calls, suggesting that human activities in the area could mask the sounds produced by fish. Additionally, louder vessel noise had a negative correlation with herbivore abundance. Finally, integrating ecological and acoustic indicators revealed a high-opportunity site with high habitat quality and low anthropogenic noise that remains unprotected under current zoning regulations. Conversely, two reefs where high coral cover and juvenile abundance overlap with loud vessel noise represent current conservation challenges. Our study highlights the potential of combining ecological and acoustic assessments to identify priority sites where low human presence may render more fruitful conservation efforts. Noisy recreational activities in no-take zones may pose an underestimated threat that can be addressed by shifting current tourist activities to quieter alternatives; supporting sustainable tourism. This approach provides practical insights for improving spatial planning and management in MPAs.
22fd1123: Coral reefs provide critical social and economic benefits to coastal communities, supporting tourism and protecting shoreline infrastructure during large storms. Given the high economic value of coral reefs to coastal tourism, new initiatives such as coral insurance programs have begun to engage the private sector in funding coral restoration. However, it is unclear whether coastal businesses - key beneficiaries of reef ecosystem services - perceive coral reefs as an asset to business activities. In addition, further research is needed to identify common features of businesses that will likely participate in restoration initiatives like coral insurance. We surveyed coastal tourism businesses on Oʻahu and Hawaiʻi Island, Hawaiʻi, to measure business perception of coral reef health and value. We also identified variables correlated with business willingness to pay for coral restoration through a coral insurance. Our results indicate that many coastal businesses, even those operating on the ocean, see limited economic value in protecting coral reefs. However, most businesses showed strong willingness to pay for coral reef insurance. Pro-nature/stewardship norms, concern for business reputation, and Native Hawaiian ethnicity were significantly correlated with willingness to pay for coral insurance, while length of employment was correlated with decreased payment. We find that appealing to stewardship norms, social networks, and reputational concerns can motivate tourism businesses to participate in coral restoration. Finally, survey results indicate that high transparency, democratic decision-making, and leadership by well-known and trusted community groups are critical to ensuring that private-sector coral financing is successful. Based on follow-up interviews with key decision makers in coral insurance, we discuss considerations for the design and implementation of the policy, as well its present outlook for renewal.
9785a4b8: Evaluating the status of coral reefs is challenged by the different focuses on taxa, environmental changes, and ecological responses. We demonstrate how commonly collected coral and fish community field metrics can be combined into producing a useful single metric of Coral Reef Ecological Integrity (CREI). We used 5 ecological variables (coral cover, coral taxa, coral susceptibility to thermal stress, fish biomass, and fish taxa) and evaluated their associations with 31 environmental variables using an expansive study of 536 sites in 423 x 6.25 km2 mapped reef cells in 6 countries in the Western Indian Ocean (WIO). We found that ~ 26 environmental variables provided good predictions of these coral reef metrics and produced high testing and training predictions (~85%) for CREI. We then compared the CREI metric with common cominations of heat stress, bleaching, and coral cover metrics used to project the future reefs states, referred to as Coral Reef Climate Change Index (CCCI). Both categories of metrics indicated temporal stability in 26 sites over the 2008 and 2021 study period but higher integrity in high compliance protected areas for the CREI index. The CCI index suggests that corals are likely acclimating to heat stress and therefore not reflecting rapids declines in coral cover over this time series. Mapping CREI for the 7700 reefs cells in the WIO indentified high integrity locations notably in Tanzania and eastward to northern Madagascar. Evaluating CREI can help to set priorities for local conservation and fishery interventions and potentially applicable on a global scale to inform monitoring and evaluating the impacts of climate change and management interventions.
6e32abbc: Understanding climate-resilient coral reefs is essential in a warming world, particularly for extracting actionable lessons that can drive broader systems change. These pockets of resilience offer rare opportunities for cumulative learning, yet doing so requires a conceptual foundation that explains not only where resilient reefs occur, but why they persist. This matters because resilience may originate in ecological processes but ultimately unfolds through the social–ecological systems in which reefs are embedded and through their capacity to absorb, adapt, or transform in the face of disturbance. Drawing on a synthesis of more than 70 studies on “bright spots”—cases that outperform expectations despite local constraints—this talk develops a conceptual model for learning from resilience grounded in bright-spot logic. It includes: (1) a typology of methods for systematically identifying exceptional performance; and (2) a framework for understanding enabling conditions and how they might be replicated or scaled elsewhere. The talk concludes with a synthesis of the existing coral-reef bright-spots literature to distill recurring patterns and conditions, providing a pathway for cumulative learning across sites and enabling meaningful comparisons among refugia of reef resistance and recovery. Ultimately, this work lays the groundwork for iteratively advancing our understanding of socio-ecological resilience through the science of success, both in coral reefs and beyond.
f00a26c9: As climate change intensifies, identifying locations of climate-resilient coral reefs and better managing them is critical to sustaining coastal ecosystems and livelihoods.  This study presents a data-driven approach to identifying climate refugia reefs using <3,680 benthic transects collected between 2009 and 2025 from a range of NGO and government agencies in Fiji, stored within the open-source MERMAID platform. Notably, this is the first study in Fiji to apply climate refugia classifications based on coral life history categories and identify sites for a climate refugia strategy according to avoidance, resistance and recovery (ARR) benthic typologies. We use field records to validate and extend climate refugia locations identified in a global prioritisation, focusing on coral cover, diversity and life history categories to assess ecological resilience. We present spatial patterns of ARR reef systems, including sites showing signatures of high resistance or rapid recovery potential. Our findings demonstrate how existing ecological data can be combined with next generation modelling outputs to provide information pathways for national policy development and enable evidence-based adaptation in the Pacific Island context. These analyses provide the first national-scale evidence to guide climate-smart reef protection in Fiji, supporting policy decisions, marine spatial planning, and targeted management of resilient reef areas.
045a8e72:
In April 2024, NOAA Coral Reef Watch confirmed the world remains in the 4th Global Bleaching Event. To assess the impacts of this disturbance and identify potential coral reef refugia in the Philippines, the Wildlife Conservation Society–Philippines collaborated with the University of the Philippines–Marine Environment and Resources Foundation, the De La Salle University–Br. Alfred Shields FSC Ocean Research Center, and the Mindanao State University–College of Technology and Oceanography to conduct Bleaching Surveys using rapid bleaching method (McClanahan et al., 2019) in selected sites across Negros Island, Siquijor, and Tawi-Tawi Province. Field data on bleaching prevalence, coral composition, and reef condition were integrated with NOAA Degree Heating Weeks (DHW) to quantify thermal exposure. To contextualize ecological patterns within local management effectiveness, historical interventions were analyzed to evaluate how governance influences observed resilience indicators.
Results demonstrated clear spatial and temporal variability in bleaching severity across sites. In Negros Occidental, pale and bleached coral colonies decreased by 34.61% between June 2024 and July 2025. In Negros Oriental and Siquijor, pale and bleached colonies recorded at 11.38% and 11.14% respectively in November 2024 and declined to 1.23% and 1.11% respectively by July 2025. In Tawi-Tawi, little to no bleaching was observed in 2025. 
Overall, the study highlights how integrating biophysical assessments with governance analyses to identify and prioritize climate-resilient reef areas, guide conservation planning, strengthen community-based MPA management, and support adaptive strategies amid increasing thermal stress.
08af07cd: Climate change-induced coral bleaching is expected to intensify in the coming years, disproportionately affecting coral reefs worldwide. Efforts to determine potential climate resilient reefs are important to prioritize conservation efforts. While global scale analyses are essential for strategic planning, local scale data are needed to validate these findings as well as to document spatial variation in bleaching response, especially in highly vulnerable regions. This study examines the impact of the 4th global bleaching event that occurred in 2024 on shallow reefs (4-8 m depth) along the southern Negros climate refuge, facing the Sulu Sea (SS, southwestern Negros) and Bohol Sea (BS, southeastern Negros and western Siquijor). Using quadrat-based rapid bleaching method, we surveyed 6 SS and 9 BS reef sites at the onset of bleaching (June 2024), four months after (November 2024), and one year after (July 2025) (SS = 275 quadrats in June, 363 in November, 148 in July; BS = 399, 404, 336). Preliminary analyses indicated substantial spatial and temporal variation in bleaching response. In June 2024, bleaching severity and recently coral mortality were higher at SS reef sites (27.6% - 46.3% of colonies showing signs of bleaching) than at BS reef sites (0.8% - 4.4%). By November, bleaching severity was similar across basins but lower than June at SS reef sites (9.1% - 19.4%) and slightly higher at BS reef sites (7.8% - 14.6%). All reef sites, except for one site in SS, exhibited a decline in hard coral cover (SS: from an average of 42% to 25%; BS: from 48% to 32%). By July-August 2025, hard coral cover remained low at SS reef sites but showed slight recovery at BS reef sites. This study is among the first to provide reef-scale quantitative insights to a potential climate change refuge for coral reefs in the Philippines.
0e2f045b:
Climate change threatens the persistence of coral reef ecosystems worldwide, yet some reefs retain the capacity to survive future warming. We conducted a global analysis to map the distribution of climate-resilient coral reefs and identified 167,000 km² of high-priority habitat across 83 countries. This work expands the original “50 Reefs” climate-refugia assessment from 42 to 83 countries, revealing new opportunities for strategic protection and investment. Five countries—the Bahamas, Cuba, Australia, Indonesia, and the Philippines—contain more than half of all identified resilient reef areas, and because the analysis focuses on shallow reefs, all priority sites fall within national jurisdictions.

To identify these areas, we combined almost 50,000 field observations from 1960-2024 with 42 environmental predictors to train machine-learning model ensembles to predict coral cover and life history types across 8.7 million reef pixels at 250 m resolution. First, we show that losses in coral cover are widespread but highly heterogeneous across space and time, with almost 50% (+/- 12% sd) of reef pixels losing cover from 1960 to 2020, with a corresponding global drop in average coral cover from 24% to 20% (+/- 4% sd). Second, we identify multiple refugia types—avoidance, resistance, and recovery—linked to competitive, stress-tolerant and weedy life histories, respectively, providing a high-resolution global blueprint for guiding national action and climate-smart conservation funding for global coral reefs. Our results show that climate-resilient coral futures remain possible, and governments can safeguard them by aligning national plans, investments, and 30×30 commitments with these priority areas.
98944d5f: Peak global warming is currently projected to reach upper limits of 2.7–4.5 °C above pre-industrial levels. Even at today’s warming of 0.8–1.2 °C, the frequency and intensity of global mass coral bleaching events (most recently 2014–2017 and 2023–2025) is accelerating. The Great Barrier Reef (GBR), as the world’s largest coral ecosystem, is experiencing increasingly severe and recurrent heat stress, culminating in the hottest event on record in 2024, causing widespread mass coral bleaching events six times in the last 10 years.
