This presentation synthesises large-scale aerial assessments, in-water monitoring, and colony-level severity observations to describe how rising heat exposure has reshaped reef condition across all regions of the GBR from 2016 to 2024. We examine how both the intensity and duration of thermal anomalies, combined with the timing of peak temperatures, influence bleaching prevalence and the distribution of severity categories at colony, site, and regional scales. The 2024 event stands out not only due to record SST anomalies but also because very high and extreme levels of coral bleaching occurred across the widest spatial latitudinal range to date within the Northern, Central, and Southern management regions of the GBR for the first time during a single summer. Integrating colony-level responses with spatial heat-stress metrics demonstrates how bleaching severity predicts risks of mortality, and subsequent declines in coral cover across diverse habitats and depths. Coral mortality was strongly linked to bleaching severity and the intensity and duration of heat stress, with declines in coral cover ranging from −20% to −60% at reefs surveyed repeatedly across all three regions of the GBR.  This broad overview of the event will provide the ecological and thermal context across the Great Barrier Reef region during the unprecedented global marine heatwave from 2023-2025.
9b1bebd9: Despite recent reports of record-high hard coral cover on the Great Barrier Reef (GBR), the summer of 2024 saw the fifth mass coral bleaching event since 2016, part of the fourth global mass bleaching event. This event exposed some parts of the GBR to unprecedented levels of heat stress, and the broadest spatial footprint of bleaching recorded to date. We document substantial coral mortality directly linked to the severity of thermal stress and bleaching observed at the peak of the event. Coral loss ranged from 4% to 72% of pre-bleaching levels among individual reefs, with the northern and central GBR most severely affected. Many losses represent the most severe single-event declines in coral cover since monitoring began and highlight the impacts of increasing frequency and severity of climate-driven disturbances. These findings reinforce the urgent need for global emissions reductions to limit warming to 1.5–2°C and preserve coral reef ecosystems.
23701132: In early 2024 the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) experienced its sixth mass bleaching event along its 2,300 km length. Bleaching was severe in the southern region including in one of the most protected reef systems on the GBR, One Tree Reef. This platform reef located 80 km off the Australian mainland has had maximum protection (Scientific Zone) for 40 years. Heatwave conditions triggered bleaching across diverse coral species and a disease epizootic. We tracked 462 individual coral colonies in health assessments from bleaching onset in February 2024 through winter to the following summer in seven surveys over one year. In February and April, 66% and 80% of the tracked colonies were bleached, respectively. By May, 43% of these colonies were dead indicating that many colonies succumbed quickly. Collapse of reef structure was evident in Acropora and Seriatopora which had 100% mortality by October and degraded to algal covered rubble. For massive, long-lived Goniopora, bleaching coincided with a black band disease (BBD) epizootic. This necrotic wasting disease, typically rare on the GBR, appeared along the edge of bleached polyps. Of the 112 tracked Goniopora colonies 75% were bleached in February 2024 and 4% of these had BBD. By April, 61% of bleached colonies had an aggressive black band invading the tissue and 75% of the colonies were dead by October. The other 25% of Goniopora colonies recovered but with extensive partial mortality. Porities also bleached with a ~ 50% recovery by one year. With in-water monitoring of individual colonies we show that rapid bleaching, disease onset, high mortality and the transition to rubble were unprecedented. Understanding the connection between bleaching and disease is important as corals are faced with unprecedented thermal anomalies. The inhospitable conditions and rapid pace in coral health decline has not been previously reported in this region.
131374d4:
Prolonged, anomalously warm sea surface temperatures (SST) surrounding the austral summer 2024- 2025 triggered widespread and severe coral bleaching across most of Western Australia’s (WA) coral reefs, including the remote and ecologically significant oceanic reefs of Ashmore Reef, Scott Reef, and the Rowley Shoals. Heat stress exceeded 12 Degree Heating Weeks (DHW), a threshold typically associated with widespread bleaching and mortality across most coral groups.
These reefs have distinct heat stress histories, some having experienced multiple bleaching events over the last three decades, while the Rowley Shoals has, until the recent event, largely escaped the impacts of marine heatwaves. This raises questions about resilience, recovery, and adaptation under accelerating climate change. We summarise long-term community and demographic trends, integrated with complementary ecological and physical information to provide context to bleaching observations following peak heat stress in March and April 2025 and compare to previous bleaching events.  
This was the worst bleaching event on record for the Rowley Shoals, causing >75% mortality. In contrast, more moderate impacts were recorded for Ashmore and Scott Reefs, suggesting their previous exposure to severe bleaching may have afforded them some resilience. These trends provide valuable insights into community trajectories critical to informing management strategies aimed at improving resilience of reefs under future climate scenarios.
91adacb7: Coral reef ecosystems are being subjected to increasingly frequent and severe bleaching events. This results in shorter recovery periods between bleaching events. In 1980, it was estimated that severe mass bleaching happened approximately every 27 years globally, but by 2016 the frequency appeared to have increased to every 6 years. Diminished recovery periods can lead to reduced growth, reproductive output, recruitment success, and reef flattening. This can stem from the loss of branching and corymbose corals such as acroporids and pocilloporids. The resulting loss of structural complexity can cause habitat loss for reef species and increased macroalgae proliferation, thereby driving phase shifts. Despite our knowledge of the ecological importance of reef structural complexity, we do not fully understand how its components are affected by repeated bleaching events across different reef communities. Here, we use the agent-based model CoralPatchSim to investigate how increasing bleaching intervals affect reef structural complexity. NOAA monitoring data from three sites in the Indo-Pacific were analyzed (Rose Atoll, Fagamalo and Fugaal’u) using four coral metrics as proxies for structural complexity: percentage hard coral bottom cover, coral surface area, amount of calcium carbonate in live corals and fish refuge volume. For each site, 30 years of reef growth was simulated across four scenarios: no bleaching events, and bleaching events every 10 years, 5 years, and 2 years. Increasing bleaching frequency resulted in diminished structural complexity metrics. It was observed that consecutive bleaching events produced progressively smaller reductions in complexity metrics, indicating community-level adaptation. These adaptations were dominated by weedy, stress-resistant corals, suggesting that even if reefs can adapt to increasing thermal stress, they will do so with lower complexity. As bleaching increases, we must find ways to maintain not only coral cover but also structurally complex sets of corals that support habitat provisioning, fisheries, and tourism.
349984f0: Standing genetic variation in fitness-related traits is critical to determine how fast populations can adapt to climate warming but is unknown for many species. Here, we used a quantitative genetics approach to evaluate heritable genetic variation in thermal tolerance across the Indo-Pacific distribution of a common reef-building coral. A total of 360 familial crosses of Platygyra daedalea were bred from 105 parental corals from 10 reef populations and 6 ocean regions. We tracked the survival of more than 56,000 larvae from these crosses under local ambient and a standardized elevated temperature known to induce mortality. Overall coral larval survival declined under heat stress but varied widely among individual crosses within all populations, typically ranging from less than 15% to more than 90%. Additive genetic effects contributed up to half of this variation in heat survival, where narrow-sense heritability (h2) ranged from 0.19 to 0.49. Differences in the amount of additive genetic variance (VA) for heat survival among populations were explained by historical heatwave conditions that induce coral bleaching. VA declined by 13% for every 0.01 increase in the frequency of moderate heatwaves (>4 DHWs) since 1985 (R2 = 0.90), consistent with directional selection on thermal tolerance traits. Our findings suggest that coral populations may be adapting to warming consistent with recent increases in their upper thermal limits.
329cca96: Persistence of reef-building coral populations hinges on adaptation to rising sea temperatures through natural selection acting on heritable variation in heat tolerance. Yet, the discovery of genetic variants associated with heat tolerance and genomic predictions of adaptive potential remain in early stages, partly due to limited large-scale genotypic and phenotypic datasets. Their application to conservation and restoration is also limited by uncertainties regarding the effect of candidate genetic variants on coral fitness in the wild, the consistency of heat tolerance genomic architecture under varying duration and intensities of thermal stress, and the degree to which heat adaptation is repeatable across populations and species. To address these gaps, we used a comprehensive whole genome sequencing dataset from ~1,000 Acropora spathulata colonies across 29 Western Pacific reefs to conduct genome-wide association analyses of heat tolerance phenotypes and thermal environments. Genetic variants associated with long-term climatic conditions indicate largely non-parallel polygenic adaptation across distant populations, but overlapping signals point to key known and novel genes under repeated selection by temperature. Several of these variants had measurable effects on coral bleaching and mortality in the wild, and collectively improved predictions of these outcomes, suggesting that genome-environment associations successfully detected adaptive variants. Association studies revealed that tolerance to acute heat stress involves distinct genetic variants and genes across populations, but similar molecular pathways. Importantly, we found little overlap between genomic regions underlying experimental acute heat tolerance, and in-situ tolerance to marine heatwaves, indicating that the genetic architecture of heat tolerance may vary across thermal exposures. Overall, our results emphasize the complex interplay between genetic architectures and types of heat stress in shaping selection under climate warming across populations, and highlight the value of integrating multiple approaches and taxa to identify genomic markers with broad relevance for coral management under climate warming.
0ed985c4: Coral reefs worldwide are increasingly subjected to climate change-induced threats such as marine heatwaves and extreme storms. These pressures are increasing in frequency and intensity. Understanding how they shape the genetic composition of natural coral populations could help us predict how coral species adapt to change. We analysed the rich whole-genome sequencing data of the Pachyseris speciosa species complex (n=318). Our data encompassed two out of the four distinct lineages, sampled across multiple sites in Indonesia, Philippines, and Great Barrier Reef. Specifically, we investigated the genomic differences between populations exposed to varying typhoon strengths and recent marine heatwaves by integrating outlier detection methods, and explicitly linking genetic and environmental data. This approach allowed us to identify genomic regions showing signatures of selection potentially driven by these environmental pressures. We verified signatures across the different lineages of P. speciosa. The patterns observed across lineages suggest that both heat stress and mechanical disturbance each impose selective forces on corals, influencing traits related to stress tolerance and recovery capacity. Overall, we show that Pachyseris populations harbor alleles that contribute to resilience against thermal anomalies and typhoon disturbance. Identifying these genomic regions enhances our understanding of coral adaptive potential and provides a foundation for conserving climate-resilient reef populations.
59b21c72: Coral reefs are biologically rich and culturally significant ecosystems that are facing increasing degradation from ocean warming, pollution, and coastal development.  Using seascape genomics, I investigated how environmental gradients and genetic variation interact to shape the adaptive potential of three common reef-building coral species (Montipora capitata, Pocillopora acuta, Porites compressa) around O‘ahu, Hawai‘i. 640 coral samples were collected from 194 sites across O‘ahu using a stratified random design to capture environmental heterogeneity and assess environmental drivers of coral population structure.  Coral genomic DNA was processed using reduced representation genomic sequencing (RAD-seq) to generate high-density SNP data, and Symbiodiniaceae community structure was characterized using ITS2 metabarcoding. Distance-based RDA (dbRDA) was then used to assess the influence of eight environmental factors (sea-surface temperature, wave action, sedimentation, nutrient effluent, irradiance, primary production, reef complexity & coastal development) on coral holobiont population structure. Significant genotype-environment associations (GEAs) were identified using the SAMβADA pipeline, and outlier loci associated with GEAs were annotated and mapped spatially to locate potential hotspots of adaptive capacity. Overall, this approach seeks to identify individuals and populations with putative tolerance to specific environmental stressors. By integrating genetic, symbiont, and environmental data, this project provides the most spatially extensive seascape genomics assessment in Hawai‘i to date. The results will offer critical, spatially explicit data to support coral reef ecosystem management and conservation strategies that prioritize resilient coral populations and adaptive genetic diversity in the face of climate change.
e2ebf08b: Acroporid branching corals, once dominant reef-builders across Florida’s Coral Reef (FCR), have suffered dramatic declines in recent decades. Following the 2023 marine heatwave, severe losses of these branching species have rendered them functionally extinct in Florida. Coral restoration efforts in Florida have historically focused on asexual fragmentation of acroporids. However, the recent incorporation of ex situ sexual propagation into restoration pipelines has led to innovative methods intended to increase coral survival and resilience, including selective breeding, stress hardening, and genetic rescue. Because research is still needed to inform genetic management of these new practices, this project experimentally tested the influence of maternal genetic lineage on outplanted Acropora palmata sexual recruit fitness. Recruits from three batch crosses with single, unique egg donors were outplanted at three locations representing distinct ecological regions along FCR: Broward County, the upper Florida Keys, and the middle Florida Keys. Outplant survivorship, condition, and growth were monitored for one year and compared across locations and genetic lineages to assess differential fitness. Tissue samples were taken before outplanting and at one month and six months post-outplanting to determine if symbiont shuffling occurred after acclimating to in situ conditions or in response to thermal stress and if these shifts were influenced by genetic lineage or outplant location. Initial outplant survival was high, with both outplant location and genetic lineage influencing survival. Genetic lineage also appeared to influence resistance to and recovery from thermal stress. Therefore, genetic factors may be important components of breeding strategies and outplanting designs aimed at increasing the survivorship and resilience of sexual recruits.
c17a3430: Trace metals remain among the most persistent contaminants in marine ecosystems, yet their bioaccumulation dynamics across different reef matrices remain poorly resolved in the Red Sea. The region is undergoing rapid coastal development to support the expansion of the economy, while baseline contaminant monitoring remains limited. Understanding how metals transfer from seawater and sediments into key reef organisms along the foodwebs, from primary producers to higher trophic levels, is therefore essential for evaluating ecosystem health and exposure risks. Here, we quantified concentrations, bioaccumulation potential, and trophic transfer of ten trace metals (Cr, Mn, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, As, Sr, Cd and Pb) across five matrices: seawater, sediments, macroalgae (Dictyota cervicornis), corals (Pocillopora favosa), and  six reef fish species spanning multiple trophic positions (Siganus luridus - herbivorous, Acanthurus gahhm - herbivore/detrivore, Chaetodon austriacus - corallivore, Chaetodon larvatus - corallivore,  Parupeneus barberinus - invertivore, and Epinephelus malabaricus - carnivorous) from central Red Sea reefs. Across all six fish species, including the commercial species E. malabaricus, Zn and As consistently exhibited bioaccumulative potential (log BAF > 3.3; in some cases surpassing the bioaccumulation threshold > 3.6). Arsenic concentrations exceeded FAO guideline levels (> 2 mg kg⁻¹) in every fish species examined, underscoring its ecological and food-safety relevance. Corals and macroalgae displayed distinct accumulation profiles: Pb and Cu were bioaccumulative in corals, whereas Mn, Co, Zn, and As accumulated preferentially in macroalgae, reflecting taxon-specific uptake mechanisms. Preliminary trophic-level analyses indicate evidence of biodilution pattern in some metals. The widespread bioaccumulation of multiple trace metals across ecologically and commercially important species underscores the need for broad, systematic, and consistent  monitoring programs in the Red Sea. These findings provide an integrated ecological baseline of trace metal transfer across reef matrices in the Red Sea and highlight emerging contaminant risks as coastal development accelerates.
53b1094c:
The majority of Florida’s Coral Reef (FCR) is located close to shore and more vulnerable to runoff and pollution than offshore reefs. These local stressors can negatively impact coral’s resilience and ability to recover from periodic stress events (i.e. increased temperatures), as well as reducing the capacity for adaptation to increasing climate change stress.  Due to the urbanization of SE Florida, FCR is subjected to many sources of pollution including a range of chemical contaminants, such as antifouling compounds, hydrocarbons, trace metals, pesticides, herbicides, plastics, personal care products, cleaning products, and general waste from the large urban population. These pollutants can impact coral growth, photosynthesis, metabolism, reproduction and survival.  Previous research identified copper, arsenic, and nickel as emerging dissolved contaminants of concern in the water column , with several others found in concentrations above limits recommended by the FDEP  and EPA.  Additionally, high concentrations of numerous chemicals were found in sediments but there is a lack of data examining the effects of individual contaminants in sediment to corals.  In order to mitigate the effects of these contaminants, it is crucial to understand how these contaminants move through the watershed from their terrestrial source(s) to the reef.  
Water and sediment quality data were collected from several databases including the Water Quality Portal from the National Water Quality Monitoring council, NOAA National Status and Trends Data, and the South Florida Water Management District’s DBHydro database. Contaminants with concentrations above suggested thresholds from the FDEP and EPA were identified as contaminants of concern and specific locations (i.e. specific inlets or canals) were identified as areas of concern.  This data will allow us to model the flow of contaminants from source to sea and help to inform the concentration and duration of exposure that corals receive to assist in environmental impact assessments.
81b65c39: Both natural and human activities introduce heavy metals into the marine environment, pollute coral reefs, and can be toxic to marine organisms. While most studies of heavy metals have focused on corals from tropical regions, some of the most polluted areas are inhabited by understudied temperate coral species. We quantified the concentration of fourteen elements, in the skeleton of Astrangia poculata from Jamestown, RI collected in the fall, winter, spring, and summer seasons of 2018-19. Seasonal variation was observed in most elements and concentrations of Fe/Ca, Mn/Ca, Zn/Ca, Ni/Ca, Ba/Ca, Cu/Ca, As/Ca, Cr/Ca, and Pb/Ca gradually increased from fall to summer. This trend may indicate bioaccumulation of these metals into the coral skeleton through the year, with corresponding removal of some metals (Zn, As, Br, and Pb) from coral tissue. Significant correlation among Cu, Pb, and Ni indicate pollution associated with landfills and mining sites, while Zn and Ni are commonly introduced from wastewater. Marine-based pollution from oil spills (Pb and Ni) and antifouling paint from ship hulls (Cu and Zn) may also contribute to concentrations in the skeleton. We found that Ba, Fe, and Zn were at least 140% higher and Ni was at least 500% higher in the skeleton of A. poculata compared to other species from tropical reefs. A. poculata may have a higher tolerance for metal pollution, making it a potential indicator of pollution in the environment.
3cf759f0:
The concurrent action of climate change and the accumulation of anthropogenic pollutants represent a major threat to coastal marine environments. In this context, the effects on scleractinian corals are particularly relevant as they sustain marine biodiversity. Corals, as long-living benthic organisms, are capable of bioaccumulating heavy metals in response to environmental factors and anthropogenic impacts. Their skeletons incorporate these metals by absorbing those dissolved in seawater, which are then retained indefinitely as they become encapsulated within the overlying carbonate formed during new skeletal growth. This characteristic makes corals valuable sentinels for monitoring environmental contamination and its potential ecological consequences. Most studies quantifying trace-element concentrations in scleractinian corals have been conducted in the Red Sea, the Persian Gulf, and coastal regions of Australia; however, analogous studies in the Mediterranean Sea are scarce.   
This study explores trace element accumulation in Mediterranean scleractinian corals, using the zooxanthellate solitary coral Balanophyllia europaea as a reporter species. Populations from eight sites along the Italian coastline, encompassing a gradient from heavily impacted to relatively pristine conditions, were sampled to assess the status of this species across the Mediterranean Sea. Coral samples were separated into three biological compartments, host tissue, symbiotic algae, and skeleton, to characterize the compartment-specific distribution of trace elements. Concentrations of Al, As, Cd, Cr, Cu, Fe, K, Mg, Mn, Na, Ni, Pb, Se, Sn, and Zn were measured using Inductively Coupled Plasma - Optical Emission Spectrometry (ICP-OES). Intra and inter-population comparisons provide new insights into differential metal accumulation patterns among coral compartments and highlight the potential of B. europaea as a bioindicator of heavy metal pollution in the Mediterranean Sea.
cbd37485:
Ultraviolet (UV) filters are a diverse group of chemicals with a wide range of commercial applications, including sunscreens, and have been found in coastal locations. Despite the potential for recreational source inputs to vary over the day, typically monitoring programs only analyze one or a few discrete samples and do not capture this temporal variation. To investigate this, three composite samples were collected along two 100m transects  at different distances from the shoreline (T1; nearshore bathing zone and T2 ~50m from the shoreline) at multiple times over a 24 hour period, in March and in May. Samples were processed by filtering (GFF) and solid phase extraction to prepare the ‘dissolved fractions’. The GFF filters were extracted via hexane to prepare the ’particulate’ fractions. All extracts were analyzed for 12 organic UV filters using LC-MS/MS.

Eight UV filters were measured in all samples, with higher levels in May and at T1 reflecting the number of recreational users. UV filters and the septic-tracer caffeine followed similar temporal trends with peak concentrations at mid-afternoon. Higher concentrations of octocrylene were observed in the ‘particulate’ phase reflective of its lower solubility and higher logKow, whereas, oxybenzone concentrations were nearly all in the ‘dissolved’ phase matching their physio-chemical properties. Concentrations of lower solubility UV filters were not as different between March and May compared to oxybenzone suggesting saturation. 

Overall, these results indicate that aquatic organisms in popular recreational areas may encounter UV filter concentrations that are highly variable over a 24-hour period rather than a steady environmental exposure, and that concentrations rapidly decline in the bathing zone after people leave. These results highlight that both location and time are important in environmental risk assessments for UV filters. Furthermore, to understand total concentrations and exposure routes it is important to conduct assessment of partitioning by filtering samples.
4b200719: Anthropogenic activities pose a significant risk to vulnerable marine ecosystems like coral reefs, particularly those near human population centers. Despite coral reefs being one of the systems most at risk, the relationship between contaminant exposure and coral health across gradients of ecosystem disturbance remains poorly understood. Our metabolomic analysis of 380 corals from 16 sites spanning an anthropogenic impact gradient revealed that the coral metabolome is predictive of anthropogenic ecosystem disturbance. We found that human activities both within catchments and in the marine ecosystem altered the chemodiversity of coral metabolomes with a direct relationship to historic trends in coral cover: nitrogen reserves and metabolome energetic potential were reduced, while stress metabolites were enriched at more impacted sites. We further identified 25 anthropogenic contaminants from agricultural, cosmetological, industrial, and pharmaceutical sources accumulated in coral metabolomes. Our results suggest that the bioaccumulation of pollutants in corals impacts all but the most remote reef ecosystems, which will disproportionately threaten communities reliant on reefs. Together, our findings demonstrate a direct relationship between coral health, accumulation of dangerous contaminants within corals, and anthropogenic disturbance that is consistent across species.
68b0a621: Pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs) are emerging contaminants characterized by their pseudo-persistence at low concentrations and potential toxicity to marine organisms. While PPCPs have been widely documented globally, their presence and biological impacts in the Red Sea remain understudied, despite increasing contamination risks associated with rapid coastal and aquaculture development under Saudi Arabia’s Vision 2030. Sponges are proposed bioindicators and bioremediators of pollution due to their high filtration and retention capacity, yet their ability to accumulate and withstand chronic exposure to PPCPs is not well understood. The common Red Sea sponge Stylissa carteri provides an ideal model for evaluating PPCP dynamics. This study examines the distribution and effects of targeted PPCPs in S. carteri through (1) field collections of seawater and sponge tissue along a gradient from an aquaculture effluent site in the central Red Sea, and (2) a 14-day laboratory exposure to environmentally relevant concentrations of ciprofloxacin. Field surveys revealed widespread, low-level PPCP contamination in seawater, dominated by caffeine and paracetamol. Paracetamol accumulated disproportionately in sponges, whereas caffeine, ciprofloxacin, fluoxetine, and carbamazepine exhibited low or inconsistent uptake. Antioxidant activity and lipid peroxidation were highest at the nearshore and offshore reefs, respectively. Interpretations of biochemical responses are limited by confounding environmental factors. Under experimental conditions, no significant physiological or biochemical responses were observed, suggesting S. carteri tolerated environmentally relevant concentrations of ciprofloxacin. Lipid peroxidation only increased in experimental groups relative to pre-exposure groups, likely reflecting handling stress. Overall, S. carteri displays compound-specific uptake and biological resistance, indicating potential adaptation to chronic low-level contamination. Despite its high uptake of paracetamol, S. carteri may not be a robust bioindicator of PPCP pollution. This research provides baseline knowledge on chemical contamination in Red Sea reefs, demonstrates the need for multi-species and multi-endpoint monitoring, and highlights the potential of sponges as bioremediation tools.
f25ccd30: Coral reefs, globally recognized for their biodiversity and ecosystem services, are increasingly threatened by local, regional, and global stressors, particularly in coastal areas where degradation is accelerating. Among emerging threats is the pollution by contaminants of emerging concern (CECs), including pharmaceuticals and personal care products, which persist despite conventional wastewater treatment. This study assesses the occurrence, distribution, and bioaccumulation of 10 selected CECs in the coral Pocillopora favosa across 15 reefs in three central Red Sea regions (Thuwal, Jeddah, and Al Lith), representing a gradient of human pressure. Using SPE and HPLC-MS, CECs, including antibiotics (e.g., Ciprofloxacin), stimulants (e.g., Caffeine), bronchodilators (e.g., Salbutamol), and pesticides (e.g., Atrazine), were quantified in both coral tissue and seawater. Most CECs were detected in both matrices, with salbutamol and atrazine showing the highest detection frequencies. Bioaccumulation factors (BAFs) indicated that most of the selected CECs, except for caffeine, tramadol, and ranitidine, were bioaccumulated (log BAF > 3.7). Contrary to expectations, some contaminants showed higher concentrations in coral tissues in mid/offshore than in nearshore reefs, suggesting complex dynamics in contaminant transport and coral uptake. Regional patterns varied by CEC category; for example, antibiotic prevalence in Al Lith may be linked to intensive aquaculture. Our findings highlight the widespread presence and bioaccumulation of CECs in Red Sea coral reefs, emphasizing the need for region-specific environmental monitoring and tailored regulatory measures to mitigate local pollution pressures, particularly as coral reefs face increasing vulnerability from climate change.
6212b40a: Coral reefs and the ecosystem services they provide are at risk in the Anthropocene. Investment in coral restoration is thus becoming increasingly important to safeguard their future. Despite the substantial resources needed for restoration efforts, the long-term benefits to humans are only expected to accrue decades after the intervention. Systems models of intervention outcomes that are compatible with economic valuation are thus crucial for decision making and maximizing expected benefits. In this paper, we develop a mechanistic, spatially explicit model to project restoration outcomes in terms of hard coral cover, which is based on recruitment, intrinsic growth, and mortality from stochastic disturbances. Using this model, we develop a site selection tool that is compatible with tools of economic analysis, such as benefit-cost analysis and cost-effectiveness, and assess the trade-offs of passive and active restoration at the reef site level in the Philippines. Conducting a Value of Information sensitivity test, we highlight the need for regular and long-term monitoring of coral reefs in the Philippines.
93f21db7: The science and practice of coral reef restoration is rapidly developing, yet guidelines on governance and long-term management of restored reefs are limited, particularly in developing countries like the Philippines. Current guidelines for managing restored reefs often overlook governance structures and policies, risking inefficiencies and wasted financial resources. Thus, ensuring effective management is crucial for sustaining restoration efforts. The Philippines has a decentralized governance system that grants local governments the autonomy and responsibility to manage coastal areas within 15 km of the coastline. While various stakeholders have implemented coral reef restoration projects, information on how the restored reefs are managed and sustained is limited, and no national policy provides clear governance guidelines. This study documents lessons from three municipalities, Lobo (Batangas), San Vicente (Palawan), and Cordova (Cebu), where we facilitated the integrated management of restored reef areas into local management plans. Based on our experiences, we identified key governance challenges, such as unclear permitting processes, limited resources, and weak enforcement mechanisms. To address these issues, we propose a management framework to regulate human activities in restored reefs by integrating these into local government management structures and plans. The framework emphasizes stakeholder engagement, adaptive management, long-term monitoring, funding strategies, and policy recommendations to streamline permitting and enhance coordination between national agencies and local governments. Strengthening governance mechanisms is essential for sustaining restoration success and improving reef resilience. Our findings offer insights for enhancing coral reef restoration management in the Philippines and similar contexts, underscoring the need for stronger policy support, stakeholder engagement, and sustainable governance strategies.
41a40f1d: Adaptive management and resilience-based design in coral restoration requires monitoring, reporting, and evaluating coral restoration outcomes. Globally, the Philippines is among the leading countries in coral restoration research but evaluation of restored sites is seldom done due to lack of standard protocols in the past. So we investigated aspects of monitoring, reporting, and evaluation of coral restoration outcomes in the Philippines at national and local scales by 1) synthesizing extracted data from peer-reviewed and non-peer-reviewed reports, and 2) evaluating select publications using an Evaluation Tool developed by the Coral Restoration Consortium in 2020. We collected data in 2023 on the scale of restoration, coral survival, and live tissue cover for both review and evaluation. Then we used empirical and published data accompanied by long-term temperature in evaluating select publications. A total of 128 reports from primary, grey, and online sources published from 1982 to 2023 were included. Coral growth and survival dominated among 27 monitored outcomes and the median percent survival was calculated at 63%. However, during evaluation of select publications, all four coral species having different life histories experienced partial mortality and decreased survival >6 years post-outplanting. This may be linked to increased frequency of thermal anomalies recorded from 2005 to 2023 in select restoration sites, as heat-stress was also among the most reported causes of coral mortality in our review. Furthermore, results showed that long-term monitoring with community stakeholders, reporting of live tissue cover, and documentation of changes in restoration scale are seldom conducted. Moreover, 41% of the total reports did not monitor outcomes at all. The identified causes of restored coral mortality from published and empirical data can help guide future coral restoration planning. This will enable restoration interventions to adopt measurable goals and improve effectiveness  through adaptive management strategies like selecting optimal outplanting sites and season.
8cafb653:
As coral restoration expands globally, practitioners face growing need to ensure meaningful ecosystem recovery rather than short-term project outputs. Yet monitoring frameworks remain inconsistent, and commonly used indicators may not adequately capture long-term recovery trajectories. This study evaluates whether current monitoring practices measure indicators which can assess ecosystem-level progress, with a focus on improving approaches relevant to practitioner-led projects in the Coral Triangle.

A systematic review of peer-reviewed and grey literature is combined with interviews and surveys from restoration practitioners, NGOs, and community groups. This mixed-method approach captures a comprehensive set of monitoring practices that are often absent from academic publications, especially for regions where restoration is widespread but under-reported. 

This study provides a pathway toward a standardized, capacity-appropriate monitoring framework inclusive of practitioner-led efforts. By identifying essential ecological indicators and proposing criteria for selecting effective reference sites, the study supports the development of trajectory-based monitoring systems that are scientifically robust yet feasible for multi-stakeholder implementation. Strengthening these foundations is essential for ensuring restoration contributes genuinely to long-term reef resilience across the Coral Triangle and beyond.
3ad0ae8d: Coral reef restoration has been increasingly used to rehabilitate degraded reefs in the Philippines, yet there is no national standardised system to track, evaluate, and learn from these projects. We updated and refined an existing catalog of restoration efforts to make it accessible to different stakeholders and to support governance integration. We then created a set of adaptive management criteria, based on the Conservation Standards and the environmental governance literature, to evaluate restoration projects in the database. Our analysis revealed three key gaps: 1) project objectives for restoration did not fully align with broader threat contexts; 2) permitting frameworks lagged behind the emerging science of restoration; and,  3) database entries showed limited completeness for Monitoring, Evaluation, and Learning (MEL) reporting. Given these gaps, we recommend pathways for the database and the adaptive management criteria to contribute to the development of a MEL Framework for coral restoration to strengthen regulation, improve documentation, and guide policy implementation. This initiative aims to support the Philippine government in systematically tracking coral restoration projects, ensuring compliance with best practices and advancing national reporting towards Target 2 of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (Restore 30% of degraded ecosystems by 2030). By bridging data gaps, standardising permitting processes, and linking these to monitoring, this effort will contribute to more effective, transparent and science-driven coral restoration in the country.
8a0a3d93:
Community-led coral reef restoration is expanding across the Coral Triangle yet assessing ecological and socio-economic outcomes remains difficult. We share insights from a 10-year coral restoration program on a small island near Seraya Besar, Flores (Indonesia). The initiative started in response to severe dynamite fishing that destroyed reef structure and contributed to a challenged access in fish resources for the local population. The project was co-designed with the fishing village and has transitioned to local leadership local fisher composing our restoration field team. Ecological outcomes include approximately 83,000 coral fragments transplanted on about 700 artificial reef tables; rebuilding substrate lost to blast damage. Mean coral survival across cohorts is 79%. Fish abundance indicators show strong local recovery, with fish catch round restoration structures six times higher in 2019 than pre-restoration baselines.
We focus on two intertwined challenges. First, implementing restoration in a remote, livelihood-dependent setting requires constant negotiation among ecological goals, logistics, and fishing needs. Second, sustaining scientifically robust monitoring with non-scientist community observers over long-time horizons is difficult. Protocols necessarily evolved as capacity, funding, and field realities changed, creating gaps and inconsistencies that complicate longitudinal interpretation. We present the compromises made between long-term, high-resolution monitoring and simpler methods that community monitors can reliably apply while still generating consistent data on coral survival and restoration efficiency. To reduce observer bias and improve comparability, we used photographic monitoring with image-based coral growth analyses, complemented by periodic video transects for fish population monitoring. We also maintained continuous collaboration with fishers through local fishing logs and co-management measures leading to an official regional protected in 2019.
Our experience highlights both the promise and the practical limits of participatory monitoring for long-term restoration and offers recommendations for inclusive evaluation frameworks that remain rigorous, feasible, and community-owned across the Coral Triangle.
653f59d9: The coral-killing sponge Terpios hoshinota was first recorded in the Banda Islands (Mollucas, Indonesia) in 2023, and has since increased in spatial coverage and number of affected sites. Terpios hoshinota is a fast-growing, highly encrusting sponge. We found 48% of the sponge to grow on hard corals and 52% on non-living hard substrates. Based on local observations and emerging research, several factors are hypothesised to drive outbreaks. The most likely cause is elevated nutrient input from land-based sources, including residential wastewater discharge, and poorly managed nearshore aquaculture. Addressing these causes is essential to prevent further loss of coral cover, maintain ecosystem services, and protect the tourism and fisheries economy of Banda. Given the ecological and socioeconomic importance of these reefs, timely and collaborative action is required to prevent long-term shifts to degraded, sponge-dominated reefs. Here, we report monitoring, impact and innovative pilot-research results obtained from two years of work aimed at combating T. hoshinota. We also discuss our recent collaborative initiative with the ‘Banda Sea Marine Park’ management on prevention, local capacity building, restoration, and community engagement to combat T. hoshinota outbreaks. Multi-stakeholder collaborative approaches strengthen long-term management effectiveness. Innovative rehabilitation of affected reefs using coral larvae seeding supports ecosystem recovery, enhances coral genetic diversity, and provides long-term improvements to habitat structure. The techniques developed are expected to be integrated into the Marine Park’s standard operating procedures and shared with other MPAs through our networks in Indonesia.
c638b3bf: Coral reefs in Tebok Batang, a nearby estuary of Bontang Regency, have a vital role in the threatened ecosystem due to non-environmental-friendly fishing practices and a global threat in the form of coral bleaching. In response to these conditions, PT Pupuk Kalimantan Timur and local communities joined together to support damaged coral reef recovery through community-based restoration by developing and maintaining artificial reef modules. The private sector and the community work together to design, develop, select a site, maintain, and monitor. This study will show and share the restoration efficacy in Tebok Batang, through coral growth informed by colony diameter. Data were collected by purpose sampling in eleven clusters, each comprising three modules: pyramid, cube, and dome. The result shows that coral had been found attached to all fourteen clusters. Clusters deployed in 2011 have the highest average colony diameter among all clusters and modules, 32.54 cm (SE±4,83), in the form of a pyramid module. While the cube module was found highest in clusters deployed in 2017,  16.27 cm (SE±3,08), and the dome module was also found highest in the same cluster (2017), 24.84 cm (SE±3,60). All these clusters have 500 units of the modules, with a pyramid that has a polygonal base and flat triangular faces, 60x60x40cm, while the Dome module is curved, 40x60cm, and the Cube has a length of 60x60x60cm. All these cluster groups have provided new habitats in shallow waters, indicating that the cluster has a good position and good material composition that can trigger coral juveniles naturally to attach, settle, and grow, which may become a unique habitat for some targeted reef fish. This study showed that the location and these three modules were proven effective for rehabilitating methods in damaged coral reefs.
8ee3382a: Sexual reproduction, the primary process for generating new genetic lineages, is fundamental to our understanding of biodiversity, biogeography and evolution at large. One of the most common methods of reproduction in the sea, is broadcast spawning – the synchronized shedding of gametes into the surrounding water where fertilization occurs externally. Consequently, for fertilization to occure, spawning events must be tightly timed and regulated. However, despite decades of extensive marine phenology research, spawning documentation still largely depend on limited and often biased field observation data. Here we present a novel approach for species-specific, high spatiotemporal resolution, detection and monitoring of spawning events based on eDNA. We developed and utilized quantitative, probe-based ddPCR assays for selected taxa, to precisely identify species-specific spawning events based on daily eDNA samples spanning two consecutive years from the Gulf of Aqaba, Red Sea (totaling 2,725 samples across sites and and depths). Spawning was validated through seven years of field spawning monitoring in the region, and controlled experiments of induced spawning. Spawning events were detected based on deviations from the annual baselines of each species, and the limits of detection for each assay were established using synthetic target DNA (Gblocks). We show that eDNA extracted from water samples offers a transformative, scalable, highly sensitive, cost- and labor-effective alternative to traditional spawning observations. This method has the potential to revolutionize the marine phenology toolkit at a time of a rapidly changing environment.
663a4c83: Coastal islands along the southeastern Brazilian coast host unique marine communities that remain insufficiently described due to logistical challenges associated with traditional biodiversity surveys. Environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding offers an efficient, non-invasive alternative for broad-scale biodiversity assessments. This study applied eDNA metabarcoding using COI gene to characterize the rocky reef biodiversity across four coastal islands of São Paulo State, Brazil: Alcatrazes Archipelago, Laje de Santos, Queimada Grande, and Búzios Island. Seawater was sampled at the surface and 10 m depth during the summer and winter of 2023 at six sites around each island. Sequence reads were processed into OTUs and taxonomically assigned using the Midori database, resulting in 11,524 OTUs across all samples, representing 19 phyla, 72 orders, 75 families, 62 genera and 30 species. Alpha diversity analysis (Shannon index) revealed a consistent seasonal pattern across all sites, with indices consistently higher in winter than summer, indicating pronounced seasonal shifts in local biodiversity. Alcatrazes Archipelago displayed the highest mean diversity, whereas Búzios Island exhibited the lowest overall values. Taxonomic composition showed moderate spatial and seasonal variation among the islands, with patterns consistent with rocky-reef–associated communities. Across most sites, Arthropoda was the predominant phylum in both summer and winter, while Queimada Grande showed high abundance of Cnidaria in summer, indicating an increased detection of benthic cnidarians typical of shallow rocky reefs. Multivariate analyses demonstrated consistent ecological structuring across the region. NMDS ordinations revealed clustering driven by both island and season. PERMANOVA results showed that location (R² = 0.14) and season (R² = 0.13) have significant and moderate effects on community composition, while depth had a smaller but detectable influence (R² = 0.02). Overall, the findings indicate that spatial and seasonal gradients are the primary drivers of eDNA-based community patterns across São Paulo’s coastal islands.
5bce231f:
Coral reefs across the Indo-Pacific support extraordinary biodiversity but are increasingly threatened by anthropogenic stressors, including coastal development, nutrient inputs, overfishing, and climate-driven bleaching. Around Okinawa, such pressures have accelerated ecological degradation and facilitated phase shifts from coral-dominated communities to assemblages dominated by soft corals, macroalgae, and other non-coral taxa. Understanding how these altered reef states differ taxonomically and functionally is essential for predicting ecosystem trajectories.
Environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding has transformed biodiversity monitoring by providing non-invasive, sensitive community profiles, but DNA persistence might obscure short-term ecological signals. Environmental RNA (eRNA), in contrast, degrades more rapidly and may better capture present community states. This study evaluates the complementary roles of eDNA and eRNA for biodiversity assessment and wants to introduce environmental metatranscriptomics as a tool for diagnosing reef ecosystem health.
Water samples were collected across nine coral reef sites spanning a gradient of anthropogenic stress on Okinawa at three points: April 2024, August 2024, and April/May 2025. The first time point captured early summer warming, the second coincided with the onset of severe visual bleaching in shallow reefs, and the third occurred one year later after high bleaching mortality. eDNA and eRNA were co-extracted, with eRNA reverse-transcribed to cDNA. Both templates were amplified using 18S rRNA gene markers to compare taxonomic resolution, community composition, and each method to detect spatial and temporal ecological differences. eRNA will additionally be sequenced to explore community-wide gene expression profiles, providing functional signatures that may reveal molecular stress responses and early indicators of ecosystem decline or resilience.
By integrating eDNA, eRNA, and metatranscriptomic approaches, this study aims to establish a framework for high-resolution biodiversity monitoring and ecosystem health assessment in coral reefs. The findings will evaluate whether eRNA-based methods can complement eDNA in detecting active communities and diagnosing emerging stress in rapidly changing marine environments.
53686a8f: Monitoring marine biodiversity in complex and vulnerable habitats such as coral reefs is critical, yet traditional survey methods often struggle to capture cryptic taxa and fine-scale community turnover. Environmental DNA (eDNA) offers a promising solution, but its adoption in coral reef science methodology has been slow due to optimisation of assay capabilities, and the incompleteness of reference databases which constrains taxonomic resolution. Using eDNA metabarcoding of the cytochrome oxidase subunit I (COI) gene to target invertebrates, and the 16S rRNA gene for vertebrates, we examined temporal and spatial patterns of α- and β-diversity along the coastline of Singapore over one year. Results revealed differences in marine metazoan communities driven by high spatial and temporal turnover without strong directional trends across sites, including a coral reef. These patterns reflect distinct, cohesive communities with limited seasonality, characteristic of equatorial climates. There were also specific taxa that showed monsoon-associated distributions. This demonstrated that existing eDNA approaches are versatile and effective in reef settings, recovering diverse assemblages of invertebrates and vertebrates across multiple phyla, including rare and endangered species. While further refinement—such as primer sets tailored to reef taxa and more mid- to long-term surveys —will enhance precision and elucidate the community of resident species, current methods already provide a cost-effective and scalable addition to the coral reef monitoring toolbox. Importantly, eDNA samples can be archived in biobanks, establishing baselines for future surveys, and enabling retrospective analyses as technologies advance. By integrating eDNA into reef monitoring, managers and scientists gain a powerful tool to safeguard biodiversity in urban seascapes by using it for long-term ecological monitoring and conservation planning.
062b4929: Coral reefs are among the most productive and biologically diverse ecosystems on Earth. Corals cover 0.1-0.5% of the ocean floor but are home to 25% of marine life, representing a valuable resource to biodiversity. A central challenge in evaluating biodiversity using eDNA metabarcoding is that each primer set has biases resulting in selective amplification of a unique composition of taxonomic diversity. Here we test eleven primer pairs (Amaral-Zettler 18S V9, Govinarajan 18S V9, Guardiola 18S V7, Stat 18S V4, Berry 16S, MiFish E 12S, MiFish U 12S, Riaz 12S, Leray CO1, Martineau 28S, and Sherwood 23S) to assess Central Pacific coral reef diversity in the five kingdoms of marine eukaryotes: Chromista, Fungi, Metazoa, Plantae, and Protozoa. Zero-width OTUs (ZOTUs) were compared to public sequence databases and resolved to the finest taxonomic rank possible. The highest number of unique taxa was recovered by Govinarajan 18S V9 primers (127) largely comprised of Chromista and Metazoa, followed by Martineau 28S primers (100) with similar taxonomic composition.  All primers except the algae-focused Sherwood 23S recovered Metazoa, while all markers except Riaz 12S recovered taxa in the kingdom Plantae. None of these primers performed particularly well for Fungi, Cercozoa or Protozoa. At the phylum level, Riaz 12S, Berry 16S, MiFish 12S U and MiFish 12S E recovered the most taxa of Chordata. Marineau 28S, Govinarajan 18S V9, and Amaral Zettler 18S V9 recovered the most Porifera taxa, with highest counts coming from the most diverse class Demospongiae. At the class level, the “fish” primers (Riaz 12S, MiFish 12S U, Berry 16S, and MiFish 12S E) preferentially recovered Actinopteri (ray-finned fishes). While each primer set proved uniquely informative for some taxa, the taxon recovery profiles provided here should aid researchers in matching the taxonomic specificity of environmental metabarcoding primers with research questions of interest.
9acb0a40: Mission: Iconic Reefs (M:IR) is an ambitious restoration initiative to holistically restore seven degraded reefs within Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary (USA). The primary objectives are to increase coral cover from approximately 2% to 25%, enhance reef biodiversity, and restore overall ecosystem health. However, traditional monitoring approaches are insufficient for fully capturing ecosystem-level changes resulting from coral restoration. Current methods rely largely on annual fish surveys and biennial invertebrate visual assessments, which may fail to detect migratory species, seasonal population variability, and cryptic taxa that are difficult to observe or accurately identify. The goal of this study was to document the baseline biodiversity of four M:IR sites by evaluating key coral reef health indicators over one year (September 2024-September 2025). To achieve this, five eDNA markers were used to assess temporal and spatial changes in biodiversity and to detect the presence of ecologically and management-relevant indicator fish and invertebrate species. Our results demonstrate that eDNA successfully detected several management-priority species, including groupers, parrotfishes, snappers, and lobsters. While overall species richness did not significantly change over time and space, the reef site was a primary driver of differences in community composition, which could be explained by spatial variability that may influence ecosystem restoration outcomes. Comparing our results to visual surveys, we also find discrepancies and commonalities between both methods. In all, we find that increased monitoring using eDNA allowed for a more comprehensive evaluation of how fish and invertebrate communities are responding to restoration efforts.
8bb28808: Environmental DNA (eDNA) represents a powerful, non-invasive tool for monitoring coral reef biodiversity and detecting distributions of biodiversity across spatial and temporal scales. However, its application to understanding coral population structure in complex tropical reef systems remains underexplored. Here, I present preliminary work from an eDNA-based study conducted across five reef sites in Moorea, French Polynesia, designed to evaluate how well DNA recovered from seawater can capture fine-scale patterns in biological diversity and community composition. At each site, surface water samples were collected during morning and evening periods to explore potential diel variation in eDNA abundance and taxonomic representation. In parallel, benthic invertebrate surveys were conducted to provide an independent measure of community structure for comparison with eDNA signals. The primary goals of this work are to assess (1) how consistently coral taxa are detected across sites and sampling times, (2) whether spatial variation in eDNA signatures aligns with benthic survey patterns, and (3) whether the diversity and quantity of coral eDNA may be sufficient to inform future population-level analyses. While bioinformatic workflows are ongoing, early findings suggest that eDNA can capture meaningful spatial differences in reef community composition. This study contributes to growing efforts to integrate eDNA into coral reef monitoring and provides an initial framework for evaluating the feasibility of using water-derived DNA to investigate coral diversity and connectivity.
4615db3f:
Recent efforts to reclaim and rebuild loko i‘a, traditional Hawaiian aquaculture systems, combine traditional Hawaiian management with cutting edge biological technologies to expedite restoration. Loko i‘a are situated in coastal marine areas and support fish abundance by protecting and monitoring fish nursery habitats. Our study used environmental DNA (eDNA) sampling to quickly and accurately produce a species list for eight protected fishponds on the windward coast of O‘ahu, HI. We worked with the wildlife preserve managers to produce an eDNA-based species list which included common English and Hawaiian names and species’ endangered or invasive status in Hawai’i. The sampling and re-formatting of our data allowed resource managers to quickly and easily identify a number of endangered species present in the loko i‘a. We are now in the process of creating a single marine database containing both taxonomic and species information useful to local stewards in Hawai’i to increase the usefulness of eDNA biodiversity surveys in management.  
eDNA sampling was paired with environmental measures in order to model which environmental drivers were most associated with biological groups of interest (fishes, algae, annelids etc.). Redundancy analysis across all sites and biological groups identified temperature as a significant environmental driver. However, different factors played a significant role in species composition when biological groups were modeled separately. For example, Actinopteri (bony fishes) were most influenced by turbidity, while Porifera (sponges) most corresponded with pH. These results indicate that species or biological groups of interest need to be considered individually as they respond differently to environmental drivers. Our study can be used as a quick guide to help managers focus their efforts. By knowing which abiotic factors drive biotic communities, restoration of loko i‘a can be curated to enhance an environment that caters to desired species.
bb2f0ebf: Detecting rare reef species remains a major challenge for coral reef research and management. The giant triton snail (Charonia tritonis), a large predatory gastropod and natural predator of crown-of-thorns starfish, occurs at extremely low densities across the Great Barrier Reef (GBR), making traditional visual surveys challenging for assessing its distribution. Here, occupancy of the giant triton snail across the GBR was estimated by integrating diver sighting records captured by the AIMS Long-term Monitoring and Crown-of-Thorns Starfish Control programs with environmental DNA (eDNA) detections. A hierarchical Bayesian occupancy framework was used to separate ecological occurrence from imperfect detection and to integrate both visual and eDNA-based observations. The spatial hierarchy of the sampling design (reef → site → transect/filter replicate) was explicitly accounted for, allowing inference across multiple scales. Preliminary model outputs indicate that the eDNA method is a valuable complement to visual surveys, improving detection and revealing occurrences that may otherwise go undetected. This integration of molecular and traditional survey data provides the first quantitative assessment of giant triton occupancy across the GBR. The approach demonstrates how eDNA can extend the reach of existing monitoring programs, improve detection probability estimates, and inform conservation management of rare reef species critical to ecosystem balance.
4f1d6e2a: Effective coral reef monitoring in dynamic shallow-water environments requires approaches that simultaneously capture fine-scale structural detail and broader spatial patterns. In this contribution, we present an integrated, multi-sensor workflow developed for the coral reefs of Kish Island, Persian Gulf, combining UAV-derived photogrammetry, satellite-based remote sensing, and diver-based ecological surveys. Ultra-high-resolution drone imagery (~2 cm ground sampling distance) enabled precise mapping of individual coral colonies, bleaching signatures, and benthic classes, validated by comprehensive in situ observations and underwater photo quadrats. Building on this, we incorporated bathymetric and geomorphometric variables extracted from field-measured depth data and tidal-corrected profiles collected during the same period, allowing us to link benthic patterns to local topography and reef structural complexity. Integration with high spatial resolution multispectral satellite imagery (QuickBird and WorldView-2) further contextualized colony-level UAV outputs within broader reef geomorphic zones, enabling cross-scale comparisons and improving classification consistency. The merged workflow demonstrates substantial benefits: enhanced accuracy from multi-layer validation, improved detection of bleaching and degradation, and clearer interpretation of depth-dependent habitat distributions. We also highlight practical limitations, including water clarity, scale mismatches, and positional uncertainties, and outline solutions for standardized acquisition and processing in data-limited regions. This study provides a scalable model for merging “eyes above and below,” supporting innovation in coral reef monitoring while strengthening regional capacity for conservation-focused mapping, particularly for understudied reef systems such as those of the Persian Gulf.
21660aa1: Caribbean coral reefs are experiencing rapid changes in benthic composition due to a myriad of environmental stressors. Recent coral mortality events, including bleaching and Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD), have left left populations of highly susceptible stony corals severely depleted. Current monitoring programs in the U.S. Virgin Islands lack the resolution to capture rare species distributions, prompting the development of the Endangered Coral Mapping Program (ECMP). ECMP aims to assess the population size and distributions of ESA-listed and SCTLD-susceptible corals in shallow waters (<10m) across the territory. Using a stratified random sampling design, coral demographic surveys were conducted at 600 sites across the U.S. Virgin Islands. Preliminary results suggest that Dendrogyra cylindrus (1.6% occurrence) is functionally extinct in the region, and Acropora palmata (21.8% occurrence) is at risk of genetic isolation. Species distribution models will be used to determine areas of probable occurrence and potential habitats suitable for restoration. These findings aim to guide management actions to support the conservation of these vulnerable species amidst rapidly changing reef conditions.
e60fd9c0: Zanzibar reefs such as Chumbe, Bawe and Changuu reefs are located in two separate marine protected areas (MPAs) in the west of the island and have been affected by sea surface temperature (SST) anomalies. Since the establishment of these MPAs, coral cover monitoring has been consistently conducted by local academic institutes using Line Intercept Transects (LIT) method, making it possible to compare coral cover (CC) changes across 30 years. Field observation data using LIT method was also collected in April and November 2024 during and after the global coral bleaching event and included in this study. Despite recognising the role of high SST in causing coral bleaching and mortality, little is known about the relationship between post-events CC changes and SST changes measured from satellites or in-situ loggers. Hence, our study utilises statistical methods to compare between 30 years’ time series of CC changes to in-situ daily logger SST data and two similar satellite products, coral reef watch (CRW) and climate change initiative (CCI) 5km high-resolution daily SST data. Similarities were found between time series of satellite SST products in all three reefs and between satellites. However, differences were found between time series of CC changes in all three reefs, regardless of whether they are in the same MPA or in a different MPA in close proximity. In addition, logger SST time series showed differences with satellite SST time series and CC time series changes within the same reef. The results of our study hints at difficulties of remote sensing SST methods to predict changes on a local reef scale. Within local reefs, there are differences that appear standardized when relying on remote sensing measurements that are known to impact coral reefs. Hence, caution should be taken when using either in-situ logger or remote sensing methods to determine reef changes.
3a842658: Coral reefs in the Arabian Gulf exist in one of the most extreme marine environments, subject to significant temperature fluctuations that trigger bleaching events resulting in degradation over time. Accurate monitoring of coral reef health and recovery is crucial for understanding their resilience and informing conservation strategies. This study presents a robust methodological framework for assessing changes in coral reef cover across multiple years (2016, 2019, 2021, 2022, 2024, 2025) using multi-temporal Sentinel-2 satellite imagery and insitu bleaching observations in 2023 in the Saudi offshore island of Karan. A change detection analysis was conducted using the raster calculator to compute band ratio indices for each date after an iterative relative radiometric normalization procedure allowing for the demarcation of areas with reef loss. The analysis revealed a maximum cumulative transect total of 380 m and 300 m of coral reef loss along the northeastern and northern reef crests, respectively over the study period with varying extent across different zones of the reef. A linear directional progression pattern in coral reef erosion along the northern reef crests suggests susceptibility of degraded reefs to wave action. The largest change was detected in 2019 relative to 2025 with estimated loss of 432,607 m2 and 303,025m2 for the northern and southern halves of the reef crest, respectively. Insitu observations along the Karan Island reef crests in August 2023 reported varying levels of bleaching across several coral taxonomic groups including Acropora, Porites, Platygyra, Dipsastraea, and Favites. This work underscores the critical importance of combining remote sensing time-series analysis and insitu coral bleaching observations for monitoring coral reef health in challenging environments like the Arabian Gulf.
c947a82e: The National Coral Reef Monitoring Program (NCRMP) currently conducts fish and coral diver visual surveys in the U.S. Caribbean using a stratification scheme classified, in part, according to qualitative characteristics (aggregate reef, patch reef, pavement, scattered coral/rock and bedrock). The present NCRMP sample frame and stratification scheme was developed in 2013 using the best available habitat maps at the time. In the past decade, multiple high-resolution, remote-sensed mapping products have become available for bathymetry and substrate rugosity. Our goal was to incorporate these newer mapping products into NCRMP’s sample frame and stratification scheme to more accurately, precisely, and cost-effectively survey reef fish and corals in the U.S. Caribbean. Using analytical methods recently developed in Florida, we incorporated quantitative map metrics of benthic characteristics into the existing 50x50 m grid sample frame in Puerto Rico and U.S. Virgin Islands (USVI). A formal stratification analysis of reef fish and coral species using the improved sample frame and species-specific abundance metrics were used to refine NCRMP’s stratification scheme by improving the partitioning of variance in abundance within the survey design. Better partitioning of variance resulted in more effective sampling effort and more precise relative abundance indices. Moreover, the improved sample frame with quantitative map metrics can be applied to improve accuracy of previous survey data for reef fish and coral species via improved estimates of habitat area. Ultimately, the incorporation of new mapping products and a revised stratification scheme improves NCRMP’s ability to inform U.S. Caribbean reef fish stock assessments, ESA mandates for multiple coral species, and ecosystem-based fisheries management models that all aim to provide the best management advice to conserve coral reef ecosystems in the U.S. Caribbean.
0b65d337: Coral reefs provide critical ecosystem services but face increasing threats from climate change, disease, and human impacts. Monitoring these ecosystems at scale is essential for conservation and restoration, yet traditional diver-based surveys are costly, time-consuming, and prone to observer bias. Deep learning offers a transformative solution by enabling automated, accurate detection of coral colonies from large volumes of remotely sensed imagery. In this study, we developed and evaluated a deep learning model specifically designed to identify Elkhorn coral (Acropora palmata)—a keystone reef-building species in the Caribbean—and distinguish it from other coral species and non-coral elements such as dead coral, rocks, sargassum, and soft corals. To build a robust dataset, we collected high-resolution uncrewed aerial system (UAS) imagery (~2 cm spatial resolution) under varying environmental conditions across a ~3 km reef section in the U.S. Virgin Islands, using a WingtraOne VTOL platform equipped with a 61 MP Sony RGB61 camera. Additional ultra-high-resolution imagery was acquired for visual verification, and snorkelers captured several hundred georeferenced images for field-referencing. These combined datasets were used to train and validate multiple deep learning models for colony-level detection. Our models demonstrated strong capability in accurately classifying A. palmata while minimizing false positives from other reef features. This approach highlights the potential of integrating advanced computer vision techniques with aerial and in-water imagery to improve coral monitoring and restoration planning at scale. Beyond species identification, such technology enables early detection of bleaching, disease, and structural damage, supporting rapid conservation responses. Accurate mapping of coral colonies also informs restoration strategies, including prioritizing outplanting sites and modeling larval connectivity. By incorporating deep learning into reef monitoring workflows, researchers can process data efficiently, enhance precision, and deliver actionable insights for adaptive management and climate resilience.
edee0556: Coral reefs' habitat maps are fundamental data for spatial planning, resource management, and the development of coastal and marine management policies. Zoning within marine conservation areas ideally protects critical habitats, making the precision and accuracy of habitat maps crucial for effective planning. Remote sensing provides an efficient approach to mapping coral reef habitats across regions, using the unique spectral characteristics of these habitats. Random forest is one of the most frequently used machine learning algorithms in habitat mapping due to its ability to handle high spectral variation. However, this method can still produce noise and class fragmentation, resulting in habitat patterns that do not always reflect actual conditions. In addition to single-pixel-based analysis, segmentation methods also have potential for mapping coral reef habitats, such as Simple Non-Iterative Clustering (SNIC), which can group pixels based on colour, texture, size, and connectivity. SNIC-based approach groups pixels into spatially coherent objects before classification, potentially producing habitat maps with more representative boundaries and structures that better reflect field conditions. Although SNIC has not been widely applied in coral reef habitat mapping, its object-based characteristics offer the potential to produce spatial patterns that better reflect field conditions. To compare the mapping capabilities of SNIC and random forest, this study used high-resolution (1.5 m) SPOT-6 imagery, which provides sufficient spatial detail to distinguish coral reef habitat features. Imagery was modelled using ground truth data acquired through the spot check method and visually interpreted data, utilising the interpretation key developed during the ground truth survey. This study will also evaluate how the results of those two algorithms affected estimates of the extent of protected critical habitat. Through this combined assessment, which considered both accuracy and zoning implications, the study demonstrated how algorithm selection can influence the design and effectiveness of marine conservation areas.
a56c9c17: Traditionally, monitoring biodiversity and ecosystem function in underwater environments has been constrained by funding and difficulties with safely accessing remote or deep-water sites. Recent technological advancements have led to the development of autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs), submersibles that operate independently without a pilot or tether. These AUVs enhance the scale, precision, and speed of environmental research, and can mitigate the risks associated with SCUBA surveys, such as decompression illness. However, it is unknown to what extent the noise caused by AUVs and their mechanical thrusters affects fish behavior and abundance estimates compared to surveys conducted on open-circuit SCUBA, which is influenced by bubble exhalation, or nearly silent closed-circuit rebreathers. Herbivory is a key ecological process in coral reef communities that regulates the balance between corals and macroalgae, presenting a need for accurate quantification of herbivores, including parrotfish (Scarus spp. and Sparisoma spp.) and damselfish (Stegastes spp. and Microspathodon spp.). We are conducting AUV- and diver-based surveys on coral reefs in the U.S. Virgin Islands to compare count estimates, with a focus on differences between herbivore families and subsequent herbivory estimates. Preliminary surveys at a grouper spawning aggregation site suggested that behavior differs between AUV- and diver-based surveys, with individuals appearing less avoidant around AUVs at distances of 2-3 m. However, at close ranges (< 2m), AUVs disturbed fish behavior. When thrusters activated to turn, groupers made rapid avoidance maneuvers, affecting nearby fish. Groupers, although carnivorous predators, demonstrate variability in presence across methods; however, it remains unknown whether herbivores exhibit similar patterns, highlighting the need for further research. Once comparisons are validated and extended to coral reef fish and key herbivore species, AUV-based fish surveys can be deployed at scale for coral reef monitoring, which can be applied to tracking the long-term herbivory effects on U.S. Virgin Island reefs.
0c54aae6: Shipwrecks are distinctive features on the seafloor. There are three million known shipwrecks worldwide. Whether accidentally lost or intentionally scuttled, they are long-lived anthropogenic structures that shape seascapes. In some contexts they are protected as underwater cultural heritage, in others they are valued as artificial reefs. At the same time, these structures can pose risks like trapped fuel, leaching metals and antifouling paints and altered currents and sediment dynamics. Yet these roles and impacts are rarely considered together. Instead, shipwrecks are typically managed in disciplinary silos. Heritage agencies prioritise history and site integrity, while conservationists and policymakers may treat them as reef restoration infrastructure. Fragmenting the same physical structure into separate categories obscures the fact that all of these are expressions of a human-made intervention in the marine environment. I propose bringing wrecks together under one heading as anthropogenic structures with intertwined material, ecological, and social trajectories makes it possible to follow their full lifecycle and design more coherent, sustainable approaches to their monitoring and management.
