Using Mauritius as a case study, I present a study of shipwrecks of differing ages and materials, using nearby natural reefs as comparators to develop an eco-archaeological monitoring framework that links material/ structural states of shipwrecks through photogrammetry with ecological assemblages and functions across selected wrecks, generating repeatable indicators of the shipwreck assemblage. I also assess, through policy documents and interviews with stakeholders, particularly  divers and managers, to examine what wrecks do above water, how they are perceived, governed as either heritage, reefs or tourism assets. Together, these strands show how material decay, ecological function, and governance framings interact over time. I outline how this transdisciplinary framework can inform lifecycle-aware decisions about at sea infra, including criteria for identifying when shipwrecks remain beneficial and when they may require active intervention or reclassification.
171bc7e0: Artificial reefs in the form of shipwrecks are frequently adopted around the world to enhance reef associated fish populations and recreational opportunities to various stakeholders in tropical marine ecosystems. Despite widespread employment, the monitoring of their post-deployment ecological performance is often insufficient. The role of shipwrecks acting as fish producers (increasing the overall abundance and biomass of species) or as fish attractors (pulling fish from surrounding natural environments and fragmenting populations) is highly debated; largely due to a lack of experimental matched controls and temporal datasets. The current study investigated the attraction vs production value of two newly deployed shipwrecks in The Gulf of Thailand by comparing fish assemblages between paired artificial and natural reef sites pre and post deployment. Recent deployments demonstrated increased fish densities and concurrent declines at paired natural reefs, indicating an attraction effect. High-trophic level predators and planktivorous species were shown to be more strongly attracted to shipwrecks than other functional groups and likely act as first colonizers, supporting previous literature. In contrast, long established, ‘mature’ shipwrecks supported stable fish assemblages while adjacent natural reef environments displayed significant increases in fish abundances, suggesting a degree of biomass spillover. Size estimates of fish assemblages highlighted signatures of enhanced production on shipwrecks, acting as elevated nursery habitats for some juvenile fish taxa compared to nearby NR environments, however this finding was limited to newly deployed ARs, and was dissolved in mature shipwrecks. These findings suggest a nuanced perspective to the attractor-producer debate positing that shipwrecks may first act as attractors during periods of initial colonization, as well as stimulating early pulses of enhanced production, later maturing into established ecosystems with assemblages more consistent with natural reef environments. Site specific differences emphasize the need for careful planning of future wreck deployments to maximize the ecological benefits of artificial reef deployments and reduce the risk of over-exploitation of commercially targeted fish taxa.
81dba755: Artificial coral reefs (ACRs) are increasingly recognised for their ability to provide ecosystem services by mimicking natural reef structures. Their positive effects on biodiversity have led to growing interest in their utilisation for reef rehabilitation and marine conservation, driving the use of diverse materials and technologies in ACR manufacture and deployment. Despite their benefits, a thorough understanding of the lifecycle impacts of ACRs—from production through to end-of-life—remains lacking. To address this gap, this study employs a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to evaluate six distinct ACR designs, quantifying carbon emissions and other environmental impacts associated with material sourcing, manufacturing, deployment, use, and decommissioning. Our results indicate that ACRs containing metal components and produced using silicone moulds have substantially higher carbon footprints, primarily due to material extraction and energy-intensive production. Potential strategies to mitigate environmental impacts, such as replacing Ordinary Portland Cement with supplementary cementitious materials, incorporating recycled aggregates, and adopting alternative technologies, are discussed. By integrating lifecycle analysis with design and material innovation, this research provides practical recommendations for sustainable ACR design and use. The findings support evidence-based decision-making and strategic management, aiming to optimise ecological benefits while minimising adverse environmental effects throughout the entire lifespan of artificial reefs.
793f6127: Artificial reefs are widely deployed to enhance fish populations, yet their ecological equivalence to natural reefs remains uncertain, partly because comparisons are typically limited to fringing coral reefs. Such assessments may overlook how structural features and varying reef morphologies influence ecological outcomes. Fish assemblages were compared across shipwrecks, pelagic pinnacles, and fringing reefs around an island in the Gulf of Thailand to evaluate how artificial structures of differing relief align with natural community and trophic patterns. Bayesian multivariate models applied to 350 surveys from 14 sites showed that shipwrecks resembled pelagic pinnacles in mesopredators and higher trophic level predators, but had consistently lower representation of grazers and invertivores relative to both natural reef types. This functional divergence likely reflects reduced benthic complexity and forage availability; shipwrecks may support predator biomass but not fully function as ecological analogues of natural reefs. Findings highlight the importance of aligning artificial reef design with ecological objectives and considering structural context in management strategies across tropical reef systems worldwide.
c8f537df: Coral reefs are among the world’s most diverse ecosystems and contribute substantially to ocean productivity and the carbon budget. Disturbances are shifting coral reefs to altered community states, yet how these new communities indirectly alter organismal physiology by modifying the local biogeochemical environment remains largely unknown. We investigated this biogeochemical feedback in a 25-day mesocosm experiment in Mo’orea, French Polynesia, exposing the common coral Porites rus to the biogeochemical environments generated by three distinct communities (coral-, macroalgae-, and rubble/crustose coralline algae (CCA)-dominated) and a seawater-only control. While net community production (NCP) was similar among communities, net community calcification (NCC) was highest in the coral- and rubble/CCA-dominated state. Community metabolism drove changes in the organic and inorganic carbon pool, with NCP showing a positive relationship with both pH and dissolved organic carbon (DOC). These community-driven shifts affected corals at both the community and organismal level: elevated pH significantly enhanced NCC and P. rus chlorophyll a (Chl a) content. The increase in Chl a cascaded to the holobiont, correlating positively with endosymbiont density, tissue biomass, gross photosynthesis, and respiration. This study reveals a critical indirect pathway of disturbance where reef community structure modulates local carbon chemistry, which in turn regulates the physiological performance of key reef-builders. The relationships found in this study highlight a feedback loop that can influence future ecosystem trajectories on coral reefs.
a1c9adcb:
Real-time observation of coral physiological processes is essential for understanding how calcification and photosynthesis respond to rapid environmental fluctuations. However, conventional approaches rely on discrete sampling or end-point measurements, which limit the ability to capture short-term dynamics. In this study, we applied a continuous total alkalinity (TA) monitoring system—originally developed for flow-through seawater analysis—to quantify high-frequency changes in calcification and photosynthesis in a colony of Acropora aff. tenuis under controlled laboratory conditions. Dissolved oxygen (DO) was measured simultaneously to characterize photosynthetic activity.
When light conditions were switched from dark to illumination, DO concentrations began to rise after approximately 5–10 minutes, indicating the onset of photosynthesis. TA decreased nearly concurrently, reflecting enhanced calcification. This near-synchronous activation of photosynthesis and calcification demonstrates tightly coupled metabolic responses that are difficult to resolve with traditional incubation methods. These results highlight the ability of continuous TA measurements to detect rapid biogeochemical transitions on sub-hourly timescales.
Building on these findings, this system offers new opportunities for reconstructing carbonate chemistry dynamics during incubations, as continuous TA records allow estimation of CO2 system changes without relying on start–end averages. Future work will evaluate coral responses under different CO2 and temperature conditions and expand the approach to multiple species. By providing the first high-temporal-resolution observations of TA-based calcification dynamics, this study establishes a new framework for understanding coral physiological responses to rapid environmental change.
7ff52cdc: Coral reefs are essential for the foundation of marine ecosystems. However, ocean acidification (OA), driven by rising atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) threatens coral growth and biological homeostasis. This study examines two Hawaiian coral species—Montipora capitata and Pocillopora acuta to elevated pCO2 simulating OA. Utilizing pH and O2 microsensors under controlled light and dark conditions, this work characterized interspecific CBL traits and quantified material fluxes under ambient and elevated pCO2. The results of this study revealed that under increased pCO2, P. acuta showed a significant reduction in dark proton efflux, followed by an increase in light O2 flux, suggesting reduced calcification and enhanced photosynthesis. In contrast, M. capitata did not show any robust evidence of changes in either flux parameters under similar increased pCO2 conditions. Statistical analyses using linear models revealed several significant interactions among species, treatment, and light conditions, identifying physical, chemical, and biological drivers of species responses to increased pCO2. This study also presents several conceptual models that correlate the CBL dynamics measured here with calcification and metabolic processes, thereby justifying our findings. We indicate that elevated pCO2 exacerbates microchemical gradients in the CBL and may threaten calcification in vulnerable species such as P. acuta, while highlighting the resistance of M. capitata. Therefore, this study advances our understanding of how interspecific microenvironmental processes could influence coral responses to changing ocean chemistry.
2821769e:
Corals exhibit remarkable architectural diversity, and this morphological variation governs how water moves across the tissue surface and transports ions across the boundary layers. Although it has long been recognized that morphology can enhance or limit a coral’s ability to regulate local carbonate chemistries, the geometric variables and length scales that mediate turbulence, residence time, and flux remain poorly understood.

We addressed this challenge by examining skeletons of 32 western Atlantic coral species representing common gross morphologies. High-resolution 3D meshes were generated for each colony with a white light 3D scanner and used to calculate fine-scale morphometric measurements and widely applied continuous geometric descriptors, including surface complexity, volume compactness, and top-heaviness. These metrics were then paired with 2D particle image velocimetry (PIV) derived flow fields measured in a controlled laboratory flume to resolve turbulence structure, shear stresses, and boundary-layer thicknesses around the corals. By comparing geometric variables with observed flow characteristics, we elucidate the key geometric variables that structure a coral’s capacity to modulate microenvironment carbonate chemistry.

Preliminary results indicate that geometric variables capturing fine-scale roughness better predict mass transfer than bulk descriptors alone. When contextualized alongside literature-derived estimates of species-specific metabolisms, these biophysical models establish a mechanistic link between morphology and OA sensitivities. Rather than identifying a single predictive metric, this work outlines a pathway for constraining which morphometric scales matter and how they shape mass transport across coral surfaces. Ultimately, this work advances a form–function framework for forecasting demographic shifts and accretion potential under future ocean conditions.
6f241efc: Ocean acidification and its role in disrupting ecosystem dynamics have been extensively documented in contemporary marine science. Governing bodies worldwide are incentivizing marine Carbon Dioxide Removal (mCDR) technologies to simultaneously mitigate changes in seawater chemistry and capture atmospheric CO₂. Among these strategies, Ocean Alkalinity Enhancement (OAE) has been proposed as a dynamic method to remove carbon and offset ocean acidification. However, OAE remains a ‘black box’ with many foundational questions still unanswered. There is growing evidence for potential co-benefits of OAE at the ecosystem level, but further research is needed to capture how OAE integrates at the organismal level. Here we present a novel dataset highlighting how naturally occurring OAE analogues positively modulate coral physiology. Three biogeochemically distinct substrates were identified in Bocas del Toro, Panama and equipped with multiparameter sondes to continuously measure seawater chemistry. Ten coral colonies of the branching species, Porites porites (n = 10), were deployed within 50 m x 50 m matrices in dense and sparse T. testudinum meadows (n = 3) as well as in intact coral communities (n = 3; >30% live coral cover). We found that belowground respiration within intact communities of T. testudinum generated an alkalinity-rich environment that significantly improved coral health via the accumulation of lipid stores and elevation of the pH of the calcifying fluid. Perhaps most compelling, this phenomenon persisted despite corals experiencing weak to moderate hypoxia ( 4.5 mg L¹) due to nighttime respiration. We believe this research provides critical evidence for how Ocean Alkalinity Enhancement might modulate organismal physiology in at-risk marine communities.
9981a9ea: The coral microbiome has been proposed to be critical of coral health and resilience, yet it is unclear what factors drive coral microbiome composition, especially within the context of coral bleaching. Here, we use whole genome sequencing data combined with a machine learning approach (RDAforest) to assess various predictors of the microbiome in 208 colonies of Acropora millepora from 12 reef sites in the Central Great Barrier Reef during a 2016 bleaching event. We find that microbiome variation is primarily predicted by the first principal component of coral host genetic structure, prevalence of Durusdinium, and some environmental variables (most prominently chlorphyll seasonal range and maximum degree heating weeks), with negligible predictive power of bleaching score. Strong associations of the coral microbiome to symbiont genus has not been reported before and is likely due to the difference in exudate availability for microbiomes from different photosymbiont genera. In turn, the association of the microbiome with the environment suggests that the coral microbiome can serve as a proxy for environmental variation when environment cannot be measured directly, which may be especially effective in ancient DNA studies.
4079cfcf: Climate-linked disturbances, including coral bleaching and disease, are becoming increasingly common, emphasizing the need to view resilience as an emergent property of both the coral host and its microbial partners. Although the host provides the foundational capacity for immunity and stress tolerance, variation in coral-associated algal symbiont communities (Family Symbiodiniaceae) can drive differences in host phenotype, shaping thermal tolerance and disease susceptibility. Thus, Symbiodiniaceae composition can strongly influence coral resilience. Here, we reciprocally transplanted corals between inshore (near the Port of Miami) and offshore (Emerald Reef) environments off southeast Florida to assess how environmental conditions structure Symbiodiniaceae communities. We followed 208 fragments of four species evenly distributed between the environments, for one year, collecting tissue biopsies quarterly to track Symbiodiniaceae changes and document the 2022 bleaching event. After one year, fragments were returned to the laboratory, where a disease challenge was conducted to understand how environmentally driven symbiont shifts shape disease response. Field monitoring shows that corals transplanted to inshore environments shuffled in favor of Durusdinium (p<0.05), while those sourced from offshore environments tended to associate more readily with Breviolum and/or Cladocopium. Inshore corals maintained strong associations with Durusdinium, whereas offshore corals moved inshore rapidly shifted in favor of Durusdinium, suggesting persistent, environment-driven partnerships. Corals hosting Durusdinium during the summer showed significantly less bleaching than those hosting other algal genera. In the laboratory assay, corals containing more Durusdinium showed decreased disease incidence than fragments generated from the same colony associating with Breviolum (or Cladocopium) (p<0.05). These findings suggest inshore environments select for Durusdinium, resulting in higher thermal tolerance and disease resistance, benefits that may persist under changing conditions. Leveraging these resilient inshore corals in restoration and intervention efforts may help reinforce vulnerable populations and enhance reef persistence under continued climate stress.
d5133089: In Florida, the endangered coral Acropora cervicornis is often dominated (up to 99% relative abundance) by the parasitic intracellular bacterium Aquarickettsia rohweri, and this bacterium is associated with decreased host fitness and increased disease susceptibility. Despite its intracellular nature and host dependence, A. rohweri is not vertically transmitted, making it essential to identify vectors and mechanisms of its horizontal transmission. To evaluate potential vector-mediated routes, we utilized naïve coral outplanting studies, ex situ transmission studies, and short- and full-length 16S rRNA gene sequencing to track A. rohweri in coral hosts, potential vectors, and the water column. We found that when naïve corals were outplanted, only corals within 50m of an infected coral acquired A. rohweri. Further, we explored whether this transmission was potentially due to vectoring via the corallivorous snail Coralliophila abbreviata or via infectious coral host mucocytes. Our results suggest that A. rohweri is only transiently present in the snails, highest 24-hours after feeding (4% relative abundance), and almost undetectable after a two-week depuration. Although A. rohweri does not become an established microbiome member in C. abbreviata, snails may still vector this bacterium via direct feeding or fecal transmission. We therefore conducted a transmission trial in which snails fed on A. cervicornis sexual recruits – containing little to no A. rohweri –after feeding on high-Aquarickettsia corals. Mucus from recruits was repeatedly sampled and screened by qPCR to detect A. rohweri acquisition. To uncover dynamics of waterborne transmission, the water from coral only and snail tanks was collected and treated with a bacterial viability dye to determine whether live A. rohweri bacterial cells are shed into the water from either snails or infected hosts and if they retain their infectivity. Such information can inform restoration strategies and guide future investigations into other routes of transmission in vulnerable A. cervicornis populations.
c6881b41:
Western Atlantic octocorals are important components of Caribbean reef assemblages. Despite facing unprecedented threats from anthropogenic and natural stressors, including disease and marine heating waves, octocoral populations have remained relatively abundant and have become increasingly dominant in some locations. A healthy bacterial assemblage contributes to important host processes such as nutrient cycling and stress resilience, but pollution and thermal stress can break down these symbiotic relationships, which can result in disease, bleaching, and ultimately mortality. Although significant advances have been made in characterizing scleractinian coral microbiomes, we are only beginning to progress in octocoral microbiome research. Understanding octocoral-bacterial symbioses is critical for predicting their persistence amid escalating environmental threats.
Here, we used 16S amplicon sequencing to characterize bacterial communities in four common Florida octocoral species under different conditions: healthy colonies from annual field surveys (2023-2025), disease-infected Briareum asbestinum from a controlled infection experiment, and colonies experiencing natural thermal bleaching. Our study included four abundant Florida reef octocorals representing distinct morphologies: two encrusting species (B. asbestinum and Erythropodium caribaeorum) and two branching species (Eunicea flexuosa and Muricea muricata). Analyses of healthy field-collected colonies reveal distinct, species-specific bacterial community signatures, as well as ubiquitous associates (e.g. Endozoicomonas). Ongoing comparative analyses will examine how bacterial community composition, relative abundance patterns, and specific ASV distributions shift in response to disease exposure and thermal stress. By comparing microbiome dynamics across these three stress settings, we aim to identify core bacterial taxa that remain stable versus flexible community members that respond to environmental perturbations. These findings will provide critical insights into the role of host-microbe interactions in octocoral resilience and vulnerability to environmental stress, with implications for understanding their continued dominance on degraded Caribbean reefs.
c2748c71: Understanding temporal and spatial variability in reef-associated microbial communities is essential for predicting ecosystem responses to environmental change. Yet, long-term microbial datasets remain scarce in reef systems worldwide, despite the high sensitivity of microbial assemblages to environmental fluctuations and disturbance events. This gap is especially critical in light of the severe coral mortality caused by the 2023 and 2024 mass-bleaching events, which represent some of the most extreme thermal disturbances recorded in the Red Sea. Here, we examine three years of temporal variability (2022–2025) in seawater bacterial communities across 11 reefs in the central Red Sea, spanning seasonal cycles and two major warming periods (2023 and 2024). Using 16S rRNA sequencing integrated with high-resolution environmental data (temperature, nutrients, chlorophyll a), we assess patterns of alpha- and beta-diversity and functional potential across seasons and shelf positions. We anticipate pronounced seasonal shifts, with summer warming and nutrient pulses driving shifts in community composition, favoring copiotrophic and opportunistic taxa such as Vibrio, Rhodobacterales and Flavobacteriales. In contrast, we expect to find declines in key oligotrophic groups such as Prochlorococcus, and nitrite-oxidizing Nitrospinae, which are typically sensitive to thermal stress and reduced water column stability. Functionally, warm periods are expected to enhance pathways associated with organic matter degradation, chitin and sulfur cycling, DMSP metabolism, and stress-response functions, while reducing contributions from autotrophic carbon fixation and nitrification. Overall, this study provides the first multi-year microbial baseline for Red Sea reefs and evaluates the sensitivity of seawater bacterial communities to both seasonal and interannual environmental variability, including the changes in the benthic composition resulting from extreme bleaching-driven events of 2023-2024. Our multi-year approach provides critical baseline knowledge that can support the future development and implementation of microbial bioindicators in Red Sea reef monitoring efforts.
156623a1: The thermal bleaching percentage of coral holobionts shows interspecific differences under heat-stress conditions, which is closely related to the coral-associated microbiome. However, the ecological effects of community dynamics and interactions between Symbiodiniaceae and fungi on coral thermal bleaching susceptibility remain unclear. In this study, we analyzed the diversity, community structure, functions, and potential interaction of Symbiodiniaceae and fungi among 18 coral species from a high thermal bleaching risk atoll using next-generation sequencing. The results showed that heat-tolerant C3u sub-clade and Durusdinium dominated the Symbiodiniaceae community of corals, and that there was no core amplicon sequence variants (ASV) in the coral-associated fungal community. Fungal richness and the abundance of confirmed functional animal-plant pathogens were significantly positive correlated with coral thermal bleaching percentage. Fungal indicators, including Didymellaceae, Chaetomiaceae, Schizophyllum and Colletorichum, were identified in corals. Each coral species had a complex Symbiodiniaceae-fungi interaction network (SFIN), which was driven by the dominant Symbiodiniaceae sub-clades. The SFINs of coral holobionts with low thermal bleaching susceptibility exhibited low complexity and high betweenness centrality. These results indicate that the extra heat tolerance of coral in HYI may be link to the high abundance of heat-tolerant Symbiodiniaceae. Fungal communities have high interspecific flexibility, and the increase of fungal diversity and pathogen abundance was correlated with higher thermal bleaching susceptibility of corals. Moreover, fungal indicators were associated with degrees of coral thermal bleaching susceptibility, including both high and intermediate levels. The topological properties of SFINs suggest that heat-tolerant coral have limited fungal parasitism and strong microbial network resilience. We first constructed an interaction network between Symbiodiniaceae and fungi in corals, which indicated that restricting fungal parasitism and strong interaction network resilience would promote heat acclimatization of corals. This study provides insights into the role of microorganisms and their interaction as drivers of interspecific differences in coral thermal bleaching.
fbac5b99: Metabolomic approaches are increasingly be used to understand interactions within complex microbial systems. In particular, metabolomic profiling coupled with stable isotope labelling can help understand overall carbon balances and nutrient exchanges between different community members, identify novel pathways not predicted from genome-wide analyses and metabolic signatures that reflect community health. The application of advanced metabolomics to coral reef systems offers particular benefits, including the identification of stress and/or resilience biomarkers which are based on mechanistic understandings and go beyond changes in the metagenome, and will likely be a powerful tool for improving conservation decisions and reef restoration outcomes. This systematic review is centred around central carbon metabolism of coral and Symbiodiniaceae, focussing on the current state-of-play and highlighting shared and contrasting cellular mechanisms underlying heat stress response. Knowledge on coral and Symbiodiniaceae metabolism is primarily derived from the coral genera Montipora and Pocillopora and the coral model Exaiptasia from the Great Barrier Reef (Australia) and Hawaii (US), subjected to an acute heat stress. These studies have highlighted many changes in central carbon metabolism during heat stress, including changes in the TCA cycle, amino acid metabolism, glycolysis/carbohydrate metabolism and the urea cycle, although a unified hypothesis linking these changes to heat tolerance/susceptibility remain debated. Identified challenges in these studies include; 1) sample processing and throughput 2) coverage and annotation of coral/Symbiodiniaceae-specific metabolites, 3) knowledge on metabolic fluxes, and 4) the lack of in situ, spatial information. While stable isotope labelling (13C, 15N) has provided valuable insights into the turnover rate of secondary metabolites, its potential on tracing metabolic pathways is yet to be explored. We reveal knowledge and technological gaps that are critical to establish metabolomics as reef conservation tool, drawing examples from the biomedicine field on how these gaps can be overcome and where the next frontier will be.
7fb61d54: Coral reefs are experiencing an unprecedented decline primarily due to the increasing frequency and intensity of marine heatwaves, which are causing mass coral bleaching events and leading to extensive coral mortality worldwide. While the molecular processes underlying coral bleaching have been extensively studied at the genomic and proteomic levels, the post-translational responses of corals, particularly the metabolic pathways involved in disrupting their symbiotic relationship, remain less well understood. This study used an untargeted LC-MS metabolomics approach to identify the metabolic signatures of two common coral species, Stylophora pistillata and Pocillopora damicornis, under heat-stress-induced bleaching. Even under control conditions (25 °C), the two species relied on distinct pathways for metabolic energy supply. After 10 days of heat stress (31 °C), both species underwent metabolic reprogramming, which appeared to be species-specific. Stylophora pistillata downregulated ATP synthesis metabolites and upregulated alternative energy production pathways. Conversely, P. damicornis enhanced ammonia recycling pathways and amino acid metabolism, strengthening its cellular defence mechanisms. Our findings indicated that even closely related coral species can adopt different strategies in response to thermal stress. Although often underutilized, the metabolomic approach has proven valuable in providing new insights into the molecular dynamics of coral bleaching.
a079d7df:
Coral phenotypic plasticity facilitates acclimation and adaptation to environmental variability. Coral species often display a variety of color morphs, yet, key biological and ecological implications of such phenotypic variation remain underexplored. Here we present the first proteomic and untargeted lipidomic and metabolomic survey to explore the biological characteristics and potential ecological significance of different color morphs (pink and brown) of healthy Pocillopora verrucosa sampled along a latitudinal gradient. Our multi-omic approach elucidated distinct mechanisms associated with these dominant color morphs. We discovered bacterial indicators specific to each morph: putative pathogens such as Salmonella, Escherichia-Shigella, and carotenoid-producing Gemmatimonas were notably associated with the pink morph, whereas the brown morph was associated with potentially beneficial bacteria, such as Lysobacter, Acinetobacter, and Endozoicomonas. Despite these microbiome differences, the
lipidome and metabolome of P. verrucosa were surprisingly homogeneous across colors and locations, suggesting similar metabolic performances during summer conditions. Key polar and apolar lipid classes, such as fatty acids, glycerophosphocholines, and retinoids were prevalent. Notably, our proteomic analysis
revealed morph-specific expressions, with pink morphs exhibiting enhanced levels of 2 GFP-like proteins, Ankyrin, and the enzyme pullulanase, suggesting novel protective roles. In contrast, the brown morphs showed a higher abundance of heat shock
proteins, indicating putative differential stress response capabilities. This comprehensive study provides not only the first proteomic survey of P. verrucosa but also critical insights into the physiological underpinnings and potential ecological trade- offs of coral color morphs, which can further contribute to enhancing our understanding of coral resilience in the face of climate change.
03f949a7: Widespread coral bleaching driven by ocean warming continues to degrade reefs. It drives the need to better understand the cellular events initiating this process across the holobiont to support the development of effective mitigation strategies. To examine how different corals cope with acute heat stress, we compared responses of the expectedly more heat-tolerant Heliopora coerulea and the well-studied model species Stylophora pistillata. Both were exposed to a ramp from 25.5 °C to 32.5 °C over four days, followed by a three-day heat hold, and their photophysiology, holobiont proteomes and methanol-extractable metabolomes were analysed. Despite almost identical reductions in symbiont density and photochemical efficiency, the two species showed distinct molecular responses. Core thermal stress elements were conserved, including heat shock and unfolded protein responses, and both corals exhibited markers of increased host amino acid catabolism, β-oxidation, membrane and organelle lipid remodelling, and activation of immune pathways. However, S. pistillata showed signs of host mitochondrial dysfunction and energy deficit on multiple levels (potential mitochondria fragmentation, impairment of autophagy and proteolysis), and its symbionts displayed signatures of chloroplast damage (an increase in chlorophyll-related metabolites and more frequent chlorotic dinoflagellate cells), whereas H. coerulea largely maintained metabolic stability. These differences suggest that although both holobionts activated a shared stress response module, increasing photodamage and energy imbalance put S. pistillata at greater risk under prolonged warming. One possible explanation for H. coerulea’s paling without notable symbiont’s chlorosis may stem from a more effective regulation of its symbiont population. In contrast, S. pistillata’s response may reflect a later-stage symbiont-expulsion process triggered after algal impairment disrupts host homeostasis. These interpretations might offer a new route to enhance coral heat tolerance by strengthening host-driven symbiont regulation. Additionally, macroscopic bleaching readouts can mask divergent cellular responses, highlighting the need for diagnostic tools beyond visual paling and pulse-amplitude modulated fluorometry.
262b3e73: Coral reefs face severe threats from climate change, underlying the need to understand the mechanisms behind coral stress responses and resilience. Metabolomic approaches are increasingly being used to fill this knowledge gap. However, key challenges around sample preparation and throughput as well as metabolite coverage and annotation remain. We developed a high-throughput, comprehensive liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry pipeline for analyzing coral samples (host and algal symbiont), and generated baseline metabolomics data on the corals Galaxea fascicularis and Pocillopora acuta. Metabolic changes in the host and symbiont fraction were also monitored over three weeks (five timepoints) at ambient temperature (27 °C) or under a gradual increase in temperature from 27 °C to 32 °C. The elevated temperature treatment resulted in heat stress as confirmed by a decline in photosynthetic health, cell density, and protein concentrations. Unique metabolomic profiles were observed in both the heat-stressed coral host and symbiont, with changes in the host fraction being more pronounced. Heat stress led to changes in intermediates in host glycolysis and TCA cycle (including citrate, PEP, cis-aconitate and succinate). Alterations in metabolic fluxes were also detected by labelling coral polyps with heavy water, providing an orthogonal approach for assessing changes in coral central carbon metabolism in response to heat stress.
b543613e: Coral mucus is an important carbon source for the reef ecosystem, hosts a distinct suite of microorganisms, lubricates the coral surface, and aids heterotrophic feeding. Despite these roles, past research has focused on characterising mucus biochemical properties, including carbohydrate composition, lipid content, and bacterial community composition. However, despite the identification of mucin-like proteins nearly two decades ago, our knowledge of the proteins contained within the mucus remains poor. Here, we characterised the first mucosal proteome of Acropora millepora under control and thermal stress conditions using liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry.  Our results clearly distinguish the coral host tissue and mucus proteomes: 3179 proteins were detected in the mucus, including identification of mucin isomers. 1250 differentially abundant proteins that were enriched in control mucus vs. host tissues were putatively associated with immunity and homeostatic processes, such as mucociliary clearance across coral surfaces and detoxification. 1075 differentially abundant proteins that were enriched in thermally stressed mucus were similar to those in control mucus, again including those associated with ciliary clearance, as well as ion transmembrane transport. These proteins may form the ‘core mucosal proteome’ of A. millepora. However, other mucosal proteins that were specific to thermal stress were associated with inflammation and immunity, including chaperone proteins, detoxifying enzymes, macrophages, and immunomodulators of the NF-κB pathway. In addition, we found evidence of bacterial pathogenesis in thermally stressed mucus through the presence of mucin-degrading enzymes. This research emphasises the potential role that mucus plays in the innate immune immunity of scleractinian corals under control and thermal stress conditions, while providing a foundation for enhancing our understanding of the complex coral holobiont as we seek new tools to combat the ‘coral reef crisis'.
4aae7269: Coral reefs along the United Arab Emirates (UAE) coastline are subjected to extreme thermal stress and episodic turbidity pulses. However, the role of physical oceanographic forcing in shaping bleaching dynamics in this region remains understudied. To investigate the environmental controls on bleaching, we analyzed Copernicus satellite products (1998–2025) across six reef sites, using Secchi depth (ZSD) and diffuse attenuation (Kd490) as proxies for turbidity, and sea surface temperature (SST) as a thermal stress indicator. We hypothesize that mechanisms such as wind-driven resuspension, wave-induced sediment mobilization, and reduced vertical mixing mediate turbidity variability and, in turn, the underwater light environment experienced by corals by altering light penetration reaching their tissues. To enhance data reliability and avoid analytical artifacts, satellite retrievals were filtered using uncertainty thresholds of ≤30% for both ZSD and Kd490. Bleaching periods consistently aligned with reduced water clarity, with ZSD minima shallowing to 5–9 m. Site-specific extremes ranged from 5–7.5 m at Delta Reef and 5–7 m at Ras Ghanada, to 5–12.5 m across northern reefs. Kd490 commonly exceeded 0.127m-1 during bleaching (0.12–0.18 m-1), especially at Delta Reef, Drinkel Shoal, and Ras Ghanada. More moderate turbidity (0.080–0.127 m-1), which may reduce light stress and partially buffer bleaching under moderate temperatures, was more frequent prior to 2010. SSTs at bleaching ranged from 32–35°C and approached 36°C during severe events, supporting prior evidence that any protective turbidity effect collapses under extreme heat. Decadal patterns show an increase in high-temperature and high-turbidity anomalies, suggesting a reduced refuge potential in naturally turbid environments as summer maxima intensify. These findings underscore the need for Gulf-specific monitoring frameworks that integrate turbidity and thermal thresholds, uncertainty screening, and oceanographic drivers such as wind, waves, and water-column stratification when assessing bleaching susceptibility indicators in the hottest coral reef system.
89a7362e: Ocean warming driven by climate change poses a growing threat to the long-term resilience of coral reefs. The persistence of coral populations under rising temperatures depends partly on their ability to adapt to new thermal regimes. Reef connectivity may facilitate the spread of adaptive traits, such as enhanced heat tolerance, through larval exchange between environmentally distinct reefs. The Red Sea provides an ideal natural system to test this hypothesis, as its fringing reefs span a pronounced latitudinal gradient with substantial variation in temperature conditions. Here, we simulated larval dispersal among 3,150 reefs across the Red Sea in 2019 using a biophysical connectivity model to assess how larval exchange affects thermal stress thresholds (TSTs) and consequent bleaching susceptibility. We examined whether connectivity can enhance thermal resistance, and connectivity-modulated bleaching metrics can improve bleaching prediction patterns along the basin. Our results show that incorporating connectivity into TST calculations virtually increased thermal tolerance in northern Red Sea sites (Max. ΔTST = +0.5 °C), while decreasing it in many central and southern sites (Max. ΔTST = –0.4 °C). These changes translated into an 86% and 54% reduction in bleaching alerts per reef site, based on Degree Heating Week (DHW) values, for reefs in the Gulfs of Aqaba and Suez, respectively. These findings suggest that connectivity from warmer to cooler reefs can modestly enhance TST estimates, particularly in the northern Red Sea, a region often considered a potential climate refugium. However, connectivity-modulated predictions were not able to fully explain the spatial patterns of bleaching observed across the Red Sea system. Therefore, our findings suggest that larval connectivity can potentially enhance coral resistance, but this effect may operate over longer ecological and evolutionary timescales than those captured within our simulation framework.
4f9176bc: The Red Sea hosts coral reefs that persist under some of the warmest ocean conditions globally, supporting high biodiversity and providing essential ecosystem services to surrounding coastal communities. Meanwhile, major development initiatives in Saudi Arabia (e.g. NEOM and the Red Sea Project) have placed growing emphasis on conservation and large-scale coral restoration. Yet, reef building corals are increasingly threatened by climate change-induced coral bleaching, which has led to significant coral cover loss during recent major bleaching events. To that end, radical interventions like regional shading and localized cooling to mitigate thermal stress have been proposed as potential solutions. As a tool to test the impact of such interventions, we propose a mechanistic bioenergetic model that simulates coral-symbiont physiological traits at the colony scale, as well as symbiosis breakdown under stressful conditions. Based on the Dynamic Energy Budget (DEB) theory, the model simulates coral bleaching dynamics considering temperature, light irradiance, nutrients and prey availability. We parametrized this DEB model using physiological data from literature (photosynthesis rate, seasonal zooxanthellae density, nutrient and prey uptake) for common Red Sea species (Stylophora Pistillata) and evaluated it against bleaching field observations, showing good agreement in the Red Sea. Following model evaluation, we used the model to test various scenarios of shading and cooling, applied individually or in combination, that were hypothetically designed to prevent the 2024 bleaching event in the northern Red Sea. By linking physiological rates to environmental variability, the model becomes a useful tool for such applications. Beyond intervention testing, the DEB model allows for the investigation of species-specific bleaching dynamics. Since the analysis can be expanded to other species, it can potentially provide an enhanced understanding of observed differences in bleaching dynamics among species.
f9d62bea: Effective marine spatial planning is often constrained by limited ecological data and the need to balance diverse conservation priorities across rapidly changing seascapes. In the Red Sea, research has historically focused on highly localised, in-depth studies, leaving gaps in broad-scale conservation insights. This study develops a large-scale, low-resolution spatial framework to identify priority conservation areas. We integrate species’ habitat suitability with anthropogenic pressures and then evaluate priority regions under a range of conservation frameworks. We compiled an updated inventory of fish species in the Saudi Red Sea and constructed environmental envelopes for each species using depth, habitat type (coral reefs, mangroves, seagrass beds, and soft sediments), salinity, temperature, and primary productivity. This produced spatially explicit, species-level relative probabilities of occurrence (0-1). Multiple conservation scenarios were explored to reflect different regional priorities, including species of concern (IUCN Red List), absolute biodiversity (species richness), and phylogenetic uniqueness. Anthropogenic pressures – fisheries, climate change, and coastal development – were incorporated through a set of standardized indices. By integrating environmental envelopes, conservation frameworks, and anthropogenic pressures, we (1) mapped consensus areas of high conservation priority, (2) identified key biodiversity regions that remain underrepresented in current research, and (3) highlighted mismatches between existing marine protected areas and regional conservation needs. Our framework demonstrates how low-resolution, yet integrative spatial approaches can inform conservation planning in data-poor regions such as the Red Sea, providing an accessible tool for regional authorities and marine spatial planners in other data-poor seascapes. The framework is transparent and easily updated, allowing decision makers to explore trade-offs among alternative conservation goals as new data become available.
77ea1a2e: The Red Sea supports coral assemblages that tolerate some of the highest seawater temperatures recorded for reef systems, yet species-level biodiversity data remain limited at the national scale. This study presents initial outcomes from a Saudi national coral conservation program led by SHAMS, which conducted the first comprehensive species-level coral assessment along the eastern Red Sea coast. Standardized in situ surveys were combined with IUCN Red List categories and endemicity records to establish a national baseline for evaluating coral diversity patterns and conservation status. Coral communities displayed clear spatial variation related to depth, shelf morphology, and reef geomorphology. Across reef sites, areas with high species richness often coincided with the presence of globally threatened taxa and were typically dominated by Acroporidae. These sites represent important reservoirs of coral diversity but also show elevated susceptibility to thermal stress and physical disturbance. In contrast, other sites supported more simplified assemblages that appear adapted to local environmental conditions, reflecting variable ecological responses across the region. Endemic species were recorded throughout the study area, highlighting the biogeographic distinctiveness of the Red Sea and providing useful indicators for conservation planning. The resulting dataset consolidates previously fragmented information into a coherent national framework that captures spatial patterns of diversity and vulnerability. Identifying locations where biodiversity value overlaps with ecological sensitivity enables clearer prioritization for management, protected area design, and long-term monitoring. This baseline provides foundational information for Saudi Arabia’s marine spatial planning and contributes to national 30×30 targets. It also offers a reference for assessing coral resilience and biodiversity trends across the wider Red Sea.
389682a4: Coral-associated microbiomes are increasingly being recognised as key drivers of reef-building corals' health, resilience, and adaptive capacity to environmental change. While most research has focused on the microbiomes of scleractinian corals, far less attention has been given to octocorals, despite their ecological importance. In particular, the microbiomes associated with reef-building octocorals in the Red Sea remain largely unexplored. Here, we characterize the bacterial and Symbiodiniaceae communities associated with two endemic species of the hermatypic octocoral genus Tubipora in the central Saudi Arabian Red Sea. Using high-throughput sequencing of the bacterial 16S rRNA gene and the Symbiodiniaceae ITS2 region across 82 colonies collected at three reef sites ranging 0 to 30 m depth, we found that microbial assemblages were broadly similar between the two Tubipora species. However, combined species-depth effects significantly influenced both bacterial and algal community compositions, with remarkable species-specificity of ITS2 type profiles. Moreover, Tubipora hosted distinct bacterial assemblages compared to co-occurring scleractinian and octocorals species in the same region. Our findings provide the first insights into the Tubipora-associated microbiomes in shallow-water coral reefs of the Saudi Arabian Red Sea. While enriching the inventory of coral-associated bacteria from the basin, this study establishes a baseline for understanding octocoral holobionts in the area and their potential adaptive strategies in response to climate change.
1cd08f0c: The northern Saudi Arabian Red Sea coast is characterized by heterogeneous, interconnected benthic habitats, including coral reefs, seagrass beds, and unconsolidated substrate, all structured by depth and complex spatial metrics. Achieving Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 goals requires a foundational understanding of these ecological seascapes for long-term viability; therefore, collecting baseline data on fish populations, assemblage structure, and anthropogenic pressures across multiple strata remains essential. In 2024, a series of 228 baited remote underwater video (BRUV) deployments in northern Saudi Arabia documented 16,424 individual fishes across 58 families and 292 species, with 90% identified to species level. Coral habitats exhibited the highest predatory and overall species richness, whereas sand habitats supported moderate richness and several unique and threatened elasmobranchs. Overall species richness was also significantly higher in 10–30 meters (m) versus 0–10 m or >30 m strata. The analysis of predatory and commercially targeted families (e.g., emperors, snappers, groupers, jacks, sharks) showed the highest relative abundances between 0–40 m. Lethrinidae abundance was highest between 0–10 m but remained markedly higher between 10–30 m than in deeper strata. Lutjanidae and Epinephelidae showed similar abundance peaks between 10-30 m; however, Epinephelidae were consistently present across all sampled depths, extending to 60−70 m. Carangidae maintained a broad presence across 0–40 m, with abundances peaking in 0–10 and 20–30 m strata, while Carcharhinidae abundances were widespread but low between 20−50 m. Distance from fish landing sites was strongly positively correlated with species richness, abundance, and predatory biomass, revealing greater fishing pressure near access points and high recovery potential in remote areas. Integrating a 2023 dataset, predictive modelling identified priority zones for possible spatial conservation, underscoring the need for multi-year, habitat-specific, depth-stratified, and trophic-level analysis, with future refinements incorporating 2025 and historical 2020 datasets.
991d0e11: Loss of habitat may reduce niche availability and force sympatric species into competition. Stable isotope analysis can help to quantify trophic niches and identify partitioning strategies in sympatric species with similar ecologies. For instance, the bluespotted ray Taeniura lymma (IUCN: Least Concern) and honeycomb whipray Himantura uarnak (IUCN: Endangered), two functionally similar species, co-occur in the shallow waters and mangrove systems of the Saudi Arabian Red Sea. Both are benthic bioturbators, using mangroves and neighboring sand and reef habitats as sheltered feeding grounds. While these species broadly overlap in their functional niches, the fine-scale niche partitioning preventing competition is effectively unstudied. This study addresses this gap by specifically focusing on their respective trophic niches, collecting white-muscle tissue from local T. lymma and H. uarnak off of Thuwal, Saudi Arabia and investigating their diets via stable isotope analysis (SIA). Samples from each species undergo urea and lipid extraction (ULE) tests to determine the influence of these treatments on δ¹³C and δ¹⁵N values and are standardized to account for these biases. The pre-treated muscle samples, along with specimens from locally available prey, were processed and analyzed to estimate trophic position (TP), dietary contribution of prey (DCP), isotopic niche width (INW), and isotopic niche overlap (INO) to get a holistic view of each species’ diet and better understanding of the relationship between these co-occurring Red Sea stingrays. This study offers a local glimpse into interspecific dietary partitioning and will help inform conservation of interconnected nearshore habitats to protect these rays in a rapidly changing world.
1ac8a8af:
Coral reefs of the Arabian Peninsula, including the Red Sea and adjacent Arabian seas, thrive under some of the warmest, saltiest, and most nutrient-poor conditions of any reef system. Most Red Sea fisheries depend on reef-associated fishes, yet the diversity, distribution, and evolutionary history of these fishes are still being revised. This setting offers an opportunity to use reef fishes as indicators of regional adaptation to environmental extremes that resemble likely coral reef futures worldwide.

This presentation will synthesize recent work on the diversity and biogeography of Red Sea and Arabian reef fishes, emphasizing new and revised species, high endemism, and faunal breaks between the Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, and western Indian Ocean. Updated checklists, survey data, and depth records show that lineages that tolerate the Red Sea often have restricted distributions, ecological specialization, or strong genetic structure (albeit with interesting exceptions). Emerging comparative genomic data from butterflyfishes, groupers, and related taxa illustrate how demographic history and selection across thermal and salinity gradients can be separated, and how this history shapes population connectivity and trait variation.

Building on this foundation, the talk will introduce a framework for in-versus-out-of-Red-Sea comparisons that explicitly incorporates the region's deep-reef communities, which remain even more poorly known than shallow reefs. Depth-stratified fish assemblages inside and outside the basin, linked to seascape structure and targeted genomics, can test whether the Red Sea functions as a source, sink, or refugium under climate change. Because many Red Sea lineages appear largely isolated from the broader Indian Ocean, these results inform discussions of reef-fish fisheries management, marine protected area design, and the definition of management units within the Red Sea. Integrating shallow and deep reef fishes into such plans will be important if Arabian coral ecosystems are to retain their unique biodiversity and continue supporting coastal societies.
49fbad01: Seagrass meadows and coral reefs are ecologically and economically valuable coastal ecosystems that are functionally linked via nutrient transport, nursery habitat functions, hydrodynamic regulation, and prey subsidies. Seagrass invertebrate communities in particular serve as a key conduit for energy transfer from primary producers to higher-level consumers and often support many predatory coral reef-associated fish (e.g. grunts) that periodically leave reef habitats to forage in the adjacent seagrass beds. Yet, despite their critical role in strengthening seascape connectivity through trophic linkages, tropical seagrass invertebrate communities are largely understudied and undervalued. Consequently, seagrass meadows remain underrepresented in Sanctuary Preservation Areas (SPAs) within the Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary (FKNMS). Here, we assess seagrass habitat structure and benthic invertebrate community composition across the FKNMS to increase our understanding of the proximate drivers shaping seagrass habitat quality and invertebrate prey availability to coral reef-associated predators. Preliminary analyses suggest that seagrass structure (i.e., canopy height and shoot density) in the lower and middle regions of the FKNMS were strong predictors of invertebrate biomass and secondary production, indicating that taller, more dense meadows support higher prey availability for coral reef-associated predators. However, this relationship was not detected in the upper region, suggesting that other factors (i.e., water quality, predation pressure, patterns in recruitment) may serve to obscure the influence of habitat structure. More broadly, our results provide evidence for spatial variation in invertebrate biomass and secondary production across the FKNMS regions. Currently, seagrass and coral reef ecosystems alike are experiencing declines due to rapidly changing environmental conditions. Therefore, understanding the drivers of variation in invertebrate prey will be crucial to predict how functional connectivity between the two habitats may shift in the future.
096eee49: Coral reefs persist in inshore turbid environments, yet their adaptive feeding strategies and nutrient sources remain poorly understood. As climate change and coastal development accelerate turbidity in nearshore waters worldwide, understanding these adaptive feeding strategies has become urgent for predicting how inshore reef ecosystems will persist. The Pilbara coastline of Western Australia offers a rare natural laboratory—a gradient of turbidity and anthropogenic influence where coral communities persist despite harsh environmental conditions including fluctuating turbidity. Using compound-specific stable isotope analysis (CSIA), we investigate the trophic dynamics and strategies of four ecologically important coral genera (Acropora, Turbinaria, Favites, and Porites) across three nearshore sites within Exmouth Gulf. This research assesses trophic strategies and connectivity between coral host and symbiont and focuses on potential nutritional sources from adjacent intertidal habitats, including productive cyanobacterial mats. By identifying the trophic sources that sustain these reef communities, we can better inform conservation needs for both inshore reefs and their adjacent intertidal habitats, which face increasing pressures from coastal development.
8b6e8add: Nursery habitats play a key role in the replenishment of fish populations, yet the contribution of macroalgal habitats to this function remains poorly quantified. Previous work on juvenile density and habitat associations shows that macroalgal habitats can support higher juvenile densities than adjacent coral, seagrass or non-vegetated habitats, particularly where canopies are structurally complex, reinforcing their potential nursery role. However, these studies rarely quantify growth and survival, the key performance metrics of nursery habitats. To address this gap, we conducted a global systematic review and Bayesian causal meta-analysis to assess the role of macroalgal habitats as nurseries, with a specific focus on their effects on juvenile fish growth and survival. We also analysed manipulative field experiments that altered macroalgal structure and measured juvenile density and recruitment. Macroalgal habitats were likely associated with enhanced growth and survival relative to alternatives, suggesting genuine nursery potential. Nonetheless, uncertainty remains in the direction and magnitude of the effects due to substantial geographic and taxonomic biases, and the prevalence of single-habitat designs that limit side-by-side comparison. We recommend that future work prioritise comparative studies of growth and survival across habitat types—especially in tropical reef seascapes—along with experimental manipulation of nursery habitat structure and explicit integration of habitat connectivity. Together, these advances will clarify when and where macroalgal meadows most effectively contribute to recruitment and resilience of reef fish populations.
6196a8a7: Coral reefs exist within an interconnected mosaic of habitats, and reef fish rely on this habitat mosaic for foraging. Identifying areas within the habitat mosaic of high resource quality can improve the design and inform the location of marine protected areas (MPA). However, due to the reliance of multiple energetic resources, it can be difficult to identify areas within the seascape with high resource quality, especially since these areas can differ between species. In this study, we constructed energetic resource landscapes, or E-scapes, for four mesoconsumer reef fish to identify areas of high resource quality across the Florida Keys. E-scapes combine stable isotope analysis and habitat maps to generate consumer specific maps of energetic resource distribution and trophic function. We found that for all species, both reefs and shallow water seagrass beds displayed high trophic function, but this was not the case for deeper seagrass beds near reefs. Results from this study indicate that not all seagrass beds provide the same energetic resources for reef fish, and this should be incorporated into MPA design.
cd849f88: Across tropical coastal seascapes, escalating climatic and anthropogenic stressors are degrading biogenic habitats (e.g., mangroves, seagrasses, and corals), with ramifications for species that rely on multiple habitat types and their inter-connectedness. Many coastal fishes perform ontogenetic habitat shifts, transitioning from nearshore nurseries to adult habitats on offshore coral reefs. Despite their central roles in supporting ecosystem health, the habitat and connectivity needs of these fishes are rarely considered in site selection for restoration or conservation. Using the Florida Keys, USA, as a case study, we examined and compared the drivers of habitat use for five habitat-shifting fishes from two feeding guilds: mesopredators (gray snapper [Lutjanus griseus] and bluestriped grunt [Haemulon sciurus]) and herbivores (blue parrotfish [Scarus coeruleus], midnight parrotfish [Scarus coelestinus], and rainbow parrotfish [Scarus guacamaia]). We then predicted habitat suitability across the region to assess current protective and restorative management zones. Benthic habitat type was the strongest driver, with patch- and aggregate-reefs emerging as the most suitable habitats across species. Nearshore habitats were more suitable for mesopredators, while the offshore reef tract was most suitable for herbivores—apart from rainbow parrotfish, which preferred mid-channel patch reefs. Most species pairs exhibited significantly less spatial overlap than randomized expectations, demonstrating spatial partitioning even among species with similar diets and preferred habitat types. All management zones were more suitable than the region-wide average, with restorative zones outperforming protective zones. Although our results confirm the importance of patch reefs for subadults of these species, they are underrepresented in current management practices. These findings highlight the need for connected habitat corridors that span nearshore–offshore gradients, and encourage practitioners to incorporate habitat suitability into site selection for management efforts. To maximize ecosystem benefits, future work should consider the habitat and connectivity needs of a broader range of species and functional groups.
0d3d7a7c: Estuaries are widely recognized as essential juvenile habitats for numerous fish species, yet the foundational ecological studies supporting this concept primarily originate from temperate continental systems, limiting our understanding of insular estuaries adjacent to coral reefs. Here we investigated the habitat use of juvenile fishes in an insular setting, using seven years of monthly baited remote underwater stereo-video (BRUV) surveys in the Wailoa River estuary in Hawai‘i. The structure of fish assemblage as a whole was analyzed based on the MaxN metric and Bray-Curtis dissimilarity, confirming statistically significant temporal shifts and a pronounced cyclical seasonal pattern in the assemblage structure. Of the 30 taxa recorded, 10 consistently utilized the habitat, including the endemic Kuhlia xenura and the introduced Lutjanus fulvus, as well as several other culturally important reef-associated species. Size-distribution analysis demonstrated that the median lengths of key reef fish species (Acanthurus triostegus, Acanthurus blochii and Mulloidichthys flavolineatus) peaked in summer, indicating that these species rely on the estuary for a transient, developmental phase before emigrating to coral reef and other nearshore marine habitats. Temporal changes in the abundances of native and introduced mullets (Mugil cephalus and Osteomugil engeli), as well as some changes in the size distribution and abundances of other species over time, also indicated potential ecological changes specific to the BRUV deployment site, prompting the need for further investigations into the habitat use and partitioning by these different species within the estuary. Our findings revealed regular reliance of juvenile fish, including reef fish species, on insular estuarine habitat, emphasizing that effective coral reef management should consider the important functional role of estuary-reef connectivity.
e2adcef4:
Coral reefs function within mosaics of interconnected habitats, where mobile consumers create critical trophic linkages by transferring energy and nutrients across ecosystem boundaries. White grunt (Haemulon plumierii), an important species for local fisheries in Florida and the Caribbean, exemplifies this connectivity by sheltering on patch reefs during daytime and foraging nocturnally in adjacent seagrass beds. Understanding how seascape configuration influences these cross-habitat trophic pathways is essential for holistic reef management. We used DNA metabarcoding to characterize the diet composition of the White Grunt across nine patch reefs varying in seagrass continuity and connectivity in the Florida Keys.
Stomach contents from 90 white grunt (10 per reef) were analyzed using COI gene metabarcoding (Leray primers, mlCOIintF/jgHCO2198) targeting a 313 bp fragment, sequenced on Illumina NovaSeq 6000. After quality filtering and host DNA removal (21.4%), we retained 331 Exact Sequence Variants (ESVs) from 9.3 million reads.
Diet analysis revealed remarkable prey diversity spanning 15 phyla, 25 classes, 43 orders, and 70 families, demonstrating extensive utilization of seagrass-associated invertebrate communities. Malacostraca (crustaceans, 26.8% Relative Read Abundance) and Polychaeta (bristle worms, 23.3%) dominated prey composition, followed by Trematoda (10.8%), Actinopteri (ray-finned fishes, 7.8%), and Ophiuroidea (brittle stars, 7.8%). Despite significant variation in fish size among reefs (Kruskal-Wallis H=26.09, p=0.001), diet richness and Shannon diversity did not differ significantly among reef patches (H=7.66, p=0.47 and H=10.11, p=0.26), suggesting consistent prey availability across the seascape.
Future analyses will integrate seascape variables (seagrass fragmentation, edge habitat, reef connectivity) to evaluate how habitat configuration influences cross-habitat energy flow. These findings provide baseline data on reef-seagrass trophic linkages mediated by this ecologically and commercially important mesopredator, informing multi-habitat management strategies for interconnected coastal seascapes.
09831ad3: Increased anthropogenic impacts are degrading global coral reef ecosystems. Following disturbances, the skeleton of scleractinian coral can fragment into coral rubble, which can then accumulate to form coral rubble beds. Despite being comprised of dead stony corals, the interstitial space in the matrix of the coral rubble can provide a habitat for a variety of marine organisms, yet their functional role for juvenile reef fishes is not currently considered. This study examines how juvenile reef fishes exploit the structural complexity of coral rubble and evaluates the extent to which dead coral frameworks support early life-stage assemblages. Using replicated stationary video analysis from the Turks and Caicos Islands, we quantified juvenile fish abundance, species richness, and diversity in coral rubble beds and live coral habitats. Small-bodied Labridae, Acanthuridae, and Scaridae were the most abundant in the coral rubble beds, whilst Pomacentridae, small-bodied Labridae, and Scaridae were the most prevalent on live coral reefs. Over 70% of species that have distinguishable juveniles had more juvenile individuals observed than adults in coral rubble beds. This result demonstrates that juvenile reef fishes utilise interstitial spaces within the dead coral matrix as shelter and highlights the importance of rubble as a physical refuge. Our findings demonstrate that coral rubble beds provide critical structural habitat for juvenile fishes and can temporarily maintain recruitment functions following disturbance. Recognising the ecological value of rubble frameworks is essential for post-disturbance reef management, restoration planning, and assessments of habitat resilience.
fe22d74e:
Seagrass ecosystems in the Florida Keys National Marine Sanctuary (FKNMS), USA, provide critical foraging habitat for reef-associated fish communities, yet a comprehensive understanding of spatial patterns and cross-habitat linkages for mesopredator and upper predator diversity remains limited. The FISHSCAPE project aims to answer how much seagrass is required to support different foraging reef fishes across connected coastal seascapes. As part of this project, we assessed the fish assemblage of FKNMS using  Baited Remote Underwater Video Stations (BRUVS), across 102 deployments in the Upper, Middle, and Lower Keys, examining relationships with depth, temperature, turbidity, distance to shore and reef, reef connectivity (100m, 250m, 500m buffers), seagrass continuity, and fishing pressure (gravity index).
We documented 95 species with a predicted richness of 120 species. Upper Keys presented significantly lower species richness (~9 species) than Middle and Lower Keys (~12-13 species, p<0.001). The Middle Keys exhibited highest diversity (~2.1 H', p<0.001). Dominant species included Yellowtail Snapper, White Grunt, Yellow Jack, Pinfish, and Blue Runner. Great Barracuda was the most common predator (60% occurrence). Seven shark species were documented, highlighting seagrass importance for upper trophic levels.
Boosted Regression Tree analysis revealed depth (46% relative influence) and distance to shore (16%) as primary drivers of species richness. Yellowtail Snapper abundance was most influenced by distance to shore (56% relative influence). White Grunt responded to distance to reef (25%) and reef connectivity at 500m buffer (17%), while Great Barracuda showed responses to distance to shore (22%) and reef connectivity (15%). This study demonstrates BRUVS' effectiveness for assessing seagrass-reef linkages through fish diversity patterns, particularly for mobile predators utilizing multiple habitats. These findings, which include mesopredators of high relevance for local fisheries, provide crucial data for evaluating future changes and can inform holistic multi-habitat management strategies that recognize the drivers of non-cryptic fish abundance in tropical seascapes.
2236bc70: Climate change stressors such as ocean warming, acidification and deoxygenation are severely threatening coral reefs and the vital ecosystem services they provide. Corals found to survive in mangrove habitats that naturally possess stressful conditions, are being increasingly studied to investigate the impact of multiple co-occurring stressors on coral growth. However, the water quality within mangrove-coral habitats and how this changes with varying distance from the mangroves remains largely unknown. We used the Foraminifera in Reef Assessment and Monitoring Index (FORAM Index) to assess the suitability of environments for coral growth across a mangrove to reef gradient. Sediment samples were collected from five sites at varying proximity to the Low Isles mangroves in the northern Great Barrier Reef. Sites were located: amongst the inner mangroves, the mangrove fringe, the adjacent leeward and windward reef margins and at Opal Reef (control) 38 km away from the mangroves. Foraminiferal assemblages were used to determine the environmental suitability for coral growth and recovery. The poorest seawater conditions for coral occurred at the inner mangrove site with water quality improving outside the mangroves. Leeward and windward margin sites differed in their suitability for coral growth despite being a similar distance from the mangroves, indicating that water conditions were not necessarily constrained by proximity to mangroves alone. These findings have important implications for studies investigating mangrove-coral habitats as refugia and for assessing linkages between coral reefs and mangroves – two highly threatened ecosystems.
f33963b7: Coral reefs are vital marine ecosystems that support tourism, fisheries, and various ecosystem services. Their complicated designs provide habitats, feeding grounds, and nursery areas for numerous marine species, resulting in high biodiversity and significantly contributing to the livelihoods of coastal communities. However, coral reefs are increasingly degrading due to a combination of natural factors and human activities. Notably, rising sea surface temperatures have triggered severe coral bleaching events in many regions, which points to the importance of effective and continuous ecological monitoring. Citizen science has emerged as a practical and inclusive approach to coral reef monitoring, allowing public participation in scientific data collection under expert supervision. This method improves the coverage of spatial data, lowers the costs of monitoring, and makes participants more aware of the environment. In this study, a citizen science framework was implemented in Trang Province along Thailand’s Andaman coast in collaboration with SCUBA dive operators. Divers received pre-dive training on reef health assessment, biodiversity recording, and invasive species detection. After each dive, participants submitted written observations, photographs, and videos through operators for expert validation. Results indicated that volunteer-generated datasets were highly accurate and reliable, achieving a validation score of 97 percent. The program also successfully documented coral bleaching events in 2024, demonstrating its ability to detect ecological changes across time and space. The study highlights that citizen science-based coral monitoring is effective only when researchers, SCUBA operators, and the diving community collaborate closely. The collected data can support coral reef management, ecological restoration planning, and evidence-based policy development at local and national levels. This study shows that citizen science is a useful tool for protecting coral reefs. It also gives power to local communities, helps marine-resource economies, and encourages responsible and sustainable ecotourism.
cbd86c33: Coral reefs in Mu Ko Surin are under increasing climate-driven and local ecological pressures, having endured major disturbances including the 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami and the 2010 and 2024 regional mass coral-bleaching events, which together caused extreme coral mortality exceeding 90% of benthic colonies, leading to severe reef-structural degradation and posing persistent challenges to long-term resistance, ecosystem persistence, and natural recovery trajectories. This study assessed coral reef resilience across shallow reef habitats of Mu Ko Surin using the Coral Reef Resilience Index (CRRI), which integrates critical ecological indicators including live coral cover, non-living coral proportion, bleaching resistance, coral recovery capacity, algal cover, substrate suitability for coral recruitment, coral health (disease presence), and functional fish biomass. Reef surveys were conducted across representative sites in the Surin Islands, and resilience scores were calculated through weighted normalization of indicator contributions to generate a composite CRRI value for each reef. The highest CRRI score was recorded at Ao Suthep, demonstrating strong resistance to bleaching and rapid post-disturbance stabilization, while the lowest CRRI score was observed at Hin Phrae, reflecting reduced bleaching tolerance, lower recruitment suitability, and greater ecological competition from algae and zoanthids. Stress-tolerant coral families, most notably Poritidae and Faviidae contributed positively to resistance capacity, while sites dominated by persistent algal competition exhibited structurally constrained recovery trajectories and weaker larval settlement feedbacks. The findings highlight that coral resilience indexing can be applied as an evidence-based tool for reef monitoring, priority site selection for conservation action, and long-term adaptive management planning. This study provides a proposed CRRI benchmarking for Mu Ko Surin, supporting Thailand’s coral-focused marine biodiversity resilience assessments and national reef sustainability strategies.
7b167063: Coral reefs are highly productive, biodiverse ecosystems that support coasts, fisheries, and reef tourism, yet are increasingly threatened by ocean warming, pollution, and coastal development. Shallow reef flats are key coral reef habitats with high hard-coral diversity, driving reef growth and structural complexity that support many reef-associated species. Corals from the shallow reef flat are resilient and adapted to survive in environments with high temperatures, intense light, and high ultraviolet radiation. This study aims to establish a prototype coral nursery for reef restoration by selecting stress-resistant coral fragments from shallow reef flats, and to use the nursery as a key learning resource for promoting sustainable marine ecotourism.
