Coral fragments from dominant species (Porites lutea, Pocillopora acuta, and Dipsastraea favus) were selected to evaluate growth performance on dome-shaped nursery units deployed at Ko Rang Kachiu, Chumphon Province, in the Western Gulf of Thailand. Each coral fragment was collected from the shallow reef flat and reef slope to compare the survival and growth rate on the dome-shaped nursery of transplant. The results showed high overall coral fragment survival rates, reaching 96.9% in Porites lutea, 93.2% in Pocillopora acuta, and 82.7% in Dipsastraea favus. Additionally, fragments of these species on the shallow reef flat exhibited higher growth rates than fragments from the reef slope. Following transplantation, a major coral bleaching event occurred in 2024, during which fragments sourced from shallow reef flats exhibited only 5% bleaching incidence and showed stronger thermal tolerance than those from deeper reef slopes. The findings demonstrate that corals from shallow reef flat hold strong resilience value for climate-adaptive reef restoration. Coral nurseries also function as ecotourism learning hubs, boosting awareness and stewardship among visitors and local stakeholders, reinforcing long-term sustainable reef-tourism engagement.
4f2951b0:
Local scale site stewardship has been practiced in the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park by tourism operators for several decades. Early efforts were unidimensional and often reactive to respond to immediate disturbance such as crown-of-thorns starfish outbreaks. Today site stewardship efforts have evolved into a pro-active multi-dimensional approach to combat multiple and accumulative disturbances driven by anthropogenic climate change. I will present several examples of a site stewardship framework in practice that highlights the critical steps to deliver efforts to assist reef resilience, protect diversity and the people that rely on coral reefs. 

The principal step is developing a high frequency monitoring program to create intimate knowledge of multiple reef habitats. The long-term data is used to show trends in disturbance and recovery cycles across reef habitats. Secondly we created a functional decision tree with the Traditional Custodians of the Great Barrier Reef that informs decisions on if and what site assisted recovery actions are required. Thirdly, building partnerships with Traditional Owners, scientific researchers, and government management agencies combines Traditional Ecological knowledge with modern science and policy in our decision making. This delivers a holistic approach to monitoring and site stewardship activities at high value tourism sites.

Informed decision making is a critical in selecting positive actions to promote diversity and enhance climate resilience. Examples of ongoing site assisted recovery actions include juvenile coral predator management, coral rubble stabilisation, and coral larval reseeding to enhance local reef resilience at a high standard tourism site. Ongoing research provides further understanding of local resilience and evaluates the effectiveness of site assisted recovery actions. The work will show that Great Barrier Reef tourism provides a great platform to collaborate with multiple reef stakeholders and to showcase local action to a global audience that can lead to global conservation of coral reefs.
c344fd36:
Coral reef tourism is often associated with negative ecological impacts due to high human pressure. However, when guided by sustainable management strategies, tourism may contribute positively to reef biodiversity. In Rangko, Indonesia, coral reef tourism has recently emerged as an alternative livelihood, using a community-based approach to facilitate snorkeling activities while promoting conservation goals.
This study applied a two-tiered assessment, first evaluating ongoing reef tourism practices using the Other Effective Area-Based Conservation Measures (OECM) framework, followed by participatory ecological monitoring at four reef sites (Batu Dua, Toroh Mirah, Gusoh Bendera, and Pasir Timbul) to assess the ecological outcomes of community-based reef tourism. Coral cover, coral breakage, and tourism practices were recorded to evaluate the effectiveness of community-led management interventions.
Results indicate that community-based coral reef tourism in Rangko demonstrates the foundational elements of OECM, including area-based management, opportunities for functional local governance, low-impact tourism practices, and strong community support. Ecological monitoring revealed that the community-managed site in Batu Dua had the highest hard coral cover (54%) and moderate coral damage (8%), while the unmanaged site had a lower coral cover and higher levels of breakage, for example, Pasir Timbul recorded 19% coral breakage.
Taken together, these findings suggest that community-based reef tourism in Rangko not only aligns with OECM criteria and may be positioned toward potential OECM recognition, but also functions as an effective approach to in-situ biodiversity conservation, underscoring the significance of ecological monitoring and active local stewardship.
2b09f6b6:
Gili Matra, the small-islands marine protected area in Lombok, Indonesia, serves as a critical area where coral reefs form the ecological foundation and function as key economic assets, particularly for tourism and fisheries. This study applies the ocean accounting framework to examine how spatial changes in ecosystem condition translate into economic outcomes, helping identify management priorities that strengthen resilience and support long-term livelihoods.
Applying geospatial imagery for the data 2015 and 2021, and field surveys in 2021 coupled with economic valuation utilising travel cost and effect on production methods, this study reveals the ecosystem dynamics. The coral reef ecosystem remains central for tourism and fisheries, yet ecosystem conditions reveal important shifts. Of the 259.50 ha recorded in 2015, only 235.50 ha remained by 2021, with 8.25 ha converting to seagrass and 15.75 ha shifting to other substrates. 
Despite this decline, these reefs remain a significant source of economic benefits to the community, bringing in an estimated IDR 856 billion every year. Most of that value comes from provisioning value in around IDR 851 billion and cultural value around IDR 22 billion. This high number highlights the community’s heavy reliance on the ecosystem, creating a tough cycle. Economic activities inevitably generate pressures, such as solid and liquid waste, that can further degrade reef conditions and undermine the values that support the local livelihoods. The COVID-19 pandemic exposed additional vulnerabilities, as reduced tourism amplified dependence on fisheries, tightening the connection between ecosystem health and community well-being.
Overall, the findings confirm that reef value, resource use, and governance interact within a dynamic socio-ecological system. As ecosystem shifts alter risks and pressures, adaptive and sustained management becomes essential to protect habitats and secure long-term livelihoods in Gili Matra.
5a4a314c: The tourism industry is central to the Caribbean economy, with reef-based activities like scuba diving playing a significant role. Climate change poses serious threats to marine ecosystems, prompting a shift toward marine eco-tourism, which is a business model that seeks to integrate sustainability into tourism operations. While the general concept of eco-tourism has been studied since the 1960s, marine eco-tourism, particularly in the Caribbean, remains underexplored despite its economic importance. This study presents a comprehensive analysis of the environmental, economic, and social impacts of marine eco-tourism in the Caribbean, with a special focus on the scuba diving industry through the Green Fins membership. The study employed a mixed-methods approach, combining a literature review and conducting interviews with dive shops that were Green Fins members in Mexico, Honduras, Bonaire, and the Dominican Republic. Results revealed both positive and negative impacts of marine eco-tourism and the Green Fins membership on the sustainability dimensions: environmental, economic, and social. Positives of marine eco-tourism included the development of marine protected areas, education and awareness of marine ecology, citizen science, implementation of a sustainable business model, employment opportunities, preservation of local heritage, and the overall improvement of local livelihood. Negatives of marine eco-tourism included the degradation of marine ecosystems, tourism carbon footprint, economic vulnerability of the tourism industry, economic leakage, cultural commodification, and the exploitation of workers caused by overtourism. Green Fins is a great business tool that helps dive shops stay accountable to their sustainable goals. However, Green Fins has a gap in its sustainability model, focusing primarily on environmental impacts and not incorporating the economic and social dimensions of sustainability. Overall, marine eco-tourism requires a holistic approach that balances environmental protection, economic resilience, and environmental justice and that fosters greater collaboration among dive shops, tourism operators, local communities, and government agencies.
2fd1d967: Increasing climate stressors highlight the urgent need for management that fosters coral adaptation and resilience. Although high genomic variance drives evolutionary adaptation, assessing genetic diversity in the field requires significant technical and financial resources – prohibitive barriers for low-income nations with coral reefs. To address this gap, our interdisciplinary collaboration tested possible proxies for coral genomic biodiversity by extracting habitat and environmental variables from remotely sensed datasets including: habitat heterogeneity and “reef gravity” metrics from the Allen Coral Atlas, fine resolution SST from NASA’s ECOsystem Spaceborne Thermal Radiometer Experiment on Space Station (ECOSTRESS) instrument, and chlorophyll-a microclimate maps from Sentinel-2 satellite imagery. Reduced-representation and whole genome sequencing were used to estimate intraspecific genetic diversity of a range of coral species (with varying morphologies, reproductive modes, and sensitivities to climate stressors) from major coral reef regions including Honduras, Mozambique, the Great Barrier Reef, Indonesia and the Philippines. We assessed the ability of candidate environmental predictors and their combinations to reconstruct observed variation in genomic diversity across our locations and regions using an ensemble-modeling framework, incorporating hierarchical and spatially explicit models including Bayesian Generalized Linear and Additive Mixed Models (GLMMs and GAMMs) that allow local estimates to be informed by response variation among species, global estimates to reflect differences across species, locations and ocean basins, and that account for spatial autocorrelation. Preliminary results from Honduras suggest that habitat characteristics that support multi-species diversity may not sustain high genetic diversity within a single species, as relationships differ between cryptic species and across environmental variables and spatial scales. Additional analyses across regions are needed to determine whether these environmental variables can serve as reliable proxies for genomic diversity. Developing a freely available global product to estimate coral reef adaptive capacity would significantly advance marine conservation planning and benefit coral reef managers and planners worldwide.
098d143d: Implementing reef restoration under a changing climate critically requires that both functional traits and ecological resilience are retained within a system. Not only must coral growth, survival, and robust symbiotic relationships be sustained, but also heat tolerance must be maintained — or even enhanced — throughout the active management process to ensure ongoing resilience to an ever-warming climate. Selection of coral individuals with greater heat tolerance is therefore beneficial to re-population strategies, either through identification of tolerant individuals within a target population, or from adjacent populations known to house more resilient individuals, such as systems subject to naturally extreme environmental conditions. However, it is also crucial to ensure selection of individuals and/or species that continue to contribute key ecological traits necessary for system function and stability. Through development of community-driven restoration activity over the past seven years on the Great Barrier Reef, we present recent advances in trait-based approaches to reef restoration, including assessments of potential trade-offs between heat tolerance and other key functional traits such as calcification, elemental balance (e.g., carbon:nitrogen ratios), and skeletal integrity. We discuss the role of corals selected for under natural extremes in restoration efforts, alongside implications of trait-focused approaches for site resilience and ecological functioning. This work aims to guide practitioners in selecting species that combine enhanced climate resilience with essential functional roles, thereby optimising stock selection and strengthening the effectiveness of future restoration efforts that depend on robust resilience strategies.
47995766: The main consequence of global warming for coral reefs is coral bleaching, which often results in large-scale mortality. Although this phenomenon is well documented globally, the thermal stress and bleaching affecting the reefs of the South Atlantic remain poorly studied. This study aimed to investigate turbidity Kd(490), surface current velocity, cloud cover, and high-frequency variability of Sea Surface Temperature (SST) across 40 provinces subdivided into six regions of the South Atlantic (North, Northeast, East, South, Oceanic Islands, and Africa). The objective was to identify areas with lower intensities of these variables, which may be more vulnerable to bleaching, and areas with higher intensities, which may show greater resistance to climate change. The method was based on remote-sensing and reanalysis datasets. Cloud cover was obtained from the ERA5 reanalysis (1993–2024). Turbidity Kd(490), which represents light attenuation, was extracted from CMEMS (2003–2024). Surface current velocity was obtained from the GLORYS12V1 ocean reanalysis (1993–2021). Daily SST was taken from the MUR-SST product (2003–2024), and thermal amplitude was used to quantify high-frequency variability. The comparative analysis revealed consistent patterns. Current velocity was highest in the North region and lowest in the South. Turbidity reached its greatest values in the South and along the northwestern African coast, while the North and Oceanic regions showed clearer waters. Cloud cover was higher in the South and northwestern Africa and lower in the North and East. High-frequency thermal variability was strongest near the subtropical boundary. These environmental patterns are known to mitigate bleaching through shading, heat dissipation, reduced light exposure, and increased thermal tolerance. The South region and the northwestern African coast exhibited the strongest intensities of these variables, suggesting greater potential protection, while regions with lower intensities may be more vulnerable. These findings contribute to identifying areas that may act as climate refugia in the South Atlantic.
a0c648c6:
Ocean warming has become one of the main concerns for the reefs of the future. The extent of their decline will largely depend on the capacity of reef organisms to withstand these conditions. Therefore, identifying naturally thermotolerant corals is crucial for understanding the environmental factors that may contribute to such thermotolerance, with the aim of integrating these factors into conservation efforts. We compared the thermal tolerance of Pocillopora spp. corals at reef sites either exposed to or protected from tidally generated internal gravity waves in the Verde Island Passage between April and May 2025 considering thermal history and short term acute heat stress testing. We assessed thermal history parameters such as cumulative thermal stress, daily cooling intensity, and daily temperature variability. This information was overlaid with coral thermal tolerance data (ED50 standardized thermal tolerance thresholds) under acute heat stress, using the standardized and portable Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System (CBASS). 

Our findings suggest that corals from protected and exposed sites experienced similar cumulative thermal stress. However, they differed in daily cooling intensity and daily temperature variability. Importantly, corals from exposed sites, where daily temperature variability is higher, seemed to retain their thermal tolerance even under cumulative thermal stress. This suggests that daily temperature variability may help buffer corals against the harmful effects of long-term heat exposure, as suggested in previous studies. In conclusion, this study suggests that high daily temperature variability, as inferred by tidally generated internal gravity waves, may enhance coral resilience to heat stress. It remains to be determined whether the increased thermal tolerance leads to lower bleaching rates in natural settings. Further research is encouraged in reefs with similar conditions to the Verde Island Passage to better understand their potential to act as thermal refugia as ocean temperatures continue to rise.
4ef7c4aa:
South Atlantic coral reefs show distinctive ecological and physiological traits when compared with reef systems from the Caribbean and Indo Pacific. Reefs located in Brazil have been suggested as a potential climate refugium due to its lower susceptibility to bleaching caused by biological and oceanographic atypical factors (Mies et al., 2020). Although species level mechanisms of resistance remain unclear. To investigate this, we evaluated oxidative stress, a physiological factor closely related to bleaching, to compare the thermal tolerance of South Atlantic corals with species from the Caribbean and Indo Pacific.

Aquarium experiments included nine different species, three representative corals from each province, with at least one massive and one branching coral. Control and treatment groups were kept at 27°C and 30°C, to induce bleaching. Biomarkers were measured by lipid peroxidation, protein carbonylation, DNA damage, and total antioxidant capacity. Statistical analyses included ANOVA, Tukey tests, and cluster analysis to identify patterns among species and biomarkers. Withal this is the first fully controlled experiment done comparing worldwide corals.

Results don’t support the initial hypothesis as South Atlantic species did not show higher resistance than those from other provinces. Thereby it’s not possible to elect one only region as a climate refugium, since the response did vary so much among coral from the same place, but the main findings contribute with a baseline profile of oxidative stress in healthy status for such species, which can be used as a monitoring tool. Overall, massive corals tended to be more resistant than branching species, although substantial species-specific variability was observed. We also ranked the species from the most resistant to most sensitive to heat stress. Therefore, we highlight the importance of considering species specificity for more effective conservational efforts against climate change.
ebff1bdd: Close associations of reef building corals with photo-symbionts can modulate the resilience of coral reefs to environmental perturbations such as thermal stress. While genetic diversity among coral photo-symbionts has been well documented, the link to physiological and functional trait differences remains less clear. This study used a custom-built fluorometer to capture the algal symbiont’s photo-physiological response to rapid changes in light, allowing for a more nuanced and highly parameterized assessment of physiological differences across coral colonies. These light response profiles were recorded from individual colonies for four different coral species commonly found along the Fijian coastline. Based on light response profiles alone, our samples largely clustered in concordance with the underlying genetic identity of the dominant photo-symbiont species within each coral colony. For example, Cladocopium C3, C1, and C50 dominated colonies were functionally distinct from those dominated by Durusdinium D1 symbionts. Additionally, light response profiles for Cladocopium C15 dominated corals formed a distinct cluster separate from all other Cladocopium and Durusdinium dominated colonies, highlighting the unique physiological differences that separate C15 from other species. Next, coral colony tolerance to high temperature was evaluated with an acute thermal stress test. Corals dominated by Cladocopium C15 or Durusdinium D1 photo-symbionts tolerated heating better than those harboring other Cladocopium species. The link between coral photo-physiology and thermal response was further explored with a predictive model developed by coupling light response measurements prior to heating with results from the acute heating experiments. The resulting model accounted for almost 80% of the variance in bleaching responses across all coral species. We show how non-invasive light response profiles capture critical functional trait differences that align with photo-symbiotic algal genotype and coral thermal response. Phenomic methods that capture these relationships may provide a rapid and scalable method for assessment of coral colonies in restoration and conservation practices.
eb7882af: Marine heat wave events are increasing in frequency and severity, which is a critical threat to coral reefs worldwide. Observed variation in bleaching susceptibility during marine heat wave events suggest that corals have some potential for adaptive capacity for the future. Many studies have sought to identify if this phenomenon has a genetic basis and if this tolerance is a heritable trait that can help corals adapt sufficiently to keep pace with current warming trends. Yet, understanding the underlying genetics of heat tolerance and survival of marine heat waves has remained an elusive goal. During the global bleaching event of 2023-2024, we surveyed 1020 colonies of Acropora spathulata across 14 reefs in the central and northern Great Barrier Reef (GBR) spanning a gradient of thermal exposure. We quantified symbiont community composition, percent bleaching, percent mortality, binary survival outcome, as well as bleaching recovery in these colonies during the peak heat of the bleaching event and during a recovery survey in late 2024. High coverage whole genome sequencing was combined with standardized in-situ phenotyping in a genome-wide association study (GWAS) framework to identify genomic variants underlying these key bleaching-response phenotypes. A genetic relatedness matrix (GRM) informed narrow sense heritability estimates for each trait revealing adaptive potential. This study presents one of the most comprehensive genomic analyses of in situ coral bleaching resilience, revealing candidate markers for adaptive potential to support genomic prediction informed conservation strategies for the GBR under accelerating climate change.
9f2ac1a9: As climate change accelerates coral reef degradation, conservation strategies increasingly rely on evolutionary approaches like selective breeding and assisted gene flow to enhance resilience in vulnerable species. Staghorn coral (Acropora cervicornis), a foundational yet critically endangered Caribbean coral, has been a major focus of restorative outplanting for over 15 years, but long-term survivorship remains low. In Florida’s Coral Reef, mounting environmental challenges have drastically reduced standing genetic diversity in A. cervicornis, a problem compounded by restoration practices reliant on asexual propagation and a lack of natural sexual recruitment. Consequently, essential conservation goals now include characterizing remaining staghorn diversity to mitigate further loss and identifying genetic factors underlying traits linked to survival and adaptive potential. To this end, we implemented high- and low-coverage whole-genome sequencing to survey 372 A. cervicornis colonies across the Caribbean, with concentrated sampling in Florida’s Coral Reef and the Dry Tortugas. We developed a haplotype reference panel and assessed population structure and historical demography throughout the species’ range. Additionally, using multiple measures of heat stress tolerance, including in-water bleaching/mortality and acute heat tolerance assays (CBASS), we identify candidate genes associated with thermal resistance through genome-wide association studies (GWAS). Our findings provide the genomic foundation for actionable strategies to improve A. cervicornis’ adaptive capacity and restoration outcomes that fortify populations against further decline.
50724c2b: Coral reefs are under mounting threat from climate-driven disturbances, and understanding how demography, genomic variation, and symbiont communities drive patterns in thermal resilience is imperative for designing conservation practices and interventions. Here, we focus on the genomic, symbiotic, and environmental drivers of thermal tolerance in Acropora pulchra, a dominant reef-building coral across Mo’orea, French Polynesia. From 2022–2024, we sampled 396 colonies of A. pulchra representing 340 unique genets from sites spanning the entire coastline of Mo’orea for acute heat tolerance phenotyping (using the Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System (CBASS)), Symbiodinaceae ITS2 amplicon sequencing, as well as whole-genome sequencing at 10–15x coverage. Coral host genomic analyses revealed little population structure across the island, but a 10 fold greater migration rate within the north shore of the island, relative to the rest of the island. These sub-population barriers to gene flow across the island may facilitate adaptation to local thermal regimes. CBASS assays (31 - 38 °C exposure) showed a mean thermal tolerance metric (Fv/Fm ED50) of 35.7 ± 0.7°C , a wide range in the tolerance metric (33.3 °C – 37.4°C), and a trimodal distribution that corresponded to distinct Symbiodiniaceae genera. Colonies hosting Durusdinium exhibited higher thermal tolerance, on average, than those with Cladocopium, while colonies hosting Symbiodinium displayed the widest range (4 °C), implicating host genomic factors in stress response. Using a genome-wide association analysis from the 2.7 million SNPs in our dataset will allow us to identify candidate loci predictive of resilience and assess whether these genomic signatures are shared across staghorn corals with distinct population histories at the host and symbiont level. This comparative framework allows us to distinguish and quantify the contributions of genomic, symbiotic, and environmental factors to individual thermal tolerance phenotypes, advancing the ability to forecast adaptive potential and inform restoration and assisted evolution strategies.
dfc30404:
The Tara Coral expedition builds on the Tara Pacific expedition (2016–2018) that surveyed 32 Pacific archipelagos using standardized protocols to generate contextualized datasets linking coral physiology with detailed in situ and environmental measurements. Tara Coral complements the previous expedition by focusing on reefs in the Coral Triangle, the only region globally where hard coral cover has thus far remained stable, despite ocean warming trends that closely mirror global temperature trajectories. This striking divergence between global reef decline and regional stability highlights a critical scientific question: why do corals in this region appear resilient to warming while reefs elsewhere deteriorate?
The overarching objective of the expedition is to elucidate the factors underlying coral reef resilience to global warming using a transdisciplinary approach integrating climate, environmental, physiological, and genomic research. Current hypotheses suggest that historical exposure to high maximum sea surface temperatures or strong interannual variability may have pre-adapted these corals to present-day conditions. However, alternative explanations involving reef macro- and coral microbiome composition, physiological plasticity, or lineage-specific evolutionary processes remain largely untested. Understanding the mechanisms will contribute to predictions for coral reef futures worldwide and inform conservation and active intervention.
The presentation will outline the scientific framework, international partnerships, and operational objectives of the expedition, emphasizing how an integrated design can contribute to the understanding of processes sustaining coral reef functioning under climate stress. The Tara Coral expedition seeks to provide an open-access, multidimensional reference framework for a mechanistic understanding of coral reef resilience to generate predictive indicators that can be operationalized for conservation planning, environmental monitoring, and future restoration approaches. Ultimately, Tara Coral aims to shift the trajectory of coral reef science from documenting decline to decoding the biological foundations of persistence—providing critical knowledge to help safeguard reef ecosystems in a rapidly warming world.
2c166e05: Acute heat‐stress assays, such as the Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System (CBASS), are used to characterize coral thermal tolerance. Yet we still lack empirical tests of how well experimental acute heat stress performance predicts performance during natural heat extremes and how both map onto genomic variation. We combine ex situ thermal profiling via a modified CBASS and in situ measurements of coral phenotypes during a natural bleaching event along the eastern Red Sea to quantify the genetic architecture of thermal resilience in three Acropora species (A. variabilis, A. hyacinthus, and A. hemprichii). For the acute heat-stress experiment, colonies were exposed to temperature ramps following a CBASS-like methodology, and their thermal performance was estimated from dark‐adapted Fv/Fm and RGB‐derived bleaching scores. Independently, the 2024 bleaching event allowed us to collect samples of bleached and non-bleached colonies from the same reefs and microhabitats, for which bleaching severity was quantified using image-based metrics. Both approaches reveal substantial among‐genotype and among‐reef variation in thermal performance, consistent with genetic variation and regional differences in thermal tolerance. Physiological phenotyping from both datasets is being combined with whole-genome sequencing to uncover the genetic basis of thermal resilience through genome-wide association studies. These analyses are expected to identify alleles and genomic regions associated with increased thermal tolerance, evaluate whether these loci are species-specific or shared across Acropora species, and compare the extent of overlap in genomic signals between experimental and natural contexts. By testing concordance between loci and pathways associated with CBASS-derived thermal tolerance and those associated with natural bleaching resilience, this work clarifies how well experimental assays capture thermal resilience in the field. These results advance our understanding of coral adaptation to climate change and inform conservation and reef restoration strategies by identifying tolerant source populations and genomic targets for assisted gene flow and selective propagation.
30817a43: Adaptive evolution is an important process by which species may cope with environmental change. Reef-building corals are especially impacted by rapidly rising ocean temperatures and represent priority taxa to uncover genomic signals associated with heat tolerance. Here, we examine the genetic underpinnings of heat tolerance in over 450 individuals of an ecologically important coral, Acropora tersa. We estimate marker-based heritability across acute heat tolerance phenotypes, conduct genome wide association analyses, and combine marker effects in genomic prediction models to evaluate the genomic basis of heat tolerance traits. We find that heritabilities for chlorophyll metrics were considerably higher than for photochemistry metrics indicating a greater influence of the coral host on bleaching responses compared to symbiont health under heat stress. In association studies, while no single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) reached genome-wide significance, the most significant associations were with chlorophyll traits. Associated SNPs varied spatially and their genotypes exhibited differing chlorophyll values both among and within reefs. Genomic prediction models highlight limited capacity to predict phenotypes from genotypes alone, likely due to strong environmental influences on the heat tolerance traits measured. Although larger sample sizes or reduced environmental contributions to phenotypes are likely to uncover small marker effects, our findings lay important groundwork for developing predictive models of coral heat tolerance for promoting natural or assisted evolution interventions.
4aef8ada: Scleractinia (stony corals) are a diverse taxonomic order within the phylum Cnidaria, comprising more than 1,600 species described to date. Most corals play a key role as reef builders by secreting calcium carbonate, forming rigid skeletons that provide the structural foundation of tropical coral reef ecosystems. Through colonial growth and symbioses with their photosynthetic microalgae (Symbiodiniaceae) and a suite of other microorganisms, scleractinian corals support high primary productivity and underpin the vast biodiversity of coral reefs. Corals are, however, under severe pressure from climate change, including ocean warming and acidification, which threaten their survival and consequently, the ability of coral reef systems to persist globally. In recent years, significant efforts have been made to increase the number of sequenced genomes from scleractinian corals, thereby providing crucial insights into their biology, evolution, resilience, and vulnerability. However, most species are still missing a high-quality reference genome, and many available genomes remain unannotated, creating barriers to collaboration and scientific insight. To address these issues, through the Aquatic Symbiosis Genomics (ASG), we at the Coral Genome Project have produced and openly released 40 chromosome-level and 8 scaffold/contig genomes. These genomes have been annotated using a standardised pipeline to generate high-quality gene models. Here, we will present the curated resource of annotated high-quality genomes, and show how they are already being used by our community. We hope this resource will provide essential molecular tools at a critical time for coral reef conservation.
1c5643d3: Understanding how genomic variation shapes coral resilience is essential for forecasting responses to ocean warming and guiding conservation and restoration strategies. Red Sea corals thrive under extreme conditions, experiencing high salinity and fluctuating elevated temperatures, making them an ideal system for studying coral adaptation to environmental extremes. To gain insight into the molecular mechanisms of thermal tolerance in reef-building corals, we applied a quantitative genetic approach to identify loci associated with thermal tolerance in two Pocilloporidae species: Pocillopora favosa and Stylophora pistillata. We generated high-quality genome assemblies for both species and collected colonies across the central to northern Red Sea. Colonies were phenotyped using the Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System (CBASS) and scored using a Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM) fluorometry and RGB imaging analysis. Colonies were also collected during the 2024 natural bleaching event, allowing to assess bleaching responses under field conditions. Whole-genome sequencing data were generated for all colonies, and genome-wide association studies (GWAS) were performed to identify loci and genomic regions associated with variation in thermal tolerance and bleaching response. Comparative analyses across species revealed both shared and species-specific adaptations. Integrating ex situ and in situ approaches allowed us to compare thermal tolerance across controlled and natural conditions, uncovering unique and overlapping genetic loci associated with each type of stress and highlighting the advantages and limitations of each method. This study provides essential genomic resources and knowledge to support genomic-informed conservation and restoration strategies aimed at enhancing the thermal resilience of coral reefs under climate change.
7168ec99:
Predicting the ecological consequences of petroleum hydrocarbon (HC) spills in tropical marine environments remains a major scientific challenge. Each spill exhibits a dynamic and highly variable chemical profile, limiting the broader applicability of toxicity thresholds generated from traditional oil‐exposure ecotoxicity tests. An emerging alternative is the use of species-specific critical target lipid body burdens (CTLBBs), which quantify the inherent sensitivity of marine taxa to representative HCs. When paired with measured or modelled dissolved HC compositions, CTLBBs enable prediction of acute or chronic toxicity for any oil type and at any stage of weathering, offering a mechanistic, transferable framework for assessing potential impacts on coral reef species.
This presentation synthesises recent advances in generating and validating CTLBBs for tropical marine taxa and explores their application in predicting toxicity of complex petroleum mixtures. CTLBBs are derived from structure–function relationships developed through targeted ecotoxicity testing with individual HCs, assuming additivity of effects within mixtures. These predicted toxicity values are contrasted with empirical outcomes from ecotoxicity tests conducted with partially weathered oils in a tropical marine case study. Newly derived CTLBBs for tropical species such as corals enable direct comparison of their sensitivity against all taxa within the broader Target Lipid Model (TLM) database and substantially improve the ability to model toxicity for any oil composition relevant to coral reef ecosystems.
The results highlight both the promise and current limitations of CTLBB- and TLM-based approaches for coral reef applications. While widely used internationally to support spill damage and risk assessments, further work is required to refine model parameters for tropical species and reef-specific environmental conditions. Priority research areas include validating model performance under tropical light and temperature regimes, expanding CTLBB datasets for reef taxa, and integrating model outputs with ecological risk frameworks tailored to coral reef habitats and time variable exposures that characterize oil spills.
7d03056b: Plastics are now established as widespread pollutants, and their degradation into micro- and nanoparticles poses risks to coastal ecosystems such as coral reefs. Emerging advanced materials may follow comparable environmental pathways, but their engineered nanoscale and surface features could lead to different biological interactions and ecological outcomes. Anticipating the risks associated to such pollutants requires evaluating their effects for corals across organismal levels. We developed a mid- to long term closed system exposure protocol to analyse the impact of common polymeric nanoparticles and advanced carbon nanomaterials, and tested their impacts on tropical coral physiology and metabolism. Clonal fragments of four reef building corals (Stylophora pistillata, Heliopora coerulea, Montipora foliosa, Acropora valida) were exposed for eight weeks to fluorescent polystyrene, polypropylene, polyethylene, and silica beads, green-emitting graphene quantum dots, or multi walled carbon nanotubes with weekly water and treatment renewal. The coral holobiont photophysiology responses were monitored regularly by pulse-amplitude modulated fluorometry, and final outcomes were assessed through liquid chromatography coupled to mass spectrometry-based metabolomics and proteomics. Graphene quantum dots caused the most pronounced visual responses, removing the white tip coloration indicating growth in S. pistillata, and inducing tissue recession in S. pistillata and M. foliosa. Polymeric nanoparticles (polyethylene, polypropylene) did not induce visible tissue damage, but significantly elevated photosynthetic efficiency from week three, while symbiont density was not altered. A. valida and H. coerulea showed minimal responses across all treatments. These preliminary findings demonstrate that certain advanced nanomaterials, such as the graphene quantum dots in our study, can affect coral health, underscoring the urgency of incorporating advanced materials into coral-focused ecotoxicology. Ongoing complementary metabolomic and proteomic analyses will resolve pathway-level alterations and host-symbiont stress signatures, enabling mechanistic interpretation of the observed phenotypes.
37c6cbae:
Sunscreen ingredients released into coastal waters at tourist sites are estimated to reach approximately 14,000 tons annually, raising concerns about their impacts on marine organisms, including reef-building corals. Although several organic UV filters have recently been regulated due to their ecological risks, the use of so-called “reef-safe” inorganic filters, particularly zinc oxide (ZnO), has increased. However, ZnO is also classified as hazardous to aquatic life under the UN GHS, and its potential to inhibit photosynthesis in symbiotic algae and induce coral bleaching has been suggested. Thus, the environmental safety of ZnO remains insufficiently evaluated. This study aimed to assess the effects of different ZnO concentrations on corals and to identify candidate genes associated with ZnO-induced bleaching.

Fragments of Acropora solitaryensis, a common Indo-Pacific species, were collected by SCUBA diving in Tateyama in September 2024. Four colonies were assigned to each treatment (control, 0.3 mg/L, 0.5 mg/L, 1.0 mg/L) and exposed to ZnO for 48 hours. Photosynthetic activity (Fv/Fm) was measured using PAM fluorometry, RNA-seq analysis was performed, and a time-lapse camera documented changes in appearance.

Clear differences were observed between the low-concentration groups (0.3 and 0.5 mg/L) and the high-concentration group (1.0 mg/L). Corals at 0.3 and 0.5 mg/L showed minimal bleaching, little visible change, and Fv/Fm values similar to baseline. In contrast, complete bleaching occurred within 48 hours at 1.0 mg/L, accompanied by a marked decline in Fv/Fm. RNA-seq results showed no significant differences in the number of expressed genes among the control, 0.3 mg/L, and 0.5 mg/L groups, whereas overall gene expression decreased at 1.0 mg/L. Notably, several genes exhibited more than a 1,500-fold increase in expression under the 1.0 mg/L condition. These highly responsive genes may serve as potential molecular markers for the non-invasive detection of ZnO-induced stress.
3db089a4:
Recurrent environmental disturbances, particularly marine heatwaves, are reducing the fitness and abundance of breeding corals, creating feedback loops where populations struggle to recover after bleaching and stress events. The molecular processes underlying reproductive status remain difficult to study in-situ because current reproductive monitoring methods are destructive, labour-intensive, and often miss early physiological changes that precede visible reproductive failure. To address this gap, we investigated whether environmental RNA (eRNA) can detect active physiological signals in the broadcast-spawning coral Acropora millepora during the autumn 2025 spawning event at Ningaloo Reef, Western Australia.

Replicate colonies were collected during an intense marine heatwave, maintained under controlled aquarium conditions, and repeatedly sampled for paired tissue RNA and water eRNA across day/night cycles and reproductive timepoints. Expression of genes linked to circadian regulation, gametogenesis, thermal stress, and apoptosis was quantified using cDNA synthesis and RT-qPCR. Diel markers showed the strongest environmental signal; Cryptochrome-1 exhibited clear, synchronised daytime peaks in both tissue RNA and eRNA, demonstrating that transcriptional activity can be detected non-destructively from surrounding seawater. Other genes, including Clock, Vitellogenin-A2, HSP70, Bax, and Caspase-3 were consistently detectable in tissue but produced weaker or inconsistent environmental signals, likely reflecting low transcript abundance, rapid degradation, or heat-stress-induced disruption.

Microscopy and gene expression analysis revealed divergent reproductive outcomes among colonies, including premature spawning and gamete resorption, consistent with thermal stress disrupting normal gametogenesis. These findings provide preliminary evidence that eRNA can capture meaningful aspects of coral physiology; however, repeating the experiment with non-stressed breeding colonies will be essential to identify gene-specific sensitivity limitations. This work highlights priorities for eRNA-method development, including improved assay sensitivity, detection limits, degradation characterisation, and integration with transcriptomics. Ultimately, advancing eRNA approaches may enable high-resolution, non-destructive monitoring of coral health, reproductive readiness, and stress physiology at scales necessary for understanding the resilience of declining reefs.
49523dd1: Coral reefs are under increasing stress from rising seawater temperatures and nutrient enrichment from land-based sources (runoff, coastal development, sewage). These stressors can diminish ecosystem resilience, causing transitions toward alternative benthic states beyond simple coral-to-algae shifts. To implement appropriate management strategies, we need monitoring tools sensitive to these environmental changes. Large Benthic Foraminifera (LBF) have been used as bioindicators of reef health, as their community assemblages are inextricably linked to the dominant benthic substrate. However, while previous research has focused on substrate associations, the direct influence of water quality drivers, specifically nutrient gradients, on these assemblages remains underexplored. Here, we address this gap by analyzing how LBF assemblages shift under specific benthic dominances and varying nutrient conditions (e.g., nitrogen and phosphorus). We surveyed 16 reefs across a gradient of human impact and four marine lakes (landlocked seawater bodies) in Raja Ampat, West Papua, Indonesia. The LBF community identification was performed via high-throughput metabarcoding of sediment eDNA samples, and using a recently developed cytochrome c oxidase subunit 1 (COI) marker. Results indicated that foraminiferal assemblages in macro-algae-dominated reefs were distinctly different from those in all other benthic habitats. Chlorophyll-a, dissolved organic nitrogen, and temperature were significant predictors of assemblage structure; however, the explanatory power of these variables varied by habitat type. This study provides a baseline assessment of the effects of benthic dominance and water quality on foraminifera in tropical reefs. We also highlight the utility of sediment eDNA for monitoring LBF community shifts associated with benthic phase transitions
6f52ee7a: Sponges (phylum Porifera) are sessile suspension feeders, ubiquitous throughout aquatic freshwater and marine benthic ecosystems. Their predisposed ability to filter large volumes of water enables them to trap genetic material from surrounding biological communities – effectively functioning as natural DNA samplers (nsDNA). To verify the accumulation of eDNA within sponges, we used MiFish 12s rRNA-specific primers, to target fish signatures based on paired sponge-tissue biopsies and water samples from distinct reef habitats in Shirahama, Japan (n=21) and eastern Hong Kong waters (n=18). While water-derived eDNA revealed site-specific reef fish community compositions, sponge-derived eDNA showed relatively consistent compositions, suggesting that sponge eDNA may reflect community structure across a broader spatiotemporal scale. Furthermore, to fully utilise sponges as nsDNA, we conducted a comparative analysis with an additional mechanical lysis step employing BioMasher II to disrupt the sponge tissue matrix and evaluate the final eDNA yield. Validation of the sponge nsDNA technique offers the opportunity to infer temporal biodiversity patterns exhibited by historical eDNA (heDNA) from sponges held in museum collections, which we are exploring through comparison of heDNA and contemporary samples collected in Hong Kong, aiming to reconstruct changes in reef fish communities over recent decades. By harnessing the natural capability of filter feeding sponges that potentially accrue and concentrates eDNA, this humble organism has the potential to become a powerful method for monitoring the biodiversity of reef ecosystems.
a7ee8090: The power to sequence environmental DNA (eDNA) at scale has rapidly expanded our ability to observe and monitor marine ecosystems. However, its true potential lies beyond its routine use for species detection. In this talk, I showcase how eDNA can serve as a versatile forensic tool across ecological scales to 1) assess shifts in coral communities following catastrophic heating; 2) estimate population-level allele frequencies of elusive megafauna  with multi-locus genotyping; and 3) track individual coral larval dispersal with gene editing and synthetic barcodes. Together, these examples demonstrate that eDNA can bridge disciplines, from molecular biology to conservation forensics, to deliver ecologically relevant, scalable insights into tropical marine communities of ecological and economic concern.
63cbf3d7: Assessing the impacts of rapid environmental change on coral reefs is hindered by a major discrepancy between the regions with the greatest need and those that receive the most research funding. Remote sensing, particularly through the use of drones (or unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs)), has the potential to significantly enhance the accessibility and efficiency of high-quality data collection in remote, biodiverse areas such as the Coral Triangle. To test this, we compared a UAV-based method, a common citizen science method (Reef Check), and a research-grade method (photo quadrats) to assess hard coral cover, coral bleaching, and coral growth forms across reef types (inshore, mid-shelf, fringing) and depths (crest, 5 m, 10 m, 15 m) in Kimbe Bay, Papua New Guinea, during the fourth Global Mass Coral Bleaching Event. We found that, compared to in-water methods, the UAV delivered accurate reef health data for shallow reef crests of all reef types, including hard coral cover, bleached hard coral cover, and coral growth forms. We also found that data collected from the UAV was representative of overall reef health, with some limitations. Hard coral cover did not differ significantly between the crest and the other depths. However, bleaching was most significant at the reef crest and decreased significantly at deeper parts of the reef slope. Coral growth form composition varied significantly between reef types, but the scale of these differences decreased with depth. Our study demonstrates that UAVs can provide accurate health and community composition data for reefs with high biodiversity, significantly enhancing the availability of high-quality reef health data in the areas of highest need.
15893bab: Coral reef–seagrass mosaics are foundational to Florida Keys (USA) coastal ecosystems, providing critical habitat for reef fish that support commercial and recreational fisheries. For many reef fish species, ecological functions such as feeding, sheltering, ontogenetic habitat shifts, and movement pathways depend on the spatial arrangement between coral reefs and seagrass meadows. Yet, the spatial structure of coral–seagrass mosaics in the Florida Keys remains poorly quantified, limiting the ability of managers to design marine protected areas (MPAs) that effectively support fish populations. To address this gap, this study applies a remote sensing approach to understand how multi-scale spatial patterns of coral reef–seagrass habitat mosaics vary across the Florida Keys Reef Tract, and how these patterns can inform strategic MPA design. To quantify current seascape configuration, we used medium-resolution PlanetScope imagery to develop benthic habitat maps tailored to the seascape properties of the Upper, Middle, and Lower Keys. Using supervised Random Forest models trained with field observations, we developed region-specific benthic maps capturing multiple seagrass, coral reef, bare sediment, and hardbottom features to reflect differences in habitat complexity. Furthermore, we integrated coarse-resolution Sentinel-2 imagery, which provides a multispectral archive suitable for mapping spatiotemporal shifts in seascape mosaics. The resulting benthic maps served to measure spatial pattern metrics of amount, proximity, and structural complexity of seagrass meadows adjacent to coral reefs, revealing regional differences in coral–seagrass arrangement and temporal dynamics across spatial scales. By combining satellite imagery, field observations, and seascape ecology analysis, this work identifies areas in the Florida Keys that maintain more connected and structurally complex seascapes, and highlights their contrast with others that exhibit more fragmented or simplified configurations. Our study directly supports MPA planning by identifying habitat mosaics most critical for maintaining seascape stability and sustaining reef fish functional connectivity across the Florida Keys.
fa874fb0:
Direct measurement of reef flat vertical accretion in the field remains rare, with most estimates inferred from geological or ecological proxies of vertical accretion. Wave-dominated reef flats pose logistical challenges for deploying instruments capable of monitoring elevational change over time. Yet, understanding whether reef flats can keep pace with rising sea levels is critical. Close-range Structure-from-Motion (SfM) photogrammetry offers millimetre-scale vertical accuracy for monitoring growth of reef calcifying organisms, but close-range applications are rare and typically limited to individual colonies and small quadrats. It remains challenging to overcome limitations in co-registering SfM models across time and scaling up spatial coverage of models, while maintaining high elevational accuracy. 
This study evaluates the potential of drone-based SfM to overcome these limitations by comparing digital surface models (DSMs) derived from close-range and aerial imagery of a fringing reef flat in Tongatapu. Close-range DSMs were generated from handheld digital camera surveys of 1 m² quadrats at ~1 m elevation. Aerial DSMs were produced from drone imagery captured with a DJI Mavic 3E at 10–15 m elevation, covering a much larger area of reef flat, while overlapping with the location of the close-range quadrats. All orthomosaics and DSMs were processed in Agisoft, with ground control points surveyed using a Leica total station to ensure a consistent and accurate vertical datum across time.
We present a comparative analysis of accuracy, spatial coverage, and repeatability between the two approaches, assessing their suitability for detecting elevational changes of the reef flat’s morphologically differing calcifying organisms and substrates through time. Findings highlight the capabilities and limitations of drone-based SfM for scaling up reef monitoring while maintaining the precision required to quantify vertical growth. These insights inform the development of cost-effective, repeatable monitoring strategies essential for predicting reef flat resilience under accelerating sea-level rise.
72631d15: Coral reefs are globally valuable and threatened ecosystems that are particularly challenging to study due to lack of historic baseline data and the difficulties of monitoring underwater habitats. Significant progress has been made in developing imaging and remote sensing technologies to improve upon monitoring techniques, but there are new solutions yet to be explored using novel multispectral sensors mounted on aerial drones, achieving centimeter-level spatial resolution and spectral resolution surpassing what is available on many satellite systems. We utilized a 10-band multispectral drone system combined with high-resolution RGB imagery to map reefs on the scale of tens to hundreds of acres and developed a specialized photogrammetric and radiometric processing pipeline to yield high-quality and artifact-free benthic reflectance maps. Using co-registered underwater large area imaging datasets to collect training and validation data, were able to train a Random Forest classifier to achieve an 86% accuracy at identifying 13 benthic classes, including 7 distinct coral species and genera. A binary Random Forest model was also able to detect the presence or absence of live coral with 92% accuracy, showing the promise of this technology for characterizing benthic condition at the scale of hundreds or thousands of acres. We are currently exploring improved machine learning and AI classifiers to improve our reef mapping capability with this system. We present details of our workflow, visualizations, and benthic maps of key reef sites in The Bahamas and San Andrés, Colombia, demonstrating a case study for how to combine aerial and underwater remote sensing tools to assess reef condition, drive conservation decision making and establish baseline data to compare with future conditions.
a1264b93: Globally, coral reefs face significant threats from climate change and other human-induced factors. Unconventional methods to monitor them especially in areas where it is not safe due to the potential hazards and other logistical constraints are much needed to understand the impact of these threats. In Tambisan waters, recurring crocodile sightings restricts conventional diver-based surveys for assessing coral reef conditions. To address this challenge, we explored the feasibility of utilizing an underwater Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) using CHASING M2 S model in the shallow fringing reefs of Tambisan (approximately 2-3m depth) for benthic reef monitoring. The ROV was assessed for manoeuvrability, stability, and practicality under varying currents and visibility conditions. Guided by suspended transect lines to conduct the photo quadrat transect survey, the ROV was equipped with a laser scaler to provide non-intrusive size references, eliminating the need for physical quadrats. Preliminary findings indicate that the ROV was deployable across fringing reefs along the Tambisan coast, although strong currents reduced manoeuvrability and positional stability, occasionally causing current-induced drift. These limitations did not substantially affect image acquisition, and comparable image quality suitable for benthic classification was achieved. However, the image processing workflow still revealed several challenges. Turbid sites reduced clarity, requiring extensive close-up imaging to ensure sufficient usable data. Over uneven substrates, the laser scaler beams sometimes landed on different elevations, causing slight beam deflection. This issue was mitigated by referencing measurements to the brightest laser point, which corresponded to the original beam position prior to deflection. In cases where laser beams failed to reach the substrate in wide-angle images, wide shots were paired with corresponding close-up images where laser points were visible. Overall, ROV-based surveys show strong potential for expanding benthic survey coverage, provided that appropriate and workable field adaptations are implemented.
8d36ad86: Invasive marine macroalgae threaten coral reef health by altering habitat structure, suppressing coral recruitment, and driving ecosystem phase shifts, yet monitoring the extent and spread remains difficult in remote reef environments. The use of remote sensing on coral reefs remains a persistent challenge due to water-column optics, surface conditions, and the spatial scale of ocean environments. Here, we present three technologically advanced uncrewed marine systems (UMS) that combine environmental DNA (eDNA), computer vision, and hyperspectral imaging to monitor invasive macroalgae. Our primary target species is the invasive red alga Chondria tumulosa, first discovered in 2019 smothering large areas of reef and currently known only from Papahānaumokuākea National Marine Sanctuary (PNMS), one of the most remote marine protected areas on Earth. Field trials to test and develop a methodology for these systems, are conducted on Oʻahu, Hawaiʻi, where multiple invasive species (Kappaphycus/Eucheuma complex, Gracilaria salicornia, and Acanthophora spicifera) provide a controlled testbed for molecular, visual, and spectral detection. The UMS systems consists of two surface UMS and one submersible platform. One surface system is equipped with a McLane RoCSI eDNA sampler to determine species presence/absence, and a second equipped with a computer vision camera system for optical identification of target species. The submersible UMS is equipped with RGB cameras and a Headwall hyperspectral imager to generate 3D habitat maps and in-situ hyperspectral scans. Used in conjunction, these three UMS will provide independent methodologies to map the extent of invasive species with a high degree of confidence. This work demonstrates how AI-enabled, multi-sensor uncrewed systems can support scalable monitoring strategies for coral reef management and early detection of invasive species in some of the most inaccessible marine environments on Earth.
f71bb26f: Coral reef monitoring increasingly relies on data from multiple platforms, including satellites, uncrewed aerial systems (UAS), and uncrewed marine systems (UMS), yet these datasets are often difficult to integrate due to differences in scale, resolution, and geolocation accuracy. This study proposes a framework to co-register and integrate satellite imagery, UAS orthomosaics, and UMS-derived products (e.g., SfM mosaics and 3D benthic models) over a ~1 km² reef test site. The UAS orthomosaic is georeferenced using GNSS, and a spatially accurate intermediate product between satellite imagery and in-water data products. A primary challenge addressed in this study is the merging of above water and in-water product, particularly the geolocation uncertainty associated with underwater data. We outline an approach to automatically align in-water datasets with drone imagery, and subsequently with high-resolution satellite products, in order to produce spatially consistent, multi-scale benthic datasets. The objectives of this work are to (1) develop an automated or semi-automated workflow for multi-platform co-registration, (2) assess the minimum spatial footprint, feature requirements  and ocean conditions needed to reliably align underwater and aerial imagery, and (3) evaluate uncertainties in stacking datasets across air–sea boundaries. Ultimately, this framework aims to support scaling of detailed in-water observations to drone and satellite platforms, to improve interpretation of benthic habitats from remotely sensed imagery.
74e38263:
Mesophotic reef systems (30–150 m) remain chronically understudied due to the inherent difficulty of accessing these environments. Traditional remote-sensing approaches offer limited insight: satellite imagery cannot penetrate to mesophotic depths, while acoustic mapping lacks the resolution required to identify benthic organisms or characterise fine-scale reef structure. As a result, major questions surrounding species composition, community connectivity, and the ecological function of these deeper reefs persist.
To address this gap, we present a multi-scale survey framework that integrates seafloor-imaging Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) with targeted diver-based sampling to produce spatially contiguous, georeferenced datasets spanning shallow–mesophotic reef gradients. Central to this approach is a novel, hover-capable AUV engineered for precision photogrammetry over highly complex reef terrain. The vehicle carries downward-facing stereo cameras, high-intensity strobes, and an accurate navigation system, enabling repeatable low-altitude, georeferenced “lawnmower” transects over complex geomorphology.
Across multidisciplinary expeditions in Tonga, the Solomon Islands, and Samoa, this framework produced landscape-scale 3D reef models covering areas exceeding 12,000 m² at ~0.8 mm pixel resolution, among the highest-resolution, spatially contiguous photogrammetric reconstructions achieved at mesophotic depths. These models form the geospatial backbone for subsequent diver operations: within the AUV survey envelope, technical divers conducted ~0.1mm resolution photogrammetry and sample collection in 100m² focal plots at set depth intervals. The AUV models enabled precise georeferencing and ecological contextualisation of each diver-captured plot, directly linking colony-level observations to broader geomorphic and environmental patterns.
This coordinated AUV–diver framework supports new investigations into species connectivity across depth gradients and the environmental drivers shaping coral community structure. We describe the methodology in detail, emphasising the AUV design, the operational challenges of navigating complex terrain, site-selection strategies, co-registration of multi-scale datasets, and the coordination required between AUV and diver teams. This integrated approach provides a scalable blueprint for advancing mesophotic reef science in remote and data-limited regions.
a42a1f18: Coral reef restoration is an extremely complicated endeavor that requires complementary models and experimental systems. The Fk Framework is a thermodynamic-based theoretical system for predicting how living systems convert environmental energy into structured, information-rich states. The framework predicts many well-known ecological and evolutionary dynamics, including the collapse of climax ecosystems with increasing temperature. To empirically test and parameterize the framework’s key variables (Energy, Work, Certainty, and Heat), we are deploying Coral Reef Arks; large marine mesocosms designed as experimental analogs of natural reef systems. These Arks enable real-time measurement of thermodynamic and ecological parameters under controlled perturbations. Beyond their scientific role, the Arks also serve as innovative tools for conservation and restoration, supporting the recovery of coral reef biodiversity and ecosystem function while providing a quantitative basis for predicting ecosystem responses to environmental change.
d57c945f: The three-dimensional architecture of natural habitats is a key determinant of species biodiversity, harvestable biomass and resilience to disturbance. Indeed, some species, including trees, corals and oysters, alter resource availability and modify biotic and abiotic pressures through their own three-dimensional structures — thereby enhancing their own survival. However, which aspects of the three-dimensional architecture of these ‘ecosystem engineers’ shape ecosystem dynamics and species survival is rarely examined by empirical studies, leaving much of the broader ecological and conservation impact of ecosystem engineering underexplored. In this study, we show that oyster reefs exhibit combinations of geometric variables that maximise recruit survival, which is a key factor influencing oyster reef growth and persistence. Using 3D habitat designs that capture the full spectrum of natural oyster reef architectures and a geometric theory that links habitat surface area, fractal dimension and height range, we show that oyster settlement and survival are greatest at particular combinations of fractal dimension and height range that minimise predation. Our study provides a template for understanding optimal 3D habitat configurations for habitat restoration projects that are proliferating globally without targeting key aspects of the architecture of habitat space that can maximise restoration success.
3d3e16bd:
Artificial reefs (ARs) are increasingly deployed to support coral restoration, providing hard substrate in areas degraded by sedimentation, mechanical damage from coastal development, or destructive fishing practices. The 2025 Global Tipping Point Report emphasizes that reducing local and regional stressors, alongside climate action, is essential for maintaining coral reef resilience. Manual propagation of corals onto ARs remains labour-intensive and difficult to scale, making strategies that enhance natural coral settlement a critical pathway for scalable restoration. This work investigates how AR design interacts with hydrodynamics to influence larval settlement.

An integrated modelling framework was developed, coupling Computational Fluid Dynamics with Lagrangian passive particle tracer simulations to examine how AR geometry shapes near-surface flow and larval transport. Thirteen ARs and two reference geometries were analysed using geometric and complexity metrics, such as curvature, surface area, and packing, alongside hydrodynamic features including flow velocity, wake zones, and vorticity. These outputs were incorporated into a particle simulator to visualise three-dimensional larval trajectories and quantify interactions with reef surfaces under hydrodynamically informed flow conditions.

Results show that while AR geometry can significantly influence modelled larval settlement, the patterns are not always strong or linear and vary between structures. Identifying which specific structural features promote larval settlement and how they alter local hydrodynamics provides an evidence-based foundation for informed AR design. Beyond guiding the creation of new structures, these insights can support strategic propagation of corals on existing ARs to locally modify flow and enhance recruitment.

This approach provides a visually interactive platform for assessing larval trajectories and settlement, bridging engineering, ecology, and design. The tools developed also offer avenues for visualising conservation work on screens or in VR, serving as a powerful medium for science communication to engage both local and global communities in reef restoration and protection.
a4b50deb:
Coral reef restoration increasingly requires approaches that strengthen coral physiological resilience while supporting the ecological processes that maintain reef function. We present an integrated biomimetic restoration technology based on three dimensional terracotta structures that act simultaneously as ecological substrates, living microhabitats, and scalable restoration units. The ceramic material provides a natural and porous surface for early biofilm formation and secure coral attachment, while the three dimensional architecture creates hydrodynamic regimes that enhance mixing and facilitate nutrient and oxygen exchange around the coral tissue. A central feature of this technology is its capacity to accumulate beneficial cryptic invertebrates and microbial communities when conditioned at a healthy reef. Once established, these living microhabitats are transferred to degraded reefs and used as attachment platforms for coral fragments. Corals planted onto these biologically enriched structures exhibit improved physiological performance, including higher photochemical efficiency, increased densities of symbiotic algae, and elevated antioxidant capacity. These responses align with the greater richness of invertebrates and the presence of key microbial groups retained on the conditioned units, providing a strong proof of concept that healthy microhabitats can be transplanted to boost coral resilience in impacted environments.
To ensure scalability, the same ceramic technology is produced in parametrically designed three dimensional forms with controlled levels of structural complexity. Both simple and more complex designs support high coral survivorship and healthy photosynthetic activity, demonstrating the suitability of the substrate for restoration. Flow modelling indicates that increased structural complexity generates slower and more turbulent flow patterns that can promote ecological succession and the development of diverse benthic communities over longer deployment periods.
This unified ceramic microhabitat platform offers a practical, adaptable, and ecologically grounded solution that enhances coral health, supports beneficial reef communities, and contributes to long term reef recovery.
7f0d076b: Global warming and ocean acidification pose serious threats to the persistence of coral reef ecosystems, which support high biodiversity. The carbonate budget of coral reefs is governed by the balance between carbonate production and carbonate dissolution; however, research on the dissolution of reef sediments—one of the dominant contributors to this budget—has progressed slowly. Consequently, the mechanisms controlling sediment dissolution and the quantitative prediction of these processes remain inadequately understood. Under these circumstances, there is a strong demand for high-precision, in situ techniques capable of monitoring carbonate ion concentrations. To address the limitations of conventional measurement approaches, we report the development of an all-solid-state carbonate ion sensor based on a field-effect transistor (FET). By combining a carbonate-selective ionophore with an FET-based detection platform, the device enables solid-state detection of carbonate ions (CO₃²⁻). Unlike traditional potentiometric sensors, which often exhibit signal drift due to contamination or instability of the internal solution, the present sensor eliminates the need for an internal electrolyte entirely. This structural advantage ensures stable measurements even in seawater containing multiple interfering ions. In addition, the sensor demonstrates a rapid response on the order of seconds and offers inherent miniaturization benefits, making it highly suitable for real-time and on-site monitoring applications. In this study, the fundamental performance of the sensor was evaluated. The device was immersed in three standard carbonate ion solutions of different concentrations, in artificial seawater, and in natural seawater, and its response characteristics were examined in detail. Reproducibility was assessed by repeatedly using the same sensor under identical conditions. This presentation highlights the linear response to carbonate ions and the high measurement reproducibility achieved. Establishing this sensing technology is expected not only to advance carbonate budget assessments in coral reef environments but also to facilitate applications in other scientific fields, including biomedical sensing.
e7f33144: Ocean acidification poses a threat to marine carbonate sediments by enhancing the geochemical dissolution of calcium carbonate while reducing its biogenic replenishment. Coral reef sediments, which predominantly consist of calcium carbonate, are expected to shift to net dissolution by ~2050. However, empirical data from the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) is limited. This study employed in situ advective benthic chamber incubations in shallow reef sediments to quantify current and future carbonate sediment dissolution across the GBR. Sites across north-south and inshore-offshore gradients were selected to capture natural variability in temperature, aragonite saturation, and organic matter enrichment. Preliminary results indicate that sediments at 3 of 7 reefs are currently in a state of net dissolution. Simulated future ocean acidification conditions (pH shift of 0.2) increased sediment dissolution rates. Carbonate sediment dissolution rates were not simply driven by latitudinal or inshore-offshore gradients, highlighting the complex interaction of local environmental factors. This presentation will discuss the drivers of carbonate sediment dissolution across the GBR and provide insight for predicting future shifts in reef sediment budgets under ocean acidification.
bea18de5: Predicted sea-level rise and increased storm frequency caused by climate change drastically threaten low-lying inhabited coral atolls. Coral reef frameworks are the atolls’ primary defence from these changes. The growth and integrity of these frameworks is reflected in their carbonate budgets, a dynamic balance between biogenic accretion through coral growth and erosional forces, both of which are affected by factors acting at different spatial scales. We explored how carbonate budgets, estimated using the ReefBudget methodology, vary between three inhabited atolls that face differing anthropogenic stressors in the Lakshadweep Archipelago in the Northern Indian Ocean. We surveyed ten reefs, at two depths each, across the three islands. Overall, net carbonate budgets of reefs across all atolls were below optimal production rates needed to continue protecting shorelines (5 G measured on healthy reefs). This was a result of repeated mass bleaching events as well as local impacts. Carbonate production was influenced by a recent mass bleaching event in 2016 and a cyclone in 2018, and varied between depths and exposures, potentially due to differential recovery and mortality dynamics. Erosional processes were locally mediated with both urchin and parrotfish density showing large differences between islands, possibly linked to nutrient outflow and fishing intensity. We also find that by the year 2100, a large proportion of shallow sites will experience an increase in water depth above half a metre under moderate and high emissions scenarios, but none will breach this threshold under a low emissions scenario. Our results show that patterns of carbonate production were largely mediated by the history of global/regional disturbances, while erosional rates were much more dependent on local factors.
cd0d18ed: Tropical coral reefs are undergoing rapid declines in coral cover, leading to reduced carbonate production and diminished reef accretion capacity. While the role of primary reef-building corals in reef bioconstruction is well established, the ecological dynamics and calcification contributions of secondary reef builders (e.g., calcifying algae, bryozoans, serpulid polychaetes and vermetid gastropods) remain comparatively understudied. These organisms can significantly influence reef framework development and generate substantial quantities of detrital carbonate sediment, especially in the absence of corals. However, spatial variability in net calcification among secondary reef builders and across environmental gradients remains poorly understood. Here we use Calcification Accretion Units (CAUs; n = 62) deployed at 13 reefs across three Southeast Asian countries (the Philippines, Singapore and Thailand) for a one-year period to quantify carbonate production by secondary reef builders across clear-water and turbid settings.
