Though shades tempered paling visually, they had little effect on symbiont:host ratios during this event. 
Overall these studies identify widespread survival of brain and boulder corals through a major marine heatwave, but also spatial and species-specific variation. Differences in survival as well as temperature exist at both large (100km) and small (100m) spatial scales. Temporally, excessive temperatures in 2024 had minimal impact on 2023 survivors, ostensibly because of a shift in symbionts. Yet even in this high temperature but mild paling year, in situ intervention through shading likely minimized stress on targeted corals.
e69ae4c4: Coral reefs have declined sharply over the past half century as increasingly frequent and intense marine heat waves are driving widespread coral bleaching and mortality. Understanding how bleaching susceptibility varies across life-history stages is therefore crucial for predicting a population’s demographic structure. Although juvenile corals have been reported to have a higher tolerance to marine heatwaves in nature than adult corals, experimental confirmation of this capacity has been lacking. Here we investigated the size-dependence of heat tolerance for the corymbose reef-building coral Acropora digitifera across a broad range of colony sizes from 1 to 29 cm geometric mean diameter (GMD). We collected 89 colonies from a fringing reef in Palau and exposed them to a simulated 6-week heatwave reaching 32.5 °C and a cumulative stress of ~20 °C-weeks, measured in Degree Heating Weeks. Surprisingly, we found that heat tolerance increased with colony size, such that larger colonies (~25 cm GMD) could withstand ~1.3 additional °C-weeks of heat stress to reach 50% mortality compared to the smallest colonies (~1.5 cm). Using a Generalized Additive Model and segmented regression, we identified a breakpoint in the LD50–size relationship at 12–15 cm, representing the size threshold above which heat tolerance rises. The high tolerance of juveniles in field surveys has been linked to micro-habitat protection (e.g., shading, reduced flow) and physiological traits such as enhanced heat dissipation, symbiont flexibility, and plasticity. Our findings are at odds with such observations and highlight the importance of disentangling physiological and ecological drivers across size classes. Our results suggest that juveniles appear more tolerant in the wild not due to intrinsic physiology, but because microhabitat complexity may reduces thermal and irradiance stress.  Incorporating these size-specific responses into demographic models will enhance the accuracy of future predictions under continued ocean warming.
a7e98c7b: Cryptobenthic communities play fundamental roles in coral reef functioning, but their long-term responses to disturbance remain poorly understood in the Red Sea. Autonomous Reef Monitoring Structures (ARMS) provide a standardized monitoring framework that integrates visual and molecular approaches to assess these assemblages. Although ARMS photo-analysis is rarely used, it provides fine microhabitat resolution that complements whole-unit traditional molecular methods, which overlook differences across plates and surface orientations. Here, we analyse a time-series of ARMS images from representative plates deployed at four reefs across two central Red Sea locations, spanning more than a decade, including the 2015–2016 and 2023–2024 mass-bleaching events, and representing five retrievals. Focusing on the sessile cryptobenthic community (SCC), we examined colonization patterns across contrasting microhabitats created by the ARMS architecture, including high- and low-flow surfaces and downward versus upward surface orientations. Percent cover of SCC taxa was estimated using stratified random point counts, with taxa identified to the lowest possible taxonomic level. We hypothesize that both time (thermal stress; stochastic) and microhabitat structure (flow regime and surface orientation; deterministic) will strongly influence the patterns of SCC assemblages. We expect that temporal beta-diversity will reflect major bleaching events, with turnover patterns indicating cryptobenthic restructuring linked to broader reef degradation, and that reefs experiencing greater coral mortality will exhibit more homogenized sessile communities over time. By generating the first microhabitat-resolved, photo-based ARMS dataset for the Red Sea, this study demonstrates the value of ARMS as a scalable, non-destructive tool for assessing cryptobenthic responses to climate-driven reef disturbance.
6bbff75f: Molecular ecological studies have been instrumental in advancing our understanding of coral reefs. Although initially focused primarily on broad-scale connectivity patterns, they have gradually uncovered extensive local genetic structuring and cryptic diversity. Genome-wide sequencing has accelerated the discovery of these patterns, but because studies are typically conducted on individual species with population-level metadata, it has remained challenging to evaluate the broader ecological relevance of this biodiversity. In this study, we assess cryptic diversity and associated fine-scale niche partitioning using a reefscape genomics approach, where we conducted reef-wide photogrammetric mapping and genotyping across 25 scleractinian coral species in Curaçao (Southern Caribbean). Reduced-representation genome sequencing revealed cryptic diversity across the vast majority of species, highlighting the extent of cryptic diversity across Caribbean corals. For more than half of the species, cryptic lineages showed distinct depth distributions, underscoring the central role of depth in generating and maintaining this diversity. Using a newly developed package to streamline the extraction of environmental metrics from photogrammetry data, we further characterized 3D microhabitat associations for >2,000 genotyped corals. These results confirmed that nominal species exhibit much stronger niche differentiation than is apparent from depth distributions alone, and that cryptic lineages themselves are separated by marked ecological differences. Overall, these results highlight how much coral species biodiversity has been underestimated on Caribbean reefs, and the degree of environmental specialization underlying this cryptic diversity. They stress the importance of overcoming the methodological barriers to detecting cryptic lineages in situ, ensuring that our attempts to understand these rapidly changing ecosystems are not confounded by species units that do not accurately reflect underlying biological diversity.
2bbbf5a9: Reef restoration practitioners aim to preserve coral genetic diversity by protecting diverse reefs and cultivating diverse genotypes in coral nurseries. However, the recent discovery of cryptic genetic lineages within nearly all coral species complicates restoration strategies, as it is unclear what role between-lineage genetic divergence could play in adaptation. In the great star coral, Montastraea cavernosa, previous research has identified six cryptic lineages, some of which are strongly depth-segregated. Here, we performed a reciprocal transplantation experiment using two cryptic lineages restricted to shallow water (<10m depth), with one lineage more common on nearshore reefs and the other on offshore reefs. Our goal was to quantify lineage-specific responses to the environment that explain the genetic and ecological divergence between the two lineages. Contrary to our expectations, the strongest response we observed was not lineage-specific. Instead, both lineages exhibited strong and similar changes in growth proxies and metabolomic profiles, depending on the transplantation habitat. Preliminary analysis suggests that the local environment has a more significant influence on the gene expression profiles of transplanted corals than their lineage. Together, these results suggest that cryptic lineages employ similar mechanisms of adaptation and acclimatization to environmental challenges, despite their genetic distinction, raising the possibility of adaptive introgression between them. If this is indeed the case, adaptive evolution of the M. cavernosa lineage complex would benefit from the diversity gained through cultivating lineages together,  rather than selecting one particular “super coral” type.
0ea4e46e: Rising ocean temperatures and an increase in the frequency and intensity of marine heatwaves threaten the persistence and functioning of the world’s tropical reefs by directly increasing mortality of reef-building corals. The extent to which coral populations will be able to adapt to altered environments is therefore a question of critical importance. If heritable, diversity in thermal tolerance among coral colonies may provide the raw material on which selection may act, yielding increased population fitness over time. To better understand the range, spatial patterns, and eco-evolutionary drivers of thermal tolerances present within coral populations in the tropical Pacific, we used the Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System (CBASS) to assay the heat stress response of >700 colonies of Acropora hyacinthus (hyacinth table coral) and Pocillopora acuta (cauliflower coral), two common shallow reef species/complexes in American Samoa. Using a multivariate modeling approach combined with whole-genome and amplicon sequencing, we evaluated the roles of location, depth, cryptic host species, clonal lineage, and algal symbiont community in driving variation in thermal tolerance across space and species. Although there were some interesting consistencies, we found mostly species-specific patterns in the distribution and drivers of thermal tolerance, revealing divergent evolutionary strategies across the two species groups that are likely driven by key differences in life history.  A. hyacinthus thermal tolerance was driven primarily by algal symbiont community composition and location, followed depth and cryptic species. In contrast, the strongest drivers of thermal tolerance in P. acuta were location and genet, with depth and symbiont composition contributing little additional explanatory power. These data contribute a comprehensive understanding of the drivers of thermal tolerance in Pacific shallow reef corals and highlight the need for confident cryptic species assignment and species-specific ecological evaluations prior to planning adaptive management and conservation initiatives.
08168f53: The genetic diversity, size and connectivity of reef-building coral populations play central roles in their demographic resilience and adaptation to ocean warming. For broadcast spawner species with high dispersal and environmentally acquired microalgal symbionts, genetic diversity can be shaped by the interaction of local adaptation and gene flow across wide geographic ranges. Yet, limited datasets exist to jointly examine the spatial genetic variation of coral symbiotic partners across local and broad regional scales. Here, we present a comprehensive investigation of both coral hosts and Symbiodiniaceae genetic diversity in Acropora spathulata , using whole-genome sequencing data from 1,088 colonies across the Western Pacific. We found considerable dispersal of coral larvae in this taxon, around ~100 km per generation, that results in weak genetic structure across vast spatial scales and large breeding populations. Due to geographic isolation, populations separated by thousands of kilometers show stronger genetic differentiation with onset of divergence estimated ~0.27–0.65 million years ago. However, we also provide evidence that coral populations from New Caledonia and the Great Barrier Reef have maintained gene flow throughout their evolutionary history due to occasional long-distance dispersal. By contrast, the associated Cladocopium symbionts exhibit finer-scale genetic structure driven by environmental gradients, and independent of coral host population boundaries or symbiont phylogeny. Our results demonstrate how hologenome data can reveal the interplay between evolutionary processes and environmental factors shaping coral holobiont genetic diversity, as well as aiding in the delineation of conservation units and informing their adaptive potential.
259bed45: Growing evidence suggests that many morphologically defined coral species contain multiple genetically distinct groups, or cryptic taxa. These complex patterns of genetic diversity present a challenge to traditional conservation, but also an exciting opportunity to reveal the fundamental processes by which species diverge genomically and ecologically. The table coral, Acropora hyacinthus, is one such cryptic complex, with recent population genetic and phylogenetic work describing numerous cryptic sympatric groups, some of which may represent independent species. However, previous studies have been limited in geographic extent, sample size per location, or in the number of genetic markers surveyed. As such, the relative strength of within-location population structure vs. isolation by distance at the oceanic scale, the extent of historical and contemporary gene flow between taxa, and the degree to which cryptic taxa may share adaptive genetic variation, remains unclear. I will present the work of a collaborative network combining 1300 new and existing A. hyacinthus samples from over 40 sites throughout the species’ Pacific range. We will subject these sequences to a standardized whole-genome sequencing and genotyping pipeline, and use this unique dataset to create a clearer and broader view of diversity within the A. hyacinthus complex. We will distinguish cryptic population structure from the effects of allopatric divergence, and estimate effective migration rates across the range of each cryptic taxa. We will also identify admixed individuals and infer the history of gene flow across the complex, revealing the degree of long-term reproductive isolation between taxa. Finally, we will perform genotype-environment association within each taxa to infer loci involved in local adaptation, and quantify the extent to which adaptive alleles are shared across taxa. Together, our work will reveal the evolution of this ubiquitous coral at the Pacific scale, and create a rich resource for future A. hyacinthus research and conservation.
a05346d1: Japan’s coastal waters are shaped by the Kuroshio Current, a major warm current flowing from Okinawa through the Tokara Islands to the temperate Pacific coasts of Shikoku and Honshu. Although the Kuroshio appears to form a direct corridor from the tropics to the north, coral biogeography reveals a substantial divide between tropical and temperate ecosystems. Climate change is often assumed to transport tropical corals northward along the current, but population genetic evidence shows a more complex reality. Across multiple reef-building taxa—from the Acropora hyacinthus species complex to Pocillopora and Goniopora—genetically distinct temperate lineages have been identified that do not occur in the tropics. These findings match larval dispersal models indicating a physical and oceanographic barrier between tropical and temperate waters. Environmental factors also shape distributions: in Pocillopora damicornis, turbidity and temperature jointly explain the contrasting ranges of two major lineages. In Goniopora spp., morphological species boundaries fail to predict genetic structure, and strong genetic breaks occur between temperate and subtropical regions.Moreover, many coral species remain restricted to waters south of Tanegashima–Yakushima, suggesting that simple northward expansion is not feasible for numerous taxa. Corals that do expand poleward tend to be species already native to temperate regions, often showing distinct Symbiodiniaceae associations and localized adaptation at their northern limits. In contrast, temperate specialists such as Acropora pruinosa exhibit strong genetic structure, no recent northward movement, and signs of population decline driven by inbreeding and environmental degradation.Overall, climate-driven redistribution of corals is strongly constrained by pre-existing biogeographic barriers, lineage-specific adaptations, and the fragility of isolated temperate populations, emphasizing the need for conservation strategies that incorporate genetic diversity and limited connectivity.
e45d8307: Many reef-building corals present a continuum of phenotypic traits which make studying demography challenging. Across the Hawaiian Archipelago, this phenomenon is especially pronounced within the Porites genus, which exhibits non-discrete morphologies, variable pigmentation, and differences in corallite size and microstructure. These traits complicate species identification and make long-term monitoring of community composition and species-specific responses to disturbance difficult. Traditional molecular identification methods do not provide sufficient resolution as Porites spp. genomes are highly conserved, known to hybridize, and lack fully annotated references. Here, we use ezRAD, a form of restriction-site associated DNA sequencing (RADseq), to investigate species boundaries, reproductive strategies, and intraspecific relatedness of Porites compressa and Porites lobata populations across Maui. Additionally, this study leverages a decade-long monitoring program that utilizes structure-from-motion photogrammetry and large-area imaging technology to map genetic structure onto 3D models of coral reefs. Analyses show distinct loci present in clearly established morphotypes likely corresponding to species-level distinctions, yet corals with nondescript growth forms and unclear colony boundaries displayed shared genetic signatures, pointing to gene flow and potential introgression/hybridization. Further, geographic distance matrices do not inform trends in genetic relatedness and Porites show little evidence for isolation by distance in our sample set. By integrating genomic and spatial datasets, this project provides new insights into the genetic structure of Porites populations and demonstrates an approach for linking community-scale monitoring and in situ reef management strategies with fine-scale genetic resolution. Beyond these biological insights, this work offers a scalable tool for characterizing connectivity and population structure in morphologically convergent coral genera like Porites.
b387e021: Ocean transgressions and regressions during the last Pleistocene Glaciation exposed and submerged extensive portions of the Brazilian continental shelf. Because the Abrolhos reef complex is the only place that harbors all shallow-water coral species described for Brazil, it was traditionally hypothesized as a refugium for coral populations, such as those of Favia gravida. To test this scenario, we analyzed approximately 20,000 SNPs obtained through RAD-seq from nine populations distributed along the Brazilian coast. Phylogenetic and population structure analyses (svdQuartets, SNAPP, and SNMF) consistently revealed three genetically distinct lineages (Northern, Northeastern, and Eastern) whose geographic distributions mirror their genetic boundaries, indicating independent evolutionary histories rather than a recent split from a single ancestral population. Demographic reconstructions with Stairway Plot 2 revealed pronounced population bottlenecks in the Eastern and Northeastern lineages. Importantly, we tested multiple combinations of mutation rates and generation times, and all parameter sets converged on the same bottleneck signature, reinforcing that the demographic contraction is a robust signal in the data rather than an artifact of model assumptions. Bayesian and spatial migration analyses (BayesAss, EEMS) revealed minimal recent gene flow, with migration rates < 2% of individuals per generation among lineages. DIYABC simulations further supported a scenario of multiple, isolated refugia (PP = 0.91). Together, these findings challenge the prevailing single refugium paradigm and suggest that Favia gravida persisted in at least three semi-independent refugia, each maintaining unique genetic diversity. Conserving these regions is therefore essential for preserving the species’ evolutionary potential.
8c2a0baf: Rising sea temperatures are driving widespread coral decline, yet recent sequencing has revealed extensive unexplored cryptic diversity within many reef-building taxa. These morphologically similar but genetically distinct lineages may differ in their ecological roles and environmental tolerances. We examined three cryptic lineages (L1–L3) of massive Porites corals distributed across “classic” (typical) and “extreme” (warmer, low-light) reef habitats in Palau to assess how genetic and environmental factors shape their performance. Algal symbiont and bacterial community composition varied by lineage and reef type. Thermal stress experiments showed that lineages dominant in extreme habitats (L2, L3) were more resistant to heat stress but had lower light-harvesting efficiency compared to the lineage common in classic habitats (L1). In reciprocal transplantation experiments, L1, but not L2, colonies experienced high mortality when moved from classic to extreme reefs, indicating strong environmental filtering. Thermal resistance after transplantation was primarily explained by lineage identity and colony origin, suggesting that thermal tolerance is shaped by a combination of genetic factors and environmental history. Gene expression profiles and physiological metrics, including energy reserves and pigment concentrations, differed consistently among lineages and were minimally affected by reef type. Together, our results show that cryptic Porites lineages differ in thermal tolerance, physiology, and symbiosis, leading to their contrasting distributions across reef environments. This lineage-level variation likely contributes to spatial patterns in bleaching susceptibility and mortality within massive Porites populations and highlights the need to incorporate cryptic genetic diversity into reef management and restoration planning.
e36a3f8d: Glacial cycles led to a series of biodiversity contraction and expansion events, culminating in many known refugia of diversity at high latitudes. In contrast, how these glacial cycles shaped present-day genetic diversity in tropical corals remains uncertain. Specifically, the mechanisms by which periods of low sea-level stand contributed to the diversification of widely distributed coral species and their remaining signatures to within species genetic diversity are mostly undescribed. Here, we tackle this question by leveraging existing genomic data of widely distributed lineages of Pachyseris speciosa across the Indo-West Pacific and complementing them with new whole genome sequences from the Coral Triangle (i.e., the Philippines and Indonesia). The Coral Triangle is a region of interest because its basins were repeatedly isolated during glacial cycles. We first examined patterns of divergence between lineages (i.e., strict isolation, isolation with migration, or isolation with introgression) while incorporating potential contributions of selection. Using Approximate Bayesian Computation, we show that pairwise divergence between lineages follows isolation with introgression, highlighting the roles of isolation and reconnection during low-sea-level stands. Importantly, the support on heterogenous genomic effective population size and gene flow indicates potential roles of selection on the divergence of P. speciosa lineages. We further examined the role of introgression focusing on two lineages we sampled within the Coral Triangle. Using Site Frequency Spectrum-based demographic modeling, we show that a model with series of isolation and gene flow is supported in both lineages of P. speciosa. Overall, our results reveal the impacts of barriers during low-sea-level stands for both between and within lineages. In diverging lineages, barriers result in isolation, which is likely sustained by selection, leading to divergence between widely distributed lineages. Within lineages however, low-sea-level stands resulted in a series of isolation and reconnection, potentially “pumping” genetic diversity into these coral lineages.
79894422: Large-area imagery (LAI) has seen exponential growth among coral reef scientists and practitioners for monitoring and science over the past decade as the tools and techniques required become increasingly accessible. The adoption of LAI is still not without its challenges, as it has dramatically increased data volumes and compute demands. Combined with the need for multi-application workflows, data handling and extraction from LAI is burdensome compared to traditional approaches. Here we describe an organizational framework for handling LAI datasets, using over 3500 surveys from more than 2000 unique 100m² reef sites mapped at sub-mm resolution over the last 14 years. We present ways in which this dataset, consisting of approximately 1.2 petabytes of source imagery and derived 3D and 2D products, is managed within a single project using the Viscore platform to facilitate large-scale analyses across space and time. By collating this dataset, we provide examples of how computational routines can be applied across all surveys for a standardized analysis of structural complexity for monitoring or reporting on data quality. Support for web hosting image-based workflows such as percent cover analyses are also available, enabling collaborative contributions by annotators while centralizing computational system demands. Additional web hosting of 2D imagery supports exploration and visualization by the public, or access to high-resolution imagery in support of detailed annotation of derived products, such as segmentation for demographic analyses in TagLab. Finally, we include metadata standards associated with raw imagery for individual LAI datasets that have been archived and made available in a digital repository for users to access and apply to their own established LAI workflows.
3b218ad1: The Hawai‘i Monitoring and Reporting Collaborative (HIMARC) program was established to support marine resource management by combining data into a collaborative database using underwater benthic and fish survey data collected by a network of monitoring programs across the Hawaiian Islands. Each monitoring program uses unique methods, formats, and workflows, which creates challenges when combining datasets. To address this complexity, HIMARC developed a standardized data integration pipeline that includes a series of data formatting and comprehensive Quality Assurance and Quality Control (QA/QC) protocols. All datasets shared with HIMARC are: 1) reviewed thoroughly to ensure a clear interpretation of the original data and overall structure of the survey design, 2) transformed into a consistent format designed to retain key features of original data structure, 3) checked for missing data, errors, and duplicates, and 4) integrated into a combined multi-partner database. These data formatting and QA/QC protocols not only produce a unified database but also strengthen communication and relationships between data providers regarding methods, metadata, and data quality. By following this process of data integration, HIMARC contributes to capacity building for data QA/QC within each partner organization, bridges gaps created by different methodologies, and supports statewide analyses. The collaborative database has served as a vehicle for enhancing the quality and collaborative efforts of coral reef monitoring and resulted in a shared effort between scientists and managers to make informed decisions.
361115d6: The FAIR Guiding Principles, published in 2016, significantly boosted data sharing (findability and accessibility) in the scientific community, yet achieving true interoperability and reusability remains a persistent challenge.The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration's Coral Reef Conservation Program (CRCP) mirrored this open data movement, shifting from offline to online data access. However, despite making data accessible, CRCP found that stakeholder needs were not met because the Program was not producing the desired data products. A major hurdle to interoperability within CRCP is integrating data from multiple sources to gain a cohesive regional perspective on reef health. Even when using the same methodology, data collected by different offices often varies in formatting, such as column names, units of measurement, or granularity. These inconsistencies complicate national-scale data integration, analysis, and reporting. Historically, this has led to reports taking years to produce due to the extensive time required for data translation, and the lack of data consistency has hindered the development of data visualization tools. We will detail the evolution of CRCP's data management, including the shift from offline to online data distribution, and share key lessons learned. Increased data sharing initially exposed quality control concerns, prompting workflow improvements and additional data access points. Discussions with stakeholders captured further issues and identified the need for aggregated data summaries to answer managers’ questions. Recognizing this demand led to a redesign of the CRCP website and the development of the National Coral Reef Monitoring Program Data Visualization Tool. This tool allows users to graph and download summarized fish, benthic, sea water chemistry, and socioeconomic data variables across various geographic scales. The effort to make the underlying raw data for the NCRMP tool accessible has driven significant quality improvements across the entire data life cycle and established a solid foundation for data management across all funded projects.
7013e3bf: The soft coral Unomia stolonifera has invaded numerous areas since its introduction in 2005 along the eastern coast of Venezuela, causing severe negative impact on coral reefs native biodiversity. In 2017, it was reported on the rocky reef of Valle Seco, Choroní (central coast). The objective of this study was to estimate the substrate recolonization by U.stolonifera. Fixed 50 × 50 cm quadrats were established: five served as controls and five were manipulated by removing all Unomia from 25% of the quadrat area (625 cm2). Photographs of each quadrat were taken over an 11-month period (March 2023 to January 2024), and the images were analyzed using CPCe software to estimate temporal changes in percent cover. The difference in mean percent cover at the beginning and the end of the study was 14.55% (SE=4.95) in the control quadrats and 15.22 % (SE=3.61) in the experimental and there were no significant differences between treatments (F = 0.0345, p = 0.85). High variability was observed over time, including decreases in cover (“retreats”). For the control quadrats, there was a trend toward increased Unomia cover during July, August, and September, but no significant differences were found between months (F = 2.97, p = 0.090). Only at the area removed, a higher Unomia cover was observed, with 76.23% (SE=1.75) of difference in initial vs final cover, and significant difference between the time of the cut and the fourth month (t≥ 3.475, p≤ 0.05). Understanding the temporal growth dynamics of U. stolonifera is essential for designing and implementing effective control measures to manage its spread. Currently, different control methods developed by scientists and civil society are being tested to apply them and avoid a greater expansion of this species, which has significantly affected the livelihoods of local communities that depend on fishing and tourism.
a71dae31:
Harbor Porites is a taxonomically ambiguous and unusually resilient coral found in high abundance in Honolulu Harbor, an environment hostile to most corals due to sedimentation, reduced water quality, and chronic human disturbance. The success of this little-understood species in such degraded conditions raises questions about its ecological role: specifically, whether it poses a risk as a potential invasive species that could spread to healthy reefs, or functions as a pioneer species supporting reef growth in disturbed habitats.	
To assess its invasive potential, we conducted a pairwise competition experiment with five common Hawaiian corals. Harbor Porites was consistently dominated by most competitors, showed dominance only against another stress-tolerant species, and interacted neutrally with the closely related Porites lobata. These outcomes indicate low competitive ability in mixed-species assemblages and suggest that its distribution may be limited to disturbed environments where competition is reduced. 
To assess its pioneer potential, we conducted larval settlement experiments across multiple coral species, testing both the suitability of Harbor Porites skeleton as a settlement substrate and the influence of chemical cues released by live colonies on larval settlement behaviors. Larvae showed a significant preference for settling on Harbor Porites skeleton relative to other substrates, while chemical cues did not significantly affect settlement rates or timing. These findings suggest that Harbor Porites may act as a pioneer species by providing stable settlement substrate that facilitates coral recruitment and early reef development in marginal environments.
Overall, Harbor Porites is unlikely to threaten healthy reef communities through competitive dominance but may play a pioneer role in disturbed habitats by providing stable reef structure and settlement substrate that facilitate coral recruitment. Understanding how such highly resilient corals function within extreme urban reef systems can inform management and restoration strategies for coastal ecosystems increasingly impacted by human activity.
ffddfb08: Lionfish (Pterois volitans/miles) are considered established throughout the northwestern Atlantic Ocean, Caribbean Sea, and Gulf of Mexico, but their invasion front continues into the Mediterranean Sea and southern Atlantic. At the onset of the invasion, lionfish were thought to lack native predators with populations largely released from top-down regulation. While divers have reported successfully feeding speared lionfish to sharks, eels, grouper, and snapper, studies examining natural control of lionfish by predators are limited. In regions where lionfish are considered established, a better understanding of the processes that control lionfish densities is needed. Using acoustic telemetry, we deployed novel predation tags and examined changes in movement patterns to evaluate the frequency of predation on tagged lionfish within a complex, healthy, coral reef system. Predation on lionfish was remarkably high, with movement patterns suggesting that both reef-associated and pelagic species preyed upon lionfish. While it is unclear at what point in the invasion native predators began consuming lionfish as prey, predation clearly plays an important role in population control in their established range. These findings further support the important role that native predators play in regulating the population dynamics of invasive species and maintaining healthy ecosystems .
6e5db3ba: The number of benthic marine species that are becoming invasive worldwide is increasing. Azooxanthellate corals of the Tubastraea genus are recognized globally as invasive marine species and have caused significant environmental, economic, and social impacts. These corals have two distinct phases: the larval phase, in which they are mobile, and after recruitments, the sessile adult phase. The success in the recruitment phase which is influenced by the reproductive rate, substratum type and inclination and the composition the receptor community are crucial for the establishment of their population. Here we investigated the settlement of Tubastraea spp. onto submerged acrylic plates (artificial substrate) at different angles using as well as the abundance of adult colonies on different rocky shores in a Marine Protected Area in Brazil. We also related the settlement of Tubastraea to the encrusting benthic community and the topographic complexity of the rocky shore. There was an exponential growth in the number of individuals settling throughout the experiment, which lasted one year and Tubastraea settled at all four angles of inclination of the artificial substrate. Adult colonies on natural substrate were also found at all available angles on the rocky reef, but mostly vertically. On the natural substrate, the abundance of Tubastraea spp. was lower in the presence of mat-forming species. On the artificial substrate, the number of settled individuals was lower in the presence of encrusting benthic species, such as ascidians and crustose coralline algae. Although the host community may offer some biotic resistance, the high reproductive rate and the ability to grow in different orientations make these invasive corals successful.
13cc0751: The introduction of invasive species into the marine environment is a global problem, as it often results in biodiversity loss. Over the past two decades, exotic Cnidaria species have been found on the bottoms of Venezuela, Southern Caribbean. The most critical and widely reported case is that of the soft coral Unomia stolonifera, first reported in 2014 in the eastern region of the country. More recently, in 2019, we found and registered, the first exotic stony coral species, classified within the Acropora tenuis group (Dana 1846), according to phylogenetic and morphological analysis. At that time, only one colony of this species was found, but by April 2023, at least 50 colonies were observed at the same location, occupying an area of 11 m². Considering this fast population increase and the fact that several colonies could be sexually mature, the species was controlled by removing it from the invaded area and continuously monitoring the zone for new outbreaks. In 2024, two other exotic species from the Acroporidae family were found, Montipora aequituberculata Bernard, 1897 with tiered, overlapping plates that form whorls, and the branching coral Montipora digitata (Dana, 1846). Both species were morphologically identified. These three species were found in Morrocoy National Park, on the west-central coast of the country. Unlike the first encounter with the exotic Acropora, numerous colonies of M. aequituberculata were found together, forming a shallow reef of approximately half a hectare (200 m long x 20 m wide). Many colonies of M. digitata were also found, but they were much more dispersed throughout the invaded area. All three species are native to the Indo-Pacific region, and their presence in the Caribbean is concerning due to their high growth rates and successful asexual reproduction (through fragmentation), leading them to be considered potentially invasive species.
3f258008: Coral reefs exist within a broad seascape of abiotic conditions. The combination of these conditions – the environmental envelope – defines the physiological limits within which reef-building organisms construct the geological structures that support extraordinary biodiversity. Yet, despite early efforts nearly three decades ago, we still lack a clear, quantitative framework that explains the conditions under which reefs develop. Here, we integrate three major recent advances – high-resolution coral reef maps, large-scale environmental data, and cutting-edge machine-learning tools – to: (1) quantify the multivariate abiotic conditions of reef occurrences; (2) delineate global reef limits and identify marginal environments; and (3) forecast future changes in reef occurrence under climate change. Leveraging sixteen key abiotic variables, we show that coral reefs occupy a remarkably narrow proportion (~16%) of the available environmental space in shallow oceans. Machine-learning models accurately reproduced this envelope, identifying minimum monthly sea surface temperature and light-penetration depth as the two strongest predictors of reef occurrence globally. Interrogating model outputs revealed two major patterns. First, locations with suitable environments but without reefs are rare, suggesting that reefs already occupy nearly all conditions that permit growth. Second, many reefs occur near their environmental limits. These areas provide the first strictly quantitative, multivariate definition of marginal reefs, moving beyond traditional single-variable thresholds. Finally, we use our framework to forecast shifts in the probabilities of reef occurrence under future warming. By 2100, mean probabilities decline by 8.5% and 13.7% under moderate (SSP1-2.6; +1.8 ºC) and high emissions (SSP3-7.0; +3.6 ºC) scenarios, respectively, with marginal reef environments more than doubling in both cases. Nonetheless, some temperate and currently reef-free regions show localised increases in suitability. Taken together, our work establishes a robust global framework for understanding, mapping, and forecasting the environmental limits of coral reefs, providing an empirical basis for coral reef conservation prioritisation.
d5af1377:
Reef-building corals are the quintessential marine example of “Hutchinson’s paradox”. Like tropical trees, the number of coexisting species within communities seems to far exceed the relatively few environmental axes along which corals partition resources, in violation of the Principle of Competitive Exclusion. Although several classical biodiversity theories were conceived, at least in part, with coral reefs in mind (e.g., the Intermediate Disturbance Hypothesis, the Lottery Hypothesis, and Neutral Theory of Biodiversity), coral ecology still lacks mathematical coexistence theory that can reproduce anything remotely approaching the diversity that we observe on the world’s most species-rich reefs. Without such a theory, we cannot anticipate how species interactions may mediate the impacts of environmental change, which severely threatens our ability to anticipate how reef coral assemblages will transform in coming decades. 

In this talk, I will review earlier attempts to construct a tractable, predictive theory of biodiversity, and identify what we can learn from them. I also outline a framework of model formulation and analysis, and empirical calibration and testing, to quantify the relative importance of different coexistence mechanisms, drawing on advances in coexistence theory for tropical forests. A key part of this program is bridging the gap of empirical demonstration of the existence of a mechanism (e.g., Janzen-Connell effects) at local scales, with estimates of the magnitude of the mechanisms’ effects on species coexistence at community and metacommunity levels. I will present new findings from this program of research, including the surprisingly weak coexistence-promoting effect of differential responses to environmental fluctuations (i.e., the lottery mechanism), and the potential for micro-scale niche partitioning and other processes that promote aggregation at small scales to have greater coexistence-promoting effects that previously recognized. A comprehensive, rigorous theory of coral reef biodiversity that can form a foundation for anticipating reef futures is beginning to take shape.
f975c311: Marine heatwaves and nutrient enrichment increasingly destabilize coral–algal symbioses, driving low-oxygen conditions that cause hypoxia or anoxia and disrupt reef ecosystem function. Several environmental factors strongly influence coral health, bleaching, and mortality such as seawater temperature, nutrient availability, dissolved oxygen concentration, solar irradiation levels, coral colony morphology, and fine-scale hydrodynamics. However, how these variables jointly govern coral stress remains poorly quantified. Here, we present a model that links in-situ thermal stress and reduced flow around and through coral colonies to observed bleaching patterns on the reef. Reduced flow weakens daytime oxygen supersaturation and intensifies nighttime oxygen minima, producing diel oscillations that may destabilize the coral holobiont. To evaluate these dynamics, we draw on long-term field measurements collected during the record marine heatwave in 2023, including continuous dissolved oxygen records from loggers positioned within branching thickets and adjacent to Pocillopora favosa colonies, co-located temperature and light sensors, and hydrodynamic monitoring. Colonies located in high-flow areas remained unbleached, while those in low-flow zones bleached during the heatwave (with subsequent recovery), providing a natural contrast for model validation. The model couplies fluid motion, oxygen dynamics, temperature, and solar irradiance to estimate near- and far-field oxygen levels around coral colonies, with boundary conditions informed by holobiont oxygen metabolism derived from prior laboratory studies. Model outputs reproduce the major diel and seasonal trends captured in the field observations, indicating that flow physics governs a substantial portion of the oxygen stress experienced by corals during marine heatwaves. The results highlight how integrative biophysical models can help anticipate coral vulnerability under future warming scenarios. We outline potential pathways for further model refinement.
d5583183: The geological record demonstrates that some warm water corals can expand to higher latitudes under ocean warming, but it remains uncertain whether they can expand quickly enough to keep up with anthropogenic climate change. Using a global model, we recently suggested that climate change will significantly outpace coral range expansion this century. However, simplifying assumptions used in that model for coral ecological and evolutionary dynamics (such as fixed colony size structure and genetic variance) are unlikely to hold under rapid climate change. Here, we present a new model for coral range expansion dynamics, explicitly simulating individual genomes and colony sizes across a realistic latitudinal environmental gradient, while also resolving environmental variability at a sub-reef level. This model simulates an idealised stepping-stone metapopulation, but emergent genetic variance and ecological dynamics reasonably resemble real coral populations. We find that model corals with parameters resembling typical tropical species are unable to keep pace with 21st century climate change, instead requiring centuries to expand beyond their modern distribution (if at all). While ecological and evolutionary dynamics are sensitive to model parameters, and coral futures under climate change are therefore strongly species-dependent, a lack of range expansion is a persistent feature. Latitudinal light attenuation acts as a further limitation on coral range expansion; this is not yet incorporated into our model but would be expected to further throttle range expansion potential. On the flip side, our simulations suggest that strong thermal gradients between geomorphic zones within reefs may result in considerable local differences in coral resilience under climate change. As a result, while earlier studies may have over-simplified future projections, the conclusion that range expansion potential is limited for most tropical coral species appears to remain robust.
ccfcc814:
Thermal tolerance is widely assumed to be the primary determinant of coral persistence under climate change. However, the importance of thermal tolerance relative to other population and species characteristics such as growth rate and genetic variation for long-term persistence has not been systematically evaluated. Using a simple eco-evolutionary model, we investigated coral reef futures under climate change across millions of simulations. We explored a comprehensive parameter space encompassing optimal population growth rate, additive genetic variance, thermal tolerance breadth, thermal stress tolerance, effective fecundity, larval immigration rate, and immigrant thermal optima. We forced the model with projected 21st century temperatures for Hawaiian reefs under three emissions scenarios (SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0) from a dynamically downscaled ocean model. We used random forest regression and permutation importance to quantify the sensitivity of coral cover trajectories to each parameter.

Across all emissions scenarios, population growth rate and additive genetic variance were the strongest predictors of long-term coral persistence, with thermal tolerance consistently the least important parameter. While corals with low thermal tolerance experienced greater short-term population collapse, populations with high optimal growth rates (>0.1 y⁻¹) and additive genetic variance (>0.1 °C²) best maintained coral cover throughout the century, regardless of thermal tolerance. The relative importance of genetic variance increased under more pessimistic warming scenarios. Our findings suggest that fast-growing coral taxa such as acroporids and montiporids may outperform slower-growing, stress-tolerant counterparts post-2050, provided they survive the acute threats of coming decades. These results underscore the critical importance of maintaining genetic diversity in both natural populations and restoration efforts, rather than focusing solely on selecting thermally tolerant genotypes.
8e953ece: The Janzen-Connell hypothesis proposes that host-specific pathogens accumulate near adult organisms, reducing the survival of nearby conspecific juveniles; this disproportionately hurts common species (whose juveniles more frequently encounter same-species adults) and thereby promotes coexistence. Applied to coral reef systems, the Janzen Connell hypothesis predicts that juvenile colonies situated near conspecific colonies will exhibit lower survival and growth rates than those near heterospecifics. Prior experimental tests of these predictions in corals have produced mixed results and have been limited to laboratory conditions. Here, we evaluate Janzen-Connell effects using observational data derived from the large-area imaging of the Palmyra Atoll, with 4 morphospecies across 4 sites and 7 years. Results show patterns consistent with Janzen-Connell effects: conspecific adults exert more negative effects on the survival and growth of juveniles, with the effect magnitude increasing with closer proximities and smaller juvenile colony area. These patterns are consistent across several model specifications, though they do not reach statistical significance in all cases. To assess biological significance, we used the Palmyra data to parameterize a spatially explicit simulation model. Virtual experiments show that Janzen-Connell effects are weakly stabilizing but alone are insufficient for coexistence. These findings suggest that coral diversity may be maintained by Janzen-Connell effects operating at earlier life stages (i.e., larvae or settlers), by the cumulative action of multiple weak coexistence mechanisms, or by alternative mechanisms such as habitat partitioning.
225f9be6:
Accurate predictions of coral reef trajectories rely on models that incorporate key demographic processes. A major uncertainty in these models lies in the early life stages, a critical bottleneck where density-dependent interactions, like competition, are hypothesized to affect coexistence and shape community structure. Empirically quantifying intra- and interspecific interactions for microscopic coral settlers has been a major challenge. We bridged this gap by utilising a vital staining technique to differentiate recent settlers by species and by tracking individual fates with high spatial resolution, enabling us to quantify how species interactions and larval behaviour shaped settlement patterns and the potential for coexistence.
We conducted multi-species settlement experiments using larvae from three key Indo-Pacific species: Acropora aff. hyacinthus, Acropora kenti, and Montipora aequituberculata. Our analysis used these high-resolution data to parameterise models of settlement success and spatial patterns. We tested a fundamental condition for stable coexistence—whether negative intraspecific interactions were stronger than interspecific interactions. We identified two potentially stabilizing mechanisms. First, species exhibited strong spatial niche partitioning: Montipora preferred tile tops while Acropora preferred sides. Second, settlers clustered significantly more with conspecifics than heterospecifics. Such spatial aggregation leads individual settlers to interact more with conspecific than heterospecific settlers, which intensifies intraspecific competition relative to interspecific competition, a phenomenon that tends to promote species coexistence.
This study calibrates early intra- and inter-specific competition and spatial patterning of settlement for these foundational species. This critical information about micro-scale processes is necessary to parameterize community models, which help us understand how meso-scale patterns in community structure are generated and maintained, ultimately improving our ability to forecast coral reef futures.
2f28580d: Recent warm temperatures driven by climate change have caused mass coral bleaching and mortality across the world, prompting managers, policymakers, and conservation practitioners to embrace restoration as a strategy to sustain coral reefs. Despite a proliferation of new coral reef restoration efforts globally and increasing scientific recognition and research on interventions aimed at supporting reef resilience to climate impacts, few restoration programs are currently incorporating climate change and resilience into project design. As climate change will continue to degrade coral reefs for decades to come, guidance is needed to support managers and restoration practitioners to conduct restoration that promotes resilience through enhanced coral reef recovery, resistance, and adaptation. Using a combination of literature review and expert information, we address this critical implementation gap by presenting 14 recommendations that integrate resilience principles into restoration design and practice, including for project planning and design, coral selection, site selection, and broader ecosystem context. In this session we will also discuss future opportunities to improve restoration methods to support enhanced outcomes for coral reefs in response to climate change. As coral reefs are one of the most vulnerable ecosystems to climate change, interventions that enhance reef resilience will help to ensure restoration efforts have a greater chance of success in a warming world. They are also more likely to provide essential contributions to global targets to protect natural biodiversity and the human communities that rely on reefs.
3be67631:
The Coral Nurture Program is a community-science initiative established in 2018 on the Great Barrier Reef and has since expanded from a single reef site to multiple sites across the region and, more recently, to international locations. Throughout its evolution, the program has emphasised resilience-based management of coral planting activities to maximise the return on restoration effort by enhancing the growth, survival, and long-term performance of outplanted corals.

To achieve this, the Coral Nurture Program employs a multi-pronged, evidence-informed approach. First, the program integrates detailed local site knowledge, supported by scientific validation, to optimise site selection for restoration success. Second, it applies innovative phenotyping technologies grounded in in situ observations during marine heatwaves to identify and prioritise high-tolerance coral taxa for outplanting. Third, the program increasingly incorporates corals sourced from unique extreme environments, with the aim of strengthening resilience within local reef communities. Fourth, the program is optimising trait-based approaches to community restoration to maximise the functional diversity retained through the planting practice. Finally, the program is working to consider the optimal biogeochemical niche of coral species, including opportunities for targeted nutritional supplementation to support resilience during periods of stress.

In addition to biological considerations, the Coral Nurture Program has navigated the practical challenges of planning, monitoring, and operational logistics, while fostering strong community engagement and social licence for the implementation of restoration activities. Through close collaboration between scientists, tourism operators, and local communities, the program has generated a model for scalable and adaptive reef-restoration practice.

This presentation will outline the practical tools developed, key scientific insights gained, and both the successes and failures of approaches trialled to date. Collectively, these outcomes demonstrate how the Coral Nurture Program is pioneering locally tailored resilience-based restoration strategies to adapt and refine coral-reef restoration activities in the face of climate change.
4c52a82d: Coral reef restoration is growing in response to global coral cover declines triggered by mass bleaching mortality events. With marine heatwaves only set to worsen under most future warming projections, there is a critical need for coral restoration initiatives to be resilient and adaptable to future heatwave impacts. Here, we present a climate-smart adaptive management framework designed to integrate climate change impacts and adaptation to future threats within coral restoration in Palau, a small Island Nation located in the western Pacific Ocean. The framework includes the adaptive potential of the ecosystem itself, the associated human communities that depend on them, and the governance structures that oversee the management of these ecosystems. It combines innovative scientific research, such as assisted evolution and heat stress-testing, to enhance the survival and performance of corals used in restoration efforts admits climate change. Co-developed through inclusive collaboration with Palauan state representatives and national government agencies, NGOs, scientists, and local communities, the framework aims to empower local stakeholders and restoration practitioners with innovative tools needed to help ecosystems recover from and adapt to future climate-related disturbances through coral restoration efforts. Ideas and lessons learned from this framework can be translated to other Pacific Island nations' contexts, offering a practical and scalable model for linking community-based action with national and regional policy goals. As climate pressures intensify, climate-smart approaches to coral restoration will be essential for safeguarding high-value reef ecosystems, local livelihoods, and ocean governance across the Pacific.
9dfcedbb: Coral heat tolerance has gradually increased over recent decades, but the rising frequency of marine heatwaves may well outpace future rates of adaptation. As the climate continues to warm, coral reef conservation and restoration must harness corals’ evolutionary potential. However, there is currently limited operational guidance on how to translate evolutionary principles into actionable, spatially explicit management strategies across reef seascapes. We present a framework that integrates patterns of thermal exposure with larval dispersal networks to classify reefs into functional units with distinct management priorities. Our framework addresses two practical questions: (1) which management intervention—among protection, restoration, or assisted evolution via deployment or collection of heat-tolerant corals—is most appropriate for a given reef, and (2) where should each intervention be prioritised to maximise long-term adaptive capacity across reef seascapes? Applying the framework to the Great Barrier Reef as a case study, we identified complementary regional priorities: southern reefs emerge as priorities for protection and the deployment of heat-adapted corals, while northern reefs serve as valuable heat-tolerant sources and targets for restoration. We also tested the sensitivity of the classification criteria across three threshold scenarios and found that stricter criteria progressively identify smaller, more confident sets of priority reefs, demonstrating how threshold choices influence management flexibility. In the coming years, our framework can be updated to support adaptive management as new insights become available from the rapidly advancing field of coral evolutionary biology. This framework offers restoration practitioners and reef managers a practical, evidence-based tool for translating evolutionary principles into strategic, climate-informed restoration planning that supports both ecological resilience and long-term adaptive potential as climate pressures intensify.
9307b468: The CoralCarib project, led by The Nature Conservancy (TNC), is a six-year initiative (2023–2028) funded by Germany’s International Climate Initiative (IKI) to restore and protect coral reefs across Cuba, the Dominican Republic, Haiti, and Jamaica. The project’s overarching goals are to enhance coastal resilience, support sustainable livelihoods, and strengthen policy frameworks for long-term reef conservation. We present two critical components of CoralCarib: (1) ecosystem service valuation of coral reefs and (2) coral larval connectivity modeling to inform restoration strategies. Our ecosystem service valuation estimates that CoralCarib reefs provide approximately $500 million annually in benefits, distributed across coastal protection (53%), tourism (41%), and fisheries (6%). Coastal protection services alone prevent an estimated $264 million in flood damage each year, and when combined with mangroves and seagrass, this figure rises to $346 million during a major hurricane event. Tourism benefits include both on-reef activities (e.g., snorkeling and diving) and reef-adjacent recreation (e.g., beach visits, boating), reflecting the reefs’ role in sustaining local economies. Fisheries contribute roughly $29 million per year, calculated using site-level data such as average daily catch, number of active boats, fishing effort, and market prices for key species. To guide restoration, we developed a coral larval dispersal and connectivity model using the Delft3D Flexible Mesh (Delft3D FM) hydrodynamic framework at 250 m and 1 km resolution. This model simulates density-driven currents, salinity and temperature gradients, tidal forces, and wave propagation, enabling high-resolution tracking of larval dispersal from current and potential outplant sites. Connectivity outputs identify reefs most likely to benefit from restoration, providing actionable insights for optimizing coral outplanting strategies and enhancing recruitment across the region. Together, these analyses demonstrate the substantial economic and ecological value of CoralCarib reefs and provide science-based tools for maximizing restoration impact and resilience under climate change.
01cf25c6: Climate change has driven widespread coral bleaching and mortality, yet some corals persist through repeated marine heatwaves. In the Galápagos, extreme conditions provide a natural laboratory for studying resilience, and some corals have persisted even after declines during the 1982–83 El Niño event. This study investigates coral resilience in a coral restoration site during a natural warming event associated with the 2023-2024 El Niño. We monitored coral survival, bleaching and growth using structure-from-motion photogrammetry, and evaluated how symbiont composition and genotype affected thermal tolerance throughout El Niño. Clonal fragments of Pocillopora spp. were monitored within the Reef Revival nursery on Isabela Island, Galápagos and at two nearby depth-matched outplanting sites with differing environmental characteristics. Coral growth varied within and between outplanting sites, with 96% of colonies growing faster at the more protected site (Bahía), where corals showed significantly higher growth rates (2.96 cm/year) compared to the more exposed site (Calera, 2.1 cm/year). Differing from the rest of the Eastern Tropical Pacific, no widespread bleaching was observed despite elevated sea surface temperatures in the region (April-May 2024 Degree Heating Weeks = 9). Genotype and symbiont composition contributed to interindividual differences in growth rates. Coral haplotypes thought to be less heat tolerant (haplotype 3) grew faster than those thought to be more heat tolerant (haplotype 1). Interestingly, haplotype 3 hosted a higher ratio of the heat-tolerant symbiont Durusdinium even though this haplotype is thought to associate less with Durusdinium than haplotype 1. This pattern suggests active ecological and evolutionary shifts within local coral populations that were once considered entirely clonal, with novel host–symbiont combinations emerging in regions where they were historically absent. Identifying host–symbiont pairings associated with higher growth during warming conditions will strengthen the development of climate-resilient coral nurseries in the Galapagos and guide similar restoration strategies worldwide.
aea98f49:
From the warm subtropical Kermadec Islands to the cold, deep and dark subantarctic plateaus and all habitats in between, Aotearoa New Zealand’s marine realm supports a bountiful array of coral life. The Earth Sciences New Zealand Invertebrate Collection (formerly NIWA Invertebrate Collection) holds the most representative collection of corals from Aotearoa’s waters. We are custodians of 15,058 samples of anemones (actiniarians, cerianthids, corallimorpharians, zoantharians), black corals, stony corals, soft corals, sea pens, and hydrocorals. Of these samples, 580 are types, including 143 holotypes. Specimen collections began from 1953 with recent 2025 voyages continuing to return these taxa from the entire ocean depth - from rockpools to trenches at a maximum depth of 9,980 metres! Collection records informed an update to the inventory of these taxa in 2023, showing that there are 526 described extant species in the region, and a further 346 known undescribed extant species. 

In Aotearoa, corals of orders Scleralcyonacea and Malacalcyonacea (previously Alcyonacea and Gorgonacea), Antipatharia, Scleractinia, and family Stylasteridae, are protected whether they are dead or alive under the New Zealand Wildlife Act (1953). Additionally, many of these species alongside anemones and sea pens are listed as Vulnerable Marine Ecosystem indicator taxa in the High Seas and Antarctic fisheries regions. Sea pen fields are recognised as sensitive environments under the New Zealand Exclusive Economic Zone and Continental Shelf (Environmental Effects) Act 2012.

More information about Aotearoa’s abundant and diverse coral fauna will be presented, highlighting where further research is needed. This will include insights about how the Collection supports protected coral research, management and conservation.
8aaf4a57: In early 2025, the RV Sonne voyage SO309 ‘CoralNewZ’ explored Aotearoa New Zealand cold-water corals using the ROV Squid 2000, a towed-camera system, and a variety of direct sampling equipment. Here we provide a summary of scientific objectives and findings of the research voyage, including preliminary results of in situ observations on transects over disturbed and undisturbed seamount habitats. Among the highlights were, for the first time in Aotearoa NZ, the discovery of ancient coral communities (coral mounds) indicating the long-term persistence of hard corals on deep seamounts, surveys of deep-water coral communities of the deep fjords of the Fiordland Marine Region and the location of remnant areas of corals on a seamount known to have been heavily damaged by commercial trawling. We also continued a nearly 30-year time series to examine recruitment dynamics and recovery potential of deep-water corals on a Chatham Rise seamount complex to the east of New Zealand, with a particular focus on coral recovery following the cessation of fishing on closed seamounts. Live specimens of several deepwater scleractinians were also collected for ongoing research to elucidate their reproductive biology, data that is crucial to infer coral resilience and longevity in light of ongoing disturbance.
75ec2858:
The Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) of Aotearoa New Zealand (NZ) encompasses over 3.5 million square kilometres of ocean that exceeds 200m in depth. This vast deep-sea environment harbours diverse cold-water coral assemblages, including some of the highest levels of recorded diversity for three octocoral families: bamboo corals (Keratoisididae), bubblegum and precious corals (Coralliidae), and primnoid corals (Primnoidae). However, for these and other octocoral families, the scale of the EEZ-impedes an understanding of the full extent of species diversity and distribution.

The areas of the EEZ targeted by commercial fisheries overlap with deep-sea coral habitat. As such, bottom trawling currently represents the largest direct anthropogenic impact on deep-sea corals in NZ. For more than 25 years, government fisheries observers aboard trawlers have been sampling coral bycatch and submitting specimens to the collections of Earth Sciences NZ, for taxonomic identification and to document the extent of interactions between commercial fishing and protected corals. These samples represent an important source of biodiscovery since bycatch is often recovered from areas not otherwise  accessed by research voyages.

We present data on cryptic, undocumented octocoral diversity obtained from genetic and genomic analyses of observer samples. Initial DNA barcoding examined the breadth of octocoral genera recovered as fisheries bycatch across the EEZ, with subsequent genomic examinations of species-level diversity within several regularly encountered families. We discovered cryptic, undocumented diversity at every taxonomic level, uncovering new and undescribed families, genera and species. These findings demonstrate the utility of fisheries bycatch samples for documenting both octocoral diversity and the impacts that commercial bottom-trawling has on native biodiversity.
c2a56da4:
During the latest scientific collaboration between New Zealand and Germany, marine researchers joined the CoralNewZ expedition aboard the German research vessel SONNE between January and February 2025. A central aim of this joint mission was the detailed documentation of cold-water corals within the Fiordland region. Parasound mapping and ROV surveys were carried out across the Thompson, Milford, and Dusky Sound systems, as well as along the Acheron Passage. The resulting high-resolution bathymetry and video imagery provide a new perspective on the distribution, structure, and ecological significance of Fiordland’s deep-water coral communities, revealing several previously unrecorded occurrences and associations.
Across four ROV dives, coral habitats and associated fauna were systematically documented and sampled whenever feasible. Specimens were photodocumented on board, with tissue material preserved for subsequent DNA barcoding. Among the most notable observations were extensive frameworks of the zig-zag coral (Madrepora oculata), alongside diverse assemblages of stylasterids, octocorals, and solitary scleractinians. Newly recorded coral–fish associations were observed between M. oculata and multiple perch species, including the wavyline perch (Lepidoperca tasmanica) and the orange perch (L. aurantia), underscoring the role of corals as key habitat formers in Fiordland’s steep-walled fjords.
A further remarkable discovery was the presence of the deep-sea oyster Pulvinites exempla between 315 and 226 m depth, an extant representative of a lineage otherwise known almost exclusively from fossil deposits, indicating overlooked hard-substrate refugia within the region.
The collected material highlights the scientific importance of these underexplored fjord systems and advances our understanding of the structure, biodiversity, and ecological complexity of Fiordland’s deep-water coral habitats.
3e59464b: Aotearoa (New Zealand) hosts some of the world’s most extensive and diverse cold-water coral (CWC) habitats, yet the biodiversity of their associated fauna remains critically understudied. During the RV SONNE expedition SO309 CoralNewZ (Jan-Feb 2025), deep-water coral assemblages were surveyed across multiple locations, including Steward Island, Fiordland, Chatham Rise, and the Graveyard Seamounts. Using the MARUM ROV Squid, CWCs were collected together with their associated invertebrate communities, providing dedicated material for investigating peracarid crustaceans in these habitats. This study focuses on isopods (Valvifera), small-bodied, filter-feeding peracarids known for limited dispersal, potential host specificity towards CWCs, and regionally restricted distributions. Preliminary analyses reveal an exceptionally rich and previously undocumented peracarid fauna associated with New Zealand’s deep-sea CWCs. DNA barcoding and proteomics, combined with detailed morphological examination, indicate at least four Valvifera species new to science, enabling the first comprehensive biogeographic comparison across the sampled CWC habitats. Beyond species discoveries, the dataset also offers a rare opportunity to assess host associations between peracarids and New Zealand coral taxa. These findings help to fill major knowledge gaps in New Zealand’s deep-sea biodiversity and underscore the importance of integrating taxonomy, genetics, and deep-sea exploration to advance the understanding of Aotearoa’s threatened CWC ecosystems.
b39812cf: Determining levels of genetic variation within and among populations is fundamental for effective genetic management for conservation. In temperate mesophotic ecosystems (TMEs), sessile and long-lived taxa, such as black corals, play key ecological roles. By forming complex three-dimensional structures, black coral populations support diverse associated fauna and contribute to nutrient cycling and habitat complexity. Despite their ecological significance, levels of genetic variation and population structure of temperate black corals remain largely unexplored. In Aotearoa New Zealand’s Fiordland system, the endemic black coral Antipathella fiordensis dominates shallow mesophotic zones (< 20 m), where low-light conditions are more typical of deeper habitats. Fiordland’s environmental conditions provide an opportunity to address key questions about black coral population connectivity, adaptive capacity, and resilience to environmental change. Through whole-genome sequencing, here we investigate the population structure and adaptive variation in A. fiordensis across multiple Fiordland populations. By generating a high-resolution SNP dataset, we aim to assess patterns of genetic differentiation. We focus on identifying both neutral SNPs, reflecting demographic history and connectivity, and putatively adaptive loci that may underpin resilience to environmental gradients. Our findings will provide a genomic baseline for black corals and support management of key TME species. Ultimately, our work aims to highlight the potential of genomic tools to address conservation issues in non-model taxa.
7d880cd8: Marine heatwaves (MHWs) are intensifying across Aotearoa New Zealand, with Fiordland experiencing two of its most severe events on record within the past decade. The 2022 Fiordland MHW triggered severe bleaching and mass mortality in the sponge Cymbastella lamellata and may have had other ecological impacts. Fiordland supports one of the world’s largest black coral populations, with an estimated seven million Antipathella fiordensis colonies forming complex biogenic habitat for diverse fish, sponges, and invertebrate communities. Although A. fiordensis showed no obvious decline during the 2022 event, these corals were not closely monitored, and their thermal tolerance and susceptibility to future warming remain unknown. Here, we present results from a controlled laboratory experiment that simulated a MHW mimicking conditions from the 2022 event. Juvenile A. fiordensis were exposed to MHW and control treatments to assess visual stress indicators, survival, respiration, and associated microbial community dynamics. No differences in coral physiology were detected between treatments, indicating short-term resilience to this level of warming. However, 16S rRNA sequencing revealed significant shifts in microbial community composition under MHW conditions, indicating sub-lethal disturbance to coral holobiont at proximity to the upper thermal limits. Together, these findings suggest that A. fiordensis is resilient to the magnitude of recent warming events, aligning with field observations. Yet, microbial shifts suggest possible vulnerability or early adaptive response under future warming, with potential consequences for Fiordland’s coral-dominated ecosystems. This study provides the first experimental evidence of thermal sensitivity in A. fiordensis and offers important insight for the conservation of Aotearoa’s unique cold-water coral ecosystems.
fb164c81: Coral reef ecosystems depend on exchanges of energy and material with surrounding oceanic waters, especially around islands and atolls in the tropical oligotrophic ocean. However, satellites cannot resolve shallow nearshore waters, and ship-based surveys are costly, sparse, and typically avoid coastal waters and near-island slopes. As a result, the magnitude, variability, and pathways of pelagic subsidies to reefs remain poorly quantified. To address this knowledge gap, we deployed wave-propelled autonomous surface vehicles (Wave Gliders) to conduct repeated, island-scale surveys of ocean conditions across the Hawaiian Islands. Over three missions from late 2023 to mid-2025, three vehicles operated in tandem at fixed distances from shore (1.5, 3, and 6 km), completing a full circumnavigation of all eight Main Hawaiian Islands. The surveys covered 31,083 km of trackline (equivalent to four-fifths of the Earth’s circumference) and collected over 3,000 vertical profiles to 150 m depth, spanning contrasting seasonal cycles and ENSO phases. Our surveys provide the first high-resolution, three-dimensional observations of near-island ocean conditions across an entire archipelago, closing a critical data gap in the very areas that link pelagic processes to nearshore ecology. In particular, they quantified the depth, strength, and vertical structure of chlorophyll (a proxy for phytoplankton biomass), revealing both surface and subsurface productivity hotspots and the oceanographic processes that create them. This presentation will highlight emergent patterns in ocean-reef connections from these surveys and demonstrate how these data can improve our predictions of reef ecosystem responses to changing ocean conditions. We will also discuss their integration into ecosystem and fisheries models to support ecosystem-based management across tropical archipelagos.
c1a2b0cc: Internal waves (IWs) are a major pathway through which oceanic water masses influence coral reef environments, yet the cross-scale mechanisms governing their generation, arrival, and ecological consequences remain unclear. Dongsha Atoll, in the northern South China Sea, sits ~400 km downstream of the Luzon Strait (LS), where some of the world’s most energetic IWs are generated, offering a rare opportunity to resolve how mesoscale oceanographic forcing structures reef-level conditions.
