Here, we integrate long-term temperature records, high-frequency biogeochemical measurements, and basin-scale oceanographic datasets to reveal how mesoscale circulation, ocean weather, and tidal-band IWs interact to shape the physical and biogeochemical environment across Dongsha’s IW-exposed reefs. Variability in intrusions of the Kuroshio Current into the LS, and associated mesoscale eddies and sea-level gradients, jointly modulate IW generation in the LS and their subsequent arrival at Dongsha. Anticyclonic eddies suppress IWs, leading to sustained periods of reduced reef-slope upwelling. Cyclonic eddies and elevated thermoclines enhance the reef’s exposure to IWs, leading to an energetic diurnal and semidiurnal thermal regime, with cooling events often exceeding 10 °C at mid-slope depths – substantially offsetting surface heat accumulation during peak summer heating. High-resolution forereef observations further reveal that typhoon–IW interactions create short-lived hotspots of dissolved oxygen and chlorophyll-a variability around 20 m depth, but can also suppress the nitrate-rich upwelling normally driven by IWs. These processes also influence lagoonal communities that experience hypoxia driven by the respiration of upwelled organic matter transported across the reef flat. These results show how mesoscale currents and eddy dynamics remotely influence local reef-scale IW arrivals, which govern upwelling, pelagic water-mass delivery, thermal exposure, and biogeochemical structure across Dongsha’s reef system. Observations at Dongsha highlight the importance of integrating oceanographic processes into predictions of reef function and climate-refuge potential in internal-wave-influenced coral ecosystems.
e1ea67e0: Heterotrophy plays a critical role in corals’ resilience and recovery following thermal stress. Although coral trophic strategies can vary by species, location, and season, we lack a comprehensive understanding of how these strategies differ across spatial scales. As a result, we do not fully understand the extent to which scale-dependent feeding strategies contribute to the patchiness of coral survival and mortality observed across reefs after bleaching events. In this study, we investigated the trophic strategy and physiology of the reef-building coral Pocillopora verrucosa at 10 m depth, comparing variability among colonies (<3 m), within reefs (<1.5 km), and between reefs (patch versus outer rim reefs, >20 km) in North Male’ Atoll, Maldives, Indian Ocean. Coral nutritional modes were quantified using stable isotope analyses (δ¹³C, δ¹⁵N, and δ³⁴S) and paired with tissue biomass measurements to assess how trophic differences affect coral fitness across scales. We quantified benthic community composition at each site to provide context on environmental conditions that may influence resource availability. Finally, we traced energetic nitrogen resources and circulation in the atoll using isotope analyses of the common reef algae Tydemania expeditionis. Isotope and physiological analyses substantiating multi-scale variation of energetic sources, trophic strategy, and fitness will be discussed. Preliminary observations appear to corroborate initial assumptions of cross-scale variability. Outer rim reefs were characterised by massive Porites spp. encrusting corals, and tunicates, whereas inner atoll reefs were characterised by Pocillopora spp., Acropora spp., algal cover and greater overall variability in benthic communities. These distinct benthic assemblages reflect cross-scale differences in environmental conditions and suggest that resource availability for corals may also vary across the atolls.  By elucidating the patterns and magnitude of variability in the trophic strategies of P. veruccosa, this study contributes to a deeper understanding of coral resilience under escalating climate pressures.
c8078de7: Dongsha Atoll is one of the largest atolls in the northern South China Sea, featuring high coral cover and diversity, especially on its outer reef slopes. It lies in the path of some of the world’s largest internal solitary waves that propagate westward from the Luzon Strait. As these waves interact with underlying topography and break, they drive deeper, cooler, nutrient-rich waters onto the shallow parts of the reef. Such upwelling has been linked to reduced coral bleaching during thermal anomalies. However, the extent to which these oceanic inputs may nutritionally support Dongsha’s outer reef communities, and how spatial variability in internal-wave forcing shapes community structure, remain open questions. To investigate, we combined high-resolution time-series of temperature and chlorophyll records across depths (between 10–42 m) at three outer-reef sites (north, east, south), together with tissue stable carbon (δ13C) and nitrogen (δ15N) isotopes measured in a range of reef organisms (fish, seagrass, crinoids, corals) from the same sites. Our results demonstrate strong temperature variability that increases with increasing depth at all outer sites, consistent with internal-wave forcing. Time-series of chlorophyll and temperature-derived nitrate estimates indicate episodic delivery of oceanic nutrients onto the reef, corroborating the observed patterns of cooling of shallow reef slopes. Preliminary tissue isotope results suggest two potential nutrient pathways: (1) an oceanic pathway, with δ13C of small planktivorous damselfish, crinoids and some corals aligning with pelagic particulate organic matter (~ −20 to −18 ‰); and (2) a benthic or seagrass-influenced pathway, where some larger predators and corals presented more enriched δ13C (~−16 to −11 ‰) suggesting utilisation of seagrass or benthic sources. Further source elucidation using more source-specific carbon and nitrogen compound-specific isotope analysis of amino acid (CSIA-AA) is warranted to shed light on internal waves’ influences on the nutrient dynamics of communities around Dongsha’s outer reef.
bf68c129: Pelagic subsidies are increasingly recognized as a vital component of coral reef ecosystem functioning. Yet empirical observations of ocean-reef connections are scarce, and much remains unknown about the physical processes that drive subsurface resource supply and the timescales over which reef organisms respond. Here, we present one of the first continuous, year-round records of plankton, nutrients, and oceanographic conditions linking offshore processes to a coral reef ecosystem. For 15 months (2024–2025), we conducted bi-weekly monitoring of physical and biogeochemical dynamics, nutrient uptake, phytoplankton production, and planktonic community structure on an offshore reef in the central Red Sea. During winter, the water column is fully mixed to >100 m homogenizing resources across the reef slope. In summer, stratification and wind-driven thermocline dynamics regulate upwelling, which pushes the subsurface chlorophyll maximum onto the reef slope, delivering cool, nitrate- and plankton-rich waters that enhance food availability for zooplankton and benthic suspension feeders. Stable isotope analysis (δ¹³C, δ¹⁵N) of phytoplankton, zooplankton, and corals revealed how pelagic resource pools vary across seasons and how they are assimilated by reef-building corals along a 5–30 m depth gradient. Our study highlights that pelagic subsidies are not ephemeral inputs to coral reefs but originate from persistent subsurface resource pools. Delivery of these subsidies to shallow reef communities is structured by physical forcing and governs reef functioning across depths and through time. Notably, an intense heatwave in the summer of 2024 shut down upwelling, restricting the delivery of cool, plankton-rich waters to shallow depths and resulting in widespread coral mortality. The abrupt halt of upwelling caused by surface warming and increased stratification reveals an emerging threat to ocean-reef connections under climate change. Our multidisciplinary approach to ocean observation provides a valuable framework for quantifying the biophysical processes that unite coral reefs with their surrounding seascape.
9676af77: Satellite derived chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) measurements are widely used to estimate pelagic subsidies and ocean-reef connections. However, satellite observations are restricted to the upper water column (i.e., first optical depth, ~0–20 m) and cannot measure Chl-a in shallow, reef-adjacent waters. These limitations, along with the paucity of empirical in-situ Chl-a measurements on reefs, have hindered our ability to accurately quantify the magnitude and variability of pelagic subsidies  that reach and sustain reef ecosystems. To evaluate the accuracy of offshore satellite Chl-a estimates as indicators of on-reef conditions, we conducted a 15-month bi-weekly sampling campaign in the central Red Sea, measuring vertical Chl-a profiles at the reef slope and 2 km offshore. We compared in-situ Chl-a concentrations within the first optical depth to the nearest satellite-derived pixel from ocean-color products at 4 km², 1 km², and 300 m² spatial resolutions, quantifying differences between empirical concentrations and satellite measurements.  Satellite measurements underestimated on-reef Chl-a concentrations by 28-81% and failed to capture key temporal dynamics at the surface and at depth. A persistent deep chlorophyll maximum  (DCM) at 53 ± 18 m revealed a resource pool invisible to satellites that is occasionally upwelled onto the reef . To demonstrate the relevance of DCMs as a resource pool to reefs worldwide, we evaluated fluorescence profiles spanning the length of the Red Sea and six tropical Pacific archipelagos. We show that DCMs are a ubiquitous feature in the upper 88 ± 36 m  of the water column near reefs. Notably, the depth and intensity of DMCs vary across space and time, revealing how this overlooked subsurface resource can fuel shallow and mesophotic reef productivity from below.  Reliance on satellite estimates alone will obscure vital ocean-reef connections and limit our ability to accurately quantify pelagic subsidies that may be critical for reef persistence under future ocean conditions.
ffb29f2c: Pelagic resources fuel coral reef food webs and can shape reef trajectories under climate change. Upwelling regularly supplies cool, plankton-rich water to coral communities, yet how marine heatwaves impact upwelling and resource supply is poorly understood. To quantify how a common coral modulates its trophic strategy in response to upwelling before, during, and after a heatwave, we conducted paired coral and oceanographic sampling on an outer reef slope in the central Red Sea. Fifteen tagged colonies of Pocillopora favosa at 5, 15, and 25 m depth were sampled bi-monthly from February 2024 to February 2025. Upwelling was continuously monitored with an in-situ temperature sensor array, and we conducted bi-weekly sampling of particulate organic matter (POM), zooplankton, and inorganic nutrients on the reef and 2 km offshore to quantify biogeochemical variability in the pelagic resource pool. Bulk stable isotopes (δ¹³C, δ¹⁵N) and fatty-acid profiles of coral hosts, endosymbionts, POM, and two zooplankton size classes were used to quantify autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition in P. favosa. Seasonal patterns in coral trophic strategies across depth were driven by water column structure. Winter mixing homogenized heterotrophic resources across depths, leading to similar trophic strategies among colonies. As temperatures increased during summer, stratification reduced upwelling to shallower corals, leading to greater coral heterotrophy with depth.  By August, upwelling to 5 and 15 m ceased entirely, coinciding with bleaching and mortality of all colonies. In contrast, upwelling persisted below 20 m, and all deeper colonies remained unbleached. By integrating colony-level trophic strategies with oceanographic sampling, this study provides new insights into how corals shift their trophic strategy in response to resource availability and thermal stress. Our results demonstrate that strong stratification can restrict upwelling and exacerbate the impacts of heatwaves on shallow coral communities by concurrently reducing food supply and the opportunity for temperature relief.
31fdeab5:
Symbiosis is no longer considered an exception - but rather a rule to the success of life on Earth. Multi-Kingdom symbioses are ubiquitous and corals are excellent model organisms for understanding how symbiosis has driven their success in deep time, counter balanced by their precipitous decline to dysbiosis in the Anthropocene. Here, I will argue that corals reefs and their symbiotic microbiota are analogous to other foundational habitats including forests, and even human beings - with convergent metabolic characteristics that have evolved to overcome severe nutritional deficits. With the great homogenization of Earth's biogeochemical cycles, symbiosis is no longer advantageous as evidenced by the collapse of coral reefs, forests, and human health alike. 

Drawing upon examples of research conducted over the past decade, I will present evidence that 1) growth limiting nitrogen is the primary driver of the coral-Symbiodiniaceae mutualism, 2) that excess nitrogen at the holobiont level induces dysbiosis, and 3) that these perturbations further reduce ecosystem biodiversity and loss of ecosystem functioning. Reversing these trends means not only curbing ocean warming through decarbonization, but also curtailing the amount of bioavailable nutrients including nitrogen.
733de3d9:
Coral reef health depends on microbial partnerships, yet the interactions that sustain or destabilize these symbioses occur in a hidden world we have rarely observed directly. Although sequencing has illuminated the taxonomic composition of coral microbiomes, it cannot capture the fleeting, intercellular behaviors that determine whether microbial partners coexist, aggregate, or enter competitive conflict. These microscale processes ultimately influence microbiome stability on the reef, but they remain poorly understood. Here we establish a new experimental framework to coral microbiome ecology, allowing real-time visualization of community dynamics that were previously inaccessible in coral systems. We study microbial communities sampled from Diploria labyrinthiformis, green and pink phenotypes dervied from reefs in Bermuda. To do so, we use microfluidics coupled to automated timelapse microscopy, enabling visualization and pertubation of cell-to-cell interactions between microbial types within controlled microenvironments. This approach allows us to observe emerging behaviours such as mixed-species aggregation, directional movement toward neighbouring groups, and even antagonistic encounters in which one phenotype disrupts established microcolonies of microbial communtieis. By quantifying these
interaction patterns, we can begin to determine the behavioral mechanisms like cooperation, competition, aggregation, and interference that regulate how coral-associated microbial communities form and persist within mucus on the reef. These mechanistic measurements, in turn, reveal how microbiomes from different coral phenotypes assemble and maintain distinct community structures on the reef. Linking microscale behaviors to patterns observed in natural mucus samples can help bridge sequencing datasets and allows us to identify which interaction strategies promote stability, exclude opportunistic taxa, or predispose communities to dysbiosis under stress. By revealing the hidden dynamics of mucus-associated bacteria, this work opens new avenues for understanding themolecular mechanisms underlying microbial symbioses and help identify microbial traits that could be leveraged in future to support coral survival under environmental stress.
Keywords: Coral microbiome, Microbial interactions, Microfluidics, Symbiosis.
593a4bd2: Coral reef health is increasingly understood to transition from viralized (i.e., pristine, coral-dominated) to microbialized (i.e., degraded, algal-dominated) states. The viralized state is characterized by high viral predation that constrains microbial abundances, whereas the microbialized state has elevated pathogenic microbial abundances and lower oxygen concentrations. The Virus-to-Microbe Ratio (VMR), defined as the virus-like particles abundance (VLPs mL⁻¹) divided by the microbial cell abundance (cells mL⁻¹), tracks the viralized-to-microbial transition, and we hypothesize that it is the best indicator of relative reef health identified so far. Here we use VMR to access the potential for guiding coral reef restoration efforts. Our study sites include an established microbialized benthic reef system serving as a control, three deployed midwater reef mesocosms (‘Coral Reef Arks’) spanning a gradient of reef health, and 15 deployed Autonomous Reef Monitoring Systems (ARMS) distributed across the Arks and control site. From each site we sampled VMR, microbial biomass (independent of cell counts), dissolved oxygen, total nitrogen, and dissolved organic carbon (DOC). By randomly subsampling the benthic control site, we will estimate the mean VMR and its natural range of variation to create a site-specific null distribution. We will then calculate the Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence to quantify how strongly each experimental Ark departs from this baseline. Values outside of the null distribution range indicate significant divergence in reef state, supporting VMR as an indicator of reef health. To interpret the direction of change, we will examine the components of VMR by plotting cells mL⁻¹ versus VLPs mL⁻¹ to determine whether systems are shifting toward viralized or microbialized conditions. These results, paired with principal component analysis (PCA) and random forest models across the whole dataset will identify the strongest predictors of reef health and evaluate the utility of VMR for guiding reef restoration.
0d1eb65c: Symbiodiniaceae are a group of dinoflagellates linked to the emergence and the evolutionary diversification of reef-building corals. Their high genetic diversity underpins great ecological and functional differences that shape thermal tolerance and bleaching  susceptibility in coral hosts. Given the critical role that coral-Symbiodiniaceae association plays in holobiont adaptation and acclimatization, several studies have focused on describing the diversity and distribution of symbiodiniaceans worldwide. However, only few studies have investigated Symbiodiniaceae diversity in the Southwestern Atlantic Ocean (SAO). Here we carried out a review to compile the full extent of Symbiodiniaceae diversity and distribution in the SAO to address knowledge gaps and guide future research directions. Peer-reviewed literature was surveyed via ISI Web of Science and Google Scholar using the search terms (Symbiodiniaceae OR Symbiodinium) AND (South Atlantic Ocean OR Brazil). To date, Brazilian corals have been reported in association with a total of 82 ITS2 phylotypes belonging to Breviolum (36), Cladocopium (34), Fugacium (1), Gerakladium (2), and Symbiodinium (9) genera. Breviolum exhibited the highest ITS2 phylotype diversity, while Cladocopium showed the widest geographic distribution, spreading across all Brazilian coastline sectors, including oceanic islands. Although Brazilian corals are capable of associating with multiple ITS2 phylotypes, Symbiodiniaceae symbionts exhibited high host specificity. A total of 54 putative host-specific symbionts were identified in Millepora alcicornis, Madracis decactis, Favia gravida, and Mussismilia harttii. Notably, Symbiodiniaceae communities have been characterized for only 8 of the 21 photosymbiotic scleractinian and milleporid species in Brazil. Moreover, clustering analyses indicated that Symbiodiniaceae compositions of examined coral species were influenced by similarities among databases and analytical methods. This review highlights the urgent need to expand Symbiodiniaceae diversity studies in the SAO, particularly for endemic coral species whose Symbiodiniaceae assemblages remain uncharacterized, as well as for coral reef areas with limited research coverage, such as the Northern sector.
88745a93:
Coral reefs are rapidly declining under accelerating climate change, highlighting the urgent need for rehabilitation strategies that enhance long-term resilience rather than short-term survival. To address this challenge, we developed a novel experimental framework that integrates coral microbiology and microfusion to generate coral microbiome chimeras, an emerging ecological engineering approach that expands phenotypic and microbial diversity within coral reef rehabilitation strategies.
Focusing on the staghorn coral Acropora aspera in Guam, we combined long-term nursery culture, in situ assessments, and controlled heat-stress experiments to evaluate microbial plasticity, physiological responses, and resilience potential. Source populations exhibited distinct phenotypic traits, environmental memories, and microbial assemblages, emphasizing the importance of local acclimatization in shaping coral performance. Corals maintained for over two years in mid-water nurseries displayed modified microbiomes and physiological states, highlighting the need for transitional acclimatization phases prior to outplanting.
Using microfusion, we produced chimeric colonies composed of clonal host fragments acclimatized with functionally different microbiomes or phenotypic traits. Microbiome analyses revealed that fused zones hosted more diverse communities than fragments mainly dominated with Endozoicomonaceae, and chimera types retained distinct microbial signatures linked to site of origin. Gene expression analyses identified differential heat-stress responses in coral hosts and Symbiodiniaceae, but not in the endolithic alga Ostreobium, offering new insight into multi-partner stress dynamics within the coral holobiont.
Overall, this work demonstrates that integrating phenotypic traits, environmental memories, and microbiome manipulation within single colonies can enhance potential for adaptive flexibility. Chimera-based coral engineering may provide a scalable, sustainable, and nature-based solution for reef rehabilitation, offering a foundation for selecting locally adapted, functionally compatible candidates to strengthen coral resilience in a rapidly changing ocean.
d8fb84a6: Shallow hydrothermal vents at Kueishan Island present one of the most chemically extreme coastal environments, characterized by persistently low pH, high sulfide concentrations, dynamic changes in water temperatures, and reduced dissolved oxygen. Yet, the azooxanthellate coral T. aurea persists and even thrives in this habitat. Comparative analyses between vent and non-vent corals reveal coordinated morphological, physiological, and reproductive shifts, highlighting a distinctive form of niche-specific adaptation. Vent corals exhibit significantly higher skeletal porosity and reduced skeletal density relative to non-vent conspecifics, demonstrating weakened calcification under chronic acidification. This structural cost is actively counterbalanced by the upregulation of calcification-related genes (ca2/16 and ca3), suggesting robust molecular compensation to sustain skeletal deposition in low-pH, high-sulfide waters. Reproduction emerges as a key axis of resilience. Sexual reproduction is maintained or enhanced, with vent corals producing larger, fully grown oocytes and exhibiting elevated vitellogenin expression during gametogenesis. Under controlled laboratory conditions, vent corals also display greater asexual reproductive capacity, reflected by higher budding frequency and regeneration. These findings indicate a strategic reallocation of energy toward rapid population renewal, a beneficial strategy for vent coral to adapt under extreme environments. However, thermal-stress assays reveal an important trade-off: vent corals are more sensitive to elevated temperatures than non-vent conspecifics. Long-term specialization for acidified, sulfide-rich environments appears to enhance chemical resilience while diminishing investment in broad-spectrum heat-stress protection pathways. To uncover the regulatory foundation of these coordinated shifts, we employ a multi-omics framework that integrates whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS), ATAC-seq, m⁶A-seq, and transcriptomic analyses. These approaches will allow us to precisely identify epigenetic signatures and regulatory pathways underlying acidification tolerance, reproductive enhancement, and stress-response trade-offs. As shallow vents mirror multiple components of future ocean conditions, T. aurea in hydrothermal vent serves as a powerful natural model for predicting coral resilience and vulnerability in rapidly changing ocean environments.
608ff75d: Biodiversity conservation increasingly calls for the integration of multiple dimensions of biological variation to predict and assist the evolutionary potential and resilience of natural populations. Genetic approaches have driven major advances in characterizing population structure and demography, and underpinning management strategies. Yet, genetic studies mostly rely on neutral genetic variation that reflect long-term evolutionary history, offering a partial view of the current ecological status and short-term adaptive capacity of populations. This gap underscores the need to complement genetic assessments with non-genetic components. In this context, epigenetics, particularly DNA methylation, have recently emerged as promising yet unexplored candidates for capturing a supplementary layer of biodiversity. DNA methylation is sensitive to environmental changes and influences both plastic and adaptive phenotypes. As such, it may affect ecological and evolutionary processes at all biological levels, from a biological function to the ecosystem, and are more likely to reflect the short-term ecological background of individuals. To address this question, we sampled three coral species with contrasted life history traits (Acropora hyacinthus, Pocillopora acuta, Porites lobata) across the South Pacific (New Caledonia, Fiji, French Polynesia) and investigate their genetic (SNPs) and epigenetic (CpGs) structure at the genome wide level (EM-seq). We first distinguished CpG methylation patterns that are genetically associated (methQTL) from those that arose independently of genetic background. Using comparative analytical frameworks including Discriminant Analysis of Principal Component (DAPC) and Mantel tests, we showed that epigenetic structuring, particularly for genetically independent CpG sites, is stronger and more spatially refined than genetic structure. These methylation patterns closely track local ecological variation, revealing population differences undetectable at the genetic level. These findings highlight the power of epigenetic information in conservation biology by its ability to capture short-term ecological signals, bridging the gap between macro (genetic) and micro (epigenetic) evolutionary patterns.
e8360207: Corals rely on complex molecular regulation to maintain physiological function under natural environmental variability and anthropogenic perturbations of increasing frequency and severity. Epigenetic mechanisms, which dynamically influence expression without directly altering the nucleotide sequence, are central to this regulation and may be key determinants of phenotypic plasticity and acclimatory potential. However, while individual epigenetic mechanisms (e.g., DNA methylation or miRNA activity) have been described in some coral species, their combined contributions to gene expression and phenotype remain largely unresolved in non-model systems. Here, we present a cross-species, multi-omic analysis integrating matched time-series datasets of gene expression, miRNA and lncRNA expression, and DNA methylation from three reef-building coral species (Acropora pulchra, Porites evermanni, and Pocillopora tuahiniensis). Using a suite of statistical and machine-learning frameworks, we identify gene networks associated with key physiological processes (e.g., biomineralization), and evaluate the degree to which combined epigenetic feature sets can jointly predict these genes’ expression. This approach allows us to explore putative interactions among regulatory layers, disentangle their relative contributions, and build preliminary quantitative models of epigenetic influence on coral expression and physiology. Our results reveal both shared and species-specific strategies for expression regulation, and provide insight into the interactions among layers of epigenetic control. More broadly, this integrative approach advances understanding of the epigenetic architecture shaping environmental response in corals, and offers a foundation for studying how epigenetic mechanisms may work together to facilitate acclimation or resilience in reef organisms facing rapid environmental change.
9a76156a:
Corals experience widely fluctuating environmental conditions, yet the molecular basis of their response to such fluctuations remains poorly understood. Epigenetic mechanisms provide gene regulatory layers beyond genetic sequence, for instance through chemical DNA modifications and post-translational chromatin marks. In corals, differential DNA methylation has been associated with acclimatization to ocean acidification, light exposure, and bleaching. Here we aim to link phenotypic diversity and environmental history by studying patterns of coral DNA methylation, including their persistence and divergence across clonal lineages.
 
We present a framework to resolve epitypes by classifying DNA methylation patterns into dynamic and stable regions. We generated single-molecule long-read sequencing (PacBio HiFi) from two Caribbean reef-building corals, Acropora palmata and Orbicella faveolata. This approach allows for haplotype phasing and methylation identification on the same DNA molecule. We sampled multiple genets and ramets from locations with different thermal histories, enabling construction of a haplotype-resolved methylation atlas from which epitype classes can be defined. Dynamic epitypes are expected to vary across environmental or physiological contexts and may modulate transcriptional responsiveness, alternative splicing, or pathway activation. Conversely, stable epitypes are predicted to show minimal responsiveness during short-term acclimatization, such as constitutive gene-body methylation of housekeeping genes, and reflect long-term maintenance of cellular state. Such regions are also candidates for exploring a coral epigenetic clock, where stochastic deviations from canonical methylation patterns accumulate along somatic lineages. Identifying loci that exhibit progressive accumulation of epimutations would offer new tools to reconstruct relative lineage history and evaluate epigenetic inheritance in modular organisms.
 
In sum, we quantify how epitypes interact with genetic variation to generate phenotypic diversity, and assess their contributions to persistent population differences. This framework establishes a foundation for incorporating epigenetic variation into potential for environmental adaptation and coral reef resilience.
78b43b83: As coral mortality reaches all-time highs on reefs around the world, identifying the mechanisms underpinning coral resilience is critical. While genetic adaptation may confer resistance in coral populations over time, phenotypic plasticity, mediated by epigenetic regulation of gene expression, offers a rapid mechanism for acclimatization. Recent transcriptomic studies reveal divergent profiles between heat tolerant and heat sensitive corals; however, the epigenetic drivers governing these expression patterns remain largely unexplored in reef-building corals. To bridge this gap, we adapted Chromatin Immunoprecipitation Sequencing (ChIP-seq) for the reef-building coral Pocillopora damicornis, leveraging protocols established in cnidarian model systems (Exaiptasia). We developed an optimized ChIP-seq workflow to isolate high-quality chromatin from hard corals and interrogate protein-DNA interactions under heat stress versus control conditions. Specifically, we targeted post-translational modifications (PTMs), which demonstrate conserved regulatory functions across eukaryotes, to map the distinct chromatin landscape of active transcription (H3K27Ac) and gene repression (H3K27Me3). We present a validated pipeline capable of measuring epigenetic remodeling in hard corals to distinguish between genes that are silenced during heat stress versus those that are “poised” for rapid activation, a distinction invisible to standard RNA-seq. We hypothesize that corals exhibiting higher thermal tolerance possess a more dynamic epigenetic remodeling capability, characterized by the rapid up/downregulation of stress-response genes resulting in higher physiological plasticity and improved thermal tolerance. Establishing ChIP-seq in reef-building corals paves the way for uncovering the regulatory logic of coral resilience to heat stress. By identifying the epigenetic mechanisms that precede transcriptional plasticity, we can better predict adaptive potential and refine restoration strategies to select individuals capable of withstanding future environmental change.
b9bc7553: Survival in the Anthropocene is dictated by the ability of organisms to respond to increasing environmental change. For thermally sensitive organisms, understanding the mechanisms that govern their response to environmental perturbation, and the plasticity therein, is key to predicting survival. Epigenetic mechanisms, such as DNA methylation, underlie phenotypic plasticity, regulating gene function in response to the environment. However, how the methylome responds to sublethal thermal exposure, the temporal dynamics of the response, and whether this is dose dependent are poorly understood. Corals are sessile and have a narrow thermal envelope, making them an ideal model to explore epigenetic mechanisms underlying rapid responses to thermal variation. We exposed fragments of the staghorn coral Acropora cervicornis to +3°C and +5°C of heating for 1 and 2 days and compared the DNA methylation profiles of each dose to the ambient treatment immediately following the exposure and one week later. We present the first study to demonstrate the alteration of the methylome after acute sublethal thermal exposure in A. cervicornis. We observed a rapid response to thermal exposure in as little as 1 day, and no differences among doses applied immediately following heating. After a week-long recovery period, the DNA methylation profiles of heated corals no longer differed from those of the ambient treatment, suggesting the wash out of DNA methylation marks. This demonstrates the dynamic nature of the methylome, capable of rapidly changing to meet environmental conditions, though marks from acute exposure may not be retained in the long term.
25100627: Assessing the vulnerability of corals is essential for informing conservation and management efforts for those taxa most at risk of extinction. The implementation of conservation measures relies upon an understanding of a species’ resilience to primary threats (e.g., ocean warming), the current population size, distribution, and essential biological processes like growth, mortality, and reproduction as well as environmental drivers that influence these factors. For fifteen Indo-Pacific corals listed as threatened under the U.S. Endangered Species Act, these life history data are largely lacking. Here we showcase our approach developed in collaboration with NOAA, the Cooperative Institute for Marine and Atmospheric Research, and the Pacific Islands Regional Office focused on addressing knowledge gaps in the fundamental biology of two of these listed coral species that occur in the U.S. Territory of American Samoa, Acropora globiceps and Isopora crateriformis. This includes modeling species’ distribution and preferred habitats, establishing fixed sites to monitor coral colony vital rates, and experimentally predicting heat stress tolerance using standardized Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System (CBASS) assays. We highlight how these approaches and tools can serve as a model for generating data that directly inform species management and capacity building across the wider South Pacific.
b28872ba:
Fiji’s coral reefs are among the most ecologically and culturally significant in the Pacific, yet research to date has been geographically and temporally fragmented, limiting the ability to understand national-scale patterns and inform cohesive marine management. To address this gap, the Government of Fiji, in partnership with the Waitt Institute through the Blue Prosperity Fiji Program and the Scripps Institution of Oceanography, undertook the nation's largest coral reef assessment.  Between May and August 2023, scientific teams surveyed 270 sites across 13 Provinces, and covered reefs in all regions of the country except for Rotuma and southern Kadavu.

At each site, a suite of surveys were conducted to assess fish community structure, benthic percent cover, juvenile coral density, reef rugosity, macroinvertebrate diversity, and water quality. Nationwide coral cover was moderate for the region, at 19.7%, and fish biomass was higher than nearby nations in the region. Notably, cyanobacteria was found to comprise on average 4.4% of benthic cover, and thick mats were observed in many locations. Juvenile coral densities were, on average, approximately 11 individuals/m². Commercially valuable macroinvertebrate species were encountered infrequently on surveys, suggesting that their densities are low overall. However, no evidence of sewage pollution was detected on 10m forereefs in any province. Importantly, variability between provinces was high, highlighting the need for detailed, science-backed marine spatial planning. 

These findings provide Fiji’s first nationwide ecological baseline and highlight both areas of resilience and regions that may require focused management or protection. Coupled with Fiji’s strong foundation of customary governance and ongoing commitment to marine spatial planning, this dataset offers a powerful basis for targeted coral reef management.
43833165: In the era of climate change coral reefs around the world are predicted to experience increased frequency of bleaching events. Given this it is key if we are to understand future coral reef ecosystem structure we must understand how corals recovery from bleaching events. As with other reefs around the world the sub-tropical reefs of Norfolk Island and Lord Howe have experience repeated coral bleaching events over the last 6 years. This study examines how the coral reef populations recovery from bleaching events. The results highlight that recovery time in sub-tropical reefs is significantly slower than that seen in topical reefs with substantially different recovery rates between different species. These slower recovery rates mean that sub-tropical reefs are uniquely vulnerable to repeated coral bleaching events as recovery may not have occurred before subsequent bleaching events,
4373db30: Coral reefs in the South Pacific are globally significant, supporting extraordinary biodiversity and sustaining coastal communities through fisheries, coastal protection, and cultural values. In Melanesia, climate change, population growth, and resource pressure are intensifying threats to reef ecosystems, highlighting the need for evidence-based conservation strategies. Locally Managed Marine Areas (LMMAs) have emerged as community-driven tools for stewardship, yet their design often emphasises social or logistical factors rather than key ecological processes such as larval dispersal, which drive population replenishment, resilience, and fisheries productivity. This project investigates ecological connectivity and source–sink dynamics of reef fish across Fiji’s LMMA network to inform management strategies that integrate ecological and socioecological objectives. We focus on two culturally and ecologically important species, Chlorurus microrhinos and Lutjanus gibbus, sampled across six sites. Population genomic analyses using DArTseq-derived SNP markers will quantify gene flow, genetic structure, and metapopulation connectivity. These empirical estimates are integrated with biophysical larval dispersal models to identify ecologically coherent reef networks and evaluate how connectivity influences LMMA performance. By combining genomics, biophysical modelling, and seascape-scale perspectives, this research clarifies how connectivity underpins reef resilience and fisheries sustainability. The results will guide LMMA design and governance to maximise ecological function and community benefits, demonstrating how foundational science, local stewardship, and applied management can converge. This approach contributes to sustaining biodiversity, enhancing ecosystem services, and strengthening the resilience of Fiji coral reef systems under increasing environmental pressures.
ccc9bf6c: In the Pacific region, community-managed areas are emerging as conservation strategies to reverse coral reef degradation and overfishing. However, as the population grows and climate change intensifies, significant challenges remain in balancing socioeconomic needs, sustainable fisheries, and biodiversity conservation.  The challenge is magnified as fishery resources are not static because of migration between fishing grounds and conservation areas, and therefore require coordinated management strategies that align with ecological processes to promote recovery and sustainable fisheries. In reef systems, fish larval dispersal across the seascape is critical to maintaining species and fishery stock persistence through gene flow and population replenishment. Therefore, incorporating connectivity into the design of community-managed area networks can support conservation and sustainability goals whilst revealing the degree of coordination required among communities connected by fish movement. This project combined genetics, biophysical models, and underwater surveys to map and understand the role of fish connectivity within Fiji's network of community-managed marine areas. First, we evaluated population structure and genetic diversity among reef fish populations across Fiji using genetic data from a keystone fishery species, Chlorurus microrhinos. We obtained a single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) dataset containing 81,901 loci across 179 fish individuals from six widely geographically distributed Fijian islands. Second, using outputs from a series of biophysical models simulating fish larval dispersal across Fijian coral reefs and 125 underwater video surveys across 24 reefs within six Fijian iqoliqolis (fishing grounds), we tested the relationship between connectivity metrics and reef fish biomass and functional diversity. Finally, using fish connectivity and socioeconomic data, we evaluated the degree of alignment between ecological and social networks in the region. Combined, our results demonstrate the high interconnectedness of fish populations among the Fijian Islands and elucidate pathways of socioecological alignment through fisheries management coordination that can benefit small-scale fisheries in the region.
328df148: Coral reefs surrounding Funafuti, Tuvalu, play a critical role in coastal stability, ecosystem productivity, and community livelihoods. However, climate-driven stressors are reshaping reef health and shoreline dynamics. We examine how recurrent coral bleaching events—driven primarily by rising sea surface temperatures—have weakened natural coastal protection from significant coral mortality that reduces reef structural complexity. As live coral cover declines, wave energy reaching shorelines increases, contributing to erosion and shoreline change that compound the effects of regional sea-level rise. Simultaneously, rapid expansion of the floating seaweed Sargassum within the lagoon further hinders reef recovery. Dense mats reduce light penetration, inhibiting coral growth and recruitment, while their proliferation may itself be linked to ocean warming and altered nutrient regimes. The combined impacts of bleaching-induced reef degradation, Sargassum overgrowth, and rising sea levels create a feedback loop that threatens both ecological resilience and human habitation on low-lying atolls. Understanding how these stressors interact is essential for developing effective management strategies to support reef restoration and coastal protection in Funafuti and similar Pacific atoll systems.
7f98bfeb:
The year 2024 marked one of the hottest years on record, intensifying global coral bleaching events and placing unprecedented stress on reef ecosystems. While reefs such as the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) have been extensively studied and monitored, the same cannot be said for coral reefs across the South Pacific. These reefs, often small, remote and dispersed across vast oceanic distances, remain critically understudied, with many having no formal ecological assessments or long-term monitoring records. Limited institutional capacity means that even national and local authorities frequently lack baseline information on reef extent, condition and biodiversity. This lack of foundational data poses a significant barrier to understanding the true scale of climate-induced degradation across the region.
For South Pacific Island communities, coral reefs are far more than ecological treasures; they are the foundation of food security, economic stability and coastal protection. Reefs provide essential fish stocks, support local livelihoods through tourism and subsistence fishing, and act as natural barriers against storms, cyclones and tsunamis. Yet these same reefs face mounting pressures from overfishing, pollution and increasingly frequent extreme weather events driven by climate change.
Unlike well-funded systems such as the GBR, island nations in the South Pacific often lack the technical, financial and institutional resources necessary to implement regular monitoring programs or restoration initiatives. This lack in scientific attention and conservation capacity leaves many reefs, and the communities that depend on them, vulnerable and overlooked within the global conservation agenda.
This research highlights the urgent need for foundational ecological data, capacity building and reasonable resource allocation to safeguard South Pacific reefs. By bringing visibility to these neglected ecosystems, we aim to promote international collaboration and data-sharing efforts that empower island communities to protect the reefs that sustain their livelihoods and shield their shores from the advancing impacts of a warming world.
abb8d6d5:
In 2017, the Government of Tonga, the Vava’u Environmental Protection Association, and the Blue Prosperity Coalition conducted a coral reef survey expedition across Tonga to support Marine Spatial Planning and Special Management Area (SMA) development. Following this assessment, in January 2022, the eruption of the Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai (HTHH) volcano generated a series of destructive tsunamis that impacted communities and ecosystems across Tonga and beyond. In August 2022, the partnership conducted a second expedition, representing the first systematic post-eruption evaluation of coral reef condition across the Kingdom of Tonga.

On both expeditions, the team surveyed reef fish and benthic composition. In 2022, macroinvertebrate assemblages,  juvenile coral density, reef structural complexity, and water quality data, as well as qualitative evidence of tsunami impacts were collected at 49 forereef sites. Of these, 13 sites were resurveys from 2017, allowing for comparisons of benthic cover from before and after the tsunami.

Findings reveal that the eruption and tsunami caused clear but spatially variable damage to benthic communities. Coral cover declined notably in Haʻapai and was low at exposed sites in Tongatapu & ʻEua, while Vavaʻu reefs showed an increase in coral cover relative to 2017. Juvenile coral densities were low across most districts, with a few exceptions, suggesting limited short-term recovery potential in some areas. Coral communities were dominated by mounding and encrusting taxa indicating disproportionate loss of branching and tabular species.

In contrast, reef fish communities appeared largely unaffected by the eruption, showing consistent biomass, density, and diversity patterns with previous surveys. Conversely, macroinvertebrates, particularly sea cucumbers, showed substantial declines in diversity and density, likely reflecting a combination of tsunami impacts and ongoing harvesting pressure.

Together, these results provide baseline data and a cross-district assessment of post-HTHH reef condition and offer critical insights to guide recovery, fisheries management, and long-term monitoring.
22836c31: Coral disease is an escalating global threat to reef ecosystems, driving rapid declines in foundational coral taxa and undermining ecosystem resilience. Even remote, seemingly pristine regions are not insulated from these impacts. At the subtropical reefs of Norfolk Island, multiple disease states are occurring on the inshore coral reef—including tissue-loss syndromes and growth anomalies— and these disease are occurring simultaneously in multiple key reef-building corals. This multi-species outbreak has been associated with rapid colony mortality, disrupted tissue structure, altered microbial interactions, and evidence of reduced reproductive capacity, indicating limited potential for natural recovery. Together, these patterns highlight the vulnerability of isolated reef systems and emphasise the need to better understand and manage the cumulative impacts from land-based sources that drive coral disease dynamics under increasing environmental stress.
16d02e4a: Benthic ecologists are inundated with increasingly big-data assets, including high-resolution image data, yet image annotation remains a significant and costly bottleneck within the analysis pipeline. Historically, image annotation has been conducted manually using general purpose software (e.g., PhotoShop, ImageJ), or with software developed focused on ecological analysis (e.g., Coral Point Counts). With advancements in computer vision has come a notable shift towards integrating tools into ecological-focused annotation software, including CoralNet and TagLab, to assist or completely automate tasks. Yet, significant challenges persist for ecological researchers, including a lack of interoperability between available software, annotation types supported (e.g., points, polygons, masks), and the degree of control granted to the users to train and flexibly deploy deep learning models. CoralNet-Toolbox addresses these challenges, by (1) serving as a central hub that ingests existing annotations from other widely used software, (2) supporting common annotation types, thereby enabling a wider range of ecological analysis (e.g., community composition, tracking individuals) within a single platform, and (3) facilitating workflows that allow users to re-use and combine different annotation type to train deep learning models for a full spectrum of tasks, from classification to instance, semantic, and ultimately, panoptic segmentation. The Toolbox is a free, open-source, cross-platform, desktop application for annotating raster data (e.g., images, orthomosaics). Written in Python and easily installable with pip, it uses PyQT to create a graphical user interface, accessible to users with various degrees of technical proficiency, while still offering state-of-the-art, off-the-shelf AI-assisted tools that greatly expedite annotation tasks. To encourage collaborations between ecologists and out-of-domain AI practitioners, the Toolbox adopts widely used standards by exporting model files and “AI-Ready Datasets” that can immediately be shared with other researchers, or on popular deep learning platforms (e.g., HuggingFace).
045b6a2d: CoralNet is one of the most widely used online platforms for annotating benthic images through sparse points, providing an efficient semi-automatic way to monitor benthic composition. However, while point-based annotations enable statistical estimation of benthic cover and functional group composition, they fail to capture the dense spatial distribution of benthic organisms, potentially introducing under- or over-estimation bias due to the sparse downsampling of points from the full imagery. This limitation hinders the wider adoption of CoralNet in downstream applications, including 3D reef reconstruction, detailed habitat mapping, and automated restoration planning. To address this gap, we introduce DenseCoralNet, an AI-based system that transforms sparse point annotations to dense pixel-wise segmentation masks. Through a scalable sparse-to-dense conversion, we can obtain more reliable benthic cover statistics and also dense pixel-level annotations for downstream 3D semantic benthic understanding. The proposed method leverages the learned features of the powerful foundation models optimized on a significant scale of training data and strengthened self-similarity within the same corals. By utilizing learned contextual relationships between various benthic regions and strengthening the self-similarity within the same region via self-supervised guidance, we can effectively propagate the labeled points to unlabeled areas and generate spatially and semantically coherent masks. We evaluate DenseCoralNet on multiple public sources from CoralNet and SeaView datasets. The dense masks generated from 50 available sparse point annotations achieve 85% relative pixel accuracy compared with expert-labeled masks, while reducing the time required to produce dense masks to 0.45% of that for manual labeling. The dense outputs enhance 3D photogrammetric modeling, coral morphology estimation, and benthic habitat analysis. By bridging ecological point annotations with powerful AI foundation models, DenseCoralNet provides a scalable pathway to convert sparse CoralNet data into fully dense and 3D‑ready reef representations, significantly reducing manual labeling effort and enabling long-term, high‑resolution coral reef monitoring and restoration.
f4770756:
As coral reef ecosystems experience unprecedented global decline, effective conservation and management are critically dependent on accurate, large-scale monitoring of benthic community composition and health. However, current methodologies for analyzing visual survey data remain labor-intensive, creating a critical bottleneck between data acquisition and actionable ecological insight. Existing approaches for automating coral reef image analysis are constrained by the heterogeneity and limited size of annotated datasets, which often only contain sparse pixel labels. As a consequence, existing models usually predict classes from a flat list of classes that disregard the hierarchical structure of benthic classes, and perform point classification instead of dense segmentation, discarding a majority of image information.

To address these limitations, we introduce a unified framework for global-scale coral reef segmentation, trained on a large meta-dataset, including data from CoralNet, Coralscapes and MERMAID, that integrates disparate imagery, label types and taxonomic resolutions from reefs across the globe. By jointly training a DINOv3-based model on both sparse pixel annotations and dense polygon masks, we leverage the information of co-occurring benthic organisms and substrates to obtain dense pixel-wise predictions of the benthos for all images. Furthermore, by mapping all sub-datasets into a joint hierarchical concept space and incorporating a concept bottleneck in our proposed model, we are able to explicitly capture the inherent taxonomic, morphologic and functional multi-hierarchy of the benthos. This approach allows us to mitigate long-tailed class distributions, enables flexible prediction at any level of the hierarchy, and provides a step towards explainable AI. We demonstrate that our model outperforms baseline methods in segmentation performance and is able to better generalize across global reef ecoregions. This work paves the way for highly accurate, automated benthic surveys, streamlines data acquisition workflows, and can be deployed as a core model with modern monitoring systems like DeepReefMap and MERMAID.
2daa5b89: Large-area imaging (LAI) is increasingly used for benthic monitoring, yet manual data extraction can be time consuming, particularly as imagery is collected at ever-increasing temporal frequencies and spatial extents. Using benthic monitoring data collected from 2023-2025 in support of Mission: Iconic Reefs as a case study, we describe an approach that exploits the inherent characteristics of LAI, leveraging access to overlapping high-resolution source imagery to combine efficient manual annotation workflows with automated labeling approaches and build a robust AI-accelerated human-in-the-loop workflow. We use expert manual annotations comprising randomly sampled 3D points (~2500 points/plot) labeled to the species level (requiring ~6 hours of effort per plot) from 159 100m² plots to train a multi-view patch-based image classifier by projecting each labelled 3D point onto all the relevant camera images. The classifier is then used to generate new independent predictions for each source image (from 28 test plots), with voting logic used to generate a final prediction for each 3D point (with the multi-view/multi-voting approach resulting in increases in per-class accuracy of up to ~40% relative to single-view predictions). We also describe view-filtering approaches to address non-stationary or overstory objects (e.g., sea-fans), which are not represented in the 3D reconstructed scene, but are abundant in source images. We then generate automatic labels over a separate LAI data set of 2500m² plots collected with a multi-camera array covering 3,800,000 m² of reef that overlap with all 28 of the 100m² test plots. We compare automated and manual cover estimates for these larger-area plots with those for the 100m² plots, and present an interactive multi-view workflow for efficient per-point validation of AI-generated labels. Ultimately, this approach exploits the speed of automation to enable work at larger scales than before possible, while incorporating human expertise to support robust data validation and increase overall classification quality.
86705429:
Rapid advances in deep learning are creating new opportunities to reduce the cost of coral reef monitoring while simultaneously enhancing thematic, spatial, and temporal detail. In this study, we develop an ensemble deep-learning workflow to achieve automated, high-resolution classification of underwater hyperspectral imagery, attaining 91% accuracy across 48 coral reef benthic classes. Using hyperspectral transects collected in 2016 with the HyperDiver system in the Caribbean island of Curaçao, we generate densely classified, thematically detailed community maps spanning approximately 4,500 m² of reef (over 2.2 billion samples), refined to more than 600 million high-confidence predictions after filtering by model confidence. The workflow integrates per-sample depth readings, enabling continuous analysis of benthic community patterns along the depth gradient to 18 meters.

The high thematic detail and dense spatial sampling achieved through our approach demonstrate that traditional methods relying on sparse observations or coarse thematic categories can substantially underestimate true reef diversity and obscure intra-class ecological dynamics. To illustrate the utility of the approach and as a methodological demonstration,  we conduct an island-wide case study along the leeward coastline of a Caribbean island. The case study reveals clear spatial patterns: southern sites displayed comparatively higher coral cover and intra-class diversity; northern sites showed signs of degradation with elevated macroalgae and sponge cover, despite being as remote as southern sites; and central sites near urban infrastructure were the most deteriorated, dominated by cyanobacterial mats, turf algae, and bare sediment with reduced coral diversity.

This study highlights the value of combining underwater hyperspectral sensing with ensemble deep learning to scalably produce thematically rich and  densely sampled benthic maps, to reveal fine-scale ecological patterns. By integrating high-resolution community maps with assessments of the environmental drivers, the approach offers a powerful framework for detecting stressors and guiding targeted, spatially informed conservation and management actions.
a1b1052a: Coral reef fisheries contribute substantially to the delivery of nutritional benefits (e.g., essential amino acids, fatty acids, vitamins and minerals) throughout the tropics, with over 500 million people, mostly in low- and middle-income countries, estimated to depend on reefs for food and income. However, most reef fish stocks are overfished, and this together with our lack of understanding on how to manage reef-based food systems to enhance nutritional outcomes limits our ability to address malnutrition and other health problems in tropical coastal communities. Nutrition-sensitive coral reef management is needed to nourish current and future reef-dependent populations. In this talk, we will (i) define and set the principles that underpin nutrition-sensitive coral reef food systems, (ii) identify the different pathways by which coral reef food systems can become nutrition-sensitive, and (iii) integrate coral reef fish nutrient profiles with reef fish surveys from > 2,000 reefs to evaluate the nutritional status and potential of reef fisheries production systems. Overall, our work shows how coral reef food systems can be tailored to support human nutrition and highlights priority locations where nutrition-sensitive fisheries management could deliver the greatest public health benefits.
3ca772a3:
Small-scale fisheries provide essential sources of protein and micronutrients for coastal communities and small island developing states, where alternative livelihoods and agricultural resources are often limited. Globally, 90% of all fishers operate in small-scale fisheries, making them central to local food sovereignty and nutritional security. However, climate change is rapidly altering tropical marine ecosystems, restructuring food webs and shifting species compositions. Because nutrient identity and concentrations vary among fish species, changes in fish community structure may directly influence the availability of key micronutrients. Climate change is predicted to alter aquatic food webs by affecting trophic interactions, biomass transfer and productivity of functional groups.
In this study, we compiled conceptual food-web models informed by fish community data from paired coral and macroalgal habitats across tropical reef systems in order to study potential changes in nutrient provision from food web restructuring due to climate change. Macroalgal habitats were used as ecological proxies for future reef states. We quantified differences in trophic structure, fish biomass, and nutrient content, and assessed functional group vulnerabilities based on published thermal tolerances and climate sensitivity. This framework allowed us to evaluate how projected environmental change may influence fisheries productivity and nutritional outcomes under multiple climate scenarios.
Our results show pronounced declines in large and small-bodied planktivores and corallivores in degraded reef habitats. In contrast, low trophic level species, such as herbivores, benefited from increased primary production and increased in relative biomass. This and other studies indicate that lower trophic level fish may sustain fish production and micronutrient provision in coral reef fisheries. However, fish from lower trophic levels may be more vulnerable to elevated temperatures than fish from higher levels due to metabolic needs, underlining the importance of considering multiple consequences of climate change when evaluating coral reef fish communities and consequences for fisheries depending on them.
e3b9f8b2: Dual biodiversity and climate crises have exacerbated fisheries declines around the world and subsequent food insecurity for communities that depend on fish as a key form of subsistence. As fish populations change and species shift their geographic distributions, long-term planning of fisheries management becomes critical for ensuring food security. Here we present physiology-informed species distribution models (SDM) of seven finfish species in the Caribbean that are important for subsistence in some coastal areas. These models highlight areas at risk of climate-driven declines and areas of increased suitability and potential refugia, to understand how shifts in climate suitability for key finfish species may affect long-term food security in coastal communities. The SDMs indicate reductions in climate suitability across species with more severe decreases under high warming scenarios, and in models assessing climate vulnerability across life stages compared to models that only evaluate adult thermal niche. We then examined whether climate vulnerability estimated from our physiological SDMs explains spatial variation in population biomass trends in the Mesoamerican Reef region using long-term monitoring data. This analysis suggests that changing climate suitability is already associated with spatial shifts in biomass of several species, reducing resource availability for subsistence fisheries in some areas. These models highlight potential for substantial future shifts in distributions of species important for subsistence in the coming decades. Information on changing climate suitability can support regional planning efforts to help bolster food security and climate resilience within coastal communities.
b2151167: Enhancing the climate resilience of fisheries food systems is critical to safeguarding food security and supporting local livelihoods. This study adapts and applies a global resilience framework to investigate the effects of climate change on Indigenous fisheries. We used 15 climate resilience attributes under five resilience domains (i.e., assets, flexibility, social organization, learning, agency) spanning ecological, socioeconomic, and governance dimensions to assess the resilience of multi-species coral reef fisheries that are important to iTaukei (Indigenous) fishers from three villages in Fiji. We found local fishers were significantly impacted by climate change, and that rapid-onset climate events accelerated adaptation and resilience-building processes. Our findings suggest that the  perceived immediacy and the acute nature of an event may be a strong driver of socio-ecological transformations for long-term resilience. The resilience framework provided valuable insights on the resilience of Indigenous fisheries; however, socioeconomic attributes were easier to interpret compared to ecological and governance. Livelihood diversity and opportunity, social capital, agency, traditional ecological knowledge, learning capacity, and a strong resilient mindset contributed to socioeconomic resilience in fishers. We also examined resilience attributes through a gender and broader social inclusion lens. While we found culturally embedded agency empowered Indigenous women and enabled them to contribute to their personal, family and community wellbeing, their participation in decision-making was low or absent. Our research offers insights into how the resilience framework can be adapted for local contexts, with potential applications for other coral reef-dependent communities globally.
765f0913:
Small-scale fishing communities are critically dependent on coral reef ecosystems for both livelihoods and food security. This reliance renders them acutely vulnerable to climate-induced pressures on fisheries, including coral bleaching, rising sea surface temperatures, sea level rise and ocean acidification. This study investigates the socioecological linkages between small-scale fishing households and climate-vulnerable reef fish populations. Interdisciplinary data from household-level socioeconomic surveys and underwater reef fish assessments was examined across more than 1500 fishing households and 60 reef locations in Indonesia and the Philippines.
We examined relationships between household-level livelihood diversity, income dependence on fishing, fishing gear use, and fish consumption, against the vulnerability of reef fish populations to climate impacts. Fishing households were categorised along a gradient of dependence on fisheries for livelihoods, fishing income dependence, gear use and fish consumption frequency. Similarly, we classified reef sites as having low, medium, or high vulnerability of reef fish assemblages, using published species-level climate vulnerability indices. 
Preliminary findings indicate that households with more jobs, more fishing gears and with the highest fish consumption, are associated with reefs containing a lower proportion of highly climate-vulnerable reef fish species. We explore these identified relationships between reef fish vulnerability and multiple interacting socioeconomic domains, to advance our understanding of the benefits of climate-resilient reefs on coastal people’s livelihoods and food security. The research underlines the need for context-specific, community-based coastal management strategies that strengthen ecological integrity and enhance socioeconomic resilience to climate change.
e0169ccd: Ciguatera poisoning is the most common form of seafood poisoning in the Pacific Islands, yet it is understudied in much of the region. The physical, social, and economic impacts of ciguatera can affect access to fisheries and intergenerational knowledge transfer that supports sustainable management. Ciguatera is caused by the consumption of seafood that has accumulated ciguatoxins produced by epiphytic dinoflagellates (typically Gamberidiscus spp.), and can cause a range of acute or chronic symptoms. The Pacific Ciguatera Network was developed through a partnership between Hawaiʻi Sea Grant and the Pacific Integrated Ocean Observing Systems at the University of Hawaiʻi in collaboration with other Universities and more than 30 organizations representing managers, communities, and fishers across the Pacific. The Network was established to address long-standing questions about ciguatera poisoning, and aims to support standardized data collection to improve regional comparisons and analyses, while also promoting collaboration and capacity building. Online surveys and targeted interviews were conducted in Hawaiʻi (n = 106 and 20, respectively) and American Samoa (n = 102 and 12) to characterize the impacts of ciguatera in these locations, and standardized dinoflagellate sampling was conducted in partnership with local agencies and organizations in Hawaiʻi (n = 20 sites), American Samoa (n = 6), and the Marshall Islands to determine the abundance and distribution of Gambierdiscus spp. using microscopy, species diversity using DNA metabarcoding, and toxin profiles. Additional studies have been initiated that explore links between environmental variables with measured Gambierdiscus abundances to better understand potential drivers and improve predictive capacity in the region. In addition to generating critical fisheries data, building connectivity, and addressing urgent questions about seafood safety in the Pacific, this Network is strengthening partnerships and offering technical training through workshops, student engagement, and a post-baccalaureate fellowship program for recent graduates from the Pacific Islands.
39a69741: Fishing has been central to human societies for millennia, however growing human populations and continued reliance on fish as a source of protein are contributing to continued declines in global fish stocks. Conventional management often depends on catch-per-unit-effort (CPUE) as an indicator of population status, which relies on the assumption that catchability is a fixed, population-level parameter. However, catchability is likely to fluctuate with individual-level vulnerability as fish shift between states of susceptibility and resistance to capture. Consequently, CPUE can often be decoupled from population size because vulnerability to capture is dynamic and may be shaped by a range of factors. Here, we examine literature that explores how fish internal states, fisher behaviour, and environmental variability interact to shape individual-level vulnerability to fishing gears, with a focus on hook-and-line fisheries. We further assess how this vulnerability at the individual scale translates into variation in population-level catchability. We thereby reframe catchability as a dynamic, emergent property driven by shifting fish vulnerability within coupled fish-fisher systems embedded in broader socio-ecological contexts. This perspective elevates catchability from a nuisance parameter in fisheries-dependent biomass data to a central concept in fisheries science that offers insight into the dynamic links between individuals and populations and between biological and human processes. We aim to stimulate discussion toward more adaptive, trait- and environment-based frameworks that better reflect the dynamic state of both fish and fisheries in time and space as they respond to increasingly rapid environmental change.
5baf6740: Many coastal communities are heavily reliant on coral reef fishes as an important dietary source of bioavailable essential micronutrients and fatty acids. Climate change-induced coral reef degradation can alter total fish biomass and species composition. These changes in fish assemblages may influence the nutrient content of reef-derived food available to humans. Understanding how climate change affects the nutritional content of reef fish is critical for supporting adaptative management strategies that are anticipatory and based on local nutritional requirements. Using Underwater Visual Census (UVC) survey data, we estimated the nutrient content (calcium, iron, omega-3, protein, selenium, vitamin A, and zinc) of reef fish communities across 60 reef sites in Indonesia and the Philippines based on species estimates from FishBase. We evaluated how spatial variation in environmental drivers affects nutrient availability to fishing communities using GLMMs. Overall, we found lower total reef fish biomass from sites in the Philippines, compared to reef sites in Indonesia, resulting in lower overall fishable biomass. Reefs in the Philippines were dominated by macroalgae and a higher proportion of herbivores and omnivores, while sites in Indonesia were dominated by coral and had higher proportions of piscivores. We explore how these spatial patterns and different environmental and human pressures can shape nutrient availability across reef systems. While climate change effects are global, local activities, including high-fishing pressure may have a strong influence on the nutritional availability of local reefs. Given almost 120 million people depend on the Coral Triangle for food and livelihoods, stakeholders and resource managers should use nutritional information to inform climate-smart and nutrition sensitive fisheries policies in the Coral Triangle region.
533e546c: Small RNAs have been proposed as a mechanism for epigenetic inheritance across generations, but their presence and potential function has not been examined in corals. Small RNAs are crucial regulators of gene expression and genomic stability and are influential in early development of bilaterian species. However, their roles in gametes and early development remain poorly understood in early-diverging (non-bilaterian) metazoans, including corals. In this study, we characterize two major classes of small RNAs–microRNAs (miRNAs) and piwi-interacting RNAs (piRNAs)–in sperm from three phylogenetically and ecologically distinct cnidarian species: the sea anemone Nematostella vectensis, the temperate stony coral Astrangia poculata, and the reef-building tropical coral Acropora hyacinthus. Through comparative analyses, we found that miR-100, the only miRNA conserved between cnidarians and bilaterians, was the only shared miRNA expressed by all three species. Most miRNAs were species-specific, but predicted target analysis revealed that approximately half of the targeted genes in each species shared homologs involved in developmental and signaling pathways. piRNAs were abundant across the three species, mapping primarily to protein-coding genes with additional overlap on transposable elements, indicating a potential dual role in gene expression regulation and genomic integrity maintenance. Indeed, functional enrichment of the genes overlapped by piRNAs revealed terms associated with DNA binding, repair, and integration, supporting their potential involvement in preserving genomic stability in gametes and through development. Together, these findings provide the first evidence that cnidarian sperm carry a rich repertoire of small RNAs, including both conserved and species-specific types. Their presence suggests that paternal small RNAs may contribute important regulatory information to the zygote, implicating paternal small RNAs as possible mediators of epigenetic inheritance and early development in corals and their relatives.
309d5734:
Assisted fertilization and larval propagation are now central tools in efforts to rescue Caribbean reef‑building corals, highlighting the need to understand how segregating genetic variation influences offspring survival. Studies of highly fecund marine invertebrates show that segregating deleterious variants can drive intense mortality during early life stages. To investigate how inherited variation affects early life‑stage survival in corals, this study uses controlled within‑species reciprocal crosses among three Caribbean broadcast‑spawning species to generate multiple full‑ and half‑sib families with samples spanning gametes, larval time points, and settled polyps. High‑coverage whole‑genome sequencing of all parents and individual offspring from selected families provides dense variant data for each reciprocal cross. Parent–offspring trios are used to phase parental haplotypes and to perform segregation and transmission ratio distortion tests, quantifying where and to what extent surviving larvae and settlers deviate from Mendelian inheritance across the genome.

Genomic regions that show consistent underrepresentation of parental haplotypes among surviving larvae and settlers, relative to Mendelian expectations, are identified and mapped as candidates for loci influencing early life‑stage survival. These candidate regions provide a genome‑wide set of targets for future functional and multi‑omic investigation. The viability map generated from these analyses offers a practical starting point for integrating genomic information about early life‑stage survival into coral breeding and larval propagation strategies.
81854840: Artificial Light at Night (ALAN) is an emerging stressor for marine ecosystems, especially those near densely populated and urbanized coastal areas. ALAN disrupts the natural light-dark patterns that govern rhythmic biological processes.
