Corals subjected to ALAN exhibit changes in physiology and behavior, as well as disrupted spawning, yet the molecular mechanisms underlying these responses remain poorly understood. In this study, we determined the effect of ALAN on key cellular processes in the coral, Acropora digitifera. Coral colonies were subjected to “cool white” light, “warm white” light, or to natural moonlight for two months during gamete maturation and prior to the spawning period. Delayed gametogenesis and disrupted spawning were observed in corals exposed to ALAN. Correspondingly, we detected shifts in expression of gene clusters associated with development, signaling, immune response, and cell cycle regulation in ALAN-exposed corals, which could have contributed to impaired reproductive timing. Findings of this study provide insight into the molecular responses of corals to ALAN, and contribute to conservation plans to mitigate its impacts.
ef8f7d10: Coral larvae represent a critical yet vulnerable stage in reef life cycles, constrained by limited energy reserves and susceptibility to nutrient depletion. Maternal provisioning and environmental nutrient availability strongly influence larval survival, dispersal, growth, and settlement success. However, nutrient scarcity remains a major bottleneck in reef recovery, limiting the effectiveness of larval reseeding and restoration initiatives. Lipids - constituting up to 70% of coral larval biomass - are key energy sources that fuel dispersal, metamorphosis, and early development. Here, we present a novel nanotechnology-based approach to enhance coral larval performance through targeted delivery of specific lipid supplements, including triacylglycerols, fatty acids, wax esters, phospholipids, and sterols. Supplementing Acropora spathulata larvae with nanoparticle-encapsulated triacylglycerols and sterols doubled swimming speed and tripled dispersal distance, indicating improved energetic capacity. Sterol supplementation, essential for membrane integrity and metamorphosis, also increased settlement rates, with 47% of larvae settling by day compared to 20% in unfed controls. Juveniles derived from larvae fed with sterols and omega-3-rich fish oils exhibited twofold higher survival under both control and elevated temperature conditions, while Calanus oil supplementation doubled survival under heat stress. Lipidomics revealed specific sphingolipids, glycerophospholipids, and fatty acids associated with enhanced fitness. Building on these findings, we developed and field-tested an in situ larval feeding strategy in partnership with local tourism operators and Traditional Owners. Our results underscore the critical role of lipid composition and availability during early coral life stages and demonstrate that targeted nutritional supplementation can overcome nutrient limitations, enhance resilience, and advance coral aquaculture and reef restoration outcomes.
79052f72: In reef-building corals, symbiosis with dinoflagellate algae is the driver of host metabolism. Algae transfer photosynthetically-fixed nutrients to the host and host metabolism is restructured to support and sustain the intracellular symbionts. Lipids in particular are an important nutrient source during early stages of offspring development in corals. However, it is uncertain how the diversity and abundance of these compounds differs in offspring based on symbiosis and different modes of reproduction. Therefore, we aimed to characterize the effect of symbiosis and host life stage on the lipidomic profiles of the sea anemone Exaiptasia diaphana (commonly referred to as Aiptasia), a model system for the study of coral symbiosis. We sampled symbiotic and aposymbiotic Aiptasia adults, asexually-derived pedal lacerates, and sexually-derived larvae and isolated lipid fractions for liquid-chromatography mass-spectrometry (LC-MS) analysis. Over 200 lipid compounds were identified across all sample groups. At every life stage, there were more compounds in symbiotic compared to aposymbiotic samples. Lipidomic profiles of samples separated primarily based on symbiotic state and formed distinct clusters within life stages, except for symbiotic larvae which overlapped with aposymbiotic larvae and lacerates. Triglycerides, ceramides, and phosphatidylethanolamines (PEs) were the major lipid classes identified among test samples, and all of them were more abundant in symbiotic test groups. Our results highlight the major influence of symbiotic state on lipid profiles in Aiptasia at different life stages and provide insight into how the lipidome differs between sexual and asexual offspring.
6488d6d4: Mass coral bleaching events are growing in frequency, duration, and intensity. Besides causing extensive mortality, the shorter time between events disrupts the ability of reefs to recover. The unique reefs of the Southwestern Atlantic (SWA), mainly restricted to Brazilian waters, are known for their reduced diversity and high endemism. Historically, the SWA is often considered a climate refugium, suffering less bleaching-related mortality compared to Indo-Pacific and Caribbean reefs. However, their recovery capacity requires investigation. In 2019, an unprecedented regional heatwave triggered the most severe bleaching episode recorded for SWA reefs. This study aimed to (i) document the bleaching incidence and mortality during the 2019 heatwave, and (ii) assess coral recovery over 3 years, using Degree Heating Weeks (°C-weeks) as a metric of cumulative heat stress. We measured bleaching incidence and monitored coral cover through surveys in three reefs in Southern Bahia (central Brazilian coast), an area that hosts the richest reef formations in the SWA, before, during, and after thermal stress. Our findings show that coral assemblages were exposed to a 5-month thermal anomaly, experiencing thermal stress peaking at 14.1 ºC-weeks. Roughly 70% of the coral cover bleached, resulting in a cover decline of ~40%. Key reef-building species, including Millepora alcicornis and the endemic Mussismilia braziliensis and Mu. harttii suffered mortality exceeding 50%. After 3 years, corals showed no increase in cover, neither at the assemblage nor species levels. This constrained recovery may indicate the breakdown of the refugium and a trade-off for resistance. Typical features of the region, such as high turbidity and dominance of massive corals, provide bleaching resistance, but likely also limit recovery. Although recent studies indicate the 2023–24 El Niño–Southern Oscillation caused less intense thermal stress with minimal cover changes in central Brazilian coast, data on coral recovery in the region remain limited.
680c31a6: Coral reefs of the Florida Keys were hit with the hottest summer on record in 2023, causing mass bleaching and mortality of scleractinian corals. Sea surface temperatures remained above 31ºC for 41 days, exceeding all prior records for the region. Branching corals fared especially poorly through this heatwave, such that Acropora palmata was recently deemed functionally extinct in the region. High temperatures and prolonged thermal stress were the major causes of mortality. However, we show that prior disease increased the onset of death for both the disease lesions and neighboring tissue. Using a Cox proportional hazard model, we found that, compared to initially healthy tissue on the same colony, disease lesions and neighboring tissue had a 3.8 times and 1.5 times higher risk of death, respectively. We show that multiple stressors, at different spatial scales, acted synergistically, increasing mortality. Furthermore, we found that close proximity to the reef edge significantly increased coral survivorship. The reef edge is protective due to potentially faster local velocity, as water courses around the reefs, which would help reduce the boundary layer and enhance the removal of cytotoxic metabolic wastes. These findings reinforce the importance of the interactive effects of disease and reef topography on coral bleaching susceptibility and mortality, and suggest that restoration efforts should be focused on reef edges where the chance of survival is improved relative to the reef interior.
9035222c: Coral reefs are threatened by rising global sea-surface temperatures, and a greater understanding of macro and micro-scale community responses to these impacts is needed. While traditional reef monitoring methods evaluate observable ecosystem changes under stress conditions, these changes can take time to manifest and may not capture early warning signs of stress. Marine microbes play a critical role in coral reef ecosystems, facilitating the movement and exchange of organic and inorganic materials across the broader food web. Marine heatwaves interrupt microbial processes including nutrient cycling, although the resulting community changes may be difficult to identify within traditional monitoring frameworks.  Seawater microbial communities respond rapidly to environmental changes and analysis of reef-associated seawater microbiomes has identified microbial indicators that inform shifting physicochemical variability and can also predict reef health and zoning status. During the 2023-24 Austral summer, the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) was exposed to above average sea-surface temperatures (1-2.5°C) with some sites experiencing up to 12.47 Degree heating week thermal exposure. Prevalent coral bleaching was observed at 73% of reefs surveyed in the marine park, with very high or extreme bleaching reported at 39% of surveyed reefs. Seawater was collected at 71 reefs across the GBR throughout the duration of the sustained bleaching event, and metagenomes and water chemistry values were generated from these samples. The thermal stress event shifted the seawater microbiome composition and impacted microbial processes. Using Integrative multi-omics these microbial changes were correlated with water chemistry and site-specific bleaching severity, coral mortality and recovery metrics to identify microbial indicators that forecast and predict reef recovery outcomes, providing insight into dynamic reef community impacts.
38c0456a: Coral reefs are declining rapidly, punctuated by the most extreme marine heatwave on record in the southern Great Barrier Reef in 2024. Diel temperature variability can “prime” corals for heat tolerance, yet earlier heat stress events resulted in unexpectedly high mortality in the most variable habitats, raising questions about when variability helps or harms. Here, we quantified host physiology and microbial communities of the reef-building coral Pocillopora damicornis across six distinct thermal environments spanning 2.4–7.7 °C d⁻¹ and three timepoints: pre- (Sept 2022), during (May 2024), and post-heat stress (Sept 2024). Pre-heatwave, P. damicornis from the Shallow Lagoon—an intermediately variable habitat—displayed the clearest “optimal priming” phenotype, with elevated host protein content, mycosporine-like amino acids, and  phenoloxidase (PPO) activity, indicating enhanced antimicrobial and immune potential prior to thermal stress. Microbial communities from the most thermally variable sites contained a greater proportion of rare and specialized taxa, suggesting  microbiome structuring along the variability gradient. During the heatwave, corals exhibited synchronous declines across most physiological metrics regardless of site, and microbiomes underwent significant beta-diversity shifts while remaining broadly Endozoicomonas-dominated. Post-heatwave, many physiological metrics rebounded—often approaching or exceeding pre-heatwave levels—yet trajectories diverged sharply between lower-, intermediate-, and higher-variability sites. Differential abundance analyses revealed that taxa with putative probiotic and antimicrobial properties became enriched primarily at intermediate variability , a pattern absent at both the least and most variable sites. Together, these results reveal nonlinear, site-specific regimes in which variability promotes resilience, induces vulnerability, or both, with direction and effect dependent on the level of thermal variability By coupling natural microbial assemblages with host physiological status across an environmental variability gradient and through an extreme marine heatwave, this work advances mechanistic forecasts of coral persistence and helps prioritize reef habitats most likely to withstand continued ocean warming.
ba3bc16b: The 2023–2024 global mass coral bleaching event—the fourth worldwide episode following 1998, 2010, and 2015–2017—caused extensive coral loss across major reef regions. Yet, despite widespread reports of ecosystem collapse, certain small islands with unique hydrodynamic regimes experienced markedly reduced bleaching impacts. These rare refuges provide crucial insights into how local physical processes can buffer coral communities under accelerating climate change. Here, we assessed spatial variation in bleaching severity around Lyudao (also known as Green Island), a volcanic island 40 km off the southeastern coast of Taiwan. The island lies within the Kuroshio Current, where downstream island-wake upwelling produces two distinct thermal environments: the northern and eastern coasts are approximately 1.5 °C cooler than the southern and western coasts. We surveyed 13,823 coral colonies across nine sites at 5 m and 10 m depths, using a five-level scale to quantify bleaching severity. Only 31.39% of colonies exhibited severe bleaching (levels 4–5), and 12.73% were fully bleached or dead—substantially lower than many Indo-Pacific/ Caribbean reports from the same event. Juvenile corals showed slightly higher susceptibility (mean severity 2.72) than adults (2.65). Dominant genera (Montipora, Porites, Isopora, Acropora, and Pocillopora) consistently showed higher bleaching in the warmer south/west, whereas colonies in the cooler north/east exhibited less bleaching. Site bleaching response index (SBRI) values ranged from 3.29 to 4.41 in the south/west and from 2.25 to 2.74 in the north/east. Linear regression demonstrated a strong positive relationship between accumulated heat stress and bleaching severity, confirming the role of Kuroshio-driven upwelling in shaping spatial thermal resilience. Our findings show that local hydrodynamic cooling can meaningfully mitigate bleaching impacts even during a global mass bleaching event. Lyudao emerges as a rare “hope spot” in Taiwan, highlighting the importance of natural physical refugia for sustaining coral reefs in a rapidly warming world.
efdfc853: Climate refugia are regions where the effects of climate change manifest more slowly, with specific environmental conditions conferring greater resistance or resilience to thermal stress, preventing severe coral cover declines. A putative example of a climate refugium is the Raja Ampat Archipelago, the global epicenter of marine biodiversity, located in the heart of the Coral Triangle. However, the region in question lacks quantitative data and detailed assessments of thermal stress and coral cover loss. In this sense, the objective of this study was to assess the intensity of thermal stress and quantify the incidence of coral bleaching and mortality in Raja Ampat, hypothesizing that these reefs act as a climate refugium and exhibit high resilience potential and low coral mortality even in the face of thermal stress events. For this purpose, bleaching monitoring was conducted across 19 sites in December 2024 (during the fourth global bleaching event) and April 2025 (post–thermal stress). Fixed 20 m transects were positioned, and along each transect, 1×1 m quadrats were placed and photographed, analyzed via PhotoQuad® to determine benthic cover. Bleaching incidence and coral mortality (cover reduction) were calculated between the two monitoring periods. The analysis of Degree Heating Weeks (DHW) data confirmed severe thermal stress in Raja Ampat, reaching a maximum of 13°C-weeks, and the results showed that bleaching reached a maximum incidence of ~18 ± 5.42% during the event but dropped drastically to ~5 ± 1.93% four months after the stress peak. Mean coral cover remained stable, at ~34 ± 3.8% during the event and ~36 ± 3.9% post-thermal stress, which did not indicate significant coral mortality. Thus, the low mortality and high resilience observed, even under thermal stress, reinforce Raja Ampat’s potential as a putative climate refugium, pending further investigation, and provides crucial data for its strategic management and conservation.
ec321922:
Global warming is severely affecting the Mediterranean Sea, determining mass mortality events in long-lived benthic organisms. Among the most vulnerable is the purple sea-whip, Paramuricea clavata, a key habitat-forming octocoral that mostly thrives on coralligenous reefs and supports several ecosystem functions. While shallow populations, <40 m, are typically the most exposed to thermal anomalies (TA), showing mass mortality events in several locations, deeper populations have not yet been affected by comparably extensive events. Here, we report the effects of an unprecedented TA affecting P. clavata at 50 m depth at Scilla Italy, during summer 2024.
Surveys conducted via video transects at depths of 35, 40, and 50 m revealed a dramatic deterioration in colony health following the 2024 summer TA. In January 2024, severe necrosis affected 46.9% of the colonies at 35 m, while its incidence was markedly lower at 40 m (2.9%) and 50 m (2.1%). After the TA, in September 2024, severe necrosis of P.clavata became widespread across all surveyed depths, reaching 94.5% at 35 m, 98% at 40 m, and 48.5% at 50 m.
On-site underwater temperature loggers recorded 28 distinct TA events in 2024 at 50 m depth, during which sea temperatures reached or exceeded the species’ physiological threshold of 25 °C. Global Ocean reanalysis data (obtained from Copernicus Marine Service, 1994–2024) were used to identify the Marine Heat Waves in the period 2011-2024 at 35, 40 and 50 meters. The result show that the summer 2024 produced an unusually prolonged (64-day) warm anomaly at 50 m depth, highlighting an unprecedented downward penetration of thermal stress.
Our findings provide the first evidence of a thermal anomaly in waters deeper than 40 m causing extensive necrosis in P. clavata in the central Mediterranean Sea, and highlight that deep thermal refugia for this species may be compromised.
85d6d031: Climate change-induced coral bleaching events are increasing in frequency and severity, threatening the persistence of coral reefs. The third (2014–2017) and fourth (2023–2025) global coral bleaching events had substantial impacts on reefs worldwide, including those at Aldabra Atoll, Seychelles. Remote, well protected coral reefs such as those at Aldabra, serve as unique natural laboratories to study bleaching impacts and recovery trajectories in the absence of substantial direct human stressors. The Seychelles Islands Foundation (SIF), management authority of this UNESCO World Heritage Site, has conducted annual monitoring of Aldabra’s reefs since 2014, tracking changes in coral reef communities and their resilience over time. Severe bleaching in 2016 led to a 33–60 % decline in coral cover on seaward reefs and a 39% decline inside its semi-enclosed lagoon. Recovery trajectories also varied across locations, whereby lagoonal reefs recovered to 95% of their pre-bleaching cover by 2023 and seaward reefs to 84%. The 2024 bleaching event was associated with substantially higher heat stress, peaking at 12 Degree Heating Weeks (DHW) compared to just over 5 DHW in 2016. Nevertheless, despite the higher thermal stress in 2024, coral mortality was not higher than in 2016 (11–46% decline on seaward reefs; 23% decline inside lagoon), suggesting potential increases in thermal tolerance or resilience mechanisms over time. This presentation outlines the impacts of these two mass bleaching events, comparing coral mortality, observed recovery trajectories, and associated environmental drivers such as heat stress and temperature variability across reef locations. By presenting the latest insights from Aldabra’s long-term monitoring programme, the aim of the presentation is to contribute to understanding how (remote) coral reefs respond to escalating thermal stress. These insights offer valuable benchmarks for coral conservation strategies, emphasising both the resilience and vulnerability of coral reefs in a rapidly warming ocean.
fff29f4c: World Heritage (WH) inscription recognises places, processes and peoples considered of outstanding and universal significance to humankind.  Among the 235 natural WH properties globally, 29 contain coral reefs.  These reef locations were the subject of two UNESCO reports (published 2017 and 2018) that described historical heat stress and observed bleaching, as well as the projected onset timing for recurrent exposure under future climate scenarios.  Here, we evaluate the past decade of satellite-derived heat stress to compare with the projections from those reports (based on modelling used for the IPCC Fifth Assessment Report).  The onset of recurrent heat stress at a level associated with severe coral bleaching (DHW ≥ 8°C-weeks) has already occurred in several WH reef locations – years-to-decades earlier than the projected timing.  This demonstrates the underlying acceleration of ocean warming and the potential for catastrophic impacts on coral reefs, both within and beyond WH properties.  These observations, from reef locations that are internationally recognised for their natural values, are a clarion call for urgent action to limit future impacts to coral reef systems globally.
a3923fa2:
Genomic studies increasingly reveal that many coral taxa are comprised of morphologically indistinguishable yet genetically distinct species occurring across diverse habitats in sympatry. There is also evidence that these cryptic coral species may be specialised to distinct environmental niches, suggesting potential for differential adaptive responses to changing ocean conditions. However, investigations of cryptic coral diversity have predominantly focused on well-studied broadcast spawning genera such as Acropora, with comparatively little attention given to brooding corals despite greater opportunities for local adaptation in the context of limited dispersal and gene flow.

This study aims to investigate the genomic basis of cryptic diversity and local adaptation in brooding corals across environmental gradients of the Great Barrier Reef (GBR). To do this, we sampled corals within the species complex Seriatopora hystrix along an expansive latitudinal gradient of the GBR. We employed low-coverage whole-genome sequencing to assess genome-wide variation and divergence within and among putative cryptic species. Our analyses of over 100,000 single-nucleotide polymorphisms revealed at least three distinct genetic lineages, with two co-occurring in sympatry at several sampling locations. We further identified genetically distinct populations associated with specific depths, habitat types, and reef locations, suggesting fine-scale local adaptation.

Ongoing analyses are focused on identifying candidate genes under environmental selection using genotype-environment association analyses and genome scans. We also aim to examine micro-morphological skeletal characteristics and symbiont community composition across the three distinct taxa to determine the functional basis of their divergence. As coral reefs face increasing threats from climate change, our results highlight the importance of recognizing evolutionarily distinct and differentially adapted coral lineages within nominal species. Our findings have important implications for protected area design and restoration initiatives. Conservation efforts should consider preserving and harnessing this cryptic diversity to maintain adaptive potential and resilience in coral reef ecosystems.
f128f2b5: Early studies of reef-building corals highlighted their broad environmental distributions, often remarking on their high degree of phenotypic plasticity across habitats. Subsequent quantitative assessments revealed greater depth specialization than initially recognized, a pattern reinforced by genomic studies exposing depth-partitioned cryptic lineages. However, as only a small fraction of recognized depth-generalist species have been examined genomically, it remains unclear what proportion of reef-building coral species are truly depth opportunistic. Focusing on the Southern Caribbean—where a disproportionate number of such assessments have been conducted—we addressed this question using an ecological and reefscape genomic analysis. By combining quantitative surveys of species depth distributions (5–60 m) with evidence of depth-associated genetic partitioning, we identified Montastraea cavernosa and Stephanocoenia intersepta as the remaining depth-generalist species whose genetic structure has yet to be evaluated in this region. Genome-wide sequencing of both species (n = 321) across the depth gradient in Curaçao revealed three and five sympatric cryptic lineages, respectively, each with distinct yet partially overlapping depth distributions. To explore whether cryptic lineages are ecologically partitioned at depths where they overlap, we leveraged photogrammetric mapping to characterize colony-level microhabitat and morphology. We uncovered microhabitat niche partitioning among syntopic lineages, evinced by differences in colony angle, local rugosity, and degree of occlusion. Although colonies from different lineages were visually indistinguishable, we found differences in colony size and polyp density at depths where they co-occurred. Overall, our results demonstrate that the last apparent depth-generalist species on Southern Caribbean reefs comprise specialist cryptic lineages that are partitioned across both depth and microhabitats. These findings therefore challenge the existence of depth-generalism among corals in this region and, more broadly, the longstanding assumption that wide depth distributions reflect high phenotypic plasticity. Appreciating this genetic diversity and its ecological significance will greatly improve our ability to predict—and ultimately bolster—coral responses to climate change.
1884bd84: Ecological speciation through adaptation to different habitats can readily occur without strong geographic isolation when the same traits underlie both ecological divergence and reproductive isolation. In light-dependent corals with environment-mediated spawning, adaptation to varying light regimes across depths provides opportunities for such speciation. We show that depth-related distributions are common among sister lineages of corals within the photic zone. We then investigated molecular drivers of depth-associated adaptive divergence by analyzing sequence variation in proteins related to environmental sensing in depth-segregated and light-dependent lineages in the Orbicella species complex. Specifically, we analyzed 1) two genetically divergent ecotypes of Orbicella faveolata across a depth gradient, and 2) two depth-segregated sister species, O. annularis and O. franksi, with different spawning times following sunset. Genome-wide analyses indicate divergence across depths occurred through adaptation via positive selection on G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs). These molecules mediate chemo/photo/thermo-reception, enhancing physiological adaptation across environments, and are also involved in reproductive isolation via differences in spawning time. Our study proposes a molecular mechanism for the origin of depth-segregated coral species, common across anthozoans, in which ecological divergence operates at spatial scales smaller than their larval dispersal potential, and highlights avenues contributing to generating biodiversity in the sea.
9ada11d4: Cryptic species, or genetically distinct lineages with similar morphologies, are increasingly recognized across marine systems, yet their functional roles and ecological significance remain underexplored. On coral reefs, genetic diversity interacts with environmental gradients to shape community structure. In Bocas del Toro, Panamá, three cryptic lineages in the Siderastrea siderea complex occur along a light-driven depth gradient, including a depth-generalist lineage that coexists with both shallow (<3 m) and deep (5–15 m) specialists. While genomic data suggest little to no gene flow between lineages, functional differences in ecologically-relevant phenotypes have rarely been evaluated. In this study, we assessed potential adaptation to specific light and temperature regimes among lineages across two sites using field-based experimental assays. Photosynthesis vs. irradiance (PvsE) curves were generated using a custom respirometry system to evaluate photosynthetic performance across a light gradient, and thermal tolerance was quantified using a Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System (CBASS). Shallow-lineage colonies reached photosynthetic maxima at irradiance levels below typical in situ conditions, while depth-generalist and deep lineages required irradiance levels more than 150% higher than those measured in their habitats. CBASS assays revealed that the shallow lineage maintained stable photosynthetic efficiency (Fv/Fm) values up to +9°C above control temperatures, contrasting the depth-generalist and deep lineages which exhibited stepwise declines in Fv/Fm beginning at +3°C. Ongoing work is evaluating how physiological traits, algal and bacterial symbioses, and in situ gene expression might explain the light and temperature responses observed between lineages. These findings highlight the risks of treating cryptic species with functional variation as genetically uniform and the importance of characterizing genetic and functional diversity to inform species-specific responses to environmental change.
680697c4: In northeastern Brazil, the scleractinian coral Siderastrea spp. persists along an expansive depth gradient ranging from tidepools to mesophotic reefs. Although population-level studies of Siderastrea have been conducted elsewhere in the South Atlantic along latitudinal gradients, no work has yet examined how genetic structure varies across depth in this region. Working in Rio Grande do Norte, Brazil, we sampled tissue from 120 Siderastrea colonies from four sites spanning <1 m to 30 m depth and used 2bRAD sequencing to resolve population structure. Our analyses revealed three genetically distinct, cryptic lineages with variable connectivity among them. Notably, the lineage dominant at Rio do Fogo, the mid-depth site (2–3 m), appears to indirectly facilitate gene flow between the lineage prevalent in the shallowest site (a tide pool) and the lineage characteristic of the deeper sites. Rio do Fogo was also the only location where all three lineages co-occurred, suggesting it may function as a key genetic bridge between shallow and deeper reef habitats. In addition to exploring genetic structure across this depth gradient, we also assessed microbial community patterns within our samples by performing 16S rRNA sequencing. From this analysis we found that microbiome composition varied significantly by reef environment but not genetic lineage. Together, these findings uncover genetic complexity in Siderastrea spp. of northeastern Brazil and identify a focal site that may support genetic connectivity across a broad depth distribution. This work provides critical insight into the population structure and microbial composition of Siderastrea spp. in Brazil and offers guidance for conservation strategies aimed at maintaining connectivity across depth-stratified reef systems.
9ee80ebc: As seawater temperature rises, a clear understanding of how genotype-by-environment interactions influence coral responses to environmental change is required for successful restoration. The recent discovery of cryptic (morphologically similar but genetically distinct) coral lineages can potentially limit restoration success as lineages may differ in their potential for plasticity. In the Bocas del Toro Reef Complex (BTRC), Panama, three cryptic lineages of Siderastrea siderea (L1–3) exist along depth, light, and temperature gradients at two sites. One lineage (L1) is a shallow specialist residing in high light and high thermal variability environments, and exhibits increased thermal tolerance compared to lineages 2 and 3, which prefer deeper environments. To test local adaptation and plasticity potential between lineages, we conducted a 1-year reciprocal transplantation between shallow (L1) and deep (L2, L3) lineages at these sites in BTRC. Coral growth, color, mortality, and symbiont Fv/Fm were measured at 6 months and will be measured again at one-year post-transplantation (March 2026). At 12 months a thermal challenge assay will determine how transplantation influences thermal tolerance in all lineages. Transplanted fragments will also be assessed for host and symbiont physiological metrics, baseline respiration, gene expression, and symbiont (ITS2) and microbiome (16S) community compositions. We hypothesize that L1 from shallow environments will exhibit increased plasticity compared to other lineages, while also maintaining higher thermal tolerance due to their increased energy reserves. Lastly, in parallel, we are conducting a common garden mesocosm experiment to test the light-acclimation potential of these lineages. We hypothesize that L1 will maintain more stable performance under lower light conditions as typically experienced by L2, relative to L2 under higher light conditions. Together, these experiments will disentangle the plasticity potential of cryptic lineages to inform restoration and management of large, long-lived corals.
0b4216d8: Corals exhibit diverse sexual systems that can strongly influence their population ecology and resilience. In gonochoric corals, where individual colonies produce only eggs or sperm, differences in reproductive investment are expected to drive sex-specific vulnerability to environmental stress. For example, greater energetic costs of oogenesis may reduce the resources available for stress tolerance, making egg-producing colonies more susceptible to disturbances, such as repeated or extended heatwaves. In addition, physiology, symbiosis, and resilience can also be influenced by cryptic genetic diversity within coral species, yet the role of cryptic diversity in shaping coral reproductive ecology remains largely unexplored. This study investigates how environment, physiology, energetics, and symbiosis interact to shape the reproductive traits and thermal tolerance of two cryptic lineages of Siderastrea siderea across a thermal variability gradient in Bocas del Toro, Panamá. Three complementary investigations were conducted: (1) To assess how habitat characteristics and lineage identity influence sex ratios and reproductive investment, colony lineage, sex, and fecundity were quantified at six reef sites over two reproductive cycles (2024–2025). (2) To identify physiological and symbiotic mechanisms underlying observed sex ratios, host energetic reserves, trophic status (stable isotopes), gene expression, and microbial (16S) and Symbiodiniaceae (ITS2) community composition were characterized for each colony. (3) Then to test how thermal tolerance varies among sexes, lineages, and environments, an acute heat stress experiment (CBASS) was conducted in 2025. By integrating spatial, physiological, and symbiotic perspectives, this work moves beyond lineage identification to uncover how thermal environment, reproductive mode, and symbiotic partnerships interact to influence coral energetics, sex expression, and reproductive potential. These findings reveal not only mechanisms by which hidden genetic diversity mediates coral responses to environmental variability, but also provide new insights into how sex- and lineage-specific traits may determine responses to warming oceans.
51e2d9d2: Cryptic depth-segregated coral species are common, with shallow and deep colonies often appearing morphologically similar while forming genetically distinct lineages. However, little is known about the specific genes or genomic architecture underlying such divergence. Theoretical models predict that, under the high gene flow conferred by pelagic larval dispersal, linkage among co-adapted genes should be favored to reduce reshuffling by recombination. Eunicea flexuosa is a nominal species of Caribbean octocoral that includes two depth-segregated lineages (Deep and Shallow) that diverged about 800 Kya, initially with ongoing gene flow, followed by transient isolation and recent secondary contact.  We hypothesize two modes of differentiation: (i) light–associated genes will be highly differentiated and linked, while (ii) modest differentiation will be spread across the genome, potentially associated with hitchhiking (with adapted genes) or arising from historical periods of reduced connectivity. We assembled a de novo genome for each ecotype and used whole genome sequencing to assess the genomic architecture of differentiation between these two ecotypes. The two assemblies were similar in size (530 Mb) and had high contiguity (Shallow: N50 = 25 Mb, L50 = 6; Deep: N50 = 18.7 Mb, N90 = 3.9 Mb) and completeness (95.3–96.4% BUSCO). Across the genome, FST revealed a small number of strongly divergent regions set in a broader background of moderate differentiation. These patterns of differentiation suggest an interplay between selection and gene flow at the onset of speciation, while historical reductions in connectivity may contribute to broader genomic differentiation.
90024991: Cryptic species challenge the assessment of biological diversity and its distribution. In scleractinian corals, key components of coral reef ecosystems increasingly threatened by global change, the lack of diagnostic morphological characters blurs species boundaries and limits biodiversity monitoring. Here, using genomics, we investigated the genetic structure and potential cryptic diversification of the widely studied coral Galaxea fascicularis (Linnaeus, 1767) across the western extent of its distribution. Targeted sequence capture of ultraconserved elements and exons from 202 G. fascicularis colonies collected across the Arabian Peninsula (AP), Maldives, and western Indian Ocean (WIO), was used to delimit evolutionary lineages using phylogenomic reconstructions and species delimitation analyses. Genomic, biogeographic, symbiotic, ecological, and morphological differentiations among lineages were further explored to characterise the origin of this diversification. Nine genetically distinct, morphologically cryptic, and geographically restricted lineages were identified, with the Maldives lineage being the most divergent. Four lineages occurred allopatrically in the WIO, while four others were sympatric around the AP, for which new genetic barcoding markers were developed to allow reliable identification. Significant variation in Symbiodiniaceae community composition was detected across localities and lineages, and redundancy in gene functions associated with divergence was also found. Ecological differences, including depth distribution, were observed among some lineages. Together, these findings reveal extensive cryptic diversity and fine-scale geographic structure within G. fascicularis, reflecting complex evolutionary dynamics potentially driven by a combination of allopatric or peripatric (e.g., following repeated Red Sea isolation events) and parapatric (associated with depth zonation) speciations. By uncovering this hidden diversity and providing accessible genetic tools for lineage identification, this study advances our understanding of coral speciation and supports more accurate biodiversity assessments and conservation strategies.
99b8d17a: Accurate species identification is fundamental to biological diversity assessments and critical for understanding ecological and evolutionary dynamics. High morphological variation in scleractinian corals have collapsed our understanding of species boundaries. Pocillopora corals are the main reef builders in the eastern tropical Pacific (ETP), despite growing under broad interannual changes that are unfavorable for reef development. Associated with these environmental conditions, Pocillopora corals exhibit cryptic diversity and highly variable skeletal morphology, leading to confusion and debate over the taxonomic validity of morphologically defined species. In this study, we found that most of the nuclear genes analyzed present low resolution to delimit species, but the histone loci allow us to identify most of the species present in ETP. Based on mitochondrial and nuclear data, we found that up to five species could be distributed in the ETP: P. cf. effusa, P. villosa, P. cf. verrucosa, and two cryptic species of the P. grandis-meandrina complex. We found that the colonial morphology and micro-morphology of corallites are not useful for identifying Pocillopora species. We characterize in situ morphological plasticity of Pocillopora corals and examine associated changes of environmental conditions in the Gulf of California and morphological variation along ETP. More than 200 tagged colonies of P. meandrina/eydouxi species complex (mtORF Type 1) were followed for 15 years (2011-2025) in locations with different flow conditions. During the study, several patterns of morphological plasticity were documented. Coral colonies switched between morphological species in as little as 6-8 months (short-term plasticity) or in two-three years (long-term plasticity). This study shows that much of the morphological diversity seen in Pocillopora corals in the ETP is driven by changes in environmental conditions independently of genetic lineage.
f8ced32c: This presentation  will focus on U.S. national-level action  to address coral reef disturbances of significant impact including coral disease outbreaks, thermal stress events, major storms, invasive/nuisance species, and coastal construction activities. It will also include examples of major disturbances in U.S. coral reef jurisdictions and innovative strategies in place to prevent, prepare for, and respond to these disturbances.
0fb08691: MPAConnect is a network of 40 coral reef marine protected areas (MPAs) in 18 Caribbean countries and territories, in partnership with the US National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s Coral Reef Conservation Program and the Gulf and Caribbean Fisheries Institute. Since 2011, MPAConnect has worked to address the priority capacity building needs of MPA managers, especially related to core MPA management topics such as sustainable financing, law enforcement and management planning. Since 2015, the network has also worked to build management capacity for climate change resilience and response to thermal stress events. In 2018, the network turned its attention to helping MPA managers respond to a new coral disease outbreak in the Caribbean. We describe the multi-faceted approach to building management capacity for response to stony coral tissue loss disease and lessons learned by the network. In 2024, MPAConnect’s regional management capacity assessment systematically examined the network-wide response to coral disturbance events and, for the first time, assessed management capacity for coral restoration among Caribbean MPAs. We present these findings and describe how they are shaping the network’s current innovative work on developing and implementing MPA-level coral restoration plans, and the complementary management capacity building needed on sustainable financing, partnerships and stakeholder engagement, monitoring and communications, to ensure the long-term success of Caribbean coral reef recovery efforts.
7d208324:
In the wake of Hurricane Maria in 2017 and the rapid spread of Stony Coral Tissue Loss Disease (SCTLD) across Puerto Rico, the Department of Natural and Environmental Resources’ Coral Reef Conservation and Management Program (the Coral Program) elevated coral reef emergency response as a top management priority. These escalating threats required coordinated local action, which led DNER to developing formal emergency response initiatives and securing sustainable funding pathways. A major milestone was the passage of Law 72, declaring coral reefs as essential natural infrastructure for coastal protection. This designation enabled new interagency collaborations and allowed the use of significant external resources—notably over $4 million in FEMA Hazard Mitigation funding—for coral reef restoration.

The government further strengthened these efforts through Executive Order OE-2021-066, which declared an ecological emergency due to SCTLD, mandated mitigation actions, and allocated $1 million to support response efforts. This directive enabled the development and government approval of the SCTLD Emergency Response Strategic Plan in 2022. Consequently, since 2023, coral disease response and emergency/disturbance management have become central components of the Coral Program’s mission.

Using the Rapid Response Protocol for Coral Reef Emergencies as a guiding framework, the program now coordinates strategic responses to a range of disturbances, including high-energy ocean events such as strong swells, tropical storms, and hurricanes. The scope has recently expanded to include surveillance of invasive pulse soft corals (family Xenidae) (via benthic surveys and innovative technologies such as automated underwater vehicles) and evaluating response frameworks for vessel groundings that may trigger reef emergencies.

A public-facing ArcGIS-based dashboard integrates emergency response data with information collected through citizen science, enhancing transparency and situational awareness. This presentation will outline a practical roadmap for developing an integrated coral reef emergency and disturbance response initiative, offering insights from the perspective of a local resource management agency.
d7dcdc4b: Stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD) causes unprecedented damages to infected reefs, including large-scale mortality on over 20 species, reduced coral cover and diversity, and diminished ecosystem services. An in-water topical medicine developed to halt active disease lesions exhibited high efficacy and has been used throughout much of the Caribbean to treat affected colonies. Treatments, however, are not prophylactic. New lesions can develop on previously-treated corals, though how frequent this is has not been well quantified. By fate-tracking nearly 2,500 treated corals across seven Florida Keys reef sites, we quantified reinfection rates through the first year of treatment as well as overall survival rates of treated corals. For corals that were part of a regular monitoring program, new lesion development continued to decrease with time, and survival after three years was 86%. The tissue saved from SCTLD-related mortality through intervention efforts in the Florida Keys is estimated at nearly 10,000 m2 of tissue, or the equivalent of 3.75 million coral outplants. Though there was some variation among species and habitats, 36% of colonies remained disease free for at least a year after just one treatment visit, and 58% remained disease-free if colonies were visited again 1 to 2 months after initial treatment. This suggests that for affected reef sites that can only be visited one or two times, treatments still have substantial positive impacts on coral survival. We use intervention efforts in a remote national park to calculate that 25 days of single-treatment dive operations likely equated to saved tissue equivalent to 547,000 coral outplants. We further outline the pros and cons of concentrated intervention efforts on a small number of sites versus dispersed effort over many reefs.
525767b2: More than a decade since it was first reported, stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD) remains the most devastating coral epizootic in the western Atlantic region due to its high species susceptibility (34 species), broad endemic range (33 jurisdictions), and high mortality rate (30-60%). SCTLD has yet to be documented on Pacific reefs, however, urgent questions remain regarding the potential for SCTLD introduction and whether Pacific corals are susceptible to the disease. Several lines of evidence point to ship’s ballast water transfers as potential sources of disease transport to new reefs, speeding up disease spread in the Caribbean. The Panama Canal, which supports tens of thousands of ship crossings between ocean basins annually and has historically facilitated pathogen and nonnative species introductions, represents a potential avenue of disease transmission to Eastern Tropical Pacific reef systems. To evaluate the vulnerability of Pacific reefs to SCTLD, we conducted a laboratory transmission experiment using Pavona clavus and Pocillopora sp. native to the Pacific side of the Panama Canal. Both species presented lesions following exposure to waterborne and direct contact disease, and had equal risk of developing lesions as the moderately-susceptible Caribbean species Orbicella faveolata. Lesions were confirmed to be histopathologically similar to SCTLD, while molecular characterization of algal symbiont communities did not reveal any distinct patterns in disease susceptibility among Symbiodiniaceae genera. Finally, to assess the potential for spread of SCTLD pathogens from disease-laden ballast water discharge in the region, we modeled the dispersal of neutrally buoyant particles released from the Pacific anchorage. Particles reached biodiverse reef habitats at the nearby Pearl Islands within three days, which is within the established window of infectiousness for waterborne SCTLD. These findings indicate that the threat of SCTLD to Pacific reefs may be significant, and that the consequences of transport, exposure, and transmission could be far-reaching.
2657d094: While many Pacific Islands are at the forefront of terrestrial biosecurity initiatives to address invasive species, the protection of the marine environment has lagged behind. Emerging threats such as stony coral tissue loss disease and invasive soft corals highlight the need for action in these areas. This talk will provide a  overview of the challenges and opportunities managers face and strategies to improve marine biosecurity.
ad21f540: Reef-building corals are increasingly threatened by recurrent marine heatwaves driven by anthropogenic disturbances and climate change, resulting in widespread and destructive bleaching events. A rapid increase in coral thermotolerance can arise from multiple molecular mechanisms, including genetic and epigenetic variation, shifts in holobiont composition, and phenotypic plasticity. Identifying the functional drivers underlying thermotolerance is therefore essential for optimizing reef restoration strategies and informing the design of effective marine protected areas. To address this challenge, we investigated three ubiquitous scleractinian coral species: Acropora hyacinthus, Pocillopora acuta, and Porites lobata (~130 colonies per species), representing contrasting functional groups and sampled across a large-scale gradient of thermal stress in the South Pacific (New Caledonia, Fiji, French Polynesia). We combined ecologically relevant, laboratory-controlled thermal-stress experiment to quantify both early and late thermaltolerance with an integrative multi-omic framework. This included genomic and epigenomic profiling (EM-seq), transcriptomics (RNA-seq), and holobiont characterization (metabarcoding of Symbiodiniaceae and bacteria). Multi-Omic Factor Analysis (MOFA) captured ~50% of genomic variation, 20% of epigenomic variation, 45% of transcriptomic variation, 45% of Symbiodiniaceae variation, and 15% of bacterial community variation. Variance partitioning and association with key structuring factors (island, site, early and late thermotolerance phenotypes) revealed several multi-omic factors significantly associated with thermaltolerance. These factors encompassed hundreds of SNPs, CpG loci, gene-expression profiles, and multiple Symbiodiniaceae clades. Many thermotolerance-associated biomarkers were involved in canonical stress-response pathways. The resulting candidate biomarkers now require validation to support the identification of highly thermotolerant corals and guide targeted conservation actions. Overall, this study advances our understanding of the genetic and non-genetic adaptive mechanisms shaping coral resilience under climate change, while providing innovative tools to enhance the conservation and restoration of reef ecosystems.
f486e0fd: Acute thermal stress tests are gaining widespread adoption as a standardized approach for diagnosing thermally tolerant corals for research and restoration applications. The Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System (CBASS) and short-term thermal stress assay was developed as a portable platform and high-throughput approach to enable rapid thermal tolerance assessments and widespread comparative analyses. However, accurate benchmarking and evaluation of the utility and limitations of the approach are still very much a work-in-progress. Here, we will present a mini-review of the strengths, limitations, and remaining unknowns concerning the use of the CBASS and similar techniques for coral thermal tolerance assessment. Primary strengths center around the portability, low-cost, flexibility in configuration and deployment, high-throughput capacity, limited supporting infrastructure required, and ecological relevance of response differences. Limitations have been found in consistency across different equipment types, tank volumes, and flow rates, repeatability of measurements over time, precision of different response variables, and associated cost of high-precision instrumentation. Remaining unknowns concern the ecological relevance of results, consistency in acute versus chronic thermal responses, underlying drivers affecting variation in responses, potential tradeoffs to increased thermal tolerance, and contributions of phenotypic plasticity versus adaptation to thermal tolerance differences. Some unknowns are technical in nature, while others are biological. We maintain these techniques and approach still hold great promise for research and restoration applications, though argue it is important for the community to recognize the limitations of the various systems and assays when designing experiments and interpreting results. Additionally, it is important as a community that we work together in continuing to answer the unknowns in order to optimize and further our understanding of how best to utilize these promising tools.
45b3b685:
Corals exhibit substantial intrapopulation variation in their response to ocean warming, a phenomenon that restoration efforts aim to harness by selectively propagating more resilient individuals. Yet, the molecular traits underpinning holobiont heat tolerance – emerging from complex host-symbiont interactions – remain poorly resolved. Here, we tested the predictive power of coral metabolomes for determining thermal tolerance in a multi-species community with diverse symbiont associations.  
We sampled 5mm biopsies from 15 colonies of each of eight coral species from Kāneʻohe Bay, Oʻahu, Hawaiʻi, for untargeted metabolomics analysis. Colony fragments were then subjected to each of five distinct six-month temperature regimes representing both historical and projected conditions in Kāneʻohe Bay. We quantified thermal tolerance at the end of the experiment based on the rates of decline in symbiont photosynthetic efficiency (effective dose – experimental degree heating weeks – causing 25% Fv/Fm reduction). Using machine learning and correlation analysis, we evaluated how accurately metabolome signatures predicted thermal resilience across and within species and identified metabolite classes and molecular species most strongly associated with heat tolerance. 
Our results demonstrate that metabolome signatures predicted heat tolerance with moderate to high accuracy. Despite individual metabolites most strongly correlated with thermal tolerance being highly species-specific, the underlying metabolic pathways were common and included energy metabolism, membrane composition and oxidation, and lipid-mediated signalling, and related to the algal symbiont, the coral host, and their interaction. Increased abundances of specific symbiont-derived lyso lipids and triacylglycerols containing polyunsaturated fatty acids were especially strong biomarkers of decreased bleaching susceptibility. Based on our findings, we hypothesise that highly tolerant corals have a more cooperative and metabolically advantageous symbiosis. This research highlights the promising utility of biochemical biomarkers as tools for resilience-based reef restoration and provides new mechanistic insight into metabolic interactions within the coral holobiont that confer climate tolerance to reef-building corals.
1487b4db: The number of coral restoration programs has been rapidly increasing worldwide to combat the intensification and frequency of marine heatwaves. Yet, most practitioners lack accessible, scalable tools for identifying thermally resilient corals. Conventional approaches for assessing thermal tolerance remain costly, invasive, and impractical for many restoration groups, creating a critical need for rapid, non-invasive, and inexpensive methods. To address this gap, we used the Multispectral Aquatic Photosynthesis Profiler (MAPP) and a global deep-learning framework to generate and analyze non-invasive photophysiological predictors of coral thermal tolerance. From 2023 to 2025, we compiled a multinational dataset spanning Australia (AIMS), Fiji (Reef Explorer), Florida (Mote Marine Laboratory), Guam (University of Guam), and the Gulf of Aqaba (KAUST), incorporating both short-term CBASS (<24 h) and multi-week chronic thermal stress assays. Currently, the MAPP generates over 4,000 photophysiological metrics per colony, which we reduce to 10-120 key predictors using feature selection techniques and stacked autoencoder reductions. Deep-learning models were then trained using an 80/20 split and evaluated with a 10-fold cross-validation and leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV) to assess the generalizability and predictive robustness. Predictive accuracy varied across regions, but was consistently strong, with the highest LOOCV performance in Guam (R2 = 0.91) and in the Fijian summer of 2023 (R2 = 0.83). Fijian winter, Gulf of Aqaba, and Florida datasets produced moderate, but informative results (R2 = 0.55-0.65), reflecting regional and seasonal physiological variability. Across all regions, LOOCV exceeded the 80/20 split, highlighting improved model stability as the global dataset expands. Together, these results demonstrate that MAPP-derived photophysiological metrics, when paired with a global deep-learning framework, offer a powerful, scalable, inexpensive, and non-invasive approach for identifying thermally resilient corals to support restoration decision-making in a rapidly warming ocean.
416d36e2: Coral reef ecosystems are threatened by increasingly frequent and severe marine heatwaves. One proposed intervention is to propagate thermally tolerant coral colonies for outplanting to restoration sites, but identifying reliable predictors of thermal stress resistance or resilience remains a challenge. Here, we conducted a 3-month artificial thermal stress and recovery experiment on 38 genets of the staghorn coral, Acropora cervicornis, to determine whether common physiological metrics and/or 16S microbial communities were predictive of long-term survival. At peak heat stress (4 Degree Heating Weeks), stressed colonies featured reduced Symbiodiniaceae cell densities and altered microbial communities when compared to their paired ambient control fragments. After a 1-month recovery period, 26 out of 38 genets exhibited partial to full mortality in at least one of their replicate fragments. Resilient genets had higher symbiont densities and host protein concentrations than susceptible genets under control conditions, suggesting that genotypes with greater biomass were more likely to recover. Most fragment microbiomes were dominated by sequences assigned to “Candidatus Aquarickettsia rohweri,” but high abundances of this putative parasite did not appear to impact the response to thermal stress. Instead, bacteria linked to endolithic algae in coral skeletons were associated with survival. Outside of photochemical efficiency, the physiological metrics in this medium-term experiment did not correlate with acute heat stress rankings (ED50) for these genets, potentially due to the narrow ED50 range for A. cervicornis in the Florida Keys. Heat stress responses can be difficult to quantify, but integrative heat stress experiments remain a valuable tool to discover traits that predict improved bleaching outcomes.
6fb5ed3c: Decades of Coral Reef Watch data have revealed a strong latitudinal gradient in thermal stress accumulation across the Caribbean, shaping the realized thermal niche of coral populations. To examine the effect of contrasting thermal histories on thermal performance, we conducted standardized CBASS thermal profile assays on colonies of the endangered reef-building coral, Acropora palmata from Curaçao (highest frequency of heat-stress events), U.S. Virgin Islands and the Florida Keys (prior to the functional extinction of A. palmata at this site). Colonies were exposed to a Control+6°C temperature ramp up (0–120 min), a heat-load (180–360 min), and a subsequent 10-hour recovery phase (ending at 1020 min). Physiological phenotyping (ED5, ED50, ED95, Decline Width, Tissue Absorptance) and inferred transcriptional stress trajectories using Slingshot (top 2,000 most variable genes) consistently identified Curaçao as the most thermally resilient population. The dominant axes of inferred stress progression and recovery, in correlation with gene expression, resolved regulatory programs associated with complete (thermally tolerant) versus incomplete (sensitive) returns to homeostasis. Colonies from the thermally tolerant population were characterized by a dominant set of positively correlated genes representing a single, highly integrated protein-quality control / proteostasis (PCP) network. The core temporal gene markers of this pathway included canonical heat-shock proteins (HSPs), co-chaperones, ubiquitin ligases, deubiquitinases, and proteasome components. Our results show that the presence and strong induction of HSPs and ubiquitination-related genes is not just a signature of excessive stress but a signature of resilience and recovery. Curaçao colonies maintain an expanded and extremely efficient, constitutively primed PCP network that enables efficient damage control, mitigation of the toxicity of aberrant proteins, and recovery of nuclear functions under thermal stress conditions. Complementary SNP-based analysis confirms Curacao colonies harbor important genetic diversity for survival under a high frequency of heat-stress events and are therefore obvious targets for conservation action.
f70d61dc: The elkhorn coral, Acropora palmata, is a keystone species and the primary builder of western Atlantic reef crests. After the functional extinction of the species from Florida’s coral reef from the 2023 marine heatwave, resource managers want advice on how to rehabilitate elkhorn populations from near-zero baselines. Importantly, many Florida native elkhorn genets were collected and placed into ex-situ protective care for propagation before the extirpation event. Further work involved breeding these corals with other Florida genets using assisted reproduction. We report on a field experiment in Biscayne National Park (near Miami, U.S.A.) where the growth of fifteen purportedly unique and new genets from Florida-by-Florida-parent crosses created at The Florida Aquarium (TFA) is compared to fifteen founder genets originally collected from throughout the Florida Keys. All 30 corals were sourced from TFA and were relocated to three sites in the field in March 2025. After nine months, phenotypic performance including survival, calcification rates, gains in height and planar surface area, and growth morphology were calculated. Results for summer 2025 indicate equal survival between sexual vs. asexual treatments (80% overall), with minor site-specific survival. Calcification rates were 5.7 ± 2.5 mg cm-2 d-1 overall and did not differ significantly by treatment or site. These rates are about half of those measured previously in other western Atlantic locations, indicating conditions were less optimal for A. palmata during this period at our sites. Localized differences in heat stress likely played a role, as the reefs in this area experienced 8 degree-heating weeks in summer 2025. These results are important in guiding restoration strategies to optimize the balance of native founder genets and new genets created through assisted reproduction to obtain the most effective mix of resilient genets for reestablishing a self-perpetuating population of this critical shoreline-protecting and habitat-creating coral species.
cd636a2f: Ocean warming is driving widespread coral bleaching, destabilizing the host–symbiont relationship and further impeding calcification, the core process underpinning reef accretion already compromised by intensifying seawater acidification. Reef restoration and future-proofing efforts typically emphasize the propagation of thermally tolerant coral species or individuals, yet acid–base regulatory traits remain largely overlooked despite their key roles in modulating bleaching sensitivity and conferring tolerance to ocean acidification. Using live-tissue imaging, we examined pH regulation in calcifying and symbiont-carrying cells of three coral species (Stylophora pistillata, Pocillopora effusa, and Porites lobata) with contrasting sensitivities to seawater pH and temperature, and assessed whether this trait serves as an integrative predictor of species-specific responses to environmental perturbations. P. effusa showed strong thermal sensitivity, with calcification declining by 45% (light) and 68% (dark) at 30°C relative to 25°C, yet remained unaffected by reduced pH (7.9 → 7.6). Conversely, P. lobata experienced a ~40% decrease in calcification under low pH but responded little to warming, while S. pistillata was largely insensitive to either driver. Photosynthetic responses were similarly variable: P. effusa exhibited reduced gross photosynthesis under warmer and more acidic conditions; P. lobata maintained stable photosynthesis across treatments; and S. pistillata photosynthesis increased under warming. Of the three species, only P. effusa paled visibly following prolonged heat exposure. Interactive effects on calcification and photosynthesis were predominantly additive, with occasional deviations typically reducing cumulative impacts. In this presentation, we evaluate whether variation in proton-removal capacity at the site of calcification explains these contrasting calcification responses, and whether altered acid–base regulation in symbiont-carrying cells underlies differences in photosynthetic stability and bleaching susceptibility. Together, these findings provide mechanistic information for identifying acid–base regulatory traits that enhance resilience to climate-driven ocean change, thereby enabling more informed coral selection for restoration efforts that leverage live-tissue imaging approaches.
aa92b0db: Severe marine multi-host epizootic disease outbreaks are increasing rapidly around the globe even as their underlying ecological mechanisms remain unclear. Coral reefs of the tropical western Atlantic have suffered particularly widespread mortalities in the last decade due to stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD). Local loss of cover is often high, despite many reefs hosting historically low coral densities prior to disease emergence. SCTLD transmits rapidly among reefs via ocean currents and then persists, generally after first depleting the most highly susceptible species. The role of reefs’ oceanographic connectivity and resident metacommunities in facilitating invasion potential and endemicity must be better understood. Here, we first produced gapless species-specific maps of coral presence and abundance in the US Caribbean region down to 60 m depth, using rich monitoring data and oceanographic predictors. We then used these maps coupled with prior identified within-reef SCTLD epidemic rates to produce a multi-scale, multi-susceptibility model of SCTLD spread after its estimated emergence locally in late 2018. Mapping indicated hotspots of high-risk reefs featuring highly susceptible corals Colpophyllia natans, Diploria spp., and Pseudodiploria spp., as well as extensive upper mesophotic coverage of a moderately susceptible genus thought to contribute to long-term SCTLD maintenance, Orbicella spp. Modeling demonstrated that when sensitive parameters controlling local disease initiation and external transmission flux were tuned in concert with realistic hydrodynamic connectivity, simulations closely matched observed spread. Local outbreak intensity, and thus export of diseased tissue mass, was directly related to a sites’ community and total coral cover. More difficult to validate and optimize were susceptibility group-specific quantities of coral cover loss, suggesting ways in which future work might more flexibly model inter-group transmission. Findings support prior work linking disease spread with ocean currents, while also highlighting the critical importance that heterogeneous host density and community have to infectious marine diseases.
61005e66: The growth and performance of coral colonies is supported by energy and nutrients derived from photosynthetic microalgal symbionts. Symbiont density within coral tissue is an important factor influencing coral performance – for example, corals with low symbiont densities (e.g., following bleaching events) and correspondingly low energy inputs often exhibit reduced growth rates. Yet, the functional relationship of symbiont density to coral growth rate remains unclear. Here, we evaluate the link between symbiont density and coral growth rate. We do this by developing a mechanistic model describing the flux of resources from symbiont populations into coral biomass. We then test this model against experimental datasets showing how reductions in symbiont population density driven by restricted inorganic nutrient supply (nitrate, phosphate) affects the growth of different coral species (including 10 species from several functional groups). We found evidence for three functional relationships of symbiont density to coral growth rate. For six species, coral growth rate simply increased linearly with increasing symbiont density, as more symbionts leads to increased resource supply rates. For two species, growth rate saturated at high symbiont densities due to per-capita reductions in symbiont resource production at high densities. Finally, the growth rates of two coral species showed no response to changes in symbiont density. Our study offers a new analytical approach for linking symbiont density to coral growth and provides empirically validated predictive models of coral colony growth rate in environments that lead to fluctuating symbiont densities.
92f9a5e4:
Symbiotic cnidarians have thrived for millions of years in oligotrophic marine environments, where nitrogen scarcity has driven the co evolution of metabolically integrated holobionts. Nutrient homeostasis in these associations relies on photosynthesis powered nitrogen recycling that maintains a narrow carbon–nitrogen (C–N) balance, increasingly vulnerable to anthropogenic stressors. Understanding the mechanistic basis of C-N regulation is essential, yet experimentally challenging, given their microscopic scale and tight metabolic entanglement, making numerical models valuable tools for isolating subprocesses and quantifying system behaviour.

We present a numerical mechanistic model of symbiosis between a coral host and colonizing Symbiodiniaceae that, unlike steady-state or flux-based approaches, uses explicit rate laws to solve a compartmental model. This captures dynamically C-N coupling via glutamate-based amino acid synthesis, which drives growth of both partners. Diurnal dynamics of dissolved species (glucose, ammonium, oxygen), particulate biomass, and spatial volumes are tracked through coupled differential-algebraic equations.

Model predictions reveal a N driven feedback loop that mediates the transition from early exponential symbiont colonization to stable, isometric host–symbiont growth. An initially large host nitrogen pool supports rapid symbiont proliferation; its depletion imposes N limitation, and triggers photosynthate translocation. This organic carbon fuels host ammonium reassimilation into amino acids and thereby growth, hence a minimum nitrogen flux is defined to maintain a productive yet bounded symbiont population. We validated these predictions using continuous flow chemostats of S. microadriaticum (CCMP-2467) under in hospite like pH and ammonium conditions. Predicted C  and N-turnover rates match published single cell NanoSIMS measurements of nutrient fluxes, supporting views of cnidarian symbiosis as a self regulating chemostat governed by a C–N feedback loop. A phase transition analysis across external ammonium supplies and photosynthetic productivities maps dynamical regimes and nitrogen thresholds, linking molecular metabolism to holobiont level stability and identifying environmental conditions that push the symbiosis beyond its safe operating space.
e87ad349: Coral connectivity plays a fundamental role in the persistence and resilience of reef ecosystems, yet remains challenging to quantify due to the complex interplay between poorly quantified larval traits and ocean dynamics. This challenge is amplified in physically intricate regions such as the Aruba-Bonaire-Curaçao (ABC) islands, where sharp bathymetric gradients and fine-scale hydrodynamics strongly influence larval transport and recruitment. In this study we apply the multi-scale, GPU-accelerated ocean model SLIM3D to simulate several years of hydrodynamics in the ABC basin, enabling the fine-scale (∼100 m) representation of coastal circulation in reef areas. We then use SLIM’s Lagrangian Particle Tracker (LPT) to simulate the dispersal of millions of virtual larvae including species-specific biological traits, such as competency acquisition/loss, mortality, and settlement behaviour, thereby providing a robust assessment of connectivity pathways at the regional scale. Sensitivity analyses on the LPT’s physical and biological parameters further help us identify the most influential factors shaping connectivity. We analyze dispersal patterns for the broadcast spawner Diploria labyrinthiformis (Dlab) and the brooder Favia fragum (Ffra) and find very contrasted results. While Ffra shows very limited inter-island exchanges due to rapid larval settlement, resulting in largely isolated reef populations, Dlab exhibits broader dispersal enabled by longer pelagic larval durations and advection by the strong northwestward Caribbean Current. Additionally, the formation of (sub)mesoscale eddies in the lee of the islands also promotes larval retention and self-recruitment, leading to lower genetic differentiation for the broadcaster than for the brooder. Our findings align with in-situ genetic measurements, revealing numerous distinct genetic groups for Ffra and only two major groups for Dlab. This study underscores the role of coral larval early-life history traits in self-recruitment and long-distance dispersal, giving insights into how dispersal potential varies with reproductive mode and local hydrodynamics.
881490c2:
Coral recruitment is increasingly recognized as a key bottleneck for reef persistence and restoration. Recent work shows that competent larvae are weak swimmers that depend on fine-scale reef structure to escape fast flows and complete settlement, and that vertical swimming and gravitaxis differ strongly among species and through early ontogeny. At the same time, restoration efforts are rapidly deploying textured tiles, 3D-printed modules and novel ``microrefugia'' geometries to boost settlement, often with limited guidance on how larval behavior, wave climate and substrate design interact.

We present a behaviorally realistic numerical model that tracks individual coral larvae as they swim, sink and are transported over millimetre-scale ridges under wave-driven flow. The model is grounded in laboratory measurements of gravitactic and vertical swimming behavior for Acropora and Pocillopora larvae, including their tendency to switch between upward and downward swimming modes near competence. Hydrodynamic fields over ``d-type'' and ``k-type'' ridge arrays are first resolved with a flow model, then used to move thousands of virtual larvae with simple rules for active swimming, buoyancy, random reorientation and attachment. Larvae are allowed to settle only when they encounter crustose coralline algae (CCA) in relatively low-shear microhabitats, reflecting experimental observations that calm, structured microrefugia and CCA cues jointly promote settlement and post-settlement survival.

By varying ridge height and spacing, wave period and basic larval traits in a set of proof-of-concept tests, we explore how structure and flow might interact to influence where larvae are more likely to be retained or swept away. The framework aims to link larval behaviors to restoration-oriented substrate design to maximize larval capture and settlement under local wave climates.
b932519e: Pacific nations depend on reefs and are thus highly vulnerable to their degradation. In Hawaiʻi, coral reefs are foundational in Native Hawaiian genealogy, support high levels of endemic biodiversity, and generate over $850 million annually. Mitigating land-based pollutants — one of the top three threats to coral reefs globally — can improve reef resilience and preserve these ecosystem services. Sediment runoff is a primary contributor to coral decline (and algal dominance), and its deleterious effects are exacerbated by nutrients, which bolster the growth of algae competing with corals for benthic space. A leading management strategy to prevent shifts from coral- to algal-dominated states is maintaining the biomass of herbivorous fish, which consume algal competitors. However, the efficacy of this method can be hindered by sediment-driven changes in fish herbivory.  This study constructs a mathematical model to improve management targets by identifying (1) pollutant thresholds past which herbivore biomass may abruptly decline and (2) primary drivers of resilience. We expand upon a coral-algal competition model by incorporating dynamic herbivorous fish functional groups (browsers, grazers, and scrapers), which interact with the model’s benthic components via algal herbivory. The responses of benthic taxa and herbivore groups to land-based pollutants are parameterized using literature and field data. Specifically, algae growth and herbivory rates were measured across three independent riverine runoff gradients in Hawaiʻi for one year.
