Following the resumption of outplanting in 2024, orthomosaic baselines were produced for outplant plots at each reef. Because high post-bleaching coral cover was used to select these plots—a proxy for resilience—surviving wild colonies and a 2022 restored cohort were also incorporated. In 2025, one year later, these plots were re-imaged to evaluate recovery trajectories. Orthomosaics were processed using the open-access CeruleanAI platform to extract standardized and reproducible metrics of live coral coverage and colony dimensions.
Preliminary results reveal distinct first-year recovery trajectories. At Cheeca Rocks, Orbicella annularis and Orbicella faveolata increased in live tissue area (+0.070 m² and +0.254 m²). At Looe Key, O. annularis exhibited neutral to slightly negative tissue change (–0.025 to +0.012 m²), and O. faveolata showed small decreases (–0.008 to –0.011 m²). These patterns suggest stronger early recovery at Cheeca Rocks, whereas mixed responses at Looe Key reflect local stress history and environmental constraints. These preliminary results provide site-specific insight into post-disturbance recovery and enable direct comparison of wild and restored corals. By integrating photogrammetric reconstruction with CeruleanAI-driven outputs, this workflow enhances the consistency, transparency, and decision relevance of coral recovery monitoring. The approach supports site evaluation and restoration planning, underscoring the importance of advanced monitoring tools for guiding climate-resilient coral restoration.
2d4f11a9:
Coral reef ecosystems are experiencing unprecedented pressure under accelerating climate change, underscoring the urgent need for scalable, repeatable, and high-resolution monitoring systems. This paper positions underwater AI as a major research frontier and analyzes the factors that have transformed marine perception from a niche application into a catalyst for AI innovation. We examine the evolution of underwater perception and discuss how current research is reshaping our ability to observe, interpret, and respond to reef change.

We identify three converging forces behind this emerging trend: (1) escalating environmental necessity, as risks to reef ecosystems become increasingly urgent and reef managers seek ecosystem-scale assessments that are faster and more cost-effective; (2) the increasing availability of large-scale annotated reef image datasets, enabled through citizen science platforms, consolidation of datasets, Corporate Social Responsibility, and large collaborative monitoring programs; and (3) a migration of computer vision researchers toward underwater domains as advances in terrestrial benchmarks reach plateaus in performance. Together, these drivers have led to a significant uptick in publications and research interest in AI-driven monitoring and restoration of marine ecosystems.  

Our analysis reveals how unique underwater challenges - turbidity, cryptic species detection, expert annotation bottlenecks, and cross-ecosystem generalization - are driving fundamental advances in weakly supervised learning, open-set recognition, and robust perception under degraded conditions.  We survey emerging capabilities in large-scale reef datasets, semantic and instance-level scene understanding, and 3D reconstruction for habitat change detection. We also highlight a paradigm shift from passive visual assessment toward targeted, AI-enabled intervention, for tasks such as reef restoration.  

Ultimately, this work demonstrates that constraints encountered in coral reef environments are pushing the boundaries of foundation models, self-supervised learning, and perception, leading to methodological innovations that extend beyond marine applications to benefit general computer vision, robotics, and environmental monitoring.
1c280bd7: Long-term monitoring of coral reefs increasingly relies on automated, image-based machine learning tools to deliver standardised and scalable assessments of benthic community composition.  ReefCloud is a reliable platform for rapidly assessing changes in reef conditions across diverse habitats; however, like most machine-learning approaches, its performance may decline following large-scale disturbances such as mass coral bleaching and mortality, which can rapidly alter the taxonomic composition of reef communities in ways that are not represented in the training data. To evaluate model robustness under disturbance-driven ecological change, we assessed ReefCloud’s performance before and after the 2024 mass bleaching event on the Great Barrier Reef using case studies spanning inshore to offshore habitats. Although models trained on long-term monitoring datasets perform well from year to year, post-2024 bleaching imagery showed a drastic shift in benthic assemblages that reduced annotation accuracy and reliability. We examine how model performance varies across a range of habitat types and bleaching impact categories to assess sources of error linked to training data composition. Each case study provides specific recommendations for model assessments to support the production of reliable data at the benthic composition level. We also discuss broader implications of machine learning for long-term coral reef monitoring, highlighting the need for transparent reporting of model capabilities and limitations, especially amid accelerating climate impacts. As disturbance events such as coral bleaching increase in frequency and intensity, sustaining the robustness, accuracy, and interpretability of long-term coral reef monitoring datasets while leveraging automated approaches is essential for reliable ecological assessment and management.
9db7ebf0: Pacific reefs are declining under increased disturbances, with hard coral cover declines leading to increasing prevalence of alternative benthic groups such as algae and benthic cyanobacterial mats (BCMs). Monitoring these alternative groups is important for assessing reef health, yet they are frequently lumped together or misclassified, particularly BCMs. Photo-based ex-situ monitoring may increase this risk due to visual identification challenges. AI-powered tools, such as ReefCloud (https://reefcloud.ai/), have emerged as efficient tools to reduce the bottleneck of slow manual image annotations, but can be limited by image quality and annotator skill. This study used ReefCloud to compare conventional PIT surveys with image-based surveys (n = 75 images per site; both methods covering a total 150m of reef) where annotations (n = 20 per photo) were done by two expertise levels and AI models trained on 20-50% of the dataset, across shallow (~2m) and deeper (~10) reef. In situ PIT data (every 50 cm) was collected by an experienced surveyor, quadrats (1 m2) were photographed every 2 m along the transect line, 1 m above the substrate. The objectives were to evaluate (1) agreement between PIT and image-based observations of BCM, (2) how AI model performance varies with annotator expertise and training level, and (3) performance across depths. Hard coral annotations were well aligned with PIT across expertise levels and depths, but BCMs were consistently significantly underestimated, particularly for the beginner annotator. BCM estimates were most accurate when AI models were trained up to 20% by an experienced annotator, and further machine training did not improve performance for any group. While experienced in situ observers likely remain the most reliable for complex taxa groups, image-based methods provide a feasible alternative when field surveys are constrained. However, considerations of annotator expertise, survey depths and photo quality remain critical for ensuring reliable data quality.
642541fb: Coral reef ecosystems face increasing and diversifying impacts from both environmental and anthropogenic pressures, with devastating outcomes for reef-associated fisheries. Climate change is driving widespread mortalities across reef-building corals, resulting in a loss of diversity and changes to fish community composition. Further, overfishing threatens the function and service provision of coral reef ecosystems as well as the livelihoods and food security of millions of people. These environmental and ecological changes across coral reefs and their fisheries have been intensively evaluated and continue to receive considerable, and imperative, attention across research and management contexts. However, very little consideration has been given to changes across socioeconomic dimensions and their relationship to sustainability and resiliency in coral reef fisheries. Elucidating these dynamics is critical to developing a multidimensional understanding of socioecological transformation across coral reef ecosystems and their fisheries. To address this gap, we characterize archetypal trajectories of socioeconomic change across the world’s tropical coral reefs between 2000 and 2020. Using spatial and national-level data, we employ a global-scale cluster analysis across eight indicators of distal human pressure on coral reef fisheries: GDP, population density, urbanization, deforestation, agriculture, regulatory quality, marine aquaculture, and marine protected areas. Our results identify 6 archetypes of socioeconomic change and determine the relative importance of our indicators in differentiating these archetypes. This work contributes to our broader understanding of coral reefs as complex social-ecological systems and supports the development of theories of change for coral reef fisheries—which can inform innovative, interdisciplinary fisheries management decisions that are applicable across geographies while remaining responsive to local context.
421f4f9e:
Determining how social and ecological processes jointly shape resilience in coral reef fisheries systems remains a key challenge. While resilience is shaped by the ability to cope, adapt, and transform in response to environmental or social change, the specific social-ecological configurations that support these processes are not well understood. It remains unclear how household behaviour, ecological dynamics, and institutional factors align across countries to shape the likelihood of adaptation.
We examine how reef ecological state, fisheries dependence, local governance arrangements, and household socioeconomic conditions interact to shape adaptive intent among reef-dependent households in Indonesia and the Philippines. We combine surveys from over 2,000 households with underwater ecological surveys and satellite-derived reef metrics to construct an integrated, social-ecological dataset across multiple coastal communities. We use clustering to identify socioeconomic archetypes capturing distinct livelihood structures, asset portfolios, and dependence on fishing. Ecological adaptive capacity is quantified through indicators including fish biomass, coral cover, and habitat complexity, while governance captures perceived fairness and legitimacy, as well as participation in decision-making.
Using multilevel models, we test how ecological conditions and governance directly and indirectly influence household adaptive intent, and whether these pathways differ across socioeconomic typologies. We find that governance is a mediating factor in the relationship between ecological and social adaptation, providing opportunities for, or constraining, adaptation. This approach advances understanding of resilience as an emergent property of multiple interacting domains rather than isolated drivers. By identifying empirically grounded social-ecological configurations of adaptation, this study provides practical insights for designing policies that support the resilience of reef-dependent fishing communities in a rapidly changing climate.
b1803bef: Indonesia, the world’s largest archipelagic nation, is the third largest capture fish producer in the world, next to China and Peru. In addition to this high production, Indonesian fisheries are distinguished by diversity in terms of species and trophic levels harvested, the variety of gear and methods used, the size and types of fishery vessels, and the expansive area and depths fished. As such, fisheries management in Indonesia is equally complex, embracing multiple levels of governance, decentralization, and traditional fishing tenure rights. During 2019-2021, the Supplemental Technical Assistance (STA) project was implemented to complement the activities of the USAID Sustainable Ecosystems Advanced (SEA) project to support the Government of Indonesia to improve fisheries and marine resource governance and conserve biological diversity with a focus on the small-scale fisheries of Fishery Management Area 715 in Eastern Indonesia. The STA project was developed based on the recommendations provided from a preliminary needs assessment report that summarized input from Indonesian fishery managers and scientists. From a series of stakeholder surveys, the needs assessment identified capacity building activities to contribute to a theory of change in three key areas: stock assessment and data management, scientific reviews and management recommendations, and management policies and protocols. The talk will detail the lessons learned from the needs assessment and capacity building activities for Indonesian fishery scientists, students, and managers implemented during STA with a highlight on in-person and online workshops in histology-based rapid reproductive analysis for fishes and the development of a national-level review process for fishery stock assessments.
d34e80bb:
Coral reef fisheries across the Caribbean are essential for food security, livelihoods, and local economies, yet they face growing pressures from climate change, overfishing, and limited access to timely, standardized fisheries information. In many small-scale coastal communities, monitoring programs operate with limited resources, and while data are often collected with great local effort, constraints in time, tools, or analytical capacity can make it difficult to process and use this information for decision-making. To address these challenges, the Coral Reef Alliance and its co-management partners co-developed FishApp, a bilingual, near real-time fisheries monitoring platform designed to integrate biological, ecological, and socioeconomic data into a single decision-support system. 
FishApp compiles catch and effort data, size-based indicators, maturation proportions, biomass spectra, multispecies indices, and long-term CPUE trends to support data-limited stock assessments and ecosystem-based decision-making. The platform enables co-managers, community organizations, and government authorities to detect spatial and temporal trends, identify early warning signs of resource decline, and interpret environmental change alongside fishing dynamics. These analyses are automatically translated into accessible dashboards that facilitate transparent communication and shared understanding among stakeholders.
A core feature of FishApp is its strong integration of community participation. Local fishers, community scientists, and co-managers contribute directly to data collection, validation, and indicator co-design, ensuring that traditional knowledge and field expertise inform management recommendations. FishApp represents a scalable model for how digital tools, developed collaboratively with coastal communities, can enhance fisheries assessment, environmental resilience, and inclusive governance. By expanding across the Caribbean the platform aims to support sustainable coral reef fisheries that balance ecological integrity with the livelihood needs of coastal populations.
557ade56: Environmental challenges associated with climate change, like rising temperatures, can produce beneficial or negative legacy effects in reef-building corals. Negative effects of marine heatwaves have been documented on the resilience and survival of corals, yet conversely, beneficial transgenerational acclimatization has also been demonstrated in marine invertebrate offspring following exposure to environmental stress. Understanding marine heatwaves effects on non-genetic inheritance of environmental memory is essential information for reef-building corals. This study tested the hypothesis that spawning Acropora pulchra corals exposed to a simulated marine heatwave during gametogenesis would result in crossgenerational acclimatization through environmental memory. Adult colonies (n = 12 genets) from the north shore backreef in Mo'orea, French Polynesia, were split in half and exposed to diel fluctuations in ambient temperature or a simulated heatwave for four weeks in March/April 2022. Heatwave-exposed colonies exhibited bleaching and declines in symbiont densities (-20%), chlorophyll content (-20%), photosynthesis (-3%), and respiration (-11%), indicating a reduction in energetic capacity that could limit parental provisioning to offspring. Six months later, spawning success was high in both the ambient (100%) and heatwave parental histories (83%). Gametes from the heatwave history had significantly larger egg sizes (38%) and higher fertilization success (25%) than ambient history. When larvae reared to the swimming stage were subjected to a reciprocal thermal stress experiment, offspring from heatwave parents had greater survivorship under stress than those from ambient (28%) or wildtype (30%) colonies. RNASeq revealed differential gene expression following parental heatwave treatments and in the subsequent offspring due to parental history, with protein synthesis processes and maintenance of cellular structure enriched in both the adults and offspring. These results demonstrate that offspring of parents challenged by thermal stress exhibit enhanced fitness through environmental memory. Such acclimatization may allow early developmental stages to survive stress events and has critical implications for reef recruitment.
8833b923: Coral mass spawning is an annual event in which numerous colonies release gametes with precise synchrony. As most reef-building corals spawn only once a year, the timing of gamete release is critical for maximizing fertilization success. Recent studies demonstrated that moonlight suppresses spawning, and that the dark period between sunset and moonrise serves as a spawning cue. However, the mechanisms underlying this regulation remain poorly understood. In this study, we investigated the effect of the dark cue on gametogenesis and gene expression in the coral Galaxea fascicularis. Coral fragments were maintained in tanks with and without cue, and samples were collected daily until spawning occurred. Histological and transcriptomic analyses were performed to examine changes in gonadal development and molecular pathways associated with the cue. Under dark condition, all colonies spawned synchronously, whereas no spawning occurred in the control under artificial moonlight. Our results show that the dark cue regulates critical stages of gametogenesis, enabling progression toward gamete maturation and spawning. Transcriptomic analysis further revealed sets of genes responsive to the cue, including both common and sex-specific patterns of regulation. Together, these findings provide new insights into the molecular and cellular basis of how environmental cues, particularly lunar cycles, regulate coral reproduction.
648dbf3b: Sexual recruitment of scleractinian corals is essential for maintaining genetic diversity and population resilience. Larvae actively search for suitable settlement substrate and an essential part of that search involves chemosensory processes. Many studies have assessed larval settlement behavior and metamorphosis in response to settlement cues but the neurobiological mechanisms underlying these responses remain unresolved. In this study, we quantified physiology (lipid content, fertilization rate, sperm motility) and global gene expression in families of Acropora cervicornis larvae across a developmental time series and following crustose coralline algae (CCA) exposure. The developmental time series included larvae from two unique biparental crosses at daily intervals spanning 12 to 86 hours post fertilization. In the CCA exposure experiment, larvae from a third biparental cross were sampled based on their behavioral response in two categories: those that remained swimming at the surface and those which were actively probing and attaching to the substrate in settlement arenas with and without a CCA cue. Larvae were collected at two timepoints, one and four hours post exposure, as some larvae reacted quickly to the CCA. After four hours, 46% of larvae exposed to CCA cues displayed active probing whereas only 34% of control larvae exhibited these behaviors. RNA was extracted from all samples and the resulting shotgun RNA-seq libraries were sequenced on the Illumina NovaSeq X Plus. These data will be used to identify genes responsible for sensing settlement cues and triggering behavioral and metamorphosis responses throughout embryogenesis.The results of this study will provide foundational knowledge on key sensory pathways involved in critical developmental transitions and give insight into neural development from a molecular perspective.
a946966f: In this work, we generated a curated database of high-quality, chromosome-level genomes and gene annotations for 42 scleractinian species and 49 non-scleractinian cnidarians. The availability of these resources allowed us to characterise cnidarian genome structure, construct a robust phylogeny and investigate in detail the core genes underlying key physiological processes. Our genomic analyses revealed strong chromosomal conservation across species, together with clade-specific rearrangements and multiple independent gene family expansions. We then focused on understanding the gene sets that control budding in corals. Using our curated database, we performed a detailed comparative annotation of transcription factors and downstream genes previously associated with budding, regeneration, and larval development in other cnidarians. In parallel, by experimentally following coral budding in the laboratory, we generated bulk and single-cell RNA-seq datasets across multiple developmental time points. This approach enabled us to identify temporal expression changes and cell type–specific expression patterns of key genes regulating coral budding. We further validated gene expression and spatial patterns using immunohistochemistry (IHC). Together, our analyses reveal that corals possess a highly specialised molecular machinery that partially recapitulates larval developmental programs, establishing developmental axes that guide the formation of new polyps and highlighting the involvement of specific cell types. Overall, this study provides the first comprehensive insight into the genomic foundations of coral budding, illuminating previously unknown gene dynamics. It also underscores the value of comparative genomics and integrative bioinformatics for understanding both evolutionary processes and the molecular basis of key developmental mechanisms in corals.
4fa8e993:
Understanding how early-diverging marine invertebrates regulate development and environmental responses at the molecular level is crucial to identifying mechanisms of resilience under rapid environmental change. Neuropeptide signalling is one of the earliest-evolving systems of intercellular communication, it regulates a large diversity of physiological processes, in many animals, yet its role in corals remains largely unknown.

This project investigates the evolution of neurotransmission using the coral Stylophora pistillata, a scleractinian cnidarian. The research builds upon previous studies in the cnidarian Nematostella vectensis, where neuropeptides and their receptors were identified and functionally characterized, laying the groundwork for exploring these mechanisms in reef-building corals. The main goal is to identify and functionally characterize neuropeptides and their G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) in S.pistillata, marking the first such exploration in corals.  We identify coral neuropeptides and their GPCRs using bioinformatic prediction and phylogenetic analyses, followed by molecular cloning and heterologous expression to perform deorphanisation assays and characterise ligand–receptor interactions.

Applying candidate neuropeptides to live coral tissues and coral larvae, we test their roles in processes fundamental to early-life success, including larval settlement, growth, reproductive physiology, stress responses, and symbiosis. Linking these pathways to larval fitness and stress tolerance will provide mechanistic insight into how signalling networks shape developmental and ecological outcomes.

This work introduces neuropeptide signalling as an overlooked regulator of coral biology and connects evolutionary molecular mechanisms to phenotypic resilience. By integrating evolutionary biology, comparative genomics, molecular physiology, and functional assays, the project provides new perspectives on conserved pathways underlying early-life robustness and identifies potential molecular targets relevant to coral adaptation and conservation under global climate change.
73b8269f: Increasingly frequent and intense bleaching events can disrupt natural coral reproduction cycles by reducing energy reserves for gamete production. In 2023 and 2024, the U.S. Virgin Islands experienced mass coral bleaching events caused by unprecedented thermal stress followed by a lack of observed spawning. Thirty Orbicella spp. and 10 Diploria labyrinthiformis colonies were monitored through the bleaching events of 2023 and 2024. Over the summer of 2024, 17 of these colonies (7 Orbicella spp. and 10 D. labyrinthiformis) were brought to a land-based nursery for spawning observations. No spawning was observed from any colony. This is the first study to our knowledge to report a complete lack of observed spawning in both bleached and unbleached corals. Samples were taken from wild and tagged colonies for histological and lipid analysis (30 Orbicella spp. and 10 D. labyrinthiformis). Histology showed no signs of gamete development in either species. Corals which bleached during the 2023 event had significantly lower lipid concentrations than those that retained their pigmentation. Negative physiological effects on all corals in the study over the course of the two bleaching events suggest the impacts of bleaching go deeper than visual health. Due to the lack of spawning in both visually bleached and unbleached corals, the future of sexual coral restoration may rely on bringing broodstock colonies into controlled ex situ conditions for multiple years prior to reproduction.
874d1de5: Corals play essential roles in reef ecosystems by increasing structural complexity, providing shelter, and enhancing biodiversity. Nevertheless, coral populations worldwide have been increasingly affected by rising sea temperatures, which induce bleaching and mortality. We evaluated the health and demographic structure of coral populations over time and examined the effects of two major thermal anomalies in reefs from the Southwestern Atlantic. Sampling was carried out at five sites in Arraial do Cabo, a coral “oasis” in southeastern Brazil that hosts high species richness and represents the southern distribution limit of several species. Sampling took place from 2019 to 2025 through at least semiannual surveys, encompassing two significant thermal events: the 2019 El Niño and the 2023/2024 marine heatwave. At each site, nine random 10 m × 1 m transects were surveyed along a 2–9 m depth gradient. All colonies were identified, measured, and categorized according to the Coral Health Chart. Colonies smaller than 5 cm were considered juveniles, and those below 2 cm as recruits. Six coral species were recorded, with Siderastrea stellata, Millepora alcicornis, and Mussismilia hispida being the most abundant. In 2019, a mass bleaching event affected up to 80% of M. alcicornis colonies, yet mortality reached only 12%, indicating considerable thermal resilience. the size structure of M. alcicornis remained unchanged; however, the density of S. stellata recruits decreased, and M. hispida consistently exhibited very low densities of both recruits and juveniles. Bleaching in S. stellata and M. hispida ranged from 50–60%, with mortality near 20%. In contrast, during 2023/2024, while reefs elsewhere experienced severe bleaching, corals in southeastern Brazil showed no significant impacts, likely due to coastal upwelling buffering local temperatures. Overall, the findings reveal species-specific responses to thermal anomalies and highlight both the vulnerability and notable resilience of Brazilian corals to increasing thermal stress.
a3d77ba4:
As anthropogenic stress continues to ravage coral reefs, researchers and managers alike are playing a desperate game of catch-up. Given the limited chance of halting climate change, priorities now shift towards enhancing reef resistance and recovery in the dwindling intervals between bleaching events. Yet, while abundant literature exists on the processes up to and during bleaching, little is understood about the aftermath. Symbiont populations may be rapidly re-established, for example, but other important health indicators like growth and fecundity may take years to be fully restored, with exact timelines remaining unclear. In the last decade, the number of studies addressing coral recovery has started to increase, but still only represent a fraction of those focused on bleaching. Fundamental questions, from species-specific recovery potential to when exactly a coral is considered “recovered”, remain unanswered. 
In this study, we present an overview of the existing literature on coral recovery, aiming to (1) summarise the current knowledge of how critical functions are restored post-bleaching, from a cellular to ecosystem level; (2) examine important research gaps that may improve management and risk prediction efforts; and (3) highlight the challenges of designing and executing recovery experiments. We further explore how recovery potential may be altered by recurrent heat stress, presenting a case study of Pocillopora damicornis exposed to different seasonal temperature regimes. Finally, we propose the development of a multi-species framework to predict recovery potential across different levels of bleaching severity. The fate of coral reefs is not solely determined by which species will bleach, or not. Rather, it’s those species that will recover, and recover quickly, that can hold the line against future climate change.
31b9497e:
Coral bleaching events are becoming more frequent, more severe, and are occurring on a global scale. Enhancing the efficiency of monitoring tools to document these events is essential to better understand how coral reefs will be impacted by the accelerated pace of stress. However, quantifying bleaching impacts from field imagery or in-water observation is currently a highly time-consuming process, as expert annotators must manually assign a bleaching score to each colony, a process that is difficult to scale up to the ecosystem level of disturbance. 
To address this challenge, we developed a tool that automatically estimates the severity of bleaching from large-extent (tens to hundreds of square meters, containing hundreds to thousands of colonies) orthomosaic. In this study, we integrated a five-class bleaching detection model into the RapidBenthos orthomosaic instance segmentation pipeline to automatically quantify colony-level bleaching severity.  The workflow begins by segmenting the orthomosaic using a coral detection YOLO (You Only Look Once) model. Next, a point annotation model trained in ReefCloud is applied to the underlying images during the annotation phase. Finally, a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model assigns a bleaching score to each colony based on segmented underlying images.
With this tool, researchers can gain deeper insights into the demographic impacts of bleaching, as colony taxa, size, relative location, and bleaching score are all extracted automatically. Automating bleaching detection from images enables faster data generation and reporting—an essential capability in a time when disturbances are increasing in frequency.
a45836a1: Monitoring coral recruitment is essential for assessing the long-term health, resilience, and recovery potential of coral reef ecosystems. Early coral recruitment serves as a critical indicator of reef vitality, as it can highlight declines in the capacity of coral populations to recover, even when adult coral abundance and cover appear stable. Coral recruitment and settlement patterns remain uncharacterized along the central Red Sea, despite the region being recognized as one of the world’s most biodiverse coral ecosystems. Here, we assessed coral recruitment patterns on lagoonal reefs in the Saudi Arabian Red Sea using two deployment types: short-term tiles placed during the spring spawning season (March–July 2023 and 2025) and year-long tiles (March 2023–March 2024 and March 2024–March 2025). These deployments spanned the marine heatwaves (MHW) of 2023 and 2024. Paired settlement tiles were deployed at four reefs prioritized for coral restoration efforts, including a coral nursery. A total of 588 and 43 coral recruits were identified, measured, and photographed across all tiles deployed during the 2023 four‑month and year‑long deployments, respectively. Across all reef sites, recruit assemblages were dominated by Acroporidae and Pocilloporidae, paralleling the approximate proportion of these taxa on the adjacent benthos. Reefs in this region of the Saudi Red Sea were significantly impacted by the MHW in 2024, as reflected by a virtual collapse in coral recruitment (99% decrease) across all coral families at the four reef sites in 2025. Together, these baseline rates provide one of the first insights into coral recruitment in the Red Sea, highlighting both the dominance of fast‑growing taxa and the vulnerability of early life stages to prolonged heat stress. As one of the few recruitment datasets from this region, these findings establish a critical benchmark for evaluating reef resilience and guiding restoration and management priorities under intensifying climate stress.
da71313a: Despite more than 50 years of research on the coral life cycle, there are still major limitations in data describing the core vital rates. This is particularly true of the early life history processes, such as post-recruitment survival, even though they represent a critical bottleneck to population growth. Even less is known about how these processes are affected by warming oceans and ever more frequent heat stress events. We monitored survival of >3000 0.3–10 cm coral colonies across four reefs in the eastern Indian Ocean which experienced drastically contrasting levels of heat stress. Under background conditions survival ranged between 0.43 to 0.92 across the reefs, and clear Type III survivorship patterns emerged with coral size, illustrating the importance of escaping the bottleneck of high mortality when corals are small. Following moderate bleaching, survival was 0.67 at Ashmore Reef, higher than expected given the similarity to the background conditions measured at other reefs. In contrast, severe bleaching at Mermaid Reef reduced survival odds by 35% from background conditions and resulted in a complete breakdown of the Type III survivorship for structurally complex, bleaching-susceptible taxa (Acropora spp. and Pocillopora spp.). Taxa known to be more resistant as adults (Goniastrea spp. and Porites spp.) maintained the Type III size-survival relationship, but overall survival was reduced under severe heat stress, albeit to a lesser extent than for the structurally complex taxa. These taxon-, size-, and heat stress-specific results clarify previously equivocal understanding of this essential life cycle transition which will help improve predictions of coral population trajectories and inform restoration and management of coral reefs.
f53a9160:
Extensive coral bleaching was observed in Sekisei Lagoon—the largest coral reef system in southern Japan—during the summer of 2024. Although multiple bleaching events have occurred in the lagoon in the past, the effects on the fecundity of surviving coral populations and subsequent recovery trends remain poorly understood.
 To address this gap, we analyzed fecundity survey data collected annually since 2012, alongside juvenile density surveys conducted at 31 sites across the region from 2003 to 2025. Fecundity assessments of five Acropora species were carried out each April, recording bundle maturity status on a five-point scale. Juvenile coral density within quadrats was also measured annually in April. Additionally, bleaching surveys (five-point scale) targeting four Acropora species were conducted each September.
Our results identified five major bleaching peaks (2003, 2007, 2016, 2022, and 2024). Both the reproductive capacity of surviving colonies and the density of visible juvenile corals—key indicators of post-bleaching recovery—showed a gradual decline over time. Juvenile coral densities dropped to less than 20% of peak levels in the years preceding major bleaching events, followed by slow recovery trends.
 Furthermore, since 2016, when substrate and coral composition began to be assessed using transect methods, an increase in turf algae has been observed, followed by macroalgal blooms. These widespread bleaching events have significantly impacted coral larval supply in the region. In particular, algal dominance in certain areas may inhibit the survival of newly settled polyps.
5ea79b6f: Coral reefs throughout the Tropical Northwestern Atlantic have suffered frequent mortality events of various origins since at least the late 1970’s, including record-setting marine heatwaves in 2023 and 2024, associated with the ongoing Fourth Global Coral Bleaching Event (GCBE4). While the scale and intensity of the heat exposures can be monitored in near-real-time via satellite-derived sea surface temperatures, the resulting ecological impacts are only now starting to be understood. Leveraging long-term monitoring plots established as part of NOAA’s National Coral Reef Monitoring Program (NCRMP), photomosaic imagery collected via structure from motion (SfM) was analyzed from sites in Florida, St. Thomas (USVI), and St. Croix (USVI). Utilizing pre-and-post-bleaching timepoints of 100m² plots (N = 6 per site), we quantified species and community-level mortality through a combination of virtual point intercept method and fate-tracking of colonies. The data-rich nature of these plots allows for a thorough assessment of impacts from marine heatwaves, demonstrating the value of photogrammetry as part of a long-term monitoring toolkit. Despite reaching >18 (Florida) and >20 (USVI) °C-weeks during this event, these sites experienced lower mortality than would be expected at this level of heat stress. This presentation will report on these results and explore the hypotheses for why these coral communities did not experience mass mortalities despite the extreme heat stress. Benthic community structure impacted survivorship, with sites dominated by the thermally tolerant Orbicella spp. complex showing lower relative mortality compared to those dominated by weedier species. Similarly, despite higher decreases in relative percent coral cover, the changes in absolute cover were low, signifying already low cover at certain sites and a possible shift towards overall thermal tolerance. Continued monitoring of these sites will be of critical importance in the coming years to document secondary and tertiary impacts, such as disease and corallivore outbreaks.
186c18a3: Anthropogenic climate change is driving more frequent, severe, and prolonged marine heatwaves, leading to mass coral bleaching and mortality across the globe, including on the Great Barrier Reef. Coral bleaching resilience varies widely across reef regions, yet the drivers underpinning this variability remain poorly resolved. Increasing evidence suggests that heterotrophic plasticity—fuelled in part by ambient food availability—may influence a coral’s capacity to survive thermal stress. Chlorophyll-a is frequently used as a proxy for phytoplankton biomass and particulate food supply, but its functional links to post-bleaching survivorship have not been evaluated for a variety of genera and at such broad and varied spatial scales. Here, we integrate regional chlorophyll-a climatologies with bleaching and mortality observations from the 2024 mass bleaching event across the Great Barrier Reef to test whether background productivity, and therefore increased heterotrophy, mediates coral thermoresilience. Using satellite-derived chlorophyll-a data paired with in-water health assessments and changes in coral cover, we show that reefs within more highly-productive regions exhibited significantly lower bleaching-related mortality prevalence. These findings indicate that regional differences in heterotrophic feeding capacities may buffer coral energetic deficits during heating events, potentially enhancing post-bleaching resilience. This study links patterns in trophic flexibility to spatial heterogeneity in bleaching-related mortality and highlights the importance of trophic context in predicting future climate-driven reef outcomes, particularly in an era of accelerating ocean warming.
37243acb: The sympatric corals Acropora palmata and A. cervicornis form a hybrid (“A. prolifera”) whose abundance continues to increase even as the both parental species have declined. The parent species, each with a distinct morphology, build the framework associated with highly diverse Caribbean reefs. How the morphologically intermediate hybrids have contributed to reef structural complexity through time is less understood. Using genomic data from populations across the Caribbean, we detected low rates of bidirectional gene flow since speciation between the parent species, with a slight excess of gene flow from A. palmata into A. cervicornis. We found no evidence of later-generation hybrids in our contemporary colonies, consistent with previous studies. While common causes of hybrid sterility such as repeat expansions and large chromosomal inversions were absent, we identified small homozygous regions, copy number variants, and expression bias in the hybrids that may contribute to their sterility. Because skeletal morphology is critical to reef complexity, we correlated eight morphometrics of the three taxa to the genomic data. A composite score of all morphometrics and three individual traits (branch diameter, branch radius of the first bifurcation and the number of bifurcations) showed significant associations with genomic variants. We highlight key genes involved in development, calcification and skeletal organization that may underlie the distinct morphologies of the parent species. Together, these results suggest that ongoing but infrequent hybridization between A. palmata and A. cervicornis is unlikely to genetically swamp either parent species or provide evolutionary rescue. The morphological diversity of the hybrids, however, may prove valuable for ecosystem restoration in the near term and offers important insights into the genetic basis of morphological traits of reef-building corals.
4711f5b7: Survival of the fittest assumes that, given genetic variability, some individuals of a given species will fare better than other individuals in the face of challenges, be it escaping an enemy, or surviving adverse environmental conditions. Chimeric individuals, entities composed of two or more genotypes of one species, offer a twist and a different dimension to who may be the fittest. Here we present the current state of chimeric knowledge in cnidarians, where nuances exist in the spatial distribution of the genotypes within a chimera, representing a panorama of ecological and conservation ramifications. We discuss the theoretical framework of five different states of spatial chimerism, alongside current data on spatial distribution of genotypes in coral chimeras.  Chimerism can be sectorial, whereby the genotypes co-exist, but each in their own zone.  Alternatively, the genotypes within the chimera may be mixed, either as a mosaic, evenly distributed, multi-chimerism or micro-chimerism. With the spatial distribution of genotypes, cnidarian chimerism may affect interpretation of data in cnidarian population studies. Likewise, depending on the spatial arrangements of genotypes within a chimeric coral, fragmenting such a coral, either for experimental or conservation efforts, may result in the formation of fragments that are genotypically different, either keeping the within-species genetic variants, or genetic combinations that may be less fitted to current or future environmental conditions. However, whether all partners within a chimera have equal access to the germ line remains unresolved. We conclude by highlighting how chimerism can be harnessed for cnidarian conservation efforts, primarily for coral reef restoration.
049c8821: Chimerism is increasingly recognized as a major contributor to intraspecific genetic-variation in reef-building corals. Stylophora pistillata, a brooding branching species from the Red-Sea, acts as a model for exploring the ontogeny and ecological perspectives of allorecognition. Here, we synthesize a suite of experimental and field-based studies that illuminate the mechanisms and consequences of chimerism in this species and its application in active restoration. Vital staining of coral-larvae and kinship-tracking experiments revealed that S. pistillata planulae consistently settle into dense aggregations regardless of relatedness, forming more aggregates than expected by random settlement. Similar clustering was observed in natural juvenile populations, confirming that such interactions are ecologically common. During an early alloimmune window of <6 months, these contacts lead to either fusion or structured rejection boundaries, with the probability of fusion decreasing as the temporal distance from first contact increases, reflecting the combined influence of age and kinship on allogeneic outcomes in forming chimeric or rejecting entities. Long-term in situ monitoring observations demonstrated that young chimeras outperform genetically homogeneous colonies in size and survivorship, where chimeric entities achieved larger ecological volume, initiated distinct early branching patterns, and exhibited significantly higher survival during the first two years. These results suggest a fusion effect on overall performance, supported by transcriptomic analysis showing that chimeric colonies frontload stress-associated pathways that promote greater tolerance during early development, further preparing them for environmental change. Together, these findings help explain why early allogeneic interactions may be less physiologically costly than previously assumed. Considered together, the results position S. pistillata as a powerful model system and support the idea that chimerism is a natural strategy for resilience and phenotypic variability among corals. Integrating chimerism into restoration activities may improve adaptive capabilities and enhance long-term restoration outcomes in a rapidly changing ocean.
543590c3: Reef restoration initiatives are under rapid development to counteract coral declines globally. Many restoration initiatives rely on ex situ sexual propagation of coral larvae and subsequent deployment of juvenile corals onto reefs. Such aquaculture-based efforts often utilise high-density settlement techniques to increase the chance of survival of deployed corals, which can result in chimeric fusion of coral spat. However, the fate of chimeric corals has not been investigated. Here, we undertook genome-wide SNP analyses to identify chimerism among triplicate biological replicate samples from two-year-old coral colonies derived from deployment of coral seeding units populated with aquaculture-propagated corals. We found that a significant proportion of surviving seeded colonies were chimeric, and the probability of chimerism was not related to initial settlement density. Conversely, chimerism was rare in wild-settled corals. Parentage analyses identified that chimeras formed between full siblings, half siblings, and unrelated genotypes, and, furthermore, four chimeric colonies contained both wild and seeded genotypes. Notably, chimeric colonies were slightly larger than clonal colonies, suggesting some potential fitness benefit. Given the high prevalence of chimeras resulting from coral seeding, it is critical to understand any consequences for fitness, and in this talk, we highlight some priorities for future research on chimeras in reef restoration initiatives.
a15b26d0: The unique bipartite life cycle of corals provides an early ontogenetic window for the natural occurrence of chimerism. Although common and described in nature, few studies have focused on chimerism aside from observation, and even fewer have explored the degree of associated fitness tradeoffs. This study generated coral chimeras derived from spat across varying levels of relatedness and subjected them to thermal stress, to investigate fusion likelihood and fitness implications among juveniles. Using controlled gametic crosses stained with inert dye (e.g., Nile Blue and Neutral Red), we measured the extent of fusion among larvae across degrees of relatedness (e.g., full siblings, half siblings, and unrelated) and parental identity (shared maternity versus shared paternity), to understand when fusion is likely to occur. Following settlement, juvenile chimeras and singletons were exposed to heat stress, with temperature ramped from 28℃ to 34℃ over four weeks, with the aim to induce mortality. We monitored growth and mortality of spat every two days to understand if chimeras retain a mosaic of traits (e.g., differential tolerance to temperature, and therefore partial mortality), and hence, if intracolonial genetic variation within chimeras allows intracolonial phenotypic variation. The findings of this study improve our understanding of chimerism and the implications of intracolonial genetic variability on chimeric fitness. Furthermore, the study informs the unit of selection for juvenile corals, providing a preliminary understanding of how targeted settlement may increase the diversity of traits within chimeric individuals to influence adaptive potential and survival.
4c39a01a: Interspecific hybridisation has the potential to increase genetic diversity and enhance adaptive capacity in corals, with some first generation (F1) hybrid corals exhibiting greater fitness relative to their purebred parental species. Yet the genomic consequences of coral hybridisation, including those that may lead to hybrid vigor, remain poorly characterized. Here we report haplotype-resolved, chromosome-level assemblies for five F1 coral hybrids (Acropora loripes (dame) × Acropora cf. kenti (sire)) generated using PacBio HiFi long read sequencing and Hi-C chromatin conformation data from adult coral tissue. Our reproducible workflow yields two phased haplotypes for each F1 hybrid, incorporating duplicate purging, decontamination, and quality assessment. The availability of a chromosomal-level assembly of A. loripes reference genome facilitated anchoring of each haplotype’s contigs into chromosomes, with further validation and error correction using Hi-C contact maps. The resulting assemblies show high contiguity and completeness, with consistent recovery of core cnidarian single-copy orthologues. These chromosomal-scale assemblies enable pangenome-based structural variant analysis, and long-read sequencing of F1 sperm provides complementary insight into the meiotic stability of hybrid genomes by enabling chromosome-wide assessments of aneuploidy, segregation fidelity, and large-scale chromosomal abnormalities, offering an initial characterization of meiotic behavior in hybrid Acropora corals.
554c5f11: Genetic diversity and connectivity are key determinants of coral adaptive capacity.  On the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) many broadcast spawning Acropora species have large and highly connected geographic ranges, potentially supporting high genetic diversity.  However, this is not always the case, especially if population substructure leads to isolation of genetically distinct lineages within a nominal species. Acropora kenti is an abundant and widely dispersed coral species on the GBR. It forms at least four genetic clusters, most of which have large geographic distributions (1000’s of kms). However, the lineage from Magnetic Island (Yunbenun) has a potentially restricted geographic range and may be vulnerable to genetic decline if gene flow from adjacent lineages is limited. Here, we demonstrate that although the Magnetic Island lineage of A. kenti dominates on reefs surrounding the island it is not found elsewhere. We performed a genetic assessment of the Magnetic Island lineage, revealing that its effective population size is low, placing it on a trajectory toward loss of genetic diversity and therefore reduced adaptive capacity.  Hydrodynamic modelling shows reduced opportunities for dispersal between Northern and Southern bays of the Island, leaving populations in the south of the island especially isolated and vulnerable to the effects of inbreeding. We consider the options for genetic interventions based on available data about natural gene flow both within Magnetic Island and between Magnetic Island and the adjacent lineage. Our talk highlights the importance of genetic work to identify threats to genetic diversity, even for species that appear to be widespread and highly connected.
112a7f72: The 2023–2024 global coral bleaching event severely impacted coral communities and reefs across the South Pacific of Costa Rica. Understanding coral species and genotype responses to extreme heat stress is critical to improving restoration resilience under accelerating climate change. Objective: To evaluate the effectiveness of a Coral Bleaching Contingency Plan (CBCP) implemented by Raising Coral Costa Rica in mitigating bleaching and mortality during the 2023–2024 thermal anomaly in Golfo Dulce. Methods: Ecological monitoring was conducted at two reef sites (Islotes and Punta Adela) using the PRONAMEC protocol, while survival and bleaching of transplanted colonies were assessed at five restoration sites (Platanares, Punta Estrella, Sándalo 1, Sándalo 2, and Punta Gallardo). Fragment survival data from 17 Pocillopora donors/genotypes were analyzed with generalized linear mixed models (GLMMs) to test site, time, and donor effects. Results: Coral bleaching was widespread and severe, with 100 % of colonies bleached and 90% mortality for Pavona and Pocillopora species. Porites maintained the highest live cover (up to 54 % at Punta Adela). Survival of outplanted colonies decreased from > 88 % in 2021–2022 to 13.6 % after the event. Among genotypes, survival differed significantly over time (Donor × Time, p < 0.001), with Pocillopora donors D20, D26, and D2 maintaining the highest percentages of survival. Site-level differences were significant: the reef star cluster at Punta Gallardo experienced 55 % survival of fragments, while the cluster at Platanares experienced total loss. Conclusions: The CBCP facilitated rapid response and survival monitoring during the global bleaching event. Results highlight the role of local environmental conditions and genotype-level variability in shaping restoration outcomes under thermal stress. Incorporating multiple sites, genotypes, and depth ranges can enhance restoration resilience during future extreme events. This is especially valuable for low-resource countries lacking in situ facilites or gene-banking capacity, where field-based strategies are essential.
b28433b8: Corals around the world are experiencing unprecedented levels of mortality leading to the potential extirpation of several coral species, particularly on Florida’s Coral Reef. Mote Marine Laboratory’s International Coral Gene Bank is a large-scale, land-based living coral biorepository that aims to preserve coral species and the remaining genetic diversity of coral populations. Corals held within the Gene Bank will support future research and facilitate the creation of  novel genetic combinations through ex situ assisted sexual reproduction, and utilize cryopreservation for non-synchronous coral spawning events. Currently, Mote’s Coral Gene Bank has the capacity to hold up to 15000 fragments (~3 cm diameter) or 500 adult-sized colonies (~20+ cm) within its existing infrastructure. This capacity allows for triplicate holding of each genet collected specifically for live banking, focusing initially on Florida sourced corals; a location already experiencing regional extinction of several species. To capitalize on the remaining available genetic diversity, the Gene Bank has identified nine ecoregions and four environmental delineations along Florida’s coastlines to utilize within a hierarchical collection plan. In total, 8-9 genotypes per region, 2 - 3 representatives per environment, will be collected, and held within the Gene Bank in triplicate long-term holding. The 2023 Florida summer heat wave underscored the need for a living coral biorepository, as Mote’s International Coral Gene Bank safeguarded over 200 irreplaceable genotypes of critically endangered Acropora palmata and A. cervicornis. Corals transferred before the heat stress had the highest survival, while those already pale or bleached suffered high mortality despite careful handling. This event highlights the urgency of proactive biobanking to protect vulnerable species as increasingly severe and frequent thermal events threaten Florida’s Coral Reef and reefs around the world. Managers, therefore, should prioritize living land-based coral biobanking as a critical conservation strategy to prevent species loss as environment conditions continue to deteriorate.
cbb45dde: Coral reefs are immersed in rich biological soundscapes that provide unique opportunities to investigate the behavior of soniferous organisms and ecosystem processes that are often difficult to observe using traditional visual methods. In particular, underwater soundscapes offer a powerful tool for tracking reef degradation and recovery driven by extreme weather events such as tropical storms. To examine how reef ecosystems respond to these climatic stressors, we established long-term passive acoustic monitoring stations across subtropical and tropical reefs in Taiwan, spanning shallow to mesophotic depths. In parallel, we mapped the spatial heterogeneity of reef soundscapes using GPS-equipped drifting recorders. This integrated approach enabled us to model the spatio-temporal dynamics of reef soundscapes and to identify reef areas exhibiting post-typhoon degradation. Data from Xiaoliuqiu, a coral reef island off southwestern Taiwan, show that crustacean snapping activity declined markedly following typhoons associated with intense wave energy and gradually recovered thereafter. Moreover, the spatial patterns of degradation were strongly linked to distinct typhoon tracks, which delivered extreme wave forces to different sides of the island. These results demonstrate that soundscape monitoring can effectively capture both the degradation and recovery processes of coral reef ecosystems and reveal their spatial dependence on storm exposure. Our findings highlight the value of ecoacoustic monitoring for improving reef vulnerability assessments and for prioritizing management actions in reefs subjected to repeated typhoon disturbances. Continued monitoring of reef soundscape dynamics will provide essential evidence to support climate-adapted conservation strategies and marine spatial planning.
a922cf0a: Rapid response to coral reef threats is vital for effectively documenting impacts to reefs, minimizing reef damage, and enhancing reef resilience.  The Hawai'i Division of Aquatic Resources (DAR) is the state agency responsible for managing Hawaiian coral reefs and responding to threats such as coral disease, coral bleaching, aquatic invasive species, coral predator outbreaks, and reef damage.  Although DAR monitors coral reefs throughout the state, DAR heavily relies on community reporting to rapidly identify threats to coral reefs.  Eyes of the Reef (EOR) Hawai’i is a community-based organization dedicated to training community members on identifying, documenting, and reporting coral reef threats in Hawai’i.  To enhance the reporting process, DAR and EOR collaborated to create a new web form reporting system using ArcGIS Survey123.  Reports automatically populate into an ArcGIS Dashboard that visualizes the location of each report with all associated survey data and media.  This reporting system allows DAR to efficiently collect information on the nature and scale of reef threats and to determine if a.) further information is needed to verify reports, b.) if delineation surveys are needed to determine the extent of reef threats and c.) if interventions are necessary.  EOR therefore serves as a vital part of the rapid response process for DAR by providing the information needed for timely decision making on whether intervention is needed to address threats to Hawaiian coral reefs.  DAR will continue to grow the EOR network by working with community groups to serve as EOR training hubs for how to identify, document, and report threats to coral reefs.  Over time, this will allow DAR to better manage and protect coral reefs by creating capacity for community reporting throughout Hawai’i and increasing the number of “eyes on the reef” to more rapidly detect and effectively respond to coral reef threats.
764e9ef1:
Coral restoration urgently requires fast, ecologically relevant, in-situ approaches to proactively identify thermotolerant colonies. The 2024 mass-bleaching event provided a rare opportunity to classify Acropora nasuta and Acropora hyacinthus in the field across multiple Mo’orea sites as resistant or sensitive, providing verified phenotypes for assay development. We compared three complementary heat-stress designs using clonal fragments: (i) a CBASS acute assay with four increasing temperature treatments; (ii) an ET25 acute assay targeting the temperature that induces a 25% decline in photosynthetic efficiency (Fv/Fm) relative to baseline; and (iii) a two-week medium-term exposure at +2°C above the local bleaching threshold. For each assay we quantified non-destructive photophysiology (Fv/Fm and standardized image-based bleaching score) and biochemical oxidative status (superoxide dismutase, catalase, total protein, total antioxidant capacity, and lipid peroxidation, LPO). We then tested the ability of individual marker and multi-marker combinations to discriminate fragments into resistant versus sensitive categories, using cross-validated models. Non-destructive photophysiology alone showed highly variable predictive power across assays: the two-week exposure reached 73% correct classification, whereas CBASS dropped to 53%, ET25 performed intermediately.
Taken together, the results show that Fv/Fm and bleaching scores alone offer limited power to correctly recover the resistance vs sensitive status documented in the field in 2024. Incorporating antioxidant enzymes levels, protein content, total antioxidant capacity, and LPO often improved accuracy across assays and species, highlighting their value for detecting physiological resilience not captured by photophysiology alone.
Overall, assay performance is strongly context-dependent and influenced by the suite of markers measured. Integrating additional physiological markers substantially enhances the predictive accuracy of heat-stress assays for identifying thermotolerant donors in restoration programs. This framework offers pragmatic, evidence-based guidance for selecting  thermotolerant donors and fragments for climate-smart restoration during future heatwaves.
6f9dfae9: As climate change accelerates coral reef decline, identifying resilient genotypes is critical for restoration success. This study evaluated twelve Colpophyllia natans genotypes under ocean warming (OW), ocean acidification (OA), and combined stressors (OAOW) for one month. Corals from three Florida Keys nurseries experienced four treatments: control (26.5°C, 500 ppm pCO₂), OA (26.5°C, 1100 ppm pCO₂), OW (31.5-33°C, 500 ppm pCO₂), and OAOW (31.5-33°C, 1100 ppm pCO₂). Coral health was assessed through tissue growth, photochemical efficiency (Fv/Fm), metabolism, and calcification rates. A multispectral algal photosynthesis profiler (MAPP) phenotyped algal symbionts, and Symbiodiniaceae communities were characterized using ITS2 sequencing. Results revealed significant negative effects of ocean warming on all metrics. OW and OAOW treatments substantially reduced tissue surface area, photosynthetic efficiency (Fv/Fm), photosynthesis, and calcification compared with controls. Significant genotypic variation in stress responses was documented, with genotypes CN11 and CN12 showing greatest susceptibility to warming. These genotypes were dominated by Breviolum algal symbionts, whereas most others harbored the more thermally tolerant Durusdinium. Conversely, genotypes CN83, CN73, CN21, and CN18, all collected from Key West Harbor seawalls, demonstrated greater physiological robustness. The MAPP analysis differentiated algal phenotypes across treatments, revealing that functional responses of symbiont types to OA and OW are distinct and interactive. Source nursery significantly influenced outcomes, with Looe Key in-water nursery corals experiencing greater tissue loss and Fv/Fm reductions under warming. This correlated with symbiont composition, as all Looe Key genotypes contained Breviolum, while land-based nursery corals hosted only Durusdinium. Notably, just one week of warming exposure was sufficient to detect significant reductions in calcification and photosynthesis, indicating rapid physiological responses to stress. These findings demonstrate substantial genotypic variation in climate resilience and underscore the importance of incorporating symbiont composition and genetic screening into coral restoration planning to maximize long-term success under future ocean conditions.
3d081878: The capacity for corals to resist and recover from environmental stress is vital for their survival under a rapidly changing climate. Effectively maintaining photosynthetic processes under heat stress is a key factor in determining stability of symbiosis between coral hosts and algal endosymbionts, along with recovery and survival outcomes after disturbance. Measured with novel chlorophyll fluorometers, informative photobiological metrics are often linked to coral thermal tolerance. However, photosynthetic resilience will vary across algal genera and coral hosts. Therefore, we evaluated if plasticity in five photobiological metrics served as predictors of thermal resistance and resilience across several reef-flat corals in Guam. Species representing distinct functional groups included Acropora pulchra (competitive), Pocillopora damicornis (weedy), and Pavona decussata (generalist) and encompassed a range of predicted thermal sensitivities. Thirty genets of each species were subjected to experimental chronic warming (3C above ambient temperature for 16 days) and the heated and control corals were monitored for an additional 26 days following heating. Thermal resistance (the retention of chlorophyll after heating) and resilience (the probability of survival throughout and following heat exposure) both varied across coral species. Photobiological metrics, including the maximum quantum yield of photosystem II (PSII), PSII functional absorption cross section, connectivity between PSII reaction centers, and PSII reoxidation rate constants, were measured at five timepoints (i.e. accumulated heat doses) and dose-responses were species-specific. The photophysiological dose-response is predicted to correspond with coral bleaching and mortality risks. Further, the most informative photobiological metric is expected to differ between coral species. This study evaluates the link between photosynthetic resilience and thermal tolerance with the aim of identifying effective photobiological predictors to select resilient corals for targeted restoration.
eadbc525: Galápagos corals in the Eastern Tropical Pacific inhabit marginal, suboptimal environments and experience frequent, intense thermal stress–increasingly common conditions for reefs under recurrent climate anomalies. Marginal populations may host resilient individuals, providing insight into climate-driven acclimatization and adaptation. In the Galápagos, coral community resilience has been highest in the far northern sites of Darwin and Wolf, and experimental thermal assays show that Pocillopora colonies from these sites are more heat- and cold-tolerant than colonies from the southwestern island of Isabela. To identify possible drivers of this variation, we sequenced the mitochondrial open reading frame (mtORF) to determine host haplotype, used actin-targeted quantitative PCR to assess symbiont community composition, and performed Tag-Seq to evaluate transcriptional responses to heat and cold stress in colonies from seven sites representing the thermal range experienced by corals across the archipelago. Pocillopora from resilient far northern sites were mtORF Haplotype 1 and hosted the highest relative abundances of stress-tolerant Durusdinium symbionts, while thermally sensitive colonies from Isabela were clonal Haplotype 3 individuals hosting exclusively stress-sensitive Cladocopium. Across all seven sites, host haplotype was the strongest predictor of thermal sensitivity. Preliminary gene expression analyses from Isabela revealed that corals induced the same core biochemical pathways under acute heat and cold stress. However, regulatory patterns diverged by stressor. Heat stress broadly activated molecular chaperones, antioxidants, and immunity and cell-death regulators while suppressing metabolic functions. Cold stress elicited widespread repression of these same pathways with targeted induction of genes stabilizing membranes, proteins, and cytoskeletal structures. Together, these results demonstrate that geographic variation in host lineage and symbiont identity, combined with conserved and stressor-specific molecular responses, underlies the observed differences in thermal tolerance across the archipelago. This work improves understanding of how corals persist in highly variable environments and provides a framework for predicting resilience in vulnerable reef systems worldwide.
129b302f: Conservation practitioners are contemplating ambitious restoration projects to restore reef ecosystems, including by futureproofing for larger heatwaves using corals that are more bleaching resistant. Bleaching resistance arises from the underlying genetics of the host, its symbiont, and their interactions, and they can acclimate to local conditions. Thus, infusing a reef with currently heat-resistant colonies might not have a durable effect if those colonies switch partners upon transplantation or the successful interaction between partners is environmentally dependent. When transplanting corals across reefs, local adaptation on the native reef may in fact lead to reduced success. Here we present the results of a large reciprocal transplant experiment using three species of Acropora to measure how local adaptation and acclimation in hosts and symbionts contribute to bleaching resistance. We collected nubbins from 240 individuals on three environmentally divergent reefs across the Palauan archipelago (total N=720) and reciprocally deployed them to transplant racks after measuring their bleaching resistance in acute heat stress tests. After one year, we re-assessed transplanted colonies for survival and growth. Overall we found little evidence for local adaptation for these three species on these three reefs. However, we found clear site-level effects. For example, growth was highest on the most wave exposed, shallow reef, where transplant success also trended higher.  Across all species and locations, higher symbiont density in heated nubbins of our experimental stress test was associated with higher transplant survival: the probability of survival increased if a colony exhibited a larger symbiont density in heated nubbins at the end of our heat stress experiment. In ongoing work, we will combine these phenotypic results with population-genomic datasets for both the hosts and their symbionts to begin isolating the role that host and symbiont genetic identity plays in determining phenotypic responses to warming conditions.
115b02cd: Losses in living coral cover due to a multitude of stressors have produced reefs on the brink of functional extinction, but some individuals have persisted. Seven experiments over seven years utilized similar methodological approaches to characterize resistance to ocean acidification and warming of five coral species critical for ecosystem recovery and prioritized for restoration: Acropora cervicornis, A. palmata, Montastraea cavernosa, Orbicella faveolata, and Pseudodiploria clivosa. During each two month long experiment, several genotypes within each species were exposed to four environmental conditions: control, high temperature, low pH, and combined high temperature & low pH. Phenotypic metrics to assess resilience included growth, photochemical efficiency, and metabolism. Using a meta-analysis approach, we combined experiments’ results to characterize responses of multiple species, genotypes, and replicate fragments to ocean warming and acidification in isolation and in concert after one month and two months of exposure. The combination of ocean warming and acidification had an additive, negative effect on the corals’ phenotypic responses, but the magnitude varied among species. We identified A. palmata and O. faveolata as more heat tolerant for most of the traits measured, but they were still susceptible to ocean acidification and the combined treatment. Our results support consideration of exposure duration in experimental designs, as there were differences in phenotypic responses after one month and two months exposure, both between and within species. Some species, particularly A. cervicornis, had more variation in phenotypic response among genets compared to others. Those genets that performed well under ocean warming experiments also had higher outplant survival after the 2023 severe bleaching on Florida’s coral reef. Species with larger genotype-phenotype diversity may have greater potential for more informed restoration plans via resilience screening. Our results support the need to prioritize immediate intervention responses for ocean warming in concert with ocean acidification for long-term restoration success.
fa89edd6: Globally, corals face an increased frequency of mass mortality events (MMEs) as populations experience repeated marine heatwaves which disrupt their obligate algal symbiosis. Despite greater occurrences of MMEs, the relative roles of the environment, host, and symbiont genetic variation in survival, subsequent recovery, and carry-over effects to the next generation remain unresolved. High-resolution temporal and spatial whole genome sequencing of corals before, after, and several years following an MME reveal that host genetics has an impact on bleaching and mortality and that selected alleles important for adaptation persist through the next generation, demonstrating rapid evolution in this coral population. Bleaching resistance and survival following the bleaching event were highly polygenic and allele frequency shifts show reef habitat specificity, emphasizing the spatial complexity of environmental selection and how it shapes population recovery following an MME. This study reveals how MMEs reshape the genomic landscape and the spatial and temporal distribution of genomic diversity within coral populations facing severe threats from global change.
6181cccd: The persistence of coral reefs increasingly depends on restoration approaches, such as assisted migration; however, the thermal physiology of target outplant corals is often unknown. Restoration outcomes could be improved by identifying holobiont traits that are associated with thermal tolerance. Active chlorophyll-a fluorescence based on fast-repetition-rate fluorometry captures dynamic changes in symbiont light harvesting, photochemistry, and photoprotection, allowing these traits to be integrated into comprehensive photochemical phenotypes, offering a ‘phenomic’ framework linking environmental history, stress response, and coral fitness. We used two marine protected areas in the Coral Coast of Fiji to evaluate how relocating ramets of Acropora humilis across different shallow-reef microhabitats influences their photochemical phenotype and how their photochemical traits relate to thermal tolerance and growth. Fluorescence phenotypes from corals living in a common garden nursery showed strong site-specific separation driven by differences in the maximum quantum yield of photosystem II (PSII) (Fv/Fm), PSII functional absorption cross-section (σPSII), and both photochemical and non-photochemical fluorescence quenching. Replicate ramets from 50 colonies were then transplanted from their common garden nurseries to two nearby back-reef lagoon sites for one year, followed by further fluorescence phenotyping. Corals were then subsequently subjected to an acute heat-stress assay to determine if transplantation led to possible differences in thermal response. Results from these experiments will be presented in the context of using rapidly assessed photochemical phenomic signatures to help inform short-term success in assisted migration.
db62e763: The capacity to project ecosystem dynamics under future climates is critical for conservation planning. Yet this task is challenged by uncertainties arising from limited data, simplification of complex ecological processes, and substantial biological and environmental variability. True variability, disentangled from uncertainty, can offer opportunities: for example, differences in vital rates (i.e., growth, survival, fecundity) can afford some individuals an advantage, while environmental heterogeneity can provide populations with spatial refuges. Here, we integrate vital rate data from ~10,000 coral colonies across environmental gradients on the Great Barrier Reef (GBR) into a metacommunity model to project coral dynamics to 2100. We explore variation in projections as a function of: (i) demographic vital rates, (ii) spatial context (site, reef and geographic region), and (iii) future climate scenarios (three Shared Socioeconomic Pathways, SSPs) informed by an ensemble of downscaled Global Climate Models (GCMs). All factors influenced coral dynamics, with much higher variation across projections in the near-term decades. Under each SSP, the largest source of variation stemmed from the GCMs, while spatial variation was also substantial, with the site scale contributing the highest amount, followed by the geographic region and individual reef identity.
