Ultimately, we show that while future coral dynamics remain uncertain, a clear reduction in heterogeneity by the century's end, across ecological scales and the ensemble of GCMs, suggests higher certainty in a major loss of coral cover under all but SSP1-2.6 (warming <2°C), even while accounting for natural adaption via selection of heat tolerance. Thus, achieving SSP1-2.6 or lower is essential to maintain opportunities for natural adaptation and ecological intervention, as reef systems will be more likely to retain individuals and areas, both within reefs and across larger regional scales, capable of persisting longer.
7a227e0f: Climate-driven environmental degradation poses a critical threat to coral reef ecosystems. While some individuals may adapt to changing environmental conditions, large portions of existing communities are likely to move to locate favourable conditions or perish under climate change. These responses inevitably lead to changes in community composition and community-level environmental tolerance limits over time. Despite the dynamic nature of ecological communities, ecosystem modelling studies incorporating climate hazard metrics commonly apply a fixed baseline to the metrics over time. In this study, we compare climate hazard metrics conventionally used in coral ecosystem modelling with emerging concepts that can incorporate the temporal dynamics of coral reef communities. We use a high-resolution atmosphere-ocean coupled climate model to dynamically downscale an ensemble of CMIP6 sea surface temperatures in Australian waters and project characteristics of extreme temperature events at different global warming levels. Conventional hazard metrics project significantly greater intensity and duration of extreme temperature events than dynamic metrics. Difference between the metrics is spatially variable, with the central and southern GBR and high-latitude reef locations along the east coast of Australia exhibiting greater discrepancies between the metrics than other Australian reef locations. An inferential threshold (e.g., a hazard category) amplifies the difference between the metrics. We highlight the complementarity of conventional and dynamic thresholds and showcase a portfolio approach to climate hazard modelling that can benefit multiple stakeholder groups.
32590fa9: The Bolinao–Anda Reef Complex (BARC) in the northwestern Philippines has undergone profound ecological shifts driven by the interplay of local and global stressors. Once dominated by branching and plating corals, the system is now characterized by stress-tolerant taxa such as Heliopora coerulea and turfing macroalgae. Chronic eutrophication from intensive mariculture and sedimentation from fish-farm effluents have reshaped benthic communities and constrained coral recruitment, while recurring thermal anomalies continue to erode recovery potential. Yet the mechanistic interactions among these stressors—how nutrient enrichment and sediment loading compound bleaching impacts and reshape coral–algal competition—remain poorly understood. Here, we develop a spatially explicit model, ReefMod-BARC, to examine how these drivers generate demographic and ecological cascades. By integrating satellite-derived nutrient and turbidity data, hydrodynamic connectivity, and heatwave-induced bleaching mortality, we simulate the combined influence of eutrophication, sedimentation, and thermal stress across representative BARC reefs. We test whether these stressors act synergistically to amplify coral decline, particularly near fish farms, and evaluate the resulting shifts in community structure. Our findings demonstrate possible future trajectories for BARC reefs and identify resilience thresholds that can guide mitigation strategies in this rapidly changing reefscape.
365b85f3:
Spatial models are powerful tools to identify optimal areas for marine protected areas (MPA) that balance ecological and fisheries targets. In Indo-Pacific coral reefs, MPAs are established to mitigate the over-exploitation of commercially targeted reef fishes, yet their effectiveness is threatened by climate-induced disturbances that are becoming more frequent and severe. For example, coral bleaching and tropical cyclones reduce live coral cover, degrade structural complexity, and alter food availability for fish populations. These changes can modify fish population dynamics by lowering habitat carrying capacity, restricting adult movement, and reducing juvenile recruitment success. This study used a spatially explicit, age-structured, discreet-time fisheries model to simulate climate-induced disturbances and evaluated their impacts across 1000 hypothetical reef patches. We tested how factors such as the type (biological vs physical), spatial scale (localised vs widespread), and temporal scale (immediate vs gradual) of disturbance can influence fish populations at their juvenile and adult stages. 

We found that all three factors significantly altered the prediction of fish biomass and catches after 100-year simulations, and models that ignore bottom-up effects of habitat quality degradation may risk overestimating long-term MPA benefits. In our model, biological disturbances such as coral bleaching reduces live coral cover but temporarily maintains structural complexity. This setting allows for patchy turf algal growth that can induce adult herbivore aggregation but still provide shelter for all juvenile fish. In contrast, cyclones cause immediate structural loss and generate rubble fields on their pathway. This physical damage negates potential short-term benefits seen after bleaching and can produce more pronounced declines in fish biomass. All in all, incorporating habitat quality dynamics into spatial models can improve predictions of how climate disturbances affect coral reef fish populations and improve MPA site selection for effective management.
e1ab6f24: Reefs on oceanic islands are among the least impacted marine ecosystems on the planet, still supporting trophic networks with abundant mid- and top-level predators. However, due to their unique characteristics, such as low species diversity, these systems are particularly vulnerable to fishing and climate-driven pressures. Understanding the flow of energy and matter within their food webs is essential for predicting how different stressors may affect key ecological processes in these environments. Here, we used time-series data to build an ecosystem model and predict the effects of ocean warming and fishing on fish biomass and consumption rates in the reef food web of the St. Paul’s Archipelago. This remote equatorial archipelago sits on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, off the northeastern coast of Brazil, and forms one of the most isolated reef systems in the Atlantic Ocean. Specifically, we asked: (1) How will warming affect primary and secondary consumers? and (2) How are these lower-level consumers impacted by the fishing of mesopredators? Our results indicate that fish biomass and consumption rate is expected to remain stable until approximately 2050, followed by a 50% and 34% decline, respectively, by the end of the century under the strongest warming scenario (SSP5). These results suggest that major warming-driven impacts may only become pronounced near the end of the century. In contrast, mesopredator fishing produced rapid and substantial changes in the reef ecosystem within the first years of the projections. These findings suggest that direct anthropogenic pressures such as fishing may trigger much faster ecosystem degradation and biodiversity loss than warming alone, while climate-driven changes, although significant, appear to unfold over longer timescales.
2f7f417c:
Climate-driven declines in coral calcification and the increasing prevalence of net erosional processes on reefs threaten their long-term persistence worldwide. This loss of healthy reef habitat is partly driven by ocean acidification, which reduces carbonate availability and weakens reef accretion potential. There is growing interest in interventions that directly modify seawater chemistry to support calcifiers. 
Ocean alkalinity enhancement has emerged as a potential marine carbon dioxide removal strategy, that has the co-benefit of buffering local carbonate chemistry, improving aragonite saturation state, and creating more favorable conditions for corals and other calcifiers. Although ocean alkalinity enhancement has been explored as a global carbon dioxide removal method, its impact on coral reefs and direct application within reef restoration remains largely untested. Ecological outcomes, scalability, and operational feasibility of alkalinity enhancement in real-world restoration settings remain poorly understood. 
At the same time, aquarists have manipulated alkalinity for decades to enhance coral growth in controlled systems. Recognizing the disconnect between aquarium practices, new experimental research, and implementation needs in restoration, we initiated a coordinated network of scientists, aquarists, and practitioners in Florida to evaluate alkalinity enhancement as a potential conservation tool. 
We synthesize the current state of alkalinity enhancement science for coral reefs, present baseline calcification and updated carbonate chemistry data from ongoing field measurements, and outline a research pathway developed through the newly established Florida Alkalinity Enhancement for Restoration Working Group. This framework identifies priority experiments, monitoring approaches, and decision points needed to determine when, where, and how alkalinity enhancement could responsibly support resilient reef restoration, while emphasizing the need for rigorous, collaborative science before any large-scale deployment.
This work outlines a coordinated research pathway for evaluating when, where, and how alkalinity enhancement could contribute to resilient reef restoration, while emphasizing the need for rigorous, collaborative science before deployment at scale.
f2e93de4: As climate change intensifies, mass coral bleaching events are occurring more frequent and with greater severity, accelerating global decline of coral reefs. Global conservation efforts are hindered by the high variability in post-bleaching outcomes across regions and among individual colonies. To date, limited research has examined how physical factors (such as geomorphology and hydrodynamics) interact with biological traits (such as coral physiology) to shape patterns of coral recovery. This study investigates how different environmental conditions and coral morphologies influence recovery post bleaching, and how they vary at the reef scale. Oceanographic instruments, in-water coral surveys, and micro-computed tomography (micro-CT) were used to examine the hydrodynamic regime and coral health at three geomorphologically distinct sites within One Tree Reef in the Great Barrier Reef, following the 2024 mass bleaching. Results show that reef geomorphology and water flow modulate seawater temperatures and strongly influence coral recovery, as the site with the greatest depth range (3–5 m) and highest flow had the highest number of healthy corals nine months post-bleaching. Between the two shallower sites (1–3 m), the one exposed to swell wave energy supported greater recovery, while the site with no wave energy experienced the highest mortality, despite similar flow velocities across both sites. Both simple and complex morphotypes benefited from greater hydrodynamic energy, whereas simple colonies were generally more resilient to heat stress with higher survival rates across sites. Preliminary Micro-CT findings showed that bleaching did not manifest in corals’ skeletal structure 9-month post heatwave, and that site-specific hydrodynamics were more significant in shaping corals’ skeletons. These findings can guide coral reef management by directing conservation efforts towards areas at higher risk of mortality, while prioritising restoration in regions with greater survival and recovery potential.
6168f91d: As global coral cover continues to decline under increasing heatwaves, reef restoration practitioners are pressed to find effective and scalable solutions to improve long-term coral survival. Moreover, there is added benefit to streamline scientific advances in coral resilience methods to be adopted and effective for local practitioners in remote areas. Critical to restoration success is integrating climate resilience, where one promising approach is the identification and incorporation of bleaching resistant coral individuals via thermal assays. Although some experiments have shown that the response to experimental bleaching aligns with natural bleaching stress, and that some bleaching tolerance is retained after nursery or outplanting stages, more careful exploration of these questions in multiple systems and species is needed. To determine the efficacy of bleaching assays and tolerance metrics in a restoration setting, using multiple coral species, we compared a four-day increasing heat pulse against 1) a slow two-week hold exposure, 2) retesting after one year, and 3) natural bleaching responses of nursery corals. We observed similar Individual coral responses between the short and long-term heat stress across multiple bleaching traits. Additionally, our pre-tested corals with the highest thermal tolerance maintained their heat resistance durability after one-year in a common-garden nursery and had higher survival following the 2024 bleaching event, compared to corals with the lowest tolerance. This is the first encouraging demonstration in a restoration setting that short-term heat pulse exposures can recapitulate ecologically relevant thermal stress and offers a rapid, accessible way to scale up stocking coral restoration nurseries for future climate resilience in restoration.
b43346d0:
Understanding how coral genotypes respond to thermal stress is central to designing climate-resilient restoration strategies. While substantial effort has gone into testing thermal tolerance, limited in-situ data exists to test whether genotypes identified as thermally tolerant actually perform better in restoration or field settings. We evaluated 44 genotypes of Acropora cervicornis with varying thermal tolerance, based on Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System (CBASS) data in 2020, to test whether heat tolerance phenotypes translate to differential outcomes once outplanted to the reef. In 2025, 10 genotypes with high thermal tolerance and 10 with low to average tolerance were selected for outplanting in Dry Tortugas National Park. Survival and growth of these ramets will be measured after 8 months to test for differential performance based on CBASS-measured heat tolerance. Data from Coral Restoration Foundation’s (CRF) Tavernier nursery will also be used, including through the 2023 mass bleaching event, to test whether survival and growth in nursery settings are consistent with performance on the reef. 

Additionally, evaluation of CBASS data, combined with CRF’s nursery performance records, revealed an unexpected pattern: several genotypes with high thermal tolerance had comparatively low biomass in the nursery, whereas some low-tolerance genotypes exhibited the highest biomass. This raised a key question for restoration planning: do low-tolerance genotypes persist because they survived the bleaching better than expected, or do they exhibit superior growth and survival under non-stress conditions? 

These analyses will provide a critical test of whether acute heat stress tolerance serves as a useful predictor of genet performance, as well as highlight the value of diversifying genetic portfolios across different metrics. Multi-metric approaches align with climate-resilient restoration strategies by improving our capacity to identify genotypes most likely to persist, recover, and contribute to reef function.
0d1b0c29: The ongoing fourth global coral bleaching event raises critical questions about coral resilience and reef conservation under accelerating climate change. In Saipan, Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands, the 2013–2017 global bleaching events caused an estimated 60% loss of shallow-water corals, with Acropora pulchra thickets among the most severely impacted. In 2019, 25 genotypes were collected from lagoon sites and propagated in an open-water nursery to support reef restoration. Given the survival history of these colonies, we hypothesized that the nursery stock represented thermally tolerant genotypes capable of enhancing the heat resistance of restored thickets when outplanted at scale. However, previous studies show mixed evidence that corals maintain enhanced thermal tolerance after prolonged nursery residence, and that thermal tolerance is often associated with trade-offs such as reduced growth and fecundity. To evaluate the thermal tolerance of nursery genotypes, in June 2024 we used the Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System (CBASS) to test 25 nursery genotypes of A. pulchra alongside 25 wild colonies sampled from the same lagoonal sites. Thermal tolerance was quantified using color reference cards and Pulse Amplitude Modulated (PAM) fluorometry. In May 2025, we established two common-garden plots, one in the lagoon backreef and one at the open-water nursery, and reciprocally transplanted 10 wild and 15 nursery genotypes previously tested with CBASS. This experiment was designed to evaluate potential trade-offs associated with elevated thermal tolerance. We monitored survival, tissue loss, paling, PAM fluorescence, and volumetric growth monthly, and measured symbiont density and skeletal density at the start and again after six months. Preliminary results indicate that the nursery genotypes exhibit higher thermal tolerance than the recovering wild populations and show minimal evidence of trade-offs linked to this tolerance. However, we observe signs of acclimation to nursery and wild environmental conditions, which influenced survival and growth trajectories across sites.
66b24e01: Developing methods to effectively identify and propagate resilient coral individuals will be key to the management of tropical coral reef ecosystems under future climate scenarios. By strategically selecting thermally tolerant corals for reef restoration via propagation and outplanting practitioners may sustain reef health under future ocean warming. While studies have evaluated whether relative coral thermotolerance is retained in novel environments, such as coral nurseries, whether the predicted thermotolerance matches real world performance in nursery environments remains untested. Consequently, we quantified the thermotolerance of 16 genotypes of three coral species (Acropora millepora, Pocillopora damicornis and Acropora loripes; n = 54 genotypes total) from three sites on the Northern Great Barrier Reef (GBR) using the Multi-Taxa Phenotyping (MTP) system, propagated these individuals in coral nursery across two sites, and quantified their respective performance during the 2023-24 global mass coral bleaching event. We evaluated the relationship between Effective Dose 50 (ED50) – the temperature at which Fq’/Fm’(max) (maximum photochemical efficiency) reached 50% of baseline performance – and quantitative changes in coral colour (Mean Intensity Grey (MIG)) from before heat stress over time in the two nursery environments. ED50 broadly aligned with nursery coral performance during real world heat stress. Higher ED50 values correlated with less coral paling (or increased darkening), however, there were environment-specific and species-specific trends. Top ranked P. damicornis individuals consistently outperformed bottom ranked corals across both sites, top ranked A. millepora outperformed bottom ranked corals over time at site A but exhibited similar performance at site B, and top ranked A. loripes only outperformed bottom ranked corals at site B but performed similarly or worse at site A. Our results highlight the potential for effectively identifying and propagating thermotolerant corals using the MTP system, but we emphasise the importance of considering site- and species- specific traits to maximise benefits.
20392da4: Recent work has increasingly used acute heat-stress experiments as standardized, rapid tools for identifying thermally tolerant corals. While these assays reduce the cost, time, and labor of coral screening, survival and potential growth trade-offs must still be evaluated to ensure successful reef restoration. In March 2023, the Coral Bleaching Automated Stress System (CBASS) was used to assess the thermal thresholds of Acropora hyacinthus in Tutuila, American Samoa in the form of upper thermal limits (ED50: the Effective Dose temperature at which Fv/Fm declines by 50% relative to initial values). Three A. hyacinthus populations with distinct ED50s were identified: Maloata (36.5 °C), Faga’alu (36.96 °C), and Fagasa (37.29 °C). In February 2025, these populations were re-assessed for ED50s. Five colonies from each population were also propagated and transplanted to a common garden at Faga’alu Reef to investigate survival and growth. The upper thermal limits of the three populations were not significantly different in 2025 (37.14 °C, 37.16 °C, and 36.88 °C for Maloata, Faga’alu, and Fagasa, respectively). After one month, survival also did not differ significantly among populations. Interestingly, the Faga’alu population exhibited the highest growth rate (0.23 ± 0.09 g/day), compared to Maloata (0.08 ± 0.03 g/day) and Fagasa (0.11 ± 0.08 g/day). The lack of significant differences in ED50s in 2025 may be due to the smaller sample sizes (n = 40 in 2023 vs. n = 10 in 2025 per population) or the possibility that colonies were still recovering from the 2024 marine heatwave. No trade-off between thermal tolerance and calcification was observed between the lowest (Maloata) and highest (Fagasa) ED50 populations. The higher growth observed in Faga’alu may reflect the buffering effect of rearing propagated corals in their source reef. This study highlights the importance of coral donor screening, selection, and fragment rearing in coral restoration practice.
f8c59ee7: Record-breaking seasonal temperature increases foreshadowed Caribbean-wide mass bleaching in 2024. Prior to peak bleaching, members of the Reef Response coral restoration program at the University of the Virgin Islands outplanted fragments of wild Acropora cervicornis colonies to deeper reefs in St. Thomas, U.S. Virgin Islands. Outplant sites were chosen based on observed wild populations of A. cervicornis. Along a single continuous reef slope, replicate cohorts of A. cervicornis were outplanted at 20ft, 85ft, 100ft, and 120ft. Maximum temperatures were comparable among sites throughout the bleaching event; however, the shallow site sustained elevated temperatures longer than the deep sites. There was variable bleaching and survival among depths and among genotypes. In general, wild colonies on their native reef and corals outplanted to the shallow site bleached more and had more mortality than those outplanted to deeper sites. However, among the deeper sites there were notable genotypic differences in survival, with the majority of the genotypes performing the best at the 85ft site. These patterns suggest that the shortened period of thermal stress on the deeper sites may create a bleaching refuge for A. cervicornis restoration populations, but that genotype can play a significant role in bleaching and survival. These results also demonstrate that deep outplanting can be an effective gene banking strategy to complement or replace ex situ populations, which can be extremely important for facilities that do not have the capacity for extensive land-based nurseries. The survivors of this study represent the majority of the in situ A. cervicornis genotypic diversity in the northern U.S. Virgin Islands after wild populations were decimated in the 2024 bleaching event.
08ab1c28: The year 2015, the warmest on record in West Hawai‘i, led to widespread, severe coral bleaching causing a ~50% loss of coral cover in the region. Projected ocean warming will cause severe coral bleaching similar to that experienced in 2015 on an annual basis by 2040 in West Hawai‘i. The ʻĀkoʻakoʻa Reef Restoration Program, a partnership between Arizona State University (ASU) and the Hawaiʻi Division of Aquatic Resources (DAR), is poised to improve reef-wide thermal stress outcomes by integrating resilient coral genotypes into coral reproduction efforts on the West coast of Hawaiʻi. As Pocillopora meandrina is prone to bleaching yet provides essential fish habitat and is readily bred, we identified resilient lineages through stress testing in the summer of 2025. We evaluated thermal tolerance of Pocillopora meandrina from 5 environmentally-distinct and community-prioritized sites from throughout West Hawaiʻi to assess local adaptation to past stress. Exposure of these corals to an ecologically-relevant warming regime produced a quantitative ranking of 58 genotypes of P. meandrina as well as assessments of genetic relatedness for a species with limited population genetics data. We also present preliminary data on the efficacy of coral stress-hardening using hypervariable temperature regimes to enhance coral temperature tolerance over measured thresholds. Symbiont and host traits were correlated with historical site environmental parameters (e.g., benthic rugosity and other habitat characteristics, subsurface groundwater discharge and other water quality characteristics) to determine whether genetic identity or site history is the strongest predictor of resilience. Stress-tested corals will be cultured and propagated at the Ridge-to-Reef Restoration Center and integrated into reproduction efforts conducted by ASU, potentially producing millions of thermally-tolerant coral larvae. DAR will use this data to develop guidelines for the integration of resilience interventions into restoration efforts in West Hawaiʻi.
dcfd8ada: To keep up with projected climate change, several assisted evolution interventions have been proposed to expedite adaptation in corals. These interventions include: 1) clonal fragmentation of thermally tolerant adult colonies, 2) selective breeding of adults, and 3) genetic engineering via genetic editing of new recruits (e.g., CRISPR-Cas9). However, little is understood regarding the efficacy of these interventions within natural populations, where size-dependent demography and genetic recombination with “wild” coral colonies may dampen adaptative responses in a myriad of ways. To explore the efficacy of these interventions, we use a genetically explicit individual-based simulation of corymbose corals (e.g., Acropora tenuis). Our simulation models 100s of loci conferring thermal tolerance in each individual, includes realistic size-dependent demography (e.g., size-dependent survival, growth, and fecundity), parental recombination, and is calibrated to observed population heritability estimates. We find that the scalability of some interventions such as clonal fragmentation may limit its utility in promoting climate resilience in corals. Gene editing provides the greatest benefit to coral populations, but the technology has yet to be developed. Finally, selective breeding may provide the greatest benefit for reefs at present, providing both increased thermal tolerance and being increasingly scalable. Our results aid to inform coral reef restoration efforts on a larger scale and highlight the differences in outcomes across assisted evolution interventions that would otherwise be assumed equivalent in many genetically implicit models of corals.
6b656c9e: Global warming is compromising the function and adaptive potential of coral populations worldwide. The ability of corals to withstand rising sea temperatures and associated mass bleaching will depend on their adaptive capacity, yet how natural selection can enhance heat tolerance amidst frequent marine heatwaves remains uncertain. We used a field-validated simulation model of coral populations, incorporating mechanisms of selection and heritability of thermal tolerance, to explore coral adaptation on Australia’s Great Barrier Reef under multiple warming scenarios. Our projections show rapid coral decline by mid-century under all emission scenarios, with further loss under the current trajectory of global warming. While keeping global warming below 2°C allows thermal adaptation to keep pace in the long term, management interventions may be required to promote recovery and persistence locally. We thus explored innovative interventions to enhance thermal adaptation on heavily degraded reefs by simulating coral seeding scenarios over decadal timescales. These included deploying aquaculture-produced coral settlers with enhanced heat tolerance but low diversity, and seeding wild-captured settlers from larval slicks, which are more diverse and adapted to their local thermal environments. We found limited benefits of coral seeding for thermal adaptation, with impacts mostly confined to the seeding locations and minimal spillover through larval connectivity. While culture-reared, thermally-enhanced corals may support coral persistence on severely degraded reefs, wild-captured larvae better preserve local diversity, particularly on reefs that have the capacity to adapt to their changing thermal environment. Our simulations show that management opportunities exist to promote adaptation in a range of thermal environments, but they also highlight that curbing global warming is essential for enabling thermal adaptation across larger spatial scales.
1bcb2241: Anthropogenic climate warming is driving thermal evolution across the tree of life, and coral populations are already exhibiting signs of selection for heatwave tolerance. Yet, the pace of ocean warming may soon exceed the pace of natural adaptation. Whether conservation strategies can effectively harness the adaptive potential of corals remains uncertain. To address this, we extended an eco-evolutionary meta-population model of corymbose Acropora at a remote West Pacific coral reef system to incorporate theoretical non-thermal disturbance regimes—such as storms, crown-of-thorns outbreaks, or destructive fishing or land-use practices—and simplified conservation actions. We asked how natural gradients of heatwave exposure across reef seascapes could be leveraged to enhance system-wide coral cover and evolution of heat tolerance. We simulated two management approaches—local protection against non-thermal disturbances and coral restoration via outplanting—and evaluated whether targeting heat exposed “hotspot” reefs, thermal refugia, or random reefs yields better ecological outcomes. Overall, any local reef management outperformed the counterfactual of no action, but network-wide benefits were modest. Targeting hotspots consistently produced the strongest gains in coral heat tolerance evolution for both protection and restoration. However, strategies diverged when considering coral cover: for protection, a focus on thermal refugia increased cover more effectively, but for restoration, the better spatial strategy depended on the time horizon and future warming. Under a higher warming scenario, prioritising refugia produced greater coral cover by the 2050s, whereas targeting hotspots generated larger gains by 2100, reflecting delayed benefits of accelerated heatwave tolerance evolution. Our findings highlight that the optimal spatial management strategies to support coral persistence and adaptation are inherently context-dependent, shaped in part by local heatwave exposure gradients and the severity of future warming.
0140df6d: The northern coast of New South Whales, Australia has many intertidal rock pools that host a small but very hardy group of corals. Environmental conditions including temperature and salinity can reach values comparable to the most extreme region where corals occur – the Persian Gulf. In this study, we describe corals living in rock pools along the Solitary Island Marine Park. Five pools were monitored over the course of a year. The temperature in these pools was extremely variable throughout the year (daily variation over 10 degrees Celsius) and significantly different from the surrounding sea water as these pools experience isolation from the sea during tidal cycles. Corals in these pools included species in the genera Micromussa, Paragoniastrea, Psammocora, Turbinaria, Homophyllia, Acanthastrea, and Acropora. The most common of these were monitored for growth (Micromussa, Turbinaria), fecundity (Micromussa, Paragoniastrea), and symbiont community composition (Micromussa, Paragoniastrea). We will discuss these results in the context of conspecifics living in submerged reefs in the Solitary Islands Marine Park and the insights these corals provide for extreme conditions that may be experienced by future corals.
e1a82426: Latitudinal reef systems in the Western Indian Ocean offer an important framework for examining how coral lineages respond to environmental variability and repeated thermal stress. The genus Stylophora spans tropical and subtropical habitats across Mauritius, Rodrigues, St Brandon, and the Saya de Malha and Nazareth Banks. The 2019 and 2024 marine heatwaves induced extensive bleaching and mortality in Stylophora populations, prompting investigation into species persistence, population connectivity, and the potential of higher-latitude and offshore reefs to function as thermal refugia. This study investigated the present-day distribution of Stylophora species across this regional gradient and evaluated how recent thermal events may have shaped their occurrence. Coral tissue samples collected across island and bank systems were analysed using three genetic markers: ITS2, mitochondrial ORF, and the putative control region. The control region was amplified using primers FNAD5.2deg and RCOI3deg, while the ORF ( primers FATP6.1, RORF) and ITS2 (primers ITSc2-5, R28S1) regions were amplified following established protocols originally applied to Pocillopora. Phylogenetic analyses revealed clear lineage structuring across sites. Stylophora madagascarensis (morph A) was identified in Mauritius and the Saya de Malha Bank, reflecting persistence across mid-latitude and remote offshore environments. Stylophora mordax (morph B) was present in Rodrigues and St. Brandon, consistent with long-term isolation among outer-island populations. Stylophora pistillata, previously reported from Mauritius, was absent from all samples, suggesting local extinction following consecutive bleaching events. These results demonstrate distinct latitudinal patterns in Stylophora lineages and highlight the impact of repeated thermal stress on regional species composition. Understanding these dynamics is essential for assessing the potential of higher-latitude and offshore reefs to act as refuges for thermally sensitive corals and for informing future conservation planning under accelerating climate-driven warming.
ce41018c: Environmentally adapted and cold-resistant symbiotic hard coral communities inhabiting the high-latitude Asia–Pacific marginal distribution zone are flourishing despite strong thermal seasonality and suboptimal light conditions. Jeju Island, Republic of Korea, represents a global hotspot of this transition, where temperate, subtropical, and tropical benthic organisms currently coexist. Over recent decades, rising sea surface temperatures have driven a regime shift characterized by rapid proliferation of subtropical hard corals accompanied by widespread declines of indigenous kelp forests and soft coral assemblages. Here, we integrate long-term field observations, in situ spatial competition experiments, experimental thermal physiology, and stable isotope–based trophic analysis to elucidate the mechanisms restructuring this marginal reef ecosystem. Long-term in situ experiments and observations reveal a clear trajectory of regime shift driven by spatial competition, in which hard corals consistently outcompete both macroalgae and soft corals, ultimately occupying most available substrate. Thermal performance experiments combined with SIBER (Stable Isotope Bayesian Ellipses in R) analyses demonstrate pronounced species-specific physiological niches and contrasting trophic strategies among key foundation species, indicating that trophic flexibility enhances persistence under high-latitude environmental constraints. Predictive modeling based on contemporary coral distributional expansion and long-term seawater temperature trends further validates and forecasts continued transitions toward coral-dominated benthic communities. Extreme thermal disturbances amplify these transitions. During the marine heatwave, shallow habitats experienced severe bleaching and high mortality under temperatures exceeding 29 °C, whereas deeper habitats remained with stable ~22 °C, and functioned as effective thermal refugia. Collectively, our results demonstrate that coral tropicalization in Jeju waters is progressing through tightly coupled poleward (boreal), downward (bathymetric), and trophic functional shifts driven by ocean warming, trophic flexibility, competitive dominance, and disturbance-mediated habitat restructuring. These findings improve forecasts of coral expansion, species turnover, and habitat shifts at the geographic limits of coral distributions.
60aa28c8: Global changes are rapidly reshaping reef ecosystems, by eroding coral cover and driving shifts in species distributions. Using space-for-time substitution approach and integrating multiple modeling and experimental frameworks we projected how corals, reef fish communities, and their trophic interactions will respond to future warming across the Western Atlantic. We estimated current and future probabilities of coral occurrence, cover, and bleaching along the Brazilian coast and found that current coral occurrence probabilities peak in tropical reefs but is projected to decline sharply in this region while expanding toward subtropical latitudes. These shifts threaten endemic and range-restricted species such as Mussismilia braziliensis and M. harttii, whereas more generalist taxa (Siderastrea, Porites, Millepora) are likely to expand southward. We further modeled climate-driven changes in trophic interactions of reef fishes between 34°N and 27°S in the Western Atlantic. Warming is expected to reduce key trophic processes (herbivory and invertivory) in tropical reefs, reinforcing algal dominance and weakening critical trophic pathways. In contrast, tropicalization of feeding intensity is expected primarily in the northern hemisphere (US East Coast), with tropical herbivores expanding poleward. A large-scale standardized experiment spanning 115° of latitude in the Americas corroborates these projections. Predators exert stronger consumption and greater impacts on prey biomass and assemblage structure in warmer tropical waters, providing robust evidence for temperature-dependent interaction strength. Finally, ecosystem modeling of a near-pristine atoll food web revealed substantial declines in biomass (1–44%), reduced trophic transfer efficiency, shorter biomass residence times, and increased dependence on pelagic subsidies under future warming. Collectively, these geographic comparisons show warming impacts on the population, community and ecosystem levels, with tropical reefs trending toward structural simplification and functional erosion, while subtropical reefs may serve as limited refugia. Anticipating these shifts is essential for designing ecological corridors, prioritizing conservation, and guiding adaptive management in a warming ocean.
d78be5f3: The climate crisis has far-reaching consequences for the health and integrity of ecosystems. In marine systems, warming causes shifts in species distributions and interactions, ultimately altering the structure and functioning of marine food webs and ecosystems. The response and sensitivity of reef fish organisms to climate change have commonly been assessed using temperature-based indices. However, the exclusive focus on temperature overlooks effects of other key environmental stressors affecting marine life (e.g, deoxygenation and freshening). Here, we introduce a new and integrative framework to quantify reef fish sensitivity to multiple climate-related stressors. Using a global dataset of over 3,200 reef species from Aquamaps, we estimate single and cumulative Environmental Safety Margins (ESMs) for temperature, oxygen, and salinity across reef habitats worldwide. We find ESMs and their underlying stressors to differ between marine ecoregions with generally unsafe conditions in lower latitudes, oceanic islands and estuarine systems. Furthermore, we demonstrate regional differences in the relative importance of environmental stressors, where temperature, oxygen limitation and low salinities constrain species and communities differently across marine provinces. Reef areas with higher safety margins are characterized by species with large body size, high trophic levels, late maturation and high fecundity. These results show that vulnerability is driven not by a single environmental factor but by a combination of stressors and species-specific adaptations shaped by historical and current oceanographic conditions. The ESMs approach provide a quantitative, global-scale assessment of climate risk, highlighting priority regions for conservation fulfilling the global target of 30% protection of marine and coastal areas by 2030.
ac114e9d:
The competitive balance between corals and macroalgae underpins the structure and functioning of reefs, and shifts in this balance are a defining feature of tropicalisation. This equilibrium is influenced by benthic-feeding guilds, whose foraging benefits one competitor over the other. Herbivores reduce macroalgae cover and can promote coral expansion, while corallivores weaken coral colonies and reduce their capacity to compete for space. Omnivores, which exert both herbivory pressure and sometimes feed on coral polyps, add further complexity to these dynamics. Despite their central roles, how these taxa, and the functions they perform, are reorganising under climate-driven tropicalisation remains poorly understood.
Here, we quantified key biological and abiotic drivers of herbivore, corallivore and omnivore abundance for multiple taxa (fishes, echinoderms, and molluscs) across 29 reefs along Japan’s tropical to temperate environmental gradient (~24-34.5 degrees North). Using visual census data from 2015 and 2023, we fitted generalised additive mixed models (GAMMs) to characterise nonlinear latitudinal abundance patterns and temporal changes and used model outputs to generate projections forecasting how benthic-feeding pressure may reorganise under future climate change. 
While the overall fish abundance has shifted poleward since 2015, this response is neither uniform between guilds nor representative of all taxa. Corallivorous fishes have increased broadly across the gradient but not shifted north. Herbivorous fishes have declined, particularly in the tropics, signalling redistribution of benthic-feeding pressure. Contrastingly, less mobile taxa such as molluscs exhibit comparatively stable patterns, highlighting differences in the pace and direction of change among groups. Future projections suggest poleward intensification of both grazing and corallivory, with temperate reefs likely to experience increasing pressure from multiple feeding guilds as ocean warming accelerates range expansion. Together, these findings demonstrate divergent and guild-specific trajectories rather than a uniform poleward shift, highlighting the need for multi-taxa, trait-based approaches to understand feeding pressure reorganisation with tropicalisation.
5f980fde: The Red Sea, long considered a climate refugium for corals, experienced its fourth mass bleaching event in 2024, providing an opportunity to assess reef resilience under extreme thermal stress. SHAMS conducted three consecutive surveys (pre-, peak-, and post-bleaching) across 130 reef sites, including 40 repeatedly monitored sites, quantifying bleaching extent with photo quadrats and colony-level severity along belt transects. Results revealed marked regional variability when expressed as a proportion of hard coral (HC). During peak surveys in September, the Central Red Sea (CRS) recorded the highest bleaching intensity, with ~69.5% of HC bleached, compared to ~47.8% in the Northern Red Sea (NRS). Post-bleaching surveys in November at common sites showed live coral cover and recent mortality of 50.5% and 30.1% in the CRS, versus 75.4% and 10.5% in the NRS. The Southern Red Sea (SRS) exhibited lower bleaching (~20%) early in the season, with earlier warming (May–June) and slower cooling, suggesting impacts began before monitoring. Colony-level analyses indicated that Pocillopora and Goniastrea were most affected in the CRS, while Montipora and Porites were heavily impacted in the NRS. Despite substantial bleaching in the NRS (~40% of colonies at peak), ~41% of colonies remained unbleached, and 46.8% had recovered by the final survey, while 21.5%, predominantly Pocillopora, Porites, and Montipora, experienced the highest mortality. These patterns highlight the vulnerability of key genera, reveal strong regional differences in bleaching susceptibility and recovery, and indicate that even historically resilient refugia may be challenged under prolonged marine heatwaves. The high recovery and low mortality in the NRS support its resilience potential but demonstrate that refugia mechanisms were partially breached, suggesting that future, longer heatwaves may further jeopardize reef condition. These findings underscore the need for region-specific monitoring and proactive management to sustain Red Sea coral reefs under increasing thermal stress.
77474286: The bacterial family Endozoicomonadaceae is globally prevalent in a diversity of marine animal hosts, with Endozoicomonas being the genus most consistently associated with cnidarians worldwide. Long considered key mutualists of corals due to their metabolic versatility, evidence of host co-diversification, and their frequent decline during stress, bleaching, and disease, Endozoicomonadaceae have become emblematic of coral symbiosis. Yet, definitive empirical evidence for their functional roles, interactions within the holobiont, and the breadth of their lifestyles remains limited. Emerging reports of parasitic and pathogenic behaviors in other marine hosts further challenge the prevailing assumption of mutualism and highlight the need to re-examine their roles in cnidarian biology. In this talk, we will provide an overview of the state-of-the-art of this rapidly developing field, and showcase recent functional studies aimed at unraveling the biology of this enigmatic and ubiquitous bacterial family.
f187d24d: Endozoicomonadaceae are widely associated with marine animals, particularly with invertebrate hosts, and are considered closely related to host health. In coral holobionts, this group is regarded as a key symbiont. Using a novel approach that integrates data from different 16S rRNA gene variable regions, we revealed the global distribution patterns of Endozoicomonas through a big-data analytical framework. From an ecological–evolutionary perspective, we investigated the phylosymbiosis and cophylogenetic patterns between Endozoicomonas and hosts from the order Scleractinia and the phylum Porifera. By quantifying the cophylogenetic signal strength for each detected Endozoicomonas–host pair, we found differences in signal intensity. This result implies the varying degrees of intimacy in their symbiotic relationships. Further genomic evolutionary analyses revealed that strains with smaller and more eroded genomes exhibited stronger cophylogenetic signals with their hosts. Besides, we isolated one Endozoicomonadaceae strain, SCSIO 12664, from the coral Pocillopora damicornis. This species is a dominant member of microbial aggregates within Pocillopora acuta tissues and is widely distributed in pocilloporid corals. The microscopy observation demonstrated that the inoculated strain SCSIO 12664 could colonize the actinopharynx, body wall, and mesenterial filaments of P. damicornis. The results of transcriptomic analysis revealed, at 25°C, corals inoculated with SCSIO 12664 showed downregulation of immune-related genes, upregulation of nutrient transport-related genes, and enhanced amino acid synthesis and protein turnover in symbiotic algae. Under 32°C, inoculation with SCSIO 12664 reduced the number of differentially expressed genes in both coral and symbiotic algae. In addition, the upregulated photosystem II-related genes and improved photosynthetic efficiency were observed in symbiotic algae. Moreover, in the bacterial communities associated with corals inoculated with SCSIO 12664, the relative abundance of Flammeovirgaceae was significantly decreased. At the same time, the stability of the bacterial co-occurrence network enhanced. These findings contribute to our understanding of the relationships between Endozoicomonadaceae and their animal hosts.
86fd24d3: Among the microbial partners associated with reef-building corals, bacteria of the family Endozoicomonadaceae are globally prevalent and frequently occur within coral tissues as dense clusters known as Coral-Associated Microbial Aggregates (CAMAs). Although Endozoicomonas associations can vary over the coral life cycle, current evidence from several coral species points to early larval stages as a key window for successful colonisation. Newly settled recruits also appear permissive to colonisation, with recent experimental supplementation of Acropora kenti with Endozoicomonas acroporae inducing CAMAs within host tissues. However, the precise timeframe and cellular mechanisms underlying Endozoicomonas colonisation and subsequent CAMA formation during early development remain unclear. Here, we inoculated Acropora kenti larvae with native Endozoicomonas acroporae across five key developmental stages from gastrula (36 h post-fertilisation) to early settled juveniles and monitored Endozoicomonas colonisation and CAMA formation using fluorescent in situ hybridisation (FISH). This detailed sampling approach enabled the selection of specific timepoints for transcriptomic analysis, comparing host responses to E. acroporae with those to the non-CAMA-forming Halomonas smyrnensis. In addition, 16S rRNA gene sequencing and FISH were performed in parallel pre- and post-spawning to characterise the bacterial composition of parent colonies and assess the potential impact of spawning on CAMAs within adult coral tissues. By resolving the temporal dynamics of Endozoicomonas colonisation in early coral life stages and characterising host gene expression responses during colonisation and CAMA formation, this study provides new insights into early-life host–microbe interactions in corals.
5e20ce56:
The microbiome of octocorals is frequently dominated by Endozoicomonadaceae. However, most studies rely on relative abundance data from metabarcoding and metagenomics and lack spatial resolution across coral body parts. Imaging-based evidence of microbial organization within the host and absolute abundance estimates of octocoral-associated bacteria remain largely unavailable.
Here, we quantify and visualize Endozoicomonadaceae symbionts and other key bacterial groups in the gorgonian Leptogorgia sarmentosa using qPCR, and fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), and resolve the co-phylogeny of dominant Endozoicomonadaceae phylotypes through 16S rRNA gene amplicon data and metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs). We developed new primers and FISH probes to detect Endozoicomonadaceae phylotypes of the Candidatus Gorgonimonas lineage, uniquely associated with temperate gorgonians.
Our results reveal substantial intraspecies variability in 16S rRNA gene copy numbers, with differences up to 2–3 orders of magnitude among host specimens. Specimens with the highest estimated bacterial abundances were enriched in uncultivated Gorgonimonas (1.98×10⁷ copies/µg DNA), whose gene copy numbers largely exceeded those of other Endozoicomonas phylotypes (4.24×10⁴ copies/µg DNA). In individuals depleted of their main Gorgonimonas symbiont, no other bacterial taxa filled this niche, including Mycoplasmataceae that appeared dominant in 16S rRNA gene relative abundance datasets. This highlights the limitations of compositional data for identifying dominant phylotypes.
FISH imaging showed Gorgonimonas cells forming host intracellular aggregates (~10 μm diameter) in polyps. These structures were absent in specimens with low estimates of Gorgonimonas abundance. MAGs indicate that these symbionts affiliate at the same-species level (ANI>95%) with L. sarmentosa-derived Gorgonimonas leptogorgiae MAGs sampled a decade earlier and at genus level (ANI>85%) with MAGs from Eunicella host species. Phylogenetic analyses reveal a distinct clade of Gorgonimonas across multiple Mediterranean and Northeast Atlantic gorgonians, suggesting strong phylosymbiosis.
Overall, our study identifies Gorgonimonas as intracellular, aggregate-forming symbionts of temperate gorgonians, while raising the possibility of a facultative association for the host.
a94af7c9: Endozoicomonas is the most consistently associated bacterial genus of Cnidaria, including corals and anemones. Functional -omics, their often-high abundances, and the loss of these enigmatic bacteria from the host tissues under host stress, suggest that Endozoicomonas may be coral mutualists. However, their functions within the holobiont remain yet to be determined. To elucidate metabolic interactions of Endozoicomonas in their symbiotic context, we leverage a newly established temperate photosymbiotic sea anemone model to employ a multipronged approach combining genetic transformation, experimental microbiome manipulation, and correlative high-resolution imaging. Using two genetically and morphologically distinct Endozoicomonas strains from the sea anemone Aiptasia couchii, we here elucidate their ecological niche in the intact symbiosis. First, we will identify their specific localization with FISH-imaging in freshly collected anemones. Then, we will harness fluorescently-tagged and 15N labelled isolates to quantitate and link their metabolic capabilities to their spatial context at (sub)cellular resolution using a correlative fluorescence microscopy-SEM-NanoSIMS imaging approach. Thereby, this approach promises to shed light on the metabolic interactions of two distinct Endozoicomonas strains, and ultimately their contribution to cnidarian holobiont functioning.
77920429: Corals engage in a nutritional symbiosis with Symbiodiniaceae, algal symbionts which are fundamental for coral nutrient cycling and, ultimately, reef formation. Beyond the coral-algae symbiosis, corals engage with a diverse suite of other microbial partners. Among these, Endozoicomonas is the most consistently associated bacterium of Cnidaria globally. Importantly, the metabolic interplay between Endozoicomonas and the algal symbionts is barely explored. Using the temperate Aiptasia couchii as a new sea anemone model system, we here set out to assess the molecular and metabolic interactions between the native symbionts Philozoon 3a1c (Symbiodiniaceae) and two Endozoicomonas strains (API-3A and API-4B) co-occurring in the same host. Intriguingly, differences in morphology, genomes, and relative abundances in the holobiont suggest distinct roles and perhaps even ecological niches of the two Endozoicomonas isolates. In co-cultures, the two Endozoicomonas strains API-3A and API-4B exhibited no antagonistic behaviour on marine agar at ambient (17°C) or elevated (30°C) temperature, but antagonistic effects were detected in suspension co-culture at 30°C. Analysis of gene expression and metabolite profiles will help identify the specific molecular and metabolic cross-talk underlying the behaviour of the two Endozoicomonas strains in the presence of each other. Building on these findings, ongoing algal-bacterial co-culture experiments will reveal whether and how each Endozoicomonas strain differentially interacts with Philozoon 3alc, and whether these interactions are modulated by temperature. This integrative approach will thereby provide fundamental insights into the functional roles of Endozoicomonas in cnidarian holobionts and help elucidate their functional diversity.
245066e2: Coral-associated bacteria are increasingly recognised as key players in holobiont function, yet the mechanistic bases of their contributions remain poorly understood. Members of the genus Endozoicomonas are globally abundant, frequently dominating the microbiomes of healthy corals, yet despite their prevalence and intimate association within host tissues, their contributions to coral physiology and stress responses remain largely unknown. Here, we provide evidence that coral-associated Endozoicomonas possess the capacity to biosynthesise and transform steroid-like compounds, including under thermal stress conditions. Using a combination of genomic prediction, culture-based assays, and metabolomic profiling, we show that multiple Endozoicomonas strains encode key enzymes involved in steroid biogenesis and modification pathways. In vitro incubations under both ambient and elevated temperatures revealed the production of steroid derivatives, with thermal stress enhancing the abundance of several putative hormone-like metabolites. Notably, Endozoicomonas was the only microbe capable of metabolising coral-derived testosterone, and under thermal stress converted ubiquitous cholesterol into testosterone and progesterone. These metabolites accumulated within the coral during heat stress, suggesting active microbial involvement in immune priming and stress mitigation. This work provides the first direct evidence linking Endozoicomonas metabolism to steroid production in the coral holobiont and establishes a new framework for understanding how bacterial symbionts may chemically mediate coral responses to environmental change. Further research into the signalling, immunological, and physiological consequences of these metabolites will help clarify the functional significance of Endozoicomonas during coral thermal stress.
56bdfb3f: Endozoicomonas bacteria regularly associate with reef-building corals, but the nature of the association and contribution to the coral holobiont functioning and health is still uncertain. Caribbean Porites astreoides corals are an ideal model system for study of this relationship; they consistently either harbor high (>50%) or low (<5%) relative abundances of Endozoicomonas bacteria compared to other members of their bacterial and archaeal microbiome. Here, we present the results of field studies examining P. astreoides-Endozoicomonas associations over time which employ a quantitative PCR assay specifically for the P. astreoides-Endozoicomonas (EC10) associates. In the United States Virgin Islands, we identified that bleached corals lacking algal symbionts harbored either high or low Endozoicomonas association patterns over three reef sites (n = 3 – 11 colonies each of high or low Endozoicomonas association per site). We tracked the growth of the colonies using photogrammetry and found that the majority of colonies recovered coloration within six months, but all lost surface area and volume over this time. This  demonstrates quantitatively that Endozoicomonas associate with bleached corals capable of recovery, but that corals without Endozoicomonas can also recover. Secondly, we conducted reciprocal transplant experiments with P. astreoides between shallow (10 m) and mesophotic (40 m) Cayman Island reefs (n = 10 colonies at each depth and replicated at two sites). Our results showed that mesophotic colonies gain Endozoicomonas when moved to the shallow reef, and that shallow colonies lose Endozoicomonas when moved to the mesophotic reef. These ecological observations suggest that algal symbiont load or bleaching level (health) is not needed to maintain Endozoicomonas association, and that mesophotic-related reef or host features likely prohibit Endoizoicomonas from residing within P. astreoides.
515cf529: Cnidarians, including anemones, jellyfish, and reef-building corals, form dynamic and complex symbioses with a range of microbial species, collectively creating a metaorganism. Interactions between these microbial taxa and their hosts directly impact the health, longevity and survival of the metaorganism. However, the functional roles of many microbial partners remains unknown, and it is unclear how their interactions support metaorganism metabolism. Here, we experimentally manipulated the composition of the Exaiptasia diaphana (Aiptasia) microbiome to investigate the roles of three bacteria species on host and symbiont metabolism. For our study, we chose two species of sulfur cycling bacteria (Labrenzia aggregata and Ruegeria mobilis) and a putative pathogen (Vibrio alginolyticus), all cultured from Aiptasia. Individual Aiptasia were first treated with antibiotics to remove their native microbiome. Then, we inoculated individual anemones with either single bacterial species or a synthetic community of all three species at equal densities. When the bacteria were introduced to the host as single inoculates, our qPCR data showed that they successfully colonized the host and were maintained throughout the experiment. However, when we inoculated individuals with all three species at once, we only observed successful colonization by L. aggregata and R. mobilis, which suggests these two species outcomplete the putative pathogen V. alginolyticus for space within the host. Full-length 16S rRNA sequencing data confirmed that the inoculation of the bacterial isolates directly shaped the overall structure and development of the microbiome as the Aiptasia were recolonized following the antibiotic disturbance. Metatranscriptomics results further revealed that the inoculation of each bacteria significantly altered key metabolic pathways. Together, our results demonstrate how L. aggregata and R. mobilis modulate Aiptasia metabolism and influence overall assembly of the Aiptasia microbiome, highlighting the functional importance of specific microbial partners in cnidarian metaorganisms.
699da059:
Lab cultures of cnidarian endophotosymbionts (Symbiodiniaceae) harbor distinct microbiomes but also host a number of bacterial genera that are consistently shared between most Symbiodiniaceae cultures. Labrenzia (Alphaproteobacteria) and Marinobacter (Gammaproteobacteria) have been consistently found in most Symbiodiniaceae cultures regardless of differences in isolation method, culture maintenance practices, growth media, and growth conditions. We hypothesize that Labrenzia and Marinobacter are common in Symbiodiniaceae cultures as they perform functions that support the physiology of Symbiodiniaceae such as promoting growth rate and nutrient acquisition. Assuming the microbiomes in Symbiodiniaceae lab cultures could act as host surrogates for Symbiodiniaceae, a better understanding of the ways in which bacteria support Symbiodiniaceae physiology in culture could fill knowledge gaps about the nutritional requirements of Symbiodiniaceae in hospite, thus informing coral conservation strategies. 

We investigated the core pangenomes of Symbiodiniaceae-derived Labrenzia and Marinobacter isolates to identify the genes that make Labrenzia and Marinobacter common and beneficial to Symbiodiniaceae cultures. We identified gene functions and metabolic pathways that are enriched in Labrenzia and Marinobacter sequenced from Symbiodiniaceae cultures compared to coral hosts, and other hosts/environments. Adaptations unique to symbiotic Symbiodiniaceae, free-living Symbiodiniaceae and coral hosts were found and could be further studied to inform our understanding of coral-symbiont-microbe interactions.
d799e2fd: Stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD) affects over 22 species of reef-building Caribbean corals and is characterized by rapid tissue degradation. Notably, Acropora cervicornis appears relatively resilient to SCTLD, while Montastraea cavernosa, Orbicella faveolata, and Porites astreoides tend to exhibit higher susceptibility. While previous work has underscored the importance of microbial communities in SCTLD progression, the functional contributions of the microbiome to SCTLD exposure remains less understood and its effect on the holobiont. In this study, we conducted a controlled SCTLD transmission experiment using these four coral species, selected for their contrasting susceptibility, ecological importance, and relevance to ongoing restoration efforts. We characterized microbial communities (16S rRNA gene abundance, n=102) and functional profiles (metatranscriptomes, n=77) from the coral host, algal symbiont, and microbiome across all species. Our goal was to better understand the molecular responses associated with SCTLD exposure, and how these responses may relate to resilience or susceptibility. We report evidence of direct and indirect mechanisms of virulence, the interactions of these processes across the coral holobiont, and holobiont dependence at mitigating disease transmission. Together, these observations contribute to advancing our knowledge on the molecular mechanisms of SCTLD and the role of the coral holobiont in mediating disease outcomes. By disentangling species-specific traits from disease-driven responses, this work offers a framework for exploring the complex interplay between host, symbiont, and microbial functions in coral disease dynamics.
2a9dfe21:
Viruses are powerful yet understudied drivers of coral reef ecosystem dynamics through the modulation of microbial communities within and around the coral holobiont. Here, we aim to investigate how viral activity and interactions with bacterial hosts are shaped by host abundance, organic matter composition, and upwelling-driven variability using metagenomics, proximity ligation, microscopy, metabolomics, and metatranscriptomics.
In reef waters of Curaçao, we reconstructed a virus-bacteria interaction network by integrating metagenomics and proximity-ligation data. This revealed 3013 virus-bacteria links and demonstrated that free viral particle abundance is decoupled from viral activity. Abundant bacterial hosts were enriched in lysogenic infections, where bacterial viruses (bacteriophages) integrate into host genomes and allow the bacterial host to persist. When combined with metabolomics, these patterns indicated that integration was more strongly associated with energy-rich organic compounds, suggesting that dissolved organic matter may influence viral infection strategies.
To determine whether these organic matter-driven patterns are observed within the coral holobiont, our ongoing work examines viral activity in 112 Pocillopora verrucosa and Pocillopora grandis colonies from upwelling and non-upwelling environments in Panama over a 15-month period. We hypothesize that corals exposed to seasonal upwelling, which brings nutrient-rich waters to the surface, will show elevated viral activity and a high frequency of viral integration. Metatranscriptomic analyses have identified 2090 putative viral genomes, 98% belonging to the realm Riboviria. We assess shifts in the activity of bacteriophages and RNA viruses infecting Symbiodiniaceae across periods of upwelling, aiming to understand how this seasonal variation modulates viral communities and the downstream effects these may have on coral health.
Together, our findings suggest that viral infection strategies are linked to host identity and abundance, associated with energy-rich dissolved organic matter, and may scale up to influence coral health by shaping coral holobiont members.
dea44c24:
Reef-building corals form coral holobionts through associations with diverse symbiotic microorganisms, including symbiotic dinoflagellates and prokaryotes. These microbial partners play crucial roles in coral growth and in the response to environmental stress. Although high-throughput sequencing has facilitated extensive characterization of coral-associated microbial communities worldwide, such studies remain limited in Japanese coastal regions. Moreover, coral-associated microbial assemblages are known to be highly sensitive to environmental stressors, such as thermal stress.
  In this study, we aimed to elucidate the microbial community structure of Acropora digitifera inhabiting the coastal waters of Japan and to determine how these communities relate to thermal stress conditions. We collected A. digitifera colonies along a latitudinal transect across Japan, as well as colonies exposed to different levels of thermal stress around Sesoko Island, Okinawa Prefecture. Metabarcoding analyses were conducted to assess microbial community composition and its relationship with seawater temperature parameters.
 Across Japan, Cladocopium was the dominant symbiotic dinoflagellate associated with A. digitifera, whereas Durusdinium was detected in regions where environmental pollution has been reported. Prokaryotic communities were dominated by the family Endozoicomonadaceae, consistent with previous global observations. Prokaryotic diversity tended to increase with rising seawater temperatures. No clear correlation was observed between latitude and prokaryotic community composition, and substantial variation in prokaryotic communities was detected even among colonies from the same sampling site.
 These results suggest that the structure of prokaryotic communities associated with A. digitifera in Japanese coastal waters is influenced by a combination of localized environmental factors, including microhabitat-scale thermal stress.
aee8d518: Sri Lanka’s coral reefs have experienced substantial degradation over recent decades due to the combined effects of natural disturbances and intensifying human pressures. Major bleaching events in 1998, 2016, 2020, and 2024 caused extensive coral mortality, while unsustainable fishing, sedimentation, coastal development, and land-based pollution continue to degrade reef structure and resilience. Despite these threats, relatively little is known about the diversity, functional capacity, and ecological roles of reef-associated microbial communities across the island’s coastal regions. This study provides the first integrated assessment of taxonomic and functional metagenomic variation in water-column microbial assemblages across seven coral reef sites in northern (Coral Island, Small Island, Pigeon Island, Kuchchaweli) and southern (Weligama, Polhena, Paravewella) Sri Lanka. Reef condition differed between these regions, with northern sites exhibiting higher coral cover and lower algae cover (37.2 ± 3.4% and 23.5 ± 5.7%, respectively) than southern sites (24.3 ± 3.2% and 21.3 ± 3.2%). Samples collected from the water column were subjected to whole-metagenome sequencing, functional gene profiling, GINI inequality metrics, and average copy-number contribution (ACC) analysis. Results showed strong biogeographic partitioning linked to reef condition. Northern reefs were dominated by oligotrophic taxa such as Candidatus Pelagibacter, Synechococcaceae, and Rhodobacteraceae, reflecting stable, low-nutrient conditions. In contrast, southern reefs showed elevated abundances of copiotrophic and disturbance-associated genera, including Alteromonas, Vibrio, and Pseudoalteromonas. Functional pathways enriched at southern sites included organic matter degradation, peptide transport, stress responses, and traits associated with rapid heterotrophic growth. ACC values, highest at Polhena and lowest at Paravewella, suggested reduced functional evenness under disturbance. Elevated GINI coefficients across all sites indicated conserved inequality in dominant pathways such as peptidoglycan biosynthesis and DNA competence systems. Overall, these findings reveal a distinct microbial gradient from oligotrophic northern reefs to more disturbed southern systems. This study establishes  baseline for microbial monitoring and offers new insights.
c1a4f44b: Coral-associated microbial communities play a key role in thermal tolerance, yet the extent to which microbiomes from more heat-resilient coral populations can enhance the resilience of vulnerable populations remains unclear. Ongoing assisted gene flow (AGF) efforts with elkhorn coral (Acropora palmata) from an unusually warm site (Tela, Honduras) in the Caribbean offers an ideal opportunity to test this. In 2024, A. palmata recruits were produced by crossing broodstock from Florida (USA) and Honduras to generate “Flonduran” (Florida x Honduras) offspring, alongside Florida x Florida controls. Flonduran juveniles were then reared in closed systems with either Florida corals or their Honduran parents to expose them to different microbiomes. After 18 months of growout, juveniles were fragmented into four replicates and exposed to a controlled heat-stress assay, together with fragments from the Honduran broodstock. Throughout the experiment, corals remained segregated into Floridian or Honduran microbiome tank systems to prevent microbial exchange. Photosynthetic efficiency was measured every 2–3 days to track bleaching progression, and microbiome samples (16S and 18S rRNA gene metabarcoding) collected at four key timepoints: pre-stress, post-ramp, peak bleaching, and post-recovery. During recovery, control and heated replicates were reciprocally transferred into the other microbiome systems to test how microbial communities are restructured during recovery when exposed to a different microbial pool. Assessing microbial diversity and plasticity is a critical component of ongoing AGF efforts and also helps us determine whether microbiomes from resilient populations can enhance resilience in less heat-tolerant populations, helping test whether microbiome-based interventions such as microbial seeding or probiotic applications are viable tools for improving restoration outcomes under climate change.
03ea75a8: Western Indian Ocean (WIO) was classified as vulnerable under the Red List of Ecosystems (RLE) due to climate change stress and overfishing. This status indicates widespread risk but masks country-level differences in vulnerability. Consequently, national-scale assessments are important to generate context-specific recommendations accounting for national priorities and transboundary ecological linkages.  To address this need, we applied the National Coral Reef Assessment (NCRA) using the IUCN RLE Criterion D, disruption of biotic processes, across Kenya, Tanzania and Mozambique. The initiative informs national assessment and the Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF) reporting, using a framework and indicators aligned with RLE: hard coral cover, algae-to-coral ratio (ACR), herbivores and piscivore. Data were compiled from the regional RLE and respective national reef monitoring networks. In each country, an inception workshop gathered stakeholder input on assessment design, followed by a validation workshop to refine results for national use. At national level, Kenyan coral reefs were assessed as endangered, Tanzania vulnerable and Mozambique critically endangered reflecting variation in coral-algae dynamics and herbivore-piscivore status. Mozambique exhibited extreme decline, underscoring widespread functional depletion and heightened vulnerability to collapse.  In Tanzania, hard corals and herbivores were least concerned, ACR vulnerable and groupers endangered, highlighting emerging ecological pressures. In Kenya, hard coral was near threatened, ACR and parrotfish vulnerable and groupers endangered, highlighting stress from overfishing and algal overgrowth. These patterns pinpoint areas of high biotic disruption and associated functional decline providing basis for targeted intervention. Building on this, stakeholders from each country outlined how the findings would inform management actions, update biodiversity targets, guide monitoring priorities and support GBF reporting.  This comparative synthesis underscores the value of national‐level assessments using a common framework, enabling benchmarking and informing locally tailored conservation priorities. By applying a globally standardized framework, these assessments enable consistent risk evaluation, support evidence-based management and inform GBF reporting.
f3b32b11: In the Anthropocene, a major threat to biodiversity is the ongoing rapid changes in climatic conditions. The Indian Ocean is already experiencing the fastest rate of ocean warming of any ocean, along with oxygen depletion and ocean acidification, severely impacting the biodiversity. As species are expected to move and track their optimal climate niche with changing climatic conditions, this raises an important question about the viability of current conservation plans and marine protected areas (MPAs) designed to protect coral reef habitats in the region.  Playing an important role in systematic conservation planning, spatial prioritization is an optimization tool to provide cost-effective solutions by incorporating ecological and socio-economic information.  By incorporating climate change projections in spatial prioritization, it is possible to optimize conservation plans to be resilient to future climate change. This approach is also proven beneficial in regions lacking robust ecological data for regional conservation and management applications. In this study, we showcase multiple climate metrics using data from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) and examine how environmental complexity can improve projections of climate hazards. We found spatial variation in the climate hazard across MPAs and highlighted the most at risk MPAs in the region. We applied these climate metrics to prioritization frameworks to identify areas across the Indian Ocean that can be recommended for long-term management. This study highlights the spatial variability in climate hazard across reef habitats and contemporary MPAs, emphasizing the importance of region-specific climate action plans, and presents an approach to investigate climate-smart MPAs in the Indian Ocean.
e92155f3:
Connectivity of coral populations is critical for coral recovery following disturbances such as bleaching events. Consequently, understanding reef connectivity and integrating source/sink dynamics into area and network-based management is key to effective management, particularly for remote and isolated reef systems. Remote reefs in the western and central Indian Ocean have been impacted by multiple bleaching events but have also exhibited high capacity for recovery, and therefore provide a model system to assess the mechanism by which connectivity between isolated reef systems influences resilience of coral populations. Here, we investigate the genomic population structure of a species of the important reef-building coral Acropora (Acropora aff. tenuis) across six locations in the Chagos, Seychelles, and Iles Glorieuses islands in the central-western Indian Ocean.
